title: THE FOX AND THE GRAPES
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A hungry fox spotted some delicious-looking bunches of grapes hanging from a vine that was stretched high up on a trellis. He tried everything he could to reach them, jumping as high as he possibly could. But no matter what he did, the grapes were always just out of reach. Eventually, the fox gave up and walked away, trying to look calm and unbothered. As he left, he said, "I thought those grapes were ripe, but now I see they're actually sour."

title: THE GOOSE THAT LAID THE GOLDEN EGGS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A man and his wife were lucky enough to own a goose that laid a golden egg every day. Even though this made them wealthy, they grew impatient and wanted to get rich even faster. They started to believe that the goose must be filled with gold inside, so they decided to kill it to get all the treasure at once. But when they cut the goose open, it was just like any other goose—there was no extra gold inside. In the end, they didn’t become instantly rich like they’d hoped, and now they no longer had the goose that brought them a golden egg each day.

Greed can cause you to lose everything.

title: THE CAT AND THE MICE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
Once there was a house that became completely overrun with mice. A cat heard about this and thought, "That sounds perfect for me." So she moved into the house and started catching the mice, eating them one by one. Eventually, the mice couldn't take it anymore. They decided to stay hidden inside their holes. "Well, that's inconvenient," the cat thought. "I guess the only way to get them out is to trick them." After thinking for a while, she climbed up the wall, hung herself upside down from a peg by her hind legs, and pretended to be dead. Soon, a mouse peeked out and saw the cat hanging there. "Ha!" the mouse called out, "You might think you're clever, madam, but you could hang there looking like a sack of flour and we still wouldn't come anywhere near you!"

If you're wise, you won't trust the innocent appearance of those who have already proven themselves to be dangerous.

title: THE MISCHIEVOUS DOG
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
There was once a dog who would snap at people and bite them for no reason at all, making life difficult for anyone who visited his owner’s house. To warn others, his owner tied a bell around his neck. The dog felt very proud of his new bell and strutted around, making it jingle with every step, completely satisfied with himself. But then, an old dog approached him and said, "You shouldn't act so proud, my friend. Do you really think the bell was given to you as a reward? Actually, it's a mark of shame."

Being notorious is often confused with being famous.

title: THE CHARCOAL-BURNER AND THE FULLER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Once there was a charcoal-burner who lived and worked all by himself. One day, a fuller moved into the neighborhood. The charcoal-burner got to know him and, since he seemed like a friendly guy, suggested, "Why don’t you come live with me? We could really get to know each other better, and besides, it would save us both some money." The fuller thanked him but said, "I appreciate the offer, but I can’t. All the things I work so hard to get clean and white would be covered in charcoal dust in no time at your place."

title: THE MICE IN COUNCIL
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Once, all the mice gathered for a meeting to talk about how they could protect themselves from the cat. Many suggestions were brought up and discussed. Then, an older, more experienced mouse stood up and said, "I think I've come up with a plan that could keep us safe from now on, if everyone agrees to it and we actually do it. We should tie a bell around the cat's neck so that the ringing will warn us whenever she's nearby." The other mice loved the idea and were ready to make it happen, but then an old mouse spoke up and said, "I agree that it's a wonderful idea, but can anyone tell me who is actually going to put the bell on the cat?"

title: THE BAT AND THE WEASELS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A bat once fell to the ground and got caught by a weasel, who was just about to kill and eat him. The bat quickly pleaded for his life. The weasel replied, 'I can't let you go—I’m an enemy of all birds.' The bat argued, 'But I’m not a bird. I’m actually a mouse.' The weasel took a closer look and said, 'Oh, you’re right—now that I see, you do look like a mouse.' So he let the bat go. Not long after, the bat found himself in the same predicament with another weasel. As before, he begged for his life. But this weasel said, 'I never let a mouse escape.' Thinking fast, the bat countered, 'But I’m not a mouse—I’m a bird!' The weasel examined him and said, 'Ah, so you are,' and he let the bat go, too.

Pay attention to your situation before you decide what to do.

title: THE DOG AND THE SOW
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Dog and a Sow were having an argument, each boasting that their own babies were the best compared to any other animal. Finally, the Sow said, "At least my babies can see when they're born, but yours come into the world blind."

title: THE FOX AND THE CROW
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A crow was perched on a tree branch, holding a piece of cheese in her beak. A fox spotted her and immediately started thinking about how he could get the cheese for himself. He walked over and stood right below the tree, looking up. "What a magnificent bird I see above me!" he exclaimed. "Your beauty is unmatched, and the color of your feathers is stunning. If your voice is as lovely as your appearance, you definitely deserve to be queen of all birds." The crow felt incredibly flattered. Eager to impress the fox and prove she could sing, she opened her beak and let out a loud caw. Of course, the cheese fell straight down. The fox quickly grabbed it and said, "You do have a voice, madam, but what you really need is some smarts."

title: THE HORSE AND THE GROOM
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Once there was a groom who spent hours every day brushing and grooming the horse he looked after. But each day, he secretly took some of the horse's oats and sold them for his own gain. Over time, the horse grew thinner and weaker, until one day it said to the groom, "If you really want me to look healthy and shiny, you should spend less time brushing me and more time feeding me."

title: THE WOLF AND THE LAMB
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Wolf came across a Lamb that had wandered away from its flock. The Wolf hesitated for a moment, feeling a little guilty about attacking such a defenseless animal without a good reason. So he started looking for an excuse and finally said, "Last year, you insulted me." The Lamb replied, "That can't be true, sir. I wasn't even born then." The Wolf shot back, "Well, you've been eating grass on my land." The Lamb answered, "That isn't possible; I've never even tasted grass." The Wolf pressed on, "Then you must have been drinking from my stream." The frightened Lamb said, "Honestly sir, the only thing I've ever had is my mother's milk." The Wolf shrugged and said, "Either way, I'm not skipping my dinner," and with that, he pounced on the Lamb and ate him up without another word.

title: THE PEACOCK AND THE CRANE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Peacock made fun of a Crane’s plain feathers. "Look at my gorgeous colors," she bragged. "Aren't my feathers much more beautiful than your dull ones?" The Crane replied, "I won’t deny, your feathers are definitely more colorful than mine. But when it comes to flying, I can soar high up into the clouds, while you’re stuck on the ground like a farmyard chicken."

title: THE CAT AND THE BIRDS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A cat heard that the birds in a big cage were feeling unwell. So, he dressed up as a doctor, took along some medical tools, and went to the cage door to check on the birds. "How are you all feeling?" he asked. But the birds replied from inside, keeping the door closed, "We'll be just fine—once you're gone."

A bad person can pretend to be someone else, but wise people won't be fooled.

title: THE SPENDTHRIFT AND THE SWALLOW
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A man who had spent all his money and now owned nothing but the clothes on his back spotted a swallow on a beautiful early spring day. He thought summer had arrived and believed he no longer needed his coat, so he sold it for whatever money he could get. But soon after, the weather turned cold, and a harsh frost set in, which ended up killing the poor swallow. When the man saw the swallow's lifeless body, he exclaimed, "Poor bird! Because of you, I’m now freezing out here too."

Just because you see one swallow doesn’t mean summer has arrived.

title: THE OLD WOMAN AND THE DOCTOR
author: Aesop
text_chunk: An old woman was nearly blind because of an eye disease. After seeing a doctor, she made a deal with him in front of witnesses: if he cured her, she would pay him a large fee, but if he failed, he wouldn’t get anything. The doctor started treating her, but each time he came to visit, he took something from her house. Finally, on his last visit, when she was supposedly cured, there was nothing left in the house. When the old woman saw her empty home, she refused to pay him. After she kept refusing, the doctor took her to court to get his money. In court, she was ready with her defense. She said, “Yes, we made that agreement. If he cured me, I’d pay him, and if he failed, he’d get nothing. Now he claims I’m cured, but I say I’m even more blind than before, and I can prove it. When my eyes were bad, I could at least see that my house had furniture and other things. But now, according to him, I’m cured, yet I can’t see anything at all in the house!”

title: THE MOON AND HER MOTHER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One day, the Moon asked her mother if she could make her a dress. Her mother answered, "How could I possibly do that? Your shape is always changing. Sometimes you’re a New Moon, sometimes you’re a Full Moon, and the rest of the time you’re something in between."

title: MERCURY AND THE WOODMAN
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
One day, a woodcutter was chopping down a tree by the side of a river when his axe slipped from his hands, flew through the air, and splashed into the water. Standing at the riverbank, he mourned his lost axe. Suddenly, Mercury appeared and asked why he was so upset. After hearing what happened, Mercury felt sorry for him and dove into the river. He came back up with a shining golden axe and asked the woodcutter if it was his. The woodcutter shook his head and said it wasn't. Mercury dived in again, this time bringing up a silver axe, and asked if that one belonged to him. 'No, that's not mine either,' the woodcutter replied. On his third dive, Mercury returned with the woodcutter's actual axe. Overjoyed, the woodcutter thanked Mercury, who was so impressed by his honesty that he also gave him the golden and silver axes. Later, when the woodcutter told his friends about his good fortune, one of them became jealous and decided to try his luck. He went to the riverbank, started chopping a tree, and purposefully let his axe fall into the water. Mercury appeared again and, after hearing about the 'lost' axe, dove in and brought up the golden axe, just as before. Without waiting to be asked, the jealous man immediately called out, 'That one's mine!' and reached for it. But Mercury, disgusted by his dishonesty, not only refused to give him the golden axe but also left his own axe at the bottom of the river.

Being honest is always the best choice.

title: THE ASS, THE FOX, AND THE LION
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A donkey and a fox decided to team up and search for food together. Not long after they set out, they spotted a lion approaching and both were terrified. Thinking quickly, the fox came up with a way to save himself. He walked confidently up to the lion and whispered, 'I'll make sure you catch the donkey easily without having to chase him, but you have to promise to let me go.' The lion agreed, so the fox went back to the donkey and cleverly led him right to a hidden pit that a hunter had dug to trap animals. The donkey fell straight in. Once the lion saw the donkey was caught and couldn't escape, he turned on the fox first, took care of him quickly, and then took his time feasting on the donkey.

If you betray your friends, you might end up destroying yourself, too.

title: THE LION AND THE MOUSE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Lion was fast asleep in his den when a Mouse scurried across his face and woke him up. Annoyed, the Lion caught the Mouse under his paw, ready to kill it. Terrified, the Mouse begged for its life. "Please let me go," pleaded the Mouse. "Someday, I’ll find a way to repay your kindness." The idea that such a tiny creature could ever help him made the Lion laugh out loud. Amused, he lifted his paw and set the Mouse free. But the Mouse did get its chance. Another day, the Lion was trapped in a net set by hunters. Hearing his angry roars, the Mouse ran over and started gnawing at the ropes with its sharp little teeth. Before long, the Lion was free. "See," said the Mouse, "you laughed when I said I’d repay you. Now you know—even a little Mouse can help a big Lion."

title: THE CROW AND THE PITCHER
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A thirsty crow came across a pitcher that had a little bit of water at the bottom. No matter how hard she tried, her beak couldn't quite reach the water, and it felt like she might die of thirst even though the solution was right in front of her. Then, she came up with a smart idea. She started dropping pebbles into the pitcher, and with every pebble, the water level rose a bit more. Eventually, the water reached the top, and the clever bird was finally able to drink and satisfy her thirst.

When you really need something, you'll find a way to make it happen.

title: THE BOYS AND THE FROGS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A group of playful boys were hanging out by the edge of a pond when they noticed some frogs swimming in the shallow water. Thinking it would be fun, they started throwing stones at the frogs, hitting and killing several of them. Finally, one of the frogs poked his head above the water and pleaded, "Please stop! What you see as a game is life or death for us."

title: THE NORTH WIND AND THE SUN
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
The North Wind and the Sun got into an argument, each boasting that they were stronger. Eventually, they decided to put their powers to the test using a traveler to see who could get him to take off his cloak first. The North Wind went first, summoning all his strength to blast fiercely at the man, almost ripping the cloak away. But the harder the wind blew, the tighter the traveler held his cloak around himself. Then it was the Sun’s turn. He started shining gently on the traveler, who soon loosened his cloak and let it hang loosely over his shoulders. As the Sun shone even brighter, the traveler quickly became so warm that he tossed aside his cloak and continued his journey feeling much lighter.

It’s better to win someone over with kindness than by using force.

title: THE MISTRESS AND HER SERVANTS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A hardworking, thrifty widow had two servants who she kept busy with lots of chores. She made sure they never slept in and always got them out of bed as soon as the rooster crowed. The servants really hated having to wake up so early, especially during the cold winter. They figured that if the rooster didn’t wake their mistress so early, they could enjoy more sleep. So, they decided to catch the rooster and kill it. But things didn’t go the way they had hoped. Without the rooster’s crow to wake her, their mistress woke them up even earlier—right in the middle of the night—and put them straight to work.

title: THE GOODS AND THE ILLS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Long ago, when the world was still young, both Good things and Bad things were part of people’s lives in equal measure. The Goods didn’t make life perfect, and the Ills couldn’t make it completely miserable either. But because of people’s foolish choices, the Bad things began to multiply and grow stronger, until it seemed like they would push the Good things out of the world entirely. The Goods, feeling overwhelmed, went up to heaven and complained to Jupiter about how they were being treated. They asked him to protect them from the Ills and to give them advice on how they should deal with humans. Jupiter agreed to protect them, and he decided that from then on, Goods shouldn’t visit people openly all at once, because that made them easy targets for the Ills. Instead, the Goods would come to people one at a time, quietly and rarely, and never when expected. That’s why the world is filled with Ills—because they come and go as they wish and are always close by—while Goods, unfortunately, only appear one at a time and have to make the long journey from heaven, so it’s rare to see them.

title: THE HARES AND THE FROGS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One day, all the hares gathered together, feeling miserable about how hard their lives were. Dangers seemed to be everywhere, and they didn’t feel strong or brave enough to protect themselves. It seemed like everyone—humans, dogs, birds, and wild animals—were after them, hunting and eating them every day. Tired of living in constant fear, they all decided they couldn’t take it anymore and thought the only way out was to end their lives. So, desperate and determined, they all raced toward a nearby pond, planning to jump in and drown. But as they reached the edge, a group of frogs sitting on the bank heard the pounding footsteps and, startled, all leaped into the water to hide from the hares. Seeing this, an older and wiser hare called out to the others, “Wait, friends! Don’t give up hope. Look, there are creatures even more afraid of us than we are of everything else! That means we aren’t the most timid ones after all.”

title: THE FOX AND THE STORK
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A fox once invited a stork over for dinner. When the stork arrived, the only thing to eat was a big, flat dish filled with soup. The fox happily lapped up the soup, but the stork, with her long beak, just couldn’t manage to get any of the tasty broth. Watching her struggle made the sly fox laugh to himself. But before long, the stork invited the fox over for dinner at her place. This time, she served the meal in a tall pitcher with a long, narrow neck. The stork had no trouble reaching in with her beak and enjoyed her meal, while the fox sat there, hungry and helpless, unable to get to the delicious food inside.

title: THE WOLF IN SHEEP'S CLOTHING
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Wolf decided to wear a disguise so he could attack a flock of sheep without being caught. He put on a sheepskin and quietly blended in with the sheep while they were out grazing. The shepherd had no idea anything was wrong, and when he brought the flock in for the night, the Wolf was locked in with them. But that same night, the shepherd wanted some meat for dinner and, by chance, grabbed the Wolf, mistaking him for a sheep, and killed him right there with his knife.

title: THE STAG IN THE OX-STALL
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A stag, chased out of his hiding place by a pack of hounds, ran into a farmyard looking for safety. He slipped into a stable filled with oxen and hid himself under a pile of hay in an empty stall, leaving only the tips of his antlers exposed. Soon, one of the oxen noticed and asked, "Why did you come in here? Don’t you realize how risky it is? The herdsmen could catch you at any moment!" The stag replied, "Please, just let me stay for now. When it gets dark, I’ll be able to leave unnoticed." Throughout the afternoon, several farmhands came in to tend to the cows, but none of them saw the stag hiding. Feeling relieved, the stag thanked the oxen for their help. "We wish you luck," said the same ox as before, "but you’re still in danger. If the farmer comes, you’ll be found for sure—he never misses a thing." And sure enough, the farmer arrived. He fussed about the care of his cattle and shouted, "These animals are starving! Give them more hay, and put down fresh bedding!" As he spoke, he grabbed an armful of hay from the very pile where the stag was hiding, instantly spotting him. The farmer called for his workers, and they quickly seized the stag and killed him for meat.

title: THE MILKMAID AND HER PAIL
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A farmer’s daughter had just finished milking the cows and was walking back to the dairy, carefully balancing a pail of milk on her head. As she strolled along, she began dreaming: "This milk will give me enough cream to make butter. I'll take the butter to the market and sell it for a good price. With that money, I'll buy some eggs, and when they hatch, I'll have a bunch of chickens. Soon, my poultry yard will be full! Then, I'll sell a few of the hens and use the money to buy myself a brand-new dress for the fair. All the young men will notice me in my new dress, but I’ll just toss my head and ignore them." She became so caught up in her thoughts that she actually tossed her head just like she imagined. In that instant, the pail tipped over, spilling all the milk and ending her dreams in a second.

Don’t count your chickens before they hatch.

title: THE DOLPHINS, THE WHALES, AND THE SPRAT
author: Aesop
text_chunk: The dolphins got into an argument with the whales, and soon enough, their disagreement turned into a fierce fight. The battle dragged on for a long time with no sign of stopping. Watching all this from nearby, a little sprat thought maybe he could help. He approached the fighting groups and tried to convince them to stop and make peace. But one of the dolphins looked at him with disdain and said, 'We’d rather keep fighting until we’re all gone than be talked into making up by a little sprat like you!'

title: THE FOX AND THE MONKEY
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A Fox and a Monkey were walking together down the road, arguing about which of them came from the nobler family. Their debate went on and on, until they reached a cemetery filled with monuments. There, the Monkey stopped, glanced around, and let out a heavy sigh. 'Why are you sighing?' asked the Fox. The Monkey pointed at the tombstones and said, 'All these monuments were built to honor my ancestors, who were all important people in their time.' The Fox was taken aback at first, but quickly replied, 'Go ahead, make up whatever stories you like. None of your ancestors are going to rise from their graves to prove you wrong.'

People tend to brag the most when there's no way for anyone to call them out on it.

title: THE ASS AND THE LAP-DOG
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Once there was a man who owned both a donkey and a little lapdog. The donkey lived in the stable, where he had plenty of oats and hay to eat, enjoying as good a life as any donkey could hope for. The lapdog, on the other hand, was his master's favorite—cuddled, pampered, and allowed to curl up in his owner's lap. Whenever the master went out to dinner, he would return with special treats for the little dog, who would rush to greet him at the door. The donkey, meanwhile, spent his days working hard—carting loads, grinding corn, and carrying heavy bags around the farm. Before long, he started to envy the lapdog's easy life, comparing his own hard work to the dog's carefree existence. One day, unable to hold in his jealousy any longer, the donkey broke free from his halter and trotted into the house just as his master was sitting down for dinner. He began prancing around, trying to copy the little dog's playful antics, but only managed to knock over the table and break the dishes with his large, awkward movements. Not stopping there, the donkey even tried to climb onto his master's lap, just like he had seen the dog do so many times. The servants, seeing how dangerous this was, rushed in with sticks and shouted, beating the poor donkey until they drove him back to the stable, bruised and battered. "Oh no," the donkey thought miserably, "I've brought all this upon myself. Why couldn't I just be content with my own honest work, instead of trying to copy the silly tricks of that useless little lapdog?"

title: THE FIR-TREE AND THE BRAMBLE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A Fir-tree was showing off to a Bramble. With a bit of a sneer, the Fir-tree said, "You poor thing, you’re not good for anything. Just look at me: I’m valuable in so many ways, especially when people build houses. They can’t do it without me." But the Bramble replied, "That’s easy for you to say now. Just wait until people come with axes and saws to cut you down. Then you’ll wish you were a Bramble instead of a Fir-tree."

It’s better to live with little and have peace of mind than to have riches that come with many worries.

title: THE FROGS' COMPLAINT AGAINST THE SUN
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Once, the Sun was getting ready to marry. When the Frogs heard about it, they panicked and started croaking loudly, filling the air with their cries. Jupiter, bothered by the noise, asked them why they were making such a racket. The Frogs answered, "The Sun makes life hard enough when he's single—his heat dries up our marshes. But what will happen to us if he gets married and has more Suns?"

title: THE DOG, THE COCK, AND THE FOX
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Dog and a Rooster became close friends and decided to go on a journey together. When night came, the Rooster flew up into the branches of a tree to sleep, while the Dog curled up inside the hollow trunk. At sunrise, the Rooster woke up and crowed as he always did. A Fox, hoping for an easy meal, heard him and came over, standing beneath the tree and asking the Rooster to come down. "I'd really love to meet someone with such a lovely voice," the Fox said. The Rooster replied, "Could you please wake up my porter sleeping at the base of the tree? He’ll open the door and let you in." So the Fox knocked on the trunk, only to have the Dog burst out and attack him.

title: THE GNAT AND THE BULL
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A gnat landed on one of the bull's horns and stayed there for quite a while. After it had rested enough and was ready to leave, the gnat asked the bull, "Is it okay if I go now?" The bull just glanced up and replied with little interest, "It makes no difference to me. I didn’t notice when you arrived, and I won’t notice when you leave."

Sometimes we think we matter more to others than we actually do.

title: THE BEAR AND THE TRAVELLERS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
Two travelers were walking down the road together when, out of nowhere, a bear suddenly appeared. One of them reacted quickly, rushing to a nearby tree, climbing up into the branches, and hiding. The other wasn’t as fast. With no way to escape, he fell to the ground and pretended to be dead. The bear walked over and sniffed all around him, but the man stayed completely still and held his breath, because people say that bears won’t touch a dead body. Thinking the man was dead, the bear lost interest and wandered away. Once the bear was gone, the traveler who had hidden in the tree climbed down and asked, 'What did the bear whisper to you when he put his mouth near your ear?' The other replied, 'He told me never again to travel with a friend who abandons you as soon as trouble appears.'

Hard times reveal who your true friends are.

title: THE SLAVE AND THE LION
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A slave, mistreated terribly by his master, decided to run away to escape his misery. To avoid getting caught, he fled into the desert. Hungry and looking for a place to hide, he came across a cave that seemed empty and went inside. But, unknown to him, it was actually a lion’s den. Almost right away, the lion returned, and the terrified slave thought he was doomed. But to his shock, the lion didn’t attack him. Instead, the lion gently approached, whining and lifting up its paw. When the man looked closer, he saw the paw was swollen and sore, with a big thorn stuck in the pad. Carefully, the slave pulled out the thorn and did his best to treat the wound. In time, the lion’s foot healed completely. Grateful beyond words, the lion became friends with the man, and they shared the cave together for a while. But eventually, the slave started to miss being around other people. He said goodbye to the lion and returned to town. Sadly, he was quickly recognized, captured, and dragged back in chains to his old master, who wanted to punish him as an example. He ordered that the slave be thrown to the wild beasts at the next public show in the arena. On the day of the spectacle, the wild animals were released, including a huge, fierce-looking lion. The slave was thrown in among them, and everyone expected the worst. But to everyone’s surprise, the lion glanced at the man, rushed over, and lay at his feet, showing joy and affection. It was the same lion the slave had helped! The crowd demanded mercy for the slave, moved by this display of gratitude and loyalty. Impressed, the governor set both the slave and the lion free.

title: THE FLEA AND THE MAN
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A man was bitten over and over by a flea until he couldn't take it anymore. Frustrated, he carefully searched for the tiny pest and managed to catch it at last. Holding it tightly between his fingers, he shouted, 'Who do you think you are, you miserable little creature, biting me like that?' The flea, terrified, squeaked out, 'Oh, please, sir! Let me go! Don't kill me! I'm so tiny—I can't really hurt you!' But the man just laughed and replied, 'I'm going to kill you right now. No matter how small the harm, anything bad needs to be gotten rid of.'

Don't waste your sympathy on someone who causes trouble.

title: THE BEE AND JUPITER
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A Queen Bee from Hymettus flew up to Mount Olympus carrying some fresh honey from her hive as a gift for Jupiter. Jupiter was so delighted with her present that he promised to grant her any wish she wanted. The Queen Bee thanked him and asked if he could give bees stingers, so they could protect themselves by harming anyone who tried to steal their honey. Jupiter wasn’t happy with this request, because he cared deeply for people. Still, since he had made a promise, he agreed: bees would get stingers. But there was a catch – whenever a bee stung someone, the stinger would stay behind in the wound, and the bee would die.

Bad intentions can come back to hurt you.

title: THE OAK AND THE REEDS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: An Oak tree growing along the riverbank was ripped from the ground by a fierce wind and thrown across the stream. As it landed among a group of Reeds by the water, the Oak asked them, "How is it that you, so fragile and thin, survived the storm, while I, with all my strength, was pulled up by my roots and tossed into the river?" The Reeds answered, "You were too stubborn and tried to fight against the wind, but it was stronger than you. We bend and go with each breeze, so the storm passed over us without harm."

title: THE BLIND MAN AND THE CUB
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
Once there was a blind man whose sense of touch was so sharp that he could tell what kind of animal he was holding just by feeling it. One day, someone handed him a young wolf cub and asked him to guess what it was. He carefully felt the cub for a while and then said, "Honestly, I can't tell if this is a wolf cub or a young fox. But I do know one thing—it definitely shouldn't be let into a sheep pen."

Bad habits and tendencies often show up early on.

title: THE BOY AND THE SNAILS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A farmer’s son was out searching for snails. When both his hands were full, he started building a fire to roast them, planning to eat them for a meal. As the flames grew stronger and the heat reached the snails, they slowly pulled back deeper into their shells, making the soft hissing noise they always made as they did. Hearing these sounds, the boy said, “You silly things. How can you make that noise as if you’re whistling, when your homes are burning?”

title: THE APES AND THE TWO TRAVELLERS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Two men were traveling together, one who always lied and one who always told the truth. During their journey, they arrived in the land of Apes. The King of the Apes heard they were there and ordered them to be brought before him. Wanting to impress them, the King sat grandly on his throne, with rows of Apes lined up proudly on both sides. When the travelers entered, the King asked what they thought of him as a ruler. The liar answered, “Your Majesty, anyone can see you are a powerful and noble king.” The King continued, “And what about my subjects?” The liar replied, “They are every bit as worthy as their royal master.” Pleased with this answer, the Ape King rewarded him with a generous gift. Thinking he’d get an even better reward for telling the truth, the honest man waited for his turn. When the King asked his opinion, the truthful man replied, “Well, I think you are a very fine ape, and your subjects are fine apes too.” This answer made the Ape King furious, and he ordered the honest traveler to be dragged away and clawed to death.

title: THE ASS AND HIS BURDENS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A peddler once owned a donkey. One day, he bought a large amount of salt and loaded as much as the donkey could carry. On their way home, the donkey tripped while crossing a stream and fell into the water. The salt dissolved, so when the donkey got back up, his load was much lighter. However, the peddler turned around, returned to town, and bought even more salt, packing it onto the donkey along with what was left. As soon as they reached another stream, the donkey lay down in the water on purpose, and just like before, he stood up with a much lighter load. But the peddler realized what the donkey was doing. This time, he went back to town and bought a bunch of sponges, loaded them onto the donkey's back, and set off again. When they came to the stream, the donkey laid down once more. But now, instead of being lighter, the soaked sponges became even heavier, and the donkey found he had a bigger burden to carry than before.

You might get away with something once, but if you keep pushing your luck, it can turn against you.

title: THE SHEPHERD'S BOY AND THE WOLF
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A shepherd boy was watching over his sheep near a village. Thinking it would be funny to trick the villagers, he decided to pretend that a wolf was attacking his flock. So he shouted, "Wolf! Wolf!" and when the villagers came running to help, he just laughed at them for believing him. He pulled this prank a few more times, and each time, when the villagers arrived, they realized there was no wolf—he had fooled them again. But then, one day, a real wolf appeared. The shepherd boy cried out, "Wolf! Wolf!" as loudly as he could, but the villagers were so tired of his false alarms that they ignored his pleas for help. The wolf was free to do as he pleased and killed sheep after sheep while the boy watched helplessly.

If you lie often, people won’t believe you, even when you’re telling the truth.

title: THE FOX AND THE GOAT
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A Fox accidentally fell into a well and couldn’t climb out. After a while, a thirsty Goat came by and noticed the Fox inside. Seeing him, the Goat called down, “Is the water any good?” The Fox replied, “Good? It’s the best water I’ve ever tasted in my life! You should come down and try it yourself.” The Goat, eager to drink, didn’t think about anything else and jumped right in. Once his thirst was quenched, he started looking for a way out—just like the Fox had earlier—but there was no escape. Then the Fox said, “I have an idea. If you stand up on your hind legs and push your front legs against the wall, I can climb onto your back. From there, I’ll step onto your horns and climb out. Once I’m out, I’ll help pull you up too.” The Goat agreed and did exactly as the Fox asked. The Fox climbed onto the Goat’s back, used his horns to get out of the well, and then just walked away without a second thought. The Goat shouted after him, reminding the Fox of his promise to help. But the Fox just turned and said, “If you used your head as much as you use your beard, you wouldn’t have jumped down here without making sure you could get out again.”

Think before you act.

title: THE FISHERMAN AND THE SPRAT
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A fisherman threw his net into the sea, and when he pulled it back in, there was only a single little sprat inside. The tiny fish pleaded, "Please, let me go back into the water. I'm just a small fish now, but if you let me grow, one day I'll be big and useful to you if you catch me again." But the fisherman shook his head and said, "No way. I’m going to keep you now that I have you. If I let you go, what are the chances I'd ever catch you again? Not likely!"

title: THE BOASTING TRAVELLER
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
Once, a man traveled to faraway places, and when he returned home, he shared amazing stories about his adventures abroad. He claimed that during his time in Rhodes, he took part in a jumping contest and made an incredible jump that nobody else could match. 'You can just go to Rhodes and ask anyone—everyone will tell you it’s true,' he insisted. But one person listening to his story said, 'If you're really as good at jumping as you say, we don't need to go all the way to Rhodes to prove it. Let’s pretend this place is Rhodes right now. Go ahead—show us your jump!'

Actions speak louder than words.

title: THE CRAB AND HIS MOTHER
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
One day, an old crab asked her son, "Why do you walk sideways like that? You should be walking straight." The young crab answered, "Why don't you show me how, Mom? I'll do it if you show me." The old crab tried to walk straight, but she just couldn't do it. That's when she realized it was silly to criticize her child for something she couldn't do herself.

Leading by example is better than just giving advice.

title: THE ASS AND HIS SHADOW
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A man once rented a donkey for a trip during the summer, with the donkey’s owner coming along to guide the animal. As the day grew hotter, they decided to take a break. The traveler wanted to rest in the donkey’s shade, but the owner, who also wanted to escape the sun, wouldn’t allow it. He argued that the man had only rented the donkey, not its shadow. The traveler insisted that renting the donkey meant he could use all of it while he had it. Their disagreement quickly turned into a heated argument, and soon they were fighting. Meanwhile, the donkey ran off and disappeared from sight.

title: THE FARMER AND HIS SONS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A farmer, sensing the end of his life was near and wanting to pass along something important, gathered his sons around him. He said, "My sons, I'm not going to be here much longer. I want you to know, there's hidden treasure buried in my vineyard. Dig for it, and you'll find it." After their father passed away, the sons grabbed shovels and forks and dug up the vineyard again and again, searching for the treasure they thought was buried there. Though they found nothing hidden in the ground, the vineyard, thanks to all their digging and hard work, ended up producing the biggest, healthiest crop they'd ever seen.

title: THE DOG AND THE COOK
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
Once, a wealthy man invited several of his friends and acquaintances over for a big dinner party. The man's dog thought this was the perfect chance to invite another dog—his friend—along as a guest. So he said, "My master is throwing a feast tonight, and there will be plenty of food. Come and have dinner with me." The invited dog arrived, and when he saw all the delicious food being prepared in the kitchen, he thought to himself, "Wow, this is my lucky day! I’m going to eat enough tonight to last me for two or three days." He wagged his tail enthusiastically, showing his friend how happy he was to be invited. But just then, the Cook noticed him. Annoyed to see a strange dog in the kitchen, the Cook grabbed him by his hind legs and tossed him out the window. The poor dog took a bad fall and hurried away, limping and howling miserably. Soon after, some other dogs saw him and asked, "So, how was the dinner?" He answered, "Oh, it was fantastic! The wine was so good, and I drank so much of it that I can’t even remember how I left the house!"

Be careful about accepting favors that come at someone else's expense.

title: THE MONKEY AS KING
author: Aesop
text_chunk: During a gathering of all the animals, the Monkey put on such an entertaining dance that everyone was delighted and they chose him as their King. The Fox, however, was not happy about the Monkey's new position. One day, the Fox came across a trap baited with a piece of meat. He brought the Monkey to the spot and said, "Look, Your Majesty, I found this delicious treat. I didn't take it for myself, because I thought it should be saved for you, our King. Would you like to have it?" The Monkey immediately went for the meat and got caught in the trap. He angrily blamed the Fox for leading him into danger, but the Fox just laughed and replied, "Oh Monkey, you say you're King of the Animals, but you still got fooled so easily!"

title: THE THIEVES AND THE COCK
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Some thieves broke into a house but couldn't find anything valuable to steal—except for a rooster. They grabbed the rooster and took him away. Later, as they were getting ready to make dinner, one of the thieves picked up the rooster and was about to wring his neck. The rooster pleaded, 'Please don't kill me! I'm actually very helpful because I wake up honest people every morning with my crowing.' But the thief snapped back, 'That's exactly why you're a problem for us. You make our lives harder by waking people up early, which makes it tougher for us to steal. Into the pot you go!'

title: THE FARMER AND FORTUNE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
One day, a farmer was out plowing his field when his plow struck something solid. To his amazement, he discovered a pot filled with gold coins buried in the earth. Overjoyed by his luck, he began making daily offerings at the shrine dedicated to the Goddess of the Earth, thanking her for his newfound fortune. However, Fortune was not happy with this. She appeared before the farmer and said, 'Why are you giving all the credit to Earth for the treasure I gave you? You never thought to thank me for your good luck; but if you ever lost what you’ve gained, I know you’d be quick to blame me, Fortune, for your misfortune.'

Always show your gratitude to those who truly deserve it.

title: JUPITER AND THE MONKEY
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Jupiter announced a contest to all the animals, promising a prize to whoever he judged had the most beautiful baby. Among them was the Monkey, who proudly carried her baby in her arms—a little one who was hairless, flat-nosed, and not exactly cute. When the gods caught sight of the baby, they couldn't stop laughing. But the Monkey hugged her baby tightly and said, "Jupiter can choose whomever he wants for the prize, but to me, my baby will always be the most beautiful of them all."

title: FATHER AND SONS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
There was once a man who had several sons, but they were always fighting and arguing with each other. No matter how hard he tried, he couldn’t get them to get along or live peacefully together. Wanting to teach them a lesson, he came up with an idea. He asked his sons to bring him a bundle of sticks. Then, he handed the whole bundle to each son and told them to try to break it over their knee. Each son tried, but none of them could snap the bundle. After that, the father untied the bundle and gave each son a single stick. Breaking just one stick was easy for all of them. So their father said, "See, boys, when you’re united, you’re stronger than any enemy. But if you’re always fighting and go your separate ways, you’ll be weak and easy for anyone to defeat."

Together, you are stronger.

title: THE LAMP
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A lamp, full of oil, glowed with a bright and steady light. Feeling proud, it started bragging that it shined even brighter than the sun. At that moment, a gust of wind came by and blew the lamp out. Someone struck a match, relit the lamp, and said, "You just focus on staying lit and don’t worry about the sun. Even the stars never need someone to relight them like you just did."

title: THE OWL AND THE BIRDS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: The Owl was known for being incredibly wise. Long ago, when the very first oak tree poked up through the forest floor, she gathered all the other birds and told them, “Look at this little tree. If you listen to me, you’ll get rid of it now while it’s still small. When it grows, mistletoe will appear on it, and people will use that to make sticky birdlime that’ll be used to trap you.” Later, when people first planted flax seeds, she warned them again. “Go and eat those seeds, because from flax, humans will make nets to catch you.” And when she spotted the first archer in the woods, she cautioned, “Watch out for him—he’s your most dangerous enemy. He’ll use your own feathers for his arrows and hunt you down.” But none of the birds paid her any attention; in fact, they thought she was a bit crazy and laughed at her. But, just as she predicted, everything happened exactly as she’d said. Eventually, the birds realized how wise the Owl was, and they greatly respected her. Now, whenever the Owl appears, all the birds try to listen in, hoping she’ll share advice that could help them. But these days, the Owl keeps quiet, just sitting by herself, thinking about how foolish her fellow birds had been.

title: THE ASS IN THE LION'S SKIN
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One day, a donkey came across a lion’s skin and decided to wear it like a costume. Disguised as a lion, he roamed around scaring everyone he met—people and animals alike—since they all thought he was a real lion and ran away at the sight of him. Thrilled by how well his trick was working, the donkey let out a loud bray to celebrate. But when a fox heard the sound, he immediately realized the truth and said, "Ah, so it’s you! I might have been scared, too, if I hadn’t heard your voice."

title: THE SHE-GOATS AND THEIR BEARDS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Jupiter gave beards to the female goats when they asked for them, but the male goats were really upset. They thought it was unfair and that it took away from their own importance. The male goats decided to send a group to complain to Jupiter. But Jupiter told them not to worry about it. 'It's just a bit of hair,' he explained. 'Let them have beards if they want. They'll never be as strong as you anyway.'

title: THE OLD LION
author: Aesop
text_chunk: An old Lion, too weak with age to hunt for his own food, decided to rely on cleverness instead. He went into a cave, lay down, and pretended to be sick. Whenever another animal came in to check on him, he would pounce and eat them. Many animals were caught this way until one day, a Fox came by. Sensing that something was off, the Fox stayed outside the cave and called out to ask how the Lion was feeling. The Lion replied that he was very ill and asked, 'But why are you standing out there? Please come inside.' The Fox answered, 'I might have come in, but I noticed that all the tracks lead into the cave and none come out.'

title: THE BOY BATHING
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A boy was swimming in a river when he accidentally went too far and found himself struggling in the deep water, in real danger of drowning. As he shouted for help, a man walking by on the road heard his cries and came to the riverbank. Instead of helping, the man started lecturing the boy for being careless enough to swim in deep water, not offering any help. "Please, sir," the boy begged, "help me first and scold me later!"

In an emergency, give help first—save advice for later.

title: THE QUACK FROG
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
One day, a Frog hopped out of the marsh and announced to everyone around that he was an expert doctor, skilled with medicines and able to cure any illness. As the crowd listened, a Fox spoke up and said, "You? A doctor? How can you claim to heal others when you can't even fix your own crooked legs and rough, wrinkled skin?"

Doctor, take care of your own health first.

title: THE SWOLLEN FOX
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A hungry fox came across some bread and meat inside a hollow tree, left there by some shepherds for when they returned. Excited by his discovery, the fox squeezed in through a small opening and gobbled up all the food. But after his feast, he was so stuffed that he couldn’t fit back through the hole. Stuck and miserable, he started whining and moaning about his bad luck. Soon, another fox wandered by and asked what was wrong. After hearing the story, the second fox said, "Well, looks like your only option is to stay put until you slim down to your old size. Then you’ll be able to get out just fine."

title: THE MOUSE, THE FROG, AND THE HAWK
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Mouse and a Frog became friends, even though they were quite different. The Mouse lived on land, while the Frog could live both on land and in the water. Wanting to always stay together, the Frog tied one of his legs to one of the Mouse’s legs with a piece of thread. Everything was fine while they stayed on dry ground. But when they reached the edge of a pond, the Frog leapt into the water, pulling the Mouse in with him, and started swimming and croaking happily. Unfortunately, the Mouse couldn’t swim, and soon drowned. His lifeless body floated in the water, still attached to the Frog. A Hawk flying by spotted the Mouse, swooped down, and grabbed him in its talons. Because the Frog was still tied to the Mouse, he couldn’t escape and was carried off and eaten by the Hawk along with his friend.

title: THE BOY AND THE NETTLES
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A boy was picking berries from a hedge when he accidentally brushed his hand against a nettle, which stung him sharply. Upset and in pain, he rushed to his mother, crying, "I barely even touched it, Mom!" His mother gently explained, "That's exactly why it stung you, my son. If you had grabbed it firmly, it wouldn't have hurt you at all."

title: THE PEASANT AND THE APPLE-TREE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A farmer had an apple tree in his garden that never produced fruit. Instead, it just gave shade to sparrows and grasshoppers, who would perch in its branches and chirp all day. Frustrated by the tree’s lack of fruit, the farmer decided to cut it down. He grabbed his axe and got ready to chop. When the sparrows and grasshoppers realized what was happening, they pleaded with him, saying, "If you cut down the tree, we’ll lose our home and you’ll no longer hear our cheerful songs while you work." But the farmer didn’t listen. He started chopping, and with just a few swings, he discovered the trunk was hollow. Inside, there was a hive filled with bees and a large supply of honey. Excited by this discovery, the farmer dropped his axe and said, "This old tree is worth keeping after all!"

Most people judge value by how useful something is.

title: THE JACKDAW AND THE PIGEONS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A jackdaw watched a group of pigeons in a farmyard and grew jealous of how well they were fed. Wanting a share of their food, he decided to disguise himself as one of them. He painted himself white from head to toe and joined the flock. As long as he stayed quiet, the pigeons never suspected he wasn't one of them. But one day, he made the mistake of chattering, and the pigeons immediately realized he was an impostor. They pecked him so harshly that he ran away to join other jackdaws. However, the jackdaws didn't recognize him in his white feathers and refused to let him eat with them, driving him away. In the end, he became a lonely wanderer because of his actions.

title: JUPITER AND THE TORTOISE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Jupiter was preparing to get married and decided to throw a huge party to celebrate, inviting all the animals. Every animal showed up except for the Tortoise, which really surprised Jupiter. The next time Jupiter saw the Tortoise, he asked why he hadn’t come to the party. The Tortoise simply answered, “I don’t really like going out. There’s no place like home.” Jupiter was so annoyed by this that he decided from then on, the Tortoise would have to carry his house on his back and never be able to leave home, even if he wanted to.

title: THE DOG IN THE MANGER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A dog was stretched out in a manger, lying right on top of the hay that had been placed there for the cows to eat. Whenever the cows came over and tried to get to their food, the dog snarled and snapped at them, refusing to let them have any. One of the cows turned to the others and said, "What a selfish animal—he can't eat the hay himself, but still won't let anyone else who can have it."

title: THE TWO BAGS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Every person carries two bags everywhere they go — one hanging in front and one on their back, and both are stuffed with faults. The bag in front is filled with the faults of other people, while the one behind holds their own faults. That’s why people so easily notice what’s wrong with others, but rarely see what’s wrong with themselves.

title: THE OXEN AND THE AXLETREES
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
Two oxen were pulling a wagon packed with a heavy load down the road. As they strained and pulled under the weight, the axles of the wagon started to creak and groan loudly. Annoyed, the oxen turned around and said, "Hey, what's all that noise about? We're the ones doing all the hard work!"

The ones who complain the most are often the ones who suffer the least.

title: THE BOY AND THE FILBERTS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A boy reached into a jar full of filberts and grabbed as many as he could possibly hold in his hand. But when he tried to pull his hand out, he realized he couldn’t because the neck of the jar was too narrow for his full fist to fit through. Not wanting to let go of any of the nuts but unable to free his hand, he started to cry. Seeing what was happening, a bystander said, “Hey there, don’t be so greedy. If you take just half as many, you’ll be able to get your hand out easily.”

Don’t try to do too much at once.

title: THE FROGS ASKING FOR A KING
author: Aesop
themes: be careful what you wish for
text_chunk: Once upon a time, the frogs grew restless and unhappy because they didn’t have anyone to lead them. So, they sent a group to ask Jupiter to give them a king. Amused by their silly request, Jupiter tossed a log into their pond and declared it their king. At first, the frogs were frightened by the huge splash and darted away to the deepest parts of the water. But soon, when they saw that the log didn’t move, they slowly came back to the surface. As they grew braver, they even began to sit on the log, eventually thinking it was ridiculous to have such a lifeless king. Feeling insulted, the frogs begged Jupiter again to give them a better and more fitting ruler, asking him to take away the lazy king. Frustrated by their constant complaints, Jupiter sent a stork to be their king instead. As soon as the stork arrived, he began catching and eating the frogs one after another.

title: THE OLIVE-TREE AND THE FIG-TREE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: An olive tree teased a fig tree about losing its leaves at a certain time of year. "Look at you," she said. "Every fall, you lose all your leaves and stand there bare until spring. But as you can see, I stay green and healthy all year long." But before long, a heavy snow began to fall. The snow piled up on the olive tree's leaves, weighing down her branches until they bent and broke. Meanwhile, the snowflakes slipped right through the fig tree's bare branches, and she stood strong, ready to grow fruit again when the seasons changed.

title: THE LION AND THE BOAR
author: Aesop
text_chunk: On a scorching summer day, both a lion and a boar arrived at a small spring at the exact same time, each eager for a drink. Almost instantly, they started arguing over who should have the first sip. Their argument quickly turned into a fierce fight as they attacked each other with all their strength. After a while, as they paused to catch their breath, they noticed some vultures perched on a nearby rock, clearly waiting for one of them to fall so they could swoop down and feast on the loser. Realizing this, the lion and the boar immediately calmed down and ended their quarrel. They agreed, saying, “It’s much smarter to be friends than to fight and end up as food for the vultures.”

title: THE WALNUT-TREE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A walnut tree stood by the side of the road and, every year, it produced a lot of nuts. But everyone who walked by would throw sticks and stones at its branches to knock the nuts down. The poor tree took a lot of damage because of this. "It’s not fair," the tree complained, "that the people who enjoy my nuts treat me so badly with their blows and insults."

title: THE MAN AND THE LION
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A man and a lion were traveling together, and along the way, they started bragging about their strength and bravery, each insisting they were stronger than the other. Their argument got more intense as they went, until they reached a crossroads where a statue showed a man overpowering a lion. The man pointed to it proudly and said, "See that? That proves we're stronger than you!" The lion replied, "Hold on a second, my friend. That’s just your perspective. If lions could make statues, you can bet that most would show the man underneath."

Every story has more than one side.

title: THE TORTOISE AND THE EAGLE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Tortoise, unhappy with his simple life on the ground and jealous of the birds flying freely above, asked an Eagle to teach him how to fly. The Eagle explained that trying to fly would be pointless since Tortoises weren't made with wings, but the Tortoise kept begging and promised the Eagle treasures if he would help. Believing it was all just about learning how to fly, the Tortoise wouldn't give up. Eventually, the Eagle agreed to do his best. He grabbed the Tortoise in his talons, soared high into the sky, and then let go. The poor Tortoise plunged straight down and was shattered on a rock below.

title: THE KID ON THE HOUSETOP
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A young goat climbed up onto the roof of a shed, drawn by the grass and other plants growing in the thatch. While munching away, he noticed a Wolf walking by below and started mocking him, knowing the Wolf couldn’t reach him up there. The Wolf just looked up and said, "I hear you, kid, but it’s not really you making fun of me—it’s the roof you’re standing on."

title: THE FOX WITHOUT A TAIL
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Once, a fox fell into a trap. After struggling for a while, he eventually managed to escape, but he lost his tail in the process. Feeling embarrassed about how he looked, he started to think life wouldn’t be worth living unless he could convince all the other foxes to get rid of their tails too, so no one would focus on his missing tail. He called a meeting with all the foxes and suggested they all cut off their tails. "They’re ugly anyway," he said, "and besides, they’re heavy and it’s annoying to always be dragging them around." But another fox spoke up, "My friend, if you hadn’t lost your own tail, you wouldn’t be so eager for us to get rid of ours."

title: THE VAIN JACKDAW
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Jupiter announced he was going to choose a king for all the birds and set a specific day for them to come before his throne. He would pick the most beautiful bird to be their ruler. Eager to look their best, all the birds gathered at a riverbank to wash and groom their feathers. The Jackdaw was there too, but he realized he had no chance of being chosen with his plain, scruffy feathers. So, after everyone else left, he gathered the most colorful feathers they'd dropped and stuck them onto himself until he looked brighter and fancier than any of the others. On the chosen day, all the birds presented themselves to Jupiter. As Jupiter was about to make the Jackdaw king, the other birds crowded around, pulled off the stolen feathers, and revealed the Jackdaw for who he really was.

title: THE TRAVELLER AND HIS DOG
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A traveler was getting ready to leave for a trip and saw his dog relaxing by the door, stretching. "Hey, why are you just lying there yawning? Come on, get moving! You're coming with me," he said. But the dog just wagged his tail calmly and replied, "I'm ready, master. It's actually you I'm waiting for."

title: THE SHIPWRECKED MAN AND THE SEA
author: Aesop
text_chunk: After being washed ashore, a shipwrecked man collapsed onto the sand, finally falling asleep after fighting the wild waves. When he woke up, he angrily blamed the sea for luring people in with its calm, inviting appearance, only to suddenly turn fierce and violent, destroying ships and drowning sailors. Then, the sea appeared before him as a woman and said, "Don't blame me, sailor. Blame the winds. By nature, I'm as calm and safe as land, but when the winds attack me with their wild gusts, they stir me into a raging storm that I can't control."

title: THE WILD BOAR AND THE FOX
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Deep in the forest, a Wild Boar was busy sharpening his tusks against the trunk of a tree. Just then, a Fox strolled by and noticed what the Boar was doing. Curious, the Fox asked, "Why are you bothering with that? There aren’t any hunters around today, and I don’t see any danger nearby." The Boar replied, "You’re right, my friend. But if I ever find myself in danger, I’ll need my tusks to be ready. I won’t have time to sharpen them when trouble arrives."

title: MERCURY AND THE SCULPTOR
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Mercury was really curious about what people thought of him, so he disguised himself as an ordinary man and visited a sculptor’s workshop. In the studio, there were lots of completed statues, all lined up and ready to be sold. Spotting a statue of Jupiter among them, Mercury asked the sculptor how much it cost. "One crown," the sculptor replied. "Really? That’s all?" Mercury chuckled. Then, pointing at a statue of Juno, he asked, "What about that one?" The sculptor answered, "That’s half a crown." Finally, Mercury nodded toward a statue of himself and asked, "And how much would you want for this one over there?" The sculptor shrugged and said, "Oh, I’ll toss that one in for free if you buy the other two."

title: THE FAWN AND HIS MOTHER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A mother deer said to her young fawn, who had grown strong and sturdy, "Son, you have a powerful body and strong antlers. I just don’t understand why you’re still so scared and always running from the hounds." Right then, they both heard the distant barking of a hunting pack. The mother added, "You stay here and don’t worry about me." With that, she dashed away as quickly as she could.

title: THE FOX AND THE LION
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Fox who had never seen a Lion before came across one for the first time and was so scared, he felt like he might die from fear. The second time they crossed paths, he was still nervous, but not nearly as terrified as before. By the time he saw the Lion for the third time, he wasn’t afraid at all. In fact, he walked right up to the Lion and started chatting like they’d been friends forever.

title: THE EAGLE AND HIS CAPTOR
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Once, a man caught an eagle. He clipped the eagle’s wings and put him in a henhouse with the chickens. The eagle sat sadly in the corner, looking miserable and lonely. Eventually, the man got tired of him and sold him to a neighbor. The neighbor took the eagle home and let his wings grow back. When the eagle could fly again, he caught a hare and brought it to his new owner as a gift. A fox saw what the eagle did and said, “Don’t waste your gifts on him! You should give your thanks to the man who caught you in the first place. Maybe then he won’t capture you and clip your wings again.”

title: THE BLACKSMITH AND HIS DOG
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A blacksmith had a little dog who loved to nap while his owner worked, only waking up when it was time to eat. One day, the blacksmith acted annoyed by this habit. After tossing the dog a bone like usual, he said, "Why should I keep a lazy dog like you around? When I'm busy hammering at the anvil, you just snooze. But the moment I pause to eat, you're wide awake, wagging your tail and waiting to be fed."

People who refuse to work don't deserve to be fed.

title: THE STAG AT THE POOL
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A thirsty stag came down to a pool for a drink. As he leaned over to sip the water, he caught sight of his reflection and couldn’t help but admire his large, impressive antlers. At the same time, though, he felt disappointed by how thin and weak his legs looked. While he was busy gazing at himself, a lion spotted him and attacked. The stag bolted, quickly outrunning the lion as long as he stayed in the open field without any trees. But as soon as he ran into the forest, his antlers got tangled in the branches. Trapped, he couldn’t escape, and the lion caught him. With his final breath, the stag cried out, "How unlucky I am! I looked down on my legs, which could have saved me, but I was proud of my horns, and now they’ve brought about my end."

We often fail to appreciate what’s truly valuable, focusing instead on things that matter less.

title: THE DOG AND THE SHADOW
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A dog was walking across a plank bridge over a stream, carrying a piece of meat in his mouth. As he looked down into the water, he saw his own reflection and mistook it for another dog with a much bigger piece of meat. Greedy for the larger portion, he dropped his own meat and jumped toward the "other dog" to snatch the bigger piece. But, of course, he ended up with nothing—the other dog was only his reflection, and his own meat drifted away with the current.

title: MERCURY AND THE TRADESMEN
author: Aesop
text_chunk: When Jupiter set out to create humans, he asked Mercury to mix up a batch of lies and add just a small amount to each person that would become a tradesman. So Mercury went down the list—giving a little bit to the candle maker, a bit to the grocer, some to the clothing seller, and so on. But when he finally got to the last one—the horse dealer—Mercury realized he still had plenty of the mixture left. So he poured all of it into the horse dealer. That’s why people say tradesmen might stretch the truth, but no one lies quite like a horse dealer.

title: THE MICE AND THE WEASELS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
There was a war between the Mice and the Weasels, and the Mice kept losing badly, with many of them getting caught and eaten by the Weasels. So they held a war council to figure things out. During the meeting, an old Mouse spoke up and said, "No wonder we keep losing—we don’t have any generals to lead us or set our battle plans." Taking his advice, they chose the largest Mice to serve as their leaders. To stand out from the rest, these new generals made special helmets decorated with long plumes of straw. Feeling confident, the leaders marched the Mice back into battle, but things ended up the same as before: they lost, and everyone ran for their lives, racing back to their holes. All the regular Mice managed to escape easily, but the leaders couldn’t fit into their holes because of their bulky hats and were quickly caught by the chasing Weasels.

Being important often comes with its own problems.

title: THE PEACOCK AND JUNO
author: Aesop
text_chunk: The Peacock was unhappy because he didn't have a beautiful singing voice like the nightingale did. So, he went to Juno and complained, saying, "Everyone envies the nightingale's song, but when I try to sing, everyone just laughs at me." Juno tried to comfort him. She said, "It's true that you can't sing like the nightingale, but look how beautiful you are! Your neck shines like an emerald, and your tail is a spectacular rainbow of colors." But the Peacock wasn't satisfied. He replied, "What good is being beautiful if I sound like this?" Juno, her tone growing a little stern, said, "Fate has given everyone their special gifts. You have beauty, the eagle has strength, the nightingale has song, and every other creature has its own talent. Yet, you are the only one not happy with what you have. Stop complaining. If I gave you what you want now, you'd just find something else to be unhappy about."

title: THE BEAR AND THE FOX
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
One day, a Bear was boasting about how generous and sophisticated he was compared to other animals. He even mentioned a saying that Bears never touch a dead body. Listening nearby, a Fox smiled and said, "You know, Bear, I wish you really would stick to eating only what's already dead and stop bothering the living when you're hungry."

A hypocrite only fools himself.

title: THE ASS AND THE OLD PEASANT
author: Aesop
text_chunk: An old peasant was sitting in a grassy meadow, watching his donkey as it grazed nearby. Suddenly, he noticed a group of armed men sneaking up on them. He jumped to his feet right away and pleaded with his donkey to run with him as quickly as possible. "Otherwise," he warned, "we’ll both be captured by the enemy." But the donkey just turned around lazily and said, "And if we are, do you really think they’ll make me carry heavier loads than I do now?" "No," replied the peasant. "In that case," said the donkey, "I don’t care if they take me, since things won’t be any worse for me."

title: THE OX AND THE FROG
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Two young frogs were playing by the edge of a pond when a large ox came down to drink. Without noticing, the ox stepped on one of the frogs, killing him instantly. When the mother frog realized her child was missing, she asked his brother where he had gone. "He's dead, Mother," said the little frog. "A huge creature with four legs came to our pond this morning and stepped on him, crushing him in the mud." "Huge, you say? Was it as big as this?" the mother frog asked, swelling up her body to look as large as she could. "Oh, much bigger than that," replied her son. The mother frog puffed herself up even more. "What about now? Was it as big as this?" she asked. "Yes, yes, Mother, it was much bigger!" insisted the little frog. Determined to compete, the mother frog kept puffing herself up until she was almost round like a ball. "Was it as big as..." she started to say, but before she could finish, she burst.

title: THE MAN AND THE IMAGE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A poor man owned a small wooden statue of a god, and every day he would pray to it, asking for wealth. He kept this up for a long time, but he stayed just as poor as ever. One day, frustrated and fed up, he grabbed the statue and threw it as hard as he could against the wall. The statue's head split open from the impact, and a pile of gold coins spilled onto the floor. The man quickly gathered up the coins, saying, "You old trickster! When I respected you, you did nothing for me. But the moment I insult and smash you, you make me rich!"

title: HERCULES AND THE WAGGONER
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A wagon driver was steering his team of horses down a muddy road, his wagon loaded with goods. Suddenly, the wheels got stuck deep in the thick mud, and no matter how much the horses pulled, they just couldn’t budge it. The driver stood by, feeling helpless, and kept calling out loudly for Hercules to help him. Suddenly, Hercules appeared and said, "Try pushing the wheel yourself and encourage your horses to pull harder. Then, if you need help, you can call on me. But if you aren’t willing to do anything to help yourself, you can’t expect me—or anyone else—to come to your rescue."

Heaven helps those who help themselves.

title: THE POMEGRANATE, THE APPLE-TREE, AND THE BRAMBLE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A pomegranate tree and an apple tree were arguing over whose fruit was better. Each one insisted that theirs was superior, and soon, the argument heated up and a real fight was about to break out. Just then, a bramble bush stuck its prickly head out from the nearby hedge and called out, 'Alright, that's enough, you two. There's no need to argue.'

title: THE LION, THE BEAR, AND THE FOX
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A lion and a bear both grabbed a young goat at the exact same moment, and instantly started to fight over who would keep it. The struggle was intense and lasted a long time, until eventually they were so worn out that they collapsed on the ground, covered in wounds and struggling to catch their breath. Meanwhile, a fox had been sneaking around, watching the whole fight from a distance. When the lion and the bear were too weak to move, the fox quietly darted in, snatched up the kid, and took off. As the lion and the bear watched helplessly, one sighed and said, "We've been beating each other up this whole time, and in the end, the only one who gained anything was the fox!"

title: THE BLACKAMOOR
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A man once bought an Ethiopian slave, who had dark skin just like all Ethiopians. However, the new owner believed his skin was only dark because his previous master hadn’t taken good care of him. He thought that all the man needed was a thorough wash. So, he grabbed lots of soap and hot water and scrubbed the man vigorously. But no matter how hard he tried, the man’s skin stayed just as black as before, and the poor slave nearly fell gravely ill from the cold after all that washing.

title: THE TWO SOLDIERS AND THE ROBBER
author: Aesop
themes: the difference between words and actions
text_chunk: Two soldiers were walking together when suddenly a robber attacked them. One soldier took off, running for his life, while the other stood his ground and fought fiercely with his sword. The robber, finding the fight too tough, decided to run away and left the brave soldier alone. Once the danger had passed, the cowardly soldier returned, waving his weapon and shouting loudly, "Where is he? Let me at him and I'll show him who he's dealing with!" The brave soldier looked at him and said, "You're a bit late for that. I just wish you had helped me a moment ago, even if you'd only shouted some encouragement. That would have helped, if I could have believed you meant it. But now, put your sword away—there's no use for it. You can pretend to others that you're as brave as a lion, but I know that when trouble starts, you run away like a rabbit."

title: THE LION AND THE WILD ASS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A lion and a wild ass decided to go hunting together. The wild ass would use his incredible speed to chase down the prey, and then the lion would catch and kill it. They were very successful, and soon it was time to split the catch. The lion divided the prey into three equal parts. 'I'll take the first part,' he said, 'because I'm the king of the animals. I'll take the second part too, since as your partner, I deserve half of what's left. And as for the third part—if you don't give it to me and leave right now, trust me, you'll regret it!'

The strong make the rules.

title: THE MAN AND THE SATYR
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A man and a satyr once became friends and decided to live together. Things went smoothly at first, until one cold winter day, the satyr saw the man blowing on his hands. "Why are you doing that?" the satyr asked. "To warm my hands," the man replied. Later that same day, when they sat down for supper, they both had steaming bowls of porridge. The man lifted his bowl and blew on it. Again, the satyr asked, "Why are you doing that?" The man answered, "To cool my porridge." The satyr stood up from the table. "Goodbye," he said, "I can't be friends with someone who blows both hot and cold from the same mouth."

title: THE IMAGE-SELLER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A man once carved a wooden statue of Mercury and took it to the market to sell. But no one seemed interested in buying it. Hoping to get buyers’ attention, he started shouting about how amazing the statue was. 'A god for sale! A god for sale! This one brings good luck and keeps your fortune strong!' he called out. After a while, someone in the crowd stopped him and asked, 'If your god is as powerful as you say, why don’t you keep it and use it yourself?' The man answered, 'Well, it’s true that he brings profit, but it takes a long time—and I need money right now.'

title: THE EAGLE AND THE ARROW
author: Aesop
text_chunk: An eagle was perched high up on a rocky ledge, scanning the area below for prey. Meanwhile, a hunter, hidden in a crack of the mountain and waiting quietly for an opportunity, spotted the eagle and fired an arrow at him. The arrow hit the eagle square in the chest and went right through him. As the eagle lay dying in pain, he looked at the arrow. "Oh, how cruel fate is!" he cried. "It's bad enough that I'm going to die like this, but it's even worse that the arrow that kills me is tipped with my own eagle feathers!"

title: THE RICH MAN AND THE TANNER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A wealthy man moved into a house right next to a tanner, but the strong smell coming from the tannery bothered him so much that he told the tanner he needed to move out. The tanner kept putting it off, promising each time that he’d be leaving soon. This went on for a while until, eventually, the rich man became so accustomed to the smell that it didn’t bother him anymore, and he stopped complaining to the tanner altogether.

title: THE WOLF, THE MOTHER, AND HER CHILD
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A hungry wolf was wandering around, looking for something to eat. Eventually, he heard a baby crying and followed the sound to a nearby cottage. Crouching under the window, he listened as the mother said to her child, “Stop crying or I’ll throw you to the wolf.” The wolf took her seriously and decided to wait, hoping he’d get a meal. As evening fell, the wolf heard the mother lovingly reassure her child, “If that bad wolf shows up, don’t worry—Daddy will protect you and get rid of him.” The wolf, feeling frustrated, stood up and left. He muttered to himself, “You really can’t trust anything these people say in that house.”

title: THE OLD WOMAN AND THE WINE-JAR
author: Aesop
text_chunk: An old woman came across an empty wine jar that used to hold a special and expensive wine. Even though the jar was empty, the wonderful scent still lingered inside. She brought it up to her nose and smelled it over and over. "Wow," she exclaimed, "the drink that left behind such an amazing fragrance must have been truly delicious!"

title: THE LIONESS AND THE VIXEN
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A lioness and a fox were chatting about their children, as mothers often do. They admired how healthy and strong their little ones were, bragged about their lovely fur, and remarked on how much their kids resembled them. The Fox proudly said, “My litter of cubs is wonderful to see.” Then, with a sly smile, she added, “But I’ve noticed you only ever have one.” The lioness replied firmly, “That's true, but that one is a lion.”

It's better to have quality than quantity.

title: THE VIPER AND THE FILE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A viper slithered into a carpenter's workshop, moving from tool to tool, hoping to find something to eat. Eventually, the viper came to the File and politely asked if it could spare some food. The File responded with a mix of pity and scorn, saying, "You must be quite foolish to think I’d give you anything. I always take from everyone else and never give anything back."

People who are greedy aren’t generous.

title: THE CAT AND THE COCK
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
