A cat leaped on a rooster, looking for a good reason to eat him. Normally, cats don’t eat roosters, and she knew it wasn’t right. Finally, she said, “You’re such a nuisance at night, always crowing and keeping everyone awake. So I’m going to put an end to it.” The rooster tried to defend himself, saying he crowed to wake people up, so they could get started with their day on time, and that folks really needed him. “Maybe so,” said the cat, “but whether they do or not, I’m not missing my dinner.” And with that, she killed and ate him.

A villain never lets the lack of a good excuse stand in the way of doing wrong.

title: THE HARE AND THE TORTOISE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
One day, a Hare was teasing a Tortoise about being so slow. "Hold on a second," said the Tortoise. "How about we have a race? I bet I can beat you." The Hare laughed at the idea, but agreed, saying, "Alright, let's give it a try." They decided to let the Fox set the course and be the judge. When the race started, both began together, but the Hare quickly pulled far ahead. Confident he had plenty of time, the Hare decided to lie down and take a nap. Meanwhile, the Tortoise kept moving steadily forward, and eventually reached the finish line. The Hare finally woke up with a jolt and sprinted as fast as he could, but when he arrived, the Tortoise had already won.

Slow and steady wins the race.

title: THE SOLDIER AND HIS HORSE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: During the war, a Soldier gave his Horse plenty of oats and took care of him, wanting the Horse to be strong enough to face hardships and fast enough to carry his master out of danger if needed. But when peace returned, the Soldier made his Horse do hard labor, barely paid attention to him, and fed him nothing but scraps of chaff. When another war began, the Soldier saddled and bridled his Horse, put on his heavy armor, and got ready to ride into battle. The poor, underfed Horse collapsed under his weight and said, 'You'll have to fight on foot this time. Because of all the hard work and poor food, you’ve turned me from a horse into a donkey. And you can't expect me to change back into a horse instantly.'

title: THE OXEN AND THE BUTCHERS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Once, the Oxen decided they wanted revenge on the Butchers for all the suffering they had caused. They made a plan to get rid of the Butchers on a specific day, and soon all the Oxen gathered to discuss their strategy. Some of the more aggressive ones were already sharpening their horns, ready for a fight. Just then, an old Ox stood up and said, "My friends, I understand why you want to get back at the Butchers—they’ve given us plenty of reasons to be angry. But remember, at least they know what they're doing and don't make us suffer more than they have to. If we kill them, people who don’t know anything about the job will take over, and their mistakes will only make us suffer more. You can be sure that, even if all the Butchers disappear, humans will never stop wanting beef."

title: THE WOLF AND THE LION
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A wolf snatched a lamb from the flock and was carrying it away to enjoy on his own. As he was about to do so, a lion appeared, took the lamb from the wolf, and walked off with it. The wolf didn’t dare to fight back, but after the lion had gone a bit further, he called out, “It’s really unfair that you take what’s rightfully mine!” The lion just laughed and replied, “Oh, it was yours, was it? Was it a gift from a friend, maybe?”

title: THE SHEEP, THE WOLF, AND THE STAG
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
One day, a Stag asked a Sheep if he could borrow some wheat, promising that his friend the Wolf would guarantee he paid it back. But the Sheep was suspicious and worried they might trick her. She politely refused, saying, "The Wolf is known for grabbing whatever he wants and running off without ever paying, and you’re much faster than I am, too. So how could I ever catch up to either of you if you didn’t pay me back?"

Two wrongs don’t make a right.

title: THE LION AND THE THREE BULLS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
Three bulls were grazing together in a field, watched closely by a lion who was eager to catch and eat them. But as long as the bulls stayed united, the lion knew he stood no chance against them. Determined to divide them, the lion began spreading lies and sneaky rumors, causing suspicion and jealousy among the bulls. His plan worked perfectly; soon the bulls grew distant, stopped trusting each other, and eventually started grazing alone, each one separated from the others. As soon as the lion noticed they were alone, he attacked them one after another and killed them all.

When friends argue, it gives their enemies a chance to take advantage of them.

title: THE HORSE AND HIS RIDER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A young man, who liked to think he was a skilled rider, climbed onto a horse that hadn't really been trained and was incredibly hard to handle. As soon as the man sat in the saddle, the horse took off at full speed, and nothing could make him stop. While the young man raced down the road, a friend saw him and shouted, "Where are you going in such a rush?" The young man pointed at the horse and answered, "I have no idea—ask him!"

title: THE GOAT AND THE VINE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A goat wandered into a vineyard and started nibbling on the delicate new shoots of a vine that was heavy with beautiful bunches of grapes. The vine spoke up and said, "Why are you hurting me like this? Haven’t I done nothing to you? Isn’t there plenty of grass for you to eat? Still, even if you strip away every leaf and leave me completely bare, I’ll still make enough wine to be poured over you when you’re taken to the altar for sacrifice."

title: THE TWO POTS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
Two pots—one made of clay and the other of brass—were swept away together by a strong river current. The brass pot told his clay friend to stay close, promising to protect him. The clay pot thanked him, but pleaded for the brass pot to keep his distance. "That's exactly what worries me," said the clay pot. "If you even bump into me once, I'll shatter to pieces."

People who are equals make the best friends.

title: THE OLD HOUND
author: Aesop
text_chunk: An old Hound had spent years loyally working for his master, chasing down many animals during his prime. But as he grew older, he started to lose both his strength and speed. One day, out hunting, his master sent him after a fierce wild boar. The Hound managed to grab the boar by the ear, but with his teeth worn down by age, he couldn’t keep his grip, and the boar got away. Angry, his master scolded him harshly. But the Hound spoke up, saying, "I still have all the determination I ever had, master, but my body is tired and weak with age. Instead of blaming me for what I can’t do now, you should remember all that I’ve done for you."

title: THE CLOWN AND THE COUNTRYMAN
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Nobleman announced he was hosting a public show at the theater and promised amazing prizes to anyone who could present something new during the event. The news drew in a huge crowd of magicians, jugglers, acrobats—and among them, a Clown beloved by the people, who spread the word that he had an exciting new act to show. On the day of the performance, the theater was packed long before things started. Several acts amazed the crowd with their skills, and then the Clown, empty-handed and alone, took the stage. The audience immediately grew quiet with anticipation. Bowing his head to his chest, the Clown perfectly imitated the squeal of a pig, so convincingly that the crowd demanded he reveal the real animal they were sure he’d hidden somewhere. But he showed them there was no pig—just his talent—and the audience erupted in applause. Among them sat a Countryman who criticized the Clown’s act, saying he could do it even better, and challenged that he'd prove it the next day. When the day arrived, the theater was again filled to the brim, eagerly waiting. The Clown repeated his pig impression to wild cheers. Meanwhile, the Countryman had secretly hidden a young piglet under his clothes before going onstage. When the audience teasingly called on him to top the Clown, he pinched the pig’s ear, making it squeal loudly. But everyone in the theater insisted that the Clown’s imitation was far more realistic. The Countryman then pulled out the piglet from under his smock and said with a smirk, 'See? That just proves what kind of judges you are!'

title: THE LARK AND THE FARMER
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A lark had built her nest in a cornfield and was raising her chicks hidden beneath the ripening stalks. One day, before the chicks were ready to fly, the farmer came to check on his crop and noticed the corn was turning yellow quickly. He said, "I should let my neighbors know that I need help harvesting this field." One of the young larks overheard him, became really scared, and asked their mother if they should move right away. "There's no need to rush," she replied. "If a man waits for his friends to help, he won't do anything any time soon." A few days later, the farmer returned and saw the grain was overripe and starting to fall to the ground. He said, "I can't wait any longer. Today, I'll hire workers and get the job started right away." Hearing this, the mother lark told her chicks, "Now it's time to go, kids. He’s not relying on his friends anymore—he’s planning to do it himself."

Helping yourself is the best help of all.

title: THE LION AND THE ASS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A lion and an ass decided to team up and go hunting together. After a while, they found a cave full of wild goats. The lion waited at the cave entrance, ready to catch anything that ran out, while the ass went inside and made a loud, echoing bray to scare the goats out into the open. As the frightened goats ran out, the lion took them down one by one. When the cave was finally empty, the ass walked out and said, "I really scared them, didn’t I?" The lion replied, "You definitely did! Honestly, if I hadn’t known it was just you making all that noise, I might have run away too."

title: THE PROPHET
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A prophet sat in the busy marketplace, offering to tell anyone’s fortune if they wanted to know their future. Suddenly, someone ran up to him and shouted that thieves had broken into the prophet’s house and stolen everything they could find. The prophet immediately jumped up, frantically tore at his hair, and yelled curses at the thieves as he ran off. The people around him were entertained by this, and one of them remarked, “This guy claims to see everyone else’s future, but apparently he can’t even tell what’s going to happen to himself.”

title: THE HOUND AND THE HARE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A young hound chased after a hare. When he finally caught up to her, sometimes he would snap at her with his teeth like he wanted to hurt her. Other times, he'd loosen his grip and bounce around her playfully, like she was just another puppy. Eventually, the hare said, "I wish you'd show your real intentions! If you’re my friend, why do you bite me? If you’re my enemy, why do you play with me?"

Someone who acts both ways is not really a friend.

title: THE LION, THE MOUSE, AND THE FOX
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Lion was napping at the entrance of his den when a Mouse scurried across his back, tickling him and waking him up with a jolt. Confused, the Lion glanced around, trying to figure out what had disturbed his sleep. Nearby, a Fox watched the whole thing unfold and decided to poke fun at the Lion. He joked, "Wow, I’ve never seen a Lion get scared by a Mouse before!" The Lion, sounding annoyed, shot back, "Scared? Not at all! I just can’t stand such rude behavior."

title: THE TRUMPETER TAKEN PRISONER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Trumpeter led the charge at the front of the army, inspiring his fellow soldiers with his bold music. When the enemy captured him, he pleaded for his life, saying, "Please don't kill me. I haven't hurt anyone—I don't even carry weapons, just my trumpet." But his captors answered, "That's exactly why we should take your life. Even though you don't fight yourself, you're the one who encourages others to do it."

title: THE WOLF AND THE CRANE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One day, a Wolf got a bone stuck in his throat and couldn't get it out. Desperate, he asked a Crane if she would use her long beak to reach down his throat and pull the bone free. "I'll make it worth your while," he promised. The Crane agreed, and easily removed the bone. As the Wolf turned to leave after thanking her, the Crane called after him, "Hey, what about my reward?" The Wolf showed his sharp teeth and snapped, "Reward? You should be glad you were able to put your head inside a Wolf's mouth and came out unharmed. Isn't that enough for you?"

title: THE EAGLE, THE CAT, AND THE WILD SOW
author: Aesop
text_chunk: An Eagle made her nest at the top of a tall tree. Halfway down the trunk, a Cat lived with her kittens in a hollow, and at the base of the tree, a Wild Sow settled in with her piglets. They could have lived peacefully as neighbors if it hadn’t been for the Cat’s sneaky ways. Climbing up to the Eagle’s nest, the Cat whispered, "You and I are in serious danger. That awful Sow down there, always digging at the roots, is planning to knock over the tree so she can eat your babies and mine whenever she wants." The Eagle became terrified after hearing this. Next, the Cat climbed down and told the Sow, "You need to watch out for that scary Eagle. She’s just waiting for a chance to swoop down and snatch up one of your piglets to feed her babies." The Sow was just as scared as the Eagle. After that, the Cat went back to her hollow in the tree and pretended to be afraid, never coming out during the day. Only at night, when no one could see her, did she sneak out to find food for her kittens. Meanwhile, the Eagle was too scared to leave her nest, and the Sow was too frightened to leave her spot among the roots. Eventually, both the Eagle and the Sow, along with their families, died of hunger, and their bodies gave the Cat plenty of food to feed her growing kittens.

title: THE WOLF AND THE SHEEP
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A wolf had been attacked by dogs and lay on the ground for a long time, badly injured and seeming almost dead. Eventually, he started to recover and, feeling very hungry, spotted a sheep passing by. He called out, "Could you please bring me some water from the nearby stream? If I had something to drink, I could figure out the food part myself." But the sheep wasn’t easily fooled. He replied, "I understand that if I brought you water, you wouldn’t have any trouble getting your food—meaning me. Goodbye."

title: THE TUNNY-FISH AND THE DOLPHIN
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A tunny-fish raced through the water, desperate to escape a dolphin chasing him at full speed. Even though the tunny tried his best, the dolphin kept getting closer. Just as the dolphin was about to catch him, the tunny's frantic swimming pushed him onto a sandbank. The dolphin, caught up in the chase, followed right behind and ended up stranded, too. Both of them lay out of the water, struggling to breathe. Seeing his enemy trapped like he was, the tunny said, "I don't mind dying now—at least the one who led to my end will share the same fate."

title: THE THREE TRADESMEN
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
The people of a certain city were discussing what would be the best material to use for building strong new walls to protect their town. A carpenter stood up and suggested they use wood, explaining that it was easy to find and simple to work with. Then a stone-mason disagreed, pointing out that wood could catch fire too easily and argued that using stone would be much better. Finally, a tanner spoke up and said, "Honestly, I think you can't beat leather for this."

Everyone looks out for their own interests.

title: THE MOUSE AND THE BULL
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A bull chased after a mouse that had bitten his nose, but the mouse was too fast and darted into a hole in the wall. The bull, furious, charged at the wall again and again, but couldn’t get to the mouse. Eventually, he tired himself out and collapsed onto the ground, exhausted. When everything was quiet, the little mouse ran out, bit the bull again, and quickly slipped back into the hole before the bull could react. Frustrated and angry, the bull bellowed, but he couldn’t do anything. Then, the mouse’s tiny voice called out from the wall, 'You big guys don’t always win—you see? Sometimes us little ones come out on top.'

Strength doesn’t always guarantee victory.

title: THE HARE AND THE HOUND
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A hound startled a hare from her hiding spot and chased her for quite a while, but as the hare began to pull ahead, the hound eventually gave up. A local farmer who had watched the whole chase met the hound on his way back and made fun of him for losing. “That little hare was too fast for you,” he said. The hound replied, “Remember, there’s a big difference between running for your dinner and running for your life.”

title: THE TOWN MOUSE AND THE COUNTRY MOUSE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Town Mouse and a Country Mouse were friends. One day, the Country Mouse invited his friend to visit his home out in the fields. The Town Mouse came over, and they sat down to a meal of barley grains and roots, which tasted a bit too earthy for the Town Mouse’s liking. Soon, he exclaimed, “My dear friend, you hardly live better than the ants! You should see how I live. My pantry overflows with food. You have to come stay with me, and I promise you’ll experience true luxury.” So, when the Town Mouse went back to the city, he took the Country Mouse along. He brought him to a pantry filled with flour, oatmeal, figs, honey, and dates. The Country Mouse had never seen such food before, and eagerly sat down to enjoy the feast his friend offered. But before they even started, the pantry door opened and someone walked in. The two mice dashed off and hid in a tiny, very uncomfortable hole. After things seemed quiet, they came out again, only to have someone else enter, so they ran to hide once more. That was the last straw for the Country Mouse. “Goodbye,” he said, “I’m leaving. You may live in luxury, but you’re always surrounded by danger. At home, I can eat my simple meal of roots and grain in peace.”

title: THE LION AND THE BULL
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
One day, a Lion noticed a plump, healthy Bull grazing with the herd and started thinking of a way to catch him. So the Lion sent the Bull a message, saying he was hosting a feast and sacrificing a sheep, and would the Bull do him the honor of being his guest. The Bull agreed, but when he got to the Lion's den, he saw lots of cooking pots and spits ready, but no sheep at all. Quietly, the Bull turned and started to leave. The Lion called out, sounding hurt, and asked why he was leaving. The Bull replied, "I have a good reason. When I saw all your preparations, it was clear to me that it was a Bull, not a sheep, who was meant to be the meal."

There's no point setting a trap if your intended target can see it coming.

title: THE WOLF, THE FOX, AND THE APE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A Wolf accused a Fox of stealing from him, but the Fox denied it. They decided to take the matter to an Ape to settle the dispute. After listening to both sides, the Ape announced his judgment: "Wolf, I don't think you actually lost what you're talking about. Still, Fox, even though you deny it, I believe you're guilty of stealing."

Dishonest people don't get trusted, even when they tell the truth.

title: THE EAGLE AND THE COCKS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
There were two roosters living on the same farm, and they fought to see who would be in charge. Once the fight was finished, the rooster who lost went to hide in a dark corner. The winner, feeling proud, flew up onto the roof of the stables and crowed loudly. But as he boasted, an eagle flying high above spotted him, swooped down, and carried him away. As soon as that happened, the other rooster came out from his hiding spot and ended up ruling the farmyard with no one left to challenge him.

Being too proud can lead to your downfall.

title: THE ESCAPED JACKDAW
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A man caught a jackdaw and tied a piece of string around one of its legs before giving it to his children as a pet. The jackdaw, however, didn't enjoy living with people at all. After some time, when he seemed to have become somewhat tame and the children weren't watching him as closely, he slipped away and flew back to the places he used to know. Unfortunately, the string was still tied to his leg, and it soon got tangled in the branches of a tree. The jackdaw struggled, but he couldn't break free. Realizing there was no escape, he cried in despair, "Sadly, by winning my freedom, I've lost my life."

title: THE FARMER AND THE FOX
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A farmer was really frustrated with a fox that kept sneaking into his yard at night and stealing his chickens. Determined to put an end to it, he set a trap and eventually caught the fox. Wanting to get back at him, the farmer tied a bunch of dry straw to the fox’s tail, set it on fire, and let the fox go. Unfortunately, the fox ran straight into the fields, where the ripe corn was ready for harvest. The flames quickly spread to the crops, burning them all down, and the farmer lost his entire harvest.

Getting revenge can end up hurting you as much as it hurts someone else.

title: VENUS AND THE CAT
author: Aesop
themes: the importance of staying true to oneself
text_chunk: A cat fell in love with a charming young man and went to the goddess Venus, asking her to turn her into a woman. Venus kindly agreed and instantly transformed the cat into a beautiful young woman. The young man met her, fell in love right away, and soon they were married. Later, Venus decided to check if the cat had changed her nature as well as her appearance. She let a mouse loose in the room where the couple were. The moment the former cat saw the mouse, she completely forgot about everything else, jumped up, and chased after it. Seeing this, Venus was so disappointed that she turned her back into a cat.

title: THE CROW AND THE SWAN
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A crow was jealous when he saw the swan’s beautiful white feathers. He thought the swan’s color came from all the time it spent swimming and bathing in the water. So the crow left the altars, where he used to find bits of meat from sacrifices, and moved to live by the ponds and streams instead. Even though he washed and bathed his feathers many times every day, they never got any whiter, and in the end, he also ended up starving to death.

You can change your habits, but you can’t change your nature.

title: THE STAG WITH ONE EYE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A stag who was blind in one eye was grazing near the sea shore. To stay safe, he always kept his good eye watching the land, hoping to spot any hounds that might come after him. He turned his blind eye toward the sea, never imagining that danger could come from that direction. But, as luck would have it, some sailors passing by in a boat noticed the stag and shot an arrow at him from the sea, hitting him with a fatal wound. As the stag lay dying, he thought, "How foolish I was! I worried about threats from the land, but nothing happened from there. I never suspected the sea, and that's where my downfall came from."

Trouble often comes from where we least expect it.

title: THE FLY AND THE DRAUGHT-MULE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A fly landed on the shaft of a cart and looked over at the mule pulling it. 'You’re so slow!' complained the fly. 'Hurry up, or I’ll have to sting you to make you move faster.' But the mule didn’t pay any attention. 'My master is sitting behind me in the cart,' the mule replied. 'He’s the one holding the reins and snapping the whip, and it’s him I listen to—not you. I know when I’m allowed to take my time and when I need to hurry.'

title: THE COCK AND THE JEWEL
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A rooster was pecking around in the dirt, looking for something to eat, when he uncovered a jewel that someone had dropped by accident. "Wow!" he exclaimed, "you must be quite valuable, and your owner would be thrilled to find you. But for me, I’d take just one kernel of corn over all the jewels in the world."

title: THE WOLF AND THE SHEPHERD
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Wolf lingered near a flock of sheep for a long time, but never made any move to harm them. At first, the Shepherd watched him closely, since he naturally assumed the Wolf was up to no good. But as the days passed and the Wolf didn't bother the flock, the Shepherd started to see him more as a guardian than a threat. One day, when the Shepherd had to run an errand in the city, he wasn't worried about leaving the Wolf with the sheep. But as soon as the Shepherd was out of sight, the Wolf attacked and killed most of the sheep. When the Shepherd returned and saw the devastation, he said, "I guess this is what I get for trusting a Wolf with my sheep."

title: THE FARMER AND THE STORK
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A farmer set up some traps in his field, which he had just planted with corn, hoping to catch the cranes that kept eating the seeds. When he came back to check on the traps, he found several cranes caught in them, and among them was a stork. The stork pleaded, "Please let me go! I’m not a crane — I’m a stork, you can tell by my feathers. I’m honest and wouldn’t harm anyone." But the farmer answered, "It doesn’t matter what you are. I found you here with these cranes, who are ruining my crops, so you’ll be treated just like them."

If you spend time with the wrong crowd, people will think you’re just like them, no matter who you really are.

title: THE CHARGER AND THE MILLER
author: Aesop
themes: the ups and downs of life
text_chunk: A horse who used to carry his rider into battle realized he was getting old, so he decided to work at a mill instead. Gone were the days of marching proudly to the sound of drums—instead, he had to spend his days tirelessly grinding corn. Feeling sorry for himself, he told the Miller one day, "Once, I was a magnificent war-horse, dressed in fine gear and cared for by a groom whose only job was to look after me. Things couldn’t be more different now! I wish I’d never traded the battlefield for the mill." The Miller answered sharply, "There's no point regretting the past. Life has its ups and downs, and you just have to deal with whatever comes your way."

title: THE GRASSHOPPER AND THE OWL
author: Aesop
text_chunk: An Owl made her home in the hollow of a tree. She was used to hunting at night and sleeping during the day, but her daytime naps kept getting interrupted by the loud chirping of a Grasshopper who had settled in the branches nearby. The Owl asked the Grasshopper several times to quiet down so she could rest, but the Grasshopper just chirped even louder. Eventually, the Owl lost her patience and decided to get rid of the Grasshopper by using a clever trick. Putting on her sweetest voice, she said, "Since I can’t sleep because of your singing—which, honestly, is as lovely as Apollo’s lyre—I’m thinking about enjoying some nectar that Minerva gave me recently. Would you like to come in and try it with me?" The Grasshopper was pleased by her compliments about his song, and the idea of a tasty drink made him eager, so he happily agreed. As soon as he hopped inside the hollow tree where the Owl waited, she grabbed him and ate him up.

title: THE GRASSHOPPER AND THE ANTS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One crisp winter day, a group of Ants were hard at work spreading out their corn to dry, since it had gotten pretty damp after a long stretch of rain. Suddenly, a Grasshopper wandered over and asked if they could spare a few grains. 'Please,' she pleaded, 'I'm starving.' The Ants paused their work for just a moment, even though it went against their usual habits. 'Can we ask,' they said, 'what you were doing all summer? Why didn’t you set aside any food for the winter?' The Grasshopper sighed and answered, 'Honestly, I was so caught up singing that I just didn’t have the time.' The Ants replied, 'Well, if you spent your summer singing, you might as well spend the winter dancing.' They chuckled to themselves and got back to work.

title: THE FARMER AND THE VIPER
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
One cold winter day, a farmer found a viper frozen and unable to move. Feeling sorry for the creature, he picked it up and tucked it into his coat to warm it. As soon as the viper came back to life from the farmer's warmth, it turned on him and delivered a deadly bite. As the farmer lay dying, he said, "This is what I get for showing kindness to such a dangerous creature."

Kindness is wasted on those who are evil.

title: THE TWO FROGS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Two frogs lived as neighbors. One made his home in a marsh, surrounded by plenty of water—which frogs adore. The other lived farther off, along a lane, where the only water he could find was rainwater collected in small ruts. The Marsh Frog often warned his friend, encouraging him to move to the marsh. He promised his friend would be much more comfortable there—and, even more important, much safer. But the Lane Frog refused, insisting he just couldn’t leave a place he’d grown so used to. A few days later, a heavy wagon rumbled down the lane, and the Lane Frog was tragically crushed under its wheels.

title: THE COBBLER TURNED DOCTOR
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A not-so-skilled cobbler, struggling to make a living fixing shoes, decided to quit his trade and try his luck as a doctor instead. He claimed to have discovered a universal antidote that could cure any poison, and quickly gained a reputation, mostly due to his knack for promoting himself. But one day, he became seriously ill. The King of the country saw an opportunity to test the supposed power of the cobbler’s remedy. He called for a cup, poured in a dose of the so-called antidote, added a bit of water while pretending it was poison, and told the cobbler to drink it. Terrified that he’d been poisoned, the cobbler admitted that he knew nothing about medicine and that his antidote was useless. The King then gathered his people and said, “What greater foolishness is there than this? Here’s a cobbler so lacking in skill that no one will trust him with their boots, yet all of you were willing to trust him with your lives!”

title: THE ASS, THE COCK, AND THE LION
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A donkey and a rooster were inside a pen together. Soon, a lion—who hadn’t eaten in days—came by, ready to pounce on the donkey for a meal. But just then, the rooster stood tall, flapped his wings wildly, and let out a powerful crow. Lions are terrified of a rooster’s crow, and as soon as the lion heard it, he took off running. The donkey felt super proud and figured that if the lion was scared of a rooster, he’d definitely be afraid of a donkey. So, the donkey chased after the lion. But once they were far enough away from the rooster's crow, the lion suddenly spun around and gobbled up the donkey.

Having false confidence can end in disaster.

title: THE BELLY AND THE MEMBERS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Once, the parts of the body decided to rebel against the stomach. They said to the stomach, "You get to relax and live in comfort while we do all the hard work. We're basically your servants, always taking care of you. Well, we're done with that. From now on, you can take care of yourself." True to their word, the body parts stopped working for the stomach, leaving it to go hungry. But soon, things turned out exactly as you’d expect—the whole body started to weaken, and every part suffered together as everything began to fail. It was only then that they realized, too late, how silly their decision had been.

title: THE BALD MAN AND THE FLY
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A fly landed on a bald man's head and bit him. Trying to get rid of it, the man slapped his own head hard, but the fly got away. Mocking him, the fly said, "You tried to kill me over a tiny bite. What will you do to yourself now after that big smack you just gave yourself?" The man replied, "I don’t hold a grudge for that hit because I didn’t mean to hurt myself. But as for you, you annoying little insect who lives by feeding on people, I would have put up with much more just for the chance to squash you!"

title: THE ASS AND THE WOLF
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A donkey was grazing in a field when he spotted his enemy, the wolf, approaching in the distance. Thinking quickly, the donkey began to limp, acting as if he were badly injured and struggled to move. When the wolf drew near, he asked the donkey why he was limping. The donkey explained that he had stepped on a thorn while squeezing through a hedge and pleaded with the wolf to remove it with his teeth. "If you eat me with the thorn still in my foot, it might get stuck in your throat and cause you a lot of pain," the donkey said. Wanting an easy meal, the wolf agreed and told the donkey to lift his foot so he could pull the thorn out. But just as the wolf concentrated on the task, the donkey kicked back hard, delivering a powerful blow to the wolf's mouth and breaking his teeth. With that, the donkey took off running as fast as he could. Once he recovered enough to speak, the wolf muttered to himself, "I deserve that. My father taught me to hunt, and I should have stuck to my own trade instead of trying to play doctor."

title: THE MONKEY AND THE CAMEL
author: Aesop
text_chunk: At a big gathering of all the animals, the Monkey put on a dance show that really entertained everyone. The whole crowd cheered when he finished, which made the Camel feel jealous. Wanting to get the same attention, the Camel got up and tried to dance too. But he looked so awkward and silly as he stumbled around that the other animals burst out laughing and ended up chasing him away with their jokes.

title: THE SICK MAN AND THE DOCTOR
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A sick man was visited by his doctor, who asked how he was feeling. "Pretty well, Doctor," the man replied, "but I’m sweating a lot." "That’s actually a good sign," the doctor assured him. On the doctor's next visit, he asked again, and the patient said, "I feel about the same, but now I keep getting chills that make me feel cold all over." "That’s another good sign," the doctor said. On the third visit, the doctor inquired again, and the sick man answered that he now felt very feverish. "That's a very good sign," the doctor replied. "You’re really improving." Later, a friend came by and asked how he was doing. The sick man said, "My friend, I’m dying from all these ‘good signs.’"

title: THE TRAVELLERS AND THE PLANE-TREE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
Two travelers were making their way down a dry, dusty road on a hot summer day. Eventually, they spotted a plane tree and eagerly stepped aside to rest in the cool shade beneath its wide, leafy branches. As they relaxed and looked up at the tree, one traveler commented to the other, "This plane tree sure is useless. It doesn’t grow any fruit or do anything helpful for people." Just then, the plane tree spoke up, clearly annoyed. "How ungrateful you are!" it said. "Here you are, enjoying my shade and escaping the blazing sun, yet you insult me by saying I’m good for nothing!"

Many acts of kindness go unappreciated.

title: THE FLEA AND THE OX
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One day, a Flea spoke to an Ox and said, "Why is it that someone as big and strong as you lets humans boss you around and do all their hard work? Meanwhile, I'm tiny, but I live on their bodies, drink their blood whenever I want, and don't have to lift a finger for it." The Ox answered, "Humans treat me kindly, and I'm thankful for that. They give me good food, shelter, and sometimes even show their affection by patting my head and neck." "Well, they'd pat me, too," said the Flea, "if I let them—but I make sure they can't, or else there'd be nothing left of me."

title: THE BIRDS, THE BEASTS, AND THE BAT
author: Aesop
text_chunk: The Birds and the Beasts went to war, battling each other in a series of fierce fights where victory kept shifting sides. The Bat refused to fully join either group. When the Birds were winning, you'd see the Bat fighting alongside them. But if the Beasts started to win, the Bat would sneak over and join their ranks instead. While the conflict lasted, nobody really noticed what the Bat was doing. But once the war was over and peace returned, both the Birds and the Beasts turned their backs on the Bat. They wanted nothing to do with someone so two-faced, and ever since, the Bat has lived as a lonely outcast from both sides.

title: THE MAN AND HIS TWO SWEETHEARTS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A middle-aged man whose hair was starting to go gray had two girlfriends—one older and one younger. The older woman didn’t like that her boyfriend looked younger than she did, so every time he visited her, she would pluck out his dark hairs to make him seem older. The younger woman, though, didn’t want to be with someone who looked older than her, so whenever she could, she would pull out his gray hairs to make him look younger. In the end, both women kept plucking hair from his head until he didn’t have any left and became completely bald.

title: THE EAGLE, THE JACKDAW, AND THE SHEPHERD
author: Aesop
themes: the danger of pretending to be something you're not
text_chunk:
One day, a Jackdaw watched as an Eagle swooped down, grabbed a lamb in its talons, and soared away. "Wow," thought the Jackdaw, "I can do that too!" It flew up high into the sky, then dived down with a loud flapping of wings onto the back of a large ram. But as soon as it landed, its claws got tangled in the thick wool, and no matter how hard it tried, it couldn’t get free. The more it flapped, the worse it got. Soon, the Shepherd walked over. "Well, well," he said, "so you thought you could do that, did you?" He picked up the Jackdaw, clipped its wings, and took it home to his children. The bird looked so strange that the kids couldn’t figure out what kind it was. "What kind of bird is this, Dad?" they asked. "It’s a Jackdaw," he replied, "just a Jackdaw. But it wants everyone to think it’s an Eagle."

If you try to do something that’s beyond your abilities, not only will your efforts be wasted, but you might end up facing embarrassment or even misfortune.

title: THE WOLF AND THE BOY
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A wolf, feeling playful after enjoying a satisfying meal, noticed a boy lying flat on the ground. Realizing the boy was trying to hide out of fear of him, the wolf approached and said, "Well, I’ve found you! But if you can tell me three things that are absolutely true, I’ll let you go." The boy gathered his courage, thought for a moment, and responded, "First, it’s unfortunate that you noticed me. Second, it was foolish of me to let myself be seen. And third, everyone dislikes wolves because you’re always attacking our flocks without reason." The wolf replied, "Everything you’ve said is true from your side, so you can go."

title: THE MILLER, HIS SON, AND THEIR ASS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A miller and his young son were taking their donkey to the market, hoping to sell it. As they walked along the road, they came across a group of laughing, chatty girls. One of the girls called out, "Have you ever seen such silly people? They're walking on the dusty road when they could just ride the donkey!" The miller thought they had a good point.

title: THE STAG AND THE VINE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A stag, chased by hunters, hid himself beneath the thick branches of a vine. The hunters lost track of him and walked past his hiding spot, not realizing he was so close. Thinking he was safe, the stag started munching on the vine's leaves. But as the leaves rustled, the returning hunters noticed the movement. Guessing that an animal was hiding there, one of them shot an arrow into the greenery. The unlucky stag was struck in the heart, and as he breathed his last, he said, 'I deserve this for betraying my protector by eating its leaves.'

Sometimes, being ungrateful leads to your own downfall.

title: THE LAMB CHASED BY A WOLF
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A wolf was chasing a lamb, who ran into a temple to escape. The wolf tried to convince the lamb to come back outside, saying, "If you stay in there, the priest will probably catch you and sacrifice you on the altar." The lamb answered, "Thanks, but I’d much rather take my chances here. I’d prefer being sacrificed than being eaten by a wolf."

title: THE ARCHER AND THE LION
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
An archer went up into the hills to practice his archery, and as soon as the animals saw him, they all ran away—except for the lion, who stood his ground and challenged the archer to a fight. The archer quickly fired an arrow at the lion and hit him, then called out, "See what my messenger can do? Just wait a moment and I'll take you on myself." But as soon as the lion felt the pain of the arrow, he ran off as fast as he could. A fox who witnessed everything called out to the lion, "Come on, don't be a coward! Why not stay and fight?" The lion answered, "I'm not sticking around, no way! If his messenger causes that much pain, imagine what the man himself could do!"

It's best to stay away from those who can cause harm from a distance.

title: THE WOLF AND THE GOAT
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A wolf spotted a goat nibbling on the sparse grass at the top of a steep cliff. Since he couldn't reach her, he tried to convince her to come down. "You're taking a real risk up there, ma'am," he called out. "Why not listen to me and come down here? There’s much better food waiting for you." The goat glanced at him with a wise look. "You're not worried about me finding good grass," she replied. "You just want to eat me."

title: THE SICK STAG
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A stag got sick and was lying in a sunny clearing in the forest, too weak to move. When the other animals heard he wasn't well, many of them came by to check on him. Each visitor nibbled some of the grass around where he lay, and before long, there wasn't a single blade of grass left within his reach. After a few days, the stag started to feel a little better, but he was still too weak to stand up and find food. Because there was nothing left to eat nearby, the poor stag ended up starving to death—all because his friends hadn't thought about what he needed.

title: THE ASS AND THE MULE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Once, a man owned both a donkey and a mule, and one day he loaded them up with goods and set off on a journey. For a while, as long as the road was pretty flat, the donkey managed just fine. But eventually, they reached a hilly area where the path became rocky and steep, and the donkey grew exhausted. Struggling, the donkey asked the mule to help by taking some of his load, but the mule refused. Eventually, completely worn out, the donkey stumbled, fell down a steep slope, and died. The owner was distraught, but he did what he had to do: he put all of the donkey's burden onto the mule, and even took the donkey’s skin and placed it on top of the doubled load. The mule barely managed to carry the heavy weight, and as he trudged along, he thought to himself, "I deserve this. If I had just helped the donkey earlier, I wouldn't be carrying both his load and his skin now."

title: BROTHER AND SISTER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Once there was a man who had two kids—a boy and a girl. The boy was very handsome, while the girl wasn’t as lucky when it came to looks. One day, as they played together in their mother’s room, they happened to find a mirror and saw their own reflections for the first time. The boy, noticing how good-looking he was, began bragging to his sister about how handsome he looked. His sister, upset to see that she was plain, felt hurt by his words and started to cry. She ran to tell their father about her brother’s boasting and mentioned he’d been messing with their mother’s things. Their father just laughed, hugged them both, and said, “My children, this is a lesson—use the mirror for good. You, my son, should try to be as good as you are handsome, and you, my daughter, can make up for your looks by having a kind and sweet nature.”

title: THE HEIFER AND THE OX
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Heifer approached an Ox who was working hard, pulling a plough through the field. The Heifer, sounding a bit smug, told the Ox how sorry she was that he had to work so much. But soon after, the village held a festival, and everyone took the day off. While the Ox was let out to relax in the pasture, the Heifer was grabbed and led away to be sacrificed. The Ox looked at her with a knowing smile and said, 'Now I understand why you had such an easy life—it was because you were always meant for the altar.'

title: THE KINGDOM OF THE LION
author: Aesop
text_chunk: When the Lion ruled over all the animals, he wasn’t harsh or unfair. Instead, he was as kind and fair as a king should be. One day, he called all the animals together and created a set of laws so everyone could live together in complete equality and harmony. The wolf and the lamb, the tiger and the deer, the leopard and the young goat, the dog and the rabbit — everyone would live peacefully, side by side. The rabbit said, "Oh, I’ve wished for this day for so long! Finally, the weak can stand beside the strong without any fear."

title: THE ASS AND HIS DRIVER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A donkey was being led down a mountain path. For a while, he walked along sensibly enough, but suddenly he veered off the trail and rushed straight toward the edge of a steep cliff. Just as he was about to jump over, his driver grabbed his tail and tried desperately to pull him back. No matter how hard the driver pulled, the donkey wouldn't move away from the brink. Finally, the driver let go in frustration and said, "Fine, go ahead—get to the bottom your own way. But you'll find out soon enough that it's a quick trip to your death."

title: THE LION AND THE HARE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A lion came across a hare fast asleep in her nest and was about to eat her when he suddenly noticed a deer passing by. Wanting the bigger meal, he let the hare go and chased after the deer. But after running for a long time, he realized he couldn't catch up to the deer, so he gave up and returned for the hare. When he got back, the hare was gone, and he was left with nothing for dinner. 'I deserve this,' he admitted to himself. 'I should've been happy with what I already had instead of chasing after something better.'

title: THE WOLVES AND THE DOGS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
Once, the Wolves said to the Dogs, "Why should we keep being enemies? We’re actually pretty similar. The only real difference is that you go through training. We live free, but you’re kept as slaves by humans. They beat you, lock heavy collars around your necks, and force you to guard their flocks and herds. And worst of all, all you get to eat are bones. Stop putting up with it. Give the flocks to us, and we’ll all live well together, feasting on the best of everything." The Dogs listened and let themselves be convinced, so they went with the Wolves to their den. But as soon as they were inside, the Wolves attacked and tore them apart.

Those who betray others get exactly what they deserve.

title: THE BULL AND THE CALF
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A big, grown-up bull was trying to squeeze his massive body through the tight doorway of the cow barn to get to his stall. Just then, a young calf walked up and said, "If you move over for a second, I can show you how to get through." The bull looked at him, amused. "I already knew how to get through here long before you were even born," he replied.

title: THE TREES AND THE AXE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A woodcutter came into the forest and politely asked the trees for a favor—he needed some wood to make a handle for his axe. The tallest and most important trees thought this was a small request, so they quickly agreed, offering him a young ash sapling. He used it to craft the axe handle he wanted. But as soon as he finished, he began chopping down the grandest trees in the forest. When the trees realized what he was doing with the gift they had given, they cried out, "Oh no! We're ruined—but it's our own fault. The small favor we granted has cost us everything; if only we hadn't given up the ash, we might have stood tall for many years to come."

title: THE ASTRONOMER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: There was once an Astronomer who loved nothing more than heading outside at night to study the stars. One evening, while walking just outside the town gates, he was so busy looking up at the sky that he didn't notice where he was going. Completely lost in thought, he accidentally fell into a dry well. Lying at the bottom, groaning in pain, he drew the attention of a passerby. The person heard his cries, walked over to the well, peered down, and after learning what happened, said, "So you were so focused on the stars that you didn't even watch where you were stepping? Honestly, it seems like you got exactly what you deserved."

title: THE LABOURER AND THE SNAKE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A laborer's young son was bitten by a snake and died from the bite. Devastated and overcome with grief, the father was furious with the snake. He grabbed an axe, went over to the snake's hole, and waited for a chance to kill it. Soon, the snake appeared, and the man swung his axe, but he only managed to cut off the tip of the snake's tail before it quickly slithered back inside. Afterwards, the man tried to lure the snake out again by pretending he wanted to make peace. But the snake replied, "I can never be your friend after losing my tail, and you can never be mine after losing your child."

Wounds are never forgotten when those who caused them are still around.

title: THE CAGE-BIRD AND THE BAT
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A songbird was kept in a cage that hung just outside a window. She had a habit of singing at night, while all the other birds were fast asleep. One evening, a bat flew over, landed on the bars of her cage, and asked why she stayed quiet during the day but sang only at night. The bird replied, "I have a very good reason. Once, I sang during the day, and a hunter heard me. He set out his nets and caught me. Ever since, I only sing at night." But the bat answered, "It's no use doing that now that you're a captive. If only you had been this careful before you were caught, you might still be free."

Taking precautions after the danger has already happened doesn't help.

title: THE ASS AND HIS PURCHASER
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A man looking to buy a donkey went to the marketplace. After spotting one that seemed promising, he made a deal with the owner: he could take the donkey home for a trial run to see what it was like. At home, he led the new donkey into the stable with his other donkeys. The newcomer glanced around, then went straight over to stand next to the laziest and greediest donkey in the group. When the man saw this, he immediately put a halter on the new donkey, led it out, and returned it to its owner. The donkey’s owner was surprised to see him back so quickly and asked, “Already done testing him?” The man replied, “I don’t need any more trials—I could tell what kind of donkey he was by the company he chose.”

People are judged by the company they keep.

title: THE KID AND THE WOLF
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A young goat wandered away from the herd and was soon chased by a hungry wolf. Realizing he had no chance of escape, the kid faced the wolf and said, "I know there's no way you'll let me go. Since my time is almost up, could you at least let me enjoy it for a moment? Would you play a tune for me so I can dance before you eat me?" The wolf thought there was no harm in having a little music before dinner, so he pulled out his pipe and started playing while the kid danced. But before long, the gods who watched over the herd heard the music and came to see what was happening. As soon as they saw the wolf, they chased him away. While running off, the wolf looked back at the kid and said, "I got exactly what I deserved. I'm a butcher by trade, not a musician, and I never should have tried to please you."

title: THE DEBTOR AND HIS SOW
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A man from Athens found himself deep in debt, and his lender was demanding repayment right away. Since he didn’t have the money, he pleaded for more time, but the creditor wouldn’t hear of it—he insisted that the debt be paid immediately. Left with no other option, the debtor brought his only sow to the market to sell her. By chance, his creditor happened to be there too. Soon, a potential buyer approached and asked if the sow produced good litters. The debtor quickly replied, 'Oh yes, very fine ones! The amazing thing is, she gives birth to female piglets during the Mysteries and to males during the Panathenea.' These were local festivals—Athenians would sacrifice a sow at one and a boar at the other. At that point, the creditor, who had been listening in, cut in and said, 'That’s nothing! At the Dionysia, this sow even has kids!'

title: THE BALD HUNTSMAN
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A man who had lost all his hair decided to wear a wig. One day, he went out hunting. It was pretty windy, and before long, a big gust blew off both his hat and his wig, making everyone on the hunt laugh. But he joined in the joke and said, "Well, the hair that made this wig couldn't stay on the head it grew on, so it's no surprise it won't stay on mine."

title: THE HERDSMAN AND THE LOST BULL
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A herdsman was watching over his cattle when he noticed that one of his young bulls, one of the best in his herd, had gone missing. He immediately set out to find it, but after searching without luck, he promised that if he found the thief, he would offer a calf to Jupiter. He kept searching and eventually walked into a thicket, where he saw a lion eating the lost bull. Stricken with fear, he lifted his hands to the sky and called out, 'Great Jupiter, I promised to sacrifice a calf if I found the thief, but now I swear I’ll give you a full-grown bull if I can just escape from this lion unharmed!'

title: THE MULE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One morning, a Mule who had eaten too much and had nothing else to do, started feeling pretty proud of himself. He trotted around and bragged, "My father was definitely a noble horse, and I’m just like him!" But soon after, he was put in a harness and had to pull a heavy load for a long distance. By the end of the day, completely worn out from all the hard work, he sighed and thought, "Maybe I was wrong about my father; he must have been a donkey after all."

title: THE HOUND AND THE FOX
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Hound was wandering through the forest when he spotted a lion. Used to chasing smaller animals, the Hound thought the lion would make an impressive catch and started the pursuit. But the lion soon realized he was being followed. He stopped, turned to face the Hound, and let out a thunderous roar. Instantly, the Hound turned and ran as fast as he could in the opposite direction. Watching this, a Fox laughed and shouted, "Ha! There goes the coward who chased after a lion, only to run away as soon as it roared!"

title: THE FATHER AND HIS DAUGHTERS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A man had two daughters. He married one off to a gardener and the other to a potter. After some time, he decided to visit them and see how they were doing. First, he went to see the gardener's wife and asked how she and her husband were getting along. She told him that, overall, they were well, but said, "I really wish we'd get some good, heavy rain—the garden really needs it." Then, he visited the potter's wife and asked the same questions. She answered that everything was fine with her and her husband, but added, "I only wish we had some nice, dry weather to help dry all our pottery." Their father looked at her with a wry smile and said, "So, you want dry weather and your sister wants rain. I was about to pray for both your wishes, but it looks like I’d better just leave it out of my prayers altogether."

title: THE THIEF AND THE INNKEEPER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A thief checked into an inn, planning to stay for several days while he searched for something to steal. He waited and waited, but no chance ever seemed to come. Then, one day, during a festival, the innkeeper appeared wearing a brand new coat and sat outside the inn to enjoy some fresh air. As soon as the thief caught sight of that coat, he felt an intense desire to have it for himself. With no business to attend to, he strolled over and took a seat next to the innkeeper, sparking up a conversation. They talked for a while, and then, without warning, the thief let out a huge yawn and howled like a wolf. Concerned, the innkeeper asked what was wrong. The thief replied, "Let me tell you about myself, sir, but first, I need you to look after my clothes for me. I plan to leave them with you. These yawning fits are troubling—I can't say why I have them. Maybe it's punishment for my past misdeeds. The truth is that after I yawn three times, I turn into a ravenous wolf and attack people!" With that, he yawned again and howled just like before. The innkeeper, believing every word, panicked at the thought of facing a wolf, leaped up, and rushed to run inside. But the thief grabbed hold of his coat, pleading, "Wait, sir! Stay and look after my clothes, otherwise I won't be able to get them back!" As he spoke, he opened his mouth and yawned yet again for the third time. Terrified of being devoured by a wolf, the innkeeper hurriedly took off his coat, which the thief now held, dashed into the inn, and locked the door behind him. With that, the thief calmly made off with his prize.

title: THE PACK-ASS AND THE WILD ASS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
One day, a Wild Ass was roaming around when he stumbled upon a Pack-Ass lying comfortably in a sunny spot, clearly enjoying himself. Curiously, he approached and remarked, "You’re so lucky! Your nice shiny coat shows how well you’re taken care of. I really envy you!" However, not long after, the Wild Ass encountered the same Pack-Ass again, but this time he was struggling under a heavy load, with his driver trailing behind and hitting him with a thick stick. "Oh, my friend," the Wild Ass said, "I don’t envy you anymore. I can see that you definitely pay a high price for your comforts."

Benefits that come with a steep cost are often just questionable gifts.

title: THE ASS AND HIS MASTERS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A gardener had an ass who really struggled. He was underfed, carried heavy loads, and got beaten often. The ass decided to ask Jupiter for a better situation and requested to be handed over to a different master. In response, Jupiter sent Mercury to the gardener with the message to sell the ass to a potter, which he did. However, the ass was still unhappy because he ended up working even harder than before. Once again, he appealed to Jupiter for help, and Jupiter kindly arranged for him to be sold to a tanner. But when the ass realized what this new master did for a living, he cried out in despair, "Why didn’t I just stay with my previous masters, no matter how tough it was? They would have at least given me a decent burial, but now I’ll end up in the tanning vat!"

Servants often don’t realize how good they have it until they find themselves with someone worse.

title: THE PACK-ASS, THE WILD ASS, AND THE LION
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A Wild Ass spotted a Pack-Ass trudging along with a heavy load and couldn't help but mock him about his life of servitude. "Your life is so miserable compared to mine! I'm as free as the wind and never lift a finger to work. When I'm hungry, I just head to the hills, and there's more than enough food for me. But you? You rely on your owner for meals, and he forces you to carry heavy loads every day while he beats you mercilessly!" Just then, a Lion appeared, but he didn't bother the Pack-Ass since the driver was there. Instead, he pounced on the Wild Ass, who had no one to defend him, and without hesitation, made a meal out of him.

There's no point in being your own master if you can't defend yourself.

title: THE ANT
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
Once, ants were actually men who earned their living by working the land. However, they were never satisfied with the fruits of their own labor. They constantly eyed their neighbors’ crops and fruits with envy, stealing whatever they could whenever the opportunity arose, adding it to their own stash. Eventually, their greed angered Jupiter so much that he transformed them into ants. Yet, even though their bodies had changed, their true nature stayed the same. To this day, you can find them scurrying through the fields, collecting and hoarding the hard-earned produce of others.

You can punish a thief, but their greedy nature never changes.

title: THE FROGS AND THE WELL
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
Two frogs were living together in a marsh. During a particularly hot summer, the marsh dried up, so they went off in search of a new home because frogs love damp spots if they can find them. Eventually, they stumbled upon a deep well. One of the frogs peered down into it and said to the other, "This looks like a cool spot. Let’s jump in and settle here." However, the other frog, who was more sensible, replied, "Not so fast, my friend. What if this well dries up like the marsh? How will we get out then?"

Think carefully before you take action.

title: THE CRAB AND THE FOX
author: Aesop
themes: the importance of staying true to oneself
text_chunk:
One day, a Crab decided to leave the shoreline behind and move to a lush, green meadow a little inland. It looked inviting and seemed like a great place to find food. However, a hungry Fox was nearby, spotted the Crab, and caught him. Just before the Fox was about to make the Crab his meal, the Crab said, "I guess I brought this on myself; I shouldn't have left my home in the sea and tried to fit in here on land."

Be happy with what you have.

title: THE FOX AND THE GRASSHOPPER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Grasshopper was perched high up, chirping away in the branches of a tree. Below her, a Fox listened intently, thinking about how delicious she would be as a snack. So, he devised a clever trick to lure her down. Standing right beneath her, he showered her with compliments about her song, saying he would love to get to know the owner of such a beautiful voice. However, the Grasshopper was wise to his game. She replied, “You’ve got it all wrong, my good sir. There’s no way I’m coming down. I've kept my distance from you and your kind ever since I spotted the wings of many grasshoppers scattered at the entrance of a fox's den.”

title: THE FARMER, HIS BOY, AND THE ROOKS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A farmer had just planted a field of wheat and was keeping a close eye on it because a bunch of rooks and starlings kept landing there and munching on the seeds. His boy was with him, carrying a sling, and every time the farmer needed the sling, the starlings would pick up on it and warn the rooks, making them fly off instantly. So, the farmer came up with a clever plan. "Hey, son," he said, "we need to outsmart these birds. From now on, instead of saying 'sling,' I’ll just say 'humph!' and you need to hand me the sling right away." Soon enough, the whole flock returned. "Humph!" the farmer called out, but the starlings ignored him this time, giving him a chance to throw several stones into the group, hitting one on the head, another in the leg, and another in the wing before they managed to escape. As they hurried away, they bumped into some cranes, who asked what was going on. "What’s the problem?" one of the rooks replied. "It’s those troublemakers, humans, causing all the issues. Don’t get close to them. They have a way of saying one thing but meaning another, and that’s already cost some of our poor friends their lives."

title: THE ASS AND THE DOG
author: Aesop
text_chunk: An Ass and a Dog were traveling together when they stumbled upon a sealed package lying on the ground. The Ass picked it up, broke the seal, and began to read the contents out loud to the Dog. As he continued, it turned out to be all about grass, barley, and hay—basically, everything that Asses love to eat. The Dog quickly grew bored with this and, losing his patience, exclaimed, "Can't you skip ahead a bit and see if there's anything about meat and bones?" The Ass looked through the whole packet but didn’t find anything like that, so he responded, "Sorry, there's nothing like that here." The Dog then remarked in frustration, "Just throw it away! What’s the use of something like that?"

title: THE ASS CARRYING THE IMAGE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A man placed an Image on the back of his donkey to take it to one of the town's temples. As they traveled down the road, every person they passed uncovered their heads and bowed in reverence to the Image. However, the donkey misinterpreted this gesture, thinking everyone was showing respect to him, which made him feel quite important. Eventually, he became so full of himself that he believed he could do anything he wanted. In a show of defiance against the weight he was carrying, he suddenly stopped and refused to move any further. His driver, frustrated by the donkey's stubbornness, struck him repeatedly with his stick while saying, "Oh, you foolish creature, do you really think anyone would worship a donkey?"

People who take credit for others' accomplishments are often in for a rude awakening.

title: THE ATHENIAN AND THE THEBAN
author: Aesop
text_chunk: An Athenian and a Theban were traveling together, chatting away like travelers often do. After covering a lot of different topics, they shifted their conversation to heroes, a subject that's always interesting but not always enlightening. Each one eagerly praised the heroes from his own city until the Theban claimed that Hercules was the greatest hero to have ever lived and now held a top spot among the gods. The Athenian argued that Theseus was far more impressive, pointing out that Theseus had enjoyed a life full of blessings, while Hercules had once found himself in a position as a servant. The Athenian was smooth with his words, typical of his kind, so the Theban, unable to keep up with his banter, finally said in frustration, "Fine, you win; I just hope that when our heroes are upset with us, Athens faces the wrath of Hercules, while Thebes only deals with the anger of Theseus."

title: THE GOATHERD AND THE GOAT
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
One day, a Goatherd was rounding up his goats to take them back to the fold when one of them wandered off and refused to come back. He spent a lot of time calling and whistling for her, but the Goat completely ignored him. Frustrated, he finally threw a stone at her and accidentally broke one of her horns. Feeling panicked, he pleaded with her not to tell his master. But she shot back, 'You foolish guy, my horn would shout loud enough even if I stayed quiet.'

There's no point in trying to hide something that can't be hidden.

title: THE SHEEP AND THE DOG
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Once there was a flock of Sheep who were unhappy with how the shepherd treated them compared to his Dog. "Your behavior is really odd and, frankly, unfair," they said. "We give you wool, lambs, and milk, yet all you give us is grass, and we have to find that ourselves! The Dog does nothing for you, and still, you share delicious scraps from your table with him!" The Dog overheard their complaints and immediately spoke up, "You're absolutely right! Just think about it: where would you be without me? Thieves would take you, and wolves would eat you! If I didn't watch over you, you would be too scared to even eat grass!" The Sheep had to admit he was right, and from then on, they never complained about how their master treated the Dog.

title: THE SHEPHERD AND THE WOLF
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
One day, a Shepherd came across a Wolf's Cub wandering in the fields. He decided to bring the Cub home and raise him alongside his dogs. As the Cub grew up, he started helping the dogs chase after any wolf that tried to steal a sheep from their flock. There were times when the dogs couldn’t catch the thief and, feeling defeated, would head back home. But the Wolf would keep going on his own. When he finally caught up to the thief, he would pause to share the feast with him before returning to the Shepherd. Whenever there was a stretch of time without any sheep being taken, he would go ahead and snatch one for himself, sharing it with the dogs afterward. The Shepherd began to have his doubts and, eventually, he caught the Wolf in the act. In response, he tied a rope around the Wolf’s neck and hanged him from the nearest tree.

What’s bred in the bone will surely come out in the flesh.

title: THE LION, JUPITER, AND THE ELEPHANT
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Despite being a massive and powerful creature with sharp teeth and claws, the Lion had a secret fear: he couldn’t stand the sound of a rooster crowing. Whenever he heard it, he would run away in terror. Frustrated by this weakness, he complained to Jupiter, blaming him for making him this way. Jupiter responded that it wasn’t his fault and pointed out that, aside from this one fear, the Lion had every reason to be content with his strengths. Yet, the Lion couldn’t shake his feelings of shame over his cowardice, wishing he could just disappear. While in this troubled state, he crossed paths with the Elephant and started a conversation. The Lion noticed that the Elephant was constantly perking up his ears as if he were listening intently for something. Curious, the Lion asked him about it. Just then, a gnat buzzed by, and the Elephant replied, "Do you see that tiny little insect? I’m so scared it might fly into my ear; if it does, I’m finished!" Hearing this made the Lion feel better. He thought to himself, "If such a massive creature like the Elephant is afraid of a gnat, then I shouldn’t feel too ashamed about being scared of a rooster, who is so much bigger than that little bug."

title: THE PIG AND THE SHEEP
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One day, a Pig stumbled into a meadow where a group of Sheep were grazing. The shepherd spotted him and was about to take him away to the butcher when the Pig started squealing loudly and fighting to escape. The Sheep scolded him for causing such a ruckus and said, "The shepherd catches us all the time and takes us away like that, but we don't make a fuss." The Pig replied, "That's true, but our situations are completely different: he wants you for your wool, but he wants me for my bacon."

title: THE GARDENER AND HIS DOG
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A gardener's dog fell into a deep well, the same well where his master drew water to nourish the plants in his garden using a rope and bucket. When the gardener couldn’t get the dog out with those tools, he decided to climb down into the well himself to rescue him. However, the dog thought his master was there to drown him, so as soon as the gardener got close, the dog bit him hard, causing a lot of pain. Realizing this, the gardener left the dog behind and climbed back out of the well, saying, "I guess it’s my own fault for trying to save someone who clearly wants to die."

title: THE RIVERS AND THE SEA
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Once upon a time, all the Rivers came together to protest against the Sea for turning their waters salty. "When we flow into you," they said to the Sea, "we are fresh and drinkable. But once we mix with you, our waters become as salty and unpleasant as yours." The Sea simply responded, "Stay away from me, and you'll stay fresh."

title: THE LION IN LOVE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Lion fell head over heels for the daughter of a cottage owner and wanted to marry her. However, her father wasn't keen on the idea of such a fierce husband. Not wanting to upset the Lion, he came up with a clever plan. He approached the Lion and said, "You know, I believe you would be a great husband for my daughter, but I can’t allow your marriage unless you let me remove your teeth and trim your nails, since my daughter is really scared of them." The Lion, completely smitten, agreed without hesitation to this idea. But once he had been disarmed, the Cottager no longer felt threatened and chased him away with a club.

title: THE BEE-KEEPER
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A thief sneaked into a beehive when the beekeeper was away and took all the honey. When the beekeeper returned and discovered the hives were empty, he felt devastated and stared at them for a long time. Soon, the bees came back from foraging for nectar. They found their hives in disarray and saw the beekeeper standing there, so they buzzed over to him, ready to defend their home with their stingers. This made him angry, and he shouted, "You ungrateful little insects! You let the thief who stole my honey walk away without consequences, and now you come after me, the one who has always cared for you!"

When you strike back, make sure you're targeting the right person.

title: THE WOLF AND THE HORSE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
One day, a Wolf was wandering around when he stumbled upon a field of oats. Since he couldn’t eat them, he was about to move on when a Horse trotted by. "Hey there!" said the Wolf, "check out this amazing field of oats. I’ve left it completely untouched just for you, and I can’t wait to hear the sound of you munching on that delicious grain." The Horse replied, "If wolves could eat oats, my dear friend, you wouldn’t have hesitated to fill your belly instead of just enjoying the sound of me eating."

It's not a noble thing to give away what doesn't benefit you.

title: THE BAT, THE BRAMBLE, AND THE SEAGULL
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A Bat, a Bramble, and a Seagull decided to team up and go on a trading adventure together. The Bat borrowed some money to fund their journey, the Bramble stocked up on a variety of clothes, and the Seagull brought along a supply of lead. They set sail, but eventually, a massive storm hit, causing their boat and all their cargo to sink. Thankfully, all three managed to swim to safety. Since then, the Seagull has been flying across the ocean, diving underwater every now and then to search for the lost lead. Meanwhile, the Bat has become so fearful of facing his lenders that he stays hidden during the day and only comes out at night to find food. As for the Bramble, it grabs the clothes of anyone passing by, hoping to recognize and reclaim the lost garments.

People are generally more eager to get back what they've lost than to seek out what they don't have.

title: THE DOG AND THE WOLF
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A Dog was basking in the sun in front of a farmyard gate when a Wolf suddenly leaped out and was about to devour him. The Dog pleaded for his life, saying, "Look at how thin I am. I'd be such a poor meal for you! But if you just wait a few days, my master is throwing a feast soon. I'll be enjoying all the rich leftovers, and by then, I'll be nice and plump. That’s when you can feast on me." The Wolf thought this sounded like a great idea and decided to leave. Later, he returned to the farmyard and spotted the Dog sitting safely on the stable roof. "Come down," he called. "It’s time for you to keep our deal, remember?" The Dog coolly replied, "Listen, my friend, if you ever catch me lounging by the gate again, don’t wait for a feast."

Once bitten, twice shy.

title: THE WASP AND THE SNAKE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A wasp landed on the head of a snake and not only stung him several times but stubbornly held on. The snake, frantic with pain, tried everything he could think of to shake off the wasp, but nothing worked. Finally, in a moment of desperation, he shouted, "I will kill you, even if it costs me my own life!" Then, he placed his head, with the wasp still clinging to it, under the wheel of a passing wagon, and they both met their end together.

title: THE EAGLE AND THE BEETLE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
An Eagle was chasing a hare that was sprinting for its life, completely frantic and unsure of where to turn for help. Suddenly, it spotted a Beetle and asked it for assistance. As the Eagle approached, the Beetle warned it not to harm the hare, claiming it was under its protection. However, the Eagle overlooked the tiny Beetle, snatched the hare, and devoured it. The Beetle never forgot this betrayal. It kept a close watch on the Eagle's nest, and whenever the Eagle laid an egg, the Beetle would fly up, knock it out of the nest, and break it. The Eagle eventually became so distressed about her missing eggs that she went to Jupiter, the guardian of Eagles, and pleaded for a safe place to nest. Jupiter allowed her to lay her eggs in his lap. But the clever Beetle saw this and fashioned a ball of dirt the size of an Eagle's egg, then flew up and placed it in Jupiter's lap. When Jupiter noticed the dirt, he stood up to shake it off his robe, forgetting about the eggs, which tumbled out and broke just like before. Since then, it’s believed that Eagles never lay their eggs during the time when Beetles are around.

Sometimes, the weak find unexpected ways to take revenge on those who insult them, even when they appear far stronger.

title: THE FOWLER AND THE LARK
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A birdcatcher was busy setting up his nets to catch small birds when a lark approached him and asked, "What are you up to?" The birdcatcher replied, "I’m working on founding a city," and then he moved a short distance away and hid. The lark, intrigued, inspected the nets closely, and soon spotted the bait. Excited, she hopped onto the nets to grab it but ended up getting stuck in the tangled mesh. The birdcatcher rushed over and caught her quickly. "What a fool I was!" the lark exclaimed. "But if this is the kind of city you’re building, it’ll take forever to find enough fools to fill it."

title: THE FISHERMAN PIPING
author: Aesop
themes: the relationship between effort and reward
text_chunk: One day, a Fisherman who could play the flute headed down to the seashore with his nets and his trusty flute. He found a spot on a large rock and began to play a cheerful tune, hoping that the music would coax the fish to leap out of the water. He played for a while, but to his disappointment, not a single fish showed up. Finally, he put down his flute and tossed his net into the sea, managing to catch a massive haul of fish. Once he had landed them and watched as they flopped around on the shore, he exclaimed, "You little rascals! You wouldn’t dance when I played, but now that I've stopped, you can’t help but jump around!"

title: THE WEASEL AND THE MAN
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One day, a man captured a weasel that had been sneaking around his house. Just as he was about to drown it in a tub of water, the weasel pleaded for its life, saying, "Please, you can't really have the heart to kill me! Think about how helpful I've been in getting rid of the mice and lizards that used to overrun your home. Show me some gratitude and spare my life." The man replied, "I admit you’ve been somewhat useful, but who was it that killed the chickens? Who swiped the meat? No, you cause far more trouble than you’re worth, and you’re going to die."

title: THE PLOUGHMAN, THE ASS, AND THE OX
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A ploughman hitched up his ox and his donkey together to get his field plowed. It wasn’t the greatest team, but it was the best he could manage since he only had one ox.
