At the end of the day, when they were finally unhitched from the yoke, the donkey turned to the ox and said, "That was a tough day! Who's going to carry the master home now?" The ox was taken aback by the question. "Well," he replied, "it’s you, as always."

title: DEMADES AND HIS FABLE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Demades the orator was giving a speech in the Assembly in Athens, but the crowd was completely uninterested in what he was saying. So, he paused and said, "Gentlemen, I’d like to share one of Aesop's fables with you." This caught everyone’s attention. He continued, "Once, Demeter, a Swallow, and an Eel were on a journey together and came across a river that had no bridge. The Swallow flew over it, and the Eel swam through it." Then he stopped. "What about Demeter?" shouted several people from the audience. "Demeter," he answered, "is quite upset with you for focusing on fables instead of the important affairs of the state."

title: THE MONKEY AND THE DOLPHIN
author: Aesop
text_chunk: When people set off on a journey, they often take small pets like lap dogs or monkeys to keep them company. So, it happened that a man returning to Athens from the East had a pet monkey traveling with him. As they approached the coast of Attica, a fierce storm suddenly hit, and the ship capsized. Everyone on board was thrown into the water and scrambled to save themselves by swimming, including the monkey. A dolphin spotted him and, thinking he was a human, swam over. The dolphin let the monkey climb onto his back and started swimming towards the shore. When they got close to Piraeus, the port of Athens, the dolphin asked the monkey if he was Athenian. The monkey replied that he was and boasted about his noble family. 'Then, you must know the Piraeus,' the dolphin continued. The monkey, misunderstanding, thought he meant some important official and said, 'Oh, yes, he's a very old friend of mine.' Realizing he had been tricked, the dolphin was so disgusted that he dove underwater, leaving the poor monkey to drown.

title: THE CROW AND THE SNAKE
author: Aesop
themes: the unexpected dangers in seeking quick gains
text_chunk: A hungry Crow noticed a Snake dozing in a sunny spot. Seizing the opportunity, he grasped the Snake in his claws, intending to take it to a quiet place for a meal. Just as he was about to enjoy his prize, the Snake lifted its head and bit him. It was a venomous Snake, and the bite was deadly. As the Crow felt life slipping away, he lamented, "What a cruel twist of fate! I thought I had found a treasure, and now it has cost me my life!"

title: THE DOGS AND THE FOX
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One day, a group of dogs stumbled upon a lion's skin and started gnawing at it with their teeth. Just then, a fox walked by and said, "You all think you’re really tough, huh? But if that were a real lion, you’d soon discover that his claws are much sharper than your teeth."

title: THE NIGHTINGALE AND THE HAWK
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Nightingale was perched on a branch of an oak tree, singing as she usually did. Suddenly, a hungry Hawk spotted her and swooped down, capturing her in his claws. Just as he was about to tear her apart, she cried out, begging for her life. "I'm not big enough to make a decent meal for you," she pleaded. "You should look for bigger birds to hunt instead." The Hawk looked at her with disdain and replied, "Do you really think I'm that foolish? Why would I give up an easy catch for a chance at something better when there's no sign of it?"

title: THE ROSE AND THE AMARANTH
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Rose and an Amaranth grew side by side in a garden. The Amaranth looked at her neighbor and said, "I really envy your beauty and wonderful fragrance! It’s no surprise that everyone loves you so much." The Rose replied, her voice tinged with sadness, "Oh, my dear friend, I only bloom for a little while; my petals soon wither and fall, and then I’m gone. But your flowers never lose their charm, even when picked; they last forever."

title: THE MAN, THE HORSE, THE OX, AND THE DOG
author: Aesop
text_chunk: On a winter day when a fierce storm raged outside, a Horse, an Ox, and a Dog came to the door of a Man, asking for shelter. The Man welcomed them in without hesitation. Since they were cold and soaked, he quickly lit a fire to warm them up. He provided oats for the Horse, hay for the Ox, and shared the leftovers of his own meal with the Dog. Once the storm calmed down and it was time for them to leave, they wanted to express their gratitude in a special way. They decided to divide the life of the Man among themselves, giving each part the qualities unique to them. The Horse claimed youth, which is why young men can be so spirited and restless. The Ox took middle age, making men in that stage diligent and dependable. Finally, the Dog took on old age, explaining why older men can often be grumpy and irritable, and why, like dogs, they tend to be loyal to those who care for them, while being snappy towards strangers or those they find unappealing.

title: THE WOLVES, THE SHEEP, AND THE RAM
author: Aesop
text_chunk: The Wolves sent a group to talk to the Sheep about making a lasting peace, but they had a catch: the Sheep had to give up their loyal sheep-dogs to be killed immediately. The naive Sheep agreed to these terms, but an old Ram, who had gained wisdom over the years, stepped in and said, "How can we trust that we can live peacefully with you? Even with the dogs here to protect us, we’re still not safe from your deadly attacks!"

title: THE SWAN
author: Aesop
text_chunk: It's said that a swan only sings once in its life, and that’s when it knows its time is almost up. One day, a man who had heard stories about the swan’s beautiful song spotted one for sale at the market. Excited, he bought the swan and took it home. A few days later, he hosted a dinner for some friends and wanted to impress them with the swan's singing. But to his disappointment, the swan stayed completely silent. As time passed and the swan grew older, it finally realized its end was near and let out a hauntingly beautiful song. Upon hearing it, the man felt a surge of anger. "If this bird only sings when it's about to die, how foolish I was to want to hear its song! I should have just put it out of its misery instead of hoping for a performance!"

title: THE SNAKE AND JUPITER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A snake was constantly getting stepped on by both people and animals. This was happening because he was long and couldn't lift himself off the ground. Frustrated, he decided to go talk to Jupiter about the dangers he faced. However, Jupiter didn't feel much empathy for him. "I bet if you'd bitten the first person to step on you, the rest would have been more careful where they placed their feet," he replied.

title: THE WOLF AND HIS SHADOW
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One day, a Wolf was wandering across the open field as the sun began to set. He was taken aback by the size of his own shadow and thought to himself, "I had no idea I was this big. Can you believe I was ever scared of a lion? I should be the King of the beasts, not him!" Confident and oblivious to the danger around him, he strutted about, fully convinced of his superiority. But just then, a lion pounced on him and started to eat him. In despair, he lamented, "If only I hadn’t lost touch with reality, I wouldn’t have fallen victim to my own delusions."

title: THE PLOUGHMAN AND THE WOLF
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One day, a Ploughman unhitched his oxen from the plow and took them to the water to drink. While he was gone, a half-starved Wolf showed up and went straight for the plow, starting to chew on the leather straps connected to the yoke. As he gnawed away desperately, hoping to find something to eat, he got himself tangled in the harness. Frightened, he struggled to escape, pulling at the straps as if trying to drag the plow with him. Just then, the Ploughman returned and, seeing the chaos, shouted, "Hey, you old rascal! I wish you’d stop stealing and start doing some honest work instead!"

title: MERCURY AND THE MAN BITTEN BY AN ANT
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One day, a man watched a ship sink, taking its entire crew down with it, and he angrily remarked on how unfair the gods were. "They don't care at all about a person's character," he said, "allowing both the good and the bad to meet the same fate." Nearby, there was an ant hill, and just as he was finishing his rant, an ant bit his foot. Frustrated, he turned to the ant hill and stomped on it, crushing hundreds of innocent ants. Suddenly, Mercury appeared and struck him with his staff, saying, "You hypocrite, where’s your sense of justice now?"

title: THE WILY LION
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Lion was eyeing a plump Bull munching away in a meadow, and he couldn't help but imagine the grand feast that Bull would make. But he hesitated to pounce, worried about the Bull's sharp horns. However, hunger soon pushed him to take action. Since a direct attack didn’t seem smart, he decided to use trickery instead. Approaching the Bull in a friendly way, he said, "I just have to say, I really admire your amazing build. What an impressive head! Your shoulders and thighs are so powerful! But, my friend, why on earth are you using those ugly horns? They must be so clumsy and unsightly. Honestly, you'd be much better off without them." The Bull, foolishly swayed by this flattery, agreed to have his horns removed; and without his only means of defense, he easily became the Lion's next meal.

title: THE PARROT AND THE CAT
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A man once got a parrot and let it roam around his house. The parrot loved its newfound freedom and quickly flew up to the mantelpiece, squawking loudly to its heart's content. The racket disturbed a cat that was sleeping comfortably on the hearth rug. The cat looked up at the noisy intruder and asked, "Who are you, and where did you come from?" The parrot replied, "Your owner just bought me and brought me home." The cat sneered, "You cheeky bird, how dare you come in here and make all that noise? I’ve lived here my entire life, and if I dare to meow, they throw things at me and chase me all over the place." The parrot shot back, "Hey, lady, keep it down. People love my voice, but yours? That's just a total headache."

title: THE STAG AND THE LION
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A Stag was running from the hounds and found a cave, thinking it would be a safe place to hide. But as luck would have it, the cave was already occupied by a Lion, and the Stag quickly became the Lion's next meal. "How unfortunate I am," he exclaimed, "I escaped the dogs only to end up in the jaws of a Lion."

Out of the frying pan and into the fire.

title: THE IMPOSTOR
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A man became very sick, and while he was in a desperate state, he made a vow to the gods that he would sacrifice a hundred oxen if they helped him recover. The gods, curious to see if he would keep his promise, allowed him to get better quickly. However, he didn’t have a single ox, so he crafted a hundred small oxen out of wax and placed them on an altar, declaring, "Gods, I call you to witness that I have fulfilled my vow." The gods decided to teach him a lesson, and sent him a dream instructing him to go to the beach where he would find a hundred crowns waiting for him. Excited, he rushed to the shore but ran into a group of robbers. They captured him and sold him as a slave, and when they sold him, he fetched a price of exactly one hundred crowns.

Don't promise more than you can actually deliver.

title: THE DOGS AND THE HIDES
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Once upon a time, a group of hungry Dogs spotted some Hides soaking in a river. They realized they couldn't reach them because the water was too deep. So, they came together and decided to keep drinking from the river until it became shallow enough for them to grab the Hides. However, long before that could happen, they overindulged and ended up bursting from all the drinking.

title: THE LION, THE FOX, AND THE ASS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
One day, a Lion, a Fox, and a Donkey set out to hunt together. They quickly managed to catch a huge prize, and the Lion told the Donkey to divide it among them. The Donkey split the spoils into three equal parts and politely suggested that the others choose their share. This infuriated the Lion, who lunged at the Donkey and ripped him apart. After that, the Lion turned to the Fox and commanded him to redistribute the catch. The Fox cleverly collected almost everything into a big pile for the Lion's share, leaving just the tiniest piece for himself. "My good friend," the Lion said, "how did you manage to do that so expertly?" The Fox replied, "Me? Oh, I learned from the Donkey's mistake."

It's wise to learn from the mistakes of others.

title: THE FOWLER, THE PARTRIDGE, AND THE COCK
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One evening, as a Fowler settled down to a meager meal of herbs and bread, a friend dropped by unexpectedly. With nothing to serve, he went outside and caught a tame Partridge that he used as a decoy. Just as he was about to wring her neck, she pleaded, "You can't kill me! What will you do without me the next time you go fowling? How will you lure the birds to your nets?" Hearing this, he decided to let her go and headed to his hen-house, where he had a plump young Cock. When the Cock saw what was happening, he too begged for his life, saying, "If you kill me, how will you know when it's night? Who will wake you up in the morning for work?" The Fowler replied, "I know you're helpful for keeping track of time, but I can't send my friend to bed without supper." And with that, he caught the Cock and wrung his neck.

title: THE GNAT AND THE LION
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One day, a gnat flew up to a lion and declared, "I'm not scared of you at all! I don't even think you're stronger than I am. What does your strength really mean? You can scratch with your claws and bite with your teeth, just like an angry woman! That's it. But I'm stronger than you—if you don’t believe me, let’s have a fight and find out!" With that, the gnat sounded its horn and zipped in to bite the lion on the nose. When the lion felt the sting, he rushed to swat the little gnat away, scratching his own nose in the process and making it bleed, but he couldn’t harm the gnat at all, which buzzed away proudly, reveling in its success. However, not long after, it got stuck in a spider's web and was captured and eaten by the spider, proving that it fell victim to a tiny creature after boasting about defeating the King of the Beasts.

title: THE FARMER AND HIS DOGS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A farmer found himself trapped at his farm by a fierce snowstorm, unable to go out for supplies for himself and his family. First, he had to kill his sheep for food. As the storm raged on, he moved on to his goats. Finally, with no end in sight for the bad weather, he was forced to slaughter his oxen to survive. Watching their owner go through the animals one by one, the dogs exchanged worried glances and said, "We need to get out of here, or we might be next!"

title: THE EAGLE AND THE FOX
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
An Eagle and a Fox became close friends and decided to live near each other, thinking that being together would make their friendship even stronger. So, the Eagle built her nest high up in a tall tree, while the Fox settled at the base of it in a thicket and gave birth to a litter of cubs. One day, while the Fox was out searching for food, the Eagle, who was also hunting for her young, swooped down into the thicket, grabbed the Fox's cubs, and flew them up to her nest to feed her family. When the Fox returned and realized what had happened, she wasn't just sad about losing her cubs; she was boiling with rage because she couldn't reach the Eagle to get back at her for her betrayal. So, she sat nearby and cursed her. But it didn't take long for the Fox to get her revenge. Some villagers were sacrificing a goat at a nearby altar when the Eagle flew down and snatched a piece of burning meat to take back to her nest. A strong wind blew, causing the nest to catch fire, and as a result, her fledglings tumbled to the ground, partially roasted. It was then that the Fox rushed over and devoured them right in front of the Eagle.

A false sense of security may evade human consequences, but it can never escape divine judgment.

title: THE BUTCHER AND HIS CUSTOMERS
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
Two men were at a butcher's stall in the marketplace, looking to buy some meat. While the butcher's back was turned for just a moment, one of the men quickly grabbed a piece of meat and shoved it under the other man's cloak, hiding it from view. When the butcher turned back around, he immediately noticed the missing meat and accused them of stealing it. The man who had taken the meat insisted he didn’t have it, while the man who was hiding it claimed he hadn’t taken it. The butcher was convinced they were lying, but simply said, "You can fool me with your dishonesty, but you can't fool the gods, and they won’t let you escape so easily."

Dishonesty often leads to serious consequences.

title: HERCULES AND MINERVA
author: Aesop
text_chunk: Hercules was traveling down a narrow road when he noticed something on the ground in front of him that looked like an apple. Curious, he stepped on it with his heel. To his surprise, instead of being crushed, it doubled in size. When he hit it again with his club, it grew to an enormous size and completely blocked the road. He dropped his club, standing there in shock. At that moment, Minerva appeared and said to him, "Just leave it be, my friend. What you’re looking at is the apple of discord. If you don’t interfere with it, it stays small like it was originally; but if you act violently, it will grow into something huge like you see now."

title: THE FOX WHO SERVED A LION
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A Lion had a Fox who served him, and every time they went hunting, the Fox would find the prey while the Lion pounced on it and made the kill. They would split their catch according to an agreed ratio, but the Lion always ended up with a massive portion while the Fox barely got anything. This didn’t sit well with the Fox, so he decided to go out on his own. He started by trying to snatch a lamb from a flock of sheep, but the shepherd spotted him and unleashed his dogs. Suddenly, the hunter became the hunted, and the dogs quickly caught up with him and finished him off.

It's better to serve safely than to be free and face danger.

title: THE QUACK DOCTOR
author: Aesop
text_chunk: There was a man who became ill and had to stay in bed. He saw several doctors over time, and all of them, except for one, said that his life was not in immediate danger, but his sickness might stick around for a while. The last doctor he consulted took a very different approach. He told the man to brace himself for the worst: "You have less than twenty-four hours to live," he said, "and unfortunately, there’s not much I can do for you." In an unexpected twist, the doctor was completely wrong. Just a few days later, the man got out of bed and went for a walk, looking pale, almost like a ghost. While on his walk, he ran into the doctor who had predicted his death. "Well, well," the doctor said, surprised, "how are you? You must have just come back from the other side. Tell me, how are our friends doing over there?" "They're doing just fine," the man replied, "because they’ve drunk the water of forgetfulness and left all their life troubles behind. By the way, just before I departed, the authorities were talking about prosecuting all the doctors for not letting sick people die naturally, instead using their skills to keep them alive. They were going to charge you as well until I told them you weren’t really a doctor, just a fake."

title: THE LION, THE WOLF, AND THE FOX
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A sick and elderly Lion lay in his den, and every animal in the forest came to check on him, except for the Fox. The Wolf saw this as a great chance to settle some scores with the Fox, so he pointed out the Fox's absence to the Lion, saying, "Look, Your Majesty, everyone is here to see how you are doing, except for the Fox. He hasn’t bothered to come near you and clearly doesn’t care if you’re well or unwell." Just then, the Fox walked in and overheard the Wolf's remark. The Lion, visibly upset, roared at him, but the Fox quickly asked for the chance to explain why he hadn’t shown up. He said, "No one cares for you more than I do, Your Majesty. While you were ill, I’ve been visiting doctors in search of a remedy for your condition." The Lion asked, "And have you found a cure?" The Fox replied, "Yes, Your Majesty! Here’s what you need to do: skin a Wolf and wear his pelt while it’s still warm." Acting on this advice, the Lion turned and struck the Wolf dead with a single blow of his paw to try out the Fox's strange prescription. The Fox then chuckled to himself, thinking, "That’s what happens when you stir up trouble."

title: HERCULES AND PLUTUS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: When Hercules joined the gods and attended a feast hosted by Jupiter, he greeted everyone warmly except for Plutus, the god of wealth. When Plutus came over to him, Hercules looked down at the ground, turned away, and pretended not to notice him. Jupiter noticed this behavior and was puzzled, so he asked Hercules why he was warm with all the other gods but not with Plutus. "Your Highness," replied Hercules, "I can't stand Plutus, and here’s why: back when we were on earth, I always saw him hanging out with dishonest people."

title: THE FOX AND THE LEOPARD
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Fox and a Leopard were having an argument about who looked better, with both insisting they were the most handsome. The Leopard boasted, "Check out my sleek coat; you can't compete with that!" The Fox countered, "Your coat might be nice, but my smarts are even better."

title: THE FOX AND THE HEDGEHOG
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Fox was trying to swim across a raging river when the current pulled him away. No matter how hard he fought, he was carried far downstream until, bruised and exhausted, he finally managed to drag himself onto dry land in a calm spot. As he lay there, completely drained of energy, a swarm of horseflies settled on him and began to suck his blood without any interruptions, since he was too weak to even swat them away. A Hedgehog spotted him and asked, "Should I help and brush the flies off you?" The Fox responded, "Oh no, please don’t! Those flies are already full and are taking hardly anything from me now. If you scare them away, another group of hungry flies will come and drain every last drop of blood I have left!"

title: THE CROW AND THE RAVEN
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
A Crow grew extremely jealous of a Raven because people saw the Raven as a bird that predicted the future and was highly respected. The Crow desperately wanted that same kind of reputation for herself. One day, when she spotted some travelers coming by, she perched on a branch of a tree by the road and cawed as loudly as she could. The travelers were startled by the sound, fearing it might be an ominous sign. But then one of them noticed the Crow and said to his friends, "Don’t worry, everyone, we can continue on our way without fear. It’s just a crow, and that doesn’t mean anything."

Those who pretend to be something they aren’t just end up looking foolish.

title: THE WITCH
author: Aesop
text_chunk: There was a Witch who claimed she could calm the gods’ anger with her special charms, a secret only she knew. She was quite successful and made a comfortable living off it. However, some people accused her of practicing black magic and took her to court, insisting that she should be executed for her supposed dealings with the Devil. The judges found her guilty and sentenced her to death. As she left the courtroom, one judge questioned her, saying, "You claim you can prevent the gods' wrath. If that’s true, why haven’t you been able to soften the hatred of people?"

title: THE OLD MAN AND DEATH
author: Aesop
text_chunk: An old man was gathering a bundle of sticks in the woods and started to carry them home. He had a long journey ahead and was feeling exhausted, having barely made it halfway. Frustrated, he dropped his load to the ground and shouted for Death to come and free him from his hard life. Hardly had he finished speaking when, to his shock, Death appeared before him, ready to oblige. He was nearly terrified, but managed to muster the courage to say, "Sir, if you'd be so kind, could you please help me lift my burden again?"

title: THE MISER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: There was a Miser who sold everything he owned and melted down his stash of gold into a single lump, which he buried secretly in a field. Every day, he would go visit that spot and often spend hours admiring his treasure. One of his servants noticed how often he went there, and one night, he decided to follow him. Waiting for the right moment, he dug up the gold and stole it. The next day, when the Miser returned to the spot as usual and found his treasure missing, he started to tear his hair out and moan over his loss. A neighbor saw him in this state and asked what was wrong. The Miser shared his misfortune, but the neighbor replied, "Don’t be so upset, my friend; just put a brick in the hole and check on it every day. You won’t be any worse off than before, because even when you had your gold, it didn’t help you at all."

title: THE FOXES AND THE RIVER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A group of Foxes gathered on the riverbank, eager to get a drink. However, the current was strong, and the water looked deep and intimidating, so they hesitated and stood at the edge, trying to boost each other's courage. Finally, one Fox, wanting to prove his bravery and make the others feel ashamed, declared, "I’m not scared at all! Watch me, I’ll just step right into the water!" No sooner had he done that than the current knocked him off his feet. When the others saw him being swept downstream, they shouted, "Don't leave us behind! Come back and show us how we can drink safely too!" He called back, "I’m afraid I can’t right now. I want to go to the seaside, and this current will take me there just fine. When I return, I’ll happily show you how to drink safely."

title: THE HORSE AND THE STAG
author: Aesop
themes: the dangers of trading freedom for help
text_chunk: Once upon a time, there was a Horse who roamed freely in a meadow that was all his own. But one day, a Stag wandered into the meadow and claimed that he had every right to graze there, even going after the best patches of grass for himself. The Horse, not thrilled to have an uninvited guest, decided to get back at the Stag. He approached a man and asked for help to drive the Stag away. "Sure," replied the man, "I can help, but you need to let me put a bridle in your mouth and ride on your back." The Horse agreed, and with the man's help, they quickly managed to chase the Stag out of the meadow. However, once the Stag was gone, the Horse realized with a sinking heart that he had traded one unwanted visitor for a master.

title: THE FOX AND THE BRAMBLE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: As a Fox was making his way through a hedge, he slipped and grabbed onto a Bramble to keep from falling. He ended up getting scratched pretty badly, and in frustration, he shouted at the Bramble, "I needed your help, and look how you’ve treated me! I would have preferred to fall!" The Bramble interrupted him and said, "You must have lost your sense, my friend, to grab onto me when I'm always snagging others."

title: THE FOX AND THE SNAKE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: As a Snake swam across a river, a strong current swept it away. Luckily, the Snake managed to grab onto a bundle of thorns that was floating by. It was soon racing down the river. A Fox spotted the scene from the riverbank and shouted, "Wow! That passenger really matches that ship!"

title: THE LION, THE FOX, AND THE STAG
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Lion was lying sick in his den, unable to hunt for himself. He called out to his friend, the Fox, who had come to check on him, saying, "My good friend, I need you to do me a favor. Could you go to that nearby wood and trick the big Stag who lives there into coming to my den? I have a craving for a stag's heart and brains for dinner." The Fox went to the wood, found the Stag, and told him, "Hey there! You're in luck. You know our King, the Lion? Well, he’s on his deathbed and has chosen you as his successor to rule over all the animals. Don't forget, I was the first to bring you this good news. Now I need to head back to him, and if I were you, I’d come along and pay him a visit for his last moments." The Stag, feeling flattered, followed the Fox to the Lion's den, oblivious to the real danger. As soon as he stepped inside, the Lion lunged at him, but misjudged his leap, allowing the Stag to escape with just torn ears, racing back to the safety of the woods. The Fox felt embarrassed, and the Lion was also upset, growing hungrier despite his illness. So, he urged the Fox to make another attempt to lure the Stag. "It'll be really tough this time," the Fox replied, "but I'll give it a shot." He headed back to the woods for a second time and found the Stag trying to calm down after his narrow escape. As soon as the Stag saw the Fox, he shouted, "You deceitful creature! What do you mean trying to trap me like that? Get lost, or I'll use my horns on you!" But the Fox was unbothered. "Oh, come on! You were such a coward! Did you really think the Lion meant you harm? He just wanted to share some royal secrets with you before you ran off like a frightened rabbit. You've really annoyed him, and there's a chance he might pick the wolf as King instead, unless you come back and show some courage. I swear he won't hurt you, and I’ll be right there by your side." Foolishly, the Stag was convinced to return, and this time the Lion didn't miss his chance. He overpowered the Stag and enjoyed a sumptuous feast. Meanwhile, the Fox waited for the perfect moment, and when the Lion looked away, he snatched the brains as a reward for his trouble. Eventually, the Lion started searching for the missing brains, of course, without any luck. The Fox, who had been watching, said, "Honestly, looking for the brains is pointless! Anyone who walked into a Lion’s den twice surely didn't have any brains to begin with."

title: THE MAN WHO LOST HIS SPADE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A man was busy digging in his vineyard, and one day when he arrived to work, he realized his spade was missing. Suspecting that one of his workers might have taken it, he asked them all about it. They each adamantly denied knowing anything, but he wasn't convinced by their claims. He insisted that they all go to town and swear an oath in a temple that they weren’t guilty of the theft. He didn't think much of the simple gods of the countryside and believed that the cleverer gods of the town would catch the thief. Once they got to town, the first thing they heard was the town crier announcing a reward for information about a thief who had stolen something from the city temple. "Well," thought the man, "maybe I should just head back home. If the gods in this town can’t even catch the thieves stealing from their own temples, there's no way they’ll help me find my spade."

title: THE PARTRIDGE AND THE FOWLER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A fowler trapped a partridge in his nets and was just about to snap its neck when the bird pleaded with him to let it live. "Please don’t kill me! If you spare me, I promise to lure more partridges into your nets," it said. The fowler replied, "No, I won’t spare you. I was going to kill you anyway, and after that deceitful speech, you’ve certainly earned your fate."

title: THE RUNAWAY SLAVE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A slave, unhappy with his situation, decided to escape from his master. It didn't take long for the master to notice he was gone, and he quickly jumped on his horse and set off to track down the runaway. Before long, he caught up with him, and the slave, hoping to dodge capture, slipped into a treadmill and hid inside. "Aha! That's the perfect spot for you, my friend!" the master exclaimed.

title: THE HUNTER AND THE WOODMAN
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Hunter was wandering through the forest, trying to find signs of a lion. As he walked, he spotted a Woodman chopping down a tree, so he approached him and asked if he had seen any lion footprints or knew where the lion made its home. The Woodman replied, "If you come with me, I can show you the lion itself." The Hunter instantly turned pale with fear, and his teeth began to chatter as he said, "Actually, I'm not looking for the lion itself, just his tracks. Thanks anyway!"

title: THE SERPENT AND THE EAGLE
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
An Eagle swooped down and grabbed a Serpent in his powerful talons, planning to fly off with it for a meal. However, the Serpent was quick and coiled around the Eagle in no time, leading to a fierce battle for survival between the two. A nearby countryman, who witnessed the struggle, rushed in to help the Eagle. He managed to free the bird from the Serpent's grip, allowing it to escape. In retaliation, the Serpent spat some of its venom into the man's drinking-horn. Just as the man, catching his breath after the effort, was about to drink from the horn, the Eagle swooped in and knocked it out of his hand, spilling the poison onto the ground.

One good deed deserves another.

title: THE ROGUE AND THE ORACLE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A rogue made a bet that he could prove the Oracle at Delphi to be unreliable by getting a false answer from it. So, on the day of the challenge, he arrived at the temple with a small bird hidden under his cloak. He asked the Oracle whether the bird he was holding was alive or dead. If the Oracle said 'dead,' he planned to reveal the live bird; if it said 'alive,' he would snap its neck and show that it was actually dead. However, the Oracle was too clever for him. The answer he received was this: 'Stranger, whether what you hold in your hand is alive or dead depends entirely on your own intent.'

title: THE HORSE AND THE ASS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Horse, feeling proud of his shiny harness, encountered an Ass on the main road. As the Ass slowly moved aside with his heavy load to let him pass, the Horse impatiently shouted that he could barely hold back from kicking him to hurry up. The Ass chose to stay quiet, but he couldn’t forget the Horse’s arrogance. Not long after, the Horse lost his strength and was sold by his owner to a farmer. One day, while hauling a cart filled with manure, he ran into the Ass again, who mocked him and said, "Ha! You never imagined you’d end up here, did you? You, who used to be so full of yourself! Where are all your fancy decorations now?"

title: THE DOG CHASING A WOLF
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Dog was chasing after a Wolf, and as he sprinted along, he couldn't help but feel proud of himself. He thought about how great he was, how strong his legs were, and how fast he was covering the ground. "Look at that Wolf," he mused to himself, "what a weak little creature he is. He knows he can't keep up with me, that's why he's running away!" Just then, the Wolf turned around and replied, "Don’t think for a second that I’m afraid of you, my friend. It’s your master that I’m worried about."

title: GRIEF AND HIS DUE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: When Jupiter was handing out privileges to the various gods, Grief was absent. Once all the others had received their shares, Grief finally showed up to claim what was rightfully his. Jupiter was unsure of how to respond since there was nothing left. Eventually, he decided that Grief would receive the tears that people shed for their loved ones who have passed away. Just like the other gods, Grief thrives on devotion; the more people grieve, the more he offers in return. So, it's not wise to mourn for too long after someone is gone, because Grief, who delights in such sorrow, will quickly find new reasons to bring more tears.

title: THE HAWK, THE KITE, AND THE PIGEONS
author: Aesop
themes: the dangers of trusting the wrong protector
text_chunk: In a particular dovecote, the Pigeons were being hunted by a Kite, who would swoop down from the sky now and then to snatch one of them away. To protect themselves from this threat, they decided to invite a Hawk into the dovecote for defense. However, they quickly regretted their decision, as the Hawk ended up killing more of them in just one day than the Kite had managed to take in an entire year.

title: THE WOMAN AND THE FARMER
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A woman who had recently lost her husband would visit his grave every day, grieving her loss. Nearby, a farmer, busy with his plowing, noticed the woman and felt drawn to her. He left his work to sit beside her and started crying too. She asked him, "Why are you crying?" He responded, "I've just lost my wife, who meant the world to me, and crying helps ease my pain." She replied, "I understand; I've lost my husband as well." They shared a moment of silence, mourning together. After a while, the farmer suggested, "Since we both share this sorrow, why don't we get married and live together? I can be your husband's replacement, and you can take my wife's place." The woman thought it was a reasonable idea and agreed, so they both wiped away their tears. However, while they were having this conversation, a thief had come and stolen the oxen the farmer had left tied to his plow. When the farmer realized his oxen were gone, he beat his chest and cried out in distress. Hearing his lament, the woman approached him and asked, "Are you still crying?" He replied, "Yes, and this time it's for real."

title: PROMETHEUS AND THE MAKING OF MAN
author: Aesop
text_chunk: When Jupiter asked him to, Prometheus began the task of creating Man and the other animals. Jupiter noticed that humans, the only rational beings, were vastly outnumbered by the animals that acted on instinct. He instructed Prometheus to fix this imbalance by transforming some of those animals into men. Prometheus followed his orders, which is why some people look like men but have the instincts of beasts.

title: THE SWALLOW AND THE CROW
author: Aesop
text_chunk: One day, a Swallow was bragging to a Crow about her origins. "I used to be a princess," she exclaimed, "the daughter of a King in Athens. But my husband treated me poorly and took away my tongue for a minor mistake. To keep me safe from any more harm, Juno transformed me into a bird." The Crow replied, "You talk plenty as it is. I can’t imagine how much you would have talked if you hadn’t lost your tongue."

title: THE HUNTER AND THE HORSEMAN
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Hunter was out looking for game and managed to catch a hare. As he was heading home with it, he met a man on horseback. The man said, "Looks like you had some fun, sir," and offered to buy the hare. The Hunter quickly agreed, but as soon as the Horseman had the hare in his grasp, he urged his horse into a fast gallop and took off. The Hunter dashed after him for a short while, but soon realized he had been conned. To save face, he shouted after the Horseman as loud as he could, "No worries, sir, no worries! Take your hare; I meant it as a gift all along!"

title: THE GOATHERD AND THE WILD GOATS
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A Goatherd was looking after his goats in the pasture when he noticed a group of Wild Goats approaching and mixing in with his flock. At the end of the day, he brought everyone home and put them all in the pen together. The next day, the weather took a turn for the worse, making it impossible for him to take them out as usual. So, he kept them all in the pen and fed them there. He gave his own goats just enough food to keep them from starving, but he provided the Wild Goats with as much as they could eat and then some. He was really hoping they would stay, thinking that if he fed them well, they wouldn’t want to leave. Once the weather cleared up, he took all of them out to pasture again, but as soon as they reached the hills, the Wild Goats broke away from the flock and dashed off. The Goatherd was incredibly frustrated and scolded them for their lack of gratitude. "You rascals!" he yelled. "How can you run away after everything I’ve done for you?" One of the Wild Goats turned around and replied, "Sure, you treated us well—too well, actually. That’s what made us cautious. If you treat newcomers like us way better than your own goats, it's likely that if another group of strange goats joins, we would be the ones neglected in favor of the new arrivals."

title: THE NIGHTINGALE AND THE SWALLOW
author: Aesop
text_chunk:
One day, a Swallow was chatting with a Nightingale and urged her to leave her leafy home and join her in living with humans, nesting under their roofs. The Nightingale responded, "There was a time when I, like you, lived among people. But the painful memories of the cruel treatment I endured back then have made me despise them. I will never go near their homes again."

The memories of past suffering brought back hurtful feelings.

title: THE TRAVELLER AND FORTUNE
author: Aesop
text_chunk: A traveler, worn out from a long journey, sat down right at the edge of a deep well and soon dozed off. He was just about to tumble in when Lady Fortune appeared and gently tapped him on the shoulder, urging him to move back. "Wake up, good sir, please," she said. "If you had fallen into the well, everyone would have blamed me, Fortune, instead of recognizing it was your own carelessness."

title: A Riddling Tale
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Three women were turned into flowers that grew in a beautiful field.

However, one of them had the special privilege of returning to her own home at night.

One evening, as dawn approached, she realized it was time to go back to her friends in the field and transform into a flower again.

Before she left, she said to her husband, "If you come to gather me this afternoon, I’ll be free and will stay with you for good."

He agreed to her plan.

Now, here's the puzzle: how did her husband recognize her when all the flowers looked exactly the same without any differences?

The answer lies in the fact that while she was at home at night, the dew didn’t fall on her like it did on the others, and that’s how he knew.

title: Allerleirauh
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a king who had a wife with golden hair.

She was so beautiful that no one on earth could compare to her.

Eventually, she fell ill, and feeling that she wouldn't be around much longer,

she called for the king and said, "If you decide to marry again after I’m gone,

please promise me you won't choose anyone who isn’t just as beautiful as I am,

with hair as golden as mine. You must make this promise."

The king promised her, and shortly afterward, she closed her eyes and passed away.

For a long time, the king was heartbroken and had no thoughts of marrying again.

But soon, his advisors said, "This can't go on. The king deserves to have a queen.

So messengers were sent out to find a bride who matched the beauty of the late queen.

However, no one in the whole world could be found who resembled her.

Even if they did, none had hair like hers. So the messengers returned empty-handed.

The king had a daughter who was just as beautiful as her late mother,

with the same golden hair. As she grew up, the king looked at her one day,

and saw that she was just like his late wife in every way. Suddenly, he felt a strong love for her.

He spoke to his advisors, "I will marry my daughter, as she is the only one who resembles my wife.

Otherwise, I can’t find another bride who is as beautiful as her."

When the advisors heard this, they were horrified and said, "God has forbidden a father to marry his daughter.

Nothing good can come from such a sin; it would bring ruin upon the kingdom."

The daughter was even more shocked upon learning about her father's intentions,

but she hoped to change his mind. So she said to him, "Before I agree to this,

I need three dresses: one as golden as the sun, another as silvery as the moon,

and a third as bright as the stars. Additionally, I want a mantle made of a thousand different furs,

title: Allerleirauh
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
and each animal in your kingdom must provide a piece of its skin for it."

She thought that would be impossible to gather, and it would help divert her father from his dark intentions.

But the king didn't back down, and he ordered the most talented maidens in the kingdom to weave the dresses,

one as golden as the sun, one as silvery as the moon, and one as bright as the stars.

His hunters were sent out to catch one of each kind of animal in the kingdom,

so they could make the mantle out of all their furs. Eventually, once everything was ready,

the king had the mantle spread out before her and declared, "The wedding will be tomorrow."

Seeing that she could no longer convince her father to change his mind,

the king's daughter decided to run away. That night, while everyone was asleep,

she got up, took three items from her belongings: a golden ring,

a golden spinning wheel, and a golden reel. She packed her beautiful dresses into a nutshell,

put on her fur mantle, and blackened her face and hands with soot.

Then she prayed to God and left, walking all night until she found herself in a vast forest.

Exhausted, she climbed into a hollow tree to rest and fell asleep.

The sun rose, and she continued to sleep even past noon.

Meanwhile, the king who owned the forest was out hunting.

When his dogs reached the tree where she was hiding, they sniffed around and barked.

The king turned to his hunters and said, "Look and find out what kind of wild animal is hiding in there!"

The hunters checked it out, and when they returned, they said,

"We've found a wondrous beast asleep in the hollow tree, and we've never seen one like it before.

Its coat is made of a thousand different furs, but it's still asleep."

The king ordered, "Try to catch it alive, and bring it to my carriage. We will take it with us."

When the hunters approached the girl, she awoke in a panic and cried out,

"I'm just a poor child abandoned by my parents. Please have mercy on me and take me with you!"

title: Allerleirauh
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The hunters replied, "Allerleirauh, you can help us in the kitchen, come on with us,

and you can clean the ashes." So they brought her back to the royal palace.

They told her to sleep in a closet under the stairs where no sunlight reached,

saying, "Hairy creature, this is where you will live." Then she was sent off to the kitchen,

where she collected wood and water, cleaned the hearth, plucked the chickens,

prepared the vegetables, and tidied up.

Allerleirauh endured this miserable life for a long time.

Oh, fair princess, what fate awaits you now? One day, a feast was held in the palace,

and she asked the cook, "May I go upstairs for a bit to watch the festivities? I’ll stay close to this door."

The cook replied, "Yes, go ahead, but be back in half an hour to clean the hearth."

So she took her oil lamp, returned to her corner, removed her fur dress,

and washed the soot off her face and hands, revealing her stunning beauty.

Then she opened her nutshell and took out the dress that sparkled like the sun.

Once she was ready, she went to the celebration, and everyone made way for her,

as no one recognized her, believing she was a princess.

The king approached, took her hand, and danced with her, thinking,

"I have never seen anyone so beautiful before."

After the dance, she curtsied, and when the king turned back around,

she had disappeared, leaving everyone baffled.

The guards were summoned and questioned, but no one had seen her.

Meanwhile, she had dashed back to her hiding spot, quickly changed out of her dress,

blacked her face again, put on her fur mantle, and reverted to being Allerleirauh.

As she returned to the kitchen, ready to sweep the ashes, the cook said,

"Leave that for tomorrow and prepare the king's soup instead.

I will also take a moment to enjoy the festivities, but don’t let any hairs fall in it,

or you’ll be punished by not getting any food."

title: Allerleirauh
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
So the cook headed upstairs, and Allerleirauh prepared the soup for the king,

making delicious bread soup. When it was ready, she fetched her golden ring from her den,

and placed it in the bowl the soup was served in.

After the dance, the king was given the soup to eat, and he found it so delightful that he thought,

"I've never had anything this good before." But when he reached the bottom of the bowl,

he discovered the golden ring and couldn’t figure out how it ended up there.

He ordered the cook to appear before him.

The cook trembled when he heard the command and said to Allerleirauh,

"Surely you let a hair fall into the soup, and if you did, you'll be punished."

Once in front of the king, the king asked him who had prepared the soup.

The cook replied, "I made it." But the king said, "That's not true,

because it was much better than usual, cooked differently."

Finally, the cook admitted, "I didn't make it; it was prepared by the hairy creature."

The king commanded, "Go and bring her to me."

When Allerleirauh arrived, the king asked, "Who are you?"

She replied, "I'm just a poor girl without parents."

He asked, "What use are you in my palace?"

She answered, "I'm only good for having boots thrown at my head."

Then he continued, "Where did you get the ring that was in the soup?"

She said, "I don't know anything about the ring." The king couldn't learn anything more and had to send her away.

Another festival took place after a while, and as before,

Allerleirauh asked the cook if she could go to watch the celebrations.

He agreed, but told her to return in half an hour to make the king his favorite bread soup.

She quickly ran to her den, washed herself, and took out the dress that was as silvery as the moon.

Then she went up, looking like a princess, and the king was thrilled to see her again.

title: Allerleirauh
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
As the dance began, the two danced together, but after the dance ended,

she once again slipped away so quickly that the king couldn’t follow her.

Once in her den, she transformed back into her hairy self and returned to the kitchen to prepare the soup.

When the cook left to head upstairs, she pulled out the little golden spinning wheel,

placing it in the bowl and covering it with soup.

The soup was served to the king again, and he enjoyed it just as much as before.

He summoned the cook again, who had to admit that it was Allerleirauh who made the soup.

Allerleirauh stood before the king once more, and when she said she only deserved to have boots thrown at her head,

she claimed to know nothing about the golden spinning wheel.

When the king held another festival for the third time, everything followed the same pattern.

The cook accused her of being a witch for always putting something in the soup that made it special,

but he let her go upstairs again when she begged so hard.

She adorned herself in the dress that shimmered like the stars and entered the hall.

The king danced with her again and believed she was the prettiest girl he had ever seen.

As they danced, he cleverly slipped a golden ring onto her finger, ensuring that the dance would last a long time.

When it finished, he tried to hold onto her hands, but she broke free and darted away,

disappearing into the crowd.

She raced back to her den beneath the stairs; however, since she had stayed too long,

she couldn’t fully change out of her lovely dress and only tossed her fur mantle over it.

In her haste, she didn’t completely blacken her face, leaving one finger white.

Allerleirauh hurried into the kitchen and made the king's bread soup,

and since the cook was away, she added her golden reel to it.

When the king discovered the reel in his bowl, he summoned Allerleirauh,

and noticed the white finger and the ring he had placed on it during the dance.

He took her hand and held her tightly, and when she tried to escape,

her fur mantle parted slightly, revealing the star dress underneath.

The king grasped the mantle, pulled it off her, and revealed her golden hair.

She stood before him, radiant and unable to conceal herself any longer.

Once she washed off the soot and ashes from her face, she was more beautiful than anyone ever seen.

The king said, "You are my beloved bride, and we will never part again."

So they got married and lived happily ever after.

title: Bearskin
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a young man who joined the army and conducted himself with great bravery.

He was always the first to charge into battle, even when bullets were flying all around.

Everything was fine as long as the war continued, but when peace was declared, he was dismissed.

The captain told him he was free to go wherever he wanted.

With no home to return to, as his parents had passed away, he sought out his brothers.

He pleaded with them to take him in and provide a place to stay until the war began again.

However, his brothers were heartless and said, "What use are you to us? You’re on your own; go fend for yourself."

Left with nothing but his gun, the soldier slung it over his shoulder and stepped out into the world.

He walked until he reached a vast heath, where he saw only a circle of trees.

Sitting down under the trees, he felt sorrowful and started to ponder his situation.

"I have no money," he thought, "I haven't learned any trade other than fighting, and now that there’s peace, I'm no longer needed. I can already tell I'm going to starve."

Suddenly, he heard a rustling sound and when he turned around, a strange man appeared before him.

The man wore a green coat and seemed quite dignified, but he had a horrible cloven foot.

"I know what you need," the man said. "You'll have gold and riches, more than you can spend, but first, I need to see if you’re truly fearless, so I don’t waste my money."

The soldier replied confidently, "A soldier and fear? Those two don’t mix! You can test me if you wish."

"Alright then," the man said, "turn around."

The soldier turned and saw a large bear approaching, growling menacingly.

"Oh, you've picked the wrong guy to mess with," the soldier called out. "I’ll tickle your nose and you won’t want to growl anymore!" He aimed and shot the bear right in the muzzle, which fell lifeless on the ground.

title: Bearskin
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"I can see you have courage," the stranger commented, "but there’s one more condition you must meet."

"As long as it doesn’t jeopardize my soul," the soldier replied, knowing exactly who he was dealing with. "If it does, I’m out."

"You can take care of that yourself," the man in the green coat said, "For the next seven years, you must not wash, comb your beard or hair, or cut your nails. You can’t even say the Lord's Prayer. I will give you a coat and a cloak that you must wear during this time. If you die within these seven years, you belong to me; if you live, you’ll be free and wealthy for the rest of your life."

The soldier, considering his dire situation and recalling how often he had faced death before, decided to take the risk and accepted the terms.

The devil removed his green coat and handed it to the soldier, saying, "As long as you wear this and reach into the pocket, you'll always find it filled with money."

Then he skinned the bear, stating, "This will be your cloak and your bed. You must only sleep on this skin; no other bed is allowed. Because of this attire, you will be known as Bearskin."

The soldier put on the coat, checked the pocket, and confirmed the truth of the man's words. He donned the bear skin and ventured into the world, enjoying himself without caring about the damage it might do to his wealth.

At first, he looked somewhat normal, but by the second year, he started to resemble a monster.

His hair nearly concealed his entire face, his beard felt rough and coarse, his fingers had grown into claws, and the dirt on his skin was so thick, it could have sprouted cress.

Whenever someone spotted him, they would run away in fear, but he still found shelter, thanks to his habit of giving money to the poor, asking them to pray that he wouldn’t die during the seven years.

title: Bearskin
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
In the fourth year, he tried to enter an inn, but the landlord refused to accommodate him, worried that the horses would be frightened.

However, when Bearskin pulled a handful of ducats out of his pocket, the landlord changed his mind and gave him a room in an outbuilding.

Bearskin had to promise not to be seen, to avoid damaging the inn's reputation.

One evening while he sat alone, wishing desperately for the seven years to end, he heard loud sobbing in a nearby room.

Feeling compassionate, he opened the door and saw an old man crying uncontrollably and wringing his hands.

Bearskin approached, but the man jumped up in alarm and tried to flee. However, when he realized Bearskin’s voice was human, he calmed down and through gentle words, Bearskin encouraged him to share his troubles.

The old man explained that his wealth had dwindled away, leaving him and his daughters on the brink of starvation, and he couldn’t afford to pay the innkeeper, who threatened to imprison him.

"If that’s your only problem," Bearskin said, "I have plenty of money." He called the innkeeper over, paid him, and even gave the old man a purse full of gold.

Overjoyed at being freed from his troubles, the old man didn’t know how to express his gratitude.

"Come with me," he said to Bearskin, "my daughters are all stunningly beautiful; choose one of them as your wife. Once she hears what you’ve done for me, she won’t refuse you.

You might look a bit odd, but I’m sure she can help you out." Bearskin was pleased by this suggestion and went along.

When the oldest daughter saw his face, she was terrified and ran away screaming.

The second sister stood still, looking him up and down, then said, "How can I marry someone who no longer looks human? I’d prefer the bear who wore a hussar's uniform and white gloves, at least he looked somewhat presentable!"

"If he were just ugly, I might get used to it," she added.

However, the youngest said, "Father, he must be a good person to have helped you, so if you promised him a bride, you have to keep that promise."

title: Bearskin
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
It was unfortunate that Bearskin's face was covered with dirt and hair, for otherwise, they might have noticed how happy he felt when he heard those words.

He removed a ring from his finger, broke it in two, and gave one half to her, keeping the other for himself.

Then, he wrote his name on her half and hers on his, asking her to take care of her piece. He bid her farewell, saying, "I still have to wander for three more years, and if I don't return by then, you’re free; I’ll be dead. But please pray to God for my life."

The heartbroken bride dressed in black, tears filling her eyes whenever she thought of her future husband.

Her sisters mocked her mercilessly.

"Be careful," said the eldest, "If you take his hand, he’ll dig his claws into you." "Watch out," the second added, "Bears have sweet tooth, and if he grows fond of you, he might eat you!"

"You’ll have to obey him, otherwise he’ll start growling," the first sister warned again, while the second said, "But the wedding will certainly be entertaining, as bears dance well."

The bride stayed silent, refusing to let them get to her. Meanwhile, Bearskin roamed around the world, doing good where he could and generously giving to the poor to pray for him.

Finally, on the last day of the seven years, he returned to the heath and sat under the trees once more.

Before long, the wind howled, and the devil appeared, glaring at him, then threw Bearskin his coat back, demanding his own green coat in return.

"Not yet," Bearskin replied. "You have to clean me up first."

Whether the devil wanted to or not, he had to fetch water, wash Bearskin, comb his hair, and cut his nails.

Once that was done, Bearskin looked like a valiant soldier and was far more handsome than he ever had been.

After the devil left, Bearskin felt lighthearted. He walked into town, donned an exquisite velvet coat, climbed into a carriage pulled by four white horses, and made his way to his bride’s house.

No one recognized him. The father mistook him for a high-ranking general and ushered him into the room where his daughters were seated.

He found himself between the two oldest, who served him wine and the finest cuts of meat, believing they had never seen anyone so handsome.

However, the bride sat across from him in her black dress, staring at her plate and saying nothing.

When he eventually asked her father if he could have one of his daughters as a wife, the two older sisters dashed off to their rooms to put on extravagant dresses, each convinced she would be the chosen one.

Once alone with his bride, the stranger revealed his half of the ring, tossing it into a glass of wine before handing it to her.

She took a sip, and when she found the half-ring at the bottom, her heart raced.

She fished out the matching piece, which she wore on a ribbon around her neck, and, when they were joined together, they fit perfectly.

Then he said, "I am your betrothed, the same man you saw as Bearskin, but by God's grace, I've regained my human form and have once again become clean."

He approached her, embraced her, and kissed her.

Just then, the two sisters returned fully dressed, and when they realized the handsome man had chosen the youngest and learned of his true identity, they fled in anger.

One sister drowned herself in a well, while the other hanged herself from a tree.

Later that evening, someone knocked on the door, and when the groom opened it, it was the devil in his green coat, who said, "Now I have two souls in place of your one."

title: Brides On Their Trial
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once, there was a young shepherd who really wanted to get married.

He knew three sisters, all of whom were just as beautiful.

It became tough for him to choose, and he couldn't decide which one to prefer.

So, he turned to his mother for advice.

She suggested inviting all three sisters over,

then serving them some cheese to see how they would eat it.

The young man followed her suggestion; the first sister swallowed the cheese with the rind still on.

The second sister quickly cut off the rind, but she did it so fast that she wasted a lot of good cheese, throwing it away.

The third sister carefully peeled off the rind, cutting just the right amount.

When the shepherd shared this with his mother, she told him to choose the third sister as his wife.

He followed her advice and lived happily ever after with her.

title: Brother and Sister
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The little brother took his sister’s hand and said, 'Ever since our mom passed away, we've had no happiness. Our stepmother,

punishes us every day. If we get too close to her, she kicks us away.

We only get scraps of hard bread for meals, the leftovers that no one else wants. It's even worse for the little dog under the table,

because she often tosses it bits of food. Oh, how we wish our mother could see us now! Let's venture out together into the big world.'

They spent the entire day walking across meadows, fields, and rocky paths. When it rained, the little sister remarked, 'Heaven and our hearts are both crying.'

By evening, they reached a vast forest and were so exhausted from hunger and sorrow that they climbed into a hollow tree and fell asleep.

The next day, they woke up to the sun shining brightly down into the tree. The brother said, 'Sister, I’m really thirsty. If only I could find a little brook to drink from! I think I hear one nearby.'

He stood up, took his sister's hand, and they went in search of the brook. But their wicked stepmother, who was a witch, had followed them in secret.

She had done something terrible; she bewitched all the brooks in the forest.

When they finally found a little brook bubbling happily over the stones, the brother was about to take a drink, but the sister heard it say as it flowed, 'Whoever drinks from me will become a tiger.'

'Whoever drinks from me will become a tiger!' The sister cried out, 'Please, dear brother, don’t drink, or you’ll turn into a wild beast and tear me apart.'

Although the brother was very thirsty, he decided not to drink. 'I’ll wait for the next spring,' he replied.

When they came to the next brook, the sister again heard it say, 'Whoever drinks from me will become a wolf.'

'Whoever drinks from me will become a wolf!' The sister begged, 'Please, dear brother, don’t drink or you’ll turn into a wolf and devour me.'

title: Brother and Sister
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The brother still didn’t drink, saying, 'I'll wait until we find the next spring, but I must drink soon, because I’m too thirsty.'

At the third brook, the sister heard it say, 'Whoever drinks from me will become a roebuck.'

'Whoever drinks from me will become a roebuck!' The sister pleaded, 'Oh, please, dear brother, don’t drink or you’ll turn into a roebuck and run away from me.'

But the brother knelt by the brook and took a sip. As soon as the first drops touched his lips, he transformed into a roebuck.

The sister cried for her poor bewitched brother, while the little roebuck also wept and sat sadly beside her.

Eventually, the girl said to the little roebuck, 'Please be quiet, dear friend. I will find a way to help you.'

She untied her golden garter and placed it around the roebuck's neck, then gathered rushes and wove them into a soft leash.

With the leash tied to the roebuck, she walked deeper into the forest.

After a long journey, they finally stumbled upon a small house. The girl peeked inside and, finding it empty, thought, 'We can live here.'

She started gathering leaves and moss to create a cozy bed for the roebuck.

Every morning, she would go out to collect roots, berries, and nuts for herself, while bringing tender grass for the roebuck, who would nibble happily from her hand.

In the evenings, when the sister was tired, she'd say her prayers, rest her head on the roebuck's back, which served as her pillow, and sleep peacefully.

If only her brother could return to his human form, their happiness would be complete.

They lived together like this for some time in the wilderness. But one day, the king organized a grand hunt in the forest.

The sound of horns, barking hounds, and the joyful shouting of the hunters echoed through the trees, which made the roebuck anxious to join in.

He said to his sister, 'I need to go to the hunt; I can’t stay away any longer.' He begged so much that she finally agreed.

title: Brother and Sister
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But she warned him, 'Promise you'll come back to me by evening. I have to lock the door to keep out the rough hunters, so knock and say, 'My little sister, let me in,' so I know it's you.

If you don’t say that, I won’t open the door.' The young roebuck bounded away, feeling excited and full of joy.

The king and his hunters spotted the beautiful roebuck and tried to catch him, but no matter how hard they tried, he always escaped.

When darkness fell, he dashed back to the cottage, knocked on the door, and said, 'My little sister, let me in.'

The door swung open, and he leaped inside, resting comfortably all night.

The next day, the hunt began again. When the roebuck heard the horns and cheers of the hunters, he couldn’t take it anymore.

He said, 'Sister, let me out! I have to join them!' She opened the door, reminding him, 'But you must come back again in the evening and say your pass-word.'

The king and his huntsmen chased the young roebuck again, but he was too quick and agile to catch.

This went on all day long, until evening came, and the hunters finally surrounded him.

One of them managed to wound him in the foot, causing him to limp and run slowly.

A hunter crept behind him to the cottage and heard him say, 'My little sister, let me in.' He saw the door fly open and shut quickly behind the roebuck.

The hunter reported everything to the king.

The king replied, 'Tomorrow we’ll hunt again.'

The little sister was terrified when she saw her dear roebuck was hurt. She cleaned the blood off his foot, bandaged it with herbs, and said, 'Rest now, my sweet roebuck, so you can heal.'

The wound was minor, and by morning the roebuck didn’t feel it at all.

But when he heard the hunting sounds again, he insisted, 'I must go back; I can’t bear to miss it.'

title: Brother and Sister
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
'They won’t catch me that easily!' The sister cried, 'This time they could kill you, and I would be left alone in the forest, all by myself.'

'Don’t leave me to despair,' the roebuck pleaded. 'When I hear the horns, I feel I have to leap!' Reluctantly, the sister opened the door for him, although her heart was heavy.

Joyful and full of life, the roebuck bounded out.

The king told his huntsmen, 'Chase him all day but make sure no harm comes to him.'

As soon as the sun set, the king said to the huntsman, 'Show me where the cottage in the woods is.'

Upon arriving at the door, he knocked and called out, 'Dear little sister, let me in.'

The door opened, and the king stepped inside to find a maiden more beautiful than anyone he’d ever seen.

The maiden was frightened to see not her roebuck but a man with a golden crown.

But the king smiled warmly at her, extended his hand, and said, 'Will you come with me to my palace and be my beloved wife?'

She happily replied, 'Yes, but my little roebuck must accompany me. I can’t leave him behind.'

The king assured her, 'He can stay with you for as long as you like and will want for nothing.' Just then, the roebuck dashed in.

The sister quickly tied him up with the rush rope, took him by the hand, and left the cottage with the king.

The king lifted the beautiful maiden onto his horse and brought her to his palace, where they held a grand wedding celebration.

Now she was the queen, and they lived happily together for a long time.

The roebuck was well cared for and would run happily around the palace.

Meanwhile, the wicked stepmother, who had caused the children to leave, had thought that the sister had been eaten by wild animals and the brother had been shot by hunters.

But when she heard of their happiness and good fortune, envy and jealousy festered in her heart, driving her to seek their downfall.

title: Brother and Sister
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Her ugly daughter, who had only one eye, scolded her mother, 'A queen? That should have been my fate!'

The old woman told her to be quiet, promising, 'In due time, I’ll make my plan.'

As time passed, the queen had a beautiful little boy, while the king was away hunting.

The old witch disguised herself as a maid and entered the queen’s chamber.

She told her, 'Come, the bath is ready! It’ll refresh and strengthen you—hurry before it gets cold.'

Her daughter was right there with her. They helped the weak queen into the bath, then shut the door and ran away.

But inside the bath, they’d made a fire so hot that it soon harmed the lovely young queen.

Once this was done, the old woman put a nightcap on her daughter and laid her in bed to impersonate the queen.

She made her daughter appear just like the queen, except she couldn’t fix her missing eye; so, her daughter had to lie on her good side.

That evening, when the king came home and heard about his son’s birth, he was overjoyed and went to see his beloved wife.

But the old woman quickly called out, 'Don’t let her see the light yet; she needs her rest.'

The king left, completely unaware that a fake queen lay in the bed.

But at midnight, when everyone slept, the nurse in the nursery noticed the door opening and the real queen entering.

She took the baby from the crib, cradled it in her arms, and nursed it. Afterward, she fluffed its pillow, laid the child back down, and tucked it in.

She didn’t forget about the roebuck either; she went over to stroke its back before quietly slipping out again.

The next morning, the nurse asked the guards if anyone had come into the palace last night, but they replied, 'No, we didn’t see anyone.'

This went on for many nights, during which the queen came back but never spoke a word.

The nurse saw her every time but didn’t dare to tell anyone.

After a while, the queen began to say, 'How is my child? How is my roebuck?'

'I’ll come twice more, and then never again.'

The nurse didn’t respond, but after the queen left, she told the king about it.

The king was alarmed and said, 'Tomorrow night I’ll keep watch by the child.'

That evening, he went into the nursery, and at midnight, the queen came back again, saying, 'How is my child? How is my roebuck?

I’ll come once more, and then never again.'

She nursed the child like usual before disappearing.

The king was afraid to speak to her, so he stayed hidden.

He waited another night, and when she returned, she said, 'How is my child? How is my roebuck? This time I’ll come, and then never again.'

Unable to contain himself, the king sprang forward and said, 'You must be my dear wife.'

She replied, 'Yes, I am your beloved wife.' In that moment, she regained her former beauty, fresh and rosy.

Then, she revealed to the king what the wicked witch and her daughter had done to her.

The king ordered both women brought before the judge, where justice was served.

The daughter was sent into the forest to be torn apart by wild beasts, while the witch was burned to ashes.

As soon as the witch was gone, the roebuck transformed back into his human form, and the brother and sister lived happily together for the rest of their lives.

title: Brother Lustig
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a big war, and when it was over,

many soldiers got their discharge papers. Brother Lustig was among those who received his

discharge, but he left with nothing except a small loaf of ammunition bread,

and four kreuzers in cash.

As he walked away, St. Peter appeared in his path, disguised as a

poor beggar. When Brother Lustig approached, the beggar asked him for some help.

Brother Lustig replied, "Dear beggar, what can I give you? I

just got out of the army and all I have is this tiny loaf of ammunition bread and four kreuzers.

When those are gone, I'll have to beg just like you. Still, I will

do my best." He then divided the loaf into four pieces and handed one piece and a kreuzer over to the apostle.

St. Peter thanked him and continued on his way. Shortly after, he transformed himself back into a beggar.

When Brother Lustig approached him again, he asked for another gift.

Again, Brother Lustig offered a quarter of his loaf and another kreuzer. St. Peter expressed his gratitude and went on.

For a third time, he appeared in yet another beggar's form and approached Brother Lustig.

Once again, Brother Lustig willingly gave him another quarter of bread and a third kreuzer.

St. Peter thanked him once more, and Brother Lustig kept walking, now with just one quarter of the loaf.

He decided to stop at an inn, where he ate the bread and ordered a beer worth one kreuzer.

After enjoying his drink, he continued on his journey.

Soon, St. Peter, disguised as a discharged soldier, met Brother Lustig and said, "Good day, comrade! Can you spare a bit of bread and a kreuzer for a drink?"

Brother Lustig answered, "Where am I to get it? I’ve been discharged and all I have is a loaf of ammunition bread and four kreuzers.

I met three beggars along the way and gave each one a quarter of bread and a kreuzer. I ate the last quarter at the inn, so now my pockets are

empty. If you have nothing either, we can beg together."

title: Brother Lustig
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"No," replied St. Peter, "we don't need to do that. I know a bit about medicine, and I can earn what we need."

"Well, I don’t know anything about that, so I’ll just go beg on my own," said Brother Lustig. "Come with me, and if I earn anything, you can have half of it."

"All right," Brother Lustig agreed, and they started off together. They arrived at a peasant's house, from which they heard loud

sobs and cries. Curious, they walked in and found the husband lying there, gravely ill, while his wife wept uncontrollably.

"Stop your howling and crying!" shouted St. Peter. "I’ll make your husband well again!" He then took out a salve from his pocket and cured the sick man instantly.

The husband and wife were overjoyed and asked how they could repay him.

"What would you like to give me?" But St. Peter refused to accept anything, and the more the peasants insisted, the more he declined.

Brother Lustig nudged St. Peter and said, "Take something! Surely we could use it!"

Finally, the woman brought forward a lamb, urging St. Peter to take it, but he refused. Then Brother Lustig gave him a nudge and said, "Please take it, you fool! We really need it!"

At last, St. Peter relented and said, "Fine, I'll take the lamb, but I won’t carry it. If you insist on giving it to me, you’ll have to carry it yourself."

"That’s nothing," Brother Lustig said confidently. "I can handle it!" He slung the lamb over his shoulder.

They set off again but soon Brother Lustig started to feel the weight of the lamb and felt hungry, so he suggested to St. Peter, "Look, there’s a good spot; we could cook the lamb and eat it there."

"Sure, do what you like," St. Peter replied, "but I won’t help with the cooking. I’ll walk around a bit until it’s ready.

title: Brother Lustig
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Just don’t start eating until I get back; I’ll return at the right time."

"Okay, go ahead," Brother Lustig said. "I know how to cook."

St. Peter walked away, and Brother Lustig killed the lamb, built a fire, tossed the meat into a pot, and began to cook.

When the lamb was nearly done and St. Peter still hadn’t returned, Brother Lustig took it out, cut it up, and found the heart.

"The heart is the best part!" he exclaimed and tasted it, but in the end, he ate the whole thing.

Finally, St. Peter returned and said, "You can finish the entire lamb by yourself; I’ll just have the scraps."

Brother Lustig picked up a knife and fork, pretending to search through the lamb's meat, insisting he couldn't find the heart.

"But where could it be?" asked St. Peter.  "I don’t know," Brother Lustig replied, "but look at us, what fools we are! We both forgot that a lamb doesn’t have a heart at all."

St. Peter was baffled. "Every animal has a heart; why wouldn’t a lamb?" "No, I promise you, a lamb has no heart. Just think about it, and you’ll see it’s true."

"Fine, if there’s no heart, I don’t want any of the lamb," St. Peter finally decided. "You can eat it all yourself."

"What I can't eat now, I'll take with me in my knapsack," Brother Lustig declared, eating half the lamb and stashing the rest.

They continued on until they encountered a large stream of water blocking their path.

St. Peter said, "You can go first." "No," replied Brother Lustig, "you go first." He thought, if the water is too deep, I'll just stay behind.
