St. Peter confidently waded through, and the water reached his knees. Brother Lustig followed but found it rising to his throat.

title: Brother Lustig
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Help me!" he cried to St. Peter.

St. Peter called out, "Will you confess you ate the lamb’s heart?" "No!" he yelled back, "I didn’t eat it."

Even as the water was about to swallow him, St. Peter didn’t let him drown, making the water recede and pulling him to safety.

Continuing on, they arrived at a kingdom where they heard that the king’s daughter was gravely ill.

Brother Lustig said to St. Peter, "This is our chance! If we can heal her, we’ll be set for life!"

But St. Peter was moving too slowly for him. "Come on, move your legs, brother, we need to get there before it's too late!"

However, St. Peter continued to walk at his own pace, despite Brother Lustig’s urging, and they soon found out that the princess had died.

"Now we’re doomed," Brother Lustig lamented. "This is what happens when you dawdle."

"Just be quiet," St. Peter replied. "I can do more than heal the sick; I can bring the dead back to life."

"Well, if you can do that," Brother Lustig said, "then we’re all set, but you should at least get half the kingdom for that!"

So they reached the royal palace where everyone was mourning, but St. Peter told the king he could bring his daughter back to life.

He was led to her, and said, "Bring me a kettle and some water," and when it was prepared, he ordered everyone to leave the room, leaving only Brother Lustig.

Then he cut off all the dead girl’s limbs, tossed them in the water, lit a fire underneath, and boiled them.

As the flesh fell away from the bones, he took out the clean, white bones and positioned them back together on a table.

Once arranged, he stepped back and said three times, "In the name of the holy trinity, dead woman, arise!" At the third invocation, the princess awakened, alive, healthy, and beautiful.

title: Brother Lustig
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The king was overwhelmed with joy and said to St. Peter, "Ask for your reward! I’d give you half my kingdom!" But St. Peter humbly refused.

Brother Lustig thought to himself, "You fool! Don’t be so naïve. If you don’t want anything, I do!" St. Peter still wouldn’t accept anything.

The king noticed Brother Lustig's eagerness and told his treasurer to fill Brother Lustig’s knapsack with gold.

Afterward, they continued on their way, and when they reached a forest, St. Peter said to Brother Lustig, "Now, let’s divide the gold."

"Sure!" Brother Lustig replied.

So St. Peter divided the gold into three piles. Brother Lustig wondered, What kind of weird idea is this? There are only two of us.

But St. Peter assured him, "I divided it correctly. One pile is for me, one for you, and one for the one who ate the heart."

"I ate that," Brother Lustig said quickly, gathering all the gold.

"You can trust my word!"

St. Peter questioned, "How can that be true if lambs don’t have hearts?"

"What are you thinking, brother? Of course lambs have hearts like any other animal!" "Well, if that's the case, I guess I’ll keep the gold for myself, but I won’t travel with you any longer."

"Suit yourself," Brother Lustig replied.

St. Peter went on his own path, and Brother Lustig thought, It's probably better this way. That saint is something else.

He had plenty of money, but didn’t know how to use it wisely, and soon squandered everything.

Before long, he found himself with only four kreuzers left and passed by an inn.

Deciding the money must go, he ordered three kreuzers’ worth of wine and one kreuzer’s worth of bread.

As he sat there drinking, the mouthwatering aroma of roast goose wafted into his nose.

Curious, Brother Lustig looked around and saw the innkeeper roasting two geese in the oven.

title: Brother Lustig
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Remembering what St. Peter had said about his knapsack, he thought, why not try and get those geese?" He stepped outside.

Once outside, he shouted, "I wish those two roasted geese would come out of the oven and into my knapsack!" When he opened it, there they were!

"Perfect!" he exclaimed, thrilled at his luck. He went to a meadow and began to enjoy his feast.

While he was eating, two journeymen came by, eyeing the untouched goose with hungry stares.

Brother Lustig thought, One goose is enough for me, so he called the two men over and said, "Take the goose and enjoy it in my honor!"

They thanked him and went to the inn, ordering a half bottle of wine and some bread, excited to eat the goose.

When the hostess saw them, she exclaimed to her husband, "Those two are eating a goose! Check if it’s one of ours from the oven!"

The landlord rushed over and realized the oven was empty. "What?" he shouted, "You thieves think you can get away with eating a goose like that? Pay up now, or I'll give you a beating!"

The two men insisted, "We’re not thieves! A discharged soldier gave us this goose out there in the meadow!"

"Don’t try to trick me! The soldier was here, but he left by the door, an honest man. You two are the real thieves and must pay!"

When they couldn’t pay, the landlord grabbed a stick and chased them out.

Brother Lustig moved on and found a magnificent castle beside a terrible inn.

He approached the inn and asked for a place to sleep, but the landlord turned him away, saying, "We’re full of nobility here!"

Brother Lustig said, "It surprises me they’d choose you over that fancy castle!" The host replied, "Trust me, it’s no small fee to stay there. No one has survived a night in that castle yet."

"If others have failed, I’ll give it a try," Brother Lustig asserted.

title: Brother Lustig
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Leave it! You'll lose your neck over this!" the host warned. "If it won't kill me right away," Brother Lustig retorted, "just give me the key, along with some good food and wine."

The innkeeper reluctantly handed over the key, along with food and wine, and Brother Lustig went to the castle.

He enjoyed his supper, and when he got tired, laid down on the ground since there were no beds.

Though he quickly fell asleep, he was awakened by a terrible noise.

When he opened his eyes, he found nine ugly devils dancing in a circle.

Brother Lustig said, "Dance all you want, but stay away from me!" But the devils kept getting closer.

Feeling annoyed, Brother Lustig shouted, "Stop! You’ll see how I handle you!" and grabbed a leg from a chair.

But nine devils against one soldier were still too many, and while he fought the ones in front, others grabbed him from behind.

"Enough!" he yelled. "All nine of you get in my knapsack!" In an instant, they were trapped inside.

He quickly buckled it up and tossed it into a corner.

Suddenly, everything went quiet, and Brother Lustig lay down, falling back asleep.

The innkeeper and the nobleman who owned the castle came in to check on him, and when they saw he was unharmed, they were shocked.

"Have the spirits done you no harm?" the nobleman asked.

Brother Lustig replied, "The reason they didn’t harm me is that I have all nine of them locked up in my knapsack.

You can now live in peace in your castle; they won’t bother you again." The nobleman thanked him and rewarded him handsomely.

He even wanted Brother Lustig to remain in his service, providing for him for life.

But Brother Lustig replied, "No, thank you. I prefer being a wanderer. I want to explore more."

Brother Lustig set off again, coming across a blacksmith. He placed the knapsack with the nine devils on the anvil.

He asked the smith and his apprentices to strike it. They hammered away, producing howls of horror from the devils inside.

title: Brother Lustig
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When he opened the knapsack afterward, eight of the devils were dead, but one managed to slip out and escape back to hell.

Then Brother Lustig continued traveling the world, and many stories are told about him. Eventually, he grew old and sought a change.

He visited a hermit known for his wisdom and said, "I’m tired of wandering around and want to be good enough to enter heaven."

The hermit replied, "There are two paths: one is wide and pleasant, leading to hell, the other is narrow and challenging, leading to heaven."

Brother Lustig thought, I’d be foolish to choose the narrow, rough road.

So he set off on the wide, nice road and eventually reached a big black door, the entrance to hell.

He knocked, and the door-keeper peeked out to see who it was.

When he saw Brother Lustig, he became terrified, for he was the very same devil who had once been trapped in the knapsack.

The keeper slammed the door shut and ran to warn the highest devil, "There’s someone outside with a knapsack who wants to come in!"

"Don’t let him in! He might trap us all again!" said the highest devil, remembering the beating he received.

They yelled at Brother Lustig from inside the door to leave, stating he couldn’t come in.

"If I can’t stay here," Brother Lustig said to himself, "I’ll find a place in heaven instead," and he moved on.

He walked until he reached the door of heaven and knocked.

St. Peter was there as the door-keeper. When Brother Lustig recognized him, he thought, I’ll do better here.

But St. Peter said, "I find it hard to believe that you want to come to heaven. Why should I let you in?"

"Please let me in, brother. I need a place to go. I wouldn’t have come here if they’d welcomed me in hell!"

"No. You may not enter," St. Peter replied.

"If you won’t let me in, take your knapsack back; I’ll not take anything from you."

St. Peter said, "Give it back then," and Brother Lustig handed the knapsack through the bars.

St. Peter hung it beside his seat. Then Brother Lustig declared, "Now I wish to be inside my knapsack." In an instant, he was inside it, and in heaven. St. Peter was left with no choice but to let him stay.

title: Cat and Mouse in Partnership
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
There once was a cat who became friends with a mouse.

The cat talked so much about the love and friendship she felt for the mouse that eventually, the mouse agreed to live together and share a home.

But we need to prepare for winter, or we’ll go hungry, the cat warned.

And you, little mouse, can’t go everywhere, or you might get caught in a trap one day.

Following this good advice, they bought a pot of fat, but they weren’t sure where to store it.

After thinking it through, the cat suggested, "I can’t think of any better place than the church, because nobody dares to take anything from there.

We’ll put it under the altar and only touch it when we’re really in need."

So they safely stored the pot, but it wasn’t long before the cat started craving it.

She said to the mouse, "I have something to share, little mouse! My cousin just had a little baby, and she asked me to be its godmother.

The baby is white with brown spots, and I’ll hold it at the christening.

Let me go out today while you take care of the house by yourself."

"Sure!" the mouse replied. "You go ahead, and if you get anything really tasty, don’t forget about me; I’d love a sip of sweet red christening wine too."

However, everything the cat said was a lie; she had no cousin and wasn’t invited to be a godmother.

Instead, she went straight to the church, sneaked to the pot of fat, and started licking the top off.

Then she walked around on the rooftops of the town, looking for chances to enjoy herself, and basked in the sun, licking her lips whenever she thought of the pot.

It wasn’t until evening that she returned home.

"Look who’s back!" the mouse greeted. "You must have had a fun day!"

"It was all fine," the cat replied. "What name did they give the baby?"

"Top Off," the cat answered casually.

title: Cat and Mouse in Partnership
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Top Off?" the mouse exclaimed. "That’s a strange and unusual name. Is it common in your family?"

"What does it matter?" the cat said. "It’s no worse than the name Crumb-Stealer, like your godchildren are called."

Soon enough, the cat was hit with another craving.

She said to the mouse, "You need to do me a favor again and watch the house by yourself without me.

I’ve been asked to be a godmother again, and since the baby has a white ring around its neck, I can’t say no."

The good-natured mouse agreed, but the cat snuck behind the town walls to the church and gobbled half of the pot of fat.

"Nothing tastes as good as what you keep all to yourself," she thought, feeling satisfied with what she’d done.

When she returned home, the mouse asked, "What name did they give the child this time?"

"Half-Done," the cat replied.

"Half-Done? What do you mean? I’ve never heard that name in my life; I bet it’s not even in the calendar!"

The cat, eager for more food, said, "All good things come in threes, and I’ve been invited to be a godmother again.

The baby is all black but has white paws, and other than that, there isn’t a single white hair on it.

That’s a rare occurrence; please let me go again, won’t you?"

"Top Off, Half-Done," the mouse replied, "they are such odd names, and they really make me think.

You just stay home in your dark gray fur and bushy tail, filled with your own worries because you don’t go out during the day."

While the cat was away, the mouse tidied up the house, but the greedy cat finished off the entire pot of fat.

The cat thought to herself, "When everything is eaten up, there’s peace.

I’m all full and won’t come home until night."

The moment she arrived back home, the mouse asked, "What did they name the third baby?"

"You won’t find it any better than the others," the cat answered. "He’s called All-Gone."

"All-Gone?" the mouse exclaimed. "That’s the most suspicious name yet! I’ve never seen it written down anywhere.

All-Gone? What could that even mean?" As she shook her head, she curled up.

From that moment on, no one invited the cat to be a godmother anymore.

But as winter arrived and there was nothing left outside, the mouse remembered their stored food and said,

"Come on, cat, let’s go to our pot of fat that we saved up for ourselves—we’ll enjoy it!"

"Oh, you’ll enjoy it as much as you’d enjoy putting your fancy little tongue out the window," the cat replied.

They made their way to the pot, and although it was still in its place, it was completely empty.

"Oh no!" the mouse said. "Now I understand what happened; everything is clear now. You’re not a true friend.

You devoured all the food while you were pretending to be a godmother! First Top Off, then Half-Done, and now..."

"Will you be quiet?" the cat yelled. "One more word and I’ll eat you too!"

The words "All-Gone" were already on the poor mouse's lips, and before she could say them, the cat leaped on her, seized her, and swallowed her whole.

Indeed, that’s just how it went.

title: Cinderella
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
A wealthy man's wife fell ill, and feeling her time was short,

she called her only daughter to her bedside.

"My dear child, always be good and kind, and trust in God,

because He will protect you. I will watch over you from heaven.

With that, she closed her eyes and passed away.

Every day, the girl visited her mother’s grave and cried, staying true to her sweet and gentle nature.

As winter came, a blanket of snow covered the grave, and by the time the spring sun melted it away,

her father had already remarried.

His new wife brought along two daughters,

both beautiful and fair on the outside, but cruel and mean on the inside.

This was the start of a tough time for poor Cinderella.

"Why should that silly goose sit with us in the parlor?" they sneered.

"If you want to eat, you need to earn your keep. Out you go, kitchen maid!"

They took away her lovely clothes, dressed her in an old gray gown, and forced her into wooden shoes.

"Look at our fancy princess, all dressed up!" they laughed, leading her into the kitchen.

There, she had to work hard from dawn until dusk, waking up before sunrise to carry water, light fires, cook, and wash.

On top of that, her stepsisters treated her cruelly, mocking her and throwing her lentils and peas into the ashes, forcing her to pick them out again.

At night, after toiling until she was exhausted, Cinderella had no bed to sleep in and was left to curl up by the hearth, covered in soot.

Because of this, she was always filthy and unkempt, and they mocked her by calling her Cinderella.

One day, her father was going to the fair, and he asked his two stepdaughters what he should bring back for them.

"Beautiful dresses!" said one. "Pearls and jewels!" chimed in the other.

And then he turned to Cinderella and asked, "What would you like, Cinderella?"

"Father, please bring me back the first branch that touches your hat on the way home," she replied.

title: Cinderella
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
So he bought beautiful dresses, pearls, and jewels for his two stepdaughters, and on his way home, as he rode through a green thicket,

a hazel twig brushed against him and knocked off his hat. He broke off the branch and took it home.

When he got back, he handed his stepdaughters the gifts they had wished for and gave Cinderella the branch from the hazel tree.

Cinderella thanked him, went to her mother's grave, and planted the branch there, weeping so much that her tears watered it.

It grew into a beautiful tree. Three times a day, Cinderella went to sit beneath it, weeping and praying, and a little white bird would always come.

If Cinderella made a wish, the bird would drop down what she needed.

One day, the king announced a three-day festival to which all the lovely young women in the land were invited, so his son could find a bride.

When the two stepsisters heard they could go, they were thrilled and called for Cinderella.

"Come brush our hair, tie our shoes, and fasten our buckles—we’re going to the king's palace for the wedding!"

Cinderella did what they asked but cried because she wished she could go to the ball too. She begged her stepmother to let her join.

"You want to go, Cinderella?" her stepmother replied. "You look like a filthy mess. You don't have any clothes or shoes, and you want to dance?"

But when Cinderella pleaded again, her stepmother finally said, "I’ve emptied a dish of lentils into the ashes. If you can sort them out in two hours, you can come with us."

Cinderella hurried outside to the garden and called, "You gentle pigeons, turtle-doves, and all the birds of the sky, come help me!"

Two white pigeons flew in through the kitchen window, then the turtle-doves, and eventually all the birds of the sky landed among the ashes.

The pigeons nodded their heads and began to peck at the lentils while the others joined in, picking up all the good seeds and gathering them into the dish.

title: Cinderella
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Before an hour had passed, they finished and flew away. Cinderella took the dish back to her stepmother, feeling hopeful about going to the festival.

But the stepmother said, "No, Cinderella. You don't have clothes, and you can't dance—you’d just embarrass us."

As Cinderella cried, the stepmother added, "If you can sort two dishes of lentils from the ashes in one hour, you can join us."

She thought deep down that there’s no way Cinderella could manage that. After the stepmother scattered the lentils in the ashes, Cinderella once again turned to the garden.

"You gentle pigeons, turtle-doves, and all you birds of the sky, come help me!" she cried.

Two white pigeons flew in through the kitchen window again, followed by the turtle-doves and all the birds of the sky.

The doves began to nod and pick, and the rest followed, quickly gathering the good seeds into the dishes. In less than half an hour, they'd finished and flew away.

Cinderella was overjoyed, thinking she could finally go to the wedding. But her stepmother said, "That won’t change anything. You can’t go; you have no clothes and can’t dance. We’d be ashamed of you."

Then she turned her back on Cinderella and left with her two haughty daughters.

With everyone gone, Cinderella rushed to her mother’s grave beneath the hazel tree and cried,

"Shiver and quiver, little tree, silver and gold, throw down over me."

The bird dropped a stunning gold and silver dress and slippers embroidered with silk and silver. She quickly put on the dress and went to the wedding.

Her stepsisters and stepmother had no idea who she was, believing she was a foreign princess because she looked so beautiful in the golden gown.

They didn’t think of Cinderella at all and assumed she was home in the dirt, picking lentils from the ashes.

The prince approached her, took her hand, and danced with her.

He wouldn’t dance with anyone else and held onto her hand tightly. If someone else approached to invite her, he said, "This is my partner."

title: Cinderella
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Cinderella danced until evening fell and then wanted to go home.

But the prince said, "I will walk with you; I want to see where you belong."

Cinderella managed to slip away and jumped into the pigeon house. The prince waited until her father arrived and told him that the mysterious maiden had jumped into the pigeon house.

The old man thought, could it be Cinderella? They had to bring him an axe and pickaxe to tear down the pigeon house, but no one was inside.

When they got home, Cinderella lay in her dirty clothes among the ashes, as a dim oil lamp flickered above, because she had quickly jumped down from the pigeon house,

run to the hazel tree, taken off her beautiful clothes, laid them on the grave, and the bird had taken them away.

Then she settled back among the ashes in her gray gown.

The next day, when the festival began again, and her family went out once more, Cinderella went back to her mother’s grave.

"Shiver and quiver, my little tree, silver and gold, throw down over me."

The bird provided an even more beautiful dress than the day before. So when Cinderella arrived at the wedding in this stunning gown, everyone gasped at her beauty.

The prince had waited for her, took her by the hand, and danced only with her. When others tried to invite her, he declared, "This is my partner."

When evening came, she wanted to leave, and the prince followed her, wanting to see where she disappeared.

But she evaded him, darting into the garden behind the house.

In the garden stood a magnificent tall tree, laden with the most magnificent pears. She climbed swiftly like a squirrel, and the prince couldn’t find her.

He waited until her father returned and said, "The mysterious woman has escaped; I believe she climbed up the pear tree."

The father thought, could it be Cinderella? They brought an axe and cut the tree down, but no one was there.

And when they went back into the kitchen, Cinderella lay in the ashes, as usual, because she had jumped down from the other side of the tree,

title: Cinderella
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
returned the beautiful dress to the bird at the hazel tree, and put on her old clothes again.

On the third day, when her family had left again, Cinderella went to her mother’s grave once more.

"Shiver and quiver, my little tree. Silver and gold, throw down over me."

This time, the bird dropped an incredibly beautiful dress that was more splendid than any she had yet seen, and the slippers were pure gold.

When she went to the festival in this dress, everyone was left speechless in amazement.

The prince danced only with her; if anyone invited her to dance, he said, "This is my partner."

As evening came, Cinderella decided to leave, and the prince wanted to follow her, but she escaped him so quickly that he couldn’t keep up.

The prince employed a trick and had the entire staircase smeared with pitch; when she ran down, one of her golden slippers got stuck.

He picked up the tiny golden slipper; it was delicate and beautiful.

The next morning, he went to her father’s house and announced, "No one shall be my wife but the one whose foot this golden slipper fits."

The stepsisters were thrilled because they had pretty feet. The older one took the shoe into her room to try it on.

But her big toe couldn’t fit, and the shoe was clearly too small for her.

Her mother handed her a knife and said, "Cut off your toe. When you’re queen, you won’t need to walk anymore."

The girl cut off her toe, forced her foot into the shoe, hidden the pain, and went to the prince.

He took her on his horse as his bride and left with her. However, on their way back, they passed the grave, where the two pigeons sat.

"The shoe is too small for her!" they cried out.

The prince looked at her foot and saw blood dripping from it.

He turned his horse around and brought the false bride back, declaring she wasn’t the right one, and told the other sister to try on the shoe.

title: Cinderella
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
This sister went into her room and managed to squeeze her toes into the slipper, but her heel was too big.

Once again, her mother provided her a knife and said, "Cut off a piece of your heel. When you’re queen, you won’t need to walk anymore."

The girl cut her heel, wedged her foot into the shoe, endured the pain, and approached the prince.

He took her on his horse as his bride, but when they passed the hazel tree, the two pigeons cried out, "The shoe is too small for her!"

The prince looked down and saw blood oozing from her shoe and staining her white stocking red.

He turned around once more, bringing the false bride home.

"This one is not the right one either," he stated, "Do you not have another daughter?"

"Well, yes," her father replied, "but there’s just a little scullery maid left from my late wife, and she can’t be the bride."

The prince insisted that she be brought to him; the mother protested, saying, "Oh no, she’s much too dirty to show herself."

But he was adamant, and Cinderella was called.

She first washed her hands and face until they were clean, then came forward and bowed before the prince, who gave her the golden shoe.

Cinderella sat down on a stool, took off her heavy wooden shoe, and slipped her foot into the slipper, which fit perfectly.

When she stood up and the prince looked at her face, he recognized her as the beautiful maiden who had danced with him.

"That is the true bride!" he shouted.

The stepmother and stepsisters were horrified, their faces pale with anger. But he took Cinderella on his horse and rode away with her.

As they passed by the hazel tree, the two white doves cried out, "The shoe is not too small for her!"

When they cried that, the birds flew down and perched on Cinderella's shoulders, one on the right and the other on the left.

When it was time for Cinderella and the prince's wedding, her two false sisters came to win her favor and share in her luck.

As the couple walked to church, the older sister stood on the right, and the younger on the left. The doves pecked out one eye from each.

Later, on their way back, the older sister was on the left and the younger on the right, and the doves pecked out the other eye from each.

Thus, for their wickedness and deceit, they were punished with blindness.

title: Clever Elsie
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a man who had a daughter named Clever Elsie.

As she grew up, her father decided it was time for her to get married.

The mother chimed in, saying she would like to see someone come to marry her.

Eventually, a man named Hans came from afar and began courting her,

but he made it clear that Clever Elsie truly needed to be clever.

The father replied, "Oh, she's got plenty of common sense!"

The mother added, "She can even sense the wind coming from down the street and hear the flies cough!"

Hans replied, "If she’s not really smart, then I won’t marry her."

One day, while they were having dinner and had finished eating, the mother asked,

"Elsie, could you go down to the cellar and get some beer?"

So, Clever Elsie took the pitcher from the wall and headed to the cellar.

As she walked, she tapped the lid to make the time feel shorter.

Once inside the cellar, she grabbed a chair and placed it in front of the barrel,

so she didn't have to bend down and hurt her back.

She set the pitcher down and turned on the tap, watching the beer flow.

Instead of just standing there, she looked around and noticed something unusual.

After peering around for a while, she spotted a pick-axe hanging directly above her.

Then, Clever Elsie started to cry, saying, "If I marry Hans and we have a child, and that child grows up to draw beer from here,

the pick-axe could fall on his head and kill him!"

She sobbed loudly, feeling overwhelmed by the misfortune that lay ahead.

Meanwhile, the people upstairs were waiting for the drink, but Clever Elsie didn’t return.

Then the mother told the servant, "Go downstairs and check on where Elsie is."

The maid went down and found Elsie sitting in front of the barrel, crying her heart out.

"Elsie, why are you crying?" asked the maid.

"Oh, do I not have a reason to weep?" said Elsie.

title: Clever Elsie
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"If I marry Hans and we have a child, and that child grows big,

and has to draw beer from here, the pick-axe might fall on his head and kill him!"

The maid responded, "What a clever Elsie we have!" And she sat down beside her, starting to cry as well.

After a while, since the maid didn't come back, the people upstairs grew thirsty.

The man told the boy, "Go down to the cellar and see where Elsie and the girl are."

The boy went down and found Clever Elsie and the maid both crying together.

He asked, "Why are you both crying?"

"Oh, do I not have a reason to weep?" Elsie replied.

"If I marry Hans, and we have a child, and that child grows big, and has to draw beer from here,

the pick-axe could fall on his head and kill him!"

The boy said, "What a clever Elsie we have!" and sat down next to her, beginning to cry as well.

They all waited for the boy, but since he didn't return, the man said to the woman,

"Go down to the cellar and check on Elsie."

The woman went downstairs and found all three of them in tears.

She asked what was wrong, and Elsie told her that her future child would be killed by the pick-axe,

that would fall if they sent him to the cellar to draw beer.

The mother exclaimed, "What a clever Elsie we have!" and joined them in their tears.

The man upstairs waited a little, but as his wife did not come back and his thirst grew,

he decided to go down to the cellar himself to see where Elsie was.

When he arrived, he found them all crying together.

After hearing the reason, that Elsie's potential future child could be harmed by the pick-axe,

he said, "Oh, what a clever Elsie!" and he sat down to cry with them.

The groom stayed upstairs for a long time, and when no one appeared,

he thought they might be waiting for him downstairs, so he went to see.

title: Clever Elsie
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
As he stepped down, he found all five of them lamenting loudly, each trying to outdo the other.

"What misfortune has happened?" he asked.

"Oh, dear Hans," said Elsie, "if we marry and have a child, and he gets big enough to draw something to drink,

the pick-axe left up there might fall and crush him. Don't we have reason to cry?"

Hans replied, "I don’t need anything more complicated than that for my household. Since you’re such a clever Elsie,

I will have you as my wife!" He took her hand, led her upstairs, and married her.

After some time, Hans said, "Wife, I'm going out to work and earn some money for us.

Please go cut the corn in the field so we can have some bread."

"Sure thing, dear Hans!" she replied.

After he left, she made herself a tasty broth and took it out to the field.

Once there, she thought, "What should I do first? Cut the corn or eat?

I think I'll eat first!" She enjoyed her broth until she felt satisfied.

Then she pondered, "Should I cut the corn or take a nap? I'll nap first."

So she lay down in the corn and fell asleep.

Hans had been home for a while, but Elsie was still missing.

He thought, "What a clever Elsie I have; she's so hardworking, she didn't even come home to eat!"

As night fell and she still hadn't returned, Hans went out to see what she had done.

He found she hadn't cut any corn and was asleep in the field.

Hurriedly, he went home, grabbed a fowler's net with little bells,

and hung it around her while she continued to sleep.

Then he went home, locked the door, and sat down to work.

Finally, when night arrived, Clever Elsie woke up to the sound of bells jingling with every step she took.

Startled, she began to doubt if she was really Clever Elsie.

"Is it really me, or not?" she wondered, unsure of what to think.

Eventually, she decided to go home and ask if it really was her, since they'd know.

She ran to her house's door, but it was shut.

She knocked on the window and called, "Hans, is Elsie in there?"

"Yes, she is here," answered Hans.

This frightened her, and she said, "Oh, heavens! Then it’s not me!"

She ran to another door, but when people heard the jingling,

they wouldn't open it, and she couldn't get in anywhere.

Finally, she ran out of the village, and no one noticed her go.

title: Clever Grethel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a cook named Gretel who had a pair of shiny shoes with red heels.

Whenever she strutted out wearing them, she would twirl and admire herself, feeling like the prettiest girl around.

She felt so happy and confident, thinking to herself, 'You really are a beautiful girl.'

After returning home, she poured herself a drink, feeling overjoyed and ready to enjoy the evening.

Just like how wine makes you want to eat, she sampled the delicious food she was preparing, enjoying the flavors until she was satisfied, saying, 'A cook must know how good the food is.'

One day, her master told her, 'Gretel, we have a guest coming this evening. Please prepare two chickens with great care.'

'I'll take care of it, master,' Gretel replied confidently.

She killed the two chickens, scalded them, plucked their feathers, placed them on the spit, and set them before the fire so they could roast.

The chickens were starting to turn a golden brown and were nearly ready, but the guest had yet to arrive.

Gretel called out to her master, 'If the guest doesn't come soon, I'll have to take the chickens off the fire, but it would be such a shame if they aren't eaten at their juiciest.'

Her master replied, 'I'll go fetch the guest myself.'

As soon as her master turned away, Gretel moved the spit with the chickens aside, thinking to herself, 'Standing here by the fire is making me sweat and thirsty. Who knows when they will arrive? I'll just pop down to the cellar for a quick drink.'

She hurried down, filled a jug, and said, 'Cheers to you, Gretel!' before taking a long drink.

She thought, 'Wine should flow freely and not be wasted,' so she took another big gulp.

Then she returned to the chickens, placed them back over the fire, basted them, and turned the spit cheerfully.

But the aroma of the roasting chickens was too tempting, and she thought, 'Something must be wrong here, I need to taste it.'

title: Clever Grethel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She dipped her finger in and said, 'Oh, how good these chickens are! It would be a crime not to eat them while they're perfect.'

She dashed to the window to see if her master was returning with the guest, but there was no one in sight.

She went back to inspect the chickens, realizing, 'One of the wings is starting to burn.'

So she cut it off and ate it, relishing every bite, and then thought, 'If I don’t take the other wing, my master will notice something's missing.'

After finishing one wing, she searched for her master but didn’t see him.

Then it hit her, 'What if they aren't coming at all? What if they’ve stopped somewhere else?' So she said to herself, 'Well, Gretel, why not enjoy yourself? You've already started on one chicken, so take another drink and finish it off. After it's gone, you'll have some peace and quiet—why waste good food?'

With that, she dashed back to the cellar, took an enormous drink, and joyfully devoured the entire chicken.

Once she finished one chicken and still her master didn’t show up, she looked at the other and thought, 'If one chicken is gone, the other should be too. They come as a pair, after all.'

Recognizing that what’s good for one is good for the other, she said, 'I think another drink wouldn't hurt at all.'

So she took another big gulp and soon finished off the second chicken.

Suddenly, her master arrived, shouting, 'Gretel, hurry up! The guest is coming right behind me!'

'Yes, sir, I'll have everything ready in no time,' Gretel responded, trying to keep her composure.

Meanwhile, her master was checking to ensure the table was set correctly and sharpened the big knife he planned to use to carve the chickens.

Just then, the guest knocked politely at the door.

Gretel rushed to see who it was, and upon spotting the guest, she put her finger to her lips and whispered, 'Shh! You need to leave quickly—if my master sees you, it won't end well for you.'

She added, 'He invited you for dinner, but his real intention is to cut off your two ears. Just listen to the sound of him sharpening that knife.'

The guest heard the knife being sharpened and quickly dashed back down the steps.

Meanwhile, Gretel ran in a panic to her master, crying, 'You’ve invited quite a guest!'

Her master, confused, asked, 'What do you mean by that?'

Gretel explained, 'He took the chickens I had just finished and ran off with them!'

Her master was furious and lamented, 'What a clever trick! If only he had left me one chicken, so I would have something to eat.'

He called out for the guest to stop, but the guest pretended not to hear.

With the knife still in hand, her master chased after him, shouting, 'Just one! Just one!' meaning that the guest should leave him at least one chicken.

However, the guest thought he was being told to give up one of his ears and ran off as if he were on fire, eager to escape.

title: Clever Hans
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Hans' mother asked, "Where are you off to, Hans?" Hans replied, "To Gretel."

"Be good, Hans." "Oh, I will! Goodbye, Mom!"

"Goodbye, Hans." Hans arrived at Gretel's house. "Good day, Gretel!"

"Good day, Hans! What do you have that’s good?" "I have nothing, I want something given to me."

Gretel handed Hans a needle. "Goodbye, Gretel." "Goodbye, Hans!"

Hans took the needle, stuck it into a hay cart, and followed the cart home.

"Good evening, Mom!" "Good evening, Hans! Where have you been?"

"With Gretel." "What did you bring her?" "I didn’t take her anything; she gave me something."

"What did Gretel give you?" "She gave me a needle." "Where is the needle, Hans?"

"I stuck it in the hay cart." "That wasn’t very smart, Hans. You should have put the needle in your sleeve."

"I’ll do better next time, don’t worry."

"Where are you off to now, Hans?" "To Gretel, Mom." "Be good, Hans."

"Oh, I will! Goodbye, Mom!" "Goodbye, Hans!" Hans went to see Gretel.

"Good day, Gretel!" "Good day, Hans! What good thing do you bring?"

"I have nothing, I want something else given to me." Gretel gave Hans a knife.

"Goodbye, Gretel." "Goodbye, Hans!"

Hans took the knife, tucked it into his sleeve, and went home.

"Good evening, Mom!" "Good evening, Hans! Where have you been?"

"With Gretel." "What did you bring her?" "I took nothing; she gave me something."

"What did she give you?" "She gave me a knife." "Where is the knife, Hans?"

title: Clever Hans
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"It's stuck in my sleeve." "That wasn’t very smart, Hans. You should have put the knife in your pocket."

"I’ll do better next time, don’t worry."

"Where are you off to now, Hans?" "To Gretel, Mom." "Be good, Hans."

"Oh, I will! Goodbye, Mom!" "Goodbye, Hans!" Hans went to see Gretel.

"Good day, Gretel!" "Good day, Hans! What good thing do you bring?"

"I bring nothing; I want something given to me." Gretel gave Hans a young goat.

"Goodbye, Gretel." "Goodbye, Hans!"

Hans took the goat, tied its legs, and stuffed it into his pocket. When he got home, it was suffocated.

"Good evening, Mom!" "Good evening, Hans! Where have you been?"

"With Gretel." "What did you bring her?" "I didn't take anything; she gave me something."

"What did Gretel give you?" "She gave me a goat." "Where is the goat, Hans?"

"I put it in my pocket." "That wasn’t very smart, Hans. You should have tied a rope around the goat's neck."

"I’ll do better next time, don’t worry."

"Where are you off to now, Hans?" "To Gretel, Mom." "Be good, Hans."

"Oh, I will! Goodbye, Mom!" "Goodbye, Hans!" Hans went to see Gretel.

"Good day, Gretel!" "Good day, Hans! What good thing do you bring?"

"I have nothing; I want something given to me." Gretel handed Hans a piece of bacon.

"Goodbye, Hans!" Hans took the bacon, tied it to a rope, and dragged it behind him.

Suddenly, dogs came and devoured the bacon. By the time he got home, he was left with just the rope.

title: Clever Hans
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Good evening, Mom!" "Good evening, Hans! Where have you been?"

"With Gretel." "What did you take her?" "I took her nothing; she gave me something."

"What did she give you?" "She gave me a bit of bacon." "Where is the bacon, Hans?"

"I tied it to a rope, tried to bring it home, but the dogs took it." "That wasn’t very smart, Hans; you should have carried the bacon on your head."

"I’ll do better next time, don’t worry."

"Where are you off to now, Hans?" "To Gretel, Mom." "Be good, Hans."

"I’ll be good. Goodbye, Mom!" "Goodbye, Hans!" Hans went to see Gretel.

"Good day, Gretel!" "Good day, Hans! What good thing do you bring?"

"I have nothing but would like something given to me." Gretel said to Hans, "I will go with you."

Hans tied Gretel up with a rope, led her to a rack, and secured her there.

Then Hans went back to his mother. "Good evening, Mom!" "Good evening, Hans! Where have you been?"

"I was with Gretel." "What did you take her?" "I didn't take anything."

"What did Gretel give you?" "She gave me nothing; she came with me." "Where did you leave Gretel?"

"I tied her with a rope, secured her to the rack, and scattered some grass for her."

"That wasn’t very kind, Hans; you should have treated her better." "I’ll do better next time, don’t worry."

Hans went into the stable, got some calves' and sheep's eyes, and threw them at Gretel.

Gretel got really angry, broke free, and ran away, no longer willing to be Hans' bride.

title: Death's Messengers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
In ancient times, a giant was making his way along a vast highway.

Suddenly, an unfamiliar man appeared right in front of him and said,

"Stop! Don’t take another step!" The giant, taken aback, shouted,

"What? You, a person I could crush with my fingers, want to block my path? Who

are you to speak to me like that?" The man replied calmly,

"I am Death. No one can resist me, and you must follow my orders."

But the giant wouldn’t back down; he engaged in a fierce battle with Death.

In the long and tumultuous fight, the giant eventually gained the upper hand,

striking Death down so hard that he fell against a stone.

As the giant continued on his way, Death remained there, defeated and too weak to rise.

"What’s going to happen now?" he wondered. "If I stay here, no one will die,

and the world will become so crowded that people won't even have enough room to stand!"

Just then, a young man appeared on the road, strong and healthy,

whistling a cheerful tune as he took in his surroundings. When he spotted the

fainting figure of Death, he approached compassionately, helped him up,

and offered him a drink from his flask until he regained his strength.

"Do you know who I am?" asked the stranger as he got to his feet.

"No, I don't. Who are you?" the young man replied. "I am Death," he said,

"I have no mercy for anyone, and I can’t make exceptions for you. However,

to show my gratitude, I promise I won’t come for you without a warning.

Instead, I will send my messengers ahead to let you know when it's time for me."

"Well," said the youth, "it's good to know you'll give me a heads-up when you arrive,

and that I’ll be safe from you for now." He continued on, feeling carefree,

and enjoying life without a worry.

But youth and health don’t last forever. Before long,

illnesses and troubles began to plague him by day, robbing him of rest by night.

"I won't die yet," he reassured himself. "Death promised to send his messengers first,

but I wish these miserable days of sickness would just end."

Once he finally felt better, he dove back into his merry life.

But one day, he felt a tap on his shoulder. He turned around and saw Death standing behind him.

"Come with me. Your time to leave this world has come." "What?" the man exclaimed.

"You’re breaking your promise! Didn't you say you’d send messengers before coming yourself?

I haven’t seen any!" "Silence!" Death replied. "Haven't I sent you messengers after messengers?

Didn’t fever come and strike you down? Haven't you felt dizzy? Didn’t gout twist through your limbs?

Didn’t toothaches gnaw at your cheeks? Wasn’t it dark before your eyes? Besides that, hasn't my brother Sleep

reminded you of me each night? Didn't you lie awake as if you were already dead?"

The man had no reply; acceptance washed over him, and he went along with Death.

title: Doctor Knowall
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a poor peasant named Crabb who was hauling a load of wood to the town with his two oxen.

He sold the wood to a doctor for two talers.

As the doctor counted out the money, Crabb noticed that the doctor was enjoying a lavish meal.

Seeing how well the doctor was eating and drinking made Crabb long for that kind of life, and he thought to himself that he wouldn't mind being a doctor too.

After a moment of hesitation, he finally asked the doctor if he could also become a doctor.

"Oh, sure!" the doctor replied, "That's easy to do."

"What do I need to do?" asked the peasant.

"First, buy an ABC book with a rooster on the cover.

Then, sell your cart and oxen for money so you can buy some clothes and whatever else is needed for practicing medicine.

Third, get a sign made that says, 'I am Doctor Knowall,' and hang it above your front door."

The peasant followed all the doctor's advice.

After practicing medicine for a while, a wealthy lord found out his money had been stolen.

He was informed about Doctor Knowall in a nearby village and figured he would know what happened to the money.

So, the lord hitched up his carriage and headed to the village, asking Crabb if he was Doctor Knowall.

"Yes, that's me," Crabb replied.

The lord then told him to come with him to retrieve the stolen money.

"Of course, but my wife, Grete, needs to come too," he insisted.

The lord agreed and allowed both of them to ride in the carriage as they set off together.

When they arrived at the lord's castle, a meal had been laid out, and Crabb was invited to sit and eat.

"But my wife, Grete, should also be invited," he said, taking a seat at the table with her.

When the first servant came in with a fancy dish, the peasant nudged Grete and remarked, "Grete, that was the first one."

He meant it was the first servant with the first dish, but the servant thought he was implying he was the first thief.

title: Doctor Knowall
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Frightened, the servant rushed out to tell his colleague, "The doctor knows all; we're in trouble because he just called me the first!"

The next servant didn't want to enter at all, but was forced to do so.

As he walked in with his dish, Crabb nudged Grete again and said, "Grete, that’s the second one."

That servant also panicked and hurried back out.

The third servant had the same fate; Crabb nudged Grete once more, saying, "Grete, that’s the third one."

By now, the atmosphere was tense, and the fourth servant entered carrying a covered dish.

The lord instructed Doctor Knowall to demonstrate his skills by guessing what was under the cover.

Underneath were crabs, but the doctor couldn't figure it out and exclaimed, "Ah, poor Crabb!"

Upon hearing that, the lord declared, "There! He knows; he must also know who took the money!"

The servants looked visibly shaken and motioned for the doctor to step outside for a moment.

Once outside, all four servants confessed that they had stolen the money and promised they would return it, along with a hefty bribe, if he kept their secret.

They led him to where they had hidden the money.

Satisfied, the doctor returned to the table, sat back down, and said, "My lord, now I will consult my book to find where the gold is hidden."

Meanwhile, the fifth servant snuck into the stove to eavesdrop, hoping to find out if the doctor knew even more.

But Doctor Knowall was just sitting still, flipping through his ABC book, looking for the rooster picture.

After a moment of searching, he said, "I know you're there, so you might as well come out."

Thinking the doctor was talking to him, the servant jumped out in fright and yelled, "That man knows everything!"

Eventually, Doctor Knowall revealed to the lord where the money was concealed but didn’t disclose who had actually taken it.

As a result, he earned a lot of money as a reward and became quite famous.

title: Domestic Servants
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Where are you headed? To Walpe. I'm going to Walpe, you're going to Walpe, so,

Do you have a man? What's his name? Cham. My man is Cham, your

man is Cham. I'm going to Walpe, you're going to Walpe, so, so, we'll go together.

Do you have a child? What's their name? Wild. My child is Wild, your

child is Wild, my man is Cham, your man is Cham. I'm going to Walpe, you

are going to Walpe, so, so, we’ll go together.

Do you have a cradle? What do you call your cradle? Hippodadle. My

cradle is Hippodadle, your cradle is Hippodadle, my child is Wild, your

child is Wild, my man is Cham, your man is Cham. I'm going to Walpe, you

are going to Walpe, so, so, we'll go together.

Do you have a servant? What name does your servant have?

From-work-do-not-budge. My servant is From-work-do-not-budge, your

servant is From-work-do-not-budge, my cradle is Hippodadle, your cradle

is Hippodadle, my child is Wild, your child is Wild, my man is Cham, your

man is Cham. I'm going to Walpe, you’re going to Walpe, so, so, we’ll go together.

title: Donkey Cabbages
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a young huntsman who ventured into the forest to lie in wait.

He had a cheerful and eager spirit, and as he made his way there,

he whistled happily to himself, when suddenly, an ugly old woman approached him.

"Good day, dear huntsman! You seem so cheerful and content," she said,

"but I’m suffering from hunger and thirst. Please, could you give me a coin or some food?"

The huntsman felt pity for the poor old woman, reached into his pocket,

and gave her what little he could spare.

Just as he was about to continue on his way, the old woman stopped him.

"Listen, dear huntsman, I want to repay your kindness. You can go on your way now, but soon you'll reach a tree,

where you’ll find nine birds fighting over a cloak. Take your gun and shoot right in the middle.

The birds will drop the cloak, but one will be hurt and fall dead.

Keep the cloak as it's a wishing cloak; just throw it over your shoulders and wish to be anywhere, and you'll get there instantly.

Then, take the heart of the dead bird, swallow it whole, and you will find a gold coin under your pillow each morning."

The huntsman thanked the wise woman, thinking, "That sounds like amazing gifts, I hope they come true."

Sure enough, after walking a little while, he heard a loud screeching overhead.

Looking up, he saw a group of birds tugging at a piece of cloth, squawking and fighting over it.

"Wow!" the huntsman said. "This is just as the old woman said!" He took his gun off his shoulder, aimed, and fired right in their midst.

Feathers flew everywhere; the birds scattered in chaos, but one fell down, dead, and the cloak fluttered to the ground.

Following the old woman’s instructions, he cut open the bird, found the heart, swallowed it, and took the cloak home.

The next morning, he remembered the promise and was eager to check if it had come true.

title: Donkey Cabbages
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When he lifted his pillow, a shiny gold coin greeted him, and the same thing happened the next day, and the day after that.

He started to accumulate a small fortune, but eventually thought, "What good is all this gold if I stay at home? I need to see the world!"

So he said goodbye to his parents, strapped on his huntsman’s pouch, grabbed his gun, and set off into the world.

One day, while traveling through a thick forest, he emerged into a beautiful clearing, and in front of him stood a grand castle.

Standing at one of the windows was an old woman and a remarkably beautiful young lady.

The old woman, who was a witch, said to the maiden, "Look! There comes someone from the forest who carries a wonderful treasure within him.

We need to take it from him; it's better suited for us than him. That bird's heart in him brings a gold coin every morning!"

She explained to the girl what to do and how to act, ending with a harsh warning, "If you don't obey me, you will face consequences."

As the huntsman got closer, he noticed the maiden and thought to himself, "I've been traveling for so long; I want to take a break and rest in that magnificent castle.

I certainly have enough money to do so!" But the real reason was that he was captivated by her beauty.

He entered the castle and was warmly welcomed and treated with great courtesy.

Soon, he found himself in love with the young witch, oblivious to everything else, only wanting to please her.

The old woman then schemed, "Now we must get the bird’s heart; he won't even notice it's gone." She prepared a potion, poured it into a goblet,

and handed it to the maiden to give to the huntsman.

She did so, saying, "Drink this, my love, for me."

Taking the goblet, he drank it down, and immediately brought up the bird's heart.

The girl had to take it in secret and swallow it herself, following the old woman's orders.

title: Donkey Cabbages
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
From that moment on, the huntsman found no more gold under his pillow; it was now under the maiden's.

Every morning, the old woman collected it, but the huntsman was so smitten and oblivious that he focused only on spending time with her.

Then the old witch declared, "We have the bird’s heart, but we must also take his wishing cloak!"

The girl replied, "Let’s leave that; he has lost his wealth already." The old woman was furious and retorted, "That cloak is a rare gem, I want it!"

She scolded the girl into obedience, telling her to look out the window as if she’s sad.

The huntsman asked, "Why do you look so sorrowful?" "Oh, my beloved," she replied, "over there is the garnet mountain, where precious stones grow.

I long for them so much that thinking about them makes me sad, but how can I get there? Only the birds can fly to them, but a man cannot."

"Is there nothing else bothering you?" the huntsman asked. "I'll remove your burden from your heart!" With that, he drew her under his cloak,

wished himself onto the garnet mountain, and in a flash, they were standing there together.

Precious stones sparkled around them, and they began gathering the finest gems.

But the old woman had used her magic to make the huntsman sleepy. He said to the maiden, "Let’s sit and rest; I'm too tired to stand up."

So they sat down, and he laid his head in her lap and quickly fell asleep.

While he slept, she took the cloak off his shoulders, wrapped it around herself, picked up the garnets, and wished herself back home.

When the huntsman finally woke up and realized she had betrayed him, leaving him alone on the mountain, he thought, "Oh, what treachery exists in this world!"

He sat down, filled with sorrow, uncertain of what to do. But this mountain was inhabited by wild giants,

and soon enough, he saw three of them approaching, so he lay down as if he were asleep.

title: Donkey Cabbages
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The giants came closer, and the first one kicked him with his foot and grumbled, "What kind of earthworm is this, lying here without a care?"

The second proposed, "Let's just step on him and be done with it." But the third scoffed, saying, "Why bother? Let him live; he won’t get far.

Once he tries climbing higher, the clouds will just sweep him away." So they walked on.

The huntsman listened to them and once they left, he stood up and climbed to the mountain's peak.

As he sat on top, a cloud approached, picked him up, and drifted for a long time through the sky.

Eventually, it descended, dropping him gently into a vast cabbage garden surrounded by walls.

Looking around, the huntsman thought, "If only I had something to eat; I'm so hungry! Moving on from here looks tough.

I see no apples or pears or any other fruit, just these cabbages. But maybe just a few leaves will suffice; they won’t taste great, but they’ll refresh me."

He picked a nice cabbage and ate it, but hardly had he swallowed a few bites when he felt very strange.

Suddenly, he sprouted four legs, a thick head, and two long ears, and he realized in horror that he was turning into a donkey.

Yet, as his hunger grew, the delicious cabbage leaves seemed fitting for his new form, so he kept munching away happily.

Eventually, he came across a different kind of cabbage, and as soon as he ate it, he transformed back into his human self.

Exhausted, the huntsman lay down and fell asleep to recover from his long day.

When he woke up the next morning, he took one head of bad cabbage and one of the good cabbage, thinking,

"These will help me regain my true form and exact revenge for my betrayal." He carried the cabbages over the wall and set off to find his sweetheart’s castle.

After wandering a few days, he finally found it again. He covered his face with brown dye, ensuring no one would recognize him, and sought shelter.

title: Donkey Cabbages
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"I'm so tired," he said, "that I can't go any further." The witch asked, "Who are you, and what do you want?"

"I’m a king’s messenger," he replied, "sent to find the finest salad in the world. I’ve been lucky enough to find it, and I’m carrying it, but it's wilting in this heat.

I’m worried I won’t make it any farther before it goes bad."

When the old woman heard about the exquisite salad, her greed kicked in.

"Dear traveler, let me try this wonderful salad you speak of!" "Of course!" he said. "I have two heads and I’ll give you one of them." He opened his pouch and handed her the bad cabbage.

The witch suspected nothing, and her mouth watered at the thought of tasting this new dish. She hurried to the kitchen to prepare it.

As soon as it was ready, she couldn’t wait for it to be served on the table; she grabbed a handful and stuffed it into her mouth.

But hardly had she swallowed than she was transformed back into an ass, rushing out into the courtyard.

Then the maid came into the kitchen, saw the salad waiting there, and was about to carry it upstairs.

However, she too was overcome by the urge to taste it and ate a few leaves. Instantly, the magic took effect, and she became an ass as well, running outside to join the old woman.

The salad then fell to the ground.

Meanwhile, the huntsman sat beside the beautiful girl, and since no one had come back with the salad, she commented, "I wonder what became of the salad."

Thinking that perhaps the salad was already working its magic, he said, "I'll go check on it in the kitchen."

As he walked down, he saw the two donkeys running around in the courtyard, while the salad lay discarded on the ground.

"Looks like they got their share," he said, and picking up the remaining salad leaves, he placed them on a dish and took them to the maiden.

title: Donkey Cabbages
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"I brought you this delicacy myself," he said, "so you won't have to wait any longer." She took a bite,

and like the others, she lost her human form and ran out to the courtyard, transformed into an ass.

After washing his face so they could recognize him, he went out into the courtyard and said, "Now it’s time for you to pay for your treachery!"

He tied all three of them up with a rope and led them to a mill.

He knocked at the window, and the miller looked out asking what he wanted. "I have three unruly beasts," he replied.

"I want to get rid of them. Will you take them in and care for them, and I'll pay you whatever you ask?"

The miller agreed, saying, "Sure, but how do I handle them?" The huntsman instructed him,

"Give three beatings and one meal daily to the old one— that's the witch,

then one beating and three meals to the younger one, who’s the servant girl,

and to the youngest, who’s the maiden, give no beatings but three meals, for I can’t bear to have her hurt."

He then returned to the castle and found everything he needed.

A few days later, the miller reported that the old donkey receiving three beatings and just one meal had died.

The other two were not dead but said to be so sad that they couldn’t last much longer.

The huntsman felt compassion, set aside his anger, and ordered the miller to return them.

When they arrived back, he gave them some of the good salad and they transformed back into their human forms.

The beautiful maiden fell to her knees and said, "Oh, my love, forgive me for the harm I've done.

My mother forced my hand. I did it against my will because I love you dearly.

Your wishing cloak is in a cupboard at home, and I will take a potion to vomit up the bird's heart."

But he had different thoughts and replied, "Keep it. It doesn’t matter, as I want you to be my true wife."

Thus, they celebrated a wedding and lived happily ever after.

title: Eve's Various Children
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When Adam and Eve were thrown out of paradise, they had no choice but to make a home for themselves on barren land.

They had to work hard to put food on the table.

Adam tended to the soil while Eve spun fabric.

Every year, Eve welcomed a new child into their family, but each one was different from the others.

Some children were beautiful, while others were quite the opposite.

After some time had passed, God sent an angel to inform them that He would be coming to check on their family.

Eve, thrilled that the Lord would be visiting, diligently cleaned her house,

decorated it with flowers, and

laid rushes on the floor.

Next, she gathered her children,

but only the beautiful ones.

She washed them, bathed them, combed their hair, dressed them in clean clothes, and reminded them to

behave properly and modestly in front of the Lord.

They were to bow respectfully, extend their hands, and answer His questions politely and thoughtfully.

The less attractive children, on the other hand, were told to stay hidden.

One child hid under the hay, another beneath the roof, one in the straw, another in the stove, another in the cellar,

one under a tub, one beneath a wine barrel, one under an old fur coat, and two more beneath the very cloth she used to make their clothes and the leather for their shoes.

Just as Eve finished preparing, there was a knock at the door.

Adam peeked through a crack and saw that it was the Lord.

Adam opened the door with respect,

and the heavenly Father entered.

Standing in a row were the beautiful children, bowing before Him, extending their hands,

and kneeling.

The Lord began to bless them, placing His hands on the first and saying, "You will be a mighty king,"

to the second, "You shall be a prince," to the third, "You will be a count," to the fourth, "You will be a knight," to the fifth, "You shall be a nobleman," to the sixth, "You shall be a burgher," to the seventh, "You will be a merchant," and to the eighth, "You shall be a learned man."

title: Eve's Various Children
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He also poured out His richest blessings upon them.

When Eve saw how kind and gracious the Lord was, she thought, "I should bring my less fortunate children, too; perhaps He will bless them as well."

So she hurried to gather them from the hay, the straw, the stove, and all the other hiding places.

The whole scruffy, dirty, patchy group finally emerged.

The Lord smiled, looked at them all, and said, "I will bless these children as well."

He laid His hands on the first and said, "You shall be a peasant," to the second, "You shall be a fisherman," to the third, "You will be a blacksmith," to the fourth, "You will be a tanner," to the fifth, "You shall be a weaver," to the sixth, "You shall be a shoemaker," to the seventh, "You will be a tailor," to the eighth, "You will be a potter," to the ninth, "You will be a waggoner," to the tenth, "You shall be a sailor," to the eleventh, "You will be a messenger," and to the twelfth, "You will be a scullion all your life."

Once Eve heard all this, she said, "Lord, why do you distribute your gifts so unevenly? After all, they are all my children whom I have brought into this world, and your blessings should be shared equally."

But God replied, "Eve, you don't quite understand.

It is important that the entire world is sustained through your children.

If they were all princes and lords, who would grow crops, harvest them, grind grain, and bake bread? Who would be the blacksmiths, weavers, carpenters, masons, workers, tailors, and seamstresses? Each of them has their role, so they can support one another, and together they will thrive like the members of one body.

Then Eve replied, "Oh, Lord, please forgive me; I was too hasty in my words.

Let your divine will be done with my children."

title: Fair Katrinelje and Pif-Paf-Poltrie
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Good day, Father Hollenthe."  "Thank you, Pif-Paf-Poltrie."  "Can I please

ask for your daughter's hand?" "Of course, if Mother Malcho,

Brother High-and-Mighty, Sister Kasetraut, and Fair Katrinelje are okay with it, you can have her."  "Where is Mother Malcho,

then?"  "She’s in the cowhouse milking the cow."

"Good day, Mother Malcho."  "Thank you, Pif-Paf-Poltrie."  "Can I please

ask for your daughter's hand?"  "Sure, if Father Hollenthe,

Brother High-and-Mighty, Sister Kasetraut, and Fair Katrinelje agree, you can have her."  "Where is Brother High-and-Mighty then?"

"He’s in the room chopping some wood."

"Good day, Brother High-and-Mighty."  "Thank you, Pif-Paf-Poltrie."

"Can I please ask for your sister's hand?"  "Sure, if Father

Hollenthe, Mother Malcho, Sister Kasetraut, and Fair Katrinelje all agree, you can have her."  "Where is Sister Kasetraut then?"  "She

is in the garden cutting cabbages."

"Good day, Sister Kasetraut."  "Thank you, Pif-Paf-Poltrie."  "Can I please

ask for your sister's hand?" "Sure, if Father Hollenthe,

Mother Malcho, Brother High-and-Mighty, and Fair Katrinelje all agree, you can have her."  "Where is Fair Katrinelje then?"  "She is

in the room counting her farthings."

"Good day, Fair Katrinelje."  "Thank you, Pif-Paf-Poltrie."  "Will you be my bride?"  "Sure, if Father Hollenthe, Mother Malcho,

Brother High-and-Mighty, and Sister Kasetraut are all on board, I am.

"Fair Katrinelje, what will your dowry be?"  "Fourteen farthings in cash, three and a half groschen that are owed to me, half a pound of

dried apples, a handful of pretzels, and a handful of roots.

And I have many other things as well.

"Pif-Paf-Poltrie, what do you do for a living? Are you a tailor?"  "Something better."  "A shoemaker?"  "Something better."  "A farmer?"

"Something better."  "A carpenter?"  "Something better."  "A blacksmith?"

"Something better."  "A miller?"  "Something better."  "Maybe a broom-maker?"  "Yes, that's what I am; isn’t it a fine trade?"

title: Ferdinand the Faithful
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a man and a woman who, as long as they were wealthy, had no children. But when they fell into poverty, they had a little boy.

They struggled to find a godfather for him, so the man decided to travel to another village to see if he could find one there.

On his way, he came across a poor man who asked him about his journey. The man explained he was looking for a godfather because he was so poor that no one would agree to the role.

The poor man replied, "Oh, you're poor, and so am I. I can be your godfather, but I can’t give the child anything. Just go home and tell the midwife to come with the child to the church."

When they all arrived at the church, the beggar was already there, and he gave the child the name Ferdinand the Faithful.

As they were leaving the church, the beggar said, "Now go home; I can't give you anything, and you shouldn't feel obligated to give me anything either." However, he handed a key to the midwife and instructed her to give it to the father when they got home.

He told her that the father should keep it safe until the child turned fourteen. At that point, he was to go to the heath where there was a castle the key would unlock, and everything that was inside would belong to Ferdinand.

When Ferdinand turned seven and had grown quite a bit, he went out to play with other boys. Each of them bragged about the gifts they received from their godfathers, and Ferdinand felt frustrated because he had nothing to share.

He went home and asked his father, "Did I get nothing from my godfather?" His father responded, "Oh yes, you have a key. If there's a castle on the heath, just go there and open it." Ferdinand set out, but there was no castle in sight.

Seven years later, when Ferdinand was fourteen, he returned to the heath and there stood the castle. He opened it to find nothing inside except for a beautiful white horse. Overjoyed, he hopped on it and raced back to his father, saying, "Now I have a white horse!"

title: Ferdinand the Faithful
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Excited for the adventures ahead, he decided to ride off. On his way, he spotted a pen lying on the road. At first, he thought about picking it up, but then hesitated, thinking, "I might find another pen where I'm going if I need one."

As he rode away, a voice called out to him, "Ferdinand the Faithful, take it with you!" He looked around but saw no one, so he continued on his way.

Further along, he came across a lake, where a fish was flopping on the bank, struggling for breath. "Hang on, little fish! I'm here to help you back to the water," he said, grabbing it by the tail and tossing it back into the lake.

The fish popped its head out of the water and said, "Thanks for saving me! I’ll give you a flute. Whenever you're in trouble, play it, and I’ll be there to assist you. If you drop anything in the water, just play it, and I'll fish it out for you."

Ferdinand rode away and encountered a man who asked where he was headed. "Oh, just to the next place," Ferdinand replied. The man asked, "What’s your name?" Ferdinand said, "Ferdinand the Faithful." The man replied, "Funny, my name is Ferdinand the Unfaithful," and they both headed to the inn.

Unfortunately, Ferdinand the Unfaithful was skilled in all sorts of dark arts and knew everything Ferdinand the Faithful had ever considered or was about to do.

In the inn, there was a lovely girl with a bright smile who fell for Ferdinand the Faithful because he was handsome. She asked him about his travels. "Oh, I'm just exploring a bit," he said.

She mentioned that the king was looking for an attendant or outrider and encouraged him to apply for the position. He replied that he felt uncomfortable just offering himself to the king.

The girl responded, "Don't worry, I’ll help you with that!" And without hesitation, she approached the king and told him about an excellent servant she knew.

title: Ferdinand the Faithful
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The king was intrigued and had Ferdinand the Faithful brought to him, intending to make him a servant. However, Ferdinand preferred to be an outrider because he wanted to be with his horse.

When Ferdinand the Unfaithful learned of this, he complained to the girl, "What? You’re helping him and not me?" The girl replied, "I’ll help you too." She knew that keeping on Ferdinand's good side was wise because he couldn’t be trusted.

She went back to the king and suggested him as a servant, and the king agreed.

Every morning, when the king met with his lords, he lamented, "Oh, if only I had my love with me!" Ferdinand the Unfaithful, however, was always plotting against Ferdinand the Faithful.

So, one day, when the king was complaining, he suggested, "Why not send the outrider to fetch her? If he fails, his head should roll!"

The king called for Ferdinand the Faithful and told him about a girl he loved and commanded him to bring her to him. If he failed, he would be executed.

Feeling hopeless, Ferdinand went to the stable to his white horse, lamenting, "Oh, what an unfortunate man I am!" Suddenly, a voice called out, "Ferdinand the Faithful, why are you crying?" He turned around, but saw no one and continued to grieve.

"Oh, my dear little white horse, I must leave you and might perish!" The voice called again, "Ferdinand the Faithful, why do you weep?" This time, he realized it was his little white horse who spoke.

"You can talk?" he exclaimed. The horse said, "Yes. Listen, I know you have to fetch the bride. Here’s what you should do."

The horse instructed, "Go to the king and tell him that if he provides what you need, you’ll bring her back. You’ll need a ship full of meat and another full of bread. There are giants on the lake and if you don’t have food for them, they’ll tear you apart. Plus, there are huge birds who will attack you if you don’t have bread for them.

title: Ferdinand the Faithful
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The king then ordered all the butchers to slaughter and all the bakers to bake so that the ships could be loaded.

Once the ships were full, the little white horse said, "Now get on me, and let’s head to the ship. When the giants come, say, 'Peace, peace, my dear giants, I have something for you!' When the birds arrive, do the same.

They will not harm you, and once we reach the castle, the giants will help you. When you get there, take a couple of giants with you. The princess is sleeping. Whatever you do, don’t wake her; the giants must lift her bed and carry her to the ship."

Everything unfolded just as the little white horse had predicted. Ferdinand the Faithful shared food with the giants and birds, which made them friendly, and they carried the princess's bed to the king.

When she arrived, the princess cried that she couldn't live without her writings that had been left behind in her castle.

At the instigation of Ferdinand the Unfaithful, Ferdinand the Faithful was summoned again. The king ordered him to fetch the writings from the castle, or he would be executed.

Back in the stable, Ferdinand lamented to his little white horse, "Oh, I have to go again; how can I manage this?" The little horse instructed him to fill the ships again.

Once more, everything happened just like before, and the giants and birds were pleased with the offerings.

When they reached the castle, the white horse informed Ferdinand that he needed to go in and retrieve the writings from the princess's room.

Ferdinand entered and fetched the documents. On the lake, he accidentally dropped his pen into the water. The white horse said, "I can’t help you this time," but then Ferdinand remembered the flute and began to play.

The fish popped up, holding the pen in its mouth, and brought it back to him. With the writings in hand, he returned to the castle, where the wedding was about to take place.

However, the queen didn’t love the king because he had no nose; instead, she wished she could love Ferdinand the Faithful.

One day, when all the lords were gathered, the queen boasted that she could do magic—she could cut someone's head off and put it back on. She dared someone to try.

None were brave enough, so Ferdinand the Faithful, spurred on by Ferdinand the Unfaithful again, stepped forward.

The queen beheaded him but then returned his head with ease, healing it instantly so it looked like he was wearing a red thread around his neck.

The king questioned her, "My dear child, where did you learn that?" She replied, "I mastered this art. Shall I try it on you next?"

"Oh, yes," the king responded eagerly. She then beheaded him as well, but this time she didn’t put it back, pretending she couldn’t.

The king was buried, and she married Ferdinand the Faithful.

From then on, he always rode his white horse. One day, while riding, the horse instructed him to gallop three times around the heath, which he did.

Afterward, the white horse stood on its hind legs and transformed into a magnificent knight.

title: Fitcher's Bird
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a wizard who could transform into a poor man.

He would go from house to house, begging for food while luring away pretty girls.

No one knew where he took them, as they were never seen again.

One day, he showed up at the door of a man who had three beautiful daughters.

He appeared to be a frail, homeless beggar, carrying a basket on his back, like he was collecting donations.

He asked for some food, and when the eldest daughter came out to hand him a piece of bread, he simply touched her, and she was pulled into his basket.

Without delay, he strode off quickly, taking her to his house in a dark forest.

The house was filled with lavish things.

He offered her everything she could wish for and said, "My dear, you will surely be happy with me because you have everything your heart desires."

This lasted a few days, and then he said, "I must go out for a while and leave you alone. Here are the keys to the house."

"You can explore everywhere and look at anything except one room, which this little key unlocks. I forbid you to enter there under the penalty of death."

He also handed her an egg and said, "Take good care of this egg and keep it with you at all times, for losing it would bring great misfortune."

She accepted the keys and the egg, promising to obey his orders.

Once he was gone, she explored the entire house, top to bottom.

The rooms glittered with silver and gold, and she marveled at the opulence.

Finally, she arrived at the forbidden door.

She wanted to walk past it, but her curiosity wouldn't let her go.

She examined the key; it looked just like any other.

In a moment of weakness, she inserted it into the lock and turned it slightly, causing the door to swing open.

What she saw inside was horrifying. A large bloody basin sat at the center of the room, filled with the remains of human beings—dead and chopped into pieces.

Nearby, a wooden block was set up with a gleaming axe resting on it.

title: Fitcher's Bird
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She was so frightened that the egg she held fell into the basin.

She fished it out and tried to wipe away the blood, but it reappeared almost instantly.

She scrubbed and washed, but the stain just wouldn't come off.

Before long, the wizard returned from his journey and immediately asked for the key and the egg.
