She handed them to him, trembling as she did, and he quickly noticed the red stains indicating she'd been in the bloody chamber.

"Since you went into the room against my wishes," he said, "you'll have to go back into it against your own. Your life is over."

He threw her down, dragged her by her hair, beheaded her on the block, and then cut her into pieces—her blood pooling on the ground.

He tossed her body into the basin alongside her sister's.

"Now I'll go for the second one," the wizard said, and again he transformed into a beggar and begged at their house.

The second daughter brought him a piece of bread, and just like the first, he touched her, capturing her instantly, and took her away.

She met the same fate as her sister. Driven by her curiosity, she opened the door to the bloody chamber, looked inside, and paid for it dearly when the wizard came back.

Finally, he went after the third sister, who was clever and shrewd.

After he gave her the keys and the egg and left, she carefully stored the egg away.

Then she explored the house and eventually found her way into the forbidden room.

Alas, what a sight met her eyes! Both of her dear sisters lay in the basin, brutally murdered and dismembered.

But instead of panicking, she began to gather their pieces—heads, bodies, arms, and legs.

After she had everything in place, the pieces began to move and fit together until both sisters opened their eyes and came back to life.

They were filled with joy and embraced one another.

When the wizard returned, he immediately called for the keys and the egg, but since he found no blood on it, he said, "You have passed the test; you shall be my bride."

title: Fitcher's Bird
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
With that, he lost all power over her and was obligated to fulfill whatever she wished.

"Alright then," she said, "first take a basket full of gold to my parents and carry it on your back yourself. I'll prepare for the wedding while you’re away."

She rushed to her sisters, whom she had hidden in a small chamber, and told them, "The time has come to save you. The monster will carry you home, but as soon as you arrive, send help to me."

She placed them both in a basket, covering them with gold so no one could see them.

Then, she called for the wizard and directed him, "Now, carry this basket away. I'll be watching you through my little window to see if you stop or sit down along the way."

The wizard hoisted the basket onto his back and trudged away, but it was so heavy that sweat poured down his face.

He took a seat to rest for a moment, but one of the girls in the basket cried out, "I'm looking through my little window, and I can see you resting! Keep moving!"

He thought it was his bride speaking to him, so he stood up and continued on.

He tried to sit again, but she shouted, "I'm looking through my little window, and I see that you are resting! You need to keep going!"

Every time he stopped, she would call out this, forcing him to keep moving until, completely exhausted, he finally delivered the basket filled with gold and the two sisters to their parents' house.

Meanwhile, the bride prepared for the wedding feast, sending out invitations to the wizard's friends.

She took a skull with grinning teeth, dressed it up with ornaments and a floral wreath, and positioned it at the attic window to peek out.

When everything was ready, she climbed into a barrel of honey, then cut open a feather bed and rolled herself in it until she looked like a stunning bird that nobody would recognize.

As she stepped out of the house, she encountered some of the wedding guests, who asked, "Oh, Fitcher's Bird, how did you get here?"

"I came from Fitcher's house, which is very close by," she replied.

"And what is the young bride doing?" they inquired.

"She’s cleaned the house from cellar to attic, and now she’s peering out the window, I suppose."

Finally, she met the bridegroom, who was returning slowly.

"Oh, Fitcher's Bird, how did you end up here?" he asked.

"I came from the house of Fitcher, just over there," she answered.

"And what might the young bride be doing?"

"She’s tidied from cellar to attic, and now she’s peeking out from the window, I suppose."

The bridegroom looked up, saw the decorated skull, mistook it for his bride, and greeted it kindly.

But just as he and his guests entered the house, the bride's brothers and relatives, sent to rescue her, arrived.

They locked all the doors to prevent anyone from escaping, set the house on fire, and the wizard and all his friends were trapped inside.

title: Frau Trude
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once, there was a little girl who was stubborn and curious.

Whenever her parents asked her to do something, she simply refused.

It's no surprise she didn't get along well. One day, she told her

parents, 'I've heard so much about Frau Trude. I want to visit her one day.'

People say everything about her is bizarre, and her house is filled with strange things. I'm really curious.

Her parents firmly prohibited her from going, insisting that Frau Trude is a wicked woman who does terrible things.

'If you go to her, you're no longer our child,' they warned.

But the girl wasn't swayed by her parents' warnings.

She still decided to go see Frau Trude.

When she arrived, Frau Trude asked, 'Why do you look so pale?'

'Oh,' the girl replied, trembling all over, 'I've seen some frightening things.'

'What did you see?' the witch inquired.

'I saw a black man on your steps.' 'That was a coal miner.' 'Then I saw a green man.' 'That was a huntsman.'

'After that, I saw a blood-red man.' 'That was a butcher.' 'Oh, Frau Trude, I was so scared! I looked through the window and didn't see you, but, as I truly believe, the devil himself with a head of fire.'

'Oho!' she exclaimed. 'So you've finally seen the witch in her true form.'

'I've been waiting for you and wanting you for quite some time. You will bring me some light.'

Then, with a snap, she transformed the girl into a block of wood and tossed her into the fire.

As it blazed brightly, Frau Trude sat down next to it, warming herself and said, 'Now that's a lively fire for a change!'

title: Frederick and Catherine
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a man named Frederick.

He was married to a woman named Catherine.

Together, they lived as a young married couple. One day,

Frederick said, "I'm going to go plow now, Catherine. When

I return, there better be some roast meat waiting for me to eat

and a cool drink to satisfy my thirst." "Sure thing, Frederick,"

Catherine replied. "I’ll have it all ready for you." So when

it was almost dinner time, she pulled a sausage from the chimney,

placed it in a frying pan with some butter, and set it on the

fire. As the sausage started to sizzle, Catherine stood by,

holding the pan and lost in her own thoughts. Suddenly,

it struck her that while the sausage was cooking, she could go to the cellar and get some beer.

With that, she set the frying pan on the fire, grabbed a can,

and headed down to the cellar to draw some beer. The beer started pouring

into the can, and as she watched it flow, she panicked, thinking,

"Oh no! The dog upstairs isn’t tied up! It might get the

sausage from the pan!" Thank goodness I remembered!" Without wasting time,

she rushed up the cellar steps, but by the time she got there,

the dog had already snatched the sausage and was dragging it along.

Catherine, never one to sit idle, chased after it, running

a long way into the field. However, the dog was faster than

Catherine and wouldn’t drop the sausage, hopping deftly over

the furrows. "What’s lost is lost," she said, turning around.

After running until she was tired, she decided to walk back,

taking her time to cool down. Meanwhile,

the beer continued to pour out of the cask since Kate hadn’t

shut off the tap. By the time the can was full and there was no

more room for it, the beer overflowed into the cellar and didn’t stop until

the entire cask was drained. When Kate finally reached the steps,

she exclaimed, "Oh no! What will I do to keep Frederick from finding out?"

title: Frederick and Catherine
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She thought for a moment and finally remembered that she had a sack of

the finest wheat flour from the last fair in the attic.

She would bring that down and sprinkle it over the beer. "Yes," she said,

"if you save something when you should, you’ll have it when you need it." So she climbed

up to the attic and brought the sack down, and dumped it straight

into the can of beer, which toppled over, causing Frederick's drink to spill into the cellar.

"It’s fine," Kate assured herself. "Where one is, the other should be too," and she spread

the flour all over the cellar. Once she was done, she felt really proud of her work.

"Look how clean and fresh it all looks now!" At noon, Frederick came home.

"Now, my dear wife, what have you prepared for me?" "Oh, Freddy," she answered,

"I was frying a sausage for you, but while I was drawing beer to drink

with it, the dog grabbed it from the pan! And while I was chasing after the dog,

all the beer spilled out, and while I was using the flour to clean up the beer, I knocked the can over too.

But don’t worry, the cellar is all dry again." Frederick was not pleased.

"Catherine, Catherine, you shouldn’t have allowed the sausage to be taken, and let the beer run out of the cask,

and then wasted our flour in the process!" "Well, Frederick," she replied,

"I didn’t know that—you should have told me!" Frederick thought, "If this is the kind of wife I have,

I better take care of things myself." He had been saving quite a few thalers,

which he exchanged for gold, and said to Catherine, "Look,

these are yellow coins for playing games. I’m going to bury them in a pot

under the cow’s trough in the stable. But promise me you won’t go near them,

or you’ll regret it." "Of course not, Frederick, I promise I will stay away from them."

title: Frederick and Catherine
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
While Frederick was gone, some peddlers came into the village selling cheap earthen bowls and pots.

They asked Catherine if there was anything she wanted to trade.

"Oh, kind people," said Catherine, "I don’t have any money to buy anything.

But if you have any use for yellow coins, I can trade with you." "Yellow coins, you say? Why not?

But first, let’s see them!" "Go into the stable and dig under the cow’s trough.

You’ll find the yellow coins there!" "I’m not allowed to go there." The conmen didn't care,

they went to the stable, dug up the gold, and ran off, leaving all their pots and bowls behind.

Catherine thought she might as well use her new things, and since she already had plenty in the kitchen,

she smashed the bottom out of every pot and hung them as decorations around the house.

When Frederick returned and saw the new ornaments, he asked, "Catherine, what have you done?"

"I bought them, Frederick, with the coins that were under the cow's trough.

I didn’t go there myself—the peddlers had to dig them out!" "Oh my dear wife," said Frederick,

"what have you done? Those weren’t coins, they were pure gold, our entire fortune! You shouldn’t have done that!"

"Really, Frederick," she replied, "I didn’t know! You should have told me.

Catherine paused for a moment, then said, "Listen, Frederick, we’ll get the gold back.

Let’s run after the thieves!" "Alright then," Frederick agreed, "but take some butter and cheese with you,

so we have something to eat on the way." "Sure, Frederick, I’ll grab those!" They set off,

and since Frederick was the faster walker, Catherine followed him.

"This works for me," she thought, "because when we turn back, I’ll be a bit ahead." Then

title: Frederick and Catherine
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
she reached a hill where deep ruts carved the sides of the road.

"You can really see how they’ve damaged the earth here, it will never heal as long as it lives," she thought.

Feeling compassion, she took her butter and spread it along the ruts to ease their suffering.

While she was tending to the earth, one of the cheeses rolled out of her pocket down the hill.

"Once I’ve made my way up here, I’m not going down again," she decided.

"Someone else can go fetch it back." So she rolled another cheese down the hill.

But the cheeses didn't return, prompting her to let a third one roll down.

"Maybe they’re waiting for company and don’t want to go alone." After all three stayed away,

she thought, "It’s possible the third got lost and needs help.

I'll send a fourth to find it!" But the fourth cheese didn’t return either.

Catherine grew frustrated and threw down the fifth and sixth as well,

those being her last. She stood there a while, waiting for them to come back,

and when they still didn’t appear, she said, "You’re a fine bunch,

sending me out looking for death; you’re taking your sweet time.

I won’t wait for you any longer. I’m heading off, and you can run after me.

You’ve got younger legs than I do." With that, Catherine continued on and found

Frederick, who was waiting for her because he wanted something to eat.

"Now let’s see what you brought back," he said. She handed him the dry bread.

"Where’s the butter and the cheeses?" he asked. "Oh, Frederick,"

Catherine replied, "I spread the butter on the ruts and sent the cheeses after the runaway.

They’ll be here soon!" "You shouldn’t have smeared butter on the road, Catherine,

or let the cheeses roll down the hill!" "Honestly, Frederick," she said,

title: Frederick and Catherine
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"I didn’t know!" They ended up sharing the dry bread.

Frederick asked, "Did you secure the house before leaving?" "No, Frederick,

you should have told me to do that beforehand!" "Then go home and make sure the house is secure

before we go any further, and bring something else to eat.

I’ll wait here for you." Catherine went back, considering,

"Frederick must want something more to eat; he doesn’t like the butter and cheese.

So, I’ll grab a handkerchief filled with dried pears and a pitcher of vinegar for him to drink."

She thoroughly bolted the top part of the door, unhinged the bottom door,

and carried it with her, believing she had secured the house.

Catherine took her sweet time, thinking Frederick could rest a bit longer.

When she finally reached him, she said, "Here’s the door for you, Frederick.

Now you can take care of the house yourself!" "Oh heavens," he exclaimed,

"What a clever wife I have! You take the lower door off its hinges,

so everything can come in, and bolt the upper one. It’s too late to head back now,

but since you've brought the door here, you might as well carry it further.

"I will carry the door, Frederick, but the dried pears and the vinegar jug will be too heavy,

so I’ll hang them on the door. That way, the door can carry them!"

Soon, they entered the forest in search of the thieves, but came up short.

As it started getting dark, they climbed a tree to spend the night.

Just as they settled in, the thieves arrived,

who were known for taking what didn’t want to leave and discovering things before they were lost.

They gathered around the very tree where Frederick and Catherine were hiding,

lit a fire, and prepared to share their loot.

Frederick looked around, gathered some stones,

and jumped back down to throw them at the thieves.

However, his stones missed, prompting the thieves to say, "It’s almost morning; I think the wind is shaking down the fir cones."

title: Frederick and Catherine
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Catherine, still carrying the door on her back, felt the weight.

Attributing it to the dried pears, she said, "Frederick, I have to throw these pears down!"

"No, Catherine, not yet," he responded. "They might give us away!"

"But, Frederick, I really have to! They weigh me down too much!" "Then go ahead, but be careful."

Catherine tossed the pears down, and they rolled down through the branches.

The robbers below commented, "Look, it’s just bird droppings."

A little later, still burdened by the door, Catherine said, "Oh, Frederick, I must pour out the vinegar!"

"No, Catherine," he insisted. "You can’t! They might catch on!" "But I really must. It’s too heavy for me!"

"Then do it, but be cautious!" Catherine dumped the vinegar, splattering it on the robbers.

The thieves exclaimed, "The dew is falling!" Eventually, Catherine wondered,

"Is it really the door that's weighing me down?" She said, "Frederick, I have to drop the door!"

"Not now, Catherine! It could give us away!" "Oh, but, Frederick, I must!"

"It’s too heavy for me!" "Don’t let it fall!" "I’m letting it go, Frederick!"

"Then let it go, for all I care!" With that, the door crashed down,

making a loud noise, and the robbers shouted, "The devil is coming down the tree!" and fled,

abandoning everything behind. The next morning, when they descended,

they found all their gold and carried it home.

Back at home, Frederick said, "Now, Catherine, you also need to be diligent and work." "Of course, Frederick,

I'll head to the field and cut some corn!" When Catherine arrived in the field,

she wondered, "Should I eat before I cut or sleep first? Oh, I think I'll eat.

title: Frederick and Catherine
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But after eating, I feel sleepy, and then I began cutting.

Half-dreaming, she ended up slicing her apron, gown, and shift to shreds.

When Catherine finally awoke after her long nap, she realized she was almost naked,

saying to herself, "Is that me, or is it someone else? Oh no, it’s not me!" That evening,

Catherine ran into the village, knocked on her husband’s window, and called,

"What’s going on? I’d really like to know if Catherine is inside?" "Yes, yes," Frederick replied,

"She must be here and fast asleep." "Great! Then that means I’m definitely home!" Outside,

Catherine stumbled upon some thieves who were plotting to steal.

She approached them and said, "I’ll help you steal!" The robbers thought she knew the area well.

Catherine stepped in front of the houses and shouted,

"Hey folks, do you have anything we can take? We’re here to steal!"

The thieves thought to themselves, "This is an odd way of doing things,"

and wished they could be rid of Catherine once and for all.

They told her, "Just outside the village, the pastor has some turnips in his field.

Go over there and pull up some turnips for us!" Catherine went to the field,

but she was so lazy that she never stood upright.

A man saw her, stopped, and thought she was the devil digging up turnips.

He rushed back to the village and told the pastor,

"Pastor, the devil is in your turnip field, pulling up your turnips!" "Oh goodness," the pastor replied,

"I can’t go out to chase him away because I have a lame foot." The man said,

"Then I’ll carry you on my back!" And he did. When they arrived at the field,

Catherine stood up straight.

"Oh no, the devil!" the pastor shouted, and they both fled,

with the pastor running faster on his lame foot than the man could on his good legs.

title: Fundevogel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a forester who went into the woods to hunt.

As he entered the forest, he heard a desperate sound, like a child screaming.

Curious, he followed the noise and finally came to a tall tree.

At the top of the tree sat a little child. The child's mother had fallen asleep under the tree with him.

While she was napping, a bird of prey spotted the baby, swooped down, and snatched him away, placing him high in the tree.

The forester quickly climbed the tree, rescued the child, and thought to himself that he would take him home to raise alongside his own daughter, Lina.

So, he took the child home, and the two kids grew up together. The little boy, whom he had found in the tree, was called Fundevogel because a bird had carried him off.

Fundevogel and Lina loved each other so much that whenever they were apart, they felt sad.

One evening, the forester's old cook, Sanna, took two buckets and kept going back and forth to the spring to fetch water.

Lina noticed and asked, "Why are you fetching so much water, Sanna?"

"If you promise never to tell anyone, I'll tell you why," Sanna replied.

Lina nodded, assuring her that she wouldn't tell a soul. Sanna continued, "Early tomorrow morning, when the forester goes hunting, I'm going to heat up the water. When it's boiling, I'll throw Fundevogel in and cook him."

The next morning, the forester left for his hunt, and the children were still in bed.

Lina turned to Fundevogel and said, "If you promise to never leave me, I promise I won’t leave you either."

Fundevogel responded, "Neither now nor ever will I leave you."

Lina then said, "Then I need to tell you something. Last night, Sanna carried so many buckets of water into the house, I asked her why. She said that if I wouldn’t tell anyone, she would explain. I promised I wouldn’t say a word, and she told me she plans to boil you in the kettle once Father is out hunting. But we need to get up right now, get dressed, and leave together."

title: Fundevogel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The two kids quickly got out of bed, dressed, and slipped away.

Just as the kettle began to boil, the cook entered the bedroom to grab Fundevogel.

However, when she reached the beds, both children were gone.

Panic-stricken, she thought to herself, "What will I say when the forester returns and finds the children missing? I must follow them and get them back immediately!"

She sent three servants after them, instructing them to run and catch the kids.

Meanwhile, the children were sitting outside the forest, and when they saw the three servants approaching, Lina said to Fundevogel, "Never leave me, and I won’t leave you."

Fundevogel replied, "Neither now nor ever."

Then Lina said, "Transform into a rosebush, and I’ll be the rose on it."

When the three servants arrived, all they found was a rosebush with a single rose, and the children were nowhere to be seen.

Disappointed, they announced, "There's nothing here, let's go back and tell the cook."

They returned and informed the cook, "We didn't find them; all we saw was a rosebush with one rose on it."

The old cook scolded them, saying, "You fools! You should have cut the bush in half and brought the rose home! Now go back and search again!"

Reluctantly, the servants went out to look again.

However, the children spotted them coming from a distance.

Lina quickly told Fundevogel, "Never leave me, and I won’t leave you."

Fundevogel assured her, "Neither now nor ever."

Then Lina said, "Become a church, and I'll be the chandelier inside it."

When the servants arrived, they encountered a church with a chandelier inside, but no trace of the children.

They looked at each other and said, "What should we do now? Let’s head back home."

Upon their return, the cook asked if they had found the kids.

They replied, "No, we only found a church, and there was a chandelier inside it."

The cook scolded them again, "Why didn’t you take the church apart and bring the chandelier back?"

Now, the cook decided to join the servants in chasing after the children.

The children noticed from a distance that the cook was waddling after the three servants.

Lina said to Fundevogel, "Never leave me, and I won’t leave you."

Fundevogel replied, "Neither now nor ever."

Then Lina said, "Turn into a pond, and I'll be the duck swimming on it."

When the cook caught up with them, she spotted the pond and lay down beside it, ready to drink.

But the duck quickly swam over, seized the cook's head in its beak, and pulled her into the water, where the old witch drowned.

Afterward, the children joyfully returned home, and if they haven’t passed away, they’re still living happily to this day.

title: Gambling Hansel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a man who spent all his time gambling,

so everyone just called him Gambling Hansel.

He gambled constantly and eventually lost his home and everything he owned.

The day before his creditors were set to take his house, the Lord and St. Peter came to him.

They asked if they could stay with him for the night.

Gambling Hansel replied, "You're welcome to stay, but I can't offer you a bed or food."

The Lord told him it was fine; they would buy their own food. Gambling Hansel didn't argue.

Then St. Peter gave him three groschen and said he should go to the baker's and get some bread.

Gambling Hansel set off, but when he arrived at the house where other gamblers were hanging out,

they, who had already won everything from him, called out, "Hansel, come in!"

He replied, "Oh, do you want to win three groschen too?"

They wouldn't let him leave, so he went in and ended up losing the three groschen as well.

Meanwhile, St. Peter and the Lord were waiting for him, and when he took too long, they went to find him.

When Gambling Hansel finally showed up, he pretended that the money had fallen into the gutter,

and while rummaging around in it, he knew he had lost it while playing.

St. Peter gave him three groschen again, but this time he didn’t let himself be distracted.

He went and brought back the loaf. Our Lord then asked if he had any wine.

Gambling Hansel replied, "Oh no, sir, all the casks are empty."

But the Lord told him to go down to the cellar because the best wine was still there.

At first, he didn't believe it, but he finally said, "Fine, I'll check, but I doubt there's any left."

When he turned the tap, to his surprise, the best wine started pouring out.

He took it to them, and they spent the night there together.

The next morning, our Lord told Gambling Hansel he could ask for three favors.

title: Gambling Hansel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The Lord figured he would wish to enter heaven, but Gambling Hansel asked for a deck of cards to win everything,

dice that would make him win every time, and a tree that bore every kind of fruit,

from which no one could come down until he said so.

The Lord granted him all his wishes and left with St. Peter.

Immediately, Gambling Hansel dove into gambling for real this time, and soon he had acquired half the world.

Seeing this, St. Peter said to the Lord, "This can't continue, soon he'll win the entire world.

We need to send Death to him."

So they sent Death to him.

When Death arrived, Gambling Hansel was sitting at the gaming table.

Death said, "Hansel, come out for a moment."

But Gambling Hansel replied, "Just give me a minute; I need to finish this game.

In the meantime, why don’t you climb that tree and grab some fruit for us to snack on while we walk?"

So, Death climbed up, but when he tried to come down, he couldn’t.

Gambling Hansel left him up there for seven years; during that time, nobody died.

St. Peter said to the Lord, "This cannot continue.

People are living forever; we need to go ourselves."

So they went, and the Lord told Gambling Hansel to let Death come down.

Gambling Hansel immediately went to Death and said, "Come down now."

Death took him away right then, and that was the end for him.

They went off together to the next world, and Gambling Hansel knocked on the door of heaven.

"Who’s there?" they asked.

"Gambling Hansel," he replied.

"We don’t want anything to do with him. Go away!"

Next, he went to the door of purgatory and knocked again.

"Who’s there?" they asked.

"Gambling Hansel," he replied.

"We have enough crying and wailing here already.

We don't want him; just go away!"

Finally, he went to the door of hell, and they let him in.

However, the only ones home were old Lucifer and the crooked devils; the straight ones were busy elsewhere.

As soon as Hansel got there, he started gambling again.

Lucifer had nothing of value left to lose except his ugly devils, and Gambling Hansel easily won them over.

Now he was off again with his crooked devils, and they went to Hohenfurt.

They pulled up a hop-pole and headed toward heaven, using it to poke at the door, causing it to crack.

St. Peter said to the Lord, "We can't let this go on; if we don’t let him in, he’ll bring down all of heaven."

So they admitted him.

But Gambling Hansel immediately started playing again, creating such chaos that nobody could hear themselves speak.

Therefore, St. Peter once again said, "This cannot continue. We have to throw him out, or he will make all of heaven rebel.

So they went to him and threw him back out, shattering his soul into pieces that mingled with the gambling vagabonds still roaming today.

title: God's Food
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there were two sisters. One sister was wealthy but childless, while the other had five children, was a widow, and was so poor that she could barely feed herself and her kids.

Desperate to provide for her family, the poor sister went to her wealthy sibling and said, "I’m starving, and my children are too. Please, could you spare us a bit of bread?"

But the wealthy sister was cold-hearted as a stone. She replied, "I have nothing for myself, so why should I give you anything?" and turned her away with harsh words.

Eventually, the rich sister's husband came home and was about to cut himself some bread. However, as he sliced the loaf, blood poured out.

Frightened, the wife told him what had happened. He rushed to assist the widow and her children, but when he entered their home, he found her in prayer.

She cradled her two youngest children, while her three older ones lay lifeless. He offered her food, but she replied, "We no longer crave earthly food. God has already filled the hunger of three of us, and He will hear our prayers too."

Just as she finished speaking, her two little ones took their final breaths, shattering her heart and causing her to collapse.

title: Godfather Death
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
A poor man had twelve children and worked day and night just to put food on the table. When his thirteenth child was born, he felt overwhelmed and didn't know what to do. In his distress, he ran out to the main road and decided to ask the first person he encountered to be the child's godfather.

The first person he met was God, who already knew what was on his mind. God said, "I feel for you, poor man. I will be your child's godfather and take care of them, ensuring they find happiness in their life."

The man replied, "Who are you?" God answered, "I am God." The man quickly responded, "I don't want you to be my child's godfather. You only help the wealthy and leave the poor to suffer." While the man spoke his truth, he failed to understand how God distributes wealth and hardship.

Turning away from God, he continued down the road until he encountered the devil. The devil asked, "What are you looking for? If you choose me as your child's godfather, I’ll provide a wealth of gold and all the pleasures this world has to offer."

The man asked, "Who are you?" The devil replied, "I am the devil." The man said, "I don’t want you as a godfather; you deceive people and lead them astray." He kept walking, and soon after, he came across Death, who approached him with thin legs.

Death said, "Choose me as your child's godfather." The man asked, "Who are you?" Death responded, "I am Death, and I make everyone equal." The man realized that Death was the right choice since he takes both the rich and the poor, without difference, so he agreed.

Death then said, "I will make your child rich and renowned. With me as a friend, they will want for nothing." The man replied, "The christening is next Sunday; make sure you come at the right time." Death promised to appear.

title: Godfather Death
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
On the day of the christening, Death showed up and served as godfather just as one would expect.

As the boy grew, one day his godfather appeared and took him into a forest. Death pointed out a special herb and said, "Now you shall receive your godfather's gift. I will make you a famous doctor. Whenever you get called to a patient, I will always be there for you.

"If I'm standing by the patient's head, you can confidently say you will heal them. If you give them this herb, they will recover. However, if I stand by their feet, they are mine, and you must admit that no medicine can save them. Just be careful not to use the herb against my wishes, or you may regret it."

Before long, the young man became the most renowned physician in the world. He could simply look at a patient and instantly know their fate—whether they would recover or not. People traveled from far and wide to seek his help, and he earned so much money that he soon became wealthy.

One day, the king fell ill and summoned the physician to determine if the king could recover. When the physician arrived, Death stood by the foot of the bed, and no herb existed that could save him. "If only I could outsmart Death just this once," thought the physician. He figured that since he was Death's godson, he might look the other way if he did.

Taking a chance, he turned the king’s body so now Death was at the head. Then he administered the herbs, and the king got better and became healthy again.

But Death appeared to the physician, looking very displeased and angry. He shook his finger and said, "You've betrayed me. Since you're my godson, I won’t take immediate action against you, but if you ever try that again, it will cost you your life; I'll come for you next."

Not long after, the king's only daughter fell gravely ill. The king was distraught, crying day and night until he could barely see. He announced that whoever saved her life would marry her and inherit the throne.

title: Godfather Death
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When the physician arrived at the sick girl’s bedside, he saw Death at her feet. He should have recalled his godfather's warning, but he was so enchanted by the beauty of the princess and the prospect of becoming her husband that he cast caution to the wind. He ignored the warning signs of Death raising his hand and glaring at him.

Disregarding everything, he turned the sick girl around, placing her head where her feet had been. He fed her some of the herb, and in no time, her cheeks flushed with color, and life returned to her.

Seeing that his property had been misused once again, Death approached the physician, long strides taking him closer. He declared, "It's all over for you now, and you will pay the price," and seized him with his icy grip, making it impossible for him to resist as he was led away into an underground cave.

Inside, the physician saw thousands of candles burning in endless rows—some large, some medium-sized, and some small. Flames flickered in constant change as some candles burned out while others flared up.

Death gestured and said, "These are the lights of people's lives. The big ones belong to children, the medium ones are for married folks in their prime, and the small ones belong to the elderly. Even children often have only a tiny candle.

"Show me the light of my life," said the physician, assuming it was still tall. But Death pointed to a little flicker that looked like it would go out at any moment. "Behold, this is it."

The physician gasped, "Oh, dear godfather, please light a new candle for me, for the love of me! I want to live, to be king, and marry the beautiful princess!"

"I cannot," Death replied, "until a new one is lit. Just place the old one on top of a new one so it will continue to burn after the old one goes out," pleaded the physician. Death pretended to humor him and grasped a tall new candle.

But in his desire for revenge, he purposely made a mistake and the small piece fell, extinguishing it entirely. In that moment, the physician collapsed to the ground, and now he faced his own end.

title: Going A-Travelling
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a poor woman who had a son eager to travel.

But his mother replied, "How can you travel? We don’t have any money at all for you to take with you."

The son responded, "I’ll manage just fine on my own."

"I’ll just say, not much, not much."

He walked for a long time, repeating, "Not much, not much, not much."

Eventually, he came across a group of fishermen and called out, "Good luck, guys! Not much, not much, not much."

"What do you mean, you fool?" one fisherman retorted. "Not much?"

When they pulled up their net, they discovered they hadn't caught much at all.

Angry, one of the fishermen hit the boy with a stick and exclaimed, "Haven’t you ever seen me working? What should I say then?"

"You should say, ‘Get it full, get it full!" the youth replied.

After that, he walked on for a long while, now saying, "Get it full, get it full!" until he reached the gallows.

There, he found a poor sinner who was about to be hanged.

He cheerfully said, "Good morning! Get it full, get it full!"

"What do you mean, you rascal? Get it full? Are you suggesting there are even worse people in the world? Isn’t this enough?"

In response, he received a few hits on his back.

"What should I say then?" he asked.

"You must say, ‘May God have pity on the poor soul."

Continuing on his way, the boy began to say, "May God have pity on the poor soul."

Soon he arrived at a pit where a knacker was chopping up a horse.

"Good morning! May God have pity on the poor soul," the youth said.

The knacker, annoyed, replied, "What do you say, you ill-tempered brat?" and slapped him so hard that he couldn't see straight.

"What should I say then?" he complained.

"You must say, ‘Let the carrion lie in the pit."

He walked on, repeating, "Let the carrion lie in the pit, let the carrion lie in the pit."

Soon after, he encountered a cart full of people and said, "Good morning! Let the carrion lie in the pit."

At that moment, the cart tumbled into a pit, and the driver lashed out at the youth with his whip.

The boy was forced to crawl back to his mother, and from then on, he never ventured out again.

title: Gossip Wolf and the Fox
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The she-wolf welcomed a little pup into the world and invited the fox to be the godfather.

"After all, he’s a close relative of ours," she said.

"He’s intelligent and talented; he can teach my little one and help him succeed in life." The fox, too,

seemed quite sincere and replied, "Dear Mrs. Gossip, I appreciate the honor you’re giving me.

I’ll make sure I repay you for this gesture." He enjoyed himself at the celebration, laughing and eating.

Later, he said, "Dear Mrs. Gossip, we have a responsibility to care for the child.

He needs good food to grow strong. I know a sheepfold where we can find a tasty treat."

The wolf liked the idea and went with the fox to the farmyard.

He pointed to the fold from a distance, saying, "You can sneak in there without being noticed.

In the meantime, I’ll scout around the other side to see if I can find a chicken."

However, he didn’t do that. Instead, he settled at the edge of the forest, stretched out his legs, and rested.

The she-wolf crept into the barn. There was a dog lying there, and it barked so loudly that the farmers rushed out,

caught the gossip wolf, and poured a strong, burning mixture meant for washing over her.

Finally, she managed to escape and dragged herself outside.

The fox was lying there, pretending to be injured, and said, "Oh, dear Mrs. Gossip, I've had such bad luck!

The farmers attacked me and broke every bone in my body! If you don’t want me to lie here and die, you must carry me."

The she-wolf was moving slowly herself, but because she was so worried about the fox, she placed him on her back and slowly took him back to her den.

Once they arrived, the fox said, "Goodbye, dear Mrs. Gossip! I hope that roasting you just endured does you well!" He laughed heartily and hopped away.

title: Hans in Luck
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
After working for his master for seven years, Hans said to him,

"Master, my time is up, and I'm eager to head back home to my

mother. Please give me my wages." The master replied, "You have served me

faithfully and honestly, and you will receive a reward that matches your service."

With that, he handed Hans a piece of gold as big as his head. Hans pulled out his

handkerchief, wrapped up the heavy gold, placed it on his shoulder, and started on his

journey home.

As he made his way, steadily putting one foot in front of the other, he saw a

horseman trotting happily by on a spirited horse. "Ah," exclaimed Hans, quite loudly, "what a

wonderful thing it is to ride! You sit like you're in a chair, avoid stumbling

over stones, save your shoes, and cover more ground than you'd think!"

The rider, who heard him, stopped and called out, "Hey there, Hans, why are you walking then?"

"I have to," replied Hans, "because I'm carrying this lump home. It's true it is gold,

but it's so heavy that I can't keep my head up!"

"Tell you what," the rider said, "let's make a trade; I'll give you my horse, and you can

give me your lump." "I'd be happy to, but I must warn you, you'll have to crawl with it."

The rider dismounted, took the gold, helped Hans up, and then handed him the reins, saying,

"To ride fast, just click your tongue and say, 'jup, jup!'"

Hans was thrilled as he climbed onto the horse and rode away feeling bold and free.

After a little while, he thought it should go faster, so he clicked his tongue and shouted,'jup.'

Suddenly, the horse broke into a sharp trot, and before Hans realized it, he was thrown off and

landed in a ditch separating the fields from the highway.

title: Hans in Luck
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The horse would have bolted too, if not for a farmer passing by who stopped it.

Hans gathered himself, stood up, and feeling frustrated, said to the farmer, "This

riding business is a poor joke, especially when you end up on a horse that kicks and throws.

I’d have a better chance of breaking my neck! I won't ride again. But I like your cow; at least you

can walk peacefully behind her and get milk, butter, and cheese without issue. What wouldn't I give for a

cow like that!"

"Well," the farmer said, "if that would make you happy, I'm willing to trade the cow for your horse."

Hans eagerly agreed, and as the farmer mounted the horse, Hans led the cow along, thinking about his lucky

bargain. "If only I can find a piece of bread—and that should be easy enough—then I can enjoy butter and cheese as often as I want. If I'm

thirsty, I can milk my cow and have fresh milk. Oh, what a life!"

When he arrived at an inn, he stopped and, feeling very hungry, ate everything he had—his dinner,

supper, and whatever else he brought, finishing with a small glass of beer with his last few coins.

After that, he continued down the road with his cow toward his mother's village.

As midday approached, the heat became quite intense, and Hans found himself crossing a moor that took about an hour.

He felt very hot, and his tongue stuck to the roof of his mouth from thirst. "I know how to solve this," Hans thought.

"I'll milk the cow and refresh myself with some milk." He tied her to a withered tree, and since he had no bucket,

he tried to catch the milk in his leather cap. But despite his best efforts, not a single drop came.

Frustrated and clumsy, the old cow kicked him so hard on the head that he fell to the ground and couldn't remember where he was for a while.

Fortunately, just then a butcher strolled by with a wheelbarrow that had a small pig inside.

title: Hans in Luck
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"What are you doing here?" he exclaimed, helping Hans up. Hans explained what had happened.

The butcher handed him his flask and said, "Take a drink to cool off. That cow won't give you any milk; she's too old.

She's more useful in the field or for someone like me."

"Well, well," said Hans, smoothing his hair down, "who would have guessed? It's quite the treat when you can take down an animal at home.

Just think of the meat! I'm not a fan of beef, though; it doesn't taste juicy enough for me. But a young pig like that now, that's the ticket.

Listen, Hans," said the butcher, "out of kindness to you, I'll trade the pig for your cow."

"Heaven bless you for your generosity!" Hans said, handing over the cow, while the butcher untied the pig.

As Hans continued on, he thought about how everything was going the way he wanted.

Even when little annoyances came up, they quickly resolved themselves. Soon, he met a boy carrying a beautiful white goose.

They greeted each other, and Hans began to share his good fortune and how he'd always made great trades.

The boy said he was taking the goose to a christening feast. "Just lift her," he added, grabbing her by the wings, "she's heavy—she's been fattened up for the last eight weeks.

Whoever gets a piece of her when she's roasted will have to wipe the fat off both sides of their mouth!"

"Yeah, she's definitely a good weight," said Hans, holding her with one hand, "but my pig is just as good."

As they spoke, the boy glanced around suspiciously, finally saying, "Look, it might not be right with your pig.

In the village I just came from, the mayor's pig was stolen from its sty. I'm worried that you might have gotten it in that village.

They've sent out search parties, and getting caught with that pig would land you in a lot of trouble, at the very least, you could end up in jail!"

title: Hans in Luck
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Hans was terrified. "Oh my goodness, help me out of this! You know more about this place than I do, just take my pig and let me have your goose!"

"I don't want to risk it entirely," replied the boy, "but I don't want you to get in trouble either."

With that, he took the rope and quickly took the pig away.

Now free from worry, Hans went home with the goose under his arm.

As he thought about it, he realized he had gained from the trade. First, there’s the roast meat, then the fat that will drip from it.

That will give me dripping for my bread for months, and not to mention those lovely white feathers.

I'll stuff my pillow with them, and I'll sleep so well! Oh, my mother will be so happy!

As he approached the last village, he noticed a scissors-grinder with his barrow. His wheel spun as he sang,

"I sharpen scissors and grind them quick, my coat flies out behind me like a flick!"

Hans stopped to watch him. After a while, he spoke up, saying, "Everything seems good for you, you're so cheerful while grinding!"

"Yes," the scissors-grinder replied, "this trade is made of gold. A true grinder is a man who always finds gold in his pocket.

But where did you get that fine goose?"

"I didn't buy it; I traded my pig for it."

"I gave a gold lump as big as my head for that pig; it's what I earned for seven years of service."

"You really know how to make the best of things every time," said the grinder. "If you can make it so that every time you stand up, you hear that jingle of coins in your pocket, you'll have done well for yourself."

"How do I manage that?" Hans asked.

"You just have to be a grinder like me. All you need is a grindstone, and the rest will take care of itself.

title: Hans in Luck
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
I have one here; it's a bit worn but certainly rocks, and I only need your goose for it. Want to do it?"

"How could you even ask?" Hans replied. "I'll be the luckiest guy ever. If I have money every time I reach into my pocket,

why would I ever worry again?" With that, he handed over the goose and got the grindstone in return.

"Now," said the grinder, picking up a heavy stone nearby, "here's a sturdy stone for you, too. It’ll be great for hammering and straightening nails. Keep it safe."

Hans happily loaded himself with the stones and headed out, filled with joy, his eyes sparkling.

"I must have been born under a lucky star!" he exclaimed, "Everything I want seems to happen like magic!"

But as the day wore on, after being on his feet since dawn, he started to feel tired.

Hunger was hitting him hard, as in his excitement about getting the cow, he had consumed all his food at once.

Finally, he could only continue with great effort, having to stop often because the stones weighed him down.

Then he couldn't help but think how nice it would be to lack such a burden.

He crawled like a snail to a well in a field, hoping for a refreshing drink of cool water.

However, so as not to damage the stones, he placed them gently on the edge of the well.

As he sat down and leaned to drink, he accidentally nudged the stones, and both fell into the water.

When Hans saw them sink, he jumped for joy, then knelt down with tears in his eyes, thanking God for delivering him from those heavy stones.

"No one on earth is as lucky as I am!" he exclaimed. With a light heart, and now free from all burdens, he sprinted onward until he reached his destination.

title: Hans Married
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a young peasant named Hans, and his uncle had big plans for him.

His uncle was determined to find Hans a wealthy wife. He positioned Hans behind the stove and made it very hot.

Then, he grabbed a pot of milk and a bunch of white bread, handed Hans a shiny, freshly minted farthing, and said, 'Hans, hold onto that farthing tightly, crumble the white bread into the milk, and don’t move from this spot until I return.'

'Sure,' said Hans, 'I’ll do everything you've asked.'

With that, his uncle put on a pair of old patched trousers, went over to the daughter of a rich peasant in the next village, and asked, 'How about marrying my nephew Hans? You'll get an honest and sensible man who is perfect for you.'

The greedy father asked, 'What about his finances? Does he have bread to break?'

'Oh, dear friend,' the uncle replied, 'my young nephew has a good position, some money saved up, and plenty of bread to share. Plus, he has just as many patches as I do.'

As he spoke, he pointed at the patches on his trousers, but in that area, small pieces of land were also called patches.

'If you come home with me, you’ll see that everything I’ve said is true.'

The miser didn’t want to miss out on such a good deal, so he replied, 'If that's the case, I have no objections.'

The wedding took place on the scheduled day, and when the young bride stepped outside to check out her groom's property, Hans switched from his Sunday coat to his patched smock.

'I might ruin my nice coat,' he said.

They walked out together, and whenever a vineyard or a field came into view, Hans would point and then slap either a large or small patch on his smock, saying, 'That patch is mine, and so is that one, my dear, just look!

He meant that his wife shouldn’t gaze at the expansive land, but rather at his garment.

'Were you at the wedding too?' 'Absolutely! I was right there, all dressed up.'

'My headpiece was made of snow, but then the sun came out and it melted away. My coat was crafted from cobwebs, and while passing by some thorns, they snagged it and tore it off me. My shoes were made of glass, and when I stepped on a stone, they went 'klink' and broke in half.'

title: Hans the Hedgehog
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a wealthy farmer who had lots of money and land, but

despite his riches, he felt something was missing in his life -

he had no children. Often when he went to town with the other

farmers, they teased him about not having kids. Eventually,

this made him angry, and when he got home, he declared, "I will have a child,

even if it's a hedgehog." Shortly after, his wife gave birth to a child who had the

body of a boy but the upper part of a hedgehog, and

when she saw the baby, she was terrified, saying, "Look, you’ve brought bad luck upon us." The man replied,

"What can we do now? The boy needs to be christened, but we won’t be able to find a

godfather for him." His wife said, "We can't even name him." After the christening, the parson remarked,

"He can't sleep in a regular bed because of his spikes." So they put a bit of straw behind

the stove, and that's where Hans the hedgehog was placed. His mother couldn't nurse him because his quills would have pricked her.

So he stayed behind the stove for eight years, and his father grew tired of him, often wishing he would just die.

But Hans didn't die.

One day, there was a fair in town, and the farmer was getting ready to go,

so he asked his wife what he should bring back for her. "A bit of meat and a few white rolls for the house," she said.

He then asked the maid what she wanted, and she asked for a pair of slippers and some stockings with patterns. Finally,

he turned to Hans and asked, "And what would you like, Hans my hedgehog?" "Dear father," he said, "please bring me bagpipes." So, when the father

returned home, he gave his wife the meat and white rolls, the maid her slippers and stockings, and then he went behind the stove to

hand Hans the hedgehog his bagpipes.

title: Hans the Hedgehog
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once he had the bagpipes, Hans said, "Dear father, please go to the blacksmith and get the rooster shod, and then I will ride away,

never to return." His father was overjoyed at the idea of getting rid of him, so he had the rooster shod,

and when it was ready, Hans the hedgehog climbed on it and rode off, taking some pigs and donkeys with him that he planned to keep in

the forest. When they arrived, he managed to get the rooster to fly up to a tall tree with him,

and there he sat for many years, taking care of his pigs and donkeys until his herd grew quite large, while his father had no idea where he was.

While sitting in the tree, he played his bagpipes, and the music was truly beautiful.

One day, a king stumbled upon the music while traveling, and being intrigued, he sent his servant to investigate.

The servant searched but only found a small creature sitting on a tree that looked like a rooster with a hedgehog on it making the music.

Then the king instructed his servant to ask why the creature was there and if it knew the way to his kingdom.

Hans the hedgehog came down from the tree and said he would show the way if the king promised him whatever the first thing was

he met in the royal courtyard upon returning home. The king thought, I can easily do that; Hans the hedgehog knows nothing, and I can

write whatever I want. So the king took a pen and penned a promise,

and once he was finished, Hans the hedgehog led him home safely. When his daughter saw him from a distance, she was so happy that she rushed to him and gave him a kiss.

Then he recalled Hans the hedgehog and told her what had happened, including that he’d been forced to promise whatever would greet him when he got home, to a very odd creature on a rooster.

He explained to her that instead of writing what he wanted, he had written that he could not have it. Hearing this, the princess felt relieved and said he did well, for

she would never have gone with the hedgehog.

Meanwhile, Hans the hedgehog kept taking care of his pigs and remains happy as he continued to sit in the tree playing his bagpipes.

title: Hans the Hedgehog
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Another day, a different king came by with his entourage, also lost in the forest.

Again, he heard the enchanting music and asked his messenger what it could be, instructing him to find out.

The messenger approached the tree and spotted the rooster with Hans the hedgehog riding on it.

He asked him what he was doing up there. Hans replied, "I'm taking care of my pigs and donkeys; what do you want?" The messenger explained that they were lost and couldn't find their way back home.

Hans the hedgehog climbed down from the tree with his rooster and told the elderly king that he would show him the way if he promised him whatever the first thing was

that met him at the royal palace. The king agreed and wrote him a promise.

Once that was settled, Hans rode ahead on the rooster, guiding the king back to his kingdom.

When they arrived at the courtyard, there was much celebration.

The king had a single beautiful daughter who rushed to embrace him, overjoyed to have her father back.

She asked him where he had been all this time. He told her about losing his way and how he had been nearly lost forever, but the half-man, half-hedgehog had helped him find it.

However, in return, he had promised the creature whatever first met him in the royal courtyard - which happened to be her, and that made him quite unhappy.

Nonetheless, she reassured him that for her father's sake, she would agree to go with Hans.

Meanwhile, Hans the hedgehog tended to his pigs, and they multiplied to the point that the forest was filled with them.

Hans the hedgehog decided he didn’t want to live in the forest anymore and sent word to his father to prepare all the pig pens in the village because he was coming with an enormous herd.

Upon hearing this, his father was troubled, believing Hans had died long ago.

But Hans the hedgehog climbed back on his rooster and led the pigs into the village while commanding the people to kill as many as they wished.

The scene turned into a slaughter that could be heard for miles.

When all was done, Hans the hedgehog said, "Father, let me have the rooster shod again at the blacksmith, and I'll ride away and never come back again." So, his father had the rooster shod once more, happy that Hans was about to leave.

title: Hans the Hedgehog
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Hans rode to the first kingdom, where the king had ordered that anyone who arrived riding a rooster and carrying bagpipes should be shot, cut down, or stabbed to keep him from entering the palace.

As Hans approached, everyone attacked him with their pikes, but he urged his rooster upward, and it soared over the gate right in front of the king's window.

Hans yelled that the king needed to fulfill his promise, or he would take both his life and his daughter’s.

The king then asked his daughter to run away with Hans to save both their lives.

She put on a white dress, and her father prepared a carriage with six horses and lavish attendants, along with gold and riches.

She climbed into the carriage, sitting beside Hans with the rooster and bagpipes,

they took off, and the king believed he would never see her again.

But he was wrong. As they got a bit away from the town, Hans the hedgehog took away her beautiful clothes and poked her with his spikes until she bled.

"This is the reward for your deception," he said. "You can go; I don't want you," and he chased her back home, leaving her shamed.

Meanwhile, Hans the hedgehog continued on his rooster with his bagpipes to the second kingdom where he had previously led the way.

This king had instructed that anyone resembling Hans the hedgehog should be honored, given safe passage, praised, and lead to the royal palace.

When the king's daughter saw him, she was frightened because he did look quite strange.

But she knew she couldn't go back on her promise to her father, so she greeted Hans the hedgehog and married him.

He went to the royal table beside her, and they shared a meal together.

Later that night, when it was time to sleep, she was anxious about his quills, but he assured her she had nothing to worry about,

and asked the old king to have four men guard their chamber and start a big fire.

He explained that when he was ready for bed, he would shed his hedgehog skin and leave it by the bedside, and those men needed to grab it quickly,

throw it into the fire, and stay by it until it was burned away.

When the clock struck eleven, he entered the room, stripped off his hedgehog skin, and left it by the bed.

The men swiftly took it and threw it into the fire, and once it was burned away, he was free, lying in bed as a handsome young man,

though he was soot-covered, as if he had just emerged from a fire.

The king summoned his physician, who washed him with fine ointments and restored him to a handsome appearance.

When the king's daughter saw how he had transformed, she was overjoyed.

The next day, they woke with happiness, feasted together, and then they properly celebrated their marriage, with Hans the hedgehog receiving the kingdom from the old king.

Years later, Hans went to visit his father and proclaimed that he was his son.

His father, however, insisted that he had no son, only one who was born looking like a hedgehog with spikes, and that he had gone off into the world.

Then Hans revealed himself, and his old father was filled with joy and accompanied him to the castle.

title: Herr Korbes
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a rooster and a hen who decided they wanted to go on an adventure.

So, the rooster built a stunning carriage that had four bright red wheels, and he hitched four mice to pull it.

The hen hopped in alongside the rooster, and off they went together.

Before long, they ran into a cat who asked, "Where are you headed?"

The rooster replied, "We’re on our way to the house of Herr Korbes."

“Take me with you,” said the cat.

The rooster agreed, saying, "Of course! Just sit behind me so you won’t fall off in the front."

"Be careful not to get my lovely red wheels dirty! And you, little wheels, keep rolling. And you, little mice, make some noise as we travel to Herr Korbes' house!"

Then a millstone joined them, followed by an egg, then a duck, then a pin, and finally a needle, all jumping onto the carriage to ride along.

When they arrived at Herr Korbes' house, they found that he wasn't home.

The mice pulled the carriage into the barn, while the hen and rooster perched themselves up high.

The cat lounged by the fireplace, the duck sat atop the well-pole, the egg rolled onto a towel, the pin lodged itself into the chair cushion, the needle jumped on the bed right in the middle of the pillow, and the millstone positioned itself over the door.

Eventually, Herr Korbes came back home and headed for the fireplace to light a fire, but the cat tossed a bunch of ashes right in his face.

Startled, he rushed into the kitchen to wash it off, only for the duck to splash water in his face.

He tried to dry his face with a towel, but the egg rolled against him, broke, and glued his eyes shut.

Wanting to take a break, he sat down in a chair, but the pin pricked him.

Fuming, he threw himself onto his bed, but as soon as he rested his head on the pillow, the needle jabbed him again, causing him to scream.

In a fit of rage, he stormed toward the house door to escape into the wide world, but just as he reached the door, the millstone fell and struck him down.

Clearly, Herr Korbes must have been a very wicked man.

title: How Six Men Got On in the World
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once there was a man skilled in many crafts, and he served in

the military where he showed bravery and good judgment. However, when the war ended, he was dismissed and given just three farthings to cover his travel expenses.

"This is unacceptable!" he exclaimed. "I won’t settle for this! If I can just meet the right people, I know the king will have to reward me!"

Out of frustration, he headed into the forest, where he spotted a man who was lifting six trees as if they were mere blades of grass.

"Will you join me as my servant?" the man asked. "Sure," the tree-lifter replied, "but first I need to take this bundle of sticks home to my mother." He grabbed one of the trees, wrapped it around the other five, hoisted the bundle onto his back, and set off.

After that, he returned to the man, and together they ventured forward. "With the two of us, we'll surely do well in the world!" the man said.

As they walked for a short time, they came across a huntsman who was kneeling with his gun ready to fire. "What are you about to shoot?" asked the man.

"About two miles away is a fly sitting on an oak tree, and I’m aiming for it!" the huntsman replied.

"Come along with us!" the man urged. "If the three of us stick together, we can surely make it in the world!" The huntsman agreed and joined them.

Soon they stumbled upon seven windmills with their sails spinning wildly, even though there wasn't a breath of wind in the air.

"What’s powering those windmills?" the man wondered aloud, noticing that not even a leaf was stirring.

They continued walking until they spotted a man perched on a tree, blocking one nostril and blowing out of the other.

"What’s going on up there?" the man called out. "I’m blowing at seven windmills two miles away to make them turn!" the man replied.

"Join us! If the four of us combine our strength, we can take on the world!" the man insisted, and the blower climbed down and came along.

title: How Six Men Got On in the World
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
As they traveled, they met a man standing on one leg with the other one resting beside him. "Looks like you're making yourself comfortable!" the man said.

"I’m a runner," the man explained. "To keep from running too fast, I took off one leg—if I had both on, I'd run faster than any bird can fly!"

"Join us! If we five are together, we can conquer the whole world!" the man encouraged. So the runner joined them.

It wasn’t long before they encountered another man wearing a cap cocked to one side. "Why wear your cap like that?" the man questioned.

"If I straighten it, a terrible frost blows in, freezing all the birds out of the sky!" the man responded.

"Come along with us! If we six join forces, there’s nothing we can’t handle!" the man said.

The six men then arrived at a town where the king announced that anyone who could win a race against his daughter would marry her, but anyone who lost would lose their head.

The man stepped forward and declared, "I’ll have my servant run for me." The king replied, "Then both of your lives will be at stake in this race."

Once this was settled, the man attached the runner’s other leg back on and said, "Now be quick and help us win!" The rules stated that whoever brought back water from a distant well would win the race.

Both the runner and the king's daughter received pitchers to carry. They started running at the same moment, but before long, the spectators could no longer see the runner.

It was as though he vanished in the blink of an eye! Soon, he reached the well, filled his pitcher, and began his way back.

But halfway home, exhaustion hit him hard, and he dropped his pitcher, lay down, and fell asleep, using a horse’s skull as an uncomfortable pillow.

Meanwhile, the king's daughter, who could also run quite well, had reached the well and was rushing back with her pitcher filled with water.

title: How Six Men Got On in the World
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When she spotted the runner asleep, she felt relieved, thinking, "My enemy is defeated!" She emptied his pitcher and continued on.

Everything would’ve been lost if luck hadn’t had the huntsman stationed watchfully at the castle turrets, who saw everything happening.

He said, "The king's daughter will not triumph over us." So he quickly loaded his gun and shot with incredible aim, knocking the horse's skull from under the runner's head without injuring him.

The runner woke up, jumped to his feet, and saw that his pitcher was empty and the king's daughter had dashed ahead.

But he didn't give up; he raced back to the well, filled his pitcher once more, and returned home ten minutes before the king's daughter.

"Look!" he exclaimed, "Now I’ve really begun to run! What I did before wasn’t even close to real running."

But the king was pained, even more so his daughter, that they should be bested by a mere common soldier like him. They began to plot how to eliminate him and his companions.

The king said to his daughter, "I have an idea. Don't worry; they won’t be coming back." He then told the six of them, "You’re all going to celebrate, eat and drink well, and I’ll show you to a special room."

He led them to a chamber with an iron floor, iron doors, and barred windows. Inside, a table was spread with delicious food.

"Go on in and enjoy yourselves," the king instructed. Once they were inside, he shut and bolted the doors.

Then, he called for the cook and ordered, "Make a fire beneath that room until the iron is red hot."

The cook complied, and as the six were seated, they began to feel warm, thinking it was from the food.

But as the heat intensified and they realized they couldn’t escape, panic set in; they understood the king had a malicious intent to roast them.

"He won't succeed!" declared the man with the cap. "I’ll summon a frost that will make this fire shrink away!"

title: How Six Men Got On in the World
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He straightened his cap, and instantly a chill swept through the room, freezing everything, causing the food to become solid on the plates.

After an hour or two passed, the king, believing they had perished in the heat, commanded the doors to be opened so he could see.

When the doors swung open, all six stood there, alive and well, saying, "We’d like to step out and warm ourselves; our food is stuck to the plates!"

Furious, the king stormed down to the cook, berating him for not following orders. The cook replied, "There’s enough heat, see for yourself!"

When the king looked, he saw a fierce fire burning beneath the iron room and realized escape was impossible.

The king thought again about how to rid himself of these unwanted guests and summoned their leader.

"If you agree to take gold and renounce my daughter, I’ll give you as much as you want."

"Sure, Your Majesty," the man replied, "Give me as much gold as my servant can carry, and I’ll happily forfeit your daughter."

The king felt pleased with the response. The man then added, "In fourteen days, I’ll come to collect it."

The king then gathered all the tailors in the kingdom and ordered them to sew a massive sack in fourteen days.

When it was done, the strong one who could uproot trees lifted it onto his back and set off for the king.

The king noticed the strong man carrying a sack as big as a house and was alarmed, thinking, "How much gold could he take?"

He ordered a ton of gold to be brought, needing sixteen of his strongest men to haul it, yet the strong one grabbed it with ease.

He put the gold into his sack and joked, "Why don’t you bring more? That barely covers the bottom!"

Gradually, the king filled his sack with all his treasure, but it still wasn't half full.

"Bring in more!" he urged, "This isn’t enough to fill it!"

Eventually, the king summoned seven thousand carts filled with gold from across the kingdom, and the strong one stuffed them into his sack.

"I won’t question this anymore; I’ll just take what I can fit," he said, ignoring the weight limit.

After he stuffed it all in, there was still room for way more.

"I’m just going to wrap this up here. Sometimes, people manage to tie up a sack even when it’s not full," he said, hoisting it onto his back.

He walked off with his companions, and when the king saw one man carrying away the entire wealth of the kingdom, he became furious.

He commanded his horsemen to chase after the six and ordered them to seize the sack from the strong one.

Two regiments quickly caught up with the six and shouted, "You’re under arrest! Drop the sack filled with gold or we’ll execute you!"

"Prisoners?" the blower laughed. "I’d rather all of you dance around in the sky!"

He blocked one nostril and blew with the other, sending the two regiments scattering into the air.

They were lifted high, flying over the mountains, one here, another there.

A sergeant who had been wounded nine times cried out for mercy. He was brave and didn’t deserve poor treatment.

The blower eased him down gently and said, "Now go back to your king and warn him to send more horsemen, or I'll blow them all into the air too!"

Once the king heard this, he said, "Let them go! There’s some sort of magic at work here!"

The six men returned home with their treasure, shared it among themselves, and lived happily ever after.

title: Jorinda and Joringel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was an ancient castle nestled in the heart of a vast and thick forest.

Inside the castle lived an old woman who was a witch, all by herself.

During the day, she transformed into either a cat or a screech owl, but as evening fell, she returned to her true human form.

She had the ability to lure wild animals and birds to her, only to catch and cook them.

If anyone stepped within a hundred paces of the castle, they were forced to remain still and couldn’t move unless she allowed it.

But whenever an innocent maiden entered this enchanted area, she would turn her into a bird and imprison her in a wicker cage, then carry that cage into a room in the castle.

She owned around seven thousand cages filled with rare birds.

Now, there was a maiden named Jorinda, who was more beautiful than any other girl.

She had promised to marry a handsome young man named Joringel.

They were in the days of their engagement, and nothing made them happier than being together.

One day, wanting to enjoy each other's company in peace, they decided to take a walk in the forest.

"Be careful, Jorinda," said Joringel, "Don’t venture too close to the castle."

It was a lovely evening; the sun shone brightly through the tree trunks, casting light into the dark green forest, while turtle-doves cooed sadly from the beeches.

Jorinda cried occasionally, sitting in the sunlight with a heavy heart.

Joringel felt just as sorrowful; they were both as sad as if they were facing the end.

They looked around, completely bewildered, unsure of which way to head home.

The sun was still partly above the horizon.

Joringel peeked through the bushes and spotted the ancient castle walls nearby.

Fear engulfed him, and he was paralyzed with dread.

Jorinda started singing,

"My little bird, with the necklace red,\nHe sings that the dove must soon be dead,\nSings sorrow, sor - jug, jug, jug."

Joringel searched for Jorinda. She had been transformed into a nightingale and was singing, "Jug, jug, jug."

title: Jorinda and Joringel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
A screech owl with bright eyes circled around her three times and hooted three times.

Joringel couldn’t move. He stood there like a statue, unable to cry out, speak, or even move his hands or feet.

The sun had set. The owl flew away into the bushes, and shortly after, a crooked old woman emerged from them.

She was yellow and thin, with large red eyes and a hooked nose that jutted down to her chin.

Muttering quietly to herself, she snatched the nightingale and carried it away.

Joringel was still unable to speak or move from the spot. The nightingale had vanished.

Eventually, the woman returned and said in a hollow voice, "Greet you, Zachiel. If the moon shines on the cage, Zachiel, let him go free right away."

At that moment, Joringel was freed from his enchantment. He fell to his knees before her, pleading for his Jorinda back.

But she coldly replied that he would never see her again before walking away.

He cried, lamented, and called out, but it was all in vain. "Oh, what’s going to happen to me?" he thought.

Joringel wandered off and eventually reached a strange village, where he worked as a shepherd for some time.

He often roamed around the castle but kept his distance.

One night, he dreamed about finding a blood-red flower, in the center of which lay a beautiful large pearl.

He envisioned picking the flower and taking it to the castle, discovering that everything he touched with it would be freed from the witch's spell.

He also dreamed that he would use it to get back his Jorinda.

When morning came, he set out searching through hills and valleys for that flower.

After searching for nine days, he finally found the blood-red flower early one morning.

In the center of it was a large dew drop, as big as the finest jewel.

He traveled day and night toward the castle with this flower.

When he was within a hundred paces of it, he no longer felt trapped but walked up to the door.

Joy filled Joringel as he touched the door with the flower, and it swung open.

He stepped into the courtyard and listened for the sound of the birds.

At long last, he heard it. He continued onward until he found the room where the sound was coming from, and there was the witch, feeding the birds in the seven.

As soon as she spotted Joringel, she became furious, scolding him and hurling her curses, but she couldn't come within two paces of him.

Ignoring her, he continued to look at the cages filled with birds, but with so many hundreds of nightingales, how would he ever find his Jorinda?

Just then, he noticed the old woman quietly carrying a cage with a bird in it, heading towards the door.

He sprang towards her, swiftly touched the cage and the old woman with the flower.

Suddenly, she couldn't cast her spells anymore.

Then, Jorinda appeared, wrapping her arms around his neck, and she was as beautiful as before.

In that moment, all the other birds turned back into maidens, and Joringel took his Jorinda home, where they lived happily together for many years.

title: King Thrushbeard
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
There was a king with a daughter whose beauty was unmatched.

However, she was so proud and arrogant that no suitor seemed worthy.

One by one, she turned them all away.

One day, the king threw a lavish feast, inviting all the eligible young men from near and far.

They were lined up according to their status.

First came the kings, then the grand-dukes, followed by princes, earls, barons, and gentlemen.

The king’s daughter was led through the lineup, but she found fault with each one.

One man was too heavy—"like a wine barrel," she remarked.

Another was too tall—"thin and tall isn't very impressive."

The third was too short—"short and stocky never moves quickly."

The fourth was too pale—"as pale as a ghost."

The fifth was too ruddy—"a real rooster."

The sixth wasn’t upright enough—"like a crooked stick."

With every man, she had some criticism, but she particularly enjoyed mocking a good king standing near the top of the line whose chin was a bit crooked.

"Look at him!" she exclaimed with laughter, "he has a chin like a thrush’s beak!" From that day on, he was known as King Thrushbeard.

The old king, upset that his daughter only mocked the suitors and had no respect for them, declared he would see her married to the very first beggar who came to their door.

A few days later, a fiddler appeared, singing beneath the windows to earn a few pennies.

When the king heard him, he ordered him to come inside.

The fiddler entered, wearing tattered clothes, and performed before the king and his daughter.

After he finished, he requested a small reward.

The king responded, "Your song pleased me so much, I'll give you my daughter's hand in marriage."

The princess recoiled, but the king declared, "I swore to give you to the first beggar, and I will keep my promise."

No argument could change his mind. A priest was summoned, and she found herself married to the fiddler right there.

title: King Thrushbeard
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once the ceremony was over, the king said, "As you are now a beggar-woman, it's not proper for you to stay in my palace. You can leave with your husband."

The fiddler took her by the hand, and she had to leave on foot with him.

When they reached a beautiful forest, she asked, "Who does this lovely forest belong to?"

"It belongs to King Thrushbeard," he replied. "If you had married him, it would have been yours."
