"Oh, what a miserable fate!" she lamented. "If only I had chosen him!"

Next, they reached a green meadow, and she inquired again, "Whose beautiful meadow is this?"

"It belongs to King Thrushbeard. If you had accepted him, it would have been yours," he said.

"Oh, what a miserable fate!" she cried out. "If only I had chosen King Thrushbeard!"

Then they came to a large town, and once again she asked, "To whom does this grand town belong?"

"It belongs to King Thrushbeard. If you had married him, it would have been yours," he replied.

"Oh, what a miserable fate!" she moaned. "If only I had chosen King Thrushbeard!"

The fiddler said, "It doesn’t make me happy to hear you wishing for another husband. Am I not good enough for you?"

Finally, they reached a tiny, run-down hut, and she gasped, "Oh goodness! What a small house! Who lives in this miserable little hovel?"

"This is my house, and yours too, where we will live together," the fiddler answered.

She had to duck to enter through the low door. She asked, "Where are the servants?"

"What servants?" the fiddler replied. "You’ll have to do everything yourself."

"Start a fire and boil some water for my dinner; I’m exhausted," he instructed.

But the princess had no idea how to start a fire or cook, so the fiddler had to help her prepare a meal.

title: King Thrushbeard
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
After their meager supper, they went to bed, but he forced her to wake up early to manage the tasks.

They carried on this way for a few days until they ran out of food.

The fiddler said, "We can’t keep eating without earning anything. You need to make baskets."

He went out, cut some willow branches, and brought them home.

She began to weave baskets, but the rough willows hurt her delicate hands.

"This isn’t working for you," the fiddler said. "You should try spinning; maybe you’ll be better at that."

She sat down to spin, but the harsh thread soon cut her soft fingers, causing them to bleed.

"Look," he remarked, "you’re not cut out for any kind of work. I made a poor bargain with you. I’ll try my hand at making pots and dishes instead. You can sell them in the market."

She thought, "Oh no! If anyone from my father’s kingdom sees me selling this stuff, they will mock me!" But it was no use; she had to comply or face starvation.

Surprisingly, her first day selling went well—the villagers were eager to buy her wares since she was pretty, and they paid what she asked.

Many even gave her money and left the pots behind.

They managed to survive on what she earned for a while, and then her husband bought more pottery.

She set up her new stock at a corner of the marketplace, ready for sales.

But suddenly, a drunken soldier came galloping through and trampled her pottery, shattering it all.

"Oh no! What will happen to me?" she cried. "What will my husband say?"

She rushed home to tell him about the disaster.

The fiddler scoffed, "Who would sit in a corner of the market selling pottery?"

"Stop crying. You can’t handle ordinary work, so I visited our king’s palace to find out if they need a kitchen maid, and they agreed to take you."

So, she became a kitchen maid, having to obey the cook and do the dirtiest of chores.

title: King Thrushbeard
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She tucked a small jar into each of her pockets to take home her share of leftovers, and that’s how they survived.

One day, the king’s eldest son was getting married, so the poor woman went to the door of the hall to watch.

When all the candles were lit and people arrived, each one more beautiful than the last, she thought sadly of her own situation and cursed her pride that had led to her downfall.

The delicious aroma of fancy dishes wafted by, and sometimes the servants tossed her a few morsels, which she collected in her jars.

Suddenly, the prince entered, dressed in velvet and silk, adorned with gold chains.

When he spotted the beautiful woman at the door, he took her hand and wanted to dance with her.

But she shrank back in fear, realizing it was King Thrushbeard, the suitor she had scorned.

Despite her protests, he pulled her into the hall.

But the string holding her pockets snapped, her pots fell, and soup spilled everywhere, spraying scraps around.

The crowd erupted in laughter, and she felt so humiliated that she would have preferred to disappear.

She rushed to the door to flee, but a man stopped her on the stairs and brought her back.

When she looked up, it was King Thrushbeard once again.

He said gently, "Don’t be afraid. I and the fiddler you’ve been living with in that run-down hut are one and the same.

I disguised myself for the sake of love and was also the soldier who rode through your pottery.

I did all this to humble your proud nature and teach you a lesson for mocking me."

She broke down in tears, saying, "I have wronged you greatly and am unworthy to be your wife."

But he reassured her, "Don’t be sad; those difficult days are behind us now. Let’s celebrate our wedding."

The attendants dressed her in the finest clothing, and her father and his entire court came to wish her happiness with King Thrushbeard.

And that’s how their joy truly began.

title: Knoist and His Three Sons
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
In a place called Werrel, there lived a man named Knoist.

He had three sons. One was blind, the second was lame, and the

third was completely naked.

One day, they ventured into a field,

where they spotted a hare. The blind son shot it, the lame son

caught it, and the naked son put it in his pocket. After that,

they came across a huge lake that had three boats: one that sailed,

one that sank, and a third that had no bottom. They all climbed

into the one with no bottom. Their journey took them to a vast

forest where they found an enormous tree. Inside that tree,

there was a large chapel. In this chapel was a sexton made of

title: Lazy Harry
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Harry was a lazy guy, and even though all he had to do was take his goat to the pasture every day,

he still complained about it.

After finishing his daily task and heading home, he felt exhausted.

"It’s such a heavy burden!" he grumbled, "Driving a goat into the field year after year until late autumn is exhausting."

If only I could just lie down and take a nap! But no, I have to keep my eyes open to make sure the goat doesn’t damage the young trees, squeeze through the hedges into a garden, or run off completely.

How can anyone find rest or enjoy life like this?" He sat down, gathered his thoughts, and wondered how he could free himself from this burden.

After thinking for a long time with no useful ideas, suddenly, it felt like a light bulb went off in his head.

"I've got it!" he exclaimed. "I’ll marry Fat Trina, who has her own goat, and we can take both goats out together, so I won’t have to worry about it anymore!"

With that, Harry got up, pushed his tired legs into action, and crossed the street to where Fat Trina’s parents lived, asking for their hardworking daughter’s hand in marriage.

The parents didn’t think long about it. "Birds of a feather flock together," they figured, and agreed.

Soon, Fat Trina was Harry’s wife, and she took both goats out to pasture.

Harry was enjoying his time, having no work to recover from except for his own laziness.

He only went out with her occasionally, saying, "I'm doing it so I can enjoy my rest later; otherwise, I might lose my appreciation for it."

But Fat Trina was just as lazy. "Dear Harry," she said one day, "why should we make our lives so hard when it's unnecessary and ruin the best days of our youth?"

Wouldn’t it be better to trade our two bleating goats, which wake us up every morning, to our neighbor in exchange for a beehive? We can place the beehive in a sunny spot behind the house and not have to worry about them anymore.

title: Lazy Harry
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Bees don’t need to be taken care of or driven into fields; they find their way home and collect honey all on their own, without giving us any trouble!"

"You've made a sensible suggestion," Harry replied. "Let’s do it right away. Besides, honey tastes better and is more nourishing than goat's milk, and it lasts longer too."

The neighbor happily traded a beehive for the two goats. The bees flew in and out from early morning until late evening, tirelessly gathering nectar and filling the hive.

In autumn, Harry was able to fill a whole pitcher with honey.

They placed the jug on a shelf fixed to the wall of their bedroom, and since they were afraid it might get stolen or that mice might get it, Trina brought in a sturdy hazel stick and kept it by her bed. This way, she could easily reach it if they needed to scare away any intruders.

Harry, on the other hand, didn’t like to get out of bed before noon. "Those who get up early just waste their time," he said.

One morning, still lounging in his soft bed and enjoying the sunlight, he told his wife, "You know, women love sweet things, and you’re always sneaking tastes of the honey.

We should swap it for a goose with a young gosling before you eat it all!"

But Trina replied, "Not until we have a child to help take care of them."

"Am I supposed to exhaust myself with those little geese, only to find it’s pointless?" Harry countered. "Do you really think a kid would help with the geese? Nowadays, kids just do what they want and think they know better than their parents, just like that boy who was sent to find the cow but ended up chasing three blackbirds instead."

"Oh," Trina replied, "that kid will regret ignoring me! I’ll take a stick and give him more whacks than he can count!"

Look, Harry!" she exclaimed in her excitement, grabbing the stick she used for shooing away mice. "This is how I'll deal with him!"

She swung the stick, but unfortunately, she knocked the honey pitcher above the bed.

The pitcher hit the wall and shattered, with honey streaming all over the floor.

"Well, there go our goose and gosling," Harry remarked, "and they don’t need any looking after now. But at least the pitcher didn’t land on my head. We should be thankful for what we have."

Then, noticing that there was still some honey left in one of the shards, he reached for it and said cheerily, "We can still enjoy this, my wife! Let’s have a little rest after our scare. What’s the harm in getting up a bit later?"

The day feels long enough as it is.

"Yes," Trina answered, "we’ll always get to the end of the day on time. And remember, the snail was once invited to a wedding but only arrived at the christening.

It fell over the fence in front of the house and said, 'Hurrying doesn't help at all'."

title: Lean Lisa
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Lean Lisa thought very differently than Lazy Harry and Fat Trina, who let nothing disturb their peace.

While they lazed around, Lisa worked tirelessly from morning to night.

She overwhelmed her husband, Long Laurence, with so much work that he had more burdens to carry than a donkey loaded with three sacks.

In the end, it was all in vain, as they had nothing to show for it and ended up with nothing.

One night, as she lay in bed, barely able to move a muscle from exhaustion, her mind wouldn’t let her rest.

She elbowed her husband in the side and said, "Listen, Lenz, I’ve been thinking. If I found one florin and someone gave me another, I would borrow one more from you, and then with those four florins, I’d buy a young cow."

Her husband was pleased with this idea. "It’s true, I don’t know where you expect to get that florin from me as a gift, but if you can gather the money and buy a cow with it, then go ahead. I’d be happy if the cow has a calf because I would love to have some fresh milk."

"But the milk isn't for you," Lisa replied. "We need to let the calf drink that so it can grow big and fat, and we can sell it for a good price."

"Of course," the man agreed, "but we’ll still take a little milk; that won’t hurt."

"Who taught you how to handle cows?" said Lisa. "I won’t allow it, no matter what, and even if you stand on your head, you won't get a drop of that milk. Do you think you can just eat up everything I work so hard for?"

"Wife, quiet down, or I’ll give you a smack," the man retorted.

"What? You’re threatening me, you glutton, you scoundrel, you lazy Harry?" she exclaimed.

She was about to grab his hair, but Long Laurence got up, grabbed both of Lean Lisa’s thin arms with one hand, and with the other hand, pushed her head into the pillow, letting her argue while he held her down until she finally fell asleep from sheer exhaustion.

Whether she continued to complain when she woke up the next morning or went out to search for the florin she wanted to find, I can't say.

title: Little Briar-Rose
author: Brothers Grimm
themes: courage in the face of danger
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a king and queen who often sighed, wishing for a child.

But their wish never came true. One day, while the queen was bathing, a frog hopped out of the water and approached her.

The frog said to her, "Your wish will come true. Within a year, you will have a child."

What the frog promised came true, and the queen gave birth to a little girl who was so beautiful the king couldn't hide his joy.

He organized a grand feast and invited not only family and friends but also the wise women of the kingdom.

He wanted them to bless the child with kindness and good fortune.

There were thirteen wise women, but since he only had twelve golden plates for them to eat from, one of them had to stay home.

The feast was magnificent, and at its conclusion, the wise women began to give magical gifts to the baby.

One granted her virtue, another beauty, a third offered riches, and each one gave gifts representing the best things in the world.

Just as the eleventh woman finished her blessing, the thirteenth arrived.

She had come to take revenge for not being invited, and without greeting anyone, she shouted, "The king's daughter will prick her finger on a spindle and fall down dead on her fifteenth birthday!"

And with that, she turned and left.

Everyone froze in shock, but the twelfth woman, who still had her blessing to give, stepped forward.

She could not reverse the curse, but she said, "Instead of dying, the princess will fall into a deep sleep for a hundred years."

The king, desperate to protect his beloved child, ordered that every spindle in the kingdom should be destroyed.

Meanwhile, the gifts from the wise women flourished in the young girl, who grew up to be so beautiful, humble, kind, and wise that anyone who saw her was enchanted.

On the day she turned fifteen, the king and queen were away, leaving the princess alone in the palace.

Curious, she explored every corner of the castle, peeking into rooms and chambers until she stumbled upon an old tower.

title: Little Briar-Rose
author: Brothers Grimm
themes: courage in the face of danger
text_chunk:
She climbed the narrow, winding staircase to reach a small door.

A rusty key was in the lock, and when she turned it, the door swung open to reveal an old woman spinning flax.

"Good day, old lady," said the princess, "what are you doing here?"

"I’m spinning," replied the old woman, nodding her head.

"What is that thing that makes such a cheerful noise?" the girl asked, as she reached for the spindle.

But as soon as she touched it, the wicked curse took effect, and she pricked her finger.

At that very moment, she fell onto the bed and fell into a deep sleep.

This sleep spread throughout the entire palace; even the king and queen, who had just returned and entered the grand hall, began to doze off.

Everyone in the court also fell asleep, from the horses in the stable to the dogs in the yard, the pigeons on the roof, and even the flies on the walls.

The roaring fire in the hearth quieted down, the roast meat stopped sizzling, and the cook, about to scold a boy for a mistake, let him go and went to sleep instead.

The wind whispered softly, rustling the trees surrounding the castle.

But around the castle, a hedge of thorns began to grow.

Every year, it grew higher until it completely surrounded the castle, hiding it from view, including the flag flying on top.

Despite the castle’s obscurity, tales of the beautiful, sleeping Briar-Rose spread across the land,

and over time, many princes came and tried to cut through the thorny hedge.

But each prince found it impossible, as the thorns seemed to cling to them like hands, trapping them until they met tragic ends.

After many long years, a prince traveled to that land and overheard an old man talking about the thorny hedge.

The man said there was a castle behind it, where a supremely beautiful princess named Briar-Rose had been asleep for a hundred years.

He mentioned that the king, queen, and the entire court were under the same spell.

The prince learned from his grandfather that many young men had tried to break through the thorns but had perished in the attempt.

title: Little Briar-Rose
author: Brothers Grimm
themes: courage in the face of danger
text_chunk:
But with courage, the young prince declared, "I’m not afraid; I want to see the beautiful Briar-Rose!"

Though the old man warned him, the prince paid no attention.

By this time, a hundred years had passed, and the day for Briar-Rose to wake finally arrived.

As the prince approached the thorny hedge, he found it transformed into beautiful, flowering shrubs that parted to let him through.

After he passed, the flowers closed back up behind him.

In the castle courtyard, he saw horses and spotted hounds sleeping peacefully, and pigeons with their heads tucked under their wings on the roof.

When he entered the castle, the flies were still resting on the walls, and the cook was stuck in time, hand raised in the act of punishment.

The maid sat beside a hen that had stopped clucking.

The prince moved on and entered the grand hall to find all the court in a deep slumber.

Above the throne lay the king and queen, also asleep.

He continued, and the silence was so deep that he could hear his own breath until he finally arrived at the tower.

He opened the door into the small room where Briar-Rose lay sleeping.

She was so breathtaking that he couldn't take his eyes off her.

Leaning down, he kissed her. As soon as their lips met, Briar-Rose opened her eyes and looked at him.

Together, they went down the stairs, and everyone in the palace awoke and shared looks of wonder.

The horses in the courtyard got up and shook themselves, the hounds jumped up happily, and the pigeons popped their heads out to see before flying off.

The flies buzzed back to life, the fire flared up in the kitchen, cooking the meat again.

The cook, roused from his nap, smacked the boy on the ear, causing him to yell, while the maid completed her task.

Soon after, the king's son and Briar-Rose had a magnificent wedding celebration, and they lived happily ever after.

title: Little Red-Cap
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a sweet little girl who was cherished by everyone who laid eyes on her, but most of all by her grandmother.

Her grandmother loved her so much that there was nothing she wouldn’t give to the child.

One day, she gave her a little red velvet cap that suited her perfectly, so much so that the girl decided she would wear nothing else.

From then on, everyone called her Little Red-Cap.

One day, her mother said to her, "Come here, Little Red-Cap. I have a piece of cake and a bottle of wine for you to take to your grandmother.

She’s feeling ill and weak, and these will do her some good. Set out early before it gets hot, and remember to walk nicely and quietly.

Don't stray off the path, or you might trip and break the bottle, and then your grandmother won’t get anything.

And when you enter her room, don’t forget to say good morning, and don’t peek into every corner.

"I’ll be careful, Mom," Little Red-Cap assured her mother.

The grandmother lived deep in the woods, about half a league from the village.

As soon as Little Red-Cap stepped into the woods, she came across a wolf.

Little Red-Cap had no idea how wicked he was, so she wasn’t afraid of him at all.

"Good day, Little Red-Cap," the wolf said.

"Where are you off to this early, Little Red-Cap? What do you have in your apron?"

"Cake and wine," she replied. "It’s my grandmother’s baking day, so I’m bringing her something delicious to help her feel better."

"Where does your grandmother live, Little Red-Cap?" asked the wolf.

"A good quarter league further into the woods. Her house is by the three big oak trees; the nut trees are right below them.

Surely you know it," Little Red-Cap answered.

The wolf thought to himself, What a sweet, young girl. What a delicious meal she will make! She’ll be better than the old woman.

title: Little Red-Cap
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
I need to be clever to catch both. So he walked alongside Little Red-Cap for a short time and then said, "Look at all the pretty flowers around here.

Why don’t you take a look? Don’t you hear how beautifully the little birds are singing? You walk so seriously as if you were on your way to school, while everything else around you is so enchanting!"

Little Red-Cap lifted her eyes, and when she saw the sunlight dancing through the trees and the lovely flowers blooming all around, she thought to herself that perhaps she could bring her grandmother a fresh bouquet.

That would surely make her happy! Since it was still early in the day, she believed she would have plenty of time to get there.

So she ran off the path into the woods to pick some flowers.

As soon as she picked one, she imagined there was an even prettier one further ahead, so she chased after it and lost herself deeper in the woods.

Meanwhile, the wolf hurried straight to the grandmother’s house.

"Little Red-Cap is bringing cake and wine," the wolf replied.

"Lift the latch," called the grandmother, "I’m too weak to get up."

The wolf lifted the latch, the door swung open, and without saying a word, he rushed to the grandmother’s bed and devoured her.

Then he put on her clothes, dressed himself in her cap, settled into bed, and drew the curtains.

Little Red-Cap, however, had been gathering flowers, and when she had picked so many that she couldn’t carry anymore, she remembered her grandmother and set off toward the house.

She was surprised to find the cottage door wide open, and as she stepped inside, a strange feeling washed over her.

"Oh dear, I feel uneasy today, even though I usually enjoy spending time with grandmother," she thought.

She called out, "Good morning," but there was no answer.

Curious, she went to the bed and pulled back the curtains.

There lay her grandmother with her cap pulled down over her face, looking very odd.

title: Little Red-Cap
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Oh, grandmother, what big ears you have!" she exclaimed.

"The better to hear you with, my dear," was the reply.

"But grandmother, what big eyes you have!" she remarked.

"The better to see you with, my dear," the wolf answered.

"But grandmother, what big hands you have!" she exclaimed.

"The better to hug you with," said the wolf.

"But grandmother, what a huge mouth you have!" she cried.

"The better to eat you with!" and without hesitation, the wolf sprang out of bed and swallowed Little Red-Cap whole.

After finishing his meal, the wolf lay back down in bed, fell asleep, and began to snore loudly.

Just then, a huntsman was passing by the house, and thought to himself, how the old woman is snoring.

I should check to see if she needs anything.

So he entered the room and, upon seeing the wolf lying in bed, exclaimed, "Well, well, what do we have here, you old sinner! I’ve been looking for you!"

Just as he was about to shoot the wolf, he suddenly thought it might be possible that the wolf had eaten the grandmother, and she could still be saved.

So instead of shooting, he grabbed a pair of scissors and began to cut open the sleeping wolf's stomach.

After two snips, he spotted Little Red-Cap glimmering, and made a couple more cuts, and out popped the little girl, shouting, "Oh my gosh, I was so scared! It was so dark inside the wolf!"

Soon after, the grandmother emerged too, barely able to catch her breath.

Little Red-Cap quickly fetched heavy stones to fill the wolf's belly, and when he woke up wanting to flee, the stones were so heavy that he collapsed and died.

The three of them were overjoyed.

The huntsman took the wolf's skin and headed home.

The grandmother enjoyed the cake and wine that Little Red-Cap had brought, feeling much better.

Little Red-Cap promised herself that as long as she lived, she’d never leave the path to wander into the woods again.

It was later said that one time when Little Red-Cap was again bringing treats to her grandmother, another wolf spoke to her and tried to lure her off the path.

However, Red-Cap was cautious and walked straight ahead, reporting to her grandmother that she had met the wolf, who greeted her, but with such a sinister look that she was sure he would have eaten her if they hadn’t been on a public road.

Well, said her grandmother, let’s close the door so he can’t come in.

Soon after, the wolf knocked and called, "Open the door, grandmother! It’s Little Red-Cap, and I’m bringing you some cakes!"

But they didn’t answer or open the door, so the crafty wolf circled the house two or three times, and finally jumped onto the roof, planning to wait until Little Red-Cap went home in the evening so he could sneak after her and devour her in the dark.

But the grandmother was wise to his tricks. In front of the house was a large stone trough.

So she told Little Red-Cap, "Take the pail, dear. I made some sausages yesterday, so carry the water in which I boiled them to the trough."

Little Red-Cap filled the trough until it was completely full.

The smell of the sausages reached the wolf, who sniffed and peeked down.

Finally, he stretched his neck out so far that he lost his balance and began to slip, falling from the roof straight into the trough, where he drowned.

Meanwhile, Little Red-Cap joyfully returned home, and no one ever bothered her again.

title: Hansel and Grethel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Near a large forest lived a poor woodcutter and his wife.

They had two children: a boy named Hansel and a girl named Gretel.

Life was hard for them, and when a severe famine struck the land, they struggled to find enough food.

One night, as he lay awake in bed worrying, he groaned and said to his wife, "What are we going to do? How can we feed our poor children when we can barely feed ourselves?"

His wife replied, "I have an idea. Tomorrow morning, we’ll take the kids into the deepest part of the forest.

We’ll light a fire for them, give each of them a piece of bread, and leave them there.

They won’t find their way home, and we’ll be free of them."

"No, wife," said the man, "I can’t leave my children alone in the forest.

The wild animals would come and hurt them!"

"You fool," she replied, "then we'll all starve together. You might as well start making our coffins!"

After arguing for a while, he finally gave in, although his heart ached for the children.

Both kids were too hungry to sleep and overheard their stepmother's plan.

Gretel cried and told Hansel, "Now it's all over for us!"

"Don’t worry, Gretel," Hansel reassured her. "I’ll find us a way to escape."

That night, while their parents were asleep, he got up, put on his little coat, opened the door, and sneaked outside.

The moon shone brightly, illuminating the white pebbles in front of their house, which sparkled like silver coins.

Hansel bent down and filled his coat pocket with as many pebbles as he could hold.

Then he returned and said to Gretel, "Don’t worry, dear sister. Sleep peacefully; God will help us." He then lay back down in bed.

When morning came, before the sun rose, their mother came to wake them, saying, "Get up, you sleepyheads! We’re going to the forest to gather wood!"

title: Hansel and Grethel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She gave each child a small piece of bread for lunch and instructed them not to eat it before then, warning them they would get nothing else.

Gretel tucked the bread under her apron while Hansel hid his pebbles in his pocket.

They all set off together into the forest.

After a while, Hansel stopped and kept looking back at their house.

His father said, "Hansel, why are you lagging behind? Pay attention and keep moving!"

"Oh, father," Hansel replied, "I’m just looking at my little white cat sitting on the roof. I think she’s waving goodbye!"

"You silly boy, that’s not your cat!" the stepmother scoffed. "That’s just the morning sun shining on the chimney!"

Meanwhile, Hansel had been cunningly tossing the pebbles behind him.

When they reached the middle of the forest, the father said, "Alright children, gather some wood. I’ll light a fire so you won’t be cold."

Hansel and Gretel collected branches until they formed a little hill.

Once they had a fire going, their mother said, "Now, lay down by the fire and rest.

We’ll go deeper into the forest to cut wood, and when we’re done, we’ll return to get you."

Hansel and Gretel sat by the fire, and at noon, each had a bite of their bread.

They thought they heard their father's axe but it was really just a branch swinging in the wind.

Eventually, exhaustion overtook them, and they fell sound asleep.

When they finally woke up, it was dark outside.

Gretel began to cry, saying, "How are we going to get out of this forest?"

But Hansel comforted her, saying, "Just wait until the moon rises, and then we’ll find our way home."

When the full moon rose, Hansel took Gretel’s hand and followed the shining pebbles that now sparkled like new silver coins.

They walked all night and by dawn, arrived back at their father's house.

They knocked on the door, and when their mother opened it and saw Hansel and Gretel, she scolded them, saying, "You naughty kids! Why did you sleep so long in the forest? We thought you were never coming back!"

title: Hansel and Grethel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Their father, however, was overjoyed and felt relief flood over him; he had been heartbroken leaving them behind.

Not long after, famine struck the land again, and that night, the children heard their mother telling their father, "We’ve eaten everything. We have half a loaf left, and that’s it! The children have to go.

We’ll take them deeper into the woods so they can’t find their way back.

It’s the only way to save ourselves!"

The father’s heart ached, and he thought it would be better to share that last piece of bread with the kids.

But the wife wouldn’t listen and scolded him instead.

He had already given in once, so he felt forced to agree again.

Hearing everything, the children stayed awake.

Later that night, Hansel tried to go out to gather more pebbles, but the door was locked.

Still, he reassured Gretel, saying, "Don’t cry, Gretel. Sleep peacefully; God will help us."

Early the next morning, their mother woke them up and took them out of bed.

This time, the piece of bread was even smaller.

As they walked into the forest, Hansel crumbled his bread in his pocket, stopping often to drop bits on the ground.

Their father asked, "Hansel, why do you keep stopping?"

"I’m just saying goodbye to my little pigeon on the roof!" Hansel replied.

Their mother mocked, "That’s not a pigeon; it’s just the morning sun shining!"

Little by little, Hansel dropped all the crumbs along the way.

The stepmother led them deeper into the forest than ever before.

Again, they stopped to build a fire, and the mother said, "Sit tight, children. You can sleep a little while we cut wood.

We’ll come back for you later."

At noon, Gretel split her piece of bread with Hansel, who had already dropped his breadcrumbs.

They fell asleep, but when evening came, no one returned for them.

They woke up in the dark, and Hansel comforted Gretel, saying, "Just wait until the moon rises, and we’ll find the breadcrumbs I dropped.

title: Hansel and Grethel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
They will lead us home."

But when the moon shone bright, they looked for the crumbs and found that the birds had taken them all.

"We’ll find our way back, don’t worry!" Hansel tried to reassure her, but they wandered all night through the trees.

All day long, they roamed as well, without finding an exit from the forest.

They were very hungry, eating only a few berries from the bushes.

After growing too tired to walk, they lay down under a tree.

It had been three days since they left home.

As they trudged on, they kept getting deeper into the woods, and if help didn’t come soon, they would perish from hunger and exhaustion.

Around noon, they spotted a beautiful white bird singing delightfully.

Entranced by its song, they stood still and listened.

As the song ended, the bird flew away, and they followed it to a tiny house.

When they got closer, they saw the house was made of bread and decorated with cakes, and its windows were made of clear sugar.

"Let’s dig in!" Hansel exclaimed. "I’ll have some roof, and you, Gretel, can nibble at the window! It’ll be sweet!"

Hansel reached up and broke a piece of the roof, while Gretel leaned against the window and munched on the panes.

Suddenly, a voice called out, "Who’s nibbling on my house?"

They didn’t stop eating.

Hansel loved the taste of the roof, taking a big chunk, while Gretel knocked out a whole window pane and sat down to enjoy.

Out of nowhere, the door creaked open, revealing an old woman with crutches.

Hansel and Gretel were so scared that they dropped everything.

The old woman smiled and said, "Oh dear children, who brought you here? Come in and stay with me—you’re safe here."

Taking their hands, she led them inside her cozy little house.

Delicious food appeared in front of them: milk, pancakes, sugar, apples, and nuts.

Later, she showed them to two pretty beds with clean white sheets, and they lay down, believing they had found paradise.

title: Hansel and Grethel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But the old woman wasn’t really kind; she was a wicked witch who lured children in to capture them.

When she caught a child, she would cook and eat them, celebrating with a feast.

Witches have red eyes and poor eyesight but a keen nose, sensing when humans are near.

When Hansel and Gretel arrived, she laughed in delight, thinking, "I’ve got them, they won’t get away this time!"

Before the children even woke up the next morning, she was already up, watching them sleep and marveling at their rosy cheeks.

Muttering to herself, she thought, "What a tasty morsel they will make!"

She grabbed Hansel with her gnarled hand and locked him in a small stable.

Despite his cries, no one could save him.

Next, she shook Gretel awake and said, "Get up, lazy girl! Fetch some water and prepare a feast for your brother.

He’s in the stable, and it’s time for him to fatten up for my dinner!"

Gretel wept but had no choice but to follow the witch’s orders.

The best food was cooked for Hansel while Gretel received only scraps.

Every morning, the witch would sneak to the stable and say, "Hansel, stretch out your finger so I can check if you’re growing fat."

But Hansel would stretch out a thin bone instead, and the witch, unable to see well, thought it was his finger.

After four weeks of this, she grew impatient since Hansel showed no sign of getting fat.

"Hurry up, Gretel, bring me some water! Whether Hansel is fat or thin, I’m cooking him tomorrow!"

Poor Gretel wept as she fetched water, crying, "Dear God, help us! If only the wild animals had eaten us, we could have died together!"

"Keep it down," the witch yelled. "It won’t help you!"

The next morning, Gretel had to hang the cauldron to boil water for the witch.

"We’ll bake first," the witch said. "I’ve warmed the oven and kneaded the dough.

title: Hansel and Grethel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
You need to check if it’s hot enough, so you’d better crawl inside."

But Gretel saw through her plan, pretending to be confused.

"I don’t know how to get in; it looks tricky!"

"What’s wrong with you? It’s easy!" the old woman scoffed, "Look, I can get in myself!" She bent over and thrust her head into the oven.

Seizing her chance, Gretel pushed the witch inside, shut the heavy iron door, and bolted it.

The witch screamed in protest, but she was soon burned to death.

Gretel raced to Hansel’s stable, flung open the door, and shouted, "Hansel, we’re saved! The wicked witch is dead!"

Hansel jumped up like a bird escaping a cage.

They celebrated, hugged, and danced together without fear any longer.

Feeling secure, they explored the witch's house and found it filled with treasure—chests overflowing with pearls and gemstones.

"These are way better than pebbles," said Hansel, filling his pockets with them.

Gretel said, "I want to take something home too!" She gathered the treasures in her apron.

"But we should leave now," Hansel insisted, "We need to get out of this witch's forest!"

After walking for two hours, they faced a big body of water.

"We can’t cross!" Hansel exclaimed. "I don’t see a bridge or a ferry!"

"But look!" Gretel replied, pointing at a white duck swimming. "If I ask, it might help us across."

Then she called out, "Little duck, little duck, can you see, Hansel and Gretel are waiting for thee.

There's not a plank or bridge in sight, so take us across on your back so white."

The duck swam over, and Hansel got on its back, telling Gretel to join him.

"No, that will be too much for it!" she said. "Let it take us one at a time!"

The kind little duck agreed and safely brought them across.

As they walked on, the forest began to look familiar, and soon they spotted their father's house.

They rushed inside and threw themselves around their father's neck.

He hadn’t felt a moment of happiness since they were lost.

Their stepmother was gone.

Gretel emptied her apron, spilling pearls and jewels across the room, and Hansel contributed his own treasure as well.

All their worries vanished, and they loved happily ever after.

And that's the end of my story! There goes a mouse! Whoever catches it can make themselves a lovely fur cap!

title: Iron John
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a king who owned a huge forest close to his palace, filled with all sorts of wild animals.

One day, he sent a huntsman to catch a roe, but the huntsman didn’t return.

"Something must have happened to him," the king thought, so the next day he sent two more huntsmen to look for him.

But they also did not come back. On the third day, he called for all his huntsmen and ordered them to scour the entire forest until they found all three.

Yet, none returned, and the pack of hounds they had taken with them also vanished.

From that moment on, no one dared to enter the forest, which remained silent and isolated, only occasionally disturbed by a soaring eagle or hawk.

Years passed, and one day, an unknown huntsman approached the king, looking for a job and offering to venture into the dangerous forest.

The king, however, wasn’t willing to give his permission and said, "It’s too unsafe in there. I’m afraid you’ll meet the same fate as the others and never come back."

But the huntsman replied, "My lord, I’ll take the risk on my own terms."

So, the huntsman set off into the forest with his dog.

Not long after entering, the dog caught a scent and sprinted after it but quickly halted before a deep pool.

Before the huntsman could react, an arm emerged from the water, grabbed the dog, and pulled it under.

Seeing this, the huntsman hurried back, gathered three men with buckets, and together, they bailed out the water.

When they finally cleared the pool, they discovered a wild man whose body was brown and rusty like iron, with hair hanging to his knees.

They bound him with ropes and took him to the castle.

The wild man astonished everyone, but the king ordered him to be locked in an iron cage in the courtyard, forbidding anyone to open it under penalty of death.

The queen was entrusted with the key.

From then on, people felt safe to return to the forest.

The king had an eight-year-old son who was playing in the courtyard one day when his golden ball fell into the cage.

title: Iron John
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The boy ran over and said, "Please, give me my ball back!"

"Not until you open the door for me," replied the wild man.

"No," said the boy, "I can’t do that; the king has forbidden it," and he ran away.

The next day, he returned and asked for his ball again.

The wild man said, "Open my door," but the boy refused.

On the third day, the king had gone out hunting, and the boy came back saying, "I can’t open the door, even if I wanted to, because I don’t have the key."

Then the wild man replied, "It's under your mother’s pillow; you can get it there."

The boy, eager to get his ball back, threw caution to the wind and fetched the key.

The door creaked open, and though he pinched his fingers in the process, when it swung wide, the wild man stepped out.

He handed the boy his golden ball and hurried away.

The boy felt scared and called after him, "Oh wild man, don’t leave, or I’ll get in trouble!"

The wild man turned back, picked the boy up, and put him on his shoulder before striding hastily into the forest.

When the king returned home, he noticed the cage was empty and asked the queen what had happened.

She had no idea and searched for the key, only to find it missing.

She called for the boy, but there was no response.

The king sent out searchers to look for him in the fields, but they found nothing.

From this, the king guessed what had happened, and deep sadness spread through the royal court.

Once they reached the dark forest, the wild man set the boy down and said, "You will never see your parents again, but I will keep you with me because you set me free, and I have compassion for you.

If you follow my instructions, life will be good for you.

I have enough treasure and gold to last a lifetime."

He made a bed of moss for the boy to sleep on, and the next morning, he took him to a well and said, "Look, this golden well is as bright and clear as crystal.

title: Iron John
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
You’ll sit beside it and make sure nothing falls in, or it will be ruined.

I’ll check in every evening to see if you've followed my order."

The boy sat by the edge of the well and often saw golden fish or snakes swim by, making sure nothing fell in.

As he sat there one day, his finger suddenly hurt so badly that he accidentally dipped it into the water.

He quickly pulled it out but saw that it was shining and covered in gold.

No matter how hard he tried, he couldn’t wash the gold off.

When evening came, Iron Hans returned, glanced at the boy, and asked, "What happened to the well?"

"Nothing, nothing at all," the boy answered, hiding his finger behind his back.

But Iron Hans said, "You’ve dipped your finger in the water.

This time it may slide, but be careful not to let anything else fall in."

At dawn, the boy was already at the well, watching it closely.

His finger hurt him again, and he absentmindedly brushed his hair back, which caused a hair to fall into the well.

He quickly fished it out but saw it was already gilded.

Iron Hans arrived and immediately knew what had happened.

"You’ve let a hair fall into the well," he said.

I'll let you try to guard it one more time, but if it happens again, the well will be polluted.

On the third day, the boy sat by the well and didn't touch his injured finger, no matter how much it hurt.

Time dragged on, and as he looked at his reflection in the water, he leaned in closer to see himself more clearly.

As he leaned down, his long hair slipped into the water.

He quickly straightened up, but his hair was already golden and shone like the sun.

The poor boy was terrified.

He tied a handkerchief around his head so Iron Hans wouldn’t see.

When Iron Hans showed up, he knew everything and said, "Take off the handkerchief."

When the boy did, his golden hair tumbled down, and despite any excuses he offered, it was futile.

title: Iron John
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"You failed the test and can’t stay here anymore.

You must go out into the world, where you’ll learn about poverty.

However, since you're kind-hearted and I wish you well, there’s one thing I can offer you.

If you find yourself in trouble, return to the forest and call for me, 'Iron Hans,' and I will come to aid you.

My power is greater than you think, and I have much to give.

With that, the king's son left the forest and wandered along beaten paths and others until he finally reached a large city.

There, he looked for work, but couldn’t find any suitable jobs since he had learned nothing useful.

Eventually, he went to the palace and asked if he could be hired.

The court wasn't sure what to do with him, but they liked him enough to let him stay.

Eventually, the cook took him in, assigning him to fetch wood and water and to tidy up the ashes.

One day, when no one else was around, the cook asked him to serve food at the royal table.

But to avoid revealing his golden hair, he kept his cap on.

The king had never seen anything like that, and he said, "You must take off your hat at the royal table."

The boy replied, "I can’t, my lord. I have a sore spot on my head."

The king called for the cook and scolded him, insisting he send the boy away immediately.

However, the cook felt sorry for the boy and swapped him for a gardener’s assistant.

Now the boy had to plant and water the garden, hoe and dig, enduring the wind and rain.

One summer day, while working alone, the weather was hot, so he took off his cap to cool himself.

As the sun shone on his hair, it shimmered and sparkled, catching the attention of the king’s daughter in her bedroom.

She leaped up to see what it was.

When she saw the boy, she called out to him, "Hey, bring me a wreath of flowers!"

title: Iron John
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He quickly pulled his cap back on and gathered wildflowers, tying them together.

As he headed up the stairs with them, the gardener saw him and said, "How can you bring the king’s daughter such plain flowers? Go quickly and find the most beautiful ones you can get."

But the boy replied, "No, these wildflowers smell better and will please her more."

When he entered the room, the king’s daughter said, "Take off your cap; it's not polite to wear it in my presence."

He again insisted, "I can’t; I have a sore head."

She grabbed his cap and yanked it off, revealing his golden hair flowing down his shoulders, breathtaking to see.

He tried to run away, but she held onto his arm and gave him a handful of ducats.

He left the room but didn't care much for the gold coins, saying, "Here, take this for your children to play with."

The next day, the king's daughter called him again for another bouquet of wildflowers.

When he brought it to her, she immediately tried to pull off his cap again, but he held on tightly.

She offered him more ducats, but he refused to keep them and instead continued giving them to the gardener for his kids.

On the third day, the situation repeated itself; she could not get his cap off, and he wouldn’t accept her money.

Not long after, the kingdom was overtaken by war.

The king gathered his soldiers and wasn’t sure if he could resist the invading enemy, who had a superior army.

Then the gardener’s boy spoke up, "I’m grown now, and I’ll join the fight, but you need to give me a horse."

The others laughed, saying, "Find one for yourself after we leave; we’ll leave one behind in the stable for you."

After they departed, he went into the stable and led out a three-legged horse.

It limped along, but he mounted it anyway and rode off to the dark forest.

When he reached the edge, he called out, "Iron Hans!" three times, loud enough for the sound to echo through the trees.

title: Iron John
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Immediately, the wild man appeared and asked, "What do you want?"

"I need a strong horse because I’m going to war."

"You’ll have that, plus even more than you ask for," replied Iron Hans.

Then the wild man vanished back into the forest, and before long, a stableboy returned leading a mighty steed that snorted and was hard to control.

Following closely was a whole troop of warriors, all clad in armor, their swords gleaming in the sunlight.

The boy transferred his three-legged horse to the stableboy, mounted the strong one, and rode at the front of the soldiers.

As they approached the battlefield, many of the king’s men had already fallen, and the rest were ready to retreat.

But the boy charged in with his iron soldiers, crashing down on the enemy like a tornado and defeating everyone in their path.

They began to flee, but the boy chased them relentlessly until none remained.

Instead of returning straight to the king, he took his troops back through the forest and called for Iron Hans.

"What do you want?" asked the wild man.

"Please take back your horse and your warriors and give me my three-legged horse back."

Iron Hans complied, and soon the boy was back on his three-legged horse.

When the king returned to the palace, his daughter welcomed him and congratulated him on the victory.

"I wasn’t the one who won, but a mysterious knight came to my rescue with his army."

The princess wanted to know who this stranger was, but the king replied that he hadn’t seen him again after he had chased the enemy.

The daughter asked the gardener about his boy, but he just smiled and said, "He just came back on his three-legged horse, and the others made fun of him, calling out, 'Here comes our hobblety jig!"

They mocked, "So, where have you been sleeping under a hedge all this time?"

He replied, "I did the best of all, and it would have been bad without me," yet his peers laughed even harder.

title: Iron John
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The king told his daughter, "I’ll host a grand feast that will last three days, and you will toss a golden apple into the crowd.

Perhaps the unknown knight will show himself again."

When the event was announced, the boy headed back to the forest and called for Iron Hans.

"What do you want?" asked the wild man.

"I want to catch the golden apple thrown by the king’s daughter."

"That’s as good as done," Iron Hans replied.

"You’ll also receive a suit of red armor and ride a spirited chestnut horse for the occasion."

On the day of the feast, he galloped to the spot, took his place among the knights, and nobody recognized him.

The princess stepped forward and tossed the golden apple into the air, but he was the only one to catch it.

However, as soon as he turned to leave, he galloped off.

On the second day, Iron Hans gave him white armor and a white horse.

Again, he caught the apple and rode off immediately.

The king became furious, stating, "That’s against the rules!"

He ordered a search for the knight who caught the apple, threatening consequences if he didn’t return.

On the third day, Iron Hans provided him with black armor and a black horse, and once again, he caught the apple.

But while he was riding away, the king’s attendants chased after him.

One managed to get close enough to wound the boy's leg with his sword.

Even so, the boy escaped, but his horse jumped abruptly, and his helmet fell off, revealing his golden hair.

They rushed back to inform the king.

The next day, the king's daughter asked the gardener about his boy.

"He’s in the garden working,

that peculiar one was at the festival but only returned last night."

"He’s also shown my children three golden apples, which he just got."

The king had him summoned, and when he arrived, he had his cap on again.

The king’s daughter approached him, took it off, and his golden hair flowed down beautifully, stunning everyone present.

"Are you the knight who came to the festival every day in different outfits and caught the three golden apples?" the king asked.

"Yes," the boy replied, pulling the apples from his pocket and offering them to the king.

"If you need more proof, here’s the wound your men gave me when they chased me.

And I’m also the knight who aided you in defeating your foes.

If you can do such brave deeds, you’re no mere gardener's boy, but tell me, who is your father?"

"My father is a mighty king, and I have gold enough to satisfy me.

The king replied, "I see I owe you my thanks; how can I please you?"

"Yes," he said, "you can indeed. Give me your daughter’s hand in marriage."

The princess laughed, saying, "He doesn’t go for formalities, but I’ve already known from his golden hair that he wasn’t just a gardener's boy," and then she kissed him.

His parents celebrated the wedding with joy, as they never thought they’d see their beloved son again.

As they sat at the wedding feast, the music suddenly stopped, the doors swung open, and a regal figure entered, accompanied by a large retinue.

He approached the young man, embraced him, and said, "I am Iron Hans, and thanks to your bravery, I am no longer enchanted to be a wild man.

All the treasures I possess are now yours."

title: Little Snow-White
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs

Once upon a time in the depths of winter, as snowflakes fell softly from the sky,

a queen sat by her window, sewing. The window frame was made of sleek black ebony.

As she sewed and gazed out at the snowy landscape,

she pricked her finger with the needle, and three drops of blood fell onto the snow.

The red droplets looked beautiful against the white snow, and she thought to herself, 'If only I had a child with skin as white as snow, lips as red as blood, and hair as black as ebony.'

Soon after that, she had a little daughter who was as white as snow, as red as blood, and with hair as black as ebony.

They named her little Snow White. Sadly, the queen died when the child was born.

After a year had passed, the king remarried.

His new wife was beautiful but vain and couldn't stand the thought of anyone surpassing her in looks.

She owned a magical mirror, and every time she looked into it, she would ask,

"Mirror, mirror, on the wall, who in this land is the fairest of all?"

To which it replied,

"You, oh queen, are the fairest of all."

Satisfied, she felt reassured that the mirror always spoke the truth.

However, Snow White was growing up, and as she blossomed into a young girl,

when she turned seven, she became even more beautiful than the queen.

One day, when the queen asked her mirror,

"Mirror, mirror, on the wall, who in this land is the fairest of all?"

It replied, "You are fairer than all who are here, my lady queen.

But more beautiful still is Snow White, as I believe."

The queen was shocked and turned pale with envy.

From that moment on, whenever she laid eyes on Snow White, her heart filled with hate.

Her envy and pride grew like a weed in her heart, robbing her of peace day and night.

She summoned a huntsman and said, "Take the child into the forest. I can't bear to see her any longer.

title: Little Snow-White
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Kill her and bring me back her lungs and liver as proof."

The huntsman obeyed and took Snow White away,

but just as he was about to drive his knife into her innocent heart, she began to cry and begged,

"Oh dear huntsman, please spare my life! I will run away into the wild woods.

"Feeling pity for her beauty, the huntsman replied, "Run away, then, poor child.

The wild beasts will soon eat you alive. Yet, it feels like a weight has been lifted from my heart since I no longer have to kill you."

As a young bear came running by, he stabbed it, cut out its lung and liver, and brought them back to the queen as proof that the child was dead.

The cook was ordered to prepare them, and the wicked queen ate them, believing she had consumed Snow White's heart.

Meanwhile, the poor girl was all alone in the vast forest, frightened and confused.

She looked at the leaves on the trees, unsure of what to do. So she started to run, through sharp stones and thick thorns.

Wild animals ran past her, but she kept going, not stopping until evening.

At last, she spotted a little cottage and entered to rest.

Though everything inside was small, it was tidier and cleaner than anything she’d ever seen.

There was a table set with a white tablecloth, seven tiny plates, each with a little spoon on them.

Additionally, there were seven small knives and forks, and seven mugs.

Against the wall stood seven little beds lined up next to each other, all covered with white counterpanes.

Little Snow White was so hungry and thirsty that she ate a little bit of vegetables and bread from each plate and took a sip of wine from each mug, as she didn’t want to take all from just one.

Feeling tired, she snuggled into one of the little beds, but none of them felt quite right.

One bed was too long, another too short, but finally, she found the seventh bed was just right.

So she settled in, said a prayer, and fell asleep.

When it got dark, the owners of the cottage returned home.

title: Little Snow-White
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
They were seven dwarfs who mined in the mountains.

After lighting their seven candles, they noticed someone had been in their home since things weren’t in the same order they had left them.

The first one said, "Who has been sitting in my chair?"

The second one asked, "Who has eaten from my plate?"

The third said, "Who has taken some of my bread?"

The fourth asked, "Who has eaten my vegetables?"

The fifth said, "Who has used my fork?"

The sixth one inquired, "Who has cut with my knife?"

The seventh said, "Who has been drinking from my mug?"

Then the first dwarf noticed a little indentation in his bed and asked, "Who has been sleeping in my bed?"

The others came over and each exclaimed, "Someone's lying in my bed too!"

But when the seventh one looked at his bed, he found little Snow White fast asleep.

He called the others over, and they gathered around, astonished, shining their seven little candles on her.

"Oh, heavens, oh, heavens!" they cried, "What a beautiful child!"

They were so delighted that they didn’t want to wake her, so they let her sleep.

The seventh dwarf took turns sleeping with his six companions, spending an hour with each.

The next morning, Snow White woke up and was startled to see the seven dwarfs.

But they greeted her kindly and asked her name. "My name is Snow White," she replied.

"How did you end up in our home?" they asked. She told them how her stepmother wanted her dead,

but the huntsman had spared her life, leading her to run all day until she found their cottage.

The dwarfs said, "If you take care of our home, cooking, making the beds, washing, sewing, and keeping everything tidy, you can stay with us and want for nothing."

"Yes," replied Snow White, "with all my heart." So she stayed with them,

keeping the house in order each day. In the mornings, they went to the mountains to seek gold and silver, returning in the evenings to a ready supper.

title: Little Snow-White
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Being alone all day, the good dwarfs warned her, "Be cautious of your stepmother; she will soon find out you're here, so don’t let anyone in."

But the queen, believing she had consumed Snow White's lungs and liver, thought she was still the prettiest, so she went to her mirror and asked,

"Mirror, mirror, on the wall, who in this land is the fairest of all?"

The mirror replied, "Oh queen, you are the fairest of all I see,

but over the hills where the seven dwarfs dwell, Snow White is still alive and well."

The queen was stunned, realizing the mirror never lied. She now knew the huntsman had betrayed her and that Snow White still lived.

She devised a plan to kill her once and for all, for as long as someone else was deemed prettier, she couldn’t find peace.

Finally, she painted her face and dressed like an old peddler. No one would recognize her in this disguise.

She crossed the seven mountains to reach the seven dwarfs and knocked on their door, calling out, "Pretty things for sale, very cheap!"

Snow White peeked out the window and asked, "Good day, what do you have for sale?"

"Good things, pretty things!" the old woman responded, pulling out a brightly woven lace. "Let me in, and I’ll show you all my wares."

Thinking, I can trust this kindly old woman, Snow White unbolted the door and bought the pretty laces.

"Child," the old woman said, "what a mess you are! Let me lace you properly for once."

Snow White, unsuspecting of her true intentions, stood there while the old woman tied the laces.

But the old woman laced tightly and quickly, and Snow White soon found it hard to breathe, collapsing as if dead.

"Now I am the most beautiful," the queen thought to herself.

Later that evening, when the seven dwarfs returned home, they were horrified to find their beloved Snow White lying lifeless on the ground.

Seeing she wasn’t moving, they lifted her, and realizing the laces were too tight, they cut them.

title: Little Snow-White
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Slowly, Snow White took a breath and began to come back to life.

Upon hearing what had transpired, they warned her again, "You must be cautious of that old woman. Beware, for she might be the wicked queen!"

At home, the wicked woman stood before her mirror, asking,

"Mirror, mirror, on the wall, who in this land is the fairest of all?"

"Oh, queen, you are the fairest of all I see,

but over the hills where the seven dwarfs dwell, Snow White is still alive and well."

Hearing this made the queen fill with rage, realizing Snow White was alive.

But she swore, "This time, I will make sure you are dead!" Using her knowledge of witchcraft, she created a poisoned comb.

Changing her appearance again, she disguised herself as another old woman and went back to the seven dwarfs.

Knocking on their door, she cried, "Good things for sale, cheap!" Snow White peered out, saying, "I can’t let anyone in!"

"Just take a look," the old woman persisted, producing the comb, which sparkled enticingly.

Charmed by its beauty, Snow White let her guard down and opened the door.

Once the deal was made, the old woman said, "Let me comb your hair right!" Poor Snow White, unaware of the danger, let her do as she wished.

However, as soon as the comb touched her hair, the poison kicked in, and she fell to the ground, senseless.

"You beautiful fool," the wicked queen declared, "you’re finished now!" She then hurried away.

Fortunately, it was nearly evening when the seven dwarfs returned.

Seeing Snow White lying as if dead, they instantly suspected the evil queen and found the poisoned comb.

As soon as they removed it, Snow White began to breathe again and soon woke up to tell them what happened.

They reminded her once more to stay alert and to keep the door locked.

Back at home, the queen demanded her mirror,

"Mirror, mirror, on the wall, who in this land is the fairest of all?"

title: Little Snow-White
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Oh, queen, you are the fairest of all I see,

but over the hills where the seven dwarfs dwell, Snow White is still alive and well."

With this revelation, the queen trembled with rage. "Snow White will die!" she shouted, even if it costs me.”

She then entered a secret chamber where none could find her and made a deadly poisoned apple.

It looked beautiful, white with a red cheek, tempting anyone who saw it.

But whoever took a bite would meet their doom.

Once the apple was ready, she dressed as a farmer's wife and set off to find the seven dwarfs.

Knocking at the door, Snow White stuck her head out the window and said, "I can't let anyone in; the dwarfs have forbidden it!"

"Oh, that’s fine," replied the woman. "I need to get rid of my apples anyway."

"No, I can't take anything," Snow White insisted. The old woman countered, "Are you afraid of poison? Look, I’ll split the apple in half; you eat the red cheek, and I’ll eat the white."

The apple was so cleverly crafted that only the red side was poisoned.

Having witnessed the old woman take a bite, Snow White couldn’t resist any longer.

She reached out, took the poisoned half, and as soon as it touched her lips, she collapsed, lifeless.

The queen looked at her with a wicked grin and said, "White as snow, red as blood, black as ebony wood; this time, the dwarfs cannot wake you again."

When she asked her mirror at home,

"Mirror, mirror, on the wall, who in this land is the fairest of all?"

The mirror replied, "Oh queen, in this land you are the fairest of all."

The queen’s heart finally felt at peace. But just as a heart poisoned with envy could.

When the dwarfs returned home in the evening, they found Snow White lying on the ground without breath and lifeless.

Lifting her up, they tried to find anything that could have poisoned her, unlaced her, combed her hair, washed her with water and wine, but nothing helped; she remained dead.

title: Little Snow-White
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Saddened, they placed her on a bier and sat around it, weeping for three whole days.

When they prepared to bury her, they found she still looked alive, with rosy cheeks that would not fade.

The dwarfs decided, "We can’t bury her in the dark earth; let’s create a beautiful glass coffin so she can be seen from every side."

They placed her inside and inscribed her name in golden letters, declaring her to be a royal princess.

They set the coffin atop a mountain, where one of them stayed beside it to watch over it.

As time passed, birds came and mourned for Snow White: first an owl, then a raven, and lastly a dove.

And so, Snow White lay for a long, long time in the coffin, appearing as if she were asleep, as white as snow, as red as blood, and with hair as black as ebony.

It happened that a prince riding through the forest stumbled upon the dwarfs’ house and asked to spend the night.

He saw the coffin on the mountain, and the beautiful Snow White within it, reading the inscription that shone in golden letters.

He said to the dwarfs, "Please, let me have the coffin; I will pay you whatever you want for it!"

But the dwarfs replied, "We won’t part with it for all the gold in the world!"

Then the prince insisted, "Let me have it as a gift, for I cannot live without seeing Snow White.

I will honor and treasure her as my dearest possession." Seeing his genuine love, the dwarfs felt pity and handed it over.

The prince had it carried away by his servants.

As they were carrying it, they stumbled over a tree stump, and with the jolt, the poisoned piece of apple that Snow White had bitten fell from her throat.

Before long, she opened her eyes, lifted the coffin lid, and came back to life.

"Oh, where am I?" she cried out. The prince, filled with joy, replied, "You are with me!"

He then told her everything that had happened and proclaimed, "I love you more than anything in the world! Come with me to my father's castle; you will be my wife."

Snow White happily agreed and went with him, and their wedding was celebrated with great joy and festivity.

But the wicked stepmother was also invited to the feast.

After dressing in her finest clothes, she stood before her mirror asking,

"Mirror, mirror, on the wall, who in this land is the fairest of all?"

"Oh, queen, of all here, you are the fairest," the mirror replied.

But then it added, "yet the young queen is fairer by far."

Upon hearing this, the wicked woman was consumed with rage, unable to think clearly.

Initially, she didn’t want to go to the wedding at all; but restlessly, she pushed herself to go see the new queen.

Upon seeing Snow White, she was frozen with rage and fear.

However, iron slippers had already been heated, brought in with tongs, and set before her.

Reluctantly, she had to put on the burning shoes and was forced to dance until she dropped dead.

title: Maid Maleen
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a king who had a son wanting to marry the daughter of a powerful king.

Her name was Maid Maleen, and she was exceptionally beautiful.

However, her father had plans to give her hand to someone else.

The prince faced rejection, but because their love was strong, they couldn’t bear to part.

Maid Maleen declared to her father, "I won't take anyone else as my husband."

This made the king furious, and he commanded that a dark tower be constructed.

He ordered that no light from the sun or moon would enter it.

When the tower was complete, he announced, "You will be imprisoned here for seven years, and we'll see if you give up on your stubbornness."

Food and drink were brought to the tower, and soon Maid Maleen and her maid-in-waiting were shut inside, cut off from the world.

In the darkness, they lost track of day and night.

Meanwhile, the prince often walked around the tower, calling out their names, but his voice couldn't reach them through the thick walls.

What else could they do?

As time passed and their food dwindled, they realized the seven years were nearly over.

They eagerly anticipated their rescue, yet, no sound of a hammer echoed, no stones fell, and Maid Maleen felt her father must have forgotten them.

With only a little food left and fearing a miserable end, Maid Maleen said, "We need to try one last time to break through the wall."

Taking a bread knife, she chipped away at the mortar in the wall, and when she grew tired, her maid took over.

After a lot of hard work, they managed to remove one stone, then another, and a third.

After three days, the first ray of light broke through their darkness, and they could finally peek outside.

The sky was clear blue, and a cool breeze touched their faces, but everything around them looked desolate.

Her father's castle lay in ruins, the town and villages were charred, and the fields were barren; not a soul could be seen.

When the hole was big enough for them to escape, the maid jumped down first, followed closely by Maid Maleen.

title: Maid Maleen
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But where could they go? The enemy had ravaged the kingdom, driven away the king, and killed all the people.

They ventured out in search of another land but found no shelter or food, and their hunger forced them to eat nettles.

After a long journey, they entered a new country, hoping to find work, but everywhere they went, they were turned away with no compassion.

Eventually, they reached a large city and made their way to the royal palace.

While they were initially dismissed, the cook finally allowed them to stay in the kitchen as scullions.

The king’s son in this kingdom turned out to be the very man who had been engaged to Maid Maleen.

His father had arranged a marriage with a bride whose looks matched her wicked heart.

The betrothal was set, and the bride had already arrived; yet, due to her great ugliness, she isolated herself in her room.

Maid Maleen was then tasked to bring her meals from the kitchen.

On the day of the wedding, unable to bear her appearance, the bride worried that she would be mocked if seen in public.

"A stroke of luck has fallen upon you," she said to Maid Maleen. "I sprained my foot and can barely walk; you should wear my wedding dress and take my place. It's an incredible honor."

But Maid Maleen rejected the offer, saying, "I don’t want any honor that isn’t rightfully mine."

Even when the bride offered her gold, Maid Maleen stood firm.

Seeing Maid Maleen's defiance, the bride threatened, "If you don’t comply, it will cost you your life. Just one word, and I can end you!"

In the end, Maid Maleen was forced to obey and donned the bride’s magnificent clothes and jewelry.

When she entered the royal hall, everyone was struck by her beauty, and the king turned to his son, saying, "This is your bride, whom I have chosen for you; lead her to the church."

The prince was bewildered, thinking to himself, "She reminds me of Maid Maleen; it seems impossible, she has been imprisoned or worse."

title: Maid Maleen
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He took her hand and led her to the church.

On the way, he asked, "What are you thinking about?"

"Nothing," she replied. "I was just thinking of Maid Maleen."

He was surprised she even knew her name but chose to say nothing.

When they reached the foot of the plank, he asked again, "What are you thinking?"

"Nothing," she answered, "I was thinking of Maid Maleen."

"Do you know Maid Maleen?" he probed.

"No," she replied, "I’ve only heard of her."

Upon arriving at the church door, he inquired one last time, "What are you thinking?"

"Oh, I was just thinking of Maid Maleen," she replied.

He then took out a beautiful necklace and placed it around her neck, fastening the clasp.

Together, they entered the church, where the priest united their hands in marriage.

Afterwards, he led her home, but she didn’t say a word the entire way.

Once they arrived at the royal palace, she rushed to the bride’s chamber, changed out of the luxurious attire, slipped back into her simple gray gown, and kept only the necklace.

That night, when the bride was to be taken to the prince's chambers, she draped her veil over her face to hide her identity.

When everyone had left, he asked her, "What did you say to the nettle plant that was growing?"

"Which nettle plant?" she responded, "I don’t talk to nettle plants."

"If you didn’t, then you’re not the true bride," he said.

At that moment, she realized she had to find Maid Maleen.

"Girl, what did you say to the nettle?" she asked.

"I said nothing, just..."

The bride ran back into the chamber, proclaiming, "I remember what I said to the nettle!" And she recounted the words.

"But what did you say to the footbridge as we crossed?" the prince inquired.

"To the footbridge?" she considered. "I don’t talk to footbridges."

title: Maid Maleen
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Then you're not the true bride," he insisted, racing out to find Maid Maleen.

The bride exclaimed in anger, "You’ll pay for this!" but hurried back into her room.

"I remember what I said to the footbridge now!" she confirmed, repeating the words.

"But what about the church door?" he asked.

"I said nothing to the church door," she asserted.

"Then you're not the true bride," he concluded.

She ran out again to scold Maid Maleen, asking, "What did you say to the church door?"

"I just said that it would break your neck for you," she answered.

With fury, the bride thought, "This will cost you your life!" and rushed back into her room.

"Now I remember what I said to the church door!" she exclaimed, recounting her words.

"But where’s the necklace I gave you at the church door?" he demanded.

"What necklace? You didn’t give me any necklace," she said.

"I put it around your neck and clasped it. If you don’t know that, you’re not the true bride."

He pulled back her veil, and as he saw her hideous face, he recoiled in shock, asking, "What are you doing here? Who are you?"

"I’m your betrothed bride. I feared the people would mock me for my appearance, so I made the scullery-maid wear my clothes and go to church for me."

"Where is she? I want to see her! Go and bring her here!" he ordered.

She rushed out to tell the servants that the scullery-maid was a fraud and instructed them to take her to the courtyard for execution.

The servants seized Maid Maleen, intending to drag her away, but her desperate screams caught the king’s son’s attention.

He dashed out of his room and commanded them to set her free at once.

They brought torches, and he spotted the gold chain around her neck, the same one he had given her.

"You are the true bride!" he declared, astonished. "You are the one I took to church. Come with me to my room now."

Once alone, he said, "On our way to the church, you mentioned Maid Maleen, my betrothed. If it were possible, I would think she stands before me—you look just like her."

Maid Maleen replied, "I am Maid Maleen, imprisoned in darkness for your sake, suffering from hunger, thirst, and poverty for seven years."

"But today, the sun shines upon me again. I was married to you in the church, and I am your lawful wife."

They embraced joyfully and lived happily ever after.

As for the false bride, she was punished for her treachery.

The tower where Maid Maleen was held stood for many years, and when children passed by, they would sing:

"A king's daughter, she sits within,"

"Little Hans, with your coat so gay,"

"Follow me, follow me, fast as you may."

title: Master Pfriem
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Master Pfriem was a short, thin, and lively man who was always on the move.

His face, dominated by a turned-up nose, was marked by smallpox scars and as pale as death.

His hair was gray and shaggy, and his small eyes darted around constantly.

He noticed everything, had an opinion on everything, thought he knew better than anyone, and was always right.

When he walked through the streets, he flailed his arms as if he were rowing a boat.

Once, he bumped into a girl carrying a pail so hard that he got drenched.

"You idiot!" he yelled at her, shaking off the water. "Couldn't you see I was right behind you?"

He was a shoemaker, and while working, he pulled his thread so tightly that he ended up hitting anyone who stood too close.

No apprentice lasted more than a month with him because he always found something wrong with the best work.

One moment, it was uneven stitches; the next, one shoe was too long or one heel was higher, or the leather wasn't cut wide enough.

"Wait a minute," he said to his apprentice. "I'll show you how to soften the leather."

With that, he grabbed a strap and gave the apprentice a couple of swats on the back.

He called everyone lazy.

He himself didn't produce much work because he never stayed still for more than fifteen minutes.

If his wife got up early to start the fire, he’d hop out of bed and rush barefoot to the kitchen, shouting, "Are you trying to burn down my house?"

"That's a fire big enough to roast an ox! Doesn’t wood cost anything?"

If his servants stood gossiping while washing, he’d scold them, saying, "There they are, geese gabbing away instead of working!"

And fresh soap? "Such disgraceful extravagance and laziness! They just want to save their hands instead of scrubbing properly!"

Then he'd rush out and knock over a bucket full of soapy water, flooding the entire kitchen.

When he spotted someone building a new house, he'd hurry to the window to poke his nose in.

title: Master Pfriem
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Oh no, they’re using that red sandstone that never dries," he exclaimed. "No one will ever be healthy in that house!"

"Just look at how badly they’re laying the stones! And that mortar is worthless; it needs gravel, not sand!"

"I’ll live to see that house collapse on the people inside it!"

He sat down, stitched a couple of times, and then jumped back up, removing his leather apron.

"I have to go out and make those men see reason!"

He stumbled upon the carpenters. "What’s this?" he shouted. "You’re not working in line! Do you think the beams will be straight? One mistake will mess everything up!"

He snatched an axe from one carpenter and tried to show him how to cut it, but when a cart loaded with clay rolled by, he tossed the axe aside.
