He then rushed over to the peasant alongside the cart. "What are you thinking?" he exclaimed. "Yoking young horses to a heavy cart like this will wear them out!"

The peasant didn’t respond, and in a fit of rage, Pfriem stormed back into his workshop.

When he was about to start working again, the apprentice handed him a shoe.

"Well, what’s this?" Pfriem screamed. "Didn’t I tell you not to cut shoes so wide? Who would buy a shoe this shape? It’s practically just a sole! I expect you to follow my instructions exactly!"

"Master," the apprentice replied, "you might be right, but this is the shoe you cut out yourself. You knocked it off the table when you jumped up earlier, and I’ve just picked it back up."

"Even an angel from heaven wouldn’t make you believe that!"

One night, Master Pfriem dreamed he was dead and on his way to heaven.

When he arrived, he knocked loudly at the door.

"I wonder why they don't have a knocker," he thought. "Knocking with my knuckles is going to hurt!"

title: Master Pfriem
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The Apostle Peter opened the door and looked at him, clearly annoyed by the noise.

"Ah, it's you, Master Pfriem," said Peter. "I’ll let you in, but you’d better leave your habit of nitpicking behind, or it might not end well for you."

"You could have saved your breath," Pfriem replied. "I already know what’s proper, and thank God, everything here is perfect—there's nothing to criticize like there is on earth.”

So he entered and wandered around the vastness of heaven.

He looked left and right but occasionally shook his head or muttered to himself.

Then he spotted two angels carrying a beam. It was the same beam someone had in their eye while searching for the splinter in another's.

They weren’t carrying it straight but at an angle.

"Whoever thought this was a good idea?" Master Pfriem thought to himself, but he kept quiet and nodded in approval.

"In the end, it’s all the same—whether they carry it straight or angled—as long as they get it done and I don’t see them bumping into anything."

Soon after, he saw two angels drawing water from a well into a bucket, but the bucket was riddled with holes, and water was spilling everywhere.

They were watering the earth with rain.

"How ridiculous!" he exclaimed, but quickly reminded himself that it might just be for fun.

"If it’s a pastime, then I suppose they can waste their time doing things like this, especially here in heaven, where people seem to do nothing but lounge around."

He moved on and noticed a cart stuck in a deep hole.

"What do you expect?" he said to the guy nearby. "Who in their right mind would load something so unreasonable?"

"Good wishes," the man replied, "I couldn’t get it on the right path, but at least I’ve brought it safely this far, and they won’t let me stay stuck here."

Indeed, an angel came by and harnessed two horses to the cart.

"That’s right," Pfriem thought, "but two horses just won’t cut it; you need at least four!"

Another angel arrived with two more horses, but instead of attaching them in front, he hitched them behind.

That was the last straw for Master Pfriem. "What are you doing?" he shouted. "Has anyone in history ever seen a cart pulled this way? You think you know best, don’t you?"

He was about to say more when one of the heavenly residents grabbed him by the throat and forcefully pushed him out.

As he passed under the archway, Master Pfriem turned to take one last look at the cart and saw it being lifted off the ground by four winged horses.

At that moment, Master Pfriem woke up. "Things are certainly arranged differently in heaven than on earth," he muttered to himself, "and that explains a lot. But how could anyone remain patient watching horses harnessed both in front and behind? Sure, they have wings, but who knew?"

"It's just foolish to add wings to a horse that already has four strong legs to run with!"

"But I have to get up; otherwise, everything in my house will be a mess. It's a good thing I'm not..."

title: Mother Holle
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a widow who had two daughters—one was pretty and hardworking, while the other was plain and lazy.

Yet, she favored the ugly and idle one more because she was her own daughter.

The pretty stepdaughter had to do all the chores and be the family's servant.

Every day, the poor girl sat by a well along the road, spinning until her fingers bled.

One day, her shuttle was stained with her blood, so she dipped it in the well to wash it off, but it slipped from her hand and fell to the bottom.

She began to cry and rushed to tell her stepmother about the accident.

But her stepmother harshly scolded her and mercilessly ordered, "Since you let the shuttle fall in, you must fetch it out yourself."

Feeling hopeless, the girl returned to the well without knowing what to do.

In her sorrow, she jumped into the well to retrieve the shuttle.

As she fell, she lost consciousness.

When she woke up again, she found herself in a beautiful meadow, bathed in sunlight and filled with countless flowers.

She wandered through the meadow until she came to a baker’s oven full of bread.

The bread cried out, "Oh, take me out! Take me out! Or I shall burn! I’ve been baked for too long!"

So she approached the oven and took out all the loaves with the bread shovel.

After that, she continued on her way and found a tree filled with ripe apples.

The tree called out, "Oh, shake me! Shake me! The apples are ready to fall!"

So she shook the tree until the apples fell like rain, and after gathering them into a pile, she moved on.

Eventually, she came to a little house where an old woman peeked out.

But the old woman had such big teeth that the girl was frightened and almost ran away.

Yet, the old woman called out, "What are you afraid of, dear child? Stay with me!"

"If you do all the housework well, you’ll be rewarded. Just make sure to shake my bed so the feathers fly because that will mean snow is falling on the earth. I am Mother Holle."

title: Mother Holle
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The old woman’s friendly words gave the girl courage, and she agreed to work for her.

She fulfilled all her tasks to the satisfaction of her mistress and shook the bed so vigorously that the feathers floated around like snowflakes.

This made her life enjoyable with Mother Holle—there were no harsh words, and every day, she enjoyed boiled or roasted meat.

She stayed with Mother Holle for a while before she started to feel sad.

At first, she couldn’t understand why she felt this way, but eventually, she realized she was homesick.

Even though she was much better off here than at home, she still longed to return.

Finally, she said to the old woman, "I miss my home, and even though I’m happy here, I can’t stay any longer. I need to go back to my family."

Mother Holle responded, "I’m glad to hear you long for home. Since you’ve served me so well, I will take you back myself."

With that, she took the girl by the hand and led her to a large door.

Once the door opened, just as the girl stood beneath it, a heavy shower of golden rain poured down, covering her completely.

"You will receive this because you’ve been so hardworking," said Mother Holle, as she returned the shuttle that had fallen into the well.

Then the door closed, and the girl found herself back on earth, not far from her home.

As she entered the yard, a rooster sat on the well and crowed, "Your golden girl has returned!"

She went inside to her mother, and both she and her sister welcomed her warmly, surprised by the gold covering her.

The girl shared all that had happened, and when her mother heard how she acquired such wealth, she was eager to provide the same fortune for her ugly, lazy daughter.

So the mother made her sit by the well to spin, and to ensure her shuttle would be stained with blood, she pricked her finger on a thorn bush.

Then, she tossed her shuttle into the well and jumped in after it.

Just like her sister, she arrived in the beautiful meadow and followed the exact same path.

title: Mother Holle
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When she reached the oven, the bread again called out, "Oh, take me out! Take me out! Or I shall burn! I've been baked too long!"

But the lazy daughter scoffed, "As if I want to get dirty!" and ignored it.

Continuing on her way, she soon came to the apple tree, which cried out, "Oh, shake me! Shake me! The apples are ripe!"

But she responded, "That sounds dangerous. What if one of you falls on my head?" and continued walking.

When she finally arrived at Mother Holle’s house, she felt no fear because she had heard of the old woman's big teeth, and quickly offered her services.

At first, she forced herself to work hard, obeying Mother Holle’s instructions, dreaming of the gold she would earn.

But by the second day, she began to slack off, and by the third day, she wouldn’t even get out of bed.

She stopped making Mother Holle’s bed properly and didn’t shake it enough to make the feathers fly.

Mother Holle grew tired of this and told her she could leave.

The lazy girl was eager to go, imagining the golden rain awaiting her.

Mother Holle brought her to the grand door, but while she stood beneath it, instead of gold, she got drenched in a large pot of pitch.

"That’s your reward for your service," said Mother Holle, as she shut the door.

The lazy girl returned home, completely covered in pitch, and the rooster on the well, seeing her, crowed, "Your dirty girl has come back!"

But the pitch stuck to her and couldn’t be removed as long as she lived.

title: Odds and Ends
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a beautiful girl who was quite lazy.

She often neglected her chores. When it was time to spin flax, she would get so frustrated that if she found even a tiny knot, she'd yank out a whole bundle of it and scatter it all over the ground.

Now, she had a hardworking servant who would pick up the discarded bits of flax, clean them up, spin them into fine threads, and eventually make herself a lovely dress from them.

A young man had fallen in love with the lazy girl, and they were set to be married.

On the night before the wedding, the diligent servant was dancing joyfully in her beautiful dress, and the bride remarked, "Wow, that girl is really jumping around in my leftovers."

The groom overheard this and asked the bride what she meant.

She explained that the girl was wearing a dress made from the flax that she had tossed aside.

When the groom realized how lazy the bride was compared to the hardworking girl, he decided to choose the industrious one as his wife instead.

title: Old Hildebrand
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a peasant, his wife, and the village parson.

The parson had a crush on the peasant's wife and had longed to spend a day with her.

The peasant woman was also interested in the idea.

So one day, he told her, "Listen, my dear friend, I’ve come up with a plan for us to spend a whole day happily together."

He continued, "On Wednesday, you should pretend to be sick in bed and tell your husband you're unwell. As long as you keep acting sick until Sunday, when I have to preach, I'll announce in my sermon that anyone with a sick family member who makes a pilgrimage to Gockerli Hill in Italy, where you can buy laurel leaves for a kreuzer, will have their loved ones restored to health."

The woman eagerly agreed. So, on Wednesday, she got into bed and complained about her illness as they had planned.

Her husband did everything he could to help her, but nothing seemed to work. When Sunday arrived, she said, "I feel so ill, it’s as if I’m about to die. But before that happens, I’d really like to hear the parson’s sermon today."

The peasant replied, "Oh, my dear, don’t do that. You might get worse if you get up. Let me attend the sermon and I promise I’ll tell you everything the parson says."

"Alright," the woman said. "Just pay attention and repeat everything to me." So the peasant went to hear the sermon.

The parson announced that anyone with a sick child, husband, wife, father, mother, sister, brother, or any other relative could make a pilgrimage to Gockerli Hill in Italy, where laurel leaves cost a kreuzer. Anyone who did so would be cured right away. After the service, the peasant was overjoyed.

He hurried to the parson, who handed him the sack for the laurel leaves along with the kreuzer.

Excited, the peasant shouted as he reached home, "Hooray! Dear wife, it’s almost like you’re well now! The parson said that anyone with a sick family member could go to Gockerli Hill for laurel leaves and they would be cured instantly. I’ve got the bag and the kreuzer, so I'm off to start my journey so you can get better faster."

title: Old Hildebrand
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
As soon as he left, the peasant woman got up, and the parson was right there.

Meanwhile, let’s follow the peasant, who walked quickly so he could reach Gockerli Hill sooner. On his way, he ran into his friend, the egg merchant, who had just come back from the market.

"Bless you!" said the merchant. "Where are you rushing off to?"

"To the ends of the earth!" replied the peasant. "My wife is sick, and I just heard the parson preach that if anyone has a sick family member and makes a pilgrimage to Gockerli Hill in Italy, they can buy laurel leaves for a kreuzer, and the sick will be cured right away. So, I’ve got the bag and the kreuzer from the parson, and now I'm starting my pilgrimage!"

The egg merchant said, "Wait, are you really gullible enough to believe that? Don’t you see what’s going on? The parson just wants to have a day alone with your wife, and he put you on this quest to get you out of the way."

The peasant said, "I wonder if that’s true…"

"Alright," said the egg merchant, "get in my egg basket, and I’ll take you home so you can find out for yourself."

So the peasant climbed into the basket, and the egg merchant carried him back to the house.

When they arrived, excitement filled the air. The woman had already cooked most of the farmyard animals, made pancakes, and the parson was there with his fiddle.

The egg merchant knocked on the door and the woman asked who it was. "It’s me, your gossip!" said the egg merchant. "I need a place to stay for the night as I couldn’t sell all my eggs at the market, and they’re too heavy to carry home in the dark."

"Oh dear, you picked a bad time," the woman replied. "But since you're here, come in and sit on the bench by the stove."

She set the gossip and his basket down by the stove. Meanwhile, the parson and the woman were having a great time.

Finally, the parson said, "You sing beautifully. Sing something for me."

"I can't sing now," the woman answered. "I used to sing well in my younger days, but that’s all behind me now."

"Please, just sing a little song," the parson urged.

So the woman began, singing,

"I sent my husband away from me,

I wish it were a year before he returned,

And I’d never ask him for the laurel-leaf sack."

The gossip then started singing, but I should mention that the peasant’s name was Hildebrand.

"What are you doing, my dear Hildebrand,

sitting on the bench by the stove so near?"

And from the basket, the peasant sang,

"I’ll hate all singing from this day on,

and I won’t stay in this basket any longer."

With that, he climbed out of the basket and confronted the parson.

title: Old Rinkrank
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a king who had a beautiful daughter.

He had a glass mountain built and declared that whoever could cross it without falling

would win her hand in marriage.

Among those who loved the princess, there was a young man who asked the king for her.

"Yes," said the king, "if you can cross the mountain without falling, she will be yours."

The princess said she would go with him

and promised to hold on to him if he began to fall.

They set off together to cross the mountain, but halfway up,

the princess lost her footing and fell, causing the mountain to open and close around her.

Her fiancé couldn’t see where she had gone, as the mountain shut immediately.

He cried and mourned her loss, and so did the king, who ordered a team to break open the mountain, hoping to rescue her.

However, they couldn’t find where she had vanished.

Meanwhile, the princess had fallen deep into the earth, landing in a vast cave.

An old man, with a long gray beard, approached her and told her that if she became his servant and did whatever he asked, she could live; otherwise, he would harm her.

So she obeyed him.

Every morning, he would pull a ladder out of his pocket, set it against the mountain, and climb to the top, pulling the ladder up with him.

The princess had to cook his meals, make his bed, and do all his chores, but when he returned, he always brought heaps of gold and silver.

After many years of living with him and growing older, he called her 'Mother Mansrot,' and she had to call him 'Old Rinkrank.'

One day, while he was away, after making his bed and cleaning the dishes,

she locked all the doors and windows, except for a small window where light was coming in, which she left open.

When Old Rinkrank came home, he knocked on the door and called, "Mother Mansrot, open the door for me."

"No," she replied, "I won’t open the door for you, Old Rinkrank."

Then he said, "Wash my dishes, Mother Mansrot."

"I’ve already washed your dishes," she answered.

Then he called out again, "I’ve made your bed!"

"I’ve already made your bed," she replied.

Curious, he ran around the house and noticed the little window was open.

He thought, "I’ll peek in to see what she’s doing and why she won’t let me in."

He tried to look through, but couldn’t fit his head in because of his long beard.

So, he pushed his beard through the window, and just as it slid inside,

Mother Mansrot pulled down the window using a cord she'd tied to it, trapping his beard.

He began to cry out in pain, begging her to let him go.

But she refused, saying she would only release him if he gave her the ladder he used to climb the mountain.

Reluctantly, he told her where the ladder was.

She tied a long ribbon to the window and set up the ladder.

She climbed the ladder to the top of the mountain and opened the window.

Rushing to her father, she told him everything that had happened.

The king was overjoyed, and her fiancé was still waiting.

They dug up the mountain and found Old Rinkrank buried with all his gold and silver.

The king had him executed and took all the riches.

The princess married her beloved and they lived happily ever after, surrounded by riches and joy.

title: Old Sultan
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once, there was a farmer who had a loyal dog named Sultan, but Sultan was getting old.

He had lost all his teeth, which meant he couldn’t hold on to anything anymore.

One day, the farmer was standing with his wife in front of their house and said, "Tomorrow I'm going to shoot old Sultan."

His wife, feeling pity for the loyal dog, replied, "He has served us faithfully for so long; we should give him a better end."

"What do you mean?" said the farmer, "You’re not being very logical. He doesn’t have a single tooth left, and no thief is afraid of him now. It’s time for him to go. If he served us well, he was well-fed for it."

The poor dog, who was lying stretched out in the sun nearby, overheard everything and felt sad that tomorrow would be his last day.

He had a good friend, the wolf, so he quietly made his way to the forest that evening to share his troubles.

"Listen, my friend," said the wolf, "don’t worry, I’ll help you out of this mess. I have a plan. Tomorrow morning, your master and his wife will go to make hay, and they’ll take their little kid with them; no one will be left at home.

During their work, they usually lay the child under a hedge for shade. You should lay down there too, acting like you want to look after it.

Then, I’ll dash out from the woods and snatch the child away. You need to chase me, pretending you want to get it back.

I’ll drop the child, and you’ll return it to its parents, who will think you've saved it. They’ll be so grateful that they'll never harm you; instead, you’ll get their favor and never want for anything again."

The plan appealed to the dog, and they executed it just as discussed.

The father screamed when he saw the wolf running across the field with his child, but when old Sultan returned the child safely, the farmer was overjoyed.

title: Old Sultan
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He petted Sultan and said, "Not a hair on you will be harmed. You can eat my food free for life."

He turned to his wife and said, "Go home and make old Sultan some soft bread so he won’t have to chew. And bring out the pillow from my bed; let him lie on that."

From then on, old Sultan was as well cared for as he could ever hope.

Soon after, the wolf stopped by to check on him and was pleased that the plan had worked.

But then the wolf said, "Just a heads-up—if I ever get the chance, I might take one of your master’s fat sheep. Don’t expect me to be loyal all the time!"

Sultan replied, "I’ll stay true to my master. I can’t go along with that."

The wolf thought the dog was joking, but later that night, he crept around to steal a sheep.

However, the farmer had been warned about the wolf’s plan by the loyal Sultan and caught the wolf, giving him a good thrashing with the flail.

The wolf had to flee, but he called out to the dog, "Just wait, you rascal! You’ll pay for this!"

The next day, the wolf sent a boar to challenge Sultan to meet him in the forest to settle things.

Old Sultan had no one to support him except a cat with three legs, and as they walked together, the poor cat limped while proudly raising her tail.

The wolf and his friend were already at the meeting spot, but when they saw Sultan and the cat approaching, they thought Sultan was carrying a sword because of the cat’s raised tail.

And when the cat hopped on her three legs, they believed she was about to throw stones at them.

Frightened, the wild boar ducked into the bushes, and the wolf climbed a tree.

When the dog and cat arrived, they noticed no one was around.

But the wild boar had failed to hide fully—one of its ears was sticking out.

As the cat looked around, the boar moved its ear, and thinking it was a mouse, the cat jumped on it and bit down hard.

The boar squealed loudly and scampered away, shouting, "The culprit is up in the tree!"

Sultan and the cat looked up and saw the wolf, who felt embarrassed about being so scared, and he decided to make peace with the dog.

title: One-Eye, Two-Eyes, and Three-Eyes
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a woman who had three daughters.

The eldest was named One-Eye because she had just one eye in the middle of her forehead.

The second daughter was called Two-Eyes because she had two normal eyes like everyone else.

The youngest was named Three-Eyes because she had three eyes, with her extra eye also in the center of her forehead.

Even though Two-Eyes could see just like other people, her sisters and their mother couldn’t stand her.

They said to her, "You, with your two eyes, are just like everybody else; you don’t belong to us."

They pushed her around, tossed old clothes at her, and only left her the scraps of food.

They did everything they could think of to make her miserable.

Eventually, Two-Eyes had to go out to the fields to care for the goat, but she was still very hungry because her sisters had barely fed her.

So, she sat down on a hill and cried so hard that tears streamed down her cheeks.

As she looked up through her sadness, a woman appeared beside her and asked, "Why are you crying, little Two-Eyes?"

"Isn’t it enough reason to cry when my sisters and mother despise me just because I have two eyes like everyone else? They shove me around, throw old clothes at me, and leave me only the scraps of food. Today, they barely gave me enough to be full, so I'm still hungry," Two-Eyes told her.

The wise woman replied, "Wipe your tears, Two-Eyes. I have some advice that will end your hunger. Just tell your goat, 'Cover the table with food,' and a beautiful table will appear with plenty of delicious food for you to enjoy.

When you’ve had enough, say, 'Bleat, bleat, my little goat, please take the table away,' and it will vanish before your eyes."

After saying this, the wise woman disappeared.

Two-Eyes thought to herself, "I have to try this; I’m too hungry not to." So she said to her goat, 'Cover the table with food.'

title: One-Eye, Two-Eyes, and Three-Eyes
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
As soon as she spoke the words, a beautiful table with a white cloth appeared before her, holding delicious food that looked fresh out of the oven.

Two-Eyes offered a quick prayer, "Dear God, be our guest, amen," and began to eat.

When she had her fill, she said the words the wise woman had told her, 'Bleat, bleat, my little goat, please take the table away,' and instantly, the table and all the food disappeared.

What a wonderful way to manage things, thought Two-Eyes.

That evening, when she returned home with her goat, she found a small dish with food that her sisters had prepared for her, but she didn’t touch it.

The next day, she went out again with her goat, leaving behind the meager bread scraps she received untouched.

The first two times she did this, her sisters didn’t notice, but eventually, they realized something was off.

They said, "There’s something strange about Two-Eyes; she never eats her food anymore, and she used to finish everything we gave her. She must have found another way to get food."

Curious about the truth, they decided to send One-Eye with Two-Eyes the next time she took the goat out, to see what she did.

So, when Two-Eyes set out again, One-Eye approached her and said, "I'm going with you to the pasture to watch over the goat and make sure it has enough to eat."

But Two-Eyes knew One-Eye's real intentions, so she led the goat into some tall grass and said, "Let’s sit down, One-Eye. I'll sing a song for you."

One-Eye sat down, quickly growing tired from the unusual walk and the warmth of the sun.

Two-Eyes began to sing steadily.

Eventually, One-Eye shut her one eye and fell asleep.

Once Two-Eyes saw One-Eye was deeply asleep and couldn’t see her, she said, 'Cover the table with food.'

Then she sat at her table and ate and drank until she felt content.

title: One-Eye, Two-Eyes, and Three-Eyes
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When she was finished, she repeated, 'Bleat, bleat, my little goat, please take the table away,' and everything vanished right before her eyes.

Two-Eyes woke One-Eye and said, 'One-Eye, you are supposed to take care of the goat, but you fell asleep. What if it roams all over the place? Let's head home now.'

They went back, and again, Two-Eyes didn’t eat from the dish that her sisters prepared.

One-Eye couldn’t explain to their mother why Two-Eyes wouldn’t eat, so she fumbled, saying, "I dozed off while I was out."

The next day, their mother told Three-Eyes, 'This time, you go with Two-Eyes to see if she eats anything while she’s out and if anyone brings her food or drink. She must be eating in secret.'

So Three-Eyes went to Two-Eyes and said, 'I’m coming with you to take care of the goat and ensure it has enough to eat.'

But Two-Eyes was aware of Three-Eyes' intentions, so she drove the goat into high grass and said, 'Let’s sit down, and I’ll sing you a song.'

Three-Eyes sat down, feeling tired from the walk and the heat.

Two-Eyes began singing the same song as before.

As she sang, she teased, 'Three-Eyes, are you sleeping?'

Three-Eyes did close two of her eyes and fell asleep, but the third eye, having not been mentioned in the song, stayed wide open.

It’s true that she pretended to be asleep, but her third eye blinked, and she could see everything clearly.

When Two-Eyes thought Three-Eyes was fast asleep, she said, 'Cover the table with food.'

She enjoyed as much food as she desired before saying, 'Bleat, bleat, my little goat, please take the table away,' and all of it disappeared.

Three-Eyes had witnessed everything.

Then Two-Eyes came to her, woke her up, and said, 'Were you asleep, Three-Eyes? You are quite the vigilant watcher. Time to go home.'

title: One-Eye, Two-Eyes, and Three-Eyes
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When they got home, Two-Eyes didn’t eat again, and Three-Eyes told their mother, 'Now I see why that proud girl doesn’t eat.'

'While she’s outside, she says, 'Cover the table with food,' and a table filled with delicious food appears before her, much better than what we have. After she eats, she says, 'Bleat, bleat, my little goat, please take the table away,' and it all vanishes.'

'I watched closely. She put two of my eyes to sleep with some kind of magic, but lucky for me, the one in my forehead stayed alert.'

Their envious mother shouted, 'Do you wish to have more than we have? That desire shall end now!' and she grabbed a butcher’s knife and stabbed the goat, causing it to collapse to the ground.

When Two-Eyes saw this, she went outside, filled with sadness, and sat on the grass at the edge of the field, crying sorrowfully.

Suddenly, the wise woman appeared again and asked, 'Why are you in tears, Two-Eyes?'

'Have I not reason to cry?' she replied. 'The goat that provided my meals each day has been killed by my mother, and now I must face hunger once more.'

The wise woman said, 'Listen, Two-Eyes, here's my advice: ask your sisters for the insides of the goat, and bury them in the ground in front of your house, and you'll find good fortune.'

Then she vanished, and Two-Eyes returned home, saying to her sisters, 'Please, give me some part of the goat.'

'I don't want anything good; just give me the entrails.' So they laughed and replied, 'If that’s all you want, take it.'

Two-Eyes quietly took the entrails and buried them in the evening in front of the house door, just as the wise woman had advised.

The next morning, when they all woke up and went outside, they found a magnificent tree with silver leaves and golden fruit, more beautiful than anything else in the world.

title: One-Eye, Two-Eyes, and Three-Eyes
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
They didn’t know how such a tree could have grown overnight, but Two-Eyes recognized it as the result of the goat's entrails, right where she had buried them.

The mother told One-Eye, 'Climb up and pick some fruit from the tree for us.'

One-Eye climbed up, but when she reached for a golden apple, the branch slipped out of her grip, and it happened every time, so she couldn't pick a single apple.

Then their mother said, 'Three-Eyes, you go up; with your three eyes, you can look around better than One-Eye.'

One-Eye slid down, and Three-Eyes climbed the tree.

But she had no more luck; regardless of how hard she tried, the golden apples eluded her.

Finally, their mother grew impatient and tried to climb up herself, but she could grab hold of nothing more than One-Eye and Three-Eyes.

She only grasped at thin air.

Two-Eyes spoke up, 'Let me try. Maybe I can be successful.'

The sisters scoffed, 'You, with your two eyes, what can you do?'

But Two-Eyes climbed up, and this time, the golden apples didn’t avoid her; they came right into her hands.

She picked them one after the other and filled her apron with them.

The mother took them from her, and instead of treating poor Two-Eyes with kindness, all she and her sisters felt was envy since only Two-Eyes had been able to gather the fruit.

One day, when they all stood beneath the tree, a handsome young knight approached.

'Quick, Two-Eyes,' urged her sisters. 'Get under that barrel and don’t embarrass us!' They hurriedly rolled an empty barrel over Two-Eyes.

As they did, they also swept the golden apples she had gathered under it.

When the knight grew closer, he admired the beautiful golden and silver tree and asked, 'To whom does this gorgeous tree belong? Whoever gives me a branch can ask for anything in return.'

One-Eye and Three-Eyes quickly replied that the tree was theirs and that they would give him a branch.

title: One-Eye, Two-Eyes, and Three-Eyes
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
They tried hard to reach the branches, but they always pulled back as if they were alive.

'It's strange that the tree is yours, yet you can’t pick from it,' commented the knight.

They insisted the tree was theirs.

While they argued, Two-Eyes rolled some golden apples from underneath the barrel, right at the knight’s feet.

She was annoyed with her sisters for lying.

When the knight noticed the apples, he was amazed and asked where they came from.

One-Eye and Three-Eyes explained, 'We have another sister who can't show herself because she has only two eyes like a regular person.'

But the knight wanted to see her and called out, 'Two-Eyes, come out.'

Two-Eyes, feeling hope, stepped from behind the barrel, and the knight marveled at her beauty.

'You, Two-Eyes, can surely pick a branch from this tree for me,' he said.

'Yes, I can do that,' replied Two-Eyes confidently. 'This tree belongs to me.'

She climbed up and effortlessly broke off a branch full of gorgeous silver leaves and golden fruit.

She handed it to the knight.

'What can I give you in exchange for it, Two-Eyes?' he asked.

'Oh,' she sighed. 'I suffer from hunger and want all day long. If you would take me with you and save me, I would be so happy.'

So the knight lifted Two-Eyes onto his horse and took her back to his castle, where he provided her with beautiful clothes and plenty of food.

As he loved her dearly, they married, and their wedding was celebrated with great joy.

While Two-Eyes was being whisked away by the dashing knight, her two sisters were envious of her fortune.

'Well, the beautiful tree is still ours,' they thought, 'Even if we can’t pick its fruit, at least everyone will admire it and come to us.'

'Who knows what fortunes may await us?' But the next morning, the tree was gone, taking all their hopes with it.

To her delight, when Two-Eyes looked out of her new window, the tree was right outside her room, having followed her.

Two-Eyes lived happily for many years.

One day, two poor women came to her castle, begging for help.

As she looked at their faces, she recognized her sisters, One-Eye and Three-Eyes, who had fallen into extreme poverty.

They were now wandering around, begging for food.

Yet, Two-Eyes welcomed them warmly, showed kindness, and took care of them.

In doing so, both sisters truly regretted how they had treated their sister.

title: Our Lady's Child
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Near a vast forest, there lived a woodcutter and his wife, who had an

adorable little girl, their only child, just three years old. They were so poor,

though, that they struggled to put food on the table and didn't know how to

provide for her. One gloomy morning, the woodcutter went out to the forest, heavy-hearted

as he began his work, and while he was chopping wood, a tall and stunning woman suddenly

appeared before him, wearing a crown of sparkling stars. She said, 'I am the Virgin Mary,

the mother of Jesus. You are in need, so bring your child to me. I will take her with me and be her mother, caring for her.'

The woodcutter followed her instructions, brought his child to her, and entrusted her to the Virgin Mary, who then took her up to heaven.

In heaven, the little girl was well taken care of. She enjoyed sweet treats, drank warm milk, wore clothes made of

gold, and played with little angels. When she turned fourteen, the Virgin Mary called her one day and said,

'Dear child, I'm off on a long journey. You will need to look after the keys to the thirteen doors of heaven.

You may open twelve of them to see the wonder inside, but the thirteenth, which has this key, is off-limits. Be careful not to open it, or you will bring yourself unhappiness.'

The girl promised to obey. Once the Virgin Mary left, she began exploring the beautiful homes in heaven.

Every day, she opened one of the doors until she had visited all twelve. Inside each, she found one of the apostles bathed in light, and she delighted in

all the beauty and wonder, with the little angels by her side celebrating with her. Finally, only the forbidden door remained.

Curiosity swelled within her, and she told the angels, 'I won't open it all the way, just a little peek through the crack.' 'Oh no,' the little angels warned, 'that would be a sin.

The Virgin Mary forbade it, and it could easily lead to your downfall.' She fell silent but her curiosity wouldn’t leave her; it nagged at her and made it impossible to relax.

title: Our Lady's Child
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One day, when all the angels were out, she thought, 'Now I’m completely alone. No one will find out if I take a quick look.' She found the key and, once it was in her hand,

she inserted it into the lock and turned it. The door swung open and she saw the Trinity sitting in radiant light.

In awe, she lingered, mesmerized by everything, and when she touched the light with her finger, it turned entirely golden.

Instantly, fear gripped her. She slammed the door shut and fled. Yet, the fear still haunted her, no matter what she did.

'Yes,' she said for the second time. Then she noticed her golden finger, a clear sign of her disobedience, and realized she'd sinned. The Virgin Mary asked her again, 'Did you not do it?' 'No,' the girl insisted for the third time.

The Virgin Mary then said, 'You’ve disobeyed me and lied; you’re no longer worthy to stay in heaven.' With that, the girl fell into a deep sleep.

When she awoke, she found herself on the earth, lost in a wilderness. She wanted to scream but couldn’t make a sound.

Desperate to escape, she tried to run, but thorny hedges held her back, trapping her in their clutch. In this barren land, an old hollow tree became her home.

When night fell, she crawled into the tree for shelter. Here, fear never left her, and her heart raced constantly as the golden mark remained on her finger, no matter how much she scrubbed and washed.

It wasn’t long before the Virgin Mary returned from her journey. She called the girl to her and asked for the keys to heaven back.

As the girl handed over the keys, the Virgin gazed into her eyes and asked, 'Did you open the thirteenth door as well?' 'No,' she replied.

The Virgin Mary placed her hand on the girl’s heart, felt it pounding, and knew for sure that she had disobeyed her.

Once more, she asked, 'Are you certain you didn't open it?' Heavy rain poured, and although it was tough, she wept bitterly, remembering the joy she had in heaven and all the fun she had with the angels.

title: Our Lady's Child
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Her only food was roots and wild berries, which she scoured for as far as she could.

In the autumn, she gathered fallen nuts and leaves and stored them in her hollow. The nuts served as her winter food, and during snowy days, she snuggled among the leaves like a little animal to stay warm.

Before long, her clothes were in tatters, and bits of them fell off one after another. However, when the warm sun shone again, she would sit outside her tree, her long hair flowing like a mantle around her.

Year after year passed, and she felt the weight of the world's pain and suffering. One day, as the trees donned their fresh green, the king of the land went hunting.

He chased after a deer, and when it darted into a thicket, he dismounted, pushed the bushes aside, and swung his sword to carve a path through.

Once he broke through, he spotted a remarkably beautiful maiden seated beneath a tree, entirely enveloped in her golden hair down to her feet.

Stunned by her beauty, he paused, then asked, 'Who are you? Why are you all alone here in this wilderness?' But she couldn't respond since her mouth remained shut.

The king continued, 'Will you come with me to my castle?' She simply nodded slightly. He picked her up in his arms,

carried her to his horse, and rode back to his castle. Once there, he had her dressed in elegant garments,

and provided her with everything she could want. Although she couldn’t speak,

her beauty and charm captivated him. He fell deeply in love and soon married her. A year later, the queen gave birth to a son.

That night, the Virgin Mary appeared to her while she was alone in bed and said, 'If you confess that you unlocked the forbidden door, I will restore your ability to speak.

But if you stubbornly deny it, I will take your newborn child away with me.' The queen was allowed to reply but remained defiant, saying 'No, I didn’t open the door.'

The Virgin Mary took the child from her arms and vanished. The next morning, when the baby was gone, whispers spread that the queen was a monster who had harmed her child.

She heard the rumors but couldn’t speak up to defend herself. Yet the king trusted her completely and refused to believe the gossip. A year later, the queen had another son.

That night, the Virgin Mary appeared again, saying, 'If you admit you opened the forbidden door, I'll return your child and restore your speech.

But if you deny it, I will take this new child as well.' Again, the queen insisted, 'No, I did not open the forbidden door.'

Once more, the Virgin took the child and disappeared. The next morning, as news spread that this child had vanished too, the people cried out that the queen was a monster and demanded justice.

Despite the king's love for her, he couldn’t silence his ministers. A trial was held, and since she couldn’t defend herself,

she was condemned to be burned alive. They gathered wood, and when she was tied to the stake and flames began to surround her, pride melted away in her heart.

In a moment of despair, she thought, 'If only I could confess before I die that I opened the door.' Just then, her voice returned, and she cried out, 'Yes, Mary, I did it!'

Immediately, rain fell from the sky, extinguishing the flames, and a light shone above her. The Virgin Mary descended with her two sons beside her and her newborn daughter in her arms.

She spoke gently, saying, 'Anyone who repents their sins and acknowledges them is forgiven.' Then she handed her the three children, untied her tongue, and granted her eternal happiness.

title: Poverty and Humility Lead to Heaven
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once, there was a prince who decided to venture out into the world.

He was deep in thought and felt sad.

As he gazed up at the stunning blue sky,

he sighed and said,

"It must be wonderful for those up in heaven."

Then he spotted a poor, gray-haired man walking along the

road toward him. Curious, he asked, "How can I

get to heaven?" The old man replied, "Through poverty and humility. Wear

my tattered clothes, wander the world for seven years,

and learn what suffering is. Don’t carry money, but if you’re hungry,

ask kind-hearted people for a piece of bread.

The prince took off his royal coat and donned the beggar's rags. He stepped out into the world,

enduring great hardship. He carried only a bit of food,

spoke little, but prayed to the Lord to watch over him.

When the seven years passed, he returned to his father's palace, but no

one recognized him. He told the servants, "Go and tell my

parents that I’m back." However, the servants just

laughed and ignored him. So he pleaded,

"Please tell my brothers, I really want to see them again." The servants still refused to help,

but finally one went to inform the king's children, who didn't believe it and paid—

no attention.

The prince then wrote a letter to his mother, describing all his suffering, but he didn’t reveal that he was her

son. Out of compassion, the queen arranged for a space under the stairs

for him, where two servants brought food daily.

However, one servant was mean-spirited, wondering why the beggar should receive good food.

This servant either kept the food for himself or fed it to the

dogs, giving the starving beggar nothing but water.

On the other hand, the other servant was decent and brought the beggar his rations.

Though it was little, it sustained him for a while. During all this time,

he remained patient but grew weaker by the day.

As his health declined, he wished to receive the last sacrament.

During the mass, all the bells in the town and surrounding area began to ring on their own.

After the service, the priest approached the beggar, who lay there

lifeless. In one hand, he held a rose, and in the other, a lily, along with a paper that

contained his story.

When he was buried, a rose sprouted at one side of his grave, and a

title: Rapunzel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once there was a man and a woman who had long wished for a child.

Finally, the woman felt hopeful that God was about to grant her wish.

They had a small window at the back of their house that looked out onto a magnificent garden,

filled with the most beautiful flowers and herbs.

However, the garden was surrounded by a high wall, and no one dared to enter it because it belonged to an enchantress,

who was very powerful and feared by everyone.

One day, as the woman stood at her window gazing into the garden,

she spotted a bed filled with the most beautiful rampion—rapunzel. It looked so fresh and green that she craved it,

and her desire to eat some grew stronger every day.

Since she couldn’t get any, she started to waste away, becoming pale and miserable.

Her husband noticed her distress and asked, "What’s wrong, my dear?"

She replied, "If I can’t eat some of the rampion from the garden behind our house, I think I’ll die."

Her husband, who loved her deeply, thought, "I can’t let my wife die. I must get her some rampion, no matter the cost."

That evening, he climbed over the wall into the enchantress's garden, quickly grabbed a handful of rampion, and brought it back to his wife.

She immediately made herself a salad and ate it hungrily. It tasted so good to her—so incredibly good—that the next day she craved it three times as much.

If her husband wanted any peace, he had to go back to the garden.

So, in the twilight, he let himself down again. But when he reached the bottom, he was terrified to see the enchantress standing right in front of him.

"How dare you sneak into my garden and steal my rampion?" she demanded with an angry glare. "You will pay for this!"

"Please, have mercy," he pleaded. "I did it out of necessity. My wife looked at your rampion from our window and craved it so much that she would have died without it."

title: Rapunzel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Hearing this, the enchantress’s anger began to fade. "If what you say is true," she replied, "you may take as much rampion as you want, but I have one condition: you must give me the child your wife will have.

I will treat it well and care for it like my own child."

Terrified, the man agreed to her terms. When his wife gave birth, the enchantress appeared immediately,

named the child Rapunzel, and took her away.

Rapunzel grew up to be the most beautiful child in the world.

When she turned twelve, the enchantress locked her in a tall tower deep in the forest.

The tower had neither stairs nor doors, just a little window at the top.

Whenever the enchantress wanted to go in, she would stand beneath the window and call out, "Rapunzel!"

Rapunzel had magnificent long hair, as fine as spun gold, and when she heard the enchantress’s voice, she would unfasten her braided hair and let it down.

The enchantress would climb up using her hair.

After a year or two, a prince rode through the forest and passed by the tower.

He heard an enchanting song that made him stop and listen.

It was Rapunzel, singing sweetly in her solitude.

The prince wanted to reach her but couldn’t find the door, so he rode home, but the beautiful song lingered in his heart.

Every day, he returned to the forest just to listen to her sing.

One day, while he was hiding behind a tree, he saw the enchantress arrive and heard her call out.

Then Rapunzel let her braids down, and the enchantress climbed up.

"If that’s how she gets in, I’ll give it a try!" thought the prince.

The next evening, as the light faded, he went to the tower and called out.

Immediately, Rapunzel’s hair fell down, and the prince climbed up.

At first, Rapunzel was terrified when she saw a man, the likes of whom she had never seen before.

But the prince spoke to her like a friend, saying his heart had been so captivated by her that he had to meet her.

title: Rapunzel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Rapunzel relaxed, and when he asked if she would marry him, she noticed he was young and handsome.

"He will surely love me more than old Dame Gothel does," she thought, and she said yes, placing her hand in his.

"I’m willing to go with you, but I don’t know how to get down,

so bring a skein of silk with you every time you come, and I’ll weave a ladder with it.

Once it's ready, I’ll climb down and you can take me on your horse."

They agreed that he would visit her every evening since the enchantress came during the day.

The enchantress noticed nothing until one day, Rapunzel asked her, "Tell me, Dame Gothel, why is it harder for me to pull you up than the young prince? He climbs up in no time.

Ah! You wicked girl!" cried the enchantress. "What are you saying? I thought I had kept you away from everyone, yet you’ve deceived me."

In her fury, she grabbed Rapunzel’s beautiful hair, wrapped it around her hand, and with scissors in the other, snipped it off.

The lovely braids fell to the ground.

Without pity, she took poor Rapunzel to a desert where she had to live in great suffering.

On the same day she cast out Rapunzel, the enchantress attached the cut hair to the window’s hook.

When the prince came and called out, she let the hair down.

He climbed up, but instead of finding Rapunzel, he was faced with the enchantress, who looked at him with wicked glee.

"Ah, you came to find your beloved, but the beautiful bird is no longer singing in her nest.

The cat has caught her, and she’s lost to you forever!"

The prince was overcome with despair and in his anguish, jumped from the tower.

He survived but fell into thorn bushes that pierced his eyes.

Now blind, he wandered through the forest, living off roots and berries, lamenting the loss of his love.

He roamed in sorrow for many years until he eventually stumbled upon the desert where Rapunzel lived.

With her twins, a boy and a girl, she was living in hardship.

When she heard his voice, it sounded so familiar that she rushed toward it.

When she reached him, Rapunzel recognized him and fell into his arms, crying.

Her tears fell on his eyes, and suddenly he could see again.

He took her back to his kingdom, where they were joyfully welcomed, and they lived happily ever after.

title: Rumpelstiltskin
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once there was a poor miller who had a beautiful daughter.

One day, he went to talk to the king, and to seem important, he bragged.

He said to the king, 'I have a daughter who can spin straw into gold.'

The king replied, 'That sounds fascinating. If your daughter is as clever as you say, bring her to my palace tomorrow. I'll put her to the test.'

When the girl arrived, the king led her to a room filled with straw, handed her a spinning wheel and a reel, and declared, 'Now get to work! If you haven’t spun this straw into gold by tomorrow morning, you will die.'

Then he locked the door, leaving her alone. There sat the poor miller's daughter, utterly confused, with no idea how on earth to turn straw into gold. Her fear grew until she began to cry.

Suddenly, the door swung open, and in came a little man who asked, 'Good evening, Mistress Miller. Why are you crying?'

'Oh, I have to spin this straw into gold, and I don’t know how!' the girl replied.

'What will you give me if I do it for you?' asked the little man.

'I can give you my necklace,' she said.

The little man took the necklace, sat down at the wheel, and with a whirr, whirr, whirr, spun for three turns until the reel was full. Then he did it again, and again, until the morning came and all the straw was spun.

By daylight, the king arrived, and when he saw the gold, he was both amazed and delighted, but his greed only grew. He ordered the miller's daughter into a larger room full of straw and demanded that she spin it into gold overnight, or she would die.

Once again, the girl was at a loss, weeping, when the little man appeared again and said, 'What will you give me if I spin that straw into gold for you?'

'I can give you the ring on my finger,' she replied.

title: Rumpelstiltskin
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The little man took the ring and once more spun all the straw into shimmering gold by morning.

The king was overjoyed by the sight, but still wanted more gold, so he sent the miller's daughter into an even bigger room full of straw, threatening her to spin it that night or die.

He thought to himself, even though she is only a miller’s daughter, she would make me the richest husband in the world.

When left alone, the little man returned for the third time, asking, 'What will you give me if I spin the straw into gold for you this time?'

'I have nothing left to give,' the girl responded.

'Then promise me, if you become queen, to give me your first child.'

'Who knows if that will ever happen?' thought the miller's daughter, and feeling trapped, she agreed to his request.

He spun the straw into gold again.

The king came in the morning and found everything as he wished, and he married the lovely miller's daughter.

A year later, she gave birth to a beautiful child and completely forgot about the little man.

But then he entered her room and said, 'Now give me what you promised.'

The queen was horrified and offered the little man all the riches of the kingdom if he would spare her child.

But he firmly replied, 'No, something alive means more to me than all the treasures in the world.'

The queen was heartbroken and began to cry, so the little man took pity on her.

'I will give you three days,' he said, 'and if by the end, you can guess my name, you can keep your child.'

The queen spent the whole first night thinking of every name she had ever heard and sent out messengers across the land to find even more names.

The next day, when the little man appeared, she started guessing names: 'Is your name Caspar? No. Is it Melchior? No.'

'Maybe your name is Balthazar?' But each time, he said, 'That's not my name.'

On the second day, she asked for names from the people in her neighborhood, trying the most unusual and strange names she could come up with.

'Is your name Shortribs? Or Sheepshanks? Or Laceleg?' But he just replied, 'That's not my name.'

On the third day, her messenger returned and said, 'I couldn't find any new names. But while walking in the forest, I came across a tall mountain where a little house stood, and around the fire a tiny man was jumping and dancing, and he said, 'Today I'll spin gold for the young queen's child. Rumpelstiltskin is my name!.'

You can imagine how relieved the queen felt when she heard this name. Soon after, when the little man came in and asked, 'So, Mistress Queen, what is my name?' at first she said, 'Is it Conrad?' 'No.' 'Is it Harry?' 'No.'

'Perhaps it’s Rumpelstiltskin?' The little man screamed, 'The devil told you!' In his rage, he stomped so hard that his foot sunk into the ground, and pulling at his other leg with both hands, he lost his temper and vanished.

title: Sharing Joy and Sorrow
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once there was a tailor who was always looking for a fight, and

his wife, who was kind, hardworking, and devout, could never

make him happy. No matter what she did, he was never satisfied and

would complain, scold, and even hit her. Eventually,

the authorities caught wind of his behavior and summoned

him to face the consequences, sending him to prison to change his ways. He

was kept on a strict diet of bread and water for a while before being released. He

had to promise not to harm his wife again, and to live peacefully with her, sharing joy and

sorrow as a married couple should. For a time, things went well, but soon he fell back into his old habits and

became grumpy and argumentative again. Since he couldn’t hit her, he would grab her by the hair and pull it.

The woman managed to escape and dashed out into the yard, but he chased after her, yard-measure and scissors in hand,

throwing them at her along with anything else he could find. When he hit her, he laughed, and

when he missed, he yelled and cursed. This went on for so long that the neighbors came to the wife's aid. The tailor

was called back before the magistrates, who reminded him of his

promise. 'Gentlemen,' he said, 'I’ve kept my word! I haven’t hit her, I’ve just shared joy and sorrow with her.' 'How can that be?' the judge asked, noticing she kept coming in with such

serious complaints about him. 'I haven’t harmed her at all! She looked so unkempt that I just wanted to help comb her hair with

my hand. But she escaped from me in a huff. I chased after her to remind her of her duties, throwing whatever I could find just as a gentle nudge.' I’ve shared joy and sorrow with her too; every time I hit her, I was happy while she was sad, and when I missed her, she was happy while I was sad.' The judges were not convinced by his excuses and

decided to give him what he actually deserved.

title: Simeli Mountain
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there were two brothers: one was wealthy, while the other struggled to make ends meet.

The rich brother, however, never shared anything with the poor one, and he,

managed to scrape by selling corn, often doing so poorly enough that he couldn't provide bread for his wife and kids.

One day, while pushing a wheelbarrow through the forest, he spotted a large, bare mountain he had never seen before.

As he stood there, staring at the mountain, he noticed twelve wild men approaching him.

Believing they were robbers, he hurriedly shoved his barrow into the bushes, climbed a tree, and waited to see what would happen.

The twelve men approached the mountain and shouted, 'Semsi mountain, semsi mountain, open up!'

Immediately, the barren mountain split apart, allowing the twelve men to enter.

Once inside, the mountain closed behind them.

After a short while, it opened again, and the men emerged, carrying heavy sacks on their shoulders.

As they stepped back into the daylight, they said, 'Semsi mountain, semsi mountain, shut yourself,' and the mountain closed up once more.

With the entrance gone, the poor man climbed down from the tree, eager to discover what secrets the mountain held.

He approached it and said, 'Semsi mountain, semsi mountain, open up,' and the mountain opened for him too.

Inside, he found a cavern filled with silver and gold, and behind them lay huge piles of pearls and sparkling jewels, stacked like corn.

Overwhelmed, the poor man wasn't sure what to do or if he should take any of the treasures for himself.

Eventually, he filled his pockets with gold but left the pearls and precious stones untouched.

When he came back outside, he said, 'Semsi mountain, semsi mountain, shut yourself,' and the mountain closed up behind him as he returned home.

Now he had no reason to worry; with the gold, he could buy bread for his wife and kids, plus a little wine.

He lived happily and generously, helping the poor and doing good for everyone.

But when his money ran out, he went to his brother, borrowed a bushel measure, and brought back more, avoiding the most valuable items.

title: Simeli Mountain
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When he needed something for the third time, he borrowed the measure from his brother again.

The rich man had long been jealous of his brother's good fortune and couldn’t understand where it all came from or why his brother needed the measure.

He decided to play a trick, covered the bottom of the measure with pitch, and found a piece of gold stuck to it when it was returned.

He rushed to his brother and asked, 'What have you been measuring in this bushel measure?'

'Corn and barley,' replied the poor man.

Then the rich brother showed him the gold and threatened him, saying if he didn’t admit the truth, he would report him to the authorities.

The poor man recounted everything that had happened, just as it was.

So the rich man ordered his carriage to be readied and set off, intent on seizing better treasures than his brother had.

When he arrived at the mountain, he shouted, 'Semsi mountain, semsi mountain, open up!'

The mountain opened, and he stepped inside.

Before him lay the treasures, and for a while, he didn’t know what to take first.

Finally, he gathered as many precious stones as he could carry.

However, as he tried to leave, completely focused on his loot, he forgot the mountain's name and shouted, 'Simeli mountain, simeli mountain, open up.'

But that wasn’t the right name, and the mountain didn’t budge, remaining shut.

He started to panic, and the more he thought, the more confused he became; none of his treasures could help him.

In the evening, the mountain opened, and the twelve robbers appeared.

When they spotted him, they laughed, saying, 'Well, well, have we finally caught you! Did you think we wouldn’t notice you had been here before? This time, you won’t escape.'

title: St. Joseph in the Forest
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a mother with three daughters.

The oldest was rude and mean, the second was a bit better,

even though she had her faults, but the youngest was a kind, good

child. However, the mother was quite strange; she favored the eldest daughter,

and had little patience for the youngest.

As a result, she frequently sent the poor girl out into the vast forest,

thinking she would get lost and never find her way back.

But the guardian angel that every good child has was always there for her,

leading her back onto the right path.

One day, however, the guardian angel seemed to disappear,

and the child found herself completely lost in the forest.

She kept walking until evening fell, and then noticed a tiny light in the distance.

Excited, she hurried toward it and stumbled upon a little hut.

She knocked at the door, and it swung open, revealing a second door.

She knocked again, and an old man with a long, white beard opened it.

To her surprise, this was none other than St. Joseph.

He kindly said, "Come in, dear child, sit by my little chair by the fire.

Warm yourself. If you're thirsty, I can bring you some fresh water.

But I only have a couple of small roots here in the forest for you to eat."

St. Joseph handed her the roots. She cleaned them and then took out a pancake,

and the bread her mother had given her, mixed them in a pan,

and cooked herself a hearty soup.

When it was ready, St. Joseph said, "I'm very hungry, could I have some of your food?"

The girl was more than happy to share, giving him more than she kept for herself,

and miraculously, she found herself satisfied.

After they finished eating, St. Joseph announced, "Now it’s time for bed.

I only have one bed, so you'll sleep in it. I'll lie on the straw."

"No, please stay in your own bed," she replied.

"The straw is soft enough for me."

title: St. Joseph in the Forest
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But St. Joseph gently picked her up and carried her to the little bed.

There, she said her prayers and fell sound asleep.

The next morning, when she woke up, she wanted to greet St. Joseph,

but he was nowhere to be seen.

She got out of bed and searched for him, but he was gone.

Eventually, she noticed a bag behind the door,

filled with money that was so heavy she could barely carry it.

There was a note that said it was for the child who had slept there that night.

Excited, she took the bag and hurried back to her mother.

As she handed her mother all the money, she couldn't help but feel pleased.

The following day, the second daughter wanted to explore the forest too.

Her mother gave her a bigger piece of pancake and more bread.

Just like the first daughter, she ended up at St. Joseph’s hut.

He offered her roots for a thick soup, and when it was done,

he said, "I'm hungry; please share some of your food."

The second daughter replied, "You can have your share after I'm finished."

She ate nearly all of it, leaving St. Joseph to clean the dish.

Later, when he offered her his bed, wanting to lie on the straw,

she said, "No, there's enough space for both of us in the bed."

St. Joseph picked her up and placed her gently in the bed,

then laid down on the straw.

The next morning, when she woke up, she looked for St. Joseph,

but he was gone. However, she found a small sack of money behind the door,

about the size of her hand, with a note saying it was for the child who had slept there last night.

She took the bag and rushed home, but secretly kept two pieces for herself.

By then, the oldest daughter had become curious about what the others had received,

and insisted on going into the forest the next morning.

Her mother provided her with as many pancakes as she wanted, along with bread and cheese.

In the evening, she arrived at St. Joseph's hut just like her sisters.

title: St. Joseph in the Forest
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When St. Joseph said, "I'm hungry; can you share some food with me?"

the girl responded, "Wait until I'm full, and then you can have whatever's left."

She nearly devoured everything, leaving St. Joseph to clean the plate.

Then, when he offered her his bed and requested to sleep on the straw,

she accepted without hesitation, snuggling into the little bed.

The next morning, she awoke without seeing St. Joseph, but that didn't bother her.

She searched behind the door for a money bag, expecting to find something.

But as she bent down to examine what was on the ground, she accidentally touched it with her nose,

and to her horror, it turned out to be a second nose that was now stuck to hers.

Panicking, she began to scream, but her cries changed nothing.

She couldn't escape the sight of her elongated nose.

In a frenzy, she dashed out and screamed until she found St. Joseph;

she fell at his feet, begging him to help her.

Out of sympathy, he removed the extra nose and even gave her two pennies.

When she got home, her mother was waiting at the door and asked,

"What did you get?"

The eldest daughter lied, saying she received a big bag of money but lost it on the way.

"Lost it?" cried her mother. "Oh, we’ll find it right away!"

She took her by the hand and insisted on searching together.

At first, the girl cried and didn't want to go, but eventually, she agreed.

However, along the way, swarms of lizards and snakes appeared,

and they had no idea how to escape.

In the end, they stung the wicked daughter to death,

and they also stung the mother in the foot because she hadn’t brought her daughter back sooner.

title: Stories About Snakes
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a little girl whose mother gave her a small bowl of milk and bread every afternoon.

The girl would sit in the yard with her meal.

But as she started to eat,

a little toad came creeping out from a crack in the wall, dipped its head into her dish, and joined her for lunch.

The girl found joy in this, and whenever she waited for her little guest and it didn’t show up right away, she would call out,

"You’ll get your bread crumbs, don’t worry, and you can drink your milk too!"

Then, the toad would hurry over and enjoy its meal.

It even showed its appreciation by bringing her lovely gifts from its secret stash—shiny stones, pearls, and golden trinkets.

However, the toad only drank the milk and ignored the bread crumbs.

One day, the girl took her little spoon and gently tapped the toad on the head, saying, "You should eat the bread crumbs too, little buddy."

Her mother, who was in the kitchen, heard her talking to someone.

When she ran outside and saw her daughter tapping a toad with the spoon, she rushed out with a wooden log and killed the poor creature.

From that moment on, everything changed for the girl.

As long as the toad had eaten with her, she had grown tall and strong, but now she lost her pretty, rosy cheeks and started to decline.

It wasn’t long before the mourning bird began to cry at night, and the robin started gathering twigs and leaves for a funeral wreath; shortly after, the girl lay on her bier.

An orphan girl was sitting by the town walls spinning thread when she spotted a toad emerging from a hole low in the wall.

Swiftly, she laid out one of the blue silk handkerchiefs that toads love to crawl on.

As soon as the toad saw it, it went back for a moment, then returned, bringing a small golden crown,

placed it on the handkerchief, and then hopped away again.

The girl picked up the crown, which sparkled and was intricately crafted from fine gold.

Before long, the toad came back again, but when it couldn’t find the crown, it crawled up to the wall and, in its sorrow, banged its little head against it with all its might until it finally lay there lifeless.

If the girl had just left the crown where it was, the toad would have surely returned with even more treasures.

The toad croaked, "Huhu, huhu." The girl called out, "Come out!" The toad appeared, and the girl asked about her little sister, saying, "Have you seen little Red-Stockings?" The toad replied, "No, I haven’t. Have you? Huhu, huhu, huhu."

title: Strong Hans
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, in a quiet valley, there lived a man and a woman with their only child.

They spent their days in solitude.

One day, the mother went into the woods to collect fir branches and took her little Hans, who was just two years old, along.

Since it was spring and the child was fascinated by the colorful flowers, she wandered deeper into the forest with him.

Suddenly, two robbers jumped out from the bushes, grabbed the mother and child, and carried them deep into the dark forest, a place no one ever returned from.

The poor woman begged the robbers to let her and her child go, but their hearts were made of stone; they ignored her pleas and forced her to move further.

After navigating through thickets and brambles for about two miles, they arrived at a rock with a door.

The robbers knocked, and the door opened immediately.

They had to pass through a long, dark tunnel lit by a fire.

Swords, sabers, and other deadly weapons hung on the walls, gleaming in the light, while at a large table in the center sat four other robbers, gambling, and their captain took his place at the head.

As soon as he saw the woman, he approached her and assured her not to be afraid; they wouldn’t harm her, but she would need to take care of the household chores.

He told her that if she kept everything in order, she would be treated well.

Then they gave her something to eat and showed her a bed where she could sleep with her child.

The woman lived with the robbers for many years as Hans grew strong and tall.

His mother entertained him with stories and taught him to read an old book of knight tales she found in the cave.

When Hans turned nine, he fashioned a sturdy club from a fir branch, hid it behind his bed, and went to his mother asking, "Dear mother, please tell me who my father is. I must know!"

His mother fell silent, knowing that she couldn’t reveal this; she wanted to spare him from longing for home.

Additionally, she feared the ruthless robbers would never let him leave, and it broke her heart to think he might never meet his father.

title: Strong Hans
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One night, when the robbers returned from their thieving, Hans pulled out his club and stood before the captain.

“I demand to know who my father is, and if you don’t tell me now, I will bring you down!” he declared.

The captain just laughed and slapped him so hard that Hans fell to the ground.

Undeterred, Hans got back up and thought, "I’ll wait another year, and then try again. I’m sure I’ll succeed next time."

When a year had passed, he took out his club once more, brushed it off, examined it closely, and said, "This is a strong club."

That night, when the robbers were home and filled with wine, making their heads heavy, Hans went up to the captain again.

“Tell me who my father is!” he demanded again.

Once more, the captain slapped him, sending him under the table.

But Hans wasted no time; he quickly stood back up and, with his club, knocked the captain and the other robbers down until they couldn’t move.

His mother watched from the corner, full of admiration for his bravery and strength.

Once he finished subduing the robbers, he turned to his mother.

“Now that I’ve proven myself, I must know who my father is.”

“Dear Hans,” replied his mother, “let’s go find him.”

She took the key from the captain and Hans packed a large sack with gold, silver, and anything else beautiful he could find until it was full.

Then he hoisted it onto his back.

As they left the cave, Hans was amazed by the sight of the bright forest, colorful flowers, and singing birds outside, basking in the sunlight.

He stood there, enchanted by the beauty, feeling as if he had stepped into a dream.

His mother found the path home, and after walking for a couple of hours, they finally arrived back in their cozy valley and reached their little house.

The father was sitting by the door, and tears of joy filled his eyes as he recognized his wife and learned that Hans was his son, believing them both to be long gone.

title: Strong Hans
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Although Hans was not yet twelve, he was a whole head taller than his father.

They entered the little room together, but as soon as Hans placed his sack on the bench by the stove, the whole place began to creak.

The bench collapsed, followed by the floor, and the hefty sack dropped down into the cellar.

"Oh no!" cried the father, "What was that? You've ruined our little house!"

"Don’t worry, dear father," Hans replied. "Within that sack is more than enough to build a new house!"

Immediately, father and son got to work building a new house, buying cattle and land to start their farm.

Hans plowed the fields, and with one push of the plow, the oxen barely had to pull.

The next spring, Hans told his father to save all the money and make him a walking stick that weighed a hundred pounds so he could travel.

Once the stick was ready, he left his father's house and soon found himself in a deep, dark forest.

There he heard crunchy sounds and saw a fir tree twisted from bottom to top, and up in the tree was a strong man twisting it like it was nothing.

"Hey there!" Hans called. "What are you doing up there?"

The man replied, "I gathered some branches yesterday and am twisting them into rope."

"Sounds great!" thought Hans. "You must be pretty strong. Come down and travel with me."

The man climbed down and Hans realized he was a full head taller, and Hans was no shorty himself.

"From now on, you’ll be called Fir-Twister," Hans declared.

They continued their journey together when they heard loud banging, so powerful the ground shivered.

Soon, they came across a huge rock where a giant was smashing off chunks away with his fists.

When Hans asked what he was doing, the giant said, "At night, bears and wolves disturb my sleep, so I’m building a shelter to find peace."

"I can use you too!" Hans thought and told the giant, "Forget that house; come with me! You shall be called Rock-Splitter!"

title: Strong Hans
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The giant agreed, and the trio ventured through the forest, scaring away all the wild beasts.

Eventually, they arrived at an old, abandoned castle and decided to rest in the hall.

The next morning, Hans wandered into the garden, which had become overgrown with thorns.

While exploring, a wild boar charged at him, but he swung his club and knocked it out cold.

They carried it inside, cooked it, and shared a huge feast.

They decided that every day, two of them would go hunt while one stayed back to prepare enough meat for all.

Fir-Twister was assigned to cook first, while Hans and Rock-Splitter went hunting.

While Fir-Twister was busy cooking, a tiny, scrappy old man appeared and asked for some meat.

"Scram, you pesky little imp! You don't need any meat!" Fir-Twister snapped.

To his shock, the little dwarf launched at him, punching him until he was gasping for breath.

The dwarf didn’t stop until he had enjoyed venting his anger.

When the others returned from hunting, Fir-Twister kept quiet about the incident, and thought, "Let them try their luck with the little guy. I’m sure they’ll fare just as badly."

The next day, Rock-Splitter stayed home, but he faced the same treatment from the dwarf for refusing to share food.

When Hans and Fir-Twister got back, they could see Rock-Splitter's fight bruises but chose to remain silent, thinking, "Hans will find out for himself tomorrow."

The next day, Hans cooked as well as he could, and when the dwarf came demanding meat, he thought, "This poor fellow must be hungry, I'll share some of mine so the others won’t go without."

Hans handed him a piece, and after devouring it, the dwarf demanded more.

"That’s generous of me; keep it down, little guy!" said Hans.

But the dwarf asked again for a third piece.

"You are really pushing it!" Hans said, finally refusing him any more food.

The little dwarf jumped at him, ready to punish him as he had done to Fir-Twister and Rock-Splitter.

title: Strong Hans
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But Hans wasn’t easy prey; he landed a few solid punches that made the dwarf tumble down the castle steps.

Thinking to chase the dwarf, Hans tripped and fell flat on his face.

When he stood up, the dwarf was already far ahead; Hans chased him to the forest, where he watched him disappear into a crack in the rock.

Hans returned home but made a mental note of the spot.

When his friends came back, they were surprised by how well he had fared.

He recounted his encounter and then laughed about how they'd let themselves get beaten by the dwarf.

"It’s shameful for you big guys to be scared of a little guy like that!" he teased.

They took a basket and a rope and all three headed back to the dwarf's hole.

They lowered Hans and his club in the basket.

Reaching the bottom, he discovered a door, and inside sat a beautiful maiden, lovelier than any picture, who looked so sad.

Beside her was the dwarf, grinning at Hans like a wicked cat.

Feeling pity for the maiden, Hans decided to save her and struck the dwarf with his club, killing him instantly.

The moment the dwarf fell, the chains binding the maiden fell away, and Hans was mesmerized by her beauty.

She explained that she was a princess, captured by a savage count who imprisoned her here because she refused to marry him.

The count had set the dwarf as her jailer, and he’d made her life miserable.

Hans carefully placed the maiden in the basket and had her hoisted up.

Once the basket reached the top, it came back down, but Hans wasn’t sure if he could trust his two friends.

"What if they have plans against me?" he thought.

While the basket was halfway up, they let it drop, and had he been in it, he would have been finished.

Now stuck below, Hans felt hopeless.
