As he paced back and forth, he noticed the ring on the dwarf's finger that sparkled beautifully.

Calling upon his strength, Hans pulled the ring off and slipped it onto his finger.

Suddenly, he heard a rustling above him.

Spirits of the air appeared, announcing that he was now their master and asking for his wish.

Amazed, Hans finally told them to lift him back up.

They obeyed instantly, almost as if he were flying.

But when he reached the surface, no one was there.

His companions had fled, taking the beautiful maiden with them.

Hans again turned the ring, and the spirits revealed that the two were at sea.

He ran as fast as he could to the shore and saw a small boat far out on the water.

His traitorous friends were aboard, and without thinking, he jumped into the water, club in hand.

But the heavy club pulled him deep underwater, almost drowning him.

In the nick of time, he used the ring again, and the spirits whisked him up into the boat.

He swung his club, gave his treacherous friends what they deserved, and tossed them into the water.

Hans then sailed back to shore with the beautiful maiden, who was terrified but grateful, and returned her to her family where they celebrated their reunion.

title: Sweet Porridge
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a kind but poor little girl who lived with her mother.

They had run out of food, so the girl ventured into the forest.

In the woods, she encountered an elderly woman who understood her troubles.

The woman handed her a little pot and said,

"Cook, little pot, cook!" and the pot cooked delicious, sweet porridge for her.

To stop the cooking, she simply had to say, "Stop, little pot." The girl took the pot home.

With the pot, they were free from hunger and poverty, enjoying sweet porridge whenever they wanted.

One day, while the girl was out, her mother said, "Cook, little pot, cook!"

The pot started cooking, and the mother ate until she was full.

But when she tried to make it stop, she didn’t know the right words.

So the pot kept cooking, and the porridge spilled over the edges, flooding the kitchen.

Soon, the whole house was filled with porridge, and then it spread to the next house, and even the entire street!

It seemed as if the pot wanted to feed the whole world, causing chaos everywhere.

Nobody knew how to stop the endless flow of porridge.

Finally, when only one house was left, the girl returned home.

She simply said, "Stop, little pot," and the pot stopped cooking.

Anyone who wanted to return to town had to eat their way back through the porridge.

title: Sweetheart Roland
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there lived a woman who was truly a witch, and she had two daughters.

One was ugly and cruel, and the witch loved her because she was her own flesh and blood.

The other, her stepdaughter, was beautiful and kind, and the witch despised her.

The stepdaughter had a lovely apron that the witch's daughter coveted so much that it made her jealous.

She told her mother that she simply had to have that apron.

"Don't worry, my child," the old woman replied. "You'll get it. Your step-sister deserves to die, and tonight while she sleeps, I’ll come and take her head off.

Just make sure you're on the far side of the bed and push her towards the front." The poor girl happened to be standing in a corner and overheard everything.

Frightened, she stayed inside all day. At bedtime, the witch's daughter climbed into bed first to take the far side.

But once she was asleep, the stepdaughter gently nudged her to the front and took her place at the back, close to the wall.

In the middle of the night, the old woman crept in, axe in hand, and felt around to see if anyone was outside.

Then she grabbed the axe with both hands and struck.

After she left, the girl got up and rushed to her sweetheart, Roland, knocking at his door.

When he came out, she said, "Listen, dear Roland, we need to get away quickly.

My stepmother wanted to kill me, but ended up hurting her own child. If she realizes what she's done by daybreak, we’ll be in trouble."

"First, let's take her magic wand," Roland suggested, "or we can't escape if she chases us."

The girl grabbed the wand and left a few drops of blood on the ground: one in front of the bed, one in the kitchen, and one on the stairs.

Then she rushed away with Roland.

When the witch woke up the next morning, she called for her daughter, eager to give her the apron, but she didn't come.

title: Sweetheart Roland
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Where are you?" cried the witch.

"I'm here on the stairs, sweeping," answered the first drop of blood.

The old woman went outside but saw no one and yelled again, "Where are you?"

"I'm in the kitchen, keeping warm," called the second drop of blood.

She entered the kitchen but found nothing.

Then she cried once more, "Where are you?"

"Ah, I’m here in bed, sleeping," replied the third drop of blood.

She went into the room, and what did she see? Her very own child, beheaded and lying in a pool of blood.

The witch flew into a rage and rushed to the window. She looked out and spotted her stepdaughter hurrying away with Roland.

"You won’t get away with this," she shouted. "Even if you're far away, you’re still not safe from me."

She put on her special boots that allowed her to cover an hour's distance in a single step, and it didn’t take long to catch up with them.

Seeing the old woman coming towards them, the girl quickly used her magic wand to turn Roland into a lake and herself into a duck swimming in it.

The witch sat by the shore, tossed breadcrumbs into the water, and tried to lure the duck to her, but the duck wasn’t fooled.

The old woman eventually had to leave empty-handed.

As night fell, the girl and Roland resumed their normal forms and walked through the night until dawn.

When morning came, the girl transformed into a beautiful flower in a thorny hedge, and Roland became a fiddler.

It wasn’t long before the witch appeared, approaching the musician.

"Dear musician, may I pick that beautiful flower?" she asked.

"Sure, go ahead," he replied. "I'll play for you while you do."

As she bent down to pluck the flower, he began to play, and whether she liked it or not, she had to dance to the magical tune.

The faster he played, the more wildly she danced, her clothes ripped on the thorns, and they pricked her until she bled.

title: Sweetheart Roland
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Without stopping, she danced until she collapsed, lifeless on the ground.

Finally free, Roland said, "Now I'll go to my father and prepare for our wedding."

"I'll wait here for you," the girl replied. "To keep my identity hidden, I’ll turn into a red stone marker."

So Roland left, and the girl turned into a red marker in the field, waiting for him.

But when Roland got home, he got tangled up with another girl who captivated him, making him forget all about his beloved.

The poor girl waited for a long time. But when Roland still didn’t return, she became saddened and transformed herself into a flower, hoping someone might come by.

It just so happened that a shepherd was grazing his sheep in the field and noticed the flower.

Finding it so pretty, he plucked it and put it in his chest.

From that day on, strange things began happening in the shepherd’s house.

Every morning, when he woke up, all the chores were done; the room was clean, the table set, the fire lit, and the water fetched.

He couldn’t figure out how this was happening since he never saw anyone in his home.

While pleased, he became so anxious that he sought the advice of a wise woman.

She told him there was some magic involved and recommended he listen carefully one morning and cover whatever he saw with a white cloth.

The shepherd followed her advice, and the next morning, just as dawn broke, he saw the chest open, and the flower emerge.

Quickly, he dashed over and threw the white cloth over it.

Suddenly, the transformation was broken, and a beautiful girl stood before him.

She revealed that she had been the flower and had been helping him all along.

Impressed, he asked if she would marry him, but she said no, insisting she remain loyal to Roland despite his abandonment.

However, she promised to continue helping the shepherd.

As the day approached for Roland’s wedding, it was customary for all the girls to attend and sing for the couple.

When the faithful maiden heard this, she felt so heartbroken that she thought she might shatter, and initially refused to go.

But the other girls convinced her to join.

When it came time for her to sing, she hesitated until she was the last one standing.

At that point, she couldn’t refuse.

As soon as she began to sing, Roland sprang up and exclaimed, "I know that voice! That’s my true bride, and I want no one else!"

All he had forgotten and lost came rushing back to him.

Then the devoted maiden married her sweetheart Roland, ending her sorrow and beginning her happiness.

title: The Aged Mother
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
In a bustling town, there lived an old woman who often spent her evenings alone.

In her quiet room, she reflected on her life, mourning the loss of her husband first,

then both of her children, followed by all her relatives,

and that very day, she had lost her last friend, leaving her completely

alone and heartbroken. She felt a deep sadness, and the loss of her sons weighed the heaviest,

causing her to feel bitterness towards God in her sorrow.

As she sat there, lost in her thoughts, she suddenly heard the bells ringing for early prayer.

Surprised to realize that she had let her grief consume the entire night,

she lit her lantern and made her way to the church.

By the time she arrived, the church was already illuminated,

but not with the usual bright glow of wax candles; it had a dim light.

The place was also packed with people, and every seat was taken.

When the old woman reached her usual spot, she found it occupied too.

The entire bench was filled with others.

As she looked around, she realized those sitting there were none other than her deceased relatives,

clothed in their old-fashioned attire, but their faces were pale.

They didn’t speak or sing; instead, a gentle humming and whispering filled the air.

Then one of her aunts got up, walked forward, and said to the grieving old woman,

'Look there by the altar, and you’ll see your sons.'

The old woman glanced over and saw her two children—one hanging from the gallows,

the other tied to a wheel.

Her aunt then said, 'See? This is what would have happened to them if they had lived,

and if God had not taken them when they were innocent children.'

Shaken and filled with gratitude, the old woman returned home,

and on her knees, she thanked God for being kinder to her than she had ever realized.

On the third day, she lay down.

title: The Beam
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a sorcerer standing among a huge crowd, showcasing his amazing tricks.

He had brought in a rooster that lifted a heavy beam as if it were as light as a feather.

However, a girl in the crowd had just discovered a four-leaf clover, which made her so wise that no trick could fool her.

She saw that the beam was nothing more than a straw.

So she called out, 'Hey everyone, can’t you see that the rooster is carrying a straw, not a beam?'

As soon as she spoke, the magic faded away, and the crowd realized the truth, chasing the magician away in shame.

But the sorcerer, filled with inner fury, vowed, 'I will get my revenge soon.'

Time passed, and the girl’s wedding day arrived. She was elegantly dressed and walked in a grand procession across the fields toward the church.

Suddenly, she came across a stream that was swollen, with no bridge or plank to cross.

Quickly, the bride lifted her dress and prepared to wade through.

Just as she was standing in the water, a man—none other than the sorcerer—mockingly exclaimed beside her, 'Aha! Where are your eyes to think that’s water?'

With that, she opened her eyes and realized she was standing in the middle of a field filled with vibrant blue flax flowers.

The crowd saw her too and burst into laughter and ridicule, sending her away.

title: The Bittern and the Hoopoe
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Where's your favorite spot to feed your flocks?" asked a man.

The old cowherd replied, "Here, sir, where the grass isn’t too rich or too poor; otherwise, it doesn’t work out."

"Why is that?" the man inquired.

"Do you hear that sad sound from the meadow over there?" answered the cowherd. "That’s the bittern."

"He used to be a cowherd, just like the hoopoe. Let me tell you their story."

The bittern grazed his cows in lush green meadows filled with flowers, but his cows became wild and unruly.

In contrast, the hoopoe sent his cattle up to the high, barren hills, where the wind stirred the sand, leaving his cows weak and skinny.

When evening came and it was time to bring the cows home, the bittern couldn’t gather his herd again.

They were too spirited and ran away from him.

"Come, cows, come!" he called, but it was no use.

They ignored his calls completely.

The hoopoe, on the other hand, faced a different problem; his cows were too weak to even stand up.

"Up, up, up!" he cried, but it was pointless; they just lay there on the sand.

"That’s what happens when you lack moderation. Even now,"

"though they don’t have flocks to tend anymore, the bittern still cries, 'Come, cows, come!' and the hoopoe shouts, 'Up, up, up!'"

title: The Blue Light
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a soldier who had served his king with loyalty for many years.

However, when the war ended, he could no longer serve due to the many injuries he had sustained.

The king told him, "You can go home now; I don’t need you any longer, and you won’t receive any more pay, as only those who serve me earn wages."

The soldier was troubled, unsure how he would make a living, and wandered through the day until he eventually entered a forest.

As night fell, he saw a light shining in the distance and followed it to a house where a witch lived.

He approached her and said, "Please give me a place to stay for the night, and some food and drink, or I will starve."

"Oh, who would help a runaway soldier?" she retorted, "But since I'm feeling generous, I’ll take you in if you do something for me."

"What do you need?" asked the soldier.

"I want you to dig around my garden tomorrow," she replied.

The soldier agreed, and the next day he worked hard, yet he was unable to finish by nightfall.

"I can see you can’t do any more today," said the witch, "but I’ll let you stay another night. In return, you must chop wood for me tomorrow."

He spent the entire day chopping wood, and by evening, the witch proposed he stay for one more night.

"Tomorrow, all I need is for you to retrieve a small blue light that has fallen into an old well behind my house—it never goes out. Bring it to me."

The next day, the witch took him to the well and lowered him down in a basket.

He found the blue light and signaled her to pull him back up.

As he approached the edge, she reached down and tried to snatch the blue light from him.

"No," he said, sensing her treachery. "I won’t give you the light until I’m safely on the ground."

title: The Blue Light
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Furious, the witch let him drop back into the well.

Fortunately, he landed unharmed on the damp ground, but the blue light continued to burn—useless to him.

He realized he couldn’t escape death and sat there, feeling very sad.

Then he suddenly checked his pocket and found his half-full tobacco pipe.

"This will be my last pleasure," he thought, pulling it out, lighting it with the blue light, and starting to smoke.

As the smoke filled the cavern, a little black dwarf suddenly appeared and asked, "What can I do for you, my lord?"

"What can you do?" replied the soldier, surprised.

"I must do whatever you command," the little man replied.

"Great," said the soldier, "first, help me out of this well."

The little man took his hand and led him through an underground tunnel, and he didn’t forget to bring the blue light along.

As they traveled, the dwarf showed him the treasures the witch had hidden.

The soldier took as much gold as he could carry.

Once they reached the surface, he said to the dwarf, "Now go bind the witch and bring her to me."

Soon enough, she came rushing in on a wild tomcat, screaming in fury.

Before long, the dwarf returned and said, "It's done. The witch is already hanging from the gallows. What else do you need, my lord?"

"For now, nothing," replied the soldier. "You can go home but be ready if I call for you."

"All you need to do is light your pipe with the blue light, and I’ll come instantly."

Then the dwarf vanished.

The soldier returned to his town, went to a fine inn, ordered beautiful clothes, and had the landlord prepare a fancy room for him.

Once settled in, he called on the little black dwarf and explained, "I’ve served the king faithfully, but he has cast me aside and left me in need. Now, I want my revenge."

"What should I do?" asked the little man.

title: The Blue Light
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Tonight, when the princess is asleep, bring her to me; she’ll work as my servant."

"I can do that easily, but it’s dangerous for you if you get caught," warned the dwarf.

At midnight, the door swung open, and the dwarf carried in the princess.

"Aha, you're here!" shouted the soldier. "Get to work! Grab a broom and sweep the room!"

After she had done that, he commanded her to come to him.

He stretched out his feet and said, "Now, take off my boots," then tossed them at her face, making her pick them up and clean them.

Despite everything, she did as he asked without complaint, quietly and with her eyes half-shut.

When the rooster crowed, the dwarf returned her to the royal palace and laid her in her bed.

The next morning, the princess went to her father and recounted a strange dream.

"I was carried through the streets at lightning speed and taken to a soldier's room where I had to serve him, clean and sweep. It felt real, and I'm just as tired as if I had worked for real!"

"That dream might have been real," said the king. "Here’s my advice: fill your pocket with peas and make a small hole in it. If you're taken again, the peas will fall out and leave a trail."

Unbeknownst to the king, the little dwarf was right beside him when he said this and overheard the entire plan.

That night, as the sleeping princess was taken through the streets again, some peas did fall from her pocket, but they didn’t leave a trail because the clever dwarf had scattered peas on every street beforehand.

Once again, the princess was forced to serve until the rooster crowed.

The next morning, the king sent his men to find the trail, but it was useless, as children were sitting everywhere picking up peas, saying, "It must have rained peas last night!"

"We need another plan," the king said. "Keep your shoes on when going to bed, and before you come back, hide one. I’ll figure it out from there."

title: The Blue Light
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The little dwarf heard this new scheme, and that night when the soldier ordered him to bring the princess again, he revealed it to him.

"I don't know how to counter this plan. If your shoe is found at your house, it will go badly for you," he cautioned.

"Do as I say," the soldier replied. And once more, the princess had to work as his servant, but before she left, she hid her shoe under the bed.

The next morning, the king ordered a search for his daughter's shoe throughout the entire town.

It was discovered at the soldier's place, and he was brought back to prison after he had gone outside the gate at the dwarf’s request.

In his hasty escape, he had forgotten all his valuables—the blue light and the gold—and had just one ducat in his pocket.

Now, chained and locked up, he stood at his dungeon window when he spotted a fellow soldier passing by.

He tapped on the glass and said, "Please, could you fetch me that small bundle from the inn, and I’ll give you a reward for your trouble."

His comrade ran to the inn and returned with the bundle.

Once he was alone again, he lit his pipe and called for the little dwarf.

"Don’t worry," said the dwarf. "Wherever they take you, go along and let them do as they please, but make sure to keep the blue light with you."

The next day, he was put on trial, and even though he hadn’t done anything wrong, the judge sentenced him to death.

As they led him to his execution, he asked the king for one last favor.

"What is it?" asked the king.

"May I smoke one last pipe on my way?"

"You can smoke three pipes," replied the king, "but don't think I’ll spare your life."

So the soldier pulled out his pipe, lit it with the blue light, and as a few puffs of smoke rose up, the dwarf appeared with a small club in his hand and asked, "What does my lord command?"

"Take down that dishonest judge and the constable, and don't spare the king who has treated me so poorly!"

Without hesitation, the dwarf struck down everyone he touched, and they fell to the ground, unable to move.

The king trembled and begged for mercy, offering the soldier his kingdom as a price just to stay alive.

title: The Boots of Buffalo-Leather
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
There was once a soldier who wasn't afraid of anything, yet he worried about.

nothing at all. He had just been discharged from service,

and since he didn't know any trade and couldn't earn a living, he wandered around asking for help from kind-hearted folks.

He wore an old raincoat on his back and had a pair of buffalo-leather riding boots that were all he had left.

One day, he was wandering aimlessly, out in the open landscape, and eventually stumbled upon a forest.

He was unsure of where he was, but he noticed a well-dressed man wearing a green hunting coat sitting on a fallen tree.

The soldier shook hands with him, sat down on the grass beside him, and stretched out his legs.

'You've got nice, polished boots,' he told the huntsman, 'but if you had to walk around like I do, they wouldn't last long. Just look at mine; they're made of buffalo-leather and have been worn for ages, yet they take me anywhere.'

After a little while, the soldier stood up and said, 'I can't stay here any longer; hunger is pushing me onward. But, my friend in bright boots, where does this road lead?'

'I couldn't tell you,' the huntsman replied. 'I’ve lost my way in the forest.'

'Then we’re in the same boat,' said the soldier. 'Birds of a feather flock together. Let’s stick together and find our way out.'

The huntsman smiled slightly, and they continued walking deeper into the woods until night fell.

'It looks like we aren’t going to find our way out of this forest,' the soldier remarked, 'but there’s a light shining in the distance. Maybe we can find something to eat there.'

They walked to a stone house, knocked on the door, and an old woman answered.

'We’re looking for a place to spend the night and a little food for our empty stomachs,' said the soldier.

'You can’t stay here,' the old woman warned. 'This is a robbers’ house, and it would be wise to leave now before they return, or you'll be in trouble.'

title: The Boots of Buffalo-Leather
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
'It can’t be worse than starving, which is what will happen to me if I stay in the woods any longer,' the soldier replied. 'I’m going in.'

The huntsman hesitated but the soldier pulled him in by the sleeve. 'Come on, my dear brother. We won’t be escaping this easily.'

The old woman felt sorry for them and said, 'Crouch down behind the stove, and if they leave you anything, I’ll sneak it to you while they’re sleeping.'

Barely had they settled in the corner when twelve robbers burst in, sat at the table that was already set, and loudly demanded food.

The old woman brought out large platters of roast meat, which the robbers devoured eagerly.

When the soldier caught a whiff of the food, he told the huntsman, 'I can't hold out any longer. I'm going to sit at the table and eat with them.'

'You’ll get us killed,' the huntsman protested, grabbing his arm.

But the soldier began to cough loudly.

Hearing this, the robbers dropped their knives and forks, jumped up, and discovered the two hiding behind the stove.

'Aha! What do we have here?' they shouted. 'What are you doing here? Were you sent as spies? Wait, and we'll take care of you.'

'Please be civilized,' the soldier said. 'I’m starving. Give me something to eat, and then you can do whatever you want with me.'

The robbers were taken aback, and the leader said, 'You clearly have no fear. Well, you’ll get some food, but afterward, you’re done for.'

'We’ll see about that,' said the soldier as he sat down at the table and began to chow down on the roast meat.

'Brother bright boots, come and eat!' he called to the huntsman. 'You must be just as hungry as I am and won't find better roast at home,' but the huntsman still refused.

The robbers couldn’t believe their eyes as they watched the soldier at the table without a care.

title: The Boots of Buffalo-Leather
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
'This guy shows no manners,' they whispered.

'I’ve had enough food for now; can I get something good to drink?' the soldier said.

The robber chief, in a generous mood, called out to the old woman, 'Bring a bottle from the cellar, and make sure it's the best you have.'

The soldier pulled the cork from the bottle with a loud pop and then walked over to the huntsman.

'Watch this, brother, and you’ll see something that’ll amaze you. I’m going to toast to all of them.'

He raised the bottle above the robber's heads and shouted, 'Long life to you all!' then took a hearty drink.

As soon as he finished speaking, they all froze as if turned to stone, their mouths open and hands raised.

'You really know some tricks,' the huntsman commented. 'Now let’s go home.'

'Oh, my dear brother, it’s too soon for that. We've defeated the enemy and must first gather our spoils. These guys are stuck, and they won’t be moving until I let them.'

'Come, eat and drink!' The old woman had to bring another bottle of fine wine, and the soldier wouldn’t move until he had enough to last him three days.

Finally, when morning arrived, he declared, 'It’s time to pack up, and to make our journey quicker, the old woman will show us the shortest route to town.'

Once they arrived, he went to see his old companions and said, 'I found a nest of robbers in the forest; come with me, and we can take care of them.'

The soldier guided them back, telling the huntsman, 'You must come with me to see how they squirm when we catch them by the feet.'

He positioned his friends around the robbers and then raised the bottle.

'Long life to you all!' he called out.

In an instant, they regained their mobility but were quickly overpowered and tied up.

The soldier ordered them to be tossed into a cart like sacks.

'Now take them straight to prison.' The huntsman, however, pulled one robber aside.

'Brother bright boots,' said the soldier, 'we’ve defeated the enemy and eaten well. Let’s walk behind them like we’re lagging behind.'

As they approached the town, the soldier saw a crowd streaming through the gate, cheering and waving green branches.

Then he noticed the royal guard was coming.

'What’s going on?' he asked the huntsman.

'Don’t you know? The king has been away from the kingdom for a long time, and today he’s coming back; everyone is out to greet him.'

'But where’s the king?' the soldier inquired. 'I don’t see him anywhere.'

'Here he is,' replied the huntsman, 'I am the king, and I’m announcing my arrival.'

He opened his hunting coat, revealing his royal garments.

The soldier gasped, dropping to his knees, asking for forgiveness for treating him as an equal.

But the king shook his hand and said, 'You’re a brave soldier and have saved my life. You’ll never go hungry again; I’ll take care of you.'

'And if you ever want to enjoy a roast as good as that from the robbers’ house, just come to the royal kitchen. But you must first ask for my permission before toasting.'

title: The Bremen Town-Musicians
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a man who owned a donkey that had faithfully transported sacks of corn to the mill for many years.

However, the donkey was aging, becoming weak and less capable of working.

Seeing this, his master started to think about how he could save on feed.

Realizing that he was no longer wanted, the donkey decided to escape and set off for Bremen.

He thought, 'Here, I can surely become a town musician.' As he walked along the road for a while,

he came across a hound lying in the road, panting as if he had run a marathon.

'Why are you panting so much, big guy?' asked the donkey.

'Oh,' replied the hound, 'I’m old and getting weaker by the day. I can’t hunt anymore, and my master wanted to get rid of me, so I ran away. Now, how will I earn my keep?'

'I have an idea,' said the donkey. 'I’m heading to Bremen to become a town musician. Come with me and you can also be a musician. I'll play the lute, and you can play the drums.'

The hound agreed, and off they went.

Soon enough, they encountered a cat sitting on the path, looking as glum as could be.

'What’s wrong, old buddy?' asked the donkey.

'How can anyone be happy when their life is at stake?' replied the cat.

'I’m getting old, my teeth are worn down to stumps, and I’d rather sit by the fire and nap than chase mice, but my owner wanted to drown me, so I left. But now, I don’t know what to do.'

'Come with us to Bremen!' cheered the donkey. 'You know a thing or two about nighttime serenades.'

The cat liked this idea and decided to join them.

Next, the three of them arrived at a farmyard where a rooster was perched on the gate, crowing loudly.

'Your crow is something else!' said the donkey. 'What’s going on?'

title: The Bremen Town-Musicians
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
'I’m just predicting nice weather,' answered the rooster, 'since it's the day our lady washes the Christ child's little clothes and wants to dry them. But guests are coming for Sunday dinner, and the housewife has told the cook that she plans to cook me in soup tomorrow!'

'Oh no!' exclaimed the donkey. 'You should come with us! We're going to Bremen. There’s got to be something better out there than being dinner!'

'You have a fantastic voice,' suggested the donkey. 'If we make music together, it’s bound to sound incredible.'

The rooster agreed, and all four of them continued on their adventure.

They couldn’t reach Bremen in one day, so they decided to rest in a forest for the night.

The donkey and the hound lay down beneath a big tree, while the cat and rooster found spots in the branches.

The rooster flew to the top of the tree, feeling the safest there. Before settling down, he glanced around and spotted a flickering light in the distance.

'Hey, guys! I think there’s a house nearby,' he called to his friends.

'If that’s true, we should get moving!' said the donkey. 'This shelter isn’t great.'

'And I could really go for some bones with meat!' added the hound.

So they made their way toward the light and soon found themselves in front of a brightly lit house full of robbers.

'What do you see, my gray friend?' asked the rooster.

'Well,' answered the donkey, 'I see a table filled with delicious food and robbers sitting around enjoying themselves.'

'That sounds like the perfect opportunity for us!' said the rooster. 'If only we could get inside!'

So the animals came up with a plan to scare the robbers away.

The donkey would put his front feet on the window ledge, the hound would jump onto the donkey’s back, the cat would scramble onto the dog, and finally, the rooster would fly up and sit on the cat's head.

title: The Bremen Town-Musicians
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once they were ready, they performed their music together on cue. The donkey brayed, the hound barked, the cat meowed, and the rooster crowed.

They burst through the window, crashing down the glass. The terrible racket caused the robbers to jump up, thinking a ghost had entered, and they fled for the forest in a panic.

The four friends wasted no time; they sat down at the table and devoured whatever was left, eating like they hadn’t in ages.

After finishing their meal, they extinguished the light, and each found a cozy spot to sleep based on what felt right for them.

The donkey lay down on some straw in the yard, the hound curled up behind the door, the cat snuggled up on the hearth near the warm ashes, and the rooster perched on a rafters.

They were all exhausted from their long journey.

Around midnight, the robbers saw from afar that the light was out in the house and everything appeared calm. The captain said, 'We shouldn’t have let ourselves get scared and ordered one of them to investigate.'

The scout found everything quiet and went into the kitchen to light a candle. Mistaking the glowing eyes of the cat for burning coals, he struck a match.

But the cat didn't find this funny and leaped at him, hissing and scratching. He was terrified and dashed to the back door, only to have the dog jump up and bite his leg.

As he scurried across the yard next to the dung heap, the donkey gave him a swift kick with his back leg. The rooster, woken by the commotion, shouted down from his perch, 'Cock-a-doodle-doo!'

The robber sprinted back to his captain, trembling, and reported, 'There’s a terrifying witch in the house who spat in my face and scratched me with her claws! And there’s a man with a knife who stabbed me in the leg!'

'And in the yard, there’s a dark monster who hit me with a wooden stick! And above, on the roof, the judge yelled at me, 'Bring the rogue to me! So I fled for my life!'

After that, the robbers never dared to return to that house.

The four musicians found it so cozy that they had no intention of leaving.

And the last person to tell this tale still keeps the story alive.

title: The Bright Sun Brings It to Light
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
A tailor's apprentice was wandering through the world, looking for work.

At one point, he was unable to find any, and his situation became so dire that he didn't have a single cent to live on.

Eventually, he came across a Jew on the road.

Thinking the Jew must have a lot of money, the apprentice pushed aside any thoughts of God in his heart, confronted the Jew, and said, 'Give me your money, or I'll kill you.'

The Jew replied, 'Spare my life; I only have eight farthings.'

But the apprentice insisted the Jew had more money and pressured him until he was nearly dead.

As the Jew lay dying, his last words were, 'The bright sun will bring it to light,' and then he passed away.

The apprentice searched his own pockets and, much to his surprise, found only eight farthings, the same amount the Jew had mentioned.

He picked up the Jew's body and carried it behind a cluster of trees, then continued on his way to find work.

After a long journey, he finally found a job in a town where he also fell in love with his boss's daughter, married her, and they lived happily together.

As time went by, he and his wife had two children, but eventually, her parents passed away, leaving the couple to manage the household on their own.

One morning, while sitting at the table by the window, his wife brought him coffee.

Just as he poured it into the saucer and was about to sip it, the sunlight hit it, creating flashes of light on the wall.

The tailor glanced up and said, 'It really wants to bring it to light, but it can't.'

His wife asked, 'What do you mean by that, dear husband?'

He responded, 'I can't tell you.'

But his wife pressed him, saying, 'If you love me, you'll share it. I promise nobody will ever know,' and she wouldn't let it go.

Finally, he told her about how years ago, while searching for work and at his lowest point, he had killed a Jew, and before he died, the Jew had uttered, 'The bright sun will bring it to light.'

He explained that now the sun seemed eager to reveal it but couldn't.

After sharing his secret, he earnestly warned her never to speak of it, or he would lose his life, and she agreed.

However, when he went back to work, she shared the story with her close friend, asking her to keep it under wraps.

But within three days, the entire town knew, and the tailor was put on trial and sentenced.

Thus, in the end, the bright sun truly did bring it to light.

title: Faithful John
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was an old king who was seriously ill and thought to himself, 'I must be on my deathbed.' He then called for his most trusted servant, saying, 'Please bring Faithful John to me.'

Faithful John was the king’s favorite servant, known for his unwavering loyalty throughout his life. When John arrived at the king's bedside, the king said, 'Most faithful John, I can feel my end coming, and the only thing I worry about is my son. He’s still very young and doesn’t always know how to take care of himself. If you don’t promise to teach him everything he needs to know and be like a second father to him, I won’t be able to rest in peace.'

Faithful John immediately replied, 'I promise I won’t abandon him and will serve him faithfully, even if it costs me my life.' Hearing this, the old king said, 'Now I can die in peace.' He added, 'After I’m gone, you’ll show him the entire castle - all the rooms, halls, and vaults, along with all the treasures hidden inside. But you must never show him the last room in the long gallery, where the painting of the princess of the golden dwelling hangs. If he sees that painting, he'll instantly fall in love with her, and it could lead him into great danger. So please, protect him from that.'

After Faithful John reassured the king once more, the king laid his head down and passed away quietly.

After the old king was laid to rest, Faithful John told the young king everything he had promised his father on his deathbed. 'I will keep my word to you,' he said, 'and remain as loyal to you as I was to him, even if it costs me my life.' Once the period of mourning was over, Faithful John told the young king, 'It’s time for you to see your inheritance. I’ll show you your father’s palace.'

Then he took him around, showing him all the wealth and grandeur of the palace, but there was one room he didn’t open—the room with the perilous painting. However, the painting was situated so that it became visible as soon as the door was opened. It was painted so beautifully that it seemed almost alive, radiating charm and beauty that surpassed anything else in the world.

title: Faithful John
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The young king noticed that Faithful John always avoided this one door. Curious, he asked, 'Why don’t you ever open this door for me?' John responded, 'There’s something inside that would terrify you.' But the king insisted, 'I’ve seen everything else in the palace. I want to know what’s in this room too!' He tried to force the door open, but Faithful John stopped him, saying, 'I promised your father before his death that you wouldn’t see what’s in this chamber. It could bring great misfortune to both of us.'

'Oh, no!' replied the young king. 'If I don’t go inside, it will be my certain doom. I won’t have peace until I’ve seen it with my own eyes! I won’t leave this spot until you let me in.'

Seeing that he could no longer prevent it, Faithful John, with a heavy heart and many sighs, searched for the key among the large bunch he carried.

When he opened the door, he went in first, hoping that by standing in front of it, he could block the view of the portrait from the young king’s eyes. But it was useless; the young king stood on tiptoe and peered over John’s shoulder. As he beheld the painting of the maiden—glistening with gold and precious jewels—he immediately fainted and collapsed.

Faithful John quickly picked him up and carried him to his bed, filled with sorrow as he thought, 'Something terrible has happened to us. Oh Lord God, what will be the outcome of this?' He then gave the young king some wine to revive him.

When the king finally regained consciousness, his first words were, 'Oh, what a beautiful portrait! Whose is it?' John replied, 'That is the princess of the golden dwelling.' The young king continued, 'My love for her is so immense that if every leaf on every tree were a tongue, they couldn’t express it all. I would give my life to win her. You, my trusty John, must help me.'

title: Faithful John
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Faithful John thought for a long time about how to approach this, knowing it was already difficult just to see the king's daughter. Finally, he said to the king, 'Everything she possesses is made of gold—tables, chairs, dishes, glasses, and other household items. You have five tons of gold in your treasury; let’s have one of the kingdom's goldsmiths craft them into various vessels and decorations that might please her. Then we can go there with the gifts and try our luck.'

The king agreed and summoned the goldsmiths, who worked day and night to create the most magnificent items. Once everything was loaded onto a ship, Faithful John disguised himself as a merchant, and the king did the same to avoid being recognized. Then they set sail across the sea, heading to the town where the princess of the golden dwelling lived.

Faithful John told the king to wait on the ship while he went ashore. 'I might just bring the princess back with me,' he said. 'So make sure everything is ready on board. Set out the golden vessels and decorate the entire ship.' As he gathered various golden items into his apron, he made his way straight to the royal palace.

When he entered the palace courtyard, he saw a beautiful girl by the well, drawing water with two golden buckets. As she turned to carry the sparkling water away, she noticed the stranger and asked who he was. He replied, 'I’m a merchant,' and opened his apron for her to see the treasures inside.

She exclaimed, 'Oh, what beautiful golden things!' and set her buckets down to examine them closely. Then she said, 'The princess must see these! She loves golden things so much that she will buy all you have.' Taking him by the hand, she led him upstairs, as she was a waiting-maid.

Once the princess saw the exquisite treasures, she was thrilled. 'They are so beautifully made! I want to buy everything!' But Faithful John replied, 'I’m just a servant of a rich merchant. What I have here doesn’t compare to what my master has on his ship. He possesses the most stunning and valuable items ever crafted from gold.'

title: Faithful John
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When she requested that he bring all the goods to her, he mentioned, 'There are so many items; transporting them would take many days, and your palace doesn’t have enough rooms to showcase them all.' This only piqued her curiosity even further. Eventually, she said, 'Take me to your ship. I want to see your master’s treasures myself.'

Happily, Faithful John led her to the ship. Upon seeing her, the young king realized her beauty exceeded even the painting’s portrayal, nearly causing his heart to shatter. She stepped aboard, and as the king welcomed her inside, Faithful John stayed with the helmsman and directed the ship to depart, saying, 'Set all sails, let her fly like a bird in the air.'

Inside, the king showed her the golden vessels, each one, as well as the wild beasts and exotic animals. Hours passed while she admired everything, and in her excitement, she didn’t notice the ship sailing away.

Once she finished gazing at everything, she thanked the merchant and wanted to return home. But when she reached the side of the ship, she realized they were far from land and moving quickly across the open sea. 'Oh no!' she cried in alarm, 'I’ve been betrayed! I’ve been taken captive by a merchant—I would rather die!'

The king took her hand and declared, 'I’m not a merchant. I’m a king, just like you, and if I’ve brought you aboard in disguise, it’s only because of my great love for you. The first time I saw your portrait, I fainted.'

Hearing this from the young king, the princess was comforted and felt her heart warm toward him, willingly agreeing to become his wife.

While they sailed across the deep sea, Faithful John sat at the front of the ship playing music. Suddenly, he noticed three ravens flying toward them. Stopping his music, he listened closely to what they were saying, as he understood their language.

title: Faithful John
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One raven called out, 'Look, there he is, taking home the princess of the golden dwelling.' 'Yes,' replied the second, 'but he hasn’t won her yet.' The third said, 'But he does have her; she’s sitting beside him in the ship.' The first bird continued, 'What good will that do him? When they reach land, a chestnut horse will jump out to greet him, and if he tries to ride it, it will run off with him and take him high into the sky, never to be seen again.'

The second raven asked, 'Is there no way to escape?' 'Oh yes,' replied the first. 'If someone else can mount it quickly and grab the pistol from its holster, shooting the horse dead, the young king will be saved. But who knows that? And whoever does know and tells him will be turned to stone from the toes up to the knees.'

'I know even more,' said the second raven. 'Even if the horse is killed, the young king will still lose his bride. When they enter the castle together, a beautiful bridal gown will be resting in a dish, appearing to be woven of gold and silver. But it’s actually made of sulfur and pitch, and if he puts it on, it will burn him to the bone.'

'Is there really no way out?' asked the third raven. 'Oh yes,' replied the second. 'If someone wearing gloves grabs the gown and throws it into the fire, the young king will be saved. However, that person will become stone from the knee to the heart if they tell.

'I have even more to say,' added the third. 'Even if the bridal gown burns, the young king still won’t have his bride. After their wedding, when the dancing starts, the young queen will suddenly lose color and collapse as though dead. If someone doesn’t lift her and draw three drops of blood from her right breast, she will die. But, should anyone reveal this, they would turn to stone from head to toe.'

title: Faithful John
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once the ravens finished talking, they flew away. Faithful John understood everything but felt a deep sadness. If he kept silent, his master would face ruin; if he spoke, he would have to sacrifice his life. Finally, he resolved, 'I will save my master, even if it leads to my own demise.'

When they reached shore, everything unfolded just as the ravens had foretold. A beautiful chestnut horse leaped forward. 'Great,' said the king. 'I’ll ride it to my palace!' He started to mount it when Faithful John jumped in front, quickly hopped on, drew the pistol from its holster, and shot the horse.

The king's other attendants, who were not very fond of John, exclaimed, 'How shameful to kill such a magnificent creature! It was supposed to take the king to his palace!' But the king replied, 'Be quiet and leave him alone; he is my most faithful John. Who knows what good may come from this?'

Once they entered the palace, they found a dish with the bridal gown laid out, looking as if it was made of gold and silver. The young king approached it, ready to pick it up, but Faithful John stepped in, grabbed it with gloves on, rushed it to the fire, and burned it.

The other attendants complained again, saying, 'Look, now he’s even burning the king’s bridal gown!' But the young king said, 'Who knows what good he might have done? Leave him be; he is my most faithful John.'

The wedding finally took place, and they began to dance. During the festivities, the young queen danced too, but Faithful John kept a watchful eye on her face. Suddenly, she became pale and fell to the floor like a lifeless body.

In a panic, he rushed to her side, picked her up, and carried her into a chamber. Once inside, he laid her down and quickly sucked the three drops of blood from her right breast, spitting them out. Immediately, she breathed again and came back to life, but the young king, confused by John’s actions, became furious and shouted, 'Throw him in the dungeon!'

title: Faithful John
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The next morning, Faithful John was condemned and taken to the gallows. As he stood at the high platform, preparing for execution, he said, 'Every condemned person should have a chance to give one last speech. May I too have this right?'

'Yes,' replied the king, 'you shall have your say.' Faithful John then addressed the crowd, 'I am unjustly condemned and have remained loyal to you. I overheard the ravens’ conversation at sea, which compelled me to act in ways that I believed would save my master.'

Hearing this, the king cried out, 'Oh, my most faithful John! Forgive me, bring him down!' Just as he finished speaking, Faithful John collapsed and died.

The king and queen were struck with immense sorrow. The king said, 'Oh, how poorly I have repaid such great loyalty.' He ordered that the stone figure of John be moved to his bedroom, beside his bed.

Whenever he looked at it, he wept and said, 'If only I could bring you back to life, my most faithful John.'

Time passed, and the queen gave birth to twins—two sons who quickly became her pride and joy. One day, while the queen was at church and the king played with his children, he gazed at the stone figure again, sighed deeply, and said, 'If only I could bring you back to life, my most faithful John.'

To his astonishment, the stone began to speak, saying, 'You can bring me back to life if you’re willing to sacrifice what you hold dearest.' The king shouted, 'I would give up everything for you!'

'If you would cut off the heads of your two children and sprinkle me with their blood, I will be restored to life.' The king was horrified at the thought of killing his beloved children. But then he remembered Faithful John’s unwavering loyalty and how he died for him. Taking a deep breath, he drew his sword and, in heartbreak, cut off the heads of his children.

After smearing the stone with their blood, life returned, and Faithful John stood before him, alive and well. He said, 'Your loyalty will not go unrewarded.' John took the children’s heads, reattached them, and rubbed the wounds with their blood. They healed instantly, jumping around and playing as if nothing had ever happened.

Overjoyed, the king hid John and the children in a cupboard before the queen entered. As she came in, he asked, 'Have you been praying in church?' 'Yes,' she replied, 'but I’ve been worried about Faithful John and the misfortune that has befallen him because of us.'

'Dear wife,' said the king, 'we can give him back his life, but it comes at the cost of our two little sons, whom we would have to sacrifice.' The queen turned pale, her heart trembling, but finally agreed, 'We owe this to him for his great loyalty.'

The king felt a rush of relief that she thought the same way. He opened the cupboard and revealed Faithful John and the children, saying, 'Praise God! He is resurrected, and we’ve got our sons back.' Then he explained everything that had occurred.

From then on, they lived together happily ever after.

title: The Crumbs on the Table
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One day, George called out to his little chickens, "Come into the

living room and have some fun! There are bread crumbs on the table.

Your owner has gone out visiting."

But the chickens replied, "No, no! We can't go. If

she finds out, she'll scold us. George reassured them,

"She won't find out. Come on! She never gives you anything nice.

"Once again, the chickens insisted, "No, we really shouldn't go.

But George wouldn’t let up until they finally gave in, climbed up onto the table,

and feasted on the bread crumbs with all their might. Just then,

their owner came home, grabbed a stick in a rush,

and scolded them harshly. After they were chased out of the house,

the chickens said to George, "See what happened!"

George laughed and replied, "Didn’t I tell you it would happen?"

So they all had to run away.

title: The Crystal Ball
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was an enchantress with three sons who cared for each other deeply.

However, the enchantress didn't trust them and feared they wanted to steal her powers.

Out of suspicion, she transformed the eldest son into an eagle, forcing him to live in the rocky mountains, where he could often be seen soaring in large circles in the sky.

She turned the second son into a whale, which swam in the deep sea, only visible when it spouted a massive jet of water into the air.

Each son could return to human form for just two hours each day.

The youngest son, fearing that he might be turned into a ferocious beast—maybe a bear or a wolf—decided to sneak away.

He had heard tales of a princess who was under a spell, trapped in the castle of the golden sun, waiting to be rescued.

Many had attempted to save her, but those who did often met with tragic ends.

Twenty-three young men had already lost their lives, and only one last brave soul could attempt it, after which no more would be allowed.

With a fearless heart, he resolved to find the castle of the golden sun.

He wandered for a long time without success until he stumbled into a vast forest and got completely lost.

Suddenly, he spotted two giants in the distance, waving him over.

When he approached them, they said, "We're fighting over a cap, but neither of us can win because we're equally strong.

Since small men often outsmart us, we’ll let you decide who gets it."

The young man replied, "Why are you quarreling over an old cap? You have no idea of its true power.

It’s a wishing hat. Anyone who wears it can wish to be anywhere they desire in an instant!"

"Then give me the cap," the youth said. "I’ll move a short distance away, and when I call, we’ll race for it.

Whoever reaches me first will get to keep it."

He took the cap, walked off, and, focusing on the princess, forgot all about the giants as he kept moving forward.

title: The Crystal Ball
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Finally, he let out a deep sigh and said, "Oh, how I wish I were at the castle of the golden sun!"

No sooner had he spoken than he found himself standing on a high mountain before the castle.

As he entered, he went through all the rooms until he reached the last one, where he found the princess.

To his shock, she looked nothing like the beautiful maiden he had heard about.

Her face was ashen and wrinkled, her eyes were bleary, and her hair was red.

"Are you the princess whose beauty the world admires?" he exclaimed.

"Ah, yes, but this isn’t my true form," she replied.

"Human eyes can only see me in this pitiful state, but look in the mirror—it's not deceived and will show you my real self."

She handed him the mirror, and he saw the most beautiful maiden in the reflection, tears streaming down her cheeks.

"How can you be freed from this curse?" he asked.

"I fear no danger," he added bravely.

"The one who acquires the crystal ball and holds it before the enchanter will break his power, and I will be restored,

" she explained.

"So many others have lost their lives trying before you, and you’re so young," she added, distraught.

"I can’t be deterred from this mission. Just tell me what to do," he said.

"You will know everything when you descend the mountain where the castle sits.

A wild bull will be waiting by a spring, and you must fight it.

If you manage to slay it, a fiery bird will emerge from its body, carrying a red-hot egg inside which the crystal ball lies.

But be careful: the bird won’t drop the egg easily; if it falls, it will ignite and destroy everything around it, including the egg and the crystal ball, ruining your search."

The youth made his way down to the spring, where the bull was snorting and bellowing at him.

After a fierce battle, he stabbed the beast with his sword, bringing it down.

At that moment, a fiery bird soared into the air but was quickly chased by the young man's brother, the eagle.

The eagle swooped down and drove the bird towards the sea, striking it until it dropped the egg.

But the egg didn’t land in the sea; it fell on a fisherman’s hut on the shore, causing it to smoke and catch fire.

Suddenly, massive waves rose from the sea, crashing over the hut and extinguishing the flames.

The whale, the other brother, had come swimming to help, pushing water onto the fire.

Once the fire was out, the youth searched for the egg and found it intact; although the shell was cracked from the sudden cooling, the crystal ball inside was unharmed.

When he arrived at the enchanter's lair and held up the crystal ball, the enchanter said, "My power is now lost, and from this moment on, you are the king of the castle of the golden sun.

You have the power to restore your brothers to their human forms, too."

Eagerly, the youth rushed back to the princess, and upon entering the room, he found her transformed; she was radiant, standing in all her beauty, and they joyfully exchanged rings.

title: The Cunning Little Tailor
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a princess who was incredibly proud. If a suitor came to court her, she would give him a riddle to solve, and if he couldn’t solve it, she would dismiss him haughtily.

Word got around that whoever could answer her riddle would win her hand in marriage, no matter who they were.

Eventually, three tailors crossed paths. The two older ones were confident they had completed so many impressive jobs in the past that they would surely succeed this time too.

The third tailor, a small and somewhat reckless guy, didn’t even know his craft well, but he thought he must have a stroke of luck in this challenge. After all, where else could luck come from?

So the other two tailors told him, 'You should just stay home; you won't get far with your limited understanding.'

However, the little tailor didn't let that discourage him. He declared that he had set his mind on succeeding this time, and off he went, feeling like he could conquer the world.

All three of them went to the princess and asked her to share her riddle, confidently stating that they were the right people for it, with minds sharp enough to be threaded through a needle.

The princess then said, 'I have two types of hair on my head; what color are they?' 'If that's it,' said the first tailor, 'they must be black and white, just like pepper and salt fabric.'

The princess replied, 'That's incorrect. Let the second tailor try.'

The second tailor said, 'If it's not black and white, then it must be brown and red, like my father's Sunday coat.'

The princess shook her head. 'Wrong again, let the third tailor give it a shot. I can tell he knows the answer for sure.'

Then the little tailor stepped forward confidently and said, 'The princess has one silver hair and one golden hair on her head, and those are the two colors.'

When the princess heard this, she turned pale and almost fainted with shock. The little tailor had solved her riddle, and she thought no man could ever find the answer.

title: The Cunning Little Tailor
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once her courage returned, she said, 'You haven't won me yet. There's still one more task you must complete. In the stable below, there's a bear waiting for you. If you survive the night, you can marry me.'

She believed that this would rid her of the tailor since the bear had never spared anyone who encountered its wrath.

But the little tailor wasn’t frightened; instead, he was thrilled and declared, 'Fortune favors the bold.'

When evening arrived, the little tailor was taken to face the bear. As the bear was about to pounce on him, he said, 'Easy there, I’ll calm you down in no time.'

Then, remarkably relaxed and without a worry in the world, he pulled some nuts from his pocket, cracked them open, and started munching on the tasty bits.

Seeing this, the bear couldn't help but feel curious and wanted some nuts too.

The tailor rummaged through his pockets and handed the bear a handful, but instead of nuts, they were pebbles. The bear tried biting into them but couldn’t get a thing. 'How foolish am I,' he thought, 'that I can’t even crack a nut.'

Frustrated, he said to the tailor, 'Here, crack the nuts for me!' 'Wow, what a silly bear you are,' replied the little tailor, 'with such a big mouth and unable to crack a tiny nut.'

Then he took a pebble and quickly replaced it with a nut, and with a snap, he had it in two.

The bear thought, 'I can certainly do this too!' So he tried again and again to bite the pebble with all his might.

After a while, the tailor pulled out a violin from under his coat and began to play a tune. The bear couldn’t resist the rhythm and started to dance.

The more the bear danced, the more he enjoyed it, and soon he said to the little tailor, 'Is fiddling difficult?' 'No, it's easy enough for a child. Just put my fingers here and stroke the bow, and it plays happily, like a lively song.'

title: The Cunning Little Tailor
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
'Oh,' the bear said, 'I’d love to learn how to fiddle so I can dance anytime I want. What do you think? Will you teach me?' 'Of course,' replied the tailor, 'if you have a knack for it. But first, let me see those claws of yours; they are far too long. I’ll need to trim them.'

Then a vise was brought in, and the bear placed his claws inside. The little tailor tightened it carefully, saying, 'Now just wait while I fetch the scissors.' With that, he let the bear growl as much as he wanted and settled down in the corner on a straw pile to sleep.

During the night, when the princess heard the bear growling fiercely, she thought he was celebrating and that the tailor was doomed.

In the morning, she woke up carefree and happy, but when she peeked into the stable, she found the tailor standing there, cheerful and just as healthy as ever.

Now she couldn't say another word against the wedding since she had promised in front of others.

The king ordered a carriage to be brought so she could ride to church with the tailor.

Once they were settled in the carriage, the two jealous tailors, who envied his good fortune, rushed to the stable and unscrewed the bear again.

The enraged bear bolted after the carriage, growling and snorting.

Horrified, the princess cried out, 'Oh no, the bear is chasing us and wants to get you!'

Quick as a flash, the tailor flipped onto his head, stuck his legs out the window, and shouted, 'Do you see the vise? If you don’t back off, you’ll be put in it again!'

When the bear saw this, he turned tail and ran back.

The tailor made it to the church without any fuss, and the princess married him right away. They lived together happily ever after.

Anyone who doesn't believe this has to pay a fine.

title: The Death of the Little Hen
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, a little hen set off with a little rooster.

They went to the nut-hill and agreed that whoever found a nut kernel would share it with the other.

Soon enough, the hen found a really big nut but decided to keep it to herself.

She thought she could eat the kernel without telling the rooster.

However, the kernel was so large that she couldn't swallow it.

It stuck in her throat, making her panic because she was afraid of choking.

Desperate, she called out, "Rooster, please run as fast as you can and bring me some water, or I’ll choke!"

The little rooster dashed as fast as he could to the spring and cried out, "Stream, please give me some water! My little hen is stuck on the nut-hill and is choking on a big nut!"

The well replied, "First, go to the bride and ask her for some red silk."

So the rooster hurried to the bride and said, "Bride, can you please give me some red silk? I need it for the well so I can get water for the little hen who’s choking on a huge nut!"

The bride responded, "Sure, but first you need to bring me my little wreath that’s hanging on the willow tree."

The little rooster ran to the willow, grabbed the wreath from the branch, and returned it to the bride.

In exchange, the bride gave him the red silk, which he took back to the well.

The well gladly gave him some water in return.

The little rooster hurried back to the little hen, but sadly, by the time he arrived, she had choked and lay lifeless on the ground.

Distraught, the rooster cried out, and soon all the animals gathered to mourn the little hen.

Six mice built a tiny carriage to carry her to her grave, and as soon as it was ready, they hitched themselves to it and the rooster led the way.

On their journey, they encountered a fox, who asked, "Where are you going, little rooster?"

I’m taking my little hen to be buried," replied the rooster.

The fox said, "May I join you?" The rooster replied, "Yes, but please sit at the back of the carriage since my little horses can’t pull you in front."

And on the procession went, eventually reaching a stream.

The rooster wondered aloud, "How are we going to cross?"

Nearby, a straw lay on the bank and said, "I’ll lay myself across, and you can drive over me."

However, when the six mice stepped onto the straw, it slipped and fell into the water, causing all the mice to drown.

Now faced with a problem, a coal said, "I’m big enough. Let me lay across, and you can drive over me."

So, the coal laid down, but sadly, when it touched the water, it hissed and promptly went out.

Seeing this, a stone felt sorry for the little rooster and decided to help by laying over the water.

The rooster took charge and pulled the carriage himself.

He got it across to the other side with the lifeless hen.

But as he tried to pull the others over who were still in the carriage, it became too heavy, causing it to roll back into the water, and they all drowned.

Now left alone with the dead hen, the little rooster dug a grave for her, placed her inside, and made a mound above it.

He sat down on the mound and grieved until he, too, passed away.

title: The Devil and His Grandmother
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
There was a huge war, and the king had many soldiers, but he paid them very little.

So little that they could barely survive, which led three of them to decide to desert.

One of them said to the others, "If we get caught, we'll be hanged. How can we manage this?"

Another replied, "Look at that vast cornfield; if we hide there, no one will find us. The troops can't go in there, and they’re supposed to march away tomorrow."

They crept into the corn, but instead of the troops leaving, they remained stationed all around it.

They stayed in the corn for two days and two nights, and they were so hungry they were nearly dying. However, if they came out, it would mean certain death.

Then they said, "What’s the point of deserting if we're going to die miserably here?"

At that moment, a fiery dragon flew through the sky and landed near them, asking why they were hiding.

They replied, "We are three soldiers who deserted because the pay was terrible, and now we'll starve if we stay here or be hanged if we go out."

"If you agree to serve me for seven years," said the dragon, "I’ll help you get through the army without being caught."

"We have no choice but to accept," they answered.

Then the dragon grabbed them with his claws, flew them over the army, and set them down far away. But the dragon was actually the devil.

He handed them a little whip and said, "Use this whip, crack it, and gold will spring up around you as much as you want. You can live like lords, keep horses, and drive your own carriages. But when the seven years are over..."

He presented them with a book that they all three had to sign. "But first, I have a riddle for you," he said, "and if you guess it, you’ll be free from my power."

Then the dragon flew away, and they left with their whip, gathered plenty of gold, dressed in fine clothes, and traveled the world.

title: The Devil and His Grandmother
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Everywhere they went, they lived in joy and luxury, riding horses, driving carriages, eating, and drinking, but they never did anything wrong.

As time quickly passed, and with seven years nearly up, two of them felt anxious and worried, while the third remained calm and said, "Guys, don’t worry, I’ve got this. I’ll guess the riddle."

They went into the open countryside and sat down, while the two looked downcast.

An elderly woman approached them and asked why they looked so sad. "What’s that to you?" they replied. "You can’t help us."

"Who knows?" she said. "Just share your problem with me." So they told her how they had been serving the devil for almost seven years, that he provided them gold as if it were hay, but they were sold to him and would lose their freedom if they couldn’t guess a riddle at the end of the seven years.

The old woman said, "If you want to be saved, one of you must go into the forest. There, you’ll find a fallen rock that looks like a small house; you must enter it for help."

The two gloomy ones thought, "That probably won’t save us," and stayed put, but the third soldier, the cheerful one, got up and went into the forest until he found the rock house.

Inside, an elderly woman was sitting there, the devil's grandmother, who asked the soldier where he came from and what he was looking for.

He explained everything that had happened, and because she liked his story, she felt sorry for him and promised to help.

She lifted a heavy stone above a cellar and said, "Hide yourself there. You’ll hear everything that’s said, but stay quiet and don’t move. When the dragon arrives, I’ll question him about the riddle. He tells me everything, so listen carefully."

At midnight, the dragon flew in and asked for dinner. The grandmother set the table and served food and drinks that pleased him, so they ate and drank together.

title: The Devil and His Grandmother
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
During their conversation, she asked him about his day and how many souls he had claimed. "Not much went right today," he replied. "But I caught three soldiers, and I have them safe."

"Oh really? Three soldiers? They’re clever; they might still escape," she said.

The devil laughed and said, "They're mine. I’ll give them a riddle they'll never guess."

"What riddle is that?" she asked.

"I’ll tell you, there’s a dead dogfish in the great north sea; that’s your roast meat. The rib of a whale will be your silver spoon, and a hollow old horse's hoof will be your wineglass."

Once the devil went to bed, the old grandmother lifted the stone and freed the soldier. "Did you pay attention to everything?" she asked.

"Yes," he replied. "I know enough and can save myself now." He quickly returned another way, through the window, back to his friends.

He told them how the devil had been tricked by the old grandmother and how he had learned the answer to the riddle from her.

They were all thrilled and cheered, taking the whip and whipping themselves enough gold that it flowed all around them.

When the seven years had completely passed, the devil returned with the book, showed them their signatures, and said, "It’s time to take you to hell.

There, you’ll have a meal. If you can guess what kind of roast meat awaits you, you’ll be free from your agreement and can keep the whip as well."

The first soldier started, saying, "In the great north sea lies a dead dogfish; that’s our roast meat."

The devil got angry, mumbling, "Hmm. Hmm. Hmm." Then he asked the second soldier, "What will your spoon be?"

"The rib of a whale will be our silver spoon," the second soldier replied.

The devil grimaced and muttered again, "Hmm. Hmm. Hmm." He turned to the third soldier and asked, "And what will your wineglass be?"

"An old horse's hoof will be our wineglass," he answered.

Then the devil flew away with a loud scream and lost all power over them. The three kept the whip and whipped together enough money as they pleased, living happily ever after.

title: The Devil's Sooty Brother
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
A soldier who had been discharged had nothing to support himself and was lost about what to do next.

So, he ventured into the forest, and after walking for a little while,

he encountered a tiny man who turned out to be the devil.

The little man asked him, "What's wrong? You look very sad."

The soldier replied, "I’m hungry, but I don’t have any money."

The devil said, "If you agree to work for me as my servant, I’ll make sure you are well-fed for life.

You’ll serve me for seven years, and afterward, you’ll be free again. But there’s one condition I need to tell you about.

You must not wash, comb, or trim your hair, cut your nails, or wipe the water from your eyes."

The soldier responded, "Alright, if there’s no other choice," and went off with the little man, who led him straight into hell.

Once there, the devil explained his tasks: he was to keep the fire going under the kettles where the hell-broth was bubbling,

keep the place tidy, sweep everything behind the doors, and ensure order was maintained.

However, if he peeked into the kettles, he would be in big trouble. The soldier said, "Got it, I’ll be careful."

Then, the devil left for his own business, and the soldier started his new duties,

tending the fire and cleaning up just as he had been instructed.

When the devil returned, he checked to see if everything was done, looked satisfied, and went out again.

Now, the soldier took the chance to look around. The kettles surrounded him,

sitting over a huge fire, bubbling vigorously.

He would have given anything to look inside them, but he remembered the devil’s strict warning.

Eventually, he couldn’t hold back any longer, lifted the lid of the first kettle, and peeked in.

To his surprise, he saw his old corporal sitting inside. "Well, well, look who it is!" he exclaimed.

title: The Devil's Sooty Brother
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"You had me under your thumb back then; now it’s my turn to catch you!" He quickly closed the lid, stoked the fire, and added another log.

Next, he approached the second kettle, lifted the lid a bit, and there was his former ensign.

"Ah, look who I’ve found! You once had me in your grip, and now I’ve got you!" He shut the lid again and threw in yet another log.

Then he was curious about the third kettle and lifted the lid, only to find his general inside.

"Oh, look what we have here! You used to have me at your mercy, and now I have you trapped!" He grabbed the bellows and stoked the flames right under him.

So, for seven years, he worked in hell, never washing, combing, or trimming himself, or cutting his hair or nails, and the seven years flew by so quickly that he thought he’d only been there for half a year.

When the time was finally up, the devil came back and asked, "So, Hans, what have you done?"

"I kept the fire going under the kettles and swept all the dirt behind the doors.

"But you also peeked into the kettles, and it’s fortunate for you that you added logs to them, or else you would have lost your life.

Now that your time is over, do you want to go home?" "Yes," answered the soldier, "I’d like to see how my father is doing at home."

The devil replied, "To get your wages, go fill your knapsack with the sweepings, and take it home.

You must also leave unwashed and unkempt, with long hair and beard, uncut nails, and watery eyes.

When people ask where you’re from, say, ‘from hell,’ and when they ask who you are, say, ‘the devil's sooty brother.’"

The soldier remained quiet and did as the devil instructed, but he wasn’t pleased with his wages at all.

Once he was back in the forest, he took off his knapsack to empty it, and to his astonishment, the sweepings were all pure gold.

title: The Devil's Sooty Brother
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"I never would have thought that," he said, feeling quite happy as he entered the town.

The innkeeper, seeing the soldier approach, was terrified; Hans looked so dreadful, worse than a scarecrow.

He called out, asking, "Where do you come from?" "From hell," replied Hans. "Who are you?" asked the innkeeper. "The devil's sooty brother," Hans said, "and my king too."

The host dared not let him in, but when Hans showed him the gold, he came and opened the door himself.

Hans then ordered the best room, along with attendance, ate and drank to his heart's content, but still didn't wash or comb himself as the devil instructed.

Finally, he lay down to sleep, leaving the knapsack full of gold in view of the innkeeper, who couldn’t shake off his greedy thoughts.

During the night, the innkeeper crept in and stole the gold.

The next morning, when Hans woke up wanting to pay the landlord and continue his journey, he found his knapsack was missing.

However, he calmed himself and thought, this misfortune is not my fault.

He went back to hell, complained to the devil about his bad luck, and asked for help.

The devil said, "Sit down, I’ll wash, comb, and trim you, cut your hair and nails, and wash your eyes."

Once finished, he returned the knapsack to Hans, this time filled with sweepings, and said, "Go tell the landlord that he needs to return your money, or I will come and take him to hell to do your work."

Hans went up to the landlord and said, "You stole my money. If you don’t return it, you’ll go down to hell in my place and look just as hideous as I do now."

The landlord immediately gave him back his gold and even more, begging him to keep the incident a secret.

Now with his riches, Hans set off for his father’s house, bought himself a shabby coat, and wandered around playing music, as he had learned to do while in hell.

However, there was an old king in that land who wanted to hear him play, and the king loved it so much that he promised Hans his eldest daughter in marriage.

But when the princess heard about marrying a common man in a coat, she exclaimed, "I’d rather drown!"

The king then offered him the youngest princess, who happily agreed to marry him to please her father.

Thus, the devil's sooty brother ended up marrying the king's daughter, and when the old king passed away, the entire kingdom came under his rule.

title: The Ditmarsch Tale of Wonders
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Let me share something interesting with you. I once saw two roasted chickens soaring through the air,

they zipped around quickly with their fronts facing the sky and the backs facing down,

and an anvil along with a millstone floated gracefully across

the Rhine, moving daintily and slowly, while a frog perched on

the ice during Whitsun, munching on a plowshare. Three guys

who wanted to catch a hare went out on crutches and stilts; one

was deaf, the second was blind, the third was mute, and the

fourth couldn’t walk at all. Want to hear how they managed it?

First, the blind man spotted the hare darting across the

field, the mute called out to the lame one, and the lame one

There were some men who wanted to sail on solid ground,

so they unfurled their sails to catch the wind, gliding away over vast

fields. They even sailed over a tall mountain, but unfortunately,
