they ended up drowning miserably. A crab was in hot pursuit of a hare,

which was sprinting away at full speed, while up on the roof rested

a cow that had somehow found its way up there. In that region, the flies

are as large as goats are here. Please open the window so the

title: The Dog and the Sparrow
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
A sheepdog had a terrible master who made him suffer from hunger.

Unable to stay there any longer, he set off sadly.

On his way, he met a sparrow who asked,

"Brother dog, why do you look so sad?" The dog replied, "I’m hungry and have nothing to eat."

The sparrow said, "Dear brother, come with me to the town, and I will help satisfy your hunger."

So they went into the town together, and when they reached a butcher's shop, the sparrow told the dog, "Stay here, and I’ll grab you a piece of meat."

The sparrow landed on the stall, made sure no one was watching,

and pulled and tore at a piece lying on the edge until it fell.

The dog quickly grabbed it, ran to a corner, and gobbled it up.

The sparrow then said, "Now let’s go to another shop, and I’ll get you another piece so you can feel satisfied."

Once the dog finished that second piece, the sparrow asked, "Brother dog, are you full now?"

"Yes, I’ve had enough meat," he answered, "but I still haven’t had any bread."

The sparrow replied, "You’ll get that too. Come with me."

He took the dog to a baker's shop and pecked a couple of small buns until they rolled down.

When the dog wanted even more, he led him to another stall and got him more bread.

After devouring that, the sparrow asked, "Brother dog, have you had enough now?"

"Yes, I’m full now. Let’s go for a walk," he replied.

They both stepped out onto the highway.

The weather was warm, but after walking a bit, the dog said, "I’m tired and need to sleep."

"Go ahead and sleep," the sparrow replied. "I’ll perch on a branch while you do."

So the dog laid down on the road and fell fast asleep.

While he was sleeping, a wagon driver came along with a cart pulled by three horses, carrying two barrels of wine.

title: The Dog and the Sparrow
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The sparrow noticed he wasn't planning to steer away, so it cried out,

"Wagon driver, don’t go that way or you’ll regret it!"

But the driver muttered, "You won’t make me regret anything," and cracked his whip, driving the cart over the dog.

The wheels rolled right over him and killed him.

The sparrow shouted, "You’ve run over my brother dog and killed him! You’ll pay for this with your cart and horses!"

"Cart and horses?" scoffed the driver. "What harm can you do me?"

And he drove off.

The sparrow hid under the cart’s cover and pecked at the bung-hole until it popped out, causing the wine to flow out.

The driver, unaware, later looked back and noticed the cart was leaking, only to see one barrel was empty.

"Oh no, what bad luck!" he cried.

"Not unlucky enough yet," the sparrow replied, flying up to a horse's head and pecking its eyes out.

When the driver saw this, he pulled out an axe to strike the sparrow, but hit his horse instead, and it collapsed dead.

"What bad luck!" he lamented.

"Not unlucky enough yet," said the sparrow, and as the driver moved on with two horses, the sparrow crept under the cover again.

It pecked the bung out of the second barrel, spilling all the wine.

When the driver noticed, he cried out, "Oh, what bad luck!"

The sparrow replied, "Not unlucky enough yet," and perched on the second horse’s head, pecking its eyes out.

The driver rushed up, axe in hand, but again hit his horse in the head, killing it.

"What bad luck!" he screamed.

"Not unlucky enough yet," the sparrow said, and flew to the third horse, pecking out its eyes.

In a fit of rage, the driver swung at the sparrow without looking, but killed his third horse instead.

"What bad luck!" he cried.

"Not unlucky enough yet," the sparrow responded.

title: The Dog and the Sparrow
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The driver had to abandon the wagon, filled with anger and frustration.

When he got home, he told his wife, "Do you know what bad luck I’ve had? My wine has spilled, and all three horses are dead!"

"Oh, husband," she replied, "what a wicked bird has wandered into our lives. It’s gathered all the birds in the world, and they are devouring our corn!"

He rushed upstairs and found thousands of birds in the loft, eating up all the corn, with the sparrow right in the middle of them.

The driver cried out, "What terrible luck I have!"

"Not bad enough yet," the sparrow answered. "Wagon driver, it will cost you your life too," and flew away.

The driver had lost everything, and in frustration, he went downstairs, slumped behind the stove, feeling angry and miserable.

Outside, the sparrow called through the window, "Wagon driver, it will cost you your life!"

The driver grabbed his axe and threw it at the sparrow, but it only shattered the window, missing the bird.

The sparrow hopped inside, perched on the stove, and shouted, "Wagon driver, it will cost you your life!"

Driven mad by rage, the driver smashed the stove in pieces as the sparrow darted around the room.

Everything—his furniture, mirrors, and even the walls—suffered the same fate, yet the sparrow never got hit.

Finally, he caught the sparrow in his hand.

His wife asked, "Should I kill it?"

"No," he fumed, "that would be too kind. It will die a much crueler death."

He swallowed it whole.

But the sparrow began to flutter inside him, made its way back up to his mouth, and shouted,

"Wagon driver, it still will cost you your life!" The driver then said to his wife, "Kill the bird in my mouth for me!"

The woman swung, but missed and struck the driver on the head, causing him to collapse dead.

And so, the story went.

title: The Donkey
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a king and queen who were wealthy.

They had everything they ever wanted, except for children. The queen

grieved over this day and night, saying, 'I feel like a field

where nothing grows.' Eventually, God granted her wish, but when

the child was born, it didn't resemble a human child at all.

Instead, it was a little donkey. When the mother saw this, her lamentations

grew louder than ever. She declared that she'd rather have had no child

at all than a donkey, and insisted they should throw it in the water so the fish could devour

it. But the king said, 'No, since God has sent him, he will be my

son and heir. After my death, he will sit on the royal throne and

wear the kingly crown.' So, the donkey was raised

and grew bigger, with high, straight ears. He

had a cheerful nature, jumping around, playing, and especially

loving music. He went to a famous musician and said, 'Teach me your art. I want to play the lute as well as you do.'

'Oh, dear little master,' replied the musician, 'that would be very challenging for you; your fingers are not quite suited for it.

They're too big; I'm afraid the strings wouldn't hold up. But

the donkey was determined to play the lute.

And because he was persistent and hardworking, he eventually

learned to play as well as the master himself. One day, the young lord went out for a walk, deep in thought, and found himself at a well.

Peering into the crystal-clear water, he saw his donkey form reflected back at him.

He felt so distressed that he decided to venture into the world, taking only one loyal companion with him.

They traveled far and wide, eventually arriving in a kingdom with a single, strikingly beautiful princess.

'Let's stay here,' said the donkey. He knocked at the gate and called out, 'A guest is at the door.

'Open up so he can enter.' When the gate didn't swing open,

title: The Donkey
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
he sat down, took his lute, and began to play in the most delightful

way with his two front hooves. The doorkeeper was shocked, ran to the king, and said, 'There's a young donkey outside playing the lute like a pro.'

'Let the musician come to me,' said the king. But when the donkey entered,

everyone burst out laughing at the lute-player. When the donkey was invited to sit down and eat with the servants, he refused and said, 'I am no ordinary donkey; I'm a noble one.'

'If that’s the case, then sit with the soldiers,' they replied.

'No,' he said, 'I will sit by the king.'

The king smiled and said good-naturedly, 'Of course, little donkey, come here to me.'

Then he asked, 'Little donkey, what do you think of my daughter?' The donkey turned his head

to look at her, nodded, and said, 'I like her immensely. I've never seen anyone as beautiful as she is.'

'Well then, you shall sit next to her,' said the king. 'That's exactly what I wanted,' replied the donkey,

and he settled down beside her, eating and drinking with finesse.

After spending some time at the king's court, the noble donkey thought, 'What good is all this? I will still have to go home,' and his spirits fell.

He approached the king to request his release. But the king, fond of him, inquired, 'Little donkey, what troubles you? You look as sour as a lemon. What can I do to cheer you up?'

'Is it gold you desire?' 'No,' replied the donkey, shaking his head.

'Jewels and fine clothes?' 'No.' 'Would you like half my kingdom?' 'Not at all.' 'Then what can I do to make you happy? Would you like to marry my beautiful daughter?'

'Ah, yes,' said the donkey, suddenly cheerful and filled with joy, for that was exactly what he had been wishing for.

title: The Donkey
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
And so a grand wedding took place. That evening, when the bride and groom were taken to their bedroom, the king wanted to know if the donkey would behave well.

He ordered a servant to hide there. Once they were inside, the groom locked the door, checked around, and thinking they were alone, tossed aside his donkey skin to reveal himself as a handsome prince.

'Now, you see who I am, and that I am worthy of you,' he said. The bride was delighted, kissed him, and loved him dearly.

The next morning, he quickly put his donkey skin back on, and no one could have guessed the true form hidden beneath.

Soon, the old king came in. 'Ah,' he exclaimed, 'the little donkey is already up!' But he noticed something was off and asked his daughter, 'Aren't you sad that you didn't marry a real man?'

'Not at all, dear father; I love him just as if he was the handsomest man in the world, and I will keep him for the rest of my life.'

The king was surprised, but the servant who had hidden revealed everything to him.

'That can't be true,' the king said. 'Watch for yourself tonight; you'll see it with your own eyes. And listen, king, if you throw his skin into the fire, he will have to show his true self.'

'That sounds like a good plan,' the king agreed. Later that night, while they were asleep, he crept in. By the light of the moon,

he saw a noble-looking young man lying in bed, with the donkey skin laid out on the floor.

So he took the skin and started a fire outside, tossing the skin into it and waiting by the flames until it had completely turned to ash.

Curious to see what would happen, he kept watch all night. When the young man finally woke up, he tried to put on the donkey skin, only to find it missing.

Panicking, filled with worry, he thought, 'Now I have to find a way to escape.' But just then, the king appeared and said,

'My son, where are you in such a rush? What are you planning? Stay here; you're such a handsome man, and you won't leave me.'

'I'll give you half my kingdom, and when I die, you'll inherit it all.' 'I hope that what begins well will end well,' the young man said. 'I will stay with you.'

And so the old king gave him half the kingdom, and within a year, when the king passed away, the young man received it all.

Later, he also gained another kingdom, living in magnificence.

title: The Drummer
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One evening, a young drummer ventured out alone into the countryside,

and he stumbled upon a lake where he noticed

three pieces of white linen. "What beautiful linen!" he exclaimed, and tucked one

piece into his pocket. He returned home, forgot about what he

had found, and went to bed. Just as he was drifting off to sleep,

he thought he heard someone calling his name. He listened,

and a soft voice cried out, "Drummer, drummer, wake up."

Since it was dark, he couldn’t see anyone, but it

felt like a figure was hovering near his bed. "What

do you want?" he asked. "Give me back my shift," replied the voice,

"the one you took from me last evening at the lake. You will get

it back, but only if you tell me who you are," said the drummer.

"Ah," said the voice, "I'm the daughter of a powerful king. But a

witch has captured me, and I’m trapped on the

glass mountain. I have to bathe in the lake every day with my

two sisters, but I can’t fly back without my shift. My

sisters have gone, leaving me behind. Please give me my shift back.

"Don’t worry, dear child," said the drummer. "I’ll gladly return it to you." He pulled

it from his pocket and handed it to her in the dark. She

snatched it quickly and tried to leave. "Wait a

moment; maybe I can help you." "You can only help me by climbing

the glass mountain. Even if you were right next to it, you couldn’t climb it."

"When I set my mind to something, I always succeed," said the drummer. "I feel sorry for you, and I'm not afraid of

anything. But I don’t know the way to the

glass mountain." "The path goes through the great

forest where the man-eaters live," she replied, "and that's all I can say." Then he heard her wings as she flew.

title: The Drummer
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
At daybreak, the drummer woke up, strapped on his drums, and walked

fearlessly into the forest. After wandering for a while without encountering any giants, he thought to himself, I

need to rouse the sleepyheads, so he hung his drum in front of him,

and played such a loud roll that birds burst out of the trees.

It wasn’t long before a giant, who had been napping in the grass, stood up, towering like a fir-tree.

"What are you drumming about, you little wretch, waking me from my best sleep?" the giant roared.

"I'm drumming because

I'm leading many thousands who are coming after me." "What do they want in my forest?" the giant asked.

"They want to put an end to you and rid the forest of a monster like you!"

"Ha!" the giant chuckled. "I’ll crush you all like ants!" "Do you really think you can do anything against us?" the drummer said. "If you try to grab one, he’ll hop away out of reach. But when you're lying down and asleep,"

"they’ll come out from every thicket and creep up on you. Each one has a steel hammer strapped to his belt, and they’ll bash in your skull."

The giant scowled, feeling uneasy. Maybe meddling with these clever people could backfire on me. I can strangle wolves and bears, but I can’t

protect myself from these little earthworm folks. "Listen here, little fellow,"

he said, "head back home, and I promise to leave you and your friends in peace. If there's anything else you want, just ask; I’m more than happy

to do something for you." "You have long legs," replied the drummer, "and can run faster than I. Carry me to the

glass mountain, and I’ll give my followers a signal to turn back, and they’ll let you be this time. "Come here, worm," growled the giant. "Hop on my shoulder, and I’ll carry you wherever you want. " The giant lifted him up, and the

title: The Drummer
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
drummer began to happily drum high up.

The giant thought this was a signal for the others.

After a while, a second giant appeared on the path, who took

the drummer from the first and tucked him into his buttonhole.

The drummer grabbed the button, which was as large as a

plate, held on tightly, and smiled around. Then they arrived

at a third giant, who took him from the buttonhole and placed

him on the brim of his hat. Up there, the drummer paced back and forth, gazing over the trees, and when he spotted

a mountain in the distance, he thought, that must be the

glass mountain, and indeed it was. The giant made just two more

strides, and they arrived at the foot of the mountain, where the

giant set him down. The drummer wanted to get to the top of the glass mountain, but the giant merely shook his head,

grumbled something under his breath, and returned to the forest.

Now the poor drummer stood at the base of the mountain, towering like three stacked mountains, and looking as smooth as glass. He had no idea

how to climb it. He attempted to scale it, but every time he tried, he ended up slipping back down. If only I were a bird, he thought.

But wishing didn’t give him wings. While he stood there, unsure of what to do, he noticed, not far off, two men wrestling wildly.

He approached them and discovered they were arguing over a saddle

laying on the ground, which both wanted to claim. "You fools!" he said. "You’re fighting over a saddle when you don’t even have a horse!"

"This saddle is worth the fight," one of the men replied. "Whoever sits on it and wishes to be anywhere, even at the ends of the earth, will get there instantly.

We share this saddle. It’s my turn to ride, but the other won’t let me." "Let me resolve this dispute," the drummer said, and he stepped back and stuck a white stick into the ground.

Then he returned and said, "Now sprint to the finish line, and whoever reaches it first gets to ride first!" They both took off at a trot, but

title: The Drummer
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
before they’d even made a few steps, the drummer hopped onto the saddle, wished himself on the glass mountain,

and before anyone could even blink, he was there. At the top of the mountain was a flat area. There stood an old stone house,

and in front of it lay a big fishpond, while behind it stretched a dark forest. He saw no people or animals, everything was

silent. Only the wind whispered through the trees, and the clouds floated low overhead.

He approached the door and knocked. After knocking three times, an old woman

with a weathered face and bright red eyes answered the door. She had spectacles perched on her long nose and studied him closely. Then

she asked what he wanted. "I’m looking for a place to stay, food, and a bed for the night," the drummer replied. "You can have all that," said the old

woman, "but only if you do three tasks for me in return." "Sure thing," he answered. "I’m not afraid of hard work, no matter how tough it is."

The old woman let him in, fed him, and gave him a cozy bed for the night. The next morning, after he had slept Soundly,

she took a thimble off her wrinkled finger, handed it to the drummer, and said, "Get to work! Empty out the pond with this thimble. But you must finish it before nightfall,

and you have to sort the fish according to their kind and size."

"That’s a strange task," said the drummer, but he went to the pond,

and started emptying it. He spent all morning baling it out. But what could anyone possibly do to a big lake with just a thimble?

When noon hit, he thought, this is pointless—whether I work or not, it’s all the same. So he gave up

and sat down. Just then, a maiden came out from the house and set down a small basket of food in front of him, asking, "What troubles you? Why are you sitting here so forlorn?"

title: The Drummer
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He looked at her and saw she was astonishingly beautiful. "I can’t finish this first task," he sighed. "How will I manage the others?

I came here to find a king's daughter who is said to live here, but I can’t find her, so I’m thinking of moving on." "Stay here," said the maiden. "I can help you with your problem. You

look exhausted; lay your head in my lap and rest. When you wake up, your work will be done." The drummer didn’t need to be told twice. As soon as he closed his eyes,

she turned a wishing ring and said, "Rise, water! Fish, come out!"

Instantly, the water rose like mist and swept away with the other clouds, and the fish leapt onto the shore,

arranging themselves in rows according to their size and type. When the drummer woke up, he was astonished to see everything was done.

"But one fish is out of place," the maiden pointed out, "it’s on its own. When the old woman comes tonight and sees that you’ve completed everything she asked,

she will ask why that fish is alone. Just toss the fish in her face and say, 'This one is for you, old witch.'" Later that evening, the witch appeared, and when

she asked about the odd fish, he threw it at her. She acted as if she didn’t notice, said nothing, but looked at him with evil eyes.

The next morning, she said, "Yesterday was too easy for you; I need to give you a tougher task."

Today, you must cut down the entire forest, split the wood into logs, stack them all up, and everything has to be done by evening." She handed him an axe, a mallet, and two wedges.

But the axe was made of lead, and the mallet and wedges were tin. When he started chopping, the axe dulled,

and the mallet and wedges bent out of shape. He felt at a loss, but by noon, the maiden came again with his meal and comforted him.

title: The Drummer
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Lay your head on my lap," she said, "and sleep. When you wake, your work will be done." She turned her wishing ring, and in an instant, the whole forest

crashed down, the wood split and arranged itself into neat piles, as if invisible giants were completing the task.

When he woke up, the maiden pointed out, "Look how the wood is all stacked up, but one branch remains. However, when the old woman comes this evening and asks you about that

branch, just hit her with it and say, 'That’s for you.'" The old woman returned, and seeing the work done, said, "How easy was that work?"

"But why is that branch left here?" "For you, you old witch," he said, and swung the branch at her. She feigned not to feel it, laughed mockingly, and said, "Tomorrow morning, you’ll pile all the wood into one heap,

set fire to it, and burn it all down." He rose at dawn and began to gather the wood, but how could one man possibly gather an entire forest.

The task progressed slowly. However, the maiden didn’t abandon him. She brought him his lunch at noon, and after he ate, he lay his head on her lap and fell asleep.

When he awoke, the whole pile was ablaze, flames soaring into the sky. "Listen to me," said the maiden, "when the witch comes, she will give you all kinds of commands.

Do whatever she says, but do it without fear, or the fire will consume you. Finally, when you’ve done everything, grab her with both hands and throw her into the fire."

The maiden left, and soon the old woman snuck up to him. "Oh, I’m so cold," she said, "but that fire looks like it’ll keep me warm.

It’s good for my old bones. But, I see a log there that won’t burn. Bring it to me. When you do that, you’re free to go where you like."

title: The Drummer
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The drummer barely thought it through. He leaped into the flames, but they didn’t scorch him; not even one hair got singed.

He pulled the log out and set it down. Hardly had it touched the ground when it transformed into the beautiful maiden who had helped him.

Her sparkling golden clothes made it clear that she was the king’s daughter. But the old woman laughed wickedly and said,

"You think you’ve saved her, but not quite yet. Just as she was about to rush at the maiden and take her away, the drummer grabbed the old woman, lifted her high,

and hurled her into the fire, which closed around her like it was thrilled to have trapped an old witch.

The king’s daughter looked at the drummer, and when she saw that he was a handsome young man and remembered how he had risked

his life to save her, she took his hand and said, "You’ve risked everything for me, and I will do everything for you in return.

Promise me you’ll be true to me, and I’ll be your wife. We won’t lack for riches; we’ll have plenty from what the witch gathered here."

She led him into the house, which was filled with chests overflowing with treasures from the old woman. The maiden ignored the gold and silver,

and only took the precious stones. She was eager to leave the glass mountain. The drummer suggested, "Join me on my saddle, and we can fly down together like birds."

"I’d rather not use the old saddle," she replied. "If I turn my wishing ring, we’ll be home in no time." "Okay then,"

he responded, "Wish us right in front of the town gates." In a flash, they arrived there, but the drummer said,

"I’ll run and tell my parents the good news. Wait for me outside; I’ll be back quickly." "Oh, please be careful!" said the king’s daughter,

When you arrive, don’t kiss your parents on the right cheek, or else you will forget everything, and I’ll be left waiting here alone and deserted."

title: The Drummer
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"How could I ever forget you?" he laughed, promising to come back as fast as he could, shaking her hand on it. When he entered his parents' home, he looked so changed that no one recognized him,

since three days spent on the glass mountain felt like three years. He revealed his identity, and his parents wept with joy, hugging him.

His heart swelled with emotion, and he completely forgot the maiden's warning, kissing them on both cheeks.

But as soon as he kissed his parents on the right cheek,

every memory of the king's daughter faded from his mind. He emptied his pockets and scattered handfuls of jewels on the table.

His parents didn’t know what to make of the treasures. So, his father built a magnificent castle surrounded by gardens, woods, and meadows, as if it were meant for a prince.

When the castle was finished, his mother said, "I’ve found a bride for you, and the wedding will be in three days."

The son agreed to please his parents.

The heartbroken king’s daughter stood outside the town for a long time, waiting for the young man to return.

As evening fell, she thought, he must have kissed his parents on the right cheek and forgotten all about me.

Her heart ached with sadness as she wished herself into a lonely little hut in the woods, refusing to go back to her father’s court.

Each evening, she walked to the town and passed the young man’s home. He often caught glimpses of her,

but he no longer recognized her. Finally, she heard people talking about the wedding happening the next day.

Then she resolved, "I’ll see if I can win back his heart."

On the first day of the wedding festivities, she turned her wishing ring and said, "A dress as bright as the sun!" Instantly,

a dress appeared, shining like real sunbeams. When all the guests had gathered, she entered the hall.

Everyone was entranced by her beautiful dress, especially the bride, who loved pretty clothes. She approached the stranger and asked,

"Will you sell it to me?" "Not for gold," she replied, "but if I may spend the first night outside the door of the room where

title: The Drummer
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
your fiancé sleeps, I’ll gladly give it to you." The bride could not resist and agreed, but she secretly mixed a sleeping potion into the wine her husband-to-be had for dinner,

making him fall into a deep sleep. When everyone was asleep,

she opened the bedroom door a crack and called, "Drummer, drummer, I hope you can hear me!"

"Have you forgotten how much you cared for me?"

"Remember those hours we spent on the glass mountain?"

"That I saved your life from the witch?"

"Did you not promise me your devotion?" "Drummer, drummer, listen to me!"

But it was all for nothing; the drummer remained asleep, and when morning came, the king's daughter had to leave as she had come.

On the second evening, she turned her wishing ring again and said, "A dress as silvery as the moon!" When she appeared at

the feast in her soft moonbeam dress, it stirred the longing of the bride, who offered to trade for it, asking for a second night outside the fiancé's door.

That night, she called out in the stillness,

"Drummer, drummer, I hope you can hear me!"

"Have you forgotten how much you cared for me?"

"Remember those hours we spent on the glass mountain?"

"That I saved your life from the witch?"

"Did you not promise me your devotion?" "Drummer, drummer, please hear me!"

But the drummer, still under the spell of the sleeping potion, could not awaken. Sadly, the next morning she returned to her hut.

The household, however, had overheard the unknown maiden's lament and informed the bridegroom.

They explained he could not hear her because the girl he was to marry had slipped a sleep potion into his drink.

On the third evening, the king's daughter turned her ring again and said, "A dress sparkling like the stars!" When she arrived at the feast in that breathtaking dress,

the bride was utterly overwhelmed by its radiance.

She insisted, "I must have it!" The maiden offered it in exchange for yet another night outside the groom's door.

However, this time, the groom didn’t drink the wine prior to bed. Instead, he poured it behind the bed, and when all was quiet, he

heard a sweet voice calling to him,

"Drummer, drummer, I hope you can hear me!"

"Have you forgotten how much you cared for me?"

"Remember those hours we spent on the glass mountain?"

"That I saved your life from the witch?"

"Did you not promise me your devotion?" "Drummer, drummer, please listen to me!"

Suddenly, his memory flooded back. "Oh no!" he cried, "How could I have been so unfaithful? But that kiss on my parents' right cheek, in my joy,

that's what made me forget!" He leaped up, took the king's daughter by the hand, and joined her to his parents' bedroom.

"This is my one true bride!" he exclaimed. "If I marry anyone else, I would be making a terrible mistake." His parents, moving by how it all unfolded, agreed.

The lights in the hall sparked back to life, drums and trumpets were sounded, friends and family were invited, and the real wedding

was joyfully celebrated. The first bride received the beautiful dresses as compensation and declared herself.

title: The Duration of Life
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When God created the world, He was ready to decide how long each creature would live.

The donkey stepped forward and asked, "Lord, how long will I live?"

"Thirty years," God replied. "Does that sound good to you?"

The donkey sighed, "Lord, that seems like a long time. Just think about my tough life.

I have to carry heavy loads from dawn till dusk; pulling bags of grain to the mill so others can have bread.

All I ever get in return are blows and kicks. Please shorten my time!"

God felt compassion for him and reduced his lifespan by eighteen years.

The donkey left feeling relieved, and then the dog came by.

"How long do you want to live?" God asked the dog. "Thirty years might be fine for the donkey, but I think you’ll feel okay with that."

The dog replied, "Lord, is that your will? But think about how much I run. My feet won’t last that long.

If I lose my bark and my teeth for biting, what will I do but wander around and growl?"

God agreed and took off twelve years from his life.

Then the monkey arrived.

"You'll absolutely live for thirty years," said the Lord. "You don’t need to work like the donkey and the dog, and you'll always have fun."

The monkey replied, "Lord, it may seem that way, but it’s not what it looks like.

When millet-porridge falls from the sky, I haven't got a spoon.

Sure, I play tricks and make people laugh, but when I bite into an apple, it’s often sour.

Sadness can hide behind all that laughter. I can’t last thirty years like this."

God, feeling kind, decided to cut off ten years from his time.

Finally, a man appeared, full of life, health, and enthusiasm, and asked God for his lifespan.

"You shall live for thirty years," the Lord said. "Is that enough for you?"

"So short!" the man exclaimed. "Just when I build my house and start a fire in my own hearth,

when my trees begin to blossom and bear fruit, I have to die? Lord, please extend my years."

"I will add the donkey's eighteen years," said God.

"That still isn't enough!" replied the man.

"Then you will have the dog's twelve years as well."

"Still too little!" the man insisted.

"Alright then," God said, "you can also have the monkey's ten years, but that’s all you’ll get."

The man left feeling unsatisfied.

So, humans live for seventy years.

The first thirty years are vibrant, filled with health and joy, when they work happily and enjoy life.

Then come the donkey's eighteen years, where burdens are piled up, carrying grain for others, often met with kicks and blows.

Next come the dog's twelve years, spent lying in a corner, growling, with no teeth left to bite.

Finally, the last ten years are like the monkey's, when a person becomes foolish, does silly things, and becomes a plaything for children.

title: The Ear of Corn
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Back in the days when God walked among us,

the land was incredibly fruitful, far more than it is today.

The ears of corn didn’t just yield fifty or sixty; they grew four or five

hundred times more. Back then, the corn grew all the way from the bottom

to the very top of the stalk, and the ear's length matched the stalk's height.

However, people often take blessings for granted when they have too much.

They become indifferent and careless.

One day, a woman was walking by a cornfield when her little child,

running beside her, fell into a puddle and got her frock dirty.

In frustration, the mother grabbed a handful of the beautiful ears of corn.

Just then, God happened to walk by, saw this, and became angry.

He declared, "From now on, the stalks of corn will no longer bear ears.

People are no longer worthy of heavenly gifts."

The bystanders, hearing this, were terrified.

They fell to their knees and prayed for mercy,

hoping that He would leave something on the stalks,

even if the people didn’t deserve it, for the innocent chickens that would otherwise starve.

God, seeing their suffering, felt compassion for them and granted their request.

So, the ears of corn were left as they grow today.

title: The Elves
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a wealthy king who had three daughters.

Every day, they would stroll through the palace garden, and the king was truly passionate about fine trees.

However, there was one tree he cherished above all others.

If anyone dared to pick an apple from it, he wished them the worst fate imaginable.

When harvest time arrived, the apples on this tree were as red as blood.

Every day, the three daughters stood under the tree, hoping the wind might drop an apple, but they never found one.

The tree was so heavy with fruit that its branches nearly touched the ground.

Eventually, the youngest daughter longed for an apple and told her sisters, 'Our father loves us too much to wish us any harm; he’d only do that to strangers.'

While she spoke, she plucked a large apple from the tree and ran to her sisters, saying, 'You must taste this, my dear sisters! I’ve never had anything so delicious!'

Then both of her sisters tried some of the apple, and just like that, all three of them fell deep into the ground.

When noon arrived, the king called for his daughters to join him for dinner, but they were nowhere to be seen.

He searched everywhere in the palace and garden, but could not find them.

Worried, he announced across the land that anyone who could bring his daughters back would have the chance to marry one of them.

So many young men set out on the search that they could hardly be counted, for everyone adored the three daughters for their kindness and beauty.

Three young hunters also set out, and after eight days of traveling, they arrived at a grand castle.

Inside, they found beautiful rooms, and one had a table laid with delicious food that was still warm and steaming, yet no one was in sight.

They waited there for half a day, and the food remained hot, but eventually, hunger got the better of them.

They sat down to eat and agreed that they would stay in the castle; one of them, chosen by drawing lots, would remain inside while the others looked for the princesses.

title: The Elves
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The lots fell on the eldest, so the next day, the two younger hunters set out to search while he stayed back.

At noon, a tiny little man came by and asked for a piece of bread.

The huntsman took some of the bread he had found, cut a slice, and was about to hand it to the man.

But as he passed it over, the little man dropped it and asked the huntsman to pick it up again.

As the huntsman bent down to retrieve it, the little man took a stick, grabbed him by the hair, and gave him a good whacking.

The next day, the second huntsman stayed home and faced the same fate.

When the two returned in the evening, the eldest asked, 'How did you fare?'

'Not well at all,' he replied, and they lamented their misfortune together, but they kept quiet about it to the youngest brother.

They didn't like him at all and often called him stupid Hans for his lack of worldly knowledge.

On the third day, the youngest brother stayed home, and yet again, the little man came asking for bread.

When Hans offered some, the man dropped it again and asked for help in picking it up.

'What, can't you pick it up yourself?' Hans said. 'If you won't put in a little effort for your daily bread, then why should you have it?'

The man became angry, saying he wasn't meant to do that, but the huntsman refused to back down.

To Hans's surprise, the little man started screaming and pleaded, 'Stop, let me go, and I’ll show you where the king's daughters are.'

When Hans heard this, he stopped beating him, and the man revealed he was a gnome and promised to lead him to the king's daughters if he followed.

He pointed to a deep well that was dry, and explained that he knew Hans's companions wouldn’t treat him fairly.

To rescue the king's daughters, Hans would need to take a large basket, sit in it with a knife and a bell, and be lowered down.

title: The Elves
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
There were three rooms below, each containing a princess who would be battling a head-slicing dragon.

That evening, when the two brothers came back, they asked Hans how he had gotten on.

'Pretty well so far,' he replied, explaining he had only seen the little man who asked for bread.

'He dropped it and wanted me to pick it up, but I didn’t want to. So, I lost my temper and gave him a beating, and he ended up telling me where the king's daughters were.'

The other two grew furious upon hearing this, their faces turning green and yellow.

The next morning, they decided to visit the well together and drew lots to see who would be the first to go down.

Once again, the lot fell on the eldest brother, who took his seat in the basket with the bell.

'If I ring this, you need to pull me back up immediately,' he instructed.

After being lowered for a short distance, he rang the bell, and they quickly pulled him back up.

The second brother went down next, and he did the same as the first.

Finally, it was the youngest brother's turn, and he decided to go all the way down.

Once he stepped out of the basket, he took his knife and listened outside the first door, hearing the dragon snoring loudly.

He slowly opened the door and found one princess sitting there, lousing nine dragon heads in her lap.

Without a moment's hesitation, he drew his knife and struck them off one by one.

The princess jumped up, wrapped her arms around his neck, and showered him with kisses, giving him a golden stomacher to wear.

He then moved on to the second princess, who had a dragon with five heads, and freed her too.

Lastly, he saved the youngest princess, who had to battle a four-headed dragon.

They all rejoiced, hugging and kissing him nonstop.

He rang the bell loudly so those above would hear, and placed the princesses one by one into the basket, bringing them up.

But when it was time for him to go up, he remembered the gnome's warning about his brothers.

title: The Elves
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
So instead of climbing in, he picked up a heavy stone and placed it in the basket.

Halfway up, his false brothers cut the rope, causing the basket with the stone to fall back down, assuming he was dead.

They ran off with the princesses, convincing them to tell their father it was they who rescued them.

They went to the king and each claimed a princess in marriage.

Meanwhile, the youngest huntsman wandered through the three chambers in despair, fearing he would perish there.

He spotted a flute hanging on the wall and said, 'What are you doing up there? This place is far too gloomy.'

He gazed at the dragon heads and remarked, 'You can’t help me either.'

He paced back and forth until he smoothed the ground beneath him.

Eventually, inspiration struck, and he picked up the flute, playing a few notes.

To his amazement, a swarm of elves appeared, and with each note he played, another one joined them.

They asked him what he wished for, and he said he wanted to return to the surface.

The elves seized him by every hair on his head and soared back to the earth.

Once above ground, he hurried to the king's palace just as one of the weddings for the princesses was about to take place.

He entered the room where the king and his daughters were, and when the princesses saw him, they fainted.

The king, angered by this scene, ordered him to be thrown in prison, suspecting he had harmed the girls.

However, when the princesses regained their composure, they begged the king to set him free.

The king asked why, and they replied that they weren’t allowed to reveal the reason, but their father told them to share it with the stove.

The king listened at the door and overheard everything.

Outraged, he had the two brothers hanged and awarded his youngest daughter to the third huntsman.

At the wedding, I wore a pair of glass shoes, and as I struck them against a stone, they made a lovely sound.

title: The Elves (two stories)
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a hardworking servant girl who always kept things tidy.

She swept the house every day and tossed the dust outside.

One morning, just as she was about to return to her chores, she noticed a letter on the dust heap.

Since she couldn't read, she put her broom in the corner and took the letter to her employers.

To her surprise, it was an invitation from the elves, asking her to hold a child for them at its christening.

Unsure of what to do, she eventually agreed after they insisted that turning down such an invitation wasn't right.

Soon, three elves appeared and led her to a hollow mountain where the little ones lived.

Everything there was tiny yet more elegant and beautiful than she could have imagined.

The baby's mother lay in a bed made of black ebony, adorned with pearls, and the blankets were embroidered with gold.

The cradle was made of ivory, and the bathtub was pure gold.

The girl stood as the godmother, but when she wanted to leave, the elves eagerly asked her to stay for three days.

So, she stayed and enjoyed herself while the little folks did everything they could to make her happy.

At last, it was time for her to head home, but first, they filled her pockets with money and guided her back out of the mountain.

When she got home, she was eager to start her work again, grabbing the broom that was still in the corner.

But as she began to sweep, some strangers emerged from the house and asked her who she was and what she was doing there.

To her shock, she realized that she hadn't been gone for just three days, but for seven years, and her previous employers had passed away.

Meanwhile, a mother had discovered that the elves had taken her child out of its crib, leaving behind a changeling with a big head and wide eyes.

This changeling only wanted to eat and drink, and in her worry, the mother sought advice from her neighbor.

The neighbor told her to take the changeling into the kitchen, place it on the hearth, light a fire, and boil some water in two eggshells, which would make the changeling laugh.

If the changeling laughed, it would be over for him.

The mother followed her neighbor's instructions carefully.

When she placed the eggshells with water on the fire, the changeling said, "I'm as old as the western forest, but I've never seen anyone boil anything in an eggshell!"

And then he began to laugh.

While he was laughing, a whole bunch of little elves showed up, brought back the rightful child, and placed it on the hearth.

title: The Fisherman and His Wife
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there lived a fisherman and his wife.

They resided in a small pigsty by the sea, and every day he would head out to fish.

He cast his line over and over. One day, while sitting with his

fishing rod, staring at the sparkling water, he simply sat there.

Then suddenly, his line plunged deep into the water, and when

he pulled it up again, he found a large flounder. To his surprise,

the flounder spoke and said, "Please, fisherman, let me go! I'm not really a flounder, but an enchanted prince. What good would it do you to kill me? I wouldn’t taste good. Just toss me back into the ocean and set me free."

The fisherman replied, "Why should I waste words on that? A fish that can talk deserves to live. I’ll put you back."

With that, he released the fish back into the clear water, and the flounder swam away, leaving a trail of blood behind.

The fisherman then returned to his wife in the pigsty.

"Did you catch anything today, dear?" the wife asked.

"No," he replied. "I did catch a flounder that insisted he was an enchanted prince, so I set him free."

"Did you wish for anything first?" asked the wife.

"No, what should I wish for?" he said.

"It’s tough living in this stinky pigsty!" she complained. "You could have wished for a cozy little hut for us. Go back and call the fish. I bet he'll grant us that."

"But why should I go back?" said the man.

"You caught him and let him go again! He should be willing to help us," she insisted.

The fisherman didn’t want to, but he didn’t want to oppose his wife either.

When he got back to the sea, the water had turned a murky green and yellow, no longer calm, so he stood still and said,

"I wish he would do as I ask."

Then the flounder swam up to him and asked, "What does she want now?"

title: The Fisherman and His Wife
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"I caught you, and my wife says I should have wished for something," said the fisherman. "She doesn’t want to live in the pigsty anymore. She’d like a hut."

"Alright then, she has it," said the flounder.

When the fisherman returned home, his wife was no longer in the sty.

Instead, a little hut stood there, and she was sitting on a bench in front of the door.

She took his hand and said, "Come inside! Isn't this much better?"

They entered, and there was a small porch, a cozy living room, a bedroom, and a kitchen filled with everything they could ever need.

Behind the hut, there was a little yard with chickens and ducks, and a garden blooming with flowers and fruit.

"Look, isn’t this nice?" the wife said.

"Yes, it is," the husband replied. "We can be happy here. Let’s just enjoy it."

"Just enjoying it is fine, but..." the wife began.

They had a peaceful week or two, and then the wife said, "Listen, dear, this hut feels too small for us. The garden and yard are tiny too. The flounder could have given us a bigger house. I want to live in a grand stone castle. Go tell the flounder to give us a castle."

"Oh, wife, the hut is fine. Why should we live in a castle?" replied the man.

"Just go! The flounder can do it!" she insisted.

"No," said the husband. "The flounder just gave us this hut. I don’t want to go back too soon, or he might get mad."

"Go! He can easily do it, and he’ll be happy to help!" she urged.

The man felt heavy-hearted, but he didn’t want to refuse her. Finally, he went.

When he reached the sea, the water was now dark purple and deep blue, much rougher than before.

He stood there and said,

title: The Fisherman and His Wife
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"I wish he would do as I ask."

"What does she want, this time?" asked the flounder.

"Oh dear," said the fisherman, trembling. "She wants to live in a grand stone castle."

"Go to her, then. She’s standing at the door," responded the flounder.

The fisherman set off to go home, and when he arrived, he discovered a grand stone palace instead.

His wife was just walking up the steps as he approached.

"Come in!" she called, and he went inside with her.

Inside the castle was a huge hall with marble floors and many servants opening doors. The walls were adorned with beautiful tapestries,

and there were tables set with the finest food and drinks, nearly overwhelming the surfaces.

Behind the castle, there was a large courtyard with stables for horses and cows, as well as elegant carriages.

They had a gorgeous garden filled with vibrant flowers and fruit trees, and a park that stretched for half a mile, with deer and rabbits.

"Look, isn’t this beautiful?" the wife said.

"Yes, indeed," said the man. "This is wonderful. Let’s enjoy our time in this beautiful castle together."

"Let’s think about it later," the wife replied.

So they went to bed.

The next morning, the wife woke up first as dawn broke.

From her bed, she gazed at the stunning landscape spread out before her.

Her husband was still stretching out, so she nudged him.

"Get up, dear, and take a look out the window! Why shouldn’t we rule over all that land? We should be kings! Go tell the flounder, we want to be kings!"

"Oh, wife, why should we be kings? I have no desire to be king," replied the man.

"Well, if you won’t be king, then I will!" she declared. "Go to the flounder. I want to be king!"

"Oh, why would you want that?" the husband asked, worried.

"Just go! I must be king!" she insisted.

title: The Fisherman and His Wife
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He went, feeling unhappy and troubled by his wife’s ambition.

"This doesn’t feel right..." he thought to himself.

When he got to the shore, the sea was dark-grey, and the water roared like a storm.

He stood there and said,

"I wish he would do as I ask."

"What does she want now?" said the flounder.

"Oh, my wife wants to be king," he admitted.

"Go to her. She is king already," the flounder replied.

Reluctantly, the man returned home and found that the castle had grown even larger.

Now it had a huge tower with splendid decorations.

When he entered, everything was real marble and gold, draped in velvet.

As the grand doors swung open, he saw the court in all its glory.

His wife sat on a tall throne made of gold and diamonds, a beautiful crown on her head.

In one hand, she held a golden scepter, and beside her stood her attendants, each a little shorter than the last.

He walked up to her and said, "So, now you’re a king?"

"Yes!" she replied proudly.

The fisherman observed her and said, "Now that you are the king, let’s just leave it at that. We should wish for nothing more."

"Oh no, husband!" the wife said, her voice tinged with anxiety. "Time moves too slowly, I can’t take it! I must be emperor too!"

"Why do you want to be emperor?" he asked in disbelief.

"Just go to the flounder. I will be emperor!"

"Oh no, he can't make you emperor! There’s only one emperor! You can’t just ask him that," he tried to explain.

"But I’m the king, and you’re just my husband. Go now!" she ordered.

Reluctantly, he went back, clouds of doubt swirling in his mind.

What would happen next?

As he reached the water, it was thick and black, roiling and frothing.

title: The Fisherman and His Wife
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He shouted again,

"I wish he would do as I ask!"

"What does she want now?" asked the flounder.

"She wants to be emperor," said the fisherman.

"Go back to her. She is an emperor already," the flounder said.

When he returned, he found the palace now glimmering with polished marble and gold.

Soldiers marched out front, trumpets blaring.

Inside, nobles were acting as servants. The grand doors opened to him.

His wife sat on a colossal throne, towering two miles high, wearing a crown that was three yards tall, glistening with jewels.

In her hands she held the scepter and orb while the guards flanked her.

Before her, princes knelt in reverence.

The fisherman approached and asked, "So now you’re an emperor?"

"Yes, I am!" she declared.

He stood and said, "Now that you’re an emperor, don’t you think it’s enough?"

"Husband, now that I’m an emperor, I want to be pope too. Go tell the flounder!"

"Oh, that’s too much! You can’t be pope! There’s only one pope!" he exclaimed.

"I’ll be pope!" she insisted.

"No, I don’t want to ask him that. It doesn’t seem right!" he replied.

"If he can make me an emperor, he can make me a pope!" she snapped.

"I will be pope! Go now!" she commanded.

Fearful, he stumbled away like a lost soul.

Heavy winds whipped over the land, dark clouds rolled in, and a great storm loomed.

He saw ships struggling on the waves, cannon fire echoing in the distance.

Yet, amidst the tempest, a calm patch of sky lingered.

Filled with dread, he uttered,

"I wish he would do as I ask!"

"What does she want now?" asked the flounder.

"She wants to be like God—" the fisherman stammered.

"Go to her. You’ll find her back in the pigsty," replied the flounder.

And there they remain.

title: The Flail from Heaven
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
A farmer was heading out to plow his field with a pair of oxen.

When he arrived in the field, he noticed that both animals were starting to grow horns.

The horns kept growing, and when he decided it was time to go home, they had become so large that the oxen couldn't fit through the gate.

Fortunately, a butcher happened to walk by at that moment, and the farmer struck a deal with him.

The agreement was that the farmer would bring the butcher a peck of rape-seed, and in return, the butcher would give him a brabant taler for every seed he brought.

That seemed like a great deal. The peasant headed home, carrying the peck of rape-seed on his back.

However, on his way, he accidentally dropped one seed from the bag.

The butcher paid him fairly as agreed, but if the farmer hadn't lost that one seed, he would have received one more taler.

By the time he got back, the seed had grown into a tree that reached up to the sky.

Thinking this was a perfect opportunity, the farmer decided to see what the angels were up to up there.

So, he climbed up and spotted the angels threshing oats.

While watching them, he noticed the tree he was standing on starting to wobble.

Curious, he peered down and saw that someone was about to cut the tree down.

"If I fall from here, it would be disastrous," he thought, and in a panic, he didn't know how to save himself except by gathering some of the chaff from the oats nearby and twisting it into a rope.

He also grabbed a hoe and a flail that were lying around in heaven, then lowered himself down using the rope.

However, he landed right in the middle of a deep hole.

Luckily, he had the hoe with him, so he used it to dig himself a set of steps to climb back up, taking the flail with him as proof of his adventure, ensuring that no one could deny his tale.

title: The Four Skilful Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a poor man with four sons. When they grew up,

he said to them, "My dear children, it's time for you to head out into the

world. I have nothing to give you, so go out, learn a trade, and find your own way."

So, the four brothers gathered their sticks, said goodbye to their father, and left

through the town gate together. After traveling for a bit,

they arrived at a crossroads that branched off in four different directions.

The eldest said, "This is where we must part ways, but let's meet again here in four years.

In the meantime, let’s see what we can achieve."

With that, they each went their separate ways. The eldest met a man who asked

where he was headed and what he planned to do. "I want to learn a trade," he replied.

The man offered, "Come with me and become a thief." "No," he answered, "that's no longer considered a

respectable trade, and it usually ends badly." The man said, "Oh, don't worry about the gallows.

I will teach you how to steal things that no one else could ever get away with, and nobody will ever catch you."

Convinced, he went along with the man and soon became a skilled thief, so crafty that nothing was safe from him.

The second brother encountered a man who asked the same question: what did he want to learn in life?

"I’m not sure yet," he replied. "Then join me and be an astronomer. There's nothing better than that, for you will see everything."

He liked the idea and became such a skilled astronomer that when he had learned everything,

and was ready to move on, his master gave him a telescope and said, "With this device,

you can see everything happening on earth or in the sky—nothing will be hidden from you."

A huntsman took the third brother under his wing and provided him with excellent training in everything about hunting,

so he became an expert. When he left, his master gave him a gun and said, "This will never fail you; whatever you aim at, you'll hit."

title: The Four Skilful Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The youngest brother met a man who asked him what his plans were. "What about being a tailor?" the man suggested.

"No thanks," the boy replied. "Sitting hunched over all day pushing a needle back and forth doesn't sound appealing to me."

"But you're mistaken," the man responded. "With me, you'll learn a different kind of tailoring—one that's respectable and honorable."

So he decided to give it a chance and learned the craft from the ground up. When they parted ways, the man gave him a needle and said,

"With this, you can sew anything together—whether it's as delicate as an egg or as tough as steel, it will all become one piece.

No seams will show."

After four years had passed, the four brothers arrived back at the crossroads at the same time.

They hugged, kissed each other, and went home to their father. "So now," he said joyfully,

"the wind has brought you back to me." They shared stories of everything that had happened, each explaining their newfound skills.

They gathered in front of the house under a giant tree, and their father said, "Let's see what you can do."

He looked up and asked his second son, "There’s a chaffinch’s nest between two branches at the top of this tree.

Can you tell me how many eggs are in it?" The astronomer took his telescope, looked up, and said, "There are five eggs."

The father then turned to the eldest, saying, "Get those eggs down without bothering the bird that's sitting on them."

The skilled thief climbed up and carefully took the five eggs from under the bird, who didn't notice a thing.

He came down with them, and the father placed one egg on each corner of the table,

and the fifth one right in the middle. Then he told the huntsman, "You have one shot; shoot me five eggs in two, right through the center."

The huntsman aimed carefully and shot all five eggs as the father requested, hitting them perfectly.

title: The Four Skilful Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He must have had some magic powder to shoot around corners! "Now it's your turn," the father said to the youngest.

"Sew the eggs back together, as well as the baby birds inside, without hurting them." The tailor brought out his needle and stitched them as requested.

Once he was finished, the thief had to climb back up the tree to return the eggs to the nest without the bird noticing.

The mother bird completed her incubation, and after a few days, the hatchlings emerged with little red lines around their necks.

"Well," the old man said to his sons, "you should be praised to the skies!

You’ve used your time wisely and learned something valuable. I can't tell which of you deserves the most credit. That will be clear if you ever need to use your talents."

Not long after, there was a commotion in the land—the king's daughter was kidnapped by a dragon.

The king was very distressed and declared that whoever brought her back would have her hand in marriage.

The four brothers looked at each other and said, "This is the perfect chance to show what we can do!" They decided to work together to rescue the princess.

"I can find her easily," the astronomer said as he peered through his telescope. He pointed and said, "There she is—far away, on a rock in the sea, with the dragon guarding her."

He went to the king, requested a ship for his brothers and him, and they sailed over the sea until they reached the rock.

There sat the king's daughter, with the dragon sleeping beside her. The huntsman said, "I can't shoot; I might hit the beautiful maiden, too."

"Let me try my skills," the thief said, sneaking up and stealing the princess right from under the dragon's nose without waking it.

Overjoyed, they hurried back to the ship and sailed into the open sea.

However, when the dragon woke up and realized his princess was gone, he angrily chased after them, roaring.

Just as he swooped down on their ship, the huntsman raised his rifle and shot the dragon straight through the heart.

title: The Four Skilful Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The beast fell dead, but he was so huge that his body shattered the entire ship.

Luckily, they managed to grab some planks and swam about in the sea.

In another moment of danger, the tailor sprang into action, took out his magical needle,

and with a few quick stitches, rejoined the planks and gathered together the remaining pieces of the ship.

Soon, he skillfully sewed everything back together, and before long, their ship was seaworthy again, allowing them to head home safely.

When the king saw his daughter again, there was a grand celebration. He told the four brothers, "One of you shall marry her, but you must decide among yourselves who that will be."

This led to a heated debate, as each brother claimed the right to her. The astronomer said,

"If I hadn't seen the princess, none of your skills would have mattered—she’s mine!" The thief countered, "But if I hadn’t rescued her from the dragon, all of you would have been lost—so she's mine!"

The huntsman chimed in, "You all would have been dragon food if my shot hadn't saved you—she's mine!" The tailor concluded, "And if I hadn't stitched the ship back together, you all would have drowned. She’s mine!"

The king listened and announced, "Each of you has an equal claim, but since none can have her, I will reward you all with half a kingdom each."

The brothers were satisfied with this solution and said, "It's better this way than to fight among ourselves."

So each received half a kingdom, and they lived happily ever after with their father for as long as it pleased God.

title: The Fox and the Cat
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One day, the cat ran into the fox in the forest.

She thought to herself that he was clever, experienced, and well-respected.

So, she greeted him in a friendly way.

"Good day, Mr. Fox! How have you been? How's everything going for you during these tough times?"

The fox, full of arrogance, looked the cat over, unsure if he wanted to respond.

Finally, he sneered, "Oh, you pitiful fur cleaner, you speckled fool, you starving mouse-chaser! What makes you think you can ask how I’m doing? What have you learned? How many tricks do you know?"

"I know just one," the cat replied modestly.

"What trick is that?" the fox asked.

"When the hounds are after me, I can leap into a tree and save myself."

"Is that all?" scoffed the fox. "I’m skilled in a hundred tricks, and I have a bag full of cunning ways."

"I feel sorry for you. Come with me, and I’ll teach you how to escape from the hounds."

At that moment, a hunter and four dogs appeared.

The cat quickly climbed up a tree and nestled among the branches, where the leaves completely hid her.

"Open your sack, Mr. Fox! Open your sack!" the cat called down to him.

But the dogs had already caught him and were holding him tight.

"Oh, Mr. Fox!" the cat exclaimed. "With all your tricks, you’re left in a tight spot! If only you could climb like me, you wouldn’t be facing this fate."

title: The Fox and the Geese
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One day, a fox wandered into a meadow where a flock of plump geese was happily gathered.

Seeing them, he grinned and said, "I’ve arrived just in time."

"You all look so lovely sitting together that I can gobble you up one by one."

The geese squawked in panic, jumping up and pleading desperately for their lives.

But the fox wouldn’t hear any of it and replied, "There’s no mercy here. You must die."

Finally, one brave goose spoke up, saying, "If we have to give up our lives, please grant us just one favor: let us say one last prayer.

We want to die without regrets. After that, we can line up for you to choose the fattest one."

The fox thought for a moment and replied, "That sounds fair, and it's a respectful request. Go ahead and pray; I’ll wait."

The first goose started a long, heartfelt prayer, repeating, "Ga, ga," over and over. But she was so caught up in her prayer that the second goose didn’t wait for her turn.

The third and fourth joined in too, and soon all the geese were cackling together.

As for their prayers, they would continue on for a while longer. We’ll see what happens next after they’re done.

title: The Fox and the Horse
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
There was a peasant who had a loyal horse that had grown old and was no longer able to work.

Because of this, his master stopped feeding him.

"I have no use for you anymore," he said.

"But I wish you well. If you can bring me a lion, I’ll take care of you, but for now, you need to leave my stable."

With that, he drove the horse out into the open country.

Feeling sad, the horse went to the forest to find some shelter from the weather.

It was there that he met a fox, who asked, "Why do you look so down and wander alone?"

"Oh, it’s because greed and loyalty don’t go hand in hand," the horse replied.

"My master has forgotten all the years of service I've given him. Since I can't plow anymore, he won't give me food and has kicked me out."

The fox asked, "Didn’t he give you any chance?"

"The chance was a poor one," the horse said. "He said if I could still get him a lion, he would keep me, but he knows I can’t do that anymore."

The fox replied, "I’ll help you. Just lie down and pretend you’re dead; don’t move at all."

Following the fox's advice, the horse laid down.

The fox went to find a lion that lived nearby and said, "There’s a dead horse outside; come with me for a feast."

The lion followed the fox, and when they were both standing by the horse, the fox said, "You know, it’s not really comfortable for you here. How about I tie it to your tail so you can drag it into your cave and eat it in peace?"

The lion liked this idea, so he lay down and kept still while the fox tied the horse to him.

But instead of just tying the horse, the fox cleverly bound the lion’s legs together with the horse's tail.

It was so tightly fastened that no amount of strength could break free.

Once he was done, the fox tapped the horse on the shoulder and said, "Now, pull, white horse, pull!"

The horse sprang into action and started pulling the lion along.

The lion roared loudly, startling all the birds in the forest, but the horse continued to drag him down the path toward his master’s home.

When the master saw the lion, he changed his attitude and said to the horse, "You can stay with me and live well."

He gave him plenty to eat from that day on.

title: The Frog King, or Iron Henry
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Long ago, when wishes could still come true, there was a king

who had many beautiful daughters, but the youngest was so stunning

that even the sun, which had seen so much, was amazed every time

it shone on her face. Close to the king's castle was a dark, vast

forest, and underneath an old lime tree in that forest was a well.

On warm days, the king's daughter would go into the forest and

sit by the cool fountain. Whenever she felt bored, she would take

her golden ball, throw it high into the air, and catch it— it was

her favorite toy.

One day, the princess's golden ball didn't land in her little hand,

but instead rolled away and fell into the water. The

king's daughter watched as it disappeared into the deep well, a

place so deep that she couldn’t see the bottom. That made her

start to cry, and she sobbed louder and louder, unable to be

consoled. Just then, someone called out, "What’s wrong, princess?

You’re crying so hard that even a stone would feel sorry for you."

She looked around and saw a frog poking its big, ugly head out of the water.

"Oh, it’s just you, old water splasher," she said. "I’m crying for my golden ball,

which fell into the well."  "Don’t cry. I can help you," said the frog.

"But what will you give me if I fetch your toy for you?"  "Anything you want, dear frog,"

she replied. "My clothes, my pearls and jewels, and even my golden crown."

The frog said, "I don’t want your clothes or jewelry or your crown, but if you will love me,

let me be your friend and playmate, sit by you at your little table, eat off your golden plate,

drink from your little cup, and sleep in your little bed—if you promise me that, I will go down and fetch your ball back."

"Oh, sure," she said, "I promise you whatever you want, if only you bring back my ball."

title: The Frog King, or Iron Henry
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But then she thought, "What a silly frog! All he does is sit in the water and croak.

He can't possibly be a companion for anyone human."

But after making the promise, the frog put its head underwater and disappeared.

A little while later, he resurfaced with the ball in his mouth and tossed it onto the grass.

The king's daughter was thrilled to see her beloved toy again.

She picked it up and ran off, completely forgetting about the poor frog.

"Wait, wait!" called the frog. "Take me with you! I can't hop as fast as you can!"

But her ignoring him was no surprise as she dashed home, leaving the frog to return to his well.

The next day, while she sat with the king and all the courtiers, enjoying her meal from her golden plate,

she suddenly heard a splashing sound coming up the marble staircase.

When it reached the top, it knocked at the door and called out, "Princess, youngest princess, open the door for me."

She rushed to see who it was, but when she opened the door, there sat the frog.

In a panic, she slammed the door shut and hurried back to the table.

The king noticed her heart racing and asked, "My child, what frightens you? Is there a giant outside wishing to take you away?"

"Oh no," she said. "It’s not a giant, it’s just a nasty frog."

"What could a frog possibly want from you?" the king inquired.

"Father, yesterday while playing by the well in the forest, my golden ball fell into the water.

I cried so much that the frog brought it back for me, and I promised him he could be my friend.

But I didn’t think he could actually come out of the water. And now he wants to come in!"

Just then, there was another knock at the door. "Princess, youngest princess, open the door for me.

Don’t you remember what you promised me yesterday by the cool waters of the well?"

title: The Frog King, or Iron Henry
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Princess, please, let me in!"

Then the king said, "You must keep your promise. Go and let him in."

Reluctantly, she opened the door, and the frog hopped in, following her step for step to her chair.

He then said, "Lift me up beside you."  She hesitated, but the king insisted she do it.

Once the frog hopped onto the chair, he wanted to come to the table.

When he was finally on the table, he said, "Now, can you push your little golden plate a bit closer so we can eat together?"

She did this, but it was clear she wasn’t happy about it.

The frog enjoyed his meal, but every bite she took felt like it was choking her.

Finally, he said, "I've eaten and I'm satisfied. Now I’m tired. Can you take me to your room and make up your little silken bed?"

The king’s daughter started to cry because she didn’t want the cold frog in her nice clean bed.

But the king got angry and said, "Someone who helped you when you were in need shouldn’t be despised by you now."

So, she picked up the frog with two fingers and carried him upstairs, placing him in a corner.

But once she was in bed, he crawled over to her, saying, "I want to sleep just like you. Lift me up or I’ll tell your father!"

This made her very angry, and she hurled him against the wall with all her might, saying, "Stay quiet, you pesky frog!"

But when he landed, he was no longer a frog, but a handsome prince with kind, beautiful eyes.

He explained that he had been cursed by a wicked witch and only she could break the spell.

Now, by her father's will, he was her companion and soon to be her husband.

He told her they would return to his kingdom together the next day.

That night, they fell asleep, and the next morning, as the sun woke them,

a carriage pulled up with eight white horses, each wearing white ostrich feathers and harnessed with golden chains.

Behind them stood the young king's servant, Faithful Henry.

Faithful Henry had been so heartbroken when his master was turned into a frog that he had three iron bands placed around his heart,

to keep it from bursting with sadness.

The carriage was ready to take the young king back to his kingdom.

Faithful Henry helped them both into the carriage and took his place behind with a heart full of joy.

As they traveled partway, the prince heard a cracking sound behind him, like something breaking.

He turned and exclaimed, "Henry, the carriage is falling apart!"

"No, my lord," Henry replied. "It’s not the carriage. It’s a band from my heart, which was placed there in my deep sadness when you were a frog,

locked away in that well."

Once again, they heard a crack, and each time, the prince thought the carriage was breaking.

But it was only the bands releasing from Faithful Henry's heart because his master was finally free.

title: The Giant and the Tailor
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
There was a tailor who was full of bragging but really terrible at his craft.

He decided to take a trip to explore the world for a while.

As soon as he could manage it, he left his workshop and began to wander,

traversing hills and valleys,

sometimes going one way, sometimes another, but always moving forward. One day,

he spotted a steep hill in the distance, rising out of a dark, wild forest.

It seemed to touch the clouds.

"Thunder and lightning!" exclaimed the tailor. "What is that?" His curiosity pushed him to venture closer.

And when he did, he was completely amazed—the tower had legs and jumped over the hill in a single bound, standing before him as an enormous giant.

"What do you want here, you tiny little thing?" the giant boomed, his voice rumbling like thunder.

The tailor stammered, "I just want to look around to see if I can earn a bit of bread for myself in this forest."

"If that's what you're looking for, you can work for me," the giant replied. "If it must be, why not? What will I earn?"

"You'll find out. Every year, three hundred sixty-five days, and in leap year, one extra day. Does that work for you?"

"Sure," said the tailor, thinking to himself that a person has to cut his coat according to his cloth. "I’ll try to leave quickly."

At this, the giant told him, "Go fetch me a jug of water."

"Shouldn't I just bring the whole well too?" the boastful tailor asked as he grabbed a pitcher and headed to the water.

"What, the well and spring too?" grumbled the giant to himself, for he was a bit of a simpleton, but he was still cautious. "That fellow isn’t an idiot; he’s got a trick up his sleeve. Be careful, old Hans, this isn’t a regular servant you’ve got here."

When the tailor returned with the water, the giant commanded him to go into the forest and chop a couple of pieces of wood.

"Why not take the whole forest with one swing?" the arrogant tailor retorted.

"The entire forest, young and old, every tree, rough and smooth, along with the well and its spring, too!" the gullible giant muttered, growing even more fearful.

"This guy can do a lot more than just bake apples, and he must have a secret ability. Watch out, old Hans, he’s no ordinary servant."

Once the tailor brought back the wood, the giant insisted he should hunt down a few wild boars for dinner.

"Why not just get me a thousand in one shot and bring them here?" the cheeky tailor asked.

"What?" gasped the already frightened giant.

"Just rest for the night and get some sleep."

The giant was so terrified he couldn’t close his eyes all night, worrying about how to get rid of this troublesome servant.

As time passed, the next morning, the giant took the tailor to a marsh surrounded by willow trees.

Then the giant told him, "Listen, tailor, seat yourself on one of those willow branches. I want to see if you are strong enough to bend it down."

Suddenly, the tailor sat on the branch, holding his breath and trying to make himself heavy, so heavy that the bough bent.

But when he finally had to breathe, he flew into the air—sadly, he hadn't put his goose in his pocket—and he disappeared into the sky, which delighted the giant.

"If the tailor hasn’t landed, he must still be floating around up there!"

title: The Girl Without Hands
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once, there was a miller who had gradually fallen into poverty.

All that remained to him was his mill and a big apple tree behind it.

One day, while he was out in the forest gathering wood, he was approached by an old man he had never seen before.

The old man asked, "Why are you burdening yourself with chopping wood? I can make you rich if you promise me what's behind your mill."

The miller thought, what could that be other than my apple tree? So he agreed and signed a written promise for the stranger.

The old man laughed mockingly and said, "In three years, I'll come back for what belongs to me," and then he left.

When the miller returned home, his wife greeted him and said, "Tell me, husband, where did this sudden wealth come from? Every box and chest is overflowing, and I don’t know how it happened."

He replied, "A stranger I met in the forest promised me great riches. In return, I've promised him what’s behind the mill. We can easily give him the big apple tree."

His wife, terrified, said, "Oh, husband, that must have been the devil. He wasn’t talking about the apple tree, but rather our daughter who is standing behind the mill!"

The miller's daughter was a beautiful and devout girl, living in the fear of God and avoiding sin for those three years.

So when the time was up, and the day came for the devil to take her, she washed herself and drew a circle around herself with chalk.

The devil arrived early, but he couldn't get close to her.

Angrily, he told the miller, "Remove all the water from her so she can't wash herself, or else I have no power over her."

Frightened, the miller did just that.
