The next morning, the devil appeared again, but the girl had wept on her hands, and they were clean.

Once more, he couldn't approach her and angrily commanded the miller, "Cut off her hands, or I have no power over her!"

title: The Girl Without Hands
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The miller was horrified and exclaimed, "How could I cut off my own child's hands?"

Then the devil threatened him, saying, "If you refuse, you belong to me, and I'll take you instead."

The father, scared, agreed to do it.

He approached his daughter and said, "My child, if I don’t cut off both your hands, the devil will take me. In my fear, I've promised to do it. Please help me and forgive me for this harm."

She replied, "Dear father, do whatever you need to. I am your child."

So, she laid down both her hands and allowed them to be cut off.

The devil came for the third time, but she had cried so much on her stumps that they were still clean.

Finally, he had to give in.

The miller said to her, "Because of you, I've gained such great wealth that I will take care of you for the rest of your life."

But she replied, "I can't stay here. I'll go where kind people will give me what I need."

She had her maimed arms tied behind her back and left at dawn, walking all day until night fell.

Eventually, she reached a royal garden, and by the moon’s glow, she saw trees laden with fruit, but she couldn't get in because it was surrounded by water.

Having walked all day without eating, hunger gnawed at her, and she thought, if only I could get inside to eat some fruit before I die of hunger.

Kneeling down, she called on God and prayed.

Suddenly, an angel appeared and created a dam in the water so the moat dried up, allowing her to walk through.

She entered the garden with the angel by her side.

She spotted a tree filled with beautiful pears, but they were all numbered.

She went to it and, in her hunger, ate one pear directly from the tree with her mouth, but no more.

The gardener noticed her, but since the angel was beside her, he was too scared to speak or shout.

title: The Girl Without Hands
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
After satisfying her hunger with the pear, she hid among the bushes.

The next morning, the king who owned the garden came down to count his fruits, and noticed that one pear was missing.

He asked the gardener what had happened to it, as it wasn't lying beneath the tree but had vanished.

The gardener replied, "Last night, a spirit came and ate a pear right off the branch, using its mouth."

The king asked, "How did the spirit get over the water, and where did it go after eating the pear?"

The gardener answered, "Someone in a snow-white garment came down from heaven, created a dam, and let the spirit cross the moat."

I was terrified, so I didn’t ask any questions or shout.

The spirit left after eating the pear.

The king declared, "If what you say is true, I will watch through the night."

That evening, the king went into the garden with a priest who was tasked with speaking to the spirit.

They all sat beneath the tree and kept watch.

At midnight, the maiden crept out from the thicket, went to the tree, and once again ate a pear with her mouth, while the angel stood beside her.

The priest approached and asked, "Are you from heaven or earth? Are you a being?"

She replied, "I am not a spirit, but a miserable mortal forsaken by everyone but God."

The king said, "Even if the world forsakes you, I will never abandon you."

He took her to his royal palace, and because she was so beautiful and kind, he loved her deeply and had silver hands made for her.

After a year, the king needed to go on a journey, so he entrusted his new queen to the care of his mother, instructing her to take good care of the queen if she gave birth.

The queen did have a lovely baby boy.

The old mother hurried to write him a letter with the wonderful news.

However, the messenger stopped by a stream along the way to rest and soon fell asleep from exhaustion.

While he slept, the devil, ever eager to harm the good queen, swapped the letter for another one.

title: The Girl Without Hands
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
This new letter claimed that the queen had given birth to a monster.

When the king read the letter, he was horrified and deeply troubled, but he responded that they should take great care of the queen and nurse her well until he returned.

The messenger returned with the letter, but once again fell asleep at the same spot.

The devil appeared again and switched the letter for another one that ordered the queen and her child to be executed.

The old mother was devastated by the letter and couldn’t believe what she read.

She wrote back to the king but received no response because each time the devil replaced the letter with false information.

In the last letter, it stated that she must preserve the queen's tongue and eyes as proof of obedience.

Heartbroken at the thought of innocent bloodshed, the old mother had a hind brought in the night and cut out its tongue and eyes, keeping them safe.

Then she told the queen, "I can't have you killed as the king commanded, but you can't stay here anymore. Leave with your child, and never come back."

With tears in her eyes, the queen tied her child to her back and left.

She wandered into a vast, wild forest and fell to her knees to pray to God.

Suddenly, the angel of the Lord appeared and guided her to a small house with a sign that read, 'All live free here.'

A young woman in a white dress came out of the house, greeting her warmly and leading her inside.

She gently unbound the boy from her back, cradled him as he fed, and laid him in a cozy little bed.

The queen asked, "How did you know I was a queen?"

The white maiden replied, "I am an angel sent by God to watch over you and your child."

For seven years, the queen lived safely in the little house, and by God's grace, her hands, which had been cut off, grew back.

Eventually, the king returned from his journey, his first wish being to see his wife and child.

His old mother, in tears, said, "You wicked man! Why did you order me to take the lives of those innocent souls?"

title: The Girl Without Hands
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She revealed the forged letters and continued, "I did what you asked and saved the tokens—the tongue and the eyes!"

The king was heartbroken for his wife and child, crying even more bitterly than his mother.

Feeling compassion, the old mother comforted him, saying, "Don't lose hope. She is still alive. I secretly caused a hind to be killed and took these tokens from it. I bound her child to her back and bade her leave, making her promise never to return, for you were so angry with her."

The king vowed, "I will search as far as the sky is blue, without eating or drinking, until I find my dear wife and child. I will not rest until I know they are safe."

He set off on a long journey, searching for seven years, scouring every crevice and cave, but he couldn't find them and feared they had perished from hunger.

During it all, he did not eat or drink, for God sustained him.

Finally, he entered a vast forest and stumbled upon the small house with the 'All live free here' sign.

The white maiden appeared again, took his hand, led him inside, and greeted him, saying, "Welcome, lord king! What brings you here?"

He replied, "I've spent seven years searching for my wife and child, but I can't find them anywhere."

The angel offered him food and drink, but he declined, only seeking rest.

As he lay down for a nap, he covered his face with a handkerchief.

Meanwhile, the angel went to the room where the queen sat with her son, who she called Sorrowful, and said, "Go out with your child; your husband has come."

She went to where he lay, and as she approached, the handkerchief slipped from his face.

She said, "Sorrowful, pick up your father’s handkerchief and cover his face again."

The child picked it up and laid it over his father’s face once more.

As the king slept, he heard their conversation and enjoyed letting the handkerchief fall once more.

But the child grew impatient and said, "Dear mother, how can I cover my father's face when I don’t have a father in this world? You taught me to pray, 'Our Father, who art in heaven,' and told me my father is in heaven, who is good God. How can I know a wild man like this? He isn't my father!"

Upon hearing this, the king woke up and asked who they were.

She answered, "I am your wife, and this is your son, Sorrowful."

Seeing her real hands, he said, "My wife has silver hands!"

She replied, "The good God has made my real hands grow back," and the angel brought out the silver hands to show him.

Recognizing his dear wife and child, he embraced them, filled with joy, and said, "A heavy weight has been lifted from my heart."

The angel of God feasted with them that day, and afterwards, they returned home to the king's elderly mother.

Everywhere, there were celebrations, and the king and queen married once more and lived happily ever after.

title: The Glass Coffin
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Never let anyone say that a poor tailor can't achieve great things,

and earn high honors. All it takes is finding the right smithy,

and most importantly, having some good luck. Once, a clever and

polite tailor's apprentice set off on a journey. While wandering

through a vast forest, he realized he had lost his way.

As night fell, he had no choice but to find a place to sleep.

He could have easily rested on the soft moss, but the fear of

wild animals kept him on edge, so he decided to spend the night in

a tree instead. He climbed high up an oak and felt grateful for

his goose, which kept him company, otherwise the wind at the top

of the tree would have made him feel quite vulnerable.

After a few hours spent in darkness and trembling fear, he saw a

faint glimmer of light in the distance. Thinking there might be a

home nearby where he’d be safer than on the branches of a tree,

he carefully descended and headed towards the light.

It led him to a small hut made of reeds and rushes. With a brave

heart, he pushed the door open. The light revealed an old man

with a coat made of patches of colorful cloth.

"Who are you, and what do you want?" the man asked grumpily.

"I’m a poor tailor, caught out here in the wilderness after dark,

and I would greatly appreciate it if you could take me in until morning."

"Be on your way," the old man replied sourly. "I won’t help any

tramps—find yourself shelter elsewhere."

Just as he was about to retreat into his hut, the tailor grabbed

his coat and begged him so earnestly that the old man, not as angry

as he wanted to seem, eventually softened and let him inside.

He offered the tailor some food and a comfortable bed in a corner.

Exhausted, the tailor fell asleep with no need for rocking, but he

wouldn’t have stirred at all if not for a loud noise that woke him.

A fierce cacophony of screams and roars echoed through the thin walls.

Filled with unexpected courage, he quickly dressed and rushed outside.

title: The Glass Coffin
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Right near the hut, he spotted a massive black bull and a majestic

stag ready to engage in a fierce battle.

They charged at each other with such ferocity that the ground shook

and the air crackled with their cries. For what felt like ages,

it was uncertain who would win. Eventually, the stag drove its

horns into the bull, which fell with a thunderous roar and was

finished off quickly.

The tailor, astonished by the spectacle, stood frozen when the stag,

in full flight, leaped toward him and, before he could react,

scooped him up on its antlers. With no time to think, he clung

to the ends of the horns as they dashed across terrain—over rocks

and through valleys, woods, and fields. It felt like he was flying.

Finally, the stag halted at a rocky wall and gently dropped the

tailor to the ground. Almost lifeless, he needed a moment to

regain his senses. Once he had gathered himself,

the stag nudged a door in the rock with its horns, and it swung open.

Fire blazed out, followed by smoke that obscured the stag from view.

Confused and unsure of how to escape this desolation and return to civilization,

he stood hesitantly, when suddenly a voice resonated from the rock.

"Come in without fear; you have nothing to worry about."

He hesitated but felt compelled to follow the mysterious voice.

He passed through the iron door into a vast hall, its ceiling,

walls, and floor sparkling with polished stone, each engraved with symbols

he didn’t recognize. He looked around in wonder, just about to leave,

when the voice called out again.

"Stand on the stone in the middle of the hall, and great fortune will be yours."

His courage had grown, so he followed the instruction.

The stone beneath him started to sink slowly, taking him down,

until he found himself in another large hall, just as expansive.

However, this one had even more to admire.

Niches carved in the walls held vases of transparent glass filled

with colorful liquid or bluish mist. Positioned across from each other

were two large glass chests that instantly piqued his curiosity.

As he approached one, he saw a charming miniature castle encircled by

title: The Glass Coffin
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
tiny farm buildings, stables, and various delightful treasures.

Everything was petite, yet extraordinarily crafted, seeming as though it had

been meticulously carved by a skilled artisan.

He could have stared at this marvel for hours, but the voice pulled him away.

"Turn around and look at the glass chest opposite."

What he found there took his breath away—a stunning maiden.

She lay as if asleep, her long golden hair draped around her like a

treasure. Her eyes were closed, but the radiant hues of her skin and

the gentle rise and fall of her chest indicated that she was alive.

As the tailor admired her, she suddenly opened her eyes and gasped at

seeing him.

"Divine providence! My rescue is at hand!" she exclaimed.

"Please, hurry, help me out of this coffin. If you push back the

bolt, I can break free!"

The tailor wasted no time, and as soon as he pushed open the glass lid,

she jumped out, quickly wrapping herself in a large cloak.

Then, she invited the young man to her side, and after planting a

sweet kiss on his lips, she said, "You, my long-awaited hero,

heaven has led you to me and ended my suffering.

On this very day, when my woes come to an end, your happiness will begin.

You are the husband chosen for me by fate, and you shall enjoy a life of

unending joy, cherished by me and abundantly blessed."

"Please, have a seat and let me share my story with you."

I am the daughter of a wealthy count. When my parents passed away

during my childhood, they entrusted me to my older brother.

We shared a deep bond and were so alike in thought that we vowed

never to marry, but to stay together for life.

Our home was always filled with life—neighbors and friends often

visited, and we welcomed everyone with open arms.

One evening, a stranger arrived at our castle, claiming he couldn't

get to his next destination and requested shelter for the night.

We graciously allowed him to stay and he entertained us at dinner with

his stories and charming conversation. My brother liked him so much that

title: The Glass Coffin
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
he insisted the stranger stay a few extra days, and after some hesitation,

he agreed.

We didn’t leave the table until late at night. The stranger was

shown to his room, and I was eager to curl up in my soft bed.

Hardly had I drifted off to sleep when the faint sound of enchanting music

stirred me awake. I had no idea where it originated from, so I tried to

call my maid who slept nearby, but to my shock, I found I couldn't voice

a single word. It felt as if a nightmare was pressing down on me, making it impossible

to make any sound.

In the dim light of my night lamp, I saw the stranger entering my room,

though the doors were securely locked. He approached and claimed he had used

magic to play the music and awaken me, intending to offer his heart to me.

Despite his charm, I felt repulsed by his sorcery and refused to respond.

He stood there for a moment, seemingly waiting for me to change my mind,

but when I remained silent, he declared he would take revenge for my pride and left.

I spent the night anxiously and only fell asleep as dawn approached.

Upon waking, I rushed to my brother's room but found it empty.

The servants told me he had left at daybreak with the stranger for a hunt.

Instinctively, I feared something terrible had occurred. I dressed quickly,

ordered my horse to be saddled, and rode swiftly into the woods, accompanied by one

servant.

Unfortunately, my servant's horse stumbled, leaving him behind.

I pressed on, and moments later, I spotted the stranger leading a beautiful stag.

I asked him where my brother was and how he had obtained such a stag, noticing tears in

the creature's gentle eyes.

Instead of answering me, the stranger laughed menacingly. I was enraged.

I pulled out my pistol and shot at him, but the bullet bounced off him and hit my horse.

I fell to the ground, and the stranger muttered words that robbed me of my strength.

When I regained consciousness, I found myself trapped in this underground cave,

encased in a glass coffin. The magician appeared again and revealed that he had turned

my brother into a stag. My castle, along with everything in it, had been shrunk and sealed

in the other glass chest. My servants, transformed into smoke, were trapped in glass bottles.

He told me that if I obeyed him now, it would be simple to restore everything back to normal,

as he just needed to open the containers.

I remained silent, just as before. He vanished, leaving me alone in my prison,

where a deep sleep soon enveloped me.

During my dreams, the most comforting vision was of a young man coming to rescue me,

and today, when I opened my eyes, I saw you, and my dream has come true.

Please help me fulfill the rest of my vision. The first task is to lift the

glass chest containing my castle onto that large stone.

As soon as the stone was burdened, it began to soar high with her and the young man,

up through the opening in the ceiling into the main hall, allowing them to

easily emerge into the world above.

Once there, the maiden opened the chest, and it was a wonder to see how the castle,

the houses, and the barns expanded rapidly to their full size.

Afterward, the young maiden and the tailor returned below ground with the stone.

They carried the smoke-filled vessels up and once she opened the bottles,

the blue smoke rushed out, transforming into living people—her servants and her kin.

Her joy grew even more when her brother, who had defeated the magician in his bull form,

emerged from the forest, restored to his true self.

On that same day, as promised, the maiden gave her hand to the tailor,

title: The Godfather
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
There was a poor man who had so many children that he had asked everyone he knew to be a godfather.

When yet another child was born, he realized he had no one left to invite.

Feeling stuck, he lay down in despair and fell asleep.

In his dream, he was told to go outside and ask the first person he met to be a godfather.

When he woke up, he decided to follow his dream. He stepped outside the gate and approached the first person who came his way.

The stranger handed him a small glass of water and said, "This is magical water; you can heal the sick with it. Just make sure to check where death is standing. If he's by the patient’s head, give them some water, and they'll be healed. But if death is by their feet, all will be lost, and the sick person will die."

From that moment on, the man could always tell whether someone could be saved or not. He became famous for his healing skills and earned a good amount of money.

He was once summoned to help the king's child. When he arrived, he found death standing by the child's head, and he cured the child with the water. He did the same thing a second time, but when it happened a third time, he saw death near the child's feet, and he knew this time was different.

The man thought he would visit the godfather and tell him how well he had done with the water. But when he entered the house, the most bizarre scenes were unfolding inside.

On the first flight of stairs, he saw a broom and a shovel arguing and knocking against each other. He asked them, 'Where does the godfather live?' The broom responded, 'One flight of stairs higher.'

As he climbed to the second flight, he discovered a pile of dead fingers. Again, he asked, 'Where does the godfather live?' One of the fingers replied, 'One flight of stairs higher.'

On the third flight, he found a bunch of decapitated heads, which directed him once more to the next flight up.

On the fourth flight, he spotted fish sizzling in pans, cooking themselves. They too said, 'One flight of stairs higher.'

Once he reached the fifth flight, he came to a door and peered through the keyhole. Inside, he saw the godfather, who had a pair of long horns.

When he opened the door and stepped in, the godfather quickly hopped into bed and pulled the covers over himself.

The man said, 'Sir godfather, what an odd household you have. When I reached your first flight of stairs, the broom and shovel were fighting and hitting each other like crazy.'

'You fool,' said the godfather. 'That was just the boy and the maid talking to each other.'

'But on the second flight, I saw dead fingers lying around.'

'Oh, how silly you are! Those were just some roots of scorzonera.'

'On the third flight, there was a pile of dead men's heads.'

'You foolish man! Those were just cabbages.'

'On the fourth flight, I saw fish in a pan, sizzling and cooking themselves.'

With a chuckle, the godfather remarked that the fish then came over and served themselves up.

'And when I got to the fifth flight, I peeked through the keyhole and saw you with those long, long horns!'

'Oh, that's not true!' The godfather became alarmed, and the man quickly ran out; who knows what the godfather might have done to him if he hadn't escaped.

title: The Gold-Children
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once there was a poor man and a poor woman who owned nothing but a

small cottage, and they earned their living by fishing, always

struggling to get by. One day, while the man was sitting by the water, casting his net, he caught a fish made entirely of gold.

As he gazed at the fish in shock, it suddenly spoke to him, saying, "Listen, fisherman, if you throw me back into the water, I'll grant you a wish."

The fisherman replied, "What use is a castle to me if I can’t eat?"

The gold fish reassured him, "Don't worry about food; there will be a cupboard in your castle filled with delicious dishes, as much as you desire."

The man said, "If that's true, I could do you a favor."

"Yes, but there’s one condition: you must never tell anyone how you got your fortune. If you say even a single word, everything will be lost."

Then the man threw the magical fish back into the water.

Where their hut once stood, a grand castle appeared. He rubbed his eyes in disbelief, stepped inside, and saw his wife dressed in beautiful clothes, looking thrilled.

"Husband, how did this happen?" she exclaimed, delighted.

"It’s wonderful! But I'm so hungry; can you give me something to eat?" he replied.

"I don’t have anything and don’t know where to find food in this new house," she answered.

"You don't need to worry about that. Just look over there—there's a big cupboard. Open it up."

When she unlocked it, her eyes lit up with joy at the sight of cakes, meats, fruits, and wine.

"What more could you want, my dear?" she cried out happily. They both sat down to eat and enjoy their meal together.

After they had their fill, the woman asked, "But where does all this wealth come from?"

"Please don’t question me about it; I can’t tell you anything. If I do, our good fortune will vanish," he sighed.

title: The Gold-Children
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Alright, if I can’t know, then I won’t ask. But she wasn’t serious.

She nagged him day and night until, in a moment of frustration, he finally revealed the secret: it was due to a golden fish he had caught.

As soon as he let the secret slip, the magnificent castle vanished, and they found themselves back in their old fisherman’s hut.

"Now we have nothing again!" he lamented. "We’re back to gnawing on bones!"

"I’d rather not have wealth if I can’t know where it comes from," she replied sadly.

Once more, the man returned to his fishing. Eventually, he caught the golden fish for a third time.

"Listen, I know it seems I’m destined to end up in your hands. Take me home, cut me into six pieces. Feed two pieces to your wife, give two to your horse, and bury two in the ground. They will bring you great blessings."

The fisherman took the fish home and followed its instructions.

From the two pieces buried in the ground, two golden lilies sprang up. The horse had two golden foals, and the couple had two children made entirely of gold.

As those golden children grew tall and handsome, the lilies and horses blossomed as well.

They said to their father, "We want to ride our golden steeds and explore the world."

"But how can I bear it if you go away and I don’t know how you’re doing?" he said sadly.

After some thought, they assured him, "Leave the two golden lilies here. You can tell how we are by checking on them. If they’re fresh, we’re well. If they’re wilted, we’re sick. If they die, we are gone."

So off they rode, eventually arriving at an inn filled with people who laughed and mocked them for their golden appearances.

One child, feeling ashamed, turned back and went home to his father.

But the other rode on, reaching a vast forest. Just before entering, people warned, "It’s not safe to ride through there; it’s teeming with robbers who would harm you.

title: The Gold-Children
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
They’ll surely kill you when they see you’re made of gold. Don't go in!"

But undaunted, he declared, "I must ride through it."

He covered himself and his horse in bear skins to hide their golden glitter and bravely entered the forest.

Not long after, he heard rustling in the bushes and people talking nearby.

Some voices shouted, "There’s one!" but others responded, "Let him go; it’s just a bearskin, as poor as a church mouse—he’s of no value to us."

So the gold-child joyfully passed through the forest untouched.

One day, he entered a village and laid eyes on a maiden so stunning, he thought no one else could rival her beauty.

Overwhelmed by love, he approached her and said, "I love you with all my heart. Will you be my wife?"

The maiden, enchanted by him, replied, "Yes, I will be your wife and remain true to you forever."

They were married, and just as they basked in their happiness, the bride’s father returned.

When he discovered his daughter’s wedding, he was taken aback, asking, "Where’s the groom?"

They pointed to the gold-child, who still wore his bearskin. Wrathfully, he exclaimed, "No bearskin will ever have my daughter! I’ll kill him!"

Desperate, the bride pleaded, "He is my husband, and I love him entirely!"

Finally, the father calmed down a bit, but the thought of his daughter marrying a bearskin man lingered in his mind.

The next morning, he woke early to see if his daughter's husband was a beggar.

Peeking in, he was shocked to find a magnificent golden man in bed and the cast-off bear skins on the floor.

Relieved, he thought, "What a blessing that I didn’t act on my anger; I would’ve committed a terrible mistake."

Meanwhile, the gold-child dreamt of hunting a splendid stag. Upon waking, he told his wife, "I have to go hunting."

title: The Gold-Children
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Worried, she urged him to stay. "You could run into danger!"

But he replied, "I must go."

He got up and rode into the forest, and it wasn’t long before a beautiful stag crossed his path, just like in his dream.

He aimed to shoot, but the stag managed to escape.

He chased after it all day without tiring, but by evening, the stag disappeared.

Suddenly, he found himself at a little house where a witch lived.

He knocked, and an old woman came out, asking, "What are you doing out here in the woods? Have you seen a stag?"

"Yes, I know that stag," she replied.

But at that moment, a small dog that followed her out began barking furiously.

"Be quiet, you annoying creature, or I’ll shoot you!" he barked back.

Enraged, the witch shouted, "You dare threaten my dog?" Then she quickly transformed him into stone.

His bride waited for him in vain, thinking her worst fears had come true.

At home, the other brother stood by the golden lilies when one suddenly wilted.

"Oh no! My brother must be in trouble!" he exclaimed. "I have to go help him!"

The father replied, "No, stay! What will I do if I lose you too?" But his son insisted, "I have to go!"

He mounted his golden horse and rode into the forest, where he found his brother turned to stone.

The old witch emerged from her house, trying to trick him, but he kept his distance.

"I’ll shoot you if you don’t bring my brother back to life!" he demanded.

Reluctantly, the witch touched the stone with her finger, and he transformed back into his human form.

The two brothers rejoiced upon seeing each other again, embracing and cherishing their reunion.

Together, they rode out of the forest; one went home to his bride, while the other returned to his family.

The father smiled and said, "I knew you’d bring your brother back; the golden lily bloomed again!" Then they all lived happily and prospered until the end of their days.

title: The Golden Bird
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a king who had a stunning garden behind his palace.

In the garden, there was a magnificent tree that grew golden apples.

As the apples were ripening, they were counted, but the very next morning, one was missing.

This news reached the king, who immediately ordered that someone should watch the garden every night.

The king had three sons. He sent the eldest son into the garden as soon as night fell.

However, when midnight came, he couldn't resist falling asleep, and the next morning, another apple was gone.

On the following night, the second son took his turn to keep watch, but his fate was no different.

As soon as the clock struck twelve, he dozed off, and in the morning, yet another apple had vanished.

Now it was the youngest son's turn to keep watch. He was eager to prove himself, but the king didn't have much faith in him.

He doubted that his youngest son would do any better than his siblings but eventually allowed him to go.

The young man lay down beneath the tree but stayed awake, determined not to let sleep take over.

When midnight struck, he noticed something rustling in the air, and in the moonlight, he saw a bird with shimmering golden feathers.

The bird landed on the tree and was about to pick a golden apple when the youth shot an arrow.

The bird flew away, but the arrow hit its feathers, and one golden feather fell to the ground.

The young man picked up the feather and took it to the king the next morning, sharing what he had witnessed.

The king gathered his council, and everyone agreed that a feather like that was worth more than the entire kingdom.

"If the feather is so valuable," the king declared, "one will not be enough for me; I must have the whole bird!"

The eldest son set out, confident in his cleverness that he would easily find the golden bird.

After traveling for a while, he spotted a fox at the edge of a forest and aimed his gun at it.

The fox pleaded, "Don’t shoot me! I can offer you some valuable advice. You’re headed toward the golden bird.

title: The Golden Bird
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Tonight, you'll reach a village with two inns. One will be bright and lively inside, but don’t go there.

Choose the other, even if it looks rundown."

The prince thought, "How can a foolish animal give good advice?" and pulled the trigger.

But he missed, and the fox scampered quickly into the woods.

He continued on his way, eventually reaching the village with the two inns.

One was filled with music and laughter, while the other looked shabby.

He thought, "I'd be a fool to enter the shabby tavern, so he went into the cheerful one.

He indulged himself in revelry, forgetting about the bird, his father, and all wise counsel.

After several months without his return, the second son set out to find the golden bird.

He encountered the fox, who gave him the same advice as before, which he ignored.

When he reached the two inns, he saw his brother peering out of the window from the lively one.

Unable to resist, he went inside and spent his nights in festivity.

Eventually, the youngest son wanted to set out, but his father objected.

"It won’t do any good," he said. "He won’t find the golden bird any better than his brothers, and if he gets into trouble, he's not bright enough to help himself."

But in the end, as he was determined, the father let him go.

Again, the fox was waiting outside the woods, pleading for his life and offering good advice.

The youth was kind-hearted and said, "Don’t worry, little fox, I won’t harm you."

"You won’t regret it," the fox replied. "To help you move faster, hop on my tail."

As soon as he climbed on, the fox took off, darting through the woods until the wind whipped through their hair.

When they reached the village, the youth got off and followed the fox's advice, heading straight into the little inn for a quiet night.

The following morning, as he stepped into the open countryside, the fox was already waiting.

title: The Golden Bird
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"I have more instructions for you," he said. "Keep going straight, and you'll eventually arrive at a castle.

In front of it, a whole troop of soldiers will be sleeping. Don’t worry about them, just walk right through the castle.

You’ll find a chamber where a golden bird is kept in a wooden cage. There’s an empty golden cage next to it, but be careful.

Don’t take the bird out of the wooden cage and put it in the golden one, or you’ll be in trouble."

With that, the fox stretched out his tail again, and the king's son hopped on, galloping away again.

As he reached the castle, everything was exactly as the fox had said.

He entered the chamber where the golden bird was imprisoned, surrounded by three golden apples.

But he thought, "It would be foolish to leave such a beautiful bird in a shabby cage," so he opened the door, grabbed it, and placed it in the golden cage.

But as he did, the bird let out a sharp cry.

The soldiers woke up, rushed in, and arrested him.

The next day, he was brought to court, and since he confessed everything, he was sentenced to death.

The king, however, offered him a chance to save his life.

"You can have your freedom if you bring me the golden horse that runs faster than the wind, and I’ll also give you the golden bird."

The prince set off, feeling despondent. How would he ever find a horse like that?

Suddenly, he spotted the fox sitting on the road again.

"This happened because you ignored my advice before," the fox said. "But don’t worry, I’ll help you find the golden horse.

Just keep going in that direction, and you’ll reach a castle with the horse in the stable.

The stable attendants will be sleeping, so you can quietly lead the horse away.

But remember, use the common wooden saddle, not the shiny golden one, or you’ll find yourself in trouble."

title: The Golden Bird
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
So the fox stretched out his tail once more, and the prince hopped on.

They sped across the landscape until the wind whipped through his hair.

Everything went as the fox had predicted. The prince reached the stable with the golden horse.

As he was about to place the common saddle on it, he thought, "This gorgeous creature deserves the beautiful saddle.

As soon as the golden saddle touched the horse, it neighed loudly.

The grooms awoke and seized the young man, throwing him into prison.

The next morning, he faced the court again, now sentenced to death.

The king offered him another chance: he had to bring back the lovely princess from the golden castle.

With a heavy heart, the youth set out again, but luckily for him, he soon encountered the loyal fox.

"I shouldn't help you again," the fox said. "But I feel bad for you and will help you once more.

This path leads you straight to the golden castle. By evening, you’ll arrive.

At night, when all is quiet, the beautiful princess will head to her bathhouse.

When she goes in, run up to her and give her a kiss. Then she’ll follow you out, but don’t let her say goodbye to her parents."

Once again, the fox stretched out his tail, and the king's son climbed on.

Off they went, speeding over stock and stone, with the wind whipping through their hair.

When they got to the golden castle, everything was exactly as the fox had described.

He waited until midnight, when the whole castle was asleep and the princess was headed to the bathing house.

He leaped out to give her a kiss.

She agreed to come with him but tearfully asked if she could first say goodbye to her parents.

At first, he resisted, but when she cried more and pleaded on her knees, he gave in.

But as soon as she reached her father's bedside, everyone in the castle woke up, and the youth was caught again.

The next morning, the king declared, "Your life is forfeit, but you can earn mercy by removing the hill blocking my view from my windows.

title: The Golden Bird
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
You must finish it in eight days. If you succeed, I’ll give you my daughter."

The king's son started digging and shoveling, but on the seventh day, he realized how little he had accomplished.

He fell into despair and lost all hope.

That evening, the fox appeared and said, "You don't deserve my help, but lie down and sleep.

I'll finish the work for you."

The next morning, when he looked out of the window, the hill was gone.

The young man rushed to the king to report that he had fulfilled the task.

Whether the king liked it or not, he had to keep his promise and give him his daughter.

So the two left together, and soon enough, the faithful fox caught up with them.

"You’ve got what’s best now," he said, "but remember, the golden horse also belongs to the princess.

How do I get it?" asked the youth.

"This is how: Take the beautiful maiden back to the king who sent you for her. They'll celebrate your return and happily give you the golden horse.

Jump on it quickly, bid farewell to everyone, and last of all to the beautiful princess.

As soon as you have her hand, lift her onto the horse and ride away, for it runs faster than the wind."

Everything went according to plan, and the king's son rode off with the beautiful princess on the golden horse.

The fox didn’t lag behind, saying, "Now I’ll help you get the golden bird.

When you get close to the castle, let the maiden dismount, and I’ll look after her.

Then, ride the golden horse into the castle yard. Everyone will be thrilled, and they’ll bring the golden bird to you.

Once you have the cage, return quickly and take the maiden with you."

When the plan was successful and the king's son was ready to ride home with his treasures, the fox said, "Now you must repay me for my assistance.

What do you want?" asked the youth. "Once you're in the woods, shoot me and chop off my head and feet."

title: The Golden Bird
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"That’s quite a way to show gratitude," said the prince. "I can’t do that!"

The fox replied, "If you refuse, I must leave you. But before I go, heed my advice.

Be wary of two things: don’t buy gallows' flesh, and don’t sit at the edge of any well."

Then he scampered into the woods.

The youth thought, "That fox is quite unusual. Who would want to buy gallows' flesh? And I would never have thought to sit by a well."

He rode on with the beautiful maiden and passed through the village where his two brothers had stayed.

A commotion was happening, and when he asked about it, he learned that two men were going to be hanged.

As he drew closer, he recognized them as his brothers, who had wasted their wealth and caused trouble.

He asked if they could be freed, but the townsfolk replied, "If you pay, we can set them free, but why waste your money on them?"

Without a second thought, he paid their ransom, and they all continued their journey.

They reached the woods where the fox had first met them, and on this hot day, the two brothers suggested, "Let’s rest by the well and eat and drink."

He agreed, but while they were chatting, without thinking, he sat down at the edge of the well.

In that moment, his brothers pushed him into the well, took the maiden, the horse, and the bird, and went home to their father.

"Look what we’ve brought back!" they said. "Not only the golden bird, but the golden horse and the princess from the golden castle!"

Everyone rejoiced, but the horse refused to eat, the bird wouldn’t sing, and the maiden continued to cry.

However, the youngest brother wasn’t dead. Fortunately, the well was dry, and he landed on soft moss and was unharmed, although he couldn't climb out.

In his predicament, the loyal fox returned and scolded him for ignoring his advice.

title: The Golden Bird
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"But I can’t give up," the young man said. "I will help you out into the daylight."

He told him to grab his tail and hold on tight, and with that, the fox pulled him up.

"You’re not completely safe yet," the fox warned. "Your brothers aren’t sure you’re dead and have set up watchmen to kill you if you’re seen.

But a poor man was sitting by the road, and the youth traded clothes with him.

In this disguise, he successfully made his way to the castle.

No one recognized him, but the bird began to sing, the horse started to eat, and the beautiful maiden stopped crying.

The astonished king asked, "What does this mean?" The maiden replied, "I don’t know, but I was so sad, and now I feel so happy.

It feels like my true bridegroom has come." She explained everything that had happened, despite her brothers threatening her if she told the truth.

The king ordered all the people in his castle to come before him, and among them was the youth in his ragged clothes.

But the maiden recognized him immediately and threw her arms around him.

The wicked brothers were captured and executed, while he married the beautiful maiden and was declared the king's heir.

But what became of the poor fox? Many years later, the prince walked through the woods and came across the fox again.

"You have everything you could wish for, but my suffering never ends," the fox said with tears.

He pleaded once more for the prince to shoot him and chop off his head and feet.

He complied, and as soon as it was done, the fox transformed into a man, revealing himself as the brother of the beautiful princess who had been cursed.

Finally freed from the enchantment, they all lived happily ever after.

title: The Golden Goose
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once there was a man with three sons, and the youngest was named Dummling.

Dummling was always looked down on, mocked, and ridiculed at every opportunity.

One day, the oldest son decided to head into the forest to chop wood.

Before he set off, his mother prepared a delicious sweet cake and a

bottle of wine, hoping to keep him from going hungry or thirsty.

As he entered the forest, he met a little old man with gray hair.

The old man greeted him and said, "Please, can you spare me a piece of cake and a

sip of your wine? I'm so hungry and thirsty."

But the clever son replied, "If I give you my cake and wine, I won’t have any left for myself. Go away!"

And he left the little man standing there as he moved on.

However, when he started chopping down a tree, he quickly made a wrong move,

and the axe struck his arm, forcing him to go home for a bandage. That was thanks to the little old man.

Next, the second son ventured into the forest, and like the first, his mother gave him a cake and a bottle of wine.

The same little gray man approached him and asked for a piece of cake and a drink.

But the second son, too, replied, "What I give you will be taken away from me, so leave me alone!"

He left the little man standing there and continued on.

His consequence wasn’t long in coming; after just a few swings with the axe, he ended up hitting his leg, necessitating his return home.

Then Dummling said to his father, "Please let me go and chop wood."

His father replied, "Your brothers have already hurt themselves; it’s best if you stay away. You’re not cut out for this work."

But Dummling pleaded until his father finally relented, saying, "Fine, but you’ll learn a thing or two by getting hurt."

His mother gave him a simple cinder cake and a bottle of sour beer.

When he reached the forest, the little old gray man met him too, greeting him, saying, "Please share some of your cake and a drink—I’m so hungry and thirsty."

title: The Golden Goose
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Dummling replied, "I only have cinder cake and sour beer; if that works for you, let's sit down and share."

They sat down, and when Dummling took out his cinder cake, it was actually a sweet cake, and the sour beer turned into fine wine.

They enjoyed their meal together, and the little man said, "Since you have a good heart and are willing to share, I’ll grant you good luck. There’s a tree over there; cut it down, and you’ll find something at its roots."

Then the little man bid him farewell.

Dummling went and chopped down the tree, and when it fell, he found a goose sitting at its roots, covered in pure gold feathers.

He picked her up and took her to an inn where he planned to spend the night.

The innkeeper had three daughters who were intrigued by the magnificent goose and hoped to snag a golden feather.

The oldest thought, "I’ll find a chance to pull out a feather!" As soon as Dummling stepped out, she grabbed the goose by the wing, but her finger and hand stuck fast.

The second daughter came along next, intent on getting a feather for herself, and the moment she touched her sister, she got stuck too.

Finally, the youngest came running with the same idea, while the others yelled, "Stay back! For goodness' sake, don't come closer!"

But she didn’t understand the warning. "The others are there; I should be too!" she thought, charging ahead.

As soon as she touched her sister, she got stuck beside them. So, they all had to spend the night tangled up with the goose.

The next morning, Dummling took the goose under his arm and set off, completely indifferent to the three girls clinging to it.

They had no choice but to chase after him, weaving left and right as he moved.

In the middle of a field, they encountered a parson. Spotting the unusual procession, he exclaimed, "Shame on you girls! Why are you following this young man across the fields?"

title: The Golden Goose
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Then he grabbed the youngest by the hand to pull her away, but the moment he touched her, he got stuck too.

Before long, the sexton came by and saw his master, the parson, being pulled along with three girls.

He was baffled and called out, "Hey, your reverence, where are you going in such a hurry? Don’t forget we have a christening to attend!"

As he ran after them, he grabbed the parson's sleeve, only to find himself stuck as well.

Soon, two laborers came by with their hoes, and the parson called out to them, asking for help to free him and the sexton.

But as soon as they touched the sexton, they got stuck as well, and now there were seven of them running after Dummling and the goose.

Not long after, they arrived at a city where a king had a daughter who was so serious that no one could make her laugh.

The king declared that anyone who could make her laugh would win her hand in marriage.

When Dummling heard this, he marched up to the king’s daughter with his goose and the train of people behind him.

As soon as she spotted the strange line of seven people following Dummling, she burst into laughter, as if she could never stop.

Dummling then asked for her hand, but the king wasn’t pleased with this son-in-law and came up with all sorts of excuses, insisting that Dummling must first bring him a man who could drink an entire cellar of wine.

Dummling remembered the little gray man who had helped him before, so he went back to the forest.

Returning to the same spot where he had cut down the tree, he found a man sitting there, looking incredibly sorrowful.

Dummling asked him what troubled him, and the man replied, "I’m so thirsty and can’t quench it. I can’t handle cold water, and I just emptied a barrel of wine, but it does me no good."

Dummling said, "I can help you; come with me, and you’ll get your fill!"

He led the man into the king’s cellar, where the man bent over the enormous barrels and drank and drank until he could hardly move.

title: The Golden Goose
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
By the end of the day, he had emptied them all.

Then Dummling asked for the princess’s hand once more, but the king was irked that an ugly fellow like Dummling was about to take his daughter.

He posed another challenge: Dummling had to find a man who could eat a whole mountain of bread.

Dummling didn’t hesitate; he went straight back to the forest.

There, he found a man tying his stomach with a strap while making a grimace, saying, "I’ve eaten an entire oven full of rolls, but I’m still so hungry!"

"I need to strap myself up to avoid dying of hunger."

Dummling felt relieved and said, "Get up and come with me. You'll eat your fill!"

He took the man to the king’s palace, where all the flour in the kingdom was gathered.

They baked a massive mountain of bread.

The man from the forest stood before it, started eating, and by the end of the day, the whole mountain was gone.

Dummling asked for his bride again, but once more the king made an excuse, ordering a ship that could sail on land as well as water.

He said, "Bring it back to me, and you can have my daughter."

Dummling went back into the forest once again, where he found the little gray man.

When he shared his latest quest, the old man said, "Since you were kind to me before, I’ll grant you the ship."

Then he gifted Dummling the ship that could sail on land and water.

When the king saw this, he couldn’t deny Dummling his bride any longer.

The wedding took place, and after the king's passing, Dummling inherited the kingdom and lived happily ever after with his wife.

title: The Golden Key
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
In the winter, with thick snow covering the ground, a poor boy

had to go out on a sled to gather wood. After he collected it and packed it up, he thought, since he was freezing, that he didn't want to head home right away. Instead, he wanted to light a fire and warm himself for a bit.

So, he brushed aside the snow, and as he was cleaning the ground, he stumbled upon a tiny golden key.

He figured that if he found the key, there must be a lock nearby, so he dug into the ground and discovered an iron chest. "What if the key actually fits it?" he thought. "There could be something amazing inside that little box."

He searched for a keyhole but couldn’t find one at first. Finally, he spotted a tiny hole that was nearly invisible. He tried the key, and to his surprise, it fit perfectly.

He turned it around once, and now we must wait to see if he can completely unlock it and lift the lid. Only then will we discover the wonderful things that are hidden inside that box.

title: The Goose-Girl
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was an old queen whose husband had passed away many years ago.

She had a stunning daughter.

As the princess grew up, she became engaged to a prince who lived far away.

When it was finally time for her to marry and set off to the distant kingdom, the elderly queen packed up a collection of elegant silver and gold vessels.

She included fine trinkets of gold and silver, along with cups and jewels—basically everything that belonged to a royal dowry because she loved her daughter deeply.

She also sent along her maid, who would ride with her and deliver her to the groom.

Both of them had horses for the journey, but the princess’s horse was called Falada, and it could speak.

As the time for parting approached, the old queen went to her room, took a small knife, and cut her finger until it bled.

Then she pressed a white handkerchief to it, allowing three drops of blood to fall onto the cloth.

She gave the handkerchief to her daughter and said, "My dear child, keep this safe; it will help you in times of need."

They said a sorrowful goodbye, and the princess tucked the piece of cloth into her bosom, mounted her horse, and rode off toward her future husband.

After riding for a bit, she felt an intense thirst and told her maid, "Get down and use the cup you brought me to fetch some water from the stream. I want to drink."

The maid snapped back, "If you're thirsty, dismount and drink from the stream yourself; I’m not here to serve you."

In her desperation, the princess dismounted, bent down to the stream, and drank straight from the water instead of her golden cup.

Then she sighed, "Oh, heaven!" and the three drops of blood responded, "If your mother knew this, her heart would break in two."

But the princess remained humble, said nothing, and got back on her horse.

She rode for several more miles, but the heat of the day wore her down, and soon she felt thirsty again.

When they reached another stream, she called to her maid again, "Dismount and give me some water in my golden cup, please."

title: The Goose-Girl
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
However, the maid replied even more arrogantly, "If you want to drink, do it yourself; I won’t be your maid."

Once again, out of thirst, the princess dismounted, bent over the stream, and cried, "Oh, heaven!" and the drops of blood replied again, "If your mother knew this, her heart would break in two."

As she was drinking and leaning over the stream, her handkerchief with the three drops of blood slipped out of her bosom and floated away with the current, unnoticed due to her distress.

The maid saw this happen and was glad because now she held power over the princess—without the drops of blood, the princess became weak.

Now, when the princess tried to mount her horse, Falada, the waiting maid claimed, "Falada is better suited for me; you can ride my nag instead," and the princess had no choice but to accept.

Then the maid, with a lot of harsh words, ordered the princess to trade her royal clothes for her shabby outfit, and eventually, the princess had to swear by the clear sky above that she wouldn’t say a word about it to anyone at the royal court.

Had she refused to take that oath, she would have been killed on the spot.

But Falada saw everything and remembered it well.

The maid then mounted Falada while the true bride was left with the poor horse, and they continued their journey until they finally arrived at the royal palace.

There were great celebrations upon her arrival, and the prince hurried forward to greet her, lifting the waiting maid off her horse, believing she was his bride.

She was taken upstairs, while the true princess was left standing in the courtyard.

Then the old king peeked out of the window and saw her standing there, noticing how delicate, dainty, and beautiful she was.

He immediately went to the royal chamber and inquired about the girl standing in the courtyard.

The bride replied, "I picked her up on my way to keep me company; just find her some work to do so she doesn’t feel left out."

But the old king had no work for her, and so he said, "I have a little boy who tends the geese; she can help him."

title: The Goose-Girl
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The boy was named Conrad, and the true princess had to assist him with the geese.

Soon after, the false bride asked the young king, "Dearest husband, could you do me a favor?" He replied, "Of course, I would be glad to help."

She continued, "Please have the knacker cut off the horse’s head that I rode here; it annoyed me during the journey."

In reality, she was scared of Falada revealing what she had done to the princess.

She managed to get the king to promise this, and it soon reached the true princess that Falada was to die.

Secretly, she promised the knacker a piece of gold if he would do her a small favor.

There was a large, dark gateway in the town that she had to walk through with the geese every morning and evening; she requested him to nail up Falada’s head there so that she could see it repeatedly.

The knacker’s assistant agreed, cut off the head, and nailed it securely beneath the dark gateway.

Early the next morning, when she and Conrad drove their flock under this gateway, she sadly said, "Oh, young queen, how poorly you fare!"

They continued further out of town and drove their geese into the countryside.

When they reached the meadow, she sat down and let her golden hair loose, and Conrad spotted it, enchanted by its brilliance, wanting to snatch a few strands.

"Blow, blow, gentle wind, I wish," she said, "make him chase it everywhere until I finish braiding my hair!"

A strong wind swept through, blowing Conrad’s hat away far across the land, forcing him to run after it.

When he returned, she had already finished combing her hair and putting it up again, and he couldn’t get any of it.

Conrad, feeling frustrated, stayed silent, and they tended to the geese until evening fell, and then they went home.

The next day, while taking the geese out through the dark gateway again, the girl said, "Oh, young queen, how poorly you fare!"

She sat down again in the field to comb her hair, and Conrad, running after it, said quickly, "Blow, blow, gentle wind, I wish,"

title: The Goose-Girl
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"make him chase it everywhere until I'm done braiding my hair!"

The wind returned, whisking off Conrad’s little hat far away again, and he had no choice but to chase it down.

When he came back this time, she had been done with her hair for a long while and he couldn’t catch any.

So they continued watching their geese until nightfall.

But that night, after getting home, Conrad went to the old king and said, "I refuse to tend the geese with that girl any longer."

The aged king asked, "Why not?" Conrad replied, "Because she bugs me all day long."

Then the old king demanded that he explain what the girl did to annoy him.

Conrad explained everything: how, when they passed beneath the dark gateway, she spoke to the horse’s head and lamented, "Oh, young queen, how poorly you fare!"

He related the events that took place at the goose pasture and how he had to run after his hat.

The old king ordered him to take out the flock again the next day.

As morning came, he positioned himself behind the dark gateway and listened to the girl talking to Falada’s head.

He then went out into the fields, hiding himself in the bushes in the meadow.

Soon he saw the goose-girl and the goose-boy come with their flock, and after some time, she sat down and let her hair flow, shimmering with radiance.

"Blow, blow, gentle wind, I wish," she said, "make him chase it everywhere until I have braided my hair!"

The wind picked up and whisked Conrad’s hat away, forcing him to run far after it while the girl continued to calmly comb and braid her hair—all of which the king observed.

Then, still hidden, he slipped away, and when the goose-girl returned home that evening, he called her aside and asked why she did all these things.

She replied, "I can't tell anyone about it. I’m not allowed to share my sorrows with anyone, for I’ve sworn an oath to keep silent; had I not, I would lose my life."

title: The Goose-Girl
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He pressed her, leaving her no peace, but she wouldn’t reveal anything.

Finally, he suggested, "If you won’t tell me, talk to that iron stove over there, and he walked away.

She crept into the stove, weeping and pouring out her heart, saying, "Here I am, abandoned by everyone, but I’m a king’s daughter, forced by a false waiting maid to abandon my royal attire.

Now I must serve as a goose-girl; if only my mother knew!"

The aged king had been standing nearby, listening through the stove pipe, and he heard everything.

He returned and asked her to come out of the stove.

She emerged adorned in royal garments, showing just how stunning she was.

The old king then called for his son and revealed that the waiting maid was just that—a maid, while the true bride was the former goose-girl.

The young king was overjoyed upon seeing her beauty and youth, and preparations for a grand celebration were made.

Everyone was invited, including all their good friends.

At the head of the table, the groom sat next to the king's daughter, while the waiting maid sat opposite them, still blind to the true identity of the princess.

As they feasted and rejoiced, the old king asked the waiting maid, posing it as a riddle, what punishment she thought someone deserved for treating their master poorly.

He related the whole story and asked what such a person should receive as punishment.

The waiting maid replied, "Such a person deserves to be stripped naked and placed in a barrel lined with nails, pulled along by two white horses until she is dead."

The king said, "You, indeed, are that person, and you’ve just sentenced yourself. It shall be carried out as you said."

After the sentence was enforced, the young king married his true bride, and they ruled their kingdom together in peace and happiness.

title: The Goose-Girl at the Well
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was an elderly woman who lived with her flock of geese in a secluded clearing high in the mountains.

In this clearing, she had a cozy little house.

The area was surrounded by a vast forest,

and every morning she would take her crutch and hobble into it.

Yet, despite her age, she was surprisingly spry and active.

She gathered grass for her geese, picked wild fruits within her reach,

and carried everything back home on her back.

One would think that the heavy load would bring her down,

but she always managed to bring it back safely.

If anyone crossed her path, she greeted them warmly.

"Good day, dear farmer! It’s a lovely day, isn’t it?"

"You’re curious about why I’m carrying grass, aren’t you? But everyone must bear their own burdens."

Still, people preferred to avoid her when possible and often took the long way around.

If a father walked past with his sons, he would whisper to them, "Beware of the old woman; she has claws beneath her gloves. She’s a witch."

One bright morning, a handsome young man was walking through the forest.

The sun was shining, the birds were singing, and a gentle breeze rustled through the leaves, filling him with joy.

He hadn’t encountered anyone yet when he suddenly spotted the old witch kneeling on the ground, cutting grass with a sickle.

She had already packed a full load into her bundle, and nearby were two baskets brimming with wild apples and pears.

"But, dear mother, how can you manage to carry all that?" he asked.

"I must carry it, kind sir," she replied. "Rich kids don’t have to do these things; the saying is that for us peasants, if you don’t look behind you, you won’t see how twisted the path is."

"Will you lend me a hand?" she asked, noticing him still standing there.

"You have a strong back and young legs; it would be easy for you. Besides, my house isn’t far from here—it’s just up the hill.

title: The Goose-Girl at the Well
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
You’d be there in a flash." The young man felt sorry for the old woman.

"My father isn’t just a farmer; he’s a rich count," he said, "but if it proves that not only peasants can carry things, I’ll take your bundle."

"If you’re willing to try, I’d be very glad," she replied.

"You’ll definitely need to walk for about an hour, though, and you must carry the apples and pears too."

The young man felt a bit anxious at the idea of an hour-long walk, but the old woman wouldn’t let him back out.

She packed the bundle onto his back and hung the baskets on his arm.

"See? It’s quite light!" she said.

"No, it’s not light at all," he replied, pulling a face.

"Honestly, the bundle feels as heavy as if it’s filled with stones, and the apples and pears weigh as much as lead. I can barely breathe!"

He thought about dropping the whole thing, but the old woman wouldn’t let him.

"Look, the young gentleman won’t carry what I, an old woman, have carried so many times," she teased.

"You’re quick with your words, but when it’s time to get serious, you want to run away.

Why are you just standing there?" she insisted. "Keep moving!"

As long as he walked on level ground, it was somewhat bearable, but once they reached the hill and had to climb,

the stones rolled underfoot like they were alive, and it was too much for him.

Drops of sweat clung to his forehead and trickled both hot and cold down his back.

"Mother," he said, "I can’t go any further. I need to take a break.

"Not here," replied the old woman. "When we get to where we’re going, you can rest.

But right now you must keep moving! Who knows what good this will do you?"

title: The Goose-Girl at the Well
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"You're becoming quite shameless, old woman!" complained the count, trying to throw off the bundle, but it felt like it was glued to his back.

He twisted and turned but couldn’t shake it free. The old woman laughed merrily, moving around her crutch.

"Don’t get mad, dear sir!" she said. "You’re turning as red as a turkey! Just carry your bundle with patience.

I promise I’ll reward you well when we get home."

What else could he do? He had to accept his fate and trudged along behind the old woman.

Strangely enough, she seemed to get quicker while his burden only grew heavier.

Suddenly, she jumped onto the bundle and sat down on top of it.

No matter how frail she looked, she was heavier than the strongest village girl.

His knees began to shake, and when he hesitated, the old woman prodded his legs with a switch and nettles.

Groaning the entire way, he climbed the mountain.

Finally, just as he was about to collapse, he arrived at the old woman's house.

As soon as the geese saw the old woman, they flapped their wings, stretched their necks, and ran over, cackling happily.

Behind the flock walked a large, sturdy but ugly woman carrying a stick.

"Good mother!" she said to the old woman. "What took you so long?"

"Not at all, dear daughter," the old woman replied.

"I haven’t met with anything bad, but on the contrary, I met this kind gentleman who helped carry my burden.

Just imagine, he even took me on his back when I got tired! The way didn’t feel long at all. We’ve had quite a laugh together."

The old woman then slid down, took the bundle off the young man’s back, and set the baskets down.

She looked kindly at him and said, "Now have a seat on the bench outside and rest.

You’ve truly earned your reward, and I have plenty for you."

Then she told the goose girl, "Go inside, my dear daughter.

title: The Goose-Girl at the Well
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
It’s not appropriate to be alone with a young gentleman—it might spark his interest in you."

The count didn’t know whether to laugh or cry.

He thought, "Someone like that can’t capture my heart, even if she were thirty years younger!"

Meanwhile, the old woman was sweetly petting her geese as if they were her children before heading inside with her daughter.

The young man reclined on the bench under a wild apple tree.

The air was warm and gentle, and all around stretched a green meadow filled with cowslips, wild thyme, and countless other flowers.

A clear brook burbled through the meadow, sparkling in the sunlight, while the white geese waddled about or splashed in the water.

"It’s so lovely here," he said, "but I’m so tired that I can barely keep my eyes open. I think I’ll take a little nap.

I just hope a gust of wind doesn’t blow my legs off—they feel like they’re made of straw!"

After dozing off for a bit, the old woman woke him with a gentle shake.

"Rise and shine!" she said. "You can’t stay here.

I’ve certainly treated you poorly, but at least it hasn't cost you your life.

You don’t need money or land; I have something else for you."

With that, she handed him a small box made from a single emerald.

"Take care of it," she said. "It’ll bring you good fortune."

The count sprang to his feet, feeling rejuvenated, thanked the old woman for the gift, and left without even glancing back at the beautiful daughter.

As he walked away, he could still hear the echoes of the geese.

For three days, the count wandered through the wilds before finding his way out.

He finally reached a big town, and since he was well-known, he was taken directly to the royal palace, where the king and queen sat on their thrones.

The count knelt down, pulled out the emerald box, and set it at the queen’s feet.

title: The Goose-Girl at the Well
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She nodded for him to rise and handed the box back to her.

But as soon as she opened it and looked inside, she collapsed to the ground as if dead.

Just then, the king’s servants seized the count and began ushering him to prison, but the queen opened her eyes and demanded they set him free, wanting to speak with him privately.

Once they were alone, the queen began to weep uncontrollably. "What good are all the royal luxuries and honors surrounding me if I wake every morning in despair?"

I once had three daughters, the youngest of whom was so stunning that the whole world marveled at her.

She was as white as snow, as rosy as apple blossoms, and her hair shone like sunlight.

When she cried, her tears were not ordinary; they fell as pearls and jewels.

When she turned fifteen, the king summoned all three daughters to appear before him.

You should have seen how everyone gazed at the youngest as she entered; it was like witnessing the sunrise.

The king addressed them, saying, "My daughters, I don’t know when my final day will come, so today I’ll decide what each of you will inherit when I’m gone.

You all claim to love me, but the one who loves me the most will receive the best share."

All of them insisted that they loved him best.

"Can you express your love to me?" he asked, "so I can see just how much you mean it."

The eldest said, "I love you like the sweetest sugar."

The second said, "I love you like my prettiest dress."

But the youngest remained silent.

Then her father asked, "And you, my dearest child, just how much do you love me?"

"I don’t know, and it’s hard to compare my love to anything."

Though her father pressed her for an answer, she finally said, "The best food doesn’t taste right without salt, therefore I love my father like salt."

Upon hearing this, the king became furious and exclaimed, "If you love me like salt, then your love will be returned to you in kind."

title: The Goose-Girl at the Well
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He divided the kingdom between the two older daughters but bound a sack of salt to the youngest and sent her off deep into the wild forest.

We all pleaded and begged for her, the queen recalled, "but the king’s anger could not be undone.

How she cried when she had to leave us, pearls tumbling from her eyes all along the road."

Soon after, the king regretted his harsh decision and searched the whole forest for his poor daughter, but she was nowhere to be found.

I can’t shake the feeling that wild beasts have devoured her,

and I can barely cope with my sorrow.

Often, I comfort myself with the hope that she’s still alive, perhaps hiding in a cave or found by sympathetic souls.

But then imagine how I felt when I opened your little emerald box to find a pearl inside, just like those that used to fall from my daughter’s eyes.

You can understand how it stirred my heart.

You must tell me how you came upon that pearl!"

The count explained that he had received it from the old woman in the forest, who seemed quite strange and must surely have been a witch, but he hadn’t seen or heard anything of the queen’s daughter.

The king and queen resolved to search for the old woman.

They believed that where the pearl had come from, they would find news of their daughter.

The old woman was sitting alone at her spinning wheel. It was already getting dark, and a log burning in the hearth provided only a faint light.

Suddenly, outside, loud honks echoed as the geese returned from pasture.

Shortly after, her daughter came inside.

But the old woman barely acknowledged her, just shook her head.

The daughter took a seat next to her, took over the spinning, and twisted the threads deftly.

They sat that way for two whole hours without saying a word.

Finally, something rustled at the window, and two glowing eyes peered in.

It was an old owl, which hooted three times.

The old woman glanced up just a bit and said, "Alright, my little daughter, it’s time for you to do your work."

title: The Goose-Girl at the Well
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She got up and went outside, making her way over the meadows and into the valley.

Eventually, she reached a well surrounded by three old oak trees.

Meanwhile, the moon had risen full and bright above the mountains, so bright that one could have found a needle.

She took off the skin covering her face, leaned down to the well, and began to wash herself.

After finishing, she dipped the skin in the water and laid it out in the meadow to bleach and dry in the moonlight.

But what a transformation! No one had ever seen a change like this.

As soon as the gray mask was removed, her golden hair spilled forth like rays of sunshine, draped around her like a beautiful cloak.

Her eyes sparkled like stars, and her cheeks bloomed with a soft pink like apple blossoms.

But the lovely maiden seemed sad. She sat down, weeping bitterly.

Tears rolled down from her eyes and through her long hair, dropping to the ground.

She would have stayed there for ages if not for a rustling in the branches of the nearby tree.

She sprang up like a doe startled by a hunter's shot.

At that moment, a dark cloud covered the moon, and instantly she put the old skin back on and vanished, like a flicker of light.

Trembling like a leaf, she rushed home.

The old woman was standing at the door, and as the girl was about to explain her experience, the old woman laughed gently and said, "I already know everything."

She led her inside and lit another log.

Instead of returning to her spinning, she grabbed a broom and began cleaning.

"Everything must be clean and sweet," she told the girl. "But mother," the maiden protested, "why are you working so late?"

"What do you expect?" asked the old woman. "Do you know what time it is?"

"Not yet midnight," the maiden replied. "It's already past eleven o'clock."

"Do you not recall," the old woman continued, "that today marks three years since you came to me? Your time is up.

title: The Goose-Girl at the Well
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
We can no longer remain here together."

The girl felt terrified and said, "Oh, dear mother, will you cast me away? Where will I go?"

"I have no friends or home to return to! I've always obeyed you, and you've always been pleased with me. Please don’t send me away!"

The old woman refused to tell the girl what lay ahead.

"My time here is over, but before I go, this place must be spotless."

"So please don’t hinder me in my work. Don't worry about yourself; you’ll find a roof to shelter you, and the payment I give you will satisfy you."

But the maiden kept pleading, "Just tell me what’s going to happen!"

"I said, don’t disrupt my work. Don’t say another word. Go to your room, take off that mask, and put on the silk gown you wore when you came to me.

Then wait in your chamber until I call you."

Meanwhile, back with the king and queen, they had been traveling with the count to find the old woman.

The count had wandered off from them in the woods during the night and had to continue on his own.

The next day, feeling he was back on track, he pressed forward until it grew dark, climbing a tree to sleep, afraid of losing his way.

When the moon lit up the area, he spotted someone coming down the mountain.

She wasn't carrying a stick, yet he recognized her as the goose-girl he’d seen in the old woman’s house.

"Oh, there she is!" he thought. "If I can capture one of these witches, the other won't escape me!"

But he was astonished as he watched her approach the well, remove her skin, and wash herself.

Her golden hair fell around her, making her the most beautiful sight he had ever seen.

He held his breath, leaning forward through the leaves to catch a glimpse.

But whether he leaned too far or not, the branch cracked, and in an instant, the maiden slipped back into her skin and darted away like a deer.

title: The Goose-Girl at the Well
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
As the moon was suddenly obscured, she vanished from his view.

Hardly had she disappeared when the count climbed down from the tree and hurried after her.

Not long after, in the twilight, he spotted two figures approaching.

It was the king and queen who had noticed a light glowing from the old woman’s little house and were making their way to it.

The count eagerly told them about the incredible things he had seen by the well, and they were convinced it was their lost daughter.

Filled with joy, they moved towards the little house.

The geese were gathered around it, tucking their heads under their wings, fast asleep, with not a single movement among them.

The king and queen peered through the window to see the old woman quietly spinning, nodding her head and never looking around.

The room was spotless, as if the tiny dust folk lived there.

But their daughter was nowhere in sight.

They watched for quite a while, then finally gathered their courage and softly knocked at the window.

The old woman seemed to have been waiting for them, as she stood up and said warmly, "Come in. I already know who you are."

After they entered, she continued, "You could have saved yourself this long journey if you hadn’t unjustly banished your child three years ago.

She is good and lovable, and nothing bad has happened to her.

For three years, she has tended to the geese and learned no evil but preserved her pure heart.

You, however, have faced enough unhappiness from your own actions."

With that, she went into another room and called out, "Come, my little daughter!"

The door opened, and the princess emerged in her silken gown, golden hair cascading down and sparkling eyes shining.

It was like an angel stepping out of heaven.

She rushed to her parents, threw her arms around them, and kissed them.

They couldn’t help but weep tears of joy.

Nearby, the young count watched, and as she noticed him, her face flushed like a moss-rose, leaving her puzzled as to why.

The king said, "My dear child, I’ve given away my kingdom. What should I give you?"
