"She doesn’t need anything," the old woman interjected. "I’ll give her the tears she has shed for your sake.

They are precious pearls, finer than those found in the sea, worth more than your entire kingdom, and payment for her service."

Once the old woman finished speaking, she vanished from their sight.

The walls quaked a little, and when the king and queen looked around, they found the little house transformed into a magnificent palace.

A royal feast was laid out, and servants rushed about to attend to everything.

The tale doesn’t stop here, but my grandmother, who shared it with me, has partially forgotten the rest.

I like to believe that the beautiful princess married the young count, that they lived together in the palace, and enjoyed happiness as long as God allowed.

Regarding the snow-white geese kept near the little hut, whether they were truly young maidens under the old woman’s care or if they regained their human forms to serve the young queen is unknown to me.

However, it’s clear to me that the old woman was not a witch as many believed, but rather a wise woman with good intentions.

One can easily imagine that at the princess’s birth, she gifted her with pearls instead of tears, something that doesn’t seem to happen today, or else the poor would soon become rich.

title: The Grave-Mound
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One day, a wealthy farmer stood in his yard, inspecting his

fields and gardens. The corn was growing strong and healthy,

and the fruit trees were bursting with fruit. There were still

huge piles of grain from the previous year stacked in the loft, so

heavy that the rafters struggled to support them. Then he walked

into the stable, where well-fed oxen, plump cows, and horses that

shone like mirrors awaited him. Eventually, he returned to his

sitting room and glanced at the iron chest that held his money.

As he surveyed his wealth, a loud knock suddenly echoed beside

him. It wasn't a knock at his door, but at his heart. The door creaked open,

and he heard a voice ask, "Have you done good for your family with

this? Have you thought about the needs of the poor? Have you

shared your bread with the hungry? Have you been content with

what you have, or are you always wanting more?" The heart

didn't hesitate to answer, "I've been tough and unkind, and I've

never shown kindness to my family. If a beggar approached, I'd

look the other way. I've ignored God, focusing only on amassing

wealth. Even if everything the sky covered were mine, it still wouldn't be enough."

Realizing this, he felt a wave of fear, his knees started to tremble,

and he had to sit down.

Then there was another knock—this time at the door of his room.

It was his neighbor, a poor man with several children he could no longer feed.

The poor man thought to himself, "I know my neighbor is wealthy, but he's just as cold.

I doubt he’ll help me, but my kids are crying for bread, so I must take the chance."

He said to the rich man, "You rarely give away anything, but I'm here sounding the alarm.

My children are starving. Please lend me four measures of corn." The rich man

looked at him for a long time, and then the first hint of mercy began to melt

away the ice of his greed. "I won't lend you four measures," he replied.

title: The Grave-Mound
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"I'll give you eight, but you have to agree to one condition." "What do I need to do?" asked the poor man.

"When I die, you must watch over my grave for three nights." The peasant was disturbed

by this request, but given his desperate situation, he would have agreed to anything.

He accepted and took the corn home.

It seemed like the rich man had foreseen what was about to occur,

because just three days later, he suddenly dropped dead.

No one knew exactly how it happened, but nobody mourned for him.

When he was buried, the poor man recalled his promise.

He would have liked to be released from it, but he thought, "After all, he did act kindly towards me.

I fed my starving children with his corn, and even if that weren't the case, I must keep my word."

After nightfall, he went into the churchyard and sat by the grave mound.

Everything was quiet—the moon hung above the grave, and occasionally,

an owl swooped by, letting out its mournful cry.

When the sun rose, the poor man safely made his way back home,

and the second night passed just as quietly.

On the evening of the third day, he felt a strange anxiety, sensing that something was about to happen.

As he stepped outside, he spotted a man by the churchyard wall, someone he had never seen before.

The man was older, with scars on his face, and his eyes scanned his surroundings eagerly.

He was wrapped in a tattered cloak, with only his large riding boots visible.

"What are you looking for here?" the peasant asked. "Aren't you afraid?"

"I'm looking for nothing," the man replied, "and I'm afraid of nothing.

"I'm like a young man who set out to learn how to shudder but ended up married to a princess and gained great wealth—only, I remained poor.

"I'm just a discharged soldier, and I'm staying the night here since I have nowhere else to go."

"If you're unafraid," said the peasant, "stay with me and help me keep watch.

title: The Grave-Mound
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Watching is a soldier’s job," he replied, "whatever happens—good or bad—we'll face it together.

The peasant agreed, and they both sat down.

Everything was still until midnight when suddenly a sharp whistling cut through the air, and the two watchers saw the devil standing before them.

"Get lost, you rascals!" he shouted. "The man in that grave belongs to me. I want to take him, and if you don't leave, I'll break your necks!"

"Excuse me, red-feathered one," said the soldier, "you're not my superior.

I have no reason to obey you, and I still haven’t learned what fear is. We'll stay right here."

The devil realized money might sway them.

So, he tried a gentler approach, asking if they would accept a bag of gold and go home.

"That sounds appealing," the soldier replied, "but one bag of gold won't do.

If you give me enough to fill one of my boots, we'll leave."

"I don't have that much with me," said the devil, "but I'll be right back.

There's a money-changer in the neighboring town who's a good friend of mine, and he can lend it to me."

After the devil vanished, the soldier took off his left boot and said,

"Let's have some fun with our friend here, just hand me your knife, partner."

He sliced off the boot's sole and placed it in the grass near the grave, at the edge of a half-overgrown hole.

"That should do," he said. "Now let's wait for the devil to return."

They both sat quietly, and it wasn't long before the devil came back, clutching a small bag of gold.

"Just pour it in," the soldier instructed, lifting the boot a little, but the devil shook it out.

The gold fell through, and the boot sat empty.

"Come on, you foolish devil!" the soldier exclaimed, "this isn't going to work.

Didn't I tell you? Go back and bring more!"

The devil frowned, headed back, and returned in about an hour with a much larger bag.

"Now pour it in," cried the soldier, although he suspected the boot wouldn't fill.

The gold clinked as it fell, but the boot remained empty.

The devil glared with his burning eyes, realizing the truth.

"You have such big calves; did you think I had a cloven foot like you?" the soldier mocked. "Get more gold together or our deal's off!"

The devil stormed off again. This time, he took longer.

When he finally appeared, he was panting under the weight of a hefty sack.

He emptied it into the boot, but once again, it was far from full.

He was furious and was about to snatch the boot away from the soldier when suddenly,

the first ray of sunlight broke through the sky, and the devil disappeared, howling.

The poor soul was saved.

The peasant wanted to share the gold, but the soldier insisted,

"Give my share to the poor. I’ll accompany you to your cottage,

and together we can live in peace on what remains, as long as God allows."

title: The Griffin
author: Brothers Grimm
themes: the idea that appearances can be deceiving
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a king, but I don't know where he ruled or what his name was.

He had no son, just one daughter.

Unfortunately, she had always been sick, and no doctor had been able to help her.

Then, it was predicted that the king's daughter would regain her health by eating an apple.

So, he ordered an announcement to be made throughout the entire kingdom,

stating that whoever brought his daughter an apple that could make her well would marry her and become king.

A peasant with three sons heard of this,

and he told his eldest son to go into the garden and pick a basket of those beautiful apples with rosy cheeks.

"Take these to the court; maybe the king's daughter will be cured with them, and then you can marry her and be king," he said.

The boy agreed and set off. Shortly after leaving, he met an old man with a gray beard who asked what he had in his basket.

The boy, called Uele, replied, "Frog legs."

At this, the little man said, "Very well, so shall it be," and then walked away.

Eventually, Uele reached the palace and announced that he had brought apples that would cure the king's daughter if she ate them.

The king was thrilled and had Uele brought to him, but,

when the boy opened the basket, instead of apples, there were only frog legs still twitching.

The king became furious and had him thrown out.

Upon returning home, Uele explained what had happened.

Then the father sent the next son, named Same, to try his luck.

But everything turned out just like it had for Uele.

He too met the old man, who asked what he had in the basket.

Same replied, "Hog bristles."

"Well, so shall it be," replied the old man.

When Same reached the palace and claimed he had apples that could help the king's daughter, they refused him entry, saying that someone had already come and made fools of them.

However, Same insisted that he really did have apples, and eventually, they believed him and let him in.

title: The Griffin
author: Brothers Grimm
themes: the idea that appearances can be deceiving
text_chunk:
But when he revealed his basket, it contained only hog bristles.

This infuriated the king, who ordered him whipped and thrown out.

When he got home, he shared his misfortune, prompting the youngest son, always labeled Stupid Hans, to ask his father if he could go for some apples.

The father replied, "Oh, you would be just the right one for this. If the smart ones can’t do it, what chance do you have?"

But Hans was persistent, saying, "Please, Father, I really want to go."

"Just go on then, you foolish boy. You're only going to come back anyway," the father said dismissively.

Hans, however, was filled with excitement and jumped for joy.

"You keep getting more foolish every day," his father grumbled.

But Hans remained undeterred and decided to wait until morning to set out, since it was already nighttime.

All night he tossed and turned in bed, dreaming of lovely maidens, beautiful palaces, gold, silver, and all sorts of wonders.

When morning came, he set off, and soon after, he encountered the shabby little man in his icy clothes, who asked what was in his basket.

Hans replied that he had apples that could restore the health of the king's daughter.

"Very well, that shall be the case," said the little man.

At the court, the guards wouldn't let Hans in, saying that two others had come claiming they had apples, one with frog legs and the other with hog bristles.

However, Hans insisted he didn’t have any frog legs, just the loveliest apples in the whole kingdom.

His earnestness convinced the gatekeeper, who took him inside.

When Hans opened his basket in front of the king, beautiful golden-yellow apples fell out.

The king was overjoyed and immediately sent some to his daughter, waiting anxiously for news of the outcome.

Before long, news arrived, and to the king's surprise, the daughter herself came running in.

As soon as she had tasted the apples, she was cured and jumped out of bed.

The king’s joy was indescribable.

title: The Griffin
author: Brothers Grimm
themes: the idea that appearances can be deceiving
text_chunk:
But he didn't want to give his daughter to Hans just yet and told him he first had to build a boat that could go faster on land than on water.

Hans agreed to the task and went home to share the news with his father.

The father promptly sent him into the woods to create the boat.

He worked hard while whistling happily.

At noon, when the sun was high, the little icy man returned and asked what he was making.

Uele answered, "Wooden bowls for the kitchen."

"Very well, that shall be the case," said the little man.

By evening, Uele thought he had finished the boat, but when he tried to climb in, he found only wooden bowls.

The next day, Same went to the woods, but everything happened just like with Uele.

On the third day, Stupid Hans went, working diligently and singing joyously, causing echoes in the forest.

At noon, the little icy man appeared again, asking what Hans was making.

"I'm building a boat that will go faster on dry land than in water," Hans replied. "Once it's done, I get the king's daughter as my wife."

"Very well, so it shall be," said the little man.

By sunset, when the sky turned golden, Hans finished the boat.

He climbed in and rowed to the palace, and the boat raced like the wind.

The king saw it from afar but still wouldn’t give his daughter to Hans.

He ordered Hans to take a hundred hares out to pasture from dawn till dusk, saying that if any escaped, Hans would lose his chance.

Hans accepted this challenge and set off the next day with the hares, making sure none got away.

After a few hours, a palace servant came to tell Hans that he needed to hand over a hare right away because visitors had unexpectedly arrived.

Hans knew what this meant and replied that the king could serve hare soup to his guests the next day.

However, the maid insisted, and they argued.

Finally, Hans said he would give a hare to the king's daughter herself.

title: The Griffin
author: Brothers Grimm
themes: the idea that appearances can be deceiving
text_chunk:
The maid relayed this back to the castle, and soon, the princess herself came.

Meanwhile, the little man came again to Hans, asking how things were going.

Hans said he was watching over a hundred hares, and if he succeeded, he could marry the king's daughter.

"Good," said the little man, "here’s a whistle, and if one runs away, just blow it, and it will come back."

When the princess arrived, Hans gave her a hare for her apron, but as she walked away, he whistled.

The hare jumped out of her apron and raced back to the flock before she could turn around.

As evening came, Hans whistled again to check his hares before leading them back to the palace.

The king was astonished at how Hans managed to care for a hundred hares without losing any,

yet still refused to give him his daughter.

He then instructed Hans to bring him a feather from a griffin’s tail.

Hans set off immediately, walking straight ahead.

That evening, he reached a castle and asked for a place to stay since there were no inns at that time.

The lord of the castle gladly offered him a room and inquired where he was headed.

Hans replied, "To the griffin."

"Oh, the griffin! I've heard he knows everything. I lost the key to my iron money chest; could you ask him where it is?" the lord said.

"Of course, I will," said Hans.

The next morning, he continued on and arrived at another castle where he spent the night.

The residents learned he was going to the griffin and said they had a sick daughter.

They had tried everything to help her, but nothing worked, and they asked Hans to find out what could cure her.

Hans agreed and moved on.

Then he came to a lake, where a very tall man was waiting to carry everyone across.

The man asked Hans where he was headed.

"To the griffin," Hans replied.

"When you see him, ask him why I have to carry everyone across the lake," the man said.

title: The Griffin
author: Brothers Grimm
themes: the idea that appearances can be deceiving
text_chunk:
"Absolutely, I'll do just that," Hans assured him.

The man lifted Hans onto his shoulders and crossed the lake.

Finally, Hans arrived at the griffin's house, but only the wife was home.

She asked him what he wanted.

Hans explained that he needed a feather from the griffin's tail and that there was a castle where they had lost the money chest key.

He also mentioned a daughter who was sick and that he needed to find out how to help her.

Lastly, he talked about the man by the lake, curious to learn why he had to carry people across.

The woman said, "Listen carefully, my friend; no Christian can talk to the griffin—he eats them.

But you can hide under his bed, and while he sleeps deeply, you can pull a feather from his tail.

I will ask about the other matters myself."

Hans gladly accepted her advice and lay down under the bed.

In the evening, when the griffin came home, he sniffed the air and said, "Wife, I smell a Christian."

"Yes," she replied, "one was here today, but he has left."

And the griffin said no more.

Later that night, while the griffin snored, Hans carefully reached out and plucked a feather.

The griffin awoke immediately, saying, "Wife, I smell a Christian, and it feels like someone is tugging at my tail."

His wife replied, "You must be dreaming. Remember, a Christian was here today, but he left."

The griffin recounted that someone had lost the key to their money chest.

"Oh, the fools!" he exclaimed. "The key is in the wood shed, under a log behind the door."

He then said that a daughter was ill and they needed a remedy.

"Oh, the fools!" he repeated, "A toad has made its nest with her hair under the cellar stairs; if she gets her hair back, she'll be fine."

And finally, he commented on the lake and the man who had to carry others across.

"Oh, the fool!" he scoffed. "If he just set one person down in the middle of the lake, he'd never have to carry anyone else."

title: The Griffin
author: Brothers Grimm
themes: the idea that appearances can be deceiving
text_chunk:
The next morning, the griffin left for the day.

Hans came out from under the bed with the beautiful feather.

He had heard everything the griffin had said about the key, the daughter, and the man.

The griffin's wife repeated it to him to ensure he wouldn’t forget.

Then Hans made his way home.

First, he came to the man by the lake, who asked what the griffin had said.

But Hans told him he first needed to be carried across.

So the man agreed and ferried him across.

Once they were on the other side, Hans said that all he had to do was set one person down in the middle of the lake, and he’d never have to carry anyone again.

The man was thrilled and offered to take Hans back as a gesture of thanks.

But Hans declined, saying he was satisfied and continued on his way.

Next, he arrived at the castle where the sick daughter lived.

He carried her down the cellar steps since she couldn't walk and pulled the toad's nest from underneath the lowest step.

He handed it to her, and she jumped off his shoulders, raced up the steps, and was completely cured.

Her parents were beyond overjoyed and showered Hans with gold, silver, and anything else he desired.

When he reached the other castle, he went straight to the wood shed and found the key under the log behind the door.

He brought it back to the castle lord, who was pleased and rewarded Hans with gold from the chest and various gifts, like cows, sheep, and goats.

When Hans returned to the king with all his treasures—money, gold, silver, cows, sheep, and goats—the king asked how he acquired such riches.

Hans told him that the griffin granted whatever one desired.

The king thought he could benefit from that and set out to find the griffin.

But when he arrived at the lake, he was the first after Hans, and he was placed in the middle of it, where he drowned.

However, Hans married the princess and became king.

title: The Hare and the Hedgehog
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
This story may sound a bit unbelievable, kids, but it’s true.

I got it from my grandfather, who always insisted it had to be true or else no one would be able to tell it.

Let me share the story with you.

One Sunday morning during harvest season, just as the buckwheat was blooming,

the sun shone brightly in the sky, a warm eastern breeze swept across the stubble fields, larks chirped happily,

bees buzzed around the buckwheat, people dressed in their Sunday best were walking to church, and all the creatures were joyful,

including the hedgehog.

The hedgehog stood by his door with his arms crossed, enjoying the morning breeze and humming a little tune to himself,

which was about as good as the songs hedgehogs usually sing on a lovely Sunday morning.

As he sang softly, it suddenly hit him that since his wife was busy washing and drying the kids,

he could take a quick walk into the field and check on his turnips.

The turnips were actually quite close to his home, and he and his family often enjoyed eating them,

so he felt they were rightfully his.

The hedgehog closed the front door behind him and followed the path to the field.

He hadn’t gone far when he rounded a sloe bush that stood by the edge of the field, and there he spotted the hare,

who was out doing the same thing—checking on his cabbages.

When the hedgehog saw the hare, he cheerfully greeted him.

But the hare, who considered himself quite distinguished and was overly proud, ignored the hedgehog's greeting.

Instead, he looked down his nose and asked in a condescending tone, "What are you doing out here so early in the morning?"

"I'm taking a walk," the hedgehog replied.

"A walk?" the hare scoffed with a sly smile.  "Seems to me you could put your legs to better use."

This response made the hedgehog furious, as he couldn’t stand jokes about his legs,

especially since they were crooked by nature.

So the hedgehog shot back at the hare, "You think you can do more with your legs than I can with mine?"

title: The Hare and the Hedgehog
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"That’s exactly what I think," replied the hare.

"Let’s settle this with a race! I bet I can outpace you."

"That’s ridiculous! You with your short legs?" laughed the hare. "But if you’re so eager, I’ll accept that challenge.

What should we bet?"

"How about a golden louis-d’or and a bottle of brandy?" said the hedgehog.

"Deal," the hare responded. "Let's shake on it and get started right away!"

"Not so fast!" the hedgehog interrupted. "I haven’t eaten yet, let me head home for a quick breakfast.

I’ll be back in half an hour."

The hedgehog left, and the hare didn’t mind this delay.

On his way home, the hedgehog thought to himself, "The hare is too confident in his long legs, but I’ll outsmart him.

He might be a big shot, but he’s really quite foolish, and he’ll pay for what he said."

When the hedgehog got home, he told his wife, "Quick, get dressed! We need to head to the field."

"What’s happening?" asked his wife.

"I made a wager with the hare for a golden louis-d’or and some brandy, and I need you to be there."

"Oh dear, husband! Have you lost your mind? Why would you race a hare?" she exclaimed.

"Don't worry about it, dear," the hedgehog snapped. "This is a man’s business. Hurry up, get ready, and let’s go."

What could she do? The hedgehog's wife had to go along with him, whether she liked it or not.

Once they were on their way, the hedgehog said to his wife, "Listen carefully to what I’m about to say.

We'll use the long field for our race. The hare will run in one furrow, and as he reaches the end,

you must shout to him, 'I’m here already!'"

title: The Hare and the Hedgehog
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
They arrived at the field, and the hedgehog pointed out his wife's spot before walking to the top of the field.

By the time he got there, the hare was already waiting.

"Ready to start?" the hare asked.

"Absolutely!" said the hedgehog.

With that, they both got into their positions.

The hare counted: once, twice, thrice, and off he went like a rocket, zooming down the field.

The hedgehog, however, only sprinted a few steps before curling up in the furrow, staying perfectly still.

When the hare reached the end of the field at full speed, the hedgehog's wife greeted him with a cheerful, "I’m here already!"

The hare was stunned and puzzled; he thought the hedgehog himself was calling out to him since the hedgehog's wife looked just like him.

Yet, the hare couldn’t shake the feeling that something was unfair, and shouted, "Let’s race again!"

Once more, he took off like the wind, appearing to fly as he dashed down the field.

But the hedgehog's wife stayed calmly in her spot.

When the hare reached the top of the field, the hedgehog himself called out, "I’m here already!"

At this point, the hare was absolutely furious and shouted, "This isn’t fair! We have to race again!"

"Sure!" replied the hedgehog casually. "I’m game to run whenever you want."

And so the hare raced a total of seventy-three times more, while the hedgehog always held his ground.

Each time the hare reached either end of the field, the hedgehog or his wife would say, "I’m here already!"

By the seventy-fourth attempt, the hare was completely exhausted.

He collapsed in the middle of the field, blood pouring from his mouth, and lay still.

Meanwhile, the hedgehog grabbed the golden louis-d’or he’d won and the bottle of brandy, called his wife out of the furrow,

and they happily headed home together. If they’re still alive, they’re living there happily ever after.

This is how the hedgehog got the hare to run races with him on the heath of Buxtehude—a village near Hamburg.

Since then, no hare has ever wanted to race against a hedgehog from Buxtehude.

The moral of this story is, first, that regardless of how great someone is, they shouldn’t belittle anyone below them,

even if it’s just a hedgehog. Second, it also suggests that when a man marries, he should choose a wife who matches his own stature,

someone who looks like him. So any hedgehog should make sure his wife is a hedgehog too.

title: The Hare's Bride
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a woman and her daughter who lived in a

lovely garden filled with cabbages. One day, a little hare hopped in.

During the winter, he munched on all the cabbages. The mother then said

to her daughter, "Go into the garden and chase the hare away."

The girl called out to the little hare, "Hey, hare, you’re going to eat

all our cabbages!" The hare replied, "Come here, maiden, sit on my

little hare's tail, and let’s go to my cozy little hut." The girl refused.

The hare came back again and nibbled on the cabbages, prompting the mother to say

to her daughter once more, "Go into the garden and drive the hare away." The girl

approached the hare again, saying, "Hey, little hare, you will eat all our

cabbages!" The little hare insisted, "Come, maiden, sit on my little hare's tail and

let’s go to my hut."

On the third day, the hare returned and ate more cabbages. Once again, the mother told

her daughter to chase the hare away. The girl warned, "Shh, little hare, you'll eat all our

cabbages!" The little hare replied, "Come, maiden, sit on my little hare's tail and let's

go to my hut."

The girl finally sat on the little hare's tail, and he whisked her away to his

tiny hut. Once there, he told her, "Now cook some green cabbage and millet seed, and

I'll invite our wedding guests over." Soon, everyone was gathered.

Who were the guests, you ask? I can tell you, as someone once shared this with me.

They were all hares, with the crow acting as the parson to marry the bride and groom,

and the fox serving as the clerk. The altar was set right under a tree.

However, the girl felt sad because she was all alone. The little hare returned and said,

"Open the doors! Open the doors! The wedding guests are having a great time!"

The bride remained silent, her tears flowing. The little hare left.

He came back shortly and said, "Take off the lid! Take off the lid! The wedding guests are hungry!" The

bride said nothing and continued to cry. The little hare left again.

He returned once more and insisted, "Take off the lid! Take off the lid! The wedding guests are waiting!"

Again, the bride did not respond, and the hare walked away. But she quickly got to work,

making a straw doll dressed in her clothes, giving it a spoon to stir with, and placing it by the

pan of millet seed. Then she went back to her mother.

The little hare returned and said, "Take off the lid! Take off the lid!" He got up and hit the doll on the head.

The doll's cap fell off, and the little hare realized it wasn't his bride after all, so he left.

title: The Hazel-Branch
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One afternoon, the Christ-child had settled into his cradle.

He soon drifted off to sleep. Just then, his mother came to him, looking at him

with joy, and said, "Have you put yourself down for a nap, my child?

Sleep sweetly, and while you rest, I'll head into the

woods to bring you a handful of strawberries. I know you'll love them when you wake up."

In the woods, she found a spot bursting with the finest strawberries, but as she

bent down to pick one, a snake suddenly slithered out of the grass.

She panicked, left the strawberries untouched, and

quickly ran away. The snake pursued her, but our lady, as you

can imagine, knew exactly what to do. She hid

behind a hazel bush and waited until the snake had moved on.

Once she gathered the strawberries, she began her journey home and said, "Since the hazel bush has protected me this time, it will also protect others in the future."

So, from ancient times, a green hazel branch has been a reliable shield against snakes, adders, and anything else that crawls on the ground.

title: The Iron Stove
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
In the days when wishes still held some magic, a prince was

cursed by an old witch and imprisoned in an iron stove deep in a forest.

For many years, he remained trapped, and no one could save him. Then one day,

a king's daughter got lost in the forest, unable to find her way back to her father's kingdom.

After wandering for nine days, she finally stumbled upon the iron stove.

A voice called out from within, asking her, "Where are you coming from, and where are you going?" She replied, "I’ve lost my father's kingdom and can’t find my way home again."

Then the voice from the iron stove said, "I can help you get home quickly if you promise to do something for me. I am the son of a king much greater than your father, and I want to marry you."

This made her anxious, and she thought, "Goodness, what can I do with an iron stove?" But since she desperately wanted to return to her father, she agreed to his request.

He instructed her, "Come back here with a knife and scrape a hole in the iron." Then he gave her a silent companion who accompanied her, and in two hours, he took her home.

The castle erupted in joy when the king's daughter returned, and her father embraced her tightly, kissing her. But she was deeply troubled and said, "Dear father, I’ve suffered so much. I might never have found my way home from that wild forest if I hadn’t encountered an iron stove, but now I must return to it."

The old king was so frightened he nearly fainted, for she was his only daughter. They decided to send the miller's daughter, who was very beautiful, in her place.

They brought her to the iron stove with a knife and instructed her to scrape it. She worked at it for twenty-four hours but couldn’t remove even a sliver.

When dawn broke, a voice from the stove said, "It seems to me that it’s day outside." The miller’s daughter replied, "I think so too; I believe I hear my father’s mill."

title: The Iron Stove
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Ah, so you’re a miller’s daughter. Go away, and let the king’s daughter come here," the voice instructed.

She hurried back to the old king and told him that the man within the stove refused her—he only wanted the king’s daughter.

The old king grew anxious, and the miller's daughter cried. Meanwhile, a swineherd’s daughter, who was even more beautiful, was offered a piece of gold to visit the iron stove in place of the king’s daughter.

So, she too scraped at the stove for twenty-four hours, but without any success. At daybreak, the voice inside the stove called out, "It seems to me that it’s day outside."

She answered, "It seems that way to me too; I think I hear my father’s horn." The stove then said, "You’re a swineherd’s daughter. Leave at once and tell the king’s daughter that she must come, and remind her of all that was promised. If she fails to show, everything in the kingdom will be destroyed, and not a single stone will remain standing."

When the king's daughter heard this, she began to cry, but there was nothing left to do but keep her promise. She said goodbye to her father, tucked a knife in her pocket, and set out for the iron stove in the forest.

Upon arriving, she began to scrape, and before long, the iron started to give way. After two hours, she managed to create a small hole. She peeked in and saw a handsome young man, adorned with gold and jewels, so glorious that it warmed her heart.

Encouraged, she continued scraping until the hole was large enough. He exclaimed, "You’re mine, and I am yours; you've freed me." He wanted to take her to his kingdom, but she pleaded to visit her father one last time.

The prince agreed, but only if she promised to say no more than three words, after which she must return immediately. She went home, but unable to keep her promise, she spoke far more than three words.

title: The Iron Stove
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Instantly, the iron stove vanished, carried far away over glass mountains and sharp swords, but the prince was liberated from his confinement. After bidding farewell to her father, she took a little money with her and went searching for the iron stove, but it was nowhere to be found.

After nine days of searching, her hunger grew unbearable, leaving her uncertain of what to do. One evening, she climbed into a small tree, deciding to spend the night there out of fear of wild animals.

As midnight approached, she spotted a faint light in the distance and thought, "Perhaps salvation lies ahead." She climbed down from the tree and moved toward the light, praying as she went.

Soon, she reached a tiny run-down house, the grass grown wild around it, and a small pile of wood out front. "Where am I?" she wondered, peering through the window only to see nothing but toads of all sizes, set against a table laden with wine and roast meat, served on silver plates.

Courageously, she knocked on the door, and immediately a fat toad answered, saying, "Who’s there?" A little toad then hopped over to open it for her. Once inside, they welcomed her warmly, urging her to sit.

They inquired, "Where did you come from, and where are you going?" She shared her story of how she had broken her promise and now sought the prince once more.

The old fat toad then said, "We'll help you, but first, you must take this with you." The little toad retrieved a box from another room.

They served her food and drink, then offered her a lovely bed, as soft as silk and velvet, where she fell asleep soundly.

The next morning, the old toad handed her three needles from the box, saying she'd need them for her journey across a high glass mountain, three piercing swords, and a great lake. If she succeeded, she'd find her prince once again.

They also provided her with three important items: a large plough-wheel and three nuts, which she was to take special care of.

title: The Iron Stove
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Setting off, she reached the slippery glass mountain first, using the three needles for balance—sticking them behind and in front of her feet—to cross it safely. Once over, she hid the needles carefully.

Next, she faced the three sharp swords. Sitting on her plough-wheel, she rolled over them without a scratch. Eventually, she arrived at a sizeable, beautiful castle.

She approached them and requested a position, claiming she was a poor girl looking for work. Unbeknownst to her, the prince she had freed from the iron stove was in that castle.

She was hired as a scullery maid at a low wage, though the prince had already chosen another bride in his heart.

That evening, after cleaning, she dug into her pocket and found the three nuts from the old toad. She cracked one open and, to her surprise, discovered a stunning royal garment inside.

When the bride heard about the dress, she came to demand it and insisted, "It doesn’t belong to a servant girl." The girl replied she wouldn’t sell it, but offered to give it to the bride if she could spend a night in the prince’s chamber.

The bride, swayed by the dress's beauty and having never owned one like it, agreed.

That evening, she told the prince, "That silly girl will be sleeping in your room tonight." He simply replied, "If you’re alright with it, then so am I." But the bride secretly drugged his drink.

So the prince and the scullery maid both fell asleep in the same room, and he was so deeply asleep she couldn’t wake him. Meanwhile, she cried through the night, saying, "I set you free when you were locked in that iron stove in the wild forest. I crossed a glass mountain, faced three sharp swords, and crossed a great lake to find you, but you don’t even hear me."

The servants at the door listened to her weeping all night, sharing the tale with their master the next day.

The following evening, after her chores, she opened the second nut, revealing an even more exquisite dress. The bride, upon seeing it, wanted to buy it too, but the girl refused to take any money.

title: The Iron Stove
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She requested one more night in the bridegroom's chamber. The bride consented, but again, she slipped him a sleeping potion.

He fell into such a deep slumber that he heard nothing. Once more, the scullery maid wept throughout the night, lamenting, "I freed you from the iron stove in the wild forest, overcame a glass mountain, three sharp swords, and a great lake just to find you, but you still can’t hear me."

The servants at the door overheard her sobbing all night, and they reported it to their master at dawn.

On the third evening, after completing her tasks, she opened the last nut, revealing the most beautiful dress made of pure gold. Seeing it, the bride was determined to have it.

The girl would only give it up if she could sleep in the prince’s room one last time. This time, the prince was aware and discarded the sleeping draught.

When she started to cry, saying, "My dearest love, I freed you when you were trapped in the iron stove in the wild forest," the prince sprang up and proclaimed, "You’re the true one! You’re mine, and I’m yours!"

Without delay, they boarded a carriage together, taking the false bride’s clothes so she couldn’t follow. When they reached the great lake, they sailed across it, and as they came to the three sharp swords, they sat on the plough-wheel.

When they reached the glass mountain, they stuck the three needles into it, and at last, found their way back to the little old house, which transformed into a grand castle before their eyes.

Inside, the toads were all turned back into charming princes and princesses, full of joy. They celebrated a grand wedding, and the prince and the true princess lived in the enchanted castle, much larger than those of their parents.

As the old king felt lonely, they invited him to live with them, and together they ruled over two kingdoms, living happily ever after.

title: The Jew Among Thorns
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a wealthy man who had a servant.

This servant worked hard and was completely honest.

He was always the first one up in the morning and the last one to go to bed at night.

Whenever there was a tough task that no one else wanted to do, he was always the first to jump in.

Plus, he never complained; he was content with everything and always cheerful.

At the end of the year, however, his master didn't pay him.

He thought to himself that it was a clever move because he’d save money and the servant would stick around.

The servant said nothing and continued to work just as hard in his second year.

When that year ended and he still hadn’t been paid, he accepted it and stayed on.

After the third year, his master thought about it, reached into his pocket, but found nothing.

Finally, the servant spoke up, "Master, I’ve worked honestly for three years. Please give me what I deserve, as I’d like to explore the world a bit more."

"Oh, my good fellow," replied the old miser, "you've worked hard, and for that, I’ll reward you nicely."

Then he reached into his pocket but only counted out three farthings, saying, "Here you go, a farthing for each year. It’s generous pay, considering what you would’ve received from others."

The honest servant, who didn’t know much about money, took the coins and thought, "Now that I have some money, why should I stress myself with hard work anymore?"

So he took off, wandering happily hills and valleys, singing and jumping for joy.

As he strolled past a thicket, a little man stepped out and called out to him, "Where are you off to, cheerful friend? You seem free of worries!"

“Why should I be sad?” the servant replied. “I have enough. Three years' wages are jingling in my pocket.”

"How much do you have?" asked the dwarf. "Three farthings total," the servant answered.

title: The Jew Among Thorns
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Look, I’m a poor guy and need your three farthings. I can no longer work, but you’re young and can easily earn your keep."

Feeling sympathetic for the little man, the servant generously gave him the three farthings, saying, "Take it, and may it bring you good fortune; I won’t miss it."

The little man smiled and said, "Since you have such a generous heart, I'll grant you three wishes, one for each farthing. They'll all come true!"

"Wow!" said the servant. "If that’s the case, I wish for a gun that hits everything I aim at, a fiddle that makes anyone who hears it dance, and the ability to ask for a favor that can’t be refused."

"You’ll have all that," said the dwarf, then reached into the bushes and pulled out a fiddle and a gun, as if they had been waiting just for him.

He handed them to the servant and said, "Whatever you ask, no one will be able to refuse you."

"Wow! What more could a person want?" the servant thought and continued on his merry way.

Soon, he encountered a Jew with a long beard, who was listening to a bird singing in a tree.

"Good heavens," the Jew exclaimed, "that tiny creature has such a loud voice! If only I could catch it!"

"If that’s all you want," said the servant, "I can get it down for you." He aimed and fired the gun, and down fell the bird into the thorn bushes.

"Now go fetch the bird, you rascal!" the servant teased.

"Oh, forget the rascal part!" the Jew replied. "I'll get it myself now that you've shot it."

He laid down and began crawling into the thorns.

When he got stuck, the servant’s sense of humor got the better of him, and he took out his fiddle and started to play.

Suddenly, the Jew couldn't help but dance; the more the servant played, the wilder the Jew's dancing became.

title: The Jew Among Thorns
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Meanwhile, the thorns snagged at his shabby clothes, pulled at his beard, and pricked him all over.

"Oh dear!" cried the Jew. "What do I care for your fiddling? Leave it alone! I don’t want to dance!"

But the servant ignored him, thinking, "You’ve cheated enough people; now the thorns should give you a taste of your own medicine."

He played even more vigorously, making the Jew jump higher, leaving scraps of his coat on the thorns.

"Oh no! Please!" begged the Jew. "I’ll give you whatever you want if you'll just stop playing! A whole purse of gold!"

"If you’re so generous, I’ll stop, but I have to say, you dance really well to my music!" the servant replied, then took the purse and walked away.

The Jew watched in silence until the servant was far away, then screamed at the top of his lungs, "You miserable musician! Just wait until I catch you alone; I’ll make you regret this!"

"You ragamuffin, you wouldn't be worth a dime!" he shouted, venting all his anger.

After getting his breath back, he rushed into town.

"Your honor, I come to file a complaint! Look at what that rascal did to me on the road!" he cried, pointing to his torn clothes and scratched body.

"A stone might pity me, my little all gone, my purse with good ducats inside... Please, help me!"

"Was it a soldier who did this?" asked the judge.

"Not at all!" replied the Jew. "It was no sword; he had a gun on his back and a fiddle around his neck—easy to recognize!"

So the judge sent his men to find the servant.

They quickly found him walking slowly and also recovered the purse of money he had.

When he was brought before the judge, he said, "I didn’t touch the Jew or take his money. He gave it to me willingly just to stop my music!"

title: The Jew Among Thorns
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Heaven save us!" cried the Jew. "His lies are thicker than flies!"

However, the judge didn't buy it either, saying, "This excuse doesn’t hold up. No Jew would ever do that."

Since the servant had robbed along the highway, he was sentenced to hang.

As he was being led away, the Jew yelled after him, "You scoundrel! You music-playing dog! You're getting the punishment you deserve!"

Moving calmly up the ladder, the servant stopped at the last step.

"Could I have just one request before I die?" he asked.

"Sure, as long as it’s not asking for your life," replied the judge.

"I don’t ask for life," answered the servant, "but please let me play my fiddle one last time."

The Jew cried out in panic, "Murder! Don’t let him!"

But the judge insisted, "Why should I deny him this small pleasure? He deserves it."

Though he knew he couldn't refuse the request, he still wanted to maintain order.

"Oh no, please! Tie me up!" the Jew pleaded as the servant prepared to play.

Once the servant started to play, everyone began to quiver and shake.

The judge, the clerk, and the hangman's men couldn’t resist the rhythm.

At the second note, they all jumped up and started dancing, with the judge and the Jew dancing like pros.

Soon, a crowd gathered in the market square, young and old joining in on the wild dance.

Even the dogs that had wandered over began dancing on their hind legs.

The longer he played, the more spirited the dance became, with everyone bumping into each other.

Finally, the judge gasped, "I’ll spare your life if you just stop fiddling!"

The servant, feeling compassionate, took down his fiddle, hung it around his neck, and stepped down from the ladder.

Then he walked over to the Jew, who was lying on the ground, panting.

"Now confess where you got that money, or I’ll start playing again!" he said.

"I stole it!" the Jew cried. "But you earned it justly!"

The judge then had the Jew taken to the gallows and hanged for being a thief.

title: The Juniper-Tree
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
A long time ago, nearly two thousand years, there lived

a wealthy man who had a lovely and faithful wife, and their love for

each other was deep.

However, they had no children, which they deeply desired.

The woman prayed tirelessly, day and night, for a child—yet, none came.

In front of their house stood a courtyard with a juniper tree, and one chilly

winter day, while standing beneath it, the woman was peeling an

apple.

As she was peeling, she accidentally cut her finger, and her blood dripped onto the snow.

"Oh!" she sighed heavily, gazing sadly at the blood before her.

With a longing heart, she murmured, "If only I could have a child as red as blood and

as white as snow."

As she spoke, a warmth filled her heart, making her feel like that wish might just come true.

She returned to the house, and a month passed quickly; the snow melted away,

and then two months, and soon everything turned green.

Three months went by, and flowers burst forth from the earth.

In the fourth month, the trees in the woods thickened, their lush branches entwining.

Birdsongs filled the air, making the woods come alive as blossoms fell from the branches.

By the fifth month, the woman stood again under the fragrant juniper tree,

her heart racing with joy.

She knelt and, filled with happiness, prayed.

When the sixth month arrived, the fruit swelled to perfection.

She felt a calm settle over her; in the seventh month, she eagerly picked the

juniper berries and devoured them.

Then she felt sick and saddened.

As the eighth month rolled in, she called her husband, cried, and said, "If I die, please bury me

beneath the juniper tree."

Feeling comforted, she was happy again until the next month came.

At last, she gave birth to a child as white as snow and as red as blood.

When she saw the baby, she was so overwhelmed with joy that she passed away.

Her husband buried her beneath the juniper tree, his heart heavy with grief.

Though he cried for a long time, he eventually found some ease.

title: The Juniper-Tree
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Time went on, and he married again, having a daughter with his second wife.

The child from his first marriage, a little boy, echoed the colors of blood and snow.

The second wife adored her daughter but felt a pang of jealousy each time she looked at the boy.

She feared he might hinder her daughter's future.

Her mind became consumed with thoughts of how to ensure her daughter received all their wealth.

This darkened her heart toward the little boy.

She pushed him around, struck him here and there, and the poor child lived in constant fear.

One day, the woman headed upstairs to her room, and her little girl followed her.

"Mom, can I have an apple?" asked her daughter.

"Of course, my dear," replied the mother, handing her a lovely apple.

The apple came from a heavy chest secured with a sharp iron lock.

But the little girl wondered, "What about my brother? Can he have one, too?"

This irritated the mother, but she replied tersely, "Yes, when he gets home from school."

When she spotted him coming, it felt as if she had been possessed by a devil.

She snatched the apple and took it away from her daughter.

"You can't have it until your brother gets one first."

Then she tossed the apple into the chest and slammed it shut.

Her son walked in as she opened the door.

The devil whispered sweetly, "My boy, would you like an apple?"

Yet her gaze was sinister.

"Mom, you look frightening," said the little boy.

"Yes, please, can I have an apple?"

It felt like she had no choice but to invite him closer.

She opened the heavy lid of the chest and said, "Help yourself to an apple."

While the boy leaned inside the chest, temptation overwhelmed her.

With a swift crash, she slammed the lid down, and his head separated from his body, landing among the red apples.

Panic surged through her; she thought, "If only I can make it look like I had nothing to do with this."

title: The Juniper-Tree
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She dashed to her room, opened her drawer, pulled out a white handkerchief, and hurriedly tried to reattach his head.

She arranged it so that nothing appeared amiss and placed him on a chair by the door, apple in hand.

Just then, Marlinchen entered the kitchen where her mother was stirring a pot of hot water.

"Mom," Marlinchen said, "brother is sitting at the door, and he looks pale with an apple in his hand.

I asked him for the apple, but he didn’t say anything, and it scared me."

"Go back to him," the mother replied, "and if he doesn’t answer, slap him."

Marlinchen approached him again, "Please, give me the apple."

But he remained silent.

She slapped him, and his head fell off.

Marlinchen screamed in terror, raced back to her mother, "Mom! I knocked off my brother’s head!"

She cried and cried, unable to be consoled.

"Oh, Marlinchen," her mother exclaimed, "What have you done? You must stay quiet about this.

This can't be changed; we’ll turn him into black puddings."

The mother chopped the little boy into pieces, put him in the pan, and made black puddings.

Meanwhile, Marlinchen stood by, still weeping, and her tears fell into the pan, seasoning the mix.

Then the father came home, sat down for dinner, and asked, "Where's my son?"

The mother served a large dish of black puddings, and Marlinchen couldn’t stop crying.

The father repeated, "Where's my son?"

"Oh, he’s gone to visit his great-uncle on his mother’s side; he’ll be back soon."

"What is he doing there? He didn’t even say goodbye to me."

"He wanted to leave and asked if he could stay for six weeks; he’s being well taken care of."

"Well, that makes me uneasy. He should have said goodbye," he sighed.

But he began to eat, noting, "Marlinchen, why are you crying? Your brother will come back."

title: The Juniper-Tree
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Then he added, "Oh, this food is delicious; I want more!"

The more he ate, the more he craved, exclaiming, "Give me some more; you can have none."

It felt like it was all his.

He devoured the entire dish and tossed the bones under the table.

Meanwhile, Marlinchen went to her drawer and took her best silk handkerchief,

gathering the bones from under the table, tying them up.

She carried them outside, tears streaming down her face.

Then she lay down beneath the juniper tree on the soft grass.

After a moment, a sense of lightness filled her, and she stopped crying.

Suddenly, the juniper tree began to rustle, its branches swaying.

They parted and joined again, almost like someone was celebrating.

From the tree, a mist rose, glowing with light, and out of the fire, a beautiful bird took flight.

It soared high into the sky, singing magnificently as it flew away.

When the bird vanished, everything returned to normal, and the bones in her handkerchief were gone.

Marlinchen felt joyous, as if her brother was still alive.

She happily returned to the house and sat down for dinner.

Meanwhile, the bird landed on a goldsmith's roof, gathering all my bones,

tying them in a silken handkerchief, and laying them beneath the juniper tree.

"Kywitt, kywitt, what a beautiful bird am I!"

The goldsmith, busy making a golden chain, heard the enchanting song from his roof.

He stood up, but lost one slipper as he rushed outside.

Walking down the street in one shoe and one sock, with one hand holding the golden chain,

and the other with his pincers, the sun shone brightly on his way.

He approached the bird and exclaimed, "Bird, you sing beautifully! Sing that again for me."

"No," replied the bird, "I won’t sing for free. Give me that golden chain, and I’ll sing for you again."

"Here, take this golden chain," said the goldsmith, "now sing for me again."

The bird took the chain in its claw, perched before the goldsmith, and sang -

title: The Juniper-Tree
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Gathered together all my bones,

tied them in a silken handkerchief,

laid them beneath the juniper tree,

kywitt, kywitt, what a beautiful bird am I."

The bird then flew off to a shoemaker's and landed on his roof,

sang the same tune again.

The shoemaker, enchanted, dashed outside in his shirt sleeves.

"Bird, you sing so beautifully!" He called for his wife.

"Come see this bird; it can sing!"

He gathered his children and apprentices as they all crowded outside.

They admired the bird's stunning red and green feathers and golden neck.

"Bird, sing that song again!" the shoemaker requested.

"Nope, I won’t sing twice for free; you need to offer me something."

"Wife," the shoemaker said, "go to the attic and bring down the red shoes on the top shelf."

She returned with the shoes.

"Now, bird, here are the shoes; sing for me again!"

The bird took the shoes in its claw and perched on the roof, singing -

"Gathered together all my bones,

tied them in a silken handkerchief,

laid them beneath the juniper tree,

kywitt, kywitt, what a beautiful bird am I."

After finishing its song, it flew off, clutching the chain and shoes.

The bird flew far away to a mill, where it perched on a lime tree.

Inside, twenty millers worked hard, hewing stones.

The mill thumped away — klipp klapp, klipp klapp.

The bird settled on the tree and began to sing.

One miller paused, then two others stopped working to listen.

"Gathered together all my bones,

tied them in a silken handkerchief,

kywitt, kywitt, what a beautiful bird am I."

The last miller stopped working, enchanted.

He called out, "Bird, sing that beautifully again!"

"No," the bird said, "not for free. You need to give me the millstone."

"Sure," replied the miller, "if it were mine alone, you'd have it."

The other millers nodded, "If he sings again, he should get it."

title: The Juniper-Tree
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The bird descended, and the millers worked together, lifting the stone.

With the bird's neck through the hole, it carried the stone like a collar.

It flew back to the tree and sang -

"Gathered together all my bones,

tied them in a silken handkerchief,

laid them beneath the juniper tree,

kywitt, kywitt, what a beautiful bird am I."

After the song, it spread its wings, one claw holding the chain,

the other the shoes, and the millstone around its neck.

The bird flew back to its father's house.

Inside, the father, mother, and Marlinchen were having dinner.

"I feel so happy!" said the father.

"I don’t know why, but I feel anxious, as if a storm is brewing," the mother replied.

Marlinchen couldn't stop her tears.

Suddenly, the bird landed on the roof, and the father exclaimed, "I feel so light and happy!

It’s a beautiful sunny day; it feels like I’m about to see a dear friend."

The mother felt uneasy, shuddering, "My teeth are chattering, and I feel a fire inside my veins."

She ripped open her dress, while Marlinchen sat in a corner, crying into her plate.

Finally, the bird began to sing; the mother covered her ears and shut her eyes.

But the sound roared in her ears, like a violent storm.

Her eyes burned as the man said, "Oh, my mother, that bird sings beautifully!"

Just then, the bird let out a light-hearted tune and Marlinchen wiped her tears.

"I need to see that bird up close," the father declared, heading outside.

"Don’t go!" shouted the mother, "I feel as if our house is shaking and catching fire!"

But the father stepped outside and heard the bird sing -

"Gathered together all my bones,

tied them in a silken handkerchief,

laid them beneath the juniper tree,

kywitt, kywitt, what a beautiful bird am I!"

At that moment, the bird dropped the golden chain.

It landed perfectly around the father's neck.

He walked back in, jubilantly showing off the chain and commenting on how lovely the bird was.

But the woman fell to the floor, her cap flying off her head.

As she sang, "I wish I were a thousand feet beneath the earth, so I wouldn’t have to face this!"

The woman collapsed again, appearing lifeless.

Marlinchen, too, felt the urge to see if the bird would give her something.

Stepping outside, she heard -

"Gathered together all my bones,

tied them in a silken handkerchief,

laid them beneath the juniper tree,

kywitt, kywitt, what a beautiful bird am I!"

The bird tossed the shoes down to her.

She danced in excitement, slipping on the new red shoes.

"Oh, I was so sad before, but now I'm so happy!" she cheered.

"What a marvelous bird! It gave me red shoes!"

Her mother, rising in fury, felt fire coursing through her.

"I need to go out to see if my heart feels lighter!"

As she stepped outside, the bird dropped the millstone on her head, crushing her.

The father and Marlinchen rushed out to find smoke, flames, and chaos.

After it cleared, the little brother appeared, taking the hands of his father and Marlinchen.

They rejoiced together and returned inside for dinner, feeling grateful.

title: The King's Son Who Feared Nothing
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a king's son who found himself restless and eager for adventure.

Not satisfied with staying in his father's castle, he decided to set out into the wider world, confident that nothing could scare him.

"I want to see the wonders out there," he thought. "Time will fly by, and I’ll discover so much!"

So he said goodbye to his parents and embarked on his journey, wandering from morning till night.

No matter which path he took, he was happy to explore. Eventually, he stumbled upon a giant's house.

Exhausted, he plopped down beside the door to rest. As he looked around, he noticed the giant's enormous toys scattered in the yard.

There were giant balls and nine-pins that stood as tall as him.

Feeling playful, he decided to set up the nine-pins and roll the balls at them, cheering and laughing as the pins toppled down.

The giant, hearing the ruckus, poked his head out of the window and was surprised to see a man no taller than anyone else, playing with his pins.

"Hey there, little worm! Why are you messing with my balls? Who gave you the strength to do that?"

The prince looked up, spotted the giant, and replied, "Oh, you buffoon! You think you're the only strong one here? I can do anything I set my mind to!"

Impressed, the giant came down to watch him bowl and said, "You’re quite something. If you’re truly that impressive, go fetch me an apple from the Tree of Life."

The king's son asked, "What do you need it for?"

"It's not for me," the giant explained. "I have a fiancée who longs for it. I’ve traveled everywhere and can’t find the tree."

"I’ll find it soon enough," the prince said confidently. "Nothing can stop me from getting the apple!"

The giant warned, "You really think it will be that easy? The garden where the tree stands is surrounded by an iron fence, and wild beasts lie in front, keeping watch to ensure no one enters."

title: The King's Son Who Feared Nothing
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"They'll let me pass," the king's son declared confidently.

"Sure, but even if you make it to the garden and see the apple on the tree, you still can’t have it. A ring hangs in front, and anyone who wants the apple has to reach through it, but no one has succeeded yet."

"I'll be the one to succeed," the king's son said boldly. With that, he took his leave from the giant and set off.

He traveled over mountains, valleys, plains, and through forests until he finally arrived at the magical garden.

The beasts were all around it, but they had their heads down and were asleep. He crept up to them and stepped over their bodies, managing to climb the fence and slip safely into the garden.

In the center stood the Tree of Life, with bright red apples glimmering in the sunlight. He quickly climbed up to the top, and just as he was about to grab an apple, he noticed the ring hanging in front of it.

Without a second thought, he pushed his hand through the ring and effortlessly picked the apple.

The ring tightened around his arm, and suddenly he felt a surge of incredible strength coursing through him.

Once he climbed down with the apple, instead of climbing back over the fence, he went straight for the great gate and pulled it open with just a single push.

The lion that had been resting at the gate awoke and followed him, not with anger, but like a loyal servant.

When the prince returned to the giant, he proudly held out the apple he promised.

"Look, I got it without any trouble!"

Delighted, the giant hurried to his bride and presented her with the apple she had wished for. She was a wise and beautiful maiden, and when she noticed the absence of the ring on his arm, she said, "I won’t believe you until I see the ring!"

The giant remarked, "I can just go home and get it," thinking it would be easy to force it from the weak young man.

He demanded the ring, but the prince flatly refused.

title: The King's Son Who Feared Nothing
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Where the apple is, the ring must be too," the giant insisted. "If you won't give it freely, you must..."

They wrestled for a long time, but the giant couldn't overpower the king's son, who was bolstered by the ring's magical power.

Then the giant came up with a clever trick and said, "Let’s cool off in the river after all that fighting. We’ll feel fresher for round two."

The prince, naive and trusting, followed him to the water, removed his clothes, and absent-mindedly took off the ring before jumping in.

The giant quickly snatched the ring and ran off, but the observant lion chased him down, ripping the ring from the giant's grasp and returning it to its master.

As the prince was dressing, the giant lurked behind an oak tree, seizing the chance to attack him and blinded him with a quick strike.

Now the unfortunate king's son was left helpless and blind, unsure of how to save himself.

The giant returned, took him by the hand as if guiding him, and led him to the edge of a high cliff.

There, he thought, "Just a little shove and he’ll fall and be rid of the ring for good!"

But the faithful lion hadn’t abandoned its master. It held onto the prince's clothes and prevented him from falling.

When the giant returned to get rid of the prince, he saw that his scheme had failed.

"Is there truly no way to destroy such a weak human?" he grumbled angrily to himself.

Once again, he seized the king's son and led him back to the cliff by a different path, but still, the lion helped its master evade danger.

Close to the edge, the giant let go of the prince's hand, planning to leave him there, but the lion pushed him, sending the giant tumbling to his demise.

Once again, the loyal lion pulled its master away from danger, leading him to a tree beside a clear stream.

The king’s son sat down, while the lion lay down, playfully splashing water on his face with its paws.

title: The King's Son Who Feared Nothing
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
As a few drops hit his eyes, he slowly regained his vision and noticed a little bird fluttering nearby, hitting the trunk of a tree.

The bird splashed in the water and soared upwards between the trees as if it had regained its sight.

The king's son saw this as a sign from God and bent down to the water, washing his face.

When he stood up, his vision was clearer and brighter than ever.

The king's son thanked God for his kindness and continued his travels with his loyal lion.

Eventually, he found himself before an enchanted castle. At the entrance stood a maiden of exquisite beauty, yet she was completely black.

She spoke to him, saying, "Oh, if only you could free me from this evil spell!"

"What must I do?" asked the king's son.

The maiden replied, "You must spend three nights in the grand hall of this enchanted castle, but you cannot let fear enter your heart.

If you endure their worst torment in silence, I will be free. They can't take your life."

The prince said bravely, "I have no fear. With God's help, I will try!"

He joyfully walked into the castle, and as night fell, he settled into the large hall.

Everything was calm until midnight, when a loud commotion erupted, and tiny devils appeared from every nook.

They acted as if they didn't notice him, sat in the center of the room, lit a fire, and began to gamble.

One of them exclaimed, "This isn’t right; someone is here who doesn’t belong. It’s his fault I’m losing!"

"Just wait, you little rascal hiding by the stove! I’m coming for you!" said another.

The chaos grew louder, making it impossible to hear without feeling terrified, yet the king's son sat calmly, unafraid.

Eventually, the devils jumped on him, and there were so many that he couldn’t fight them off.

They dragged him around, pinching and poking him, beating him and torturing him, but he never made a sound.

title: The King's Son Who Feared Nothing
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
By morning, they finally left him alone, utterly exhausted and barely able to move. But as dawn broke, the black maiden appeared.

She carried a small bottle of magical water, and as she washed him, he instantly felt his pain vanish and new energy swell within him.

"You survived your first night," she said. "But two more remain."

Then she disappeared, and before he knew it, night fell again.

The following night, the devils returned to gamble once more.

They attacked the king’s son, beating him much more violently than before, leaving him covered in wounds.

Yet, because he endured in silence, they eventually had to back off, and when daylight arrived, the maiden came to heal him again.

As she left, he noticed with joy that her skin had turned white, the color returning to her hands.

Now he only had one night left, and it was bound to be the toughest. The devils came back, taunting, "Are you still alive?"

"You’ll be tormented until you can’t breathe anymore!"

They jabbed him, cursed him, tossed him around as if they intended to rip him to shreds, but he bore it all without a single yell.

Finally, the devils vanished, leaving him faint and unable to move, his eyes glued shut.

But here came the maiden, once again, sprinkling him with the magical water that revitalized him.

Suddenly, he felt all pain leave him, and he sprang up new and refreshed, as though waking from a deep sleep.

When he opened his eyes, he saw the maiden standing beside him, now pure white and radiant.

"Get up," she said. "Swing your sword three times over the staircase, and everything will be set free."

When he did this, the entire castle returned to its former glory, and the maiden revealed that she was a wealthy princess.

Then the servants announced that dinner was served in the grand hall, and so they sat to eat and drink together. That evening, they celebrated their wedding.

title: The Knapsack, The Hat, and The Horn
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there were three brothers who had fallen deeper into poverty.

Their situation became so dire that they were left enduring hunger, with nothing to eat or drink.

They said to one another, "This can’t continue; we should go out into the world and seek our fortune."

So, they set off, having walked for many long days and crossed countless blades of grass, but they still hadn’t found any luck.

One day, they entered a vast forest, and in the middle of it was a hill. As they got closer, they discovered that the hill was made entirely of silver.

The eldest brother said, "At last, I’ve found the good fortune I’ve been looking for! I want nothing more." He filled his pockets with silver and turned back home.

The other two brothers, however, thought, "We want something better than just silver," and they left it behind as they continued on.

After walking for another two days without stopping, they came across a hill that was completely made of gold. The second brother paused, deep in thought.

"What should I do?" he wondered. "Should I take enough gold to secure my future, or should I keep going?" At last, he decided.

He stuffed as much gold into his pockets as he could, said goodbye to his brother, and turned back home.

But the third brother declared, "Silver and gold don’t excite me; I won’t give up on finding something even better." He pressed on.

After three days of walking, he found himself in an even larger forest, one that seemed endless.

Since he could find nothing to eat or drink, he was nearly exhausted. Climbing a tall tree, he hoped to see the forest's edge.

But as far as he could see, all he saw were treetops. When he finally climbed down, hunger gnawed at him. He thought, "If only I could eat my fill one more time."

To his astonishment, as he descended, he spotted a table under the tree, beautifully laid out with food that smelled incredible.

title: The Knapsack, The Hat, and The Horn
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He figured, "This time, my wish has truly been granted just in time!" Without thinking about who prepared it, he sat down and enjoyed the meal until he was full.

After finishing, he thought it would be a shame for the lovely tablecloth to get ruined in the forest, so he carefully folded it and tucked it in his pocket.

Later that evening, when hunger returned, he decided to try out his tablecloth. He spread it out and said, "I wish for it to be filled with delicious food again."

As soon as he spoke, the table was filled with delightful dishes to his delight. "Now I see where my meals come from," he said, appreciating his new treasure.

This cloth made him value it more than mountains of silver or gold. But he still preferred wandering the world to sitting quietly at home.

One night, while walking through a lonely wood, he came across a dusty charcoal-burner, who was preparing a meal of potatoes over a fire.

"Good evening, friend," the traveler said. "How's life in your solitude?"

"Every day is the same, and every night is potatoes," replied the charcoal-burner. "Care to join me for dinner?"

"Thanks, but I don’t want to take your supper. I didn’t expect to drop in on you, but I can share a meal if you’ll accept my invitation."

"Who’s going to cook for you?" asked the charcoal-burner. "You have nothing, and the nearest person is two hours away."

"But there will be a meal," replied the traveler, "and it’ll be better than anything you’ve ever tasted." He took out his cloth, spread it out, and said, "Little cloth, cover yourself," and in an instant, there appeared delicious hot food.

The charcoal-burner looked on wide-eyed, but he didn’t need any convincing. He dug in and ate until he was stuffed.

After they finished the meal, the charcoal-burner smiled and said, "Your tablecloth is impressive! It’d be perfect for me out here where I can't get a good meal.

title: The Knapsack, The Hat, and The Horn
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
I have a soldier's knapsack over there, old and worn, but it has some wonderful powers. I’ll give it to you if you want it."

"What powers does it have?" asked the traveler.

"Every time you tap it with your hand, a corporal appears with six fully armed soldiers who will do whatever you command."

"If it’s like that, it’s a trade I’ll gladly take," said the traveler. He handed the charcoal-burner the cloth, took the knapsack, and bid him farewell.

After walking a bit, he decided to test out the knapsack’s powers and tapped it. Suddenly, seven soldiers appeared, and the corporal said, "What can I do for you, my lord?"

"March quickly to the charcoal-burner and demand my wishing-cloth back," he commanded.

The soldiers executed the order swiftly, retrieving the cloth without many questions.

The young man dismissed them and continued on, hopeful that good fortune would smile upon him again.

As the sun set, he encountered another charcoal-burner who was also preparing dinner. "If you want to join me for potatoes with salt, just salt, come and sit."

"No, this time, you’ll be my guest," replied the traveler. He spread out his cloth, which instantly filled with beautiful dishes.

They enjoyed a hearty meal together. After they finished, the charcoal-burner pointed to an old, tattered hat on a shelf.

"This hat has strange properties. If someone puts it on and turns it, it fires cannons that blast everything away. I no longer need it; I’d gladly trade it for your tablecloth."

"That works for me," the traveler said, taking the hat and giving up the tablecloth.

But just as he walked away, he tapped his knapsack, and his soldiers were sent to recover the cloth.

He thought to himself, "Everything seems to be going my way; my luck isn’t over yet."

After a day of traveling, he met a third charcoal-burner who also invited him for potatoes. He treated him to a feast with his wishes again.

title: The Knapsack, The Hat, and The Horn
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The charcoal-burner enjoyed it so much that he offered a horn which had entirely different powers.

When someone blows it, all the walls and fortifications fall down, and towns become ruins.

The traveler exchanged his cloth for the horn and sent his soldiers to retrieve it again, left with the knapsack, hat, and horn.

"Now I’m really set! It's time to head home and see how my brothers are doing!"

When he arrived back home, he saw his brothers had constructed a beautiful house with their silver and gold and were living quite well.

He approached them, but because he wore a ragged coat, a shabby hat, and carried an old knapsack, they didn’t recognize him.

They laughed and said, "You claim to be our brother who dismissed silver and gold for something better? A true brother wouldn’t show up dressed like a beggar!"

They shunned him and turned him away.

Angered, he tapped his knapsack, and within moments, one hundred and fifty soldiers stood before him, armed and ready.

He commanded them to surround his brothers' house, while two of the soldiers were to take sticks and beat them until they recognized him.

A commotion broke out as people rushed to help, but they stood no chance against his soldiers.

Word of the chaos reached the king, who was furious. He ordered a captain to clear out the troublemaker.

But the traveler quickly gathered an even larger army that sent the captain and his men retreating with injuries.

The king decided this vagabond couldn't be ignored any longer. The next day, he sent an even bigger force to handle the situation.

Yet they achieved nothing. The youth increased his army further, and to accelerate matters, he turned his hat twice.

Cannon fire erupted, and the king's troops were soundly defeated.

At that moment, he declared, "I won't settle until the king offers me his daughter in marriage, and I take control of the kingdom in his name!"

He made sure the king knew his demands, and the king sighed, "I can’t argue with necessity. If peace means giving you my daughter, so be it."

title: The Knapsack, The Hat, and The Horn
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The wedding took place, though the king's daughter felt resentful that she had to marry a man with a shabby hat and old knapsack. She plotted to rid herself of him.

Day and night, she schemed to find a way to escape her situation. Then she decided, "Maybe his powers are in the knapsack."

Feigning affection, she cozied up to him and, when his guard was down, said, "If only you’d take off that awful knapsack. It makes you look so unattractive; it embarrasses me."

"My dear, this knapsack is my greatest treasure. As long as I have it, I fear nothing!" he replied.

He went on to share the secret powers of the knapsack.

Then, as if to kiss him, she cleverly slipped the knapsack off his shoulders and ran away.

Once she was alone, she tapped the knapsack, commanding the soldiers to seize their previous master and remove him from the palace.

They obeyed, and the fake wife sent even more men after him to drive him out of the land.

He would surely have met his downfall if not for the little hat. As soon as he was free, he turned it twice.

Cannon fire thundered once more, demolishing everything. The king's daughter found herself pleading for mercy.

As she begged sincerely and promised to behave better, he agreed to let her off the hook.
