She acted like she cared for him and eventually convinced him that even if someone took the knapsack, they couldn’t defeat him as long as he wore that old hat.

Knowing his secret, she waited until he was fast asleep, then took the hat from him and tossed it into the street.

But he still had the horn, and enveloped in fury, he blew it.

Instantly, walls, buildings, and villages collapsed around him, burying the king and his daughter.

If he hadn’t stopped blowing, everything would have crumbled into nothing.

With no one left to oppose him, he became the king of the entire land.

title: The Lambkin and the Little Fish
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there were a little brother and sister who loved each other deeply.

Their mother had passed away, leaving them with a stepmother who was anything but kind.

She secretly did everything she could to make their lives miserable.

One sunny day, the two were playing with other kids in a meadow in front of their house.

In that meadow, there was a pond beside the house.

The children laughed and chased each other around, playing games of tag.

One of their favorite songs went like this: 'And I to you, my bird will give.'

'The little bird, it shall seek some straw,'

'The straw I'll give to the cow to eat.'

'The pretty cow shall give me milk,'

'The milk I’ll take to the baker.'

'The baker he'll bake a cake,'

'The cake I'll give to the cat.'

'The cat shall catch some mice for that,'

'The mice I'll hang up in the smoke.'

The kids gathered in a circle as they sang, and whoever got caught by the word 'snow' had to run away while the others chased after them.

They were having so much fun when suddenly the stepmother watched them from the window, filled with anger.

Knowing witchcraft, she cast a spell on them, turning the little brother into a fish and the little sister into a lamb.

The fish swam sadly around the pond, while the lamb wandered the meadow, feeling lost and unable to eat a single blade of grass.

Time passed, and soon some strangers arrived at the castle.

The wicked stepmother thought this was a perfect chance.

She called the cook and ordered him to fetch the lamb from the meadow and kill it for their guests.

The cook went to get the lamb and brought it to the kitchen, tying its feet, and the lamb endured it without complaint.

Just as the cook sharpened his knife on the doorstep to carry out the deed, he noticed a little fish swimming restlessly in front of him.

The fish was the brother, who swam after the cook when he took the lamb away.

The lamb noticed the fish and cried out, 'Oh brother, in the pond so deep!'

When the cook heard the lamb speak such sorrowful words to the fish below, he became frightened.

'This can't be an ordinary lamb; it must be under a wicked spell, ' he thought.

He replied, 'Don't worry, I won't harm you,' and chose another sheep instead to prepare for the visitors.

He took the lambkin to a kind peasant woman and recounted everything he had seen and heard.

The peasant woman recognized the lamb right away; she had nursed the little sister as a baby.

Understanding what had happened, she led the lamb to a wise woman.

The wise woman then cast a blessing over both the lambkin and the little fish.

Thanks to her magic, they regained their human forms.

After that, she took them to a tiny hut in a vast forest, where they lived happily together.

title: The Lazy Spinner
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
In a little village, there lived a man and his wife.

The wife was so lazy that she never lifted a finger to work.

No matter what her husband asked her to spin, she never finished it,

and even what she did manage to spin, she never bothered to wind up, leaving it all

in a messy heap. Whenever her husband scolded her, she always had a retort ready. "Well, how am I supposed

to wind it when I don’t have a reel? You should just go into the forest and get one for me!"

The man replied, "If that’s all it takes, I’ll head into the forest and gather some wood to make reels."

But the woman worried that if he got the wood, he would indeed make her a reel,

and then she would have to unwind her yarn and start spinning again.

She thought for a moment, and a clever idea struck her.

She secretly followed her husband into the forest, and when he climbed a tree to choose and cut the wood,

she hid in the thicket below, where he couldn’t see her, and shouted,

"He who cuts wood for reels shall die, and he who winds shall perish!"

The man paused, laid down his axe, and started to contemplate what that could mean.

After a moment, he finally said, "What could that have been? My ears must be playing tricks on me; I won't worry for nothing."

So he picked up his axe again and began to chop.

But once more, a voice called out, "He who cuts wood for reels shall die, and he who winds shall perish!"

He stopped, feeling frightened and uneasy, and thought about what he had heard.

But after a few moments, he reassured himself and reached for his axe a third time.

Just when he started to cut again, he heard the warning a third time: "He who cuts wood for reels shall die, and he who winds shall perish!"

That was enough for him; every bit of motivation left him,

so he hurried down the tree and made his way back home.

title: The Lazy Spinner
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The woman hurried ahead by a back path.

When he entered the house, she put on an innocent face, as if nothing had happened.

"So, did you bring back some nice wood for reels?" she asked.

"No," he replied. "I've realized that winding won’t work," and he told her what had happened in the forest.

From that day on, he left her alone about it.

However, after a while, the man started complaining again about the mess in the house.

"Wife," he said, "it’s really a shame that all that spun yarn is just lying there in a tangle."

"I have an idea," she said. "Since we still don’t have a reel, why don’t you go up into the loft?

I’ll stand below and throw the yarn up to you, and you can toss it back down to me. That way, we can get a skein made after all."

"Sure, that sounds good," he agreed.

So they did just that, and once it was done, he told her, "The yarn is in skeins now; it needs to be boiled."

The woman felt anxious again and replied, "Yes, let’s boil it tomorrow morning."

But she had another trick up her sleeve.

In the morning, she got up, lit a fire, and put the kettle on, but instead of yarn,

she threw in a lump of tow to boil.

After that, she went to her husband, who was still in bed, and said,

"I need to step out for a moment. You need to get up and watch over the yarn on the fire.

You must be quick about it, because if the rooster crows and you're not there,

the yarn will turn into tow!"

The man agreed and hurried out of bed.

He rushed into the kitchen, but when he peeked into the kettle,

his heart sank to see nothing but a lump of tow.

He froze, thinking he had neglected it and it was his fault, and from that moment on,

he never brought up yarn or spinning again.

But you must admit, this is a rather ridiculous situation.

title: The Little Folks' Presents
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
A tailor and a goldsmith were journeying together when, one evening,

as the sun dipped behind the mountains, they began to hear distant music,

growing clearer and clearer. It sounded unusual, yet incredibly delightful,

making them forget their tiredness.

They hurried along, and by the time they reached a hill, the moon was already shining.

On the hill, they spotted a crowd of little men and women,

holding hands and twirling around joyfully in a dance.

They sang beautifully, and that was the music the travelers had heard.

In the center sat an old man, slightly taller than the others,

wearing a colorful coat, his white beard cascading down to his chest.

The two men stood there, astonished, watching the dance.

The old man gestured for them to join, and the little ones cheerfully opened their circle.

The goldsmith, who had a hump and, like many with hunches, was quite bold, stepped in.

The tailor hesitated at first, feeling a bit scared, but when he saw everyone having so much fun,

he gathered his courage and followed.

The circle quickly closed again, and the little folks danced wildly while singing.

However, the old man pulled out a large knife from his belt, sharpened it,

and then looked at the newcomers.

They were filled with fear, but there wasn't much time to think as the old man seized the goldsmith,

swiftly shaving off all the hair from his head, and soon after, did the same to the tailor.

But their fear faded when, after finishing his work,

the old man clapped them both on the shoulder in an encouraging way,

as if to say they had behaved well by letting it all happen without a fight.

He pointed to a pile of coals nearby,

gesturing for them to fill their pockets with them.

Both complied, even though they had no idea what they would do with the coals,

and then they continued on their journey, looking for a place to stay for the night.

As they entered the valley, the clock from the nearby monastery struck twelve,

and just like that, the music stopped.

In an instant, everything vanished, leaving the hill quiet under the moonlight.

title: The Little Folks' Presents
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The travelers found an inn, bundled themselves in their coats on their straw beds,

but in their exhaustion, forgot to take the coals out of their pockets.

A heavy weight on their limbs woke them up earlier than usual.

They checked their pockets, and couldn’t believe their eyes when they discovered they were filled with pure gold.

To their delight, their hair and beards had grown back thick as ever.

Now they were rich men, but the goldsmith, who had filled his pockets more greedily,

was twice as rich as the tailor.

Being greedy, the goldsmith suggested they wait another day and return in the evening.

He wanted to get even more treasures from the old man on the hill.

The tailor declined and said, "I've got enough and I'm happy. Now I can be a master and marry my beloved—"

that’s how he referred to his sweetheart—

"and that makes me a happy man."

Yet, he stayed another day to keep his friend happy.

That evening, the goldsmith loaded up bags to carry more treasures and headed back to the hill.

Like the night before, he found the little folk singing and dancing,

and the old man shaved him clean again, signing for him to take some coal.

He stuffed his bags full, pleased with himself,

and wrapped himself in his coat.

"Even if the gold is heavy, I’ll gladly carry it," he said to himself,

and finally drifted off to sleep with dreams of waking up incredibly wealthy.

When he opened his eyes, he quickly checked his pockets,

but to his shock, they were still filled with nothing but black coals, no matter how often he searched them.

"The gold I had yesterday should still be with me," he thought, rushing to find it,

but to his dismay, he saw it had turned back into coal as well.

He smacked his forehead with his dirty black hand, then realized his whole head was bald,

including where his beard used to be.

But that wasn’t all; he now noticed that in addition to the hump on his back,

another large hump had formed on his chest.

Suddenly, he understood he was being punished for his greed and burst into tears.

The kind tailor, roused by the commotion, comforted the distressed goldsmith.

"You’ve been my traveling companion; you’re welcome to stay with me and share my wealth."

He kept his promise, but the poor goldsmith had to carry the two humps for the rest of his life, covering his bald head with a cap.

title: The Lord's Animals and the Devil's
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
God created all the animals and chose the wolf to be His dog, but He completely forgot about the goat.

Then the devil decided to create as well, making goats with long, beautiful tails.

Whenever they went out to graze, they often got their tails caught in the hedges.

This meant the devil had to come and untangle them, which was quite a hassle.

Eventually, this made him furious, and he bit off every goat's tail, which is why they all have stumps today.

After that, he let them graze freely, but it wasn't long before God noticed that they were chewing on fruitful trees and damaging noble vines, ruining other fragile plants.

This troubled God greatly, and out of His kindness, He called upon His wolves, who quickly tore apart the goats that strayed there.

When the devil saw this, he went to God and complained, "Your creatures have destroyed mine."

"Why did you create things that cause harm?" God asked.

The devil replied, "I had no choice; my thoughts are filled with evil.

Whatever I create will reflect that nature, and you owe me a substantial compensation."

"I’ll pay you once the oak leaves fall; after that, your money will be ready to count," God said.

When the leaves finally fell, the devil came back to collect his payment.

But God said, "In the church of Constantinople, there’s a tall oak that still has all its leaves.

The furious devil stormed off, cursing as he searched for the oak, wandering in the wilderness for six months.

By the time he found it, all the other oaks had grown back their green leaves.

He lost his chance at compensation, and in his rage, he gouged out the eyes of all the remaining goats, replacing them with his own.

That’s why all goats have strange, devil-like eyes and why their tails are bitten off.

title: The Louse and the Flea
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
A louse and a flea shared a house and were making beer.

They were using an eggshell for their brewing.

Suddenly, the little louse fell in and burned herself.

At that moment, the little flea began to scream loudly.

Then the little door asked, 'Hey little flea, why are you screaming?'

'Because the louse has burned herself!'

Then the little door started to creak. A little broom in the corner asked,

'Why are you creaking, little door? Have I not reason to creak?

'The little louse has burned herself,' was the reply.

So the little broom began to sweep furiously. A little cart rolled by and asked,

'Why are you sweeping, little broom? Do I not have reason to sweep?

'The little louse has burned herself,' said the broom.

Then the little cart declared, 'In that case, I will run!' and took off at full speed.

As it sped by, the ash-heap asked, 'Why are you running so fast, little cart? Do I not have reason to run?

'The little louse has burned herself,' the cart replied.

The ash-heap then said, 'In that case, I will burn fiercely!' and erupted in flames.

Nearby, a little tree said to the ash-heap, 'Why are you burning? Do I not have reason to burn?'

'The little louse has burned herself,' the ash-heap replied.

The little tree decided, 'Then I will shake myself!' and began to shake so hard that all her leaves fell off.

A girl passing by with her water pitcher noticed this and asked, 'Little tree, why are you shaking? Don't I have reason to shake?'

'The little louse has burned herself,' she replied. 'The little ash-heap is burning!'

Hearing this, the girl said, 'Then I will break my little water pitcher!'

And she smashed her pitcher. A little spring of water nearby exclaimed,

'Girl, why are you breaking your water pitcher? Do I not have reason to break it?'

'The little louse has burned herself, the little ash-heap is burning, and the little tree is shaking!'

'Oh, in that case, I will start to flow!' said the spring, and it began to flow violently.

In the flood, everything drowned—the girl, the little tree, the little ash-heap, the little cart, the broom, the door, the flea, and the louse.

title: The Maid of Brakel
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once, a girl from Brakel visited St. Anne's chapel at the base of the Hinnenberg.

She was hoping to find a husband,

and since she thought the chapel was empty, she began to sing.

But the clerk was hiding behind the altar and overheard her.

With a gruff voice, he shouted, "You can't have him!"

The girl thought that it was the child Mary, who stood next to her mother Anne, calling out to her, and she became upset.

"Fiddle de dee, you full of yourself! Be quiet and let me sing!" she shouted back.

title: The Master-Thief
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One day, an old man and his wife were sitting in front of their rundown house, taking a break from their work.

Suddenly, a magnificent carriage pulled up, drawn by four sleek black horses.

A man dressed in fine clothing stepped out.

The peasant stood up, approached the nobleman, and asked how he could help.

The stranger extended his hand and said, "I just want to enjoy a country dish. Cook me some potatoes like you always do, and I’ll gladly sit at your table and eat them."

The peasant smiled and replied, "You must be a count or maybe even a duke. Noblemen often have such fancy requests, but you shall have what you wish."

The wife went into the kitchen to wash and prepare the potatoes, shaping them into dumplings like the locals did.

While she was working, the peasant said to the stranger, "Come into my garden with me; I have a few things to finish up there."

He had dug some holes in the garden and was getting ready to plant some trees.

The stranger asked, "Don’t you have any children who could help?"

The peasant replied, "I had a son, but he left home a long time ago. He was clever but wasted his talents on mischief. Eventually, he ran away, and I haven’t heard from him since."

The old man took a young tree, placed it in a hole, secured it with a post, and packed the soil around it.

Then he tied the tree to the post with straw rope, just above, below, and in the middle.

The stranger remarked, "Why not tie that crooked old tree over there to a post too, so it can grow straight like these?"

The old man smiled and replied, "Sir, you speak from a lack of experience. That tree is old and misshapen; no one can make it straight now. Trees need training when they are young."

"That’s just like your son," said the stranger. "If you had trained him well when he was young, he wouldn’t have run away. Now he may also be hardened and bent in his ways."

title: The Master-Thief
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"It's been a while since he left," said the old man, "he must have changed. Would you recognize him if he came back?"

"Hardly by his face," the peasant replied, "but he has a birthmark on his shoulder that looks like a bean."

At this, the stranger took off his coat, bared his shoulder, and showed the old man the birthmark.

"Oh my God!" exclaimed the old man. "You really are my son!" Love for his child surged in his heart.

"But how can this be? You’re a great lord now, living in luxury. How did that happen?"

"Ah, father," replied the son, "the young tree might have been tied to a post but ultimately grew up crooked. Now it's too late for it to be straightened. As for what I have become, I’m a thief, but don’t worry, I’m a master-thief."

"For me, there are no locks or bolts; whatever I want is mine. I don’t steal like an ordinary thief; I just take from the excess of the rich. The poor are always safe. I’d rather give to them than take. If I can get something easily through skill or cleverness, I don’t touch it."

"Oh, my son," said the father, "this still troubles me. A thief is still a thief, and I fear it will end badly for you."

The old man took him to his mother, and when she realized it was her son, she cried tears of joy. But when he confessed he had become a master-thief, her tears flowed freely.

"Even if he is a thief, he is still my son, and I’ve seen him again," she finally said.

They sat down to eat, and he shared the meager meal he hadn’t had in a long time.

"If our lord, the count, finds out who you are and what you do, he won't cradle you in his arms like he did when you were born; he’ll hang you!" said the father.

title: The Master-Thief
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Don’t worry, father; he won’t harm me. I know my trade well. I’ll go to him myself today."

As evening came, the master-thief climbed into his carriage and made his way to the castle.

The count greeted him politely, assuming he was a distinguished gentleman.

But when the thief revealed his identity, the count turned pale and was silent for several moments.

"You are my godson, so mercy will replace justice, and I will treat you leniently. However, since you claim to be a master-thief, I will put your skills to the test. If you fail, you’ll have to marry the rope-maker’s daughter, and the cawing of crows will be your wedding music!"

"My lord, do think of three difficult tasks for me, and if I fail, do with me as you wish," replied the master-thief.

The count thought for a while and then said, "First, you shall steal my riding horse from the stable. Then, you will take the sheet from under my wife and me while we sleep, without us noticing. Lastly, you shall steal the parson and clerk from the church.

The master-thief went to the nearest town, where he bought the clothes of an old peasant woman and put them on.

Then he stained his face brown and painted on some wrinkles so that no one would recognize him.

Next, he filled a small cask with old Hungarian wine mixed with a strong sleeping potion.

He carried the cask in a basket on his back and walked slowly to the count's castle, putting on a shaky act as if he was an elderly woman.

It was already dark when he arrived. He sat down on a stone in the courtyard and began to cough, just like an old lady with asthma, rubbing his hands as if he were cold.

In front of the door, some soldiers were gathered around a fire. One of them noticed the woman and called out, "Come closer, old lady, and warm yourself by the fire. You’ve no bed for the night, and you should find one where you can."

title: The Master-Thief
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The old woman hobbled over, begged them to help her with her basket, and sat beside them.

"What’s in that little cask, old hag?" asked one soldier.

"A nice bit of wine," she replied. "I’ll pour you a glass for some coins and kind words."

"Let’s have it here, then!" said the soldier. After having a sip, he said, "When the wine’s good, I’d like another glass," and poured another drink, with the others quickly following suit.

"Hey guys!" one soldier shouted to those in the stable, "This old girl has wine as aged as she is! Come grab a drink! It’ll warm you up better than our fire!"

The old woman then carried her cask into the stable.

One soldier was sitting on the mounted horse, another was holding its reins, and a third was grabbing its tail.

She poured out enough wine until there was none left in the cask.

It didn’t take long before the soldier who held the reins dropped them, fell, and started to snore. The one holding the tail also let go and lay down, snoring even louder.

The soldier on the horse remained sitting, but he bowed his head down almost to the neck of the horse and dozed off, snoring like a bellows.

The soldiers outside had been asleep for a long while, sprawled on the ground as still as if they were dead.

When the master-thief saw he had succeeded, he gave the first soldier a rope instead of the reins, and the one who held the tail got a wisp of straw.

But what could he do about the one still on the horse? He didn’t want to throw him off and risk waking him.

He had a clever idea. He unfastened the saddle girth, tied some ropes that hung from a ring on the wall to the saddle, and hoisted the sleeping rider into the air.

Then he wound the rope around the posts and secured it.

He quickly unchained the horse, but if he rode over the rocky courtyard, it would make a noise that could wake the castle.

title: The Master-Thief
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
So, he wrapped the horse's hooves in old rags and led him out carefully, then jumped on.

When dawn broke, the master rode to the castle on the stolen horse.

The count had just woken up and was looking out the window.

"Good morning, Lord Count!" he called out. "Here’s the horse that I successfully snatched from your stable!"

"Look how beautifully your soldiers are sleeping outside, and if you go to the stable, you’ll see just how cozy your guards have made themselves."

The count couldn’t help but laugh.

"Well, you succeeded this time, but things won’t go as smoothly next time. If I catch you as a thief once more, I’ll treat you as one!"

That night, the countess went to bed, clutching her wedding ring tightly. The count remarked, "All doors are locked and bolted. I’ll stay up to catch the thief. But if he sneaks in through the window, I’ll shoot him."

Meanwhile, the master-thief went out into the dark to the gallows, cut down a poor soul hanging there, and carried him on his back to the castle.

He set a ladder up to the bedroom, placed the dead body on his shoulders, and started to climb.

When he got high enough that the head of the dead man was visible at the window, the count, who was watching from his bed, fired his pistol.

The master-thief quickly let the body fall, descended the ladder, and hid in a corner.

The moonlight illuminated the scene, allowing him to see the count climbing out of the window with the dead body and carrying it into the garden to dig a hole.

Now, thought the thief, the moment has come. He stealthily moved out of his hiding spot and climbed into the countess’s bedroom.

"Dear wife!" he began in the count's voice, "the thief is gone! But he was my godson, more of a scoundrel than a villain. I won’t let him suffer public shame, and I feel for his parents. I’ll bury him myself before dawn in the garden so that no one finds out. Give me the sheet to wrap him up nicely, instead of letting him be buried like a dog."

title: The Master-Thief
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The countess handed him the sheet.

"One more thing," the thief continued, "in a generous mood, let me have the ring too. The unfortunate man risked his life for it, and he deserves to take it with him to his grave."

Though reluctantly, she removed the ring from her finger and gave it to him.

The thief disappeared with both items and reached home well before the count finished his task in the garden.

The count looked very upset the next morning when the master-thief returned with the sheet and the ring.

"Are you a wizard?" he asked, "Did you rise from the grave I laid you in and come back to life?"

"I wasn’t buried, I assure you," replied the thief, "but that poor sinner hanging at the gallows was, and I will tell you exactly how it happened."

The count had to admit that the master-thief was clever and crafty.

"But your journey isn't over yet," the count added. "You still have to complete the third task. If you fail, all this will mean nothing."

The master smiled and didn’t reply.

When night fell, he set out with a big sack on his back, a bundle under his arms, and a lantern in hand towards the village church.

In the sack, he had crabs, and in the bundle, he carried some short wax candles.

He settled down in the churchyard, took out a crab, and stuck a wax candle on its back.

Lighting the little candle, he placed the crab on the ground and let it scuttle around.

He took out another crab, did the same, and continued until the last one was out.

Then he put on a long black cloak that resembled a monk's robe and glued on a gray beard.

Now, completely disguised, he took the empty sack and walked into the church, climbing up to the pulpit.

As the clock struck midnight, he cried out in a loud, piercing voice, "Listen, sinful men! The end of all things is here! The last day is upon us! Come! Come!"

title: The Master-Thief
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Anyone who wishes to go to heaven with me must crawl into the sack. I am Peter, who opens and closes the gates of heaven. See how the dead out there in the churchyard are gathering their bones. Come! Crawl into the sack; the world will soon be destroyed!"

His cry echoed throughout the village.

The parson and clerk, who lived closest to the church, heard it first. Seeing the lights moving in the churchyard, they figured something unusual was happening and went inside.

They listened for a while, then the clerk nudged the parson and said, "We should use this chance to find an easy way to heaven before the last day arrives."

"Honestly, I was just thinking that!" the parson admitted. "If you're ready, let's go!"

"Sure," said the clerk, "but you go first, Pastor; I’ll follow."

So the parson climbed up first, followed by the clerk.

The master quickly tied up the sack and hoisted it down from the pulpit, announcing, "We’re crossing the mountains!" whenever the two fools bumped against the steps.

He dragged them through the village, shouting, "Now we’re in the fog of wet clouds!"

When he finally dragged them up the castle steps, he proclaimed, "Now we're at heaven's gates, almost there!"

Upon reaching the top, he stuffed the sack into the pigeon-house, saying, "Listen to the angels flapping their wings!"

Next morning, he returned to the count and triumphantly declared that he completed the third task by carrying the parson and clerk out of the church.

"Where are they?" asked the count.

"They’re upstairs in a sack in the pigeon-house, thinking they are in heaven!"

The count went up to check for himself and found the master’s words were true.

After freeing the parson and clerk from their captivity, he said, "You’re a master thief and have won your bet. For once, you’ve escaped with your life, but make sure you leave my land. If you ever come back, expect to hang."

The master-thief bid farewell to his parents and ventured out into the great wide world, never to be heard from again.

title: The Moon
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a land where the nights were perpetually dark.

The sky stretched above like a thick black blanket because the moon,

never appeared, and no stars twinkled through the gloom. At the beginning of

the world, the night light had been enough. Three young adventurers

once journeyed from this land on an expedition.

They reached another kingdom, where, as the sun dipped behind the mountains,

a glowing orb was placed high up in an oak tree, casting a gentle light all around.

Thanks to this, everything was easily visible and recognizable,

even though it didn’t shine as brightly as the sun. The travelers

asked a passing farmer, pulling a cart, what kind of light it was.

"That’s the moon," he replied. "Our mayor bought it for three talers and hung it on the oak.

He has to refill it with oil daily and keep it clean so it always shines brightly.

We give him a taler every week for his efforts." When the farmer left, one of the travelers said,

"We could really use this lamp. We have an oak tree at home that's just as big, and we could hang it on that.

How wonderful it would be not to fumble around in the dark at night!"

"I’ve got a plan," said the second. "Let’s get a cart and some horses and take the moon with us.

The folks here can just buy another. I’m a decent climber," said the third. "I can bring it down."

The fourth traveler fetched a cart and horses while the third climbed the tree, bored a hole in the moon,

threaded a rope through it, and let it down. Once the shiny ball was in the cart, they covered it with a cloth

so nobody would notice the theft. They successfully brought it back to their own land and

set it up on a tall oak. People of all ages celebrated when the new lamp illuminated the whole land,

filling bedrooms and living rooms with its glow. The dwarfs emerged from their caves, and the tiny elves

in their little red coats danced in circles on the meadows.

title: The Moon
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The four friends made sure the moon was filled with oil, cleaned the wick, and collected their weekly taler,

but as time passed, they aged. When one of them became ill and realized he was nearing the end,

he insisted that one quarter of the moon should be buried with him. After he passed, the mayor climbed

the tree, snipped off a quarter with hedge shears, and placed it in his coffin. The moon's light

dimmed a little, but it wasn't noticeable. When the second person passed away, the second quarter was

laid to rest with him, and again the light faded a bit more. It grew even weaker after the third one

died, who also took his piece with him. Finally, when the fourth was buried, darkness returned,

and people stumbled around at night without their lanterns, bumping into each other.

However, when the pieces of the moon reassembled in the world below, where darkness had reigned,

the restless dead awoke from their slumber. They were amazed to see again; the moonlight was just right for them,

as their eyes had dimmed enough that they couldn’t handle the brightness of the sun.

They got up, feeling cheerful, and returned to their usual activities. Some went out to the theater

or to dance, while others rushed to taverns, ordering wine, getting drunk, fighting and arguing,

until they were brawling and hitting each other. The commotion grew louder until it finally reached heaven.

St. Peter, the gatekeeper of heaven, thought there was a riot in the lower world and assembled the heavenly hosts,

usually called upon to fend off evil when it tried to invade paradise. Since they didn’t arrive,

he mounted his horse and rode through the gates of heaven down to the world below.

There, he commanded the dead to return to their graves, took the moon back with him,

and hung it up in the heavens.

title: The Mouse, the Bird, and the Sausage
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, a mouse, a bird, and a sausage became friends.

They lived together, sharing their home, and enjoyed a happy, peaceful life.

Over time, they gained lots of possessions.

The bird's job was to fly into the forest every day to gather firewood.

The mouse was responsible for carrying water, lighting the fire, and setting the table, while the sausage took care of cooking.

Those who have plenty often start to yearn for something new.

One day, the bird met another bird and bragged about its wonderful situation.

The other bird, however, called it naive for working so hard, saying that the mouse and sausage were enjoying themselves.

After the mouse had made the fire and fetched the water, she would rest in her cozy little room until it was time to set the table.

The sausage would stay by the pot, ensuring the food cooked perfectly, and when dinner time approached, it would roll through the soup or veggies to mix them.

Once everything was buttered, salted, and ready, the bird would come home, and they would sit down to eat.

After their meal, they would sleep soundly until the next morning, living a splendid life.

The next day, influenced by the other bird, the bird decided it wouldn't go into the woods anymore.

It declared that it had been a servant long enough and was tired of being taken for granted.

No matter how much the mouse and sausage pleaded, the bird insisted they needed to switch roles and try something different.

They drew lots, and the sausage would gather wood, the mouse would cook, and the bird would fetch water.

What happened next? The little sausage set off toward the woods, while the little bird lit the fire.

The mouse stayed by the pot, waiting alone for the sausage to return with wood for the next day.

But the sausage took so long that both the mouse and the bird began to worry something was wrong.

So, the bird flew a bit into the air to search for it.

Not far along the path, the bird encountered a dog that had seized the poor sausage as a treat and gobbled it up.

The bird accused the dog of stealing, but the dog just shrugged and claimed that the sausage had been up to no good.

Sadly, the bird collected the wood, flew home, and shared the unfortunate news.

They were all upset but decided they needed to stick together.

So the bird set the table, and the mouse prepared the food.

Wanting to help like the sausage used to, the mouse tried to climb into the pot to mix everything.

But before she could get in, she fell in, losing her life in the process.

When the bird returned to bring in the dinner, it found no cook.

Panicked, the bird scattered the wood everywhere, calling out and searching, but no cook could be located.

In a moment of carelessness, the fire caught the wood, causing a blaze.

The bird rushed to get water, but the bucket fell into the well, dragging the bird in with it, unable to escape, and it drowned.

title: The Nail
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
A merchant had a successful time at the fair. He sold his goods and filled his money bags with gold and silver.

Feeling pleased, he decided it was time to head home so he could be back in his own house before nightfall.

He loaded his trunk with the money and placed it on his horse.

At noon, he took a break in a town, but when he was ready to continue his journey,

the stable-boy came out with his horse and said, "Sir, a nail is missing from the shoe on the near hind foot."

"Let it be missing," the merchant replied. "The shoe will hold for the six miles I have left to travel. I'm in a hurry."

Later in the afternoon, after stopping again to feed his horse, the stable-boy entered the room and said, "Sir, a shoe is missing from your horse's near hind foot. Should I take him to the blacksmith?"

"Let it be missing," the merchant responded. "The horse can manage for the couple of miles remaining."

He rode out again, but it wasn't long before the horse started to limp. Before he knew it, the horse began to stumble.

And soon, it stumbled one last time, falling down and breaking its leg.

The merchant had no choice but to leave the horse where it was, unbuckle the trunk, carry it on his back, and walk home.

He didn't arrive until late at night. "And that cursed nail," he said to himself, "has caused all this trouble."

title: The Nix of the Mill-Pond
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a miller who lived happily with his wife.

They had money, land, and their prosperity grew year after year.

But bad luck crept in like a thief in the night. Just as their wealth increased, it began to dwindle year after year, and soon he could hardly call the mill he lived in his own.

He fell into deep distress and, after his long day of work, he found no peace in sleep, tossing and turning in his bed, deeply troubled.

One morning, he rose before dawn and stepped outside, hoping that some fresh air might lighten his heart.

As he crossed over the mill-dam, the first rays of sunlight broke through, and he heard a gentle rippling sound in the pond.

He turned around and saw a beautiful woman slowly rising from the water. Her long hair, held back by her soft hands, cascaded down on either side, covering her shimmering white body.

In his fright, he wasn't sure whether to run away or to stay put. But the nixie, with her sweet voice, called him by name and asked why he seemed so sad.

At first, the miller was speechless, but her kind words gave him courage, and he shared how he used to live in wealth and happiness, but now felt so poor and desperate.

The miller thought she must be referring to a puppy or a kitten, so he promised her what she asked for.

The nixie disappeared into the water and the miller hurried back to his mill, feeling consoled and uplifted.

But he wasn't far into the mill when the maid came out of the house, calling for him to rejoice because his wife had given birth to a baby boy.

The miller froze, struck by the news. He realized the cunning nixie had known about it all along and had tricked him.

With a heavy heart, he approached his wife's bedside. When she asked why he didn’t seem happy about their beautiful son, he explained what had happened and the promise he had made to the nixie.

"What good are riches and success to me," he said, "if it means losing my child? But what can I do?" Even their relatives, who had come to celebrate, didn't know what to say.

title: The Nix of the Mill-Pond
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Meanwhile, prosperity returned to the miller's home. Everything he tried succeeded. His coffers filled themselves without effort, and money seemed to multiply overnight.

Before long, he was wealthier than ever, but he couldn't enjoy it fully because the deal he made with the nixie haunted him.

Every time he passed the mill-pond, he feared she would rise to remind him of his promise. He never let his son near the water.

"Be careful," he warned his son, "if you even touch the water, a hand will reach up, grab you, and pull you down.

As the years went by, and the nixie never appeared again, the miller began to relax. His son grew into a young man and became an apprentice to a huntsman.

Once he learned all there was to know and became an excellent huntsman, the lord of the village took him into his service.

In the village lived a beautiful and kind-hearted maiden who captured the huntsman’s heart. When his master noticed his affection, he gifted him a small house.

The two got married, lived peacefully, loved each other deeply, and enjoyed their life together.

One day, while the huntsman was chasing a deer, it darted from the forest into open land, and he pursued it until he finally shot it.

He didn't notice he was near the dangerous mill-pond, and after he had gutted the deer, he went over to the water to wash his bloody hands.

But as soon as he dipped his hands into the water, the nixie appeared, wrapping her wet arms around him and pulling him down under the waves.

That evening, when the huntsman didn't come home, his wife started to worry. She remembered his warnings about the nixie's traps and feared the worst.

She rushed to the pond and saw his hunting bag on the shore, which confirmed her worst fears.

Distraught, she cried out his name, but there was no response. She hurried to the other side of the pond and called again.

She yelled harsh words at the nixie, but got no reply. The still waters reflected only the crescent moon.

title: The Nix of the Mill-Pond
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The poor woman remained at the pond, pacing back and forth, sometimes in silence, sometimes crying out in despair.

Eventually, exhaustion took over, and she collapsed to the ground, sinking into a deep sleep.

In her dream, she found herself struggling to climb upwards through massive rocks. Thorns and brambles snagged her feet, the rain fell heavily on her face, and the wind tossed her hair around.

But once she reached the top, a completely different scene lay before her. The sky was bright blue, the air gentle, and a green meadow with blossoming flowers stretched out before her, dotted with a quaint little cottage.

She approached the cottage and opened the door. Inside sat an old woman with white hair, who kindly gestured for her to come in.

Just then, the poor woman woke up, the day had begun, and she immediately decided to follow the guidance of her dream.

She climbed the mountain with difficulty, and everything looked exactly as she had envisioned.

The old woman welcomed her warmly and pointed to a chair for her to sit. "You've faced a tragedy to have come to my lonely cottage," she said.

Tears streaming down her face, the woman recounted her tale.

'Don't worry," reassured the old woman, "I will help you. I’ll give you a golden comb. Wait until the full moon rises, then go to the mill-pond, sit on the shore, and comb your long black hair with this comb.

When you’re done, lay it down by the water, and you will see what unfolds.

The woman returned home, but the days until the full moon felt endless.

When the radiant moon finally appeared in the sky, she went to the mill-pond, sat down, and combed her hair with the golden comb. After finishing, she placed it at the water’s edge.

Before long, ripples formed beneath the surface, a wave rolled in, and swept the comb away.

In the time it took the comb to sink, the water parted, and the huntsman’s head emerged.

He didn’t speak, but looked at his wife with sorrowful eyes.

title: The Nix of the Mill-Pond
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But then, just as quickly, another wave surged and submerged him again completely.

Everything vanished, and the pond was calm again, merely reflecting the brightness of the full moon.

Overwhelmed with grief, the woman returned home, but once more a dream led her to the old woman’s cottage.

The next morning, she set out again to share her troubles.

The wise woman handed her a golden flute, saying, "Wait for the next full moon, and when it comes, play a beautiful melody, then lay the flute on the sand.

You’ll see what happens next." The wife followed her advice. As soon as the flute touched the sand, the water stirred, and a wave surged forth to carry it away.

Then the water parted again, and this time not just his head, but half of his body surged up.

He reached out longingly towards her, but just then another wave came, engulfed him once more, and drew him down again.

"What use is it to see my beloved only to lose him again?" the despondent woman cried.

Filled with despair, she was once again led by a dream to the old woman's house.

She set out again, and the wise woman gave her a golden spinning wheel, assuring her, "It’s not over yet. Wait for the full moon, then spin the wheel by the shore.

When you’ve filled the spool, place it by the water, and watch what occurs."

The woman followed every instruction. When the full moon appeared, she took the golden spinning-wheel to the shore and began to spin the flax until the spool was full.

As soon as the wheel was set by the water's edge, an intense movement rippled through the pond, and a powerful wave surged up, carrying the wheel away.

Immediately, the huntsman’s head and body emerged in a fountain of water.

He leaped to the shore, grasped his wife's hand, and they escaped.

But they had barely made it a short distance when the pond erupted with a terrifying roar, flooding the surrounding land.

In their panic, the woman called out for the old woman's help, and in an instant, they were transformed into a toad and a frog.

The flood couldn't harm them, but it swept them apart and carried them away.

When the waters finally calmed and they found dry land again, they returned to their human forms.

However, they still didn’t know where the other was, separated by high mountains and deep valleys.

To survive, they had to tend sheep, wandering the fields and forests, both filled with sadness.

As spring bloomed once more, they both happened to take their flocks to the same valley, but didn’t recognize each other.

Yet they felt comforted by no longer feeling so alone.

From then on, they drove their flocks to the same spot every day.

They spoke little, but found solace in each other's presence.

One evening, while the bright full moon illuminated the sky, and the sheep had settled, the shepherd took out his flute and played a haunting melody.

When he finished, he saw the shepherdess weeping heartily.

"Why are you crying?" he asked.

"Oh," she replied, "the full moon shone like this the last time I played this tune, and my beloved rose from the water.

As he gazed at her, it felt like a veil was lifted from his eyes, and he realized she was his beloved wife. Similarly, when she looked at him under the moonlight, she recognized him as well.

They embraced and kissed, no longer strangers to one another.

title: The Old Beggar-Woman
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was an old woman, but I'm sure you've seen an elderly person before.

This woman also begged for alms.

Whenever she received something, she'd say, 'May God bless you'.

One day, the beggar-woman approached a house, and just inside the door, a friendly little boy was standing by the fire, warming himself.

He kindly said to the poor old woman, who was shivering by the door, 'Come in, old mother, and warm yourself.'

She stepped inside but got too close to the fire, causing her tattered rags to catch fire, though she didn’t realize it.

The boy noticed this but should have helped her extinguish the flames.

Isn't it true that he should have put them out? And if he didn't have any water, he ought to have cried enough tears to create two little streams to douse the flames.

title: The Old Man and His Grandson
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once there was an extremely old man whose eyesight had faded, his hearing was nearly gone, and his knees shook when he stood.

When he sat at the table, he struggled to hold his spoon, often spilling broth all over the tablecloth.

His son and daughter-in-law were frustrated by this, and eventually, they made the old grandfather sit in a corner behind the stove.

They gave him his meals in a clay bowl and barely enough to eat.

With tear-filled eyes, he would glance towards the table.

One day, his unsteady hands couldn't manage the bowl, and it slipped from his grasp and shattered on the floor.

The young wife scolded him, but he remained silent and simply sighed.

They then bought him a wooden bowl for a few coins, and that was what he had to eat from.

One day, while they were sitting like this, their four-year-old grandson started picking up small pieces of wood from the ground.

"What are you doing?" the father asked.

"I’m making a little trough," the child replied, "for you and Mom to eat from when I’m grown up."

The man and his wife exchanged a glance and soon found themselves in tears.

From that moment, they brought the old grandfather back to the table, allowing him to eat with them and not minding if he spilled a little.

title: The Old Man Made Young Again
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
In a time when our Lord walked the earth, He and St. Peter took a break one evening at a blacksmith's shop.

As they rested, a poor beggar, weighed down by age and illness, approached the blacksmith asking for help.

St. Peter felt deep compassion for him and said, "Lord, if it’s your will, heal this man so he can earn his own living."

The Lord replied kindly, "Smith, would you lend me your forge? Heat some coals for me, and I will make this suffering old man young again."

The blacksmith agreed, and St. Peter fanned the flames. As the fire crackled and roared, the Lord took the little old man and placed him in the glowing coals, causing him to shine like a rose in bloom as he praised God.

Afterward, the Lord moved to the quenching tub, submerged the glowing man in the water, and, once he had cooled him carefully, gave him his blessing.

Suddenly, the man leaped out, looking fresh, upright, healthy, and as if he were only twenty years old.

The blacksmith, who had been watching the entire spectacle with keen interest, invited them both to dinner.

He had an elderly, half-blind mother-in-law who approached the young man and earnestly asked if the fire had hurt him.

He replied, "I’ve never felt better! It was like sitting in cool dew, not fire at all."

The young man's words echoed in the old woman's mind all night. The next morning, after the Lord had left and thanked the smith heartily, the blacksmith thought about how he could restore his own mother-in-law's youth, since he had observed everything closely, and it was within his craft.

He called out to her, asking if she wanted to feel as spry as an eighteen-year-old girl.

She eagerly responded, "Absolutely! If that young man came out so well, I want the same!"

The blacksmith then stoked a big fire and tossed the old woman into it. She twisted and writhed, screaming in terror.

"Why are you screaming and thrashing around?" the blacksmith shouted, while he fanned the flames even hotter until her rags burned away.

The old woman continued to wail, and the blacksmith thought, "Maybe I don’t have the right skill for this," and pulled her out, throwing her into the cooling tub.

Her screams were so loud that the blacksmith's wife and her daughter-in-law, both upstairs and pregnant, rushed down to see what was happening.

They found the old woman in a heap in the quenching tub, howling and twisted, looking entirely shriveled.

Seeing this, the two women, frightened out of their wits, gave birth that very night to two boys who were not born as humans but as apes, and they fled into the woods, giving rise to the ape species.

title: The Old Woman in the Wood
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once, a poor servant girl was traveling through a vast forest with the family she worked for.

As they journeyed deeper into the woods,

they were suddenly ambushed by robbers who emerged from the bushes, killing everyone in sight.

Everyone perished, except for the girl, who had jumped out of the carriage in panic and hidden behind a tree.

When the robbers left with their loot, she stepped out and witnessed the tragic scene.

Overcome with grief, she cried out, "What can a poor girl like me do now? I have no idea how to find my way out of this forest, and no one lives here, so I'm surely going to starve."

She wandered around, searching for a path, but found none. As evening fell, she sat down under a tree, put her trust in God, and decided to wait there, no matter what happened.

After some time, a white dove flew toward her, holding a tiny golden key in its beak.

The dove placed the key in her hand and said, "Do you see that massive tree over there? There’s a small lock in it; open it with the key, and you’ll find plenty of food to satisfy your hunger."

She approached the tree and opened it, discovering milk in a small dish and white bread to crumble into it, enough for her to fill her stomach.

Once she was satisfied, she said, "It's about that time when the hens at home go to roost; I'm so tired, I could use a bed, too."

Then the dove returned with another golden key in its beak and said, "Open that tree there, and you will find a bed."

She opened it and found a beautiful white bed. Grateful, she prayed to God for protection through the night and lay down to sleep.

The next morning, the dove appeared for the third time, bringing another little key and said, "Open that tree, and you’ll discover some clothes."

When she opened it, she was amazed to find garments adorned with gold and jewels, more splendid than anything a king’s daughter would wear.

title: The Old Woman in the Wood
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She lived there for a while, and every day the dove visited, ensuring she had everything she needed, and it was a peaceful, good life.

One day, the dove said, "Could you do me a favor?" "With all my heart," replied the girl.

The little dove explained, "I will lead you to a small house. Inside, an old woman will be sitting by the fire and will greet you with 'good day'. But for your own sake, don’t answer her; just let her be and walk past her on the right.

Further along, you'll find a door; open it, and you’ll enter a room filled with rings of all sorts, some stunning with shining stones. However, leave them as they are and look for a plain one among them, and bring it back to me."

The girl arrived at the little house and found an old woman sitting there who stared at her and said, "Good day, my child." The girl remained silent and opened the door.

The old woman shouted, "Where are you going?" as she grabbed the girl's dress, trying to hold her back, saying, "This is my house; no one can enter if I don’t allow it." But the girl stayed quiet, broke free from her grasp, and walked straight into the room.

Inside, there was an enormous amount of rings that sparkled before her eyes. She sifted through them searching for the plain one but couldn’t find it.

While she searched, she noticed the old woman trying to sneak away, carrying a birdcage. The girl quickly followed her, snatched the cage from her hand, and when she looked inside, she found a bird that had the plain ring.

She took the ring and joyfully ran back home, eager for the little white dove to come and collect it, but it didn’t come.

So she leaned against a tree, determined to wait for the dove.

As she stood there, it felt as if the tree was soft and bending down. Suddenly, the branches wrapped around her like arms, and when she turned around, the tree transformed into a handsome man who embraced her warmly and said, "You’ve freed me from the clutches of that wicked old woman; she's a witch.

She turned me into a tree, and every day, I spent two hours as a white dove; I could only regain my human form once she lost the ring." Then, his servants and horses, who had also been turned into trees, were released from the enchantment and stood beside him.

He led them all back to his kingdom, for he was a prince, and they married and lived happily ever after.

title: The Owl
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
A couple of hundred years ago, when people weren’t quite as crafty as they are today, something remarkable happened.

In a small town, by some unfortunate twist of fate, a large horned owl had strayed from the nearby woods into the barn of a local resident during the night.

When morning came, the owl didn't dare to leave her hiding spot, frightened by the ruckus that other birds made whenever she showed her face.

The next morning, when the stablehand went into the barn to grab some straw, he was so startled by the sight of the owl perched in a corner that he ran away.

He rushed to tell his master that a monster like nothing he had ever seen was in the barn, one that could easily devour a man.

'I know your kind,' replied the master. 'You have the guts to chase blackbirds in the fields, but when you see a dead hen, you need a stick to go near it.'

'I must see this monster for myself,' the master declared, and he confidently headed into the granary to take a look.

However, when he saw the strange, fearsome creature for himself, he felt just as terrified as the stablehand had.

He jumped back, dashed to his neighbors, and urgently asked for help against this unknown and dangerous beast, fearing the entire town would be threatened if the owl got loose.

A great commotion broke out in the streets as townspeople gathered, armed with spears, hay forks, scythes, and axes as if preparing for battle.

Eventually, the town's council members showed up, led by the burgomaster.

Once they assembled in the marketplace, they made their way to the barn and surrounded it.

One of the bravest among them stepped forward and cautiously entered the barn with his spear lowered, but he soon fled screaming, looking pale as a ghost and unable to speak.

Two others were brave enough to try as well, but they emerged just as terrified.

Finally, a large, strong man known for his past exploits said, 'Staring at the monster isn't going to chase it away; we need to take this seriously, but I see that you have all turned into cowards, and none of you will face the creature.'

title: The Owl
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He demanded armor be given to him, requested a sword and spear, and armed himself.

Everyone praised his bravery, although many worried for his safety.

The barn doors were flung open, revealing the owl perched on a great crossbeam.

He had a ladder brought over, and as he raised it and prepared to climb, the crowd shouted encouragement, urging him to be courageous and calling on St. George, who defeated the dragon.

Just as he reached the top, the owl noticed his intentions and, startled by the crowd and chaos, didn’t know how to escape.

She rolled her eyes, fluffed her feathers, flapped her wings, snapped her beak, and hooted harshly, 'Tuwhit, tuwhoo.'

'Strike now! Strike now!' the crowd yelled to their hero.

'Anyone in my position wouldn’t shout, 'Strike now,'' he replied, feeling a surge of doubt.

He managed to step one rung higher on the ladder, but then he began to tremble and, feeling weak, descended.

Now, no one was left who wanted to place themselves in such danger.

'The monster has surely poisoned the strongest among us, just by snapping at him and breathing on him,' they murmured.

'Are we really going to risk our lives too?'

They started to discuss what they should do to keep the town safe.

After some time of deliberation, the burgomaster came up with a solution.

'I think we should use the town funds to pay for this barn and whatever corn, straw, or hay is inside, compensating the owner.'

'Then we'll burn down the entire building along with that dreadful beast. This is no time to worry about money; being stingy would be foolish.'

Everyone agreed with him.

So they set fire to the barn at all four corners, and the owl met her end in the flames.

'Let anyone who doesn’t believe this go there and see for themselves.'

title: The Pack of Ragamuffins
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One day, the rooster said to the hen, "Now is the perfect time for the nuts to be ripe."

"Let’s head up to the hill and enjoy a feast before the squirrel gets to them all!"

"Absolutely!" replied the hen. "Let’s go and have some fun!"

So they made their way to the hill, and since it was such a beautiful day, they stayed until evening.

I’m not sure if they overindulged or just got a bit too proud, but when it was time to head home, they refused to walk.

The rooster ended up having to build a little carriage out of nut shells.

Once it was ready, the little hen hopped inside and told the rooster, "You can harness yourself to it."

"I find that hard to accept," said the rooster. "I’d prefer to walk home rather than be pulled around like that."

"No way! That’s not our deal. I don’t mind being the driver and sitting in front, but I won’t pull it myself."

While they were arguing, a duck waddled over and quacked, "You thieves! Who told you to raid my nut hill?"

"Just wait and see what happens to you!" The duck charged at the rooster.

But the rooster wasn’t just going to stand still—instead, he bravely attacked the duck.

Eventually, he managed to injure her with his spurs, and she cried for mercy, agreeing to be harnessed to the carriage as punishment.

Now the rooster took his place at the front and became the driver, and off they went at a swift pace, with the rooster shouting, "Duck, go as fast as you can!"

After a while, they encountered two pedestrians, a pin and a needle.

They called out, "Stop! Stop! It’s getting dark, and we won’t be able to go any further. It's so messy on the road; can we ride in the carriage for a bit?"

They had been at the tailor's pub and lingered too long over their drinks.

title: The Pack of Ragamuffins
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Since they were so small and didn’t take up much space, the rooster let them climb aboard, but only if they promised not to step on his and the hen's feet.

Later that evening, they arrived at an inn. Since they didn't want to continue traveling at night, and the duck was a bit clumsy and kept stumbling, they decided to stay.

At first, the innkeeper hesitated, saying his place was already full, and he didn’t think they looked very important.

But eventually, after they flattered him and mentioned that he'd get the egg laid by the hen along the way and keep the duck who laid eggs every day, he agreed to let them stay.

They served themselves well, enjoyed a feast, and had a great time.

Early the next morning, while everyone was still sleeping, the rooster woke the hen, brought out the egg, broke it open, and they ate it together, tossing the shell into the fireplace.

Then they tiptoed to where the needle was still sleeping, grabbed it by the head, and stuck it into the landlord's chair cushion.

The pin was placed in his towel, and without delay, they hurried away across the heath.

The duck, who enjoyed sleeping outdoors and had stayed in the yard, heard them leave and decided to have some fun herself. She found a stream and swam, which was much faster than being tied to a carriage.

The innkeeper didn’t wake up until a couple of hours later. He washed up and was about to dry himself when the pin scratched across his face, leaving a red line from one ear to the other.

After that, he went into the kitchen to light his pipe, but as he reached for it, the eggshell flew into his eyes.

"Why is everything attacking me this morning?" he grumbled as he angrily sat down in his grandfather's chair.

But he quickly jumped up, shouting in pain because the needle had pricked him even worse than the pin, and not just on the head.

Now he was really furious and suspected the guests who had arrived late the night before.

When he went to find them, they were already gone.

He then vowed never to take in ragamuffins again, as they eat a lot, pay nothing, and play tricks as a way of showing their gratitude.

title: The Peasant and the Devil
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a clever and shrewd peasant.

Everyone talked about his clever tricks, but the most famous story was how he once captured the devil and outsmarted him.

One day, after working in his field, the peasant was getting ready to head home when he noticed a strange heap of burning coals right in the middle of his land.

Curiously, he walked over to it and saw a little black devil sitting on the hot coals.

'Are you sitting on a treasure?' the peasant asked.

'Yes, indeed,' replied the devil. 'There’s a treasure here that has more gold and silver than you’ve ever seen in your life.'

'But that treasure is in my field and it belongs to me,' said the peasant.

'It’s yours,' the devil answered, 'if you agree to give me half of everything your field produces for two years.'

'I have enough money, but I crave the fruits of the earth,' the devil added.

The peasant agreed to the deal but said, 'To avoid any disputes over the division, everything above ground will be yours and everything below ground will belong to me.'

The devil liked that arrangement, but the sly peasant had planted turnips.

When harvest time arrived, the devil showed up to claim his share but found only yellow, withered leaves, while the peasant joyfully dug up his turnips.

'You’ve gotten the better of me this time,' said the devil, 'but next time, that won’t work. What grows above ground will be yours, and what’s below, will be mine.'

'I agree,' replied the peasant.

But when it was time to plant again, instead of turnips, he sowed wheat.

When the wheat ripened, the peasant went out to the field and harvested all the full stalks down to the ground.

When the devil arrived, he found only the stubble and stormed off in a rage into a crack in the rocks.

'That’s how you trick the devil,' said the peasant as he went to collect his treasure.

title: The Peasant in Heaven
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, a poor and devout peasant passed away and found himself at the gates of heaven.

At the same time, a very wealthy lord arrived, eager to enter heaven as well.

Then, Saint Peter showed up with the key.

He unlocked the door and welcomed the great man inside,

but seemed to overlook the peasant completely as he closed the door again.

Outside, the peasant heard the rich man being celebrated in heaven,

with joyful music and singing echoing from within.

Soon, everything fell silent, and Saint Peter reopened the gates of heaven.

He let the peasant enter.

The peasant expected to be welcomed with music and songs just like the rich man had been,

but it was completely quiet.

He was greeted warmly; the angels came to welcome him.

But no one sang.

So, the peasant asked Saint Peter why there were no songs for him as there had been for the rich man.

He remarked that it seemed like heaven was just as biased as life on earth.

Saint Peter replied, "Not at all; you are just as cherished by us as anyone else.

You will experience every heavenly joy that the rich man enjoys.

However, people like you come to heaven every day, while a rich man like him only comes once in a while."

title: The Peasant's Wise Daughter
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a poor peasant who owned nothing but a small house and had just one daughter.

The daughter suggested, "We should ask the king for a piece of newly-cleared land."

When the king heard about their struggles, he generously gave them a plot of land.

They dug up the land with the plan to sow a little corn and some other grains.

After they had worked nearly the entire field, they uncovered a mortar made of pure gold.

The father turned to his daughter and said, "Since the king has been so kind to us, we should give him this mortar as a token of thanks."

But the daughter disagreed, responding, "Dad, if we have the mortar but not the pestle, we’ll need to find the pestle too. So it's better if we keep quiet about it."

Despite her advice, he ignored her and took the mortar to the king instead, saying he had found it on the cleared land and offering it as a gift.

The king accepted the mortar and asked if he found anything else.

When the peasant replied no, the king insisted he must now produce the pestle.

The peasant said they hadn’t discovered it, but talking to the king was like speaking to the wind—he was thrown into prison until he could produce the pestle.

Every day, the servants brought him bread and water, and he cried out in despair, "If only I had listened to my daughter!"

He lamented, "Oh, if I had just listened to my daughter!"—he refused to eat or drink.

The king, hearing this, ordered the peasant to be brought before him and asked why he was always crying about his daughter.

The peasant explained, "She advised me not to take the mortar since I would also need to find the pestle."

The king responded, "If you have such a wise daughter, she should come here!"

So the daughter had no choice but to appear before the king, who asked her if she was truly that wise.

He proposed a riddle; if she could solve it, he would marry her.

title: The Peasant's Wise Daughter
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Immediately, she replied, "Yes, I will solve it!"

The king declared, "You must come to me not clothed, not naked, not riding, not walking, not on the road, and not off the road; if you can accomplish this, I will marry you."

So she went away, removed all her clothing, leaving herself not clothed.

Then she took a large fishing net and wrapped it around herself, so she was not naked.

She hired a donkey and tied the fishing net to its tail, making it pull her along, which meant she was neither riding nor walking.

The donkey walked in the ruts, allowing her to touch the ground only with her big toe, meaning she was neither on the road nor off of it.

When she arrived like this, the king declared she had solved the riddle and met all the conditions.

He immediately ordered her father released from prison, took her as his wife, and entrusted her with all the royal belongings.

Years later, during a parade reviewing his army, the king saw some peasants who had stopped with their wagons to sell wood.

Some of them had oxen, and others had horses. One peasant had three horses, and one gave birth to a foal, which darted off and lay down between two oxen.

The peasants began to argue fiercely, hitting each other, as the one with the oxen claimed the foal belonged to him, while the horse owner insisted it was his.

The squabble was brought before the king, who decided that the foal would stay where it was found, meaning it ended up with the peasant that didn’t own it.

The other peasant walked away, mourning over his lost foal. He had heard about the queen's kindness because she was from humble peasant roots.

So he approached her, asking if she could help him get his foal back.

She replied, "Yes, I will help, but you must promise me something first.

"Tomorrow morning, when the king inspects the guard, stand in the middle of the road where he will pass, pretend you’re fishing with a large net.

"Keep fishing and act like your net is full, and if the king questions you, I’ll tell you what to say."

title: The Peasant's Wise Daughter
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The next morning, the peasant stood in the road, fishing on dry ground.

When the king saw him, he sent a messenger to inquire about the ridiculous sight.

The peasant replied, "I'm fishing!" and the messenger questioned how he could fish without water.

The peasant explained, "It’s just as easy for me to fish here as it is for you."

The messenger returned with this report, prompting the king to summon the peasant.

The king demanded to know who had come up with such an idea.

The peasant insisted that the idea was completely his own.

Frustrated, the king had him beaten until he confessed he had gotten the idea from the queen.

Returning home, the king confronted his wife, saying, "Why have you acted so deceitfully? I won’t keep you as my wife anymore; your time is up, go back to your peasant hut.

However, I will allow you to take the one thing you cherish the most."

She replied, "I will do as you wish, my dear husband," embraced him, and kissed him goodbye.

Then she instructed a servant to bring her a powerful sleeping potion.

The king drank deeply, quickly falling into a deep sleep, while she took only a tiny sip.

Seeing he was sound asleep, she called a servant to help wrap the king in a beautiful white linen cloth.

They carried him to a carriage waiting outside, and she drove off with him.

She laid him on her little bed, and he slept uninterrupted for a full day and night.

When he finally woke up, he looked around in confusion and called for his attendants, but no one was there.

Eventually, his wife entered the room and said, "My dear lord and king, you told me I could take what was most precious to me; I have brought you with me."

Tears filled the king's eyes as he said, "My dear wife, you shall be mine and I will be yours," and he took her back to the royal palace.

They married once again, and they lived happily ever after.

title: The Pink
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a queen who had not been blessed with any children.

Every morning, she would go into the garden and pray to God in heaven.

She asked God to give her a son or a daughter.

Then, an angel appeared and reassured her, saying, "Don't worry, you will have a son who can wish for anything he desires."

"Whatever he wishes for in this world will become reality."

Excited, she rushed to tell the king the wonderful news.

When the time finally came, she gave birth to a son, and the king was overjoyed.

Every morning, she took the child to the garden where wild animals were kept, and washed herself in a clear stream.

One day, when the child was a little older and resting in her arms, she dozed off.

This was when the old cook, who knew about the child's wishing ability, stole him away.

He took a hen, killed it, and smeared its blood on the queen's apron and dress.

He then carried the child to a hidden place where a nurse was forced to care for him.

Then he went to the king and accused the queen of letting her child be taken by wild beasts.

When the king saw the blood on her clothes, he believed the cook and became furious.

He commanded that a high tower be built, one where neither the sun nor moon could be seen.

He had his wife locked in the tower and sealed in.

She was to remain there for seven years without food or drink until she starved.

However, God sent two angels in the form of white doves that brought her food twice a day, keeping her alive.

Meanwhile, the cook worried that the child's wishing power could get him into trouble.

So he left the palace and went to the now-talking child, suggesting he wish for a beautiful palace with a garden and everything that comes with it.

As soon as the boy wished for it, everything appeared just as he imagined.

Soon after, the cook told him, "It's not good to be alone. Wish for a lovely girl to join you."

title: The Pink
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The king's son wished for a companion, and instantly, a girl appeared, more beautiful than any artist could capture.

The two of them played together and cherished one another, while the old cook enjoyed his life like a noble.

However, he worried that the king's son might one day want to reunite with his father, putting him at risk.

So, he took the girl aside and said, "Tonight, when the boy is asleep, stab him in the heart and bring me his heart and tongue. If you refuse, it'll cost you dearly."

The next day, she explained that she couldn't bring herself to harm the innocent boy.

The cook, desperate, threatened her life if she didn't comply.

Eventually, she decided to sacrifice a little hind instead.

She killed it, took its heart and tongue, and placed them on a plate.

As she heard the cook approaching, she told the boy to lie down and cover himself with blankets.

The wicked cook entered and asked, "Where are the boy's heart and tongue?"

The girl handed him the plate, but the king's son uncovered himself and exclaimed, "You old sinner, why did you try to kill me?"

"Now I will pronounce your sentence: you will become a black poodle with a gold collar and will eat burning coals until flames burst from your throat."

As soon as he finished speaking, the old man was transformed into a poodle, wearing a gold collar.

The cooks were ordered to bring in live coals, which he had to eat until flames erupted from his throat.

The king's son lingered for a while, thinking of his mother and wondering about her fate.

Eventually, he said to the girl, "I want to go home to my own land. Will you come with me?"

She replied, "Oh, the journey is so long, and what will I do in a strange land where no one knows me?"

Feeling they couldn't bear to be apart, he wished for her to be transformed into a beautiful pink flower and took her along.

He returned to his homeland, with the poodle running after him.

title: The Pink
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He reached the tower where his mother was imprisoned and wished for a ladder to reach the top.

Once up there, he peered inside and called out, "Dear mother, lady queen, are you alive or have you passed away?"

She replied, "I just ate and am still satisfied," thinking the angels were providing for her.

He said, "I am your dear son, who was supposedly taken by the wild beasts, but I'm alive and will rescue you soon."

He climbed back down, went to his father, and announced himself as a stranger looking for work.

The king agreed, saying that if the huntsman was skilled and could bring back game, he would welcome him, but that deer had never been seen in the area.

The huntsman promised to bring as much game as needed for the royal table.

He gathered all the huntsmen and led them into the woods.

They formed a large circle, leaving one end open, while he stood in the middle and began to wish.

Suddenly, over two hundred deer ran into the circle, and the huntsmen shot them.

They loaded the deer onto sixty carts and took them back to the king, who was finally able to feast after years of scarcity.

The king was overjoyed and commanded a grand feast for all his servants the next day.

As everyone gathered, he called for the huntsman to sit beside him.

The huntsman replied, "Your majesty, I’m just a humble huntsman," but the king insisted.

While dining, the huntsman thought of his mother and wished that someone would ask about her.

Sure enough, the marshal asked, "Your majesty, we are enjoying ourselves here, but how is the queen in the tower? Is she still alive?"

The king retorted, "She allowed my dear son to be torn apart by wild beasts; I don’t want to hear her name!"
