The huntsman stood up and said, "Dear father, she is alive, and I am her son. I wasn't taken by beasts but by that wicked cook who stole me from her while she was asleep, and stained her apron with animal blood."

He pointed to the dog with the golden collar, saying, "That’s the one who did it," and commanded that live coals be brought.

The dog was forced to eat them until flames erupted from his throat.

Then the huntsman asked the king if he wanted to see the dog as the cook, wishing him back into his original form.

In an instant, the cook stood there, white apron and knife at his side.

The king was so enraged that he ordered the cook to be thrown into the deepest dungeon.

Then the huntsman said, "Father, would you like to meet the girl who cared for me so lovingly, even though her own life was at risk?"

The king agreed, so the son revealed her in her flower form.

He took the pink from his pocket and placed it on the royal table, and it was the most beautiful flower the king had ever seen.

Then he wished for her to return to her maiden form, and she stood before them, more stunning than any artist could render.

The king sent two ladies-in-waiting to fetch the queen from the tower.

When she entered, she would not eat and said, "The kind and merciful God who has sustained me will soon free me."

She lived three more days, then passed away content, and when she was buried, the two doves that had brought her food came to rest on her grave.

The king ordered the cook to be torn into pieces, but his own grief consumed him, and he died soon after.

His son married the beautiful girl he had brought back as a flower, and it remains unknown if they are still alive today.

title: The Poor Boy in the Grave
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once there was a poor shepherd boy whose parents had passed away.

He was placed by the authorities in the household of a wealthy man.

This man and his wife, however, had unkind hearts; they were greedy and resented anyone who gave the boy even a tiny piece of their food.

The young boy could do whatever he wanted, but he received very little to eat and earned only more hits in return.

One day, he was in charge of watching a hen and her chicks, but the hen slipped through a hedge with them.

Suddenly, a hawk swooped down and snatched her away.

The boy shouted with all his strength, "Thief! Thief! Rascal!" But it didn't matter; the hawk didn't return with its catch.

The man heard the commotion, ran over, and as soon as he realized his hen was gone, he erupted in anger.

He beat the boy so badly that he couldn't move for two days.

After he recovered, he took care of the chicks without their mother, and it was even harder now since they scattered everywhere.

Thinking he was being clever, he tied them all together with a string to keep the hawk from stealing them.

But he was sorely mistaken.

After two days of chasing them around and feeling hungry, he finally fell asleep.

The hawk returned and grabbed one of the tied chicks; since the others were attached, it took them all away.

It perched on a tree and devoured them.

The farmer came home, saw what happened, and furiously beat the boy again, leaving him bedridden for several days.

When he could finally get up, the farmer told him, "You're too stupid for this. I can't make a herdsman out of you; you'll be my errand boy instead."

He then sent him to the judge with a basket of grapes and a letter.

As he walked, hunger and thirst tormented him, and he couldn’t resist eating two grapes.

When he reached the judge, the judge read the letter and counted the grapes.

"Two are missing!" he exclaimed.

The boy honestly admitted that, driven by hunger and thirst, he had eaten the missing grapes.

title: The Poor Boy in the Grave
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The judge wrote a letter to the farmer, asking for the same number of grapes again.

The boy had to deliver these too, and once more found himself unable to resist eating two more grapes because he was so hungry.

Before doing so, he took the letter out of the basket and hid it under a stone, planning that it wouldn’t betray him.

When the judge asked about the missing grapes again, the boy said, "How did you find out? The letter couldn't possibly know, since I hid it before I ate them!"

The judge couldn't help but laugh at the boy's innocence and wrote a letter advising the farmer to take better care of the poor boy.

He said the farmer should ensure the boy had enough food and drink and teach him right from wrong.

The farmer, however, had a harsh response: "If you want to eat, you must work. If you mess up, you'll learn through punishment."

The next day, he assigned the boy a tough task.

"Chop two bundles of hay for the horses. I’ll return in five hours, and if it’s not done, I’ll beat you!" he warned.

Then the farmer, along with his wife and servants, headed to a yearly fair, leaving only a small piece of bread for the boy.

The boy sat down on a bench and put his all into the work.

As he got warm, he took off his little coat and tossed it onto the hay.

In his anxiety about finishing on time, he kept cutting the hay but accidentally chopped his coat as well.

He realized his mistake too late, feeling despair.

"Oh no! All is lost! The farmer will certainly kill me if he sees this!" he panicked.

The boy recalled that the farmer's wife once mentioned having a pot filled with poison under her bed.

But she had only said that to keep greedy people away; it actually contained honey.

The boy crawled under the bed, grabbed the pot, and ate all the honey.

"I don’t know,

he remarked, "people say death is bitter, but this tastes sweet to me!"

No wonder the farmer's wife wished for death so often.

He sat back down in a little chair, ready to accept his fate.

But instead of feeling weaker, he felt revitalized by the food.

"This can’t be poison!" he thought.

But then he remembered the farmer mentioning a bottle of fly poison in the closet with his clothes.

That must be the true poison that will end my life, he concluded.

However, it turned out to be Hungarian wine.

The boy took the bottle and gulped it down.

"This death tastes sweet too!" he said.

But shortly after, as the wine made him dizzy, he felt himself nearing death.

"I feel like I must die," he said. "I'll go to the churchyard and find a grave."

He stumbled out, arrived at the churchyard, and laid down in a freshly dug grave.

His senses began to fade.

Nearby, there was an inn hosting a wedding.

When he heard the music, he imagined he was in paradise until he finally lost all consciousness.

The poor boy never woke up again.

The strong wine mixed with the cold night air took his life, and he remained in the grave.

When the farmer heard about the boy's death, he was struck with fear.

He worried about facing justice, and his panic was so intense that he fainted.

His wife, who was nearby with a pan of hot oil, rushed to help him.

But the flames caught the pan, and soon the whole house was ablaze.

Within hours, it lay in ashes, and for the rest of their lives, they lived in poverty and misery, tormented by guilt.

title: The Poor Man and the Rich Man
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Long ago, when the Lord would walk among us, it happened that he grew tired and

the night fell before he could find an inn. At that moment,

he came across two houses sitting across from each other on the path.

One was grand and stunning, while the other was small and shabby.

The grand one belonged to a wealthy man, and the small one was the home of a poor man.

The Lord thought to himself, "I don’t want to trouble the rich man.

I'll spend the night with him." Just then, the rich man heard someone

knocking at his door. He opened the window and asked the stranger

what he needed. The Lord replied, "I just need a place to stay for the night."

The rich man examined the traveler from head to toe, and seeing that the Lord was dressed in simple clothes,

not looking like someone with plenty of money, he shook his head and said,

"No, I can't take you in; my rooms are filled with herbs and

seeds. If I let everyone in who knocks at my door,

I might end up begging myself. Please go somewhere else.

With that, he shut the window and turned away.

The Lord walked away from the rich man and approached the small house, knocking gently.

Before he even finished knocking, the poor man opened the little door and welcomed the traveler.

"Come in, please! It’s already dark, and you can't keep walking tonight," he said.

The Lord was pleased with this welcome and stepped inside.

The poor man's wife kindly invited him to make himself at home, offering what little they had.

They had a modest meal to offer, but they gave it wholeheartedly.

She put some potatoes on the fire, and while they boiled, she milked their goat,

hoping to have some fresh milk to go along with it.

When dinner was ready, the Lord sat down with the couple.

He enjoyed their simple meal, filled with laughter and smiles at the table.

After dinner, as it became bedtime, the woman pulled her husband aside and said,

"Listen, dear, let’s make a bed of straw for ourselves tonight,

title: The Poor Man and the Rich Man
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
so the tired traveler can sleep in our bed and rest well after a long day of walking."

"Absolutely," he replied. "I will ask him to take our bed and rest properly."

He approached the stranger and offered him their bed to sleep in.

But the Lord hesitated, not wanting to take away the couple's bed.

However, they insisted until he finally relented and lay down in their bed,

while the couple settled down on the straw.

The next morning, they woke up before dawn and prepared the best breakfast they could for their guest.

When the sun poured in through the little window and the Lord had risen,

he shared another meal with them before getting ready to continue on his journey.

As he stood at the door, he turned to the couple and said,

"Since you both are so kind, you can wish for three things for yourselves, and I will grant them."

The man replied, "What else could I wish for but eternal happiness? And along with that, health so we can enjoy every day, and daily bread for our meals.

For the third wish, I’m not sure what to ask for."

The Lord offered, "Would you like a new house instead of this old one?"

"Oh yes," the man said excitedly, "if that’s possible, I would love that!"

The Lord granted his wish, transforming their old house into a new one.

He blessed them once more and continued on his way.

By the time the rich man awoke, the sun was high in the sky. He peered out

of his window and was stunned to see a lovely new house with red tiles and bright windows,

where the shabby hut used to be. Curious, he called for his wife.

"What could have happened?" he asked, astonished.

Last night, a miserable little hut stood there, and now it’s a beautiful new house!

"Go see for yourself!" he ordered.

So his wife rushed over to ask the poor man what had happened.

He replied, "Last night, a traveler came and asked to stay overnight,

title: The Poor Man and the Rich Man
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
and this morning he granted us three wishes—a happy life, health, and enough food to eat,

and on top of that, a beautiful new house to replace our old one."

When the rich man's wife heard this news, she swiftly returned to her husband.

"You won’t believe it!" she exclaimed. The man responded,

"I could kick myself! If I had only known!

That traveler also came to our house, and I turned him away.

Hurry, get on your horse! You can still catch up with him and ask for your own three wishes!"

Following her urgent advice, the rich man hopped on his horse and rode off.

He soon spotted the Lord and approached him.

Speaking sweetly, he asked the Lord not to hold his earlier refusal against him.

"I was looking for the key for the front door, and by the time I found it, you were already gone.

If you come this way again, please stay with me."

"If I pass by again, I will," replied the Lord.

Then the rich man inquired if he could also wish for three things, like his neighbor had.

"Of course," the Lord replied, "but be warned—it may not be beneficial for you.

You might be better off not wishing for anything at all."

But the rich man thought that surely he could think of something that would make him happy.

As the Lord then instructed, "Ride home, and your three wishes will indeed be granted."

Now that the rich man had a chance to gain what he wanted, he rode home,

thinking hard about what he should wish for.

But as he pondered, he accidentally loosened the reins, and his horse began to prance around,

disrupting his thoughts and leaving him frustrated.

"Calm down, Lisa," he said gently, but the horse only started acting up even more.

Finally, becoming angry, he shouted, "I wish you’d break your neck!"

As soon as he uttered those words, the horse collapsed and lay still, never to rise again.

Just like that, his first wish came true. Yet, being stingy by nature,

title: The Poor Man and the Rich Man
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
he didn’t want to leave the harness behind.

He removed it and slung it over his shoulder, now forced to walk.

"I still have two wishes left," he consoled himself.

As he trudged along through the hot, sandy path, feeling more and more irritated,

the saddle pressed against his back, and he still had no idea what to wish for.

"If I wished for all the wealth and gems in the world,

wouldn't I then have to think of other wishes too? I know I would.

So I should choose something so fantastic that I wouldn't ever need to wish for anything again."

With that, he sighed. "Ah, if only I were like that Bavarian peasant,

who also had three wishes and knew precisely how to use them.

First, he wished for a lot of beer;

then he wished for as much beer as he could drink; and finally,

he wished for a barrel of beer to go with it."

Many times, the rich man thought he had it all figured out,

but it always seemed just a bit too little.

Then he realized how easy his wife's life was—staying home in a cool room, enjoying herself.

This irked him, and before he knew it, he exclaimed,

"I wish she were the one sitting on this saddle and couldn’t get off it,

instead of me suffering with it on my back!"

The moment he spoke those words, the saddle vanished from his back.

He instantly realized that his second wish had come true.

Feeling the heat of his anger, he ran straight home,

eager to have some time alone in his room to think of a great final wish.

But as he entered and opened the door,

he found his wife sitting in the middle of the room on the saddle,

crying and unable to get off.

"Just bear it for now," he told her, "and I’ll wish for all the riches on earth for you.

Just please stay put on that saddle!"

But she called him foolish, saying, "What good are riches if I’m stuck here on this saddle?

You wished this upon me, so you have to help me down!"

Left with no choice, he begrudgingly used his last wish to get her off the saddle.

As soon as he made that wish, it was granted. In the end, he gained nothing but frustration,

trouble, and the loss of his horse. Meanwhile, the poor couple lived happily and humbly for the rest of their days.

title: The Poor Miller's Boy and the Cat
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
In a small mill, there lived an old miller who had no wife or children.

Three apprentices worked for him. Having spent several years together,

the miller eventually said to them, "I'm getting old and want to relax

by the stove. Go out and find me the best horse. Whoever brings me the

best one will inherit the mill, and in return, they'll care for me until

I die."

The third boy was a bit of a fool, often mocked by the others, who didn't want

him to inherit the mill. In fact, he decided he wouldn’t want it anyway.

All three boys set off together, but once they reached the village, the

two clever ones told Hans, the fool, "You might as well stay here; you’ll never

get a horse." Hans, however, followed them, and that night they found a cave

and lay down to sleep. Once Hans was asleep, the two smart boys quietly got

up and left him behind, thinking they were quite clever, but it would soon

backfire.

When morning came and Hans woke up, he found himself in a dark cave.

Looking around, he gasped, "Oh no, where am I?" He scrambled up and climbed

out, stepping into the forest, wondering, "Now that I'm all alone, how on

earth will I find a horse?" Lost in thought, he stumbled upon a friendly little

tabby cat, who kindly asked, "Hans, where are you headed?" "Oh dear, you can’t

help me," he replied. "I know exactly what you want," said the cat. "You wish

for a beautiful horse. Come with me, serve me loyally for seven years, and I will

give you the most stunning horse you’ve ever seen."

"What a strange cat," thought Hans, "but I’ll see if she’s telling the truth."

So the cat led him to her enchanted castle, where all her servants were little kittens.

They scampered around playfully, joyful and lively. At dinner, three of them performed music.

title: The Poor Miller's Boy and the Cat
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One played the bass viol, another the fiddle, and a third blew into a trumpet, puffing up his cheeks.

After dinner, the table was cleared, and the cat said, "Now, Hans, come dance with me."

"No," he replied, "I won’t dance with a cat; I’ve never done that before." "Then take him to bed,"

the cat instructed her kittens. One guided him to his bedroom, one took off his shoes, one removed

his stockings, and finally, one blew out the candle. The next morning, they returned to help him up;

one put on his stockings, one tied his garters, another brought his shoes, one washed him, and one dried

his face with her tail. "That feels nice," Hans said.

Despite his charming surroundings, he had to work for the cat, chopping wood daily with a silver axe.

His wedge and saw were silver too, and his mallet was made of copper. He chopped wood diligently, enjoyed

good meals, but saw no one except the tabby cat and her little helpers. One day, she said, "Go mow my meadow, and

dry the grass," handing him a silver scythe and a golden whetstone, but reminded him to return them.

So, Hans did as he was told, completed the task, and brought the tools and grass back, asking for his reward.

"Not yet," the cat replied, "You must do another task for me. There’s silver timber, a carpenter’s axe, a square,

and everything you need. Use these to build me a small house." After building the little house, Hans claimed he had finished, but still no horse.

Even so, the seven years felt like just six months to him. The cat then asked if he wanted to see her horses. "Yes," Hans said.

She opened the door of the little house to reveal twelve dazzling horses, so shiny that they made his heart leap.

"Now eat and drink, but I won’t give you your horse just yet. In three days, I’ll follow you and deliver it," said the cat.

title: The Poor Miller's Boy and the Cat
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
So Hans set off home, and the cat pointed him in the right direction. However, she never provided him with new clothes, so he wore his old tattered smock.

When Hans got home, the other two apprentices were there too, and they each had a horse, but one was blind and the other lame.

They asked, "Hans, where's your horse?" "It will be here in three days," he replied. They laughed, saying, "Really, foolish Hans, where will you find a horse?"

"It'll be a great one." Hans entered the parlor, but the miller told him he couldn't sit at the table because he looked so shabby.

So they only gave him a bite to eat outside. At night, the other two didn’t even allow him a bed, forcing Hans to crawl into the goose-house to sleep.

In the morning, after the three days had passed, a carriage arrived drawn by six beautifully shining horses.

A servant brought a seventh horse, meant for the poor miller’s boy. A magnificent princess emerged from the carriage, and she was actually the little tabby cat who had

been Hans’s master for the past seven years. She asked the miller, "Where’s the miller's boy, the dunce?" The miller replied,

"We can’t have him here; he’s too ragged and is lying in the goose-house." The princess ordered them to bring him right away.

They fetched Hans, who held his little smock together to cover himself. The servants unpacked beautiful clothes, washed him up and dressed him, making him look more handsome than any king.

Then the princess wanted to see the horses that the other boys had brought. One was blind and the other lame.

She ordered a servant to bring her the seventh horse, and when the miller saw it, he remarked that such a horse had never entered his yard.

"And that's for the third miller's boy," said she. "Then he must inherit the mill," the miller said, but the princess insisted that the horse belonged to Hans and he would keep both his horse and the mill.

She took her loyal Hans and placed him in the coach. They first visited the little house he had built with the silver tools, which had transformed into a grand castle, adorned with gold and silver.

Then she married him, and he became rich, wealthier than he could have ever imagined, ensuring a life of comfort. Finally,

let no one ever say that someone who is considered silly can't ultimately achieve greatness.

title: The Queen Bee
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, two princes set out on a quest for adventure, but they ended up in a wild, chaotic lifestyle that kept them from ever returning home.

The youngest of the three, often called 'Simpleton,' decided to go after his brothers.

When he finally found them, they laughed at him for believing that he could make it through life with his simplicity, especially since they, being much smarter, struggled to get by.

Regardless, the three brothers continued on their journey together and soon arrived at an ant hill.

The older brothers wanted to destroy the hill just to watch the little ants scurry around in fear, carrying their eggs away, but Simpleton said, "Leave them alone; I won’t let you disturb these creatures."

They pressed on and came to a lake where many ducks were swimming.

The two brothers wanted to catch a few ducks for roasting, but Simpleton refused, insisting, "Leave the ducks in peace; I won’t let you kill them."

Eventually, they found a beehive overflowing with honey, and the older brothers planned to set a fire underneath the tree to smoke out the bees and steal the honey.

Once again, Simpleton intervened, saying, "Leave the bees alone; I won’t allow you to burn them."

The three brothers then reached a castle with stone horses standing in the stables, and they couldn’t see anyone around.

They wandered through the halls until they found a door with three locks.

In the center of the door was a small pane, which allowed them to peek inside the room.

Inside, there sat a little gray man at a table.

They called out to him once, then twice, but he didn’t respond. It wasn’t until they called him a third time that he got up, unlocked the door, and came out.

He didn’t say a word but led them to a beautifully set table, where they enjoyed a meal, and afterward, he took them to their sleeping quarters.

The next morning, the little gray man approached the eldest brother, motioned for him to follow, and showed him a stone table with three tasks written on it, which would free the castle from its curse.

title: The Queen Bee
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The first task involved finding the princess's pearls, a thousand of them scattered beneath the moss in the forest.

He had until sunset to gather all of them; if he failed to collect even one, he would be turned to stone.

The eldest brother went out to collect the pearls, but at the end of the day, he had only gathered one hundred, and as a result, the curse was fulfilled, and he turned to stone.

The next day, the second brother took on the challenge, but he fared little better, finding only two hundred pearls, which led to his own transformation into stone.

Finally, it was Simpleton's turn to search for the pearls. Struggling to find them, he sat down on a stone and began to cry.

While he was sitting there, the king of the ants, whom he had once saved, arrived with five thousand ants, and together they quickly gathered all the pearls into a neat pile.

The second task required Simpleton to retrieve the key to the king's daughter's chamber from the lake.

When he arrived at the lake, the ducks he had previously saved swam up to him, dove down, and brought back the key.

The third task was the most challenging; he had to identify the youngest and most beloved of the three sleeping princesses.

The princesses looked identical and could only be recognized by the different sweets they had eaten before sleeping: the eldest had eaten sugar, the second had syrup, and the youngest had honey.

The queen bee, whom Simpleton had protected from the fire, came to help; she tasted the lips of each princess and finally settled on the one who had eaten honey, helping Simpleton identify the right princess.

With the spell broken, everyone was freed from their sleep, and those who had been turned to stone regained their true forms.

Simpleton married the youngest and sweetest princess, and after her father passed away, he became king, while his two brothers were united with their respective princesses.

title: The Raven
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a queen with a little daughter who

was so young that she needed to be carried. One day, the

child was acting out, and no matter how much her mother tried to calm

her, the child just wouldn't quiet down. The queen grew impatient, and as

the ravens flew around the palace, she opened the

window and said, "I wish you could be a raven and fly away, so I could have some peace."

As soon as she spoke those words, the child transformed into a raven and flew

out of her arms and out the window. It soared into a dark forest and

stayed there for a long time, with the parents hearing nothing from their

child.

Then one day, a man was walking through this forest

and heard the raven crying. He followed the sound, and as

he got closer, the bird said, "I am a king's daughter, cursed to be a raven, but you can save me."

"What should I do?" he asked.

She replied, "Continue deeper into the forest, and you'll find a house where an old woman lives.

She will offer you food and drinks, but you must refuse them, or else you'll fall into a sleep, and you won't be able to set me free.

Behind the house, there's a huge pile of tan. You should stand there and wait for me.

For three days, I will arrive every afternoon at two o'clock in a carriage.

On the first day, there will be four white horses, then four chestnut horses, and lastly four black ones.

But if you're asleep when I come, I won't be freed."

The man promised to do everything she asked, but the raven warned him, "Sadly, I know you will not be able to set me free; you'll accept something from the woman."

Still, the man promised again that he wouldn't touch anything.

However, when he entered the house, the old woman approached him and said, "Poor man, you look faint. Come, refresh yourself with some food and drink."

title: The Raven
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"No, I won't eat or drink," replied the man.

But the old woman wouldn't take no for an answer, saying, "If you won’t eat, just have one drink from the glass, just one won’t hurt."

Eventually, he let himself be convinced and took a drink. Shortly

before two o'clock that afternoon, he went into the garden to wait by the tan pile for the raven.

As he stood there, his weariness suddenly overwhelmed him, and he couldn't fight it off.

He lay down for just a moment, determined not to fall asleep.

But hardly had he closed his eyes than he dozed off, falling into such a deep sleep that nothing in the world could wake him.

At two o'clock, the raven arrived with four white horses,

but her heart sank as she realized he was asleep.

When she came to the garden, she saw him lying there, fast asleep on the tan pile.

She climbed down from the carriage, shook him, and called out to him, but he didn’t wake up.

The next day around noon, the old woman came again, bringing him food and drink, but he still refused.

Yet she wouldn’t let him rest and eventually persuaded him to take just one drink.

As the clock approached two o'clock, he went back to the tan pile to wait for the raven again, but suddenly felt an overwhelming weariness.

His legs gave way under him, and he had no choice but to lie down, quickly falling back to sleep.

When the raven arrived with four brown horses, she was again filled with despair, saying, "I know he is asleep."

She approached him, but there he lay, blissfully unaware, and she couldn’t wake him.

The next day, the old woman asked him why he was refusing food and drink, questioning whether he wanted to die.

He replied, "I can’t eat or drink, so I won’t."

But she set a plate of food and a glass of wine in front of him, and the smell was too tempting to resist; he took a hearty sip.

title: The Raven
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When the time came, he went to the tan pile to wait for the king’s daughter,

but he fell asleep again as if he were a stone.

At two o'clock, the raven arrived with four black horses, everything around her dark.

She was devastated, saying, "I know he’s asleep and cannot set me free."

There he was, sound asleep.

She shook him and called out, but he wouldn’t wake.

She placed a loaf of bread beside him, followed by a piece of meat, and a bottle of wine, allowing him to eat and drink to his heart's content, promising that the food would never run out.

Then she took off a gold ring from her finger and placed it on his, her name engraved on it.

Lastly, she left behind a letter explaining what she had given him, assuring him that none of it would ever be less, and in it,

she wrote, "I know that you will never set me free here, but if you are still determined to try, come to the golden castle of Stromberg; I believe you can find it."

After giving him all these treasures,

she climbed back into her carriage and drove off to the golden castle.

When the man woke and realized he had been asleep, he felt a heavy sadness.

"She must have passed by while I was sleeping, and now I haven't freed her."

Suddenly, he noticed the items beside him,

and read the letter detailing what had happened.

He stood up and set off for the golden castle of Stromberg, even though he had no idea where it was.

He wandered the world for a long time, entered a dark forest, and walked for fourteen days without finding an exit.

By evening, he was so exhausted that he lay down in a thicket and fell asleep.

The next day, he continued on his way, but as night fell again and he was about to rest under some bushes, he heard loud howling and crying that kept him awake.

When people typically light candles, he spotted a flickering light and got up to move towards it.

He arrived at a house that looked quite small, dominated by a huge giant standing in front.

title: The Raven
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He thought, if I go in and the giant sees me, it might cost me my life.

But eventually, he mustered his courage and went inside.

When the giant spotted him, he exclaimed, "It’s good you’ve come; I've been hungry for a while, and I’ll eat you for dinner!"

"I'd prefer if you didn’t," the man replied, "I don’t wish to be eaten, but if you’re hungry, I’ve enough here to share with you."

"If that's true," said the giant, "then I won't eat you; I was just going to devour you because I had nothing else."

He sat down at the table, and the man pulled out his magical endless supply of bread, wine, and meat.

The giant was thrilled and ate to his heart’s content.

Then the man said, "Can you help me find the golden castle of Stromberg?"

The giant replied, "I’ll check my map; it has all the towns and villages listed."

He brought out a map from the room and searched for the castle, but it wasn’t there.

"No worries," the giant said, "I have larger maps in my cupboard upstairs; we can look at those."

Unfortunately, it still didn’t show the castle.

The man wanted to leave again, but the giant pleaded, "Wait a few more days until my brother returns with provisions."

When his brother finally returned, they asked about the golden castle.

He replied, "Once I’ve eaten enough, I’ll check the map to find it."

Then they went up to his room, searching through his map, but couldn't find it.

So he pulled out even older maps, and they didn’t stop until they located the golden castle of Stromberg, but it was thousands of miles away.

"How will I ever get there?" the man asked.

The giant said, "I can take you within two hours of the castle, but then I must return to nurse my child."

title: The Raven
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
So the giant carried the man about a hundred leagues from the castle, and then said, "You can walk the rest of the way from here."

He turned back, while the man kept walking day and night until he finally arrived at the golden castle of Stromberg.

It was perched on a glass mountain, and the enchanted maiden was driving in her carriage around the castle before heading inside.

He felt a rush of joy seeing her and tried to climb up to her,

but every time he attempted, he slipped back down the glass.

Frustrated, he thought to himself, I’ll stay down here and wait for her.

He built a hut and lived there for a whole year, watching every day as the princess rode above, but

he could never reach her.

One day, from his hut, he spotted three robbers fighting amongst themselves and shouted, "Hey! What's going on?"

They paused at the sound of his voice, but upon seeing no one, they went back to bickering.

He called out again, "Come on, guys!" They halted once more but, again seeing no one, resumed fighting.

Finally, he yelled for the third time, "Seriously, what are you fighting about?" He decided to check on them.

He inquired why they were beating each other so passionately.

One explained that he found a stick that could open any door he hit it against.

Another claimed he had found a cloak that made him invisible,

and the last boasted about a horse that could run anywhere, even up the glass mountain.

They debated whether they should share their treasures or keep them for themselves.

The man said, "I’ll trade you something for those three items. I may not have money, but I have other valuable things. But first, I need to test if what you say is true."

They let him try the horse, gave him the cloak, and the stick; suddenly, they couldn’t see him anymore.

He hit them playfully and exclaimed, "Now you see what you’ve earned; are you happy?"

Then he rode up the glass mountain, but when he reached the top of the castle, he found the door was shut.

He struck the door with the stick, and it swung open instantly.

He went inside and climbed the stairs until he arrived at the hall where the maiden sat, sipping from a golden goblet.

She couldn’t see him since he wore the cloak.

As he got closer, he took off the ring she'd given him and dropped it into her goblet, making it chime.

She exclaimed, "That’s my ring! The man meant to save me must be here somewhere."

They searched every corner of the castle but couldn’t find him.

Meanwhile, he had slipped out, riding his horse and taking off the cloak.

When they reached the door and saw him, they exclaimed with delight.

He jumped down, gathered the princess in his arms, and she kissed him, saying, "You’ve saved me! Tomorrow, we will celebrate our wedding."

title: The Riddle
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a prince who felt a strong urge to explore the world.

He decided to travel alone, except for his loyal servant who joined him.

One day, they found themselves in a vast forest, and as darkness fell,

they were unable to find a place to stay for the night.

Then, he noticed a girl walking toward a small house, and when

he got closer, he saw that she was young and beautiful.

He asked her, "Excuse me, can my servant and I find a place to sleep for the night in your home?"

"Oh, yes," she replied, her voice tinged with sadness, "you certainly can, but I wouldn’t recommend it."

"Why not?" asked the prince.

The girl sighed and explained, "My stepmother practices dark magic. She doesn’t like strangers."

Realizing he had come upon a witch's dwelling,

the prince, undeterred by fear and unable to walk further in the dark, went in.

Inside, an old woman sat in an armchair by the fire, watching him with her piercing red eyes.

"Good evening," she growled, pretending to be welcoming. "Please, take a seat and rest."

She stirred the fire, where something simmered in a small pot.

The girl warned them to be cautious, advising them not to eat or drink anything because the old woman brewed harmful concoctions.

They quietly slept through the night.

When morning arrived, they prepared to leave, and just as the prince mounted his horse,

the witch called out, "Hold on a moment, I have a parting drink for you."

But while she fetched it, the prince rode off, leaving only the servant behind to tighten the saddle.

When the servant returned to grab the saddle, he found a raven already feasting on the fallen horse.

"Who knows if we can find anything better today?" muttered the servant.

So he killed the raven and took it with him.

They continued through the forest all day, but couldn’t find their way out.

By nightfall, they discovered an inn and decided to stay there.

title: The Riddle
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The servant handed the raven to the innkeeper to prepare for their dinner.

However, they had unwittingly entered a den of murderers, and during the night, twelve of them arrived to kill the travelers and rob them.

Before carrying out their plan, they sat down for supper, joined by the innkeeper and the witch,

and they shared a bowl of soup made from pieces of the raven.

Hardly had they taken a few bites before they collapsed, poisoned from the raven's flesh.

The only one left in the inn was the honest innkeeper's daughter,

who had played no part in their evil deeds.

She opened all the doors for the prince and showed him the treasure.

But the prince insisted that she keep everything; he wanted none of it and rode away with his servant.

After a long journey, they reached a town where a beautiful but proud princess lived.

She had announced that whoever could give her a riddle she couldn't solve would marry her.

However, if she solved it, that man's head would be taken.

She had three days to guess, and her cleverness always led her to the answer well before the time was up.

Nine suitors had already lost their lives this way when the prince arrived and, dazzled by her beauty, decided to try his luck.

He approached her and presented his riddle: "What is it that one slew none, and yet slew twelve?"

She couldn’t figure it out.

She pondered for a long time, but the answer eluded her.

She consulted her riddle books, but it wasn’t there—in short, she had hit a dead end.

In her desperation, she ordered her maid to sneak into the prince’s chamber and eavesdrop on his dreams, thinking he might reveal the riddle.

But the clever servant had already taken his master’s place in bed, and when the maid arrived, he pulled off her cloak and chased her away.

On the second night, the princess sent another maid to try her luck, but the servant did the same, taking her cloak and driving her out.

Believing himself safe on the third night, the prince lay down in his own bed.

Then the princess herself came, wearing a misty-gray cloak, and sat beside him.

Thinking he was asleep, she began to speak, hoping he would answer as many do in their dreams.

But the prince was awake and heard everything.

"One slew none, what is that?" she asked.

He replied, "A raven that fed on a dead, poisoned horse, and died from it."

Then she asked, "And yet slew twelve, what is that?" He said, "That refers to twelve murderers who ate the raven and died."

Once she understood the riddle, she tried to sneak away, but he held her cloak tightly, forcing her to leave it behind.

The next morning, the princess claimed she had solved the riddle and called for twelve judges to present her answer.

But the young prince requested a moment to speak, saying, "She snuck into my room last night and questioned me; otherwise, she wouldn’t have found the answer."

The judges said, "Bring us proof of this."

The servant then produced the three cloaks, and when they saw the misty gray one belonging to the princess,

the judges declared, "Let the cloak be embroidered with gold and silver, and it shall be yours."

title: The Robber Bridegroom
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a miller who had a stunning daughter.

As she grew older, he wanted to make sure she was taken care of and married well.

He thought to himself, if a good suitor comes along, I’ll happily give her to him.

Not long after, a wealthy suitor appeared.

The miller found no faults with him, and he agreed to let his daughter marry him.

However, the daughter wasn’t as fond of him as a girl should be about her fiancé.

She had serious doubts about him.

Whenever she saw him or thought of him, she felt an unsettling fear.

One day, he said to her, "You’re my betrothed, yet I’ve never seen you at my place."

The girl replied, "I don’t even know where you live."

The bridegroom responded, "My house is deep in the dark forest."

She said she couldn’t find her way there.

The bridegroom insisted, "You have to come visit me this Sunday; I’ve already invited the guests.

To help you find your way, I’ll sprinkle ashes so you can follow the path better."

When Sunday arrived and the girl had to leave, she felt really anxious, though she didn’t quite know why.

To mark her path, she filled her pockets with peas and lentils.

At the entrance of the forest, she followed the ashes, and with every few steps, she dropped a handful of peas.

She walked nearly all day until she reached the darkest part of the forest, where a lonely house stood.

It looked eerie and uninviting.

She stepped inside but found no one there; an eerie silence surrounded her.

"Turn back, turn back, dear young maiden," a voice called out.

"You’re entering a murderer’s house."

The young woman glanced up and realized the voice came from a bird in a cage on the wall.

The bird repeated, "Turn back, turn back, dear young maiden; it’s a murderer’s house you’re entering."

Despite the warning, the girl explored further from room to room, but the house was absolutely empty.

title: The Robber Bridegroom
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Finally, she found the cellar, where a very old woman sat, her head shaking continuously.

"Can you tell me if my fiancé lives here?" asked the girl.

"Oh dear child," replied the old woman, "you've entered a murderer’s den.

You think you’re about to get married, but death awaits you.

Look, I’ve got a huge kettle boiling; when they catch you, they’ll slice you apart and eat you because they’re cannibals."

"If I don’t help you, you’ll end up as their meal," she said.

The old woman quickly ushered her behind a large barrel.

"Stay completely quiet and don’t move, or you’re done for.

When the robbers are asleep tonight, we’ll escape; I’ve been waiting for this chance for a long time."

Just then, the ruthless gang came home, dragging another young girl with them.

They were drunk and ignored her cries.

They poured three glasses of wine for her: one white, one red, and one yellow, and her heart broke.

They ripped her clothes off, laid her on a table, sliced her delicate body into pieces, and sprinkled salt on it.

The poor girl behind the barrel quaked in terror as she witnessed what fate awaited her.

One of the robbers noticed a gold ring on the dead girl's finger.

When it wouldn’t come off, he took an axe and chopped the finger off, but it flew through the air and landed in the bride's bosom.

The robber, holding a candle, tried to find it but couldn’t.

Then another robber suggested, "Have you checked behind the large barrel?"

But the old woman interrupted, "Come eat something first; don't waste time looking until morning—the finger won't run away."

The thieves agreed with her, gave up their search, and sat down to eat.

The old woman mixed a sleeping potion into their wine, and soon they were snoring on the floor.

Once the bride heard they were asleep, she carefully stepped over them.

title: The Robber Bridegroom
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She was terrified she'd wake one of them, but thankfully, God helped her to pass safely.

The old woman joined her, unlocked the door, and together they rushed out of the murderer's lair as fast as they could.

The wind had blown away the ashes, but the peas and lentils had sprouted and lit the way in the moonlight.

They walked all night until dawn finally came, and they reached the mill where the girl told her father everything that had happened.

On the wedding day, the bridegroom showed up, and the miller had invited all his family and friends.

As they sat at the table, everyone was encouraged to share stories.

The bride remained silent.

The bridegroom then said to her, "Come on, my sweetheart, don’t you have anything to share?"

She responded, "I’ll share a dream I had.

I was wandering alone through a forest and finally came upon a house that was completely empty.

On the wall, a bird in a cage cried, 'Turn back, turn back, young maiden dear; you’re entering a murderer’s house.'

It repeated this warning again.

"Darling, I only dreamt this."

So I explored all the rooms, and they felt so eerie and lifeless.

Eventually, I descended to the cellar, where I found a very old woman whose head shook.

I asked her if my fiancé lived there.

She said, 'Oh dear child, you've entered a murderer’s den.

Your fiancé does live here, but he will cut you into pieces and eat you.'

"Darling, I only dreamed this."

The old woman hid me behind a big barrel, and barely had I hidden when the robbers arrived, bringing another girl with them.

They poured three types of wine for her: white, red, and yellow, and her heart shattered.

"Darling, I only dreamed this."

Then they stripped her beautiful clothes off and chopped her lovely body into pieces, sprinkling salt over it.

"Darling, I only dreamed this."

One of the robbers noticed that the girl still had a ring on her finger.

He couldn’t get it off, so he took an axe and chopped off her finger, but it flew into the air and landed right in my bosom.

Here’s the finger with the ring!" With that, she pulled it out.

The robber turned pale as ash during her story; he jumped up to escape, but the guests restrained him and turned him over to the authorities.

In the end, he and his whole gang were executed for their terrible crimes.

title: The Rose
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a poor woman with two children. The youngest child, a girl, had to venture into the forest every day to gather firewood.

One day, after she had wandered quite far in search of wood, a strong little child appeared and eagerly helped her collect the wood and carry it back home.

Before she knew it, the mysterious child had vanished. The girl rushed home and told her mother about this strange helper.

At first, her mother didn’t believe her story. Eventually, however, the girl came home with a beautiful rose and explained that the magical child had given it to her and promised to return when the rose bloomed.

Her mother placed the rose in a vase of water. One morning, however, the girl couldn’t get out of bed.

Worried, her mother went to her room and found that her child had passed away, although she looked so peaceful and happy.

On that same morning, the rose was finally in full bloom.

title: The Sea-Hare
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a princess who lived high up in her castle,

in an apartment with twelve windows.

These windows allowed her to see in every direction, and whenever she climbed up to look around,

she could survey her entire kingdom.

When gazing out of the first window, her vision was sharper than anyone else's.

From the second window, she could see even better.

From the third window, her view was even clearer, and so it continued,

until the twelfth window, through which she could see everything above and below the ground,

with no secrets hidden from her.

However, since she was proud and didn’t want to be ruled by anyone, she declared that her husband must be someone who could hide from her so well,

that she wouldn't be able to find him at all.

Anyone who tried and was discovered would lose their head and have it displayed on a post.

Ninety-seven posts topped with the heads of dead men were already standing before the castle, and for a long time, no one had dared to try.

The princess was thrilled, thinking to herself, now I can live freely forever.

Then three brothers came to her, eager to test their luck.

The eldest believed he could hide safely in a lime pit, but she spotted him immediately from the first window, made him come out, and had his head chopped off.

The second brother hid in the palace cellar, but she also saw him from the first window, sealing his fate.

His head was added to the ninety-ninth post.

Then the youngest brother approached her, asking for a day to think it over and pleaded for her to forgive him if she found him twice; if he failed the third time, he would accept his fate.

Because he was so handsome and begged so earnestly, she agreed to his request.

The next day, he pondered for a long time about where to hide, but nothing came to mind.

Frustrated, he grabbed his gun and went hunting.

He spotted a raven, aimed carefully, and was just about to shoot when the bird called out, "Don’t shoot! I will reward you."

title: The Sea-Hare
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He lowered his gun and continued on until he reached a lake, where he caught sight of a large fish that had come to the surface.

When he aimed at it, the fish cried, "Don't shoot, and I'll reward you!"

He let it dive down again, moved along, and then met a limping fox.

He fired but missed, and the fox said, "You would be much better off coming here to pull the thorn out of my foot."

So, he helped the fox, but when he went to kill it and skin it, the fox said, "Wait! I will reward you."

The young man let it go.

The next day, he faced the daunting task of hiding again, and no matter how hard he thought about it, he couldn’t think of a place.

He headed to the forest to find the raven and said, "I spared your life, so now tell me where I can hide to avoid being seen by the princess."

The raven hung its head, pondering for a long time before finally croaking, "I have an idea."

It fetched an egg from its nest, split it in half, placed the young man inside, sealed it back up, and perched on top.

When the princess went to the first window, she couldn't see him, and the same with the others.

But when she reached the eleventh window, she spotted him.

She ordered the raven to be shot and the egg brought to her.

When the egg was broken, the young man had to come out.

She said, "This time you’re let off, but if you don’t do better tomorrow, you’re in big trouble."

The next day, he went to the lake, called out to the fish, and said, "I let you go, now tell me where to hide so the princess won't see me."

The fish thought for a moment, then exclaimed, "I’ve got it! I’ll swallow you!"

Then it swallowed him and sank to the lake's depths.

title: The Sea-Hare
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The princess checked her windows and couldn’t see him even from the eleventh one, which made her anxious.

Eventually, she looked through the twelfth window and finally spotted him.

She commanded that the fish be caught and killed, and soon the youth appeared.

You can only imagine how he felt.

She said, "Twice you've escaped, but the third time, your head will definitely go on the hundredth post."

On the final day, he wandered into the countryside, heart heavy, and ran into the fox.

"You know all about hiding places," he said. "I spared your life, so now what can you suggest so the princess doesn't find me?"

The fox seemed thoughtful and finally replied, "That’s a tough one," but then exclaimed, "I’ve got it!"

He led the young man to a spring, dipped himself in it, and emerged as a market stall-keeper selling animals.

The young man also dipped himself in the water and transformed into a small sea-hare.

The merchant went into town, showcasing the adorable creature, gathering a crowd.

Eventually, the princess arrived, loved the sea-hare so much that she bought it,

paying the merchant a handsome amount.

Before handing it over, he whispered to the sea-hare, "When the princess goes to the window, quietly crawl under her hair."

Finally, the moment came for the princess to search.

She checked each window in order, from the first to the eleventh, and saw nothing.

When she didn’t find him at the twelfth window, she was anxious and angry.

She slammed it shut so hard that the glass shattered into countless pieces, shaking the castle.

Returning to her room, she felt the sea-hare nestled in her hair.

She grabbed it and flung it to the ground, yelling, "Get out of my sight!"

The sea-hare scurried to the merchant, and together they rushed to the spring.

They jumped in and regained their true forms.

The young man thanked the fox and said, "The raven and fish were fools compared to you; you really know how to handle things."

He made his way back to the palace, where the princess was already waiting, resigned to her fate.

They had a royal wedding, and he became king, ruling over the entire kingdom.

He never told her how he had managed to hide the third time or who had helped him, so she believed he had done it all on his own,

and she respected him greatly, thinking to herself, he can do even more than I can.

title: The Seven Ravens
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a man with seven sons, but despite his wish for a daughter, he had none.

Eventually, his wife gave him hope when she became pregnant again.

When the baby finally arrived, it was a girl.

The joy was overwhelming, but the baby was frail and tiny, needing to be baptized in secret because of her weakness.

The father rushed one of the boys to the spring to collect water for the baptism.

All six brothers went with him, excited to be the first to fill the jug, but in their eagerness, the jug fell into the well.

They stood there, unsure of what to do, and none of them had the courage to go home.

As time passed and they still didn’t return, the father grew impatient and mused aloud, 'Those naughty boys must have forgotten while playing some game.'

He started to worry that his little girl might not be baptized in time and, in his frustration, exclaimed, 'I wish the boys would turn into ravens.'

No sooner had he said the words than he heard a flurry of wings overhead; he looked up to see seven black ravens soaring away.

The parents couldn’t reverse the curse, and while they mourned the loss of their seven sons, they took some comfort in their precious daughter, who began to thrive and grow more beautiful each day.

For a long time, she didn’t realize she had brothers, as her parents were careful not to mention them in front of her.

However, one day, she overheard some people discussing her, saying she was lovely but was also to blame for the tragedy that struck her brothers.

This troubled her deeply, and she approached her parents to ask if she had brothers and what had happened to them.

Unable to keep the secret any longer, her parents revealed that her brothers had been turned into ravens by a curse and that her birth was innocent.

Still, the girl couldn’t shake the guilt, and every day she felt the weight of needing to save her brothers.

Determined, she set out secretly into the wide world to find and rescue them, no matter what it took.

title: The Seven Ravens
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
All she brought with her was a small ring from her parents, a loaf of bread for hunger, a pitcher of water for thirst, and a tiny chair to rest on.

With resolve, she journeyed far and wide to the very ends of the world.

First, she reached the sun, which was scorching hot and dangerous, known for consuming little children.

She quickly fled from it and ran to the moon, but it was far too cold and wicked.

When the moon noticed her, it ominously declared, 'I smell human flesh.'

Terrified, she hurried away and arrived at the stars, which were kind and welcoming, each sitting on its own little chair.

Then the morning star offered her a chicken's drumstick and said, 'Without this, you can’t open the glass mountain.'

Grateful, she accepted the drumstick, carefully wrapped it in cloth, and continued on her journey until she reached the glass mountain.

The door was shut tight, and she decided to use the drumstick.

But when she unwrapped it, she found it was empty, and she had lost the morning star's gift.

What was she to do now? Without a key, she couldn’t get into the glass mountain to rescue her brothers.

In desperation, she took a knife, cut off one of her fingers, and used it to unlock the door.

Once inside, a little dwarf approached her and asked, 'What are you looking for?'

'I’m searching for my brothers, the seven ravens,' she replied.

'The lord ravens aren't here, but if you wait, you can come inside,' the dwarf said.

He brought in dinner for the ravens on seven little plates and filled seven little glasses.

The girl took a bite from each plate and a sip from each glass, but in the last glass, she accidentally dropped the ring she had brought.

Suddenly, she heard the sound of wings fluttering and a rush of air when the dwarf announced, 'The lord ravens are flying home.'

As they arrived, the ravens wanted to eat and drink but noticed their plates and glasses were disturbed.

'Who has eaten from my plate?' one raven asked, and another cried, 'Who has drunk from my glass?'

'Someone has used a human mouth!' they exclaimed.

When the seventh raven reached the bottom of his glass, the ring rolled against his lips.

He recognized it as the ring belonging to their parents and exclaimed, 'May our sister be here; then we will be free.'

Upon hearing this, the girl stepped from behind the door, and instantly, the ravens transformed back into their human forms.

They hugged and kissed each other with joy.

title: The Seven Swabians
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once, seven Swabians set out together. The first was Master Schulz,

the second was Jackli, the third was Marli, the fourth,

Jergli, the fifth was Michal, the sixth was Hans, and the seventh,

Veitli. All seven decided to travel the world in search of adventures and heroic deeds.

To ensure their safety and arm themselves, they came up with the idea of having one long and strong spear crafted for them.

Each of them held onto the spear together. Leading the way was Master Schulz, and all the others followed in a line, with Veitli at the back.

One evening in the hay month, after walking quite a distance with much further to go before reaching the village where they planned to stay, they found themselves in a meadow.

As twilight fell, a large beetle or hornet suddenly flew past them from behind a bush, buzzing ominously.

Master Schulz was so frightened that he almost dropped the spear, and a cold sweat broke out all over him.

"Listen!" he cried to his friends, "Good heavens, I hear a drum!"

Jackli, who was behind him holding the spear, caught a whiff of something and said, "Something’s definitely going on; I smell gunpowder and a burning fuse."

At those words, Master Schulz took off running, leaping over a hedge. However, he happened to jump right onto the teeth of a rake that had been left behind after hay-making.

The rake handle hit him in the face and gave him a hard knock. "Oh no! Oh no!" screamed Master Schulz. "Take me prisoner! I surrender, I surrender!"

The other six jumped over, one after the other, crying out, "If you surrender, I surrender too! If you surrender, I surrender too!"

Then, realizing there wasn’t actually an enemy to capture them, they saw they had made a mistake, and to avoid being seen as fools, they all promised to keep quiet about it until someone accidentally mentioned it.

They continued their journey. The second danger they faced was nothing compared to the first.

Days later, their path led them through a fallow field where a hare was lounging in the sun.

title: The Seven Swabians
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
With its ears perked up and its big, glassy eyes wide open, the hare terrified all of them.

They huddled together, trying to figure out what to do. They knew that if they ran, the beast would chase and swallow them whole.

So they agreed that they must go through a brave struggle.

"Boldly ventured is halfway won," said one, as all seven grabbed the spear, with Master Schulz in front and Veitli at the back.

Master Schulz tried to hold the spear back, but Veitli had found some courage and wanted to charge ahead, declaring, "In every Swabian’s name, strike true, or I hope you become lame!"

But Hans knew how to respond and said, "Thunder and lightning, it's easy to talk, but for dragon-hunting, you’re always late!"

"Nothing's missing, not even a hair; the devil himself must be lurking there!"

Then it was Jergli’s turn, and he chimed in, "If it’s not the devil, it’s got to be his mother, or at least his devilish stepbrother!"

Marli then had a bright idea and called out to Veitli, "Advance, Veitli, advance, advance, and I'll hold the lance from behind."

Veitli, however, didn't move, and Jackli insisted, "It’s Schulz's place to lead; no one else deserves that honor!"

Master Schulz gathered his courage and said firmly, "Then let’s boldly charge into battle; this will show our valor and strength!"

So they all charged at the 'dragon.' Master Schulz crossed himself and prayed for help, but as they got closer, he shouted in sheer panic, "Oh no, oh no, ho, ho, ho!"

This startled the hare, which sprinted away in fright.

When Master Schulz saw it fleeing, he cried out in excitement, "Quick, Veitli! Look there! The monster’s just a hare!"

But the Swabian friends continued searching for more adventures and arrived at the Moselle, a lush, calm, deep river with few bridges. In many places, people had to cross it using boats.

Since the seven Swabians were unaware, they shouted to a man working on the other side of the river to ask how to cross.

The distance and their way of speaking confused the man, and he responded, "What? What?" in the local dialect.

Master Schulz figured he was saying, "Wade through the water," so as the first one, he stepped into the Moselle.

It didn’t take long before he sank into the mud and swirling depths, while his hat was blown across the river by the wind. A frog landed next to it and croaked, "Wat, wat, wat."

The other six on the opposite side heard the croaking and exclaimed, "Oh no, friends! Master Schulz is calling us! If he can wade across, why can't we?"

So they all jumped into the water in a rush and drowned, leading to one frog costing the lives of all six, and none of the Swabian allies ever made it home again.

title: The Shepherd Boy
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a shepherd boy whose reputation spread far and wide.

He became well-known for his clever answers to all kinds of questions.

The king of the land caught wind of this, but he was skeptical and decided to summon the boy.

The king then said to him, 'If you can answer three questions I ask, I will consider you my own child, and you can live with me in my royal palace.'

The boy asked, 'What are the three questions?'

'The first is, how many drops of water are in the ocean?' the king said.

The shepherd boy replied, 'Lord king, if you dam up all the rivers on Earth so not a single drop flows into the sea until I’ve counted them, then I can tell you how many drops are in the ocean.'

'The next question is, how many stars are there in the sky?' the king continued.

The shepherd boy said, 'Just give me a large sheet of white paper.'

Then he made so many tiny points on it that they were nearly impossible to distinguish, anyone trying to count them would likely lose their sight.

'There are as many stars in the sky as there are points on this paper. Just try counting them,' he said.

But no one could manage it.

'The third question is, how many seconds of time are there in eternity?' the king asked.

The shepherd boy replied, 'In Lower Pomerania, there’s a diamond mountain that’s two miles high, two miles wide, and two miles deep.'

'Every hundred years, a little bird comes to sharpen its beak on it. When the entire mountain is worn away, that will mark the end of the first second of eternity.'

'You’ve answered the three questions like a wise man,' the king said, 'and from now on, you will live with me in my royal palace, and I will consider you my own child.'

title: The Shoes That Were Danced to Pieces
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a king who had twelve daughters, each more beautiful than the last.

They all shared a big room where their beds were lined up side by side, and every night the king would lock the door tight.

But in the mornings, when he unlocked the door, he would find their shoes completely worn out from dancing, and no one could figure out how it happened.

So the king announced that whoever could discover where his daughters danced at night would get to choose one of them as his wife and become king after him.

However, if anyone failed to solve the mystery within three days and nights, they would lose their life.

Before long, a prince arrived and volunteered for the challenge. He was welcomed warmly, and in the evening, he was shown to a room next to the princesses' sleeping quarters.

His bed was placed there so he could see where they went dancing. To ensure they couldn’t sneak away, the door to their room was left open.

But the prince felt his eyelids grow heavier and heavier, and soon he fell asleep.

When he awoke the next morning, all twelve princesses had gone dancing again because their shoes were full of holes.

This pattern continued for the next two nights, and his head felt heavier than before.

Many others came after him, trying the same challenge, but all lost their lives.

Then, a poor soldier who could no longer serve due to a wound found his way to the king's town.

He met an old woman who asked him where he was headed.

"I don’t really know," he replied, jokingly adding, "I thought I’d try to find out where the princesses dance their shoes to pieces and become king myself."

"That’s not too hard," said the old woman. "Just avoid drinking the wine that will be brought to you at night, and pretend to be fast asleep."

She then handed him a cloak, saying, "If you wear this, you’ll be invisible, and you can quietly follow the twelve princesses."

With her wise advice, the soldier gained confidence, went to the king, and presented himself as a suitor.

title: The Shoes That Were Danced to Pieces
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He was treated as nicely as the others, receiving royal garments.

That evening, he was taken to the antechamber, and just before bedtime, the eldest princess came in with a cup of wine.

But he had tied a sponge under his chin, letting the wine run into it without taking a sip.

Then he lay down, and to make it sound convincing, he began to snore as if in a deep sleep.

The twelve princesses heard him and laughed. The eldest said, "He might as well have saved himself."

With that, they got up, opened wardrobes and cupboards, and pulled out beautiful dresses, excitedly dressing up in front of the mirrors.

But the youngest princess said, "I don't know how to explain it, but I feel uneasy. Something bad must be about to happen to us."

"You're just being silly and scared," the eldest replied. "Have you forgotten how many princes have come here in vain? I hardly needed to give the soldier a sleeping-draught—he wouldn't have woken up anyway."

Once they were ready, they checked on the soldier, but he had his eyes shut tight and wasn’t moving at all, so they felt safe.

The oldest princess then went to her bed and tapped it, causing it to sink into the ground, and one by one they slipped down through the opening, with the eldest going first.

The soldier, who had been watching everything, didn’t wait any longer. He put on his cloak and followed the youngest princess.

Halfway down the steps, he accidentally stepped on her dress. Startled, she shouted, "What was that? Who's pulling on my dress?"

"Stop being so silly," the eldest said. "You must have caught it on something."

They made it all the way down, and found themselves in a beautiful avenue of trees, with silver leaves that shone and sparkled.

The soldier thought, "I have to take something as proof," so he snapped off a twig. The tree cracked loudly.

The youngest princess gasped. "Something’s wrong! Did you hear that noise?"

title: The Shoes That Were Danced to Pieces
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But the eldest dismissed her, saying, "It’s just a gun that fired in joy because we got rid of our prince so quickly."

They walked on into another avenue lined with golden leaves, and finally reached a third avenue where the leaves were bright as diamonds. He picked twigs from each, making a loud crack each time.

The youngest jumped back in fear, but the eldest insisted they were just celebratory shots.

They continued on and arrived at a grand lake with twelve little boats, each carrying a handsome prince who eagerly awaited the twelve princesses.

Each prince took a princess with him, but the soldier sat beside the youngest.

Her prince remarked, "I wonder why the boat feels so heavy today. I’ll have to row hard to get us across."

"Isn't it just the warm weather?" responded the youngest. "I feel warm as well."

Across the lake stood a magnificent, brightly-lit castle, where they could hear the joyful sounds of trumpets and drums.

They rowed over, entered the castle, and each prince danced with his beloved.

The soldier danced along invisibly, and when one princess held a cup of wine, he drank it all, leaving her cup empty when she tried to drink.

This startled the youngest princess, but the eldest hushed her once more.

They danced until 3 AM, by which time all the shoes had been so worn down that they had to stop.

The princes rowed them back across the lake, and this time the soldier sat beside the youngest again.

On the shore, they bid farewell to their princes, promising to return the next night.

As they made their way back to the steps, the soldier dashed ahead and jumped into his bed. When the twelve princesses finally came up, tired and slow, they found him snoring loudly.

They sighed, saying, "As far as he’s concerned, we’re safe." They changed out of their lovely dresses, tucked them away, and hid their worn-out shoes beneath the bed.

The next morning, the soldier was determined to stay silent and witness the magical happenings, so he joined the princesses for a second and third night.

Each night, it was just like the last, with them dancing until their shoes were completely ruined.

But on the third night, he brought back a cup as a keepsake.

When the time came for him to answer the king, he took the three twigs and the cup and went to see him.

The twelve princesses stood quietly behind the door, eavesdropping.

When the king asked, "Where have my twelve daughters danced their shoes to pieces at night?" he replied, "In an underground castle with twelve princes," and explained everything, showing the tokens.

The king called for his daughters and asked if the soldier had told the truth. Seeing they were caught, they had to confess.

Then the king asked which princess he wanted to marry.

He said, "I’m no longer young, so I’d like to marry the eldest."

The wedding took place that very day, and the kingdom was promised to him after the king’s death.

However, the princes were enchanted as many days as the princesses had danced at night.

title: The Shroud
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a mother with a seven-year-old son.

He was so handsome and lovable that everyone who saw him couldn’t help but like him.

She adored him more than anything else in the world.

But one day, he suddenly became ill, and soon after, God took him away.

The mother was heartbroken and couldn’t find comfort, crying both day and night.

After he was buried, the child began to appear at night in the spots where he had played and laughed before.

Whenever the mother cried, he would cry too, and as the morning light broke, he would vanish.

One night, since the mother wouldn’t stop crying, the child appeared wearing the little white shroud he had been buried in.

With flowers adorning his head, he stood at the foot of her bed and said, "Oh, Mom, please stop crying, or I won’t be able to sleep in my coffin because your tears keep my shroud wet."

Hearing this made the mother feel afraid, and she stopped her tears.

That same night, the child returned, holding a small light, and said, "Look, Mom, my shroud is almost dry, and I can finally rest in my grave."

Then the mother entrusted her sorrow to God, accepting it quietly and patiently.

After that, the child did not come back and peacefully slept in his little bed beneath the earth.

title: The Singing Bone
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
In a certain country, there was once a lot of grief over a

wild boar that destroyed farmers’ fields, killed cattle,

and attacked people with his tusks. The king promised

a big reward to anyone who could rid the land of this pest,

but the beast was so large and powerful that no one dared to approach

the forest where it lived. Finally, the king announced

that anyone who captured or killed the wild boar would receive

Now, in this country lived two brothers, sons of a poor man,

who decided they would take on this dangerous

challenge. The older brother, who was cunning and clever, did so out of pride,

while the younger brother, who was naive and gentle, acted from kindness.

The king advised them that to increase their chances of finding

the beast, they should enter the forest from opposite sides. So

the elder entered from the west, and the younger from the east.

Soon after the younger brother went in, a small man approached

him. He was holding a black spear and said, "I give you this spear because your heart is pure and good. With this,

you can bravely confront the wild boar, and it will not harm you."

The younger brother thanked the little man, picked up the spear, and continued on.

Not long after, he spotted the beast charging at him. He pointed

the spear toward it, and in its blind rage, it ran headlong into it, its heart split in two. Then he hoisted the

monster onto his back and started heading home to the king.

As he emerged from the forest, he found a house at the entrance where people were joyfully drinking wine and

dancing. His older brother had gone inside, thinking that since the boar wouldn’t escape,

he could drink until he felt bold. But when he saw his younger brother coming out

of the woods carrying the prize, his envious and wicked heart couldn't find peace. He shouted to him, "Come in, dear brother, and rest a bit with a drink!"

The younger brother, unaware of any malice, joyfully went inside and told him all about the

kind little man who had given him the spear that helped him defeat the boar.

title: The Singing Bone
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The older brother kept him there until evening, and then they left together. When they reached a

bridge over a brook, the older brother let the younger cross first, and as he was halfway over, he pushed him from behind,

causing him to fall to his death. He buried him beneath the bridge, took

the boar, and brought it to the king, pretending he was the one who had

killed it, which earned him the king's daughter as his wife.

When the younger brother didn’t return, he claimed the boar

must have killed him, and everyone believed it.

But as nothing stays hidden from God, this wicked act also came to light.

Years later, a shepherd was driving his herd across the

bridge when he noticed a tiny white bone in the sand. He thought it would make a perfect mouthpiece,

so he climbed down, picked it up, and crafted a mouthpiece for his horn. But when he blew into it for the first time,

he was astonished when the bone began to speak by itself,

"Ah, friend, you've blown into my bone.

For long have I rested by the water,

my brother killed me over the boar,

and took the king's young daughter for his wife."

"What an amazing horn," the shepherd marveled. "It sings on its own! I need to show it to my lord the king." And when he arrived at the king's palace with it,

the horn sang its song once more. The king understood everything,

and ordered the ground beneath the bridge to be dug up, revealing the entire skeleton of the murdered brother.

The evil brother couldn’t deny his crime, and he was thrown into a sack and drowned. However, the bones of the murdered

man were given a proper burial in a beautiful tomb in the churchyard.

title: The Singing, Soaring Lark
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a man preparing to embark on a long journey.

Before he left, he asked his three daughters what he should bring back for them.

The eldest asked for pearls, the second wanted diamonds, but the youngest said, 'Dear father, I would really love a singing, soaring lark.'

The father replied, 'Yes, if I can find one, you will have it.' He kissed all three daughters and set off.

When it was time for him to return home, he had pearl and diamonds for his two eldest daughters.

However, he had searched everywhere for the singing, soaring lark for his youngest without success, which made him very sad because she was his favorite.

As he traveled, he passed through a forest where he came across a magnificent castle.

Nearby, he noticed a tree, and at the very top, there it was—a singing, soaring lark.

"Aha, you couldn’t have arrived at a better time!" he exclaimed, delighted, and called for his servant to climb up and catch the little bird.

But just as they approached the tree, a lion leaped out from beneath, shook himself, and roared with such force that the leaves trembled.

"Whoever tries to take my singing, soaring lark," he called, "I will devour!"

The man said, "I didn’t realize the bird belonged to you. I'll make it up to you and offer a large sum of money to spare my life."

The lion replied, "Nothing will save you unless you promise to give me the first thing you meet when you return home. If you agree, I'll let you go and you'll get the bird for your daughter too."

The man hesitated, saying, "That might be my youngest daughter, who loves me best and always runs to greet me when I return."

However, the servant, frightened, objected, "Why should it be your daughter? It could just as likely be a cat or a dog!"
