Convinced, the man agreed, took the singing, soaring lark, and promised to give the lion whatever met him first.

title: The Singing, Soaring Lark
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
As soon as he got home and stepped through the door, the first person to rush toward him was none other than his youngest and dearest daughter.

She ran up, kissed him, and when she saw the singing, soaring lark, she was overjoyed.

But the father couldn't rejoice; he began to cry and said, "My dearest child, I bought this little bird for you, but in exchange, I've promised you to a fierce lion who will tear you apart!"

He explained everything just as it had happened and begged her not to go, no matter what.

Yet she comforted him, saying, "Dear father, your promise must be honored. I'll go and try to soften the lion's heart so I can come back safely."

The next morning, she found her way marked and bravely ventured into the forest.

But the lion was an enchanted prince, cursed to be a lion by day while his people were also lions.

At night, however, they transformed back into humans.

Upon her arrival, she was warmly welcomed and taken into the castle.

When night fell, the lion turned into a handsome man, and soon their wedding was celebrated with great style.

They lived happily together, staying awake at night and sleeping during the day.

One day, he said, "Tomorrow, there's a feast at your father's house because your eldest sister is getting married. If you want to go, my lions will take you there."

She replied, "Yes, I’d love to see my father again," and agreed to go accompanied by the lions.

Everyone rejoiced when she arrived, as they had thought she had been killed by the lion and was long gone.

She shared stories about her handsome husband and how happy she was, staying with them throughout the celebration.

When it was time for the second daughter’s wedding, she insisted to the lion, "This time, I will not go alone. You need to come with me."

The lion cautioned her, saying that it was too dangerous; a flame from a burning candle would change him back into a dove, and he would have to fly around with doves for seven years.

title: The Singing, Soaring Lark
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She responded, "Oh, please come! I will protect you from all light!"

They left together, taking along their little ones, and she had a room built that was so strong and thick that no light could seep through.

This was where he would hide when the candles were lit for the wedding feast.

But the door was made from green wood that warped, leaving a tiny crack no one noticed.

The wedding was grand, but as the procession returned from the church and passed by this room, a beam of light hit him, transforming him instantly.

When she entered to look for him, she only found a white dove sitting there.

The dove said, "I must fly the world for seven years, but every seventh step you take, I will drop a drop of red blood and a white feather. Follow these, and you can rescue me."

With that, the dove flew out the door, and she followed, collecting the drops and feathers that marked their path.

She pressed on, venturing further and further into the world, never stopping to rest, but as seven years slipped by, she grew hopeful they would be reunited.

Yet, they were still far apart.

One day, as she went onward, no feather or drop appeared, and when she looked up, the dove had vanished.

Feeling alone, she climbed up to the sun and asked, "You shine in every corner, have you seen a white dove?"

"No," the sun replied, "but take this casket; open it in your greatest need."

She thanked the sun and continued on until night fell and the moon arrived.

She then asked the moon, "You shine all night, have you seen a white dove?"

"No," said the moon, "but here is an egg; break it during your time of need."

Grateful, she journeyed further until the night wind brushed against her.

She asked it, "You sweep over every tree; have you seen a white dove?"

"No," the night wind replied, "but I'll consult the other winds; perhaps they have seen it."

title: The Singing, Soaring Lark
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The east wind and west wind came but had seen nothing, while the south wind said, "I’ve seen the white dove. It flew to the Red Sea, where it turned back into a lion, as the seven years have passed.

He is fighting a dragon—who is actually an enchanted princess!"

The night wind advised her, "Go to the Red Sea. Count the tall reeds on the right bank, break off the eleventh, and strike the dragon with it—this will allow the lion to defeat it, and they'll regain their human forms.

Once this is done, look for the griffin by the Red Sea, and swing yourself onto its back.

The bird will carry you home. Here's a nut; when you're above the center of the sea, let it fall. A tall nut-tree will grow, and the griffin can rest on it; if it can’t, it won’t be strong enough to carry you across.

And don’t forget the nut, or you’ll fall!"

So she followed the night wind's directions and found everything just as instructed.

She counted the reeds by the shore, cut off the eleventh, and struck the dragon, allowing the lion to defeat it.

Instantly, both returned to their human shapes again.

But when the princess, who had been cursed as a dragon, was freed, she took the young man’s arm, got on the griffin, and flew away.

The poor girl, who had journeyed so far, was left behind once more.

She sat down and cried, but eventually, she found the strength to say, "Still, I will go as far as the wind blows and until the rooster crows, until I find him."

She set off on long journeys until she arrived at the castle where they were together.

There, she learned they were about to have a wedding feast.

She thought, "God will help me still," and opened the casket the sun had given her.

Inside lay a dress as radiant as the sun.

She wore it and entered the castle, where everyone, including the bride, gazed at her in awe.

title: The Singing, Soaring Lark
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The dress caught the bride's eye, and she asked if it was for sale.

"Not for money or land," the girl replied, "but for flesh and blood, let me spend a night in the chamber where the bridegroom sleeps."

The bride agreed, wanting the dress desperately, but instructed the page to give the prince a sleeping potion.

That night, when the prince was fast asleep, she was led into the room.

She sat on the bed and said, "I’ve followed you for seven years, consulted the sun, the moon, and the winds, and helped you against the dragon. Will you forget me?"

But the prince slept so soundly, he thought it was just the wind whistling through the trees.

The next day, she was sent away and had to return the golden dress.

Disheartened, she went to a meadow and wept.

As she sat there, she remembered the egg the moon had gifted her.

She opened it, and from it came a clucking hen with twelve golden chicks, the most beautiful sight.

She got up and led them through the meadow until the bride glanced out the window.

Delighted by the little chicks, she rushed down and asked if they were for sale.

"Not for money or land, but for flesh and blood, let me sleep another night in the chamber where the bridegroom sleeps."

The bride agreed, scheming to cheat her again as she had before.

But that night, the prince asked the page, "What was that rustling sound in the night?"

The page explained everything, saying he had to give the prince a sleep potion because a poor girl had secretly entered the chamber.

The prince commanded, "Pour the potion by the bedside."

At night, she was brought back in again, and as she told him her tales of sorrow, he recognized her voice.

He sprang up and exclaimed, "At last, I’m free! I was trapped in a dream, bewitched by that strange princess, forced to forget you, but now God has set me free!"

They quietly left the castle at night, fearing the sorcerer who was the princess's father.

They climbed onto the griffin, which carried them across the Red Sea.

In the middle of the sea, she dropped the nut.

Immediately, a tall nut-tree sprouted, allowing the bird to rest.

Once rested, the griffin flew them home, where they found their child, now tall and beautiful.

From that day on, they lived happily ever after until the end of their days.

title: The Six Servants
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was an old queen who was also a sorceress. Her

daughter was the most beautiful girl anyone had ever seen. But the queen,

had only one goal: to lead men to their doom.

Whenever a suitor showed up, she declared that anyone who wanted to marry her daughter had to complete a challenging task or face death.

Many were captivated by the daughter's beauty and risked everything, but none could succeed in the tasks set by the queen.

Without mercy, they were forced to kneel down.

One day, a prince heard about the lovely maiden and turned to his father, saying,

"Father, let me go there. I want to propose to her."

"Never," replied the king. "If you go, it would mean your death." Feeling hopeless, the prince fell ill.

For seven long years he lay in bed, and no doctor could help him. When his father realized there was no more hope, he sighed heavily.

He finally told his son, "Go ahead and try your luck; I have no other way to save you."

Hearing this, the prince jumped up, feeling better, and joyfully set out on his journey.

As he rode across a meadow, he spotted something that looked like a giant pile of hay.

Getting closer, he recognized it as a man lying there, his stomach bulging.

When the hefty fellow noticed the traveler, he stood up and stated,

"If you need someone, take me into your service."

The prince remarked, "What would I do with someone so clumsy?"

"Oh," replied the large man, "this is nothing. When I puff myself up, I'm way bigger!"

"In that case," the prince said, "I could use your help. Come with me."

So, the man joined the prince, and soon they found another person lying on the ground with his ear pressed to the earth.

"What are you doing there?" asked the prince.

"I’m listening," replied the man.

"What’s so interesting?" asked the prince.

title: The Six Servants
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"I’m listening to everything happening in the world; I can even hear the grass growing!"

"Tell me," said the prince, "what's going on at the queen's court with the beautiful daughter?"

The listener said, "I hear the sound of a sword cutting off a suitor's head."

The prince replied, "I could use you—come with me."

They continued on their way and then saw feet and legs lying on the ground, but couldn't see the rest of the body.

After walking a bit, they found the body and finally the head. "Wow, you are quite tall," said the prince.

"Oh," the tall man replied, "this is nothing! If I really stretch out, I could be three thousand times taller! I’d love to join you."

"Sure, come along," said the prince.

Next, they found a man sitting along the path with his eyes covered.

The prince asked him, "Are your eyes weak? Can't you handle the light?"

"No," the man replied, "I have to keep my eyes covered, or whatever I look at shatters. My gaze is that powerful! If that’s useful to you, I'd happily serve."

"Then come with me," said the prince.

As they traveled, they saw a man shivering in the blazing sun, trembling like a leaf.

"Why are you shaking when it's so warm?" the prince inquired.

"Alas! I'm different. The hotter it gets, the colder I become; my bones chill while the warmth surrounds me."

"That's unusual," said the prince, "but I’d like you to join my team."

Finally, they came upon a man with a long neck, gazing around.

"What are you staring at so intently?" asked the prince.

The man responded, "I have sharp eyes that can see into every forest, field, hill, and valley, all over the world."

The prince replied, "Come along if you want; I still need someone like you."

title: The Six Servants
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Eventually, the prince and his six companions reached the town where the old queen resided.

He didn’t reveal his identity, but said, "If you give me your beautiful daughter, I will take on any task you set."

The sorceress was thrilled to catch such a handsome young man and said, "I’ll give you three tasks. If you succeed, you can marry my daughter."

"What’s the first task?" he asked. "You must fetch me my ring that I lost in the Red Sea."

The prince returned to his companions and said, "The first task is tough; we need to get a ring from the Red Sea. Let's figure out how."

The man with the sharp eyes said, "I can see where it is. It’s on a pointed stone."

The tall man lifted them to the spot and said, "I could grab it easily if I could see it."

"Oh, is that all?" grinned the stout man, who then lay down and drank the water until the sea was dry.

Once dried, the tall man bent over and retrieved the ring.

Overjoyed to have the ring, the prince brought it back to the queen, who was amazed.

"Yes, that’s the right ring! You’ve completed the first task. Now, for the second task."

"Do you see that meadow in front of my palace? There are three hundred fat oxen grazing. You must eat them all—bones, skin, and everything—not leaving a single hair behind.

Down in my cellar are three hundred casks of wine; you must drink every last drop. If even a hair or a drop is left, your life is forfeit."

"Can I invite guests for this feast?" asked the prince, hoping to make it fun.

The old woman chuckled maliciously and replied, "You can invite one person for the sake of company."

The prince went to his friends and said to the stout man, "You’ll be my guest today and eat your fill."

The stout man puffed himself up and devoured all three hundred oxen without leaving a single hair behind.

title: The Six Servants
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Then, without needing a glass, he drained the wine casks dry.

Once they finished eating, the prince returned to the queen and told her the second task was done.

She was astonished and said, "No one has accomplished so much before, but one last task remains. I will not let you escape easily."

"Tonight, I will have my daughter brought to your room, and you are to hold her in your arms. But don’t fall asleep! At midnight, if she’s not in your arms, you'll be in trouble."

The prince thought, "This task seems easy enough; I'll definitely keep my eyes open." However, he told his servants what the old woman had said and added,

"Who knows what trickery she has planned? Stay vigilant and ensure she doesn’t leave my room."

As night approached, the sorceress came with her daughter and placed her in the prince's arms.

The tall servant coiled around them like a protective guard, while the stout man stood by the door to block anyone.

The two sat there; the maiden didn’t say a word, but when the moonlight shone on her face, the prince was struck by her beauty.

He couldn’t take his eyes off her, filled with love and joy, and he didn’t feel tired at all.

This went on until around eleven o'clock, when the old woman cast a spell over them, causing everyone to fall asleep, and in that moment, she whisked her daughter away.

They slept soundly until fifteen minutes before midnight, when the magic wore off, and they woke up.

"Oh no!" cried the prince. "I’ve lost her now!" His faithful servants groaned in despair, but the listener said, "Wait, let me listen."

He concentrated and then said, "She’s on a rock three hundred leagues away, crying for help. You, tall one, can help her! If you stand up, you’ll get there in no time."

"Yes," replied the tall one, "but the one with sharp eyes needs to come with me to break the rock."

title: The Six Servants
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The tall man picked the one with the covered eyes onto his back, and in the blink of an eye, they arrived at the enchanted rock.

Once there, the tall one unveiled the other’s eyes, and as soon as the man looked around, the rock shattered into a thousand pieces.

The tall one swiftly took the maiden in his arms and returned her just as quickly, bringing back his companion in the same way, all before the clock struck midnight.

By the time the clock chimed twelve, they were all back where they started, joyous and oblivious.

The old sorceress crept in with a wicked grin, thinking, "Now she’s mine!" She believed her daughter was still on that rock.

But when the queen saw her daughter safe in the prince's arms, she gasped, saying, "Here’s someone smarter than me."

Seeing she had no options left, she reluctantly agreed to let her daughter marry him.

But she whispered in her ear, "It’s embarrassing for you to have to obey such commoners and not be able to choose your own husband."

These words filled the maiden with anger, and thoughts of revenge brewed.

The next morning, she had three hundred huge bundles of wood gathered and told the prince that even though he had completed the three tasks, she wouldn’t marry him unless someone was willing to sit in the middle of the wood and endure the fire.

She figured no servant would risk being burned for him, and thought surely, he'd do it himself, then she could be free.

However, the servants insisted, "Every one of us has done something, except for the icy one. It’s your turn now," and they shoved him into the center of the pile and set it ablaze.

The fire blazed for three days, consuming all the wood. When the flames died down, the frosty fellow stood there amidst the ashes, shaking like a leaf.

"I’ve never felt such cold in my life; if it had lasted any longer, I would have..."

With nothing else to stand on, the lovely maiden had to marry the unknown youth.

title: The Six Servants
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But as they headed to the church, the old queen cried, "I can’t bear this disgrace," and sent her soldiers after them to kill anyone who got in their way and bring her daughter back.

But the listener had sharpened his ears and overheard the queen’s secret plans.

"What should we do?" he asked the stout man.

The stout one knew exactly what to do: he spat out some of the seawater he had drunk, and a massive lake formed, drowning the soldiers.

When the sorceress saw this, she sent her armored knights, but the listener heard their armor clanking.

He removed a bandage from the eye of the sharp-eyed man, who looked intently at the approaching troops, causing them to shatter like glass.

The prince and his bride continued on their journey, and after they were married, the six servants took their leave.

"Your wishes are fulfilled; you won’t need us anymore. We’ll go find our own fortunes," they said.

About half a league from the prince's palace was a village with a swineherd tending his pigs.

As they passed through, the prince told his wife, "Do you really know who I am? I’m not a prince; I’m just a swineherd. That man over there is my father.

We need to help him and work together."

So, he got down from his horse and asked the innkeeper to take her royal clothes during the night.

When she woke up, she had nothing to wear. The innkeeper's wife gave her an old dress and some wool stockings, acting like it was a kind gift.

The princess thought, "I deserve this for my arrogance and pride."

This continued for a week until she couldn’t take it anymore, her feet sore and tender.

Then some people came by and asked if she knew who her husband really was.

"Yes," she replied, "he’s a swineherd who has gone out with ropes to negotiate a deal."

They insisted, "Come with us; we’ll take you to him," and led her back to the palace.

Upon entering the hall, she found her husband dressed like a king.

But she didn’t recognize him until he embraced her, kissed her, and said, "I suffered so much for you, so you had to endure for me too."

And then they held a grand wedding, and the storyteller wishes he could have been there to see it.

title: The Six Swans
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, a king was out hunting in a vast forest.

He chased a wild beast with such enthusiasm that none of his attendants could keep up.

As evening approached, he stopped to look around.

That’s when he realized he was lost.

He searched for an exit but couldn’t find one.

Then he spotted an old woman approaching him, her head bobbing as she walked.

But she was a witch.

"Good woman," he said, "can you show me the way out of the forest?"

“Oh, yes, my lord,” she replied, “but only on one condition.

If you don’t meet that condition, you’ll never escape and will starve in here.”

“What condition?” asked the king.

“I have a daughter,” the old woman said, "who's as beautiful as anyone in the world.”

“She deserves to be your queen, and if you agree to make her your wife, I will show you the way out of the forest.”

Feeling desperate, the king agreed, and the old woman led him to her small hut.

Inside, her daughter sat by the fire, and she welcomed the king as if she had been waiting for him.

He noticed she was indeed beautiful, but there was something about her that filled him with secret dread.

He placed the maiden on his horse, and soon enough, they reached his royal palace.

The king had already married once and had seven children—six boys and a girl—whom he adored more than anything.

Worried that the stepmother might mistreat them, he took his children to a secluded castle in the depths of the forest.

It was so hidden, and the path was so tricky that he wouldn’t have found it if it weren't for a wise woman who had given him a magical ball of yarn.

When he tossed it ahead of him, it unrolled and showed him the way.

As the king often visited his beloved children, the queen grew suspicious and wanted to know what he did when he was alone in the forest.

She paid a lot of money to his servants to betray his secret, and they revealed to her the existence of the magical ball that led him.

title: The Six Swans
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Now restless, she found out where the king kept the ball of yarn.

Then she crafted small shirts out of white silk, and using the witchcraft skills she learned from her mother, she sewed a spell inside them.

One day, when the king went out hunting again, she took the shirts and followed him into the forest, using the ball to guide her.

The children, seeing someone approach from afar, thought their beloved father was coming to them.

Joyfully, they ran to greet him.

Then she threw a shirt over each of them, and as soon as the shirts touched their bodies, they turned into swans and flew off over the forest.

The queen returned home thrilled, believing she had successfully gotten rid of her stepchildren, not realizing the girl had stayed behind.

The next day, the king went to see his children but found only the little girl.

“Where are your brothers?” the king asked.

“Oh, dear father,” she replied, “they’ve disappeared and left me behind.

I saw from my window how they turned into swans and flew away.”

She showed him the feathers they had dropped in the courtyard and which she had collected.

The king was heartbroken but didn’t suspect the queen had orchestrated this evil act.

Fearing the girl might also be taken from him, he decided to take her with him.

But she was terrified of her stepmother and begged the king to let her stay just one more night in the forest castle.

The poor girl thought, I can’t stay here any longer. I need to find my brothers.

That night, she fled into the forest.

She walked through the entire night and into the next day without a break until she could go no further out of sheer exhaustion.

Then she stumbled upon a small hut and stepped inside, where she found a room with six tiny beds.

Not daring to sleep in one of them, she crept under a bed on the hard ground, planning to spend the night there.

Just before sunset, she heard rustling and saw six swans fly in through the window.

They landed on the ground, began blowing at each other, and their feathers drifted away.

title: The Six Swans
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The swan skins peeled off as easily as a shirt. Seeing them, the maiden recognized her brothers and felt a surge of joy as she crept out from under the bed.

The brothers were equally thrilled to see their little sister, but their happiness was short-lived.

“You can’t stay here,” they warned her. “This is a hideout for robbers. If they find you, they will kill you.”

“But can’t you protect me?” the little sister pleaded.

“No,” they replied. “We can only take off our swan skins for a short while each evening.

After that quarter of an hour, we're swans again.”

She cried and asked, “Can’t you be freed?”

“Sadly, no,” they answered. “The conditions are too harsh.

For six years, you must neither speak nor laugh, and during that time, you must sew six little shirts of starwort for us.

If a single word escapes your lips, all your efforts will be in vain.”

Once they shared their plight, the hour was up, and they flew out the window as swans.

The maiden was determined to save her brothers, even if it cost her life.

She left the hut and found a spot in the forest where she sat in a tree and spent the night.

The next morning, she gathered starwort and began to sew.

She couldn't speak to anyone and had no desire to laugh.

She just sat there, empty-minded.

After a while, the king of the land was hunting in the same forest, and his huntsmen came upon the tree where the maiden was sitting.

They called out to her, asking, “Who are you?”

But she didn’t reply.

“Come down to us,” they urged. “We won’t harm you.”

She merely shook her head.

As they pressed her with more questions, she threw down her golden necklace, hoping to satisfy them that way.

However, they persisted, so she tossed down her girdle.

When that didn’t work, she shed her garters, giving away everything she could spare until all she had left was her shift.

title: The Six Swans
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But the huntsmen were undeterred and climbed the tree to bring her down and lead her before the king.

The king asked, “Who are you? What are you doing up in that tree?”

But she remained silent.

He tried to communicate in every language he knew, yet she stayed as mute as a stone.

Her stunning beauty moved the king deeply, and he fell in love with her.

He draped his cloak around her, lifted her onto his horse, and brought her back to his castle.

There, he had her dressed in fine clothes, and her beauty shone like sunlight.

Still, no one could coax a word from her.

He seated her beside him at the table; her graceful demeanor and kindness captivated him.

He declared, “She’s the one I want to marry, and no other woman in the world will do.”

A few days later, he wed her.

However, the king had a wicked mother who disapproved of the marriage and spread rumors about the young queen.

“Who knows,” she said, “where this silent girl came from?”

“She’s not fit to be a queen.”

A year passed, and when the queen had her first child, the old woman snatched it away from her and smeared her mouth with blood while she slept.

She then accused the queen of being a child-eater.

The king wouldn’t believe her and wouldn’t let anyone harm the queen.

But the queen kept fiercely sewing those shirts and focused on nothing else.

When she gave birth to another beautiful boy, the wicked mother-in-law used the same trick, but the king refused to believe her.

“She’s too kind and good to commit such acts,

and if she could speak for herself, her innocence would shine through.”

But when the old woman kidnapped the newborn for the third time and accused the queen, who stayed silent, the king had no choice but to hand her over to justice.

She was condemned to be burned at the stake.

On the day of her execution, it happened to be the last day of the six years she was bound to silence.

By then, she had finished the shirts and was ready to save her brothers.

All that was missing was the left sleeve of the sixth shirt.

As she was being led to the stake, she held the shirts tightly in her arms.

Just as they were about to light the fire, she looked around and saw six swans flying toward her.

Her heart leapt with joy, realizing her rescue was at hand.

The swans flew in close enough that when they landed, their swan skins fell away.

Her brothers stood there in their own forms, strong and handsome.

Only the youngest lacked his left arm, replaced by a swan’s wing.

They embraced and kissed each other, then the queen rushed to the king, who was deeply moved.

She finally spoke, saying, “Dear husband, now I can declare that I am innocent and was falsely accused.

I must tell you about the old woman's treachery who stole my three children and hid them away.”

To the king's great delight, the children were soon brought forth.

As punishment, the wicked mother-in-law was tied to the stake and burned to ashes.

The king, queen, and her six brothers lived happily ever after, filled with peace.

title: The Skilful Huntsman
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once there was a young man who had learned the craft of locksmithing.

He told his father that he was ready to venture out into the world to seek his fortune.

The father replied, "That's fine; I'm completely okay with that."

He gave his son some money for the journey, and the young man set off.

After traveling for some time, he decided he no longer wanted to be a locksmith because he had lost interest in it and instead fancied becoming a huntsman.

During his travels, he met a huntsman dressed in green who asked where he was coming from and where he was headed.

The young man replied that he was a locksmith's apprentice, but he didn't enjoy the trade anymore.

He expressed his interest in hunting and asked, "Will you teach me?"

"Of course!" replied the huntsman, "if you come along with me."

So the young man agreed, apprenticed himself to the huntsman for several years, and learned the art of hunting.

Afterward, he decided to try his luck elsewhere, and the huntsman, as a parting gift, gave him an air-gun.

The unique thing about this air-gun was that it never missed its target.

With that, he set off and soon found himself in an enormous forest that he couldn't cross in a single day.

As evening approached, he climbed up into a tall tree to keep safe from wild animals.

Around midnight, he noticed a faint light glimmering in the distance.

Curious, he looked through the branches to focus on the light, marking its location in his mind.

To help him navigate, he took off his hat and tossed it in the direction of the light, planning to follow it once he climbed down.

After descending, he retrieved his hat, put it back on, and headed directly toward the light.

As he got closer, the light grew brighter, revealing a massive fire.

He spotted three giants sitting around it, cooking an ox on a spit.

One of the giants said, "I need to check if the meat is ready to eat," and reached for a piece.

title: The Skilful Huntsman
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Just as he was about to bite it, the huntsman shot it right out of his hand.

"Well, would you look at that!" exclaimed the giant. "The wind must’ve knocked it away!"

He grabbed another piece, but the huntsman shot it away again right before he could take a bite.

This made the giant glare at the one beside him and say, "Why are you stealing my food?"

"I didn’t take it, a sharpshooter must be responsible for that!" the other replied.

The giant tried again, but yet again, the huntsman shot the meat from his hand.

The giant exclaimed, "That’s an impressive shot! Someone like that would be useful to us."

He called out, "Come here, sharpshooter! Sit with us by the fire and eat all you want. We promise we won’t harm you, but if you refuse, you’ll regret it."

With that, the young man stepped forward and confidently declared that he was a skilled huntsman, able to hit whatever he aimed at.

The giants said they would treat him well if he joined them.

"There’s a great lake outside the forest," they told him. "Behind it stands a tower where a beautiful princess is held captive, and we want to take her."

"Sure," he replied, "I’ll get her for you!"

Then they added, "But there’s one more thing; there's a small dog that starts barking as soon as anyone gets close.

As soon as it barks, everyone in the palace wakes up, which is why we can’t reach the princess.

Can you take care of the dog?"

"Absolutely!" he said. "That sounds fun!"

He hopped into a boat and rowed across the lake.

As soon as he landed, the little dog dashed out, ready to bark.

But the huntsman quickly used his air-gun and shot it dead.

When the giants saw this, they rejoiced, thinking they had the princess secured.

However, the huntsman wanted to check things out first and told them to wait outside until he called for them.

title: The Skilful Huntsman
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He entered the castle and found everything perfectly quiet, with everyone fast asleep.

In the first room he searched, he spotted a sword hanging on the wall, made of pure silver with a golden star on it.

Near it lay a sealed letter, which he opened to read that whoever possessed the sword could kill anything that stood in their way.

He took the sword, secured it at his side, and went further.

He entered the chamber where the princess lay asleep, and she was so beautiful that he paused, holding his breath to admire her.

He thought, "How can I let this innocent maiden fall into the hands of the wicked giants?"

Looking around, he noticed a pair of slippers beneath the bed.

One slipper had her father's name etched on the right side, and her own name on the left.

She also wore a silk scarf embroidered with gold, featuring her father's name on one side and her own on the other, both in golden letters.

The huntsman took a pair of scissors, snipped the right corner of her scarf, and tucked it away in his knapsack.

He also carefully took the right slipper, the one with the king's name, and stashed that too.

The princess still lay asleep, tightly sewn into her nightgown, so he snipped a small piece from it without disturbing her.

Satisfied, he left her undisturbed.

When he returned to the gate, the giants were waiting eagerly for him, believing he had brought the princess.

But he called out to them to come in, saying the princess was already theirs and he couldn't open the gate for them.

Instead, he pointed to a hole they could crawl through.

The first giant approached, and the huntsman grabbed his hair, pulled him in, and swiftly cut off his head with the sword.

He then pulled the rest of the giant inside.

He beckoned the second giant, managed to decapitate him too, and then did the same with the third.

Feeling accomplished, he had freed the beautiful princess from her enemies.

To ensure they wouldn’t bother anyone again, he cut out their tongues and placed them in his knapsack.

title: The Skilful Huntsman
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He thought, "I will return home to show my father what I’ve done, and then I’ll travel the world because good fortune will surely find me."

When the king awoke and found the three giants dead, he entered his daughter's room, woke her up, and asked who had killed them.

The princess replied, "I don't know, dear father; I’ve been asleep."

As she stood to put on her slippers, she noticed the right one was missing.

Seeing the scarf, she realized a corner was cut off, and a piece was missing from her nightdress.

The king gathered his court, soldiers, and everyone present, demanding to know who had freed his daughter and slain the giants.

Among them was a captain, a one-eyed, hideous man who claimed he was the one.

The old king announced that since he had done this deed, he should marry his daughter.

However, the princess protested, "I’d rather roam the world than marry him, father!"

The king insisted that if she refused the marriage, she should remove her royal garments, wear peasant clothes, and begin a life as a potter.

So the princess changed, borrowing enough pottery to set up a stall.

She promised the potter that if she sold it by evening, she'd pay him back.

The king arranged for peasants to smash her stall with their carts, hoping to force her into marrying the captain.

When the carts arrived and shattered her pottery into tiny pieces, she began to cry.

"How am I ever going to pay for that now?" she lamented.

The king's plan to coerce her had backfired.

Instead, she returned to the potter, asking if he would lend her more.

He refused, insisting she had to pay for her previous borrowings first.

Heartbroken, she went to her father, crying and pleading to leave the kingdom.

The king sighed and said, "I will build you a hut in the forest where you can stay for the rest of your life, cooking for everyone, but you won't charge them anything."

Once the hut was ready, a sign outside read "Today given, tomorrow sold."

title: The Skilful Huntsman
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She stayed there for a long time, and word spread that a maiden was cooking for free.

The huntsman heard the rumors and thought that he could help her out.

Since he was poor and without money, he packed his air-gun and some important tokens from the castle and ventured into the forest.

Eventually, he found the hut with the sign saying, "Today given, tomorrow sold."

With the sword that had slain the giants, he entered the hut and asked for a meal.

He was struck by the beauty of the maiden, who appeared like a work of art.

She inquired about his journey, and he replied, "I'm just wandering around the world."

Then she noticed the sword and asked him its origin, recognizing her father's name on it.

Curious, she asked, "Are you the king's daughter?"

"Yes," she confirmed.

"With this sword," he explained, "I beheaded three giants," and he revealed the tongues of the giants he'd kept as proof.

Next, he showed her the slipper and the snipped corner of her scarf.

Overjoyed, she realized he was the one who had rescued her from the giants.

Together, they returned to the old king's palace.

They brought him to the hut, where she led him to her room, explaining that the huntsman was her true rescuer.

When the king saw all the evidence, he was now fully convinced and expressed his happiness in knowing the truth.

He declared that the huntsman could have his daughter for a wife, which delighted the princess.

She dressed the huntsman as a foreign lord, and the king instructed a grand feast to be prepared.

At the dinner table, the captain sat on the left side of the princess, believing the huntsman was just a visitor.

After the meal, the old king asked the captain if he could guess a riddle.

"What if someone claimed to have killed three giants, but when asked about their tongues, found none left? How could that happen?"

The captain responded, "Then they must not have had any tongues."

"Not true," replied the king. "Every creature has a tongue!" Then he questioned what punishment should be given to anyone who answered like that.

The captain suggested, "He should be torn to pieces!"

The king declared that the captain had sealed his fate, and he was imprisoned and then executed in four parts.

Meanwhile, the princess married the huntsman.

Later, he brought his parents to live with him, and they enjoyed a happy life together.

After the old king passed away, he inherited the kingdom.

title: The Sole
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
For a long time, the fish were unhappy because there was no order in their kingdom.

No one paid attention to the others; they swam left or right freely.

Some would dart between those who wanted to stick together or just got in their way.

A strong fish would sometimes smack a weaker one with its tail, pushing it away, or even swallow it whole without hesitation.

They all thought, how wonderful it would be if we had a king who could enforce laws and fairness among us.

So, they gathered to pick a ruler, someone who could zoom through the water fastest and help the rest of them.

They lined up by the shore, and the pike signaled with his tail, prompting them all to take off.

The pike shot away like an arrow, and right behind him were the herring, the gudgeon, the perch, the carp, and the others.

Even the sole joined in, hoping to win the race.

Suddenly, a loud cry rang out: the herring is in the lead, the herring is in the lead!

Who’s in the lead? the jealous sole screamed angrily from far behind, Who’s first?

The answer came back: The herring, the herring!

The envious sole shouted, The naked herring!

Since that day, the sole has been punished with a mouth on one side.

title: The Sparrow and His Four Children
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
A sparrow had four young chicks living in a swallow's nest. When they

were ready to fly, some mischievous boys knocked the nest down, but

luckily, all the birds managed to escape in the strong wind. After that,

the worried father felt sad that since his sons had ventured out into

the world, he hadn't warned them about all the dangers they might face,

or taught them how to handle each one.

In autumn, many sparrows gathered in a wheat field, and there the old bird

reunited with his four children, joyfully bringing them home with him. “Oh, my dear sons, how

I've worried about you all summer since you flew away without my guidance. Please heed my words,

obey your father, and stay alert. Little birds have to face big dangers. Then he asked the eldest where

he had spent the summer and how he had managed to survive. “I stayed in the gardens, looking for caterpillars and small worms,

until the cherries were ripe. “Well, my son,” the father replied, “that's not bad, but there are risks involved.

Because of that, be extra careful from now on, especially when you see people in the gardens carrying long green poles,

which are hollow and have a small hole at the top. Yes,

father, but what if a little green leaf is stuck over the hole with wax?” asked the son. “Where did you see that?” “In a

merchant's garden,” said the young bird. “Oh, my son, merchants are clever folks,” said the father. “If you’ve been around the

worldly people, you’ve learned enough about their cleverness, just make sure to use it wisely and don't be too confident.”

Then he asked the next one, where have you spent your time? “At court,” said the son. “Sparrows and silly little birds don't belong

there. It’s full of gold, velvet, silk, armor, harnesses, sparrow-hawks, screech-owls, and lanners. Stick to the horses' stables, where they winnow oats,

title: The Sparrow and His Four Children
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
or thresh, and you might find your daily grain of corn in peace. Yes,”

father,” the son replied, “but when the stable boys set traps and snares in the straw, many get caught.” “Where did you see that?” asked the old bird. “At court, with the stable boys.” “Oh, my son, court boys are trouble. If you've been at court and left no feathers behind,

you’ve learned quite a bit and should know how to navigate the world, but keep your eyes open, because the wolves often devour the cleverest dogs.”

The father then asked the third son, where did you seek your fortune? “I cast my tub and rope on the roads and highways, and sometimes found a grain of corn or barley.” “That’s just fine,” said the father, “but stay alert and watch for anyone bending down to pick up a stone, because you won’t have much time then.

That’s true,” said the son, “but what if someone carries a rock or ore in their pocket?” “Where have you seen that?” “Among the miners, dear father. When they come out of the pit, they often carry little bits of ore with them. Mining folks are hardworking and clever.”

“If you’ve been around mining guys, you’ve certainly learned something, but when you go there, be careful, for many a sparrow has come to a bad end when a mining boy threw a piece.

Finally, the father turned to the youngest son. “You, little chirping nestling, were always the silliest and weakest. Stay close to me. The world has many rough, wicked birds with crooked beaks and sharp claws, waiting to catch little birds like you.

Stick with your own kind, and nibble on little spiders or caterpillars from the trees or houses, and you’ll live long in peace. “Oh, my dear father,”

the youngest said, “he who feeds himself without harming others has a good life, and no sparrow-hawk, eagle, or kite can hurt him if he entrusts himself and his food each evening and morning to God, who created and preserves all forest and village birds,

and who also hears the cries and prayers of young ravens. No sparrow or wren falls to the ground without His will.” “Where did you learn this?” asked the father. The son replied,

“When the fierce wind swept me away from you, I found my way to a church, and there throughout the summer, I’ve been gathering flies and spiders from the windows, and listening to sermons.”

The Father of all sparrows provided for me all summer and kept me safe from misfortune and fierce birds. “Indeed, my dear son, if you find shelter in churches, help clear away the spiders and buzzing flies,

and sing praises to God like the young ravens, and commit yourself to the eternal creator, all will be well with you,

even if the world is filled with wild, cruel birds.” “Whoever entrusts their path to God,”

“suffers in silence, waits, and prays while keeping their faith and conscience pure, will surely be under God’s protection.”

title: The Spindle, The Shuttle, and The Needle
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a girl whose parents passed away when she was still very young.

Left all alone in a small house at the edge of the village, she lived with her godmother.

Her godmother supported herself by spinning, weaving, and sewing.

Taking in the lonely child, the old woman provided her a home, taught her valuable skills, and raised her to be a good person.

By the time the girl turned fifteen, the old woman fell ill.

One day, she called the girl to her bedside and said, "My dear daughter, I feel my time is near."

"I'm leaving you the little house, which will shelter you from wind and rain, and my spindle, shuttle, and needle, so you can earn a living."

Then she gently placed her hands on the girl’s head, blessed her, and said, "Just keep the love of God in your heart, and everything will turn out alright."

After that, she closed her eyes, and when they buried her, the girl followed the coffin, crying heartbrokenly, and paid her final respects.

Now the girl lived alone in her little house.

She worked hard, spinning, weaving, and sewing, and everything she did was blessed by the kind old woman.

It felt like the flax in her room grew on its own, and whenever she finished a piece of cloth or a carpet or crafted a shirt, she found someone willing to buy it at a good price.

She wanted for nothing and even had some to share with others.

Around this time, the king's son was traveling around the country searching for a bride.

He declared he wouldn’t choose a poor girl, nor did he want a rich one.

He said, "I want to marry the girl who is both the poorest and yet the richest."

When he reached the village where the girl lived, he asked, as he did everywhere, who the richest and the poorest girl was.

They first named the richest girl.

Then the townsfolk said the poorest was the girl who lived in the small house at the very end of the village.

title: The Spindle, The Shuttle, and The Needle
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The rich girl was outside her house, surrounded by luxury, and when the prince approached, she greeted him with a low curtsy.

He glanced at her without saying a word and continued on.

When he reached the poor girl’s house, she wasn't standing at the door; instead, she was sitting in her small room.

He stopped his horse and saw the girl through the window, illuminated by the bright sunlight, busy at her spinning-wheel.

When she noticed the prince looking in, her cheeks flushed, and she looked down before going back to her spinning.

I can't say for sure if her thread was perfectly even at that moment, but she kept spinning until the prince had ridden away.

Once he was gone, she opened the window and said, "It’s so warm in here," watching until she could no longer see the white feathers in his hat.

Then she returned to her work, resumed spinning, and a saying the old woman often repeated while working popped into her head.

She sang, "Spindle, my spindle, hurry, hurry away, and here to my house bring the wooer, I pray."

And you won't believe what happened next: the spindle flew out of her hand immediately and out the door.

When she finally stood up in disbelief to see where it went, she watched it dance happily into the countryside, trailing a shimmering gold thread behind it.

Before long, it completely disappeared from her sight.

With no spindle left, the girl grabbed the weaver's shuttle, sat at her loom, and started to weave again.

Meanwhile, the spindle continued to dance along, and just as the thread finished, it reached the prince.

"What’s this?" he exclaimed. "The spindle seems to want to guide me!" He turned his horse around and followed the golden thread.

Back at her house, the girl was still at her work, singing, "Shuttle, my shuttle, weave well this day, and guide the wooer to me, I pray."

Instantly, the shuttle jumped out of her hand and flew out the door.

Before it crossed the threshold, it began to weave a carpet more beautiful than anything anyone had ever seen.

Lush lilies and roses bloomed on either side, while in the center, on a golden background, green branches arched overhead.

Underneath, hares and rabbits bounded, while stags and deer peeked out from among the foliage.

Colorful birds perched on the branches above but lacked the gift of song.

The shuttle darted around, effortlessly creating a masterpiece.

Since the shuttle had run off, the girl settled down to sew.

With the needle in her hand, she sang, "Needle, my needle, sharp-pointed and fine, prepare for the wooer this house of mine."

The needle leapt from her fingers, zipping around the room like lightning.

It was as if invisible spirits were at work, covering tables and benches with green cloth in an instant.

The chairs were draped in velvet, and beautiful silk curtains hung at the windows.

Just as the needle finished its last stitch, the girl spotted the prince outside the window again, thanks to the golden thread the spindle had guided him with.

He dismounted, strolled across the carpet into the house, and when he entered the room, there stood the girl in her simple clothes, but she glowed like a rose among leaves.

"You are the poorest and yet also the richest," he said to her. "Come with me; you shall be my bride."

She didn’t speak, but she offered him her hand.

He then kissed her, helped her onto his horse, and took her to the royal castle, where they celebrated their wedding with great joy.

The spindle, shuttle, and needle were stored in the treasure chamber, never to be forgotten.

title: The Spirit in the Bottle
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a poor woodcutter who worked hard from dawn till dusk.

After saving up some money, he turned to his son and said, "You are my only child. I want to invest the money I've earned through hard work into your education. If you learn an honest trade, you can support me when I grow old and my body becomes weak."

The boy attended a high school where he studied diligently, earning praise from his teachers. He stayed there for quite some time.

After progressing through two classes, the father's savings were all gone, and the boy still had much to learn. The father sighed, "Oh, I can't provide for you anymore, and during these tough times, I can barely earn enough for our daily bread."

The son replied, "Don’t worry about it, Dad. If it’s meant to be, it will work out for me. I’ll get used to it soon enough."

When the father decided to go into the forest to earn money by chopping and stacking wood, the son said, "I’ll come with you and help."

The father replied, "No, my son. That would be tough for you. You aren't used to hard labor and won't be able to handle it. Besides, I only have one axe and no money to buy another."

"Just ask the neighbor," the son suggested. "He’ll lend you his axe until I can earn one of my own."

So, the father borrowed an axe from the neighbor, and the next morning, they set out to the forest together.

The son helped his father and was cheerful and energetic about it. But when the sun was high in the sky, the father said, "Let’s take a break and have lunch; after that, we’ll work even better."

The son held his bread in his hands and said, "You take a break, Dad; I’m not tired. I’ll walk around a bit in the forest and look for birds' nests."

title: The Spirit in the Bottle
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Oh, you silly boy," the father said. "Why run around like that? You’ll just get tired and then won’t be able to lift your arm. Stay here and sit down with me."

Nevertheless, the son wandered into the forest, ate his bread, and joyfully searched among the green branches for bird nests. He walked back and forth until he stumbled upon a massive, old oak tree that was so wide it would take five men to hug it.

He paused to look at it and thought, many birds must have made nests in there. Just then, he thought he heard a voice. He listened closely and realized someone was crying softly, "Let me out, let me out."

He looked around but saw nothing. Then, he thought the voice was coming from the ground.

So he shouted, "Where are you?" The voice replied, "I’m down here among the roots of the oak tree. Let me out!"

The schoolboy started digging at the base of the tree, searching among the roots, until he discovered a glass bottle nestled in a small hollow.

He picked it up and held it up to the light, revealing a creature inside that resembled a frog, bouncing up and down.

"Let me out, let me out!" it cried again. The boy, thinking nothing was wrong, pulled the cork out of the bottle.

In an instant, a spirit sprang forth, growing larger until it stood towering over the boy, as massive as half the tree.

"Do you know what your reward is for releasing me?" bellowed the spirit.

"No," the boy replied bravely. "How would I know that?"

"Then let me inform you: I must strangle you for it."

"You should have warned me sooner," the boy said. "If I had known, I probably would have kept you locked up. But you’ll need to consult others about that first."

"More people here and more people there," the spirit huffed. "You shall have the reward you’ve earned. Do you think I was trapped in that bottle for fun? No, it was a punishment for me. I am the mighty Mercurius. Whoever lets me out must be strangled."

title: The Spirit in the Bottle
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Hold on a second," the boy said, "not so fast. I need to be sure you really were in that bottle and that you're the right spirit. If you can get back in there, then I’ll believe you, and you can do whatever you'd like with me."

The spirit replied arrogantly, "That’s an easy task." He compressed himself down, becoming small and slender as he had been originally, and slipped back through the bottle's neck.

As soon as he was inside, the boy quickly replaced the cork and tossed it back among the roots of the oak.

Then, just as the boy was about to head back to his father, the spirit wailed, "Oh, please let me out! Let me out!"

"No," the boy said firmly. "I won’t free you again. You tried to take my life once, and I won't let you go now that I've caught you again."

"If you let me go," the spirit said, "I’ll give you so much that you will have plenty for the rest of your life."

"No, I won’t fall for your tricks again, just like before," replied the boy.

"You’re passing up good fortune," the spirit said. "I won’t harm you; I’ll reward you richly."

The boy pondered, "Maybe I’ll take the chance. Perhaps he’ll keep his word, but he won't be free this time."

So he pulled out the cork, and the spirit surged from the bottle as he had before, stretching himself until he appeared like a giant.

"Now you shall receive your reward," the spirit declared, handing the boy a small piece of rag resembling a bandage.

"If you apply one end to a wound, it will heal; rub steel or iron with the other end, and it will turn into silver."

"I have to try this," the boy exclaimed, and headed to a tree, slicing off some bark with his axe and applying the bandage.

title: The Spirit in the Bottle
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Immediately, the wound sealed up and healed.

"Great!" the boy said to the spirit, "Now we can part ways."

The spirit thanked him for freeing him, and the boy thanked the spirit for the gift as he made his way home.

"Where have you been racing about?" his father asked. "Why haven’t you done your work? I always said you’d never amount to anything."

"Don’t worry, dad; I’ll make up for it," said the boy.

"Make up for it indeed," grumbled the father, feeling frustrated. "That won’t help."

"Just wait, Dad; I’ll chop down that tree so it splits," the son said, taking his bandage and rubbing it on the axe.

He swung hard, but since the iron had turned to silver, the blade bent. "Hey, Dad, look at this bad axe you gave me—it’s all crooked!"

The father was astonished, saying, "Oh no! What have you done? Now I’ll have to pay for that, and I don’t have the means! This is the reward of your hard work!"

"Don’t get mad," the son reassured. "I’ll find a way to pay for the axe."

"Oh, you blockhead," the father cried. "How do you plan to pay for it? You have only what I give you. These are just the foolish ideas of a student. You know nothing about woodcutting!"

After a while, the boy sighed, "Dad, I really can’t work anymore; we should take a break."

"What do you mean?" the father countered. "Do you think I can just sit around like you? I've got to keep working, but you can head home if you want."

"Dad, this is my first time here in this forest, and I don’t know the way back alone. Please come with me."

Finally, the father, somewhat calming down, agreed and walked home with him.

When they got home, the father said, "Go sell your damaged axe and see what you can get for it. I need to make up the difference to pay the neighbor."

The son took the axe to town to find a goldsmith. The goldsmith tested it, placed it on the scales, and said, "It's worth four hundred talers, but I don’t have that much on me."

The son replied, "Just give me what you have, and I’ll lend you the rest." The goldsmith handed him three hundred talers, keeping a debt of one hundred talers.

The son returned home and told his father, "I've got the money. Go ask the neighbor how much he wants for the axe."

"I already know," the father replied, "one taler, six groschen."

"Then give him two talers and twelve groschen—double that! I have plenty of money now." He handed his father a hundred talers and insisted, "You won’t ever have to worry again, live how you want."

"Good heavens!" the father said. "Where on earth did you get all this money?"

The boy explained how everything had happened, recounting how he’d trusted his luck and ended up with a fortune.

With the remaining money, he returned to school to continue learning. Since he could heal any wounds with his bandage, he eventually became the most famous doctor in the entire world.

title: The Star-Money
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a little girl whose parents had passed away.

She was so poor that she didn’t even have a room to live in or a bed to sleep on.

In the end, all she had were the clothes on her back and a piece of bread that a kind person had given her.

Despite her struggles, she was a good and kind-hearted girl.

Feeling abandoned by the world, she wandered into the open countryside, trusting in God.

Soon, a hungry man approached her and said, "Please, give me something to eat. I'm so hungry."

She gave him her entire piece of bread, saying, "May God bless you," before continuing on her way.

Next, a child appeared, complaining, "My head is so cold. Could you give me something to cover it with?"

Without hesitation, she took off her hood and handed it to him.

As she walked a bit further, she encountered another child without a jacket, shivering from the cold.

She gave her own jacket to that child, and shortly after, another one begged for a dress.

She gladly gave away that dress too.

Eventually, she found herself in a forest, and the night turned dark.

Then, yet another child approached her, asking for a shirt.

The compassionate girl thought, "It’s dark, and no one can see you. It’s fine to give away your shirt," so she took it off and gave it away.

Now standing there with nothing left, something amazing happened.

Suddenly, stars fell from the sky, and they were actually smooth, shiny pieces of money.

Even though she had just given away her shirt, she magically received a new one made from the finest linen.

She put the money in her new shirt, and just like that, she was rich.

title: The Stolen Farthings
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One day, a father sat down for dinner with his wife and children, along with a good friend who had come to visit.

As they enjoyed their meal, the clock struck twelve.

Suddenly, the stranger noticed the door swing open, and a very pale child, dressed in pure white, entered.

The child didn’t look around or say a word; instead, it walked straight into the next room.

A little while later, it returned and quietly slipped out the door again.

On the second and third days, the child appeared in exactly the same way.

Eventually, the stranger asked the father who the lovely child was that went into the next room every day at noon.

The father replied, 'I’ve never seen it before,' and he had no idea to whom it might belong.

The next day, when the child appeared again, the stranger pointed it out to the father, but he still couldn’t see it.

The mother and children also didn’t notice anything unusual.

Frustrated, the stranger stood up, walked to the door of the room, opened it slightly, and peeked inside.

He saw the child sitting on the floor, busily digging around in the spaces between the floorboards.

But as soon as the child spotted the stranger, it vanished.

The stranger then explained what he had seen and described the child in detail, which made the mother recognize it.

'Oh, that's my dear child who passed away a month ago,' she said.

They lifted up the floorboards and found two farthings—coins that the child had once received from its mother to give to a poor man.

However, the child thought, 'I can buy a biscuit with this,' and had kept the farthings hidden in the cracks between the boards.

Because of this, the child couldn’t rest in peace in its grave; it returned every day at noon to search for the lost coins.

The parents immediately gave the money to a poor man, and after that, the child was never seen again.

title: The Story of Schlauraffen Land
author: Brothers Grimm
themes: the interplay of reality and fantasy
text_chunk:
Once upon a time in Schlauraffen Land, I journeyed forth and saw Rome and the

Lateran hanging by a thin silk thread, and a man with no feet who

outran a speedy horse, and a sharp sword that sliced through a

bridge. I spotted a young donkey with a silver nose chasing after two

quick hares, and a gigantic lime tree where hot cakes

grew abundantly. I noticed a skinny old goat carrying around a hundred

cartloads of fat on its back, plus sixty loads of salt. Haven't I

already spun enough tall tales? I witnessed a plow working without any horses or cows,

and a one-year-old child tossed four millstones from Ratisbon to

Trier, and from Trier to Strasbourg. A hawk flew over the

Rhine, completely within its rights. I heard some fish

making such a ruckus with one another that it echoed

all the way to the heavens, and sweet honey flowed like water from a deep

valley to the very top of a high mountain—strange sights indeed.

There were two crows mowing a meadow, and I saw two gnats

constructing a bridge, while two doves tore a wolf apart. Two children

gave birth to two kids, and two frogs threshed corn

together. I watched two mice consecrating a bishop, and two cats

scratching out a bear's tongue. Then a snail unexpectedly rushed in and

defeated two ferocious lions. A barber stood nearby shaving a woman's

beard off, and two toddlers told their mother to be quiet.

I then saw two greyhounds pulling a mill from the

water, and a broken-down old horse next to it, declaring that it was

correct. Four horses were in the yard threshing corn

with all their strength, while two goats warmed the stove, and a

red cow tossed bread into the oven. Suddenly, a hen crowed,

"Cock-a-doodle-doo!" The story has been told, cock-a-doodle-doo.

title: The Straw, the Coal, and the Bean
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
In a village, there lived a poor old woman who had collected a

bowl of beans that she wanted to cook. So, she started a fire on

her hearth, and to help it burn faster, she lit it with a handful of

straw. While she was pouring the beans into the pan,

one of them fell unnoticed to the ground, landing next to a piece of

straw. Shortly after, a burning coal from the fire jumped down to join

them. The straw looked at the others and said,

"Hey friends, where did you come from?" The coal replied,

"I managed to escape from the fire, and if I hadn't done so by sheer

force, I would have turned to ash. I was so close to being burnt up!"

The bean chimed in, "I too got away with my skin intact, but if the old

woman had tossed me into the pan, I would have been boiling away without

a second thought, just like my friends. "And would I have fared any better?"

the straw replied. "The old woman has already cooked so many of my

brethren in the fire. She grabbed sixty of them at once and ended their lives.

Fortunately, I managed to slip through her fingers."

"But what do we do now?" asked the coal.

"I think," said the bean, "that since we've all escaped death, we should stick together like good friends.

And to make sure we don't encounter any more trouble, let's leave this place and find a new home together."

The other two agreed, and they set off on their journey.

After a while, they came across a small stream, but without a bridge or plank,

they didn’t know how to cross. The straw had a clever idea and said,

"I'll lay myself across as a bridge, and you can walk over on me." The straw stretched itself from one side to the other.

The coal, feeling bold, stepped confidently onto the makeshift bridge.

But when she reached the middle and heard the water rushing below, she became nervous,

stopped, and didn't dare go any further. The straw, however, started to burn,

snapped in two, and fell into the water. The coal followed,

hissing as she hit the water, and disappeared.

The bean, who had wisely stayed behind on the shore, couldn't help but laugh,

and she laughed so hard that she almost burst. It would have been the end for her,

too, if a kind tailor traveling nearby hadn't sat down to rest by the stream.

Noticing her distress, he pulled out his needle and thread and stitched her up.

The bean thanked him sincerely, but since the tailor used black thread,

all beans have looked the same ever since.

title: The Tailor in Heaven
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One beautiful day, God decided to enjoy himself in the heavenly garden and took all the apostles and saints with him.

This meant that Saint Peter was the only one left in heaven.

The Lord had instructed him to keep the gates locked until He returned.

So, Peter stood by the door, keeping watch.

Before long, someone knocked.

Peter asked, 'Who’s there, and what do you want?'

'I’m a poor, honest tailor praying for admission,' replied a smooth voice.

'Honest, huh?' Peter responded. 'That sounds like what a thief would say before going to the gallows. You’ve been light-fingered and have snipped away at people's clothes. I can't let you in. The Lord has forbidden it while He’s out.'

'Please, have mercy,' the tailor pleaded. 'The little scraps that fall from the table aren't stolen and aren't worth anything. Look at me—I’m lame, and my feet are blistered from the walk here. I can't possibly go back. Just let me in, and I’ll do all the dirty work. I promise to carry the children, wash their clothes, scrub the benches where they’ve played, and patch up their torn frocks.'

Moved by pity, Saint Peter opened the door just wide enough for the thin, lame tailor to slip through.

Once inside, the tailor had to sit in a corner behind the door and stay quiet so the Lord wouldn’t notice him when He returned.

The tailor did as he was told, but one time, when Saint Peter left the door, he couldn't resist the urge to peek into every corner of heaven and check out how everything was arranged.

Finally, he found a spot with many beautiful and precious chairs, and in the center was a golden chair adorned with shiny jewels.

This chair was much higher than the others and had a golden footstool in front of it.

This seat belonged to the Lord, where He sat when at home, able to see everything that happened on Earth.

The tailor stood and admired the grand chair for quite some time, as it fascinated him more than anything else.

title: The Tailor in Heaven
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
At last, he couldn't control his curiosity any longer and climbed up to sit in the chair.

From there, he could see everything happening down on Earth, including an ugly old woman washing by the stream, secretly laying aside two veils for herself.

This made the tailor so angry that he grabbed the golden footstool and threw it down to Earth at the old thief.

But since he couldn’t retrieve the stool after that, he quietly left the chair, returned to his spot behind the door, and pretended like he had never moved.

When the Lord came back with the heavenly company, he didn’t see the tailor hiding behind the door.

However, when He sat down, He noticed the footstool was missing.

'Peter, what happened to the stool?' He asked.

Peter replied, 'I’m not sure, but I know a lame tailor is sitting behind the door.'

The Lord had the tailor brought before Him and asked, 'Did you take the stool? Where is it?'

'Oh, Lord!' the tailor said joyfully, 'I threw it down to Earth in my anger at an old woman I saw stealing two veils while washing.'

'You knave!' said the Lord. 'If I judged as you do, how do you think you could have lasted so long? I would have had no chairs, benches, or even an oven fork left, having thrown them all down at the sinners.'

'You can no longer stay in heaven; you must go back outside again. Go wherever you like; you won’t be punished.'

Peter had to take the tailor out of heaven again, and as he had tattered shoes and blistered feet, he picked up a stick and went to find a place where good soldiers hang out.

title: The Thief and His Master
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Hans wanted his son to learn a trade, so he decided to go to the

church and pray to God, asking for guidance on what would be the best

one for him. Then the clerk stepped behind the altar and said,

"thieving, thieving." Hans returned to his son and told him that

he was to learn to be a thief because that’s what God had said.

So he set off with his son to find someone who knew the ins and outs of

thieving.

They walked for a long time until they reached a vast forest, where they

found a small house with an old woman living inside. Hans asked her,

"Do you know anyone who can teach thieving?" The woman replied,

"You can learn that quite well here; my son is a master at it." So, he

spoke with her son and asked if he was truly skilled in thieving.

The master-thief assured him, "I will teach him thoroughly. Come back in a

year, and if you recognize your son, I won’t ask for any payment. But if

you don’t know him, you’ll owe me two hundred talers."

Hans went home, and his son learned both witchcraft and thieving quite

well. When the year was over, Hans felt anxious about how he would recognize

his son. As he wandered anxiously, he encountered a little dwarf who asked,

"What’s got you so troubled?"

Hans replied, "A year ago, I placed my son with a master-thief who told me to

come back when the year is up. If I don’t recognize my son, I owe two hundred

talers, but if I do, I pay nothing. Now I’m scared I won’t recognize him and don’t

know how I’ll get the money."

The dwarf advised him to take a crust of bread and stand beneath the chimney.

He said, "On the cross-beam, there's a basket where a little bird is peeking out, and

that’s your son."

Following the dwarf's advice, Hans went and tossed a crust of black bread in front of

title: The Thief and His Master
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
the basket. The little bird emerged and looked up. "Hello, my son, is that you?"

the father called out. The son was thrilled to see his father, but the master-thief

said, "You must have had some help, or how could you possibly know your son?"

"Dad, let’s go," said the young man.

So, father and son began their journey back home. On the way, a carriage passed by,

and the son said, "I’ll transform into a big greyhound. You can make a lot of money by selling me."

The gentleman in the carriage called out, "Hey there, will you sell your dog?"

"Yes," said Hans. "How much do you want for him?"

"Thirty talers." "That’s quite a bit, but since he's such a fine dog, I’ll take him."

The gentleman took the dog into his carriage, but soon enough, the dog leaped out

of the window and ran back to his father, turning back into a human.

They went home together. The next day, a fair was happening in the nearby town.

The son said to his father, "I’ll become a beautiful horse now, and you can sell me.

But remember, once you sell me, make sure to take off my bridle, or I can’t turn back.

" Hans took the horse to the fair, and soon the master-thief bought it for a hundred talers.

However, Hans forgot to take off the bridle.

So the master-thief brought the horse home and put it in the stable.

When the maid entered, the horse called out, "Take off my bridle, take off my bridle!"

The maid, surprised, asked, "Wait, you can talk?"

She took off the bridle, and instantly, the horse turned into a sparrow.

It flew out the door, and the master-thief transformed into a sparrow, flying after him.

They met up again and decided to cast lots, with the master losing this time.

So the master turned into a rooster, and the young man transformed into a fox, and in a swift

moment, the fox bit off the rooster’s head, ending his life.

title: The Three Apprentices
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there were three apprentices who had promised to always stick together.

They agreed to travel together and work in the same town.

But one day, their masters ran out of work for them,

and soon enough, they found themselves in rags and without food.

One of them spoke up, saying, "What should we do? We can’t stay here any longer."

"Let’s travel again," he continued, "and if we can’t find work in the next town,

we’ll ask the innkeeper to keep us informed of each other’s whereabouts.

Then we can part ways if we need to." The others agreed that this seemed like a good plan.

They set out on their journey and soon met a well-dressed man who asked who they were.

"We’re apprentices looking for work," they replied. "Until now, we’ve stayed together,

but if we can’t find any work, we plan to split up."

"There’s no need for that," said the man. "If you follow my advice,

you’ll never lack for gold or work. In fact, you’ll become great lords and ride in carriages."

One of the apprentices replied, "As long as our souls aren’t in danger, we'll do it."

The man assured them, "You’ll be fine; I have no hold on you."

But one of the apprentices noticed something off — he spotted a horse's foot mixed with a man's foot,

and he wanted nothing to do with him.

Then the man said, "Don't worry, I’m only after another soul, one that is already half mine.

I just want to make sure my claim is complete."

Feeling secure, they agreed, and the man laid out what he wanted.

First, they had to respond to every question as a team.

Second, they had to say "for money," and third, they should always say, "and quite right too."

They were to repeat these phrases, one after the other, without adding anything else,

title: The Three Apprentices
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
or else they would lose all their money.

As long as they followed those rules, their pockets would always be full.

To kick things off, he gave them as much gold as they could carry and told them to go to a specific inn.

They arrived at the inn, and the innkeeper greeted them, asking if they wanted something to eat.

The first apprentice answered, "All three of us!"

"Yes, that’s what I mean," said the innkeeper.

The second apprentice added, "For money."

"Of course," replied the innkeeper.

The third chimed in, "And quite right too!"

"Absolutely right," said the innkeeper with a smile.

Good food and drink were soon served, and they enjoyed a nice meal.

After finishing dinner, it was time to settle the bill.

The innkeeper handed the bill to the first apprentice, who replied, "All three of us."

The second said, "For money," and the third added, "And quite right too."

The innkeeper nodded, saying, "That’s only fair, but I can’t give anything without payment."

However, they ended up paying even more than expected.

Other guests watched and whispered, "Those guys must be crazy."

"Yeah, they really aren’t the sharpest," the innkeeper agreed.

They stayed for a while at the inn, repeating only, "All three of us, for money, and quite right too."

Yet, they were aware of everything happening around them.

Then one day, a wealthy merchant came in with a large sum of money,

saying, "Hey, innkeeper, please keep my money safe; those three crazy apprentices might steal it!"

The innkeeper obliged. As he carried the merchant's trunk to his room,

he could tell it was heavy with gold.

So, he gave the three apprentices a room downstairs while the merchant got a private room upstairs.

Later that night, when the innkeeper thought everyone was asleep,

he came back with his wife, wielding an axe, and murdered the merchant.

After killing him, they returned to their own beds.

title: The Three Apprentices
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When morning arrived, there was a huge outcry.

The merchant was found dead in his bed, covered in blood.

Guests rushed out, but the innkeeper quickly accused, "It was those three crazy apprentices who did it!"

The other lodgers agreed, saying, "It could only be them!"

So the innkeeper summoned them and asked, "Did you kill the merchant?"

"All three of us did," the first apprentice replied.

"For money," said the second apprentice.

And the third added, "And quite right too!"

"See? They’re confessing!" the innkeeper exclaimed.

The three apprentices were jailed and awaited their trial.

Feeling scared, they began to worry as things turned serious.

That night, the devil returned and advised them, "Just hang on for one more day and don’t let go of your luck. I won’t let a single hair on your head be harmed."

The next morning, they were led to court, and the judge asked, "Are you the murderers?"

"All three of us," they responded together.

"Why did you kill the merchant?" the judge pressed.

"For money," they replied.
