"You monsters! Don’t you even feel a shred of guilt?"

"And quite right too!" they replied.

The judge, infuriated, ordered, "Take them away for execution!"

So they were taken out, and the innkeeper had to follow them to the gallows.

Just as they were about to be executed and the executioner was ready with his sword,

a carriage pulled by four chestnut horses appeared, racing so fast that sparks flew off the ground.

Someone waved a white handkerchief out of the window.

The hangman paused and exclaimed, "A pardon is coming!"

The voice from the carriage echoed, "Pardon! Pardon!"

Then the devil appeared, dressed elegantly, and stated, "You three are innocent.

You may speak and share what you have seen and heard."

The oldest apprentice then said, "We didn’t kill the merchant.

The real murderer is standing right there, and he pointed at the innkeeper."

He added, "If you check his cellar, you’ll find many more victims he has murdered."

The judge sent the executioners to investigate, and they found everything the apprentices had said to be true.

When they reported back to the judge, he ordered the innkeeper to be brought forth, and his head was taken as punishment.

The devil then smiled at the three apprentices, saying, "Now that I have the soul I sought, you are free.

You can keep the money you earned."

title: The Three Army-Surgeons
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Three army surgeons believed they had mastered their craft completely.

As they traveled around the world, they stopped at an inn where

they wanted to spend the night. The innkeeper asked them where they were coming from,

and where they were headed. "We’re exploring the world and

practicing our skills," they replied. "Show me what you can do," said

the innkeeper. The first surgeon insisted he would cut off his hand and reattach it by morning.

The second claimed he would pull out his heart and put it back by the next morning.

The third said he would gouge out his eyes and restore them by morning. "If you can

do that," said the innkeeper, "you've truly mastered your art." However,

the surgeons had a special ointment they always carried with them,

which they used to heal themselves. They had a small bottle of it with them.

Then they followed through on their promises, cutting off their hand, heart,

and eyes as they had said, placing them all on a plate to give to the innkeeper.

The innkeeper handed it to a servant girl who was supposed to store it in the

cupboard and take care of it. But the girl secretly had a boyfriend who was a soldier.

So, when the innkeeper, the three surgeons, and everyone else in the inn went to sleep,

the soldier arrived looking for something to eat. The girl opened

the cupboard to get him some food, and in her excitement, she forgot to close the door.

She sat at the table with her boyfriend, and they chatted happily together.

While she was lost in her thoughts, a cat snuck in, found the open cupboard,

and stole the hand, heart, and eyes of the three surgeons.

When the soldier finished eating, and the girl was clearing the table and getting ready to close the cupboard,

she noticed that the plate the innkeeper had given her was empty.

Panic set in as she said to her boyfriend, "Oh no, what am I going to do? The hand is gone,

the heart and the eyes are gone too. What will happen to me in the morning?"

title: The Three Army-Surgeons
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Don't worry," he reassured her. "I’ll help you. There's a thief

hanging on the gallows outside; I'll cut off his hand. Which hand should I take?"

"The right one," she replied. So, the girl handed him a sharp knife,

and he went outside to cut off the unfortunate thief's right hand.

Then he caught the cat and gouged its eyes out, but now they still needed a heart.

"Haven't you been slaughtering? Don't you have dead pigs in the cellar?" he asked.

"Yes," she said. "That's true," he said, and went down to fetch a pig's heart.

The girl placed everything on the plate and put it back in the cupboard. When her boyfriend left,

she quietly went to bed.

The next morning, when the three surgeons awoke, they asked the girl to bring them the plate with the

hand, heart, and eyes. She retrieved it from the cupboard, and the first surgeon attached the thief's hand,

rubbing it with his ointment. Instantly, it reconnected to his arm.

The second surgeon placed the cat's eyes into his own head.

The third attached the pig's heart where his own had been, as the innkeeper watched, amazed by their skill.

"I've never seen anything like this before!" he proclaimed, and he promised to tell everyone about their talents.

Then they paid their bill and continued on their journey.

As they walked, the one who had received the pig's heart didn't stay with the others at all.

Whenever he spotted a corner, he would rush towards it, sniffing around like a pig.

His companions tried to pull him back by the tail of his coat, but it didn’t work.

He broke free and darted off toward the messiest places.

The second surgeon acted strangely too. He rubbed his eyes, saying, "Guys, something's not right.

These aren't my eyes! I can't see! Can one of you help me not fall?"

title: The Three Army-Surgeons
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
So, with great effort, they pressed on until evening, arriving at another inn.

They entered the bar together, where a rich man sat counting his money at a table in the corner.

The one with the thief's hand walked around him, making awkward movements with his arm,

and finally, when the stranger turned his head, he snatched a handful of cash.

One of his companions saw this and said, "Hey, what are you doing? You can’t steal! That's shameful!"

"Oh, come on," he replied. "My hand just twitches, and I can’t help grabbing things!"

Later that night, they settled down to sleep, but it was so dark that no one could see their own hands.

Suddenly, the one with the cat's eyes woke up, nudged the others, and said, "Guys, look up! Do you see those white mice?

The other two sat up, but they couldn’t see anything. Then he exclaimed, "Something's off. We haven't received our own back.

We need to go back to the innkeeper. He tricked us!" So they returned the next morning,

and told the innkeeper that they hadn't received their own belongings; the first had a thief's hand,

the second had cat's eyes, and the third had a pig's heart.

"It must be that girl's fault," replied the innkeeper. He was about to summon her when she saw the three of them coming,

and she slipped out the back door, not to return.

The three then insisted he had to give them a lot of money, or they would set a red rooster on his roof.

- An ancient symbol of fire. - He handed them everything he had,

and whatever he could gather, and the three left with it.

It was enough for the rest of their lives, but they still would’ve preferred to have their own body parts back.

title: The Three Black Princesses
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
In East India, an enemy laid siege to the town, refusing to leave until he received a hefty sum.

He demanded six hundred dollars from the townspeople.

So, they beat a drum to announce that whoever could gather the money would become the burgomaster.

Among them was a poor fisherman who fished the sea with his son, but the enemy captured his son.

In exchange for the boy, the enemy gave the father six hundred dollars.

The fisherman handed over the money to the town’s leaders, and the enemy left, allowing him to become the burgomaster.

Then it was declared that anyone who failed to address him as 'Mr. Burgomaster' would face the gallows.

The son managed to escape the enemy and found himself in a vast forest atop a high mountain.

Suddenly, the mountain opened up, and he entered a grand, enchanted castle.

Inside, everything was draped in black—chairs, tables, and benches alike.

Three young princesses appeared, completely dressed in black but with a hint of white on their faces.

They reassured him, telling him not to be afraid and that he could rescue them.

He eagerly agreed, wishing he knew how to help.

In response, they explained that he must not speak to or look at them for an entire year.

He could ask for whatever he needed, and if they dared to answer, they would.

After a long time, he expressed his desire to return to his father, and they permitted him to go.

They instructed him to take a purse of money and wear a specific coat, and he must return within a week.

In an instant, he found himself back in East India.

However, he couldn't find his father in their old fishing hut and asked nearby people for his father's whereabouts.

They warned him not to say that, or he would end up at the gallows.

Determined, he sought out his father and asked, 'Fisherman, how did you end up here?'

His father replied, 'You must not ask that; if the townsfolk hear, you'll be hanged.'

Despite the warning, he stood his ground and was taken to the gallows.

Standing there, he pleaded, 'Oh, my masters, just let me go to the old fisherman's hut.'

Once there, he donned his old smock and returned to the town leaders, declaring, 'Can’t you see? I am the poor fisherman’s son! Did I not earn my meals in this outfit?'

His father instantly recognized him, apologized, and took him home.

Once there, he shared everything that had happened, including his encounter with the enchanted castle.

He recounted how he met three princesses dressed in black with just a touch of white.

They had comforted him and said he could rescue them.

His mother suggested it might not be wise to try, and advised him to take a blessed candle and some boiling wax to drop on their faces.

He returned, trembling with fear, and dripped the wax on the princesses' faces while they slept, causing them to turn half-white.

Suddenly, all three princesses woke up, enraged, shouting, 'You cursed dog! Our blood will cry for vengeance!'

They declared that no one in the world could save them, and warned that their three brothers, bound by seven chains, would tear him apart.

In a panic, he heard a loud commotion throughout the castle, jumped out the window, and broke his leg.

Meanwhile, the castle sank back into the earth, sealing the mountain once again, leaving no trace of its existence.

title: The Three Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a man with three sons, and that was all he had in the world.

Each son wanted the family house after their father passed away, but the father loved all of them equally.

He was uncertain about what to do; selling the house wasn't an option because it had been in their family for generations.

Instead, he came up with a plan.

He told his sons, "Go out into the world and learn a trade. When you return, whoever creates the best masterpiece will inherit the house."

The brothers were excited about this and each chose a different path: the eldest became a blacksmith, the second a barber, and the youngest a fencing master.

They agreed on a time to return home after their training.

All three brothers ended up finding skilled masters who taught them their trades really well.

The blacksmith had the honor of shoeing the king's horses, and he thought to himself, "I’m definitely getting that house."

The barber only shaved distinguished clients, and he too was already imagining the house as his own.

The fencing master took his share of hits but pushed through, thinking, "If you're scared of a hit, you'll never earn that house."

When the time finally came, the three brothers returned to their father, but they weren't sure how to showcase their skills.

So, they sat down together to brainstorm.

As they were chatting, a hare suddenly dashed across the field.

"Ah, this is perfect!" said the barber.

He quickly grabbed his basin and soap, and as the hare approached, he skillfully soaped and shaved off the hare's whiskers while it was on the run, without cutting its skin or harming a single hair.

"Well done," said their father. "If your brothers don't step up, the house is yours."

Not long after, a nobleman sped by in a coach.

"Now watch what I can do, Father," declared the blacksmith.

He dashed after the coach, quickly removed all four shoes from one of the horses while it was galloping, and put on four new shoes without pausing.

"You're impressive, just like your brother," said their father. "I’m not sure who to choose!"

Then the third son chimed in, "Father, let me have a turn, please." With the rain starting to pour, he pulled out his sword and twirled it above his head so fast that not a drop fell on him.

Even as the rain intensified, he kept swirling the sword faster, staying completely dry as if he were in a cozy home.

When their father saw this, he was astonished, declaring, "This is the masterpiece! The house is yours."

The brothers agreed with this decision, and out of their love for each other, they chose to stay together in the house.

They followed their trades, and since they had learned so well, they made a lot of money.

They lived happily together until they grew old.

Eventually, one of them fell sick and passed away, which saddened the other two so deeply that they soon fell ill and died as well.

Because they had been so clever and had loved each other so much, they were united once again.

title: The Three Feathers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a king with three sons. The first two were clever and wise, while the third was quiet and simple, earning him the name "the Simpleton."

As the king grew old and frail, he started to worry about who would inherit his kingdom. He wanted to find the best successor among his sons.

To solve this, he said to his sons, "Go out and whoever brings me the most beautiful carpet will become king."

To make sure there was no argument between them, he took them outside the castle, blew three feathers into the air, and instructed, "Follow wherever they go."

One feather flew east, another went west, and the third floated straight up, not traveling far before it fell.

The brothers split up: one went right, the other left, and they laughed at the Simpleton, who was stuck where the third feather had landed.

Feeling sad, he sat down and noticed a trap-door nearby the feather. He lifted it, discovered some stairs, and ventured down.

He reached another door, knocked, and heard a voice from inside calling, "Waiting-maid with the limping leg, hurry and see who's there!"

The door swung open, revealing a large, plump toad surrounded by a group of tiny toads. The big toad asked what he wanted.

He replied, "I’d like the most beautiful and finest carpet in the world." The toad then summoned a young toad and commanded, "Waiting-maid with the limping leg,"

The young toad brought a box, and the fat toad opened it, presenting the Simpleton with a carpet so stunning and exquisite that nothing like it existed on earth.

He thanked her profusely. Meanwhile, his older brothers thought so little of him that they were convinced he wouldn’t find anything.

"Why should we waste time searching?" they said, and simply grabbed some rough handkerchiefs from the first shepherds' wives they saw and took them back to the king.

At the same time, the Simpleton returned with his beautiful carpet, and when the king saw it, he was amazed and declared, "If justice is to be done, the kingdom belongs to the youngest."

title: The Three Feathers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
However, the two older sons wouldn’t let it go; they insisted it was impossible for the Simpleton, who lacked sense in everything, to be king, and urged their father to set a new challenge.

The king agreed and announced, "Whoever brings me the most beautiful ring shall inherit the kingdom." He led the three brothers out again and blew three feathers into the air.

As before, the feathers scattered, with the eldest two's going east and west, while the Simpleton's feather shot straight up and dropped near a door in the ground.

He went down to the big toad again and asked for the most beautiful ring. She immediately called for her big box and handed him a ring that sparkled with jewels, so beautiful that no goldsmith could ever match it.

The older brothers mocked him for seeking a golden ring. They couldn't be bothered with effort, so they pried the nails out of an old carriage ring and took it to the king.

But when the Simpleton presented his golden ring, his father once again declared that the kingdom belonged to him.

The two brothers kept nagging the king until he proposed a third condition: whoever brought back the most beautiful woman would be king.

Once again, he released three feathers, and they flew off into the sky.

The Simpleton went back to the big toad and said, "I need to take home the most beautiful woman." The toad replied, "Oh, the most beautiful woman isn't here right now, but I'll still help you.

She handed him a yellow turnip that had been hollowed out and attached to six mice. The Simpleton sadly exclaimed, "What am I supposed to do with that?"

The toad comforted him, saying, "Just put one of my little toads inside it." He randomly picked one from the circle and placed it into the yellow coach.

As soon as she sat down, she transformed into a breathtaking maiden, the turnip changed into a real coach, and the six mice became horses.

He kissed her, hopped into the coach, and rode off quickly.

Meanwhile, his brothers, who had made little effort, brought back the first peasant women they stumbled upon.

When the king saw them, he declared, "After my death, the kingdom will belong to my youngest son." Yet the older brothers were relentless, shouting that the Simpleton shouldn’t be king.

They insisted that whoever’s wife could leap through a ring hanging in the center of the hall should be chosen.

They thought the peasant women would have no trouble with that, as they were strong, unlike the delicate maiden.

The old king agreed to this challenge. The two peasant women jumped, but they were so clumsy that they fell and broke their arms and legs.

Then, the lovely maiden the Simpleton had brought leaped gracefully through the ring like a deer, leaving no room for opposition.

Thus, he was crowned king and ruled wisely for a long time.

title: The Three Green Twigs
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a hermit living in a forest at the

base of a mountain. He spent his days in prayer and doing good deeds,

and each evening, he would carry two buckets of water up the mountain,

doing it all for the glory of God. Many animals drank from it, and many plants

were refreshed by it, as a fierce wind blew up high,

drying out the air and the ground. The wild

birds, who feared humans, flew around looking for a drink.

Because the hermit was so devout, an angel from God, visible to him, accompanied

him, counting his steps. Once he finished his task, the angel brought him food,

just like how a raven had once fed a prophet of old. As the hermit grew older in his piety,

he happened to see a poor sinner being taken to the gallows.

He thought to himself, 'Well, that person is getting what they deserve.'

But later that evening, as he was carrying the water up the mountain, the angel who usually came with him didn’t show up,

and he didn’t receive any food either. This filled him with fear,

and he searched his heart, wondering how he could have sinned to make God so angry.

He couldn't find anything he had done wrong. So, he neither ate nor drank,

threw himself on the ground, and prayed day and night.

As he was weeping bitterly in the forest one day, he heard a small bird singing beautifully,

and that made him feel even more troubled. He said,

'You sing so joyfully; the Lord isn’t angry with you. If only you could tell me how I’ve offended Him,

so I could do penance and find peace in my heart again.'

Then, the bird spoke and said, 'You have acted unjustly by condemning a poor sinner being led to the gallows,

and for that, the Lord is upset with you. Only He can truly judge.

However, if you repent for your sins, He will forgive you.'

At that moment, the angel appeared next to him holding a dry branch.

'You must carry this dry branch until three green twigs sprout from it,

title: The Three Green Twigs
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
but every night when you sleep, lay it under your head.

You will need to beg for your food door to door, and can’t stay more than one night in any house.

That is the penance the Lord has set for you.'

The hermit took the branch and returned to the world he hadn’t seen in so long.

He ate and drank only what was given to him at each door. However,

many people didn’t listen to his requests, and numerous doors remained closed,

leaving him sometimes without even a crumb of bread.

One day, after going from door to door from morning till night,

and receiving nothing from anyone, he wandered into a forest.

Eventually, he found a cave where an old woman was sitting.

He said, 'Good woman, please let me stay with you tonight.'

But she replied, 'I can’t. Even if I wanted to, I have three wild sons.

If they come home from their robbing and find you here, they’ll kill us both.'

The hermit insisted, 'Let me stay. They won’t harm you or me.'

The old woman, feeling compassionate, was persuaded.

So, the hermit lay down beneath the stairs and put the piece of wood under his head.

When the old woman saw him do this, she asked why.

He explained that he carried the piece of wood as penance and used it as a pillow,

and shared that he had offended the Lord by thinking the poor sinner deserved his fate.

The woman began to weep, saying, 'If the Lord punishes you for one single word,

how will it fare with my sons when they stand before Him?'

Later that night, the robbers returned home, shouting and arguing.

They lit a fire, and when they saw the man lying beneath the stairs,

they became furious and yelled at their mother, 'Who is this man?

Didn’t we tell you no one could stay here?'

Their mother replied, 'Leave him alone; he’s just a poor sinner doing penance for his crime.'

The robbers asked, 'What has he done? Old man,' they shouted, 'tell us your sins.'

The old man sat up and explained how just one careless word had angered God,

and how he was trying to make up for that now.

The robbers were so moved by his story that they were deeply shaken,

reflected on their own lives, and began to sincerely repent.

After the hermit had converted the three sinners, he lay back down under the stairs.

In the morning, they found him dead, and from the dry wood where his head had rested,

three green twigs had sprouted.

Thus, the Lord had once again welcomed him into His grace.

title: The Three Languages
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
In Switzerland, there was once an elderly count who had only one son.

Unfortunately, the boy was not the sharpest tool in the shed and struggled to learn anything.

The father sighed and said, "Listen, my son, no matter how hard I try, I can't seem to get anything into your head.

You need to leave this place; I’m going to send you to a well-known teacher who might help you learn.

So, the young man was sent away to a new town, where he stayed with the master for an entire year.

When he returned home, his father eagerly asked him, "Well, my son, what have you learned?

The son replied, "Father, I learned what dogs say when they bark."

The father was horrified and exclaimed, "Goodness! Is that all you have learned? I’ll send you to another teacher!

The young man was taken to yet another town where he spent a year with a different master.

When he returned, his father asked once more, "What have you learned this time?

He said, "Father, I learned what birds say."

The father became enraged and shouted, "Oh, you foolish boy! You've wasted your time and learned nothing.

Aren't you ashamed to show your face to me? I will send you to a third master, but if you come back empty-handed again, I will no longer consider you my son."

So, the young man spent another year with yet another master.

Once more, when he returned home, his father asked, "What have you learned, my son?

The son replied, "Dear Father, this year I learned what frogs croak."

The father, now completely furious, jumped up, called for his servants, and declared, "This man is no longer my son!

I banish him! Take him to the forest and kill him!

The servants took him away, but when it came time to carry out the order, they felt too sorry for him and let him go.

Instead, they killed a deer and cut out its eyes and tongue to bring back as proof.

title: The Three Languages
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The young man wandered off and eventually stumbled upon a fortress where he asked for a place to stay for the night.

The lord of the castle replied, "You can sleep in the old tower, but be warned, it's dangerous in there.

It’s filled with wild dogs that bark and howl endlessly, and every so often, they must be fed a human they devour immediately.

The whole region was terrified of them, yet no one could do anything about it.

However, the young man felt fearless and said, "Just let me go to the barking dogs, and give me something to throw; they won’t harm me."

So, they handed him some food for the wild animals and led him to the tower.

When he entered, the dogs didn’t bark at him; instead, they wagged their tails joyfully, ate the food he offered, and didn’t harm him at all.

The next morning, everyone was amazed when he came out unhurt and told the lord of the castle, "The dogs revealed to me, in their own language, why they’re here and causing trouble.

They are enchanted and must guard a treasure hidden beneath the tower, and they can’t find peace until it’s taken away.

I also learned how to do that from what they told me."

All who heard this were overjoyed, and the lord of the castle said he would adopt him as a son if he succeeded.

The young man went back down, knowing exactly what to do, and successfully brought out a chest full of gold.

From that day forward, the wild dogs’ howls were never heard again; they disappeared, freeing the land from their terror.

After a while, the young man decided he wanted to travel to Rome.

On his journey, he passed a marsh full of croaking frogs.

He listened to their croaking, and as he understood what they were saying, he became thoughtful and sad.

Eventually, he arrived in Rome just as the Pope had died, and there was much debate among the cardinals about who should be the next.

They finally concluded that the next Pope should be someone marked by a divine sign.

Just then, as this was decided, the young count entered the church, and two snow-white doves flew down and landed on his shoulders.

The church leaders saw this as a sign from above and immediately asked if he would accept the position of Pope.

Though he was unsure and questioned his worthiness, the doves encouraged him to accept, and he ultimately said yes.

He was then anointed and consecrated, fulfilling what he had heard from the frogs, which had moved him deeply—he was to be the new Pope.

Next, he had to lead a mass, but he didn’t know a word of it.

Fortunately, the two doves stayed on his shoulders, whispering the prayers in his ear.

title: The Three Little Birds
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Around a thousand years ago, in a land filled with small kings,

there was one king who lived on the Keuterberg, and he loved to hunt.

One day, as he rode out from his castle with his huntsmen,

three girls were nearby, watching their cows on the mountain.

When they spotted the king and his entourage, the oldest girl shouted to the others.

"Hey! Look! If I don't get him, I don't want anyone!" The second girl,

called out from the other side, pointing to the king's right-hand man.

"Hey! If I don't get him, I don't want anyone either!" They were actually referring to

the king's two ministers. The king overheard their conversation and, after returning from his hunt,

he summoned the three girls and asked them what they had said on the mountain.

They refused to reveal it, so the king turned to the eldest and asked, "Do you really want to marry me?"

She said yes, and the two ministers married the other two sisters,

because they were all three beautiful, especially the queen, who had hair like golden flax.

However, the two sisters never had any children. One day, when the king had to leave,

he invited them to visit the queen to keep her company, as she was about to have a baby.

She gave birth to a little boy who entered the world with a bright red star.

The two sisters, feeling envious, plotted to throw the beautiful boy into the water.

After tossing him in—believed to be into the Weser—a little bird flew into the sky, singing:

"When the sisters heard the song, they were terrified and quickly fled.

When the king returned, they falsely told him the queen had given birth to a dog.

The king replied, "What God does is well done." However, a fisherman living near the river

fished the little boy out, saving him while he was still alive. Since

the fisherman's wife had no children, they decided to raise him.

A year later, the king went away again, and the queen had another boy.

title: The Three Little Birds
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The deceitful sisters once more threw him into the water.

Again, a little bird flew up, and when the king returned,

they told him the queen had given birth to another dog. The king replied,

"What God does is well done." Once again, the fisherman saved the boy.

Then the king left again, and this time the queen had a little girl,

who the sisters also threw into the water.

Yet again, a little bird flew up and sang. When the king returned, he was told the queen had

given birth to a cat. This made the king furious, and he ordered his wife to be imprisoned.

In prison, the queen became quite ill.

As the children grew up, the eldest boy once went out with some other boys to fish, but

the others rejected him, saying, "Go away, foundling!"

He was upset and asked the old fisherman if it was true.

The fisherman explained that he had fished him out of the water.

Determined, the boy decided to find his father. Though the fisherman urged him to stay,

he insisted on going. Eventually, the fisherman agreed.

The boy traveled for many days until he reached a large body of water, where he saw an old woman fishing.

"Good day, ma'am," he greeted her.

"You might fish for a long time before you catch anything," she replied.

"And you might search for your father for a long time as well. How will you cross the water?"

The old woman then lifted him onto her back and carried him across.

He searched for days but couldn't find his father.

After a year passed, the second boy set out to find his brother.

He reached the water where the same thing happened as with his brother.

At home, only the girl remained, and she grieved for her siblings.

Having lost hope, she also asked the fisherman to let her go search.

She arrived at the great stretch of water and spoke with the old woman.

"Thank you," she said.

"May God assist you in your journey," replied the old woman.

Feeling a friendly connection, she carried the girl across the water, gave her a wand, and said,

title: The Three Little Birds
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Keep going straight on this path. When you see a great black dog,

you must pass it quietly and confidently, without laughing or looking at it. Then you'll reach a high castle.

When you reach the threshold, drop the wand and walk through the castle.

There, you'll find an old fountain with a large tree growing out of it, where a bird is caged.

You must take the bird down and also fill a glass of water from the fountain.

After that, retrace your steps while picking up the wand again.

When you pass the dog again, hit it in the face with the wand, but make sure you connect.

Then come back to me."

The girl found everything just as the old woman described, and on her way back, she met her two brothers.

They had been searching for one another across the world.

Together, they reached the spot where the black dog lay.

She struck it in the face, and it transformed into a handsome prince who followed them.

At the river, the old woman was still there, delighted to see them.

She carried all three of them across the water before leaving, now free.

Back on shore, they rejoiced at being reunited, but they hung the bird on the wall.

Yet the second son felt restless at home, took his crossbow,

and went hunting. When he felt tired, he played his flute.

The king, also out hunting, heard the music and followed it.

When he met the young man, he exclaimed, "Who taught you to play so well?"

"If you don't believe me, come with me," the boy replied.

The king agreed and asked the fisherman, who told him everything.

At that moment, the little bird on the wall began to sing.

Everyone was frightened, and the king took the bird, the fisherman, and the three children back to the castle.

He ordered the prison to be opened and brought his wife out.

She was weak and ill, so the daughter gave her some of the fountain water, restoring her health.

Meanwhile, the two false sisters faced punishment for their actions, and the daughter married the prince.

title: The Three Little Men in the Wood
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a man who lost his wife and a woman who lost her husband.

The man had a daughter, and the woman had a daughter as well.

The girls knew each other and often went out for walks together.

One day, they stopped by the woman's house.

The woman said to the man's daughter, "Listen, tell your father I want to marry him, and in return, you’ll get to wash in milk every morning and drink wine, while my daughter will have to wash in water and drink water instead."

The girl went home and told her father what the woman had said.

The man pondered, "What should I do? Marrying could be both a joy and a torment."

Finally, unable to decide, he took off his boot,

and said, "Take this boot. It has a hole in the sole. Go to the attic, hang it on the big nail, and fill it with water. If it holds the water, I’ll marry again; if it leaks, then I won’t."

The girl did as instructed, but surprisingly, the hole closed up and the boot filled to the brim.

She informed her father about the outcome.

Seeing that his daughter was right, he went to the widow, courted her, and soon afterward, they were married.

The next morning, when the two girls woke up, the man's daughter found milk waiting for her to wash in, along with wine to drink.

The woman's daughter, on the other hand, had only water for bathing and drinking.

On the second morning, both girls found only water for washing and drinking.

But on the third morning, the man's daughter had milk and wine again, while the woman's daughter was stuck with water.

As time went on, the woman grew increasingly bitter towards her stepdaughter, going out of her way to treat her poorly.

She was also envious because her stepdaughter was lovely and kind, while her own child was not.

One winter, when everything was frozen solid and snow covered the land, the woman made a dress out of paper and called for her stepdaughter.

title: The Three Little Men in the Wood
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Put on this dress and go out into the woods to fetch me a basket of strawberries. I’m really craving some!"

The girl replied, "But there are no strawberries in winter! The ground is frozen, and the snow has buried everything. And why should I wear this flimsy paper dress? It's freezing outside, and I’ll be just as cold as ice. The wind will blow through it, and thorns will tear it off me!"

The stepmother snapped back, "You dare go against me? You must go out and don’t come back until you’ve brought me strawberries!"

Then she handed the girl a small piece of stale bread, saying, "This will last you for the day." She thought to herself that the girl would probably freeze or starve outside.

Nevertheless, the maiden obeyed, donned the paper dress, and left with the basket.

All around her was blanketed with snow, with not a hint of green anywhere.

Once she entered the woods, she spotted a small house and three little men peeking out.

She greeted them politely and knocked softly on the door.

They called, "Come in!" So she entered, sat down on a bench by the stove, warmed up, and ate her breakfast.

The little men asked, "Can we have some too?"

"Of course!" she replied, splitting her bread in half to share with them.

They then asked, "What brings you to the forest in such thin clothing in the winter?"

"I’m looking for a basket of strawberries and can’t return home until I find some," she answered.

After she finished her bread, they handed her a broom and said, "You should sweep the snow away from the back door."

Outside, as she swept, the little men talked amongst themselves about what to give her for her kindness in sharing.

The first said, "I’ll grant that she becomes more beautiful every day."

The second added, "My gift is that gold coins will fall from her mouth every time she speaks."

And the third declared, "My gift is that a king will come and take her as his wife."

title: The Three Little Men in the Wood
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The girl followed their instructions, and as she cleared the snow, she stumbled upon real, ripe strawberries that were miraculously growing amidst the snow.

Overjoyed, she quickly filled her basket, thanked the little men, shook their hands, and rushed home with her prize.

When she walked in and said "Good evening," a gold piece fell from her mouth.

She excitedly shared her adventure in the woods, and each word brought forth more gold coins until the entire room was filled.

Her step-sister, jealous, exclaimed, "Look at her flaunting gold like that!"

But meanwhile, she secretly wanted to venture out to the forest herself.

The mother responded, "No, my dear, it’s far too cold. You might freeze to death!"

However, since her daughter wouldn’t stop pestering, the mother eventually agreed, giving her a luxurious fur coat to wear, along with some bread and cake to take.

The girl set off for the forest to find the little house.

The three little men peeked out again, but this time she didn’t greet them.

Without a word, she clumsily stepped into the room, plopped down by the stove, and began to munch on her bread and cake.

The little men called out, "Can we have some?"

But she scoffed, "There’s hardly enough for me, so why would I share?"

After finishing her meal, they offered her a broom, saying, "You should sweep at the back door."

"Sweep for yourselves!" she shot back. "I’m not your servant!"

Seeing that they wouldn’t give her anything, she stormed out.

The little men deliberated, "What should we give her since she’s so disrespectful and has such an envious heart?"

The first said, "I’ll curse her to grow uglier each day."

The second said, "At every word she speaks, a toad will hop out of her mouth."

And the third added, "She will meet a miserable end."

When the girl searched for strawberries and found none, she angrily returned home.

title: The Three Little Men in the Wood
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
As she opened her mouth to recount her adventure, a toad leaped out with every word she spoke, horrifying everyone around her.

The stepmother’s wrath grew, and she focused entirely on how to harm her pretty stepdaughter, whose beauty continued to blossom.

Eventually, she boiled some yarn in a cauldron and threw it on the poor girl’s shoulders.

"Take this axe and go to the frozen river to rinse the yarn!" she ordered.

The girl did as she was told, cutting a hole in the ice.

While she was busy, a magnificent carriage appeared, and the king sat inside.

The carriage stopped, and the king asked, "My child, who are you and what are you doing here?"

"I’m just a poor girl rinsing yarn," she replied.

Feeling compassion for her, the king noticed her beauty and said, "Would you come away with me?"

"Oh yes, with all my heart!" she responded, eager to escape her troubles.

She climbed into the carriage, and as they arrived at the palace, a grand wedding took place, just as the little men had promised.

A year passed, and the young queen gave birth to a son.

When the stepmother learned of the fortune the girl had, she came to the palace with her daughter, pretending to visit.

When the king was out, the evil woman grabbed the queen by the head, and her daughter seized her feet.

Together, they tossed her out of bed and flung her out the window into the river below.

Then the ugly daughter hopped into the bed, and the mother covered her up.

Upon the king’s return, he approached his wife, but the old woman said, "Shh! She’s lying in a feverish sweat. You must let her rest today."

The king, unsuspecting, left until the next morning.

When he spoke to his wife, every word she said unleashed a toad, replacing what used to be gold coins.

The old woman insisted that this was simply a symptom of her sweat and that it would pass.

During the night, a scullion noticed a duck swimming up the gutter, which said, "What about my guests?"

When it received no answer, it asked again, "And my guests, what may they do?"

"They’re sleeping soundly, too," it replied.

Then, the duck transformed back into the queen, nursed the baby, tidied up the bed, and slipped away down the gutter once more.

She repeated this for two nights.

On the third night, she instructed the scullion, "Go tell the king to take his sword and swing it three times over me at the threshold."

The scullion hurried to the king and delivered the message.

The king came with his sword and swung it three times over the spirit, and on the third swing, his wife appeared, alive and well just as she was before.

The king was overjoyed but decided to keep the queen hidden until Sunday, when the baby was to be baptized.

During the baptism, he asked, "What should happen to a person who drags another from their bed and throws them into the water?"

The wicked woman replied, "That person deserves to be thrown into a barrel full of nails and rolled down the hill into the water."

The king then declared, "You have just sentenced yourself!"

He demanded a barrel be brought out, placed the old woman and her daughter inside, nailed it shut, and rolled it down the hill into the river.

title: The Three Sluggards
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, a king had three sons, and he loved each of them equally.

He was unsure of which son to choose as his heir.

As he approached the end of his life, he called his sons to his bedside.

With a gentle voice, he said, "My dear children, I've been thinking about something to share with you.

Whoever among you is the laziest will inherit the kingdom."

The oldest son replied, "Father, that kingdom is mine. I'm so lazy that if I lie down to take a nap and a drop of water falls in my eye, I won’t even bother to open it just to stay asleep."

The second son said, "No, Father, the kingdom belongs to me because I’m so lazy that while I’m warming myself by the fire, I’d rather let my heel burn than move my leg."

Then the youngest son chimed in, "Actually, Father, the kingdom is mine. I’m so lazy that if someone were about to hang me and I already had the rope around my neck, I wouldn’t lift a finger to cut it free, even if you put a sharp knife in my hand!"

Hearing this, their father said, "You boys have outdone yourselves in laziness."

title: The Three Snake-Leaves
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a poor man who struggled to take care of his only son.

The son said, "Dear father, things are going so badly that I feel like I'm a burden to you.

I would rather leave and figure out how to earn my own living."

The father, filled with sorrow, gave him his blessing and said goodbye.

At that time, the king of a powerful empire was at war, so the young man joined the army.

When he faced the enemy, a fierce battle broke out.

It rained bullets, and his comrades fell all around.

When the leader was killed, the remaining soldiers were ready to flee.

But the young man stepped forward, boldly addressed them, and shouted, "We will not let our homeland be destroyed!"

Encouraged by his bravery, the others rallied behind him, and he led them to victory.

When the king learned that he owed the triumph to this young man alone, he raised him above the rest, rewarded him with great riches, and made him the kingdom's highest official.

The king had a stunning daughter who was also quite peculiar.

She had vowed not to marry anyone who didn't promise to be buried alive with her if she passed away first.

"If he loves me completely," she declared, "what use is life to him afterwards?"

On her part, she would do the same; if he died first, she would accompany him to the grave.

This odd oath scared off all potential suitors, but the young man was so captivated by her beauty that he cared for nothing else and boldly asked her father for her hand in marriage.

"But do you understand what you must promise?" asked the king.

"I must be buried with her if I outlive her," he replied, "but my love for her is so strong that I’m not afraid of the risk."

The king consented, and they celebrated a magnificent wedding.

For a while, they lived happily together.

But soon, the young queen fell gravely ill, and no doctor could save her.

As she lay there, lifeless, the young king remembered his promise and was horrified at the thought of being buried alive with her.

title: The Three Snake-Leaves
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But there was no way to escape it.

The king had stationed guards at every exit, and he couldn’t avoid his fate.

When the day came to bury her, he was taken down into the royal vault, and the door was locked behind them.

Next to the coffin, there was a table with four candles, four loaves of bread, and four bottles of wine.

When those provisions ran out, he would perish from hunger.

So, he sat there, overwhelmed with sorrow, eating just a tiny piece of bread each day and sipping only a bit of wine, all the while watching death approach.

As he stared into space, he saw a snake slither out from a corner of the vault and move toward the corpse.

Thinking it was there to gnaw at her, he drew his sword and shouted, "As long as I live, you won’t touch her!" and he sliced the snake into three pieces.

After a while, a second snake appeared, and upon seeing the other dead, it quickly retreated.

But soon, it returned with three green leaves in its mouth.

It placed the severed pieces together, for they matched perfectly, and laid one leaf on each wound.

Instantly, the parts rejoined, the snake stirred, and it revived, racing off with its companion.

The leaves lay on the ground, and a thought struck the bereaved man watching this scene.

Could the miraculous power of the leaves that restored the snake's life help a human too?

He picked up the leaves and placed one on his wife’s mouth and the other two on her eyes.

Hardly had he done this when the blood surged back into her veins, her pale face regained color, and she drew a breath.

She opened her eyes and asked, "Oh God, where am I?"

"You're with me, dear wife," he replied, and explained everything that had happened.

He offered her some wine and bread, and once she regained her strength, they went to the door and knocked, calling out so loudly that the guards heard and informed the king.

The king came down himself, opened the door, and found them both safe and sound, rejoicing that their sorrow had come to an end.

title: The Three Snake-Leaves
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The young king, however, took the three snake leaves, entrusted them to a servant, and said, "Keep these safe for me and carry them everywhere.

Who knows when they might come in handy?"

But a change had come over his wife.

After returning to life, it felt like all love for her husband had vanished from her heart.

Later, when he planned to visit his father across the sea, and they boarded a ship, she forgot the great love and loyalty he had shown her.

Instead, she developed a wicked crush on the ship's captain.

One night, while the young king slept, she called the captain in.

She seized her husband by the head, and the captain took his feet, and together they tossed him into the sea.

Once their shameful deed was committed, she said, "Now, let’s go back home and say he died during the journey.

I’ll praise you to my father so much that he’ll marry me to you and make you his heir."

But the devoted servant had witnessed everything, unseen by them.

He untied a small boat from the ship, hopped in, and followed his master.

He retrieved the young king’s lifeless body and, using the three snake leaves he always carried, placed them on the king’s eyes and mouth.

Miraculously, he revived the young king.

They both rowed with all their might, day and night, and their little boat moved so swiftly that they reached the old king before the treacherous couple.

He was amazed to see them return alone and asked what had happened.

When he learned of his daughter’s wicked betrayal, he said, "I find it hard to believe she could act so cruelly, but the truth will soon come out. Now, go into a private chamber and stay out of sight."

Shortly after, the grand ship returned, and the ungrateful daughter approached her father looking anxious.

"Why are you back alone? Where is your husband?" the king asked.

"Oh, dear father," she replied, "I return with deep sorrow.

During the trip, my husband fell suddenly ill and died, but the kind captain helped me.

He witnessed everything and can tell you more."

The king responded, "I will bring the dead back to life," and opened the chamber door.

When she saw her husband, she was struck with shock, fell to her knees, and begged for mercy.

The king said, "There’s no mercy for you.

He was willing to die with you and brought you back to life, yet you betrayed him in his sleep. You will face the consequences you deserve."

Then she and her accomplice were placed in a ship riddled with holes and sent out to sea, where they soon sank beneath the waves.

title: The Three Sons of Fortune
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once, a father gathered his three sons and handed each of them a gift.

To the first, he gave a rooster; to the second, a scythe; and to the third, a cat.

"I'm getting old," he said. "I can feel my time is nearly up, and I wanted to set you up for the future.

I don’t have any money, but what I’m giving you might not seem valuable at first.

It’s all about how wisely you use it.

Just find a place where these things are unfamiliar.

After their father passed away, the eldest son took his rooster and set off.

Wherever he went, the rooster was already well-known; in towns, he saw them sitting on rooftops, turning with the wind.

In the villages, he heard many roosters crowing.

Nobody was amazed by the rooster, so it wasn’t looking good for his fortune.

Eventually, he arrived at an island where people had never seen a rooster.

They didn’t even know how to tell time.

They could tell when it was morning or evening, but at night, if they stayed awake, they had no clue what time it was.

"Look!" he exclaimed. "This proud bird has a ruby-red comb on its head and spurs like a knight.

It calls out three times during the night at set hours, and when it crows for the last time, the sun rises.

But if it crows during the day, pay attention—there's going to be a change in the weather!"

The islanders were thrilled; they stayed awake all night, eagerly listening as the rooster crowed at two, four, and six o'clock, announcing the time.

They asked if he would sell the bird and how much he wanted for it.

"As much gold as a donkey can carry," he responded.

"That’s a laughably low price for such a valuable creature!" they all exclaimed in unison.

When he returned home with his riches, his brothers were amazed.

The second brother thought, "Well, I’ll go out and see if I can sell my scythe for a good profit."

title: The Three Sons of Fortune
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But things didn't seem promising; everywhere he went, laborers were already carrying scythes.

Eventually, he stumbled upon an island where no one knew about scythes.

When the grain was ripe, they used cannons to shoot it down.

This method was rather unpredictable; some missed entirely, while others hit the ears instead of the stems, wasting much grain.

Plus, it created a terrible racket.

The man decided to mow the fields quietly and swiftly with his scythe, leaving the people staring in disbelief.

They agreed to pay him whatever he asked for the scythe, and he received a horse loaded with gold.

Now it was the third brother's turn to find the right buyer for his cat.

He faced the same troubles as his brothers; while on the mainland, it was hard to sell a cat.

Everywhere had cats, so many that people often discarded newborn kittens.

Finally, he sailed to an island where cats were unknown, and the mice had taken over.

The mice danced on tables and benches, whether their owners were home or not.

The people complained bitterly about the infestation; even the king didn’t know how to keep them out.

Mice squeaked from every corner, nibbling at anything they could find.

But soon, the cat went on a chase, clearing out several rooms.

The people pleaded with the king to buy this marvelous animal for their kingdom.

The king gladly paid the asking price, giving him a mule loaded with gold, and the third brother returned home with the biggest fortune.

The cat enjoyed her time in the royal palace, catching so many mice that they were uncountable.

Eventually, she got a bit tired and thirsty, so she stopped, lifted her head, and made a strange sound.

When the king and his subjects heard this unusual cry, they panicked and rushed out of the palace in terror.

Then the king called for advice on what to do.

It was decided to send a herald to ask the cat to leave the palace.

If she didn’t comply, force would be used against her.

The advisors warned, "We'd rather deal with the mice we know than risk our lives with this beast!"

So, a brave young man was sent to ask the cat if she would leave peacefully.

But the cat, now thirstier than ever, simply replied, "Mew. Mew."

The youth interpreted this as, "Absolutely not!" and conveyed the message back to the king.

Then the advisors decided it was time to use force.

Cannon were brought out, and soon the palace was engulfed in flames.

As the fire reached the room where the cat was sitting, she leaped through the window, but the crowd didn’t stop until the entire palace was reduced to rubble.

title: The Three Spinners
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a girl who was lazy and refused to spin.

No matter what her mother said, she couldn’t be persuaded to do it.

Eventually, her mother got so angry and frustrated that she hit her, which made the girl cry out loud.

At that very moment, the queen happened to be passing by.

When she heard the crying, she stopped her carriage, went into the house, and asked the mother why she was hitting her daughter so hard that the cries could be heard all the way out on the road.

The mother felt embarrassed to admit her daughter’s laziness, so she said, "I can’t get her to stop spinning. She insists on spinning forever, and I’m poor and can’t..."

The queen replied, "There’s nothing I enjoy more than hearing the sound of spinning, and nothing makes me happier than when the wheels are humming. Let me take your daughter with me to the palace; I have plenty of flax, and she can spin as much as she likes."

The mother was more than happy with this, and the queen took the girl with her.

Once they arrived at the palace, the queen led her into three rooms stacked from floor to ceiling with the finest flax.

"Now spin this flax for me," she said, "and when you’re done, you’ll marry my eldest son, regardless of your poverty. Your hard work is enough of a dowry for me."

The girl felt terrified inside because there was no way she could spin the flax, not even if she lived to be three hundred years old and worked from morning till night.

So, once she was alone, she started to cry and sat there for three days without doing anything.

On the third day, the queen returned and saw that nothing had been spun; she was surprised.

The girl explained that she hadn’t been able to start because she was so upset about leaving her mother’s house.

The queen accepted her excuse but warned her, "When I come back tomorrow, you need to start working."

title: The Three Spinners
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Left alone again, the girl was at a loss about what to do and, in her distress, went to the window.

That’s when she saw three women approaching her. The first had a broad, flat foot, the second had a very large underlip that hung down over her chin, and the third had a wide thumb.

They stopped in front of the window, looked up at her, and asked what was wrong.

She told them about her troubles, and they offered their help, saying, "If you’ll invite us to your wedding and not be ashamed of us, calling us your aunts and letting us sit at your table, we’ll spin the flax for you in no time."

"Of course!" she replied eagerly. "Just come in and start working right away!"

She let the three strange women in and cleared a spot in the first room where they settled down to spin.

One of them drew the thread and treadled the wheel, the second dampened the thread, and the third twisted it while tapping the table.

Every time she tapped, a perfectly spun skein of thread fell to the ground.

The girl kept the three spinners a secret from the queen, showing her the results whenever she came by, until the queen was so impressed she couldn’t stop praising her.

After the first room was empty, she moved on to the second room, and then to the third, which was cleared just as quickly.

Before they left, the three women reminded the girl, "Don’t forget what you promised us – it could change your fortune."

When the girl showed the queen the empty rooms and the huge pile of spun yarn, the queen ordered for the wedding to be arranged.

The prince was thrilled to have such a clever and hardworking bride, praising her endlessly.

"I have three aunts," said the girl, "and since they’ve been so kind to me, I’d like to invite them to the wedding and have them sit with us at the table."

The queen and the prince agreed, saying, "Why not?"

So when the feast began, the three women entered wearing peculiar outfits, and the bride exclaimed, "Welcome, dear aunts!"

The prince then asked, "How did you come to have such ugly friends?"

He approached the one with the broad foot and asked, "How did you get that broad foot?"

"By treading," she replied, "by treading."

Then he turned to the second woman and asked, "How did you come by your droopy lip?"

"By licking," she answered, "by licking."

Lastly, he asked the third woman, "How did you end up with your broad thumb?"

"By twisting the thread," she answered, "by twisting the thread."

Hearing this, the prince was alarmed and declared, "Neither now nor ever will my beautiful bride touch a spinning wheel!"

And that’s how she escaped the dreaded flax-spinning.

title: The True Sweetheart
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a young and beautiful girl who

lost her mother when she was just a child. Her

stepmother did everything she could to make the girl’s life miserable.

Whenever the stepmother assigned her any tasks, she put in all her effort,

working tirelessly and doing everything within her

ability. Yet, she could never win the heart of the cruel woman,

who was always dissatisfied; it was never enough. The harder the girl

strived, the more burdens were piled on her. All that the woman

thought about was how to make her more miserable and weigh her down with ever-greater tasks.

One day, she told her, "Here are twelve pounds of feathers that

you must pick, and if you don’t finish by this evening, you can expect a good beating. Do you think you can just waste the

whole day?" The poor girl sat down to work, tears streaming

down her face, as she realized it was completely impossible to finish the task in a single day. Every time she

managed to pile a handful of feathers in front of her and sighed or clapped her hands in despair, they would scatter, and she

would have to pick them up again and start all over. Then, she rested her elbows on the table, put her face in her hands, and cried,

"Is there really no one in this world who feels sorry for me?" Just then,

she heard a soft voice say, "Don’t worry, my child, I am here to help you." The girl looked up and saw an old woman next to

her. The woman took her hand gently and said, "Just tell me what’s troubling you." As she spoke so kindly, the girl

shared the story of her miserable life, explaining how one heavy burden after another was placed upon her, and that she never

could complete the tasks that were given to her. "If I don’t finish these feathers by tonight, my stepmother will beat me; she has warned me,"

she said, "and I know she always keeps her promises." Her tears began flowing again, but

title: The True Sweetheart
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
the kind old woman reassured her, saying, "Don’t be scared, my child. Rest for a bit, and I'll handle your work." The girl lay

down on her bed and fell asleep quickly. The old woman sat down at the table with the feathers, and how those feathers did flutter

from their quills as she barely brushed them with her frail hands.

The twelve pounds were finished in no time, and when the girl awoke,

she saw great piles of fluffy white feathers stacked neatly, and everything in

the room was organized. However, the old woman had disappeared. The girl thanked God and waited calmly until evening,

when her stepmother came in and was astonished to see the work done.

"What a clumsy girl you are!" she said, "What can be done if people are

diligent? Why couldn't you find something better to do?"  You sit there with your hands crossed.  When she left, she muttered, "That girl is worth more than her weight in salt. I need to give her

something even harder to tackle." The next morning, she called for the girl and said, "Here’s a spoon for you. With this, you must empty the large pond beside the garden, and if you don't finish by nightfall, you know what will happen."

The girl took the spoon, noticing it was full of holes. Yet, even if it had been perfect, she knew it was impossible to empty the pond with it. She immediately got to work, kneeling by

the water, where her tears fell, trying to scoop it out. But the good old woman appeared again, and when she learned the reason behind her tears, she said, "Be cheerful, my child. Go into the thicket, lie down, and sleep; I'll take care of your work." As soon as the old woman was alone, she only needed to touch the pond lightly, and a vapor rose up from the water and mingled with the clouds.

Slowly the pond began to empty, and when the girl woke up just before sunset and returned, she found nothing left but the fish struggling in the mud.

title: The True Sweetheart
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She went to her stepmother and showed her that the work was complete.

"That should have been done much earlier!" the stepmother exclaimed, turning pale with rage, but then she devised another plan.

On the third morning, she said to the girl, "You must build me a castle over there on the plain, and it must be finished by this evening." The girl was shocked and replied, "How can I possibly accomplish such a huge task?"

"I will not tolerate any excuses!" the stepmother screamed. "If you can empty a pond with a spoon full of holes, you can build a castle too! I’ll claim it for myself today, and if anything is missing, no matter how small, you know what to expect."

She pushed the girl out, and as she stepped into the valley, she saw the rocks piled high, larger than she could ever lift. She sat down and sobbed, still hoping the old woman would come to her rescue.

The old woman arrived quickly, comforting her. "Lie down in the shade and sleep; I’ll build the castle for you. If you'd like, you can live in it yourself." After the maiden walked away, the old woman touched the gray rocks. They began to rise and move together as if giants themselves had built the walls.

Before long, the castle was constructed as if countless hands were tirelessly at work, placing stone upon stone. There was a loud rumble from the ground as pillars pushed themselves upright and arranged themselves neatly. The tiles laid themselves in order on the roof, and by noon, a magnificent weather-cock was already swiveling at the top of the tower, resembling a radiant maiden with flowing garments.

The inside of the castle was still being designed as evening approached. How the old woman did it, I can't explain, but the rooms were decorated with silk and velvet, with embroidered chairs and fancy armchairs by marble tables. Crystal chandeliers hung beautifully from the ceilings and reflected in the polished floors, where green parrots nested in golden cages alongside rare singing birds. Everywhere there was as much grandeur as if a king were about to reside there.

title: The True Sweetheart
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The sun was setting when the girl woke up, dazzled by the brightness of thousands of lights. She rushed to the castle and entered through the wide-open door.

The steps were laid with red carpet, and the golden railings were adorned with blossoming trees. When she saw the splendor of the rooms, she stood as though turned to stone. It's hard to say how long she might have stood there if she hadn’t remembered her stepmother. "Oh, if only she could finally be satisfied and stop making my life so miserable," she thought.

The girl went to tell her that the castle was ready. "I'll move in right away!" the stepmother declared as she rose from her chair. As they entered the castle, she was forced to shield her eyes from the overwhelming brilliance. "See how easily you've done this!" she said to the girl. "I should have given you a more difficult task."

She poked around in every room to check for any deficiencies, but she found nothing wrong. "Now we must check downstairs," she said with a malicious look at the girl. "We still need to inspect the kitchen and cellar, and if you’ve forgotten anything, you won’t escape punishment. But when she entered the kitchen, she found it spotless with a fire burning on the hearth, meat roasting in the pans, and the tongs and shovel neatly placed against the wall.

Every shiny brass utensil was arranged in plain sight. Nothing was missing—not even a coal box or a water pail. "Which way to the cellar?" she yelled. "If that's not filled to the brim with wine casks, you'll be in trouble!" She herself pulled up the trapdoor and started down, but she only took two steps before the heavy trapdoor, which had merely been laid back down, crashed shut.

The girl heard her scream and quickly lifted the door to rush to her aid, but she found the stepmother lying hurt.

Now, the magnificent castle belonged to the girl alone. At first, she didn’t know how to come to terms with her good fortune. Beautiful dresses hung in the wardrobes, chests were brimming with gold, silver, pearls, and jewels; she never had a desire she couldn't fulfill.

title: The True Sweetheart
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Soon, the news of the girl’s beauty and wealth spread throughout the world. Suitors came daily, but none appealed to her.

Finally, the prince's son came, and he knew how to touch her heart, leading her to promise herself to him.

In the castle garden stood a lime tree, under which they sat one day, and he said to her, "I will go home and get my father's approval for our marriage. Please wait for me under this lime tree; I’ll be back in a few hours."

The girl kissed him on his left cheek and promised, "Stay true to me and don't let anyone else kiss you on this cheek. I will wait here under the lime tree."

The maiden remained beneath the lime tree until sunset, but he didn’t return. She waited three days, from morning till evening,

but he still didn’t appear. By the fourth day, she said, "Something must have happened to him. I’ll go search for him, and I won’t return until I have found him."

She packed three of her most stunning dresses—one embroidered with bright stars, the next with silver moons, and the last with golden suns—wrapped a handful of jewels in her handkerchief, and set off on her journey. She asked everyone about her fiancé, but no one had seen him, and no one knew anything.

She wandered far and wide but couldn't find him. Eventually, she took a job with a farmer as a cowherd and buried her dresses and jewels beneath a stone.

Now, living as a herdswoman, she tended to her cattle, feeling lonely and longing for her beloved. She had a little calf that she trained to recognize her, and would feed it with her own hands.

"Little calf, little calf, kneel by my side, and don’t forget your cowherd-maid," she sang, "just as the prince forgot his betrothed bride, who waited for him beneath the lime tree’s shade."

The little calf knelt down, and she stroked it tenderly.

After living for a couple of years, sad and missing him, news spread across the land that the king’s daughter was about to marry.

title: The True Sweetheart
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The road to the town passed through the village where the girl lived. One day, while she was taking her herd out, the bridegroom rode by.

He sat proudly on his horse, not looking back, but when she caught sight of him, she recognized her beloved, and it felt like a sharp knife shredded her heart.

"Oh no," she sighed, "I thought he was true to me, but he has forgotten all about me!" The next day he rode past again.

"Little calf, little calf, kneel by my side, and don’t forget your cowherd-maid, just like the prince forgot his betrothed bride, who waited for him beneath the lime tree’s shade."

As he heard her voice, he looked down and slowed his horse. He gazed into her face and then put his hands over his eyes, as if trying to recall something, but soon he rode away, disappearing from view.

The girl cried out, "Oh, he doesn’t recognize me anymore!" Her sorrow continued to intensify.

Soon after this, a grand three-day festival was announced at the king's court, inviting everyone from the kingdom.

"This is my last chance!" thought the girl. When evening arrived, she headed to the stone where she'd buried her treasures.

She retrieved the dress adorned with golden suns, dressed herself elegantly, and adorned herself with jewels. She let her hair down, which she had hidden beneath a handkerchief, letting it cascade down in beautiful curls, and then ventured into the town, unnoticed in the darkness.

Upon entering the brightly lit hall, everyone gasped in surprise, though no one knew who she was. The king’s son approached her, not realizing it was her. He led her out to dance, completely enthralled by her beauty, forgetting all about the other bride.

After the feast was over, she slipped away into the crowd and hurried back home before dawn, donning her cowherd clothes again.

The following evening, she wore the dress with silver moons, adding a half-moon made of precious stones to her hair. When she reappeared at the festival, everyone turned to look at her.

The prince approached her eagerly, swept away by her charm, dancing only with her and ignoring all others.

Before she left, he insisted that she promise to return for the festival's final night.

On the third evening, she donned the star-dress, sparkling with jewels, and her ribbon and girdle gleamed with starry stones. The prince had been waiting long for her return and pushed his way toward her.

"Please tell me who you are!" he exclaimed. "I feel as if I’ve known you forever. What happened when you left?"

Then she stepped forward and kissed him on his left cheek. In that moment, it was as if a veil lifted from his eyes, and he recognized his true love.

"Come with me! I won’t stay here any longer!" he said, taking her hand and leading her to the carriage.

They raced away to the magical castle as if the wind was pulling their carriage. The illuminated windows sparkled in the distance.

As they passed the lime tree, countless fireflies danced around it. It shook its branches, releasing a wonderful fragrance.

On the steps, flowers blossomed, and the hall echoed with the melodies of beautiful birds. In the great hall, the entire court gathered, and the priest awaited to marry the

title: The Turnip
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there were two brothers who both served as soldiers. One of them was wealthy, while the other was struggling.

In an effort to escape his poverty, the poorer brother took off his soldier's coat and decided to become a farmer.

He worked hard on his small piece of land and sowed it with turnip seeds.

The seeds sprouted, and soon, one turnip grew exceptionally large and strong.

It kept growing bigger and bigger, and it seemed like it would never stop, truly deserving the title of the princess of turnips, because it was unlike any turnip anyone had ever seen.

Eventually, it became so enormous that it filled an entire cart, requiring two oxen to pull it. The farmer had no idea what to do with this giant turnip or if it would bring him fortune or misfortune.

After some thought, he reasoned, if he sold it, what would it fetch? And if he ate it himself, smaller turnips would serve him just as well. It seemed best to take it to the king.

So he loaded it onto a cart, hitched up two oxen, and took it to the palace, presenting it to the king.

"What an extraordinary thing!" said the king. "Many marvelous sights have come before me, but I have never seen such a monster as this! From what seed could this have come? Or are you perhaps simply lucky by chance?"

"Oh, no," replied the farmer. "I'm not lucky at all. I'm just a poor soldier who couldn't support himself anymore, so I hung up my coat and started farming. I have a brother who is wealthy and known to you, Your Majesty, but I’ve been forgotten by everyone because I have nothing."

The king felt sorry for him and said, "I will lift you out of your poverty and give you such riches that you will be equal to your wealthy brother."

The king then presented the farmer with gold, land, meadows, and herds, making him incredibly rich, far beyond what the other brother had.

When the rich brother heard how much his poor sibling had gained from a single turnip, he became envious and started plotting how he could achieve the same luck.

title: The Turnip
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
However, he decided to go about it in a much sneakier way. He took gold and horses to the king, thinking the king would give him a much bigger gift in return.

If his brother earned so much from one turnip, surely he could get an even greater reward for such valuable items.

The king accepted his offering but told him he had nothing more valuable than the extraordinary turnip.

So the rich man had no choice but to load his brother's turnip into a cart and take it back home.

At home, he was frustrated and didn't know whom to blame for his misfortune until he came up with a wicked plan to kill his brother.

He hired assassins to ambush his brother and approached him with a friendly face, saying, "Dear brother, I’ve heard about a hidden treasure. Let’s dig it up together and share it!"

Trusting his brother, the poor man agreed and went along without any suspicion.

As they traveled, the assassins attacked, tied him up, and intended to hang him from a tree.

Just as they were about to do this, they suddenly heard loud singing and the sound of hooves approaching.

Filled with fear, they quickly stuffed their prisoner into a sack, hung it from a branch, and fled.

The poor man was able to poke his head through a hole he made in the sack.

The person approaching was a cheerful young student riding through the woods, joyfully singing.

When the captive noticed someone passing by, he called out, "Good day! You’ve come at just the right time!"

The student looked around, confused about where the voice was coming from. Finally, he asked, "Who’s calling me?"

The voice from above replied, "Look up! Here I am in the sack of wisdom. In no time, I’ve learned great things; all schools are a joke compared to this. Soon I’ll come down wiser than anyone else. I comprehend the stars, the winds, the sands of the sea, the cures for illnesses, and the powers of herbs, birds, and stones. If you were lucky enough to enter, you’d experience the wonders of knowledge firsthand."

The student, amazed by these claims, replied, "Blessed be the day I found you! Can I also climb into the sack for a bit?"

"For a while, I will let you in," the voice replied with some reluctance, "but first, you need to wait an hour, because I have one last thing to learn."

Eventually, the student grew impatient and begged to get in immediately, his thirst for knowledge consuming him.

Pretending to relent, the voice said, "To leave the house of knowledge, you must lower the sack by the rope, and then you can enter it yourself."

So the student lowered the sack, untied it, and set the voice free. Then he called, "Now pull me up quickly, and I’ll jump in!"

"Hold on!" said the voice. "That won’t work!" It grabbed him by the head and flipped him upside down into the sack, then secured it and hoisted the eager student up into the tree.

Then, swinging him in the air, it exclaimed, "How are you doing, my friend? Look, wisdom is already coming to you, and you’re gaining invaluable experience. Stay still until you become wiser!"

The figure then mounted the student's horse and rode away, but an hour later, it sent someone to help.

title: The Twelve Apostles
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Three hundred years before the birth of Jesus Christ, there

was a mother who had twelve sons, but she was so poor and struggling

that she didn’t know how to keep them all alive. She

prayed to God every day, asking Him to let all her sons be here on earth with the promised Redeemer.

When her situation grew even more desperate, she sent each of them out into the world to find food.

The eldest was named Peter, and he set out, already walking for a whole day when he stumbled into a vast forest.

He looked for a way out but couldn't find one, getting even more lost, while feeling an intense hunger

that made it hard for him to stand. Eventually, he grew so weak that

he had to lie down, thinking death was near.

Suddenly, a small boy appeared next to him, shining with brightness, looking as beautiful and kind as an angel.

The boy clapped his hands together, making Peter look up to see him. He then asked, "Why are you sitting there looking so troubled?"

"Oh, I’m searching the world for food, so I can see the dear Savior who is promised," Peter replied. "That’s my greatest wish."

The child said, "Come with me, and your wish will come true." He

took poor Peter by the hand and led him between some cliffs to a

huge cavern. Once they entered, everything glimmered with gold, silver, and crystal, and in the center stood twelve

cradles lined up side by side. Then the little angel said,

"Lie down in the first cradle and rest a while; I’ll rock you."
