Peter did as he was asked, and the angel sang to him, rocking him until

he fell asleep. While he was sleeping, the second brother arrived,

guided by his guardian angel, and he was rocked to sleep just like the first. One by one, the others came, until all twelve were there, resting in the golden

cradles. They slept, however, for three hundred years, until the

night when the Savior of the world was born. Then they awoke,

and were with Him on earth, and were known as the twelve apostles.

title: The Twelve Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a king and a queen who lived together happily.

They were blessed with twelve children, all boys.

Then the king told his wife, 'If the thirteenth child you're about to have is a girl, the twelve boys must die so her inheritance can be great.

He even ordered twelve coffins to be made, already filled with shavings.

Each coffin had a little pillow, and he had them taken to a locked room.

Then he gave the queen the key and told her not to mention this to anyone.

The queen sat and wept throughout the day, until her youngest son, whom she'd named Benjamin after the Bible character, said to her, 'Dear mother, why are you so sad?

'Oh, my dear child,' she replied, 'I can’t tell you.' But he wouldn’t let her be until she unlocked the room and showed him the twelve coffins filled with shavings.

She then said, 'My dear Benjamin, your father has ordered these coffins for you and your eleven brothers because if I have a little girl, you will all be killed and buried in them.'

As she spoke and wept, her son comforted her, saying, 'Don’t cry, dear mother; we will save ourselves and run away.'

'Go into the forest with your brothers,' she advised, 'and let one of you keep watch on the tallest tree, looking towards the castle.

'If I give birth to a boy, I will raise a white flag so you can return. But if it’s a girl, I will raise a red flag, and you must run away as fast as you can. May God protect you.'

'Each night, I will pray for you – in winter for warmth by a fire and in summer that you won’t faint in the heat.'

After she blessed her sons, they ventured into the forest.

They took turns on watch and sat on the tallest oak, looking towards the castle.

On the eleventh day, it was Benjamin’s turn, and he saw a flag being raised.

title: The Twelve Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But instead of a white flag, it was blood-red, signaling that they were all to die.

The brothers were furious, saying, 'Are we all going to suffer because of a girl? We swear to take revenge; wherever we find a girl, her blood will flow.'

So they went deeper into the forest and found a small bewitched hut hidden in the darkest part.

'We'll live here,' they decided, 'and Benjamin, the youngest and weakest, will stay at home and take care of it while we hunt for food.'

And so, they ventured into the forest, hunting hares, wild deer, birds, and whatever else they could find to eat.

They brought everything back to Benjamin, who cooked it for them to satisfy their hunger.

For ten years, they lived together in the little hut, and the time flew by.

Meanwhile, the queen had a daughter, who had grown up to be kind-hearted and beautiful, with a golden star on her forehead.

One day, during laundry, she saw twelve tiny shirts and asked her mother, 'To whom do these twelve shirts belong? They’re too small for Father.'

With a heavy heart, the queen replied, 'Dear child, they belong to your twelve brothers.'

'Where are my twelve brothers? I've never heard of them,' the girl asked.

The queen responded, 'God knows where they are; they are wandering the world.'

Then she led her daughter to the locked room and showed her the twelve coffins with the shavings and pillows.

'These coffins are for your brothers, who had to leave in secret before you were born,' she explained, sharing the whole story.

'Don’t cry, mother, I will find them,' the girl said adamantly.

She took the twelve shirts and set off into the great forest.

She walked all day and finally arrived at the enchanted hut.

There, she encountered a young boy who asked, 'Where do you come from, and where are you headed?' He was amazed by her beauty and royal attire.

She replied, 'I am a princess searching for my twelve brothers, and I will walk till I reach the end of the sky to find them.'

title: The Twelve Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
As she showed him the twelve shirts, Benjamin realized she was his sister.

'I am Benjamin, your youngest brother,' he said.

Joyfully, she wept, and he wept too as they embraced each other with heartfelt love.

'But sister,' he added, 'there's still a problem. We agreed that any girl we meet must die because we had to leave our kingdom due to a girl.'

'I’ll gladly die if it means saving my twelve brothers,' she replied.

'No, you won't die,' he insisted. 'Sit beneath this tub until our eleven brothers return, and I’ll resolve it.'

She followed his instructions, and when night fell, the others came back from hunting.

Dinner was ready, and as they sat at the table, they asked, 'What's the news?'

'Don’t you know anything?' said Benjamin.

'No,' they responded.

'While you all hunted, I stayed home and know more than you,' Benjamin revealed.

'Well, tell us,' they urged.

'Promise me the first girl we come across won’t be harmed,' Benjamin replied.

They agreed, 'Yes, we promise she'll be spared; now tell us!'

'Our sister is here,' he announced as he lifted the tub.

The princess emerged, adorned in her royal garments, her golden star shining on her forehead.

They all rejoiced and hugged her tightly, overwhelmed with love.

Now, she stayed at home with Benjamin, helping him with household chores.

The other eleven brothers continued to hunt for food, bringing back game, deer, birds, and pigeons.

Benjamin and their sister prepared the meals.

She gathered firewood and herbs for cooking, ensuring there was always a warm meal ready when they arrived back.

She kept their little home tidy and put fresh linens on their beds, making sure the brothers were content and lived in harmony.

One day, they prepared a grand feast together, joyously eating and drinking.

In the garden of the enchanted house, there were twelve beautiful lily flowers.

title: The Twelve Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Wanting to delight her brothers, she picked the twelve flowers to present to them during dinner.

But the moment she pulled the flowers, the twelve brothers transformed into twelve ravens, soaring away into the sky.

The hut and garden vanished, leaving her alone in the wild forest.

As she looked around in distress, an old woman appeared and said, 'My child, what have you done? Why didn’t you leave the twelve white flowers?'

'They were your brothers, now forever changed into ravens.'

'Is there any way to save them?' she asked, tears in her eyes.

'There’s only one way,' the woman replied, 'but it’s incredibly hard. You must remain silent for seven years, without speaking or laughing. If you say a single word before the seven years is up, all hope is lost, and your brothers will die.'

Determined, the girl thought, 'I will save my brothers,' and climbed a tall tree to spin without speaking or laughing.

One day, while she was up in the tree, a king was hunting nearby. His large greyhound ran to her tree, barking and whining.

The king came closer and saw the beautiful princess with the golden star and was so taken by her beauty that he asked if she would marry him.

She simply nodded her head, unable to speak.

He climbed the tree, carried her down, placed her on his horse, and took her home.

Their wedding was celebrated with much joy, but the bride remained silent and did not smile.

Years went by happily, until the king's wicked mother began to slander the young queen, saying, 'You’ve brought a common beggar girl home.

Who knows what she’s up to behind closed doors?'

'Even if she can’t talk, she should at least smile. Those who don't laugh must have something to hide.'

At first, the king didn’t believe her, but the old woman’s accusations piled up, finally persuading him to have his wife condemned.

They prepared a great fire in the courtyard for her execution, and the king, watching from the window, wept for love.

As she stood bound at the stake, and the flames curled towards her clothes, the last moment of her seven years of silence ended.

Suddenly, twelve ravens appeared, swooping down, and as they landed, they transformed back into her twelve brothers.

They broke through the fire, extinguished the flames, freed their sister, kissing and embracing her.

Finally able to speak, she explained to the king why she had remained silent.

The king rejoiced upon learning of her innocence, and together they lived in harmony until they passed away.

As for the wicked stepmother, she was brought before the judge and put into a barrel filled with boiling oil and venomous snakes, meeting a fitting end.

title: The Twelve Huntsmen
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a prince who was deeply in love with his bride.

While he was happily sitting next to her, he received troubling news.

His father was gravely ill and wanted to see him one last time before passing.

He turned to his beloved and said, "I must leave you now. Here’s a ring to remember me by. When I'm king, I will..."

He rode quickly to his father, who was on the verge of death.

"My dear son," the king said weakly, "I wanted to see you once more before I go. Promise me you will marry as I wish."

He named a certain princess who was to become his bride.

Overwhelmed with sorrow, the prince agreed without thinking, saying, "Yes, dear father, I will do as you wish."

With that, the king closed his eyes forever.

After the prince was crowned king and the mourning period ended, he had to fulfill his promise.

He sent a proposal to the princess and she agreed to marry him.

His first love, upon hearing about this, was so heartbroken that she nearly died.

Her father noticed her distress and asked, "My dear child, why are you so sad? You can have whatever you desire."

She thought for a moment and replied, "Father, I want eleven girls who look exactly like me in every way."

Her father promised, "If it can be done, I will make it happen," and he searched his entire kingdom.

Finally, he found eleven maidens who resembled her perfectly.

The princess had twelve identical huntsman outfits made—one for each of them.

Once her eleven companions were dressed, she donned the twelfth outfit.

She then bid farewell to her father and set off with her companions to the court of her former love.

She approached her ex-fiance and asked if he needed any huntsmen, offering to have all of them serve him.

The king looked them over, but didn't recognize her. Since they were such striking young men, he agreed to take them all on.

From then on, they became the king's twelve huntsmen.

title: The Twelve Huntsmen
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Now, the king had a magical lion that knew all hidden truths.

One evening, the lion said to the king, "You believe you have twelve huntsmen."

"Yes," replied the king, "they are twelve huntsmen."

The lion continued, "You’re mistaken; they are twelve girls."

The king was skeptical. "How can you prove this?" he asked.

"Just scatter some peas in the antechamber," the lion replied, "and you’ll see the truth. Men walk steadily over peas without disturbing them, but girls trip and scatter them."

The king liked this idea and had the peas spread on the floor.

However, a servant who liked the huntsmen overheard the plan and rushed to warn them.

"The lion wants the king to think you’re girls," he said.

The princess thanked him and told her friends, "Show your strength and walk firmly over the peas."

The next morning, when the king summoned the twelve huntsmen, they marched into the antechamber with the peas.

To the king’s surprise, none of the peas rolled or moved as they walked.

Returning to the lion, the king said, "You misled me; they walk just like men."

The lion replied, "They knew they were being tested and adjusted their steps accordingly. Just let twelve spinning wheels be placed in the antechamber, and they’ll be drawn to them, which no man would do."

The king liked this suggestion and had the spinning wheels set up.

Once again, the loyal servant warned the huntsmen about the test.

The princess then advised her eleven girls, "Hold back your curiosity and don’t look at the spinning wheels."

The following morning, when the king called for the twelve huntsmen, they walked through the antechamber without a single glance at the wheels.

The king turned to the lion and said, "You've tricked me; they must be men, for they didn’t even look at the spinning wheels."

The lion replied, "They have learned about the examination and controlled themselves."

The king, however, was no longer convinced.

The twelve huntsmen continued to accompany the king on his hunts, and he grew increasingly fond of them.

One day, as they were out hunting, news arrived that the king's bride was coming.

The true bride was horrified to hear this, feeling her heart nearly shatter as she fainted.

The king rushed to his huntsman, worried something had happened, and removed his glove.

To his shock, he saw the ring he had given his first love, and as he looked into her eyes, he recognized her.

His heart swelled, and he kissed her. When she opened her eyes, he declared, "You are mine, and I am yours, now and forever. Nothing can change that."

He sent a messenger to the other bride, kindly asking her to return to her kingdom, saying, "I already have a wife; it’s like someone finding an old key and not needing a new one."

The wedding soon took place, and the lion regained favor with the king for ultimately revealing the truth.

title: The Two Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there were two brothers: one was rich and the other was poor.

The wealthy brother was a goldsmith, but he had a wicked heart. In contrast, the poor brother made a living by crafting brooms and was a good, honorable man.

He had two children who were twin boys, looking incredibly alike, like two drops of water. The boys often played around their rich uncle's house and would scavenge for scraps to eat.

One day, while their father was out in the forest gathering brushwood, he spotted a stunning golden bird that was more beautiful than anything he had ever seen.

He picked up a small stone, threw it, and miraculously hit the bird. However, only one golden feather fell to the ground as the bird flew away.

He took the feather home to his brother, who examined it and said, "This is pure gold!" He offered a substantial amount of money in return.

The next day, while climbing a birch tree to cut some branches, the same golden bird reappeared. After some searching, he found its nest with a single gold egg inside.

Excited, he brought the egg home, and his brother again assessed it and confirmed, "This is pure gold!" He paid handsomely for it.

Eventually, the goldsmith said, "I would really like to have the bird itself." So, the poor man ventured into the forest once more.

Once again, he spotted the golden bird resting in a tree. He threw a stone at it, successfully bringing it down, and took it to his brother, who rewarded him with an impressive pile of gold.

Feeling hopeful, the poor man thought to himself, "Now I can make a living!" and he returned home happily.

However, the goldsmith was sly and greedy; he knew the bird was special. He called his wife and said, "Cook the gold bird, but make sure to save every last bit for me. I want to enjoy every morsel."

Unbeknownst to them, the bird was magical; whoever ate its heart and liver would find gold coins beneath their pillow every morning.

As the wife set the bird on the spit to roast, her husband left the kitchen for a moment.

title: The Two Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
At that very moment, the two children of the poor broom-maker rushed in, stood by the spit, and turned it around.

When a couple of bits of the bird fell into the pan, one of the boys exclaimed, "Let’s eat those small pieces! I’m starving, and no one will even notice."

So, they devoured the pieces, but soon after, the wife returned and noticed them eating something.

"What are you eating?" she asked. "Just some small bits that fell from the bird," they replied.

That must have been the heart and liver!" she gasped, panicking. To prevent her husband from getting angry, she quickly killed a young rooster, took its heart and liver, and replaced them with the golden bird's.

When the bird was ready, she served it to the goldsmith, who greedily ate it all without leaving a crumb behind.

The next morning, however, when he reached beneath his pillow, expecting to find gold, he was shocked to find nothing.

Meanwhile, the two children were unaware of the incredible fortune that had come their way. When they woke up, they heard something rattling to the ground.

When they looked, they found two gold coins lying there. Excited, they ran to their father, who was equally astonished. "How could this be?" he wondered.

The following morning, they found two more gold coins again. And this continued daily, prompting the father to share this bizarre story with his brother.

The goldsmith immediately realized how it had happened: the children had eaten the magical bird's heart and liver.

Filled with jealousy and spite, he told their father, "Your children are in league with evil. Don’t accept their gold, and don’t let them stay in your house any longer! They could bring ruin upon you."

Fearing for his family, the father reluctantly led the twins into the forest and left them there, heartbroken.

The boys wandered through the forest, looking for a way back home, but they just became more lost.

Eventually, they met a huntsman, who asked, "To whom do you children belong?" They quickly explained that they were the poor broom-maker's sons.

title: The Two Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
They admitted that their father could no longer keep them because of the gold found every morning.

The huntsman replied, "That’s not so bad, as long as you stay honest and hardworking. I like you both; I have no children of my own. I’ll take you home and raise you until you grow up."

They learned how to hunt from him, and he kept the gold pieces they found each morning for their future needs.

As they grew older, their foster father took them into the forest one day and said, "Today, you’ll take your first shots so I can release you from your training and make you full-fledged huntsmen."

They stayed hidden, waiting for game for a long time but saw nothing.

Suddenly, the huntsman spotted a flight of wild geese flying in a triangular formation and instructed one boy, "Take one down from each corner."

He did so, successfully passing his initial test.

Soon afterward, another flock flew by in a different formation, and the huntsman told the other brother, "Now bring one down from each corner here as well."

That trial shot was successful too.

"Now I declare you both free of your training," said the foster father. "You’re skilled huntsmen now!"

The two brothers set off together into the woods, strategizing and making plans.

Later that evening, as they sat down for supper, they told their foster father, "We won’t eat or take a single bite until you grant us a favor."

Curious, he asked, "What is your request?"

They said, "We’ve completed our training and are ready to forge our own paths in the world. Please allow us to travel."

The old man was delighted and replied, "You speak like brave huntsmen! What you want coincides with my own desires. Go forth, and may fortune favor you!"

They celebrated with a joyous meal together.

When the day of their departure arrived, their foster father presented them with nice guns and a hunting dog each, along with as many gold pieces as they wished.

He walked part of the way with them and handed them a shiny knife, saying, "If ever you part, stick this knife into a tree where you separate. When one of you returns, you’ll know how your brother is doing.

title: The Two Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
If the side facing the direction he went is rusted, he’s dead; if bright, he’s alive."

The two brothers continued on their journey and soon arrived at a vast forest too big to escape in a single day.

They camped there, eating from their hunting pouches, but even the second day passed, and they still hadn’t found their way out.

With nothing to eat, one brother said, "We have to hunt something or we’ll starve."

He loaded his gun and scanned the area. An old hare came hopping toward them.

He raised his gun, but the hare quickly pleaded, "Dear huntsman, please let me go; I’ll give you two baby hares!"

Before they knew it, the hare dashed into the brush and returned with two little ones.

The playful babies were so adorable that the huntsmen couldn’t bring themselves to kill them and opted to keep them.

Before long, a fox appeared, and as they prepared to shoot it, the fox begged, "Please let me live; I’ll bring you two little foxes!"

Soon enough, he too returned with two young foxes, which they decided to add to their growing group.

Then a wolf came out of the thicket, and with the same plea, he offered two baby wolves.

Next, a bear approached and requested, "Dear huntsman, just let me live, and I’ll bring you two bear cubs!"

And so, two bear cubs joined the flock.

Finally, a lion appeared, and though the huntsman initially considered aiming for the beast, it too made the same plea, offering two lion cubs.

Now they had two lions, two bears, two wolves, two foxes, and two hares following them.

However, their hunger was still not satisfied, so they said to the foxes, "Listen, you crafty ones, find us something to eat!"

The foxes replied, "There’s a village nearby where we’ve successfully nabbed plenty of chickens before. We'll lead you there!"

So they ventured into the village, bought food for themselves, fed their animals, and continued on.

title: The Two Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The foxes knew their way around and led the huntsmen straight to the poultry yards.

Afterward, they wandered for a while, but couldn’t find a place where they could stay together.

Finally, they decided, "We must split up." They divided the animals so that each had one lion, bear, wolf, fox, and hare.

They bid each other farewell, promising to love one another like brothers until the end, and stuck the knife into a tree where they had parted.

One brother traveled east while the other went west.

The younger brother ended up in a town entirely covered in black crepe.

He entered an inn and asked the host if he could find accommodation for his animals.

The innkeeper showed him to a stable with a hole in the wall. The hare quickly darted out, grabbed a cabbage head, and returned.

The fox then snuck out to catch a hen and didn't stop until he also snagged a rooster.

The bigger animals—the lion, bear, and wolf—couldn't squeeze through.

The innkeeper then guided them to a meadow with a cow lying on the grass, allowing the larger animals to eat until they were full.

Once the huntsman had seen to his animals, he asked the innkeeper why the town was draped in black.

The host replied, "It’s because our king’s only daughter is to be sacrificed tomorrow."

Surprised, the huntsman inquired, "Is she sick?"

"No," said the host. "She’s healthy, but she must be given to a dragon that devastates the land until it receives a virgin each year. Only the king's daughter remains to be delivered to him.

The huntsman shook his head in disbelief and asked why they had not killed the dragon.

"Many knights have tried," replied the host, "but they all met with their deaths. The king has promised his daughter to whoever defeats the dragon, and that person will also inherit the kingdom after his death."

The huntsman said no more, but the next morning he took his animals and climbed the dragon's hill.

At the top stood a small church with three full cups on the altar, labeled, "Whoever drinks from these will become the strongest man on Earth and wield the sword buried at the threshold."

title: The Two Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The huntsman didn’t drink, but instead searched for the sword but couldn’t budge it.

Then he walked back inside and drained the cups, and instantly felt strong enough to lift the sword.

As the hour approached for the princess's delivery to the dragon, the king, the marshal, and the courtiers escorted her.

From afar, she spotted the huntsman on the dragon's hill and mistakenly thought it was the dragon waiting for her.

Reluctantly, she began to make her way up the hill to avoid destruction.

The king and courtiers went back home, consumed by grief.

At the hilltop, however, the maiden found not the dragon but the brave huntsman, who reassured her he would save her and led her into the church, locking the door behind them.

Moments later, a furious seven-headed dragon arrived, roaring as he saw the huntsman.

"What are you doing here?" the dragon demanded.

The huntsman confidently replied, "I’m here to fight you!" The dragon countered, "Many knights have perished here; I’ll soon finish you!"

With that, he exhaled fire from all seven mouths.

The fire was intended to ignite the dry grass, suffocating the huntsman in smoke, but his animals rushed in and extinguished the flames.

Furious, the dragon lunged at the huntsman, but drawing his sword, the huntsman swung it through the air, severing three of the dragon’s heads.

Now enraged, the dragon rose into the sky, breathing flames over the huntsman and preparing to dive down.

But the huntsman wielded his sword once more and cut off three more heads, leaving the monster weakened and nearly defeated.

With one final thrust, the huntsman cut off the last head.

Exhausted, he called upon his animals, who tore the dragon to pieces.

After the battle, he unlocked the church door and found the princess on the floor, having fainted from fear.

Carrying her out, he assured her that she was free from danger, showing her the dragon's remains.

Overjoyed, she exclaimed, "You will be my dearest husband, as my father promised my hand to whoever killed the dragon!"

title: The Two Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
As a token of gratitude, she gave each of her animal saviors a piece of her coral necklace, reserving her handkerchief, embroidered with her name, for the huntsman.

He took the seven dragon tongues, wrapped them in her handkerchief, and kept them safe.

Then, both exhausted, they decided to rest.

She suggested, "Yes, let's sleep," as they settled on the ground.

Before drifting off, the huntsman instructed the lion, "You keep watch so that we aren’t surprised while we sleep."

The lion lay down beside them, but he too was fatigued from the fight.

He turned to the bear and said, "Lay down near me; I really need some rest. If anything shows up, wake me."

The bear then lay down and called to the wolf. "Come, lie beside me for a moment; I need sleep too. If anything comes, just wake me."

Then the wolf lay down and asked the fox, "Hey, you come rest next to me for a bit, but if anything comes, wake me up!"

The fox, feeling tired, sought the hare and said, "You lie down by me too. But if anything arrives, wake me immediately!"

The hare sat beside him but was utterly exhausted, with no one left to wake him, and soon fell asleep.

At that moment, both the princess and all the animals were peacefully slumbering.

Meanwhile, the king's marshal, who was tasked with watching from a distance, grew bold as he saw no dragon fly off with the princess.

When he looked down, he saw the dragon was slain, and nearby lay the prince and his animals all deep in slumber.

As a wicked and heartless man, he quickly seized the opportunity, cutting off the huntsman’s head before taking the maiden and carrying her down the hill.

She awoke in terror as he claimed, "You are in my power now; you’ll tell everyone it was I who killed the dragon!"

"I cannot do that," she replied, "because it was the huntsman and his animals who freed me."

title: The Two Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Raging, he drew his sword and threatened to kill her if she didn’t comply, ensuring she promised to remain silent.

He then took her to the king, who was ecstatic to see his daughter alive, believing her to have met her demise.

The marshal declared, "I killed the dragon and saved the princess and the kingdom, so I demand her hand in marriage!"

The king turned to his daughter, asking, "Is this true?"

She hesitated before replying, "Yes… but I won’t agree to the wedding until a year and a day have passed, as I hope to learn of my true rescuer."

While she stood by, the huntsman's animals still lay sleeping beside their master on the hill.

A great bumblebee buzzed around and landed on the hare's nose, but the creature merely wiped it off and continued napping.

The bee returned a second time but was brushed off again.

On its third visit, the bee stung the hare's nose, causing him to wake.

As soon as the hare was awake, he roused the fox, and the fox then nudged the wolf, followed by the wolf waking the bear.

Finally, the bear called the lion, but when the lion woke and saw the princess was gone, panic set in.

"Who did this?" he roared. "Bear, why didn’t you wake me?"

The bear turned to the wolf, "Why didn’t you wake me?" Then the wolf inquired of the fox, "Why didn’t you wake me?"

All eyes fell on the fox, who looked at the hare and asked, "Why didn’t you wake me?"

The poor hare had no response and realized he was solely to blame.

As anger mounted, they began to close in on him, but he pleaded, "Don't kill me! I can revive our master again!"

"I know a mountain where a magical root grows; anyone who consumes it heals from all wounds and ailments.

But it’s a long journey—two hundred miles away!"

The lion declared, "You have until tomorrow to go there and come back with the root, or else!"

title: The Two Brothers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The hare dashed away, and somehow made the round trip in twenty-four hours, returning with the life-giving root.

The lion gently placed the huntsman’s head back in position and the hare fed him the root.

Miraculously, life returned to him, his heart started beating, and he opened his eyes.

Initially alarmed at the sight of the princess’s absence, he thought sadly she must have left him.

The lion, in his hasty excitement, had placed the huntsman's head on incorrectly, but fortunately, the huntsman didn't notice.

However, when he went to eat later and saw his head was turned the wrong way, confusion filled him.

He asked the animals what had taken place while he was asleep.

The lion explained that they too had been exhausted and fell asleep, only to awaken and find their master decapitated.

The hare brought the magical root, allowing the huntsman to rise again and pursue his quest.

Following that experience, they set off together anew, but the younger brother pointed out, "You look just like me, and you're dressed like I am. We've both got animals trailing behind us.

Let's enter the palace by separate gates, and we can arrive before the old king at the same time."

So they parted ways, and as they arrived in the courtyard from opposite sides, the guards were taken aback.

They reported, "It’s not possible for both to be true; the gates are miles apart!"

But both brothers made their way inside, climbing the steps to meet the king.

The king said to his daughter, "Which of these two is your husband?"

Confused, she replied, "Each looks so much like the other, I can’t tell."

In distress, she recalled the necklace she had given to the animals, and searched for her golden clasp.

She found it on the lion and shouted with joy, "The one with this lion is my true husband!"

The young king chuckled, confirming, "Yes, he’s the one!" They then enjoyed a banquet together, celebrating their reunion.

Later that night, as they settled into bed, the queen asked, "Why have you been laying a sword in our bed every night? I feared you meant to harm me!"

Realizing this situation revealed his brother's guilt, the younger brother understood the bond was tested, yet love prevailed.

title: The Two Kings' Children
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a king who had a little boy. According to the stars,

it was foretold that this boy would be killed by a stag when he turned sixteen.

When he reached that age, the huntsmen took him out hunting.

In the forest, the king's son got separated from the others and suddenly spotted a great stag.

He wanted to shoot it but couldn't get a clear shot.

He chased the stag so far that he ended up outside the forest, and suddenly, a tall man appeared in place of the stag.

The man said, "It's good I found you. I've already ruined six pairs of glass skates chasing after you and couldn't catch you."

He then took the king's son with him and dragged him across a large lake to a grand palace.

Once there, the boy had to join him for a meal.

As they ate, the man said, "I have three daughters. You must keep watch over the eldest for one night from nine in the evening until six in the morning.

Each time the clock strikes, I will come and call. If you don’t answer, you will be put to death.

If you always answer, you can marry her."

When the young man and the princess got to the bedroom, they found a stone statue of St. Christopher.

The princess said to it, "My father will come at nine o’clock, and every hour until three.

When he calls, please answer him instead of the king's son."

The stone statue nodded quickly at first, then more slowly until it finally stood still.

The next morning, the king said, "You did well, but I cannot give my daughter to you just yet.

Now you must watch over my second daughter for a night, and then I’ll think about giving you the eldest daughter.

But I will come every hour myself, and if you don’t respond when I call, you will pay for it with your life."

They entered the next sleeping chamber, where there was a larger statue of St. Christopher.

title: The Two Kings' Children
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The princess said, "If my father calls, please answer him."

The statue nodded quickly and again more slowly until it finally stood still.

The king's son lay down at the door, resting his head on his hand and fell asleep.

The following morning, the king said, "You watched well, but I still cannot give you my daughter.

Now you must keep watch over the youngest princess.

I’ll think about giving you my second daughter after that. But I will come every hour myself.

Answer me when I call, or your blood will be spilled for me."

They walked to the next chamber, where a far larger statue of St. Christopher stood.

The princess said to it, "When my father calls, answer him."

The statue nodded for quite some time, before finally standing still again.

The king's son lay down at the threshold and slept once more.

The next morning, the king said, "You’ve really done well, but I still can’t give you my daughter.

I have a vast forest. If you can cut it down by six this morning and six tonight, I’ll consider it."

He handed him a glass axe, a glass wedge, and a glass mallet.

When he arrived at the woods, he started to chop but the axe broke in half.

He then tried the wedge, striking it once with the mallet, but it shrank to the size of sand.

He became very distressed and believed he would have to die, so he sat down and cried.

At noon, the king said, "One of you girls must take him something to eat."

The two eldest daughters replied, "No, we won’t take it to him. Let the one he last watched over do it."

The youngest daughter reluctantly went to bring him something to eat.

When she found him in the woods, she asked how he was doing.

"Oh," he said, "I’m doing terribly."

She urged him to come eat something. "No," he replied, "I can't. I’m going to die anyway, so I won’t eat anything more."

title: The Two Kings' Children
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She spoke kindly and begged him to try, so he finally came and ate a little.

Afterward, she said, "Let me pick some lice off you for a while."

As she did that, he grew weary and fell asleep.

She took out her handkerchief, tied a knot in it, struck it three times on the ground, and called, "Earth-workers, come forth!"

In an instant, numerous little earth-men emerged and asked what she commanded.

She instructed them, "In three hours, the vast forest must be cut down and all the wood stacked in heaps."

The little earth-men got to work and gathered their friends, finishing the task within the time limit.

Once it was done, they returned to the princess and informed her.

Then she took out her white handkerchief again and said, "Earth-workers, go home."

Instantly, they all vanished.

When the king's son woke up, he was overjoyed, and she told him to return when it struck six.

He did as she instructed and arrived back just as the king asked, "Did you manage to cut down the forest?"

"Yes," replied the king's son.

At the dinner table, the king said, "I still can’t give you my daughter just yet; you must complete one more task for her."

Curious, the young man asked, "What is it?"

The king said, "I have a large fish-pond. Tomorrow morning, you must clear it of mud until it's as bright as a mirror, and fill it with fish."

The next morning, the king gave him a glass shovel and said, "You must finish the fish-pond by six o’clock."

When he got to the pond, he stuck the shovel into the mud, but it broke in half.

He tried his hoe next, but that broke too.

Feeling defeated, he was troubled.

At noon, the youngest daughter returned with food and asked how he was doing.

He replied that everything was going very badly and that he felt certain he would lose his head.

title: The Two Kings' Children
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"My tools have shattered again."

"Come eat something!" she insisted. "You’ll feel better after."

"No!" he said. "I can't eat; I’m too miserable for that."

She urged him with kind words until he finally agreed and ate some food.

Then she picked lice off him again, putting him to sleep.

She then took out her handkerchief, knotted it, struck the ground three times, and declared, "Earth-workers, come forth!"

Again, a host of little earth-men appeared and asked what she desired.

She instructed them to completely clean out the fish-pond in three hours, making sure it was so clear that people could see their reflections, and to fill it with fish.

The earth-men hurried away, calling upon their kin for help, and within two hours, the job was done.

They returned to her and said, "We’ve completed your task."

The princess took her handkerchief again, struck it three times on the ground, and commanded, "Earth-workers, return home."

As they vanished, the king's son awoke to find the fish-pond finished.

The princess then instructed him to come back at six.

Upon his arrival, the king asked, "Did you finish the fish-pond?"

"Yes," said the king's son.

Once again at the table, the king said, "You’ve certainly cleared the fish-pond, but I still cannot give my daughter away.

You must do one more thing."

Intrigued, the king's son asked, "What is that?"

The king explained, "I have a mountain covered in briars that must all be cut down, and at the top of it, you need to build a strong castle with all the furnishings that a castle should have."

The next morning, the king gave him a glass axe and a glass gimlet and told him to have it all done by six.

As he cut down his first briar, the axe broke short and splintered, sending pieces flying.

He couldn’t use the gimlet either.

title: The Two Kings' Children
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Feeling utterly defeated, he sat down, hoping for her to come and help him.

At noon, she came with food. He met her, shared his troubles, and ate.

Afterward, she helped him again, and he fell asleep.

She took her handkerchief, struck the ground, and called out, "Earth-workers, assemble!"

Once more, little earth-men appeared and asked what she desired.

She said, "In three hours, you must cut down the briars and construct a mighty castle at the top of the mountain, complete with all necessary furnishings."

They hurried off to gather their friends, and when the time was up, everything was prepared.

They returned to the princess, who took her handkerchief, struck the ground, and ordered, "Earth-workers, go home."

And with that, they vanished.

When the king's son awoke, he found everything completed and was as happy as a bird.

When six o’clock struck, they walked home together.

The king asked, "Is the castle ready?"

"Yes," replied the king's son.

At dinner, the king said, "I cannot give my youngest daughter away until the two eldest are married."

The king's son and daughter were troubled, and he had no idea what to do.

But one night, he decided to sneak away with the princess.

As they ran, the princess looked back and saw her father chasing them.

"Oh, what are we going to do?" she exclaimed. "I’ll turn you into a briar, and I’ll change into a rose to hide in the bush."

When her father arrived, he saw a briar with a single rose on it.

He reached for the rose, but a thorn pricked his finger, forcing him to return home.

His wife asked why he hadn’t brought their daughter back.

He replied that he almost caught her, but a briar with a single rose had appeared.

The queen insisted that he should have picked the rose, as it would have brought the briar with it.

So he returned for the rose, but by then, the couple had made their escape.

title: The Two Kings' Children
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The princess glanced back to see her father still pursuing them and said, "We need another idea. I’ll turn you into a church and myself into a priest, and I’ll preach from the pulpit."

When her father arrived, he saw the church, and a priest was preaching.

He listened for a while before heading back home.

The queen asked him why he didn’t bring their daughter back.

He said, "I chased her for a long time, but I found a church and a priest preaching instead."

The queen responded, "You should have brought the priest, and the church would have followed."

Determined, she set out to find them.

As she walked, she eventually spotted them in the distance.

The king's daughter looked back and realized her mother was coming. "Oh no, we’re in trouble now!" she said.

"I’ll turn you into a pond and myself into a fish."

When the mother reached the spot, she saw a large pond with a fish happily jumping and splashing.

She tried to catch it, but couldn’t grasp it.

Frustrated, she drank up the entire pond, only to become so ill that she had to vomit it all back up.

Realizing her failure, she shouted, "Come back to me!"

The king's daughter returned, and the queen gave her three walnuts and said, "You can use these to help you in your time of greatest need."

The young lovers set off once again.

After a long journey of ten miles, they arrived at the castle where the king's son lived.

Near it was a village.

As they reached the village, the king's son said, "You stay here, my dear. I’ll go to the castle and fetch a carriage and servants to bring you back."

Upon arriving, everyone rejoiced at the return of the king’s son.

He told them he had a bride waiting in the village and requested a carriage to bring her.

They quickly harnessed the horses, and many attendants took their places outside the carriage.

As the king's son was about to get in, his mother kissed him, causing him to forget everything that had transpired.

title: The Two Kings' Children
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Recognizing his forgetfulness, she had the horses pulled from the carriage again, and everyone went back to the house.

Meanwhile, the princess sat in the village, waiting and watching, thinking he would return for her, but no one came.

In desperation, the king's daughter took a job at the castle mill and had to sit by the pond each day.

One day, the queen walked past the pond and noticed the beautiful girl sitting there.

She commented, "What a strong and lovely girl! She’s impressive."

But no one recognized her.

Time passed, and the girl served as a miller’s help, doing her job honorably and well.

Meanwhile, the queen was looking for a bride for her son, and a girl from far away was soon to arrive.

When the bride arrived, the wedding was promptly slated.

Many townsfolk came to witness the union.

The miller's maiden asked the miller if she could join them, and he told her she could go.

Before she left, she opened the first walnut, revealing a gorgeous dress.

She put it on and went to the church, standing by the altar.

Suddenly, the bride and groom entered and sat before the altar.

As the priest was about to bless them, the bride looked back and saw the beautiful maiden.

The bride stood up and declared she wouldn’t be given away until she also had a dress as lovely as the one the maiden wore.

They returned home to ask the maiden if she would sell her dress.

"No," the maiden said, "but the bride might have to earn it."

The bride asked how she could do so.

The maiden said, "If I can sleep one night outside of the king's son’s door, she can have what she wishes."

The bride agreed to that.

However, the servants were instructed to give the king's son a sleeping potion, and the maiden lamented on the threshold all night.

She reflected on cutting down the forest for him, cleaning out the pond, building the castle, and changing into a briar, a church, and finally a pond, only to be forgotten.

The king's son, however, did not hear her, but the servants were awake and listened, confused.

The next morning, the bride donned the dress and went to church with her groom.

In the meantime, the maiden opened her second walnut, revealing an even prettier dress.

She wore it and stood at the altar once more, with everything unfolding as it had before.

That night, the maiden lay again at the prince's door, to be given a sleeping potion once more.

But this time, a servant brought him something to keep him awake.

As he fell asleep, the maiden lamented her past deeds once more at the threshold.

The king’s son heard everything and felt deep sadness as memories flooded back.

Wanting to go to her, he found his mother blocking the way.

But the next morning, he rushed to tell her everything that happened and apologized for forgetting her.

Realizing it was time, the princess opened the third walnut to discover a stunning dress beyond compare.

She donned the dress and accompanied her groom to the church.

Candles were lit, flowers were offered, and the priest blessed them as they joyfully wed.

The false queen and bride had to depart without their wish.

As the tale goes, that is how it all ended.

title: The Two Travellers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Hills and valleys may never meet, but people can come together, both good and bad.

On their journey, a shoemaker and a tailor crossed paths.

The tailor was a cheerful little guy, always happy and ready for fun.

As he spotted the shoemaker approaching from the opposite side, he noticed the bag he carried, revealing his trade.

He couldn’t resist singing a playful song at him.

But the shoemaker didn’t appreciate the joke. He grimaced as if he’d sipped vinegar and acted like he wanted to grab the tailor.

Seeing this, the tailor burst into laughter, offered him his bottle, and said, "Chill out, have a drink, and let that anger go."

The shoemaker took a big swig, and gradually his scowl began to fade.

Handing back the bottle, he said, "I really enjoyed that drink. They say drinking too much can be a problem, but this wasn’t because I was thirsty. Shall we travel together?"

"Sure!" replied the tailor. "As long as you’re up for heading into a big city where we can find work."

"That’s exactly where I want to go," the shoemaker agreed. "There’s nothing to earn in a small village, and out in the countryside, people don’t always wear shoes."

So off they went together, putting one foot in front of the other like a pair of weasels in the snow.

They had plenty of time but not much food to eat. When they finally reached a town, they greeted the local tradesmen.

The lively and jovial tailor, with his bright red cheeks, easily earned work from everyone, and luck smiled upon him, bringing kisses from the masters’ daughters under the porch.

Whenever he met up with the shoemaker again, he always had more in his bundle. The sour shoemaker scowled, thinking that the more scoundrelous one is, the more luck one has.

Yet the tailor kept laughing and singing, sharing whatever he earned with his companion.

When a few coins jingled in his pockets, he treated them to a feast and celebrated so joyfully that the glasses danced on the table.

title: The Two Travellers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
As they continued their journey, they entered a large forest that held the road to the capital.

Within the forest were two paths: one that took seven days to walk and another that only took two, but neither traveler knew which one was shorter.

They sat down beneath an oak tree, discussing how to plan for their journey and how much bread they would need.

The shoemaker insisted, "You need to plan ahead. I’ll bring enough bread for a week."

"What?" the tailor exclaimed. "Carry bread for seven days like a pack mule and miss out on the sights? No way! I’ll trust in God and not worry about it."

"I have enough money for both summer and winter, but in the heat, bread spoils quickly and my coat won’t last. Why shouldn’t we find the right way instead? Two days of bread will be plenty."

So they each purchased their own bread and then ventured into the forest.

It was eerily silent, as calm as a church, with no wind blowing, no stream babbling, and no birds chirping. Through the thick branches, not a single sunbeam could break through.

The shoemaker said nothing, the weight of the bread on his back making sweat stream down his frowning and gloomy face.

The tailor, on the other hand, was lighthearted, skipping about, whistling a tune or singing, thinking to himself, "God must be happy to see me so cheerful."

This went on for two days until the forest seemed endless, and the tailor had eaten all his bread. With every passing moment, his spirits began to drop.

Yet, he kept his courage, depending on God and his luck. That evening, he lay down hungry beneath a tree, waking the next morning still famished.

The day passed, and when the shoemaker plopped down on a fallen tree to enjoy his lunch, the tailor could only watch.

If he asked for a crumb of bread, the shoemaker mocked him, saying, "You’ve always been so chirpy; now you know what it’s like to be sad. Those birds that sing too early are caught by the hawk in the evening."

title: The Two Travellers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He had no pity. But on the fifth morning, the poor tailor couldn’t stand any longer, barely able to speak due to weakness, his cheeks pale and eyes red.

Then the shoemaker said, "I’ll give you some bread today, but in exchange, I want to take your right eye."

The miserable tailor, desperate to save his life, had no choice but to agree. He cried from both eyes, then extended them for the shoemaker.

With a cruel heart, the shoemaker used a sharp knife to take out his right eye.

The tailor remembered what his mother had said when he snuck food from the pantry. "Eat what you can, and endure what you must." After finishing his hard-earned bread, he stood up, pushing his misery aside, finding comfort in the thought that he could still see enough.

But on the sixth day, hunger crept back and gnawed at him intensely. He collapsed by a tree, and on the seventh morning, he couldn’t get up from weakness, with death looming close.

Then the shoemaker said, "I’ll show mercy and give you bread again, but you must give up your other eye."

At that moment, the tailor realized how careless he had been with his life, prayed to God for forgiveness, and said, "Do what you need to, I will bear whatever comes, but remember that our Lord God doesn’t just sit idly by, and one day your evil deeds against me will be repaid. When times were good, I shared everything with you.

My job requires exactness in every stitch. If I lose my sight and can’t sew anymore, I’ll be left begging. Just don’t leave me here alone while I’m blind, or I’ll starve." But the shoemaker, having hardened his heart, took the knife and took out the tailor's left eye.

He gave him a piece of bread to eat, handed him a stick, and led him along.

When the sun set, they finally made it out of the forest, and ahead lay the gallows in the open country.

title: The Two Travellers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The shoemaker led the blind tailor to the gallows, then abandoned him and went his own way.

Exhausted, in pain, and starving, the unfortunate man fell asleep and slept through the night.

When daybreak came, he woke up, confused about where he was. Two poor souls hung from the gallows, and a crow was perched on each of their heads.

One of the hanged men spoke up, saying, "Brother, are you awake?" "Yes, I’m awake," replied the other.

"I have something to share," said the first man. "The dew that has fallen on us from the gallows gives anyone who washes with it their sight back.

If the blind only knew this, how many would regain their vision despite believing it impossible."

When the tailor heard this, he grabbed his pocket-handkerchief, pressed it on the grass, and once it soaked up dew, he washed his empty eye sockets.

As promised by the man on the gallows, new eyes appeared, and it wasn’t long before he could see the sun rising behind the mountains.

Before him lay the grand royal city with its magnificent gates, towering spires, and gleaming golden balls and crosses.

He could see each leaf on the trees, the birds flying by, and the insects dancing in the air. He pulled a needle out of his pocket and, threading it with ease, felt happiness swell in his heart.

He dropped to his knees, thanking God for his mercy, and said his morning prayer. He didn’t forget to pray for the poor souls swinging in the wind like pendulums.

Then he tossed his bundle on his back, soon forgetting about his previous heartache, walking happily on his way, singing and whistling.

The first thing he saw was a young brown foal running free in the fields. He grabbed its mane, wanting to hop on and ride into town.

But the foal pleaded, "I’m still too young! Even a small tailor like you would break my back. Let me grow stronger, and maybe I’ll repay you later!"

"Off you go," said the tailor, "You’re still just a silly creature." With that, he gave the foal a playful swat on the back, and it leaped away joyfully, galloping into the fields.

title: The Two Travellers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But the little tailor hadn’t eaten since the day before. "The sun may fill my eyes," he said, "but it doesn’t fill my belly. Whatever comes my way now will have to pay for it!" Then he spotted some young ducks swimming on a pond.

"Just what I need!" he exclaimed, grabbing one and trying to wring its neck. Suddenly, an old duck, hiding among the reeds, quacked loudly and paddled over, begging him to spare her little ones.

"Can’t you imagine how heartbroken your mother would be if someone tried to take you away?"

"Calm down," said the kind-hearted tailor, "You can keep your ducklings," and he returned the captured one back to the water.

Turning around, he found himself in front of a hollow old tree, with wild bees buzzing in and out.

"This must be my reward for being kind," thought the tailor. "Honey would surely give me strength!" But the queen bee came out, threatening him, saying, "If you harm my bees or destroy our hive, we’ll sting you like a thousand fiery needles.

However, if you leave us in peace, we’ll help you." The little tailor realized he couldn’t get honey either. With three empty plates and not a bite for dinner, he dragged his famished self into town.

As the clock struck noon, he found a delicious meal prepared just for him at the inn and sat down for a much-needed feast.

Once he was satisfied, he declared, "Now I'll get to work!" He wandered through town, searching for a master, and soon found a solid job.

Having mastered his craft, he quickly gained fame, and everyone wanted the little tailor to make their new coats, leading to his growing importance.

"I’ve reached my limit in skill," he said one day, "and yet I keep improving every day." Soon after, the king named him court tailor.

Funny how things work! On the same day, the shoemaker, who had once been his companion, also became the royal shoemaker.

title: The Two Travellers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When the shoemaker saw the tailor, suddenly with two healthy eyes again, guilt washed over him.

"I must set a trap before he gets revenge," he thought. However, digging a pit for someone else often leads to your own downfall.

That evening, after work, as darkness fell, he sneaked up to the king and claimed, "Your Majesty, the tailor is getting too big for his boots, boasting that he can find the ancient golden crown that was lost."

"I’d love that!" exclaimed the king, summoning the tailor for the next morning and demanding he retrieve the crown, or he’d be banished from the town forever.

The tailor thought, "That’s a tall order! If the grumpy king wants me to do the impossible, I’ll just leave town immediately!"

He packed his belongings and headed to the gate, but as he stepped outside, he felt a pang of regret for leaving his good fortune behind.

Near the pond where he’d met the ducks, the old duck, whose babies he had saved, was grooming herself on the shore. Spotting him, she asked, "What’s got you looking so glum?"

"You won’t believe the troubles I’m facing," he told her, explaining his predicament.

"If that’s all," said the duck, "we can help! The crown fell into the pond, and it’s right at the bottom. We’ll fetch it for you. Just lay your handkerchief out by the shore."

With that, she dived under with her twelve ducklings, and within five minutes, she resurfaced with the crown resting on her wings, while her ducklings swam around it.

They swam to the bank and placed the crown on the handkerchief. No one could believe how stunning it looked, shimmering like a hundred thousand jewels in the sunlight.

The tailor tied up the handkerchief, carrying it back to the king, who was overjoyed, presenting him with a gleaming gold chain.

But now, the shoemaker wasn’t done. He concocted another plot and approached the king again.

"Majesty, the tailor has grown arrogant again, bragging that he can mold the entire royal palace in wax, every inch inside and out."

title: The Two Travellers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The king called for the tailor, commanding him to replicate the palace in wax, and he’d face imprisonment if he failed to create even a single nail.

The tailor thought, "This just keeps getting worse! I can’t pull that off," and he threw his bundle on his back, stepping outside.

As he sat under the old tree, his head hung low, bees buzzed around him.

The queen bee noticed and asked if he had a stiff neck, seeing how he was sulking.

\"Oh no,\" the tailor replied. \"Something much heavier weighs me down.\" He explained what the king had demanded.

The bees began buzzing amongst themselves, and the queen suggested, \"Go home for now, but return tomorrow with a big sheet, and everything will be fine."

He did as she said, and while he was gone, the bees flew to the royal palace, checking every nook and cranny.

They quickly crafted the palace in wax so efficiently that it seemed to be forming right before their eyes.

By evening, it was ready, and when the tailor arrived the next day, the whole exquisite building was complete, with not a single nail or roof tile missing.

It was delicate, white as snow, and sweet-smelling like honey. He carefully wrapped it up to present to the king, who couldn’t stop admiring it.

Delighted, he placed the wax palace in the largest hall, rewarding the tailor with a stunning gemstone.

But the shoemaker wasn’t finished yet. He went to the king a third time and said, "Your Majesty, the tailor is claiming that water will flow up in the courtyard of the castle, clear as crystal!"

The king summoned the tailor again and warned him that if the water didn’t rise as promised, he would lose his head.

Caught in a life-and-death situation, the terrified tailor raced to the gate, tears streaming down his face.

As he was leaving, the foal he had once let free, now a beautiful chestnut horse, came trotting towards him.

"Now is my chance to repay you," said the horse. "I know what you need, so climb on, I can carry you both!"

title: The Two Travellers
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Feeling braver, the tailor jumped on, and the horse galloped into the town and directly to the castle courtyard.

It raced as fast as lightning three times around, and on the third lap, it collapsed.

At the very moment, a loud thunder crashed, and a piece of earth shot up like a cannonball into the air, followed by a fountain of water that erupted high into the air, as clear as crystal.

The sunbeams sparkled on the water. When the king witnessed this, he ran and embraced the tailor in front of everyone.

But fortune didn’t last forever. The king had many beautiful daughters, but no son.

So, the envious shoemaker approached the king for the fourth time and said, "Your Majesty, the tailor continues to boast. He claims that he could summon a son for you from the air!"

The king ordered the tailor to come forth, declaring that if he managed to bring a son within nine days, he could marry his eldest daughter.

"What a high reward!" the little tailor thought. "It’s tempting, but these cherries are too high for me. If I try to reach them, the branch will snap, and I’ll fall."

Sitting cross-legged at his worktable, he pondered his predicament. Eventually, he cried, "This is impossible! I’llleave, I can’t find peace here!"

He packed his bag and hurried to the gate. In the meadow, he saw the stork, pacing like a philosopher.

Sometimes it paused, thoughtfully examining a frog before swallowing it whole.

The stork approached and greeted him. "Looks like you’re heading out. Why are you leaving the town?"

The tailor shared the king’s demands and how he felt doomed.

"Don’t let that bring you down," said the stork. "I’ve been delivering children for a while now, so I can certainly fetch a little prince from the well. Just relax, and come back in nine days."

The little tailor went home and returned to the castle on the appointed day. Before long, the stork flew in and tapped on the window.

The tailor opened it, and the stork walked in solemnly, carrying a baby that looked like an angel in his beak.

It stretched its tiny hands toward the queen, and the stork placed the baby in her lap. She doted on it, showering it with kisses and squealing in delight.

Before leaving, the stork handed over a small bag filled with sweets to the queen for the little princesses.

But the eldest didn’t receive any; instead, she got the merry tailor as her husband.

"It feels like I’ve won the greatest prize!" he remarked. "My mother was right; she always said that those who trust in God, with a sprinkle of good luck, can never fail."

The shoemaker was tasked with making the shoes for the tailor’s wedding.

After the celebration, he was ordered to leave town forever. He took the path back through the forest to the gallows.

Exhausted from anger and the heat, he collapsed.

As he tried to rest his eyes, the crows from the heads of the hanged men swooped down and pecked out his eyes.

In his madness, he ran into the forest and perished there from starvation, for no one has ever seen or heard from him again.

title: The Ungrateful Son
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, a man and his wife were sitting by the front door of their home.

They had a juicy roasted chicken laid out before them, ready to enjoy.

Just then, the man noticed his elderly father approaching, and he quickly grabbed the chicken and hid it away, unwilling to share any with him.

The old man arrived, took a sip of his drink, and then left.

Now, the son wanted to bring the roasted chicken back to the table, but when he picked it up, it had turned into a huge toad.

The toad leaped onto his face and refused to leave.

If anyone tried to remove it, the toad looked at them so menacingly that they didn't dare touch it.

And so the ungrateful son was stuck feeding the toad every day; if he didn’t, it would feast on his face instead.

Thus, he wandered the world in constant discomfort and stress.

title: The Valiant Little Tailor
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One summer morning, a little tailor was perched on his table.

He was feeling cheerful as he sewed with all his energy.

Just then, a peasant woman passed by, calling out, "Good jams, cheap!"

Her cheerful cry was music to the tailor's ears.

He poked his head out the window and called out, "Come up here, dear lady! You can sell your goods to me."

The woman climbed the three steps to the tailor's door, carrying her heavy basket.

He had her unpack all the jars for him.

He inspected each jar, lifting them up and sniffing them, then finally said, "The jam looks good. Just weigh me out four ounces, my dear, and if it turns out to be a quarter pound, that’s fine."

The woman, hoping for a good sale, gave him what he wanted, but left feeling quite angry and grumbling.

"May this jam be blessed by God!" exclaimed the little tailor, "and grant me health and strength."

He grabbed some bread from the cupboard, sliced off a piece, and spread the jam on it.

"This won't taste bitter, " he thought, "but I’ll finish my jacket before I take a bite."

He set the bread beside him and continued sewing, and in his joy, his stitches grew larger and larger.

Meanwhile, the sweet smell of the jam attracted a swarm of flies that gathered around.

"Hey, who invited you?" the little tailor said as he swatted away the uninvited guests.

However, the flies, not understanding German, wouldn’t leave and kept coming back in increasing numbers.

The little tailor finally lost his patience.

He pulled out a piece of cloth from underneath his worktable and said, "Hold on, I’ll give you something!" as he struck the flies mercilessly.

When he pulled the cloth away, he counted seven flies, lying dead with their legs stretched out.

"Are you the sort of fellows who think you can bother me?" he said, admiring his own bravery.

"The whole town will know about this!" The little tailor quickly cut himself a belt, stitched it up, and embroidered the words, "Seven at one stroke."

title: The Valiant Little Tailor
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"What a victory! Everyone will hear of this," he thought, feeling as proud as a peacock.

He put on the belt and decided it was time to venture out into the world, believing his workshop was too small for his bravery.

Before leaving, he rummaged around the house, but all he found was an old cheese, which he popped into his pocket.

Outside his door, he saw a bird caught in the bushes, which also went into his pocket with the cheese.

Feeling light and nimble, he set off on the road, not feeling tired at all.

The path led him up a mountain, and when he reached the top, he found a powerful giant sitting there, peacefully looking out over the world.

The little tailor walked up confidently and said, "Good day, comrade! Looks like you’ve got a great view of the world. I’m on my way to try my luck. Want to join me?"

The giant looked down at the tailor with disdain and replied, "You ragamuffin. What are you doing here?"

"Oh really?" responded the little tailor, unbuttoning his coat to show the giant his girdle.

"Read that and you’ll see what kind of man I am."

The giant read it aloud: "Seven at one stroke," and felt a flicker of respect for this tiny fellow.

However, he wanted to test the tailor, so he picked up a stone and squeezed it until water dripped out.

"Do that too, if you have strength," the giant challenged.

"Is that all?" said the tailor. "That’s child's play for me!" He reached into his pocket for the soft cheese and pressed it until it oozed.

"Now that was a bit better, right?" he said with a smirk.

The giant was taken aback, not believing the little man could do that.

Then, the giant picked up a stone and hurled it so high that it was hard to follow with the eye.

"Now, tiny man, do that as well!"

"Nice throw," said the tailor, "but hold on. I’ll throw one that will never come back!"

title: The Valiant Little Tailor
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
He pulled the bird from his pocket and set it free; it flapped away and never returned.

"What do you think of that throw, buddy?" the tailor asked with a grin.

"You can definitely throw," admitted the giant, "but let’s see if you can actually carry something properly."

He led the little tailor to a massive oak tree that had been felled on the ground and said, "If you’re strong enough, help me carry this tree out of the forest."

"Sure," replied the little tailor. "You take the trunk on your shoulders, and I’ll handle the branches — they’re the heaviest part anyway."

The giant hoisted the trunk onto his shoulder, but the little tailor climbed onto a branch.

Not noticing, the giant carried the whole tree along with the tailor, who was quite happy and whistling a tune.

The giant managed to drag the heavy load for a while, but soon cried out, "Hey, I have to drop the tree!"

The little tailor jumped down, grabbed the tree as if he’d been carrying it all along, and said, "You’re such a big guy and yet can’t even manage the tree?"

They continued on together, and as they passed a cherry tree, the giant picked some ripe fruit from the top, bent it down, and handed it to the tailor.

He told him to eat, but the little tailor couldn’t hold the tree, and when the giant let go, it sprang back up, tossing the tailor into the air.

When he landed unharmed, the giant said, "What’s going on? You can’t even hold a weak twig?"

"It’s not about strength," replied the little tailor. "Do you think I could fell seven with one blow? I jumped over the tree because the huntsmen are shooting in the thicket. Try to jump like I did!"

The giant attempted to jump over the tree but got caught in the branches, leaving the tailor with the upper hand.

The giant said, "If you’re so brave, come with me to our cave and spend the night."

title: The Valiant Little Tailor
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The little tailor agreed and followed him.

Inside the cave, other giants were sitting by the fire, each munching on a roasted sheep.

The little tailor looked around and thought, "This place is way bigger than my workshop."

The giant showed him a bed and said he could sleep there.

However, the bed was too big for the little tailor, so he crawled into a corner instead.

At midnight, when the giant thought the tailor was sound asleep, he got up, took a big iron bar, and sliced through the bed with one blow, thinking he had killed the little man.

At dawn, the giants went into the forest, having completely forgotten the little tailor.

But to their surprise, he cheerfully walked up to them, and they were terrified, fearing he would strike them down.

They ran off in a panic.

The little tailor moved on, following his own nose.

After a long walk, he arrived at the courtyard of a royal palace, feeling weary.

He lay down on the grass and fell asleep.

As he rested there, people came by to inspect him and read on his girdle, "Seven at one stroke."

"What is such a great warrior doing here in peaceful times?" they wondered.

"He must be an important lord!" They rushed in to inform the king, saying that if war broke out, this would be a valuable ally.

The king was impressed and sent one of his courtiers to the tailor to offer him military service when he woke up.

The ambassador stood by the sleeping man and waited for him to open his eyes, then delivered the king’s proposal.

"I’m ready to join the king’s service," the tailor responded.

He was warmly welcomed and given a special place to stay.

However, the soldiers were wary of the little tailor and wished he’d leave.

"What’s going to happen?" they whispered. "If we spar with him, every blow will take down seven of us! Not one of us is strong enough to face him."

They decided to approach the king together, asking for their dismissal.

title: The Valiant Little Tailor
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"We can’t stay with a man who kills seven at one stroke!" they told him.

The king felt bad about losing his loyal soldiers over one man and wished he’d never met the tailor.

Though he was tempted to dismiss the tailor, he was afraid the little man would retaliate and take over the throne.

After thinking for a while, he finally devised a plan.

He summoned the tailor and told him he had one more request, since he was such a brave hero.

In a nearby forest lived two giants who caused a lot of trouble by robbing, murdering, and burning, and no one could approach them without risking their lives.

If the tailor managed to defeat these giants, he could marry the king’s only daughter and receive half of the kingdom as a dowry, along with a hundred horsemen to assist him.

That sounded pretty good to the little tailor. "You don’t get offers like this every day!" he thought.

"Oh, yes!" he said, "I’ll conquer the giants and don’t even need the hundred horsemen; anyone who can strike seven with one blow isn’t afraid of two!"

The little tailor set off, with the hundred horsemen following behind.

When he reached the edge of the forest, he told his men to wait, saying, "I’ll take care of the giants myself."

He leapt into the forest, scanning for the giants.

Before long, he spotted them sleeping under a tree, their snores causing the branches to sway.

Not wasting time, the little tailor collected two pockets full of stones and climbed up into the tree.

Settling on a branch above the giants, he began dropping one stone after another onto the chest of one of them.

The giant slept through it for a while, but eventually woke with a start and shook his companion.

"Why are you hitting me?" asked the groggy giant.

"I’m not hitting you!" protested the other.

They went back to sleep, but the little tailor threw another stone at the second giant.

"What’s that about?" the second giant exclaimed. "Why are you pelting me?"

title: The Valiant Little Tailor
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
After some back-and-forth, they finally gave up and closed their eyes.

The little tailor then threw the biggest stone he could find at the first giant, hitting him squarely.

"Now that’s not cool!" shouted the first giant, jumping up like a madman and shoving his partner towards the tree.

In retaliation, the other pushed back, and they got into such a furious fight that they ended up tearing each other apart and both fell down dead.

The little tailor jumped down, relieved and chuckled, "Thank goodness they didn’t tear the tree I was hiding in! That would’ve been a challenge! But us tailors, we’re quick on our feet."

He drew his sword and pierced each giant with a couple of thrusts, then went outside to the horsemen, saying, "I’ve done it! Both giants are taken care of, though it was tough work. They fought with trees, but none could withstand me, the man who kills seven at one blow!"

"But you’re not hurt?" asked the horsemen, shocked.

“Not at all," he replied. "Not even a hair on my head has been touched!"

The horsemen couldn’t believe him and rode into the forest, where they found the giants lying in a pool of blood and trees scattered everywhere.

The little tailor asked the king for his promised reward, but the king reneged on his promise.

"Before you can marry my daughter and take half my kingdom, you must complete one more heroic task."

In the forest lurked a unicorn that caused havoc, and the tailor needed to catch it.

"One unicorn? That’s no more trouble than two giants! One blow, seven to go — I can handle this!"

He took a rope and an axe and headed into the forest, telling the horsemen to wait outside.

He didn’t have to look long; soon the unicorn came charging at him as if to stab him with its horn.

"Easy there!" he said to himself, holding his ground until the unicorn got close, then darted behind a tree.

title: The Valiant Little Tailor
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The unicorn bolted into the tree with its full force, getting its horn stuck so it couldn’t pull free.

"Gotcha!" said the tailor, coming out from his hiding spot to put a rope around the unicorn's neck.

He freed the horn from the tree with his axe, then led the creature back.

The king still refused to honor his promise and created one final requirement.

Before the wedding, the tailor had to capture a wild boar that was destroying the forest, with the help of some huntsmen.

“No problem! This is child’s play!“ the tailor said, not needing the huntsmen's help.

They were glad, as the wild boar had given them a hard time.

When the boar charged the little tailor with foaming jaws, he sprinted into a nearby chapel, leaping up to the window.

The boar ran in after him, but the clever tailor dashed around and locked the door behind it, trapping the beast.

He called the huntsmen over to show them their captive.

The little tailor then returned to the king, who was now obliged to keep his word, and handed him his daughter along with half the kingdom.

Had the king realized he was marrying off a tailor instead of a noble hero, he would have felt even more troubled.

The wedding was a grand affair, yet joy was lacking.

After some time, the young queen overheard her husband mumbling in his sleep, saying, "Boy, sew me a doublet and patch my pants, or I’ll smack you with the yardstick!"

She then understood the true origins of her husband and, the next morning, complained to her father about her situation, asking for help to be rid of him, claiming he was just a tailor.

The king assured her, saying, "Leave your bedroom door open tonight, and my men will be outside. When he falls asleep, we’ll bind him and put him on a ship to the wide world."

The queen agreed, but the king's armor-bearer, who was friends with the little lord, overheard the plan.

"I’ll fix that," the little tailor thought.

That night, he went to bed with his wife as usual, and when she thought he was asleep, she got up, opened the door, and lay back down.

Meanwhile, the little tailor pretended to be asleep, then shouted loudly, "Boy, sew me a doublet and patch my pants! I’ve felled seven at one blow, I’ve slayed two giants, captured one unicorn, and caught a wild boar! Why should I fear those outside?"

When the men heard his bold claims, they were gripped with fear and ran off like wild animals, terrified of the tailor.

Thus, the little tailor remained a king for the rest of his life.

title: The Water of Life
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once there was a king who was seriously ill, and no one thought he would survive.

His three sons were deeply troubled and went to the palace garden to cry.
