While they were there, an old man approached and asked what was bothering them.

They explained that their father was so sick he would surely die since nothing seemed to help.

The old man replied, "I know of one last remedy: the water of life. If he drinks it, he will recover, but finding it is quite a challenge."

The eldest son declared, "I will find it!" He went to speak to his father and pleaded to go search for the water of life because it was the only thing that could save him.

"No," said the king, "the risk is too high. I'd rather die."

But he begged for a long time until the king finally agreed. The prince thought to himself, "If I bring back the water, then my father will love me the most, and I will inherit the kingdom." So he set off on his journey.

As he rode a little way, he encountered a dwarf standing in the road, who called out, "Where are you going in such a hurry?"

"Mind your own business, you little pest," the prince replied haughtily, and continued riding.

However, the angry dwarf cast an evil spell on him.

Shortly after, the prince entered a narrow ravine, and the mountains closed in around him, making the path so tight that he couldn't go forward.

He couldn't turn his horse around or get off; he was trapped like a prisoner. Meanwhile, the sick king waited for him, but the prince never returned.

Seeing that his brother did not come back, the second son asked, "Father, let me go and search for the water." He thought, "If my brother is gone, the kingdom will be mine." Initially, the king refused, but eventually, he gave in.

The second prince took the same path as his brother. He too met the dwarf, who stopped him to ask where he was rushing to.

title: The Water of Life
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"That’s none of your business, you little shrimp," the prince said, riding past without a second glance.

The dwarf, feeling scorned, cast his spell, and soon, like the first prince, the second son also found himself in a ravine, unable to move.

And so it goes for those who are proud.

Since the second brother was also missing, the youngest son begged to be allowed to search for the water. Finally, the king had no choice but to let him go.

When he met the dwarf and the dwarf inquired where he was off to in such a hurry, he stopped, explained, and said, "I'm looking for the water of life to save my father."

"You want to find it? Then you’ll need to follow my instructions. You’re heading to an enchanted castle where the water springs from a fountain, but to get there, you’ll need an iron rod and two loaves of bread."

The dwarf continued, "Strike the iron door of the castle three times with this rod, and it will open. Inside, you’ll find two lions with sharp teeth, but if you throw a loaf to each of them, they'll be calmed."

"Hurry to drink from the water of life before the clock strikes twelve, or the door will close, and you’ll be trapped!"

The prince thanked the dwarf, took the rod and bread, and set off on his adventure.

When he arrived at the castle, everything was as the dwarf had described. After three strikes of the rod, the door swung open, and he tossed the bread to the lions, quieting them.

He entered and found a grand hall where several enchanted princes sat, and he quickly took their rings. There was also a sword and another loaf of bread, which he took with him.

Next, he entered a chamber where a beautiful maiden rejoiced at his arrival, kissed him, and said he was her savior.

She told him he had freed her and that she would grant him her entire kingdom, inviting him to return in a year to celebrate their wedding.

title: The Water of Life
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
She also shared the exact location of the spring and reminded him to hurry and fill his cup with the water before the clock struck twelve.

He continued on his way and eventually arrived at a room with a beautifully made bed.

Feeling exhausted, he lay down for a moment of rest.

When he finally awoke, it was nearly twelve o'clock! He jumped up, rushed to the spring, filled his cup, and hurried back.

But just as he was about to pass through the iron door, the clock struck twelve, and the door slammed shut, taking part of his heel with it.

Despite the injury, he felt joy for having secured the water of life and headed home.

He passed the dwarf again, who saw the sword and bread and said, "With these, you have gained great power; with the sword, you can defeat entire armies, and the bread will last forever."

But the prince insisted, "I won't go home without my brothers. Can you tell me where they are?"

"They are imprisoned between two mountains," said the dwarf. "I held them captive because of their arrogance."

The prince pleaded until the dwarf agreed to release them, but warned him, "Be cautious; they have wicked hearts."

When his brothers arrived, he joyfully shared how he had found the water of life and rescued a beautiful princess who promised to wait for him.

They rode together and came across a kingdom afflicted by war and famine. The king believed he was doomed due to the dire situation.

The prince approached the king and offered him the loaf, which fed and satisfied the entire kingdom.

Then he gave the king his sword, which helped him defeat his enemies and find peace.

The prince took back his loaf and sword, and the three brothers continued on their journey.

They entered yet another famine-stricken land where the prince repeated his acts of kindness, using his loaf and sword to free two more kingdoms.

Afterward, they boarded a ship and sailed across the sea.

During the voyage, his older brothers whispered together, "The youngest has found the water of life while we haven't. Our father will give him the kingdom, taking what belongs to us."

title: The Water of Life
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
They conspired to get even and plotted to harm him. They waited until he was fast asleep, then poured the water of life out of his cup and replaced it with salty seawater.

When they reached home, the youngest took his cup to the sick king so he could drink and be cured.

Yet, as soon as the king drank a little bit of the salty water, his condition worsened.

Seeing the king suffer, the two elder brothers accused the youngest, claiming he had tried to poison their father. They offered the king the "real" water of life.

As soon as the king tasted it, he felt his health returning and grew strong again.

Pleased, they taunted the youngest, saying, "You may have discovered the water of life, but we reaped the benefits. You should have been more careful! We took it from you while you slept at sea. When the year is over, one of us will go to claim the princess."

They warned him, "Don't tell our father about this, or you’ll lose your life. Keep silent, and you’ll be fine."

Angered, the old king believed his youngest son had plotted against him, so he summoned the court and sentenced him to be executed.

One day, as the prince was out hunting, unaware of the threat to his life, the king sent the huntsman along with him.

When they were alone in the woods, the huntsman looked so sad that the prince asked, "What’s wrong, dear huntsman?"

The huntsman hesitated and said, "I shouldn’t tell you, but I must. The king ordered me to kill you."

The prince was stunned and said, "Please, let me live! I’ll give you my royal clothes if you let me go!"

The huntsman replied, "I’d gladly do that; I can’t bear to harm you." They swapped clothes, and the huntsman returned while the prince rode deeper into the forest.

Soon, three wagons filled with gold and precious stones were sent to the king as a gift from the three kings whose enemies had been defeated by the prince’s sword and bread.

title: The Water of Life
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Wondering if his son was truly innocent, the old king mourned, "I wish my son were still alive. I regret allowing him to be killed."

"He’s alive!" the huntsman declared. "I couldn’t follow through with the king's command, and here’s how it happened."

The king felt a weight lift off his heart and announced throughout the kingdom that his son was welcome to return and be forgiven.

The princess, meanwhile, had a bright golden road made leading up to her palace.

She declared that anyone who rode straight down the road would be the rightful suitor and would be welcomed, while those who strayed from the path wouldn't be right.

As the time drew closer, the eldest brother hurried to present himself as the princess's rescuer for his chance at marrying her and claiming the kingdom.

When he reached the palace and saw the gorgeous golden road, he thought, "It would be a disgrace to ride on that," and turned aside, riding along the edge.

When he reached the door, the servants told him he was not the right suitor and asked him to leave.

Not long after, the second brother set off.

Upon seeing the golden road, he worried that a piece might get broken off if he rode on it.

So he too rode alongside it and was told at the door that he wasn’t the right one and had to go away.

Finally, when the year had passed, the youngest brother was eager to see his beloved and forget his troubles.

Focused on her, he didn't notice the golden road and rode straight down the middle.

When he arrived at the door, it opened wide, and the princess welcomed him joyfully, declaring him her savior and lord, and they celebrated their wedding with great festivities.

After the wedding, she informed him that his father had invited him back and forgave him.

The prince rode back, explaining everything to his father about how his brothers had betrayed him while he had remained silent.

The old king wanted to punish his elder sons, but they had already sailed away and never returned.

title: The Water-Nix
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once, a little brother and sister were playing by a well, and while they were having fun,

they accidentally fell in. There was a water-nixie living below,

who said, "Now I've got you! You'll have to work hard for me now,"

and she took them away with her. She handed the girl some filthy, tangled flax

to spin and made her fetch water in a bucket that had a hole in it.

The boy was given the task of chopping down a tree with a dull axe, and they both got

nothing to eat except dumplings that were as hard as rocks.

Eventually, the children became so fed up that they decided to wait until

one Sunday when the nixie went to church, and then they ran away. But when

the church service ended, the nixie noticed they were gone and

took off after them in a hurry. The children spotted her from far away,

and the girl tossed behind her a brush that turned into a massive hill of

bristles, covered with thousands of spikes. The nixie had to climb over it with great difficulty. Finally,

when the children noticed this, the boy threw behind him a comb, which

formed a huge ridge with countless teeth. But the nixie managed to keep her balance and

eventually made it over.

Then the girl threw behind her a mirror, which created a hill of reflective glass, so slippery that the

nixie couldn't get across it. She thought, "I'll quickly go home and

fetch my axe to cut through this hill of glass." Long before she

returned to break the glass, the children had already escaped a great distance, and the water-nixie had to

trudge back to her well.

title: The Wedding of Mrs. Fox (two stories)
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was an old fox with nine tails who

suspected his wife of being unfaithful. Wanting to test her loyalty,

he laid himself down under the bench,

pretending to be stone dead.

Mrs. Fox went up to her room, locked the door, while her maid,

Miss Cat, sat by the fire and cooked. When word spread that Mr. Fox was dead,

suitors began to arrive. Miss Cat heard someone knocking at the front door.

She went to answer it and found a young fox who asked,

"What are you up to, Miss Cat?"

"I’m not asleep; I’m awake,"

the cat replied,

"Would you like to know what I’m making?"

"I’m boiling warm beer with butter;"

"Will you be my guest for supper?"

"No, thank you," said the young fox. "What is Mrs. Fox doing?"

"She’s crying her eyes out,"

Miss Cat said. "Just tell her that a young fox is here who wants to woo her."

"Of course, young sir."

The cat dashed up the stairs, trip trap,

knocking on the door with a tap, tap, tap.

"Oh yes, my dear little Cat!" she cried.

"There’s a suitor at the door."

"What does he look like, my dear?"

"Does he have nine beautiful tails like the late Mr. Fox?" "Oh no,"

the cat replied, "he has only one." "Then I won’t take him."

Miss Cat went downstairs and sent the suitor away. Soon after,

there was another knock, and yet another fox stood at the door,

hoping to win Mrs. Fox's heart. He had two tails, but he was turned away,

just like the first. More suitors came, each with one tail more than the last,

but they were all sent away until finally, one arrived with nine tails,

just like the late Mr. Fox! When the widow heard that,

she joyfully said to Cat,

title: The Wedding of Mrs. Fox (two stories)
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Now throw open all the doors and windows wide!"

But just as the wedding was about to happen, old Mr. Fox stirred under the bench,

swatted the crowd away, and chased Mrs. Fox out of the house.

When old Mr. Fox passed away, a wolf came as a suitor, knocking

at the door, which Miss Cat opened.

The wolf greeted her, saying,

"Good day, Mrs. Cat of Kehrewit. Why are you sitting here all alone?"

"I’m breaking sweet bread in milk,"

she replied.

"No, thank you, Mrs. Cat," said the wolf. "Is Mrs. Fox not around?

If she’s looking for a husband, she should come downstairs."

The cat quickly dashed upstairs,

her tail swishing as she went,

until she reached the parlor door.

With her five golden rings, she knocked on the door,

"Are you in there, my good Mistress Fox?"

"If you’re looking for a husband,"

"you should come downstairs!"

Mrs. Fox asked, "Does the gentleman have red stockings and a

pointed mouth?" "No," answered the cat. "Then he won't do for me."

After the wolf left, a dog, a stag, a hare, a bear, a lion,

and all the beasts of the forest came, one after the other.

But they all lacked the good qualities that old Mr. Fox had, and Miss Cat had to

keep sending the suitors away.

At last, a young fox arrived. Mrs. Fox asked, "Does he have red stockings or a

little pointed mouth?" "Yes," said the cat. "Then let him come upstairs!" said

Mrs. Fox, and instructed the servant to prepare a wedding feast.

"Clean the room as best as you can,

open the window, and throw out my old husband!"

"He brought me many fine, fat mice,

but he never thought about his wife,

greedily eating every one he caught."

Then Mrs. Fox married the young Mr. Fox, and there was much dancing and celebration.

And if they haven't stopped yet, they still might be.

title: The White Bride and the Black One
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
A woman walked through the fields with her daughter and stepdaughter, gathering fodder.

As they worked, a poor man approached them and asked, "Could you direct me to the village?"

"If you want to know," the mother replied, "you'll have to find it yourself," while the daughter added, "If you're worried about getting lost, you should find a guide."

But the stepdaughter said, "Oh, poor man, I'll help you find your way."

God was furious with the mother and daughter for their unkindness, and he turned his back on them, wishing for them to become as dark as night and as ugly as sin.

However, to the kind stepdaughter, God smiled and accompanied her. As they approached the village, He blessed her and said, "Choose three things for yourself, and I will grant your wishes."

The girl answered, "I wish to be as beautiful and radiant as the sun," and in an instant, she became as fair as day.

"Next, I wish for a purse that always has money in it." God granted this too, but He reminded her, "Don't forget the most important wish of all."

She said, "For my third wish, I desire to dwell in the eternal kingdom of heaven after I die." This wish was also granted, and then God left her.

When the stepmother and her daughter returned home to see that they had become as black as coal and ugly, while the stepdaughter was now white and beautiful, their jealousy turned into hatred, and they plotted to harm her.

The stepdaughter had a brother named Reginer, whom she loved dearly. She shared everything that had happened with him.

Reginer said, "Dear sister, I want to paint your portrait so I can keep your image always before me, because my love for you is immense."

She replied, "But please, don't let anyone see the picture."

So he painted her and hung the portrait in his room. He worked as a coachman in the king's palace. Every day, he would stand before the portrait, thanking God for such a wonderful sister.

title: The White Bride and the Black One
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Meanwhile, the king had just lost his wife, who had been so beautiful that no one could compare. His heart was heavy with grief.

The royal attendants noticed the coachman admiring the beautiful portrait daily, and their jealousy drove them to tell the king.

The king summoned the portrait, and upon seeing it was identical to his late wife, yet even more beautiful, he fell hopelessly in love with it.

He ordered the coachman to come before him and asked whose likeness the portrait was. The coachman replied that it was his sister, so the king decided he would take no one but her as his wife.

He gifted the coachman a carriage, horses, and splendid garments, sending him off with his blessings.

When Reginer returned to share the news with his sister, she was overjoyed. But the ugly stepdaughter, burning with envy, raged at her mother, saying, "What good are your tricks if you can't bring me such luck?"

"Calm down," said the old woman. "I'll fix that soon enough," and with her witchcraft, she cast a spell over the coachman, blinding him partially, while she made the white maiden half-deaf.

They climbed into the carriage, starting with the bride in her royal attire, followed by the stepmother and her daughter, while Reginer sat up front to drive.

As they traveled, he suddenly called out, "Cover yourself well, my dear sister, so the rain won't soak you, the wind won't cover you in dust, so you may be fair and lovely when you meet the king."

When the bride asked, "What does my dear brother say?" the old woman lied and said, "He says you should take off your golden dress and give it to your sister."

So, she took it off and donned a shabby gray gown in exchange. They continued on, and not long after, the brother cried again, "Cover yourself well, my dear sister, so the rain won't soak you, the wind won't cover you in dust, so you may be fair and lovely when you meet the king."

title: The White Bride and the Black One
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Again, the bride asked, "What does my dear brother say?" The old woman replied, "He says you should take off your golden hood and give it to your sister."

So she removed the hood and placed it on her sister's head, leaving her own head bare. They pressed on further.

Soon, the brother called out once more, "Cover yourself well, my dear sister, so the rain won't soak you, the wind won't cover you in dust, so you may be fair and lovely when you meet the king."

The bride asked again, "What does my dear brother say?" The old woman replied, "He says to look out of the carriage." They happened to be over a bridge, crossing deep water.

As the bride leaned forward to look out, they both pushed her out, and she fell into the water.

At the same moment she sank, a beautiful snow-white duck emerged from the smooth water and began to swim away.

Reginer, unaware of what had occurred, continued to drive the carriage until they reached the palace.

He brought the dark maiden to the king as his sister, mistaking her for his beloved because his vision was clouded by the spell.

When the king saw the hideousness of his supposed future bride, he was filled with rage and ordered that the coachman be thrown into a pit filled with snakes and adders.

However, the old witch was skilled at flattering the king and deceived him well enough that she and her daughter were allowed to stay.

One evening, as the black bride sat on the king's lap, a white duck swam up the sink to the kitchen and called to the kitchen boy, "Please, light a fire so I can warm my feathers."

The boy complied and lit a fire on the hearth. The duck settled down by it, fluffed up her feathers, and asked, "What’s my brother Reginer up to?"

The scullery boy replied, "He's trapped in the pit with snakes and adders." The duck inquired, "What’s the black witch doing?" The boy answered, "She’s happy and loved by the king."

The duck sighed, "May God have mercy on him," and swam away.

The next night, she returned, asking the same questions, and again on the third night.

Finally, the kitchen boy could no longer keep silent and told the king everything.

The king wanted to see it himself, so he went the following evening, and when the duck popped her head through the sink, he drew his sword and swiftly cut off her head.

Suddenly, she transformed into a stunning maiden, just like the portrait her brother had painted.

The king was overjoyed, and as she stood before him, drenched, he had fine clothes brought for her to wear.

She told him how she had been betrayed and thrown into the water, and her first request was to rescue her brother from the pit of snakes.

Once the king fulfilled this, he went to confront the old witch, asking if she knew the punishment for such treachery, telling her everything that had happened.

She was so blinded by rage that she responded, "She deserves to be stripped and placed in a barrel filled with nails, with a horse sent to drag her all over the world!"

So it was done to her and her dark daughter. Meanwhile, the king married the white and beautiful maiden and rewarded her loyal brother, making him a wealthy and esteemed man.

title: The White Snake
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a king known throughout the land for his incredible wisdom.

Nothing escaped his notice, and it felt like whispers of even the deepest secrets found their way to him.

The king had a peculiar habit; every evening after dinner, once the table was cleared and no one else was around, a loyal servant had to bring him a special dish.

This dish was always covered, and the servant had no idea what it contained.

No one knew what was under the cover because the king never lifted it to eat.

This went on for quite some time, until one day the curious servant, while taking the dish away, couldn't resist the urge to take a peek.

He locked his door carefully, lifted the lid, and found a white snake resting on the plate.

Unable to help himself, he took a small piece and tasted it.

As soon as the morsel touched his tongue, he heard faint whispers coming from outside his window.

He rushed over to listen, and discovered that it was the sparrows chatting and sharing everything they had seen in the fields and woods.

By eating the snake, he had gained the power to understand their language.

On that very day, the queen misplaced her treasured ring, and suspicion fell on the servant, who had access to all places.

The king summoned the servant and warned him, threatening that if he couldn’t identify the thief by morning, he would be presumed guilty and face execution.

Despite his declarations of innocence, he was sent away with no support.

In despair, he wandered into the courtyard, thinking about how to escape his predicament.

Nearby, a group of ducks were relaxing by a brook, grooming themselves while having a private conversation.

The servant listened closely as they recounted their adventures from the morning and the tasty food they had found.

One duck complained, "I feel so heavy because I hastily swallowed a ring that fell under the queen's window."

The servant immediately grabbed the duck by the neck and hurried to the kitchen.

"Here’s a prime duck for you to prepare!" he said to the cook.

The cook agreed, weighing the duck in his hands, saying, "She’s worked hard to get fat enough and has waited long enough to be cooked."

title: The White Snake
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
So, he beheaded the duck, and as it was being dressed, the queen's ring was found.

The servant could now easily prove his innocence, and the king, eager to make things right, offered him a favor and promised him any position he desired in the court.

The servant, however, turned down all offers and simply asked for a horse and some money to travel, wishing to explore the world.

Once his request was granted, he set off on his journey and one day came across a pond where he spotted three fish stuck in the reeds, struggling for air.

Even though people often say fish are silent, he could hear their desperate lamentations about facing a miserable fate, and feeling sympathetic, he dismounted and returned the three fish to the water.

They leaped joyfully, poked their heads above the surface, and called out, "We’ll remember you and repay you for saving us."

As he continued on, he thought he heard a voice from the ground beneath his feet.

He listened closely and soon realized it was the ant king complaining, "Why can’t people keep their heavy creatures off our homes? That clumsy horse has been trampling my people without mercy!"

The servant took a side path, and the ant king exclaimed, "We’ll remember your kindness!"

In the woods, he came across two elderly ravens tossing their young ones out of their nest.

"Out you go, you lazy little ones!" they crowed, "We can’t find any food for you anymore; you’re big enough to fend for yourselves!"

The frightened chicks lay on the ground, flapping their wings and crying, "What helpless little ones we are! We need to fend for ourselves, but we can’t even fly! What are we supposed to do?"

Moved by their plight, the kind young man dismounted, drew his sword, and sacrificed his horse to feed them.

The young ravens approached the offering, satisfied their hunger, and chirped, "We’ll remember your kindness!"

Now on foot, he walked for quite a while until he reached a bustling city.

The streets were filled with noise and commotion, and a man rode by on horseback, shouting, "The king's daughter is looking for a husband, but whoever wants her hand must complete a daunting task. If you fail, you’ll lose your life!"

title: The White Snake
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Many had tried and failed, but when the young man saw the princess, her beauty overwhelmed him, and he forgot all the risks as he stepped forward to express his interest.

The king summoned him to the sea and tossed a gold ring into the water right in front of him, saying, "Bring me that ring from the depths. If you come back empty-handed, you’ll be thrown back in until you drown!"

Everyone watching was saddened for the handsome youth as they left him alone by the shore.

As he stood there, contemplating his next move, he suddenly noticed three familiar fish swimming toward him.

The middle fish carried a mussel, which it placed on the beach at his feet.

When he opened it, he found the gold ring nestled inside.

Overjoyed, he rushed back to the king, expecting to receive the promised reward.

However, the haughty princess looked down on him, feeling he was beneath her due to his humble origins, and demanded he complete another task.

She went to the garden and scattered ten sacks full of millet seeds across the grass, telling him, "You must pick these up by sunrise tomorrow, and not a single grain should be left behind!"

He sat in the garden, wracking his brain for a solution but found nothing, sitting there sorrowfully waiting for dawn and his impending doom.

As soon as the first rays of sunlight entered the garden, he noticed all ten sacks standing full as if untouched, not a single grain missing.

The ant king had come during the night, bringing countless ants to gather the seeds with great care and teamwork.

The princess herself came into the garden, astonished to find that the young man had completed the task.

However, her pride wouldn't let her relent; she said, "Even though he completed both tasks, he cannot be my husband until he brings me an apple from the tree of life."

The young man didn’t know where the tree of life was, but determined, he set off on a journey, prepared to search forever, even without any hope.

After wandering through three kingdoms, he fell asleep under a tree one evening, but was stirred by rustling leaves as a golden apple dropped into his hand.

As he looked up, three ravens landed on his knee, saying, "We’re the three young ravens you saved from starvation; upon hearing you seek the golden apple, we flew across the sea to where the tree of life is and brought you the apple."

Overjoyed, he began his journey back home, intent on giving the golden apple to the king's beautiful daughter, who couldn’t think of any more excuses.

Together, they cut the apple of life in half and shared it, and in that moment, her heart filled with love for him, and they lived happily ever after.

title: The Wilful Child
author: Brothers Grimm
themes: the importance of listening to guidance
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a child who was very willful and refused to do what her mother wanted.

Because of this stubbornness, God was not pleased with her,

and allowed her to fall ill. No doctor was able to help her, and before long,

she found herself lying on her deathbed. Once she was buried,

and the earth was piled on top of her, her arm suddenly shot up,

reaching for the sky. Even after they buried her again and tried to cover the arm,

it always emerged once more. Eventually, the mother had to go to the grave,

and strike the arm with a stick. After doing that,

the arm finally went back down, and at last, the child found peace beneath the earth.

title: The Willow-Wren
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Back in the day, every sound had its own meaning and importance.

When the blacksmith’s hammer struck, it seemed to shout, "Hit it hard, hit it hard."

As the carpenter’s plane scraped along, it announced, "Here we go, here we go."

If the mill wheel started to turn, it called out, "Help, Lord God, help!"

And if the miller was dishonest and set the mill rolling, it spoke clearly, first asking, "Who’s there? Who's there?"

Then it quickly replied, "It’s the miller, the miller." And finally rushed out, "He’s swiping three pecks in a bushel!"

During this time, the birds also had their own language that everyone understood.

Now, it mostly just sounds like chirping, screeching, and whistling, sometimes even like music without words.

But then, the birds thought it was time to choose a ruler from among themselves.

Only the green plover disagreed. He valued his freedom, wanting to live and die free.

Flying anxiously here and there, he cried, "Where should I go? Where should I go?"

He decided to retreat to a quiet, lonely marsh and kept to himself.

The birds wanted to talk about the idea, so on a beautiful May morning, they all gathered from the woods and fields.

Eagles and chaffinches, owls and crows, larks and sparrows, you name it.

Even the cuckoo showed up along with his assistant, the hoopoe, who always announces him a few days in advance.

A tiny bird without a name also joined the crowd.

The hen, who accidentally missed the whole discussion, was shocked by such a large gathering.

"What’s happening? What’s happening?" she clucked.

But the rooster calmed her and explained that it was just a gathering of important birds.

They decided that whoever could fly the highest would become the king.

A tree frog sitting nearby overheard and warned, "No, no, no!" thinking it would lead to tears.

But the crow just cawed, suggesting that everything would be fine.

title: The Willow-Wren
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
They agreed to take off that very morning, so no one could say later, "I could have flown higher, but it got dark."

On cue, the whole group soared into the sky.

Dust flew up from the ground along with a flurry of wings and noise, making it look like a black cloud was emerging.

The smaller birds fell behind quickly, unable to keep up.

The bigger birds managed to stay aloft longer, but none could match the eagle, who soared so high it seemed he could pluck the sun's eyes.

Seeing that no one else could reach him, the eagle thought, "Why fly higher? You're already the king," and began to come down.

The birds beneath him immediately called out, "You must be our king! No one else has flown as high as you."

"Except me," squeaked the nameless little bird that had snuggled into the eagle's feathers.

Not tired at all, he shot up even higher until he reached heaven itself.

When he got as high as he could go, he folded his wings and shouted down with a clear voice, "I am the king! I am the king!"

"You, our king?" the birds shouted angrily. "You got up there by trickery!"

They decided to put a new rule: whoever could dig down the lowest would be the king.

The goose flapped and hopped around when it was time for the test.

The rooster quickly scratched the ground.

The duck had the worst time, jumping into a ditch and spraining her legs. She waddled off to a nearby pond, crying out that it was unfair.

The little bird without a name, however, found a mouse-hole, slipped inside, and announced, "I am the king! I am the king!"

"You, our king?" the birds yelled, even angrier. "Do you think your trickery will work?"

They decided to imprison him in the hole and starve him out.

The owl was placed as the guard and was warned not to let the little trickster out for her own safety.

When evening came and all the birds were tired from flying, they went home to rest with their families.

Only the owl stayed by the mouse-hole, staring intently with her big eyes.

Eventually, she got tired and thought, "I can close one eye and still watch with the other to keep the little rascal from escaping."

So, she shut one eye while keeping the other open on the mouse-hole.

The little bird peeked out, wanting to sneak away, but the owl quickly moved, making him pull his head back.

The owl then switched her eye positions, planning to alternate them throughout the night.

But during her next switch, she forgot to open the other eye and fell fast asleep.

Seeing this, the little bird took his chance and slipped out.

From that day on, the owl never showed herself during the day because the other birds chased her and pulled at her feathers.

She only flies at night now, and she despises and hunts mice for making those annoying holes.

The little bird, too, is very careful not to be seen, fearing for his life if caught.

He sneaks around the hedges, and when he feels safe, he sometimes shouts, "I am king!" This is why the other birds mockingly call him the king of the hedges.

Yet, no one was happier than the lark, who didn’t have to follow the little king's orders.

As soon as the sun shows up, she flies high in the sky, shouting, "Oh, how beautiful this is! Beautiful this is!"

"Beautiful, beautiful! Oh, how beautiful this is!"

title: The Willow-Wren and the Bear
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One summer day, a bear and a wolf were wandering through the forest.

As they walked, the bear heard a bird singing so beautifully that he exclaimed,

"Hey, brother wolf, what bird is singing like that?" "That’s the king

of all birds," replied the wolf, "and we must bow down to him." In truth,

the bird was just a willow-wren. "If that’s the case," the bear said,

"I really want to see his royal palace. Come on, take me there." "It’s not as simple as you think," the

wolf replied. "We have to wait until the queen arrives." Soon, the

queen showed up with food in her beak, and the king followed,

and they began feeding their chicks. The bear wanted to go right away, but the wolf pulled him back by the sleeve and said,

"No, you need to wait until the king and queen leave." So they watched the nest where the little ones were, then ambled off.

However, the bear was restless and couldn't wait to see the royal palace.

After a while, he returned. Just as the king and queen had flown off, he peeked inside and saw five or six chicks resting there.

"Is this the royal palace?" the bear shouted. "This is a terrible palace; you’re not king's children, you’re

just common brats." When the young wrens heard this, they were infuriated and shouted, "No, we’re not! Our parents

are honest creatures. Bear, you’ll pay for saying that!"

The bear and the wolf felt uneasy and quickly retreated to their dens.

Meanwhile, the young willow-wrens kept screaming, and when their parents returned with food, they said, "We won’t even touch a fly’s leg, no, not if we were starving,

until you figure out if we’re respectable kids or not. The bear came here and insulted us." Then the old king

declared, "Don’t worry, he will be punished," and he quickly flew off with the queen to the bear’s cave, calling out, "Old growler, why have you

title: The Willow-Wren and the Bear
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
insulted my children? You will face the consequences - we will punish you!"

Thus, an announcement of war was made against the bear, and all four-legged animals were called to join, like oxen, donkeys, cows, deer, and every other

creature that crawled upon the earth. The willow-wren summoned everything that flew in the sky, not just birds large and small, but also midges,

hornets, bees, and flies were to come too. When the time for battle arrived,

the willow-wren sent out scouts to find out the plan.

The gnat, being the cleverest, flew into the forest where the enemies were gathered and hid under a tree leaf to hear the password.

There stood the bear, and he called the fox to him, saying, "Fox, you’re the smartest of all animals; you’ll be our general and lead us." "Sounds great," said the fox,

"but what signal should we agree on?" No one knew, so the fox proposed, "I have a lovely long bushy tail that looks like a plume

of red feathers. When I lift my tail high, everything's going well, and you must charge; but if I lower it, run away as

fast as you can." After the gnat heard this, she quickly flew back,

and revealed everything, down to the finest details, to the willow-wren.

When day broke and the battle was set to begin, all the four-legged animals rushed in with such noise that the earth shook.

The willow-wren and his army flew through the sky with hums, whirs, and swarms that made everyone feel uneasy.

They both moved toward each other. But the willow-wren sent down a hornet with orders to settle right beneath the fox's tail and sting him mercilessly.

When the fox felt the first sting, he jumped in surprise and lifted one leg due to the pain, but he kept his tail high.

After the second sting, he had to lower it for just a moment, but by the third, he couldn't hold on any longer, screamed, and tucked his tail between his

legs. When the animals saw that, they thought they were doomed and ran off, scrambling for their homes, leaving the birds victorious.

Then the king and queen zipped back home to their little ones, exclaiming,

"Children, celebrate, eat and drink as much as you want! We’ve won the battle!" But the young wrens replied, "We won’t eat yet; the

bear must come to the nest and say he's sorry and admit that we are honorable kids before we do that." Then the willow-wren

flew over to the bear’s cave and shouted, "Growler, you need to come to my nest and apologize to my children, or else we’ll break every bone in your body." So, the bear crept over,

filled with dread, and asked for their forgiveness. Finally, the young wrens were satisfied, and they gathered round to eat, drink, and celebrate

late into the night.

title: The Wise Servant
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The master is truly lucky, and everything runs smoothly in his

house when he has a wise servant who hears his commands

but chooses to follow his own judgment instead. One such

clever servant named Hans was once sent out by his master to find a

missing cow. He was gone for a long while, and the master thought,

loyal Hans must be putting in a lot of effort. But when

he still hadn't returned, the master grew worried that something

might have happened to him and set out himself to search for

the boy. He searched for quite a while, but finally spotted

Hans running around in a large field. "Now, dear Hans,"

said the master as he approached, "have you found the cow?"

"No, master," he replied, "I haven't found the cow, and I

haven't even looked for it." "Then what have you been looking for,"

the master asked. "Three blackbirds," replied the boy.

"And where are they?" asked the master. "I can see one, I hear

the second, and I'm chasing after the third," answered the clever boy.

"Take a lesson from this: don't worry about your

masters or their commands. Instead, do what feels right to you,

and you'll become just as wise as I am.

title: The Wishing-Table, The Gold-Ass, and The Cudgel in the Sack
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a tailor who had three sons,

but only one goat. Still, that goat provided for all of them with

her milk, so she needed good food and must be taken

to pasture every day. The sons took turns with this duty.

One day, the eldest son took her to the churchyard, where the best grass grew,

and let her munch and roam freely. When it was time to

head home, he asked, "Goat, have you had enough?" The goat replied,

"Not a leaf more I'll touch, meh. Meh."

"Then come home," said the young man, taking the rope

around her neck, leading her to the stable, and securely tying her up.

Well, the old tailor asked, "Has the goat eaten enough to stay healthy?"

"Oh, yes," replied the son, "she's eaten so much that not a

leaf more she'll touch." But the father wanted to see for himself,

so he went to the stable, petted the goat, and asked,

"Goat, are you satisfied?" The goat answered,

"Among the ditches I leaped around,

I've found no leaf, so I went hungry, meh. Meh."

"What do I hear?" shouted the tailor, rushing upstairs to the

son. "Hey, you liar! You said the goat had enough, but you've let her starve!"

Fuming, he grabbed a yardstick from the wall and chased him out with blows.

The next day, it was the second son's turn. He found a spot

in the garden fence, where only the best herbs grew, and

the goat devoured them all. When evening came, he asked,

"Goat, are you satisfied?" The goat replied,

"Not a leaf more I'll touch, meh. Meh."

"Then come home," said the boy, bringing her back and tying her

up in the stable. Again, the tailor asked if the goat had had enough.

"Oh, she has eaten so much, not a single leaf more she'll touch,"

title: The Wishing-Table, The Gold-Ass, and The Cudgel in the Sack
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
the son answered. The tailor wasn't convinced, so he went down to ask.

"Goat, have you had enough?"

"Among the ditches I leaped about,

I found no leaf, so I went without, meh. Meh."

"You ungodly rascal!" cried the tailor, realizing he had let such a good goat

go hungry. Furious, he dashed upstairs and kicked the second son out.

Now it was the youngest son's turn, who wanted to do his duty

properly. He found some bushes filled with lush leaves and let the

goat munch away. In the evening, when he was ready to return, he

asked, "Goat, have you had enough?" The goat replied,

"Not a leaf more I'll touch, meh. Meh."

"Then come home," said the young man, leading her into the stable and

tying her up. Once again, the tailor asked, has the goat had her fill?

"She's eaten so much that not a leaf more she'll touch."

Still doubtful, the tailor went downstairs and asked, "Goat,

have you had enough?" The evade replied,

"Among the ditches I leaped about,

I found no leaf, so I went without, meh. Meh."

"Oh, the lot of them are liars," cried the tailor, each one as wicked and

irresponsible as the other. "You won't fool me anymore!" Enraged, he dashed upstairs

and beat the poor young man soundly with the yardstick until he ran out.

Now, the tailor was all alone with his goat. The next morning, he

went to the stable, cuddled the goat, and said, "Come, my dear animal,

I will take you to eat myself." He took her by the rope and led her to the

green hedges, amongst the milfoil and all the tasty things goats love.

"Today, you can eat to your heart's content," he said, allowing her to munch until

evening. Then he asked, "Goat, are you satisfied?" She replied,

"Not a leaf more I'll touch, meh. Meh."

title: The Wishing-Table, The Gold-Ass, and The Cudgel in the Sack
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Then come home," said the tailor, leading her back and tying her securely.

As he walked away, he turned back and asked, "Are you really satisfied now?"

But the goat just replied,

"Among the ditches I leapt about,

found no leaf, so went without, meh. Meh."

Hearing that, the tailor was horrified, realizing he had driven away his three sons unjustly.

"Wait, you ungrateful creature!" he shouted. "It's not enough to have driven you off,

I'll teach you a lesson you won't forget!" In his rage, he rushed upstairs, grabbed his

razor, lathered the goat's head, and shaved her clean, making her look like the palm

of his hand. And since a yardstick was too good for her, he fetched the horsewhip and

gave her a sound beating, causing her to jump away with huge leaps.

Left alone in his house, the tailor fell into deep sorrow, wishing for his sons to return,

but no one knew where they had gone. The eldest had become an apprentice to a joiner,

working diligently, and when it was time for him to travel, his master gave him a small table,

which wasn't fancy and was made from ordinary wood, but it had one magical feature.

Whenever someone set it up and said, "Little table, spread yourself," it would magically

be covered with a clean cloth, a plate, a knife, a fork, and delicious dishes like boiled and

roasted meats, all topped off with a glass of wonderful red wine that made you feel good.

The young craftsman thought, "With this table, I'll have everything I need for a lifetime," and

set off into the world, never worrying about whether an inn was good or bad.

If he felt like it, he didn’t even bother going to an inn; instead, he would stop in a field,

a wood, or a meadow. Wherever he chose, he would take the table off his back, set it down,

and say, "Spread yourself," and everything he desired would magically appear.

title: The Wishing-Table, The Gold-Ass, and The Cudgel in the Sack
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Eventually, he decided it was time to return home to his father, believing his father's anger

would have softened, and he would welcome him back with his magical table.

On his way home, he stopped one evening at an inn filled with guests.

They welcomed him and invited him to sit and eat with them, saying he would otherwise struggle to

get a meal. "No, thanks," replied the joiner. "I would rather treat you all as my guests."

They chuckled, thinking he was joking. He just set his wooden table in the middle of the room and

said, "Little table, spread yourself." In an instant, it was filled with such delicious food that

the innkeeper knew he could never afford it, and the aroma wafted tantalizingly to all.

"Dig in, dear friends," said the joiner. The guests, seeing that he meant it,

didn't need to be asked twice; they rushed over, pulled out their knives, and dug in.

What amazed them most was that when a dish was emptied, another would instantly appear.

The innkeeper watched from the corner, unsure what to make of it, but he thought,

"I could definitely use a cook like that in my home." That night, they feasted well.

Eventually, they lay down to sleep, and the young apprentice placed his magic table against the

wall. But the innkeeper couldn't rest, consumed by curiosity.

He remembered a little old table in his storage room that looked similar, so he fetched it,

and carefully swapped it for the magical table while the apprentice slept.

The next morning, the joiner paid for his lodging, took the swapped table,

never realizing it was a fake, and headed home. Around midday, he reached his father's house,

who welcomed him joyfully. "So, my dear son, what have you learned?"

"Father, I've learned a great trade," the youngster replied.

"That's great, my boy! What did you bring back with you?"

"The best thing I brought back is this little table!"

The tailor examined it closely and said, "This isn't a great piece of work.

title: The Wishing-Table, The Gold-Ass, and The Cudgel in the Sack
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
It's just an old, plain table." "But it spreads itself!" insisted the son.

"When I set it up and tell it to spread, the finest dishes appear, along with the wine!

Let's invite our family and friends over; they can enjoy a feast on this magical table!"

When everyone gathered, he placed the table in the middle of the room and said, "Spread yourself!"

But the little table did nothing, remaining as bare as any ordinary table.

The apprentice realized his table had been swapped and felt ashamed to stand there.

The relatives laughed and left without eating or drinking.

The father resumed his tailoring, while the son went to work at a mill.

The second son had become an apprentice to a miller.

After finishing his training, his master said, "Since you've done well, here’s an unusual ass.

This one doesn't pull carts or carry bags."

"Then what good is he?" the apprentice asked, confused.

"He produces gold!" the miller answered. "If you set him on a cloth and say 'bricklebrit,'

he will spit gold coins for you!" The apprentice was thrilled and thanked his master.

Headed out into the world, whenever he needed money, he just had to say 'bricklebrit' to his ass,

and gold pieces would rain down all around him.

Wherever he went, he never had to settle for less—he picked the best of everything.

After traveling for a while, he thought, "I should visit my father. He'll forgive me once he sees I have the gold-ass.

It just so happened he stopped at the same inn where his brother's table was swapped.

Leading his donkey up to the stables, the host tried to take it from him, but the boy said,

"No worries, I'll tie my donkey myself. I need to know where he is."

The innkeeper found this odd, thinking that someone who had to look after their ass themselves couldn’t afford much.

But when the apprentice produced two gold coins from his pocket and asked for a nice meal,

title: The Wishing-Table, The Gold-Ass, and The Cudgel in the Sack
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
the innkeeper's eyes widened, and he quickly gathered the best he could find.

After dinner, the guest wanted to settle up.

The innkeeper, seeing an opportunity, doubled the bill and claimed he needed two more gold coins.

The apprentice checked his pockets and realized he was out of gold.

"Just a moment, I'll fetch some money!" he said, grabbing the tablecloth.

Curious, the innkeeper secretly followed him. As the guest shut the stable door,

the innkeeper peered through a knot hole in the wood.

The apprentice spread the cloth under the donkey and shouted, "bricklebrit!"

Immediately, gold coins began tumbling from the donkey's backside, raining down money everywhere.

"Wow! That’s a lot of money!" thought the innkeeper.

"I could use that in my establishment." The guest paid his bill and went to bed.

But during the night, the innkeeper snuck into the stable, took the gold donkey, and tied up

his own donkey instead.

The next morning, the apprentice set off with what he thought was his gold donkey.

By midday, he arrived at his father's home, excited to be welcomed back.

"So, son, what have you become?" asked the old man.

"A miller, dear father," he replied.

"And what do you bring back from your travels?"

"Just an ass, but not just any ass, a magical one! When I say 'bricklebrit,' it spits out gold coins!"

"That sounds fantastic! Let's invite everyone, and they can all be rich!"

The tailor was intrigued, thinking he could finally retire from sewing.

He hurried to gather the relatives.

Once they gathered, the miller instructed them, "Step back, let me show you!"

He spread a cloth, shouted ‘bricklebrit,’ but instead of gold, nothing happened.

It was clear his donkey had no magic at all, as every donkey cannot be extraordinary.

The miller’s heart sank, realizing he had been tricked, and he begged for forgiveness,

but his relatives left as broke as when they arrived.

title: The Wishing-Table, The Gold-Ass, and The Cudgel in the Sack
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Disappointed, the old tailor returned to his needle, while the miller took on an apprenticeship.

The third brother had trained as a turner, taking the longest to learn.

His brothers wrote him letters, telling of their misfortunes, how the innkeeper had stolen their gifts.

When the turner completed his training, his master gifted him a sack, telling him,

"There’s a cudgel inside. It may seem useless, but it'll be very handy.

"If anyone wrongs you, just say, 'Out of the sack, cudgel!' and the cudgel will spring out and thump them.

It won’t stop until you tell it to go back into the sack.”

The apprentice thanked his master and put the sack on his back.

If someone came at him, he would say, 'Out of the sack, cudgel!' and the cudgel would jump out, giving them a thrashing.

It was so quick that before anyone realized what was happening, they'd already received a beating.

When evening came, the young turner arrived at the same inn where his brothers had been cheated.

He laid the sack on the table and began sharing exciting tales of what he had seen.

"Yes, some people have tables that spread food, or magical donkeys,

and they are indeed nice,” said he, “but nothing beats the treasure I carry in this sack.

The innkeeper's interest piqued; he wondered what amazing jewels could be inside.

That night, as the guest lay on the bench with the sack as a pillow,

the innkeeper thought he could sneak away with it.

He gently pulled and tugged at the sack, trying to replace it with another.

But the turner had been waiting for this moment. Just as the innkeeper gave a hard tug, he shouted,

"Out of the sack, cudgel!" The little cudgel sprang out and started whacking the innkeeper.

The innkeeper begged for mercy, but the harder he cried, the more the cudgel beat him.

Finally, he collapsed in exhaustion. The turner warned,

"If you don’t return the table and the gold donkey, the cudgel will start again!"

title: The Wishing-Table, The Gold-Ass, and The Cudgel in the Sack
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Oh no!" the host pleaded, "Please, I’ll get everything back, just make it stop!"

The apprentice replied, "I'll show you mercy this time, but don't get into mischief again."

He commanded, "Into the sack, cudgel!" letting the innkeeper breathe a sigh of relief.

The next morning, the turner returned home to his father with the wishing table and the gold-ass.

The tailor was overjoyed to see him again and asked about his travels.

"Dear father, I've become a turner!" he replied.

"That's a skilled trade! What valuable thing did you bring me?

"A unique cudgel, dear father!" the son exclaimed.

"A cudgel? Is that worth the trouble? You can find those anywhere.

But hold on! Not one like this, father. If I say, 'Out of the sack, cudgel,' it jumps out and gives anyone troubling me a thorough beating.

"I used it to rescue the magical table and gold donkey from the innkeeper who stole them.

Let's invite everyone over; I’ll treat them well and fill their pockets with gold!"

Though unsure, the old tailor called all the relatives together.

The turner spread out his cloth and brought in the gold donkey.

The miller called out, "Bricklebrit!" and promptly gold coins fell like rain onto the cloth.

Then the turner led in the little table and said to his brother,

"Now, dear brother, speak to it!"

As soon as the carpenter said, "Table, spread yourself!" it elegantly laid out delicious dishes.

The tailor had never experienced such a feast, and everyone stayed late into the night, filled with joy.

The tailor put the needle and thread away for good and lived happily with his three sons.

And what happened to the goat, who caused the tailor to throw out his sons? Well, here’s the tale.

Feeling embarrassed by her bald head, the goat ran off to a fox's den.

When the fox arrived home, he was startled by two glowing eyes in the darkness.

Terrified, he ran away. A bear encountered him and, noticing the fox's distress, asked,

‘What’s wrong, brother Fox? You seem worried.’

"Oh, it's just that a fierce creature is in my cave, staring at me with fiery eyes," answered the fox.

The bear said, "Let’s go together, and we’ll drive it out!"

But once they looked in and saw the eyes, fear gripped the bear too. He fled.

A bee flew by and noticed the bear's fearful expression. She asked,

"Bear, why the long face? You used to be cheerful.

It's easy for you to say," replied the bear. "There's a terrifying beast in the fox's home.

But the bee said, "I may be small, but I think I can help!"

She darted into the cave, landed on the goat's shaven head, and stung her.

The goat jumped up, bleating, "Meh, meh!" and ran off wildly, never to be found again.

title: The Wolf and the Fox
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The wolf had the fox by his side, and whatever the wolf wanted, the fox had no choice but to do. He was the weaker of the two and secretly wished to be free from his master.

One day, while they were wandering through the forest, the wolf said, "Hey, red fox, go find me something to eat, or I’ll eat you myself."

The fox replied, "I know a farm where there are two young lambs. If you're up for it, we can get one of them."

That sounded good to the wolf, so they headed to the farm. The fox snuck in, stole the little lamb, brought it back to the wolf, and quickly left.

The wolf devoured the lamb but wasn’t satisfied with just one. He wanted the other one too and decided to go back for it. However, he was so clumsy that the mother lamb heard him and started bleating loudly, which attracted the farmers.

The farmers arrived and found the wolf, beating him so badly that he limped back to the fox, howling in pain. "You tricked me really well," he said. "I tried to grab the other lamb, and those farmers caught me and beat me up."

The fox simply replied, "Well, what did you expect?"

The next day, they went out again, and the greedy wolf once more said, "Hey, red fox, find me something to eat, or I’ll eat you myself."

The fox answered, "I know a farmhouse where the lady is baking pancakes tonight. Let’s see if we can get some for ourselves."

They went over there, and the fox sneaked around the house, sniffing around until he found the dish. He quickly grabbed six pancakes and brought them to the wolf.

"Here’s something for you to eat," he said, and then went on his way. The wolf scarfed down the pancakes in no time and exclaimed, "I need more!" He went over and knocked the whole dish down, making a huge mess.

The noise caught the attention of the woman, who rushed out. When she saw the wolf, she called for help, and soon people came running, beating him until he returned to the forest, limping with two sore legs.

"You’ve really misled me this time!" he shouted, "The farmers caught me and thrashed me!" The fox replied, "What did you expect?"

On the third day, the wolf, barely able to walk because of his injuries, said again, "Hey, red fox, find me something to eat, or I’ll eat you myself."

The fox said, "I know of a man who’s been cooking, and there’s salted meat in a barrel in the cellar. Let’s go get it."

The wolf replied, "I’ll follow you so you can help me if I can’t escape." The fox agreed and guided him along the back paths until they reached the cellar.

The cellar was full of meat, and the wolf quickly dug in, thinking he had plenty of time to feast. The fox, although hungry too, kept checking the hole they entered to make sure he could still fit through.

The wolf asked, "Why are you running around and jumping like that?" The fox replied, "I just want to make sure no one is coming."

The wolf said, "Don’t worry about me; I won’t leave until the barrel is empty." Meanwhile, the farmer, hearing the noise from the fox’s jumping, stepped into the cellar.

The fox spotted him and jumped out through the hole in one swift move. The wolf tried to follow but had eaten so much that he got stuck.

The farmer approached with his club and struck the wolf dead, while the fox happily bounded back into the forest, glad to be free of the greedy wolf.

title: The Wolf and the Man
author: Brothers Grimm
themes: the strength of cunning over brute strength
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, the fox was chatting with the wolf about the

power of humans. He explained how no animal could stand against them, and how

every creature had to rely on cleverness to protect themselves

from them. The wolf responded, "If I ever get a chance to

see a human, I wouldn't hesitate to go after him."

"I can help you with that," said the fox. "Come see me bright and early

tomorrow, and I’ll introduce you to one." The wolf showed up early, and the fox led him down a path that

the hunters used every day. First, an old retired soldier strolled by.

"Is that a man?" the wolf asked. "No," replied the fox, "that was a man."

Then a little boy walked by on his way to school.

"Is that a man?" "No, but that one will be." Finally, a

hunter appeared, carrying a double-barreled gun on his back and a knife at his side. The fox said to the wolf, "Look, there’s a man.

You should go after him, but I’m going to hightail it back to my den." The

wolf charged at the man. When the hunter saw him, he muttered, "I wish I had loaded my gun with a bullet." He aimed,

then shot the wolf with a round of small shot. The wolf grimaced but wasn't deterred; he attacked again, prompting the hunter to fire a second time.

The wolf felt the pain but charged at the hunter once more, only for the hunter to pull out his sharp knife and slash at him

from both sides, causing the wolf to bleed and howl as he retreated to the fox.

"Well, my friend wolf, how did it go with the human?" the fox asked. The wolf replied, "I never expected a human to be so strong.

First, he took a stick from his shoulder and blew into it, and something flew into my face, tickling me terribly.

Then he blew into the stick again, and it shot into my nose like a bolt of lightning.

When I got really close, he pulled out a white rod from his body, and beat me so hard that I nearly fell down dead.

"Look at you, what a braggart!" the fox said. "You throw your hatchet so far that you can't even retrieve it."

title: The Wolf and the Seven Little Kids
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was an old goat who had seven little kids.

She loved them just like any mother loves her children. One day,

she decided to go into the forest to get some food. So she called

all seven to her and said, "Listen, my dear children, I have to go into the

forest. Be on your guard against the wolf; if he comes in, he'll

devour you all—skin, hair, and everything. That villain often

disguises himself, but you can recognize him right away by his rough voice

and his black feet. The kids replied, "Don’t worry, dear mother, we’ll take great

care of ourselves. You can go without worrying." Then the old

goat bleated and left with a relaxed heart.

It wasn't long before someone knocked at the door and called,

"Open the door, my dear children! Your mother is back, and she has brought

something special for each of you." But the little kids recognized

the wolf by his rough voice. "We won't open the door," they cried, "You're not

our mother. She has a soft, sweet voice, but yours is rough. You’re the wolf!"

So the wolf left and went to a shopkeeper to buy a large chunk of chalk.

He ate it to soften his voice. Then he returned, knocked at the

door of the house, and called, "Open the door, my dear children! Your

mother is back and has brought something special for each of you."

But the wolf had pressed his black paws against the window, and

the kids saw them and shouted, "We won't open the door! Our

mother doesn’t have black feet like you. You’re the wolf!" Then the wolf

ran to a baker and said, "I hurt my feet. Please rub some dough on

them for me." When the baker did this, he ran to

the miller and said, "Sprinkle some white flour over my feet for me." The

miller thought, "The wolf is trying to trick someone," and refused. But the wolf

title: The Wolf and the Seven Little Kids
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
threatened, "If you don’t do it, I’ll eat you!"

So the miller got scared and made the wolf’s paws white for him. Sure enough,

the villain went to the door again, knocked,

and said, "Open up, children! Your dear mother has come back and has something

for each of you from the forest." The little kids shouted, "First show us your

paws so we can see if you’re truly our mother." Then he put

his paws through the window, and when the kids saw they were

white, they believed him and opened the door.

But who should walk in but the wolf! They were terrified and scattered to hide.

One jumped under the table, the second dove into the

bed, the third hid in the stove, the fourth in the kitchen, the fifth

in the cupboard, the sixth under the washtub, and the seventh

in the clock-case. But the wolf quickly found them and, without any hesitation,

he gobbled them all down one by one. The youngest, who was in the clock-case, was the only one he missed.

Once the wolf had quenched his hunger, he lay down under a tree in the lush meadow outside and started to sleep.

Not long after, the old goat returned from the forest. What a sight she saw! The door was wide open.

The table, chairs, and benches were overturned, the washtub lay shattered, and the blankets and pillows were

tossed off the bed. She searched for her children, but they were nowhere

to be found. She called each of them by name, but received no answer.

Finally, when she called for the youngest, a soft voice

responded, "Dear mother, I’m in the clock-case." She took the kid out,

and he told her that the wolf had come and eaten all the others.

You can imagine how heartbroken she was over her poor children.

Eventually, in her sorrow, she went outside, and the youngest kid followed her.

When they reached the meadow, they found the wolf lying under the tree,

snoring so loudly that the branches shook. She looked at him carefully and saw something moving and struggling in his

big belly. "Oh, heavens!" she said, "Is it possible my poor

children that he swallowed for dinner might still be alive?"

Then the kid ran home to get scissors, a needle,

and thread. The goat cut the monster's stomach open, and just as she made her first cut, a little kid poked its head out.

As she cut further, all six kids tumbled out one after the other, alive and unharmed, since the greedy monster had swallowed them whole.

Oh, what a joyous reunion! They hugged their dear mother and jumped around like sailors celebrating a wedding.

However, the mother said, "Now go find some big stones so we can

fill the wicked beast's belly while he’s still sleeping." So the seven kids hurried to fetch the stones,

and they stuffed as many as they could into his stomach, while the mother sewed him up quickly,

so he remained unaware and didn’t stir at all.

When the wolf finally woke up, he got to his feet, and feeling thirsty from the stones in his stomach,

he wanted to go to a well to drink. But as he walked, the stones rattled around inside him.

He grumbled, "What’s rumbling and tumbling against my poor bones? I thought it was six kids, but it feels like big rocks!"

When he reached the well and bent over to drink, the heavy stones caused him to fall in, and he drowned.

When the seven kids saw this, they ran to the well and shouted,

"The wolf is dead! The wolf is dead!" and danced for joy around the well with their mother.

title: The Wonderful Musician
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a talented musician who wandered through a forest, feeling quite down.

As he strolled, he found himself lost in thought, and when his mind ran out of things to ponder, he said to himself,

"Time seems to be dragging on here in the forest."

So, he decided to find a good friend. He pulled his fiddle from his back and played a tune that echoed through the trees.

Before long, a wolf came trotting through the underbrush towards him. "Oh no, here comes a wolf," the musician thought.

"I really don’t want him around," he mumbled to himself, but the wolf drew closer and said, "Dear musician, your playing is so beautiful! I would love to learn how to do that, too."

"Learning is easy," the musician replied, "You just need to follow all my instructions."

"Oh, musician," said the wolf, "I promise I'll listen to you like a student listens to a teacher."

The musician instructed him to follow along, and after they walked a bit, they reached an old oak tree that was hollow inside and split in the middle.

"Look," said the musician, "if you want to learn how to fiddle, stick your front paws into this gap."

The wolf obliged, and while he did, the musician quickly grabbed a stone and with one swift move, wedged his paws tight, trapping him like a prisoner.

"Stay there until I come back," the musician said, and then he walked away.

After some time, he thought again, "This forest is getting boring. I need another companion," and took out his fiddle to play once more.

It wasn’t long before a fox emerged, sneaking through the trees.

"Ah, a fox is coming," the musician remarked. "I have no interest in him."

The fox approached him and said, "Oh, dear musician, your playing is so lovely! I want to learn it as well."

"That’s easy to learn," said the musician. "You just need to do everything I tell you."

title: The Wonderful Musician
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Oh, musician," the fox replied, "I will follow your commands just like a student follows a teacher."

"Come along then," said the musician, and as they walked a bit further, they arrived at a narrow path lined with tall bushes.

The musician stopped and bent down a young hazel bush, putting his foot on it to hold it down.

Then he bent another young tree from the opposite side and said, "Now, little fox, if you want to learn something, give me your left front paw."

The fox complied, and the musician tied his paw to the left branch.

"Now, little fox, reach me your right paw," he instructed.

He tied that paw to the right branch too. Once he checked that the knots were secure, he let go, and the branches snapped back, lifting the fox into the air.

"Wait there until I come back," the musician called out.

Once again, he thought, "Time is dragging on here in the forest. I need another companion."

So, he picked up his fiddle, and the sound echoed through the trees.

A little hare hopped toward him. "Oh, a hare is coming," the musician said, "but I don’t want him."

"Dear musician," said the hare, "you play so beautifully! I’d love to learn that too."

"Learning is quick," the musician reassured him. "You just need to do everything I tell you."

"Oh, musician," the little hare replied, "I’ll do whatever you say like a student who listens to a teacher."

They traveled together for a while until they arrived at a clearing in the forest with an aspen tree.

The musician tied a long string to the little hare and fastened the other end to the tree.

"Now run around the tree twenty times!" shouted the musician.

The little hare dashed around, and after twenty laps, the string wrapped tightly around the tree.

No matter how much the hare pulled and struggled, it only made the string dig into its soft neck.

"Wait there until I come back," the musician instructed, and he moved on.

Meanwhile, the wolf had been working hard to free himself, pushing and biting at the stone.

He finally managed to free his paws and, filled with rage, raced after the musician, eager for revenge.

When the fox saw the wolf charging, he started to cry out, "Brother wolf, help me! The musician has betrayed me!"

The wolf broke down the little tree, snapped the rope in two, and freed the fox, who joined him to get back at the musician.

They also saved the hare, and together they set off to confront their enemy.

The musician, meanwhile, had played his fiddle again as he continued on his way, this time attracting the attention of a poor woodcutter.

Without even thinking about it, the woodcutter set his work aside and came with his hatchet in hand to enjoy the music.

"Finally, the right companion has come," the musician said, pleased to see a human instead of wild animals.

He played beautifully, and the woodcutter stood mesmerized, his heart filled with joy.

As he watched, the wolf, the fox, and the hare appeared, and he could tell they were up to no good.

So, he raised his shiny axe, positioning himself in front of the musician as if to say, "Anyone who tries to harm him will have to deal with me."

The creatures, frightened, turned and ran back into the forest.

The musician, grateful, played one last song for the woodcutter before moving on.

title: The Young Giant
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, a farmer had a son who was no bigger than a thumb.

He didn't grow at all, staying the same size for many years.

One day, as the father was getting ready to plow the fields,

the little boy said, "Dad, I want to go with you."

"You want to come with me?" the father replied. "Stay here; you won't be any help out there, and you might get lost."

The boy started to cry, so the father, wanting peace, put him in his pocket and took him along.

Once they were out in the fields, the father took him out and set him down in a freshly turned furrow.

While he sat there, a huge giant came over the hill.

"Do you see that giant?" the father said, trying to scare the little boy into behaving. "He’s coming to get you!"

But the giant barely took two steps before he was standing in the furrow.

He carefully picked up the tiny boy with two fingers, examined him, and without saying a single word, walked away.

The father stood frozen in fear, thinking his child was lost forever.

But the giant took him home, let him drink from his breast, and soon the boy grew tall and strong, just like other giants.

After two years, the old giant decided to test him and said, "Pull up a stick for yourself."

At that point, the boy was already strong enough to pull out a young tree by the roots.

But the giant thought, "We need to do better than that," and suckled him for another two years.

When he was tested again, the boy's strength had increased so much that he could rip an even bigger tree from the earth.

Still, that wasn't enough for the giant; he suckled him for two more years.

When they went into the forest again, the giant challenged him once more, saying, "Now, pull up a real stick."

The boy yanked up the biggest oak tree, making it crack as if it were nothing.

title: The Young Giant
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"That's good enough," said the giant, pleased. "You're perfect now." He took him back to the field where he had found him.

The farmer was plowing when the young giant approached him, saying, "Dad, don’t you see how I’ve grown into a fine man?"

The farmer was shocked and said, "No, you’re not my son. I don't want you—leave me!"

"I am your son! Let me help you with your work!" the boy insisted. "I can plow as well as you, even better!"

"No, no! You’re not my son, and you can't plow! Get away from me!" The farmer was frightened of this giant figure.

He dropped the plow, stepped back, and sat down at the edge of the field.

The young giant then grabbed the plow with one hand, but his grip was so strong that it went into the ground effortlessly.

The farmer couldn’t bear to watch and called out, "If you’re going to plow, don’t press so hard—it ruins the work!"

But the boy ignored him, unharnessed the horses, and pulled the plow himself, saying, "Just go home, Dad, and tell Mom to prepare a big meal. I’ll take care of the field."

So the farmer went home and asked his wife to make the food, while the youth plowed the two-acre field all by himself.

After finishing, he harnessed himself to the harrow, using two at once to harrow the whole land.

When he was done, he went into the forest, pulled up two oak trees, and slung them over his shoulders.

He hung one harrow on each side, with one horse before and one behind, and carried it all like it was a bundle of straw back to his parents' house.

When he entered the yard, his mother didn’t recognize him and asked, "Who is that terrible tall man?"

The father replied, "That’s our son!"

His mother insisted, "No, that can’t be our son; he was tiny. Go away; we don’t want you!"

title: The Young Giant
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The boy stayed quiet, but guided his horses to the stable, giving them oats and hay.

After taking care of them, he came into the living room and said, "Mom, I’m hungry. Is the food ready?"

"Yes," she said, bringing in two enormous dishes filled with food, enough to feed herself and her husband for a week.

The youth devoured all of it in one go and then asked, "Do you have anything else?"

"No," she replied, "that’s all we have."

"That was just a snack; I need more!"

Not daring to refuse, she set a massive pig's trough filled with food on the fire.

When it was ready, she brought it in, but he said, "Is that all?" and gobbled everything again.

Still not satisfied, he said, "Dad, I can see I’ll never get enough food here. If you bring me a strong iron staff that I can’t break against my knees, I’ll go out into the world."

The farmer was pleased, hitched up his two horses, and fetched a staff so large and thick that the horses could barely carry it.

The boy placed it across his knees, snapped it in two like a twig, and tossed it aside.

Then the father harnessed four horses and brought back a bar so long and thick that it took all four horses to haul it.

The boy broke this in half effortlessly as well and said, "Dad, this won’t work. You need to get more horses and a stronger staff!"

So the father harnessed eight horses and retrieved an even bigger staff.

The young giant grabbed it, snapped the end off, and said, "Dad, you won’t be able to find the staff I need. I’m leaving now!"

He left and claimed to be a blacksmith's apprentice. He arrived at a village with a stingy blacksmith who never helped anyone but himself.

The young man entered the smithy and asked if he needed an apprentice.

"Yes," the smith replied, sizing him up. "That looks like a strong guy who can swing a hammer well."

title: The Young Giant
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Curious, he asked, "How much do you want for wages?"

"I don’t want any wages," the youth replied. "Just pay me every two weeks with two blows from your hammer that I’ll endure."

The miser was thrilled, thinking he would save money.

The next morning, the unwelcome apprentice began working. When the smith brought in a glowing iron bar, the boy struck once, and the iron shattered.

The anvil sank deep into the ground, impossible to retrieve.
