The blacksmith was furious and said, "I can’t use you; your strikes are too powerful! How much for the next hammer?"

"I’ll give you a gentle tap—that’s all!" he said.

He raised his foot and kicked the blacksmith so hard he flew over four hayloads.

Afterward, he found the thickest iron bar in the smithy, took it as a cane, and left.

As he walked, he reached a small farm and asked the bailiff if he needed a foreman.

"Yeah," the bailiff said. "I could use someone capable. How much do you want to be paid?"

He responded, "I’ll take no salary but will give you three blows each year that you must accept."

Satisfied, the bailiff thought this would save him money too.

The next morning, as everyone went to the woods, the other workers rose early, but the foreman stayed in bed.

One of them called out, "Get up! It’s time to go to the woods!"

The head-man grumbled, "You go on without me; I’ll be back before you know it."

The others told the bailiff about his refusal, and he instructed them to wake him up.

"Tell him to harness the horses!" the bailiff added.

But the head-man reiterated, "You go ahead, I’ll be back faster than any of you." He stayed in bed for another two hours.

Eventually, he got out, fetched two bushels of peas from the loft, made broth, and leisurely ate.

Once done, he harnessed the horses and drove into the woods.

title: The Young Giant
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Not far into the woods, there was a ravine he needed to cross.

He led the horses across, then stopped to make a barrier so they could not pass through.

As he entered the forest, the others were just driving out, ready to go home.

"Go ahead," he said. "I’ll be home before you!"

He didn’t venture far, quickly tore up two massive trees, tossed them into his cart, and headed back.

When he approached the barricade, the others were still stuck, unable to get through.

"If you had stayed with me," he remarked, "you could have gotten home faster and enjoyed some extra sleep!"

He tried to move on, but the horses were trapped, so he unhitched them, placed them on top of the cart, took the reins, and pulled the entire thing through with ease.

Once across, he told the others, "See, I’ve made it back quicker than you!"

In the yard, he grabbed a tree limb, displayed it to the bailiff, and said, "Look at the beautiful firewood I’ve brought!"

The bailiff turned to his wife and said, "This servant is quite good! Even if he sleeps late, he always gets back before the others."

He worked for the bailiff for a year, and as the others were collecting their wages, he said it was time he got his too.

The bailiff, however, feared the blows and pleaded to be excused, saying he would rather be the head-man.

"No, I won’t be a bailiff; I’m the head-man and I’ll stay that way," he replied. "But I’ll take what we agreed upon."

The bailiff was willing to give him whatever he wanted, but the head-man turned everything down.

Confused, the bailiff asked for a fortnight’s delay to figure something out.

The head-man agreed to wait.

The bailiff called in all his clerks to brainstorm and find advice.

They thought for a long time but concluded that the head-man was dangerous, capable of killing a man as easily as a fly.

title: The Young Giant
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
So, the bailiff should have him clean the well, and then, while he was down there, they would roll a millstone on him.

The idea pleased the bailiff, and the head-man agreed to lower himself down the well.

When he was at the bottom, they shoved the largest millstone down, hoping to crush him.

But he yelled, "Get those hens away from the well! They’re tossing sand in my eyes!"

The bailiff called, "Shh!" pretending to scare the hens away.

After finishing his job, the head-man climbed back up and said, "Look at this beautiful necktie I have!"

And there it was, the millstone around his neck.

Wanting his pay, he faced yet another delay, and the clerks advised the bailiff to send the head-man to the haunted mill to grind corn at night.

No man had returned from there alive.

The bailiff liked the plan and summoned the head-man that very evening, telling him to take eight bushels of corn and grind it at the haunted mill.

The head-man loaded corn into his pockets and a wallet, then headed to the mill.

The miller warned him, "You can grind during the day, but not at night; it’s haunted and no one has survived a night there."

"I’ll manage it; you just go and get some rest," he replied.

Once inside, he poured out the corn, and around eleven, he went into the miller’s room and sat on a bench.

Suddenly, a door opened, and a large table floated in, settings of wine, meats, and other delicious food preparing themselves.

Nothing was carried by anyone; it all happened magically.

Chairs rearranged themselves, yet still, no people appeared until fingers began handling knives and forks, laying food on the plates.

As he was hungry, he decided to sit at the table, joining those invisible diners.

After eating his fill, he noticed all the candles snuffed out and felt a slap on the face.

"If that happens again, I’ll punch back!" he exclaimed.

When he got slapped again, he did just that!

And thus the whole night went on; he didn’t take anything without giving something back, returning favors fiercely, but never harming anyone.

As dawn broke, everything stopped. The miller awoke and came to check on him, wondering if he was still alive.

The young man replied, "I gave as good as I got!"

The miller was thrilled, saying the mill was finally freed from its curse.

He offered the young man a fortune as a reward.

"I don’t want money; I have plenty of that," he replied.

So, he loaded his meal on his back and headed home, letting the bailiff know he had done as asked.

Hearing this, the bailiff panicked; he walked around the room, beads of sweat forming on his forehead.

Then, desperate for fresh air, he opened a window, but before he knew it, the head-man kicked him out and into the air.

And he flew away, never to be seen again.

The head-man then turned to the bailiff's wife and said, "If he doesn’t return, you must take the next blow!"

She cried, "No, I can’t take one!"

She opened the other window out of fear, releasing her own sweat.

He then gave her a kick, and she flew out as well, going much higher than her husband.

Her husband yelled, "Come back to me!" and she replied, "No, you come to me!" They hovered in the sky, unable to reach one another.

Whether they’re still in the air, I can’t say, but the young giant continued onward with his iron bar.

title: Thumbling
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once there was a poor peasant who sat by the fire in the evening.

His wife was busy spinning nearby. He sighed and said, "It's so sad that we have no children. Everything is so quiet here, while other homes are filled with noise and laughter."

"I know," replied his wife, also sighing. "Even if we had just one child, even if it were tiny, just as small as a thumb, I would be so happy, and we would love it with all our hearts."

Then something miraculous happened: the woman fell ill, and after seven months, she gave birth to a child who was perfect in every way but only as long as a thumb. They said, "It's just as we wished, and he will be our dear child." They named him Thumbling.

Even though they provided him with plenty of food, Thumbling didn't grow taller and remained the same size. However, he had keen little eyes and quickly proved to be clever and quick-witted—everything he did seemed to turn out well.

One day, the peasant was getting ready to head into the forest to cut wood and mumbled to himself, "I really wish someone would bring the cart to me." "Oh, Father!" cried Thumbling, "I can bring it to you! Just trust me! I’ll have it at the forest in no time!"

The man smiled and replied, "How will you do that? You're way too small to lead the horse by the reins." "That's no problem, Dad!" Thumbling said. "Just have Mom harness the horse, and I'll sit in his ear and tell him how to go."

"Alright then," answered the father. When the time came, the mother harnessed the horse and placed Thumbling in its ear. He shouted, "Giddy-up!" and the horse went along just like it had a real driver, heading straight to the forest.

As Thumbling was calling out directions from the horse's ear, they turned a corner and encountered two strangers. One of them exclaimed, "What on earth is going on? A cart is coming and someone is calling to the horse, but no driver is in sight!" "This doesn’t seem right," said the other. "Let’s follow the cart and see where it goes."

title: Thumbling
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The cart went straight into the forest and stopped right where the wood was cut. When Thumbling saw his father, he shouted, "See, Father! Here I am with the cart! Now take me down!" The father held on to the horse with one hand and lifted his son out of the horse's ear with the other.

Thumbling landed cheerfully in the straw, but the two men watching were completely stunned. One of them pulled the other aside and said, "Listen, that little guy could make us a fortune if we show him off in a big city! Let’s buy him!"

They approached the peasant and said, "Please, sell us the little man. He will be well taken care of!" "No way!" replied the father. "He’s the apple of my eye, and not all the money in the world could buy him!"

However, Thumbling had secretly climbed up the folds of his father’s coat and settled on his shoulder, whispering in his ear, "Dad, do sell me! I’ll be back before you know it." So, the father agreed to let him go with the two men for a good sum of money.

"Where would you like to sit?" they asked him. "Oh, just put me on the brim of your hat! I can walk back and forth and look at the world while not worrying about falling off!" They did as he said, and after saying goodbye to his father, they set off.

As night fell, Thumbling said, "Please take me down! I really need to get down now." "Just stay up there!" the man with the hat responded, not wanting to bother. "It doesn't matter to me; sometimes the birds drop things on me anyway!"

"No!" Thumbling insisted. "I have manners! Please put me down quickly!" Eventually, the man took off his hat and placed Thumbling on the ground by the roadside. Thumbling hopped around a bit on the grass, then darted into a mouse hole.

"Goodbye, gentlemen! Just go home without me!" he called, teasing them. They ran over and poked their sticks into the mouse hole, but it was useless. Thumbling crawled further in, and as darkness fell, the men had to head home in frustration.

title: Thumbling
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When Thumbling saw they were gone, he cautiously came out of his hiding spot. "It’s so dangerous wandering around outside in the dark, " he thought. "It’s so easy to break a neck or a leg!"

Luckily, he stumbled upon an empty snail shell. "Thank goodness!" he said, "I can spend the night in here safely." He crawled inside and soon dozed off.

Not long later, as he was about to fall asleep, he heard two men go by, one saying, "How can we get our hands on the rich pastor’s silver and gold?" Thumbling chimed in, "I can help you with that!"

"What was that?" one of the thieves asked, startled. "I heard someone talking!" They paused to listen, and Thumbling called out, "Take me with you, and I'll help!"

"But where are you?" one thief asked. "Look down at the ground and see where my voice is coming from!" Thumbling replied. Eventually, they found him and picked him up. "You little pest, how do you plan to help us?" they asked.

"Listen, I can sneak into the pastor's room through the bars and hand you whatever you want to take!" he said. "Alright then," they agreed, "let's see what you can do." When they reached the pastor's house, Thumbling sneaked into the room but immediately yelled as loud as he could, "Do you want everything that's here?"

The thieves jumped back in terror, and one of them hissed, "Shush, don’t wake anyone up!" But Thumbling pretended not to hear and shouted again, "Do you want everything that's here?" The cook in the next room heard him and sat up to listen.

The thieves, though frightened, gathered their courage and thought, "This little rascal is mocking us!" They returned and whispered to him, "Come on, be serious and give us something!"

But Thumbling yelled as loud as he could, "I will give you everything! Just reach in and take it!" The maid, listening carefully, heard this clearly and jumped out of bed, rushing to the door. The thieves fled as if demons were chasing them, but since the maid couldn’t see anyone, she went to light a candle.

title: Thumbling
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When she reached the spot with the candle, Thumbling, unseen, scurried off to the granary. The maid looked everywhere, but finding nothing, went back to bed, thinking she must have just been dreaming.

Thumbling had found a cozy spot in the hay and intended to sleep there until dawn, then head back to his parents. But other adventures were in store for him.

When morning came, the maid woke to feed the cows, first heading into the barn where she grabbed a bundle of hay—right from the spot where Thumbling lay sleeping. He was in such a deep sleep he didn’t notice anything until he found himself in the cow’s mouth!

"Oh my goodness! How did I end up in this situation?" he exclaimed. But he soon realized where he was. Then he had to be careful not to get stuck between the cow's teeth but eventually slid into her stomach with the hay.

"I’ve been forgotten in this tight space!" he thought. "No sunlight gets in, and there won’t be a candle!" His situation felt increasingly dire as more and more hay kept pouring in through the door.

In his anguish, he shouted as loud as he could, "Quit bringing me more food! Stop!" The maid, milking the cow, heard someone yelling, but saw no one and realized it was the same voice she heard the night before.

Terrified, she lost her balance and spilled the milk. She rushed to her master and cried, "Oh my heavens! The cow spoke!" "You’re losing it!" replied the pastor, but he went to check for himself.

Barely inside, Thumbling yelled again, "Stop bringing me more food!" The pastor, alarmed, thought some evil spirit was in the cow and ordered her to be slaughtered.

The cow was killed, and as her body was disposed of, Thumbling had a tough time making his way out. He finally found space to wiggle free, but just as he was about to escape, a hungry wolf came by and swallowed the whole stomach in one bite.

But Thumbling didn’t panic. "Maybe the wolf will listen to me," he thought. So, he called out from inside the wolf's belly, "Hey, wolf! I can tell you where to find a feast!"

title: Thumbling
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Where?" asked the wolf. "At this house over there. You just need to sneak in through the kitchen sink and you’ll find cakes, bacon, and sausages—enough to fill you up!" He described his father's house perfectly.

The wolf took this direction seriously, sneaking in through the sink at night and gorging himself in the pantry. After he had eaten enough, he wanted to leave, but he had grown too big to go back the same way.

Thumbling had planned for this, so he started making a racket inside the wolf's belly, kicking and shouting as loud as he could. "Will you be quiet?" roared the wolf. "You’re going to wake everyone up!"

"What do I care?" replied Thumbling. "You’ve stuffed yourself, so why shouldn’t I have some fun too?" And he began yelling even louder.

At long last, his parents were awakened and rushed to the room, peering through the door. When they realized a wolf was inside, they dashed away in fear. The father grabbed his axe, and the mother took the scythe.

"Stay back!" the father instructed. "If I don’t kill him with the first hit, you must help finish him off!" Hearing their voices, Thumbling cried out, "Dad! I’m here! I’m inside the wolf!"

The father rejoiced, shouting, "Thank goodness! Our dear child has found us again!" He told the mother to put down her scythe so Thumbling wouldn't get hurt. Then he swung his axe and struck the wolf hard enough to bring him down.

After the wolf was dead, they fetched knives and scissors and cut him open, pulling Thumbling out.

“Oh my,” the father said, “What sorrow we've endured for your sake!” "Yeah, Dad," replied Thumbling. "I've had my share of adventures. Thank goodness I'm finally breathing fresh air again!"

"Where have you been?" the father asked. "Well, Dad, I’ve been in a mouse hole, in a cow's belly, and then in a wolf! Now I just want to stay here with you!"

"And we will never sell you again, not even for all the riches in the world!" his parents replied, embracing and kissing their precious Thumbling. They provided him with food and drink and even had new clothes made for him, as his old ones had become ruined.

title: Thumbling as Journeyman
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once there was a tailor who had a son, and this boy was quite small.

He was no bigger than a thumb, and because of this, everyone called him Thumbling.

But Thumbling had a brave spirit inside him, and he announced to his father, "Dad, I have to go out into the world!"

The old man replied, "That’s great, my son!" He then took a long darning needle and fashioned a knob of sealing wax at the end, saying, "Here’s a sword for your journey."

Before he set off, Thumbling wanted to share one last meal with his family.

He hopped into the kitchen to see what his mother had prepared one final time.

When he arrived, the food was already served and sitting on the hearth.

He asked, "Mom, what do we have to eat today?"

"Look for yourself," replied his mother.

So, Thumbling jumped onto the hearth to peek into the dish, but when he leaned in too far, the steam from the food caught him and whisked him up the chimney.

For a while, he floated in the air on the steam until he finally came back down to the ground.

Now the little tailor was out in the wide world, and he traveled around, seeking a master in his craft, but the food he found wasn’t good enough for him.

"Mistress, if you can’t provide better food for us," Thumbling said, "I will leave by tomorrow morning – too many potatoes and not enough meat!"

"Goodbye, Mr. Potato-king! What do you want for dinner, you grasshopper?" the mistress snapped, irritated.

She grabbed a dishcloth and was about to hit him, but Thumbling quickly skated under a thimble, peeked out from underneath it, and stuck out his tongue at her.

The mistress picked up the thimble, trying to grab him, but Thumbling nimbly hopped into the cloth.

While she was pulling it out and searching for him, he slipped into a crack in the table.

"Hey there, lady!" he teased, popping his head out, and when she tried to swat him, he jumped down into the drawer.

title: Thumbling as Journeyman
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Eventually, she caught him and kicked him out of the house.

Thumbling wandered on and stumbled into a vast forest, where he encountered a gang of robbers plotting to steal the king’s treasure.

When they spotted the little tailor, they thought, "A small guy like him could slip through a keyhole and help us out!"

"Hey, you giant, will you come to the treasure chamber with us? You can slip in and throw out the cash!" one of them shouted.

After thinking it over, Thumbling said, "Sure!" and went with them to the treasure chamber.

He examined the doors to find a crack he could squeeze through.

Before long, he found an opening wide enough for him to slip in.

Just as he was about to go through, one of the guards noticed him and said to the other, "What an ugly spider is creeping there! I’ll kill it!"

"Leave it alone, it hasn't harmed you," replied the other guard.

This gave Thumbling the chance to scurry through the crack into the treasure chamber.

He opened the window just below where the robbers were waiting and began tossing out one coin after another.

When Thumbling was in full swing tossing out the coins, he heard the king approaching to check on the treasure.

He quickly found a hiding spot.

The king noticed some solid coins were missing but couldn't figure out how they had vanished, as all the locks and bolts seemed secure.

He left, telling the guards, "Keep an eye out; someone is stealing our money!"

As soon as Thumbling picked up the coins again, the guards heard the sound of clanking money.

They rushed in to catch the thief, but Thumbling was quicker and jumped into a corner, hiding under a coin so he couldn’t be seen.

While the guards called out, "Here I am!" he rather mocked with, "Found me!"

They dashed toward him, but by the time they arrived, he had sprung over to another corner under a different coin.

He teased, saying, "Here I am!"

He kept making fools of them, leading them around the chamber until they were too tired to continue.

title: Thumbling as Journeyman
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
By now, he had tossed out all the coins, and with all his might, he launched the last one out and hopped on it to fly down through the window.

The robbers praised him, exclaiming, "You’re a real hero! Want to be our captain?"

Thumbling declined the offer, saying he wanted to explore the world first.

They divided the treasure, but Thumbling only asked for a single kreuzer since he couldn't carry more.

Once more, he strapped on his sword, said farewell to the robbers, and continued on his journey.

At first, he worked with some masters, but he didn’t enjoy it, so eventually, he took a job as a servant at an inn.

However, the maids couldn’t stand him because he could see everything they did without them noticing, and he would tell their employers about the extra food they’d taken from the plates.

They decided to get back at him and planned a trick.

One day, when one of the maids was mowing the garden and saw Thumbling hopping around among the plants, she quickly mowed him up with the grass, bundled him in a cloth, and secretly tossed him to the cows.

One of the cows, a large black one, swallowed him without hurting him.

Inside the cow, it was dark; there wasn’t a candle lit.

When the milking began, the noise prevented anyone from hearing him.

Later, the master came to the barn and said, "That cow will be killed tomorrow."

Hearing this, Thumbling shouted, "Let me out first! I’m stuck inside her!"

The master heard him, but he didn't know where the voice was coming from.

"Where are you?" he asked.

"In the black cow," replied Thumbling, but the master didn’t understand and walked away.

The next morning, the cow was slaughtered.

Fortunately, Thumbling didn’t get hurt during the butchering; he ended up among the sausage meat.

When the butcher started his work, Thumbling yelled with all his might, "Don’t chop too deep! I’m in here!"

title: Thumbling as Journeyman
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
But nobody heard because of the noise of the chopping.

Poor Thumbling was in a bind, but trouble sharpened his wits.

He darted out so skillfully between the blows that none of them hit him, and he escaped intact.

However, he still couldn’t get away; he had no choice but to let himself be shoved into a black pudding with bits of bacon.

His space in there was cramped, and to make things worse, he was hung up in the chimney to be smoked.

Time dragged on painfully.

Finally, in winter, he was brought down again because the black pudding had to be served to a guest.

As the hostess began slicing it, he made sure not to stretch his head out too far, fearing to be cut.

Eventually, he saw his chance, cleared a way for himself, and jumped out.

Thumbling, however, wasn’t about to stay in a place that treated him so poorly, so he set off again.

But his freedom didn’t last long.

In the open country, a fox caught him in its jaws.

"Hey there, little tailor, I’m the one stuck in your throat! Let me go!"

"You’re right, you’re not worth much to me," the fox replied, "but promise me the chickens in your father’s yard, and I’ll let you go.

"With all my heart!" Thumbling replied. "You can have all the roosters and hens, I promise you that!"

The fox then released him and carried him home.

When his father saw his dear son again, he gladly gave the fox all the chickens he had.

"For this, I also bring you a nice bit of money," Thumbling said, handing his father the kreuzer he’d earned on his adventures.

"But why did the fox get the poor chickens?"

"Oh, you silly! Your father surely loves you far more than any chicken."

title: Wise Folks
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
One day, a peasant grabbed his trusty hazel stick from the corner.

He turned to his wife, Trina, and said, "I'm going to be gone for three days."

"If the cattle dealer comes while I'm away and wants to buy our three cows,"

"feel free to strike a deal, but only if you can get at least two hundred talers for them—nothing less, got it?"

"For goodness' sake, go peacefully," his wife replied, "I'll handle it."

"You sure?" asked the man. "Remember when you fell on your head as a child? That still affects you."

"But let me be clear: if you do something foolish, your back will feel my stick, and it won't be just paint either! You can count on that; it'll last a whole year."

The next morning, the cattle dealer arrived, and the woman didn't need to say much.

Once he saw the cows and heard the price, he said, "I'm willing to pay that. Honestly, they’re worth it. I'll take them right now."

He unchained the cows and led them out of the barn, but just as he was about to leave the yard, the woman grabbed his sleeve.

"You need to pay me the two hundred talers first, or I can't let the cows go," she said.

"True," the dealer replied, "but I forgot to put on my money belt. Don't worry, though, I can leave you something as security—I'll take two cows and leave one here. That’s a decent pledge."

Seeing the logic in that, the woman let the dealer take the cows, thinking, "Hans will be so proud of how I've handled this."

Hans returned on the third day, just as he promised, and immediately asked if the cows had sold.

"Yes, indeed, dear Hans," Trina replied, "and just like you said, for two hundred talers. They’re probably not worth that much, but the man took them without any complaints."

title: Wise Folks
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Where's the money?" Hans asked.

"Oh, I didn’t get the money yet," she responded, "He forgot his money belt but left good security behind."

"What kind of security?" he queried.

"One of the three cows he won’t get until he pays for the other two. I was really clever; I kept the smallest one, which eats the least."

Hans got angry and raised his stick, just about to beat her as he had warned, but suddenly he stopped.

"You are the biggest fool that ever lived, but I feel sorry for you. I'm going to wander the roads for three days to see if I come across someone even more foolish than you."

"If I find someone, you’ll get off scot-free, but if not, you'll get what you deserve."

He went to the main roads, sat on a stone, and waited to see what would happen.

Soon enough, he saw a peasant’s wagon coming, with a woman standing in the middle of it instead of sitting on the straw or guiding the oxen.

Thinking he’d found his next fool, he jumped up and ran back and forth in front of the wagon like he was losing his mind.

"What do you need?" the woman asked him.

"I don’t know you—where are you from?"

"I fell from heaven," he replied, "and I don’t know how to get back. Could you help me?"

"No," she said, "I don’t know the way. But if you came from heaven, surely you’ve seen my husband who’s been up there for three years. How is he?"

"Oh, yes, I've seen him," the man said, "but things aren’t going too well. He keeps the sheep, and they give him a lot of trouble. They wander off and get lost, and he has to chase after them constantly. His clothes are in tatters too, and will fall off any day now."

title: Wise Folks
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"There’s no tailor up there because Saint Peter doesn’t allow them in, as you know from the stories."

"Who would have thought it?" cried the woman. "You know what? I’ll fetch his Sunday coat still hanging in the cupboard at home. He can wear that to look respectable."

"That won’t work," the peasant said. "People can’t take clothes into heaven; they’re confiscated at the gate."

"Then listen," she said, "I sold my fine wheat yesterday and made a good amount of money. I’ll send that with you. If you hide the purse in your pocket, no one will know you have it."

"If you can't manage it some other way," said the peasant, "fine, I’ll do that."

"Just sit still, and I’ll drive home to get the purse. I’ll be back soon. I won't sit on the straw bundle, I stand in the wagon since it makes it easier to see," she added.

She drove her oxen away, and the peasant thought, "That woman has a true talent for stupidity. If she actually brings the money, my wife will be lucky, since she won’t get a beating."

Before long, she returned in a flurry with the money, placing it directly in his pocket.

Before leaving, she thanked him over and over.

When she got back home, she found her son who had come in from the field.

She excitedly told him about the unexpected encounter and added, "I’m thrilled to be able to send something to my poor husband. Who would have thought he would need anything up in heaven?"

The son was astonished. "Mom," he said, "it’s not every day that someone falls from heaven like that! I'll go find him; he must tell me what it’s like up there and how things are managed."

He saddled his horse and rode off swiftly. He found the peasant sitting under a willow tree, just about to count the money.

title: Wise Folks
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"Have you seen the man who fell from heaven?" the young man shouted.

"Yes," the peasant replied, "he headed back that way—it’ll be much quicker for him. If you hurry, you can catch up to him."

"Oh no," said the young man, "I’ve been working hard all day, and this ride has exhausted me. You know him, could you please get on my horse and go persuade him to come back?"

"Well, well," thought the peasant, "here’s another one without much sense. Why not?" He climbed onto the horse and trotted away.

The young man waited there until night fell, but the peasant never returned.

He must have been in a hurry not to look back, thought the young man. The peasant probably gave him the horse to take to my dad.

He returned home and told his mother what had happened, claiming he had sent the horse to his father so he wouldn’t have to keep running around.

"You made a wise choice," she said. "Your legs are younger than his."

When the peasant got home, he put the horse in the stable next to the cow he had kept as a pledge.

He went to Trina and said, "Well, you’re lucky; I found two people even sillier than you, so this time you’re off the hook for a beating!"

"I’ll save that for another time." Then he lit his pipe, settled into his grandfather's chair, and said,

"What a smart move it was to trade two skinny cows for a shiny horse and a bag of money. If looking foolish always paid off like this, I would proudly wear the title!"

title: A CHEERFUL TEMPER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: I inherited something wonderful from my father: a "good temper." "And who was my father?" you might ask. That’s not really important to the good temper part, but I’ll tell you he was lively, good-looking, round, and a bit chubby; he was a complete contradiction to what his job would suggest. "And what was his job and status in polite society?" you’re curious. Well, if that information were printed at the beginning of a story, some readers might put the book down and say, "This title is awful; I don’t like stories like this." But let me clarify: my father was neither a skin-dresser nor an executioner, quite the opposite. He held a respected position that placed him right at the front with the town's elite; in fact, he had to walk ahead of the bishop and even the princes. He was a hearse driver! There you have it. When people saw my dad, seated up front in the funeral carriage wearing his long black cloak and his three-cornered hat, they'd glance at his round, cheerful face and forget all about sorrow or death. That face seemed to say, "It’s nothing; things will turn out better than you think." So I inherited not just his good temper but also his habit of visiting the churchyard often, which can be lovely when you bring the right attitude. Plus, I read the Intelligencer just like he did.

title: A CHEERFUL TEMPER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: I'm not exactly young anymore; I don’t have a wife or kids, nor a library, but I read the Intelligencer, and that's enough for me. It’s a delightful newspaper, just like it was for my dad. It's super useful since it lists church preachers and newly released books, as well as where to get houses, help, clothes, and groceries. Not to mention all the charity subscriptions and sweet little poems! There are people looking for jobs or to connect, and they lay it all out so plainly. Really, a guy who subscribes to the Intelligencer can live a happy life and die content, ending up with a stack of papers that could serve as a soft mattress—unless, of course, he prefers wood shavings for a bed. The newspaper and the churchyard always fascinated me. My walks to the latter were like refreshing dips for my cheerful spirit. Sure, anyone can read the newspaper, but come on an adventure with me to the churchyard when the sun is shining and the trees are lush, and let’s stroll among the graves. Each one is like a closed book turned with the spine up, letting us read just the titles but not much else. My dad shared so much knowledge, and I’ve observed a plenty myself. I keep all that in my diary, where I jot down a history of the people resting here and a few more besides.

title: A CHEERFUL TEMPER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Now we’re in the churchyard. Behind the white iron fence, there used to be a rose bush; it's gone now, but a bit of evergreen from a nearby grave reaches out, making some appearance. Here lies a very unhappy man who, while he was alive, had a decent lifestyle. He had enough to get by and even a little extra. But his refined tastes meant that the smallest thing could irritate him. If he went to a play, instead of enjoying it, he’d get annoyed if the lighting on the moon was too bright on one side or if the painted sky was in the wrong place. Or if a palm tree showed up where it shouldn’t have, like in a scene meant to be the Berlin Zoo, or if a cactus popped up in a Tyrolean view, or if a beech tree appeared in northern Norway. As if any of that mattered! Why didn’t he just let those things go? Who would let such trivial issues bother them, especially at a comedy where everyone is supposed to have fun? Sometimes he would even complain if the audience clapped too loud or too soft. "They're like damp wood," he’d say, looking around to judge the crowd, "Nothing fires them up tonight." He would get all worked up because they didn’t laugh at the right moments or because they laughed in the wrong spots. And he worried himself to death over it until, eventually, he literally worked himself into the grave.

Here rests a happy man, meaning a man of privilege and status, which was quite lucky for him because without it, he probably wouldn’t have been noticed at all. It’s fascinating how nature balances these things. He strolled about in a coat that was entirely covered in embroidery and in the society drawing rooms, he resembled one of those fancy pearl-embroidered bell-pulls, made purely for decoration. And behind him stood a reliable friend, doing all his dirty work. Even today, these handy helpers still exist behind other fancy bell ropes. Everything is arranged so wisely that a man can be in a good mood.

title: A CHEERFUL TEMPER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Here lies—ah, it’s sad to think about him!—a man who, for sixty-seven years, was never once known to have said anything nice. He lived with the hope of coming up with a clever idea. Eventually, he became convinced that he had one and was so ecstatic about it that he actually died from sheer joy at finally having thought of a good idea. No one benefitted from it; in fact, nobody even found out what he thought was good. I can just imagine that this very idea could prevent him from resting peacefully. What if bringing that idea to light means he has to pop up at breakfast, and since he’s supposed to show up at midnight like a ghost, he wouldn’t be able to pull that off? Therefore, he’d have to take that brilliant idea back down into the grave with him—that would surely lead to an unsettled grave.

The woman who lies here was so incredibly stingy that during her life she would get up at night and meow just so her neighbors would think she had a cat. What a miser!

Here lies a young lady from a respected family, who always made sure her voice was heard in social circles. When she sang "Mi manca la voce," it was the only genuine thing she ever proclaimed in her life.

Here rests a maiden of another kind. She was engaged to be married, but that’s a story for another time; let’s leave her be.

Here lies a widow who outwardly spoke sweet words but had envy and malice in her heart. She would visit nearby families, digging up their faults, which she preyed on with every ounce of her spite. This grave belongs to a family so tightly knit in their beliefs that they wouldn’t accept any viewpoint on a subject outside their own. If newspapers or even the entire world proclaimed something was true, but a little schoolboy claimed he learned differently, they’d take his word as gospel just because he was part of the family. In fact, it’s well known that if the family rooster crowed at midnight, they would insist it was morning, even though the town watchman and all the clocks were ringing midnight.

The great poet Goethe finishes his Faust with the words, "may be continued;" so might our explorations in the churchyard. I come here frequently, and if any of my friends, or those who aren’t my friends, become too overwhelming for me, I step outside and choose a spot to imagine burying them. I picture them there—dead and powerless—until they re-emerge as better versions of themselves. I document their lives and actions in my diary, just like everyone should. This way, if our friends act foolishly, we don’t need to get upset about it. We can metaphorically bury the annoying ones and keep our good spirits. They can read the Intelligencer, a paper crafted by the people. When my life story is finally bound by the earth, I hope they’ll write on my tombstone—

[1] "I want a voice," or, "I have no voice."

title: A GREAT GRIEF
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
This story actually has two parts. We could skip the first part, but it gives us some details that are useful.

We were staying at a country estate where the owner was away for a few days. While we were there, a lady arrived from the nearby town with her pug dog. She mentioned that she came to sell some shares in her tan-yard. She had her paperwork with her, and we suggested she put it in an envelope and write the address of the estate's owner, "General War-Commissary Knight," on it.

She listened carefully, grabbed a pen, paused, and asked us to repeat the address slowly. We complied, and as she wrote, she hesitated. Sighing deeply, she exclaimed, "I'm just a woman!" Her pug dog had sat down on the floor next to her and was growling, having come along for some fun and fresh air. Certainly, a hard floor isn't suitable for a guest.

"He doesn't bite," the lady assured us. "He has no teeth. He’s part of the family, loyal but grumpy. That’s my grandchildren's fault; they tease him. They play pretend weddings and want him to be the bridesmaid, which is just too much for him, poor thing."

Afterward, she handed over her papers and picked up Puggie. That was the first part of the story that we could have left out.

About a week later, we arrived in town and checked into an inn. Our windows overlooked the tan-yard, which was divided by wooden planks. One side had many skins and hides, both raw and tanned. It was all the equipment needed for a tannery, which belonged to the widow. Sadly, Puggie had passed away that morning and was to be buried in that yard. The widow’s grandchildren—who, notably, were the tanner's grandchildren since Puggie had never married—were filling in the grave, which looked beautiful. It must have been quite a pleasing spot to lie.

The grave was surrounded by pieces of flower pots and covered with sand. At the top, they had stuck a half-buried beer bottle, neck up, which wasn’t very symbolic.

The children danced around the grave, and the oldest boy among them, a practical seven-year-old, suggested they host a viewing of Puggie's grave for everyone in the lane. The price of admission would be a trouser button since every boy would surely have one, and they could give another for a little girl. Everyone cheered, agreeing with the idea.

All the kids from the lane—even those from the small lane behind—rushed to see the grave, each one handing in a button. Many were seen that afternoon walking around with only one suspender on, but they had just seen Puggie's grave, which was worth much more.

However, outside the tan-yard entrance stood a little girl dressed in rags. She was very pretty, with curly hair and clear blue eyes that made you want to smile. She didn’t say a word or cry, but each time the little door opened, she gazed longingly into the yard. She knew she didn't have a button, so she sadly waited outside until everyone else had gone in to see the grave. Once they were gone, she sat down and covered her eyes with her small brown hands, bursting into tears; she was the only one who hadn’t seen Puggie's grave. Her grief felt as heavy as any adult’s.

We observed all of this from above. It’s fascinating how, when viewed from above, many of our own troubles and those of others can actually make us smile! That’s the story, and if anyone doesn’t get it, they can always go buy a share in the tan-yard.

title: A LEAF FROM HEAVEN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
High up in the crisp, blue sky, an angel flew with a flower he had picked from heaven's garden. As he gently kissed the flower, a tiny leaf fell from it and drifted down into the soft earth of a forest. The leaf took root, sprouted, and began to grow among the other plants.

"What a silly little shoot!" remarked one plant. "No one will even recognize it; not the thistles or the stinging nettles."

"It must be some kind of garden plant," said another, and they laughed and looked down on it as if it were something unworthy.

"Where are you going?" asked the tall thistles, their leaves lined with sharp thorns. "It's ridiculous to grow like that; we’re not here to help you."

Winter arrived, covering the plant with snow, but the snow sparkled on it as if there was sunshine beneath it as well.

When spring came, the plant blossomed into full glory, becoming the most beautiful plant in the forest. Then a botany professor appeared, one who could translate his knowledge into books. He examined and tested the plant, but it didn’t fit into his classification of plants, and he couldn’t identify what type it was. "It must be some kind of lesser species," he concluded; "I don’t recognize it, and it isn’t listed in any classification."

"Not recognized in any classification!" repeated the thistles and nettles.

The big trees surrounding it saw and heard the comments, but they didn’t say a thing—sometimes it’s better to stay silent when you don’t know.

A poor innocent girl wandered through the forest; her heart was pure, and her understanding was deepened by her faith. Her greatest treasure was an old Bible, which she read and cherished. From its pages, she heard God speaking to her, reminding her of what happened to Joseph’s brothers when they tried to harm him: "They thought of evil in their hearts, but God turned it into good." When we suffer unfairly, when we’re misunderstood or scorned, we should think of Him who was pure and holy, who prayed for those who crucified Him: "Father, forgive them; they don’t know what they’re doing."

title: A LEAF FROM HEAVEN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The girl paused before the marvelous plant, captivated by the sweet, refreshing fragrance of its green leaves. The flowers shimmered and sparkled in the sunlight like bright flames, and the air around them was alive with sweet sounds, as if each held an endless melody. With sincere gratitude, she admired this beautiful creation of God, leaning down to study the flower and smell its delightful scent. Suddenly, an idea illuminated her mind, and her heart soared. She wished she could pick a flower, but she hesitated to break it off, knowing it would quickly fade. Instead, she took just one green leaf, brought it home, and tucked it into her Bible, where it remained fresh and vibrant. Weeks later, that same Bible lay beneath her head in her coffin. A serene peace was on her face, reflecting the truth that she was now in the presence of God.

In the forest, the extraordinary plant continued to bloom, eventually growing into a nearly tree-sized specimen, and all the migratory birds bowed before it.

"That plant is definitely a foreigner," sneered the thistles and burdocks. "We could never act like that here." Even the black forest snails spat at its flowers.

Then the swineherd arrived, gathering thistles and bushes to burn them for their ashes. He uprooted the fantastic plant, roots and all, and added it to his bundle. "This will be just as useful as the rest," he said, taking the plant away.

Before long, the king of the land fell into deep melancholy. He worked tirelessly, but it did little good. They read him serious and learned texts, then switched to light and silly stories, but nothing helped. So they sought advice from one of the wise men, who sent a message back saying there was a remedy that could heal him: a plant of heavenly origin, growing in the king’s own woods. The messenger described the flower so well that it could not be mistaken.

The swineherd responded, "I think I took that plant from the forest in my bundle, and it’s been burned to ashes a long time ago. But I didn’t know any better."

"You didn't know any better? What ignorance upon ignorance!"

The swineherd took those words to heart, recognizing they applied to him; he realized he wasn't the only one who was ignorant. Not even a leaf from the plant could be found. There was one, but it lay with the dead girl; no one knew anything about it.

The king, feeling sad, wandered to the spot in the woods. "This is where the plant grew," he said; "this place is sacred." So he commanded that the area be enclosed with golden rails, and a guard was stationed nearby.

The professor of botany wrote an elaborate study about the heavenly plant, and for this, he was rewarded with gold, improving his life and that of his family.

And this part of the story is perhaps the most amusing. The plant was gone, and the king remained as sad as ever, but the guard said he had always been that way.

title: A ROSE FROM HOMER'S GRAVE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
All the songs of the East tell the story of the nightingale's love for the rose in the quiet starlit night. The songbird serenades the fragrant flowers.

Not far from Smyrna, where merchants guide their heavily loaded camels with pride as they travel beneath the tall pines over sacred ground, I spotted a hedge of roses. A turtle dove flitted among the branches of the towering trees, and as sunlight touched her wings, they sparkled like mother-of-pearl. On the rosebush grew a flower more stunning than all the rest, and the nightingale poured out his heart to her; yet the rose stayed silent, and not a single dewdrop fell like a tear of sympathy on her petals. Eventually, she bowed her head over a pile of stones and murmured, "Here lies the greatest singer in the world; I'll spread my fragrance over his tomb, and I'll let my leaves fall on it when the storm scatters them. He who sang of Troy has become earth, and from that earth, I have emerged. I, a rose from Homer's grave, am too grand to bloom for a nightingale." Then the nightingale sang himself to death. A camel-driver passed by, accompanied by his loaded camels and his workers; his little son discovered the lifeless bird and buried the beautiful songster in the great Homer's grave while the rose shuddered in the wind.

Evening arrived, and the rose wrapped her leaves around herself more tightly, dreaming; and this was her dream.

It was a beautiful sunny day; a crowd of travelers approached who had come to pay their respects at Homer's grave. Among them was a minstrel from the north, the land of clouds and the dazzling lights of the aurora borealis. He plucked the rose and tucked it into a book, carrying it far away to his homeland. The rose withered with sadness, lying between the pages of the book, which he opened back in his own home, saying, "Here is a rose from the grave of Homer."

Then the flower awakened from her dream, trembling in the wind. A drop of dew fell from her leaves onto the singer's grave. As the sun rose, the flower bloomed even more beautifully than before. The day was warm, and she still thrived in her familiar Asia. Soon, footsteps drew near; strangers like those from her dream approached, and among them was a poet from the north. He plucked the rose, pressed a kiss upon her fresh petals, and took her away to the land of clouds and northern lights. Like a memento, the flower now rests in his "Iliad," and just like in her dream, she hears him say as he opens the book, "Here is a rose from the grave of Homer."

title: A STORY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In the garden, all the apple trees were in bloom. They rushed to produce flowers before the leaves even appeared, while in the yard, the ducklings waddled back and forth, and the cat lounged in the sun, licking the warmth from its paws. Looking out at the fields, the corn stood tall and vibrant, shining bright with green, unlike anything else! Little birds chirped and flitted about, as if the day were a grand celebration; and indeed it was, for it was Sunday. Church bells chimed, and people strolled to church, looking cheerful and dressed in their finest clothes. Everything radiated happiness. The day was warm and beautiful, and it would have been easy to say, "God's kindness towards us is limitless." But inside the church, the pastor stood at the pulpit, speaking loudly with anger. He proclaimed that all men were wicked and that God would punish them for their sins, asserting that the wicked, upon dying, would be cast into hell, burning forever. His excitement painted a grim picture; the evil within them would not vanish, nor would the flames ever cool, and they would find no rest. It was chilling to hear, especially as he described hell as a dismal pit where all the world's refuse gathered. There was no fresh air, only the suffocating stench of fire, and the wicked would sink deeper into darkness with silence enveloping them. It was horrifying to listen to, and his conviction terrified the congregation. Outside, the birds sang cheerfully, and the warm sun shone brightly, as if every little flower whispered, "God, Your kindness to us is boundless." Truly, it felt nothing like the preacher's grim sermon.

That evening, as the pastor prepared for bed, he noticed his wife sitting quietly, lost in thought.

"What’s bothering you?" he asked her.

"Well, the issue is," she replied, "I can’t piece together my thoughts. I struggle to understand your message from church today—that there are so many wicked people who will burn eternally. Eternal—how long is that? I’m just a woman, a sinner in the eyes of God, but I could never imagine letting even the worst sinner suffer forever. How could our Lord, so infinitely good, allow that, knowing where their wrongdoing comes from? I can’t fathom it, even if you insist it's true."

title: A STORY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
It was autumn, and the trees dropped their leaves. The earnest and solemn pastor sat by the bedside of a dying woman. A pious and faithful soul was taking her last breaths; she was the pastor's wife.

"If anyone is to find rest in the grave and receive mercy from our Lord, it will surely be you," said the pastor. He gently folded her hands and read a psalm over her lifeless body.

She was laid to rest; two large tears streamed down the pastor’s cheeks, and the parsonage felt empty and still, for its sun had set forever. She had gone home.

That night, a cold wind brushed across the pastor's face; he opened his eyes, thinking the moon was shining into his room. However, there stood a figure at his bedside, resembling the ghost of his late wife. She looked at him with a kind, sorrowful expression, as if wanting to say something. The pastor sat up in bed and reached out his arms to her, saying, "Not even you can find eternal rest! You suffer, you most gentle and devout woman?"

The dead woman nodded as if to say "Yes," placing her hand on her heart.

"Is there nothing I can do to bring you peace in the grave?"

"Give me just one hair—a single hair—from the head of a sinner who will be condemned to eternal flames in hell," she replied.

"It should be so easy to save you, pure and faithful woman," he said.

"Follow me," said the spirit of the dead woman. "You can soar with me wherever your thoughts desire. Invisible to others, we will peek into their innermost chambers; but you must swiftly identify the one destined for eternal despair, before the rooster crows!" In an instant, they found themselves in the bustling city, where the names of the deadly sins blazed in bright lights: pride, greed, gluttony, lust—in short, the whole rainbow of sin.

"Yes, just as I believed," said the pastor. "Those destined for the eternal fire reside there." They stood before a grand, illuminated entrance, with grand steps covered in carpets and flowers, festive music echoing from within. A footman in fancy silk and velvet stood by, holding a large silver-tipped staff near the entrance.

title: A STORY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Our ball could easily rival the king’s," he remarked, disdainfully glancing at the crowd peering in from the street. His expression and gestures conveyed what he thought: "You miserable beggars elicit nothing but pity."

"Pride," the dead woman said, "do you see him?"

"The footman?" the pastor responded. "He’s just a foolish pawn, not someone fated to be burned eternally!"

"Just a fool!" echoed through the entire house of pride, revealing that they were all fools within.

Next, they glided into the stark space of a miser. Pale like a skeleton, shivering from cold and hunger, the old man was clinging desperately to his wealth. They watched him spring up feverishly from his meager mattress to pull a loose stone from the wall, revealing gold coins hidden in an old stocking. He fumbled anxiously over an old, tattered coat that held more hidden treasures, his clammy fingers trembling.

"He’s ill! This is madness—joyless madness—plagued by fear and terrifying dreams!"

They quickly moved on to where criminals lay side by side, asleep in long rows. One stirred suddenly, like a wild beast, letting out a chilling scream before jabbing his neighbor in the ribs with a sharp elbow, causing the other to turn in his sleep:

"Shut up, monster—just sleep! This happens every night!"

title: A STORY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Every night!" echoed back the other. "Yes, every night he comes to torment me! In my fury, I've done terrible things. I was born with a wicked heart, and it has brought me back here for a second time; I accept my punishments for that. But, I haven’t yet confessed something. A little while ago, while passing my old master’s yard, dark thoughts emerged and when I made a spark against the wall, it came too close to the thatched roof. Everything burned down—flames surged, just as they sometimes engulf me. I helped rescue cattle and belongings, nothing living was harmed except for a flock of pigeons that flew into the blaze and my master's dog, whom I didn’t think of. I still hear his howls, haunting me when I try to sleep, and when I finally drift off, that big rough dog comes and lays on me, howling and pressing me down. Listen! You can snore; you’re snoring the whole night while I barely manage a few minutes!" Suddenly, the enraged criminal lunged at his comrade, striking him hard in the face.

"Wicked Matz has lost it again!" the other criminals murmured among themselves. They grabbed him, struggling and bending him double until his head rested between his knees, tying him up so tightly that blood nearly burst from his eyes and pores.

"You’re torturing that poor man!" the pastor exclaimed, and as he reached out to protect the one who had already endured too much, the scene shifted. They flew through lavish halls and dilapidated homes, witnessing every deadly sin. An angel of justice stood tall, reciting their crimes along with their feeble defenses; the latter wasn’t impressive, but was presented before God, who understands the heart and knows all, embodying mercy and love. The pastor's hand trembled; he couldn’t bring himself to snatch even a hair from the sinner's head. Tears poured down his face like a stream of mercy, washing away the eternal flames of hell.

"Father of all mercy, grant her the peace that I couldn't provide!"

"I have it now!" the dead woman replied. "Your harsh words, your despair in humanity, your grim view of God and His creation pushed me to you. Come to truly understand mankind! Even the wicked hold a part of God within them—and that can extinguish and triumph over the flames of hell!"

The pastor felt a kiss on his lips; a glow of light enveloped him—God’s radiant sun illuminated the room, and his wife, alive, sweet, and full of love, awakened him from the dream God had sent.

title: ANNE LISBETH
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Anne Lisbeth was a stunning young woman with a fair complexion, sparkling white teeth, and soft, clear eyes. She moved lightly on the dance floor, but her thoughts were even lighter. She had a little boy who wasn’t considered pretty at all, so he was sent to be cared for by a laborer's wife while she took a position at the count's castle. In the lavishly decorated rooms filled with silk and velvet, she was sheltered from the elements, and no one could speak harshly to her, as she was the nurse for the count's child. This child was as fair and delicate as a prince and looked like an angel; how much she loved him! Meanwhile, her own son was at the laborer’s house, where food was scarce and no one really cared for him. He often cried, but who notices an unloved child's cries? Eventually, he would tire himself out and fall asleep; after all, when we sleep, we don’t feel hunger or thirst. Ah yes, sleep is a marvelous invention.

As the years passed, Anne Lisbeth’s child grew up, though they said his growth had been stunted. He became a part of the family he lived with, who received money to care for him, allowing his mother to completely distance herself. She had transformed into quite the lady, and now owned a comfortable home in town. When she went out for walks, she wore a bonnet, but she never visited the laborer’s home; it was too far from town, and anyway, there was no longer a reason to go, as the boy belonged to his new family. He had food and even contributed to their household by tending to Mary’s red cow, as he was skilled at taking care of animals.

title: ANNE LISBETH
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: A magnificent dog lounged proudly on top of his kennel by the nobleman’s yard gate when the sun was shining, barking at anyone who passed by. But when it rained, he would scurry back into his house to stay warm and dry. Anne Lisbeth’s boy also sat in the sunshine, up on the fence, crafting a little toy. He was aware of three strawberry plants in bloom nearby, hopeful that they would bear fruit, even though that hope often turned out to be in vain. He often sat outside in the rain, getting soaked to the bone, only for the cold wind to dry his clothes later. If he ventured near the count's estate, he was shoved around because the workers called him ugly. Yet he was used to it; nobody loved him. This was Anne Lisbeth's boy's reality, and how else could it be? It seemed destined that he would not be loved by anyone. Until now, he had been like a land crab; the land had finally cast him away. He set out to sea on a miserable boat, sitting at the helm while the skipper sat beside a jug of grog. He looked so dirty and ugly, half-frozen and half-starved, that it seemed he never had enough to eat—which was actually true.

title: ANNE LISBETH
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Late in autumn, when the weather turned rough, windy, and wet, a miserable boat set out to sea with only two men on board—or rather, a man and a half, consisting of the skipper and his boy. It had been a gloomy twilight all day, and darkness quickly enveloped them. The cold bit through their thick coats, prompting the skipper to take a dram for warmth. The bottle was old, as was the glass; the upper part was intact, but the bottom was broken and had been secured to a little wooden block painted blue. "One dram is comforting, but two are even better," thought the skipper as the boy gripped the helm tightly with his calloused hands. He was ugly, with tangled hair, and looked frail and stunted; the church register listed him as Anne Lisbeth's son, but others simply called him the field-laborer's boy. The wind howled through the rigging, and the boat sliced through the rough sea. The sails billowed with the wind, propelling them forward wildly. Above and below, everything was tumultuous, and things might worsen. Suddenly, they felt a jolt. What had hit the boat? Was it a waterspout or a large wave crashing against them?

"Heaven help us!" the boy at the helm shouted as the boat tipped over, lying on its side. They had struck a rock rising from the depths of the sea, sinking immediately like an old shoe dropped in a puddle. "It sank instantly with mouse and man," as the saying goes. There might have been mice on board, but only one man and a half— the skipper and the field-laborer's boy—were present. No one witnessed it, apart from the seagulls flying above and the fish swimming below, and even they fled in fright as the boat filled with water and disappeared. It rested barely below the surface, with the two of them buried and forgotten. The only thing that didn’t sink was the glass, which floated while the wooden piece drifted away to be washed up on shore and broken; when or where didn’t truly matter. It had served its purpose and had been cherished, unlike Anne Lisbeth's boy, who was never loved at all. But in Heaven, no one could ever say, "Never loved."

title: ANNE LISBETH
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Anne Lisbeth had been living in town for many years now. People called her "Madame," and she took pride in that title. She recalled her past noble days, driving around in a carriage and mingling with countesses and baronesses. Her beautiful son had been a sweet angel with the kindest heart; he adored her just as much as she cherished him. They would kiss and embrace, and he had been her joy, her second life. Now at fourteen, he was tall, handsome, and clever. However, she hadn't seen him since she carried him in her arms, nor had she visited the count's palace in years—it was quite a trek from town.

"I have to make an effort to go," Anne Lisbeth said to herself. "I want to see my darling, the count's sweet child, and hug him close. Surely he must miss me too, the young count; he must think of me and love me just as he did when he would wrap his little angel arms around my neck, calling me 'Anne Liz.' I cherished every note of it—yes, I need to see him again." She took a cart across the countryside, got off, and continued her journey on foot until she reached the count's castle. It looked just as grand and magnificent as she remembered, and the garden remained unchanged. All the servants were strangers to her; not one recognized Anne Lisbeth or remembered her former significance there. Still, she was positive the countess would soon inform them, and her darling boy too—how she longed to see him!

Now that Anne Lisbeth had reached her destination, she waited a long time. Time seems to stretch when you’re waiting. But before the grand dinner, she was invited in and treated kindly. She was told that after dinner she would have the chance to see her sweet son again. He had grown tall and thin since she last saw him, but his beautiful eyes and angelic smile remained unchanged. He looked at her without speaking, clearly not recognizing her. He turned to leave, but she grabbed his hand and pressed it to her lips.

title: ANNE LISBETH
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Well, well," he said while walking out of the room. He was the one she thought about constantly, the one she loved most, her pride and joy!

As Anne Lisbeth exited the castle and stepped onto the main road, she felt sorrowful and despondent; the boy she had nursed through the night and held dear, the one she still dreamed about, had been distant and unfamiliar, offering no greetings or recognition. A big black raven swooped down in front of her, cawing ominously.

"Ah," she sighed, "what ill-fated bird are you?" Soon, she passed by the laborer's home; his wife stood at the door, and the two exchanged pleasantries.

"You look well!" the woman remarked. "You’re all plump and healthy; you seem to be doing fine."

"The boat sank with them," the woman continued. "Hans the skipper and the boy both drowned; that's the end of that. I always thought the boy would be helpful with a few coins. He won't cost you anything more, Anne Lisbeth."

"So they drowned," Anne Lisbeth repeated, but said nothing more, and the topic ended there. She felt quite downcast because her count-child had shown her no inclination to acknowledge her, though she loved him dearly and had traveled so far to see him. The journey had cost money too, and she had gotten no enjoyment from it. Still, she kept her thoughts to herself, not wanting the laborer's wife to think she regretted her previous status at the castle. The raven flew overhead once more, cawing loudly.

"That wretched bird!" Anne Lisbeth exclaimed. "You’re really going to frighten me today." She had brought coffee and chicory with her, thinking it would be nice to give the laborer's wife some to brew for herself, and then she'd have a cup too.

title: ANNE LISBETH
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The woman brewed the coffee, and while waiting, Anne Lisbeth settled into a chair and fell asleep. In her dreams, she experienced something she had never dreamt before; curiously, she envisioned her own child, who had wept and gone hungry in the laborer's home, now lying in the depths of the sea, in a place only God knew. It felt like she was still sitting in the hut, where the woman was preparing the coffee, catching a whiff of the coffee beans roasting. Suddenly, a stunning young figure appeared at the door, as beautiful as the count’s child, who said to her, "The world is fading away; hold on to me, for you are still my mother; you have an angel in heaven, hold me tight;" and the angel-child extended his hand and grasped her. Then there was a violent crash, like the sound of a world shattering, and the angel-child rose from the earth, pulling her by the sleeve so strongly that she felt herself lift off the ground; but simultaneously, something heavy clung to her feet, dragging her down, as if hundreds of women were holding onto her, crying, "If you are to be saved, we must be saved too. Hold on, hold on!" In that moment, they all clung to her, but there were too many; as they tightened their grip, her sleeve tore and Anne Lisbeth fell in terror, waking up from the dream. She found herself nearly tipping over in her chair, too startled and shaken to recall the dreadful dream she had just experienced.

They drank their coffee and chatted, and then Anne Lisbeth headed towards the little town where she was supposed to meet the carrier for her ride back home. However, when she arrived, she found that he wouldn’t be ready until the next evening. She then thought about the cost and the distance she’d have to walk. Remembering that the route along the seaside was two miles shorter than the main road, and with the clear weather and moonlight, she resolved to trek there on foot immediately to make it home by the next day.

title: ANNE LISBETH
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The sun had set, and the evening bells chimed from the village church tower, but for Anne Lisbeth, it sounded more like the cries of frogs in the marshes. Eventually, silence enveloped the area; every bird had settled down, even the owl stayed hidden; deep silence cloaked the woods by the seashore. As Anne Lisbeth walked on, she could hear her footsteps crunching on the sand; even the waves were quiet, as if the sea had fallen asleep. There was peace among the living and the dead in the depths of the ocean. Anne Lisbeth walked on, her mind wandering without really focusing on anything—everyone knows that thoughts never truly leave us; they merely slumber. Many thoughts that have been dormant awaken at the right time, stirring in our minds and hearts, as if they have come to us from above. It’s said that a good deed brings blessings in return, while the wages of sin is death. Much has been discussed and written about that we overlook or don’t grasp. A light seems to flicker inside us, and forgotten things resurface; that’s how it was with Anne Lisbeth. Seeds of every vice and virtue lie in our hearts, in yours and mine; they’re there, like small seeds, until a ray of sunlight, the touch of something evil, or a choice to turn right instead of left stirs them. The seed begins to sprout, filling us with its essence, directing our paths towards good or bad. Troubling thoughts often linger in the mind, fermenting away, unnoticed as our senses remain dulled; but they are present. Anne Lisbeth walked on, with her senses half-asleep, yet thoughts brewing within her.

From one Shrove Tuesday to another, so much can occur that weighs down the heart. It’s a reflection on an entire year; many things may be forgotten—sins against heaven in word and thought, offenses against our neighbors, and even against our own conscience. We are barely aware of their presence; Anne Lisbeth didn’t think about any of her wrongdoings. She hadn’t committed any crimes against the law; she considered herself an honorable person in a good position.

title: ANNE LISBETH
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: She continued along the seashore. What did she see lying there? An old hat—a man’s hat. When could it have washed overboard? She stepped closer, stopping to inspect the hat. "What is that?" she wondered, shuddering; it was nothing but a pile of grass and tangled seaweed sprawled across a long stone, resembling a corpse. Just some tangled grass, yet it frightened her. As she turned to leave, many childhood superstitions flooded her mind: tales of ghosts by the seashore; the spirits of drowned individuals who had yet to be buried, their bodies washed ashore on desolate beaches. She knew the body itself couldn’t harm anyone, but the spirit could pursue a lonely traveler and demand to be taken to a burial ground, seeking rest in consecrated soil. "Hold on!" the specter would cry. As Anne Lisbeth murmured these words to herself, her entire dream came rushing back to her—when the mother had clung to her and cried these words, during the tumultuous, world-ending chaos when her sleeve had been torn, and she fell from her child's grasp, wanting to lift her up during that terrible moment. Her child, whom she had never loved, was now buried beneath the sea and could rise like a specter from the waters, calling out, "Hold on; take me to consecrated ground!"

title: ANNE LISBETH
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
As these thoughts whirled in her mind, fear quickened her steps, making her walk faster and faster. A chilling dread settled over her heart, almost causing her to faint. As she looked out at the water, everything seemed to darken; a thick fog rolled in, twisting the bushes and trees into grotesque shapes. She turned to glance at the moon rising behind her. It appeared pale and ghostly, and a heavy sadness weighed down her limbs. "Stop!" she thought, then turned again to look back at the moon. A white face materialized right beside her, draped in mist as if it were a shroud. "Stop! Carry me to consecrated earth!" echoed in her ears in hollow tones. The sound wasn’t from frogs or ravens; she saw no sign of such creatures. "Dig a grave for me!" rang out clear and loud. Yes, it was indeed the spirit of her child—the one beneath the ocean, who could find no peace until taken to the churchyard, until someone dug a grave for him in sacred ground. She would go there right away and dig. She turned towards the church, and the heavy burden on her heart felt lighter, almost nonexistent; yet as she attempted to cut home via the shortest path, the weight returned. "Stop! Stop!" The words came through clearly, sounding like croaks from frogs or cries from birds. "Dig a grave for me!"

The fog was cold and damp; her hands and face felt clammy with dread. A heavy weight seized her again, and her mind cleared for thoughts that had never surfaced before.

title: ANNE LISBETH
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In these northern regions, a beech tree can bud overnight, emerging in the morning sun with youthful green. Likewise, in a single instant, the recognition of past sins—those in thoughts, words, and actions—can unfold before us. Once the conscience awakens, it blooms in the heart automatically; God rouses it when we least expect it. In that moment, we find no excuses for ourselves; the deed remains, a witness against us. Our thoughts begin to take shape, resonating far out into the world. We become horrified at the notion of what we’ve carried inside us and the truth that we haven’t conquered the evil rooted in our thoughtlessness and pride. The heart conceals both vice and virtue, and both flourish even in the shallowest soil. Now, Anne Lisbeth experienced in thought what we often express in words. She succumbed to them, sinking to the ground. "Dig a grave!" echoed in her ears, and she would’ve happily buried herself if it meant forgetting her actions.

It was the first hour of her awakening, filled with anguish and horror. Superstition alternately made her shiver with cold and burn with feverish heat. Memories she had feared even to articulate flooded her mind. Silently, as shadows drift across the moonlit landscape, a spectral figure rushed by her; something she had heard about before. Nearby, four snorting steeds galloped in fiery splendor, pulling a blazing carriage, within which sat the malevolent lord of the manor who had ruled a century ago. The legend said that every night at midnight, he would drive into his castle yard and then out again. He was not as pale as the dead but as dark as coal. Nodding, he pointed towards Anne Lisbeth, shouting, "Hold on! Keep holding on! And you may ride again in a nobleman's carriage and forget your child!"

title: ANNE LISBETH
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
She composed herself and hurried to the churchyard, but black crosses and ravens danced before her eyes, leaving her unable to differentiate between them. The ravens croaked as the one she’d seen in the daytime, but now she understood their words. "I am the raven-mother; I am the raven-mother," each ravens croaked in turn, and Anne Lisbeth felt that the name fit her too; she feared she would transform into a black bird, crying out as they did, if she didn’t dig a grave. She threw herself onto the ground, clawing at the hard earth until blood flowed from her fingers. "Dig a grave!" still echoed in her ears; she feared that the rooster might crow and the first rays of dawn might appear before she completed her task—and then it would be too late. The cock crowed, and daylight broke in the east, but the grave remained only half-dug. A cold hand swept over her head and face, sinking down to her heart. "Only half a grave!" a voice wailed, then vanished. Yes, it had fled away over the sea; it was the ocean specter, and overcome with exhaustion, Anne Lisbeth collapsed to the ground, losing consciousness.

When she came back to her senses, the sun was shining brightly, and two men were lifting her up. But she wasn’t in the churchyard; she was on the beach, where she’d dug a deep hole in the sand and cut her hand on a piece of broken glass whose sharp end was stuck in a small block of painted wood. Anne Lisbeth was feverish. Her conscience had awakened all the superstitions, overwhelming her mind so much that she believed she had only half a soul, and that her child had taken the other half into the sea. She realized she could never seek the mercy of heaven until she retrieved the other half now trapped in the deep waters.

Anne Lisbeth returned home, but she was no longer the woman she once was. Her thoughts felt like a jumbled, tangled mess; yet one thread remained clear—she had to carry the specter from the seashore to the churchyard and dig a grave for him there; doing so might restore her soul. Many nights she disappeared from home, always found waiting at the seashore for the specter.

A whole year passed this way; then one night she vanished again and was nowhere to be discovered. The entire next day was wasted in an unsuccessful search for her.

By evening, when the clerk entered the church to toll the evening bell, he saw Anne Lisbeth at the altar, having spent the whole day there. She appeared exhausted, but her eyes sparkled, and her cheeks had a rosy hue. The last rays of sunlight illuminated her and glinted off the altar, shining on the open Bible, which bore the words of the prophet Joel: "Rend your hearts and not your garments, and turn unto the Lord."

"That was merely a coincidence," people said; but do things happen by chance? In Anne Lisbeth’s face, lit up by the evening sun, one could see serenity and calmness. She claimed she was happy now, for she had triumphed. The seashore specter—her very own child—had visited her the night before and said, "You’ve only dug me half a grave: but for a year and a day, you have buried me entirely in your heart, and that is where a mother can best keep her child!" Then he returned her lost soul and led her into the church. "Now I am in the house of God," she remarked, "and it is in this house that we find happiness."

As the sun set, Anne Lisbeth’s soul ascended to that place where there is no pain, and all her troubles came to an end.

title: BEAUTY OF FORM AND BEAUTY OF MIND
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a sculptor named Alfred. After winning a prestigious gold medal and earning a travel scholarship, he set off for Italy before returning to his home country. He was young then—though he's ten years older now. Upon his return, he visited a small town on the island of Zealand.
