The entire town knew who he was, and one of the wealthiest men there threw a party in his honor, inviting everyone important or wealthy. It was quite the event, and word spread quickly, making an announcement unnecessary. Boys, children from poorer families, and even the needy gathered outside the house, peering in at the illuminated windows. The streets were buzzing with excitement, as if the watchman was hosting a gathering of his own. The atmosphere was festive because Alfred, the sculptor, was there. He shared stories and anecdotes that everyone listened to with a mix of enjoyment and admiration, but no one was more captivated than the elderly widow of a naval officer. She was like a fresh piece of blotting paper, soaking up every word he said and asking for more. She was both appreciative and shockingly naive—a bit like a female Gaspar Hauser.

"I would love to see Rome!" she exclaimed. "It must be a gorgeous city, or so many travelers wouldn't keep going there. Please, tell me what it looks like when you enter through the gate!"

"I'm not sure I can describe it well," the sculptor replied. "But when you enter, you find a vast open space with a thousand-year-old obelisk standing in the center."

"An organist?" the lady exclaimed, clearly unfamiliar with the word 'obelisk.' Some guests barely stifled their laughter, and the sculptor almost lost his composure. But then he noticed a pair of striking blue eyes beside the curious lady—her daughter. Anyone with such a daughter couldn’t be foolish. The mother was overflowing with questions, while her daughter, who rarely spoke, looked like the enchanting maid of a fountain. She was beautiful, a perfect subject for the sculptor's contemplation, but not for conversation because she seldom spoke.

title: BEAUTY OF FORM AND BEAUTY OF MIND
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Does the pope have a big family?" the lady asked.

The young man answered thoughtfully, as though her question had been different. "No, he doesn't come from a notable family."

"That's not what I meant," the widow pressed. "I mean, does he have a wife and children?"

"The pope isn’t allowed to marry," he replied.

"I don’t like that," the lady remarked.

She could have asked smarter questions, but would her daughter have been there, leaning gracefully against her shoulder and gazing ahead with a smile that almost looked sad, if she hadn’t felt free to say whatever she liked?

Alfred spoke again about Italy, describing the stunning colors of its landscapes: the purple hills, the deep blue Mediterranean, and the bright azure of southern skies, whose brilliance could only be rivaled in the North by the deep-blue eyes of a girl. He said this with a particular intonation, but the one he meant to understand, the lovely girl, seemed completely unaware, and that was charming.

"Beautiful Italy!" some guests sighed.

"Charming! Just charming!" echoed through the room.

"Maybe I’ll win a hundred thousand dollars in the lottery," the widow said. "If I do, my daughter and I will travel—and you'll join us, Mr. Alfred! We can all go together, along with a few of our friends." She nodded warmly at the company, making each guest imagine they were the lucky one chosen to travel with them to Italy. "Yes, we must go," she continued, "but let’s avoid areas with robbers. We’ll stick to Rome. You’re always safe on the main roads."

title: BEAUTY OF FORM AND BEAUTY OF MIND
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The daughter sighed softly, and oh, how much weight could be held in a sigh! The young man attributed a lot of meaning to it. Those deep-blue eyes, lit up during the evening in his honor, surely held treasures far richer than anything Rome could offer. So, when he left the party that night, he had completely lost his heart to the young lady. Mr. Alfred visited the naval officer's widow frequently, but it quickly became clear that his visits were more for her daughter than for the lady herself. They called her Kaela, a name derived from her full name, Karen Malena. She was genuinely beautiful, though some remarked she was a bit dull and liked to sleep in.

"She’s just used to it," her mother claimed. "She is a beauty, and they’re always more easily fatigued. She does sleep late, but that’s what makes her eyes so clear."

What power seemed to lie in the depths of those dark eyes! The young man felt the truth in the saying, "Still waters run deep," and he found himself diving deep into their mystery. He often shared stories of his adventures, and the mother was just as curious and eager as she'd been on that first evening. It was a joy to hear Alfred talk about anything. He showed them colorful pictures of Naples and spoke of climbing Mount Vesuvius and the fire eruptions it experienced. The widow had never heard of such things.

"Good heavens!" she exclaimed. "So that’s what a burning mountain looks like! But doesn't it pose a danger to the people living nearby?"

"Entire cities have been destroyed," he replied, "like Herculaneum and Pompeii."

"Oh, those poor people! You actually witnessed that yourself?"

"No, I didn’t see any of the eruptions shown in those pictures, but I’ll show you a sketch of one I did after seeing an eruption myself."

He placed a pencil sketch on the table, and the mother, overwhelmed by the impressive images, glanced at the pale drawing and exclaimed in astonishment, "Did you really see it spewing white fire?"

title: BEAUTY OF FORM AND BEAUTY OF MIND
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
For a moment, Alfred's respect for Kaela's mother wavered, but then he was dazzled again by Kaela's presence and found it understandable that her mother didn't have a keen eye for color. In the end, it hardly mattered because Kaela's mother possessed the greatest treasure of all: Kaela herself.

Alfred and Kaela became engaged, which seemed like a natural progression. Their engagement was announced in the small town's newspaper. Her mother bought thirty copies to cut out the announcement and send it to friends and family. The happy couple felt joy, and her mother shared in their happiness, feeling as if she was connecting herself to Thorwalsden.

"You’re a true successor of Thorwalsden," she told Alfred, and he sensed that in this instance, her compliment was quite astute. Kaela remained quiet, but her eyes sparkled, her lips smiled, and every movement was graceful—she was, indeed, beautiful, a fact worth repeating. Alfred decided to sculpt a bust of both Kaela and her mother. They posed for him as he molded the soft clay with his hands.

"I suppose you’re doing this work yourself just for us, instead of having your assistant do the mundane tasks?"

"I really need to mold the clay myself," he responded.

"Ah, yes, you’re always so courteous," the mother said with a smile, while Kaela silently squeezed his hand, all covered in clay.

Then he shared with them the marvels of Nature in all her forms. He explained how, in the hierarchy of creation, inanimate objects ranked lower than living beings—the plant above the mineral, the animal above the plant, and finally, humans at the peak. He argued that the beauty of the mind could be reflected in outward appearance, and it was the sculptor’s role to capture that beauty and express it through his art. Kaela listened silently but nodded in agreement, while her mother confessed:

"It’s a bit tough to follow you, but I’m making an effort to keep up with my thoughts, even if what you say makes my head spin. I do manage to grasp some of it."

title: BEAUTY OF FORM AND BEAUTY OF MIND
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Kaela’s beauty captivated Alfred; it filled his soul and held a powerful sway over him. Beauty radiated from every feature of Kaela, sparkled in her eyes, curled at the corners of her lips, and infused each movement of her graceful hands. Alfred, the sculptor, noticed this. He spoke only to her and thought only of her, and they became a single entity. You might say she spoke a lot through her silence, as he was always engaging with her; together, they were one. Following their engagement came the wedding, complete with bridesmaids and gift-giving, all joyfully acknowledged in the wedding speech. Kaela's mother set up a bust of Thorwalsden as a guest at the table, dressed in a bathrobe, believing he should be part of the celebration. Songs filled the air, and cheers erupted because it was a merry wedding, and they made a stunning couple. "Pygmalion loved his Galatea," one of the songs mentioned.

"Ah, that’s some of your myths," the mother-in-law remarked.

The next day, the newlyweds set off for Copenhagen, where they'd be living. Kaela’s mother accompanied them to handle the "household chores," as she liked to call them. Kaela appeared like a doll in a dollhouse, surrounded by everything bright, new, and exquisite. Together, the three of them sat, and Alfred's demeanor could be captured well by a saying: he looked like a swan among geese. The enchantment of beauty had ensnared him; he fixated on the exterior without caring to discover what lay beneath, a negligence that often leads to unhappiness in marriage. The outer shell may get damaged, the gilding may dull, and then the buyer laments their choice.

In a big group, it’s frustrating to have a button come loose with no spare to remedy it, but it's even worse to realize your wife and mother-in-law are engaged in silly chatter while you struggle to maintain a little wit to cover the awkwardness.

title: BEAUTY OF FORM AND BEAUTY OF MIND
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The young married couple often sat hand in hand; he would chat away while she occasionally added a word, her melodious voice ringing out with bell-like clarity. It was a welcome change when Sophy, one of her friends, came to visit. Sophy wasn’t pretty. Still, she had no noticeable flaws, even though Kaela sometimes joked that she was a tad crooked; no one except close friends would have noticed. She was sensible, yet she never realized that she could be a threat in such a household. Her presence brought a new atmosphere to their dollhouse, and they all acknowledged they needed fresh air. The young couple and their mother decided to travel to Italy.

"Thank goodness we’re back home in our own four walls!" both mother and daughter exclaimed upon returning from a year away.

"There’s really no joy in traveling," the mother said. "Honestly, it’s exhausting. I apologize for saying this. I got tired of it quickly, even with my children around. Plus, traveling is quite costly. And all those galleries you’re expected to visit, and the endless things you need to chase after! You have to do it out of guilt; everyone will ask what you’ve seen when you return and likely tell you what you missed out on. Eventually, I grew weary of those countless Madonnas; I thought I might turn into one myself."

"And the food, mom," Kaela chimed in.

"Yes, indeed," she replied, "I couldn’t find a decent meat soup—their cooking was just terrible."

The trip had worn Kaela out too, but she was always tired, which was her biggest issue. So, they invited Sophy to come live with them, and her presence was immensely beneficial. The mother-in-law admitted that while Sophy was not only a skilled homemaker, she was also knowledgeable and accomplished, though that was somewhat unexpected from someone of her modest means. She was also a kind-hearted, dedicated girl—a fact proven during Kaela's illness when she began to fade away. When the exterior is everything, it must be strong; otherwise, all hope is lost. And all hope faded for Kaela, as she died.

title: BEAUTY OF FORM AND BEAUTY OF MIND
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"She was beautiful," her mother said. "She was so different from those beauties we call 'antiques,' who are often damaged. A true beauty should be perfect, and Kaela was perfection itself."

Alfred wept, and so did her mother. They both donned mourning attire. The black dress suited her well, and she wore it longer than anyone else. She faced another sorrow when she saw Alfred marry again—this time to Sophy, who was far from beautiful. "He’s made a drastic choice," the mother-in-law commented. "He’s gone from the most beautiful to the ugliest and has forgotten his first wife. Men lack constancy. My husband was quite different—but he passed away before me."

"'Pygmalion loved his Galatea' was part of the song at my first wedding," Alfred said. "I once fell in love with a stunning statue that came to life in my arms; however, the kindred spirit, the angelic soul who can really elevate us, I only found now. You came to me, Sophy, not wrapped in the external beauty, though you are even fairer than necessary. The main aspect still prevails. You’ve come to teach the sculptor that his work is merely dust and clay—an outer shell made from a perishable material—and that what we ought to chase is the ethereal essence of mind and spirit. Poor Kaela! Our life together was just a fleeting encounter. In the next world, where we will recognize one another through the union of our minds, we will only be acquaintances."

"That wasn’t a loving sentiment," Sophy said, "nor was it spoken with kindness. In a future state, where there’s neither marrying nor giving in marriage, but where, as you say, souls are drawn together by shared understanding, everything beautiful will reveal itself, elevating to a higher plane of existence. Her spirit will gain such completeness that it may resonate with yours even better than mine, and you’ll once again express your first rapturous proclamation of love: 'Beautiful, most beautiful!'"

title: BY THE ALMSHOUSE WINDOW
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Close to the grassy rampart that surrounds Copenhagen stands a large red house. Colorful balsams and other flowers greet us from the many windows of the building, whose interior is quite shabby. The residents living there are elderly and in need.

Look! At the window, an old maid leans out. She pulls a dry leaf from the balsam plant and gazes at the grassy rampart where many children are playing. What’s on her mind? A whole life story is unfolding in her imagination.

"Those poor little children, how happy they are—playing and laughing together! Look at their rosy cheeks and bright eyes! But they don’t have shoes or stockings. They dance on the green rampart, right on the spot where, according to an old tale, the ground used to sink, and where a playful child had been lured with flowers, toys, and sweets into a grave that was already dug, which was later covered up. The story goes that after that, the ground became stable, and the mound remained solid, quickly getting blanketed with green grass. The little ones playing there have no idea about the old tale; otherwise, they might feel they heard a child weeping deep down below, and those dewdrops on each blade of grass would seem to them like tears of sorrow. They also know nothing of the Danish King who, facing an enemy, swore an oath before his trembling courtiers that he would stand his ground with the citizens of his capital and perish here in his home; they know nothing about the brave souls who fought here or the women who boiled water to pour on the enemy, waiting in the snow to surprise the city."

"No! The little ones are just playing with carefree spirits. Keep playing, little girl! Soon enough, the years will pass—yes, those brilliant years. The priestly hands have touched the candidates for confirmation; hand in hand they stroll along the green rampart. You’re wearing a white dress; it took your mother a lot of hard work, and yet it was made from a larger dress! You’ll also wear a red shawl; who cares if it hangs too low? People will just notice how big, how very big it is. You are thinking of your dress and of the Giver of all good—how wonderful it is to wander along the green rampart!"

"And the years go by; while there are dark days, your cheerful spirit stays bright, and you’ve gained a friend without even realizing how. You met so often! You walk together on the rampart in the fresh spring, during holidays when everyone comes out to enjoy the ramparts, and all the church bells seem to sing praises for the arrival of spring."

"Hardly have the violets sprouted when, there on the rampart, just opposite the lovely Castle of Rosenberg, stands a tree adorned with its first bright green buds. Every year, this tree bears fresh green shoots. Sadly, it’s not the same for the human heart! Dark clouds, more numerous than those that blanket the northern skies, obscure the human heart. Poor child! Your friend’s bridal chamber is a black coffin, and you become an old maid. From the almshouse window, behind the balsams, you’ll watch the merry children playing and see your own story unfold again."

And that is the life story that unfolds before the old maid as she gazes out at the rampart, the bright, sunny rampart, where the children, with their rosy cheeks and bare feet, are joyfully playing, just like other free little birds.

title: CHILDREN'S PRATTLE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
At the lavish home of a wealthy merchant, children from influential families gathered for a party. The merchant was a well-educated man; his father had sent him to college and he had successfully completed his exams. Initially, his father had been just a cattle dealer, but through hard work and honesty, he built up his wealth, and his son, the merchant, continued to grow it. Smart as he was, he also had a kind heart, though people talked more about his riches than his compassion. The merchant's home welcomed all sorts of visitors—those of high status and those with intellect, and some who had neither.

Since it was a gathering for children, the air was filled with innocent chatter straight from the heart. Among them was a striking little girl, filled with pride; this attitude was installed in her by the staff, not her sensible parents.

Her father held the prestigious position of groom of the Chambers at the royal court, a fact she was quite aware of. "I’m a child of the court," she declared. But her birth right didn’t define her value, just as being born into a less affluent family wouldn't hold anyone back from making something of themselves. She went on to brag about being well-born and insisted that those without the same background couldn’t climb up in life. "And those with names ending in ‘sen’ can never amount to anything," she added. "We have to stand tall with our hands on our hips to keep them at bay!" She stuck out her arms, demonstrating how it was to be done, and her delicate arms were indeed lovely.

This comment made the merchant's little daughter very upset since her father’s name was Petersen, ending in 'sen'. She said, trying to sound as proud as possible, "But my dad can buy a hundred dollars’ worth of candy and share it with everyone. Can your dad do that?"

"Yes!" chimed in the daughter of a newspaper editor. "My dad can write about your dad and anyone else in his newspaper. Everyone is afraid of him because he can do whatever he wants with the paper." Her expression radiated pride, as if she were a real princess.

title: CHILDREN'S PRATTLE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Outside the slightly open door stood a poor boy peeking through the crack. He was of such low standing that he wasn’t even allowed inside. He had spent the day turning a spit for the cook, who had let him hover by the door to catch a glimpse of the well-dressed children having fun. For him, this was a big deal. "Oh, if only I could be one of them," he thought, listening to the talk about names which only deepened his sadness. His family couldn’t even afford a newspaper, let alone write for one; and worse, his father’s name—and his own—ended in ‘sen,’ which meant he would never succeed. But he accepted that he was given his place in life and had to be at peace with it.

And this is what unfolded that evening.

Years went by, and most of those children grew into adults.

In the town stood an impressive house, filled with all sorts of beautiful and valuable items. Everyone wanted to see it, with people visiting even from surrounding areas to gaze at its treasures.

Which of the children whose chatter we’ve shared could claim this magnificent house as their own? You might think it’s easy to guess, but it actually wasn’t straightforward. The house belonged to the poor boy who had stood outside that night. He had truly made something of himself, despite having ‘sen’ at the end of his name—it was Thorwaldsen.

As for the other three children—those of fine lineage, wealth, and intellectual pride—they were respected and admired in the world. They enjoyed privileges from birth and upbringing, and they had no reason to feel guilty about their past statements and beliefs, as it had all been just 'children's prattle.'

title: DELAYING IS NOT FORGETTING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
There was an old mansion surrounded by a swampy ditch and a drawbridge that was rarely lowered—because not all visitors are good. The house had loopholes in the walls for shooting and pouring boiling water or even molten lead on any would-be intruders. Inside, the rooms were tall, with high-beamed ceilings, perfect for the smoke that billowed from the fireplace where large, damp logs smoldered. Pictures of knights in armor and proud ladies in splendid dresses adorned the walls; the most regal of all was a woman named Meta Mogen, the mistress of the house, to whom the castle belonged.

As evening fell, robbers arrived; they killed three of her servants and the yard dog, then chained Mrs. Meta to the kennel while they feasted in the hall, enjoying the wine and ale from her cellar. Now tethered, Mrs. Meta could only watch helplessly.

However, one of the robbers' servants quietly approached her; he had to be careful not to be seen, or they would have killed him.

"Mrs. Meta Mogen," he said, "do you remember how my father had to ride a wooden horse when your husband was still alive? You prayed for him, but it was no use; he had to endure that until his limbs were scarred. You snuck down to him, just like I’m sneaking to you now, and placed small stones under each of his feet to give him support. No one saw it, or they chose not to, because you were then the lovely young mistress. My father told me about this, and I haven’t forgotten! Now I will set you free, Mrs. Meta Mogen!"

Then they brought the horses out of the stable and rode off through the rain and wind to seek help from friends.

"A small act of kindness done for the old man has now been rewarded richly!" Mrs. Meta said.

"Delaying is not forgetting," said the servant.

The mansion still stands today; it no longer belongs to Mrs. Meta Mogen but to another noble family.

We are in present times now. The sun shines on the gilded knob of the tower, small wooded islands dot the water like bouquets, and wild swans glide around them. In the garden, roses bloom; the mistress of the house is the finest rose of all, radiating joy—joy born from good deeds in her heart, deeds that remain preserved and are not forgotten. Delaying is not forgetting!

title: DELAYING IS NOT FORGETTING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Now, she walks from the mansion to a small peasant hut in the field. Inside lives a poor girl who cannot walk; her small room faces north, so the sun rarely enters. The girl can only see a tiny patch of field surrounded by a tall fence. But today, sunlight fills the room—the warm, beautiful sunshine coming from the south through a new window that replaced a wall.

The paralyzed girl sits basking in the warm sunlight, and she can see the woods and the lake; the world has become vast and beautiful, all thanks to a single word from the kind mistress of the mansion.

"The word was so simple, the act so small," she said. "The joy it brought me was immeasurable and sweet!"

And so she continues to do many good deeds, thinking of everyone in humble cottages and grand mansions where those in need reside. It may be hidden, but God does not forget. Delaying is not forgetting!

There was an old house in a bustling town. It has rooms and halls, but we won’t go inside; we'll stay in the kitchen, where it’s warm and bright, clean and tidy. The copper pots shine, and the table looks freshly polished. A single servant has done all this, yet she has time to spare as if she wants to go to church; she wears a black bow on her cap, symbolizing mourning. But she has no one to mourn—neither family nor sweetheart. She’s a poor girl who once was engaged to a kind young man; they were deeply in love.

"We have nothing! The wealthy widow across the street has made an offer to me; she’ll make me rich, but you are in my heart. What should I do?"

"Do what you believe will lead to your happiness," she advised. "Be kind to her, but remember this: once we part ways, we will never see each other again."

Years went by; then one day, she bumped into her old friend and sweetheart in the street. He looked ill and worn, and she couldn’t help but ask, "How are you?"

"I’m rich and doing well in every way," he replied. "The woman is strong and kind, but you are still in my heart. I’ve fought my battles, and they will soon come to an end; we won’t see each other again until we meet before God!"

A week passed; that morning, she read in the newspaper that he had died. That’s why she wore mourning! Her old sweetheart had passed away, leaving behind a wife and three step-children, according to the paper; it felt like a crack, but the metal was pure.

The black bow signifies loss, and the sorrow on the girl’s face reveals even deeper pain; it remains in her heart and will never be forgotten. Delaying is not forgetting!

These are three stories, you see—three leaves on the same stem. Would you like more? The little heartbook contains many more. Delaying is not forgetting!

title: EVERYTHING IN THE RIGHT PLACE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between nobility and commonality
text_chunk:
More than a hundred years ago, at the edge of the woods by a large lake, stood an old mansion surrounded by deep ditches filled with reeds and bulrushes. Nearby, a gnarled willow tree leaned over the reeds next to the drawbridge at the gate.

From the narrow path, the sounds of bugles and the pounding of hooves echoed nearby. A little girl, busy watching the geese, hurried to shoo them away from the bridge before the hunting party approached. They arrived so quickly that she had no choice but to climb onto one of the high corner stones of the bridge to avoid being run over. She was still quite young, delicate in frame, with bright blue eyes and a sweet, gentle expression. But the baron, riding by on his horse, didn’t notice any of this. Instead, he playfully swung his hunting crop and gave her a rough shove, sending her tumbling backward into the ditch.

"Everything in the right place!" he shouted. "Into the ditch with you!"

He burst into laughter, thinking it was all in good fun. The rest of the hunting party joined in, shouting and yelping while their hounds barked in excitement.

As she fell, the girl managed to grab onto a branch of the willow tree, hanging on just above the water. Once the baron and his party, along with the barking dogs, disappeared through the gate, she tried to pull herself up, but the branch snapped. Just as she was about to fall into the reeds, a strong hand reached down from above. It was a pedlar who had been watching from a distance and rushed over to help her.

"Everything in the right place," he said, mimicking the baron as he pulled the girl up to solid ground. He wanted to put the broken branch back where it had snapped, but since that wasn’t possible, he simply stuck it into the soft earth.

title: EVERYTHING IN THE RIGHT PLACE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between nobility and commonality
text_chunk:
"Grow and thrive if you can, and produce a good flute for them over at the mansion," he remarked, secretly wishing to see the baron and his companions taught a lesson. Then he entered the castle—but not to the banquet hall; he was too humble for that. Instead, he headed for the servants' hall where the workers examined his wares and bargained with him. Above, loud laughter and the barking of dogs echoed through the open windows; they called it singing and certainly put in their best effort. Wine and strong ale were overflowing in glasses and jugs, and the favored dogs feasted alongside their masters. They summoned the pedlar upstairs, but only so they could make fun of him. With their heads clouded by drink, they filled a stocking with beer for him to drink quickly, claiming it was all in good fun as they laughed. Then they carelessly wagered meadows, farmers, and entire farmyards on a single card... and lost.

"Everything in the right place!" the pedlar remarked as he finally escaped what he called Sodom and Gomorrah. "The open road is truly my right place; I felt uneasy up there."

The little goose-girl, who had returned to watching the geese, kindly nodded at him as he passed through the gate.

Weeks turned into months, and the girl noticed that the broken willow branch he had stuck in the ground near the ditch remained fresh and green—new twigs even started to sprout. She saw that the branch had rooted, and it made her happy; she claimed the tree as her own. While the tree thrived, everything else in the castle was in decline due to excessive feasting and gambling, which are precarious pastimes. Less than six years later, the baron exited his castle gate, reduced to a poor beggar, while a wealthy tradesman had purchased the baronial seat. That very pedlar they once mocked and poured beer over became the owner of the estate, proving that honesty and hard work lead to success. From then on, no card-playing was allowed there.

"That's a terrible pastime," he declared. "When the devil saw the Bible for the first time, he sought to create a mockery of it and invented card-playing."

title: EVERYTHING IN THE RIGHT PLACE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between nobility and commonality
text_chunk:
The new owner of the land found a wife—and who did he choose? The little goose-girl, who had always remained kind and good, looking stunning in her new clothes as if she were born of nobility. How everything unfolded would take too long to tell in this busy world, but it really happened, and the most important moments are still to come.

Life in the old mansion became joyful and bright: the mother managed the household while the father took care of things outside, and they flourished.

Where honesty paves the way, prosperity is sure to follow. The old mansion got a fresh coat of paint, the ditches were cleaned, and fruit trees were planted; everything felt cozy and inviting, with floors as shining and white as cardboard. In the long winter evenings, the mistress and her maids would sit by the spinning wheel in the grand hall, and every Sunday, the counselor—an honorable title the pedlar earned later in life—read aloud from the Bible. Their children received the best education, but as with all families, not everyone was particularly bright.

Meanwhile, the willow tree near the drawbridge matured into a magnificent tree, standing tall and free, never trimmed. "It's our family tree," the older folks would say to their kids, "and it deserves our respect."

title: EVERYTHING IN THE RIGHT PLACE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between nobility and commonality
text_chunk: A century passed. In modern times, the lake had turned into marshland; the entire baronial estate had seemingly vanished. Only a pool of water and remnants of crumbled walls remained where the deep ditches once lay. And there stood a magnificent old tree with drooping branches—this was the family tree. It thrived beautifully when left untouched. Though its trunk was split from root to crown due to storms, it stood resilient, with grass and flowers sprouting from every crevice. Particularly above, where the sizable branches divided, a small hanging garden flourished, with wild raspberries and ferns thriving, even a touch of mistletoe growing gracefully among the old willow's branches, reflecting in the dark waters below as the wind rustled the weeds at the edge of the pool. A footpath winding across the fields ran close to the old tree. Atop a wooded hillside, the new mansion boasted a stunning view, grand and impressive, with windowpanes so clear they seemed non-existent. The broad staircase leading to the entrance resembled a bower decorated with roses and lush plants. The lawn was brilliantly green as if each blade of grass was individually polished. Inside the foyer, priceless oil paintings hung on the walls, and chairs and sofas upholstered in silk and velvet could be easily moved on casters. Polished marble-topped tables and leather-bound books with gilt edges completed the elegant decor. Clearly, well-off and distinguished people resided there—the baron and his family. Every piece perfectly matched its surroundings. "Everything in the right place" was their motto, and thus the portraits, once the pride of the old mansion, were now relegated to the hallway leading to the servants' quarters. They had become mere relics, especially two portraits: one of a man in a scarlet coat sporting a wig, and the other of a lady with curly, powdered hair holding a rose, both encircled by large wreaths of willow. Both portraits bore spots from where the baron’s sons had used them for target practice with their crossbows. They depicted the counselor and his wife, the ancestors of the entire family. "But they weren't truly part of our family," one boy remarked, "He was a pedlar and she tended geese. They aren't like our mom and dad." The portraits were just old junk, and "everything in its rightful place"—that was why the great-grandparents' portraits hung in the passage leading to the servants' quarters.

title: EVERYTHING IN THE RIGHT PLACE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between nobility and commonality
text_chunk:
The village pastor's son served as a tutor at the mansion. One day, he took his young charges and their older sister—she had recently been confirmed—for a walk across the fields. They strolled along the road by the old willow tree, where she picked a beautiful bouquet of wildflowers. "Everything in the right place," she remarked, and indeed, the flowers looked stunning. While listening intently to the pastor’s son discuss the elements and notable figures from history, she felt a sense of joy at the knowledge shared. She possessed a healthy mind, noble in actions and filled with love for everything God had fashioned. When they reached the old willow tree, the youngest of the baron’s sons desperately wanted a flute made from its wood, similar to what he’d had before. The pastor’s son broke off a branch. "Oh, please don't!" the young lady exclaimed, but it was too late. "That’s our famous old tree! I love it dearly. My family laughs at me for it, but that doesn’t matter. There’s a story tied to this tree," she began to explain—sharing the tale we already know about the mansion, the pedlar, and the goose-girl who first met there and became the ancestors of the noble family she belonged to.

"They never sought a title, those good old folks," she continued, "their motto was 'everything in its right place,' and they believed it wouldn’t be right to buy a title. My grandfather, the first baron, was their son; they say he was quite learned, well-liked by princes and princesses, and invited to all the royal festivities. At home, everyone favors him, but I can't quite explain it, something about the old couple tugs at my heart! How warm and family-like it must have been in that old mansion, with the mistress at the spinning wheel surrounded by her maids while her husband read from the Bible!"

"They must have been wonderful, sensible people," the pastor’s son agreed. Their conversation naturally shifted to the distinction between nobles and commoners. From the way the tutor waxed eloquent about noble heritage, it almost seemed like he didn’t belong to a commoner’s family.

title: EVERYTHING IN THE RIGHT PLACE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between nobility and commonality
text_chunk:
"It's a blessing to belong to a family with a distinguished history, one that drives us to strive for goodness. Belonging to a noble lineage is a privilege; it serves as a golden ticket to the highest circles of society. Moreover, many poets express that only the poor possess brilliance, and that nobility is to blame for ill qualities, but that view is misguided. Noble families have their fair share of beautiful traits; my mother has shared many stories of such moments, and I could recount several. Once, at a noble family's mansion in town—my grandmother had served as the lady's nurse—my mom was alone in a room with the nobleman when he suddenly noticed an elderly woman limping into the courtyard, a regular visitor every Sunday to collect her gift.

"Look at that poor woman," said the nobleman, "it’s difficult for her to walk."

Before my mother could even respond, he hurried out of the room to assist her with the gift she came to claim. It may seem like a small act, but it resonates like the widow’s two mites from the Bible—echoing in the depths of every human heart. This is what poets should highlight, especially in our time; it does good, it heals, and it brings people together. But when someone thinks their noble birth entitles them to act superior—like a horse prancing in the street—calling others commoners when they enter a room, it shows how nobility can decay; it becomes mere pretense, much like a mask created by Thespis, and is amusing when such a person is ridiculed in satire."

That was the tutor’s lengthy but well-meaning speech, delivered while he finished shaping the flute.

A large gathering took place at the mansion; many guests from the region as well as the capital arrived. There were ladies in both elegant and garish attire, and the grand hall was packed. The clergymen stood in a corner, appearing solemn as though preparing for a funeral, even though it was a celebration—the festivities had simply yet to begin. A grand concert was on the agenda, which was why the baron’s young son had brought his willow flute, though neither he nor his father could get it to produce a sound, rendering the flute practically useless.

title: EVERYTHING IN THE RIGHT PLACE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between nobility and commonality
text_chunk:
What music there was and the songs sung delighted those who performed them; otherwise, it was an enchanting experience!

"Are you an artist?" asked a dandy, the baron’s son. "You created it from the willow, haven’t you? Genius deserves the spotlight!"

"Definitely not! I just go with the flow of the times, and that’s something you can’t avoid."

"I hope you’ll entertain us with your little instrument—won't you?" He then handed the tutor the flute cut from the willow tree by the pool and called out loudly that the tutor was about to perform a solo. It was clear they wanted to tease him, so he initially refused, even though he actually played quite well. However, they persisted and encouraged him until he finally took the flute and raised it to his lips.

What a mesmerizing flute! Its sound was more electrifying than a steam engine’s whistle; it carried far and wide, echoing in the yard, the garden, the woods, and many miles around. With the sound, a storm brewed and roared; "Everything in the right place." All at once, the baron, as if swept up by the wind, flew out of the hall and straight into the shepherd's cottage. The shepherd, during this chaotic event, didn’t enter the hall; instead, he stumbled into the servants' area, among the snazzy footmen who wore silk stockings—those proud servants were alarmed that an outsider dared to sit at their table. In the main hall, the baron's daughter flew to the head of the table—she definitely belonged there—with the pastor's son seated beside her. They looked like a couple in love. An elderly Count, part of one of the oldest families in the land, remained undisturbed in his place of honor. The flute played fairly, as fairness should, and it is everyone’s duty to observe it. The sharp-tongued dandy who had instigated the flute performance stumbled into the poultry house, and many followed suit.

The flute could be heard from a mile away, resulting in curious occurrences. A wealthy banking family, riding in a luxurious carriage drawn by four horses, found themselves tossed from it, unable to even spot room for their footmen inside. Two prosperous farmers who had risen above their fields were thrown into the ditch—it was a perilous flute indeed. Thankfully, it broke after producing its first sound; and that was a relief, for it was safely returned to its owner’s pocket—"its right place."

The following day, not a soul muttered a word about the startling events; hence emerged the term "to pocket the flute." Everything returned to its usual state, except the portraits of the pedlar and the goose-girl now hung in the banquet hall. They graced the wall as if they belonged there, and as an expert claimed their irreplaceable value, they remained displayed and restored. "Everything in the right place," and so it will be. Eternity lasts far longer than this tale.

title: GRANDMOTHER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Grandmother is very old, with a face full of wrinkles and hair that's completely white; yet her eyes shine like stars and radiate a gentle warmth when they look at you, which feels comforting. She wears a dress made of heavy, luxurious silk decorated with large flowers, and it rustles softly as she moves. And oh, can she tell stories! Grandmother has a wealth of knowledge, having lived before both my parents—that much is certain. She carries a hymn-book with big silver clasps that she often reads from. Inside the book, between its pages, lies a flat, dried rose. It may not be as pretty as the fresh roses in the vase, but she gazes at it with a joyful smile, sometimes even bringing tears to her eyes. "I wonder why Grandmother looks at that faded flower like that? Do you know?" Whenever her tears fall onto the rose, it seems to revive, filling the room with its sweet scent; the walls fade away like a mist, and suddenly she's surrounded by a beautiful green forest where sunlight streams through thick leaves. In that moment, Grandmother becomes young again—a lovely maiden, fresh like a blooming rose, with rosy cheeks, bright ringlets, and a graceful figure. But her eyes, those same gentle, saintly eyes, remain unchanged; they've stayed with her. By her side sits a tall, strong young man who hands her a rose, and she smiles at him. Grandmother can't smile like that anymore. Yes, she is smiling at the memory of that day, filled with many thoughts and cherished recollections; but the handsome young man is long gone, the rose has wilted in the old book, and Grandmother sits there again, an old woman gazing sadly at the faded rose.

title: GRANDMOTHER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Grandmother has passed away now. She was sitting in her armchair, sharing a beautiful, long story with us; and when it ended, she said she was tired and leaned back to take a little nap. We could hear her soft breathing as she slept; it gradually became more peaceful and calm, and her face glowed with happiness and serenity, as if lit by a ray of sunshine. She smiled one last time, and then people softly said she was gone. She was laid in a black coffin, looking serene and beautiful in the white folds of her shroud, even though her eyes were closed; the wrinkles had disappeared, her hair gleamed white and silvery, and a sweet smile lingered around her lips. There was no fear in our hearts as we gazed upon the body of our beloved grandmother. Her hymn-book, where the rose still lay, was placed under her head, as she had wished; and then they buried her.

On her grave, right by the churchyard wall, they planted a rose bush; it quickly blossomed with roses, and a nightingale perched among the flowers, singing over the grave. From the church organ, the beautiful music and words of the psalms echoed, just like those found in the old book resting under the head of the departed.

The moon illuminated the grave, but the person who once lay there was not present; any child could wander safely, even at night, and pick a rose from the bush by the churchyard wall. The departed know more than we, the living. They understand the fear that would grip us if a dead person were to appear before us. They are in a better place; the dead do not return. The earth has been piled high over the coffin, and it is only earth that lies inside it. The pages of the hymn-book have turned to dust, and the rose, along with all its memories, has crumbled to dust as well. Yet, fresh roses bloom over the grave, the nightingale sings, and the music from the organ continues, keeping the memory of our dear grandmother alive, with her loving, gentle eyes that always seemed youthful. Eyes can never truly die. One day, ours will once again meet our beloved grandmother, young and beautiful, just as she was when she first kissed the fresh, red rose that is now dust in her grave.

title: HOLGER DANSKE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In Denmark, there's an ancient castle called Kronenburg, located near the Sound of Elsinore. Every day, huge ships from England, Russia, and Prussia sail by in droves. They greet the castle with booming cannon fire, "Boom, boom," as if saying, "Good day!" The old castle replies with a single "Boom," which means, "Thank you very much." During winter, no ships can pass because the Sound freezes over, creating a surface that looks like a highway. People from Denmark and Sweden exchange friendly gestures instead of cannon fire, offering each other white bread and biscuits, believing that foreign treats taste the best.

But the most stunning sight in all of this is the old castle of Kronenburg, where Holger Danske rests deep in a dark cellar that no one ever enters. He's dressed in iron and steel, holding his head up on a strong arm; his long beard trails down onto a marble table, where it has practically become one with it. He sleeps and dreams, yet in his dreams, he sees everything happening in Denmark. On every Christmas Eve, an angel visits him and reassures him that all his dreams are true. The angel tells him he can sleep peacefully because Denmark is not in any real danger. However, if trouble ever arises, Holger Danske will awaken, and the table will break apart as he pulls his beard. Then he will emerge with great strength, ready to strike a blow that will resonate across the world.

title: HOLGER DANSKE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: An old grandfather was sitting with his little grandson, sharing this tale about Holger Danske, and the boy believed every word his grandfather said. As the old man recounted the story, he carved a figure out of wood to represent Holger Danske, which was meant to be placed on the front of a ship. The grandfather, a talented woodcarver, created Holger Danske standing tall and proud, with his long beard flowing, one hand gripping a broad battle-axe while the other rested against the Danish arms. He shared many stories about heroic Danish men and women from the past with the little boy, who felt he learned so much that he could even match Holger Danske's wisdom, who, after all, only dreamed. When the boy finally went to bed, he thought so much about Holger that he imagined pressing his chin against his quilt, pretending he had a long beard connected to it. Meanwhile, the grandfather continued carving, finishing the Danish arms last. He stepped back to admire his creation and remembered all he had heard, read, and shared that evening with his grandson. Nodding his head, he wiped his glasses clean and put them on, saying, "Ah, yes; Holger Danske may not appear in my lifetime, but perhaps the boy in bed will witness him when the time truly comes." The grandfather nodded again, feeling increasingly satisfied that he had captured the essence of Holger Danske. The figure seemed to sparkle with life; the armor shone like real steel, and the hearts in the Danish arms glowed red, while the lions with gold crowns leaped in spirits.

title: HOLGER DANSKE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"That is the most beautiful coat of arms in the world," the old man declared. "The lions symbolize strength, while the hearts represent gentleness and love." As he gazed at the top lion, memories of King Canute flooded his mind, the ruler who connected England to Denmark's throne. The second lion reminded him of Waldemar, who liberated Denmark and conquered the Vandals. The third lion brought to mind Margaret, who unified Denmark, Sweden, and Norway. But as he fixed his gaze on the red hearts, their colors burned brighter, like flames. Each heart ignited a recollection. The first led him to a dim, cramped prison where a beautiful woman, Christian the Fourth's daughter, Eleanor Ulfeld, sat. The flame transformed into a rose blooming on her chest, its petals pure like her noble heart. "Ah, yes; that’s indeed a noble heart in the Danish arms," the grandfather said, moving onto the next flame, which took him to the sea, where cannon fire echoed and smoke enveloped the ships. The flames wrapped around Hvitfeldt, marking him with an honor ribbon as he miraculously blew himself and his ship into the air to save the fleet. The third flame guided him to the miserable huts of Greenland, where preacher Hans Egede spread love with every word and action, making that flame a star on his chest, adding another heart to the Danish arms.

As the old grandfather's spirit followed the next flickering flame, he sensed where it would take him. In a humble peasant woman's room stood Frederick the Sixth, jotting his name in chalk on a beam. The flame twinkled on his breast and heart, merging in that peasant's room, signifying his love became part of the Danish arms. The grandfather wiped away tears, having known King Frederick—the man with silver hair and honest blue eyes—who he had devoted his life to see. He folded his hands and sat in silence for a while. His daughter walked in, urging him to rest because it was getting late, and dinner awaited.

"What you’ve carved is so beautiful, grandfather," she remarked. "Holger Danske and the old coat of arms; I feel like I’ve seen that face before."

title: HOLGER DANSKE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"No, that can't be," the old grandfather replied. "But I have seen it, and I tried to capture it in wood, just like I remember it. It was long ago, during the English fleet's siege on April second, when we proved we were true, historic Danes. I was aboard the Denmark, in Steene Bille's squadron, with a man at my side whom even cannonballs seemed to fear. He sang old songs with glee and fought like he was more than just a man. I still remember his face, but where he came from or where he went is a mystery; nobody knows. I've often thought it might have been Holger Danske himself, swimming down to help us in our hour of need. That's my theory, and there you can see his likeness."

The wooden figure cast a gigantic shadow on the wall and even part of the ceiling. It felt as if the true Holger Danske stood behind it, his shadow flickering, likely due to the lamp's unstable flame. Then the daughter-in-law kissed the old grandfather and guided him to a comfortable armchair by the table. She and her husband—who was the grandfather's son and the father of the little boy in bed—sat down to join him for supper. As they ate, the grandfather spoke of the Danish lions and hearts, symbols of strength and kindness, explaining that there is a different kind of strength than just that found in a sword. He pointed to a shelf filled with old books, including a collection of Holberg's plays, which are so witty and entertaining that it feels as if we’ve known the people described in them.

"He knew how to fight too," the old man added. "He challenged the follies and prejudices of people throughout his life."

Then the grandfather gestured to a spot above the mirror where an almanac depicted the Round Tower and said, "Tycho Brahe was another who wielded a sword—not one to harm but one to light up the path of the stars above and make them understandable. Then there's he, whose father was in my profession. Yes, he—the son of the old image-carver, the man with silver hair and broad shoulders, whose name resonates worldwide. He was a sculptor, and I am just a carver. Holger Danske could come alive in marble, so that people around the world learn of Denmark's strength. Now, let’s raise a toast to Bertel."

The little boy in bed could vividly picture the old castle of Kronenburg, the Sound of Elsinore, and Holger Danske deep in his cellar, his beard attached to the table as he dreamed about everything happening above him.

Holger Danske did dream of that small, humble room where the wood-carver sat; he heard every word spoken and nodded in his sleep, saying, "Ah, yes, remember me, Danish people. Keep me in your thoughts; I will come when you need me most."

The bright morning light spilled over Kronenburg, with the wind carrying the sound of a hunting horn from the nearby shores. As ships passed by, they greeted the castle with the booming of cannons, and Kronenburg responded with its own, "Boom, boom." Yet the booming cannons did not wake Holger Danske because they simply meant, "Good morning" and "Thank you." He would need another kind of firing to wake up, but rest assured, Holger Danske would awaken when needed, for he still held great energy.

title: IB AND LITTLE CHRISTINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: In a forest stretching from the banks of the Gudenau in North Jutland, a substantial ridge of land rises from the ground, creating a natural wall through the woods. To the west of this ridge, not far from the river, there’s a farmhouse surrounded by poor soil, with sandy patches visible between the few stalks of rye and wheat. It had been years since anyone here tended these fields. They kept three sheep, a pig, and two oxen; really, they managed quite well and had just enough to live on, as happy folks often do. They could have even afforded to keep a couple of horses, but local farmers often said, "A horse eats as much as it earns." Jeppe Jans worked his fields during summer and made wooden shoes in winter. He also had an assistant, a boy who was just as skilled at making wooden shoes that were sturdy yet lightweight. They crafted shoes and spoons that sold well, so it wouldn’t be fair to call Jeppe Jans and his family poor. Little Ib, a seven-year-old boy and their only child, would sit by, watching the workers or whittling a stick—sometimes even his finger instead of the stick. One day, Ib carved so well that he turned two pieces of wood into real little wooden shoes, and he decided to give them to Little Christina as a gift.

title: IB AND LITTLE CHRISTINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "And who is Little Christina?" you might ask. She was the boatman's daughter, delicate and graceful like a young lady should be; if she’d worn different clothes, no one would believe she lived in a tiny hut on the nearby heath with her father. He was a widower who made a living transporting firewood from the forest to the eel pond and eel weir on the estate of Silkborg, and occasionally all the way to the distant town of Randers. There was no one to look after Little Christina, so she was usually with him in the boat, exploring the woods filled with blossoming heath or picking ripe wild berries. Sometimes, when her father had to go into town, he would take Christina, who was a year younger than Ib, across the heath to Jeppe Jans's cottage and leave her there. Ib and Christina were in perfect harmony; they shared their bread and berries when they were hungry, tended to their small gardens together, and played everywhere. Once, they ventured quite deep into the forest and even climbed the high ridge. Another time, they discovered a few snipe eggs in the woods, which was quite exciting. Ib had never been to the heath where Christina's father lived or near the river, but that changed when her father invited him for a boat ride. The evening before, he walked across the heath to visit the boatman at his house. The next morning, they were seated on top of a high pile of firewood in the boat, munching on bread and wild strawberries while Christina's father used poles to guide the boat forward. They floated along smoothly, with the tide on their side, drifting over lakes formed by the river; at times, they were completely surrounded by reeds and aquatic plants, yet there was always a clear path ahead—even as the old trees bent over the water, their gnarled branches, like sleeves rolled up, scaring off the nearby wildlife. The water lilies waved gently on the surface, making the whole journey magical, until they finally reached the grand eel weir where the water gushed through the floodgates. The children found it awe-inspiring.

title: IB AND LITTLE CHRISTINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Things took a turn for the worse when, after unloading the firewood, Christina's father bought a whole bundle of eels and a sucking pig, placing it all in a basket at the back of the boat. They started their journey back upstream; the wind was on their side, and with two sails up, it was as if two horses were pulling them along. While they sailed, they happened to drift near the place where the boatman's helper lived, not far from the riverbank. The boat was tied up, and the two men instructed the children to sit still. They did so, but only for a moment before their curiosity got the better of them. They peeked into the basket first and, unable to resist, decided to pull out the pig to hold it. Both trying to grab it at once, they accidentally dropped it into the water, and the pig began to float away with the current.

This was a total disaster! Ib jumped out of the boat and ran a little way from the water.

"Oh, bring me with you!" cried Christina, and she jumped after him. Moments later, they were deep in the thicket, unable to see the boat or the shore. They ran a bit further, but then Christina tripped and started to cry.

Ib helped her up and said, "Don’t worry; just follow me. That house is right over there." But the house wasn't where he thought, and they kept wandering deeper, crunching through the dry leaves and stepping on broken branches that snapped under their little feet. Then they heard a loud, eerie cry and stopped to listen. Soon the scream of an eagle cut through the woods, an ugly sound that scared the children. But in front of them, in the densest part of the forest, were the most enticing blackberries, growing in abundance. They couldn’t resist stopping to eat, and they feasted so long that their mouths and cheeks turned dark with juice.

Suddenly, they heard that frightening scream again, and Christina said, "We’re going to get into trouble about that pig."

title: IB AND LITTLE CHRISTINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Oh, it’s fine," replied Ib. "We’ll just head back to my father’s house; it’s somewhere in these woods." So they went on, but the path kept leading them farther off track. No house appeared, and soon it was getting dark, making the children feel scared. The eerie silence around them was occasionally broken by the hoots of owls and birds they didn’t recognize. Eventually, they found themselves lost in the thicket; Christina started crying, and soon Ib joined in too. They sat down on the dry leaves, and eventually, they fell asleep.

The sun was high in the sky when the children woke up, feeling chilly. But they noticed the sun shining on a hill nearby and thought if they climbed up, they’d warm up. Ib thought he might be able to see his father’s house from the top. However, they were far from home in a completely different part of the forest. They struggled up to the hillside and arrived at the edge of a slope that descended toward a clear, shimmering lake. The sunlight sparkled off the surface, and they were surprised to stumble upon such a beautiful sight.

Near where they stood, there was a hazel bush overflowing with delicious nuts. They quickly gathered some and cracked them open, enjoying the tender young kernels that were just ripe. But another surprise awaited them. From the bushes emerged a tall old woman, her face sun-baked brown and her deep black hair shining; her eyes sparkled like a Moor's. On her back, she carried a bundle, and in her hand, she held a knotted stick. She was a gypsy. At first, the children didn’t fully understand her words. She reached into her pocket and drew out three large nuts, telling them there were beautiful, wondrous things hidden inside—wishing nuts. Ib looked at her and, feeling braver, asked her if she would give him the nuts. The woman handed them over and then gathered a pocketful for herself. Ib and Christina stared at the wishing nuts with wide eyes.

"Does this nut contain a carriage pulled by two horses?" asked Ib.

title: IB AND LITTLE CHRISTINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Indeed, it holds a golden carriage with two golden horses," replied the woman.

"Then I’ll take that one," said Christina, so Ib handed it to her, and the mysterious woman tied it up in her handkerchief.

Ib picked up another nut. "Does this one have a pretty neckerchief like the one Christina is wearing?" he asked.

"Yes, it has ten neckerchiefs inside, along with beautiful dresses, stockings, and a hat with a veil," she answered.

"Then I want that one too," said Christina; "it sounds lovely!" And Ib gave her the second nut.

The last one was a little black nut. "You can have that one," said Christina; "it doesn’t look as nice."

"It’s the best of all for you," replied the gypsy with a smile, as Ib held the nut tightly.

Then the woman promised to guide the children back to the right path so they could find their way home. They ended up going in a completely different direction than they intended, so nobody should blame the woman as if she meant to steal them. On a wild path, they met a forester who recognized Ib, and with his help, Ib and Christina made it home, where everyone had been worried about them. They were forgiven, even though they’d done wrong by letting the pig fall into the water and by wandering off. Christina went back to her father’s cottage on the heath, while Ib stayed in the farmhouse on the edge of the woods, near the great ridge.

That evening, the first thing Ib did was pull the little black nut from his pocket, the one that supposedly contained the best gift. He placed it carefully between the door and the doorpost, then closed the door, cracking the nut open. But when he peeked inside, he saw mostly emptiness; it appeared hollow or worm-eaten, looking like it was filled with rich black soil instead of anything good. "Just what I expected!" he exclaimed. "How could anything wonderful fit in this tiny nut? Christina will find her two nuts are just the same; no fancy clothes or golden carriage will be in them."

title: IB AND LITTLE CHRISTINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Winter came and went; a new year followed, along with many years. Eventually, Ib was old enough to be confirmed, so he spent an entire winter with the local clergyman preparing for it.

One day during this time, the boatman visited Ib's parents and shared that Christina had gotten a job and was doing very well. "Just think!" he said, "She is going to work for a wealthy innkeeper at the hotel in Herning, many miles to the west. She’ll help the landlady with housekeeping; if she behaves well and stays to be confirmed, they will treat her like their own daughter."

So Ib and Christina said their goodbyes. People were already calling them "the betrothed," and at parting, the girl showed Ib the two nuts that she had kept safe since they got lost in the woods. She also told him she’d treasured the little wooden shoes he carved for her as a child, kept neatly in a drawer. And that was how they parted.

After Ib’s confirmation, he stayed home with his mother, having become a skilled shoemaker. During summer, he managed the farm all by himself since his father had passed away and his mother didn’t have any farm hands. Occasionally, but not often, he would hear about Christina through a passing postillion or eel seller. It sounded like she was well off with the wealthy innkeeper, and after being confirmed, she wrote a letter to her father, including a warm message for Ib and his mother. In the letter, she mentioned that her new employers gifted her with a lovely new dress and some nice undergarments. This was heartening news.

title: IB AND LITTLE CHRISTINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
One day in the following spring, there was a knock at Ib’s mother’s door. When they opened it, lo and behold, the boatman and Christina stepped inside. She had come to visit and spend the day. A carriage had come from the Herning hotel to the next village, and she had taken the chance to reconnect with her friends. She looked as elegant as a lady, dressed in a beautiful outfit made just for her. There she stood, adorned in fine clothing, while Ib was in his work clothes. Words escaped him; he could only take her hand, feeling too happy to speak. Christina, however, was relaxed, full of conversation, and she greeted him warmly with a kiss. Even later, when they were alone, she asked, "Did you recognize me, Ib?" He continued holding her hand and finally replied, "You’ve become quite the grand lady, Christina, and I’m just a humble working man. But I’ve often thought about you and the good old days." They wandered up the ridge together, looking down at the stream and the heath, where little hills were dressed in blooming gorse. Ib didn’t say much, but as time to part drew near, he felt it was clear that Christina should be his wife; hadn’t they even been called betrothed in childhood? To him, it felt as if they were truly engaged, even without a word being spoken. Their time together was short, as Christina had to return that evening to prepare for the next morning’s journey back to Herning. Ib and her father accompanied her to the village. It was a beautiful, moonlit evening, and as they arrived, Ib continued to hold Christina's hand as if he didn’t want to let go. His eyes sparkled, and his words stumbled out with hesitance from the depths of his heart, "Christina, if you haven’t become too grand, and if you can be happy to live in my mother’s house as my wife, we can get married someday. But we can wait a little while."

"Oh yes," she replied; "Let’s wait a little longer, Ib. I trust you because I think I love you. But let me think it over." Then he kissed her, and that’s how they parted.

title: IB AND LITTLE CHRISTINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
On the way home, Ib told the boatman that he and Christina were practically engaged, and the boatman admitted he had always suspected it would come to this. That evening, the boatman stayed over at Ib’s farmhouse, but no more was said about the engagement. Over the next year, two letters traveled between Ib and Christina, signed "Faithful till death." But after that year, one day, the boatman came to see Ib with a friendly greeting from Christina. But he hesitated as he carried another message, and finally confessed that Christina, now a lovely young woman, had become quite sought after. Many admired her, but her master’s son, who had been home for a visit, wished to marry her. He had a good job in Copenhagen, and since Christina liked him back, his parents were willing to support the match. However, Christina often thought of Ib and knew how much he cared for her, so she felt inclined to turn down this fortunate opportunity, added the boatman. At first, Ib remained silent but turned pale and shook his head gently before declaring, "Christina must not refuse this good fortune."

"Would you write to her then?" the boatman asked.

Ib sat down to write but found it difficult to express what was in his heart. He tore up the page in frustration. The next morning, however, he managed to draft a letter and wrote:

"I read the letter you sent to your father and from it, I see you’re thriving, with better fortunes yet to come. Think carefully about what lies ahead if you choose me as your husband, for I have very little to offer. Do not think of my situation; focus only on your own happiness. You owe me no promises; if you feel bound, I release you from any vow. I wish you every joy and blessing, Christina. Heaven will give me my heart's comfort."

title: IB AND LITTLE CHRISTINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
This letter reached Christina in time. By November, her banns were called in the church on the heath and in Copenhagen, where her groom lived. Her future mother-in-law took her shopping in Copenhagen, as the groom could not make the long trip himself. On the way, Christina saw her father in a village, and they exchanged farewells. Not much was said to Ib about this, and he remained silent, though his mother noticed he had grown more withdrawn and thoughtful. As he reminisced about the past, it was no wonder that he recalled the three nuts the gypsy woman had given him when he was a child, especially the two he had given to Christina. These wishing nuts had turned out to be true fortune-tellers. One contained a gilded carriage and noble steeds; the other, beautiful clothing, which Christina would soon have in her new home in Copenhagen. Her part had come true, and for him, the nut contained nothing but empty soil. The gypsy woman claimed it was the best for him. Perhaps it was, and he now understood her words. The black earth—the dark grave—was the best thing for him now.

More years went by; not many, but they felt long for Ib. The old innkeeper and his wife passed away one after another, leaving their entire fortune, many thousands of dollars, to their son. Christina could now have that golden carriage and a wardrobe full of fine clothes. During the two long years that followed, there was no letter from Christina to her father; and when they finally received one, it didn’t mention happiness or success. Poor Christina! Neither she nor her husband had learned to manage their wealth or save, so the riches brought no peace because they hadn’t sought it.

title: IB AND LITTLE CHRISTINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Seasons shifted; the heath bloomed in summer and lay blanketed in snow during winter, the rough winds sweeping across the ridge where Ib’s cozy home stood. One spring day, as the sun beamed down brightly, he was plowing his fields when the plow scraped against something hard. He thought he’d struck a firestone, but soon spotted a gleaming piece of metal glimmering in the furrow. He dug a little more and uncovered a large, intricately designed golden armlet, only to realize he had stumbled upon a treasure from a burial site. He kept digging and found even more valuable items, which he presented to the local clergyman, who explained their worth. Next, he visited the magistrate, who notified the museum director about the discovery and suggested that Ib present the treasures himself.

"You’ve found the best treasure in the earth," the magistrate told him.

"The best treasure?" Ib thought; "What could be better than this—found in the earth? Well, if that’s the case, then the gypsy woman was right about her prophecy."

So, Ib boarded a ferry from Aarhus to Copenhagen. To someone who had only sailed the nearby river a couple of times, this felt like an ocean adventure. Eventually, he arrived in Copenhagen, where he received a substantial amount of money—for the gold was worth six hundred dollars. With his newfound wealth, Ib began to explore the bustling city.

The night before his scheduled return with the ferry captain, Ib lost his way in the streets and took a wrong turn. He wandered until he found himself in a poor neighborhood in Christian’s Haven, where no one was around. After a while, a little girl emerged from one of the shabby homes, and Ib asked her for directions. She looked up at him shyly and began to cry. Concerned, he followed her as she explained her troubles. Under a streetlamp, her face glowed in the light. In that moment, a strange sensation washed over Ib; it was like seeing Little Christina again from their childhood. He followed the girl up the rickety stairs of a shabby building, leading him to a small attic room. The air was thick and heavy; no light shone inside, but from a corner came sounds of soft moans. It was a woman on a wretched bed.

title: IB AND LITTLE CHRISTINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Can I help you?" he asked. "This little girl brought me here, but I’m lost in this city. Is there anyone nearby whom I can call for help?"

Then he gently lifted the head of the sick woman, smoothing her pillow. He gasped as he did so; it was Christina from the heath! No one had mentioned her name to Ib in years; bringing it up would have troubled him, especially since the news about her life hadn’t been good. It turned out the wealth her husband inherited made him arrogant and he ended up giving up his steady job, traveling abroad for six months, and upon returning, he lived extravagantly, racking up enormous debts. At first, he teetered on the high pedestal he had created for himself, but eventually it all came crashing down. One morning, his lifeless body was found in the canal. The cold hand of death had reached Christina before him. Her youngest child, longed for in the lap of luxury, had died just weeks after being born, leaving Christina alone and sickly. In her misery, she lay abandoned in this sad little room, surrounded by poverty. Her oldest child, also named Little Christina, was the girl who led Ib to this place where they struggled to survive.

"It breaks my heart to think that I’ll die and leave this poor child behind," she sighed. "What will become of her?" She couldn’t say anymore.

Ib struck another match and lit a candle hastily found in the room, casting a feeble light around the dismal space. He gazed at the little girl and thought about Christina in her youth. Could he find it in his heart to love this child who was a stranger to him? Just then, the sick woman slowly opened her eyes and stared at him. Did she recognize him? He never found out, for no other words came from her lips.

In the forest by the Gudenau river, not far from the heath and beneath the ridge, the little farmhouse stood newly painted and whitewashed. The air was dense and dim; the heath was bare of blossoms; autumn winds swirled yellowed leaves towards the boatman's hut, which housed strangers. The little farmhouse, however, stood safe and sheltered beneath tall trees and the high ridge. A hearty fire burned on the hearth, and within, light sparkled from the bright eyes of a child; sweet, birdlike sounds filled the room from her rosy lips, like the cheerful song of a lark in spring. Everything radiated life and happiness. Little Christina sat on Ib’s knee. To her, Ib was both father and mother, as her own parents had faded from her memory like a dream. Ib's home was neat and well-furnished; he had become prosperous. The once sorrowful tale of Christina lay buried in the churchyard of Copenhagen, where she died in poverty. Now, Ib possessed money—wealth that emerged from the dark earth, and he had finally claimed Christina as his own.

title: IN A THOUSAND YEARS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Yes, in a thousand years, people will soar through the air on steam-powered wings, gliding over the ocean! The young folks from America will travel to the historic shores of Europe. They'll come to marvel at the monuments and grand cities, which will by then be in ruins, just as we today visit the crumbling glory of Southern Asia. In a thousand years, they'll be here!

The Thames, the Danube, and the Rhine continue their journeys, Mont Blanc stands tall with its snow-covered peak, and the Northern Lights dance across the northern lands; but generation after generation passes into dust, and many of the once-mighty are forgotten, like those resting under the hill where a wealthy trader has built a bench to overlook his swaying cornfields.

"To Europe!" shout the young Americans; "to the land of our ancestors, the magnificent land of monuments and dreams—Europe!"

The airship arrives, filled to the brim with eager passengers because it’s a faster journey than by sea. An undersea electro-magnetic wire has already sent the news of the aerial adventurers. Europe is on the horizon. They spot the coast of Ireland, but the passengers remain sound asleep; they won't be woken until they are directly over England. That’s where they’ll first set foot on European ground, in the land of Shakespeare, as the educated call it; or the land of politics and machinery, as others refer to it.

They stay here for an entire day—it's all the busy travelers can spare for England and Scotland combined. Then it's onward, through the underwater tunnel to France, the land of Charlemagne and Napoleon. Names like Molière are mentioned, and scholars chat about the ancient classics. There's excitement and chatter about heroes, poets, and scientists who, right now, are unknown but will emerge after our time in Paris, the heart of Europe, and other places.

The air steamboat soars over the lands where Columbus once set sail, where Cortez was born, and where Calderón wrote his famous plays in beautiful verse. Lovely, dark-eyed women still inhabit the lush valleys, and the oldest tales tell of the Cid and the Alhambra.

title: IN A THOUSAND YEARS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Then it’s on to Italy, where the remnants of the ancient, eternal Rome lie. It’s gone! The Campagna now stretches desolate. A single crumbling wall is all that’s left of St. Peter’s, but some doubt whether this ruin is authentic.

Next, they head to Greece for a night’s rest at a grand hotel atop Mount Olympus, just to say they’ve been there; the journey continues to the Bosphorus, where they pause for a few hours to see where Byzantium once stood; legends speak of the harem from the days of the Turks, and now, fishermen are casting their nets.

Over the remains of great cities along the broad Danube, cities we don’t even know in our time, the travelers glide by; yet, occasionally, the caravan lands on the fertile grounds of those that time will one day reveal.

Below lies Germany, once crisscrossed by a vast web of railways and canals—a place where Luther spoke, where Goethe composed, and where Mozart once conducted the symphony of harmony. Remarkable names shine here in science and art, names we don’t yet recognize. They dedicate one day to explore Germany, and another for the North, the homeland of Oersted and Linnaeus, along with Norway, the realm of ancient heroes and modern Normans. Iceland is a stop on the way home. The geysers no longer erupt, Hecla is an extinct volcano, but the rocky isle remains in the midst of the swirling sea, a lasting reminder of legend and poetry.

"There’s so much to see in Europe," says the young American, "and we’ve covered it all in just one week, just like the great traveler says in his famous book, 'How to See All Europe in a Week.'"

title: IN THE NURSERY
