author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Everyone—Dad, Mom, brothers, and sisters—went out to the play, leaving just little Anna and her grandpa at home.

"Let's put on a play too," he suggested. "And we can start right away!"

"But we don’t have a stage," cried little Anna. "And there’s no one to perform; my old doll can’t because she looks terrible, and my new doll can’t because I don’t want to wrinkle her new clothes."

"You can always find actors if you use what you have," grandpa replied.

"Now, let’s set up the theater. We can stack one book here, another there, and a third one in a sloping row. Then three on the other side—that’s our side scenes! The old box over there can be the back stairs, and we can lay the flooring on top of it. The stage represents a room, as you can see. Now we just need the actors. Let’s see what we have in the toy box. First, the characters, and then we’ll get the play ready. One by one; this will be fantastic! Here’s a pipe head, and over there’s an odd glove; they’ll work perfectly for a father and daughter."

"But that’s only two characters," little Anna said. "What about my brother’s old waistcoat? Can’t that be in our play too?"

"It’s definitely big enough," grandpa replied. "It can be the lover. There’s nothing in the pockets, which makes it interesting—it’s part of an unfortunate story. And here are the nutcracker’s boots, complete with spurs. Look at them stomp and strut! They will represent the unwelcome suitor the lady doesn’t like. What kind of play do you want? A tragedy or a domestic drama?"

"A domestic drama, please," little Anna said, "because that’s what everyone else enjoys. Do you know one?"

title: IN THE NURSERY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I know hundreds," grandpa said. "The favorites are mostly French, but they’re not suitable for little girls. In the meantime, we could pick one of the prettiest, as they’re all quite similar. Now I’ll shake the pen! Cock-a-doodle-doo! Here’s the play—sparkling new! Now, listen to the playbill."

Grandpa grabbed a newspaper and read as if he were reading from it:

"And now we’re ready to begin. The curtain rises. We don’t have a curtain, so it’s already up! All the characters are here, so we’re good to go. Now I’ll speak as Papa Pipe-head! He’s upset today—one can tell he’s a colorful meerschaum.

"'Snik, snak, snurre, bassellurre! I’m the master of this house! I’m the father of my daughter! Will you hear what I have to say? Mr. de Boots is someone in whom one can see their reflection; his top half is morocco leather, and he’s got spurs to boot. Snikke, snakke, snak! He shall marry my daughter!"

"Now, listen to what the Waistcoat says, little Anna," grandpa said. "Now it’s the Waistcoat’s turn to speak. The Waistcoat has a laid-back collar and is very modest, but he knows his worth and has every right to say what he says:

"'I don’t have a spot on me! Good material should be appreciated. I’m real silk, and I have strings too.'"

"'On the wedding day, but not anymore; you can’t keep your color in the wash.' Now it’s Mr. Pipe-head speaking. 'Mr. de Boots is water-resistant, made of strong leather, yet very delicate; he can creak and clank with his spurs and has an Italian look-'"

"But they should speak in verses," Anna said, "because I’ve heard that’s the most beautiful way to do it."

title: IN THE NURSERY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"They can do that too," Grandpa replied, "and if the audience demands it, they’ll speak that way. Just look at Miss Glove; see how she’s pointing her fingers!"

"'If I could just have my love, Who would be happier than Glove? Ah! If I must be apart from him, I know it will break my heart!' 'Bah!'"

The final word was spoken by Mr. Pipe-head, and now it’s Mr. Waistcoat’s turn:

"'O Glove, my dear, Though it costs you a tear, You must be mine, For Holger Danske has sworn it!"

"Mr. de Boots, hearing this, stomps, jingles his spurs, and knocks down three of the side scenes."

"That’s wonderfully exciting!" little Anna exclaimed.

"Silence! Silence!" grandpa said. "Your quiet applause will show that you’re the sophisticated audience in the stalls. Now Miss Glove sings her big song with a dramatic flair:

"'I can’t see, oh dear! And so I’ll crow! Kikkeriki, in the grand hall!"

"Now comes the thrilling part, little Anna. This is the most important moment in the entire play. Mr. Waistcoat undoes himself and addresses his speech to you, so you can applaud; but just leave it alone—that’s considered more proper.

"'I’m driven to extremes! Take care of yourself! Now the plot unfolds! You are Pipe-head, and I am the noble head—snap! There you go!"

"Do you see this, little Anna?" grandpa asked. "That’s a truly charming comedy. Mr. Waistcoat has captured the old Pipe-head and stuffed him in his pocket; there he lies, and the Waistcoat says:

"'You’re in my pocket; you can’t come out until you promise to unite me with your daughter Glove on the left. I extend my right hand.'"

"That’s really lovely," little Anna said.

"'Although I’m all ears, I seem rather stupid: Where’s my humor? It’s gone, I fear, And I feel quite strange, you know. Oh! please let me out, And like a lamb led to slaughter, I’ll surely propose to my daughter.'"

"Is the play over already?" little Anna asked.

"Not at all," grandpa replied. "It’s just over for Mr. de Boots. Now the lovers kneel down, and one of them sings:

'Come, do what you should do— Bless your son and daughter.'

And they receive his blessing, celebrate their wedding, and all the furniture sings in chorus,

"'Klink! Clanks! A thousand thanks; And now the play is finished!"

"And now we’ll applaud," grandpa said. "Let’s call them all out, including the furniture, because it’s made of mahogany."

"Isn’t our play just as good as the ones in the real theater?"

"Our play is way better," grandpa said. "It’s shorter, the performers are natural, and it has made the time fly before tea!"

title: IN THE UTTERMOST PARTS OF THE SEA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: A few years ago, large ships set sail towards the North Pole to explore the remote coasts and find out how far people could venture into those uncharted territories. One of these ships had been making its way north for over a year, battling snow and ice, and the sailors had gone through many tough times. Finally, winter arrived, and the sun vanished completely; for weeks, it was nothing but constant night. All around them, as far as they could see, were endless fields of ice, and the ship was stuck fast in the frozen expanse. The snow piled high around them, and the sailors used it to build huts shaped like bee hives. Some were big enough to hold a whole group, while others only had space for three or four men. It wasn’t entirely dark, though; the northern lights blazed in red and blue flames, looking like a never-ending fireworks display, and the snow sparkled, reflecting the light, giving the night an eternal twilight glow. When the moon shone the brightest, the locals came to see the sailors, dressed in their rough, furry outfits and riding sledges over the ice. They brought plenty of furs and skins, so the snow-huts soon had warm carpets, and the sailors used the furs to wrap themselves in while they slept beneath their snow roofs—outside, the cold was much harsher than any winter they knew back home. While it was late autumn back in their country, they often thought of it in their distant exile, picturing the yellow leaves on the trees. Their watches still showed evening time, a signal for sleep, but here in this land, it was always night.

title: IN THE UTTERMOST PARTS OF THE SEA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: In one of the huts, two of the men settled down to sleep. The younger one had brought with him from home his most cherished possession—a Bible that his grandmother had given him when he left. Every night, he placed the sacred book under his head, and he had learned its verses by heart since childhood. Each day, he read from it, and lying on his cold bed, the holy words would come to him: "If I take the wings of the morning and fly to the uttermost parts of the sea, even there You are with me, and Your right hand will uphold me." Inspired by that faith, sleep embraced him, bringing dreams that felt like messages from God. He sensed a vibrancy within him, as if he could hear familiar melodies, as if the warm summer breezes were wrapping around him. A bright light shone above him, but it wasn’t just the reflection from the snow; it was the dazzling glow of a powerful angel whose radiant face he gazed upon. Like petals from a lily, the angel emerged from the pages of the Bible, and with a graceful sweep of its arm, the walls of the hut faded away like a delicate mist. Suddenly, green hills and meadows unfolded around him, bathed in the gentle light of a beautiful autumn day. The stork's nest was empty, yet ripe fruit still hung from the wild apple tree, even though the leaves had fallen. Bright red berries glimmered on the hedges, and the starling in the green cage outside the peasant’s hut, his true home, whistled a tune he had taught it. His grandmother had hung bird food around the cage, just as he used to do. The village blacksmith’s young, pretty daughter drew water at the well. She smiled at his grandmother, who nodded back and pointed to a letter that had arrived from far away. That very morning, the letter came from the chilly northern land where the one they missed was sleeping under God’s protective hand. They laughed and shed tears over the letter, and he, far away in the ice and snow, felt both joy and sorrow with them in spirit, for he could see and hear it all in his dream. From the letter, they read aloud the words of scripture: "In the uttermost parts of the sea, Your right hand shall uphold me." As the angel wrapped its wings around the sleeping man like a comforting veil, beautiful music filled the air along with a hymn. Then, the vision faded. It was dark once more inside the snow-hut, but the Bible remained beneath his head, and faith and hope continued to live in his heart. God was with him, even in the "uttermost parts of the sea."

title: JACK THE DULLARD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Deep in the countryside stood an ancient baronial hall, where an old proprietor lived with his two sons, both of whom thought they were incredibly clever. They were determined to woo the King's daughter, who had made it clear she would choose a husband based on how well he could express his words.

The two brothers spent an entire week preparing for their courtship—this was the maximum time allowed. But that was enough; they were well-informed already, and everyone knows how handy that can be. One brother could recite the entire Latin dictionary and three years' worth of the local newspaper, backwards or forwards, at will. The other had extensively studied corporate laws and knew exactly what every corporation needed to know, believing he could chat about state affairs and lend his opinions in council meetings. Plus, he was crafty and could skillfully embroider suspenders with roses and intricate designs.

"I will win the Princess!" both of them exclaimed. Their father gifted each of them a fine horse; the brother who could recite the dictionary had a black horse, while the one versed in corporate laws received a gleaming white steed. They even smeared fish oil on the corners of their mouths to make their speech smooth and slick. As they mounted their horses, all the servants gathered in the courtyard to watch, and just then, their third brother showed up. Although the proprietor had three sons, the third was often overlooked by his brothers because he wasn't as learned, and he was known as "Jack the Dullard."

"Hey!" Jack the Dullard called out. "Where are you two headed all dressed up in your Sunday best?"

"We’re off to the King’s court as suitors for the King’s daughter. Haven’t you heard the announcement that’s been broadcast all over the country?" They eagerly explained it to him.

"Oh wow! I want in too!" Jack the Dullard shouted. His brothers laughed at him and rode away.

"Dad," Jack pleaded, "I need a horse too. I really want to get married! If she accepts me, great; if not, I’ll make her accept me—I’ll claim her as mine!"

title: JACK THE DULLARD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Don’t be silly," the old man replied. "You can’t have a horse from me. You don’t know how to speak properly—you can’t arrange your words at all. Your brothers are nothing like you."

"Fine," Jack the Dullard said. "If I can’t have a horse, I’ll take my Billy-goat; he can carry me just fine!"

And just like that, he hopped on the Billy-goat, kicked it into gear, and galloped down the main street like a whirlwind.

"Yeehaw! That was a ride! Here I come!" Jack the Dullard shouted, singing joyfully as his voice echoed through the air.

Meanwhile, his brothers slowly rode ahead in silence, focused on the clever speeches they had planned, knowing they needed to be well-prepared.

"Hey! Check this out! Look what I found on the road!" Jack called, holding up a dead crow.

"Dullard!" his brothers exclaimed. "What are you going to do with that?"

"This crow? I’m going to give it to the Princess!"

"Sure, go ahead," they laughed, before continuing on.

"Hey everyone! I’ve got something else! You don’t find this on the road every day!"

His brothers turned around, curious about what he might have found this time.

"Dullard!" they cried. "That’s just an old wooden shoe, and it’s missing a part; are you planning to give that to the Princess too?"

"Of course I am!" Jack the Dullard replied, and once again his brothers laughed as they spurred their horses faster.

"Hey—hold on!" Jack shouted, catching up again. "It just keeps getting better! Hurrah! This is amazing!"

"What now?" his brothers asked, amused.

"Oh, you won’t believe it! I found something for the Princess!"

"Bah!" his brothers said dismissively. "That’s just clay from the ditch."

"Yes, but it’s the best kind!" Jack said. "Feel how wet it is—it just runs through my fingers!" And he stuffed his pockets with clay.

title: JACK THE DULLARD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
But his brothers raced ahead, sending sparks flying, and they arrived at the town gate a full hour before Jack could get there. At the gate, each suitor was assigned a number and lined up closely, so they couldn’t move their arms—this was a smart arrangement, as they would have surely started arguing otherwise.

A crowd gathered around the castle, eager to see the Princess greet the suitors. However, as each one stepped into the hall, they seemed to lose their ability to speak, almost like a candle extinguished. Then the Princess would declare, "He’s of no use! Out he goes!"

Finally, it was the turn of the brother who knew the dictionary by heart; but at that moment, he completely forgot everything. His footsteps echoed loudly, and when he looked up, the ceiling was a mirror, making it look like he was standing on his head. Three clerks and a head clerk were writing down every single word spoken, preparing it for the newspapers to be sold for a penny on the streets. The heat from the stove was overwhelming.

"It’s hot in here!" the first brother said.

"Yes," the Princess replied, "my father is roasting young chickens today."

"Baaa!" he stood there, speechless like a lamb. He hadn’t prepared for this kind of response and didn’t know what to say, though he meant to be witty. "Baaa!"

"He’s of no use!" the Princess said. "Out he goes!"

And so he had to leave. Then it was the second brother's turn.

"Yes, we’re roasting chickens today," the Princess said.

"Wha—what were y—you—wished to ob-" he stuttered, and all the clerks hastily wrote down, "wished to ob-"

"He’s of no use!" stated the Princess. "Out he goes!"

Then it was Jack the Dullard’s turn. He rode into the hall on his goat.

"Yes, it’s because I’m roasting young chickens," the Princess replied.

title: JACK THE DULLARD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Oh, that’s great!" Jack the Dullard exclaimed. "I assume you’ll let me roast my crow too?"

"Of course!" said the Princess. "But do you have anything to roast it in? I have neither pot nor pan."

"Absolutely!" Jack replied. "Look, here’s a cooking pot with a tin handle."

He pulled out the old wooden shoe and placed the crow inside.

"Well, that’s quite a dish!" the Princess said. "But how will we add sauce?"

"Oh, I’ve got that too!" said Jack. "I have so much of it, I can even spare some;" and he poured some of the clay from his pocket.

"I love that!" said the Princess. "You’ve shown you can respond and hold your own, so you shall be my husband. But do you realize every word we say is being recorded and will be printed in tomorrow’s paper? If you look over there, you can see three clerks and a head clerk at every window; the old head clerk is the worst because he doesn’t understand anything."

But she only said this to frighten Jack the Dullard; the clerks erupted in laughter, each dropping ink blots onto the floor.

"Oh, those are the ones?" Jack said, laughing. "In that case, I’ll give my best to the head clerk!" He emptied his pockets and tossed wet clay right in the head clerk's face.

"That was very clever!" said the Princess. "I couldn’t have done that; but I’ll learn in time."

So, Jack the Dullard became a king, received a crown and a wife, and sat upon a throne. But the story we’ve shared comes from the unreliable reports of the head clerk and the printing corporation.

title: LITTLE CLAUS AND BIG CLAUS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In a village, there were two men with the same name—Claus. One was known as "Great Claus" because he owned four horses, while the other, who had just one, was called "Little Claus." Let's find out what happened to them, as this story is true.

Throughout the week, Little Claus had to plow for Great Claus, using his single horse. But on Sundays, Great Claus would lend him all four of his horses. On that day, Little Claus would take the reins of all five horses, feeling incredibly proud as he cracked his whip and shouted, "Go on, my five horses!" The sun was shining bright, and church bells rang cheerfully while people made their way dressed in their finest clothes, prayer books in hand, heading to listen to the clergyman. They looked at Little Claus plowing with his five horses, and he couldn't help but feel proud, repeating, "Giddy up, my five horses!"

"You really shouldn't say that," warned Great Claus, "since only one of those horses belongs to you." However, Little Claus soon forgot this advice and would yell "Giddy up, my five horses!" whenever someone walked by.

"I'm telling you, you need to stop saying that!" exclaimed Great Claus. "If you don't, I'll hit your horse on the head, and it will drop dead right here!"

"I promise I won't say it again," Little Claus replied, but when he saw people passing by who greeted him with smiles and hellos, he couldn't help but feel so pleased at the thought of plowing with five horses that he shouted again, "Giddy up, all my horses!"

"I'll make your horses move for you," replied Great Claus, grabbing a hammer and striking Little Claus's horse on the head, causing it to drop dead instantly.

title: LITTLE CLAUS AND BIG CLAUS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "Now I don't have any horse left!" Little Claus cried, sobbing. However, after a bit, he took the dead horse's skin and hung it out to dry. Then, putting the dry skin into a bag, he slung it over his shoulder and set out to the nearby town to sell it. He had a long way to go and needed to pass through a dark, eerie forest. Suddenly, a storm broke out, and he lost his way. By the time he found the right path, it was already evening, and heading back home was now too far. Nearby, he spotted a large farmhouse with closed shutters, but lights flickered through the cracks. "Maybe they'll let me stay the night," thought Little Claus, so he walked up to the door and knocked. The farmer's wife answered, but when she heard his request, she turned him away, saying her husband wouldn't allow any strangers inside. "I guess I'll have to sleep outside then," he thought as she closed the door in his face. Close to the farmhouse was a big haystack, and between it and the house was a shed with a thatched roof. "I can sleep up there," Little Claus decided, noticing how comfortable the roof looked, "as long as that stork doesn’t come down and bite my legs," referring to the live stork perched on it. So, Little Claus climbed onto the shed’s roof, trying to get comfortable. As he turned, he noticed that the wooden shutters of the farmhouse didn’t reach the heights of the windows, allowing him to peek in. He saw a large table laden with wine, roast meat, and a gorgeous fish. The farmer's wife and the sexton were sitting at the table, enjoying their meal, with her filling his glass and helping him to plenty of fish, which he seemed to love. "If only I could get some of that," thought Little Claus, stretching his neck toward the window, where he spotted a beautiful pie—they were truly having a splendid feast.

title: LITTLE CLAUS AND BIG CLAUS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Just then, he heard someone riding down the road toward the farmhouse. It was the farmer returning home. He was a good man but had a strange aversion—he couldn't stand sextons. If he saw one, he'd fly into a rage. Because of this dislike, the sexton had taken the opportunity to visit the farmer's wife while the farmer was away, and she had treated him to the best food the house had. When the farmer's wife heard her husband coming, she got anxious and asked the sexton to hide in a large, empty chest in the room. He complied, knowing how furious the farmer would get at the sight of him. The wife quickly cleared away the wine and hid the rest of the delicious food in the oven, hoping her husband wouldn’t ask why it was all out.

"Oh, dear," sighed Little Claus from the top of the shed, seeing all the good food disappear.

"Is anyone up there?" the farmer called, spotting Little Claus. "Why are you up there? Come down and join me in the house!" So, Little Claus climbed down and explained to the farmer how he had lost his way and requested shelter for the night.

"Alright," the farmer said, "but first, we need something to eat."

The farmer's wife welcomed them happily, set the table, and served them both a bowl of porridge. The farmer was very hungry and devoured the porridge eagerly, while Little Claus couldn't stop thinking about the delicious roast meat, fish, and pies he knew were hidden in the oven. Under the table at his feet lay the sack containing the horse's skin, meant for selling at the next town. Little Claus didn’t enjoy the porridge at all, so he pressed his foot down on the sack beneath the table, and the dry skin let out a loud squeak. "Quiet!" he whispered to the sack, stepping on it again until it squeaked even louder.

"Hey! What’s in that sack?" the farmer asked.

"Oh, it’s a magician," replied Little Claus. "He says we don’t need to eat porridge because he has conjured up the oven full of roast meat, fish, and pie."

title: LITTLE CLAUS AND BIG CLAUS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Wow!" exclaimed the farmer, jumping up to open the oven door, thinking the delicious food was a result of the magician under the table. The wife stayed silent, quickly bringing everything out as they both dug into the fish, meat, and pastry.

Once again, Little Claus stepped on the sack, causing it to squeak. "What does he say now?" asked the farmer.

"He says, " replied Little Claus, "that there are three bottles of wine standing in the corner by the oven for us."

So the farmer's wife was forced to bring out the hidden wine, and the farmer drank until he felt quite merry. He fancied such a magician like Little Claus had a real treasure. "Can your magician summon up the devil?" he inquired. "I’d like to see him now while I'm in such good spirits."

"Oh, absolutely!" little Claus answered. "My magician can do anything I ask—right?" he queried, stepping on the sack again to make it squeak. "See, he’s saying 'Yes,' but he’s worried we won’t like how he looks."

"I’m not scared, but what does he look like?"

"Ha!" the farmer said, "then he’s definitely ugly. You know I can't stand sextons. That doesn’t concern me, though—I'll know who it is, and it won’t bother me. Now I’m feeling brave, but just don’t let him get too close!"

"Wait, I need to ask the magician," said Little Claus, so he stepped on the bag and leaned in to listen.

"He says you need to go open that large chest over there in the corner, and you’ll find the devil hiding inside; but you have to hold the lid tight so he doesn't escape!"

"Will you help me hold it?" the farmer asked, heading toward the chest where his wife had hidden the scared sexton. The farmer opened the lid slightly and peered in.

title: LITTLE CLAUS AND BIG CLAUS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Oh!" he exclaimed, jumping back in shock. "I saw him, and he looks just like our sexton! How terrifying!" After that, he felt the need to have another drink, and they sat and drank well into the night.

"You should sell me your magician," the farmer proposed. "Name your price; I’ll pay whatever you want! I would even give you a whole bushel of gold right now!"

"No way, I can’t!" replied Little Claus. "Just think about how much profit I could make with this magician!"

"But I really want him," the farmer said, continuing to persuade.

"Okay," said Little Claus finally, "since you so kindly offered me a night's lodging, I won't turn you down. You can have the magician for a bushel of money, but make sure it’s full measure!"

"You got it!" the farmer agreed, "but you must take the chest as well. I really don’t want it in the house another minute; who knows if he might still be inside!"

So, Little Claus traded the farmer the sack with the dried horse skin and in return got a full bushel of money. The farmer even gave him a wheelbarrow to carry away the chest and the gold.

"Goodbye!" said Little Claus, leaving with his fortune and the large chest concealing the sexton. At the edge of the forest, he reached a wide, deep river. The water flowed so quickly that few could swim against the current. Recently, a new bridge was built across it. In the middle of the bridge, Little Claus paused, saying loudly enough for the sexton to hear, "What should I do with this heavy chest? It's as if it's filled with stones! I’ll be exhausted if I roll it any further; maybe I should just throw it into the river. If it floats over to my place, great! If not, it won't matter much."

He picked up the chest, lifted it slightly, as if to toss it into the water.

title: LITTLE CLAUS AND BIG CLAUS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"No, don’t throw it!" cried the sexton from inside the chest. "Let me out first!"

"Oh!" exclaimed Little Claus, pretending to be scared. "He's still in there? I have to toss him into the river to make sure he drowns!"

"Oh no, please don’t!" begged the sexton, "I'll give you a whole bushel of gold if you just let me out!"

"Now that changes things," said Little Claus, opening the chest. The sexton crawled out, pushed the empty chest into the water, and returned to his house, where he measured out a full bushel of gold for Little Claus, who now had a cart full.

"I've been paid handsomely for my horse," he thought to himself as he got home, entered his room, and dumped all his gold onto the floor. "Great Claus will be furious to learn how wealthy I've become thanks to my single horse—but I won’t tell him exactly how it all played out." So he sent a boy to Great Claus to borrow a bushel measure.

"What could he want that for?" Great Claus wondered and decided to smear the bottom of the measure with tar so that some of whatever went into it would stick. When the measure came back, three new silver florins were stuck to it.

"What’s this?" Great Claus exclaimed, hurrying over to Little Claus to ask, "Where did you get all this money?"

"Oh, it was from my horse's skin, which I sold yesterday."

"Well, you got a good price then," said Great Claus. In a hurry, he dashed home, grabbed a hatchet, and struck all four of his horses on the head, skinned them, and headed to the town to sell them. "Skins, skins! Who will buy skins?" he shouted through the streets. All the shoemakers and tanners rushed to him, asking how much he wanted.

"A bushel of money for each one," Great Claus answered.

"Are you crazy?" they all shouted, "Do you think we have money to spare by the bushel?"

title: LITTLE CLAUS AND BIG CLAUS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Skins, skins!" he shouted again, "Who will buy skins?" But to all inquiries, his answer was, "A bushel of money."

"He's making fools of us," they all said, and the shoemakers grabbed their straps while the tanners picked up their leather aprons and started to beat Great Claus.

"Skins, skins!" they mocked him. "Yeah, we’ll mark your skin till it’s black and blue!"

"Get out of town!" they yelled. Great Claus, for the first time, had to run away as fast as he could, completely humiliated.

"Soon," he muttered as he reached home, "Little Claus will pay for this; I'll beat him to a pulp!"

Meanwhile, Little Claus's elderly grandmother passed away. She had always been nasty and unkind to him, but he felt sad and placed her in his warm bed, hoping to revive her. He decided she would stay there all night while he sat in a chair, as he often did. During the night, as he sat there, the door opened, and in walked Great Claus with his hatchet. He knew exactly where Little Claus's bed was and went right to it, striking the old grandmother's head, thinking it was Little Claus.

"There!" he exclaimed, "Now you'll never trick me again!" Then he went home.

"That man's wicked," thought Little Claus. "He meant to kill me. Thank goodness my grandmother was already dead, or she'd have lost her life!" He dressed her in her best clothes, borrowed a horse from his neighbor, and hitched it to a cart. He laid the old woman on the backseat, so she wouldn’t fall out as he drove, and headed through the woods. By morning, they reached a large inn where Little Claus stopped to get something to eat. The landlord was a wealthy man—and kind too—though being rather short-tempered.

"Good morning," he greeted Little Claus. "You’re up early today."

title: LITTLE CLAUS AND BIG CLAUS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Yes," replied Little Claus. "I’m taking my grandmother to town; she's in the back of the wagon, but I can’t bring her inside. Could you please take her a glass of mead? You’ll need to speak up, though; she doesn’t hear very well."

"Sure, I’ll do that," the landlord said, pouring a glass of mead and carrying it out to the dead grandmother, who sat upright in the cart. "Here’s a glass of mead from your grandson!" he announced, but the dead woman said nothing, merely sitting there. "Can’t you hear me?" the landlord shouted as loud as he could. "Here’s a glass of mead from your grandson!"

He shouted again and again, but when she didn’t respond, he lost his temper and threw the glass of mead in her face. It hit her on the nose, causing her to fall backwards out of the cart, as she'd only been sitting there and wasn’t tied in.

"Hey!" Little Claus yelled, rushing out of the inn and grabbing the landlord by the throat. "You’ve killed my grandmother! Look, there’s a huge hole in her forehead!"

"Oh, how unfortunate!" the landlord lamented as he wrung his hands. "This all stems from my fiery temper. Dear Little Claus, I'll give you a bushel of money; I’ll bury your grandmother as if she were my own—just keep quiet! If not, they'll behead me, and that would be troublesome!"

So, in the end, Little Claus received another bushel of money, and the landlord buried his grandmother as if she were his own. When Little Claus got home, he sent a boy to Great Claus again, asking to borrow a bushel measure. "What’s going on?" wondered Great Claus. "I thought I’d killed him; I need to see for myself." He headed over to Little Claus with the bushel measure in hand. "How did you come into all this money?" he asked, eyes wide, staring at Little Claus's treasures.

title: LITTLE CLAUS AND BIG CLAUS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"You killed my grandmother instead of me," Little Claus said. "So I sold her for a bushel of money."

"Well, that's a fair price at least!" said Great Claus. So he returned home, grabbed his hatchet, and killed his own grandmother in one fell swoop. Then he put her in a cart and drove into town to see if the apothecary would buy a dead body.

"Whose is it, and where did you get it?" asked the apothecary.

"It’s my grandmother!" he replied. "I killed her just to get a bushel of money for her."

"Heaven have mercy!" the apothecary gasped. "You’ve lost your mind! Don’t say things like that; or you will lose your head!" He then seriously explained how wicked that deed was and that such a man would surely be punished. Great Claus got spooked, bolted out of the shop, hopped into his cart, and raced home, terrified. The apothecary and all the townspeople thought he was mad and let him go wherever he pleased.

"You’ll pay for this," Great Claus vowed as soon as he hit the highway, addressing Little Claus. He quickly grabbed the largest sack he could find and marched over to Little Claus's house. "You’ve pulled another fast one on me," he said angrily. "First, I killed my horses, then my old grandmother. It’s all your fault! But I won't let you trick me again." With that, he caught Little Claus and shoved him into the sack, hoisting it over his shoulder. "Now I'm going to drown you in the river!"

It was a long walk to the river, and Little Claus wasn’t exactly light to carry. The road passed by the church, where the organ was playing, and the people inside were singing beautifully. Great Claus set the sack down near the church entrance, figuring he might catch a psalm before proceeding. Little Claus, unable to escape the sack, couldn’t see that everyone else was inside the church.

title: LITTLE CLAUS AND BIG CLAUS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Oh dear," sighed Little Claus from inside the sack, twisting and turning, but he couldn't loosen the string holding it shut. Just then, a gray-haired cattle driver passed by with a large staff, herding a flock of cows and oxen. They stumbled against the sack containing Little Claus and tipped it over. "Oh dear," Little Claus mused, "I’m still young and headed to heaven soon."

"And I, poor fellow," said the drover, "who am already old, can’t get there."

"Open the sack," shouted Little Claus. "Crawl in instead, and you’ll arrive there soon!"

"With pleasure!" replied the drover as he opened the sack, allowing Little Claus to spring out as quickly as he could. "Will you take care of my cattle?" asked the drover as he climbed inside the bag.

"Sure!" Little Claus replied, promptly tying the sack shut and then driving off with all the cows and oxen.

When Great Claus emerged from the church, he picked up the sack and threw it over his shoulders. It felt lighter than before, as the old drover was much less heavy than Little Claus.

"How light this feels now," he noted. "Ah, must be from going to church!" So he walked on to the river, which was deep and wide, and tossed the sack containing the old drover into the water, believing it to be Little Claus. "Now you can stay there!" he declared. Then he started back home, but when he reached a place where two roads crossed, there was Little Claus driving the cattle.

"What’s going on?" Great Claus asked. "Didn’t I just drown you?"

"Yes," Little Claus answered, "you threw me into the river about half an hour ago."

"But where did you get all these fine animals?" asked Great Claus.

title: LITTLE CLAUS AND BIG CLAUS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"These are sea cattle," explained Little Claus. "Let me tell you the whole story, and thank you for throwing me into the river; I've ended up above you in wealth! I was pretty scared while I was tied up in the sack, but when the wind whistled in my ears as you tossed me over the bridge, I sank to the bottom. Luckily, I landed on soft grass down there, and then the sack opened up. A beautiful maiden in white robes, with a crown of green leaves on her hair, came up to me and said, 'Welcome back, Little Claus! Here's your first herd of cattle. A little farther down the river, you’ll find another one.' I realized that the river was a massive highway for the sea folks who were frequently walking and herding between the sea and the land. The riverbed was filled with the loveliest flowers and fresh grass. Fish flowed past me like birds in the air, and the people were strikingly gorgeous, with marvelous cattle grazing in the hills and valleys!"

"But why did you come up if it was all so wonderful down there? I would have stayed!" Great Claus asked.

"That's good thinking on my part; you see, the sea maiden told me about the other cattle a mile further on, but she meant the river as the road since she can’t go any other way. I was aware of the river’s twists and turns, sometimes right and sometimes left. It seemed too far, so I figured taking the land route would save me half a mile and let me gather my cattle quicker!"

"Lucky you!" Great Claus exclaimed. "Do you think I'd find sea cattle if I went down the river?"

"Yes, absolutely!" replied Little Claus, "but you’re too heavy for me to carry in a sack. If you make it down there first, then get into a sack, I’ll gladly toss you in!"

"Thanks!" Great Claus replied. "But keep in mind—if I'm unable to land any sea cattle, I’ll come back and give you a good beating!"

"Now, now, no need to be fierce!" Little Claus said as they strolled toward the river. As they approached, the cattle, feeling thirsty, rushed over to drink from the stream.

"Look how eager they are," Little Claus commented. "They can’t wait to get back in!"

"Come on, help me, hurry up," urged Great Claus, "or you'll be in trouble!" He crawled into a large sack that was lying across one of the oxen.

"Put in a stone, or I won’t sink!" he hollered.

"Oh, I doubt that’s an issue," Little Claus replied but still added a good-sized stone before tying the bag tightly and giving it a shove.

"Splash!" Great Claus fell into the river and immediately sank down.

"I hope he doesn’t find any cattle," Little Claus thought, and then he walked home with his own animals.

title: LITTLE IDA'S FLOWERS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Oh no, my poor flowers are totally dead!" little Ida exclaimed. "They looked so beautiful yesterday evening, and now their leaves are all drooping and dried up. Why does this happen?" She turned to the student sitting on the sofa, whom she really liked because he told the best, most entertaining stories and made the prettiest cut-out pictures of hearts, dancing ladies, castles with opening doors, and, of course, flowers. "Why do my flowers look so faded today?" she asked again, pointing to her nosegay, which had completely withered.

"Don't you know what's wrong?" the student replied. "The flowers went to a ball last night, so it’s no surprise they’re hanging their heads today."

"But flowers can’t dance!" little Ida protested.

"Oh yes, they absolutely can," the student answered. "When it gets dark and everyone is asleep, they dance around joyfully. They have a ball almost every night!"

"Exactly," the student continued, "like little daisies and lily-of-the-valleys."

"Where do these beautiful flowers have their dances?" little Ida asked.

"Haven't you seen the big castle outside the city gates where the king spends his summers? The beautiful garden there is full of flowers. When the king and all his court go to town, the flowers run from the garden into the castle, and you should see how much fun they have! The most beautiful roses sit on the throne, playing king and queen, while the red cockscombs stand bowing as the lords-in-waiting. Then all the pretty flowers join in for a grand ball, where blue violets play the young cadets and dance with hyacinths and crocuses, whom they call the young ladies. The tulips and tiger lilies act as the old ladies who watch the dance, making sure everything is in order."

"But," little Ida said, "is there anyone who would hurt the flowers for dancing in the king’s castle?"

"No one knows about it," the student replied. "The old steward who keeps watch at night sometimes comes in, but he carries a big bunch of keys. Whenever the flowers hear the keys rattling, they scamper and hide behind the long curtains, standing completely still while peeking their heads out. The old steward sniffs and says, 'I smell flowers here,' but he can never see them."

title: LITTLE IDA'S FLOWERS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Oh, how wonderful!" little Ida said, clapping her hands. "Will I be able to see these flowers?"

"Yes," said the student. "Just remember to think about it next time you’re outside. I’m sure you’ll see them if you peek through the window. I did that today and saw a long yellow lily lying on the sofa. She was a court lady."

"Can the flowers from the Botanical Gardens go to these balls?" Ida asked. "It’s such a long way!"

"Oh yes," said the student, "whenever they wish! They can actually fly. Have you noticed those lovely red, white, and yellow butterflies that look like flowers? They were flowers once! They’ve fluttered off their stems, flapping their leaves like little wings to fly. If they behave themselves, they get the chance to fly around during the day instead of just sitting on their stems at home. Over time, their leaves become real wings. But the flowers in the Botanical Gardens might have never been to the king's castle, so they have no idea about the fun that happens there at night. I have an idea! Next time you go to the botanical professor's garden nearby, tell one of the flowers that there's a big ball at the castle. That flower will spread the word to the others, and they’ll fly to the castle right away. When the professor goes into his garden, he won’t find a single flower left. He’ll be so puzzled about where they all went!"

"But how can one flower tell another? Flowers can’t talk!"

"No, definitely not," the student answered. "But they can communicate through gestures. Haven't you noticed how they nod and rustle their green leaves when the wind blows?"

"Can the professor understand what they’re saying?" Ida asked.

"Of course he can!" the student replied. "One morning, he walked into his garden and saw a stinging nettle making signs with its leaves to a beautiful red carnation. It was saying, 'You’re so pretty, I really like you.' But the professor thought that was silly nonsense, so he clapped his hands on the nettle to stop it. That’s when the nettle stung him with its leaves, and he learned to never touch a nettle again."

title: LITTLE IDA'S FLOWERS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Oh, how funny!" Ida said, laughing.

"How can anyone fill a child’s head with such ideas?" grumbled a cranky lawyer who had come to visit and was sitting on the sofa. He didn’t like the student and scowled whenever he saw him making silly pictures. Sometimes, the pictures would be of a man hanging on a gallows holding a heart, as if he had stolen it, or an old witch riding a broom with her husband perched on her nose. But the lawyer didn’t appreciate such humor and would mumble to himself, "How can anyone put such nonsense into a child's head? What silly ideas!"

title: LITTLE IDA'S FLOWERS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: But to little Ida, all the stories the student told about the flowers felt quite funny, and she thought about them a lot. The flowers hung their heads because they had spent the whole night dancing and were now tired and probably feeling unwell. So she took them into the room with her toys on a lovely little table where the drawers were full of beautiful items. Her doll, Sophy, was sleeping in the doll’s bed, and little Ida said to her, "You need to get up, Sophy! You have to sleep in the drawer tonight because the poor flowers are sick and need your bed. Maybe that will help them get better." She pulled out the doll, who looked quite grumpy and didn’t say anything because she was annoyed at being kicked out of her bed. Ida carefully placed the flowers in the doll's bed and tucked them in with the quilt. She told them to stay still and behave while she made some tea for them to help them feel better and get up in the morning. Then she closed the curtains around the little bed so the sunlight wouldn't shine in their eyes. Throughout the evening, she couldn’t stop thinking about what the student had said. Before she went to bed herself, she had to sneak a peek behind the curtains at the garden where her mother’s beautiful flowers grew—hyacinths, tulips, and many more. She whispered softly to them, "I know you’re going to a ball tonight." But the flowers didn’t seem to understand, and not a single leaf moved; yet Ida felt absolutely sure she knew what was happening. She lay awake for a long time, dreaming about how lovely it would be to see the beautiful flowers dancing in the king's garden. "I wonder if my flowers have been there," she thought to herself before falling asleep.

title: LITTLE IDA'S FLOWERS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: In the night, she woke up after dreaming about the flowers, the student, and the grumpy lawyer who never missed a chance to complain. It was quiet in Ida's room; the night lamp was glowing on the table while her mom and dad were fast asleep. "I wonder if my flowers are still in Sophy's bed," she thought. "How much I’d like to know!" She sat up a little and glanced at the door leading to her playroom filled with flowers and toys; it stood slightly open, and she could hear someone inside playing the piano. It sounded softer and more beautiful than she had ever heard before. "Now all the flowers must be dancing in there," she thought. "Oh, how I wish I could see them!" But she didn't want to move and wake her parents. "If only they would come in here," she wished, yet they didn’t. The music continued to play beautifully, more enchanting than anything, and she couldn’t restrain herself. She tiptoed out of bed, crept quietly to the door, and peeked into the room. Oh, what a glorious sight it was! There was no night lamp lit, but the moonlight poured through the window, making it feel almost like daytime. All the hyacinths and tulips were lined up in two long rows across the floor; not a single flower remained in the window, and the flower pots were all empty. The flowers danced gracefully on the floor, turning and holding each other’s long green leaves as they spun around. Sitting at the piano was a large yellow lily, and little Ida was convinced she had seen her in the summer before. The student had said she resembled Miss Lina, one of Ida's friends, and though they had all laughed at that, now it truly seemed to Ida like the tall yellow flower was just like the young lady. She moved beautifully while playing, swaying her long yellow head from side to side, nodding in sync with the lovely music. Then, a large purple crocus bounded onto the table where the playthings were, went up to the doll's bed, and pulled back the curtains. There were the sick flowers, but they got up immediately and nodded to the others, indicating they wanted to dance with them. The old, rough doll with the broken mouth stood up and curtsied to the pretty flowers. They didn’t look sick at all anymore; they were lively and cheerful, yet none noticed little Ida. Suddenly, it appeared something had dropped from the table. Ida turned her head and saw a small carnival rod bouncing among the flowers as if it belonged there. It was smooth and neat, carried by a little wax doll sporting a wide-brimmed hat like the lawyer wore. The carnival rod leaped around among the flowers on its three red stilted feet, stomping loudly as it danced the Mazurka; the flowers couldn’t keep up, as they were too light to stomp like that. All at once, the wax doll seated on the carnival rod grew larger and taller, spinning around and saying to the paper flowers, "How can anyone put such nonsense in a child’s head? These are all just foolish fantasies!" Then, the doll transformed into a perfect likeness of the lawyer, complete with his wide-brimmed hat, looking as sour and cross as he did. But the paper dolls struck him on his skinny legs, causing him to shrink and become little again. It was hilarious, and Ida couldn’t help but giggle. The carnival rod continued dancing, and the lawyer had no choice but to dance along. No matter whether he transformed back into a tall figure or remained a tiny wax doll, he was compelled to join the dance. Finally, the other flowers took pity on him, especially those who had just been in the doll’s bed, and the carnival rod stopped its dancing. At that moment, loud knocking came from the drawer where Ida's doll Sophy lay with her other toys. The rough doll hurried to the edge of the table, laid down flat, and began to pull the drawer open.

title: LITTLE IDA'S FLOWERS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Then Sophy woke up and looked around, astonished. "There must be a ball happening here tonight," she declared. "Why didn’t anyone tell me?"

"Will you dance with me?" the rough doll asked.

"You seem like the right partner, but I’d rather not," she replied, turning her back on him.

Then she perched herself on the edge of the drawer, hoping one of the flowers would ask her to dance. But none did. So she coughed, "Ahem, ahem;" and still, not a single flower approached. The shabby doll ended up dancing alone, and surprisingly, not too badly, either. When it seemed like none of the flowers cared about her, she climbed down from the drawer to the floor, intentionally making a loud sound. All the flowers rushed over to her, asking if she was hurt, especially those who had lain in her bed. But she wasn't hurt at all, and Ida’s flowers thanked her for letting them use the nice bed and were very kind to her. They guided her to the center of the room, where the moonlight shone, and danced with her while all the other flowers formed a circle around them. Sophy felt very happy and said they could keep her bed; she didn’t mind lying in the drawer at all. However, the flowers were very grateful and replied—

"We can’t last long. By tomorrow morning, we’ll be completely dead, and you must tell little Ida to bury us in the garden, near the grave of the canary. Then come summer, we’ll wake up and be even prettier than we were before."

"No, you mustn’t die!" Sophy exclaimed, kissing the flowers.

title: LITTLE IDA'S FLOWERS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Just then, the door swung open, and a swarm of beautiful flowers danced in. Ida couldn’t believe where they came from, unless they were from the king’s garden. First, two lovely roses entered, wearing little golden crowns—those were the king and queen. Gorgeous stocks and carnations followed, curtsying to everyone present. They brought music with them. Big poppies and peonies used pea pods for instruments, puffing into them until they turned red in the face. Bunches of blue hyacinths and delicate white snowdrops jingled their bell-shaped flowers like real bells. More flowers entered, like blue violets, purple heart's-ease, daisies, and lily-of-the-valleys, all dancing together and greeting one another. It was truly a beautiful sight.

Eventually, the flowers bid each other goodnight. Little Ida crept back into her bed, dreaming of everything she had witnessed. When she got up the next morning, she hurried to the little table to check if the flowers were still there. She pulled aside the curtains of the little bed and found them all lying there, but quite faded—much more so than the day before. Sophy was still in the drawer where Ida had placed her, looking quite sleepy.

"Do you remember what the flowers asked you to tell me?" little Ida asked, but Sophy looked blank and didn’t say a word.

"You’re not kind at all," said Ida, "and yet they all danced with you."

Then she took a little paper box painted with beautiful birds and laid the dead flowers inside.

"This will be your lovely coffin," she said. "When my cousins visit, they can help me bury you in the garden so that next summer, you can grow back even more beautifully than ever."

Her cousins were two cheerful boys named James and Adolphus. Their father had gifted each of them a bow and arrow, and they brought them to show Ida. She told them about the poor flowers that had died, and as soon as they got permission, they accompanied her to bury them. The boys walked ahead, carrying their crossbows, while little Ida followed with the pretty box containing the dead flowers. They dug a small grave in the garden. Ida kissed her flowers, then gently placed them, along with the box, into the earth. James and Adolphus fired their crossbows over the grave since they didn’t have guns or cannons.

title: LITTLE TINY OR THUMBELINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once there was a woman who really wanted a little child, but her wish just couldn't come true. Finally, she decided to visit a fairy and said, "I would love to have a little child. Can you help me find one?"

"That's easy to do," replied the fairy. "Here’s a special barleycorn, different from the ones farmers grow for chickens. Plant it in a flower pot and see what happens."

"Thank you," the woman said, handing the fairy twelve shillings for the barleycorn. She went home, planted it, and suddenly a big, beautiful flower bloomed, similar to a tulip, but with its leaves tightly closed like a bud. "What a gorgeous flower!" the woman exclaimed, kissing the rich red and golden leaves. As she did, the flower opened up to reveal a delicate little girl sitting on the green velvet stamens. She was hardly half the size of a thumb, and they named her "Thumbelina" or Tiny because she was so small. A polished walnut shell served as her cradle, with blue violet leaves for bedding and a rose leaf as a blanket. At night, she slept soundly, and during the day, she played on a table with a plate of water placed for her. Around it were flower wreaths with their stems in the water, and a big tulip leaf floated that Tiny used as a boat. She would sit there, rowing herself with two oars made of fine horsehair. It was a lovely scene. Not only that, but Tiny could sing so sweetly and softly that her voice was unlike anything anyone had ever heard. One night, while she was peacefully sleeping in her little bed, a large, ugly, wet toad crept through a broken window pane and jumped onto the table where Tiny lay under her rose-leaf quilt.

"What a pretty little wife she would make for my son!" said the toad as she picked up Tiny's walnut shell cradle and jumped back out the window into the garden.

In the soggy edge of a wide stream in the garden lived the toad with her son, who was even uglier than his mother. When he saw the beautiful little girl in her lovely bed, all he could do was croak, "Croak, croak, croak."

title: LITTLE TINY OR THUMBELINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Don’t be so loud, or she’ll wake up," said the toad. "She might jump away because she's as light as a feather. Let’s place her on one of the water lily leaves in the stream; it’ll be like an island for her since she’s so small, and she won’t be able to escape. Meanwhile, we’ll prepare her room in the marsh to get ready for when you’re married."

Far out in the stream, there were several water lilies with broad green leaves floating on the surface. The largest leaf looked farther away than the others, so the old toad swam out to it, holding Tiny’s walnut shell. Tiny woke up early the next morning and cried bitterly when she saw her surroundings; all she could see was water on all sides of the big green leaf, with no way to get to land. Meanwhile, the old toad was busy decorating her daughter-in-law's new room beneath the marsh with rushes and wild yellow flowers to make it look pretty. She swam out with her ugly son to the leaf holding poor little Tiny. She wanted to take the beautiful bed to put it in the bridal chamber ready for her soon-to-be-wife. The old toad dipped her head low in the water and said, "Here is my son; he will be your husband and you will live happily in the marsh beside the stream."

"Croak, croak, croak," was all her son could manage to say, so the toad took away the lovely little bed and swam off with it, leaving Tiny all alone on the green leaf, where she sat and wept. She couldn’t bear the thought of living with the old toad and marrying her ugly son. The little fish swimming beneath the water had seen the toad and heard what she said. They popped their heads up to look at the little girl and, upon seeing how pretty she was, felt sorry that she had to live with the ugly toads. "This cannot happen!" they decided, and began to gather around the green stalk holding the leaf Tiny stood on and gnawed it at the root. Soon, the leaf floated down the stream, carrying Tiny far away from land.

title: LITTLE TINY OR THUMBELINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Tiny drifted past many towns, and the little birds in the bushes noticed her, singing, "What a lovely little creature!" The leaf floated farther and farther, bringing her into beautiful new lands. A graceful little white butterfly was constantly fluttering around her and eventually landed on the leaf. Tiny was thrilled; this meant the toad couldn’t possibly catch up with her, and the scenery was gorgeous, with the sun shining on the water, making it sparkle like liquid gold. She tied one end of her girdle around the butterfly and the other end around the leaf, which made it glide along even faster, taking Tiny with it. Soon, a large beetle zipped by and, noticing her, grabbed her by the waist with its claws and flew her up into a tree. The green leaf floated away on the stream, and the butterfly went with it, stuck to the leaf.

Tiny was so frightened when the beetle flew her to the tree! But she felt especially bad for the beautiful white butterfly tied to the leaf because if he couldn't escape, he would starve. The beetle didn’t care at all. He set himself beside her on a big green leaf, served her honey from the flowers to eat, and complimented her on her beauty, although he noted she didn’t look at all like a beetle. Eventually, all the other beetles showed up, raised their feelers, and commented, "She only has two legs! How ugly!" "She doesn’t have feelers," said another. "Her waist is so slim. She’s just like a human being."

title: LITTLE TINY OR THUMBELINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Oh! she’s ugly," declared all the lady beetles, despite Tiny being very pretty. The beetle who grabbed her believed the others and refused to talk to her again, leaving her perched on a daisy. Tiny cried, feeling so ugly that even the beetles wouldn’t talk to her. Surprisingly, she was actually the loveliest creature imaginable, as delicate as a rose petal. Throughout the summer, Tiny lived all alone in the big forest. She made herself a bed from blades of grass and hung it under a large leaf to keep dry in the rain. She ate honey from flowers and drank morning dew from their leaves. That’s how summer and autumn went by, and then winter came—the long, cold winter. All the birds that had sweetly sung to her had flown away, while the trees and flowers had wilted. The large clover leaf that had sheltered her was now shriveled and withered, leaving nothing but a dry stalk. Tiny felt terribly cold; her clothes were ragged, and being so fragile, she was nearly frozen to death. When the snow started falling, each flake felt heavy on her because she was only an inch tall. She tried to wrap herself in a dry leaf, but it cracked in half and couldn’t warm her, and she shivered from the cold. Near the woods where she had been living lay a cornfield, but it had been harvested long ago; only dry stubble remained poking up from the frozen ground. To her, it felt like pushing through a big forest. Oh, how she trembled from the cold. Finally, she reached the door of a field mouse's burrow, which was cozy and warm under the corn stubble. Inside, the field mouse lived in comfort, with a whole room filled with corn, a kitchen, and even a beautiful dining room. Tiny stood at the door like a little beggar girl and begged for just a small piece of barley, as she hadn’t eaten for two days.

"You poor little thing," said the field mouse, who was genuinely a kind old mouse. "Come into my warm room and share a meal with me." Pleased with Tiny, the field mouse added, "You can stay with me all winter if you’d like, but you have to keep my rooms tidy and share stories with me, as I really enjoy hearing them." Tiny agreed to everything the field mouse asked and found herself quite comfortable.

title: LITTLE TINY OR THUMBELINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"We’ll have a visitor soon," said the field mouse one day. "My neighbor drops by once a week. He’s much better off than I am—he has large rooms and wears a beautiful black velvet coat. If you could marry him, you’d be very well taken care of. But he’s blind, so you’ll need to share some of your best stories with him."

But Tiny didn’t feel interested in this neighbor as he was a mole. Still, he came to visit, dressed in his black velvet coat.

"He’s rich and educated, no doubt, but he always speaks disrespectfully of the sun and pretty flowers because he’s never seen them," said the field mouse.

Tiny had to sing for him, "Ladybug, ladybug, fly home," and many other pretty songs. The mole fell for her because of her lovely voice, but he remained quiet and cautious. Not long before, the mole had dug a long tunnel that connected the field mouse's home to his own, and Tiny had permission to go with the mole whenever she wanted. He warned her not to be scared of a dead bird lying in the passage. It was a perfect bird, complete with beak and feathers, and it hadn’t been dead long, lying right where the mole had dug. The mole took a piece of phosphorescent wood in his mouth, which glowed like fire in the dark, and led them through the dark corridor. When they reached the spot with the dead bird, the mole pushed his broad nose through the ceiling, causing the earth to give way, creating a large hole and letting daylight pour in. On the floor lay a dead swallow, its beautiful wings pressed against its body, head and feet tucked beneath its feathers; it was clear the poor bird had died from the cold. Seeing it made Tiny very sad because she adored the little birds, who sang beautifully for her all summer. But the mole pushed it aside with his crooked legs and said, "It will never sing again. What a miserable existence it must be to be a little bird! I thank my luck that none of my children will ever be birds, as they just cry, 'Tweet, tweet,' and end up starving during winter."

title: LITTLE TINY OR THUMBELINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Yes, you’re very wise to say that!" exclaimed the field mouse. "What good is all that singing when winter hits? They either starve or freeze to death. Still, birds are very refined."

Tiny stayed silent, but once the others turned their backs on the bird, she knelt down and brushed aside the soft feathers covering its head, kissing its closed eyelids. "Perhaps this was the one who sang so sweetly to me all summer," she murmured, "and I’m so grateful for your lovely songs, dear bird."

The mole blocked the hole that let the sunlight in, then accompanied the lady back home. But that night, Tiny couldn't sleep, so she got out of bed, collected hay, and wove a lovely carpet. She took it to the dead bird and draped it over him, using some fluff from the flowers she had found in the field mouse's home. It felt as soft as wool, and she laid some more on either side of the bird so he could rest warm in the cold earth. "Goodbye, sweet little bird," she whispered. "Thank you for your wonderful songs during the summer when everything was green and the sun shone bright." She laid her head on the bird's breast, but jumped back in shock when she felt a thump inside; it was the bird's heart—he wasn’t truly dead but just numb from cold, and her warmth was bringing him back to life. In autumn, swallows fly south for warmth, but if one lingers, the cold overwhelms it, and it falls as if dead, covered by the cold snow. Tiny shivered, frightened because the bird was so much larger than her—she was only an inch tall. But she gathered courage, layered more fluff around the poor swallow, and grabbed a leaf she used as a blanket, placing it over the bird's head. The next morning, she snuck out to check on him. He was alive but weak, only able to open his eyes briefly to see Tiny, who stood nearby holding a piece of decayed wood for light.

title: LITTLE TINY OR THUMBELINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Thank you, dear little maiden," said the recovering swallow. "You’ve warmed me so well that I’ll soon be strong enough to fly again in the warm sunshine."

"Oh," she replied, "it’s cold outside now; it’s snowing and freezing. Stay in your cozy bed; I’ll take care of you."

Tiny brought the swallow some water in a flower leaf, and after he drank, he told her that he had hurt one of his wings in a thorn bush, making it hard for him to keep up with the others, who ahead on their way to warmer places. He then fell to the earth, unable to recall how he got to where she found him. The swallow spent the entire winter underground, and Tiny cared for him with affection and attention. Neither the mole nor the field mouse knew anything about it since they didn’t like swallows. Before long, spring arrived, warming the earth. When the swallow told Tiny it was time to say goodbye, she opened the hole in the ceiling that the mole had made. The sun shined in beautifully, and the swallow invited her to go with him; he could carry her on his back, and together, they could escape from the gloomy mole and his dark home—far away over the mountains, to warmer lands where the sun shone brighter than here and flowers bloomed more beautifully. "Come along, dear little Tiny; you saved my life when I was frozen in that dark tunnel."

"Yes, I’ll go with you!" Tiny exclaimed, climbing onto the swallow's back, resting her feet on his outstretched wings and tying her girdle to his strongest feather.

Then the swallow soared into the air, gliding over forests and seas, high above the tallest snow-capped mountains. Tiny shivered in the chilly air but tucked herself under the bird's warm feathers to keep her little head exposed, cherishing the stunning lands beneath them. Eventually, they arrived at warm countries where the sun shone brightly and the sky looked so much higher. Vines heavy with purple, green, and white grapes grew along the hedges and by the roadside, while lemons and oranges hung from trees in woods that filled the air with the scent of myrtles and orange blossoms. Beautiful children raced along the country path, playing with big colorful butterflies, and as the swallow flew on, every place appeared even lovelier.

title: LITTLE TINY OR THUMBELINA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Finally, they approached a blue lake, where a dazzling white palace stood, shaded by the deepest green trees. The palace was made of ancient marble, with vines wrapping around tall pillars, and at the top were many swallow nests, including one belonging to Tiny's swallow.

"This is my home," said the swallow, "but it wouldn’t be appropriate for you to live there—you wouldn’t be comfortable. You should pick one of those beautiful flowers, and I’ll set you down upon it so that you can have anything you desire to make you happy."

"That sounds wonderful," Tiny responded, clapping her little hands in delight.

A large marble pillar had fallen, breaking into three pieces, and between these pieces, the most stunning white flowers grew. The swallow flew down with Tiny and placed her on one of the broad leaves. To her amazement, she saw a tiny little man, white and transparent, as if made of crystal, right in the center of the flower! He wore a gold crown and delicate wings; he wasn’t much larger than Tiny herself. He was the angel of the flower, for every blossom holds a tiny man and a tiny woman, and he was the king of them all.

"Oh, how beautiful he is!" Tiny whispered to the swallow.

At first, the little prince was shocked by the bird, who seemed like a giant to such a delicate creature. But when he saw Tiny, he was overjoyed, thinking she was the prettiest little maiden he had ever met. He took his gold crown off and placed it on her head, asking her name and if she would be his bride, the queen of all the flowers.

This was definitely a different kind of husband compared to the toad's son or the mole dressed in black velvet and furs, so she happily replied, "Yes," to the handsome prince. Then all the flowers bloomed, and from each one emerged a lovely lady or tiny lord, all so pretty that it was a joy to see. Each of them gifted Tiny a present, but the best of all was a pair of beautiful wings that had belonged to a large white fly, which they attached to Tiny's shoulders so she could flutter from flower to flower. There was so much happiness that the little swallow, perched above them in his nest, was asked to sing a wedding song, which he did to the best of his ability. Yet in his heart, he felt sad because he was very fond of Tiny and would have liked to be with her forever.

"You can’t be called Tiny any longer," said the spirit of the flowers. "That's an ugly name for someone so lovely. We will call you Maia instead."

"Goodbye, goodbye," said the swallow with a heavy heart as he flew back to Denmark. There, he found a nest above the window of the house of a fairy tale writer. The swallow sang, "Tweet, tweet," and from his song, the whole story unfolded.

title: LITTLE TUK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
They called him Little Tuk, but that wasn't really his name; he had chosen that for himself before he could speak clearly, and he intended it to be Charles. This was fine for those who knew him, but not so much for strangers.

Little Tuk was left at home to look after his little sister, Gustava, who was a lot younger than him. He had to learn his lessons at the same time, which made things tricky. The poor boy sat there with his sister on his lap, singing all the songs he knew, while every now and then glancing at his open geography lesson. By the next morning, he had to memorize all the towns in Zealand and everything about them.

Finally, his mother came home and gathered little Gustava in her arms. Tuk hurried to the window and read so fervently that he almost strained his eyes; it was getting darker by the minute, and his mother couldn't afford a light.

"Look, there goes the old washerwoman up the lane," his mother said as she peered out of the window. "The poor woman can hardly drag herself along, and now she has to haul a pail of water from the well. Be a good boy, Tuk, and run over to help her, will you?"

title: LITTLE TUK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
So Tuk quickly ran over and assisted her, but when he returned to the room, it was completely dark, and no one mentioned a light. He had to go to bed on his small truckle bed, lying there thinking about his geography lesson, Zealand, and everything the teacher had told him. He should really have gone over it again, but he couldn't due to the lack of light. So he tucked the geography book under his pillow, having heard that this could help with learning, though it wasn't always reliable. He continued to think deeply when, suddenly, it felt like someone kissed him on his eyes and mouth. He slept, yet he didn't sleep; it seemed like the old washerwoman looked at him kindly and said, "It would be a shame if you didn't know your lesson tomorrow morning; you helped me, so now I'll help you, and Providence always looks after those who help themselves." At that moment, the book under Tuk's pillow began to move. "Cluck, cluck, cluck," a hen cried as it approached him. "I'm a hen from Kjoge," it said, sharing details about the town, including its population and a battle that really wasn't worth mentioning.

"Crack, crack," something fell. It was a wooden bird, a parrot used as a target for Prastoe. It boasted that as many people lived in that town as it had nails in its body. The parrot was quite proud, saying, "Thorwalsden lived nearby, and here I am now, perfectly comfortable."

But suddenly, Little Tuk was no longer in bed; in an instant, he found himself on horseback. He galloped along, sitting in front of a grand knight with a flowing plume who held him steady in the saddle. They rode through the woods near the bustling old town of Wordingburg, where the king's castle was surrounded by tall towers and bright light streamed from all the windows. Inside, there were songs and dancing; King Waldemar and the young ladies of the court were dancing joyfully together. But as morning dawned and the sun rose, the entire city and the king's castle suddenly sank down together. One tower after another collapsed until, finally, only a single tower remained standing on the hill where the castle had once sat.

title: LITTLE TUK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Now the town looked small and shabby, and the schoolboys carried their books under their arms, reading that it had two thousand inhabitants. However, this was just a stretch of the truth, as it didn’t actually have that many.

And once again, little Tuk found himself back in his bed, barely able to tell if he was dreaming or not, because someone was standing by him.

"Tuk! little Tuk!" a voice called out. It belonged to a tiny figure dressed as a sailor, looking small enough to be a midshipman, but he wasn’t. "I bring you many greetings from Corsor. It's a lively town filled with energy. It used to be called ugly, but that's no longer true. I sit by the sea-shore," Corsor said; "I have highways and amusement parks; I’ve even birthed a witty and entertaining poet, which isn't something you come across every day. I once wanted to outfit a ship to sail around the world, but I didn’t manage it, although I probably could have. But I am fragrant with roses, for delightful blooms flourish at my gates."

Then a splash of color appeared before little Tuk’s eyes, red and green; it cleared away, revealing a cliff by the bay, its slopes overrun with vibrant greenery. Atop the cliff stood a beautiful old church with pointed towers. Water gushed from the cliff in thick streams, creating a constant splashing sound. Nearby sat a regal king wearing a golden crown on his silver hair. This was King Hroar of the Springs, and right by the springs was the town of Roeskilde. Then all the kings and queens of Denmark climbed the hill to the old church hand in hand, each wearing golden crowns, while music played and fountains erupted.

Little Tuk witnessed it all. "Don't forget the names of these towns," advised King Hroar.

title: LITTLE TUK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Suddenly, everything disappeared, but where? It felt like flipping through the pages of a book. Now, an old peasant woman appeared, having come from Soroe, where the grass grows in the marketplace. She wore a green linen apron over her head and shoulders, damp as if it had rained heavily. "Yes, it has," she replied, preparing to share delightful stories from Holberg's comedies, and tales about Waldemar and Absalom. But just as she was about to begin, she shrunk suddenly and wiggled her head as if she were a frog getting ready to leap. "Croak," she said; "it’s always wet and eerily quiet in Soroe." Then little Tuk saw that she transformed into a frog. "Croak," and again she became an old woman. "One must dress for the weather," she said. "It’s wet, and my town is like a sealed bottle. We must enter by the cork and exit by the cork. Long ago, I had beautiful fish, and now I have fresh, rosy-cheeked boys at the bottom of the bottle, learning wisdom, Hebrew, and Greek."

"Croak." It sounded like the call of frogs on the moor or the creaking of heavy boots marching—always the same tone, monotonous and grating, until little Tuk finally fell fast asleep, and then the sounds no longer bothered him. But even in this sleep, a dream or something similar occurred. His little sister Gustava, with her blue eyes and curly blonde hair, grew into a beautiful young woman all at once and, without wings, she could fly. Together, they soared over Zealand, gliding above green forests and blue lakes.

"Listen, do you hear the rooster crow, little Tuk? 'Cock-a-doodle-doo.' The chickens are flying out of Kjoge. You’ll have a big farmyard. You'll never go hungry or be in want. The bird of good fortune will be yours, and you’ll grow into a rich and happy man; your house will rise like King Waldemar’s towers, beautifully adorned with marble statues like those at Prastoe. Understand me well; your name will travel around the world like the ship that was supposed to sail from Corsor, and at Roeskilde—Don’t forget the names of the towns, as King Hroar mentioned—you will speak well and clearly little Tuk, and when you finally lay in your grave, you’ll sleep peacefully, as—"

"As if I lay in Soroe," little Tuk murmured as he awakened. It was bright daylight, and he couldn’t recall his dream, but that wasn't important, because we aren't meant to know our future. He quickly jumped out of bed, reviewed his lesson in the book, and suddenly understood it all perfectly. The old washerwoman peeked in through the door, kindly nodding at him and saying, "Thank you, good boy, for your help yesterday. I hope all your wonderful dreams come true."

Little Tuk didn’t remember his dream at all, but One above did.

title: OLE THE TOWER-KEEPER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"In this world, things are always going up and down, and right now, I've hit my limit!" said Ole, the tower-keeper. "Most people have to experience both the highs and the lows; when you think about it, we all become like watchmen, looking down on life from higher ground."

That was Ole's way of speaking, my friend the old tower-keeper. He was a quirky guy, always chatting away, sharing whatever popped into his mind, yet he held many serious thoughts close to his heart. Born to respectable parents, there were whispers that he might be the son of a privy councillor. Ole had studied and even worked as an assistant teacher and deputy clerk, but none of that helped him out much. Back then, he lived in the clerk's house, supposedly getting free room and board, but he still acted like a refined gentleman. He wanted his boots polished with fancy blacking, but the clerk could only provide plain grease. They clashed over this—one accused the other of being stingy, while the other defended his self-respect. Eventually, the disagreement over blacking drove a wedge between them, and they parted ways.

What Ole wanted from the world was that fancy blacking, but all he received was grease. So, he distanced himself from everyone and took on a hermit's life. Yet, in a big city, the church tower is the only spot where one can find solitude while also having a job and a place to sleep. Ole found himself up there, smoking his pipe during his solitary watch.
