He looked up and down, lost in his thoughts, sharing his unique interpretations of what he read in books and within himself. I often lent him books—good ones too; you could tell by the company he kept. He had no love for the English or French novels, which he dismissed as a mix of empty chatter and leftover scraps. Instead, he craved biographies and accounts of the world's wonders. I visited him at least once a year, generally right after New Year's, and he would always share his thoughts inspired by the changing year.

title: OLE THE TOWER-KEEPER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Let me share the story of three of those visits with Ole, and I'll do my best to quote him whenever I can.

Among the books I had recently lent Ole was a geological text that had really sparked his interest. It was all about boulders.

"Yes, those boulders are such ancient fellows!" he exclaimed. "Can you believe we walk right past them without a second thought? And those paving stones on the street—fragments of the oldest remnants of history—we walk over them without noticing. I’ve done it myself! But now I look at every paving stone with respect. Thank you for the book! It's filled my mind with thoughts and made me eager to learn more about the subject. The earth's story is the most incredible romance of all. It's a shame we can't read its first chapter because it's written in a language we don't understand. We must read between the layers, even in the pebbles, for each different time period. Yes, it’s a beautiful story that has been unfolding for thousands of years and is still going on. Thank you for the book about the boulders. They have stories to tell if only they could speak! It's refreshing to feel insignificant at times, especially when one is in a position like mine. To think that despite our fancy titles and honors, we are merely fleeting insects on this great ant-hill called earth! I pondered all this last New Year's Eve, lost in the book, completely forgetting my usual New Year's adventure—the wild ride to Amack. Ah, you don't know what that is!"

title: OLE THE TOWER-KEEPER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "You know about the witches flying on broomsticks—that journey is famous, taken on St. John's Eve to the Brocken; but we also have our unique wild ride, which is national and modern—the ride to Amack on New Year's night. All the mediocre poets, musicians, and artistic souls hop on their broomsticks or quill pens—steel pens are too rigid for them! I witness this every New Year's Eve and could name most riders, but I wouldn't want to earn their ire by talking about them. My niece, a fishwife, proudly claims to supply three notable newspapers with their spicy slander, and she’s even been to Amack as a guest. She was carried there because she doesn’t own a quill pen and can’t ride. She shared some amusing tales about it, though half were likely exaggerated. The festivities kick off with everyone singing their own songs—they each think they’ve written the best! Bells chime in turn, little drummers beat their rhythm, and the guests introduce themselves under pseudonyms, which is pretty much like using grease instead of fancy blacking. Then, there's the beadle and his boy—who usually goes unnoticed—and the street sweeper turning over dustbins, proclaiming, ‘Good, very good!’ Amidst this gathering, a massive tree or mushroom rises from the dirt at Amack, holding everything the guests had contributed in the past year. Sparks like flames shoot from it, symbolizing all those borrowed ideas and thoughts, now freely flying away like fireworks. The young poets play silly games, and laughter echoes like pots shattering against doors. "It was all so amusing!" my niece remarked. She said a lot of spiteful yet funny things, but I won't repeat them because a person should be kind and not a constant critic. You'll see, once you know the truth about the ride to Amack, it’s only natural to watch for it on New Year’s Eve. If I notice some familiar faces missing in the New Year, I also spot newcomers. But this year, I got too lost in the boulders, rolling back through millions of years, witnessing the stones breaking loose far up north, drifting on icebergs long before Noah’s Ark, sinking to the ocean floor, and re-emerging as a sandbank—a sandbank that peeked from the flood and declared, ‘This shall be Zealand!’ I envisioned them housing unknown birds and wild chieftains carving their Runic signs into these stones, marking them through time. In that moment, I lost all sense of time, becoming nothing and no one. Then a handful of beautiful shooting stars whizzed by, clearing my mind and changing my thoughts. You know what fallen stars are, right? Scholars debate what they are, but I’ve got my own theory about shooting stars that many people might call them. How often are silent thanks sent up for those who've done great and noble deeds? They may not be spoken, but they aren’t lost. I believe these thanks are gathered up, carried by sunbeams as hidden gratitude over the heads of the benefactors; and if a whole people expresses thanks over time, what emerges is like a bouquet of flowers, eventually falling as a shooting star over the good person’s grave. Seeing a shooting star always brings me joy, especially on New Year’s night when I try to figure out who the gratitude is for. Recently, a brilliant star fell in the southwest, a tribute of thanks for many—a tribute for all those who deserved it. 'Who was that for?' I wondered. It landed on the hill by the Bay of Plensberg, where the flag of Danebrog flies over the graves of Schleppegrell, Lasloes, and their companions. Another star fell in the heart of the land, landing in Soro, a flower on Holberg’s grave—thankfulness from countless people—for his delightful plays!

title: OLE THE TOWER-KEEPER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"It's a beautiful thought that a shooting star might fall on our graves. For mine, none will fall—no sunbeam brings gratitude to me; I guess I’m only destined for grease in this life," Ole sighed.

It was New Year’s Day, and I climbed up to the tower. Ole spoke of the toasts people made when transitioning from the Old Year to the New—"from one grave to another," as he put it. He shared a story about those toasts, and it carried a deep meaning.

"When the clock strikes midnight on New Year’s Eve, everyone at the table raises their full glasses and drinks a toast to the New Year. This is a strong start for those who drink too much. They begin the New Year by going to bed, a good beginning for the lazy. Sleep, after all, plays a huge role in the New Year, just like the drink. Do you know what’s in the glass?" Ole asked. "Let me tell you. Inside the glass is health, then pleasure, and eventually the most overwhelming delights; but also misfortune and the deepest sorrows dwell in the glass too. Now, let’s count the glasses—of course, I’m counting the different types of glasses for different people."

"You see, the first glass is for health, and in it grows the herb of health! Hang it from a beam in the ceiling, and by year’s end, you might find yourself sitting in the garden of health."

"If you take the second glass, a little bird will soar up, joyfully chirping, inviting all to listen to its song. You might even join in, singing, 'Life is beautiful! Keep your spirits up and march on!'"

"From the third glass, a whimsical creature rises, not quite an angel, but certainly not harmful—a bit of a trickster. He'll whisper joyful thoughts in your ear, sneaking into your heart and warming you, making you feel merry, as far as your peers can judge."

"In the fourth glass, there’s neither herb, bird, nor creature. This glass symbolizes reason, and one should never go beyond that boundary."

title: OLE THE TOWER-KEEPER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Take the fifth glass, and you’ll find yourself weeping over your emotions; or perhaps you'll feel differently. Out of that glass leaps Prince Carnival, nine times more exuberant than ever. He’ll pull you into a frenzy, making you forget your dignity—if you’ve got any—and you’ll end up dancing and singing, lost in the moment. The daughters of vanity, draped in silk and satin, will come with flowing hair and irresistible charms; good luck tearing away from them!"

"The sixth glass! And guess what? A charming little demon inhabits it, dressed smartly and incredibly captivating, who fully understands you and agrees with everything you say, becoming like your shadow. He carries a lantern to guide you home. There’s an old legend about a saint who got to choose one of the seven deadly sins and went with drunkenness, thinking it the least harmful. But that sin led him to commit the others. His blood mingles with that of the demon. This sixth glass plants the seeds of all evils within us, growing strong like mustard seeds, spreading throughout the world, forcing many into a dire fate."

"That’s the tale of the glasses," Ole concluded, "and it can be told with flair or without, but I give it to you in both styles!"

On this occasion, I chose moving day for my visit to Ole, which is hardly pleasant in the streets; they’re filled with scraps and leftovers to wade through. But that day, I spotted two kids playing in this mess. They were pretending to go to bed, which seemed like the perfect game for the occasion. They crawled under a heap of straw and pulled an old ragged curtain over themselves as a blanket. "It was fantastic!" they said, but I found it a bit too much, and I had to head up to Olé’s.

title: OLE THE TOWER-KEEPER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "It’s moving day today," he said. "Streets and houses look like one giant dustbin—a big mess! But I’m happy with just a cartload. Rarely, I find something good in all this rubble; for example, right after Christmas, I encountered a dust cart filled with debris, looking like the perfect representation of moving day. It was rough weather, wet and muddy—just the kind you catch a cold in. At the back of the cart stood a still-green fir tree, draped in tinsel; it had been used during Christmas and now tossed out onto the street. Watching it was comical or perhaps sad—it all depends on your perspective. I pondered about it and everything in the cart. Perhaps the glove from an old lady was there as well—what thoughts might it have? Maybe it’s thinking, 'I feel sad for that tree; I was once part of the festivities, shining brightly. My life was a ball night, full of handshakes, then I ended up here, ruined! That's all I can think about now!' That’s one way the glove might reflect. Meanwhile, the shattered pottery chimed in, "That tree’s a ridiculous concern! When you’re in the dust cart, you shouldn’t put on airs or wear tinsel! I know just how useful I’ve been in the world—way more than that pathetic green stick!" A valid view, though perhaps a bit peculiar. But despite everything, the fir tree stood out like a bit of poetry amidst the debris; and on moving day, there’s enough dust to make anyone want to escape the chaos. When I’m on the tower, I stay up and watch it all—it’s pretty entertaining.

title: OLE THE TOWER-KEEPER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Look at the folks down below, playing the game of 'changing houses.' They’re hauling their belongings, while the household spirit rides along in an old tub with them. All their little worries and real troubles stick with them from old homes to new; what’s the point for either side? A wise saying comes to mind: 'Reflect on the big moving day of death!' It’s a weighty thought. I hope you don’t mind me mentioning it? Death is the most certain messenger in the end, no matter his busy schedule. Yes, Death is the bus driver, the passport manager, and he stamps our service books, handing us our entry to eternity. Do you get my meaning? All the moments in our lives—big or small—go into this savings bank. So when Death summons us into his bus, we leave for the afterlife, he hands us our service book as our ticket, taking along this or that good deed we did. It can be comforting or terrifying. No one escapes the bus ride. People speak of one who can’t board—known as the Wandering Jew; he must follow behind the bus. I sometimes wish he would simply step on board and avoid the poets altogether.

"Now picture that busy bus. The passengers are diverse; kings and beggars, geniuses and fools all sit side by side. They leave behind their possessions and status; all that comes along is the service book and the gift from the savings bank. But what deeds will Death select to bestow upon us? Perhaps something small that we forgot, a deed recorded in our past—tiny like a pea, but that pea could sprout into something magnificent. The carefree villager who faced ridicule may receive his old stool as provision; and that stool might become a throne, bright and beautiful in the land of eternity. The one who spent his days indulging in pleasures to forget his wild ways might find his barrel with him on the journey, to drink from as they ride along. The drink would be pure and sweet, awakening noble feelings he never acknowledged in life. But in that moment, he finds the source of his punishment—the nagging guilt that never fades. While oblivion is written on the glasses, 'remembrance' is inscribed on the barrel."

title: OLE THE TOWER-KEEPER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Whenever I dive into a good historical book, I can’t help but think about the poetry in what I’m reading, and how it ties back to Death’s bus ride—I wonder which heroic deeds Death selected for his journey and what provisions he received for eternity. Take this one French king—right now, I can’t recall his name; sometimes the good ones slip my mind; he once helped his people during a famine, and they erected a snow monument in his honor, inscribed with, 'Faster than this melts, you brought relief!' I picture Death glancing back at that monument and bestowing on him a single snowflake as his provision—one that will never melt. This flake then dances over his royal head like a white butterfly on its way to eternity. Similar is the case with Louis XI. I’ve remembered his name because we often recall the bad—he was a character I wish the tales didn’t tell. He had his lord high constable executed, which he could do rightly or wrongly, and to ensure the punishment was extra cruel, he placed the innocent children of the constable, aged seven and eight, under the scaffold to be splattered with their father's blood, then locked them up in iron cages without even a blanket for warmth. Every week, King Louis would send in an executioner to pull a tooth from each of the boys, so they wouldn’t get too cozy. The older child bravely offered, 'My mother would be heartbroken if she knew my younger brother had to suffer so much. Pull out two of my teeth, but spare him.' The hangman couldn’t help but shed tears, but the king's orders prevailed. Thus, every week, two tiny teeth ended up on a silver plate for him; it was his demand, and he received. I can only assume that Death took those two teeth from the life's savings bank and gave them to Louis XI as he drove into eternity; they soar ahead of him like burning flames, glowing and stinging him for all time.

"Yes, it’s a serious journey, this bus ride on the big moving day! And when will it take place? That’s the tricky part. It could happen any day, any hour, any minute. The bus may pull up any time! Which of our deeds will Death pull from the savings bank to provide us for the journey? Let us not forget the real moving day that isn’t marked on any calendar."

title: OLE-LUK-OIE, THE DREAM-GOD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Nobody knows as many stories as Ole-Luk-Oie, or can tell them quite as beautifully. Each evening, as children sit at the table or in their little chairs, he quietly tiptoes up the stairs in his socks, opens doors without making a sound, and sprinkles just a bit of fine dust in their eyes, enough to make them sleepy so they don’t see him. Then he sneaks behind them and gently blows on their necks until their heads start to droop. Ole-Luk-Oie loves children and only wants them to be still so he can share his lovely stories, but they can’t be still until they’re in bed and asleep. Once they are cozy and dreaming, he sits on the bed. He’s dressed very nicely; his coat shimmers in colors that shift from green to red to blue as he moves. He carries two umbrellas—one covered in pictures he opens over the good children, allowing them to dream the most wonderful tales all night long. The other umbrella, plain, is held over the naughty kids, making sure they sleep heavily and wake in the morning without dreaming at all.

Now let’s find out how Ole-Luk-Oie visited a little boy named Hjalmar every night for a whole week and what he shared with him. There were seven stories, just like the seven days in a week.

"Listen closely," said Ole-Luk-Oie one evening when Hjalmar was in bed, "because I’m going to make your room magical."

Suddenly, all the flowers in the pots transformed into tall trees with long branches reaching up to the ceiling and stretching across the walls, turning the space into a greenhouse. Each branch was loaded with blossoms, every one just as gorgeous and fragrant as a rose; if anyone had tasted them, they would have found them sweeter than jam. The fruit sparkled like gold, and there were cakes bursting with plums. It was astonishingly beautiful. But then, dismal sounds came from the table drawer where Hjalmar’s school books were stored.

"What’s that noise?" Ole-Luk-Oie wondered aloud as he walked over to the table and opened the drawer.

title: OLE-LUK-OIE, THE DREAM-GOD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Inside was a slate, so distressed over a wrong answer in a math problem that it seemed like it was about to fall apart. The pencil tugged at its string like a little dog wanting to help but being unable to.

Then came a sad moan from Hjalmar’s copybook. It was horrible to hear! Each page had rows of capital letters each followed by little letters. They were supposed to be a copy, but the letters Hjalmar had written looked all crooked, leaning as if they might topple off the pencil lines.

"Look, you should stand up straight like this," the copy scolded. "See how nicely I’m positioned with a graceful curve?"

"Oh, we would love to, but we can’t," Hjalmar’s letters lamented. "We’re made so poorly."

"Then you’ll have to be erased," said Ole-Luk-Oie.

"Oh, no!" the letters cried, and suddenly they stood up straight in a way that looked quite lovely.

"Now we must set aside our stories and practice making these letters nice," Ole-Luk-Oie told them. "One, two—one, two—" So he led them until they stood elegantly and looked as beautiful as they could be. But when Ole-Luk-Oie departed and Hjalmar looked at them in the morning, they were just as awkward as ever.

As soon as Hjalmar was tucked in, Ole-Luk-Oie waved his magical wand over the furniture in the room, which immediately starting chattering away, each object speaking only of itself.

title: OLE-LUK-OIE, THE DREAM-GOD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Above the chest of drawers hung a large picture in a gilded frame, showing a lovely landscape filled with ancient trees, flowers in the grass, and a broad stream winding through the woods, passing several castles out into the wild ocean. Ole-Luk-Oie tapped the picture with his wand, and instantly the birds began to sing, the trees rustled, and the clouds drifted across the sky, casting shadows on the scene below. Then Ole-Luk-Oie lifted little Hjalmar up to the frame, placing his feet in the picture, right on the soft grass. He stood there with the sun shining down through the leaves. He dashed to the water and climbed into a small red-and-white boat that was waiting there. The sails sparkled like silver, and six swans, each with golden crowns around their necks and bright blue stars on their foreheads, pulled the boat through the green forest, where the trees talked about robbers and witches while the flowers whispered of lovely little elves and fairies, sharing their stories with the butterflies. Dazzling fish, their scales shimmering like silver and gold, swam alongside, leaping out of the water and splashing around, while birds of red and blue trailed after them in long lines. The gnats danced about, and the cockchafers buzzed—"Buz, buz"—all of them eager to follow Hjalmar and share their tales. It was such a delightful sail. Sometimes the woods were thick and dark, other times like a beautiful garden lit up by sunshine and flowers; eventually, he drifted past magnificent glass and marble palaces, where princesses stood on balconies with faces like little girls Hjalmar knew and often played with. One held out her hand, offering a heart made of sugar, lovelier than any candy he'd ever seen. As Hjalmar floated by, he grasped one side of the sugar heart, and they held on tight until it split in two. Hjalmar got the bigger piece, while the princess kept the smaller one. At every castle, little princes acting as guards snapped to attention, showing off their golden swords while showering him with plums and tin soldiers. They must have been real princes!

Hjalmar sailed on, passing through more forests, grand halls, and eventually massive cities. He finally arrived at the town where his nurse lived, the kind woman who had carried him in her arms when he was very small. When she saw him, she waved and smiled, then started reciting the little verses she had composed just for him.

title: OLE-LUK-OIE, THE DREAM-GOD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"How often I fondly think of you, My dear Hjalmar, forever near! When I could watch your tiny giggles, Or kiss away your pearly tears. In my arms, your first words were light, As I helped you take your early steps, My watchful care was your delight, My love for you, a treasure kept. Proudly I pray the skies above Keep you safe till the end of your days."

And all the birds joined in the same melody, the flowers danced on their stems, and the old trees swayed, as if Ole-Luk-Oie had been telling them tales too.

How the rain poured down! Hjalmar could hear it even while asleep; when Ole-Luk-Oie opened the window, the water flooded right up to the window-sill. It looked just like a big lake outside, and a beautiful ship lay close to the house.

"Would you like to sail with me tonight, little Hjalmar?" Ole-Luk-Oie asked. "We’ll visit far-off lands, and you can be back here by morning."

In an instant, Hjalmar stood on the deck of the grand ship, dressed in his best clothes, as the weather brightened instantly. They sailed through the streets, passing by the church, surrounded by the vast, rolling sea. They sailed until the land vanished, and soon they spotted a flock of storks migrating to warmer regions. The storks, flying in a line, had been traveling for a long time. One of them looked so tired that its wings barely worked anymore. It was the last in line and soon fell far behind. Eventually, it descended lower and lower, wings spread out, struggling until it landed on the ship's rigging and slid down to the deck, standing before them. A young sailor scooped it up and tucked it into the hen-house alongside the hens, ducks, and turkeys, leaving the poor stork confused in their company.

"Just look at that guy," the chickens giggled.

The turkey puffed himself up as big as he could and asked who the newcomer was, while the ducks waddled back a bit, quacking, "Quack, quack."

title: OLE-LUK-OIE, THE DREAM-GOD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The stork eagerly shared stories of warm Africa, the pyramids, and wild ostriches dashing across the deserts. But the ducks didn’t understand his tales and quacked among themselves, "We all think he’s silly."

"Of course, he’s silly," said the turkey, then gobbled.

The stork fell silent, lost in thoughts of his home in Africa.

"Those are some tall, elegant legs you have," the turkey remarked. "What do they cost per yard?"

"Quack, quack, quack," the ducks snickered, but the stork pretended not to hear.

"You might as well laugh," said the turkey. "That was a witty comment, or perhaps it was too clever for you. Ah, how clever he is! He will surely entertain us while he’s here." Then he gobbled, and the ducks echoed, "Gobble, gobble; Quack, quack."

What a ruckus they created while having so much fun!

Hjalmar went to the hen-house, opened the door, and called to the stork. He hopped out onto the deck. Now he looked rested and happy, nodding at Hjalmar as if to thank him. He spread his wings and flew away to warmer lands, while the hens clucked, the ducks quacked, and the turkey turned bright red.

"Tomorrow you’ll become soup," Hjalmar told the birds, and then he woke up, finding himself back in his little bed.

What an incredible adventure Ole-Luk-Oie had taken him on that night!

"What do you think I brought for you?" said Ole-Luk-Oie. "Don’t be scared; it’s a little mouse!" And he extended his hand to reveal a lovely little creature. "It’s come to invite you to a wedding. Two little mice are getting married tonight, living under the floor of your mother’s pantry; it must be a lovely place to live."

"But how can I squeeze through the little mouse-hole in the floor?" Hjalmar asked.

"Leave that to me," replied Ole-Luk-Oie. "I’ll make you small enough." With a wave of his magic wand, Hjalmar shrank down until he was as tiny as a finger. "Now you can borrow the tin soldier's uniform. It will look nice to wear when you attend the wedding."

title: OLE-LUK-OIE, THE DREAM-GOD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Absolutely," Hjalmar replied, and in an instant, he was dressed neatly like the best of tin soldiers.

"Could you please sit in your mom’s thimble so I can pull you to the wedding?" asked the little mouse.

"Would you really go through all that trouble, young lady?" said Hjalmar. And that’s how he rode to the mouse’s wedding.

First they went under the floor, then through a long passage that barely allowed the thimble to slide through. The whole path sparkled with the phosphorescent light of rotten wood.

"Doesn’t it smell wonderful?" asked the mouse as she guided him along. "The walls and floor are smeared with bacon fat; it’s delicious!"

Before long, they arrived at the wedding hall. On the right side stood all the lady-mice, whispering and giggling among themselves. To the left, the gentleman-mice were smoothing their whiskers with their paws. In the center stood the bride and groom, side by side in a hollow cheese rind, kissing as everyone watched. They were already betrothed and soon to be married. More guests kept arriving until the mice were nearly squished together, with the couple standing in the doorway, blocking entry.

The room had also been smeared with bacon fat, just like the passage—this was the only refreshment offered to the guests. For dessert, they had a pea where a mouse from the couple had nibbled the first letters of their names. It was quite the rarity. All the mice agreed it was a beautiful wedding and that they were really well entertained.

Afterward, Hjalmar returned home. He had certainly attended a grand event, but it had required crawling through passages and shrinking to fit into a tin soldier’s outfit.

title: OLE-LUK-OIE, THE DREAM-GOD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"You wouldn't believe how many grown-ups would love to have me visit them at night," Ole-Luk-Oie said. "Especially those who’ve done something wrong. They say, 'Good little Ole, we can’t close our eyes; all night we see our bad deeds showing up like little demons sitting on our beds, splashing us with hot water. Can you come and chase them away so we can sleep well?' Then they sigh deeply, saying, 'We’ll pay you for it. Goodnight, Ole-Luk, the money’s on the window sill.' But I don’t do anything for cash." "What shall we do tonight?" asked Hjalmar. "I’m not sure if you’d want to join another wedding," Ole-Luk-Oie replied, "though this one’s different from last night’s. Your sister’s big doll, dressed like a man and called Herman, is getting married to the doll Bertha. It’s also their birthday, and they’ll be receiving plenty of gifts."

"Yeah, I already know," Hjalmar said. "My sister always lets her dolls celebrate their birthdays or have weddings whenever they need new clothes; I’m sure this has happened more than a hundred times already."

"That might be true, but tonight is the hundred and first wedding, and after that, it has to be the last one, so it has to be extra special. Just look!"

Hjalmar glanced at the table, where a little cardboard dollhouse stood with all the windows lit up. In front, tin soldiers were presenting arms. The bride and groom were seated on the floor, leaning against the table's leg, looking quite pensive for good reason. Ole-Luk-Oie, dressed in grandma’s black gown, officiated their marriage.

Once the ceremony finished, the furniture in the room joined together to sing a lovely song composed by a pencil, set to the tune of a military march.

"What merry sounds are on the wind, As marriage rites together bind A quiet and a loving pair, Though formed of kid, yet smooth and fair! Hurrah! If they are deaf and blind, We’ll sing, though weather prove unkind."

title: OLE-LUK-OIE, THE DREAM-GOD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Then came the gifts, but the bride and groom had nothing to eat; love was to be their feast.

"Shall we go to a country house, or travel?" asked the groom.

They consulted the swallow who had traveled far and the old hen in the yard who had raised five sets of chicks.

The swallow spoke of warm places where grapes hung in large clusters on vines, where the air was soft and gentle, and mountains glowed with colors more beautiful than we can imagine.

"But they don’t have red cabbage like we do," argued the hen. "I once spent a summer with my chicks, and there was a big sandpit where we could scratch around. We found a garden with red cabbage growing there; oh, it was so delicious, I can’t think of anything better!"

"But one cabbage plant is just like another," the swallow countered. "And here, we often have bad weather."

"True, but we’re used to it," said the hen.

"But it gets so cold here that it freezes sometimes."

"Cold weather is good for cabbages," the hen asserted. "Plus, we enjoy warm days sometimes. Remember, four years ago, we had a summer that lasted over five weeks where it was so hot we could hardly breathe. And here, we don’t have poisonous animals and we’re free from thieves. Anyone who doesn’t love this place must be wicked!" The hen then started to cry a lot and continued, "I’ve traveled before too; I once went twelve miles in a coop, and that was no fun at all."

"The hen is a sensible lady," said the doll Bertha. "I don’t care for traveling over mountains just to go up and come back down again. Let’s go to the sandpit in front of the gate and take a walk in the cabbage garden instead."

"Am I going to hear more stories?" asked little Hjalmar, as soon as Ole-Luk-Oie put him to sleep.

title: OLE-LUK-OIE, THE DREAM-GOD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"We won’t have time for that tonight," Ole-Luk-Oie replied while spreading his prettiest umbrella over Hjalmar. "Look at these Chinese figures," said he, as the whole umbrella appeared to be a massive china bowl showcasing blue trees and pointed bridges, with little Chinamen nodding their heads. "We must make the world beautiful for tomorrow morning," Ole-Luk-Oie announced, "because it’s a holiday, it’s Sunday. Now I have to visit the church steeple to see if the little sprites living there have polished the bells so they’ll sound sweetly. After that, I’ll check the fields to ensure the wind hasn’t blown dust from the grass and leaves. And my hardest task is to retrieve all the stars and make them shine again. I must note their numbers first before putting them in my apron, as well as numbering the spots I took them from, so they find their way back; otherwise, we would see a lot of falling stars, one after another."

"Listen, Mr. Luk-Oie," said an old portrait hanging in Hjalmar’s room. "Do you recognize me? I’m Hjalmar's great-grandfather. I appreciate you telling the boy stories, but you mustn’t confuse his ideas. The stars cannot be taken down from the sky; they are spheres like our Earth, and that’s a good thing for them."

"Thank you, old great-grandfather," Ole-Luk-Oie replied. "I appreciate that; you may be the head of the family, but I’m older than you. I’m an ancient spirit; the Romans and Greeks called me the Dream-God. I’ve visited the most esteemed homes and know how to treat high and low people alike. Now you may tell the stories yourself," and with that, Ole-Luk-Oie walked off, taking his umbrellas with him.

"Well, it seems one should never voice an opinion," muttered the portrait. This roused Hjalmar from his sleep.

Hjalmar nodded, then jumped out of bed and turned his great-grandfather’s portrait to face the wall, not wanting it to interrupt them as it had the day before. "Now, you must tell me stories about five green peas living in one pod; or about the chickweed that courted the chickweed; or the proud darning needle who thought she was an embroidery needle."

title: OLE-LUK-OIE, THE DREAM-GOD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"You can have too much of a good thing," Ole-Luk-Oie remarked. "You know I prefer to show you something, so let me introduce you to my brother. His name is also Ole-Luk-Oie, but he only visits once and when he does, he takes you away on his horse, sharing tales as you ride. He knows just two stories—one is so incredibly beautiful that no one can imagine anything like it; but the other is dark and scary, making it impossible to describe." Then Ole-Luk-Oie lifted Hjalmar up to the window. "There, now you can see my brother, the other Ole-Luk-Oie; he’s also known as Death. You’ll notice he’s not as frightening as picture books portray; instead of a skeleton, he’s wearing a silver-embroidered coat and a splendid hussar uniform, with a velvet cape flying behind him as he rides. Look how he gallops!" Hjalmar watched as this Ole-Luk-Oie scooped up both young and old, carrying them away on his horse. Some he placed in front and others behind, but he always asked first, "How are your report cards looking?"

"Yes, but let me see for myself," he would respond, and they had no choice but to show him the books. Those who had "Very Good" or "Exceedingly Good" in their grades sat at the front, hearing the beautiful story, while those with "Middling" or "Tolerably Good" stayed at the back, listening to the frightful tale. They trembled and cried, wanting to jump off the horse but couldn't seem to unfasten themselves.

"Why, Death is quite a splendid Ole-Luk-Oie," Hjalmar concluded. "I’m not scared of him one bit."

"You needn’t fear him," said Ole-Luk-Oie, "if you take care to keep a good conduct record."

"Now that’s very enlightening," murmured the great-grandfather's portrait. "Sometimes it is useful to express an opinion;" and with that, he was quite satisfied.

These are just some of the tales and adventures of Ole-Luk-Oie. I hope he visits you tonight too and shares some more.

title: OUR AUNT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
You should have met our aunt; she was delightful! Not in the way you usually think of charm, but she was genuinely kind and had her own unique sense of humor. She was everything a memorable person should be—someone others could talk about and laugh at. She could easily have stepped right out of a play, living entirely for the theater and everything that happened there. She was a dignified matron, but Agent Fabs, who affectionately called her "Flabs," claimed she was obsessed with the stage.

"The theater is my school," she would say, "it’s where I learn everything. I’ve even revived Biblical stories through it. Take 'Moses' and 'Joseph in Egypt'—those are operas to cherish! I gain insights into universal history, geography, and understanding people from the theater. The French plays teach me all about the vibrant, albeit tricky, life in Paris. I’ve wept countless tears over 'La Famille Roquebourg'—how that poor man must drink himself to death just so the lady can marry another! In the fifty years since I’ve been subscribed to the theater, I've shed more tears than I can count!"

Our aunt knew every play, every backdrop, every character, and every actor who had graced the stage. It seemed like her life only truly began when the theater was open for nine months each year. Summers without a summer theater only reminded her how old she was getting; on the flip side, a midnight performance felt like extra life added to her years. Unlike everyone else who announced spring with news of the stork or the first strawberries, she would declare the arrival of autumn with, "Did you hear? They’re selling theater boxes now; the performances will start soon!"

She evaluated living spaces based entirely on how close they were to the theater. It broke her heart to leave her cozy lane behind the playhouse to move to a bigger street farther away, where she had no neighbors across from her.

title: OUR AUNT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"At home," she would exclaim, "my windows should be my opera box! You can’t just sit and introspect all day; you need to see people. But now I live just like it’s the country. If I want to see anyone, I have to sit on my kitchen sink since that’s the only place I can see neighbors. Back in my charming lane, I could look straight into the ironworker’s shop, and it was only a quick stroll of three hundred steps to the theater. Now, it feels like three thousand!"

Our aunt sometimes fell ill, but no matter how she felt, she never missed a performance. Once, the doctor advised her to soak her feet in a bran bath, and sure enough, she followed his orders while making her way to the theater, keeping her feet in bran even as she sat down. She would have considered it a happy ending if she had passed away at the theater, just like Thorwaldsen did.

She could never fathom that heaven wouldn’t have its own theater. While it wasn’t promised, it seemed entirely reasonable to imagine. Surely, countless talented actors and actresses who had passed on would have a stage to showcase their talents.

Our aunt had an ‘electric wire’ connecting her room to the theater. Every day around coffee time, she received a telegram with the words, "Herr Sivertsen is at the machinery;" he was the one who signaled when the curtain would go up and down and changed the scenes.

From him, she got quick summaries of each performance. His take on Shakespeare’s 'Tempest' was, "What nonsense! So much fuss for something that opens with ‘Water in front of the wings.’" In other words, the water needed to be positioned a certain way. But if the same backdrop stuck around for five acts, he would call it a sensible, well-written play, one that performed itself without needing much effort.

title: OUR AUNT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Back in the day—around thirty years ago, as our aunt liked to say—she and Herr Sivertsen were younger. During that time, he was already working the stage machinery and was, as she called him, her benefactor. It used to be common practice to admit audiences into the ‘flies,’ the area above the stage, during performances, and every machinist had a few tickets to share. Often, the flies were filled with notable company; it was rumored that the wives of generals and privy councillors would join up there. It was fascinating to watch how people moved around the stage when the curtain was down.

Our aunt had been there multiple times, whether it was a tragedy or a ballet; the performances with the most characters on stage were the most thrilling to view from the flies. The view was dim up there, and many people brought supper with them. One time, three apples and a sizable piece of bread with butter and sausage fell into the dungeon of Ugolino, where that poor man was meant to starve. This caused quite a stir, and laughter erupted from the audience. The sausage was one of the main reasons the management decided to stop allowing spectators in the flies.

"But I attended thirty-seven times," our aunt proudly stated, "and I’ll always remember Mr. Sivertsen for that."

On the very last night when the flies were still open, the 'Judgment of Solomon' was performed, which our aunt clearly remembered. Through her connection with her benefactor, Herr Sivertsen, she was able to secure a free admission for Agent Fabs, even though he hadn’t earned it at all—he always joked about the theater and teased our aunt; still, she got him a ticket to see the play from above because he wanted to check out the actors from a different perspective.

"Those were his exact words, just like him," our aunt recalled.

He peered down from above during the 'Judgment of Solomon' and fell asleep. You would think he’d just come from a dinner filled with toasts. He dozed off and ended up locked in. When he woke up, he started telling a story, but our aunt wouldn’t believe a word of it.

title: OUR AUNT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"The 'Judgment of Solomon' was over," he insisted, "and all the spectators had left, but that’s when the real show began, the after-piece, which was the best part of all. Then real life unfolded. It wasn’t just the 'Judgment of Solomon' being performed—it turned into a real courtroom drama on stage." And Agent Fabs had the audacity to try to convince our aunt of this story. That’s the gratitude he showed for her getting him a ticket.

What did the agent say? It was amusing but riddled with sarcasm.

"It was pretty dark up there," the agent remarked, "but then the magic kicked in—a tremendous performance, 'The Judgment in the Theater.' The box-keepers were ready, and each viewer had to show their ghostly passbook to determine if they could enter with their hands free or bound, and whether they needed a muzzle. Those who arrived late when the show had already started, along with youngsters struggling with their timing, were tied outside and given soft slippers to wear, which allowed them inside just before the next act started—and yes, they had muzzles, too. And that’s when the 'Judgment on the Stage' began."

"All nonsense, without a shred of truth," our aunt responded flatly.

The artist wishing to reach Paradise had to climb a staircase he had drawn, but which no one could actually ascend. That was his punishment for mishaps in perspective. Every plant and building that the props guy had painstakingly placed in inappropriate locations, the poor guy had to correct before dawn if he wanted to enter Paradise. Let Herr Fabs figure out his entry—whatever he said about the actors, the tragedians, the comedians, the singers, and the dancers was the most outrageous of all. Mr. Fabs—more like Flabs! He didn’t deserve to go in, and our aunt refused to dignify his comments.

title: OUR AUNT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once, our aunt found herself scared and trembling in her sacred space of joy, the theater. It was a winter day, one of those long days where the sky stays gray. It was brutally cold and snowy, but our aunt had to be at the theater. They were performing a little opera and a grand ballet, along with a prologue and an epilogue to boot; it would last until late at night. She absolutely had to go, so she borrowed her lodger's fur-lined boots—warm boots that went all the way up her legs.

She made it to the theater and to her box; the boots were cozy, and she kept them on. Suddenly, there was a shout of "Fire!" Smoke started streaming from one of the side scenes, pouring down from the flies, and chaos erupted. People rushed out, and our aunt was the last one in her box, "on the second tier, left-hand side, because that’s where the scenery looks best," she would often say. "The sets are always arranged to be most visually appealing from the King’s side." She wanted to exit, but in the panic, people in front of her had slammed the box door shut. There she was, trapped, unable to go forward or back into the next box because the divider was too high. She called out, but no one heard her. Peering down at the empty row of boxes below, she felt surprisingly young and nimble. She thought about jumping down, and with one leg over the partition and the other resting on the seat, she sat there like she was on horseback, snug in her flowered cloak and one leg dangling in that enormous fur boot. It was a sight to see; once onlookers spotted her, they heard her cries and saved her from the flames—thankfully, the theater wasn’t burned to the ground.

That night became the most unforgettable evening of her life, and she was thankful she couldn’t see her own reflection because it would have been mortifying.

Every Sunday, her benefactor from the machinery department, Herr Sivertsen, would visit her, but the days in between felt so long. During those times, she had a small child come over "for the scraps;" meaning, to eat up the leftovers from her meals. This little one worked in the ballet and certainly needed the nourishment. Sometimes she appeared as an elf, other times as a page; her most challenging role was the lion's hind leg in 'The Magic Flute.' But as she got older, she was able to portray the forelegs of the lion instead. She only earned half a guilder for that, while the hind legs fetched a whole guilder. However, she had to walk bent over and get no fresh air. "That was fascinating to hear," said our aunt.

She deserved to outlive the theater itself, but she wasn’t meant to last that long; she didn’t pass in the theater but rather peacefully in her bed. Her final words had significant meaning.

At her passing, she left behind about five hundred dollars. We deduce this from the interest, which amounted to twenty dollars. Our aunt planned this legacy for an elderly spinster who had no close friends; it was meant to fund a yearly subscription for a seat in the second tier, on the left side, for Saturday evenings because, as her will stated, "two pieces were always performed on that night"; the only condition for the recipient of this legacy was that she think of our aunt every Saturday evening as she rested in her grave.

title: POULTRY MEG'S FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Poultry Meg was the only one living in the new, elegant house built for the chickens and ducks belonging to the manor. It was located where the old knight's residence once stood, complete with its tower, pointed rooftops, moat, and drawbridge. Nearby was a wild tangle of trees and bushes; that was where the garden used to be, stretching out to a large lake, now turned into moorland. Crows and choughs flew overhead, squawking loudly among the ancient trees. The flocks of birds didn’t seem to diminish when someone shot at them; in fact, they often grew larger. You could hear their cries even inside the poultry house, where Poultry Meg sat, surrounded by ducklings scampering around her wooden shoes. She knew every bird and duck from the moment it hatched, and she cared deeply for them, taking pride in the fancy house built just for her fowls. Her own little room was tidy and welcoming; that was what the gracious lady who owned the house wanted. The lady often visited, bringing along noble guests to whom she proudly showcased "the hens' and ducks' barracks," as she affectionately called it.

Inside the house, there was a cupboard for clothes, an armchair, and even a chest of drawers. On top of the drawers was a shiny metal plate engraved with the name "Grubbe," belonging to the noble family that once lived there. They found the brass plate while digging the foundation; the clerk mentioned it had little value aside from being an antique. He was knowledgeable about the place and its history, thanks to his books, and had written numerous notes kept in his desk. But perhaps the oldest crow knew even more than he did, cawing in its own way about the past, although the clever clerk couldn’t understand that.

title: POULTRY MEG'S FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
After those hot summer days, mist sometimes hung over the moor, making it seem like a lake was hidden behind the old trees where crows and jackdaws fluttered about. This was how it appeared in the time of the good Knight Grubbe—when the manor house had its thick red walls. Back then, the dog chain reached all the way over the gate; you entered through the tower into a paved hallway that led to the rooms. The windows were narrow, with small panes, even in the grand hall where dancing used to take place. But by the time of the last Grubbe, no one remembered dancing in that hall, although an old drum still sat there, a remnant of the music that had once filled the space. A beautifully carved cupboard held rare flower roots because Lady Grubbe loved her plants, nurturing trees and shrubs. Her husband preferred hunting wolves and boars, often taking their little daughter Marie along for part of the journey. Even at five, she sat proudly on her horse, her large black eyes scanning her surroundings. She found it amusing to whip the hunting dogs, while her father wished she would play with the peasant boys who would come running to gawk at their lord.

Nearby, in a clay hut next to the knight's residence, lived a peasant named Jon who had a son, Soren, the same age as the young noble lady. Soren was a skilled climber, often fetching bird nests for her. The birds squawked in protest, and one of the larger birds even pecked him above the eye, causing a scare that he might lose it; luckily, he was unharmed. Marie Grubbe called him her Soren, which was a great privilege for him and a benefit for his father, Jon, who had once been punished by being made to ride a wooden horse. This horse had four wooden legs and a single narrow plank for a back; Jon had to straddle it, with heavy bricks tied to his feet to ensure he couldn't sit comfortably. He made all kinds of grimaces, and Soren cried, pleading for Marie to help. She immediately demanded that her father's punishments stop, and when they didn’t listen, she stamped her foot and tugged at her father's sleeve until it tore. She got what she wanted, and Jon was freed from his awkward situation.

title: POULTRY MEG'S FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Lady Grubbe walked over to her daughter, gently brushing her hair back from her forehead, gazing at her with affection; but Marie didn’t understand the reason behind her mother’s soft look.

Marie wanted to visit the hounds instead of following her mother down to the garden, where the lake bloomed with water lilies and the bulrush heads nodded in the reeds. "How lovely!" she exclaimed. At that time, a rare tree she had planted stood proudly in the garden. It was called the blood-beech—a dark tree, almost like a shadow among the others, with deep brown leaves. This tree needed plenty of sunshine; otherwise, it would turn bright green like all the other trees, losing its unique beauty. The tall chestnut trees housed many birds' nests, as did the thickets and grassy meadows. It seemed like the birds knew they were safe here, no hunters allowed.

Little Marie often came to this place with Soren. He was a good climber, just as we’ve mentioned, and they would return with eggs and fluffy chicks. The birds of all sizes flew around in a panic; the peewits from the fields and the crows and jackdaws from the treetops squawked loudly; it made for quite a ruckus, just like the uproar families create today.

"What are you kids up to?" scolded the gentle lady, "That's not right!"

Soren felt embarrassed, and even the little noble lady looked down a bit; but then he responded in a quick, funny way,

"Caw-caw! Get out of here!" shouted the large black birds, and they flew off; but they returned the next day because they felt at home here.

Sadly, the gentle lady didn’t stay with them long, as the good Lord took her away; truly, her home was more with Him than in the knight's house. The church bells tolled sadly as they carried her to her grave, with tears in the eyes of the poor folk, for she had been kind to them.

After she was gone, no one tended to her gardens, and they fell into disrepair. People said that the knight Grubbe was harsh, but even his young daughter managed to get her way with him. He would laugh and let her have what she wanted. Now twelve years old, she had a strong build. With her dark eyes, she looked right through people, rode her horse like a pro, and could shoot a gun as well as any seasoned hunter.

title: POULTRY MEG'S FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
One day, important visitors came to the area—none other than the young King and his half-brother, Lord Ulric Frederick Gyldenlowe. They were eager to hunt wild boar and spend a few days at Grubbe Castle.

Gyldenlowe sat beside Marie at the dining table, took her hand, and kissed her, treating her like family; but she slapped him on the face, protesting that she couldn’t stand him, which sparked great laughter, as it seemed rather amusing.

And indeed, it was quite funny, for five years later, on her seventeenth birthday, a messenger arrived bearing a letter where Lord Gyldenlowe asked for her hand in marriage. What a surprise!

"He is the finest and most gallant gentleman in the entire land," Grubbe the knight said; "that’s not a thing to take lightly."

"I’m not that interested in him," replied Marie Grubbe; but she didn’t dismiss the idea of the most distinguished man in the land who sat next to the king.

Silverware and fine linens headed to Copenhagen via ship, while the bride traveled by land over ten days. But fate threw them a curveball; contrary winds delayed the ship for four long months until it finally arrived—but by then, Lady Gyldenlowe had vanished.

"I’d rather sleep on coarse sackcloth than in his soft beds," she declared. "I’d prefer to walk barefoot than ride in a carriage with him!"

Late one evening in November, two women rode into the town of Aarhuus. They were Lady Gyldenlowe (Marie Grubbe) and her maid. They had traveled from Weile after arriving by ship from Copenhagen. They checked in at Lord Grubbe's stone mansion in Aarhuus. Grubbe was not thrilled about this visit. Marie faced unpleasant remarks; however, she did get a bedroom assigned and was served her morning beer soup, but her father's darker side flared up against her, which she was not used to. She wasn’t known for being docile, and people often respond in kind. She spoke openly with bitterness and resentment about her husband, declaring she wouldn’t live with him; she was too honorable for that.

title: POULTRY MEG'S FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
A year passed, but it wasn’t a happy one. There were harsh words exchanged between father and daughter, which should never happen. Bad words lead to bad outcomes. What would become of such a tumultuous circumstance?

One day, her father stated, "We can’t coexist under the same roof. Move to our old manor house, but you’d better hold your tongue and not spread any lies among the townsfolk."

And so, they parted ways. She went with her maid to the old castle where she had been born, near where her gentle, devout mother lay in the church vault. Only an old cowherd lived in the courtyard, making him the sole inhabitant of the place. Cobwebs hung thick with dust in the rooms, and the garden overran with wild growth; hops and climbing plants tangled over the trees and shrubs, while the hemlock and nettle grew wilder and stronger. The blood-beech had been overshadowed by other trees and now stood among them, its once-proud leaves fading to green, losing its distinct charm. Crows and choughs flew overhead in large groups, cawing away as if they had something vital to share, as if announcing, "She’s back! The girl who took our eggs and chicks! And that thief who helped her? He’s punished now for not behaving!"

This was what the clerk recounted in our time; he had collected this information from books and notes. It was safely stored away in his desk.

"The world spins both up and down," he remarked. "It’s odd to think about."

Now let’s see how Marie Grubbe fared. We shouldn't forget Poultry Meg, who is cheerful and content in her charming hen house, even in our time. Marie Grubbe sat in her time, but not with the same spirit that old Poultry Meg showed.

title: POULTRY MEG'S FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Winter passed, and spring and summer came and went, eventually giving way to autumn, with its damp and chilly sea mist. Life in the old manor house was lonely and desolate. Marie Grubbe took her gun in hand and went hunting on the heath, aiming for hares, foxes, and any birds she could bag. Several times, she bumped into the noble Sir Palle Dyre of Norrebak, who roamed with his own gun and hunting dogs. He was tall and strong, proud of his prowess when they chatted. He often bragged that he could match the late Mr. Brockenhuus of Egeskov, whom many still spoke about. Palle Dyre had even hung an iron chain with a hunting horn at his gateway, and when returning home, he would grab the chain to lift himself and his horse off the ground while blowing the horn.

"Come and see me do it, Dame Marie," he challenged. "You can breathe freely at Norrebak."

When she visited his castle is unclear, but it was inscribed on the altar candlestick in Norrebak Church that they were gifts from Palle Dyre and Marie Grubbe from Norrebak Castle.

Palle Dyre was a big, hefty man who had a hearty appetite for drink. He could down spirits like a sponge and snored like a barn full of pigs, appearing red-faced and bloated.

"He’s treacherous and malicious," said Dame Palle Dyre, Grubbe's daughter. She soon grew tired of her life with him, but that didn’t improve things.

One day, the table was set, and the food went cold. Palle Dyre was out hunting foxes, and the gracious lady was nowhere in sight. By midnight, Palle Dyre returned, but Dame Dyre didn’t show up until the next morning. She had turned her back on Norrebak and ridden away without a word.

The weather was gray and wet; the wind turned cold, and a swarm of squawking black birds flew overhead. They still had a home, unlike her.

title: POULTRY MEG'S FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
First, she traveled south, deep into German land. A couple of gold rings with gems were sold for money; then she turned east, then west again. With nothing to eat in sight, she cursed everything, even the good Lord above, consumed by misery. Soon, her body grew feeble too, and she could hardly lift a foot. The peewit erupted as she stumbled over the mound where it built its nest, screaming, "You thief! You thief!" She had never stolen her neighbor's belongings, but she remembered how she’d once caused eggs and baby birds to be taken from their nests.

From her spot, she could see the sand dunes. Fishermen lived by the shore, but she was too weak to reach them. The large white seagulls flew above, shrieking just like the crows and jackdaws in the manor garden. As the birds flew close to her, she felt as if they turned black as crows, and then everything faded to darkness.

When she opened her eyes, she found herself being lifted. A strong man had her in his arms, and she looked up into his bearded face. He sported a scar over one eye that split his eyebrow. Despite her frail state, he carried her onto the ship, where he faced a reprimand from the captain.

The next day, the ship set sail. Madame Grubbe hadn’t been put ashore, so she traveled with it. But surely she will return, right? Yes, but when and where?

The clerk could recount this too; it was no tale he spun himself. He had the entire strange saga from an old, authentic book, one that we can read ourselves. The Danish historian, Ludwig Holberg, who penned many insightful texts and comedies, chronicled his encounter with Marie Grubbe. It’s an interesting read, but we still can’t forget Poultry Meg, who sits happily in her cozy hen house.

The ship sailed away with Marie Grubbe. That’s where we left off.

title: POULTRY MEG'S FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The plague was sweeping through Copenhagen in 1711. The Queen of Denmark retreated to her German home, the King left the capital, and everyone who could hurried away. Even the students, including those who had free room and board, abandoned the city. The last student to remain at the free college finally departed too. It was two in the morning. He lugged his knapsack, filled more with books than clothes. A chill mist enveloped the town; not a soul was in sight, while the doors around were marked with crosses to indicate the plague was inside, or the occupants had perished. A clattering wagon rolled by; the coachman waved his whip, and the horses sped past. The wagon was full of corpses. The young student covered his nose with his hand and inhaled from a sponge soaked in strong spirits he carried in a brass case. From a small tavern down the street came sounds of revelry and laughter from people drinking through the night, trying to forget the plague that lurked outside, ready to claim them as it had others. The student made his way to the canal at the castle bridge, where a few small ships were docked; one prepared to sail away to escape the afflicted city.

"If God spares our lives and grants us a fair wind, we’re heading to Gronmud, near Falster," said the captain, asking the student his name if he wished to join them.

"Ludwig Holberg," the student replied, a name that at the time sounded like any other. But in the future, it would carry the weight of one of the most distinguished names in Denmark; back then, it was just the name of an unknown young scholar.

The ship smoothly glided past the castle, and it wasn’t yet light when they were out at sea. A gentle breeze filled the sails, and the young student settled back, facing the wind, and dozed off—perhaps not the wisest decision.

By the third day, the ship anchored near the island of Falster.

"Do you know anyone here with whom I might find inexpensive lodging?" Holberg asked the captain.

"You might have good fortune with the ferry-woman at Borrehaus," the captain advised. "But tread lightly; her name is Mother Soren Sorensen Muller. She might fly into a rage if you’re too polite. Her husband is in custody for a crime, which is why she runs the ferry herself—she can hold her own."

title: POULTRY MEG'S FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The student took his knapsack and made his way to the ferry house. The door was unlocked, swinging open as he entered a room with a brick floor. There, a large bench, covered with leather, was the main piece of furniture. A white hen had her chicks with her and had knocked over the pipkin of water, causing a puddle on the floor. The place was empty except for a cradle with a child inside. The ferry boat returned with just one passenger, and it was difficult to tell if that person was male or female. Wrapped in a large cloak with a hood, the figure approached.

The figure turned out to be a woman who stepped into the room. She carried herself with a certain elegance when straightening her back; proud eyes sparkled beneath her dark eyebrows. It was Mother Soren, the ferryman's wife, although the crows and jays might have used another name we know better.

Her demeanor seemed grim, and she wasn’t keen on chatting; nonetheless, it was agreed that the student could stay at her house indefinitely while things remained grim in Copenhagen.

Occasionally, honest townsfolk visited the ferry house from the nearby town. Individuals like Frank, the cutler, and Sivert, the exciseman, would enjoy a mug of beer together with the student, who was an intelligent young man. He had a good grasp of his lessons, as they referred to them; he could read Greek and Latin and was well-versed in various scholarly topics.

"The less you know, the less it weighs on you," Mother Soren remarked.

"You need to work hard," Holberg said one day while she was washing clothes in strong soapy water and had to chop the logs for the fire by herself.

"Have you had to labor like this since childhood?"

"You can read it in my hands," she replied, extending them to show hew small they were, yet tough and sturdy, with bitten nails. "You're educated and can read."

title: POULTRY MEG'S FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
As Christmas approached, heavy snow began to fall. The cold grew harsh; the wind blew sharply as if filled with acid to sting people's faces. But this didn't faze Mother Soren; she wrapped her cloak around her and pulled her hood tight over her head. Early in the afternoon, as darkness descended upon the house, she prepared the hearth with wood and turf, then sat to mend her stockings since no one else was around to do it. Towards evening, she spoke to the student more than usual, discussing her husband.

"He accidentally killed a sailor from Dragor, and for that, he has to spend three years in chains. But he's just an ordinary sailor, and the law remains firm."

"The law applies to nobles as well," Holberg interjected.

"Do you think so?" responded Mother Soren before gazing into the fire for a moment. "Have you heard of Kai Lykke? He had a church torn down; when clergyman Master Martin preached against it, he tossed him into chains and judged him, condemning him to death. Yes, the clergyman had to bow his head at the sentence. Yet somehow, Kai Lykke went free."

"He had the right to act as he did at the time," Holberg argued, "but we have moved beyond those days."

"You might fool a simpleton into believing that," Mother Soren scoffed, rising to tend to the child in the next room. She adjusted the baby for comfort and then prepared the student’s bedding. He received the green coverlet because he felt the cold more than she did, even though he was born in Norway.

On New Year’s morning, the sun shone brightly. The frost was so intense that the fallen snow had become hard, making it possible to walk on it. The bells from the nearby town rang for church. Student Holberg wrapped himself in his woolen cloak, heading to the town.

Above the ferry house, crows and jays soared about with loud calls; their commotion made it hard to hear the church bells ringing. Mother Soren stood outside, filling a brass pot with snow to melt for drinking water. She glanced up at the flock of birds, lost in her own thoughts.

title: POULTRY MEG'S FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
While heading to church, Holberg passed by the tax collector Sivert's house near the town gate. He was invited in for a mug of brown beer mixed with treacle and sugar. The conversation turned to Mother Soren, but the tax collector didn’t know much about her. In truth, not many did. He noted that she didn’t hail from Falster; she once owned some property herself. Her husband was a common sailor with a fiery temper, who had killed another sailor from Dragor, and though he beat her, she defended him.

"I wouldn’t put up with such treatment," said the tax collector's wife. "I come from better stock. My father was a stocking-weaver for the Court."

"And that’s why you married a government official," replied Holberg, bowing to both her and the collector.

On Twelfth Night, the festive evening commemorating the Three Kings, Mother Soren lit a Three Kings candle for Holberg—one made of animal fat with three wicks that she had prepared herself.

"For each man?" she asked, staring at him intently.

"For each of the wise men from the East," Holberg responded.

"You mean it that way," she replied, before falling silent for a long while. This evening, he learned more about her than he had up to that point.

"You speak fondly of your husband," Holberg observed, "yet people say he mistreats you daily."

title: POULTRY MEG'S FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"That's my affair," she shot back. "The beatings might have done me some good when I was a child; now, I suppose I earn them for my sins. But I know the good he’s done me," she continued, standing tall. "When I lay sick out on that desolate heath, and no one pitied me, only the crows and jays came to feast upon me, he lifted me in his arms and had to take the heat for carrying me on board the ship. I’m not the sickly type, and I recovered fine. Every man has his way, and Soren has his; but you can’t judge a horse by its saddle. Overall, I've had a more pleasant existence with him than I did with the so-called most noble and gallant man in the King’s service. I had the Stadtholder Gyldenlowe, the King’s half-brother, as my husband; and then I took Palle Dyre. Each man has his merits, just as I have mine. That was quite a lot of gossip, but now you know everything about me."

That was Marie Grubbe! Fate played a peculiar hand with her. She didn’t live to celebrate many more Three Kings' festivals; Holberg recorded that she passed away in June 1716. However, he didn’t note, for he wasn’t aware, that many large black birds circled above the ferry house when Mother Soren, as she was known, lay there deceased. They didn’t squawk, as if they understood that silence should prevail at a funeral. Once she was buried, the birds vanished, although that evening an enormous number of crows and choughs appeared at the old manor house in Jutland, shrieking as if there was something important to convey. Perhaps they were talking of the little boy who once took their eggs, of the peasant’s son made to wear an iron garter, and of the noble young lady who ended up as a ferryman's wife.

And the whole family shouted, "Bravo! Bravo!" as the old house was torn down.

title: POULTRY MEG'S FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"They’re still cheering, but there’s nothing to celebrate," the clerk remarked while recounting the tale. "The family is extinct, the house has been demolished, and in its place stands a fine poultry house with gilded weather vanes and old Poultry Meg. She’s quite happy in her beautiful residence. If she hadn’t ended up here," the old clerk added, "she would have had to go to the workhouse."

The pigeons cooed around her, the turkeys gobbled, and the ducks quacked.

"Nobody recognizes her," they muttered. "She doesn’t belong to any family. It’s just kindness that has her here. She has neither a drake for a father nor a hen for a mother, and no descendants to speak of."

Despite that, she came from a noble lineage, although she was unaware of it, and the old clerk remained oblivious too; even with all his records. But one of the old crows knew her history and shared it. Her own mother and grandmother had told tales of Poultry Meg's lineage. And we remember the grandmother. We saw her, as a child, riding across the bridge with a proud look, as though this entire world belonged to her and all the birds' nests too; we caught glimpses of her on the heath, by the sand dunes, and finally, in the ferry house. The last descendant of her line returned to the old home, where the old castle had once stood, amidst the screeching of the wild black birds; yet here she was among the gentle birds, who recognized her and held her dear. Poultry Meg had nothing more to wish for; she looked forward to her inevitable passing, being old enough to accept it.

And Poultry Meg has a fine grave, known only to the old crow, if that same crow hasn’t passed on by now.

And now we know the full story of the old manor house, its past owners, and all of Poultry Meg's family.

title: SOMETHING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I want to be someone and make a difference in the world," said the oldest of the five brothers. "I don’t mind how humble my role is, as long as I can do some good, which will still mean something. I plan to be a brickmaker; bricks are always in demand, and I’ll be genuinely contributing."

"Your 'something' isn't enough for me," replied the second brother. "What you're suggesting is nothing more than basic work that could even be done by a machine. No, I’d rather be a builder; that’s something real. A builder gains respect, becomes a citizen, has his own signage, and his own place for workers. So, I’ll become a builder. If all goes well, I’ll grow to be a master and have my own crew, and my wife will enjoy the status of a master’s wife. Now that’s what I call something."

"I think it's all nothing," said the third brother. "In reality, there are many people in town who have a higher status than a master builder. You may be honest, but even as a master, you’ll just rank with the average. I know a better path. I want to be an architect, placing myself among those with wealth and intellect who appreciate art. I’ll have to start as a laborer or an apprentice, wearing a paper cap even though I now wear a silk hat. I’ll have to bring beers and spirits for the other workers, and they'll address me in an informal way, which I’ll take as an insult. But I’ll endure it, seeing it as just a disguise, a play-acting phase that will disappear once I complete my apprenticeship. After that, I’ll enter an academy to learn drawing and earn the title of architect. I might even achieve recognition and have my name honored, allowing me to build memorable structures. Isn’t that worth living for?"

"That’s not for me," declared the fourth brother. "I won’t just follow in others’ footsteps or mimic their work. I want to be a genius and surpass all of you. I’ll create a new style of architecture, using materials that suit the local climate and reflect our national identity. I’ll develop a unique blueprint that embodies my creative vision."

title: SOMETHING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"But what if the climate and materials aren’t suitable?" asked the fifth brother. "That could be a big setback for you and affect your designs. National pride can easily turn into pretentiousness, and trends can spiral out of control, just like youth often does. It's clear to me that none of you will ever truly amount to anything significant, despite your optimism. But go ahead, do what you want; I refuse to imitate you. I plan to stay above it all and critique your efforts. I’ll point out the flaws in your work, and that will definitely be something worthwhile." And he kept his word by becoming a critic.

People would remark about this fifth brother, saying, "He’s precise and sharp-minded, but he accomplishes nothing." Ironically, they believed he must amount to something.

Now, here’s a little tale that will never really conclude. As long as the world exists, there will always be men like these five brothers. So what became of them? Were they each nothing or something? You’re about to find out; it’s quite a story.

The oldest brother, the brickmaker, quickly learned that each completed brick earned him a small coin, even if it was just a copper one. And a handful of coppers can add up to a shiny shilling; when someone knocks on a door with a collection of these coins—whether it’s the baker, butcher, or tailor—the door swings open, and he can get whatever he wants. So you can see why bricks are valuable. Some bricks, however, may crumble or break, but the older brother still found a use for them.

On a high earth bank forming a dike by the coast, a poor woman named Margaret wanted to build herself a house. She received not only the imperfect bricks but also a few good ones from the eldest brother, who was kind-hearted despite never achieving anything beyond making bricks. The poor woman managed to construct a tiny house; it was small with a crooked window, a low door, and a straw roof that could have been better, yet it offered shelter and a view over the wild sea crashing against the dike. The salt waves would scatter their foam all over it, but the house stood strong for a long time, even after the brother who gave her the bricks had passed away.

title: SOMETHING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The second brother, of course, could build better than poor Margaret since he had completed an apprenticeship. Once his training was over, he packed his bag and set off on his journey, singing the journeyman’s song,

"While I’m young, I can roam with cheer, Building new houses far and near; Bright dreams of home fill my mind, No matter how far I wander behind."

"Cheers to a workman’s joyful life! There’s someone back home who thinks of me; I can never forget home and friends, and I plan to be a master someday."

And that’s exactly what he did. Upon returning home, he became a master builder—constructing house after house until they formed an entire street, which, once completed, became a true delight for the town. These houses created a home for him in return, though how could houses build a home? If you asked the houses, they couldn’t tell you, but the people understood, saying, "Certainly, the street built his house for him." It wasn’t huge, and the floor was made of lime, but when he danced with his bride on that lime-covered floor, it sparkled bright to him, and flowers seemed to bloom from every stone in the walls, decorating their space like the finest tapestries. It really was a lovely home, and within it was a happy couple. The corporate flag fluttered outside while journeymen and apprentices cheered, "Hurrah!" He had earned his place, made something of himself, and eventually passed away, which was also "something."

Next, we have the architect—the third brother—who started as a carpenter’s apprentice, wore a cap, and did odd jobs, but later attended an academy and became a respected architect. Yes, while the master builder's homes may have built his house, the street was named after the architect, and the most beautiful house on that street became his. Now that was truly something; he was recognized as "something" because he had titles before and after his name. His children were considered "wellborn," and when he died, his widow was treated as a woman of stature, which was indeed "something." His name remained etched in stone at the corner of the street and was known by everyone. Yes, this was also "something."

title: SOMETHING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
What about the family genius—the fourth brother—who aspired to create something new? He attempted to construct a grand upper story on his own, but it collapsed, and he fell with it, breaking his neck. However, he received a grand funeral, attended by city flags and music; flowers were showered upon the pavement, and three lengthy eulogies were delivered, each one longer than the last. He would have appreciated this while alive, along with the newspaper articles written about him, as he loved the attention. A monument was erected over his grave—just an upper story over him, but that was still "something." Now he lay dead, alongside his three brothers.

The youngest brother—the critic—outlived them all, and that suited him just fine. It gave him the chance to have the last say, which mattered a lot to him. People often remarked about his sharp mind. Finally, his time came, and he passed away, only to find himself waiting at the gates of heaven with another soul. Who else was there but old Margaret from the house on the dyke? "It’s clearly for contrast that I, along with this poor soul, should arrive at the same time," said the critic. "And who are you, my dear lady? Are you trying to get in, too?"
