The old woman curtsied as well as she could; she thought he might be St. Peter himself speaking to her. "I’m just a poor old woman without any family. I’m old Margaret who lived in the house on the dyke."

"Well, what have you done—what grand deed have you accomplished down there?" he inquired, mainly to keep the conversation going, as he felt weary standing and waiting.

"What have I done?" she answered. "Honestly, I haven't achieved anything that warrants these gates opening for me. If I get in, it would only be due to mercy."

"How did you leave the world?" he asked, simply to say something since he was feeling quite tired of waiting.

title: SOMETHING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "How I left?" she explained. "It’s hard to say. In the last years, I was sick and miserable, barely managing to get out of bed without suddenly facing the cold. The last winter was brutal, but I got through it. There were a few mild days, as you might know. The ice covered the lake, stretching far beyond sight. People from town came and danced and skated on it; I heard beautiful music floating into my little room. One evening, when the moon rose gracefully, though not yet full, I looked out over the wide sea. Right where the sky met the sea, I saw an unusual white cloud. I watched it, noticing a little black spot in the middle, which got larger; my experience told me what it meant—a terrifying storm was coming that would overwhelm those people on the ice. Young and old, everyone in the city was out there. If no one noticed the sign or understood its meaning, who would warn them? I felt a surge of strength and resolve, and stumbled out of bed to reach my window. Although I was weak and exhausted, I managed to open it. I saw people laughing and playing on the ice, the beautiful flags dancing with the wind. But that ominous white cloud with the black spot loomed over them. I yelled as loud as I could, but no one heard me from so far away. Soon, the storm would hit, the ice would break, and they’d be lost. They couldn’t hear me, and I couldn’t reach them. I thought if I could only bring them to safety! Then it struck me; I’d rather set fire to my bed and let my house burn down than watch so many perish. I lit a fire, and the flames climbed high, serving as a warning. I barely made it to the door before I collapsed. The flames waved towards me and danced on the window, rising above the roof. The people outside saw the fire and rushed to save the poor sick woman, thinking I was going to burn. No one hesitated to run towards me. As they hurried, I felt a rush of air, and the sound of breaking ice thundered all around. The spring flood was lifting the ice, shattering it into pieces. But the people reached the sea wall just in time, and I had saved them all. However, I could not withstand the cold and shock, so I came here to the gates of paradise. I hear they are open to those like me, and now I have no home left on earth; but I don’t think that entitles me to be allowed in here."

title: SOMETHING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Then the gates were opened, and an angel guided the old woman in. She had left behind a single straw from her bed when she lit the fire to save so many lives. This straw had turned into the purest gold—gold that blossomed into eternal flowers and fruits.

"Look," said the angel, pointing to the wonderful straw, "this is what the poor woman has brought. And what do you bring? I know you’ve done nothing; you didn’t even make a single brick. Even if you could return and at least produce a brick, it likely wouldn’t be useful unless done with good intentions, which is always something. But you cannot return to earth, and I can’t assist you."

Then the humble old mother from the dyke house pleaded for him. She said, "His brother made all the stones and bricks and sent them to me to build my little home; that was a great deal for someone like me. Couldn’t these bricks be a kind of wall for him in his favor? It’s a matter of mercy; he needs it now, and here’s the essence of mercy itself."

"Then," replied the angel, "your brother, considered the least among you all, the one whose honest deeds appeared so simple—he's the one who sent you this heavenly gift. You won't be turned away. You can stand at the gate and reflect, and repent for your life on earth; but you won’t be allowed in until you perform one good act of repentance, which will indeed be something for you."

"I could have phrased that better," thought the critic; but he didn’t voice it, which for him was, after all, SOMETHING.

title: SOUP FROM A SAUSAGE SKEWER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"You missed an amazing dinner yesterday!" said an old female mouse to another who hadn't been there. "I got to sit at table twenty-one right under the mouse-king. What a feast we had! It was top-notch. We had moldy bread, a tallow candle, and sausage. Once we finished that, we got to enjoy the same meal all over again—it felt like two feasts in one. Everyone was so friendly, joking around as if we were family. The only thing left were the sausage skewers, which sparked a conversation that eventually led us to the saying, 'Soup from sausage skins.' In the nearby country, they call it 'soup from a sausage skewer.' Everyone had heard the saying, but no one had ever actually tried the soup or knew how to make it. We even toasted to the creator of this soup idea, suggesting he should be made a relief officer for the poor. Funny, right? Then, the old mouse-king stood up and declared that the young lady mouse who figured out the best way to make that celebrated soup would become his queen, and she'd have a year and a day to accomplish it."

"That sounds like a solid offer," replied the other mouse. "But how do you actually make the soup?"

"Ah, that's where things get tricky. All the young lady mice were wondering the same thing. They were eager to become queens, but they didn't want to venture out into the world to learn how to make the soup—something that was essential. Not every mouse is keen to leave her cozy family corner or warm spot by the fireplace, even if the reward is to be a queen. It’s tough to find bacon and cheese out there, plus there’s always the chance of getting eaten by the cat!"

title: SOUP FROM A SAUSAGE SKEWER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Probably thoughts like these kept most of the mice hesitant to explore the world for answers. Only four mice stepped up, eager yet poor. Each wanted to travel to one of the four corners of the world to see which one was the luckiest. They all took a sausage skewer as a walking stick to remind them of their mission. They left home early in May, and only returned the following May, with just three of them making it back. The fourth mouse was nowhere to be found, even with the decision day fast approaching. "Ah, there's always a bit of trouble mixed in with the greatest joys," the mouse-king observed, but he quickly ordered that all mice within a wide radius be invited, and they gathered in the kitchen. The three returning mice stood in a line while a sausage skewer draped in black fabric stood in for the missing one. No one dared to speak until the king told one to share her story. Let's see what she says.

title: SOUP FROM A SAUSAGE SKEWER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "When I first went out into the world," began the little mouse, "I thought I already knew everything, as many young ones do. But I quickly learned otherwise. I jumped right onto a ship heading north, thinking the ship's cook must know how to whip up every dish at sea. Easy enough, I thought, especially with all those sides of bacon and barrels of salt meat, plus moldy flour. Sure, I found plenty of delicious food on board, but I had no chance to learn how to whip up soup from a sausage skewer. We sailed for many days and nights; the ship rocked a lot, and I definitely ended up soaked. Once we landed at the port, which was way up north, I disembarked. It’s a wild experience leaving your cozy little spot at home to hide on a ship, only to find yourself miles away in a strange land. I found myself in vast forests filled with pine and birch trees that smelled so strong I started sneezing, immediately thinking of sausage. I spotted big lakes that looked dark from a distance, as if they were ink, but were actually clear once I got closer. Large swans were gliding across them, and at first, I mistook them for mere foam until they moved. They belonged to the goose family; their walk gave them away—no disguising where you come from. I stuck with other forest and field mice, but they didn’t know much, especially about how to make the soup that had driven my travels. To them, the notion that soup could be made from a sausage skewer seemed completely absurd, and they repeated it all over the forest. They insisted it was an impossible task. Little did I know that on my first night there, I would learn the exact preparation method."

title: SOUP FROM A SAUSAGE SKEWER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "It was the heart of summer, and the mice in the forest explained that’s why everything smelled so strong, the herbs were so fragrant, and why those lakes with white swans seemed so dark yet so clear. Near the edge of the woods, close to a few houses, stood a tall pole, almost the size of a ship's mast, decorated with flower wreaths and fluttering ribbons; it was a Maypole. Young boys and girls danced around it, trying to outshine the fiddlers with their singing. They were having a blast, both at sunset and under the moonlight, but I didn’t join them in their fun. What business did a little mouse have with a Maypole dance? Instead, I settled in the soft moss, gripped my sausage skewer, and watched. The moonlight illuminated one particular spot where a tree was covered in extraordinarily fine moss. If I could say it, it was as soft and luxurious as the fur of the mouse-king, but the green hue made it easy on the eyes. Suddenly, I spotted the cutest little beings marching toward me. They barely reached my knees, and they looked human but were much better proportioned; they called themselves elves. Their clothes were delicate, crafted from flower petals and embellished with bug wings, which looked quite charming. By their behavior, it seemed they were searching for something. I had no idea what it was until one of them spotted me and came over. The leader pointed to my sausage skewer, saying, 'That’s just what we need; look, it’s sharp at the end—perfect!' The longer he inspected my staff, the more thrilled he became. 'I’ll let you borrow it,' I offered, 'but not as a gift.'"

title: SOUP FROM A SAUSAGE SKEWER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"'Oh, we won’t keep it!' they all chimed in, quickly grabbing the skewer I'd given them. They danced over to a patch of beautiful moss and planted it there, using it as their Maypole, which seemed perfectly suited for them. They adorned it so beautifully that it sparkled brightly. Tiny spiders spun gold threads around it, and it became draped with delicate veils and flags that glimmered like snow in the moonlight. Afterward, they took colors from a butterfly's wings, sprinkling them over the white decorations, which shone as if covered in flowers and diamonds. I could barely recognize my sausage skewer anymore. It became a Maypole unlike any seen in the world. Soon, a grand assembly of real elves arrived, dressed in the most exquisite outfits. They invited me to join their celebration, but I was to keep my distance since I was too big for them. Music began, enchanting and sweet, sounding like a thousand glass bells. It echoed and resonated so strongly that I believed it belonged to the swans. The tunes blended with voices from the cuckoo and blackbird. It felt as if the entire forest joined in a magnificent symphony—the laughter of children, bell chimes, and the songs of birds—all coming from the elves’ Maypole. My sausage skewer had transformed into a wondrous instrument. I could hardly believe how much music was generated from it until I remembered to whom it belonged. I was so moved I felt tears, albeit those small ones a mouse can shed, but they were tears of joy. The night was far too short; in summer, nights are never dragged out like they are here. As dawn broke, the gentle breeze ruffled the glassy surface of the forest lake, and all the delicate veils and flags fluttered away, evaporating into thin air. The webs spun by the spiders and all decorations vanished as if they’d never existed. Six elves returned my sausage skewer, requesting I make a wish they would fulfill if possible. I asked if they could tell me the secret to making soup from a sausage skewer."

"'How do we make it?' the chief elf asked with a grin. 'You’ve just seen it; I’m sure you barely recognized your own skewer!"

title: SOUP FROM A SAUSAGE SKEWER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"They think they’re so clever," I thought to myself. Then I explained everything to them, telling them about my journey and what promise I’d made to win the mouse-king's prize for discovering the soup recipe. 'What good will this do for the mouse-king or our grand kingdom?' I asked. 'Can I simply wave around the skewer and say, Look, here it is, and expect soup to magically appear? That would only result in a meal meant for fasting.'"

"The elf dipped a finger into a violet's cup and said, 'Check this out; I’ll bless your pilgrim's staff. When you return home and enter the king's castle, just touch the king with it, and violets will bloom to cover the whole place, even in the dead of winter. I'd say I've sent you home with quite the gift—and then some.'"

Before I could explain what that 'something more' was, I stretched my staff toward the king, and when it touched him, the most stunning bunch of violets sprang forth, filling the air with fragrance. The smell was so intense that the mouse-king ordered the nearest mice to push their tails into the fire to counterbalance the overwhelming scent of the violets, which wasn't a crowd-pleaser for everyone.

"What was this extra 'something' you mentioned?" asked the mouse-king.

title: SOUP FROM A SAUSAGE SKEWER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Well," I replied, "I think it’s what you’d call 'effect.'" I then twirled the staff, and suddenly, there wasn’t a single flower left on it! All I held was the bare skewer, lifting it like a conductor ready to start a concert. 'Violets, as the elf told me,' I continued, 'are for sight, smell, and touch; now we just need to create the feeling of sound and taste.' As I waved my staff, sounds filled the air—not the melodious tunes from the forest or the elf celebration but kitchen sounds—the sizzling and boiling of food. It came all at once, like a gust of wind whistling through the chimneys. Everything seemed to be in chaos, with pots and kettles boiling over. The fire-shovel clattered onto the brass fender, and just as abruptly, all sound ceased. The only thing to hear was the gentle, vaporous tune of the teapot, a rather exceptional melody. It was impossible to tell whether the kettle was just starting to boil or about to cease boiling. The little pot steamed, while the big pot simmered, seemingly with no regard for the other. As I waved my baton more wildly, the pots bubbled over and foamed while the commotion erupted again, causing me to drop my stick.

"That seems like one peculiar soup," said the mouse-king. "Shall we now learn about its preparation?"

"That’s all there is to it," I replied with a bow.

"That’s it!" exclaimed the mouse-king. "Then let's hear what the next mouse has to share."

title: SOUP FROM A SAUSAGE SKEWER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "I was born in the library of a castle," said the second mouse. "Very few members of my family ever got the chance to step into a dining room, let alone a storeroom. The only times I’ve known a kitchen are during my journey and when I’m here today. We often felt starvation in the library, yet we managed to gain significant knowledge. We heard rumors about the royal prize for anyone who could make soup from a sausage skewer. My old grandmother dug up a manuscript she couldn’t read herself, but had heard read, saying, 'Those who are poets can make soup from sausage skewers.' Then she asked me if I was poetic, and I felt completely unqualified. She told me that I needed to become a poet. I asked again what that required because it seemed just as challenging as discovering how to make the soup. Being well-read, my grandmother mentioned three primary qualifications: understanding, imagination, and feeling. 'If you can manage to get those, you’ll be a poet, and making sausage-skewer soup will be a piece of cake.'"

title: SOUP FROM A SAUSAGE SKEWER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "So I set out into the world, heading west to become a poet. I knew understanding was the foundation of everything, as the other two qualities aren't given much value. My first step was finding understanding. Where could I find it? 'Go to the ant and learn wisdom,' said a wise old king. I remembered that from spending time in the library, so I made my way to the nearest ant hill and started observing, hoping to gain wisdom. The ants are the wisest creatures; their actions are like a well-orchestrated equation. They work diligently and lay eggs worriedly for future generations. They see themselves divided into clean and dirty ants, ranked by numbers, with the queen at the top being number ONE; her word is the ultimate authority on anything. She shared plenty of clever thoughts, but to me, they sounded ridiculous. She claimed the ant hill was the highest thing in the world, yet right next to it was a tall tree, which we all could agree was much taller, but she ignored mentioning it. One evening, an ant lost her way on this tree. Though she hadn’t reached the top, she climbed higher than any other ant dared. When she returned, she shared she'd found something above the ant hill, and the others got offended. They punished her by making her wear a muzzle and live alone forever. Not long after, another ant made the same discovery but spoke of it delicately and vaguely. Since she was one of the esteemed ants, they believed her, and when she passed, they honored her memory by erecting a monument from an eggshell, showing their deep respect for science. I observed the ants running around with bags on their backs. Once, I saw an ant drop her load; despite her struggle to lift it, she failed. Two other ants rushed to help her, nearly dropping their loads as well. They eventually had to pause to recover because you must always put yourself first. The ant queen noticed their actions, praising them for kindness and understanding. 'These two qualities,' she stated, 'set us ants apart from all other beings. Understanding is paramount; my wisdom surpasses all.' Saying this, she stood tall on her hind legs so no one could mistake her for someone else. I couldn’t let this chance pass, so I devoured her. If ants teach wisdom, I had just gotten the queen!"

title: SOUP FROM A SAUSAGE SKEWER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "I turned and went closer to the towering tree I mentioned, which was an old oak. With its broad trunk and lush canopy, it was home to a dryad, born with the tree and destined to die with it. I had learned all this in the library, and finding just such a tree thrilled me. When the dryad saw me, she screamed like many women do at sight of mice; she had more than enough reasons to be scared since I might chew through the tree supporting her life. I spoke kindly, calming her down. Eventually, she lifted me gently and I shared my quest with her. She promised that she might help me obtain one of the treasures I sought that very evening. She told me her dear friend Phantaesus was as charming as a love god, often spending hours beneath the rustling branches with her. He called her his dryad and the tree his. The massive oak with its gnarled trunk suited him perfectly. Its roots extend deep into the earth while its branches reach high into the sky, grasping all the values of snow, wind, and sunlight, as it ought to. 'Yes,' the dryad said, 'the birds flit in the branches above, recounting tales of beautiful valleys they have visited in distant lands. A stork has even built a nest on one of the craggy branches, beautifully arranged, and tales of the land of the pyramids are a delight to share with Phantaesus. All of this excites him, but it’s never enough. I have to recount my entire life in the woods, from days as a youngling when the tree was small enough that a stinging nettle overshadowed it, up until now with this great, strong tree. Come, sit beneath the vine and listen. When Phantaesus arrives, I will discreetly grab one of his feathers from his wing for you. That feather is a prized gift for any poet, and it will do marvels for you.'"

title: SOUP FROM A SAUSAGE SKEWER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"When Phantaesus showed up, I plucked the feather," I explained. "I placed it in water until soft. It was heavy and hard to digest, but eventually, I managed to nibble it down. Becoming a poet isn’t easy; there are obstacles along the way. But now with both understanding and imagination, I knew the final ingredient was in the library. A wise person once spoke of novels whose sole purpose seemed to absorb human tears—a sort of emotional sponge. I remembered a few I found utterly tempting; they had been read so much they carried an aura of emotions. I retraced my steps to the library and literally devoured an entire novel, at least the soft part; I left the binding behind. Once I digested that and then a second, I felt a stirring inside. I grabbed a small bit from a third romance and knew I had finally become a poet. I could feel it and began claiming it to others as well. Mind you, I suffered from headaches and backaches, among others. I reflected on every tale involving sausage skewers, anything tied to sticks, staves, or splinters came flooding back to mind. The ant-queen must have had sharp insights. I even recalled a man with a white stick that made him invisible. I considered sticks used as hobby horses, as symbols in music, and even sticks breaking over backs. Countless phrases related to sticks and skewers swarmed my thoughts. Now that I was officially a poet and had toiled hard for it, I figured I could craft poetry about anything. Hence, I promised to share a poetic history of a skewer with you every day of the week. That, you see, is my soup."

"In that case," replied the mouse-king, "let’s hear what the third mouse has to say."

"Squeak, squeak," shouted a little mouse at the kitchen door; it was the fourth mouse, previously presumed dead. She dashed in like a bullet, knocking over the sausage skewer covered in black fabric. She had been running day and night, waiting for a chance to sneak into a goods train and rode the rails, yet arrived almost too late. Pressing forward, she looked disheveled but still managed to confidently speak as if everyone was solely there to hear her. Let’s find out what she said.

title: SOUP FROM A SAUSAGE SKEWER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I immediately headed to the biggest city," she began, "though I can’t recall its name—I have a poor memory for names. I was taken from the railway with some forfeited goods to the jail, but managed to escape and snuck into the turnkey's home. The turnkey was gossiping about his prisoners, particularly one who had said something silly. Those words spiraled into a whole story, eventually noted down that declared, 'The whole thing is like making soup from sausage skewers, but it could cost him his life.'"

"This piqued my interest in the prisoner," the little mouse continued. "I watched for a chance to slip into his cell, as there’s always a mouse-hole behind every locked door. The prisoner looked pale, sporting a big beard and bright, sparkling eyes. A lamp lit the space, but the black walls made it look gloomier. He scratched pictures and verses onto the walls with white chalk, although I didn’t read them. He seemed quite bored with his confinement, so I became a welcome guest. He lured me with breadcrumbs, whistling, and kind words, and I grew fond of him. We shared meals; he gave me cheese and sausage, and I truly cherished him. Ultimately, we formed a delightful bond. He let me scamper about on his hand, arm, and even into his sleeve; I even burrowed into his beard, and he called me his little friend. I forgot why I set out in the first place; I lost the sausage skewer I had tucked away in a crack in the floor—it’s still there, you know. I wanted to stay with him forever because I realized he would have no one to befriend him if I left, which is truly sad. I remained, but he eventually departed. He expressed such sorrow as we said goodbye for the last time, giving me extra bread and cheese before kissing his hand to me. Then he was gone and never returned. I have no clue what happened to him after I left."

title: SOUP FROM A SAUSAGE SKEWER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "The jailer got hold of me after that. He mentioned something about soup from a sausage skewer, but I didn’t trust him. He picked me up, alright, only to toss me into a cage like I was a mere performer. Oh, what a horrifying situation! I had to run around in circles with no progress, just to amuse everyone. The jailer’s granddaughter, however, was a lovely girl with curly hair like the brightest gold, sparkling eyes, and a wonderfully sweet smile."

title: SOUP FROM A SAUSAGE SKEWER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "One day, as she peeked into my cage, she said, 'Oh, you poor little mouse, I'll set you free.' Then she opened the iron latch, and I leaped out onto the windowsill and jumped to the roof. Free! Free! All that occupied my thoughts was that—nothing about the reason I set out in the first place. It was getting dark as night approached, and I found shelter in an old tower shared by a guard and an owl. I was wary of both, especially the owl since she resembles a cat and has a terrible habit of eating mice. But, I might have been incorrect in my assumptions; turns out she was a well-educated and decent old owl, far more knowledgeable than the watchman, and even as wise as I. The younger owls squabbled endlessly about everything, but she calmly told them, 'You should consider making soup from sausage skewers.' She was very nurturing and affectionate towards her young ones. Her demeanor instilled confidence in me, so from my nook, I called out 'squeak.' This pleased her so much that she promised to protect me, insisting that not a soul would harm me. But she was crafty since she planned to reserve me for her winter dinner when food would be scarce. However, she was quite a brilliant lady owl; she informed me that the watchman could only hoot using the horn hanging from his side, making him think he was an owl in the tower. He aspired to do great things but could only manage small tasks—like making soup from sausage skewers. I then asked the owl for the secret recipe for this soup. 'Soup from a sausage skewer,' she replied, 'is just a saying among humans, and it can mean many things. Everyone thinks their own interpretation is the best, but the saying itself amounts to nothing.' 'Nothing!' I gasped, struck by that truth. Truth isn't always pretty, but it's the utmost essential thing, as the wise old owl pointed out. I pondered this deeply and realized that if truth truly stood above everything, it had to be far more valuable than the soup from a sausage skewer. So, without delay, I rushed to return home in time to present the highest and best gift—truth. Mice are an enlightened society, and the mouse-king presides over them all, so I believed he would gladly make me queen for truth's sake."

title: SOUP FROM A SAUSAGE SKEWER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Your truth is merely a lie," shouted the mouse who hadn’t spoken yet; "I can whip up the soup, and I intend to."

"I didn't travel," said the third mouse; "I stayed put in this country: that was the smarter choice. You gain nothing by wandering; all can be accomplished here just as easily, so I remained home. I didn't acquire what I know from magical beings. I didn’t gorge it down nor learn it by chatting with owls. I gained it strictly from thinking and reflecting. Would you now be so kind as to place the kettle on the fire—like this? Now pour the water in—fill it to the brim; set it on the flames, stoke a vigorous fire, and keep it boiling; it needs to bubble over. Now, I toss in the skewer. Would it please the mouse-king to dip his tail into the boiling water and swirl it around? The more the king stirs, the richer the soup will become. That’s all you need to do—just keep stirring."

"Can no one else do this?" questioned the king.

"No," declared the mouse. "Only the tail of the mouse-king holds this power."

The water bubbled and foamed as the mouse-king stood next to the kettle. It seemed a perilous task. But he swivelled around and dipped his tail, like mice do when skimming cream from milk, into the steaming pot. Just as his tail brushed the hot steam, he jolted back from the fireplace, crying out, "Oh, absolutely, you must be my queen; let’s set aside the soup debate for our golden wedding fifty years from now! That way, the less fortunate in my kingdom will have something to look forward to for a long while with great joy."

And so the wedding took place not long after. But, as many mice made their way home, they murmured that the soup couldn't really be called "soup from a sausage skewer," but rather "soup from a mouse's tail." They admitted that some stories were captivating but also felt the entire situation could have been handled differently. "I would have told it like this—" and "I’d have done it that way—" These were the critics always so wise in hindsight.

When this tale spread across the land, opinions were mixed, but ultimately, the story itself remained unchanged. In the end, the best approach in everything you do, big or small, is to expect no thanks for any of it, even when it comes to 'soup from a sausage skewer.'

title: THE ANGEL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Whenever a good child passes away, an angel of God comes down from heaven, cradles the child in their arms, spreads out their magnificent white wings, and flies over all the places that the child cherished during their life. Then the angel gathers a big handful of flowers to take up to the Almighty, so they can bloom more vividly in heaven than they do on earth. The Almighty holds the flowers close to His heart, but He kisses the one that pleases Him the most, giving it a voice so it can join the heavenly chorus of joy."

These words came from an angel of God as he carried a lifeless child up to heaven, and the child listened as if caught in a dream. They soared over familiar spots where the little one had often played and through beautiful gardens filled with delightful flowers.

"Which of these should we take with us to heaven to be planted there?" asked the angel.

Nearby stood a slender, lovely rosebush, but a wicked hand had broken its stem, leaving the half-opened rosebuds wilted and drooping on the trailing branches.

"Oh, poor rosebush!" exclaimed the child. "Let’s take it with us to heaven so it can bloom in God’s garden."

The angel picked up the rosebush, kissed the child, and the little one opened his eyes just a bit. The angel also collected some beautiful flowers, along with a few humble buttercups and heart's-ease.

"Now we have enough flowers," the child declared, but the angel merely nodded; he didn’t fly upward to heaven.

It was nighttime and very quiet in the big town. They stayed in place, hovering over a narrow street that held a large heap of straw, ashes, and discarded bits from the homes of people who had moved away. Among the clutter lay pieces of broken plates, chunks of plaster, rags, old hats, and other unsightly debris. Amid all this mess, the angel pointed to shattered pieces of a flower pot and a clump of earth that had fallen out, still held together by the roots of a wilted field-flower tossed among the trash.

"We'll take this too," the angel said. "I'll explain why as we fly on."

title: THE ANGEL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
As they flew, the angel told the story.

"Down in that narrow lane, in a dim cellar, there lived a poor sick boy. He had been unwell since childhood, and even on his best days, he could only manage to walk around the room on crutches a couple of times. During summer, the sunlight would stream into the cellar for about half an hour each day. In that sunny spot, the sick boy would sit, warming himself in the sunshine and watching the red blood swirl through his delicate fingers. He would jokingly say he had been outside, but he knew nothing of the vibrant green forest in spring until a neighbor's son brought him a green branch from a beech tree. He would place it above him, imagining he was in the beech woods while the sun shined and the birds chirped happily. One spring day, the neighbor’s boy gifted him some field flowers, and among them was one with the root still intact. The boy carefully planted it in a flower pot and set it in the window near his bed. It was a fortunate planting since it thrived, sprouting fresh shoots and blooming each year. It became a marvelous flower garden for the sick boy, his cherished connection to the world. He nurtured it, making sure it got every sunbeam that filtered into the cellar from dawn until dusk. The flower even made its way into his dreams—for him, it bloomed and spread its fragrance. It brought him joy, and he turned to it even in death when the Lord called him. He's been with God for a year now. During that time, the flower stood forgotten by the window until it was tossed out among the rubbish on moving day. We’re adding this little wilted flower to our bouquet because it brought more genuine happiness than the most beautiful flower in a queen's garden."

"But how do you know all this?" asked the child being carried to heaven.

"I know it," replied the angel, "because I was that poor sick boy who walked on crutches, and I know my own flower well."

At that moment, the child opened his eyes and gazed into the angel's radiant, joyful face. Suddenly, they found themselves in the heavenly home where happiness overflows. God embraced the deceased child and gave him wings, so he could fly alongside the angel, hand in hand. Then the Almighty cherished all the flowers, but He kissed the wilted field flower, giving it a voice. It joined the angels' song surrounding the throne, both close by and further away, yet all equally filled with joy. They all sang together in praise—the good, happy child, and the poor field flower that had once lain wilted and forgotten amid the debris of a dark, narrow street.

title: THE BEETLE WHO WENT ON HIS TRAVELS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was an Emperor who had a horse with golden shoes. Each of the horse's feet wore a golden shoe, and there was a good reason for that. This horse was stunning, with slender legs, bright, intelligent eyes, and a mane that cascaded down his neck like a shimmering veil. He had bravely carried his rider through battles, dodging bullets and charging at the enemy. With his master on his back, he galloped over fallen foes, saving both the Emperor’s life and his crown, which was worth more than the shiniest gold. That’s why the Emperor’s horse wore golden shoes.

A beetle scuttled out from the stable where the farrier had been fitting the horse with those golden shoes. "Of course, the big ones get the special treatment first," he said. "But remember, size doesn't always mean greatness." He stretched his skinny leg as he spoke.

"What do you want?" the farrier asked.

"You must be joking," the farrier exclaimed. "Golden shoes for you?"

"Yes, definitely! Golden shoes," said the beetle. "Am I not just as valuable as that magnificent creature over there? He is pampered and served while I belong to the royal stables!"

"But why does the horse wear golden shoes?" the farrier questioned. "You understand the reason, don’t you?"

"Understand? It’s a personal insult to me!" the beetle shouted. "They’re doing this to annoy me, so I’m off to seek my fortune elsewhere!"

"You're so rude!" yelled the beetle as he stormed out of the stable. He flew a short distance until he landed in a beautiful flower garden filled with the sweet scents of roses and lavender. Ladybugs with red and black shells flitted around, and one of them said, "Isn't it lovely here? Everything is just so beautiful!"

"I’m used to better places," the beetle scoffed. "You think this is beautiful? There isn’t even a dung-heap!" Moving on, he found a caterpillar crawling beneath the shade of a large haystack. "What a beautiful world!" the caterpillar exclaimed. "The sun feels so nice! Soon, I’ll go to sleep and then die, but I’ll wake up with lovely wings to fly like a butterfly."

title: THE BEETLE WHO WENT ON HIS TRAVELS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Who do you think you are?" the beetle retorted. "You think you can just flutter around like a butterfly? I just came from the Emperor's stable, and even the horse there—who wears my old golden shoes—can't fly, except for me! I can already spread my wings!" Saying that, he opened his wings and took off. "I don’t want to be disappointed," he thought, "but I can’t help it." He later landed on a spacious lawn and pretended to snooze, only to drift off for real. Then, a heavy rainstorm hit. The beetle woke up and wished he could burrow into the earth for cover, but he was stuck. The raindrops tossed him around, sometimes making him swim on his stomach and sometimes on his back. Flying was out of the question. Doubts crept in about whether he’d survive, so he lay still. After a while, the storm eased enough for him to wipe the water from his eyes and notice something shiny. He crawled toward it and found some linen laid out to bleach in the grass. He snuggled into a fold of the damp linen, which was far from as cozy as the warm stable, but he had no other options, so he stayed there for a whole day and night while the rain continued to pour. By morning, he crawled out of his damp hiding place, feeling grumpy about the weather. Two frogs were perched on the linen, their bright eyes sparkling with delight.

"What wonderful weather!" one frog said. "It’s so refreshing! This linen holds the water perfectly, making my hind legs quiver as if I’m about to swim!"

"I wonder," said the other, "if the swallow who flies to foreign lands ever finds a better climate than this. The moisture is delightful, just like lying in a wet ditch! Anyone who doesn’t enjoy this doesn’t love their homeland."

"Have you ever been in the Emperor's stable?" the beetle asked. "There, moisture is warm and revitalizing; that’s the climate I prefer! But I can’t take that with me on my travels. Is there seriously no dung-heap in this garden, where someone of my rank could make a comfortable home?" The frogs either didn’t get it or ignored him.

title: THE BEETLE WHO WENT ON HIS TRAVELS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I don’t ask questions twice!" the beetle shouted after repeating the question three times without a response. He moved a little farther and tripped over a broken piece of pottery that shouldn’t have been lying around. But since it was there, it provided decent shelter for several families of earwigs living inside. Their needs were simple, they were social, and each mother was so proud that she considered her own child the most beautiful and smartest.

"Our son is engaged, dear innocent boy! His greatest ambition is to one day creep into a clergyman’s ear. Isn’t that a sweet wish? It keeps him grounded. What happiness for a mother!"

"Our son," said another mother, "barely left the egg before embarking on adventures. He’s full of energy and spirit; I expect he’ll wear his horns out running! Isn't that charming for a mother, don’t you think, Mr. Beetle?" She recognized him by his shiny coat.

"You’re both absolutely right," he replied, and they invited him to come in, which meant as far as he could fit under the broken pottery.

"Now you must meet my little earwigs, who are absolutely adorable and entertaining. They rarely misbehave, except when they have an upset stomach—which happens often at their age!"

Each mother talked about her little one, and the babies mimicked them in their own way, even using the tiny pincers in their tails to nip at the beetle's beard.

"They’re always up to something, those cheeky little ones," said one mother, beaming with pride, but the beetle found it tedious. He asked how to reach the nearest dung-heap.

"That’s way out in the big world, on the other side of the ditch," said an earwig. "I hope none of my kids ever go that far; it would break my heart!"

"Well, I intend to try to get there," the beetle declared, walking off without any formal goodbyes, which is considered polite.

When he arrived at the ditch, he met a group of fellow beetles. "We live here," they said, "and we're quite comfortable. Would you like to join us in this rich mud? You must be tired from your journey."

title: THE BEETLE WHO WENT ON HIS TRAVELS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Absolutely!" said the beetle. "I’d love that. I’ve been caught in the rain and had to lie on linen, and keeping clean is exhausting! Plus, I have a sore wing from standing in the draft under broken pottery. It's refreshing to be with my kind again."

"Maybe you came from a dung-heap?" the oldest beetle suggested.

"No way! I came from a much grander place!" the beetle replied proudly. "I emerged from the Emperor's stable, where I was born with golden shoes on my feet. I’m on a secret mission, but you can’t ask me anything about it!"

The beetle then stepped into the rich mud, where he found three young lady beetles who giggled, unsure of what to say.

"None of them are engaged yet," their mother said, and the beetle maidens giggled again, this time feeling shy.

"I’ve never seen such beauties, even in the Royal Stables!" exclaimed the beetle, who was now relaxing.

"Don’t spoil my girls!" their mother warned. "And don’t talk to them unless you have serious intentions!"

But the beetle’s intentions were quite serious, and eventually, he got engaged. The mother blessed them, and all the other beetles cheered, shouting, "Hurrah!"

Immediately after the engagement came the wedding, as there was no reason to wait. The next day was joyful, the one after that was fairly comfortable, but by the third day, he realized he needed to find food for his new wife and possibly children.

"I’ve let myself be deceived," said the beetle to himself, "and now I have to provide for them in return."

Before he knew it, he was off for the whole day and night, leaving his wife behind like a forsaken widow.

"Oh dear," the other beetles commented, "this new family member we welcomed is nothing but a drifter. He left his wife, leaving us to shoulder the burden!"

"Well, she can just unfasten her engagement and stay with my other daughters," the mother suggested. "Shame on the villain that abandoned her!"

title: THE BEETLE WHO WENT ON HIS TRAVELS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Meanwhile, the beetle, who had crossed the ditch on a cabbage leaf, was journeying on the other side. In the morning, two people came to the ditch. Upon seeing him, they picked him up and examined him closely, particularly one boy who acted very scholarly. "Allah can see the black beetle against the black stone and the black rock. Isn’t that in the Koran?" he asked.

title: THE BEETLE WHO WENT ON HIS TRAVELS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Then he translated the beetle’s name into Latin and spoke at length about its nature and history. The second person, older and a scholar, suggested taking the beetle home since they were looking for good specimens. Our beetle took great offense to this remark, so he suddenly flew out of the boy's hand. His wings were dry now, and they carried him far away until he found a hothouse where a glass sash was slightly open. He slipped inside and buried himself in warm earth. "This is quite cozy," he thought to himself and soon fell asleep. He dreamed that the Emperor’s horse was dying and had left him the golden shoes, promising him two more as well. It was delightful, and when the beetle woke up, he crawled out and took a look around. What an amazing place the hothouse was! In the back, there were large palm trees, and sunlight made the leaves shimmer; beneath them, a lush green carpet spread out, dotted with flowers as red as flames, yellow as amber, and white as freshly fallen snow. "There are so many delicious plants here," said the beetle. "How delightful they will taste once they decay! This is a great pantry! Surely, I must have some family living here; I’ll find someone to connect with! I may be proud, but I take pride in that." Then he roamed about in the dirt, reflecting on how lovely that dream had been about the dying horse and the golden shoes he’d inherited. Just then, a hand grabbed the beetle and squeezed him, spinning him around. The gardener's young son and a friend had entered the hothouse, eager to have some fun with him. They wrapped him in a vine leaf and stuck him in a warm pants pocket. He twisted and turned as hard as he could, but got squeezed again by the boy's hand, which was a gentle hint to stay still. The boy then dashed toward a small lake at the end of the garden. There, the beetle was placed into an old, broken wooden shoe with a stick stuck upright for a mast, and he was tied to this mast with a piece of string. Now he was a sailor expected to sail away. The lake wasn’t large, but to the beetle, it felt like an ocean. He was so stunned by its size that he fell over on his back and kicked his legs. Then, the little boat drifted away, sometimes caught by the water’s current; whenever it strayed too far from shore, one of the boys rolled up his pants and waded in to bring it back. But eventually, just as it was happily floating out again, the two boys were urgently called, and they hurried away, leaving the boat to its fate. It was carried further and further from the shore until it ended up in the open sea. This was a terrifying situation for the beetle since he couldn't free himself due to being tied to the mast. Then, a fly came to visit. "What lovely weather!" the fly said. "I think I’ll hang out here and sunbathe. You must be having a great time!"

title: THE BEETLE WHO WENT ON HIS TRAVELS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"You have no idea what you’re talking about," the beetle replied. "Can’t you see I’m a prisoner?"

"Ah, but I’m not a prisoner," the fly commented, and flew away.

"Now I’ve learned how cruel the world is," the beetle thought. "I’m the only respectable one around! First, they deny me my golden shoes, then I have to lie on damp linen and stand in a draft, and to top it all off, they’ve tied me down with a wife! Just when I finally find a comfy spot that I love, a human boy comes along, ties me up, and leaves me at the mercy of the wild waves while the emperor’s favorite horse struts around in his golden shoes. It drives me crazy! No one cares about my adventures; the world doesn’t deserve to hear them. It should've given me golden shoes when the Emperor's horse was fitted with them! If I had those shoes, I’d be a gem in the stable, but now I’m lost to both the stable and the world. My story is finished."

But it wasn’t over yet. A boat with some young girls soon rowed up. "Look, there's an old wooden shoe sailing by!" exclaimed one of the girls.

"And there’s a poor little creature tied up inside!" another girl noted.

The boat approached the beetle's vessel, and the young girls rescued it from the water. One of them pulled out a small pair of scissors from her pocket, snipped the string without harming the beetle, and upon reaching the shore, placed him on the grass. "There you go!" she said. "Creep away, or fly if you can! Being free is wonderful!" The beetle soared straight through the open window of a grand building and landed, exhausted, right on the mane of the Emperor's favorite horse, who stood in his stable. The beetle found himself at home again and clung to the mane to recover. "Well," he mused, "here I am on the Emperor's favorite horse, just as if I were the Emperor himself. But what did the farrier ask me? Oh yes, I recall—he wanted to know why the horse was given golden shoes! The answer is clear now: they were given for my sake." With that thought, the beetle’s mood lifted. The sun's rays streamed into the stable, illuminating him and bringing life into the space. "Traveling really opens your eyes," the beetle concluded. "The world isn't so bad if you learn to take things as they come."

title: THE BELL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In the winding streets of a bustling town, people often heard a peculiar sound in the evenings as the sun set, casting a golden glow over the rooftops. It resembled a church bell, but it was short-lived, quickly drowned out by the constant noise of traffic and the chatter of voices from the town. "The evening bell is ringing!" they would say. "The sun is going down!" Those who ventured outside the town, where the houses gave way to gardens and small fields, heard the sound of the bell much more clearly. It seemed to come from a cozy church tucked away in the peaceful, fragrant woods, and people gazed in that direction with reverent hearts.

As time passed, one person turned to another and said, "I really wonder if there’s a church in the woods. That bell has such a sweet, mysterious sound! Should we go explore and see what’s behind it?" The wealthy drove in their carriages, while the less fortunate walked, but the journey felt incredibly long. When they reached a cluster of willow trees at the edge of the woods, they settled down, gazing up at the towering branches, feeling like they were truly in the forest. A candy vendor from the town set up a stall nearby, and soon after, another vendor came, hanging a bell over his stall, which was covered in pitch to shield it from the rain, though it was missing its clapper.

When people returned home, they would say the outing had been quite romantic, which really meant something much deeper than simply enjoying tea. Three individuals claimed they went all the way to the end of the woods; they too had heard that enchanting sound, but there it seemed to them like it was coming from the town. One of them penned a poem about the bell, expressing that its sound was akin to a mother’s voice addressing a beloved and intelligent child. He declared that no melody was sweeter than the bell's chime.

The country's emperor caught wind of this and proclaimed that whoever could discover the source of the sound would earn the title of "Bellringer to the World," even if no actual bell existed.

title: THE BELL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Many people ventured into the woods in hopes of landing this prestigious title, but only one returned with some sort of answer. None had gone far enough, including him, yet he claimed the bell's sound actually came from a wise owl inside a hollow tree. This owl would continuously bump its head against the tree, but he couldn’t say for sure whether it was the owl’s head or the tree’s hollow trunk that created the noise.

He was named "Bellringer to the World" and wrote an annual short paper about the owl, but surprisingly, people didn’t gain any more wisdom from it than they had before.

It was confirmation day. The clergyman delivered a beautiful and moving sermon that touched the hearts of all the candidates. It was indeed a significant day for them, marking their transformation from children to adults; they were crossing over into a more mature state of being.

The sun shone brilliantly, and the mysterious bell's sound resonated more clearly than ever. Everyone wanted to go explore, except for three. One girl wanted to head home to try on her ball gown because it was the dress that allowed her to be confirmed this time; otherwise, she wouldn’t have been able to go. The second, a poor boy, had borrowed a coat and boots from his landlord’s son for the confirmation and needed to return them by a certain time. The third insisted he wouldn’t go into unfamiliar places without his parents; he’d always been a good child and wanted to keep it that way, even after confirmation, and he didn’t appreciate their teasing. Thus, the three stayed behind, while the others moved on. The sun was shining, birds were singing, and the confirmed children joyfully sang too, holding hands, for they were all equal in the eyes of God. Soon, two of the youngest grew tired and headed back to town, while two girls sat down to make flower crowns, which held them back. When the remaining group reached the willow trees where the confectioner had set up shop, they declared, "Now that we’re here, it’s clear—the bell doesn’t really exist; it must just be figments of our imagination!"

title: THE BELL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Just then, the beautiful and solemn sound of the bell echoed from deeper within the woods, prompting four or five kids to decide to venture further. The woods were densely vegetated, making it hard to pass through: wild lilies and anemones grew in abundance, while flowering vines and thorns hung like garlands from tree to tree. Nightingales sang, and sunlight filtered through in playful patterns. It was a stunning sight! But the path was challenging for the girls; their dresses would surely be ruined. Large moss-covered rocks lay scattered about, and fresh spring water bubbled up with a gentle sound. "I doubt that’s the bell," said one of the confirmed boys, and he lay down to listen. "We need to find out if it is!" And there he stayed, while the others pressed on.

They stumbled upon a hut made from tree bark and branches, with a large crab-apple tree arching over it as if ready to drop its fruit onto the roof, where roses bloomed. A little bell hung from a long branch. Was that the source of the sound they had heard? Everyone agreed it must be, except for one person who remarked that the bell was too small and too high-pitched to have been heard from such a distance, noting it had a different sound altogether from the one that had touched their hearts.

The one who spoke was a king’s son, and the others began to mutter that he always thought he was smarter than everyone else.

title: THE BELL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
So, they let him wander off on his own. As he ventured deeper into the woods, he felt a sense of reverence wash over him; yet he still heard the small bell chiming that brought joy to the others. Sometimes, when the wind blew in his direction, he could also hear the sounds from the confectioner's stall where the others were enjoying tea and singing. But the deeper bell’s sound was much stronger; soon it seemed to him as if an organ had joined in with a melody—the sound was coming from his left, close to where his heart was. Suddenly, something rustled in the bushes, and a little boy appeared before the king’s son, wearing wooden shoes and a jacket so short that its sleeves barely reached his wrists. They recognized each other: the boy was the one who had stayed behind to return the borrowed clothes to his landlord's son. He had done so and then set out again in his wooden shoes and old clothes, for the sound of the bell was just too tempting—he felt the need to carry on.

"We could go together," suggested the king’s son. However, the boy in wooden shoes felt embarrassed; he tugged at his jacket's short sleeves and admitted he was afraid he couldn't keep up. Plus, he thought the bell must be searched for to the right, where all things grand and magnificent could be found.

"Then we won’t meet," the king’s son replied, giving a nod to the boy, who headed into the densest part of the woods, where brambles snatched at his ragged clothes and poked his hands, face, and feet until they bled. The king’s son got his share of scratches too, but the sunlight lit his path, and we’ll follow him because he was determined. "I will find the bell!" he declared. "Even if it takes me to the ends of the earth!"

Ugly monkeys perched high in the trees and bared their teeth. "Shall we attack him?" they chattered. "Should we beat him up? He’s a king’s son!"

title: THE BELL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: But undeterred, he continued deeper into the woods, where the most enchanting flowers bloomed. Huge white star lilies with blood-red stamens stood tall, mesmerizing blue tulips danced with the breeze, and apple trees bore fruit like large shimmering soap bubbles. Just imagine how dazzling these trees looked in the sunlight! The lush green meadows were alive with deer frolicking in the grass. Majestic oaks and beeches towered overhead, and when their bark split, tufts of grass sprouted from the crevices. There were also large, tranquil lakes in the woods where swans glided gracefully, flapping their wings. The king's son often paused to listen, and sometimes he thought the sound of the bell rose from one of these deep lakes, but soon he understood it was ringing even further into the woods. As the sun began to set, the clouds turned brilliant shades of red. The forest quieted, and he sank to his knees, singing an evening hymn as he said, "I might never find what I'm looking for! The sun is setting, and the dark night is coming. Yet, I may just see the round sun one more time before it dips below the horizon. I’ll climb these rocks—they’re as tall as the trees!" Gripping the vines and roots, he scaled the slippery stones, where water snakes wriggled, and the toads croaked at him. He reached the top just as the sun was vanishing from sight. "Oh, what a wondrous view!" he exclaimed. The vast, majestic sea unfolded before him, rolling waves crashing against the shore, with the sun standing like a brilliant altar where the sea met the sky, melting together in the most radiant colors. The forest sang, and so did his heart. All of nature felt like one massive holy church, with trees and floating clouds as pillars and the flowers and grass as the rich velvet carpet. When the sun disappeared, the flame colors of the sky faded, but millions of stars twinkled into existence, shining like diamond lamps. The king's son stretched his arms toward heaven, the sea, and the woods. Just then, out of nowhere, the poor boy in the short-sleeved jacket and wooden shoes appeared; he had arrived just as swiftly through the path he chose. They ran toward each other, clasped hands in the grand cathedral of nature and poetry, as above them, the invisible holy bell rang, surrounded by happy spirits singing hallelujahs in celebration.

title: THE BELL-DEEP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Ding-dong! ding-dong!" It echoes from the "bell-deep" in the Odense-Au. Every kid in the old town of Odense, on the island of Funen, knows the Au, which gently flows around the town, washing the gardens and flowing under the wooden bridges from the dam to the watermill. In the Au, yellow water-lilies and feathery brown reeds grow; the dark, velvety flags stand tall and thick; old, weathered willows lean precariously over the stream beside the monk's meadow and the bleaching ground. On the opposite side, there are gardens upon gardens, each uniquely beautiful, some boasting lovely flowers and arbors like mini dollhouses, and often displaying cabbages and other garden veggies. But in some places, the gardens are completely hidden by towering elder trees that reach far over the water, which is deeper in some spots than an oar can reach. Right across from the ancient nunnery lies the deepest part, known as the "bell-deep," where the old water spirit, the "Au-mann," resides. The spirit rests during the day under the sunlight, but comes out on starry and moonlit nights. He is very old; Grandmother says she even heard her grandmother talk about him. He is said to live a lonely life, having no one to talk to except the great old church bell. Once, the Bell hung in the church tower, but now there’s no sign of the tower or the church, which was called St. Alban's.

"Ding-dong! ding-dong!" rang the Bell when the tower still stood tall. One evening, just as the sun was setting, the Bell, while swinging proudly, broke loose and soared down through the sky, its shiny metal gleaming in the warm light.

"Ding-dong! ding-dong! Now I’ll take a break!" sang the Bell as it dived into the Odense-Au, where it was the deepest; that’s why they call it the "bell-deep."

But the Bell didn’t find peace or rest. Down in the Au-mann’s domain, it still rings out, sending notes that sometimes break through the water’s surface. Many people believe its sounds are omens of someone’s death, but that’s not true; the Bell is just chatting with the Au-mann, who is no longer alone.

title: THE BELL-DEEP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
And what is the Bell saying? It's very old, as we’ve mentioned; it has been around long before grandmother's grandmother was born. Yet, it’s still a child compared to the Au-mann, who is a very ancient and quiet being. He’s quite peculiar, wearing eel-skin hose and a scaly jacket with yellow lilies for buttons, and a wreath of reeds in his hair with seaweed in his beard; still, he looks rather charming.

What does the Bell share? To recount it all would take years; every year, it tells ancient stories, some brief and some lengthy, depending on its mood. It shares tales of times long past, of dark and hard days, like this one:

"In the church of St. Alban, a young, handsome monk climbed into the tower. He was deeply thoughtful as he peered through the loophole at the Odense-Au, when the water bed was still broad, and the monks' meadow was still a lake. He gazed out at the rampart and the nun's hill across from the convent, where light shone from the nun’s cell. He knew the nun well, and his heart raced as he thought about her. Ding-dong! ding-dong!"

"Into the tower came the dapper servant of the bishop. And when I, the Bell made of metal, rang loudly and swung back and forth, I could have knocked him out! He sat right beneath me, fiddling with two little sticks like they were a string instrument, and began to sing. 'Now I can sing out loud, even though normally I can’t whisper. I can sing about everything hidden away behind locks and bars. Over there it’s cold and wet. The rats are eating her alive! Nobody knows! Nobody hears it! Not even now, as the bell is ringing and singing its loud Ding-dong, ding-dong!'"

title: THE BELL-DEEP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Back then, there was a king known as Canute. He bowed before the bishop and the monk, but when he angered the free peasants with heavy taxes and harsh words, they grabbed their weapons and chased him away like a wild animal. He sought refuge in the church, shutting the gate and door behind him. A violent crowd surrounded the church, and I heard stories about it. Crows, ravens, and magpies took off in panic at the noise. They flew into the tower and back out, glancing down at the throng below and peeking into the church windows, shouting out what they saw. King Canute knelt at the altar in prayer, flanked by his brothers Eric and Benedict, who stood guard with their swords drawn, but the treacherous servant Blake betrayed him. The mob knew just where to strike, and one of them hurled a stone through a window, killing the King! The screams and shouts of the crowd and the birds filled the air, and I joined in, ringing out 'Ding-dong! ding-dong!'"

"The church bell hangs high, watching the world around it, understanding the language of the birds. The wind rushes in through windows and loopholes; it knows everything because it gathers information from the air that permeates all things. The church bell understands its language and echoes it out into the world, 'Ding-dong! ding-dong!'"

"But it overwhelmed me to hear and know so much; I became too tired and heavy to ring anymore. The beam snapped, and I fell into the shimmering Au, where the water is deepest and where the Au-mann lives, solitary and alone. Year after year, I share with him what I've learned and what I know. Ding-dong! ding-dong!"

So rings the Bell from the bell-deep in the Odense-Au. That’s what Grandmother told us.

However, the schoolmaster argues there isn’t any bell down there, as it couldn’t possibly ring; and that there’s no Au-mann, claiming he doesn’t exist! He says that when all the other church bells ring harmoniously, it’s not really the bells making the sounds, but the air itself creating the music. Grandmother told us that the Bell said it was the air that informed him; so, they agree on that, and this much is certain.

"Be careful and watch out for yourself," they both advise.

The air knows everything. It surrounds us, fills us, speaks of our thoughts and deeds, and it tells of them longer than the Bell does in the depths of the Odense-Au where the Au-mann lives. It rings out into the vault of heaven, far, far out, forever and ever, until the heavenly bells sound "Ding-dong! ding-dong!"

title: THE BIRD OF POPULAR SONG
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the cycle of life and death
text_chunk:
It's wintertime. The earth is covered in a snowy blanket, looking like polished marble; the air is bright and clear, with a wind sharp like a finely honed sword. The trees stand tall, resembling white coral branches or blooming almond shoots, and the chill is just as intense as it is high in the Alps.

The night dazzles under the glow of the Northern Lights and the sparkle of countless twinkling stars.

Inside our cozy room, next to a warm stove, we reminisce about the good old days. We settle in to hear this tale:

By the open sea lay a giant's grave, and at midnight, the spirit of the buried hero, a once mighty king, sat upon the grave mound. A golden circlet shimmered on his brow, his hair danced in the wind, and he wore a suit of steel and iron. He bowed his head in deep sadness, sighing like a restless spirit.

Soon, a ship appeared on the horizon. As the sailors lowered their anchor and came ashore, one of them, a singer, approached the royal spirit and asked,

"Why are you mourning? What's causing your suffering?"

"No one has sung about the deeds of my life; they've been lost and forgotten. My accomplishments aren’t carried across the land or into people's hearts, so I find no peace or rest."

He talked about his achievements and the heroic battles he had fought, known only to those who lived at the same time, but without a singer to share his story.

Then the old bard plucked the strings of his harp and sang about the hero's youthful bravery, his strength, and the greatness of his deeds. As he sang, the face of the hero glowed like a crescent moon's reflection on a cloud. Lifted with joy and dignity, the spirit rose in brilliance and vanished like the fleeting Northern Lights. Only the grassy mound with unmarked stones remained; but at the final note from the harp, a little singing bird soared above the hill, sweet and charming, with a voice that rang like a thrush and carried the heartfelt emotions of home, like a migratory bird's call. This singing bird flew over mountains and valleys, fields and woods—it was the Bird of Popular Song, eternal and undying.

title: THE BIRD OF POPULAR SONG
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the cycle of life and death
text_chunk:
We hear his melody now in our warm room while the snowy bees swirl around outside, and the storm rages against the windows. The bird doesn’t just sing the requiem of heroes; he also shares gentle love songs—so many warm notes of Northern loyalty and truth. He weaves stories and melodies, teaching us with proverbs and bits of wisdom, crafting songs like Runes placed under a dead man's tongue that compel him to express; Popular Song tells the tale of his homeland.

Back in the days of old, in the era of the Vikings, popular speech found its home in the bard's harp.

During the time of grand castles, when strength dictated justice, and might made right—where a peasant's worth was the same as a dog's—how did the Bird of Song find a safe place? Neither brutality nor ignorance took notice of him.

Inside the turreted castle, the lady of the house sat with a scroll, recording ancient memories as songs and legends. Beside her stood an old woman from the woods and a wandering peddler. As they told their tales, around them fluttered the Bird of Popular Song, who will sing as long as there’s sunlight on the hills.

And now, he looks at us and sings. Outside, the night and snowstorm rage. He plants the Runes in our minds, enabling us to connect with our homeland. Heaven speaks to us in our mother tongue through the Bird of Popular Song. Old memories stir, fading colors become vibrant again, and stories and songs offer us a sweet potion that elevates our spirits, making the evening feel like a Christmas celebration.

Snowflakes swirl in a dance, ice cracks, the storm reigns outside with its might, but he is not the LORD OF ALL.

It's wintertime again. The wind is sharp as a double-edged sword, and snowflakes playfully chase one another; it feels like it's been snowing forever. The snow blankets the town like a heavy, dreamy shroud. Everything is hidden away, except for the golden cross of the church, a symbol of faith, peeking through the snow and glinting in the clear sky.

Above the buried town soar birds of every kind, small and large; they chirp and sing as best they can, each using their unique voice.

First come the sparrows, chirping about every little thing in the streets, alleys, nests, and homes, sharing stories of their surroundings.

"We know the town buried beneath all this!" they chirp. "Everything alive is chirp, chirp, chirp!"

The black ravens and crows glide over the pristine snow.

"Food, food!" they caw. "There’s something to be found down there; something to eat, and that’s what matters. That's the consensus down there, and it's a good one!"

Wild swans fly on their powerful wings, singing of the noble and great things that still bloom in people's hearts, right above the town resting under its snowy blanket.

There’s no death there—life thrives below; we can hear it in notes that swell like the tones from a church organ, gripping us like sounds from the spirit world, like the melodies of Ossian, like the wind rustling from the wings of wandering spirits. What harmony! This harmony speaks to our hearts and lifts our souls! It’s the Bird of Popular Song we hear.

At this moment, the gentle breath of heaven wafts down from the sky. There are openings in the snowy mountains, sunlight streams into the gaps; spring is coming, the birds are returning, and new generations are arriving with the same familiar tunes in their hearts.

Listen to the story of the year: "The snowy stormy night, the heavy dream of winter, all shall dissolve, all shall rise again in the beautiful notes of the Bird of Popular Song, who never dies!"

title: THE BISHOP OF BORGLUM AND HIS WARRIORS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
We're in Northern Jutland, in the area known as the "wild moor." The sound of the "Wester-wow-wow"—the unique roar of the North Sea crashing against Jutland's western coast—fills the air. It rolls and thunders, echoing for miles, and we find ourselves close to the source of that booming noise. Ahead of us stands a massive sand mound—like a mountain we've been heading toward, as we drive slowly through the deep sand. Atop this sandy hill is a tall, ancient building—the convent of Borglum. One of its larger wings still houses a church. It's late evening when we arrive, but the June night is clear, allowing our gaze to stretch far over fields and moors to the Bay of Aalborg, past heath and meadows, and across the deep blue sea.

Having arrived, we roll past barns and various farm structures, veering to the left of the gate toward the Old Castle Farm. Lime trees are lined along the walls, thriving in the sheltered area away from the wind, their branches and leaves nearly covering the windows.

We climb the winding stone staircase and pass through long corridors beneath heavy roof beams. The wind moans in a strange way both inside and outside. It's hard to say how it started, but people often claim—whenever they’re scared or want to scare others—that the spirits of old choir members glide silently past us into the church for mass. You can hear them in the storm, and their singing awakens eerie thoughts, transporting us back to the old times.

On the coast, a ship has run aground, and the bishop's warriors are there, showing no mercy to those the sea has spared. The ocean washes away the blood from the split skulls. The goods belong to the bishop, and there's plenty stored here. The sea has thrown up tubs and barrels filled with expensive wine for the convent cellar, along with a good stock of beer and mead already there. The kitchen is well-stocked with game, poultry, hams, and sausages, and fat fish swim in the ponds outside.

title: THE BISHOP OF BORGLUM AND HIS WARRIORS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The Bishop of Borglum is a powerful lord with vast possessions, yet he still craves more—everything must bow to the mighty Olaf Glob. His wealthy cousin in Thyland has died, leaving everything to his widow. It's strange how one relative can be harsher to another than even a stranger would be. The widow's husband owned all of Thyland except for the church property. Her son had been away on a journey since childhood, wanting to explore foreign lands and meet new people. For years, there’s been no news of him. Perhaps he’s long since passed away and won't return home to rule where his mother now reigns.

"What does a woman know about ruling?" the bishop remarked.

He called the widow to a court, but what good did it do? The widow had always followed the law and was strong in her rightful claims.

Bishop Olaf of Borglum, what is your plan? What are you writing on that smooth parchment, sealing it with your seal, and handing it to the riders and servants, sending them far away to the city of the Pope?

It's the season of falling leaves and stranded ships, and winter's icy breath is approaching.

Two winters have come and gone before the bishop welcomed the horsemen and servants back home. They returned from Rome with a papal decree—a ban, or bull, against the widow who dared to offend the pious bishop. "Cursed be she and all she possesses. Let her be cast out from the congregation and the Church. No one must help her, and let friends and family avoid her like a plague!"

"What doesn't bend must break," stated the Bishop of Borglum.

Everyone abandoned the widow, but she clung to her faith in God. He is her helper and protector.

Only one servant—a loyal old maid—remained by her side. Along with her old servant, the widow tended to the fields, and despite the land being cursed by the Pope and the bishop, the crops grew.

"You doomed child, I will still see my plans through!" shouted the Bishop of Borglum. "I will call upon the Pope's authority to summon you before a court that will condemn you!"

title: THE BISHOP OF BORGLUM AND HIS WARRIORS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The widow then hitched the last two oxen she had to a wagon, climbed aboard with her old servant, and traveled across the heath and out of Denmark. She entered a foreign land, where a strange language was spoken and different customs were practiced. She traveled further, to where green hills turned into mountains draped in vines. Strange merchants passed her by, glancing anxiously at their cargo-laden wagons, fearing attacks from the armed followers of bandit knights. The two poor women, in their simple cart pulled by two black oxen, journeyed boldly through the treacherous roads and dark forests. Soon, they found themselves in Franconia, where they met a strong knight accompanied by twelve armed followers. He stopped, looked at their unusual vehicle, and asked the women about their journey and where they came from. When one of them mentioned Thyland in Denmark and shared her sorrows, she added that her troubles would soon end, as it was willed by Divine Providence. For the knight, unbeknownst to her, was her son! He took her hand and embraced her, and the mother wept, unable to have cried for years, only holding back her tears until her lips bled.

It’s the season of falling leaves and stranded ships, and soon icy winter will set in.

The ocean rolled wine barrels onto the shore for the bishop's cellar. In the kitchen, deer roasted over the fire. At Borglum, warmth filled the inviting rooms while the bitter winter raged outside, when news arrived for the bishop. "Jens Glob of Thyland has returned, accompanied by his mother." Jens Glob was filing a complaint against the bishop, summoning him to both the temporal and spiritual courts.

"That won't be of any use to him," said the bishop. "Better give up, knight Jens."

Once again, it’s the season of falling leaves and stranded ships. Icy winter is approaching, and the "white bees" are buzzing, stinging travelers' faces as they melt.

"What a cold day!" people remark as they step inside.

Jens Glob is so lost in thought that he burns the hem of his wide garment.

"You, Bishop Borglum," he declares, "I shall conquer you after all! The law may be beyond the Pope's protection, but Jens Glob will get to you!"

title: THE BISHOP OF BORGLUM AND HIS WARRIORS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
He then penned a letter to his brother-in-law, Olaf Hase, in Sallingland, asking him to meet on Christmas Eve at mass in the church at Widberg. The bishop himself would be presiding over the mass, so he'd be traveling from Borglum to Thyland—something Jens Glob was keenly aware of.

The moor and meadow are covered in ice and snow. The marsh can support horse and rider, as the bishop travels with his priests and armed men. They took the shortest path through the swaying reeds, as the wind moaned mournfully.

Sound your brass trumpet, you trumpeter in fox-skin! It rings cheerfully in the crisp air. They ride on over the heath and moorland—what is vibrant during summer now lies icy, like the rest of the land—heading toward the church in Widberg.

The wind sounds its own trumpet—harder and harder, building up into a storm—an awful storm that intensifies more and more. They race toward the church, as fast as they can through the storm. The church stands firm while the storm swirls over the fields, moors, land, and sea.

Bishop Borglum reaches the church, but Olaf Hase will barely make it, no matter how hard he rides. He’s traveling on the far side of the bay to support Jens Glob, who is about to confront the bishop at the judgment seat of the Almighty.

The church is the hall for judgment; the altar is the council table. The candles shine brightly in heavy brass holders. The storm carries the charges and the verdict, echoing through the air across moors and heath, and over the rolling waters. No ferry can cross the bay in this kind of weather.

Olaf Hase pauses at Ottesworde. He releases his warriors, giving them their horses and equipment, so they can go home to greet his wife.
