He’s prepared to risk his life alone in the raging waters; yet, they must testify that it wasn't his fault if Jens Glob stands without backup in the church at Widberg. His loyal warriors refuse to leave him, following him into the depths of the water. Ten of them are swept away, but Olaf Hase and two of the youngest make it to the other side. They still have four miles to ride.

title: THE BISHOP OF BORGLUM AND HIS WARRIORS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
It’s past midnight, it’s Christmas. The wind has calmed. The church is lit up; the glowing light shines through the windows, spreading over the meadow and heath. The mass has long ended, silence envelops the church, and the wax from the candles can be heard dropping onto the stone floor. And now Olaf Hase arrives.

In the forecourt, Jens Glob greets him kindly and says,

"I’ve just made an agreement with the bishop."

"Is that so?" replied Olaf Hase. "Then neither you nor the bishop will leave this church alive."

The sword flies from its sheath, and Olaf Hase lands a blow that shatters the church door panel that Jens Glob hastily closes.

"Wait, brother! First, hear what the agreement was that I made. I have slain the bishop and his warriors and priests. They will have no more words to say in this matter, nor will I speak again of all the wrong my mother has endured."

The long wicks of the altar lights glimmer red; but a redder hue lies on the floor where the bishop has fallen, his skull split open, surrounded by his slain warriors, in the calm of this holy Christmas night.

title: THE BISHOP OF BORGLUM AND HIS WARRIORS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Four days later, the bells toll for a funeral at the convent of Borglum. The murdered bishop and the slain warriors and priests lie under a black canopy, surrounded by candles draped in black. The dead man is dressed in a black cloak adorned with silver, holding the crozier in his now powerless hand. The incense rises in clouds, and the monks chant a funeral hymn. It sounds like a lament—like a sentence of wrath and condemnation that must be heard throughout the land, carried by the wind—sung out by the wind—the lament that sometimes goes silent but never truly fades; it rises again, telling this tale of the Bishop of Borglum and his harsh nephew. It reaches the ears of terrified farmers passing along the heavily laden roads near the convent. It echoes in the sleepless ears of those in the thick-walled rooms of Borglum. Not only the superstitious hear the whispers and footsteps racing through the long, echoing corridors leading to the convent's long-locked door. The door still appears to open, the lights seem to flicker in the bronze candlesticks; the sweet scent of incense wafts in the air; the church dazzles in all its ancient glory, while the monks sing and celebrate mass for the slain bishop, who rests in his silver-embroidered black mantle, with the crozier in his limp hand; and on his pale, proud brow, the red wound glows like fire, signaling the worldly desires and wicked thoughts.

Descend into his grave—into oblivion—oh dreadful shapes of old times!

Listen to the furious wind roaring above the crashing sea! A storm approaches, claiming human lives. The sea remains unchanged by time. Tonight, it’s a terrifying abyss ready to swallow up lives, and maybe tomorrow it might be a smooth surface—just like in the past that we thought we buried. Sleep soundly, if you can!

The new age spills sunlight into the room. The wind still howls fiercely. A wreck has been reported—just like in the old days.

During the night, down at Lokken, the little fishing village with the red-tiled roofs—visible from our window—a ship has come ashore. It's stuck fast in the sand, but a rocket apparatus has thrown a rope on board, creating a bridge from the wreck to the mainland; everyone aboard is saved, wrapping themselves in warm blankets; they’re invited to the convent’s farm the next day. In cozy rooms, they find warm hospitality and friendly faces. They’re greeted in their own language, and the piano plays melodies from their homeland. Before those melodies fade, a connection is made—the thread of thought reaching out to the land of the rescued reassures their worries are eased; then, by evening, they join in a dance at the feast held in the grand hall at Borglum. They waltz and dance the Styrian dance to Danish folk songs and melodies from foreign lands in modern times.

Blessed be you, new times! Speak of summer and pure breezes! Send your rays of sunshine shining into our hearts and minds! On your vibrant canvas, let the dark legends of the harsh times that are behind us be painted!

title: THE BOTTLE NECK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
On a street corner, surrounded by other struggling homes, stood a very tall, narrow house that had seen better days. It looked warped and tired, with every part of it out of alignment. Inside lived a number of poor people, but the most apparent poverty was in the attic room at the top. In front of the tiny window hung an old, crooked birdcage soaking up the sunlight. Instead of a proper water dish, it used the broken neck of a bottle, turned upside down with a cork stuck to keep the water inside. At the window stood an elderly woman, who had draped chickweed over the cage. Inside, a little linnet hopped from perch to perch, happily singing and chirping.

"Sure, it's easy for you to sing," thought the bottle neck. Of course, it didn’t actually speak like we do, since bottle necks can’t talk. Instead, it quietly communicated this thought to itself.

"You might sing beautifully. You have all your limbs intact. You should try being reduced to just a neck and a mouth, with a cork stuffed in, like I am. Then you wouldn’t feel like singing, I bet. But I guess it’s good that some can be happy. I have no reason to sing, and even if I were happy, I wouldn't be able to. But back when I was a full bottle, and they rubbed me with a cork, I used to sing! They even called me a complete lark. I remember going on a picnic with the furrier's family on the day his daughter got engaged; it feels like it was just yesterday. I've been through a lot: I’ve been in fire and water, buried deep in the earth, and climbed higher in the air than most people. Now I’m just swinging here, perched outside a birdcage in the sun. Oh, my history is worth hearing, but I can't share it out loud, for a good reason—I can't talk."

title: THE BOTTLE NECK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: So the bottle neck shared his story, but only in his own thoughts. The little bird continued to sing joyfully; down on the street, people rushed by, focused on their own lives or sometimes lost in thought. Meanwhile, the bottle neck thought deeply. He remembered the blazing furnace where he was born, how hot it was when he was placed in the heated oven—his true home. At first, he wanted to escape it but slowly grew comfortable over time. He recalled being lined up with countless siblings who came from the same furnace. Some became champagne bottles, others beer bottles, which made them a bit different. But it often happens in the world that a beer bottle can hold the most exquisite wine, while a champagne bottle may end up filled with something less desirable. Even in decay, nobility retains its essence, just as a champagne bottle remains noble, even when filled with blacking. When they packed the bottles, he never expected to end up as just a bottle neck or be a water dish for a birdcage, which he liked to think was a position of honor, as it meant he had some use in the world. He didn’t see daylight again until he was unpacked in the wine merchant’s cellar, where for the first time, he was rinsed with water, creating an odd sensation. There he lay empty and uncorked, feeling as if he was missing something, and then he was filled with rich, expensive wine, corked, sealed, and labeled "first quality," as if he had won a prize. Both the wine and bottle were good, and youth is when poetry thrives. Within the bottle, he heard sounds of joy, visions of sunny green mountains where vines flourish, and merry vine-dressers sing and celebrate. "Oh, how lovely life is!" All these vibrant tones in the bottle were like the musings of an aspiring poet, who often doesn’t quite understand the melodies swirling inside them. One morning, the bottle was purchased by the furrier’s apprentice, who had been instructed to pick out one of the best bottles of wine. It was put in a basket with ham, cheese, and sausages. The furrier’s beautiful daughter herself added the freshest butter and finest bread, carefully packing it. She had sparkling brown eyes and a smile that lingered sweetly on her lips. Her hands were delicate and pale, and her neck even whiter, and it was clear she was a lovely girl who was not yet engaged. The basket sat in her lap as the family headed out to the forest. The bottle neck peeked out from between the folds of the white napkin, noticing the red wax on the cork while catching glimpses of the girl’s face and the young sailor seated nearby. He was her young friend, the son of a portrait painter, who had just successfully completed his exams as a mate, and he was set to sail to faraway shores the next morning. There had been much conversation about this while packing the basket, and during it, the furrier’s daughter wore a somewhat somber expression. The young couple wandered off into the lush green woods, talking together. What were they discussing? The bottle couldn’t tell since it was still in the basket. They stayed out there for quite a while; when finally they returned, it looked like something wonderful had occurred, as everyone was laughing. The furrier’s daughter laughed, too, though she spoke very little, her cheeks glowing like two blooming roses. Then her father picked up the bottle and the corkscrew. What an extraordinary feeling it was to have the cork pulled for the very first time! The bottle would never forget that moment—it felt a rush as the cork flew out, followed by a gurgling sound as the wine poured into glasses.

title: THE BOTTLE NECK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Long life to the engaged couple!" exclaimed the father, and everyone emptied their glasses to the last drop as the young sailor kissed his beautiful bride.

"Wishing you both happiness and blessings!" the elderly parents joined in, and the young man refilled the glasses.

"A safe return, and a wedding exactly a year from now," he toasted. After the glasses were emptied again, he held the bottle high, proclaiming, "You’ve been here on the happiest day of my life, and you’ll never be used by anyone else!" With that, he threw it high into the sky.

title: THE BOTTLE NECK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: The furrier's daughter feared she’d never see the bottle again, but she was mistaken. It fell into the reeds by a small lake in the woods. The bottle neck remembered how long it lay hidden there. "I provided them with wine, and in return they gave me muddy water," it thought, "but I suppose it was well-intentioned." He couldn’t see the happy couple anymore, nor the cheerful older folks, but for a long while, he could hear their laughter and songs. Eventually, two young boys came by, poked around in the reeds, and spotted the bottle. They picked it up and took it home, so once again, it found new purpose. At their quaint wooden cottage, the boys had an older brother who was about to embark on a long sea journey. He had been there the day before to say goodbye, and their mother was busy packing his supplies. In the evening, their father planned to take the bundle to the town, delivering a farewell message from the mother. They had already filled a small bottle with herbal tea mixed with brandy, and it was wrapped in the parcel. But when the boys arrived, they brought home a bigger, stronger bottle they had found; it could hold much more than the smaller one, and they all believed the brandy would be great for stomach pains, especially combined with medicinal herbs. The new large bottle was chosen to go instead of the little one, so the bottle began its journey once more. It was taken aboard the same ship where Peter Jensen, the young mate, was sailing. However, the mate didn’t see the bottle; and, if he had, he wouldn’t have recognized it as the one they had toasted with at the joy-filled wedding party. Although it no longer contained wine, it held something just as good, and each time Peter Jensen brought it out, his crew affectionately named it "the apothecary," since it was the best remedy for upset stomachs, which he freely shared as long as there was still some left. Those were happy days, and the bottle sounded off like a lark when it got a rub from a cork, earning the nickname "Peter Jensen's lark."

title: THE BOTTLE NECK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Time flew by through long days and months while the bottle stood empty in a corner, until a violent storm hit—whether on the outward or return journey, it couldn’t tell, as it had never been on land. The storm was fierce, waves rising high and violently tossing the ship around. The mainmast broke, the ship developed a leak, and the pumps failed, with darkness enveloping everything. Just as the ship was sinking, the young mate scrawled on a piece of paper, "We are going down: God’s will be done." He wrote the names of his betrothed and himself, along with the ship's name. Then he shoved the paper into an empty bottle that was handy, tightly corked it, and tossed it into the swirling sea. He had no idea that it was the very same bottle that once held the goblet of joy and hope for him, now drifting on the waves with his last farewell and a message from the heart. The ship sank, taking the crew down with it; but the bottle soared like a bird, carrying a loving letter from a devoted heart. With each sunrise and sunset, the bottle felt as it did when it first came into being—longing to fly away. It survived storms and calm, crashing against no rocks or falling prey to sharks, drifting for more than a year, sometimes north and sometimes south, as the current guided it. In most ways, it was free, although that too could become tedious. The note, the final farewell from the groom to his bride, would only bring sorrow once it reached her, but where were those soft hands that once set a picnic tablecloth on fresh grass in the green woods on her engagement day? Yes, where was the furrier’s daughter? Where was the land closest to her home?

title: THE BOTTLE NECK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: The bottle didn’t know; it continued onward and onward, eventually growing tired of its travels. But it had to persist until it reached a foreign shore. Not one word of the language spoken there did the bottle understand; it was a tongue it had never encountered before, and not understanding a language is truly a loss. The bottle was pulled from the water and examined on all sides. The little letter inside was discovered, taken out, and turned over repeatedly, yet the people could not decipher the handwriting. They suspected it had been tossed overboard, along with something precious written on that paper, but what it said remained a mystery. They returned the paper to the bottle and placed both into a large cupboard in one of the town’s grand homes. Whenever strangers visited, the paper was taken out and reviewed, but the penciled address became nearly unreadable over time, until eventually, no one could distinguish any letters at all. For an entire year, the bottle was confined in that cupboard, before being moved to the attic, soon covered in dust and cobwebs. How often it thought of those better times—when it poured out rich wine in the fresh green woods or carried a secret message on the waves during its flight! For a full twenty years, it remained up there, but then a reconstruction project began on that house. The bottle was discovered when they removed the roof; people talked about it, although the bottle couldn’t understand their conversations—after all, living in an attic for decades doesn’t teach a language. "If I had been downstairs in a room," it mused, "I might have learned something." It got washed and rinsed, a necessity really, and afterward, it looked clear and youthful again in its old age, but the cherished paper it once held was destroyed in the washing. They stuffed the bottle with seeds, even though it had no clue what they were. Then they corked it tightly and wrapped it up carefully. In that dark place, not even the light from a torch could reach it, much less the sunlight or moonlight. "And yet," the bottle thought, "people travel to see as much as they can, while I can see nothing." But it did something equally important; it traveled to its new destination and was unpacked.

title: THE BOTTLE NECK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "What a fuss they made over that bottle!" one person commented, "And it’s probably broken anyway." But surprisingly, the bottle wasn’t broken, and even better, it understood every word spoken. This language it had heard at the furnaces, in the wine merchant's cellar, in the forest, and on the ship—it was the well-loved language of its past life. It felt like it had returned home, and hearing that familiar language was like a warm welcome. Out of sheer happiness, it felt like jumping out of their hands, hardly noticing its cork was pulled and its contents emptied until it found itself taken to a cellar where it could be left and forgotten. "There’s no place like home, even if it is a cellar." It never occurred to it that it might lay there for years; it felt so content. For many long years, it rested in the cellar until one day, a group came to take the bottles away, and it was among them.

title: THE BOTTLE NECK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: In the garden outside, a grand celebration was underway. Brightly colored lamps hung like festoons from tree to tree, and paper lanterns glowed to appear like shimmering tulips. The evening was beautiful, with mild weather. Stars sparkled overhead, and the crescent moon, half-shadowed by the full moon, resembled a gray globe with a golden rim—an enchanting sight for those with keen eyes. The illumination spilled into even the most secluded parts of the garden, ensuring no one could easily get lost. Bottles of light lined the borders, including the one we know, destined to end up as a bottle neck for a birdcage. Everything felt lovely to our bottle, as it found itself back amid the joy and festivities of the green woods; once again, it heard music, laughter, and the lively chatter of a crowd, especially in the section where the lamps shone brightly, and the paper lanterns displayed their splendid colors. Though it stood in a distant nook, the spot was serene and contemplative. It carried a light and served not just a purpose but also added beauty. During such a moment, it's easy to forget that it had spent two decades in an attic, and indeed, forgetting is often a blessing. As the bottle sat there, it saw a couple pass by, resembling the bridal pair—it was the mate and the furrier's daughter—who once strolled together in the woods long ago. The bottle felt as if it were reliving that moment. Other guests strolled through the garden, enjoying the celebration. Among them was an old maid, alone in this world. Like the bottle, she reminisced about the green woods and the young couple tied to her memories; she recalled the happiest hour of her life. Such memories linger, no matter how old a woman becomes. But she did not recognize the bottle, nor did the bottle notice her. So often, people pass each other in life without knowing they share a connection, just as these two did, all while living in the same town.

title: THE BOTTLE NECK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The bottle was removed from the garden and sent back to a wine merchant, where it was refilled with wine and sold to an aeronaut planning to ascend in his balloon on the upcoming Sunday. A large crowd gathered to witness the event; military bands played music, and many preparations took place. The bottle observed it all from its spot in the basket, close to an excited live rabbit, who knew he was about to be taken up and lowered again in a parachute. The bottle, on the other hand, wasn’t aware of the "up" or "down," only noticed the balloon growing bigger and more restless until the ropes that held it were cut, triggering it to rise into the air, carrying the aeronaut, the basket, the bottle, and the rabbit while music played and cheers erupted from the crowd.

"This is an incredible journey up into the sky!" thought the bottle. "This is a whole new way to sail, and here there's no chance of crashing into something."

title: THE BOTTLE NECK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Thousands of people gazed at the balloon, including the old maid in the garden, who spotted it as she leaned out of her garret window, where the cage with the linnet hung without its water glass, just an old cup for its needs. A myrtle plant in a pot had been carefully positioned to avoid falling. Leaning out, the old maid watched the aeronaut in the balloon as he lowered the rabbit in the parachute, then raised the bottle to toast to everyone. After that, he hurled it into the sky. Little did she know, this was the very same bottle her friend had tossed in her honor on that joyful day in the green woods during her youth. The bottle didn't have time to think as it was launched suddenly into the air; before it realized what was happening, it soared higher than it had ever been before, looking down at steeples and rooftops that appeared minuscule from its lofty height. It began to descend much quicker than the rabbit, flipping in the air and feeling young and free, despite being half full of wine. But this was short-lived. It was such a remarkable journey that everyone down below could see the bottle glisten in the sunlight. The balloon, now drifting far away, soon left the bottle behind, and it plummeted onto a roof, shattering into pieces. Yet the pieces, with their propelled momentum, kept moving, bouncing and rolling until they landed in the courtyard and shattered into even smaller fragments; only the bottle neck remained intact, separated as cleanly as if cut with a diamond.

"That would make a perfect bird's glass," one of the cellar workers remarked. But none of them had a bird or cage, nor did they plan to get one just because they found a bottle neck suitable for such use. However, the old maid in the garret had a bird, and it might be helpful for her. So, they fitted the bottle neck with a cork and brought it up to her. As happens often in life, what had once been on top was now flipped down, and it got filled with fresh water. They hung it in the little bird’s cage, where the bird tweeted and sang merrier than ever.

title: THE BOTTLE NECK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Ah, you have every reason to sing!" noted the bottle neck, which was regarded as something special since it had been up in a balloon; nothing more was known of its past. As it hung there in the cage, it could hear the sounds and chatter of people below, as well as the conversation of the old maid inside. An old friend had come to visit, and they spoke not about the bottle neck, but about the myrtle on the window.

"No, don’t spend a dollar on your daughter’s bridal bouquet," the old maid insisted. "You shall have a beautiful little bunch of blossoms instead. Do you see how wonderfully the tree has grown? It sprouted from just a small sprig of myrtle that you gave me the day after my engagement, from which I was supposed to create my bridal bouquet when a year passed; but that day never came. The eyes that were supposed to bring me light and joy in life are now closed. My beloved sleeps peacefully in the ocean; the myrtle has matured into a tall tree, and I am now an even older woman. Before the sprig you gave me faded, I took a shoot and planted it. Now, as you see, it's grown into a large tree, and at last, a bouquet of its blossoms shall make an appearance in your daughter’s wedding flowers."

Tears welled up in the old maid's eyes as she reminisced about the love of her youth and their engagement in the woods. Waves of thoughts flooded her mind, but she never realized that right next to her, in that very same window, was a reminder of those days gone by—the neck of the bottle that had once rejoiced when the cork popped on the engagement day. Yet the bottle neck didn’t recognize the old maid; perhaps it couldn’t hear her memories because it was too preoccupied with its own thoughts of her.

title: THE BRAVE TIN SOLDIER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Once upon a time, there were twenty-five tin soldiers who were all brothers because they were made from the same old tin spoon. They stood at attention, gazing straight ahead in their beautiful red and blue uniforms. The first thing they ever heard were the words, "Tin soldiers!" spoken by a little boy who clapped his hands with joy when the box they'd been kept in was opened. The soldiers were a gift for his birthday, and he eagerly set them up on the table. They all looked identical, except for one who had only one leg. He had been the last one made, and there wasn’t enough melted tin to finish him, so he stood proudly on his one leg, making him quite unique.

title: THE BRAVE TIN SOLDIER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The table where the tin soldiers stood was cluttered with other toys, but the centerpiece was a charming little paper castle. Through its tiny windows, you could see the rooms inside. In front of the castle, a cluster of miniature trees surrounded a piece of glass that represented a shimmering lake. Wax swans floated on the lake, their reflections dancing in the water. But the most captivating sight was a tiny lady standing at the castle's open door. She was also made of paper, dressed in delicate muslin with a narrow blue ribbon draped over her shoulders like a scarf. Right in front of her was a glimmering tinsel rose, as big as her face. She was a dancer, stretching her arms out and lifting one leg so high that the tin soldier couldn’t see it and thought she, like him, only had one leg. "That's the lady for me," he mused; "but she’s so fancy and lives in a castle while I’m stuck in a box with twenty-five others—definitely not a suitable home for her. Still, I will try to get to know her." He then laid down flat on the table behind a snuff box to get a better look at the beautiful lady, who maintained her balance on one leg. When evening fell, the other tin soldiers were returned to their box, and the household settled in for the night. That’s when the toys began their own festivities, visiting one another, feigning battles, and throwing grand balls. The tin soldiers rattled in their box, wishing they could join the fun, but they couldn’t manage to open the lid. The nutcrackers played leapfrog, and the pencil bounced around the table. The noise was so lively that the canary woke up and started chirping in verse. Only the tin soldier and the dancer remained where they were, each standing firm on one leg. The soldier’s eyes never left her. As the clock struck midnight, the lid of the snuff box flew open, and out popped a little black goblin because the box was a puzzle toy.

"Tin soldier," said the goblin, "don’t long for what isn’t yours."

title: THE BRAVE TIN SOLDIER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
But the tin soldier pretended he couldn’t hear him.

"Fine, just wait until tomorrow," the goblin replied.

The next morning, when the children came in, they placed the tin soldier in the window. It’s unclear whether it was the goblin's doing or just a draft, but the window flew open, and the tin soldier, heels over head, tumbled from the third story to the street below. It was a dreadful fall; he landed headfirst, with his helmet and bayonet wedged between the flagstones and his one leg sticking up in the air. The maid and the little boy hurried downstairs to find him, but he was nowhere to be found; they almost stepped on him at one point. If he had only called out, "Here I am," everything would have been fine, but he was too proud to ask for help while wearing his uniform.

Before long, it began to rain, with raindrops falling faster and faster until a heavy downpour hit. After it stopped, two boys happened to walk by. One of them said, "Look, there’s a tin soldier! He should have a boat to sail in!"

So they made a boat out of newspaper, placed the tin soldier inside it, and sent it sailing down the gutter while they ran alongside, clapping their hands. Wow, the gutter waves were huge! The stream raced along quickly because of the heavy rain. The paper boat bobbed up and down, sometimes spinning so fast that the tin soldier trembled, yet he stood resolutely, his expression unchanged, and his musket held firmly. Suddenly, the boat passed under a bridge that led to a drain, and everything went dark, just like inside the tin soldier's box.

"Where am I heading now?" he wondered. "This is definitely the fault of that black goblin. If only the little lady were here with me in this boat, I wouldn’t mind the darkness at all."

Just then, a giant water rat appeared, living in the drain.

title: THE BRAVE TIN SOLDIER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Do you have a passport?" the rat demanded. "You better show it to me right now!" But the tin soldier remained quiet, gripping his musket even tighter. The boat continued sailing, while the rat, gnashing his teeth, shouted to bits of wood and straw, "Stop him! Stop him! He hasn’t paid his toll, and he hasn’t shown his pass!" But the current grew stronger and stronger. The tin soldier could see daylight shining at the end of the tunnel. Then he heard a roaring noise that was frightening enough to scare even the bravest of warriors. At the end of the drain, it plunged into a large canal over a steep drop, making it as dangerous for him as a waterfall would be for us. He was too close to stop, so the boat shot forward, and the poor tin soldier could only hold himself as upright as possible, not moving an eyelid, to show he wasn't afraid. The boat spun around several times and then filled up with water, inching closer to sinking. He ended up neck-deep in water while the boat sank more and more, the paper softening and loosening until, at last, the water closed over his head. He thought of the graceful dancer he would never see again, and the lyrics of a song echoed in his mind.

"Farewell, warrior! ever brave, Drifting onward to thy grave."

title: THE BRAVE TIN SOLDIER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Then the paper boat fell apart, and the soldier sank beneath the water and was quickly swallowed by a great fish. Oh, it was pitch black inside the fish, much darker than the tunnel, and narrower too. Yet the tin soldier stayed firm, stretched out, gripping his musket. The fish swam back and forth, making amazing movements, but soon it grew still. After a while, a flash of light seemed to shoot through the fish, and then daylight appeared with a voice saying, "Look, a tin soldier!" The fish had been caught, brought to market, and sold to the cook, who took him to the kitchen and cut him open with a large knife. She picked him up by the waist, holding him between her fingers, and brought him into the room where everyone was excited to see this incredible soldier who had traveled inside a fish. But he wasn’t proud at all. They set him down on the table and—oh, what strange things happen in the world!—he was in the very same room from which he had fallen out of the window, surrounded by the same children and the same toys, including that charming castle with the graceful dancer at the door, still balancing on one leg, her other leg raised just like him. Seeing her moved the tin soldier so much that he almost shed tin tears but held them back. He just gazed at her, and they both remained silent. Then one of the boys suddenly picked up the tin soldier and tossed him into the stove. There didn’t seem to be any reason for it, so it must have been the goblin’s fault for living in the snuff box. The flames brightened around the soldier as he stood there, and the heat was intense; he couldn’t tell if it was real fire or the fire of love burning inside him. He watched the little lady and she looked back at him. He felt himself begin to melt, yet he stood tall with his gun on his shoulder. Suddenly, the door burst open, and the gust of air swept up the little dancer, carrying her like a spirit right into the stove beside the tin soldier, and she was instantly consumed by flames. The tin soldier melted into a lump, and the next morning, when the maid cleaned out the ashes from the stove, she found him shaped like a tiny tin heart. But the little dancer was gone, leaving behind only the tinsel rose, which had burned black as coal.

title: THE BUCKWHEAT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Often, after a fierce thunderstorm, a field of buckwheat looks charred and scorched, as if a fire had swept over it. The local folks say this happens because of lightning, but let me share what the sparrow told me. The sparrow got this story from an old willow tree that stands by a buckwheat field, and it's still there today. This impressive tree is a bit gnarled with age; its trunk is split, and grass and brambles grow from the crack. It leans forward slightly, and its branches hang down to the ground like green hair. Surrounding it, fields of corn stretch out—rye, barley, and pretty oats that, when ripe, look like little golden canary birds resting on a branch. The corn seems to smile, and the heaviest, richest ears bow their heads in gentle humility. Not long ago, there was a field of buckwheat right across from the old willow. Unlike the others, this buckwheat stood tall, its head held high. "I'm just as valuable as the rest of you," it declared, "and I’m even more beautiful! My flowers rival the lovely apple blossoms, and we’re a joy to look at. Is there anything prettier than us, old willow tree?"

The willow tree nodded gently, as if to say, "Oh, I certainly do."

But the buckwheat puffed itself up with pride and sneered, "You silly old tree; you're so ancient that grass grows out of you!"

Then, a fierce storm arrived. All the field flowers tucked their leaves together or bowed their heads while the storm raged, but the buckwheat held its ground, refusing to bend. "Just bow your head like us," the flowers urged.

"I don’t need to," the buckwheat replied defiantly.

"Bow your head like the rest of us!" cried the ears of corn. "The storm angel is coming; his wings stretch from the sky to the earth, and he will take you down before you can even ask for mercy!"

"I won’t bow my head!" declared the buckwheat firmly.

"Close your flowers and lower your leaves," the old willow tree advised. "Don’t look at the lightning when the storm hits; even people can’t do that. In a flash of lightning, heaven opens up, and we can glimpse it, but the sight can blind even humans. What do you think will happen to us, mere plants from the earth, if we dare to look?"

"Mere plants?" scoffed the buckwheat. "I’m going to catch a glimpse of heaven myself!" Proudly and boldly, it looked up just as the lightning lit up the sky, making it seem like the whole world was on fire.

When the terrible storm passed, the flowers and corn lifted their heads in the fresh, clear air, brightened by the rain. But the buckwheat lay in the field, charred black by the lightning. The branches of the old willow swayed in the wind, and large drops of water fell from its green leaves as if it were weeping. The sparrows asked the willow, "Why are you crying when everything around you is so bright and cheerful? Look at the sun shining and the clouds floating through the blue sky. Can’t you smell the sweet fragrance of the flowers and bushes? Why do you weep, old willow?" The willow then recounted the proud buckwheat’s downfall and the consequences that followed.

This is the story that the sparrows shared with me one evening when I asked them to tell me a tale.

title: THE BUTTERFLY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once, there was a butterfly who dreamed of finding a bride, and naturally, he wanted one who was the prettiest among the flowers. He looked closely at all the flowerbeds and saw that the flowers were sitting quietly and modestly on their stems, just like young ladies should before getting engaged. However, there were so many flowers that he thought his search might become tiring. Not wanting to put in too much effort, he decided to visit the daisies. The French call this flower "Marguerite," and it’s said that the little daisy can tell fortunes. Lovers pick the petals off the daisies and ask questions about their love lives while plucking each leaf: "Does he or she love me?—Intensely? Madly? A lot? A little? Not at all?" Everyone says it in their own language. The butterfly approached Marguerite and instead of pulling her petals, he kissed each one because he believed kindness would accomplish more.

"Dear Marguerite daisy," he told her, "you are the wisest of all the flowers. Please tell me which flower I should choose as my wife. Which one will be my bride? Once I know, I'll fly straight to her and propose."

But Marguerite didn’t respond; she was upset that he referred to her as a woman when she felt she was just a girl, and there’s a big difference. He asked her again, and again, but she stayed silent. Growing impatient, he decided to fly away and start his search right away. It was early spring when the crocuses and snowdrops were blooming beautifully.

"They’re really lovely," the butterfly thought. "Charming little girls; but they seem a bit too serious."

Then, like any young guy, he started looking for the older flowers. He flew over to the anemones, but they weren't to his liking. The violets were a bit too sentimental for his taste. The lime blossoms were too small, and there were just so many of them. The apple blossoms, while resembling roses, bloomed today but could fall off tomorrow with the slightest breeze, and he worried that a marriage with one of them might be too short-lived. The pea blossom caught his eye the most; she was white and red, graceful and slender, just like those domestic girls who look pretty but are also useful in the kitchen. Just as he was about to ask for her hand, he noticed a pod nearby with a wilted flower hanging from it.

title: THE BUTTERFLY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"That's my sister," the pea blossom said.

"Oh, really? And you’ll be just like her someday," he replied, and flew away quickly, feeling quite disturbed.

A honeysuckle was blooming in the hedge, but there were so many like her, all with long faces and pale skins. No, he didn’t like her. But which flower did he prefer?

Spring passed, summer was almost over, and autumn arrived, yet he still hadn’t made a choice. The flowers were now dressed in their most vibrant colors, but it was no use; they lacked the fresh, sweet scent of youth. Even when hearts grow old, they still long for fragrance, and there wasn't much of that found in the dahlias or the dry chrysanthemums. So, the butterfly turned his attention to the mint growing at the ground level. You see, this plant doesn't have a flower, but it's pure sweetness all around, with an incredible fragrance in every leaf.

"I’ll choose her," said the butterfly, and he approached her with an offer. But the mint remained quiet and still as she listened to him. Finally, she replied,

"Let’s just be friends, if that’s okay. We’re both old now, but we can still enjoy each other's company; besides, marriage—let’s not make ourselves look foolish at our age."

And so, the butterfly ended up with no wife at all. He had taken too long to choose, and that’s never a good idea. Thus, the butterfly became what is known as an old bachelor.

Late autumn set in, bringing rainy and cloudy weather. The cold wind blew over the hunched willows, making them creak. It wasn't a day to be flitting around in summer attire, but luckily, the butterfly was sheltered. He found warmth in a room heated by a stove, feeling as cozy as summer.

"But just existing isn’t enough," he said. "I crave freedom, sunshine, and a little flower for company."

Then he flew against the windowpane and caught the attention of those inside the room, who admired him, caught him, and pinned him into a box of curiosities. They couldn’t do anything more for him.

"Now I'm stuck on a stalk like the flowers," the butterfly commented. "It’s not the best feeling; it must be something like being married, as here I am, stuck for good." With that thought, he managed to comfort himself a little.

"That isn’t much of a comfort," said a potted plant growing in the room.

"Ah," thought the butterfly, "you can’t really trust these potted plants; they have too much to do with humans."

title: THE CHILD IN THE GRAVE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
It was an incredibly sad day, and everyone in the house felt an overwhelming sense of grief; the family's youngest child, a four-year-old boy who was the joy and hope of his parents, had passed away. They still had two daughters left, one of whom was about to be confirmed. Both girls were good-hearted and charming, but the loss of the youngest boy felt the heaviest, especially since he was a son. The sisters mourned deeply, feeling especially hurt by their parents’ sorrow. The father was heartbroken, but the mother was completely overwhelmed by her grief. She had been by her sick child’s side day and night, nursing him and holding him close, as if he were part of her own being. She couldn’t accept that her little one was gone and needed to be laid to rest. She believed that God wouldn’t take her precious child away from her, and when that became the painful reality, she cried out in anguish, "God must not know! He has cruel spirits on Earth who act on their own without listening to a mother's prayers." In her deep sorrow, she drifted away from her faith, and troubling thoughts about death and the afterlife began to consume her. She struggled to convince herself that people were just dust and that life ended with death. But these doubts offered her no comfort, leaving her to spiral into despair. In her darkest moments, she stopped crying, even forgetting her two daughters who were still with her. Her husband’s tears fell onto her forehead, but she didn’t acknowledge him; her mind was only with her lost child, and her entire existence felt wrapped up in memories of him and the innocent words he once spoke.

The day of the little boy's funeral arrived. For nights leading up to it, the mother hadn’t slept, but on the morning of the funeral, she finally succumbed to exhaustion and fell into a deep sleep. Meanwhile, the coffin was taken to a distant room and sealed shut so she wouldn’t hear the sound of the hammer. When she awoke and asked to see her child, her husband, with tears in his eyes, said, "We had to close the coffin, my dear. It was necessary."

title: THE CHILD IN THE GRAVE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"When God is so hard on me, how can I expect people to be any better?" she groaned, tears streaming down her face.

The coffin was brought to the grave, and the heartbroken mother sat with her young daughters. She glanced at them, but her mind was miles away from home. She surrendered herself to her sorrow, tossed around by it like a ship lost at sea. The day of the funeral came and went, and days of heavy sorrow followed. Her daughters and husband looked at her with tearful eyes, offering words of comfort, but what could they say? They were grappling with their own grief. It seemed as though she would never sleep again, and yet that would have been her greatest ally, bringing strength to her body and peace to her soul. Eventually, they managed to persuade her to lie down, and when she did, she lay as still as if she had fallen asleep.

One night, her husband was listening to her breathing, as he often did, convinced she had finally found solace in sleep. He folded his arms and prayed, soon drifting off to a healthy sleep himself. He didn’t notice when his wife quietly got up, dressed, and slipped out of the house, drawn to the same place her thoughts always wandered to—the grave of her child. She walked through the garden and along a field path that led to the churchyard. No one saw her, nor did she see anyone; her eyes were fixed on her destination. It was a beautiful starlit night in early September, with the air calm and still. When she reached the churchyard, she stood by the little grave, which resembled a large bouquet of fragrant flowers. Lowering her head, she leaned over the grave, as if trying to see her little boy through the earth covering him. His smile was so clear in her mind, as were his gentle eyes, which she could never forget, even when he was ill. She sat by his grave, weeping freely, letting her tears fall.

"You would gladly join your child now," said a voice close by—deep and clear enough to touch her heart.

title: THE CHILD IN THE GRAVE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
She looked up to find a man next to her, wrapped in a black cloak with a hood pulled tight over his face. Despite the shadow, she could see his stern features, which held a hint of confidence, and his eyes shone with youthful light.

"Join my child?" she repeated, her voice filled with desperation and longing.

"Are you brave enough to follow me?" asked the figure. "I am Death."

She nodded, indicating her agreement. Suddenly, it felt like all the stars were glowing with the brilliance of a full moon, illuminating the colorful flowers decorating the grave. The earth above it seemed to float aside like a curtain. She sank down, and the figure draped her in a cloak of black; darkness surrounded her as she descended into death. She found herself sinking deeper than the shovel of the grave digger could reach, until the churchyard became a ceiling above her. Then the cloak was lifted, and she stood in a vast hall filled with soft twilight. In an instant, her child appeared before her, smiling and more beautiful than ever. With a silent cry, she rushed to embrace him. A glorious melody filled the space, faint at first but then growing clearer, music that seemed to come from beyond a dark curtain separating the realm of the dead from eternity.

"My sweet, beloved mother," her child said, his familiar, cherished voice surrounding her. Kiss after kiss followed in a blissful reunion. Then the child pointed to the dark curtain. "There’s nothing as beautiful here as what’s out there. Mother, don’t you see them? Oh, it’s true happiness!"

Yet the mother saw nothing beyond the curtain, only darkness. Her earthly eyes couldn’t perceive what her child could see—one chosen by God to be with Him. She could hear the music, but she couldn't understand the words—the truth she needed to trust.

"I can fly now, mother," the child said, "I can soar alongside other joyful children into the presence of the Almighty. I long to fly away, but if you keep weeping for me, you may never see me again. And yet, I would still like to go. May I not soar away? You promise to come to me soon, don’t you, dear mother?"

title: THE CHILD IN THE GRAVE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Oh, please, stay!" the mother pleaded. "Just one more moment; let me look at you, kiss you, and hold you close to my heart again."

She kissed and embraced her child. Suddenly, she heard her name called from above. What could it mean? Her name was being spoken in a sorrowful voice.

"Do you hear?" said the child. "It’s your father calling for you." Moments later, deep sighs echoed around them, like the cries of children mourning. "Those are my sisters," he said. "Mother, please don’t tell me you’ve forgotten them."

In that instant, she recalled the loved ones she had left behind, and an overwhelming fear gripped her. She glanced around at the dark night. Shadowy figures floated by, some of them familiar as they drifted toward the dark curtain, disappearing beyond it. Would her husband and daughters pass by? No, their heart-wrenching sighs still echoed from above, and she had nearly forgotten them while consumed with thoughts of her deceased child.

"Mother, now the bells of heaven are ringing," the child said. "Mother, the sun is about to rise."

A brilliant light flooded in upon her, and her child disappeared as she was lifted upwards. Cold surrounded her, and she raised her head to find herself lying in the churchyard, atop her child's grave. In a dream, the Lord had guided her feet and illuminated her spirit. She knelt down and prayed for forgiveness. She had wanted to keep a soul from its eternal journey, forgetting her responsibilities toward the living. After her prayer, she felt lighter. The sun burst forth, a little bird sang above her, and the church bells rang for the early service. Everything around her felt sacred, and her heart was renewed. She recognized the goodness of God and the duties she had to fulfill, eagerly making her way home. She leaned down over her husband, who still slept peacefully. Her warm, loving kiss stirred him awake, and heartfelt expressions of love flowed between them. Now, she was as gentle and strong as a devoted wife should be; from her lips came the words of faith: "Whatever God does is right and best."

Her husband then asked, "Where did you suddenly find such strength and comforting faith?"

As she kissed him and their children, she replied, "It came from God, through my child in the grave."

title: THE CONCEITED APPLE-BRANCH
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
It was May, and although the wind still had a chilly bite, nature was cheerful with the message, "Spring is here!" Wildflowers were bursting forth, brightening the hedges. Under a small apple tree, Spring felt particularly vibrant, showcasing one branch adorned with delicate pink blossoms ready to bloom. The branch was fully aware of its beauty; it was a knowledge as natural as breath. So, I wasn’t surprised when a nobleman’s carriage pulled up nearby, carrying the young countess. She remarked that the apple branch was the very embodiment of spring’s charm. Soon after, the branch was plucked and cradled in her delicate hand, sheltered by her silk parasol. They then drove off to the castle, where grand halls and exquisite drawing rooms awaited. Light white curtains fluttered at open windows, and gorgeous flowers filled crystal clear vases. Among them, the apple branch was placed alongside fresh beech twigs, creating a scene that was simply delightful. The branch, enamored by this attention, began to feel quite proud, reflecting a little too much of human nature.

Guests of all sorts filled the room, and their admiration varied according to their social standing. Some stayed silent, while others overshared their praises. It didn’t take long for the apple branch to realize that there’s as much variety in humans as there is in plants. Some thrive on spectacle, while others struggle to maintain their worth, and frankly, some wouldn’t be missed much if they were gone. The apple branch thought this as it gazed out the open window, looking over gardens and fields, where many flowers and plants lay—some rich and vibrant, others humble and modest.

"Poor, overlooked herbs," the apple branch lamented. "There’s clearly a distinction between them and someone like me. I can’t imagine how sad they must feel, if they experience emotions similar to mine! There must be a reason for this difference, or else we’d all be equals."

The apple branch looked down at the flowers with a mix of pity, especially a little flower found in fields and ditches. No one bothered to gather these common blooms into bouquets; they were far too ordinary and even grew stubbornly between paving stones, sprouting everywhere like weeds. They carried the rather unflattering name of "dandelions."

title: THE CONCEITED APPLE-BRANCH
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Poor, disregarded plants," sighed the apple branch, "it’s not your fault for being unattractive or having such a bad reputation; but just like people, there has to be a hierarchy among plants."

"A hierarchy!" exclaimed a sunbeam, as it warmly kissed the blossoming apple branch and then danced over the yellow dandelions in the fields. All the flowers were siblings, and the sunbeam loved them all, no matter their status.

The apple branch had never considered the vast love of God that embraces all creation, everything that lives and thrives in His presence; it had never pondered the good and the beautiful, often hidden but never forgotten by Him—not just among lower creatures, but also within humanity. The sunbeam knew better.

"You’re not seeing the whole picture," the sunbeam told the apple branch. "What about the plant you pity so much?"

"The dandelion," the apple branch replied, "No one ever includes it in a bouquet; it often gets trampled, there are just so many of them. And when they go to seed, they turn into fluffy white balls that blow away in the breeze, sticking to people's clothes. They’re just weeds; but I guess weeds are a part of life. I’m genuinely grateful I’m not like one of those weeds."

Then, a group of children came running through the fields, one little one so small that others had to carry him. When they set him down on the grass surrounded by yellow flowers, he burst into laughter, kicked his legs, rolled around, picked the yellow blooms, and showered them with kisses in pure joy. The older kids began snipping flowers off with long stems, weaving them into necklaces and belts, even crafting a crown to adorn the youngest’s head. They looked stunning in their crowns of green stems and golden flowers. Meanwhile, the eldest among them carefully collected the spent flowers, whose feathery seed heads were like delicate white crowns. The kids held them up to their mouths, blowing gently to scatter the seeds, believing what their grandmothers told them—that whoever managed to blow them away would receive new clothes by year’s end. The despised dandelion was, in that moment, transformed into a symbol of fortune.

title: THE CONCEITED APPLE-BRANCH
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Do you see?" said the sunbeam. "Can you see the beauty in these flowers? Can you see how they bring joy?"

"Yes, to children," the apple branch acknowledged.

Soon, an old woman appeared in the field, wielding a dull knife without a handle, digging around the roots of some dandelions to pull them up. She planned to make tea with some and sell the others to a pharmacist for some cash.

"But beauty is worth so much more than that," the apple branch insisted. "Only the select few are allowed to bask in the realm of beauty. There is a difference among plants, just as there’s a difference among people."

The sunbeam then spoke of the immense love of God, visible in creation and everything alive, emphasizing that His gifts are equally scattered in this life and the next.

"That’s just your viewpoint," the apple branch retorted.

Moments later, some guests entered the room, among them the young countess—the very lady who had placed the apple branch in the transparent vase, basking in the sunlight. She brought something that looked like a flower, shielded by a few large leaves to protect it from gusts of wind. It was handled with more care than the apple branch had ever experienced. When she gently peeled away the leaves, out came the feathery seed-head of the lowly dandelion. This was the treasure she had plucked, carefully covering it to ensure no delicate feathers fluttered away. As she presented it, she marveled at its beauty, lightness, and unique design, soon to be set free by the breeze.

"Look," she exclaimed, "how wonderfully God has crafted this little flower! I’ll paint it alongside the apple branch. Everyone admires the beauty of the apple branch, but this humble flower has a different kind of loveliness bestowed by Heaven. Though they seem different, both are children of the realm of beauty."

In that moment, the sunbeam kissed the humble flower and then the blooming apple branch, which blushed a rosy hue.

title: THE DAISY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Hey there! In the countryside, not too far from the main road, there stood a farmhouse; you might have passed by it yourself. In front of it was a small flower garden enclosed by painted wooden fences; nearby, a ditch ran alongside, where a tiny daisy grew on the fresh green bank. The sun shone down on it just as warmly and brightly as it did on the grand flowers in the garden, so it thrived. One morning, it fully opened, its little snow-white petals spread around a yellow center like rays of sunlight. It didn’t mind that no one could see it in the grass, or that it was often overlooked; on the contrary, it felt quite happy, turning toward the sun, listening to the lark singing high up in the sky.

The little daisy was as joyful as if it were a holiday, even though it was just Monday. All the children were at school, sitting at their desks and learning their lessons, while it stood on its slender green stalk learning from the sun and its surroundings just how kind God is. It felt joyful that the little lark's song mirrored its own feelings so sweetly. With a sense of wonder, the daisy gazed up at the bird that could fly and sing without envy. "I can see and hear," it thought; "the sun shines on me, and the forest embraces me. How rich I am!"

title: THE DAISY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In the nearby garden, many grand and magnificent flowers bloomed, and strangely, the less fragrance they had, the prouder and haughtier they became. The peonies puffed themselves up, trying to outshine the roses, but size isn’t everything! The tulips flaunted their vibrant colors, standing tall and upright like candles so everyone could see them better. In their arrogance, they overlooked the little daisy, which observed them and thought, "They’re so rich and beautiful! I’m sure the pretty bird will come down to visit them. Thank God I’m so close and at least can witness all this splendor." Just as the daisy was contemplating, the lark swooped down, chirping "Tweet," but not to the peonies or tulips—no, it came right to the grass where the humble daisy was. The joy the daisy felt was so overwhelming that it was at a loss for words. The little bird hopped around it, singing, "The grass is so soft, and what a lovely little flower with its golden heart and silver dress is blooming here!" The yellow center of the daisy did look like gold, while its delicate petals glimmered like silver.

The daisy was incredibly happy! No one could understand just how much. The bird kissed it gently with its beak, sang sweetly, and then flew back up into the blue sky. The little daisy took a long time to recover from this surprise. It glanced over at the other flowers in the garden, hoping they had seen its joy and the honor it had received; surely they understood. But the tulips stood stiffer than ever, their faces red with annoyance. The peonies looked sulky; if they could speak, they would surely have lectured the daisy. The little flower could clearly see they were uneasy, and she felt a deep pity for them.

A little while later, a girl wandered into the garden, holding a sharp knife. She headed straight for the tulips and began cutting them down one by one. "Oh no!" sighed the daisy, "This is terrible; they’re finished now."

The girl took the tulips away. The daisy felt thankful to be outside and just a small flower—it was grateful for its place. At sunset, it folded its petals and drifted off to sleep, dreaming all night of the sun and the little bird.

title: THE DAISY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The next morning, when the daisy stretched its tender petals open like little arms toward the light, it recognized the lark’s voice, but this time it sounded so sad. The poor bird had good reason to be downcast; it had been caught and was kept in a cage by an open window. It sang about the joyful days when it could fly freely, the fresh green corn in the fields, and the times it soared nearly to the clouds. The poor lark was deeply unhappy as a prisoner. The little daisy wished it could help, but what could it possibly do? It was challenging for such a small flower to find a solution. It completely forgot about the beauty surrounding it, how warmly the sun shone, and how beautifully white its own petals were. All it could think about was the poor trapped bird, for which it felt powerless. Then, two little boys came into the garden; one had a large sharp knife, similar to the one the girl had used to cut the tulips. They walked right toward the little daisy, and it couldn’t understand what they intended.

"Here’s a nice piece of turf for the lark," one of the boys said, and he started cutting a square around the daisy so that it would stay in the middle of the grass.

"Pull the flower out," said the other boy, and the daisy trembled in fear, because to be uprooted meant certain death. It desperately wanted to live, especially since it was going to go with the square of turf into the poor lark’s cage.

"No, let it stay," said the other boy, "it looks so pretty."

So it stayed and was taken into the lark's cage. The poor bird lamented its lost freedom and flapped its wings against the cage bars; the little daisy wanted to say something comforting but couldn’t find the words. Thus, the morning slipped by.

title: THE DAISY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I have no water," said the captive lark, "they've all gone out and forgotten to give me anything to drink. My throat is dry and burning. I feel like I’m on fire inside, and the air feels so heavy. Oh, I think I might die, and then I’ll leave behind the warm sunshine, the lush meadows, and all the beauty that God has created." It pecked at the piece of grass to refresh itself a little. Then it noticed the little daisy, nodded toward it, kissed it with its beak, and said, "You will fade away here, poor little flower. You and the piece of grass are all they gave me in exchange for the whole world I enjoyed outside. Each blade of grass will be a green tree for me, and each white petal of you will represent a fragrant flower. Sadly, you only remind me of what I have lost."

"I wish I could comfort the poor lark," thought the daisy. It couldn’t move any of its leaves, but the scent of its delicate petals filled the air, stronger than usual, and the bird caught wind of it. Even though it was dying of thirst, and in pain tearing up the grass around it, it ignored the flower.

Evening came, and no one showed up to give the poor bird a drop of water; it flapped its beautiful wings in distress, letting out only a faint and sad "Tweet, tweet." It bowed its little head toward the flower, heart breaking from longing and need. The daisy couldn’t fold its petals and fall asleep as it had the night before; it drooped sadly. The boys returned the next morning; when they saw the dead bird, they wept bitterly, dug a nice grave for it, and adorned it with flowers. They placed the bird's body in a lovely red box, wanting to give it a royal burial. While it was alive and singing, they had overlooked it, allowing the poor thing to suffer in the cage; now, they were crying over it and covering it with flowers. The turf with the little daisy was tossed out onto the dusty road. No one remembered the flower that had felt so deeply for the bird and longed to console it.

title: THE DARNING-NEEDLE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a darning-needle that thought she was so special that she believed she deserved to be used for embroidery. "Hold me tightly," she would say to the fingers that picked her up, "don't let me drop; if I fall, I'll be lost forever, I'm just too fancy."

"Is that what you think?" the fingers replied as they grabbed her firmly.

"Look, I'm coming with a train!" the darning-needle said, pulling a long thread behind her, but there was no knot in it.

The fingers then placed the tip of the needle against the cook's slipper, which had a tear in the leather that needed stitching.

"What rough work!" exclaimed the darning-needle, "I won't be able to handle this. I'll break!—I’m breaking!" And sure enough, she snapped. "Didn’t I say so?" she grumbled, "I know I'm too delicate for work like this."

"Now this needle is completely useless for sewing," said the fingers, but they still held on to her. The cook then dripped some sealing-wax onto the needle and used it to attach her handkerchief.

title: THE DARNING-NEEDLE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"So now I'm a brooch," declared the darning-needle; "I knew I'd come to something important someday: talent always finds a way to shine through!" She chuckled quietly to herself; of course, no one ever saw a darning-needle laugh. Proudly, she sat as if she were in a grand carriage, surveying her surroundings. "May I ask if you're made of gold?" she addressed a nearby pin. "You look quite lovely with your unique head, even though you're a bit small. You must strive to grow, for not everyone gets sealing-wax dripped on them!" As she said this, the darning-needle puffed herself up so much that she tumbled out of the handkerchief and into the sink the cook was cleaning. "Now I'm off on an adventure," said the needle as she floated away with the dirty water, "I really hope I won’t get lost." But she did get lost, ending up in a gutter. "I'm too special for this world," the darning-needle said as she lay in the gutter, "but I know who I am, and that gives me some comfort." So she maintained her proud demeanor and stayed cheerful.

As she lay there, all sorts of things floated by—chips, straws, and bits of old paper. "Look at them go," the darning-needle said, "they have no clue what's underneath them. I'm here, and that's where I'll stay. Look at that chip; it only thinks about itself. And there’s a straw twisting and turning! Don’t get too full of yourself, or you might bump into a rock. And there goes a piece of newspaper; what’s written on it is forgotten, yet it acts all important. I sit here patiently, knowing who I am, so I won't budge."

One day, something shiny nearby caught the darning-needle’s eye, and she thought it was a diamond. But it was just a piece of broken glass. She spoke to it because it sparkled, pretending to be a brooch. "I suppose you're really a diamond?" she asked.

title: THE DARNING-NEEDLE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Well, something like that," it replied, and they both thought the other was quite valuable. They started chatting about the world and all the arrogant people in it.

"I've been in a lady’s sewing kit," said the darning-needle, "and that lady was the cook. She had five fingers on each hand, and I've never seen such arrogant fingers—even though they were just used to take me out of the box and put me back in!"

"They weren't high-born!" the darning-needle scoffed, "They were just so proud. They were five siblings, all fingers but of different lengths. The one in charge was the thumb; short and stout, he could only bow once because he had just one joint in his back. But he claimed that if he were cut off, no man would be fit to be a soldier. Sweet-tooth, the next finger, loved to dive into sweet or sour, pointed at the sun and moon, and wrote letters when the fingers wrote. Longman, the middle finger, looked over the others; Gold-band, the next finger, had a golden ring around his waist, and little Playman did nothing at all, instead relishing his idleness. They were all boastful, and I was tired of them!"

"And now we sit here shining," said the piece of broken glass.

At that moment, more water rushed into the gutter, causing it to overflow, and the piece of glass was swept away.

"So he gets promoted," said the darning-needle, "while I'm stuck here. I’m too special; it’s my pride, and I don’t care!" So she sat there, proud as ever, thinking thoughts like, "I could almost believe I came from a sunbeam because I’m so fine. It feels like sunbeams are always searching for me under the water. Oh! I’m too fine for even my mother to find. If I still had my old eye, which broke off, I think I’d cry—but no, I wouldn't do that; crying isn’t elegant."

One day, a couple of street boys were splashing around in the gutter, digging for old nails, coins, and other treasures. It was a dirty task, but they enjoyed it. "Hey!" one of them shouted after pricking himself with the darning-needle, "here’s something for you!"

"I’m not something; I’m a young lady!" the darning-needle protested, but no one heard her.

The sealing-wax had come off, and she was completely black now. But she thought the black made her look slim, so she believed she was even fancier than before.

"Here comes an egg shell floating by," said one of the boys, and they stuck the darning-needle inside the shell.

"White walls, and I’m black myself," the darning-needle remarked, "that looks nice; now I can be seen, but I hope I won’t get seasick, or I might break again." She didn’t get seasick, and she didn’t break. "Having a strong constitution helps against seasickness; plus, one must never forget their own worth. Now that my seasickness is over, delicate folks can handle quite a lot."

Crack! went the shell as a wagon rolled over it. "Goodness, look how it crushes!" the darning-needle gasped. "I’m about to be sick. I’m breaking!" But she didn’t break even though the wagon passed over her, and there she lay.

title: THE DROP OF WATER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
You probably know what a magnifying glass is—those round lenses that make everything appear a hundred times larger than it really is. When someone holds one up to their eye and looks at a drop of water from the nearby pond, they see thousands of incredible tiny creatures that would otherwise go unnoticed. But they’re really there, and it’s not an illusion. It almost looks like a huge plate filled with spiders jumping around in a frenzy. And they are fierce! They pull off each other’s legs, arms, and bodies, both front and back; yet, in their own way, they seem cheerful and lively.

Once upon a time, there was an old man known by everyone as Kribble-Krabble; that was his name. He always wanted the very best of everything, and when he couldn’t get it in the usual ways, he resorted to magic.

One day, he sat down and held his magnifying glass up to his eye, gazing at a drop of water he had scooped up from a puddle by the ditch. Oh, what kribbling and krabbling there was! All those little creatures jumped and tugged at each other, even eating one another.

"That’s horrible!" exclaimed old Kribble-Krabble. "Can’t we convince them to live in peace so that each one can mind their own business?"

He pondered over this repeatedly, but nothing he thought of worked, so he decided to use magic.

"I need to give them some color so they can be seen better," he thought, as he poured what looked like a small drop of red wine into the drop of water. But it was actually witches' blood from the ear lobes, the finest kind, at a cost of ninepence per drop. Now, the amazing little creatures were bright pink all over, resembling a whole town of naked wild men.

"What do you have there?" inquired another old magician who didn’t have a name—and that was the best part about him.

"Well, if you can guess what it is, I’ll give it to you as a gift," replied Kribble-Krabble.

But guessing is not so easy if you don’t know.

The nameless magician looked through the magnifying glass.

It honestly looked like a busy city reflected in there, where everyone was running around without clothes. It was shocking! But even more shocking was how the creatures pushed and shoved one another, biting and tugging, some scrambling to climb up while others were pulled down.

"Look! Look! His leg is longer than mine! Ugh! Get rid of it! There’s one with a little bruise. It hurts him, but let’s make sure it hurts even more!"

They were all hacking away at him, pulling and eating him up because of that minor bruise. And among them was one little creature sitting still, like a sweet maiden, just wishing for peace. But they dragged her out, pulled her around, and even ate her.

"So, what do you think it is?" asked Kribble-Krabble. "Can you figure it out?"

"Oh, that’s easy enough to see," the other replied. "That’s Paris, or some other major city; they all look alike. It’s a big city!"

"Nope, it’s a drop of puddle water!" declared Kribble-Krabble.

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
We're on our way to Paris for the Exhibition.

Now we're here. What a journey it was, a flight without magic. We soared through the air on steam over the sea and land.

Indeed, we live in a time of fairy tales.

We find ourselves in the heart of Paris, in a grand hotel. Beautiful flowers adorn the staircases, and soft carpets cover the floors.

Our room is cozy, and from the open balcony door, we can see a large square. Spring has arrived in Paris, just like us, personified by a stunning young chestnut tree with its freshly opened leaves. It sparkles in its spring attire, standing out vividly among the other trees. One of those trees has been removed from the list of living trees. It lies on the ground, roots exposed. In its place, the young chestnut tree is set to be planted and thrive.

The chestnut tree is still perched high on the heavy wagon that brought it here this morning, journeying several miles to Paris. For years, it had thrived under the shelter of a mighty oak, where a wise old clergyman would often sit, sharing stories with children.

The young chestnut tree had listened to those stories, for the Dryad who lived within it was like a child. She remembered the days when the tree barely poked above the grass and ferns. These ferns would never grow taller, but the tree would rise each year, soaking in sunshine and fresh air, drinking up the dew and rain. It had also faced strong winds and rain multiple times, as is the way of nature's education.

The Dryad cherished her life, delighting in the sunlight and the songs of the birds. But most of all, she loved when human voices filled the air; she understood their language just as easily as she understood that of animals.

Butterflies, cockchafers, and dragonflies visited her. They chatted about the village, the vineyards, the forests, and the old castle with its parks and ponds. In the water lived creatures that could traverse the depths, flying with grace beneath the surface—wise beings, silent yet profound.

The swallow, who had dived beneath the surface, spoke of pretty goldfish, thick turbot, and plump brill. She described them vividly, but said, "Self is the best view," implying that one must see for themselves. But how could the Dryad ever experience such beings? She could only gaze out over the beautiful countryside, watching the industrious nature of humans.

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
It was magnificent, especially when the old clergyman sat beneath the oak tree, discussing France and the remarkable feats of her sons and daughters, whose names would resonate in admiration for all time.

Then the Dryad learned about Joan of Arc, the shepherd girl, and Charlotte Corday; she heard tales of Henry the Fourth and Napoleon the First—names that would echo in the hearts of the people.

The village children listened intently, as did the Dryad, who became a pupil like the rest. As clouds drifted past, she envisioned everything she heard—those clouds became her picture book.

She felt blissful in beautiful France, a land of creativity and freedom. Yet in her heart remained a sting, for every bird and every flying creature was much freer than she. Even a tiny fly could explore more of the world beyond her horizon.

France was vast and magnificent, yet she could only see a tiny piece of it. The land stretched out infinitely, with vineyards, forests, and grand cities. Among all, Paris was the most splendid and powerful. The birds could reach it, but she never could!

Amid the village children was a ragged little girl, yet she was beautiful. She was always laughing or singing, weaving red flowers into her dark hair.

"Don't go to Paris!" the old clergyman cautioned her. "Poor child! If you go there, it will ruin you."

The Dryad often thought of her, for she shared the same wish and longing for the grand city.

The Dryad's tree was bearing its first chestnut blossoms while birds flitted around, all under the beautiful sunshine. Then a grand carriage approached, drawn by spirited horses, and within sat an elegant lady. On the back seat, a small groom managed his balance. The Dryad recognized the lady, as did the old clergyman, who shook his head sadly when he saw her.

"So you went there after all, and it was your ruin, poor Mary!" he said.

"Poor?" thought the Dryad. "No, she's dressed like a countess" (she had transformed into one in the city of magic). "Oh, if only I could be there, surrounded by all that splendor and glamour! They shine up to the clouds at night; I can tell just by looking up where the city lies."

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
In that direction, the Dryad stared each evening. She saw the glowing cloud on the horizon at night, and would sometimes miss those drifting clouds that illustrated the city and stories from history.

The child grasped at picture-books, and the Dryad reached for the cloud world, her mental imagery. A sudden clear sky felt like an empty page; she'd only seen such blank pages for days.

During the hot summer, not a single breeze stirred on those blazing days. Every leaf and flower seemed to be in a stupor, as did the people.

Then clouds gathered above, covering the region where the shining mist signaled, "Here lies Paris."

The clouds rose like a mountain chain, hurried through the sky, and spread over the entire landscape as far as the Dryad could see.

The clouds piled up like enormous blue-black rocks, their tops illuminated by occasional lightning.

"These are also servants of the Lord God," the old clergyman had said. A brilliant flash struck, bursting the ancient oak apart, its crown shattering. It looked as if the tree stretched its arms to embrace the light bearers.

No bronze cannon could resound across the land for a royal birth as thunder echoed at the oak's demise. Rain poured down; a refreshing wind blew in, and everything felt rejuvenated. The old clergyman said a few words to honor the fallen tree, while an artist sketched it as a lasting memory.

"Everything fades away," said the Dryad, "like a cloud, never to return!"

The old clergyman also didn't return. The green roof of his school was gone, his teaching chair vanished. The children stopped coming, but autumn arrived, then winter, followed by spring. In all this seasonal change, the Dryad gazed at the area where Paris twinkled with its bright mist far on the horizon.

Engines and trains rushed from the town, whistling and howling at all hours of the day. In the evening, just before midnight, at dawn, and throughout the day, the trains arrived. People streamed in from every kingdom, summoned to Paris by a new wonder of the world.
