What did this wonder look like?

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
"A magnificent blossom of art and industry," one person explained, "has blossomed in the Champ de Mars—a gigantic sunflower, from which you can learn geography and statistics, rise to the heights of art and poetry, and explore the greatness of various lands."

"It's a fairy tale flower," another added, "a colorful lotus plant that spreads its green leaves like a velvet carpet across the sand. Spring brought it forth, summer will showcase its magnificence, and autumn winds will sweep it away, leaving not a leaf or a root behind."

At the Military School, in peacetime, there's a war arena—a blade of grass-free sandy expanse, as if plucked from the desert, where Fata Morgana reveals dazzling castles and hanging gardens. However, in the Champ de Mars, these sights were to be more splendid and extraordinary than in the East, as human artistry transformed these illusory scenes into reality.

"The palace of Aladdin in the present has been built," they proclaimed. "Day by day, hour by hour, it reveals more of its wonderful splendor."

The endless halls shimmer with marble and vibrant colors. Here, "Master Bloodless" operates his steel and iron limbs in a vast machinery hall. Works of art in metal, stone, and Gobelins tapestry announce the vibrant intellect alive in every land. Galleries of paintings and the beauty of flowers showcase everything that human ingenuity and skill can create. Even artifacts from ancient times have emerged at this major event.

To grasp such an overwhelming spectacle, it had to be divided into smaller portions, pieced together like a playful toy.

Like a grand table at Christmas Eve, the Champ de Mars presented a magical castle of industry and art, encircled by souvenirs from all nations, all on a grand scale, as every country contributed its remembrance.

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
Here was the royal palace of Egypt, there the desert's caravanserai. The Bedouin had left his sunny lands, hastily riding by on his camel. Here stood Russian stables, home to the fiery horses of the steppe. There was the simple straw-thatched house of the Danish farmer, waving the Dannebrog flag, next to Gustavus Vasa's wooden house from Dalarna, adorned with exquisite carvings. American cabins, English cottages, French pavilions, kiosks, theaters, churches—all scattered about, and between them fresh green grass, pure spring water, blooming shrubs, rare trees, and hothouses that could transport one into a tropical forest; entire gardens brought from Damascus, flourishing under a single roof. What colors! What fragrances!

Artificial grottos surrounded fresh and saltwater bodies, offering glimpses into the realm of fish; visitors felt as if they strolled at the bottom of the sea, among the fish and polyps.

"All this," they said, "the Champ de Mars provides," as the bustling crowd swept around the lavishly set table, like a busy ant colony, walking or riding in little carriages, since not all could endure such a tiring journey.

They thronged from morning until late at night. Boat after boat, packed with people, glided down the Seine. The number of carriages continued to swell. The crowd of foot and horseback grew denser. Carriages and omnibuses overflowed, stuffed with people. All these tributaries flowed toward the Exhibition. Every entrance displayed France's flag; around the world's fair waved the flags of all nations. A myriad of sounds echoed from the machines; from the towers, the melody of chimes rang out, mingling with the rough songs from the eastern cafés. It was a kingdom of Babel, a wonder of the world!

Indeed, it was. That’s what all reports claimed, and who hadn't heard them? The Dryad was aware of everything that was shared about this new wonder in the city of cities.

"Fly away, you birds! Fly away to see, and come back and tell me!" said the Dryad.

The wish grew into a burning desire—the sole thought of her existence. Then, one quiet night, while the full moon illuminated the sky, the Dryad saw a spark fly out of the moon's orb, resembling a shooting star. A grand, noble figure appeared before her tree, its leaves swaying as if stirred by a storm.

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
In a voice rich and strong, like a trumpet proclaiming the end of life and summoning souls, it declared: "You will go to the city of magic; you will root yourself there, experiencing the mighty breezes, fresh air, and sunlight. But your lifespan will be shortened; the years that would have awaited you in free nature will be condensed into a small tale. Poor Dryad! This shall lead to your downfall. Your yearning will intensify, your desire will become more turbulent, and the tree itself will become a prison for you; you will abandon your confines, surrender your nature to intermingle with humans. The years that were yours will shrink to half the life-span of a fleeting fly, which lives just a day: one night, and your light will be extinguished—the tree’s leaves will wither and drift away, never to be green again!"

Those words echoed, and the light disappeared, but not the Dryad's longing. She trembled with a feverish anticipation.

"I will go there!" she cried joyfully. "Life is unfolding, swelling like a cloud; nobody knows where it is heading."

When the gray dawn broke and the moon grew pale, the cherished hour chimed. The promised words came true.

Workers arrived with spades and poles, digging around the roots of the tree, going deeper and deeper. A wagon came, pulled by many horses, and they lifted the tree, roots and all, securing it with chains as they wrapped matting around its roots as if it wore warm shoes. Thus began the journey—onwards to Paris. The tree was destined to become a beautiful addition to the glorious city of France.

As the chestnut tree was moved, its twigs and leaves quivered in anticipation; the Dryad too felt that pleasurable tingle of expectation.

"Away! Away!" her pulse echoed in every beat. "Away! Away!" became words that fluttered and trembled in her mind. The Dryad forgot to say goodbye to her homeland; she didn't think of the dancing grass or the pure daisies, which had looked up to her as a young lady, a princess playing at shepherdess in the open air.

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
The chestnut tree stood on the wagon, its branches nodding; whether it meant "goodbye" or "let's move on," the Dryad didn't know. She could only dream of the marvelous new world that awaited, feeling excitement that no innocent child's heart, nor any passionate heart, had felt more eagerly on a journey to Paris.

Her farewell resounded in the words "Away! away!"

The wheels turned; the distant landscape approached; the present faded away, transformed like the clouds. New vineyards, forests, villages, and villas appeared—came closer—then vanished!

The chestnut tree moved forward, and the Dryad, too, went along with it. Train after train rushed by, releasing vaporous clouds that formed images, telling tales of Paris—from whence they came and where the Dryad was headed.

Everything around understood what was happening; it seemed every tree she passed reached out its leaves, pleading, "Take me with you! Take me with you!" for within every tree was a longing Dryad.

So much changed during this journey! Houses seemed to sprout from the earth—more and more—denser and denser. Chimneys rose like flowerpots arrayed side by side or stacked on rooftops. Huge signs and vibrant figures covered the walls from top to bottom, brightly illuminated.

"When does Paris begin, and when will I arrive?" asked the Dryad.

The crowd thickened; the noise and chaos grew; carriages followed one after another, people on foot and on horseback intertwined; shops crowded in every direction, filled with music, chatter, and laughter.

The Dryad, in her tree, found herself suddenly in the heart of Paris. The heavy wagon came to a halt in a small square filled with trees. The tall buildings surrounding her had balconies from which residents peered down at the fresh chestnut tree, set to replace the fallen tree on the ground.

Onlookers paused, smiling at its vibrant spring-time freshness. The older trees, still budding, whispered in welcoming gestures as the fountain shot its water high into the air, showering the newcomer with pearly droplets as if giving it a refreshing greeting.

The Dryad sensed her tree being lifted off the wagon and placed in the spot where it would grow. Its roots were buried in earth, fresh sod laid down. Blooming shrubs and potted flowers were positioned around it, creating a little garden in the square.

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
The lifeless tree that succumbed to the city's polluted air was now loaded onto the wagon and taken away. People observed. Children and elders sat on benches, admiring the vibrant new tree. And as we narrated this story, we stood on a balcony, gazing down at the fresh spring sight brought from the pure country air, and said, as the old clergyman would have, "Poor Dryad!"

"I’m happy! I’m happy!" the Dryad exclaimed with joy; "yet I can’t fully grasp or articulate what I feel. Everything is just as I imagined, and yet different from what I envisioned."

The buildings loomed so tall and close! Sunlight only graced one wall, plastered with posters that captured the attention of the passersby, forming a crowd.

Carriages rushed past; light and heavy ones blended together. Omnibuses, those overstuffed moving houses, rattled by; horsemen galloped among them, while carts and wagons asserted their presence.

The Dryad wondered if these towering structures, so closely packed, might shift forms, like clouds, and part ways just enough for her to glimpse Paris, to see Notre Dame, the Vendome Column, and the wondrous buildings that had attracted so many visitors.

But the houses remained fixed in place. Even as night fell, the lamps flickered to life. Gas flames illuminated the shops, casting light into the branches of the trees, making it feel like summer sunlight. The stars appeared—those very same stars that the Dryad had gazed upon back home. She sensed a fresh, pure breeze emanating from them. She felt herself uplifted, invigorated, as if she could see through every leaf and source of the tree’s roots. Amidst the noise, chaos, and vibrant colors, she felt the tender gaze of caring eyes upon her.

From the side streets came the cheerful tunes of fiddles and wind instruments. "Up! To the dance, to the dance!" they beckoned, inviting revelry and enjoyment. The kind of music that might make trees, houses, and horses want to dance, if only they knew how. The Dryad was engulfed in intoxicating joy.

"How wonderful, how splendid it is!" she exclaimed, beaming. "Now I'm really in Paris!"

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
The following day and night offered the same sights, similar hustle, similar energy; changing yet constant. This continued in the sequence of days.

"Now I know every tree, every flower in this square! I recognize every building, every balcony, every shop in this small corner, where I’m denied sight of this grand city. Where are the triumphal arches, the Boulevards, the amazing structures of the world? I see none of it. As if caged, I stand among these towering buildings, now familiar, with their signs, emblems, and posters; all the painted sweets that no longer tempt me. Where are the things of which I heard, the things I longed for, the reasons I wanted to be here? What have I grasped, found, won? That same longing lingers within me; I yearn for a vibrant life to embrace, to experience. I must step into the midst of life itself, mingling among the crowds. I must soar like a bird. I must see and feel, become fully human. I want to sparkle like the clouds and bask in the sunlight of life, gazing down from above, before fading into oblivion, no one knowing where I might go."

"Take away the years truly meant for me, and replace them with but half the life of a fleeting fly! Free me from my confinement! Grant me human life, human happiness—just one short night, if that’s all I can have; then punish me for wanting to live, for my yearning for life! Remove me from Your list. Let my shell, the young tree, wilt or be cut down, burned to ashes, scattered to the winds!"

A rustle swept through the tree's leaves; every leaf quivered as though fire coursed through them. A gust of wind shook the green crown, and from within it emerged a radiant female figure. In an instant, she was beneath the brightly-lit leafy branches, youthful and beautiful, much like Mary, to whom the clergyman had predicted, "The grand city will be your ruin."

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
The Dryad sat at the base of the tree—at her locked door, whose key had been discarded. So youthful! So lovely! The stars gazed down at her, blinking with intrigue. The gas lamps glimmered and beckoned her closer. How delicate she appeared, yet so blooming!—a child, and yet an alluring maiden! Her gown flowed like silk, green like the newly-budded leaves upon her tree; in her nut-brown hair, a half-opened chestnut blossom rested. She resembled the Goddess of Spring.

For a fleeting moment, she remained motionless. Then, springing into action, she hurried off, light as a gazelle. She ran and leapt like a sunlight-reflected image in a mirror—now here, now there. Whoever tried to follow her would see how her style and figure changed with the light of each building and space she moved through.

She reached the Boulevards, amidst an explosion of gaslight coming from lamps, shops, and cafés. Young slender trees lined the streets, each concealing their own Dryad, providing shade from the artificial sunlight. The entire pavement transformed into one grand festive hall, filled with tables showcasing an array of refreshments from champagne and Chartreuse to coffee and beer. Here were flowers, statues, books, and colorful textiles on display.

From the throng near the tall buildings, she looked out at the rolling current beyond the lines of trees. A flood of carriages surged forward—cabriolets, coaches, omnibuses, and cabs—while galloping gentlemen and marching troops navigated through. Crossing to the other side seemed a perilous venture for life and limb. Lanterns cast their radiance around; gas light prevailed; suddenly, a firework shot up! From where? To where?

Here, soft Italian melodies flowed; over there, Spanish songs with castanets accompanied the sound of laughter; but strongest of all was the street organ's cheerful tune, the rousing "Can-Can" music that perplexed Orpheus, never known to the likes of the "Belle Helene." Even the barrow seemed tempted to twirl upon one of its wheels.

The Dryad danced, floated, and flew, shifting colors like a hummingbird in sunlight; each house, shaped by the world around, reflected back its image upon her.

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
Like a glowing lotus bloom torn from its stem, the Dryad glided along with the wind. Every time she paused, she transformed into another being, evading recognition and a chance for anyone to draw close to her.

Everything fell away like images in the clouds. In every face she gazed, none seemed familiar; she recognized not a single form from home. The spark of memory remained—two bright eyes flashed in her thoughts. She remembered Mary, the ragged yet cheerful child who adorned her dark hair with red flowers. Mary was now here, amidst the thrumming heartbeat of the grand city, radiant and magnificent, just like that day she passed by the old clergyman's house and the Dryad's ancient oak.

Here she was certainly living, engulfed in the din. Perhaps she had just stepped down from one of the luxurious waiting carriages. Elegant vehicles, with gilded coachmen and footmen in silken attire, approached. The people emerging from them were all splendidly dressed ladies, making their way through the open gates and up the wide staircase to a building resting on marble pillars. Could this be the wonder of the world? There Mary must surely be.

"Sancta Maria!" echoed from within. Incense filled the air through the grand painted and gilded aisles, where a solemn twilight lingered.

Dressed in black garments of the finest fabric, following the latest trends, the wealthy women of Paris swept across the shining floor. Their family crests were engraved on silver shields adorning velvet prayer books and embroidered onto scented handkerchiefs edged with Brussels lace. Some ladies kneeled solemnly before the altars, while others visited confessionals.

Anxiety and fear enveloped the Dryad; she felt as if she had entered a place that didn’t belong to her. It felt like the sanctuary of silence, the hall of secrets. Every word whispered held a veil of mystery.

The Dryad envisioned herself draped in lace and silk, akin to the wealthy women around her—did perhaps each of them carry a longing like hers?

A deep, sorrowful sigh echoed. Was it from someone in a distant confessional or from the Dryad’s own heart? She tightened the veil around her; the incense filled her senses, but here was not her place of yearning.

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
Away! Away—a racing without pause. The fleeting fly knows no rest, for her existence is endless movement.

She emerged again among the lit gas lamps, beside a grand fountain.

"All the streaming waters cannot wash away the innocent blood spilt here," those words resounded. Strangers gathered around, engaging in vibrant conversation—something no one would have dared do in that grand hall of silence from whence she came.

A heavy stone slab shifted and lifted. She didn’t grasp why. She spotted an opening leading into the depths below. The strangers descended, leaving behind the starlit air and cheerful vitality of the world above.

"I’m afraid," said one woman to her husband, "I can’t pursue it; I have no interest in wonders down there. You should stay with me."

"But travel home?" replied the man, "and leave Paris without witnessing its most magnificent marvel—the true wonder of the present, crafted from one man's strength and resolve?"

"I will not go down for anything," she insisted.

"It is called the wonder of our time," the Dryad heard and understood. Her intense longing had led her here, and now she stood at the entrance. Into the depths of Paris? She hadn’t anticipated this; yet she heard it spoken of, saw the strangers go down, and followed them.

The staircase spiraled down of cast iron, broad and easy. At the bottom, lamps flickered; further down, another lamp illuminated the way. They entered a maze of endless halls and arched passages that communicated with each other. All the streets and avenues of Paris mirrored here in dim reflections. The names adorned the walls, and every building above had its corresponding number down below, with roots trapped beneath the macadamized quays of a wide canal, in which muddy water flowed. Above it, fresh water bobbed over arches, and tangled gas pipes and telegraph wires dangled overhead.

In the distance shone the lamps, resembling faint reflections from the world above. Every so often, a deep rumble echoed—the sound of heavy wagons trundling over the entrance bridges.

You may have heard of the CATACOMBS? Now they serve as vanishing points in this new underground world—a marvel of today—the sewers of Paris. The Dryad found herself here, rather than within the grand Exhibition at the Champ de Mars.

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
She heard exclamations of awe and admiration.

"From here flows health and life for thousands upon thousands above! We live in an age of progress, filled with so many blessings."

That was the consensus among men; but not for the creatures born and bred here—the rats, to be precise, who squeaked to each other within the cracks of a decaying wall, quite plainly, and the Dryad understood them.

A robust old rat with a bitten-off tail expressed his frustrations in bold squeaks, and his family echoed their agreement to every word.

"I am tired of all this human pretension," he declared, "overwhelmed by this show of ignorance. Truly, what a fine façade! All built from gas and petroleum! I can’t digest such fare. Everything shines and looks pretty now, but it’s so bright that it makes one feel ashamed, without quite knowing why. Oh, how I wish we were back in the times of tallow candles! Those were romantic days, not so far behind us."

"What is that you’re talking about?" asked the Dryad. "I've never seen you before. What are you discussing?"

"About the glorious days gone by," the Rat replied, "the happy times of our ancestors. Back then, it was a real privilege to be down here. Our rat nest was different then. Mother Plague used to live here; she would take lives, but never harmed the rats. Robbers and smugglers once roamed freely here. The time of romance is lost, even in our rat nest, as fresh air and petroleum have invaded every corner."

Thus the Rat squeaked, reminiscing about the times when Mother Plague roamed free.

A carriage halted—an open omnibus, drawn by swift horses. The passengers boarded and proceeded along the underground Boulevard de Sebastopol, which lay beneath the famous bustling street of the same name.

As the carriage disappeared into the dim light, the Dryad emerged once more into the joyful freshness above. Here, not in the shadowy tunnels filled with heavy air, was the spectacle she sought within her fleeting existence. It had to shine brighter than all the gas lamps, more brilliantly than the moon gliding past.

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
Indeed, she spotted it shining in the distance, sparkling and twinkling like the evening star in the sky.

She saw a gate to a little garden open wide, inviting her into a realm of light and music. Colorful lamps surrounded small lakes, adorned with metallic water plants that shot jets of water high into the air. Beautiful weeping willows, genuine tokens of spring, let their fresh branches drape over the lakes like a translucent, protective veil. Amidst the bushes blazed an open fire, casting a warm twilight glow over charming huts made of branches, through which the melodies of music drifted—captivating, intoxicating music that made one’s heart race.

Lovely girls in festive attire, smiling brightly, and embodying youthful exuberance—"Marys," with roses woven in their hair, without carriages or postillions—whirled around, dancing joyfully.

Where were the heads? Where were the feet? They sprang with wild abandon, as though possessed, laughing and joining in joyous revelry, as if in their ecstasy they aimed to embrace the whole world.

The Dryad felt herself swept up by the dance. Around her delicate foot, a chestnut brown silk boot clasped, echoing the ribbon that cascaded down from her hair to her bare shoulders. The green silk dress flowed in large folds, but didn’t quite hide her lovely foot and ankle.

Had she arrived at the enchanted Garden of Armida? What was the name of this place?

The name glittered in gaslights over the entrance. It was "Mabille."

The soaring rockets, splashing fountains, and popping champagne corks accompanied the wild jubilant dance. Over all of this, the moon glided smoothly, bright but with a slightly crooked face.

A wild exhilaration surged within the Dryad, as if she were intoxicated by opium. Her eyes sparkled, her lips moved, but the sound of violins and flutes drowned her words. Her partner whispered things she couldn’t understand, nor did we. He reached out his arms to pull her in, yet he embraced only the empty air.

The Dryad was carried along like a rose petal on the wind. She perceived a flame in the air, a flashing light atop a tower. The beacon gleamed from the destination of her desires, shining from the red lighthouse of the Fata Morgana in the Champ de Mars. The wind carried her there; she circled the tower, and the workers thought of her as a butterfly that arrived too soon, now falling helplessly.

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
In that bright moonlight, gas lamps illuminated the surroundings, through the halls, over the grand structures scattered around, over the rose hills and rocks shaped by human skill, from which waterfalls, powered by "Master Bloodless," cascaded down. The depths of the seas, lake abysses, and the kingdom of fish revealed themselves here. People walked as if in a pond's depth, conversing with the sea creatures within the diving bell of glass. Water pressed against the solid glass walls from every side. The polyps, resembling long-armed creatures, clung to the bottom, extending their limbs in search of prey. A sizable turbot swam comfortably, casting occasionally suspicious glances. A crab clambered over it, resembling a gigantic spider, while shrimps flitted about like the restless butterflies and moths of the sea.

In the fresh water thrived nymphaea lilies and reeds; the goldfish stood in ranks below, all turning their heads in unison to receive the flowing water into their mouths. Fat carps gazed at the glass walls with dazed expressions, knowing they were on display and recalling the seasickness they endured during their railway journey.

They had come to see the Exhibition but now stared at the people who passed by, day in and out. Every nation on earth had displayed its inhabitants for the entertainment of soles and haddocks, pike and carp, who offered their judgments.

"Those are scaly beasts," a slimy Whiting declared. "They change their scales two or three times daily, producing sounds they call speech. We don’t need scales, and we express ourselves easily by twitching our mouths and staring. We have many advantages over humans."

"But they learned to swim from us," stated an educated Cod. "You should know, I hail from the vast sea. During hot days, they swim; first removing their scales, then they kick and row about. They strive to be like us, but they cannot match us. Poor creatures!"

The fish stared, convinced that the throngs of people they had witnessed during the bright day were still moving around them; they could yet see the same figures that had initially captured their attention.

A lovely Barbel, spotted and round, commented that the "human fry" were still present.

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
"I can see a well-formed human figure," said the Barbel. "She was called 'contumacious lady' or something similar. She had a mouth and wide eyes like ours, with a large balloon-like head and something akin to a folded umbrella in front, with pieces of seaweed dangling around her. She should remove the excess and appear as we do; then she might resemble a proper barbel, as much as a human can!"

"Whatever happened to the one they lured away with a hook? He was sitting in a wheelchair, scribbling with pen and ink, cataloging everything. They called him a 'writer.'"

"They’re still around with him," said a hoarse old Carp who had transported a hook she swallowed in her youth. "A writer? That’s what we fish would call a cuttle or inkfish among humans."

Thus, they gossiped in their fishy way; but within the artificial water grotto, workers were busy, making the most of the night shifts to finish by daybreak. Their rhythmic hammering and songs accompanied the departing Dryad.

"So, at least I’ve seen you, you lovely goldfish," she said, waving hello. "Yes, I know you; I've learned of you from home; the swallow informed me about you. How stunning! How radiant! I’d kiss every one of you, as well as the others. I know you all, but you don’t know me."

The fish blinked in astonishment at the twilight. They understood her not.

The Dryad disappeared altogether. She had long been in the open air, where different lands—the regions of black bread, the codfish coast, the kingdom of Russian leather, and the banks of eau-de-Cologne, and the gardens of rose oil—exhaled their fragrances from the world-wonder flower.

When we return home after a night out, half asleep yet still aware, melodies play vividly in our minds; we hear them, recalling every note. When the eyes of the slain close, the final image lingers, like a photograph.

This was true for the Dryad as well. The vibrant life of the day had not yet faded into the quiet of night. The Dryad witnessed this; she knew it would repeat tomorrow.

title: THE DRYAD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the contrast between freedom and confinement
text_chunk:
She stood among the fragrant roses, thinking she recognized them from home. She noticed red pomegranate blossoms, just like those Mary had worn in her dark hair.

Memories from her childhood home flickered through her mind; her eyes eagerly took in the surrounding sights as an anxious, relentless feeling drove her through the marvel-filled halls.

A deepening fatigue overtook her. She longed to rest on the soft Oriental carpets indoors or lean against the weeping willow by the clear water outside. But for the fleeting fly, there is no rest. In a blink, the day completed its cycle.

Her thoughts wavered, her limbs trembled; she collapsed onto the grass by the bubbling water.

"You will forever spring forth from the earth," she said with a touch of sadness. "Moisten my tongue—grant me a refreshing sip."

"I am not living water," came the reply. "I only surge upward when the machine commands it."

"Please, bestow me some of your freshness, green grass—just a single fragrant bloom."

"We must perish if severed from our roots," replied the Flowers and Grass.

"Grant me a kiss, you gentle breeze—just a single life kiss."

"The sun will soon kiss the clouds red," answered the Wind. "Then you will join the dead, blown away like all the splendor that will fade away before the year ends. I’ll once more play with the light sand here, stirring up dust across the meadow and sky. Everything is dust!"

The Dryad felt an overwhelming dread, much like a woman who has cut her pulse in the bath yet clings to life even as she bleeds out. She rose, staggered a few steps, then collapsed again at the entrance of a small church. The door stood ajar, lights flickered on the altar, and organ music filled the air.

What music! Such notes the Dryad had never experienced before; yet it felt like she recognized several familiar voices among them. They resonated deep from the heart of all creation. She perceived echoes of tales told by the old clergyman, great deeds, celebrated names, and the gifts creatures of God must pass down to live on.

The organ swelled, and from its melody, these words emerged:

"Your wishes and desires have yanked you from your destined place upon the earth. That will lead to your demise, poor Dryad!"

The notes softened and became gentle, fading into a mournful tone.

In the sky, the clouds glowed crimson. The Wind sighed softly:

"Fade away, you souls! The sun is about to rise!"

The first light touched the Dryad. Her form radiated shifting colors, like a soap bubble as it bursts and transforms into a drop of water; like a tear that falls and evaporates.

Poor Dryad! Just a dew drop, merely a tear spilled on the earth, fading away!

title: THE DUMB BOOK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
On a busy road cutting through the woods, a lone farmhouse stood, with the path running right through its yard. The sun was shining and all the windows were wide open; inside, people were bustling about. In the yard, under an arbor of lilac bushes in full bloom, an open coffin was set up. They had brought a dead man there, scheduled to be buried that very afternoon. No one shed a tear for him. His face was covered with a white cloth, and beneath his head lay a large, thick book made of folded blotting paper, with dried flowers sandwiched between the pages. This herbarium was his collection gathered from various places, meant to accompany him to his grave, as he had wished. Each flower was linked to a chapter in his life.

"It was the old student," someone said. "They say he was once an ambitious young man who studied ancient languages and even wrote songs. Then something happened to him, and he gave up on life, turning to drink. Eventually, he ruined his health and was brought to the countryside, where someone paid for his care. He was gentle like a child, except during his dark moods; then he could become incredibly fierce, as strong as a giant, darting through the woods like a hunted deer. But once we managed to bring him back home and convinced him to look at the book filled with dried plants, he could sit for hours examining a single one, often letting tears roll down his cheeks. Who knows what memories flooded his mind? He asked us to place that book in his coffin, and now he lies there. Soon, they’ll close the lid, and he will find peace in the grave!”

The cloth covering his face was gently lifted. The dead man’s expression was serene—a sunbeam touched his features. A swallow zipped into the arbor like an arrow, circling above his head and chirping softly.

It's such a strange feeling—everyone has experienced it—to sift through old letters from our youth. It’s like a different life rises before us, filled with hopes and heartaches. Many of the friends we once knew intimately now feel like they're gone from our lives, even though they still exist. We thought they would remain in our hearts forever, sharing every joy and sorrow.

The withered oak leaf in the book reminded him of a lifelong friend and schoolmate. He had attached the leaf to the student's cap in the green woods when they promised each other eternal friendship. Where does that friend live now? The leaf remains, but the friendship is lost. Here too is an exotic hothouse plant, too delicate for the northern gardens. Its leaves seem to still carry a sweet scent! She had given it to him from her own garden—she was a nobleman’s daughter.

Here’s a water lily he picked himself, watered with salt tears—a lily from sweet waters. And here’s a nettle: what stories could its leaves tell? What memories did he hold when he picked it? Here’s a little snowdrop from the lonely woods; here’s an evergreen from a flowerpot at the tavern; and here’s a simple blade of grass.

The lilac hung its fresh, fragrant flowers over the dead man's head; the swallow flew by again, chirping "twit, twit." Now, the men arrived with hammers and nails, ready to close the lid over the man, who rested his head on the cherished dumb book—closed forever!

title: THE ELF OF THE ROSE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the cycle of life and death
text_chunk:
In the middle of a garden, there stood a rose tree bursting with blooms, and nestled among its prettiest roses was a tiny elf. So small that no human could spot him, he had tiny sleeping quarters hidden behind each rose leaf. He was perfectly shaped and as beautiful as a little child, with wings that extended from his shoulders to his feet. The scent in his chambers was delightful, and the walls were clean and lovely, made up of the rose's blushing leaves.

All day long, he basked in the warm sunshine, flitting from flower to flower and dancing with the butterflies. But then he decided to check how many steps he would need to take along the veins of a linden leaf, which he imagined to be long roads. Little did he know, those roads would take longer than he thought, and before he had even made much progress, the sun began to set. It had gotten too late. The temperature dropped, dew settled on the grass, and a chill wind blew; realizing that it was best to head home, he hurried as fast as he could. But when he arrived, he found all the roses closed tight, and he couldn’t get in—not a single rose remained open. The poor elf became frightened; he had never been out after dark, always hiding behind warm rose leaves to sleep. This could easily spell his demise! He remembered an arbor on the other side of the garden, covered in beautiful honeysuckle blossoms that resembled large painted horns. Thinking it would be a good place to sleep until morning, he flew over.

Yet when he arrived, he found two people sitting in the arbor—a handsome young man and a lovely lady. They sat next to each other, wishing they’d never have to part. Their love was deeper than any child’s love for their parents.

"But we have to say goodbye," the young man said. "Your brother doesn’t approve of our engagement, and he’s sending me away on business across mountains and seas. Farewell, my sweet bride; you are everything to me."

title: THE ELF OF THE ROSE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the cycle of life and death
text_chunk:
They kissed, and the girl wept, giving him a rose before pressing a kiss on it so passionately that the flower opened. The little elf zipped in and rested his head against the delicate, fragrant walls. He could easily hear them whispering, "Farewell, farewell," and knew the rose was placed against the young man’s chest. His heart raced! The little elf couldn’t fall asleep; it beat so loudly. As the young man walked through the dark woods, he took the rose out and kissed it repeatedly with such fervor that the little elf was almost crushed inside it. He could feel the heat of the young man's lips through the petals, and the rose bloomed wide open as if warmed by the midday sun.

Then, a sinister-looking man appeared. He was the girl’s wicked brother. Brandishing a sharp knife, he stabbed the young man while he was still kissing the rose, then cut off his head, burying it with the body right under the linden tree.

"Now he’s gone and will soon be forgotten," the wicked brother thought. "He’ll be lost forever on his journey over mountains and seas. My sister will think he is dead; he can’t come back, and she won’t dare question me about him."

He scattered the dry leaves over the loose soil with his foot and made his way home through the darkness. But little did he know, the elf followed him. He hid inside a rolled-up linden leaf that had fallen onto the wicked man’s head while he was digging the grave. With the hat on, it was very dark, and the little elf was filled with fear and dismay at the wicked act.

Morning came before the wicked brother reached home; he took off his hat and entered his sister’s room. There lay the beautiful girl, dreaming of her beloved, whom she believed was traveling far away over mountains and seas. Her wicked brother hovered over her, laughing a horrible laugh—one only devils could manage. A dry leaf slipped from his hair onto the blanket, but he didn’t notice it as he went off to catch a few hours of sleep. Meanwhile, the elf slipped out of the withered leaf, approached the ear of the sleeping girl, and told her about the dreadful murder in a dreamlike whisper. He described where her brother had killed her lover and where he had buried him, pointing out the linden tree in full bloom nearby.

title: THE ELF OF THE ROSE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the cycle of life and death
text_chunk:
"This isn’t just a dream I’m sharing with you," he reassured her. "You’ll find a withered leaf on your bed to prove it."

When she awoke and discovered the leaf, she shed bitter tears that brought her no comfort.

All day, the window stood open, and the little elf could have easily returned to the roses or any of the flowers, but he couldn’t bear to leave such a sorrowful soul. In the window bloomed a bush of monthly roses. He nestled into one of the flowers and watched the poor girl. Her brother would come into her room, always cheerful despite his wicked deeds, leaving her unable to share her grief.

As night fell, she slipped out of the house and into the woods to the place where the linden tree stood. After brushing away the leaves from the earth, she uncovered her murdered beloved. Oh, how she wept and prayed to join him in death! She longed to take his body home but that was impossible, so instead, she took the poor head with its closed eyes, kissed his cold lips, and shook the dirt from his beautiful hair.

"I will keep this," she declared. After covering the body again with earth and leaves, she took the head and a little sprig of jasmine that bloomed nearby, carefully carrying them home. Once in her room, she found the largest flowerpot she could locate, placed her loved one’s head inside, covered it with earth, and planted the jasmine twig.

"Farewell, farewell," whispered the little elf. He couldn’t stand to witness her deep sorrow any longer, so he flew back to his rose in the garden. But the rose had wilted; only a few dry leaves remained clinging to the green hedge behind it.

"How quickly everything good and beautiful fades away!" sighed the elf.

title: THE ELF OF THE ROSE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the cycle of life and death
text_chunk:
After a while, he found another rose to call home, nestled safely among its delicate, fragrant leaves. Each morning, he flew to the window of the poor girl, always finding her weeping by the flowerpot. Her bitter tears fell on the jasmine twig, and as she became paler each day, the twig grew greener and more vibrant. Shoots sprouted, and little white buds formed, which the girl lovingly kissed. But her cruel brother scolded her, asking if she had lost her mind. He couldn’t understand why she mourned over a flowerpot, and it only irritated him. He was oblivious to whose closed eyes were inside, or whose red lips were fading beneath the earth. One day, she rested her head against the flowerpot, and the little elf found her asleep. He whispered sweetly in her ear about that evening in the arbor, the lovely scent of the rose, and the love of the elves. She dreamt beautifully, and as she drifted peacefully away, her spirit joined her beloved in heaven. The jasmine opened its large white flowers, releasing its sweet fragrance—a silent tribute to the dead. However, the wicked brother saw the beautiful blooming plant as his property, an inheritance from his sister. He placed it in his bedroom, close to his bed, enchanted by its beauty and delightful scent. The little elf followed it, flitting from flower to flower, sharing the tale of the murdered young man, the wicked brother, and the poor sister with every flower spirit. "We know," the flower spirits replied, "for we sprouted from the eyes and lips of the murdered one. We understand, we understand," they nodded in unison. The elf was puzzled by their calm and flew to the bees, gathering honey, to tell them about the wicked brother. In turn, the bees shared this with their queen, who commanded them to go and kill the murderer at dawn.

But that night, right after the sister’s death, while her brother slept next to the fragrant jasmine, every flower cup opened, and the flower spirits slipped out, armed with poisonous spears. They hovered near the sleeping man’s ear, filling his dreams with nightmares before darting across his lips, pricking his tongue with their venom. "Now we’ve avenged the dead," they said, returning to the jasmine flowers.

When morning arrived, as soon as the window opened, the rose elf, accompanied by the queen bee and the entire swarm, rushed in to punish him. But he was already lifeless. People gathered around the bed, declaring that the scent of the jasmine was the cause of his death. The rose elf finally understood the revenge of the flowers and explained it to the queen bee, who buzzed around the flowerpot with her swarm. The bees were unwavering, despite attempts to drive them away. One man picked it up to move it, but as he did, one of the bees stung his hand, causing him to drop the pot, shattering it to pieces. Suddenly, everyone saw the white skull inside, and they realized the man in bed was a murderer. The queen bee hummed in the air, celebrating the flowers' revenge and the brave little elf of the rose, proclaiming that even the smallest leaf can harbor someone who uncovers deeds of evil and brings justice.

title: THE ELFIN HILL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
A few large lizards were scurrying around in the crevices of an old tree. They understood each other really well because they spoke the lizard language.

"There’s so much buzzing and rumbling coming from the elfin hill," said one lizard. "I haven’t been able to sleep for two nights because of the noise. It’s like having a toothache; it keeps me wide awake."

"Something's happening over there," said the other lizard. "They propped up the top of the hill with four red posts until dawn this morning, so it’s all aired out now, and the elfin girls have learned new dances. Something is definitely going on."

"I talked to an earthworm I know," said a third lizard. "He just came from the elfin hill, where he’s been digging around day and night. He’s heard a lot. Even though he can’t see, being a poor little creature, he knows how to wiggle about and hide. They’re expecting guests at the elfin hill—grand company too, but he wouldn’t say who they are, or maybe he just didn’t know. All the will-o'-the-wisps are supposed to be there for a torch dance. The silver and gold from the hill will be polished and set out in the moonlight."

"Who could the strangers be?" the lizards wondered. "What’s going on? Listen to that buzzing and humming!"

Just then, the elfin hill opened up, and an old elfin maiden with a hollow back came dancing out. She was the housekeeper for the old elf king and a distant relative of the family, so she wore an amber heart on her forehead. Her feet moved very quickly, "trip, trip;" oh my, how fast she could dance all the way down to the sea to the night-raven.

"You’re invited to the elf hill for tonight," she said. "But can you do me a big favor and deliver the invitations? You need to do something since you have no housework to attend to like I do. We’re having some very important guests—conjurers who always have something to say. That’s why the old elf king wants to make it a big event."

title: THE ELFIN HILL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Everyone can come to the grand ball, even humans, if they can talk in their sleep or do something our way. But for the feast, we need to be selective; we can only admit high-ranking guests. I had an argument with the elf king about excluding ghosts. The merman and his daughter must be invited first, even if they’re not keen on staying long on dry land. We’ll provide them with a wet stone to sit on or maybe something better; I think they’ll accept this time. We also need to invite all the top-tier demons with tails, along with hobgoblins and imps; we shouldn’t forget the death-horse, the grave-pig, or even the church dwarf, even if they’re part of the clergy and don’t count among our crowd. But that’s just their job; they’re closely related to us and visit frequently."

"Croak," said the night-raven as he flew off with the invitations.

The elfin maidens were already dancing on the elf hill, twirling in shawls made from moonlight and mist, which looked lovely for those who appreciated that sort of thing. Inside, the large hall of the elf hill was lavishly decorated; the floor had been washed with moonlight, and the walls were treated with a magic ointment, making them glow like tulip petals in the light. In the kitchen, frogs were roasting on the spit, dishes of snail skins mixed with children's fingers were being prepared, there was mushroom seed salad, hemlock, noses, and mouse marrow, marsh beer, and sparkling saltpetre wine from the grave cellars. Those were substantial meals. The old elf king had his gold crown polished with powdered slate; it was rare and hard for an elf king to obtain. In the bedrooms, curtains were hung and secured with snail slime; there was truly a buzzing and humming everywhere.

"Now we need to fumigate the place with burnt horsehair and pig bristles, and then I think I’ll have done my part," said the elf servant.

"Father, dear," said the youngest daughter, "can I finally hear who our high-born visitors are?"

title: THE ELFIN HILL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Well, I suppose I should tell you now," he replied. "Two of my daughters need to prepare themselves to be married, since those marriages will indeed happen. The old goblin from Norway, who lives in the ancient Dovre mountains and owns many castles made of rock and freestone, plus a gold mine, is coming with his two sons, both of whom are looking for wives. The old goblin is a true-hearted, honest old Norwegian; he’s cheerful and straightforward. I knew him before when we used to share good times together. He once came here to fetch his wife, who is now deceased; she was the daughter of the king of the chalk hills at Moen. They say he took her from chalk; I’d love to see him again. It’s said that the boys are ill-mannered and forward, but maybe that’s not entirely true, and they’ll improve as they grow older. Make sure you can teach them some good manners."

"When are they coming?" asked the daughter.

"That depends on the wind and weather," said the elf king. "They travel frugally. They’ll arrive when they have the chance to catch a ship. I wanted them to come by way of Sweden, but the old man didn’t want to follow my advice. He’s not keeping up with the times, and that annoys me."

Two will-o'-the-wisps came hopping in, with one quicker than the other, so naturally, one arrived first. "They’re coming! They’re coming!" he shouted.

"Give me my crown," said the elf king, "and let me stand in the moonlight."

The daughters put on their shawls and bowed down to the ground. There stood the old goblin from the Dovre mountains, wearing a crown of hardened ice and polished fir cones. He also had on a bear skin and big, warm boots, while his sons wore no braces with their bare necks, because they were strong men.

"Is that a hill?" asked the youngest of the boys, pointing to the elf hill. "In Norway, we’d call it a hole."

title: THE ELFIN HILL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Boys," the old man replied, "a hole goes in, and a hill stands out; can’t you see?"

Another thing that amazed them was how easily they understood the language.

"Watch out," warned the old man, "or people will think you were badly raised."

Then they entered the elfin hill, where the fancy and grand company had assembled, and they arrived so quickly it was as if they had been blown together. For each guest, the neatest and most pleasant arrangements had been made. The sea folks sat at tables in large water tubs, saying it felt just like home. Everyone behaved well, except for the two young northern goblins; they put their legs on the table, thinking it was fine.

"Feet off the tablecloth!" the old goblin exclaimed. They complied, but not without delay. Then they tickled the ladies who were serving at the table with the fir cones they had in their pockets. They took off their boots to be more comfortable and handed them to the ladies to hold. But their father, the old goblin, was quite different; he spoke fondly about the majestic Norwegian rocks and shared beautiful tales of the waterfalls that crashed over them with a loud roar, like thunder or the sound of an organ, sending white foam everywhere. He told stories of salmon leaping in rushing waters while the water god played his golden harp. He painted such vivid pictures that everyone listening could visualize it all; they could see the sawmills, the male and female servants singing songs, and doing a lively dance—when suddenly, the old goblin gave the old elfin maiden a kiss, a huge kiss, despite being almost strangers.

Then the elfin girls had to dance, first in the usual way, and then with stomping feet, which they executed flawlessly; followed by artistic and solo performances. Oh my, how they flung their legs around! No one could tell where the dance started or ended, nor which were legs or arms, as they all twirled together like shavings in a sawmill! They spun so fast that the death-horse and grave-pig got dizzy and had to leave the table.

title: THE ELFIN HILL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Wait!" shouted the old goblin. "Is that all the housekeeping they can do? Can they do more than just dance around and create a whirlwind?"

"You’ll see soon what they can do," said the elf king. Then he called his youngest daughter to him. She was as slender and fair as moonlight, and the most graceful of all the sisters. She took a white chip in her mouth and vanished instantly; that was her special talent. But the old goblin said he wouldn’t want his wife to have such a talent, and figured his boys would feel the same way. Another daughter could make a figure that looked just like her follow her, as if she had a shadow, which none of the goblin folks had. The third sister was quite different; she had learned from the brew witch of the moor how to flavor elfin puddings with glow-worms.

"She would make an excellent housewife," said the old goblin, then he greeted her with his eyes instead of raising a toast, as he didn’t drink much.

Next came the fourth daughter with a large harp to play on; when she struck the first chord, everyone had to lift their left leg (because goblins are left-legged), and at the second chord, they had no choice but to do exactly what she wanted.

"That’s a dangerous woman," said the old goblin, and the two sons exited the hill; they had seen enough. "And what can the next daughter do?" the old goblin asked.

"I’ve learned everything about Norway," she replied, "and I won’t marry unless I can go to Norway."

Then her youngest sister whispered to the old goblin, "That’s just because she's heard in a Norwegian song that when the world ends, the cliffs of Norway will still stand firm like monuments, and she wants to go there to be safe; she’s really afraid of sinking into the sea."

"Ho! ho!" chuckled the old goblin. "Is that what she means? Now, what about the seventh and last?"

title: THE ELFIN HILL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"The sixth comes before the seventh," the elf king said, as he could count; but the sixth didn’t want to step forward.

"I can only tell people the truth," she said. "No one cares for me or pays me any attention, and I have enough to do sewing my grave clothes."

Then the seventh and final daughter came forward; what could she do? Well, she could tell stories—any kind of stories, as many as you like.

"Here are my five fingers," said the old goblin. "Now give me a story for each one of them."

So she took him by the wrist and made him laugh until he nearly choked; when she reached the fourth finger, there was a gold ring on it, almost as if it knew a betrothal was coming. Then the old goblin said, "Hold tight to what you have: this hand is yours; I want you to be my wife."

The elfin girl replied that the stories about the ring-finger and Little Peter Playman hadn’t been told yet.

"We’ll hear those in winter," said the old goblin, "and also about the fir and birch trees, and ghost stories, and tales of tingling frost. You’ll tell your stories, as no one over there can tell them as well as you can; we’ll sit in the stone rooms where the pine logs are burning, and drink mead from the golden drinking horn of the old Norwegian kings. The water-god has given me two; when we’re there, Nix will come to visit and sing you all the songs of the mountain shepherdesses. We’ll have such a good time! The salmon will leap in the waterfalls, crashing against the stone walls, but he won’t be able to join us. It’s truly pleasant living in old Norway. But where are the boys?"

Where indeed were they? Why, they were running around the fields, extinguishing the will-o'-the-wisps who kindly brought their torches.

"What kind of tricks have you been up to?" the old goblin asked. "I’ve found you a mother; now you can choose one of your aunts."

But the boys claimed they wanted to make a toast and drink to good friendship; they had no desire to marry. So they made speeches and raised their glasses to show they were empty. Then they took off their coats and lay down at the table to sleep, making themselves quite at home. Meanwhile, the old goblin danced around the room with his young bride, exchanging boots with her, which is actually more fashionable than exchanging rings.

"The rooster is crowing," said the old elfin maiden who acted as the housekeeper. "Now we need to close the shutters so the sun doesn’t scorch us."

Then the hill closed up. But the lizards continued to scurry up and down the gnarled tree, with one saying to the other, "Oh, how much I enjoyed the old goblin!"

"The boys impressed me more," said the earthworm. But then again, the poor wretched creature couldn’t see.

title: THE EMPEROR'S NEW SUIT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
A long time ago, there was an emperor who loved new clothes more than anything else. He spent all his money on them, and his biggest goal was to always look stylish. He didn’t care much for his army or the theater; all he wanted to do was ride around and show off his latest outfit. He had a coat for every hour of the day, and instead of being in his office, you could often say, "The emperor is in his dressing room."

The vibrant city he lived in always welcomed visitors from all over the world. One day, two con artists arrived. They convinced everyone they were expert weavers, claiming they could create the most amazing fabric imaginable. They said their colors and patterns were not only incredibly beautiful, but the clothes made from their material had a magical quality: only those who were fit for their jobs or exceptionally intelligent could see them.

"That sounds like incredible fabric!" the emperor thought. "If I wear a suit made from this cloth, I’ll be able to identify who in my empire is unfit for their roles, and I can tell who is clever versus who is foolish. I need to have this cloth made for me immediately." He handed over a large amount of money to the swindlers upfront to get started right away. They set up two looms and pretended to work very hard, but they didn’t produce a single thread. They requested the finest silk and the most precious gold fabric, which they pocketed, and worked at the empty looms late into the night.

"I really want to know how the cloth is coming along," thought the emperor. But then he felt uneasy because he remembered that anyone not fit for their position wouldn’t be able to see it. He believed he’d have nothing to worry about, but decided it would be wise to send someone else to check on their progress. Everyone in town knew about the special qualities of the fabric and was eager to see who might be foolish or incompetent.

"I’ll send my honest old minister to check on the weavers," thought the emperor. "He’s the best judge of how the fabric looks because he’s intelligent, and nobody understands his role better than he does."

title: THE EMPEROR'S NEW SUIT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The good old minister walked into the room where the swindlers were sitting in front of the empty looms. "Oh no!" he thought, opening his eyes wide, "I can’t see anything at all!" But he didn’t say that out loud. The swindlers urged him to come closer and asked if he admired the exquisite patterns and beautiful colors they were crafting, pointing to the empty looms. The poor minister tried his hardest but saw nothing since there was nothing there. "Oh dear," he thought, "Am I really this foolish? I would have never guessed that! I can’t let anyone know! Am I actually unfit for my job? No, I can’t admit that I can’t see the cloth."

"Well, do you have anything to say?" asked one of the swindlers, pretending to be busy weaving.

"Oh yes, it’s very lovely, exceptionally beautiful," replied the old minister, peering through his glasses. "What a stunning pattern! What vibrant colors! I’ll definitely tell the emperor how much I like the fabric."

"We’re glad to hear that," the two weavers said, describing the colors and explaining the intricate pattern. The old minister listened closely so he could report to the emperor what they said, and that’s exactly what he did.

The swindlers then asked for more money, silk, and gold fabric to continue their work. They kept everything for themselves, and not a single thread ever touched the loom, yet they carried on pretending to work at the empty looms.

Soon after, the emperor sent another honest courtier to see how the cloth was progressing and if it was nearly done. Like the old minister, he looked and looked but couldn’t see anything either since there was nothing there.

"Is this not a beautiful piece of cloth?" the swindlers asked, showcasing and explaining the magnificent pattern, which, of course, didn’t exist.

"I’m not foolish," said the man to himself. "So it must be that my appointment is for a position I’m not worthy of. This is strange, but I can’t let anyone know!" So he praised the cloth he couldn’t see, expressing his delight at the beautiful colors and fine pattern. "It’s truly exceptional," he told the emperor.

title: THE EMPEROR'S NEW SUIT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Everyone in town buzzed about the fantastic cloth. Finally, the emperor himself wanted to see it while it was still on the loom. Accompanied by several courtiers, including the two who had already seen it, he went to the swindlers, who were now working harder than ever, but still without any threads.

"Isn’t it magnificent?" asked the two old advisers who had been there before. "Your Majesty must admire the colors and the pattern!" They pointed to the empty looms, assuming everyone else could see the fabric.

"What’s going on?" thought the emperor. "I don’t see anything at all. This is awful! Am I foolish? Am I unfit to be emperor? That would indeed be the worst thing that could happen to me."

"Well then," he told the weavers, "your fabric gets our highest approval;" and nodding happily, he looked at the empty loom because he didn’t want to admit he could see nothing. All his attendants, who were with him, stared and stared, and even though they couldn’t see anything more than anyone else, they said, just like the emperor, "Yes, it’s very beautiful." They all encouraged him to wear the new exquisite clothing at a grand procession that was coming up. "It’s magnificent, beautiful, and excellent," people chimed in; everyone seemed thrilled, and the emperor declared the swindlers "Imperial Court Weavers."

The night before the big day, the swindlers pretended to be hard at work and burned more than sixteen candles. They wanted everyone to see how busy they were finishing the emperor’s new suit. They pretended to lift the cloth from the loom, cut around with imaginary scissors, and sewed with needles that held no thread, finally announcing, "The emperor’s new suit is ready now."

The emperor and all his noblemen entered the hall; the swindlers held their arms up as if they were holding pieces of fabric and said: "These are the trousers!" "This is the coat!" and "Here’s the cloak!" and so on. "They’re as light as a cobweb, and wearing them feels like having nothing on at all; but that’s the beauty of it!"

title: THE EMPEROR'S NEW SUIT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Absolutely!" exclaimed all the courtiers, even though they weren’t able to see anything, because there was simply nothing there.

"Your Majesty, would you like to graciously undress?" asked the weavers, "so we can help you put on the new suit in front of the large mirror?"

The emperor took off his clothes, and the swindlers pretended to put the new suit on him, one piece after another. The emperor admired himself in the mirror from every angle.

"How well they fit! They look amazing!" everyone said. "What a beautiful pattern! What lovely colors! This is a magnificent outfit!"

The master of ceremonies announced that the bearers of the canopy, which would be carried in the procession, were ready.

"I’m all set," said the emperor. "Don’t you think my suit looks fantastic?" He turned back to the mirror, wanting everyone to believe he was admiring his clothes.

The chamberlains who were supposed to carry the train stretched their hands as if raising a train, pretending to hold something in their hands; they didn’t want anyone to realize they couldn’t see anything.

The emperor walked in the procession under the beautiful canopy, and everyone who saw him from the streets and windows exclaimed, "Wow, the emperor’s new suit is extraordinary! What a long train he has! It fits him perfectly!" No one wanted to admit they saw nothing, fearing they might seem unfit for their role or look foolish. No emperor’s clothes had ever received so much admiration.

"But he’s wearing nothing at all," finally said a little child. "Goodness! Listen to the innocent child," the father exclaimed, and he whispered to his neighbor what the child had said. "But he has nothing on!" the crowd finally shouted. This left a profound impact on the emperor, as he began to realize they were right; however, he told himself, "I must carry on to the end." And the chamberlains walked with even greater dignity as if they were carrying a train that didn’t exist.

title: THE FARM-YARD COCK AND THE WEATHER-COCK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
There were two roosters—one strutting on the dung-hill and the other perched on the roof. Both were full of themselves, but which of the two really contributed more? Share your thoughts—we’ll stick to our own conclusions regardless.

The poultry yard was separated from another yard by some wooden planks, and in that yard, resting on a dung-hill, was a big cucumber, aware that it was a hot-bed plant.

"Some are just born to it," the cucumber thought to itself. "Not everyone can be a cucumber; there are other creatures, too. The hens, the ducks, and all the animals next door are living things as well. I really admire the yard rooster on the plank; he’s definitely more important than that weather-cock up high who can’t even make a sound, let alone crow. The weather-cock doesn't have hens or chicks, only cares about himself, and sweats some sort of greenish stuff. No, the yard rooster is truly a rooster! He walks with style! His crowing is music, and wherever he goes, you know exactly what a trumpeter sounds like! If he would just come over here! Even if he gobbled me up whole, stalk and all, dissolving me in his belly, it would be a glorious end," the cucumber said.

That night, a terrible storm hit. The hens, chicks, and even the rooster sought shelter; the wind crashed down the planks between the two yards, and the tiles flew off the roof, but the weather-cock stood steady. He didn’t even flinch, because he couldn’t; despite being young and newly fashioned, he was wise and composed. He had the maturity of age and wasn’t at all like the birds fluttering about—sparrows and swallows—who he looked down on, considering them insignificant little chirpers. He thought that pigeons, large and glossy like mother-of-pearl, resembled weather-cocks in some way, but they were heavy and dull, focused only on stuffing themselves with food, and boring to talk to. The migratory birds had also visited him, sharing tales of exotic lands, lofty caravan adventures, and spooky stories that sent chills down his spine. It was all new and fascinating at first, but soon he realized they kept repeating themselves, and it grew tiresome; no one he met sparked any interest, as they were all stale and narrow-minded.

title: THE FARM-YARD COCK AND THE WEATHER-COCK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"The world is a terrible place!" he declared. "Everything in it is just plain stupid."

The weather-cock swelled with pride, which might have intrigued the cucumber if it had realized it, but all the cucumber could see was the yard rooster, who was now nearby.

The storm had passed, and the wind had scattered the planks.

"What do you think of that crowing?" the yard rooster asked the hens and chicks. "It was a bit rough—it needed some polish."

The hens and chicks gathered around the dung-hill, and the rooster strutted about like a royal.

"Garden plant!" he exclaimed to the cucumber, showing off his knowledge, while he forgot that he was pecking at it and devouring it. "A blissful end!"

The hens and chicks came over, because where one runs, the rest follow—they clucked and chirped, proud to have such a rooster among them.

"Cock-a-doodle-doo!" he crowed. "The chicks will grow up to be mighty hens at once if I announce it in the poultry-yard of the world!"

And the hens and chicks clucked and chirped, excited by the rooster’s announcement.

"A rooster can lay an egg! And do you know what’s inside that egg? A basilisk. No one can handle the sight of such a creature; everyone knows that, and now you do too—now you know what’s within me and how I stand as the champion among all roosters!"

With that, the yard rooster flapped his wings, puffed up his comb, and crowed again; all the hens and little chicks shuddered but felt proud to have such a champion among them. They clucked and chirped until the weather-cock heard them; he did hear, but he didn’t move.

"Everything is so foolish," the weather-cock muttered to himself. "The yard rooster can’t lay eggs, and I’m too lazy to do so myself; if I wanted, I could lay a wind-egg. But honestly, this world isn’t even worth a wind-egg. Everything is so silly! I don’t want to be stuck here any longer."

With that, the weather-cock gave up, but he didn’t harm the yard rooster, although the hens claimed that was his goal. And what's the moral? "It’s better to crow than to be all puffed up and walk away!"

title: THE FIR TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the importance of appreciating what one has
text_chunk:
Deep in the forest, where the sun shone warm and the air was fresh, stood a lovely little fir tree. Despite its beauty, the tree felt unhappy; it yearned to be as tall as its neighbors—the towering pines and firs encircling it. The sun beamed down, and the gentle air rustled its needles, while peasant children passed by, chatting joyfully. But the little fir tree ignored their laughter. Sometimes, the children would sit nearby with big baskets filled with strawberries or raspberries and would say, 'Isn’t this a pretty little tree?' This only made it feel more miserable. Yet, the tree continued to grow, gaining a new ring around its trunk every year—a sign of its age. But it sighed, 'Oh, how I wish I could be as tall as the others! Then I could stretch my branches wide and see the vast world below. Birds would nest in my boughs, and when the wind blew, I’d sway gracefully like my tall friends.' So discontented was the fir that it couldn’t enjoy the sunshine, the birds, or the rosy clouds drifting above. In winter, when the land was blanketed in glistening snow, a hare would hop by and jump right over the little tree, leaving it feeling even more embarrassed. After two winters, the fir had grown tall enough to prevent the hare from jumping over it. Still, it felt unfulfilled and cried out, 'If only I could grow taller and older! Nothing else in the world matters to me!' In the autumn, just like every year, the woodcutters came to chop down the tallest trees, and the now fully grown fir trembled as magnificent trees crashed to the ground. Once their branches were cut away, the trunks looked so thin and bare that they were hard to recognize. Then they were loaded onto wagons pulled by horses and taken away. 'Where are they going? What will happen to them?' The young fir tree was desperate to know and asked the swallows and storks that flew by in the spring, 'Do you know where those trees were taken? Did you see them?'

The swallows didn’t know, but the stork, after thinking for a moment, nodded and replied, 'Yes, I believe I do. When I flew back from Egypt, I saw several ships with fine masts that smelled like fir trees. Those must have been the ones; they looked very impressive.'

title: THE FIR TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the importance of appreciating what one has
text_chunk:
'Oh, how I wish I were tall enough to sail on the sea!' exclaimed the fir tree. 'What is the sea like? What does it look like?'

'It would take too long to explain,' said the stork as he flew away.

'Rejoice in your youth,' the sunbeam said. 'Embrace your fresh growth and the vibrant life within you.'

The wind kissed the tree, and the dew sprinkled it with tears, but the fir tree paid no attention.

As Christmas approached, many young trees were cut down, some even smaller and younger than the fir tree, which had neither rest nor peace because it longed to leave its forest home. These destined trees, prized for their beauty, kept their branches and were loaded onto wagons to be taken from the forest.

'Where are they going?' the fir tree wondered. 'They aren’t taller than I am; in fact, some are quite smaller. Why aren’t their branches cut off? Where are they headed?'

'We know, we know!' sang the sparrows. 'We peeked into the windows of the houses in town and learned what happens to them. They get dressed up in the most beautiful ways! We’ve seen them standing in warm rooms, covered with all sorts of lovely decorations—honey cakes, golden apples, toys, and countless wax candles.'

'And then?' asked the fir tree, trembling with excitement, 'What happens then?'

'We didn’t see any more,' replied the sparrows, 'but that was enough for us.'

'I wonder if something so dazzling will ever happen to me,' thought the fir tree. 'That would be so much better than sailing the sea. I yearn for it with all my heart. Oh! When will Christmas arrive? I’m now as tall and well-formed as the ones taken last year. Oh, I wish I were on that wagon, standing in a warm room surrounded by all that splendor! There must be something more magnificent awaiting me, or else why would the trees be so decorated? Yes, it must be even greater! What could it be? I’m tired from longing; I hardly know how I feel.'

title: THE FIR TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the importance of appreciating what one has
text_chunk:
'Rejoice with us!' said the air and sunlight. 'Savor your vibrant life in the fresh air.'

But the tree wouldn’t celebrate, even as it grew taller every day; its dark green leaves were visible in the woods while passersby admired it, saying, 'What a beautiful tree!'

Just before Christmas, the dissatisfied fir tree was the first to be cut down. As the axe sliced through its trunk, causing it to fall with a groan, it felt pain and weakness, forgetting all its dreams of happiness and grieving leaving its home in the woods. It realized it would never see its beloved companions, the other trees, nor the little bushes and colorful flowers that had grown beside it, perhaps not even the birds. The journey was anything but pleasant. The tree regained its senses only when it was unwrapped in the courtyard of a house with several other trees, and it heard a man say, 'We only need one tree, and this one is the prettiest.'

Then, two servants in grand uniforms came and carried the fir tree into a large, beautiful room. The walls were adorned with pictures, and near the grand stove stood elegant china vases topped with lion figures. There were rocking chairs, silk sofas, and big tables piled high with valuable books, toys, and pictures—at least that’s what the children said. The fir tree was placed in a large tub filled with sand, but it was hidden behind green cloth so no one would see it was just a tub, and it stood on a lovely carpet. The fir trembled, wondering what was going to happen next. Young ladies entered, and the servants helped them decorate the tree. They hung little bags made of colorful paper filled with sweets from its branches; gilded apples and walnuts dangled as if they had grown there, and all around were hundreds of red, blue, and white candles secured to its boughs. Realistic dolls were placed under the green foliage—the tree had never seen anything like it before—and at the very top, a sparkling star made of tinsel was secured. Oh, it was simply breathtaking!

title: THE FIR TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the importance of appreciating what one has
text_chunk:
'Tonight,' everyone exclaimed, 'it's going to look so bright!' 'Oh, I wish evening would come,' thought the fir tree, 'and the candles would be lit! Then I’ll finally know what happens next. Will the forest trees come to see me? I wonder if the sparrows will peek in at the windows as they fly by? Will I grow faster here and keep these decorations for both summer and winter?' Guessing didn't help; it made its bark ache, a discomfort for a slender fir tree similar to a headache for us. Finally, the candles were lit, and the tree became a dazzling beacon of light. It quivered with joy in all its branches, so much so that one of the candles fell among the green leaves and singed them. 'Help! Help!' cried the young ladies, but there was no real danger as they quickly put out the fire. After that, the tree did its best not to tremble, even though the flames scared it; it was determined not to harm any of the beautiful decorations, despite their brilliance dazzling its eyes. At that moment, the doors swung open, and a rush of children poured in as if they planned to knock the tree over, followed quietly by their parents. For a brief moment, the little ones were speechless, then they cheered with glee, dancing around the tree as they reached for one gift after another.

'What are they doing? What will happen next?' thought the fir. Eventually, the candles burned down to the branches and were extinguished. Then, the children were given permission to plunder the tree.

They charged at it, making the branches crack, and if it hadn’t been secured to the ceiling by the glimmering star, it surely would have toppled. The children danced around with their lovely new toys, while the only one who noticed the tree was the children’s maid, who peeked into the branches to see if any apples or figs had been overlooked.

'A story, a story!' shouted the children, pulling a plump man toward the tree.

'Now we’ll be in the shade,' said the man as he sat beneath it, 'and the tree will be able to listen, too, but I’ll only tell one story. Which should it be? Humpty Dumpty or Ivede-Avede, the one who tumbled down the stairs but soon got back up and ultimately married a princess?'

title: THE FIR TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the importance of appreciating what one has
text_chunk: 'Ivede-Avede,' shouted some. 'Humpty Dumpty,' cried others, their voices a delightful cacophony. Meanwhile, the fir tree remained quiet, thinking, 'Will I have anything to do with this? I’ve already amused them enough.' The old man then told them the story of Humpty Dumpty—how he fell down the stairs but got back up and married a princess.
