The children clapped their hands and urged, 'Tell another, tell another!' eager to hear about 'Ivede-Avede.' Instead, they only got 'Humpty Dumpty.' After that, the fir tree fell silent and contemplative; the birds in the forest had never told tales like 'Humpty Dumpty,' who fell down yet still achieved greatness.

title: THE FIR TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the importance of appreciating what one has
text_chunk:
'Ah! Yes, it happens this way in life,' thought the fir tree, convinced because it was narrated by such a kind man. 'Ah well,' it thought, 'who knows? Maybe I will fall too and marry a princess;' and it joyfully anticipated the next evening, expecting to be again adorned with lights, toys, gold, and fruit. 'Tomorrow, I won’t tremble,' it decided. 'I’ll relish all my splendor and hear the story of Humpty Dumpty again, or perhaps Ivede-Avede.' And all night long, the tree remained quiet and thoughtful. The morning brought the servants and the maid into the room. 'Now,' thought the fir tree, 'all my grandeur is about to begin!' But they dragged it out of the room and up to the attic, tossing it onto the floor in a dark corner where no daylight reached. 'What does this mean?' thought the tree, 'What am I supposed to do here?' It had plenty of time to wonder as days and nights passed with no one coming near. When someone finally did arrive, it was only to store large boxes in the corner, effectively putting the tree out of sight as if it had never existed. 'It’s winter now,' thought the tree. 'The ground is frozen, covered in snow, so no one can plant me. I suppose I’ll just stay here until spring comes. How kind everyone is! But I do wish this place were brighter instead of so lonely, with not even a hare to look at. Even though I didn’t like it then, it was so nice in the forest when the snow lay on the ground, and the hare would scamper by and jump over me. Oh, this is so painfully lonely!'

'Squeak, squeak,' said a little mouse as it inched cautiously toward the tree. Then another followed, both sniffing the fir and scurrying between its branches.

'Oh, it’s so cold,' said the little mouse. 'If it weren’t, we’d be quite comfy here, wouldn’t we, old fir tree?'

title: THE FIR TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the importance of appreciating what one has
text_chunk:
'I’m not old,' replied the fir tree. 'There are plenty of trees older than I am.'

'Where do you come from? What do you know?' asked the curious mice. 'Have you been to the most beautiful places in the world? Can you tell us about them? And have you been to the pantry, where delicious cheeses sit on the shelves and hams hang from the ceiling? One could run around on tallow candles there and go in thin and come out fat.'

'I don’t know anything about that place,' said the fir tree. 'But I do know about the woods where the sun shines and the birds sing.' Then it shared its youthful story with the little mice. They had never heard anything like it before. After listening intently, they said, 'Wow, you’ve seen so many things! You must have been very happy.'

'Happy!' exclaimed the fir tree. Then, reflecting on its story, it added, 'Ah, yes! Those were indeed happy days.' But when it recounted Christmas Eve and how it had been adorned with treats and lights, the mice replied, 'How lucky you were, you old fir tree!'

'I’m not old,' the tree insisted. 'I just came from the forest this winter; I’m only stuck now.'

'What amazing stories you can tell,' said the mice. The next night, four more mice joined them to hear the tree’s tales. The more it talked, the more the fir tree remembered. It thought to itself, 'Those days were joyful, and they could happen again. Humpty Dumpty fell and yet married a princess. Maybe I could marry a princess, too.' The tree envisioned the lovely birch tree in the forest that resembled his idea of a real princess.

'Who is Humpty Dumpty?' asked the little mice. The fir tree then recounted the entire story, remembering every detail. The little mice were so thrilled they could have jumped to the top of the tree. The following night, many more mice gathered, and even two rats joined in but were unimpressed with the story, leaving the little mice feeling disappointed as well.

title: THE FIR TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the importance of appreciating what one has
text_chunk:
'Do you know only one story?' asked the rats.

'Just one,' replied the fir tree, 'I heard it on the happiest evening of my life, though I didn’t realize at the time how happy I was.'

'We think it’s a miserable story,' said the rats. 'Don’t you have any tales about bacon or tallow in the pantry?'

'Thanks, but no thanks,' replied the rats, and they left.

title: THE FIR TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the importance of appreciating what one has
text_chunk: The little mice also stopped visiting after that, leaving the tree to sigh and reflect, 'It was so nice when the cheerful little mice gathered around me and listened to my stories. Now that’s gone, too. Still, I will think of myself as happy when someone comes to take me out of here.' But would that day ever come? Yes! One morning, people entered the attic to clean it out, packing away boxes, and pulled the tree from its corner, tossing it carelessly onto the attic floor. Then the servant dragged it down the stairs into the sunlight. 'Now life is starting again!' the tree rejoiced in the warmth and the fresh air. It was swiftly taken downstairs and into the courtyard, overwhelmed with excitement, eager to see everything around it. The courtyard opened into a garden bursting with life. Fresh, fragrant roses cascaded over the little fence. The linden trees were blooming while swallows darted here and there, chirping, 'Twit, twit, twit; my mate is arriving,' but they were not referring to the fir tree. 'Now I can truly live!' the tree cheered, stretching out its limbs, but alas! They were all withered and yellow, lying in a corner among weeds and nettles. The golden paper star still glittered at the top of the tree in the sunlight. Two children, who had once danced around the tree at Christmas, were playing in the courtyard. The youngest noticed the shiny star and ran over, ripping it from the tree. 'Look at this ugly old fir tree!' the child exclaimed, trampling the branches underfoot as they crunched beneath their boots. The tree gazed at all the vibrant flowers in the garden and then back at itself, regretting it hadn’t stayed in the dark attic. It reminisced about its youthful days in the forest, the joyous Christmas eve, and the little mice who had listened to the 'Humpty Dumpty' tale. 'Gone! Gone!' sighed the old tree. 'If only I had savored my time while I could! But now it’s too late.' Suddenly, a boy came along and chopped the tree into small pieces, leaving a heap on the ground. The pieces were tossed into a fire under the kettle, where they quickly erupted in flames. The tree sighed deeply, with each sigh echoing like a gunshot. Nearby, the children sat in front of the fire, watching it, calling out, 'Pop, pop!' Yet each 'pop,' which was just the sound of a deep sigh, made the tree think of a summer day in the forest, that magical Christmas evening, and of 'Humpty Dumpty,' the only story it knew or could ever tell, until finally, it was completely consumed. The kids continued to play in the garden, and the youngest wore the golden star that had adorned the tree during its happiest night. Now everything was over; the tree's life had come to an end, just as every story must eventually do.

title: THE FLAX
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The flax was in full bloom, showcasing pretty little blue flowers that were as delicate as a moth's wings—or maybe even more so. The sun was shining, and the rain was nurturing it, which was just as beneficial for the flax as it is for little children to be washed and then kissed by their mothers. They look much lovelier for it, and so did the flax.

"People say I look really good," the flax said. "They say I’m so fine and long that I’ll make a beautiful piece of linen. How lucky I am! It fills me with happiness knowing something amazing can be made from me. The sunshine lifts my spirits, and the rain is so sweet and refreshing; my joy is overwhelming—no one in the world could be happier than I am!"

"Oh, for sure," replied the fern, "but you haven’t experienced the world like I have; my stalks are all knotted," it said, then it sang a sad little tune.

"Snip, snap, snurre, Basse lurre: The song is over."

"No, it’s not over," said the flax. "Tomorrow the sun will shine again, or the rain will fall. I can feel that I’m growing. I know I’m in full blossom. I am the happiest of all creatures."

title: THE FLAX
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
One day, some people came along and yanked the flax up by its roots; it was painful. Next, they soaked it in water like they intended to drown it, and then they placed it near a fire as if to roast it. This was shocking! "We can't expect to be happy all the time," the flax thought. "Going through bad times as well as good helps us gain wisdom." And surely, there was plenty of tough times ahead for the flax. It faced being soaked, roasted, broken, and combed; it barely understood what was happening to it. Eventually, it found itself on the spinning wheel. "Whirr, whirr," went the wheel so fast that the flax couldn't gather its thoughts. "Well, I’ve been very happy," it thought amid the pain, "and I must be content with the past;" and content it remained until it was placed on the loom, becoming a beautiful piece of white linen. Remarkably, every last bit of flax was transformed into this one piece. "This is quite incredible; I never would have believed I could be so fortunate!"

"But the song hasn’t ended yet, I’m sure; it’s only just beginning! How wonderful it is that after all I’ve been through, I’m finally something special! I’m the luckiest being in the world—so strong and beautiful; look how white I am, and how long! This is a far cry from just being a simple plant with flowers. Back then, I received no attention and no water unless it rained; now, I’m cared for and looked after. Every morning, the maid turns me over, and I get a shower-bath from the watering can every evening. And the clergyman's wife noticed me, declaring that I’m the best piece of linen in the whole parish. I couldn’t be happier than I am now!"

title: THE FLAX
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Eventually, the linen was taken indoors, cut into pieces, and pricked with needles. Not very pleasant, but it eventually became twelve garments of a type that people often avoid naming, yet everyone should wear one. "Look at me now!" said the flax. "I’ve become something significant. This was my destiny; it’s truly a blessing. Now I can be useful in the world, just as everyone should be; that’s how we find happiness. I’m now divided into twelve parts, yet we all remain the same in this whole dozen. What extraordinary good fortune!"

Years went by, and eventually the linen was so worn out it could barely hold together. "It must be coming to an end soon," said the pieces to one another; "we wish we could hold on a little longer, but hoping for the impossible is futile." They fell into rags and tatters, thinking it was all over for them as they were torn apart, drenched, pulped, and dried, until suddenly they found themselves transformed into beautiful white paper. "Now, this is a delightful surprise!" said the paper. "I’m finer than ever, and I’m going to be written on—who knows what wonderful things may be inscribed on me? This is fantastic luck!" Sure enough, the most beautiful stories and poems were written on it, and there was only one blot, which was quite fortunate. People heard these stories and poems read aloud, and they became wiser and better for it; everything written contained good and meaningful lessons, a great blessing wrapped in the words on this paper.

"I never imagined anything like this when I was just a little blue flower growing in the fields. How could I have imagined I’d be the reason for bringing joy and knowledge to people? I can hardly believe it myself, yet it’s true. Heaven knows I did nothing on my own, but merely what was necessary for my survival; and still, I’ve moved from one joy and honor to the next. Just when I thought the song was over, something higher and better begins for me. I bet I’ll be sent off to travel the world to be read. It has to be; in fact, it’s likely, since I carry more splendid thoughts than I had pretty flowers back in the day. I’m happier than ever!"

title: THE FLAX
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
But the paper didn’t go on any travels; instead, it was sent to the printer, where all the words written on it were set in type to create a book—or rather, hundreds of books. So many more people could enjoy and gain from a printed book than if the paper itself had traveled the world; if it had, it would have worn out long before completing its journey.

"This is definitely the smartest plan," said the written paper. "I never thought of that. I’ll stay at home and be honored, almost like an old grandfather, which, in a way, I am to all these new books. They will do some good in the world. I couldn’t have wandered around like they do. But the one who wrote all this looked at me as each word flowed from his pen onto my surface. I am the most honored of all."

Then, the paper was tied up with other papers and tossed into a tub sitting in the washhouse.

"After work, it’s good to rest," said the paper, "and this is a perfect chance to gather my thoughts. Now I can finally contemplate my true state; knowing oneself is real progress. I wonder what will happen to me now? I’m sure there’s still more ahead. I’ve always been progressing, that much I know."

One day, all the paper in the tub was taken out and laid on the hearth to burn. People said it couldn’t be sold at the shop to wrap butter and sugar because it had been written on. The children in the house gathered around the stove, eager to see the paper burn because it flared up so beautifully, and among the ashes, countless red sparks danced about, zipping here and there like the wind. They called it kids coming out of school, and everyone claimed that the last spark was the schoolmaster. They often believed the last spark was gone, only for another spark to appear, shining brilliantly. They wondered where the sparks went. Perhaps someday we’ll find out, but for now, we don’t know.

The entire bundle of paper was piled onto the fire, and it quickly ignited. "Ugh," cried the paper as it flared into a bright flame. "Yikes!" It certainly wasn’t pleasant to be burning, but as it was engulfed in flames, they shot up into the air, higher than the flax had ever stretched its little blue flower, glimmering brighter than the white linen ever could. All the letters turned red in an instant, and all the words and thoughts blazed into flames.

"Now I’m soaring straight up to the sun!" a voice called from the flames, and it sounded like a thousand voices echoed back. The flames shot up through the chimney, disappearing out the top. Then, tiny beings, as numerous as the flowers the flax had once held, floated above; they were even lighter and more delicate than the original flowers. As the flames died down and left only black ashes, these little beings danced upon them, creating bright red sparks with every touch.

"The children are all out of school now, and the schoolmaster was the very last," exclaimed the children. It was delightful fun, and they sang above the dead ashes.

"Snip, snap, snurre, Basse lure: The song is over."

But the tiny, unseen beings said, "The song is never over; the most beautiful part is yet to come."

However, the children couldn’t hear or understand this, nor should they; there are simply some things children aren’t meant to know.

title: THE FLYING TRUNK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a merchant who was so wealthy that he could have covered the entire street with gold, and still had enough left for a small side street. But he didn't, as he understood the true value of money. He was so clever that every penny he spent brought him back a crown, and he kept this up until he passed away. His son inherited his fortune and lived a carefree life; he attended a masquerade every night, crafted kites from five-pound notes, and tossed gold coins into the sea instead of stones, having fun making them skip. Eventually, he wasted all his money. All that was left were a pair of slippers, an old robe, and four shillings. As a result, all his friends abandoned him; they couldn’t bear to walk beside him in the streets. Yet, one kind friend sent him an old trunk with a message: 'Pack up!' The son replied, 'Sure, it’s easy to say 'pack up,' but I don’t have anything left!' So, he decided to sit inside the trunk. It was no ordinary trunk; as soon as someone pressed the lock, it could fly! He closed the lid and pressed the lock, and the trunk soared up the chimney with him inside, straight into the clouds. Whenever he heard cracking sounds from the bottom, he felt scared because if the trunk fell apart, he’d have an epic tumble over the treetops. However, he managed to arrive safely in Turkey.

He hid the trunk in the woods under some dry leaves and then ventured into town. This was easy for him since the Turks typically wore dressing gowns and slippers, just like he did. He encountered a nurse with a small child and called out, 'Hey, Turkish nurse! What's that castle near the town with such high windows?'

'That’s where the king’s daughter lives,' she answered. 'It has been foretold that she will be quite unhappy about a lover, so no one is allowed to visit her unless the king and queen are present.'

title: THE FLYING TRUNK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
'Thanks!' said the merchant’s son. With that, he went back to the woods, climbed into his trunk, flew up to the castle roof, and slipped through a window into the princess’s room. She was sleeping on the sofa, and her beauty was so striking that he couldn’t resist kissing her. She woke up startled, but he quickly reassured her that he was a Turkish angel who had come down from the sky to visit her, which made her very happy. He sat beside her and chatted, telling her that her eyes looked like deep lakes with thoughts swimming around like little mermaids. He compared her forehead to a snowy mountain hiding splendid halls full of pictures. Then, he shared a delightful story about the stork that brings beautiful children from rivers. She listened intently, and when he asked if she would marry him, she immediately said yes.

'But you must come on Saturday,' she said. 'That’s when the king and queen will have tea with me. They will be so proud to find out that I’m marrying a Turkish angel! But you must think of some charming stories to impress them, as my parents love hearing stories. My mother prefers those that are deep and moral, while my father likes something funny to make him laugh.'

'Of course,' he replied. 'I’ll bring no other marriage gift than a story.' With that, they parted ways, but the princess gifted him a sword adorned with gold coins, which he could use.

Then he soared back to the town, bought a new dressing gown, and returned to the woods, where he crafted a story to be ready for Saturday. This was not an easy task, but he managed to finish it in time. Come Saturday, he visited the princess, where the king, queen, and the entire court were enjoying tea with her. He was greeted with great courtesy.

'Will you tell us a story?' asked the queen, 'one that’s enlightening and full of knowledge.'

'Yes, but don’t forget to add something funny,' chimed in the king.

title: THE FLYING TRUNK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
'Absolutely,' he answered and began: 'Once there was a bundle of matches that were exceedingly proud of their noble lineage. Their family tree was a tall pine from which they’d been cut. The matches lay tucked between a tinderbox and an old pan, reminiscing about their glorious past. 'Ah, back then, we grew on lush green branches, and the morning dew fed us like diamonds. When the sun shone, we soaked up its warmth, and the little birds sang us stories. We knew we were lucky; while other trees wore their green only in summer, our family was always dressed in green. But then the woodcutter came, cutting us down like a great upheaval. The eldest branch now serves as the mainmast on a grand ship, sailing the world, while the others were sent off to various places; now we just kindle lights for the common folk. That's how such highborn beings like us ended up in a kitchen.'

'My story is quite different,' said the iron pot next to the matches. 'From the moment I entered the world, I've been used for cooking and cleaning. I am the one everyone comes to when something solid and useful is needed. My joy comes from being clean and shiny after meals and chatting with my neighbors. Except for the water bucket, who sometimes goes outside, we all live snugly within these four walls. We get our gossip from the market basket, but he often brings unpleasant news about people and the government. One day an old pot got so frightened, he fell and shattered. He was quite a progressive, I assure you.'

'You’re talking too much,' interrupted the tinderbox, striking against the flint until sparks flew. 'Don’t we want a fun evening?'

'Absolutely,' the matches agreed. 'Let’s discuss the highborn among us.'

'No, I’d prefer not to keep talking about our status,' the saucepan interjected. 'Let’s find another way to have fun. I’ll start. Let’s share something that has happened to us. That should be easy and interesting. Once, on the Baltic Sea near the Danish shore...'

title: THE FLYING TRUNK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
'What a lovely start!' the plates exclaimed. 'We’re sure to enjoy that story!'

'Well, in my younger days, I lived in a tidy household where furniture was polished, floors were scrubbed, and fresh curtains were hung every couple of weeks.'

'What an engaging storyteller you are,' praised the carpet broom. 'It’s clear you've spent a lot of time in the company of women; your words carry a genuine purity.'

'That’s certainly true,' replied the water bucket, leaping for joy and splashing some water on the floor.

Then, the saucepan continued with his tale, and the ending was just as captivating as the beginning.

The plates rattled with delight, and the carpet broom dug out some parsley from the dustbin to crown the saucepan, knowing it would annoy the others. He thought, 'If I crown him today, he’ll crown me tomorrow.'

'Let’s have a dance!' suggested the fire-tongs, and then they all danced, lifting one leg into the air. The chair cushion in the corner burst with laughter at the sight.

'Shall I be crowned now?' asked the fire-tongs, as the broom quickly found another wreath for them.

'After all, they were just common items,' thought the matches. The teapot was then asked to sing, but she said she had a cold and couldn’t sing without being heated up. Everyone thought she was just being snooty and only wanted to perform for the fine folks in the parlor.

An old quill pen sat in the window, usually used by the maid for writing. There was nothing too special about the pen, aside from being dipped too deeply in ink, but it was quite proud of that.

'If the teapot won’t sing,' said the pen, 'she should just let it be; there's a nightingale in a cage that can sing. Sure, she hasn’t been trained much, but we can overlook that tonight.'

'I find it quite inappropriate,' stated the teapot, the kitchen's own singer and half-sibling to the teapot, 'that we give a rich foreign bird our attention. Isn’t that a bit unpatriotic? Let the market basket decide what’s right.'

title: THE FLYING TRUNK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
'I’m definitely annoyed,' replied the basket. 'Inwardly vexed more than anyone realizes. Are we truly spending our evening wisely? Wouldn’t it be smarter to tidy up? If everyone were in their proper place, I’d lead a game; that would change everything.'

'Let’s perform a play,' they all suggested at once. Just then, the door swung open, and the maid walked in. Not a single pot among them moved; they stayed perfectly still. Still, not one of them lacked a high opinion of themselves and what they could accomplish if they wanted to.

'Sure, if we had chosen,' they all thought, 'we could have enjoyed a very fun evening.'

The maid grabbed the matches and lit them. Oh, how they sputtered and flared up!

'Now,' they all thought, 'everyone will see we’re the best. Look how we shine! What a light we bring!' But even as they boasted, their flames faded out.

'What a fantastic story!' exclaimed the queen. 'I feel like I’m right there in the kitchen, watching the matches. Yes, you may marry our daughter.'

'Indeed,' agreed the king. 'You shall have our daughter.' The king addressed him as 'thou' because he was soon to be part of the family. The wedding day was set, and on the eve, the entire city was lit up. Sweets and treats were thrown to the crowd. The street children stood on their toes, shouting 'Hooray!' and whistling with their fingers; it was all quite spectacular.

'I’ll give them another surprise,' declared the merchant’s son. So he bought rockets, firecrackers, and whatever fireworks he could find, packed them all into his trunk, and flew up into the sky. What a whizzing and popping they made as they went off! When the Turks saw such a spectacle in the air, they jumped so high their slippers flew off. After this, it was easy to believe that the princess was truly going to marry a Turkish angel.

After the fireworks, when the merchant's son landed back in the woods with his trunk, he thought, 'I should head back to town and see what they think of the show.' It was only natural he’d be curious about their opinions. How strange the things people said! Every person he asked shared a different tale, yet all agreed it was beautiful.

'I saw the Turkish angel myself,' said one. 'He had eyes like shimmering stars and hair like foamy water.'

'He flew in a cloak of fire,' shouted another, 'and delightful little cherubs peeked out from the folds.'

He heard many more wonderful things about himself, including that he was to be married the very next day. After all this, he went back to the forest to rest in his trunk. But it was gone! A spark from the remaining fireworks had set it ablaze; it was now nothing but ashes! So the merchant’s son couldn’t fly anymore or meet his bride. She waited all day on the roof for him, and she’s probably still waiting there while he roams the world telling fairy tales—though none are quite as entertaining as the tale about the matches.

title: THE GARDEN OF PARADISE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a prince who owned the largest and most beautiful collection of books anyone could imagine, filled with stunning copper engravings. He could read about every culture and country, but there was one thing he could never find in any of his books: the location of the garden of paradise, which was what he yearned to discover the most. When he was a young boy, just old enough to start school, his grandmother had told him that every flower in the garden of paradise was like a delicious cake, the pistils were filled with rich wine, and that on one flower was written history, while another had geography or math. She said that anyone who wanted to learn simply had to eat some of these cake-like flowers, and the more they ate, the more knowledge they gained. He believed her then, but as he grew older and wiser, he understood that the real beauty of the garden of paradise must be so much different from that. "Oh, why did Eve pick the fruit from the tree of knowledge? Why did Adam eat the forbidden fruit?" pondered the prince. "If I had been there, none of this would have happened, and there wouldn’t be any sin in the world." The garden of paradise occupied his thoughts all the way until he turned seventeen.

One evening, while he was walking alone in the woods—a pastime he loved—dark clouds gathered, and rain started to pour as if the sky had opened up. It got as dark as the bottom of a well at midnight. He slipped on the wet grass and stumbled over sharp stones sticking out of the ground. Everything was soaked, and the poor prince was soaked to the bone. Eventually, he had to climb over big rocks, with water splattering up from the thick moss. Just when he began to feel weak, he heard a strange rushing sound and came across a large cave filled with light. Inside, there was a huge fire burning, and a majestic stag, its impressive antlers gleaming, was roasting on a spit between two sturdy pine trees. An old woman, who looked strong enough to be mistaken for a man, sat by the fire, throwing logs into the flames.

title: THE GARDEN OF PARADISE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Come in," she called to the prince, "sit by the fire and warm yourself."

"There’s a strong draft in here," the prince remarked as he settled onto the ground.

"It will only get worse when my sons come home," replied the woman. "You're currently in the Cavern of the Winds, and my sons are the four Winds. Do you get that?"

"Answering foolish questions isn’t easy," the woman continued. "My boys have a lot going on; they’re up there playing shuttlecock with the clouds in the king’s hall," she pointed skyward.

"Oh, I see," replied the prince, "but you're speaking more roughly than the women I’m used to."

"That’s because they have nothing to do, whereas I have to be stern to keep my boys in line. Believe me, I can handle them, even though they can be pretty stubborn. See those four sacks hanging on the wall? They’re just as scared of those as you used to be of the rat behind the looking glass. I can pack the boys into those sacks without a fight, trust me. They stay in there until I say they can come out. And here comes one of them now."

In strode the North Wind, bringing a biting chill with him. Hailstones pelted the floor, and snowflakes swirled around. He was dressed in a bearskin cloak, with a sealskin hat pulled down over his ears. Long icicles hung from his beard, and hailstones rolled off his coat.

"Don’t get too close to the fire," the prince warned. "You might frostbite your hands and face."

"Frostbite?" laughed the North Wind loudly. "That’s my favorite thing! And who are you, little one, and how did you find your way to the Cavern of the Winds?"

"He’s my guest," said the old woman. "And if you don’t like that, you can go into the sack. Do you get me?"

title: THE GARDEN OF PARADISE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
That put an end to the discussion. So the North Wind began to share his adventures, telling of where he had been for the past month. "I come from the polar seas," he said. "I was at Bear's Island with Russian walrus hunters. I slept at the helm of their ship as they traveled away from North Cape. Sometimes I’d wake up to see storm birds flapping around my legs. They’re peculiar creatures; they flap their wings once and soar far away, on their long outstretched wings.

"Don’t drag the story out so much," the mother of the Winds said. "What’s Bear's Island like?"

"It’s a very beautiful place, with a flat, smooth dancing floor. Half-melted snow, covered in moss, sharp stones, and the remains of walruses and polar bears litter the ground, their giant limbs decaying in green. It seems like the sun never shines there. I’d blow gently to clear the mist away, and there I’d see a little hut built from shipwreck wood, dressed with walrus skins turned outward; it looked green and red. On the roof sat a grumpy bear. Then I went to the seashore to check out birds' nests and saw the baby birds opening their mouths, crying for food. I blew into those tiny throats, and quickly silenced their cries. Further along, there were walruses with pig-like heads and teeth a yard long, rolling about like giant worms."

"You tell your stories very well, my son," said the mother, "they make my mouth water."

"After that, the hunting began. A harpoon struck a walrus, sending a stream of blood spraying like a fountain onto the ice. Then I thought of my own game; I started blowing, setting my own ships—the icebergs—adrift to crush the boats. Oh, how the sailors howled and screamed! But I howled louder. They had to toss their cargo and leave their chests and dead walruses on the ice. Then I covered them with snow and left them to drift southward and taste the salty sea. They will never return to Bear's Island."

title: THE GARDEN OF PARADISE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"So you’ve caused trouble," said the mother of the Winds.

"I’ll let others talk about the good I’ve done," he replied. "But here comes my brother from the West; I like him best of all, as he smells of the ocean and brings fresh, cold air with him as he arrives."

"Is that the little Zephyr?" asked the prince.

"Yes, that’s the little Zephyr," said the old woman, "but he isn’t little anymore. Once upon a time, he was a beautiful boy; now that’s all in the past."

He came in looking wild, wearing a slouched hat to protect his head and carrying a hefty club cut from a mahogany tree in the American forests.

"I come from the wild forests, where thorny brambles create thick hedges between the trees, where the water snake slinks through the wet grass, and humans hardly exist."

"I peered into the rushing river and watched it tumble down from the rocks. Water droplets rose to the sky, sparkling like a rainbow. I saw a wild buffalo swimming, but the strong current swept him away among a flock of wild ducks who took flight as the waters rushed by, leaving the buffalo to tumble over a waterfall. That delighted me, so I called up a storm that uprooted old trees, sending them floating down the river."

"And what else did you do?" the old woman asked.

"I zipped wildly across the savannahs; I petted wild horses and shook coconuts from the trees. Yes, I've got many stories to tell, but I don't need to share everything I know. You are well aware, aren’t you, old lady?" He kissed his mother so roughly that she nearly lost her balance. He was truly a spirited fellow.

Just then, the South Wind entered, wearing a turban and a flowing Bedouin cloak.

"It’s freezing in here!" he exclaimed, throwing more wood onto the fire. "You can tell the North Wind got here before me."

"It's hot enough to roast a bear here," the North Wind shot back.

title: THE GARDEN OF PARADISE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"You yourself are a bear," retorted the other.

"Do both of you want to end up in the sack?" said the old woman. "Now sit on that stone and tell me where you’ve been."

"In Africa, mother. I went out with the Hottentots who were hunting lions in Kaffir land, where the grass is the color of green olives. I raced with an ostrich but quickly left him behind. Eventually, I reached the desert, where the golden sands shimmer like the sea's bottom. I met a caravan whose travelers had just killed their last camel to find water; they had very little, and they toiled on under the burning sun across the vast, endless desert. I rolled myself in the sand and whipped it into burning columns over their heads. The dromedaries froze in terror while the merchants covered their heads and fell to the ground before me, as they do before Allah, their god. I buried them under a heap of sand to cover them all. When I blow that away on my next visit, the sun will bleach their bones, and travelers will see evidence that others have passed through."

"So you’ve done nothing but evil," said the mother. "Into the sack with you;" and, before he knew it, she had grabbed the South Wind and stuffed him into the bag. He rolled around on the floor until she sat on him to keep him still.

"Your boys are quite lively," the prince observed.

"Yes," she responded, "but I know how to correct them when I need to. And here comes the fourth." In walked the East Wind, dressed like someone out of a Chinese painting.

"Oh, you’re from that direction, are you?" she noted. "I thought you had been to the garden of paradise."

"I’m heading there tomorrow," he replied. "I haven’t been for a hundred years. I just returned from China, where I danced around the porcelain tower until all the bells rang. In the streets, I saw an official flogging taking place, with bamboo canes breaking over the shoulders of men from all ranks. They exclaimed, 'Thank you, my kind benefactor;' but I could tell their hearts weren’t in it, so I made the bells ring out ‘ding, ding-dong.’"

title: THE GARDEN OF PARADISE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"You’re quite the wild one," said the old woman; "it’s good you’re going to the garden of paradise tomorrow; you always learn something new there. Drink deeply from the fountain of knowledge while you’re there, and bring me back a bottleful."

"I will," said the East Wind, "but why have you put my brother South in a bag? Let him out; I want him to tell me about the phoenix. The princess always wants to hear about this bird when I visit her every hundred years. If you open the sack, sweetest mother, I’ll give you two pockets full of tea, fresh and green as when I picked it from the spot where it grew."

"Well, for the sake of the tea and because you’re my own son, I’ll open the bag."

She did, and the South Wind crawled out looking rather downcast since the prince had witnessed his embarrassment.

"Here’s a palm-leaf for the princess," he said. "The old phoenix—the only one in the world—gave it to me himself. He scratched his entire life story into it with his beak. The princess can read how the old phoenix set fire to his own nest and sat on it while it burned, like a widow from Hindoo tradition. The dry twigs crackled around the nest until the flames shot forth and reduced the phoenix to ashes. Amidst the fire lay an egg, glowing red hot, which soon burst with a loud bang and out flew a young bird. He’s the only phoenix in the world and the king of all other birds. He has bitten a hole in the leaf I give you, which is his greeting to the princess."

"Now let’s eat something," said the mother of the Winds. So they all sat down to feast on the roasted stag, and as the prince sat beside the East Wind, they quickly became good friends.

"Could you tell me who this princess is that you’ve been talking about? And where is the garden of paradise?" asked the prince.

title: THE GARDEN OF PARADISE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Ho! ho!" exclaimed the East Wind, "would you like to go there? Well, you can fly away with me tomorrow; but I must warn you, no human has set foot there since the days of Adam and Eve. You’ve read about them in your Bible, haven’t you?"

The East Wind continued, "When Adam and Eve were expelled from the garden of paradise, it sank into the earth. Still, it kept its warm sunshine, gentle breezes, and all its splendor. The fairy queen resides there, in the island of happiness, where death does not exist, and everything is beautiful. I can take you there tomorrow if you ride on my back. But for now, let’s not talk any more, as I want to sleep." The group all settled in.

When the prince woke up early the next morning, he was astonished to find himself high above the clouds. He was sitting on the back of the East Wind, who was holding him securely. They were flying so high that the woods, fields, rivers, and lakes below looked like a giant painted map.

"Good morning," the East Wind greeted him. "You could have slept longer; there’s not much to see in the flat country we’re passing over unless you enjoy counting churches, which look like spots of chalk on a green board." The green board was his term for the green fields and meadows.

"It was rude of me not to say goodbye to your mother and brothers," said the prince.

"They’ll forgive you since you were asleep," replied the East Wind, and they sped along even faster.

The leaves and branches rustled as they flew by. Over the seas and lakes, the waves grew taller, and the large ships dipped into the water like swans diving. As night fell, the great towns looked enchanting; lights twinkled like stars, appearing and disappearing like sparks on a piece of paper set on fire. The prince clapped his hands in delight, but the East Wind warned him not to show too much excitement, or he might fall and find himself hanging from a church steeple. The eagle in dark forests flies swiftly, but even faster was the East Wind.

title: THE GARDEN OF PARADISE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"There are the Himalayas, the tallest mountains in Asia," said the East Wind. "We’re getting close to the garden of paradise now."

Then they turned south, and the air filled with the sweet scent of spices and flowers. Wild figs and pomegranates grew freely, and vines hung heavy with clusters of blue and purple grapes. They both landed and lay back on the soft grass, watching the flowers sway gently with the breeze as if welcoming them. "Are we in the garden of paradise now?" asked the prince.

"Not yet," replied the East Wind. "But we will be very soon. Do you see that rocky wall with a cave underneath it, draped with grapevines like a green curtain? That’s where we need to go. Wrap your cloak tightly; while it's warm here, just a few steps further, it gets icy cold. A bird flying past the cave feels like it’s one wing in summer and the other in winter."

"So this is the way to the garden of paradise?" the prince asked as they stepped into the cave. It was cold at first, but that quickly faded because the East Wind spread his wings, which glimmered like fire. As they made their way through the enchanting cave, the prince noticed large stone formations above them, with water trickling from the ceiling in fascinating shapes. Sometimes the passage was so narrow that they had to crawl on their hands and knees, while at other times, it opened up wide like a vast sky. It felt like they were moving through a solemn chapel for the dead, adorned with stone organs and silent pipes. "It seems like we are traveling through the valley of death to reach the garden of paradise," the prince said.

title: THE GARDEN OF PARADISE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: But the East Wind said nothing, only pointed ahead to a lovely blue glow shining in the distance. The stones looked misty until they appeared like white clouds in moonlight. The air felt fresh and sweet, like a breeze from the mountains filled with the scent of roses. A crystal-clear river sparkled at their feet, revealing gold and silver fish playing in the bright water, along with purple eels that emitted sparks of light. The wide leaves of the water lilies floated above, shimmering with all the colors of the rainbow. The flower seemed to draw its life from the water, just as a lamp draws oil. A marble bridge, so exquisitely crafted it looked like it was made of lace and pearls, led to the island of happiness where the garden of paradise bloomed. The East Wind lifted the prince in his arms and carried him over, while flowers and leaves sang sweet songs from his childhood in tones so full and soft that no human voice could ever replicate them. Inside the garden, tall trees filled with sap grew, but whether they were palms or towering water plants, the prince couldn’t tell. Vines draped like garlands of green and gold, resembling the illuminations on ancient manuscripts. Birds, flowers, and decorations intertwined in beautiful chaos. Nearby, a group of peacocks stood on the grass, flaunting their colorful tails in the sun. The prince reached out to touch them and was startled to find they weren't real birds but leaves from the burdock tree sparkling in the colors of peacock feathers. The lions and tigers played gently around the green bushes, the fragrant blossoms reminiscent of olives. The feathers of the wood-pigeon shone like pearls as it flapped against the lion’s mane, while even the usually shy antelope stood nearby, nodding its head as if wanting to join in on the fun. Soon, the fairy of paradise appeared. She radiated like the sun, her joyful face glowing with the happiness of a mother cherishing her child. She was young and lovely, and a group of beautiful maidens followed her, each wearing a bright star in her hair. The East Wind handed her the palm-leaf etched with the phoenix's history, and her eyes sparkled with delight. She then took the prince’s hand and led him into her palace, whose walls were richly painted, reminiscent of a tulip leaf basking in sunlight. The ceiling looked like an upside-down flower, with colors growing deeper and more vivid the longer you stared. The prince walked to a window and saw what looked like the tree of knowledge, with Adam and Eve beside it and the serpent lurking nearby. "I thought they were banished from paradise," he remarked.

title: THE GARDEN OF PARADISE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The princess smiled and explained that time had carved each event onto a pane of glass, displaying moving pictures; the leaves rustled, and the people came and went, just like in a mirror. He gazed through another pane and saw the ladder from Jacob's dream, with angels ascending and descending on outspread wings. Everything that had ever occurred in the world was alive and moving on the glass panes, captured in images only time could create. The fairy then led the prince into a large, airy room with transparent walls that sparkled with light. Inside were portraits, each more beautiful than the last—a million joyful beings, their laughter mingling into one sweet melody. Some appeared so elevated that they seemed smaller than the tiniest rosebud or mere specks on a page. In the center stood a tree with drooping branches, bearing golden apples—both big and small—looking like oranges amidst the green leaves. This was the tree of knowledge of good and evil, the very one from which Adam and Eve had taken their forbidden fruit. Each leaf dripped a bright red dewdrop as if the tree were lamenting the consequences of their choice. "Let’s take a boat ride," suggested the fairy, "a sail on the cool waters will refresh us. We won't actually move from here, even if the boat rocks on the rising waves; the countries of the world will glide by, but we’ll remain still."

It was truly a sight to behold. First, the majestic Alps appeared, covered in snow and cloaked in clouds and dark pines. The sound of horns echoed, and shepherds sang joyfully in the valleys. Banana trees leaned over the boat, and rare animals and flowers lined the distant shores. New Holland, the fifth land of the world, drifted past, with mountains that looked blue in the background. They heard the priests’ song and watched a wild dance unfold, synchronized with the beat of drums and horns made of bone; the pyramids of Egypt rose up to the clouds, while columns and sphinxes lay toppled and buried in sand, following in pursuit; flashes of the northern lights danced over extinguished volcanoes in a performance no one could replicate.

title: THE GARDEN OF PARADISE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The prince was overjoyed, seeing countless marvelous sights beyond what could be described. "Can I stay here forever?" he asked.

"That depends on you," the fairy replied. "As long as you don’t, like Adam, yearn for what’s forbidden, you can remain here always."

"I won't touch the fruit from the tree of knowledge," declared the prince, "there’s plenty of equally beautiful fruit here."

"Look deep within your heart," advised the princess, "and if you don’t feel confident in its strength, return with the East Wind who brought you here. He’ll be flying back and won’t return for another hundred years. You might think that will pass like a hundred hours, yet even that’s a long time to face temptation and test your will. Each evening, when I leave you, I’ll have to say, 'Come with me,' and beckon you with my hand. But you mustn’t listen or move to follow me; with every step you take, your resistance will weaken. If you ever try to follow me, you’ll find yourself back in the hall where the tree of knowledge grows, for that’s where I sleep under its perfumed branches. If you leaned over me, I’d have to smile. If you kissed my lips, the garden of paradise would sink away, and it would be lost to you. A wailing wind from the desert would surround you, cold rain would splash down on your head, and sorrow would be your only future."

The East Wind then kissed him on the forehead and said, "Stay strong; we will meet again in one hundred years. Farewell, farewell." With that, the East Wind spread his broad wings, which sparkled like lightning during a harvest or like the aurora borealis on a cold winter night.

"Farewell, farewell," echoed the trees and flowers.

Storks and pelicans flew behind him in feathered groups, escorting him to the edges of the garden.

title: THE GARDEN OF PARADISE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Now we will start dancing," the fairy said. "When it’s nearly over at sunset, while I’m dancing with you, I will make a sign and ask you to follow me. But don’t obey. I must repeat this for a hundred years, and each time you resist, you’ll gain strength until resisting becomes easier. After a while, your temptation will disappear completely. This evening, since it'll be your first, I have warned you."

Then, the fairy led him into a grand hall filled with delicate, transparent lilies. The tiny golden pistils of each flower created a soft music that resembled the harmonious tunes of flutes and lyres. Beautiful maidens, slender and graceful, glided through the dance, singing about the joyous life in the garden of paradise, where death would never intrude, and where everything would bloom forever in eternal youth. As the sun dipped down, the sky turned vibrant crimson and gold, casting a rosy tint upon the lilies. The lovely maidens offered the prince sparkling wine; after drinking it, he felt a happiness greater than he’d ever known. Suddenly, the backdrop of the hall opened, revealing the tree of knowledge, surrounded by a heavenly glow that nearly blinded him. Soft, sweet voices that reminded him of his mother filled the air, calling to him, "My child, my beloved child." The fairy beckoned to him in sweet tones, "Come with me, come with me." Forgetting his promise, even on that very first evening, he rushed to her, while she continued to smile and signal him.

"Come, come," urged that enchanting voice, and the prince followed. With each step, his cheeks flushed, and exhilaration coursed through his veins. "I must follow," he thought; "it can't be a sin to pursue beauty and joy. I just want to see her sleeping, and nothing will happen if I don’t kiss her. I can hold back; I’m strong-willed."

The fairy cast off her dazzling clothing, pushed back the branches, and vanished into the trees.

title: THE GARDEN OF PARADISE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I haven't sinned yet," the prince declared, "and I won’t;" and then he pushed through the branches to follow the princess. She lay sleeping, beautiful as only a fairy in the garden of paradise could be. She smiled as he leaned over her, and he noticed tears glistening on her lovely eyelashes. "Are you crying for me?" he whispered. "Oh, don’t cry, dearest of women. Now I feel the happiness of paradise in my very being, in every thought. A new life is awakening within me. One moment of such joy is worth an eternity of darkness and sorrow." He leaned down and kissed the tears from her eyes, then brushed his lips against hers.

A deafening clap of thunder shattered the air. Everything around him collapsed. The gorgeous fairy, the beautiful garden, sank deeper and deeper. The prince watched as it sank into the dark night until it twinkled like a distant star beneath him. Suddenly, a coldness, akin to death, swept through him; his eyes closed, and he fell into a deep unconsciousness.

When he regained his senses, he was met with a cold rain pounding down on him and a sharp wind whipping through his hair. "Oh no, what have I done?" he sighed. "I have sinned like Adam, and the garden of paradise has vanished into the earth." He opened his eyes and saw the star in the distance; it was just the morning star sparkling brightly in the dark.

He got to his feet and found himself lost in a dense forest, right where the Cavern of the Winds was, and the mother of the Winds sat beside him. She looked angry and raised her arm in the air as she spoke. "The very first evening!" she exclaimed. "I knew this would happen! If you were my son, I would place you in the sack."

"And that’s where he will end up eventually too," said a strong old man with large black wings and a scythe in his hand, known as Death. "He will be laid to rest in his coffin, but not yet. I’ll allow him to roam the world for a while, to atone for his sin and give him time to improve. But I will return when he least expects it. I will place him in a black coffin, lift it onto my head, and fly away beyond the stars. There, too, blooms a garden of paradise, and if he behaves and is pious, he will be welcomed in. But if his heart is filled with sin, he will sink deeper than the garden of paradise has ever sunk. Once every thousand years, I will come for him, at which time he will either be condemned to sink further or freed to a happier existence in the world beyond the stars."

title: THE GIRL WHO TROD ON THE LOAF
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once there was a girl named Inge who stepped on a loaf to keep her shoes clean, and everyone knows the troubles she faced because of it. Inge was a poor girl, but she was proud, arrogant, and had a cruel side. As a little child, she enjoyed capturing flies and ripping off their wings to make them crawl. When she grew older, she would take beetles and pin them down, only to watch them struggle while she pretended they were reading a tiny leaf. Sadly, she only got worse with age, and her beauty made people overlook her bad behavior.

"Your stubbornness needs to be controlled," her mother often told her. "When you were little, you would trample on my apron, but one day, I’m afraid you’ll trample on my heart." Unfortunately, her mother’s fears came true.

Inge was sent to live with a wealthy family far away, who treated her like their own child and dressed her in beautiful clothes, which only made her pride grow.

About a year later, her patroness said, "You should visit your parents at least once, Inge."

So, Inge set off to see her parents, but deep down, she just wanted to show off her fancy clothes back home. When she reached the edge of the village, she spotted young men and women chatting, and her mother among them. Inge’s mother was sitting on a stone to take a break, with a bundle of sticks she had gathered from the woods. Seeing her mother’s shabby appearance made Inge turn back; despite her fancy dress, she felt embarrassed by her mother’s poverty, not out of sympathy, but because of her pride.

Another six months passed, and her mistress said, "You should go home again and visit your parents, Inge. I’ll give you a large wheaten loaf to take with you; I know they’ll be happy to see you."

title: THE GIRL WHO TROD ON THE LOAF
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Inge put on her best outfit and new shoes, lifted her dress, and set out, making sure her feet stayed clean. However, when she reached the spot where the path crossed the marsh, she saw muddy puddles, and in order to avoid wetting her feet, she tossed the loaf into the mud and stepped on it. As she stood there with one foot on the loaf and the other lifted to step forward, the loaf began to sink, pulling her down until she vanished completely, leaving just a few bubbles to mark where she had sunk. And that is how her story ends.

But where did Inge go? She sank into the ground and found herself with the Marsh Woman, who was always brewing there.

The Marsh Woman is related to the elf maidens we hear about in songs and stories, but little is known about her. When mist rises from the meadows in summer, it's said to be because she's brewing beneath them. Inge fell into a place that no one can stand for long. A heap of mud was a palace compared to the Marsh Woman's brewery; as she fell, she shivered, becoming cold and stiff like marble. Her foot remained attached to the loaf, weighing her down as a golden ear of corn bends a stalk.

Soon, an evil spirit took hold of Inge and dragged her to an even worse place where she saw countless unhappy souls in agony, waiting for mercy. Each one was plagued by an endless sense of unrest. While it would take too long to describe the torments they faced, Inge's punishment was to stand like a statue with her foot still stuck to the loaf. She could move her eyes to see the suffering around her but couldn’t turn her head; when others looked at her, she mistakenly thought they admired her beauty and fine clothes. She was unaware that her clothes were now covered in mud, a snake tangled in her hair, and toads peering out from her dress. The worst of all was the terrible hunger that tormented her, and she couldn’t bend down to break off a piece of the loaf; her body felt like a stiff pillar. Flies without wings crawled over her face and eyes, and no matter how much she blinked, they wouldn’t fly away—it was unbearable.

title: THE GIRL WHO TROD ON THE LOAF
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"If this goes on much longer," she thought, "I won’t be able to take it." But it continued, and she had to endure it without being able to help herself.

A tear, followed by many hot tears, fell on her head and ran down her face to the loaf beneath her. Who could be crying for Inge? Her mother was still alive, and a mother’s tears of sorrow for her child will always touch the child's heart, sometimes adding to their torment rather than lifting it. Inge could hear conversations from the world she had left behind, and everyone seemed cruel. The day she stepped on the loaf had been witnessed by a cowherd from a hill as she crossed the marsh.

When her mother wept and said, "Oh, Inge! What grief you’ve caused me!" Inge thought, "Oh, that I had never been born! My mother’s tears can’t help me now."

Then the kind words of the people who had adopted her reached her ears. They said, "Inge was a sinful girl who didn’t appreciate the gifts of God but trampled over them instead."

"Ah," thought Inge, "they should have punished me and driven my bad behavior out of me."

A song began circulating about "The girl who stepped on a loaf to keep her shoes clean," and it was sung everywhere. The tale of her sin was shared with children, who called her "wicked Inge" and said she deserved punishment. Inge heard this, and her heart grew hard and bitter.

One day, as hunger and grief gnawed at her, she overheard a little, innocent child listening to the story of the vain and proud Inge, who suddenly broke into tears and exclaimed, "But will she never come back?"

And she heard the response, "No, she will never come back."

"But if she were to say she was sorry and ask for forgiveness, promising never to do it again?" asked the child.

"Yes, then she might come back; but she will not ask for forgiveness," came the answer.

title: THE GIRL WHO TROD ON THE LOAF
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Oh, I wish she would!" said the child, who was very upset about it. "I would give up my doll and all my toys if she could just come back! Poor Inge! This is awful for her."

These compassionate words pierced Inge's heart, and she felt a strange warmth. It was the first time someone had said, "Poor Inge" without mentioning her faults. A little innocent child was crying and praying for her, making Inge feel odd, and she wished she could cry too. The fact that she couldn’t added to her suffering. Meanwhile, years rolled on in the world above, and she heard her name mentioned less often. One day, she caught a sigh and the words, "Inge! Inge! What a sorrow you’ve caused me! I warned you it would be like this." It was the last breath of her dying mother.

After that, Inge heard her kind mistress say, "Oh, poor Inge! Will I ever see you again? Perhaps, but we never know what the future holds." But Inge knew well that her mistress would never reach that terrible place.

Time passed—it was a long, bitter time—until Inge once again heard her name spoken and saw what looked like two bright stars shining above her. They were the gentle eyes of an old woman closing for the last time on earth. Many years had passed since that little girl had cried for "poor Inge." Now that child had become an old woman whom God was calling home. In her final moments, one often reflects on the entire life they lived, and this woman recalled how, as a child, she cried over Inge's tale and now prayed for her. As her eyes closed on earth, she saw the hidden realities of eternity, and her final thoughts were filled with Inge’s plight. Tears streamed down her face, and in heaven, just as when she was a little girl, she wept and prayed for Inge. Her tears and prayers traveled through the dark void surrounding Inge’s tormented soul, and through the mercy they brought, she found an unexpected grace. As Inge mentally replayed each sin from her earthly life, she trembled, and tears she had never shed rushed to her eyes. She thought it was impossible for mercy to ever reach her, but as she recognized this in deep remorse, a brilliant light suddenly pierced the depths around her.

title: THE GIRL WHO TROD ON THE LOAF
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
More powerful than any sunlight melting a snowman, Inge’s stiff form transformed, and like a bird, she soared upward with lightning speed to the world of the living. The little bird was timid and shy, hiding from everything around it, seeking refuge in a dark corner of an old, crumbling wall; there, it sat, unable to make a sound. Yet it quickly discovered the beauty surrounding it—the fresh air, the soft glow of the moonlight bathing the earth, and the delightful scents wafting from the bushes and trees made it feel joyfully alive in its vibrant feathers. Everything in creation seemed to sing of kindness and love. The bird longed to express the feelings inside it like the cuckoo and nightingale do in spring, but it couldn’t. Yet in heaven, even a worm can be heard singing praises, and the trembling notes in the bird’s heart were audible to Heaven, just like the heartfelt psalms of David before they found their voice.

As Christmas approached, a nearby peasant stuck a pole with some grains of corn on top so the birds could feast and celebrate the joyous holiday. On Christmas morning, the sun shone down on the grains, quickly attracting a flurry of chirping birds. Then, from a crack in the wall, the little bird burst forth in song, finally ready to perform its first act of kindness on earth—and in heaven, everyone knew exactly who that bird was.

Winter was very harsh; the ponds froze over, and there was hardly any food for the animals or the birds. Our little bird ventured onto the roads, scavenging for grains of corn in the sled ruts and picking up crumbs at rest stops. It ate only a few but shared its findings with other hungry birds and sparrows, making sure they were fed too. Throughout the winter, the bird collected many crumbs and fed them to its feathery companions until they weighed as much as the loaf Inge had stepped on to keep her shoes clean; and when the last crumb was given away, the gray wings of the bird turned white, preparing for flight.

"Look, there's a seagull!" shouted the children as they spotted the white bird diving into the sea and rising back into the sunlight, white and sparkling. Yet no one could say where it went after that, although some claimed it flew straight to the sun.

title: Snow-White and Rose-Red
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a poor widow who lived in a secluded cottage.

In front of her cottage, there was a garden with two rose bushes.

One bush had white roses and the other had red roses. She had two daughters,

who were just like the two rose bushes, one named Snow-White and the other Rose-Red.

They were good and cheerful, as busy as two children could be, but Snow-White was a bit more gentle and reserved than Rose-Red.

Rose-Red loved to run around the meadows and fields collecting flowers and chasing butterflies, whereas Snow-White stayed at home.

She helped their mother with chores or read to her when there was nothing else to do.

The two girls were so close that they always held hands when they went out, and when Snow-White said, "We won’t leave each other," Rose-Red replied, "Not ever, as long as we live." Their mother would add, "Whatever one has, the other must share."

They often wandered the forest together, picking red berries, and no animals harmed them; they trusted the girls.

A little hare would nibble a cabbage leaf from their hands, a roe grazed nearby, a stag hopped merrily by them, and the birds perched on the branches without fear.

Nothing bad ever happened to them, and if they stayed out too late in the forest and darkness fell, they would lie down on the moss and sleep until morning, which made their mother worry less.

One morning, after spending the night in the woods, they woke to see a beautiful child in a shining white dress sitting near them.

He got up and looked kindly at them without saying a word, then walked into the forest.

When they looked around, they realized they had been sleeping right next to a cliff and could have fallen into it if they had wandered a bit further in the dark.

Their mother told them that it must have been an angel who kept them safe.

Snow-White and Rose-Red kept their mother's little cottage so tidy that it was a joy to look inside.

In summer, Rose-Red took care of the home, preparing a wreath of flowers each morning for their mother to find by her bed, which included a rose from each bush.

title: Snow-White and Rose-Red
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
In winter, Snow-White would light the fire and hang the kettle on the stove.

The kettle was made of brass, polished to a shine like gold.

In the evening, when the snowflakes fell, their mother would say, "Go, Snow-White, and bolt the door," and they’d sit around the hearth while she read aloud from a large book.

The girls listened attentively as they spun, a lamb resting on the floor nearby and behind them a white dove nestled with its head tucked under its wings.

One evening, as they sat comfortably together, someone knocked at the door, as if they wanted to come in.

Their mother said, "Quick, Rose-Red, open the door; it must be a traveler looking for shelter."

Rose-Red opened the door, thinking it would be a poor man, but it was a bear, who put his broad, black head inside.

Rose-Red screamed and jumped back, the lamb bleated, the dove fluttered, and Snow-White hid behind their mother's bed.

But the bear spoke softly, saying, "Don't be afraid, I won't harm you. I'm just half-frozen and only want to warm myself."

"Poor bear," their mother said, "lie down by the fire, but be sure not to burn your fur."

Then she called, "Snow-White, Rose-Red, come out; the bear means no harm, he’s friendly."

The girls came out gradually, and soon the lamb and dove approached without fear.

The bear said, "Come here, kids, and help knock the snow off my fur."

They brought a broom and swept the bear's coat clean, and he stretched out by the fire, growling happily.

Before long, they felt at ease and started playing tricks on their clumsy guest.

They tugged at his fur, put their feet on his back, rolled him around, and tapped him playfully with a stick, laughing whenever he growled.

But the bear took it all in stride; he only said, "Just don't be too rough, kids."

title: Snow-White and Rose-Red
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When bedtime came, their mother told the bear he could stay by the hearth to stay warm, safe from the cold.

As dawn broke, the children let him out, and he trotted away.

From then on, the bear came every evening at the same time, lying down by the hearth, allowing the girls to play with him as much as they liked.

They became so comfortable with him that they never locked the door until their black friend had arrived.

When spring came and everything outside turned green, one morning the bear told Snow-White, "I must leave now; I can't return until summer is over."

"Where are you going, dear bear?" Snow-White asked.

"I need to go to the forest and protect my treasures from wicked dwarfs.

During winter, when the ground is frozen hard, they can't get out, but now that the sun has warmed the earth, they break through and come out to steal.

Once they have something in their possession, they don’t let go.

Snow-White was sad to see him go, and as she opened the door for him, he hurried away and got caught on the bolt, tearing a piece of his fur.

Snow-White thought she spotted something shiny like gold beneath the fur, but she wasn’t sure.

The bear dashed off quickly, disappearing behind the trees.

A little while later, their mother sent the girls to the forest to collect firewood.

They came across a big tree that had fallen, and next to it, something was hopping back and forth in the grass, but they couldn't tell what it was.

As they got closer, they saw a dwarf with a wrinkled face and a long white beard caught in the tree's crevice.

He was jumping around like a dog on a leash, and looked up at the girls with fiery red eyes.

"Why are you just standing there? Come here and help me!" he shouted.

"What are you doing, little man?" Rose-Red asked.

"You foolish, nosy goose!" the dwarf replied. "I was trying to split this tree for a bit of firewood.

title: Snow-White and Rose-Red
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
We’ve got no food left for cooking because we can’t burn heavy logs.

I had safely driven the wedge in, but it slipped out, and the tree fell back on me.

Now my beautiful white beard is stuck, and I can’t get free, while you silly smooth-faced ones laugh at me! Ugh! You guys are dreadful!"

The girls tried their best, but they couldn't pull the beard out; it was stuck too tight.

"I'll run and get help!" Rose-Red said.

"You thoughtless goose!" the dwarf snapped. "Why would you need to fetch someone? You're already too many for me. Can’t you think of something better?"

"Hold on!" Snow-White said. "I can help you!" She pulled out her scissors and snipped the end of his beard.

As soon as the dwarf felt free, he grabbed a bag from among the tree roots filled with gold, grumbling, "Unrefined folks! Cutting off a piece of my fine beard—bad luck on you!" He threw the bag over his shoulder and left without even looking back.

Some time later, Snow-White and Rose-Red went fishing.

As they approached the brook, they spotted something large and grasshopper-like jumping toward the water.

They rushed over and found the dwarf again.

"Where are you going?" Rose-Red asked. "You’re not trying to jump in the water, are you?"

"Of course not, you simpleton!" shouted the dwarf. "Can't you see the cursed fish is trying to pull me in?"

He had been fishing when the wind tangled his beard in the line.

Just then, a big fish bit the hook, and the little guy didn't have the strength to pull it out, being dragged towards the water.

He clung to the reeds, but it was no use; he was in danger of being pulled in.

The girls arrived just in time, holding him tightly as they tried to untangle his beard from the line, but it was hopeless.

title: Snow-White and Rose-Red
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
They had to get the scissors out again and cut through the line, losing a part of the beard.

When the dwarf saw what had happened, he yelled, "How rude! Who told you to ruin a man's face? Wasn’t it enough to cut the end of my beard? Now you've taken the best part! I can't show myself to my people anymore!"

He took out a sack full of pearls he had hidden in the reeds and without another word, he stormed off.

Soon after, their mother sent the girls to town to buy needles, thread, ribbons, and laces.

The road took them over a heath covered in large stones.

There, they spotted a big bird hovering in the air, circling slowly above them.

It came down lower and finally landed near some rocks.

Suddenly, they heard a loud, pitiful cry. They rushed over and were horrified to see that the eagle had captured the dwarf.

The girls, filled with sympathy, grabbed the little man and pulled against the eagle until he finally let go.

As soon as the dwarf recovered from his shock, he squeaked, "Couldn't you have been more careful? You pulled my coat so hard that it’s torn and full of holes, you clumsy girls!"

He picked up a sack full of precious stones and scurried back into his hole under the rock.

By this point, the girls were used to his ungratefulness and continued on their way.

On their way home, they passed over the heath again and spotted the dwarf, who had dumped out his pouch of sparkling jewels in a clear spot.

He must not have thought anyone would come by at that time.

The evening sun cast a glow on the brilliant gems.

They sparkled beautifully in all colors, leaving the girls transfixed.

"Why are you just standing there staring?" cried the dwarf, his gray face turning red with rage as he noticed them.

He was still grumbling when a loud growl echoed, and a black bear emerged from the forest.

The dwarf jumped up in fright but couldn't reach his cave as the bear was closing in.

In a desperate tone, he begged, "Please, Mr. Bear, spare me! I'll give you all my treasures, look at these beautiful jewels! Take them instead! What do you want with someone as small as me? You wouldn’t even feel me between your teeth. Just take these two innocent girls; they’re plump and juicy! Please show them mercy!"

The bear ignored his pleas and swatted the wicked little creature, silencing him for good.

The girls had fled, but the bear called out, "Snow-White and Rose-Red, don't be frightened. Wait for me; I'll come with you!"

Recognizing his voice, they paused, and when he caught up, his bear skin fell away to reveal a handsome man dressed in gold.

"I’m a prince, he said, "and I was cursed by that wicked dwarf who stole my treasures from me.

I’ve had to roam the forest as a bear until I was freed by his defeat.

Now he’s justly punished for his actions."

Snow-White married him, and Rose-Red married his brother, and together they shared the great wealth the dwarf had hoarded in his cave.

Their mother lived peacefully and happily with her daughters for many years.

She brought the two rose bushes with her, and they bloomed beautifully with white and red roses every year.

title: The Devil with the Three Golden Hairs
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a poor woman who gave birth to a little boy.

As he entered the world with a caul, it was foretold

that when he turned fourteen, he would marry the king's daughter.

Not long after, the king visited the village,

and no one recognized him,

so when he asked the villagers if there was any news, they replied,

"A child has just been born with a caul; traditionally, anyone born this way will succeed at whatever they attempt. It’s also prophesied that,

when he turns fourteen, he will marry the king's daughter."

The king, who had a wicked heart and was furious about the prophecy,

approached the child's parents and pretended to be friendly. He said, "You poor folks, let me take care of your child. I'll provide for him."

At first, they refused, but when the stranger offered them a significant amount of gold and they realized the child was destined for greatness, they eventually agreed and handed him over.

The king placed the boy in a box and rode away until he reached a deep body of water. Then he tossed the box in, thinking,

"I've rid my daughter of her unwanted suitor."

But instead of sinking, the box floated like a boat, and not a single drop of water entered it.

It drifted all the way to within two miles of the king's main city, stopping at a mill.

A miller's boy, lucky enough to be there, spotted it and hooked it out, believing he had stumbled upon treasure. But when he opened it, he found a lively, healthy boy.

He took the child to the miller and his wife, who had no kids of their own, and they rejoiced, saying, "God has given us a son!"

They cared for the foundling, raising him with kindness.

One day during a storm, the king visited the mill and asked the millers if the tall youth was their son. "No," they replied. "He’s a foundling. Fourteen years ago, he floated down to the mill-dam in a box, and the mill-boy pulled him out."

title: The Devil with the Three Golden Hairs
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Then the king realized it was the very child of good fortune he had thrown into the water. He said, "Good people, could this youth deliver a letter to the queen? I’ll reward him with two gold coins."

"As you wish," they replied, and they instructed the boy to be ready.

The king then wrote a letter to the queen that said, "As soon as the boy arrives with this letter, let him be killed and buried, and it must all be done before I return."

The boy set off with the letter but got lost. As night fell, he entered a large forest. There, he spotted a small light and headed toward it, finding a cottage.

Inside, an old woman sat by the fire alone. She looked startled when she saw the boy and asked, "Where do you come from, and where are you headed?"

"I come from the mill," he answered, "and I need to deliver a letter to the queen. But I got lost in the forest and would like to stay here for the night."

"Oh, you poor boy," the woman said. "You’ve stumbled into a den of thieves, and when they come home, they will kill you."

"Let them come," said the boy confidently. "I’m not afraid, but I’m so tired I can't go any further." He then lay down on a bench and fell asleep.

Shortly after, the robbers returned, angrily demanding to know what strange boy lay there.

"Ah," said the old woman, "this is just an innocent child who got lost in the forest. Out of pity, I let him in; he has a letter for the queen."

The robbers opened the letter and read it. It said that as soon as the boy arrived, he should be put to death.

Moved by compassion, the ruthless robbers felt sorry for him. The leader tore up the letter and penned a new one, stating that as soon as the boy arrived, he should marry the king's daughter.

title: The Devil with the Three Golden Hairs
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
They let him sleep peacefully on the bench until the morning, and when he woke, they handed him the letter and pointed him in the right direction.

When the queen received the letter and read it, she followed its instructions and organized an extravagant wedding feast.

The king's daughter married the child of good fortune, and since he was handsome and kind, they lived together happily.

Eventually, the king returned to his palace and saw that the prophecy had come true; his daughter was married.

"How did this happen?" he asked, frustrated. "I ordered something completely different!"

The queen showed him the letter and explained that he could see for himself what was written.

The king read the letter and realized it had been swapped with another. He questioned the youth about the original letter.
