"I don’t know anything about it," the boy replied. "It must have changed while I was asleep in the forest."

Furious, the king declared, "You won't have it all your way. Whoever marries my daughter must bring me three golden hairs from the devil's head. Fetch me what I want, and you can keep my daughter!"

The king hoped this task would free him from the boy once and for all.

But the boy of good fortune replied confidently, "I will get those golden hairs. I’m not afraid of the devil." He then took his leave.

His journey took him to a large town, where the gatekeeper asked him about his trade and knowledge. "I know everything," the boy replied.

"Then you can help us," said the gatekeeper. "Tell us why our market fountain, which used to flow with wine, is now dry and gives no water."

"You’ll find out later," he answered, continuing on his way.

He soon reached another town, where the gatekeeper also inquired about his trade. "I know everything," he said again.

"Then you can help us, too," said the gatekeeper. "Can you tell us why a tree in our town that once produced golden apples doesn’t even have leaves now?"

title: The Devil with the Three Golden Hairs
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
"You’ll find out later," he replied, moving onward.

Next, he approached a wide river he needed to cross. The ferryman asked him about his trade and knowledge.

"I know everything," he told the ferryman. "Then you can help me," said the ferryman. "Tell me why I always have to row backward and forward and never get set free."

"You’ll find out later," he answered, crossing the water.

Reaching the entrance to hell, he found it dark and smoky. The devil wasn't home, but his grandmother sat in a big armchair.

She asked, "What do you want?" though she didn't look very menacing.

"I need three golden hairs from the devil's head; otherwise, I can't keep my wife," he replied.

"That’s quite a big request," she said. "If the devil finds you here, it will cost you your life. But since I feel sorry for you, I’ll see what I can do."

She transformed him into an ant and said, "Crawl between the folds of my dress; you'll be safe there."

"That works fine for now," he said, "but I still want to know three things – why the fountain that once flowed with wine is now dry, why the tree that once bore golden apples no longer has leaves, and why the ferryman always has to row back and forth and can never be free."

"Those are tricky questions," she answered, "but just stay quiet and listen closely to what the devil says when I pull out a hair."

As evening fell, the devil returned home. As soon as he entered, he sensed something was amiss. "I smell human flesh; something isn’t right here!"

He searched every corner, but couldn’t find anyone. His grandmother scolded him, saying, "It’s just been cleaned and organized; don’t mess it up again!"

"You always think you smell flesh!"

After eating and drinking, he grew tired and laid his head in his grandmother's lap, asking her to pick at his hair.

title: The Devil with the Three Golden Hairs
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Soon, he fell asleep, snoring heavily. The old woman took hold of a golden hair and pulled it out, placing it beside her.

"What are you doing?" the devil exclaimed.

"I had a bad dream," the grandmother explained. "I tugged on your hair because of it."

"What did you dream?" the devil asked.

"I dreamed of a fountain in a marketplace that once flowed with wine, but now it’s dry. What causes this?"

"Oh, if only they knew!" the devil replied. "There's a toad under a stone in the well; if they kill it, the wine will flow again!"

The grandmother took care of him until he drifted off again, snoring loudly. Then she pulled out the second golden hair.

"What are you doing this time?" the devil yelled.

"Don’t be upset; I did it because of another dream," she said.

"What was it about this time?" he asked.

"I dreamed of a tree that used to bear golden apples but now doesn’t even have leaves. What’s wrong with it?"

"Oh, if they only knew!" the devil said. "A mouse is gnawing at the roots; if they kill it, the tree will bear golden apples again. But I’ve had enough of your dreams! If you wake me again, I’ll give you a slap!"

The grandmother soothed him as she picked at him until he fell asleep again. Then she pulled the third golden hair.

"What are you doing?" the devil bellowed.

"What’s the matter with dreams, after all?" he asked, genuinely curious.

"I dreamed of a ferryman who complained that he had to always row from one side to the other without being released. What causes this?"

"Ah, that fool!" the devil muttered. "Whenever someone wants to cross, they must put the oar in his hand, and then he’ll be free!"

Once the grandmother had plucked out the three golden hairs and answered the three questions, she let the devil be and he slept on.

title: The Devil with the Three Golden Hairs
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
When the devil left again, the kind old woman took the ant from her dress and returned the boy of good fortune to his human form.

"Here are the three golden hairs for you," she said. "I trust you heard what the devil said to your three questions."

"Yes," he replied, "I listened closely and will remember."

"You have what you came for, so you can head on your way now," she said.

He thanked the old woman for her help and left hell feeling fortunate that everything had worked out so well.

When he reached the ferryman, he was expected to deliver the promised answer.

"Ferry me across first," said the boy of good fortune, "and then I’ll tell you how you can finally be free." Once they reached the other side, he shared the devil's advice.

"Next time someone needs to cross, just give them the oar and set them free!"

He moved on and came to the town with the unfruitful tree, where again, the watchman sought an answer. He told the watchman what the devil revealed.

"Kill the mouse gnawing at its roots, and it will produce golden apples once more." The watchman was grateful, rewarding him with two donkeys full of gold.

Finally, he arrived at the place where the well had run dry. He shared the devil's message: "There’s a toad in the well under a stone; find it and kill it, and the well will flow with wine again." The watchman thanked him, giving another two donkeys laden with gold.

At last, the boy of good fortune returned home to his wife, who was overjoyed to see him and learned of his success.

He presented the three golden hairs to the king, and when the king saw the four donkeys filled with gold, he was pleased and declared, "Now all the conditions are met, and you can keep my daughter!"

"Dear son-in-law, where did all this gold come from? This is incredible wealth!" the king exclaimed.

"I crossed a river and got it there; it’s on the shore instead of sand," he answered.

"Can I go fetch some, too?" asked the king, eager.

"As much as you want," the boy replied. "There’s a ferryman by the river; let him take you over, and you can fill your bags on the other side."

The greedy king hurried off, and when he reached the river, he called to the ferryman to take him across. The ferryman came and instructed him to get in.

As they reached the other side, he handed the oar to the king and jumped out. From that moment on, the king had to be the ferryman as a punishment for his greed.

Perhaps he’s still ferrying today. If he is, it’s because no one has taken the oar from him.

title: The Good Bargain
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once, there was a peasant who took his cow to the fair and sold her.

He got seven talers for her. On his way home, he had to pass by a pond.

From a distance, he could hear the frogs croaking, 'aik, aik, aik, aik.'

He thought to himself, they're babbling without making any sense.

I received seven talers, not eight!

When he reached the water, he shouted at them, 'You silly creatures! Don't you know better than that? It's seven thalers, not eight!'

But the frogs continued their 'aik, aik, aik' without a care.

'Fine! If you don’t believe me, I’ll count it for you!' He took out his money.

He counted out the seven talers, always calculating four and twenty groschen to a taler.

The frogs, however, ignored his counting and kept croaking, 'aik, aik, aik, aik.'

'What?' the peasant shouted, really annoyed. 'If you think you know better, count it yourselves!' And he tossed all the coins into the water.

He stood still, wanting to wait until they returned what belonged to him, but the frogs just kept insisting with their 'aik, aik, aik, aik.'

And they didn’t return the money at all.

After waiting a long time, evening came, and he had to head home.

He yelled at the frogs, 'You splashing water creatures! You thick-headed, goggle-eyed fools! Your big mouths can croak until they hurt my ears, but you can’t count up to seven talers.'

'Do you think I’m going to stand here until you figure it out?' And off he went.

The frogs continued croaking 'aik, aik, aik, aik' as he walked home, feeling very annoyed.

Later, he bought another cow, which he slaughtered, and he thought that if he sold the meat well, he might make as much as both cows were worth, and even keep the hide.

So when he arrived in the town with the meat, a pack of dogs had gathered at the gate, led by a large greyhound.

title: The Good Bargain
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The greyhound jumped at the meat, sniffed, and barked, 'wow, wow, wow.'

Realizing there was no stopping him, the peasant replied, 'Yes, I know you want some meat, but I’d be foolish to give it to you.'

The dog, though, just kept barking 'wow, wow.'

'Will you promise not to eat it all, and will you vouch for your friends?' asked the peasant.

'Wow, wow, wow,' said the dog.

'Well, in that case, I’ll leave it for you. I know you and your master, but listen, I need my payment in three days, or else you’ll have trouble. Just bring it to me!'

With that, he unloaded the meat and turned to head back.

The dogs devoured it and barked loudly, 'wow, wow.'

The countryman, hearing them from afar, thought, 'Oh no, they all want some, but the big one is responsible for it.'

After three days, he thought to himself, 'Tonight, I’ll have my money!' and felt really happy.

But no one showed up to pay him.

'You can’t trust anyone these days,' he grumbled.

Finally, he lost patience and went into town to see the butcher and demanded his payment.

The butcher thought it was a joke, but the peasant insisted, 'Enough joking; I want my money! Didn’t the big dog bring you the entire slaughtered cow three days ago?'

The butcher grew angry and snatched a broomstick, kicking him out.

'Wait!' the peasant said. 'There’s still some justice in the world!' He went to the royal palace to ask for an audience.

He was brought before the king, who was sitting there with his daughter, and asked what wrong had been done to him.

'Alas,' the peasant said, 'the frogs and the dogs have taken what is mine, and the butcher paid me with a stick.' He explained everything that had happened.

The king's daughter laughed heartily, and the king said, 'I can’t give you justice in this matter, but you shall marry my daughter instead.

title: The Good Bargain
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
'In her whole life, she has never laughed like this before, and I’ve promised her to the one who can make her laugh.'

'You can thank your lucky stars for this fortune.

'Oh,' replied the peasant, 'I don't want her at all. I already have a wife, and one is more than enough for me. When I come home, it's like I have a wife in every corner!'

The king got angry and said, 'You're a rude guy.'

'What do you expect from an ox but beef?' the peasant replied.

'Alright,' the king said, 'you’ll receive another reward instead. Go now, but come back in three days, and I’ll have five hundred counted out for you.'

As the peasant walked out through the gate, the sentry said, 'You made the king’s daughter laugh, so you’re bound to get something good.'

'Yes, that’s what I’m expecting,' answered the peasant, 'I’m supposed to get five hundred.'

'Hey,' said the soldier, 'give me some of that money! What do you need all that for?'

'Since it's you,' said the peasant, 'you can have two hundred. Just show up before the king in three days, and they’ll pay you.'

A Jew who was nearby and heard their conversation rushed after the peasant, grabbed his coat, and said, 'Oh, wonderful! You’re quite the lucky one! I’ll exchange your money for smaller coins, what do you want with those big talers?'

'Jew,' said the peasant, 'you can still take three hundred. Just give me that in coins right now. In three days, you’ll be paid by the king.'

The Jew was thrilled with his small profit and brought back the sum in poor groschen, three of which equaled only two good ones.

After three days, as ordered by the king, the peasant went before the king.

'Take off his coat!' the king commanded. 'Give him his five hundred.'

title: The Good Bargain
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
'Ah,' said the peasant, 'they no longer belong to me. I gave two hundred to the guard, and the Jew exchanged three hundred for me, so I actually don’t have anything left.'

Meanwhile, the soldier and the Jew showed up, demanding what they had earned from the peasant, and they received the punishment counted out to them.

The soldier took it patiently, he was already used to it, but the Jew said sorrowfully, 'Oh dear, are these the heavy talers?'

The king couldn’t help but chuckle at the peasant, and when all the anger wore off, he said, 'Since you’ve already lost your reward before you even got it, I’ll give you compensation.'

'Go into my treasury and take some money for yourself, as much as you want.'

The peasant wasted no time, stuffing his pockets with anything that would fit.

Afterwards, he went to an inn to count his money.

The Jew had snuck after him and heard him murmur, 'That trickster of a king has cheated me. Why couldn’t he just give me the money himself?  Now, I have no idea if what I stuffed in my pockets is right or not.'

'Goodness,' thought the Jew, 'he’s talking disrespectfully about our lord the king. I’ll run and tell on him, and I’ll get a reward!'

When the king learned of the peasant’s words, he got furious and ordered the Jew to bring the offender to him.

The Jew hurried to the peasant, saying, 'You need to go see the king right now, wearing the very clothes you have on!'

'I know what’s right better than that,' the peasant replied. 'I need to get a new coat first! Do you think a man with so much money in his pocket should go see the king in rags?'

The Jew realized the peasant wouldn’t budge without a new coat and worried that if the king cooled off, he’d lose his reward and the peasant would escape punishment.

'Out of pure kindness, I’ll lend you a coat for a short while,' he said. Whatever people do for love.

The peasant gladly accepted, put on the Jew’s coat, and went off with him.

The king reprimanded the peasant for the disrespectful things the Jew had reported.

'Oh, what a Jew says is always false,' said the peasant, 'no truth ever comes from his mouth. That rascal is capable of claiming I’m wearing his coat!'

'What’s that?' the Jew shouted, 'is the coat not mine? Didn’t I lend it to you just to look good for the king?'

When the king heard that, he said, 'The Jew has surely deceived one or the other of us, either myself or the peasant.'

He ordered some hard talers to be counted out to the peasant.

The peasant, however, went home in the fine coat, with nice money in his pocket, and thought to himself, 'This time I got lucky!'

title: The Hut in the Forest
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
A poor woodcutter lived with his wife and three daughters in

a small hut at the edge of a lonely forest. One morning, just as he

was getting ready for work, he told his wife, "Let our oldest daughter bring me my lunch into the forest, or I won't be able to finish my tasks."

To help her find her way, he added, "I’ll take a bag of millet with me and spread the seeds along the path."

As the sun was rising high in the sky, the girl set off with a bowl of soup, but the field sparrows, wood sparrows,

larks, finches, blackbirds, and siskins had snatched up the millet before she even got there, and the girl couldn’t find her way.

Hoping for luck, she kept walking until the sun went down and night began to settle in. The trees rustled in the dark, the

owls hooted, and she started to feel scared. Then, in the distance, she saw a light flickering between the trees. "There must be people living there who can help me," she thought, and headed towards the light.

Before long, she arrived at a house where all the windows were glowing. She knocked, and a gruff voice from inside called out, "Come in!"

The girl stepped into the dark entrance and knocked on the door of the room. "Come in," shouted the voice, and when she opened the door, she saw an old man with gray hair sitting at the table, his chin resting in his hands, and his long white beard nearly touching the ground.

Next to the stove lay three animals: a hen, a rooster, and a brindled cow. The girl told her story to the old man and asked for shelter that night.

The animals replied, "Duks," which must have meant they were okay with her staying, for the old man said, "You can stay here and have food; go to the fire and prepare us some dinner."

In the kitchen, she found plenty of everything and cooked a hearty meal, but she didn’t think about the animals. She brought the full bowl to the table, sat down next to the old man, and ate until she was full.

title: The Hut in the Forest
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
After enjoying her meal, she said, "Now I’m tired. Where can I lie down and get some sleep?" The animals replied, "You didn’t think about us, so figure out for yourself where you can sleep.

Then the old man said, "Just go upstairs; there’s a room with two beds. Fluff them up and put fresh sheets on them, and I’ll come up soon to sleep too."

The girl went upstairs, and after she made the beds with clean linens, she climbed into one without waiting for the old man.

After a while, the old man entered, held his candle over the sleeping girl, and shook his head. When he saw she was fast asleep, he opened a trapdoor and let her fall through.

Later that night, the woodcutter came home and blamed his wife for letting him starve all day. "It’s not my fault," she replied, "The girl went out with your lunch and must have gotten lost, but she’ll come back tomorrow."

The woodcutter, however, woke up before dawn to head back into the forest and asked that their second daughter take him his lunch that day.

"I’ll bring a bag of lentils," he said. "They’re larger than millet, so she’ll see them better and won’t get lost."

At lunchtime, the girl took the food, but the lentils had vanished. The birds of the forest had gobbled them up, just like the day before, and left nothing behind.

The girl wandered around in the forest until night fell, and eventually, she also found her way to the old man’s house.

She was again invited in and asked for food and a place to sleep. The old man with the white beard asked the animals again.

The animals replied, "Duks," and everything went just like the day before. The girl cooked a nice meal, ate with the old man, but didn't think of the animals.

When she asked about her bed, they answered, "You didn’t think of us, so you’ll have to find a place to sleep yourself."

title: The Hut in the Forest
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
As she slept, the old man checked on her, shook his head, and let her down into the cellar.

On the third morning, the woodcutter said to his wife, "Send our youngest daughter with my lunch today; she’s always been good and obedient, and she’ll stay on the right path and not roam around like her sisters, those wild bumblebees."

The mother was reluctant, saying, "Do I have to lose my dearest child too?"

"Don’t worry," he replied, "The girl won’t stray. She’s too sensible and careful. Plus, I’ll take some peas with me; they are larger than lentils and will guide her way."

However, when the girl left with her basket, the wood pigeons had already eaten all the peas, and she didn’t know which way to go.

She was filled with sorrow and kept thinking about how hungry her father would be and how sad her mother would be if she didn’t come home.

Finally, when darkness fell, she spotted the light and arrived at the house in the forest.

She sweetly begged to spend the night there, and the man with the white beard said they would let her.

The girl then went to the stove where the animals were lying, and she lovingly petted the rooster and hen, stroking their smooth feathers, and gently caressing the brindled cow's horns.

After following the old man’s instructions to prepare a nice soup, she set the bowl on the table and said, "Should I eat as much as I want while the animals get nothing? Outside, there’s plenty of food. I’ll take care of them first."

So she went outside, fetched some barley, and cooked it for the rooster and hen, and gathered an armful of sweet-smelling hay for the cow. "I hope you like it, dear animals," she said, "and I’ll get you some refreshing water if you’re thirsty."

Then she filled a bucket with water, and the rooster and hen hopped onto the edge to drink, while the brindled cow took a hearty sip.

title: The Hut in the Forest
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once the animals were fed, the girl sat at the table with the old man and ate what was left.

Before long, the rooster and hen began tucking their heads under their wings, and the cow's eyes started to blink.

Then the girl said, "Shouldn’t we go to bed?""You have had kind thoughts for all of us," the animals replied.

So the girl went upstairs, fluffed the featherbeds, and put clean sheets on them. After she finished, the old man came up and lay down in one bed, his long beard touching the floor.

The girl lay down in the other bed, said her prayers, and slept soundly until midnight, when a loud noise woke her up.

There was cracking and splitting sounds coming from every corner, doors slammed open and banged against the walls. The beams groaned like they were being torn out of their joints; it felt like the staircase was collapsing, and finally, there was a crash as if the whole roof had fallen down.

However, once everything calmed down, and since the girl was unharmed, she relaxed and quickly fell asleep again. But in the morning, when she woke up to the bright sunlight, what did she see? She was lying in a grand hall, everything around her glimmering with royal beauty.

Golden flowers decorated the walls against a backdrop of green silk; the bed was made of ivory with a red velvet canopy, and on a nearby chair sat a pair of pearl-embroidered slippers.

The girl thought she must be dreaming. Then three elegantly dressed attendants entered and asked what she would like to do.

"If you could let me get up, I’d like to prepare some soup for the old man first, and then I'll feed the lovely hen, the charming rooster, and the sweet brindled cow."

She assumed the old man was already awake and glanced over to his bed. But instead of him, a young and handsome man lay there.

As she stared at him, realizing he was not the old man, he opened his eyes, sat up in bed, and said, "I’m a prince, cursed by a wicked witch to live in this forest as an old gray-haired man.

Only my three companions, a rooster, a hen, and a brindled cow, were allowed to be with me. The spell could only be broken when a girl with a truly good heart came along, one who showed love not just to people, but also to animals — and that’s you. Thanks to your kindness, we were freed at midnight, and the old hut in the forest has transformed back into my royal palace."

Once they had gotten up, the prince commanded his attendants to go and bring the girl’s parents to the wedding feast. But then the girl asked, "What about my two sisters?"

"I’ve locked them in the cellar," he replied, "and tomorrow they will be taken to the forest to serve as workers for a charcoal burner until they learn to be kinder and don’t let poor animals go hungry."

title: The King of the Golden Mountain
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a merchant who had two children, a son and a daughter.

They were both young and unable to walk. Two of his richly-loaded ships set sail with all his possessions on board.

As he anticipated making a fortune from them, a terrible message arrived: his ships had sunk.

Instead of being a wealthy man, he now found himself poor, with nothing left but a single field outside the town.

To escape his troubles for a bit, he went to the field, walking back and forth to clear his mind.

Suddenly, a tiny black figure appeared beside him and asked why he looked so sad.

The merchant replied, 'If you can help me, I’ll gladly share my troubles.' The little man answered, 'Maybe I can assist you.'

Then the merchant explained that he had lost everything to the sea and was left with only this field.

The dwarf said, 'Don't worry. If you promise to give me the first thing that brushes against your leg when you get home, in twelve years, I’ll make you rich.'

The merchant thought it would be his dog, forgetting about his young son, so he agreed, gave the dwarf a sealed promise, and left.

When he got home, his little boy joyfully grabbed onto his legs from where he was holding onto a bench.

The father was horrified when he remembered his promise and realized what he had agreed to do. Since he still found no money in his chest, he thought the dwarf had just been joking.

A month later, he went to the attic to collect some old tin to sell and discovered a massive pile of money.

Happy again, he made purchases, became an even better merchant than before, and felt grateful to God.

Meanwhile, the boy grew taller and became brighter and cleverer. But as the twelfth year approached, the merchant’s anxiety grew evident on his face.

One day, his son asked what was wrong, but the father wouldn’t say.

However, the boy kept asking until finally the merchant confessed that he had unknowingly promised him to a black dwarf in exchange for wealth, and now he had to give him up after twelve years.

title: The King of the Golden Mountain
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
The son reassured him, 'Don’t worry, Father, everything will be okay. The black man has no power over me.'

The boy had been blessed by the priest, and when the time came, they went together to the field. He made a circle with his father inside it.

When the black dwarf arrived, he asked the father if he had brought what he promised.

The father was silent, but the son spoke up, 'What do you want here?'

The dwarf replied, 'I’m here to talk to your father, not you.'

The son retorted, 'You deceived my father, give back the promise.'

'No,' the dwarf said, 'I won’t give up my rights.'

They argued for quite some time, but finally settled on a plan: the son would sit in a small boat, which would float away from them, while the father pushed it off.

The father reluctantly did this, believing his son would be lost as the boat capsized.

However, the boat floated safely away, carrying the boy until it reached an unfamiliar shore.

He got out and saw a magnificent castle ahead, so he set off towards it. But when he entered, he found it was enchanted.

He searched each room, but they were all empty until he found a last room where a snake was curled up tight.

To his surprise, the snake was actually a cursed princess who was thrilled to see him. 'You’ve come, my rescuer!' she exclaimed. 'I’ve waited twelve years for you to free this kingdom.'

'How can I do that?' he asked.

'Tonight, twelve black men will come, all chained up. They'll ask what you’re doing, but stay silent and let them do as they please. They’ll beat you and stab you, but hold your tongue. At midnight, their power will fade. On the second night, twelve more will arrive, and on the third night, twenty-four will try to cut off your head. But if you can endure it all without speaking, I’ll be free. I’ll come to you with the Water of Life in a bottle.'

title: The King of the Golden Mountain
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
'I’ll gladly set you free,' he promised.

Everything happened exactly as she described. The black men couldn’t force him to say a word, and by the third night, the snake transformed into a beautiful princess, who came to him with the Water of Life.

She embraced him and kissed him, and happiness filled the whole castle. They celebrated their marriage, and he became the king of the golden mountain.

Together, they lived joyfully, and the queen gave birth to a wonderful baby boy.

Eight years passed, and the king began to think about his father, his heart longing to visit him. However, the queen refused to let him go, foreseeing misery for herself.

But he insisted until she finally agreed. Before he left, she gifted him a wishing ring.

'Put this ring on your finger, and you’ll instantly be taken wherever you wish. But promise me you won’t use it to wish me away or to your father,' she cautioned.

He promised, slipped on the ring, and wished himself home, just outside the town where his father lived.

In an instant, he found himself there, heading towards the town. But when he reached the gate, the guards wouldn’t let him in because of his unusual and lavish attire.

So he went to a nearby hill where a shepherd was watching his sheep. They traded clothes, and he put on the shepherd's old coat before entering.

When he saw his father, he revealed his identity. But the father couldn’t believe that this poor shepherd was his son, insisting that his real son had died long ago.

Still, seeing the shepherd was in need, he offered him some food. The shepherd then said, 'I really am your son.'

'Can you recall any marks on my body to recognize me?'

'Yes,' his mother said, 'our son had a raspberry mark under his right arm.'

He pulled up his shirt, revealing the raspberry mark, and they no longer doubted that he was indeed their son.

He then told them about being the king of the golden mountain, how he married a princess, and they had a seven-year-old son.

title: The King of the Golden Mountain
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
'Surely that's not true,' his father said. 'What kind of king wanders around in a ragged shepherd's coat?'

The son, in frustration and forgetting his promise, turned his ring and wished for his wife and child to be with him.

In a flash, they appeared, but the queen was upset and accused him of breaking his word, bringing misfortune upon her.

'I acted without thinking, not out of malice,' he said, trying to soothe her. She pretended to accept his reasoning, but malice brewed in her heart.

He took her to the field where the stream was, pointing out the spot where the little boat had set sail.

'I’m tired. Sit down while I take a nap on your lap,' he said, resting his head on her lap.

She picked off lice until he fell asleep. Once he was out, she quietly took the ring off his finger.

Then she pulled away and left only his slipper behind, taking their child with her, wishing herself back to her kingdom.

When he woke up, he found himself alone, his wife and child vanished, and the ring gone, leaving only a slipper as a reminder.

'I can’t go home now,' he thought, 'they’d call me a wizard. I must walk until I reach my own kingdom.'

So he continued on, eventually arriving at a hill where three giants were arguing about how to divide their inheritance.

When they noticed him passing by, they called out, saying they needed a clever little man to decide how to split it.

The inheritance included a sword that could chop off anyone’s head with a command, a cloak that made the wearer invisible, and boots that could take him anywhere instantly.

'Let me check if they’re all in good shape,' he said, wanting to assess the items.

They handed him the cloak, and as soon as he wore it, he turned invisible and transformed into a fly.

After turning back, he said, 'The cloak works well; now let me have the sword.'

'No, we can’t give you that. If you commanded it, all our heads would roll, and you’d be left with yours,' they protested.

title: The King of the Golden Mountain
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Eventually, they agreed to give it to him on the condition that he would only try it on a tree.

When he did, the sword sliced through the tree trunk like it was made of straw.

He wanted the boots as well, but they warned him that if he wished to go somewhere, they would be left stranded.

'I won’t do that,' he assured them, and they gave him the boots too.

Now he had everything he needed but only thought of reuniting with his wife and child. He sighed, 'If only I could be on the golden mountain again.'

In that instant, he vanished from the giants' sight, and the inheritance was settled.

As he neared his palace, he heard joyful music and celebration, only to find out his wife was marrying someone else.

Fury consumed him, and he thought, 'This treacherous woman betrayed me while I was asleep.'

He donned his cloak and entered the palace unseen.

In the dining hall, a lavish feast was spread, with guests eating and chatting, oblivious to his presence.

There she sat in royal attire, enjoying herself. He positioned himself behind her, still unseen.

Whenever she reached for food, he snatched it away and consumed it himself. Each time she filled her glass, he drank from it instead.

Time after time, she tried to help herself, only to find her plate and cup empty.

Embarrassed and humiliated, she finally retreated to her chamber in tears, while he followed.

'Has the devil taken me, or did my hero never arrive?' she lamented. He then confronted her, saying,

'Your hero is here, you traitor. Have I deserved this from you?'

Finally, he made himself visible and stepped into the hall, declaring, 'The wedding is over; the true king is back.'

The gathered kings, princes, and advisors mocked him, but he ignored them and asked, 'Will you leave or not?'

As they tried to seize him, he drew his sword and proclaimed, 'All heads off but mine!' In that moment, their heads rolled, and he alone stood victorious, once again the king of the golden mountain.

title: The Little Peasant
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
In a certain village, everyone was incredibly wealthy, except for one poor peasant known as the little peasant.

He didn’t even own a cow, and had even less money to buy one, yet both he and his wife longed to have one.

One day, he said to her, "I have a great idea! Our friend the carpenter can make us a wooden calf and paint it brown so it looks real. Over time, it will definitely grow into a cow!"

His wife liked the idea, and soon their friend the carpenter began to carve and paint the calf, making sure it looked just right, with its head hanging down.

The next morning, when the cows were being herded out, the little peasant called the cow-herd over and said, "Look, I have this little calf, but it's still small and needs to be carried."

The cow-herd agreed and lifted the calf into his arms, carrying it to the pasture and setting it down among the grass.

The little calf stood there, pretending to eat, and the cow-herd remarked, "It will be running around by itself soon—just look at how it’s eating!"

When it was time to bring the herd home at night, he told the calf, "If you can stand there and eat, you can also walk on your own. I'm not dragging you home!"

But the little peasant stood at his door waiting for his calf, and when the cow-herd returned with the cows, he asked where it was.

The cow-herd replied, "It's still out there eating. It wouldn't stop to come with us."

The little peasant insisted, "Oh no, I have to have my calf back!" So they went back to the meadow together, but the calf was gone.

The cow-herd said, "It must have run away." But the peasant argued, "Don’t tell me that!" and took the cow-herd to see the mayor.

Due to the cow-herd's carelessness, the mayor ordered him to give the peasant a cow for his trouble.

title: The Little Peasant
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
Now the little peasant and his wife finally had the cow they’d longed for, and they were very happy, but they had no food to feed it.

Sadly, the cow soon had to be killed. They salted the meat, and the peasant went to town to sell the skin, hoping to buy a new calf with the money.

On his way, he passed a mill, where he saw a raven with broken wings. Out of pity, he picked it up and wrapped it in the skin.

As a storm rolled in, he couldn’t go any further and turned back to the mill, asking for shelter.

The miller's wife, home alone, said to the peasant, "Lie down on the straw. I can give you a slice of bread and some cheese."

The peasant ate his meal and lay down with the skin beside him, while the woman figured he was tired and had fallen asleep.

Meanwhile, the parson arrived. The miller's wife welcomed him in and said, "My husband is out, so we can have a feast!"

The peasant listened closely, feeling annoyed that he was stuck with just a slice of bread and cheese.

Then the woman began serving a spread of four delicious dishes: roast meat, salad, cakes, and wine.

Just as they were about to sit down for their meal, a loud knock came at the door.

The woman gasped, "Oh no! It's my husband!" Quickly, she hid the roast meat in the tiled stove, the wine under the pillow, the salad on the bed, and the cakes beneath it, while the parson squeezed into the closet.

She opened the door for her husband and said, "Thank goodness you’re back! It's such a terrible storm outside."

The husband noticed the peasant lying on the straw and asked, "What’s that guy doing here?"

"Oh, this poor man came in from the storm and asked for shelter, so I gave him some bread and cheese and showed him the straw," the wife explained.

The husband replied, "I have no problem with that, but hurry and get me something to eat!"

title: The Little Peasant
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
His wife said, "I only have bread and cheese."
"That's fine by me," he said. "Bread and cheese will do. Anyway, come eat with me!"

The peasant didn’t need to be asked twice; he got up and joined them for a meal.

After they finished, the miller saw the skin with the raven inside lying on the ground and asked, "What do you have there?"

The peasant replied, "I have a soothsayer inside it."

Curious, the miller asked, "Can he predict anything for me?"

"Sure!" the peasant answered, "But he only tells four things, keeping the fifth to himself."

Entranced, the miller said, "Let him predict something, then!"

The peasant pinched the raven’s head until it croaked, making a sound like "krr, krr."

The miller asked, "What did he say?"

The peasant replied, "First of all, he says there's some wine hidden under the pillow."

"Good heavens!" shouted the miller, rushing over to find the wine.

"Now what’s next?" he asked.

The peasant made the raven grunt again, saying, "Secondly, he says there's roast meat in the tiled stove."

"Wow!" the miller exclaimed, running over to find the roast meat.

The peasant continued, making the raven croak again, saying, "Thirdly, he says there's salad on the bed."

"That sounds fantastic!" the miller said, hurrying to look for the salad.

Finally, the peasant pinched the raven once more until it croaked and said, "Fourthly, he says there are cakes hidden under the bed."

"That sounds wonderful!" the miller shouted, rushing to find the cakes.

So the two of them sat down to eat together, but the miller's wife was terrified and went to bed, taking all the keys with her.

The miller wanted to know the fifth thing, but the little peasant said, "First, let’s hurry and eat the four meals. The fifth one is bad news."

title: The Little Peasant
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
They began to negotiate how much the miller would pay for the fifth prophecy until they agreed on three hundred talers.

Then, the peasant pinched the raven's head once again until it croaked loudly.

The miller asked, "What did he say?"

The peasant replied, "He says the devil is hiding outside in the closet on the porch."

The miller exclaimed that the devil must come out, and he opened the door.

The woman had no choice but to give up the keys, and the peasant unlocked the closet.

The parson darted out as fast as he could, and the miller proclaimed, "I saw that black rascal myself!"

The little peasant, on the other hand, slipped away by dawn.

Back home, the little peasant started to thrive. He built himself a beautiful house, and all the villagers said, "That little peasant must have gone to the place where golden snow falls and people carry home gold by the shovelful!"

Eventually, he was brought before the mayor and asked where his newfound wealth came from.

He replied, "I sold my cow's skin in town for three hundred talers."

When the other peasants heard that, they decided they wanted the same fortune. They rushed home, killed their cows, and stripped off their skins to sell in town.

However, the mayor declared that his servant would go first.

When she reached the merchant in town, he paid her no more than two talers for her skin, and when the others came, he gave them even less, saying, "What am I supposed to do with all these skins?"

Frustrated, the peasants blamed the little peasant for tricking them and accused him of deceit in front of the mayor.

The innocent little peasant was unanimously sentenced to death; they planned to roll him into the water in a hollow barrel.

He was led away, and a priest was brought in to hold a mass for his soul.

The townsfolk had to stand at a distance, and when the peasant saw the priest, he recognized him as the same man who had been with the miller’s wife.

He said, "I set you free from the closet; now set me free from this barrel!"

title: The Little Peasant
author: Brothers Grimm
text_chunk:
At that moment, the shepherd, who had always wanted to be the mayor, arrived with a flock of sheep and shouted, "No, I won’t do it! Even if everyone else insists, I’m not going to!"

Upon hearing this, the shepherd approached him and asked, "What’s going on? What won’t you do?"

The little peasant explained, "They want to make me mayor if I’ll just get into this barrel, but I won’t do it!"

The shepherd said, "If all it takes is getting into that barrel to be mayor, I would do it right away!"

The peasant responded, "If you get in, you’ll become the mayor."

The shepherd accepted the challenge, climbed in, and the peasant quickly closed the lid.

Then, he took the shepherd's flock for himself and drove them away.

The parson went back to the crowd and announced that the mass had been completed.

So they all brought the barrel to the water.

As the barrel rolled, the shepherd shouted, "I’m willing to be the mayor now!"

They assumed it was the peasant speaking and replied, "That’s exactly what we plan to do; but first you need to take a little look below!" And they rolled the barrel into the water.

With that, the peasants went home, and as they reached the village, the little peasant returned, peacefully driving a flock of sheep and looking quite content.

The villagers were astonished and asked, "Where did you come from? Did you just come out of the water?"

He replied, "Yes, indeed! I sank deep into the water until I reached the bottom, pushed out the barrel’s bottom, and crawled out, where I found a place with lots of lambs grazing. I brought this flock back with me!"

The villagers asked, "Are there more?" And he responded, "Oh, plenty more than I could ever want!"

The peasant’s listeners decided they would fetch their own flocks, each wanting one of their own, but the mayor insisted, "I’ll go first."

They all went to the water, and just then, they saw some cute fluffy clouds in the blue sky, which looked like little lambs reflected in the water.

The villagers shouted, "We can already see the sheep down there!" The mayor rushed ahead, saying, "I’ll jump in first to check it out, and if it looks promising, I’ll call for you!"

He jumped in with a splash, and it sounded like he was calling for them. The whole crowd jumped in after him like one.

And so, the whole village drowned, leaving the little peasant as the sole heir, suddenly becoming a wealthy man.

title: SHE WAS GOOD FOR NOTHING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The mayor stood by the open window, looking quite dapper. His frilly shirt and crisp collar had a shiny breastpin, and he wore elegant ruffles. He’d shaved his chin smooth, though he’d nicked himself a bit and stuck a piece of newspaper over the cut. "Hey, kid!" he called out.

The boy he was addressing was the son of a struggling washer-woman who happened to be passing by. He paused, taking off his cap politely. The front of his cap was bent, so he could easily roll it up and tuck it in his pocket. Standing before the mayor in his worn but neat clothes and heavy wooden shoes, he looked as humble as if he were in front of royalty.

"You're a polite young man," the mayor remarked. "I suppose your mom is busy washing clothes by the river, and you’re on your way to give her what you’ve got there in your pocket. It’s not great for her, is it? How much do you have?"

"Just half a quartern," the boy replied nervously.

"And she already had the same amount this morning?"

"Two halves make a whole," said the mayor. "She’s no good at all. It’s really unfortunate for people like her. Tell your mom she should be ashamed. Don't you become a drunkard too, though I expect you might. Poor child! Now run along."

The boy walked away with his cap in hand, as the wind tousled his golden hair. He turned the corner into a small lane leading to the river, where his mother was standing in the water by her washing bench, thumping the linen with a heavy wooden bar. The floodgates at the mill had been opened, and as the water rushed by, it tugged at the sheets and nearly tipped the bench over, forcing the washer-woman to lean against it for support. "I almost got swept away!" she exclaimed. "It’s good you’re here; I need something to warm me. It’s freezing in the water, and I’ve been at it for six hours. Did you bring me anything?"

title: SHE WAS GOOD FOR NOTHING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The boy pulled out the bottle from his pocket, and his mother took a sip from it.

"Oh, that warms me up so much!" she said. "It’s just as satisfying as a hot meal but not as pricey. Have a little, my boy; you look so pale and are shivering in those thin clothes. Autumn is definitely here. I hope I don’t get sick, but I can’t worry too much. Give me a bit more, and you can have a tiny sip too—but just a sip; we don’t want you to get used to it, my poor child." She stepped onto the bridge beside the boy as she spoke, dripping water from the straw mat wrapped around her and her gown. "I work hard and endure pain with my hands, but I do it gladly to raise you right, my dear boy."

Just then, a woman, older than the washer-woman, approached them. She looked quite miserable, limping on one leg with a large false curl hanging over her blind eye. The curl was meant to cover the defect but only made it more noticeable. She was a friend of the laundress, known among the neighbors as "Lame Martha, with the curl." "Oh, you poor thing, how hard you work standing in the water!" she exclaimed. "You really need something to keep you warm, and yet cruel people gossip about how many drops you drink." Martha then recounted everything she had overheard from the mayor. She was furious that anyone could speak that way to a mother about the little she took. What upset her even more was the fact that the mayor was hosting a dinner that night where rich wine would be flowing freely. "Many will overindulge, but they don’t call that drinking! They’re all fine, but you’re considered good for nothing!" Martha cried angrily.

"So he talked to you like that, did he, my child?" the washer-woman asked, her lips trembling. "He says your mother is good for nothing. Well, maybe he's right, but he shouldn’t have said it to you. So much has happened to me because of that house!"

title: SHE WAS GOOD FOR NOTHING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Yes," Martha replied, "I remember you worked there when the mayor's parents were alive; it's been so long. So many things have happened since then, and people must certainly be thirsty," she smiled. "The mayor’s dinner party today should have been postponed, but the news came too late. The footman told me the dinner was already cooked when he received a letter stating that the mayor’s younger brother in Copenhagen had died."

"Dead!" the laundress gasped, paling at the news.

"Yes, indeed," Martha said. "But why are you taking it so hard? I assume you knew him when you worked there?"

"Is he really gone?" she exclaimed. "He was such a kindhearted man; there aren't many like him," tears began streaming down her cheeks. "Oh dear, I feel so weak. Everything is spinning; I can’t handle this. Is the bottle empty?" She leaned against a plank for support.

"Oh dear, you’re really not well," said Martha. "Come on, cheer up; it might just pass. No, I see you really need help; I should take you home."

"I’ll handle that. Here, take my arm. The boy can stay here to watch the linen, and I’ll come back to finish the washing; it’s just a little bit left."

The washer-woman trembled, saying, "I’ve been too long in this cold water, and I haven’t eaten since morning. Oh kind Heaven, help me get home; I’m burning up with fever. Oh, my poor child!" She broke down in tears. The boy sat alone by the river, watching the damp linen and weeping for her.

The two women moved slowly. The laundress stumbled through the lane, turning into the street where the mayor lived. As they arrived at the front of his house, she collapsed onto the pavement. A crowd gathered around her, and Lame Martha dashed into the house for help. The mayor and his guests appeared at the window.

title: SHE WAS GOOD FOR NOTHING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"It’s the laundress," he said. "She’s had a bit too much to drink. She’s no good at all. It’s so sad for her sweet little boy. I really like the boy, but his mother is good for nothing."

After a while, the laundress regained her strength, and they helped her back to her rundown home, laying her down in bed. Kind Martha warmed up a mug of beer with butter and sugar—she believed that was the best medicine—and then hurried to the river, cleaning poorly but putting in her best effort. She brought the wet linen to shore and put it in a basket. By evening, she was back in the shabby little room with the laundress. The mayor's cook had given her some roasted potatoes and a generous piece of fat for the sick woman. Martha and the boy enjoyed the meal, though the mother could only comment on how nourishing the aroma was. Soon after, the boy was put to bed next to his mother, sleeping at her feet under an old quilt made of blue and white patches. The laundress was feeling a bit better now; the warm beer had helped, and the scent of good food was comforting.

title: SHE WAS GOOD FOR NOTHING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "Thank you so much, you kind soul," she said to Martha. "Now that the boy is asleep, I can tell you everything. He falls asleep quickly. How sweet and gentle he looks lying there with his eyes shut! He doesn’t know how much I’ve suffered, and I hope he never does. I once worked in service for the mayor's father, the counselor. Back then, I was a wild young girl, but I was honest—that's a promise I can make before Heaven. The youngest son came home, lively and affectionate; every bit of him was good and noble. He was the son of the house, and I was just a maid, but he loved me truly and honorably and told his mother about it. She was like an angel in the world, so wise and kind. Before he set off on his travels, he placed a gold ring on my finger. As soon as he left, my mistress called for me. With great care, she explained the clear differences between us: 'He loves your looks now, but beauty fades. You haven't had the education he has, and you’re not social equals. That's the tragedy. I value the poor,' she added. 'They can be ranked higher than many rich people before God. But on earth, we must avoid false paths, or we risk our futures, like a cart on a dangerous road. There's a good man, a widower named Eric the glovemaker, who wishes to marry you. Will you consider it?' Every word she said struck my heart like a dagger, but I understood she was right, and I felt heavy inside. I kissed her hand, cried bitterly, then wept more when I reached my room and threw myself onto my bed. That night was awful; God knows the pain I endured and the battles I fought. The following Sunday, while at church, I prayed for guidance. Just as I stepped outside, Eric approached me; and I had no more doubts. We suited each other in status and circumstance; he was already doing well financially. I took his hand and asked, 'Do you still have feelings for me?' 'Yes, always,' he replied. 'Will you marry a girl who respects and appreciates you, even if she can’t give you her love right away? That can come later.' 'Yes, it will come,' he assured me, and we joined our hands. I returned to my mistress, and the gold ring he had given me was close to my heart. I could only wear it at night when I went to bed. I kissed the ring until my lips nearly bled and then handed it to my mistress, informing her I’d be announcing our engagement to the glovemaker the following week. She embraced me and kissed me. She didn’t call me 'good for nothing;' likely, I was better back then than I am now, but back then, I was blissfully unaware of the world's misfortunes. We were married at Michaelmas, and the first year was wonderful. We had a journeyman and an apprentice, and you were our housemaid, Martha."

title: SHE WAS GOOD FOR NOTHING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Ah yes, you were such a wonderful mistress," Martha replied, "I’ll always remember how kind you and your husband were to me."

"Those were happy times, even though we didn’t have kids at first. I never saw the student again, but I caught a glimpse of him once; he was so pale and heartbroken at his mother’s funeral. She was the most important person in his life. Later, when his father passed away, he was traveling abroad and didn’t return. I know he never married; I believe he became a lawyer. He likely forgot all about me, and even if we met, he wouldn’t recognize me because I’ve lost my looks, and maybe that’s for the best." She then reflected on the dark times that followed.

"We had five hundred dollars," she recounted. "There was a house for sale for two hundred, so we thought it would be smart to tear it down and build something new in its place; so we bought it. The builder estimated the new house would cost ten hundred and twenty dollars. Eric had good credit and borrowed money from the capital city. But the captain delivering the money was shipwrecked, and we lost it. Just when our sweet boy was born, my husband fell seriously ill. For about three quarters of a year, I had to care for him day and night. We fell behind on our payments, borrowed even more, and lost everything we had. Then my husband died. Since then, I have worked ceaselessly for our child, scrubbing and washing all sorts of linens, but I’ve never managed to get ahead; it’s simply been God’s will. In His own time, He’ll take me to Himself, but I know He’ll never abandon my boy." She then drifted off to sleep. The next morning, she felt rejuvenated and thought she was strong enough to get back to work. But when she stepped into the icy water, a sudden wave of weakness struck her; she gasped for air, took a step forward, and fell. Her head rested on dry land, but her feet were still submerged; the current swept away her wooden shoes, which were held on loosely by a straw tie, and that’s how Martha found her when she came to bring coffee.

title: SHE WAS GOOD FOR NOTHING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Meanwhile, the mayor had sent a messenger to the laundress’s house to summon her, claiming he had something important to share. But it was too late; a surgeon had been dispatched to open a vein in her arm, but sadly, the poor woman had passed on.

"She drank herself to death," the cruel mayor declared. The letter notifying him of his brother’s demise stated that he had left a legacy of six hundred dollars for the glovemaker’s widow—who had also been his mother’s maid—to be distributed carefully, in either large or small sums to her or her child.

"I remember something was between my brother and her," the mayor mused, "it’s actually a relief she’s gone. Now the boy will get everything. I’ll place him with decent people so he can grow up to be a respectable worker." And with those words, the blessing of God seemed to rest upon his plan. The mayor called for the boy, promising to look after him, but cruelly added that his mother’s death was a good thing because "she was good for nothing." They buried her in the churchyard for the poor. Martha sprinkled sand on her grave and planted a rose bush, with the boy standing beside her.

"Oh, my poor mother!" he cried, tears streaming down his cheeks. "Is it true what they say, that she was good for nothing?"

"No, absolutely not," replied the old servant, raising her gaze to the heavens. "She was worth so much more; I knew it long ago, and after her final night, I’m even more convinced. She was a good, worthy woman, and God in heaven knows I’m speaking the truth, even though the world may still say she was good for nothing."

title: THE GOBLIN AND THE HUCKSTER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a typical student who lived in a tiny attic and owned nothing. Below him lived a huckster, who ran a shop on the ground floor. A goblin stayed with the huckster because every Christmas, the huckster had a big dish filled with jam and a huge chunk of butter right in the center. The huckster could afford it, so the goblin cleverly chose to remain with him.

One evening, the student slipped into the shop through the back door to buy some candles and cheese for himself—he didn’t have anyone to send, so he went himself. He got what he needed, and then the huckster and his wife both nodded good evening to him. She was the kind of woman who could say more than just a nod; she often had a lot to share. The student nodded back as he turned to leave but suddenly paused and started reading the piece of paper that had wrapped the cheese. It was a page torn from an old book—a book that definitely shouldn’t have been ripped apart because it was filled with poetry.

"There’s more like it over there," said the huckster. "I traded some coffee beans for it with an old woman. You can have the rest for sixpence, if you’d like."

"I definitely will!" said the student. "Just give me the book instead of the cheese; I can manage my bread and butter without it. Tearing up a book like this is a real sin. You’re a clever guy and practical too, but you don’t know anything about poetry—not more than that barrel over there does."

That was quite a rude thing to say, especially about the barrel, but both the huckster and the student laughed because it was said playfully. However, the goblin felt furious that anyone dared to say such things to a huckster who owned a house and sold the best butter. When night fell, and the shop was closed with everyone tucked in bed except for the student, the goblin quietly slinked into the bedroom where the huckster's wife slept and took her tongue, which she certainly didn’t need at that moment. Whatever object he placed that tongue upon gained the ability to speak, able to share its thoughts and feelings as easily as she could. It could only be used by one object at a time, which was fortunate, as multiple voices at once would have created chaos. The goblin laid the tongue on the barrel, which was filled with old newspapers.

title: THE GOBLIN AND THE HUCKSTER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Is it really true that you don’t know what poetry is?" he asked.

"Of course I know," replied the barrel. "Poetry is just something that always sits in the corner of a newspaper, and sometimes it gets cut out. I can confidently say that I have more poetry contained within me than the student does, and I’m just a simple tub in the huckster's shop."

The goblin then placed the tongue on the coffee grinder, and it really started talking! Then he put it on the butter tub and the cash register; they all voiced the same opinion as the waste-paper barrel, and a majority's opinion must always be respected.

"Now, I’ll go tell the student," said the goblin. With that, he quietly climbed the back stairs to the attic where the student lived. A candle still flickered, and the goblin peeked through the keyhole to see the student engrossed in reading the torn book, which he had taken from the shop. Wow! The room was so bright! From the book emanated a beam of light that spread wide and full, resembling the trunk of a tree with brilliant rays branching out over the student’s head. Each leaf looked fresh, and each flower resembled a lovely female face—some with dark, sparkling eyes and others with bright, clear blue. The fruit shone like stars, filling the room with enchanting music. The little goblin had never imagined, let alone seen or heard, anything that magnificent. He stood on tiptoe, peeking inside until the light faded from the attic. The student must have blown out his candle and gone to bed, but the little goblin stayed there, listening to the lingering music—a sweet lullaby for the student who had fallen asleep.

title: THE GOBLIN AND THE HUCKSTER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"This is a wonderful place," the goblin said to himself. "I never expected anything like this. I’d like to stay with the student," he thought, feeling a bit sensible. Finally, he sighed, "But the student doesn’t have any jam!" So, he headed back downstairs into the huckster's shop. Luckily, he got back just in time, for the barrel had almost drained the lady’s tongue. It was busy telling everything it held on one side and was about to flip over to describe the other side when the goblin entered and restored her tongue. From that point onward, everything in the shop—from the cash register to the pine logs—formed their opinions based on that of the barrel; they respected it so much that when the huckster read reviews about theater and art at night, they assumed it all originated from the barrel.

But after what he had seen, the goblin couldn’t just sit quietly and listen to the discussions downstairs. So, as soon as the evening light peaked into the attic, he mustered his courage. To him, it felt like the rays of light were strong cables pulling him up, compelling him to peek through the keyhole. While he was there, he experienced a feeling of vastness, like standing by an ever-moving sea during a storm, which brought tears to his eyes. He didn’t even know why he was crying, yet there was a sweet feeling mixed with his tears. "How wonderfully glorious it would be to sit with the student under such a tree;" but sadly, that wasn’t possible. He had to be content just to peek through the keyhole and feel grateful for that.

He stood there on the old landing, with the autumn wind blowing through the trap door. It was quite chilly, but the little goblin didn’t really feel it until the light in the attic went out, and the music faded away. That’s when he shivered and hurried back down to his warm corner, where it felt cozy and comforting. When Christmas returned, bringing that dish of jam and a large chunk of butter, he liked the huckster best of all.

One night soon after, the goblin was startled awake by a loud ruckus, knocking against the window shutters and the house doors, along with the sound of the watchman’s horn. A massive fire had broken out, and flames engulfed the whole street. Was it their home or a neighbor’s? No one knew, and panic took hold. The huckster's wife was so flustered that she removed her gold earrings and put them in her pocket, hoping to save something. The huckster rushed to grab his important business papers, and the servant decided to save her blue silk mantle, which she had managed to buy. Each person tried to keep their most cherished belongings. The goblin felt the same urge; in a leap, he sprang upstairs into the student’s room, finding him standing by the open window, calmly observing the fire raging across the street at a neighbor’s house. The goblin quickly gathered the precious book lying on the table and stuffed it into his red cap, holding it tight with both hands. He had saved the greatest treasure in the house, and he dashed up to the roof, settling himself on the chimney. The flames from the burning house across the street illuminated him as he sat there, holding onto his cap with his treasure—at that moment, he discovered the true feelings behind his heart and realized precisely where they pointed. Yet, after the fire was put out and the goblin began to think again, he hesitated and finally said, "I must divide my loyalty between the two; I can’t completely abandon the huckster because of the jam."

And that’s a reflection of human nature—we are all like the goblin, visiting the huckster "because of the jam."

title: THE GOLDEN TREASURE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The drummer's wife walked into the church. She gazed at the new altar, adorned with painted pictures and carved angels. The images on the canvas and above the altar were just as beautiful as the carved figures, beautifully painted and gilded. Their hair shimmered like gold in the sunlight, making them lovely to see; but the sunlight itself was even more stunning. It glowed brighter and clearer through the shadowy trees as the sun began to set. It was wonderful to gaze at the heavenly sunshine. As she admired the red sun, she thought deeply about it, and about the little one that the stork would bring. The drummer's wife felt cheerful, wishing for her child to have a bit of that sunshine, hoping it would grow up to be as radiant as one of the shining angels above the altar.

When she finally held her little child in her arms and lifted it up for its father to see, the baby looked just like one of the angels in the church, with golden hair—shimmering like the setting sun.

"My golden treasure, my riches, my sunshine!" the mother exclaimed, kissing the baby’s shiny hair, and it sounded like music in the drummer's room, filled with joy, life, and energy. The drummer started a joyful roll on his drum—the Fire-drum that was beaten when there was a fire in town:

"Red hair! This little guy has red hair! Trust the drum, not what your mother says! Rub-a-dub, rub-a-dub!"

And the townspeople echoed what the Fire-drum had announced.

The boy was taken to church and christened. There wasn't much to say about his name; he was simply called Peter. The entire town, and the Drum too, referred to him as Peter the drummer's son with the red hair; although his mother lovingly called him her golden treasure.

In the sunken path along the muddy bank, many had carved their names as a keepsake.

"Celebrity is always something!" declared the drummer, as he scribbled his and his son's names there.

The swallows flew by. On their long journey, they had seen more lasting inscriptions on rocks and temple walls in Hindostan—majestic deeds of great kings and names so ancient that no one could read or pronounce them. Quite a celebrity!

title: THE GOLDEN TREASURE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In the muddy bank, the martens built their nests. They bored holes in the deep slope, and the rain and mist washed away the names, including the drummer's and his little son's.

"Peter's name will last a year and a half longer!" the father remarked.

"Fool!" thought the Fire-drum; but it merely said, "Dub, dub, dub, rub-a-dub!"

The drummer's son with the red hair was full of life and joy. He had a lovely voice, singing like a bird in the woods. There was melody, and yet a kind of stillness.

"He must become a choir boy," his mother decided. "He should sing in the church, standing among the beautiful gilded angels who resemble him!"

"Fiery cat!" some witty townsfolk teased.

The Drum overheard this from the neighbors' wives.

"Don’t go home, Peter," the street boys shouted. "If you sleep in the attic, there'll be a fire in the house, and the fire-drum will have to sound!"

"Watch out for the drumsticks," Peter shot back, and even at his small size, he boldly ran up and punched the lead boy squarely, making him lose his balance and tumble over. The others quickly fled.

The town musician was very stylish and refined. He was the son of the royal plate-washer and had a fondness for Peter. He sometimes brought Peter home, giving him a violin and teaching him to play. It seemed the boy had a natural gift for music, wanting to be more than a drummer—he dreamed of being the town’s musician.

"I want to be a soldier," said Peter, still just a little boy. To him, it seemed the most wonderful thing in the world to carry a gun, march one, two—one, two, and wear a uniform and a sword.

"Ah, you’re learning to wish for the drum-skin, drum, dum, dum!" the Drum quipped.

"Yes, if he could just march his way up to become a general!" his father mused. "But before that can happen, there must be a war."

title: THE GOLDEN TREASURE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"We have nothing to lose," remarked the father.

"But what if he comes back as a general!" the father added.

"Without arms and legs!" cried the mother. "No, I’d much rather keep my golden treasure with me."

"Drum, dum, dum!" The Fire-drum and the other drums were sounding off because war had arrived. The soldiers were marching out, and the drummer's son followed them. "Red-head. Golden treasure!"

The mother cried; the father envisioned him as "famous"; while the town musician believed he shouldn’t go to war but stay home and learn music.

"Red-head," the soldiers chuckled, and little Peter laughed too; but when one of them teased another by saying "Foxey," he clenched his teeth and looked away, into the vast world. That nickname didn’t bother him.

The boy was lively, friendly, and good-natured; that's the best canteen, his old comrades said.

Many nights, he slept under the open sky, drenched by the rain or mist, but he never lost his cheerful spirit. The drumsticks played, "Rub-a-dub, rise and shine!" Yes, he was definitely destined to be a drummer.

The day of battle arrived. The sun hadn’t risen yet, but morning had come. The air was chilly, and the battle was fierce; mist filled the air and mingled with gunpowder smoke. Bullets and shells soared over the soldiers, hitting some while they pressed on. Now and then, one would fall to their knees, their heads bleeding and faces pale as chalk. But little Peter kept his healthy color; he was unscathed, cheerfully watching the regiment's dog, which leaped around as if the chaos was just a game.

"March! Forward! March!" was the command given to the drum. The order to retreat hadn’t come yet, although it might have made sense to do so; finally, the command came to "Retreat." But our little drummer misunderstood and beat out "Forward! march!" and the soldiers followed the drumbeat. It was a strong roll, urging the men who had started to stumble onward to victory.

title: THE GOLDEN TREASURE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Lives and limbs were lost in the battle. Bombshells tore through flesh, lighting up the straw where the wounded lay, left uncared for for many hours, maybe for all the hours they had left.

It was useless to dwell on it; yet, thinking about it was unavoidable, even far away in the peaceful town. The drummer and his wife also thought of it, for Peter was at war.

"I’m tired of these complaints," said the Fire-drum.

Another battle day dawned; the sun hadn’t yet risen, but morning was here. The drummer and his wife were sleeping. They often discussed their son at night, as they did almost every evening, wondering about him in God’s hands. The father dreamt the war was over, the soldiers had returned, and Peter wore a silver cross on his chest. But the mother dreamt she entered the church, saw the painted pictures and gilded angels, and saw her own dear boy, the golden treasure of her heart, standing among the angels in white robes, singing as sweetly as only angels can; and that he had soared up with them into the sunshine, kindly waving at his mother.

"My golden treasure!" she cried out, waking up. "Now God has taken him to Himself!" She folded her hands, buried her face in the cotton curtains of the bed, and wept. "Where does he rest now? Among the many in the big grave that they dug for the dead? Or perhaps he's in the marsh water! His grave is unknown; no holy words have been spoken over it!" And silently she offered the Lord's Prayer, bowing her head until exhaustion took over and she fell asleep.

Days passed, blending life with dreams!

It was evening. A rainbow arched over the battlefield, connecting the forest and the deep marsh.

It’s said in popular belief that where a rainbow touches the earth, a treasure lies buried—a golden treasure; and indeed, here there was one. Only his mother thought of the little drummer, and thus she dreamed of him.

Days continued to pass, merging life with dreams!

title: THE GOLDEN TREASURE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Not a hair on his head had been harmed, not a single golden strand.

"Drum-ma-rum! drum-ma-rum! There he is!" the Drum might have said if only his mother had seen or dreamed this.

With cheers and songs, adorned with green victory wreaths, they came home, as the war ended and peace was declared. The regiment's dog bounded ahead, making the path three times longer for himself than it needed to be.

Weeks and days flew by, and Peter came home to his parents. He was as tan as a wild man, his eyes sparkling, and his face glowing like sunshine. His mother embraced him, kissing his lips, his forehead, and his red hair. She had her boy back again; he didn’t wear the silver cross his father had dreamed of, but he was whole and healthy—something the mother hadn’t dreamt. And what a celebration it was! They laughed and wept, and Peter hugged the old Fire-drum.

"The old skeleton still stands!" he exclaimed.

"One would think a great fire broke out here," said the Fire-drum. "Bright day! Fire in the heart! Golden treasure! Scrat! Scrat! Scrat!"

And then, what happened? What did happen!—Ask the town musician.

"Peter’s surpassed the drum," he said. "Peter will achieve greater things than I ever did."

And yet he was the son of a royal plate-washer; but all he learned in half a lifetime, Peter grasped in just six months.

There was something so joyful about him, something so genuinely kind-hearted. His eyes sparkled, and his hair shone—there was simply no denying it!

"He should have his hair colored," suggested the neighbor's wife. "That worked wonders for the policeman's daughter, and she found a husband."

"But her hair turned a nasty green, and she constantly had to dye it."

"She knows how to fend for herself," said the neighbors, "and so does Peter. He gets invited to the most posh places, even the burgomaster's home for Miss Charlotte’s piano lessons."

title: THE GOLDEN TREASURE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
He could play! He played beautifully from the depths of his heart, creating enchanting pieces that had never been written on sheet music. He played in bright nights and dark nights alike. The neighbors found it unbearable, and the Fire-drum agreed.

He played until his thoughts soared and turned into grand visions for the future:

And Burgomaster's Charlotte sat at the piano. Her delicate fingers glided over the keys, ringing out into Peter’s heart. It felt overwhelming to him, and this wasn’t a one-time occurrence; many times, he would take her hand, kissing it and gazing into her deep brown eyes. Heaven knows what he said; we can only guess. Charlotte blushed, turning red from brow to neck, and she didn’t say a word. Then strangers wandered into the room, and one of them was the state councillor's son. He had a prominent white forehead, which he held so high, it seemed to extend back into his neck. Peter sat by her for a long time, and she looked at him with soft eyes.

That evening at home, he talked about traveling the wide world and the golden treasure waiting for him in his violin.

"Tum-me-lum, tum-me-lum, tum-me-lum!" said the Fire-drum. "Peter’s gone completely out of his mind. I think there's a fire in the house."

"Shall I share some news, Peter?" she asked upon returning home. "I have fantastic news. Burgomaster's Charlotte has gotten engaged to the state councilor's son; the engagement took place last night."

"No!" Peter exclaimed, jumping out of his chair. But his mother reaffirmed emphatically, "Yes." She had heard it from the baker's wife, whose husband got the news straight from the burgomaster himself.

Peter turned as pale as a ghost and sat down again.

"Good heavens! What’s wrong with you?" his mother asked.

"Nothing, nothing; just please leave me alone," he replied, though tears streamed down his cheeks.

title: THE GOLDEN TREASURE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"My sweet child, my golden treasure!" his mother cried, weeping; meanwhile, the Fire-drum sang quietly within.

"Charlotte's gone! Charlotte's gone! And now the song has ended."

But the song hadn't truly finished; it held many more verses, long verses, the most beautiful verses, the golden treasures of life.

"She’s acting like a madwoman," the neighbor's wife said. "Everyone can see the letters she gets from her golden treasure and read the articles about his violin playing. He even sends her money, which is quite handy since she’s now a widow."

"He plays for emperors and kings," said the town musician. "I never had that fortune, but he remembers his old master and doesn’t forget me."

"His father dreamed that Peter came home from the war with a silver cross. He may not have gotten one from the war, but it's even harder to earn one this way. Now he has a medal of honor. If only his father had lived to see this!"

"He’s become famous!" the Fire-drum proclaimed, and everyone in the town echoed this, for the drummer's son, Peter with the red hair—Peter they once knew as a little boy in wooden shoes, then as a drummer for the dancers—had become famous!

"He performed at our place before he ever played in front of kings," the burgomaster's wife reminisced. "Back then, he was quite infatuated with Charlotte. He always had ambitious aspirations. At that time, he was cheeky and full of passion. My husband laughed when he heard of that silly affair, and now Charlotte is a state councillor's wife."

A golden treasure had been hiding in the heart and soul of the poor child who had once beat a roll as a drummer—a roll of victory for those who were ready to back down. There was a golden treasure in his chest, the gift of sound; bursting forth on his violin as if it were a grand organ, as if all the spirits of a midsummer's night danced across the strings. In its melodies were the sweet sounds of birds and the pure notes of the human voice; thus his music brought joy to every heart and carried his name triumphantly across the land. That was an extraordinary spark—the spark of inspiration.
