"And look how splendid he appears!" the young ladies and the older ones remarked; the oldest of them all even got a scrapbook for famous locks of hair, simply to ask for a lock of his rich, splendid hair— his treasure, his golden treasure.

When he entered the humble room of the drummer, he looked and felt like a prince, happier than a king. His eyes were clear and his face shone like sunlight; he hugged his mother tightly as she kissed his lips, weeping from joy, as only happiness can bring. He nodded at every old piece of furniture in the room, at the cupboard with the teacups, and at the flower vase. He smiled at the daybed where he had slept as a child; then he brought the old Fire-drum into the middle of the room, addressing it and his mother:

"My father would’ve played a famous roll tonight. Now I have to do it!"

He struck a mighty roll on the instrument, and the Drum felt so honored that the parchment almost burst from pride.

"He has a wonderful touch!" said the Drum. "I now have a memory of him that will last forever. I hope his mother gets a similar joy from her golden treasure."

And this is the story of the Golden Treasure.

title: THE HAPPY FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In this country, the biggest green leaf you’ll find is definitely the burdock leaf. If you hold one in front of you, it’s large enough to use as an apron, and if you hold it over your head, it works almost like an umbrella because it’s so impressively big. Burdocks don’t grow alone; where you see one, there are always many more, creating a stunning sight that’s perfect for snails. These big white snails, which fancy people used to enjoy in fancy dishes, lived on the burdock leaves. After eating them, they would exclaim, "Oh, what a delicious dish!" because they genuinely believed they were a delicacy. For these snails, the burdock was specifically planted.

Once, there was an old estate where no one lived to enjoy the snails anymore; in fact, the owners had all passed away. Yet, the burdock kept thriving, covering all the garden beds and paths. It grew without any restraint, eventually becoming a whole forest of burdocks. Here and there, you could spot an apple or plum tree, but otherwise, the place was just a vast expanse of burdock. This forest was home to the last two surviving snails. They weren’t sure how old they were, but they could recall a time when many more of them existed. They knew they came from a family that had roots in distant lands, and that this forest had been planted for them and their relatives. They had never left the garden, but they had heard of a place named the Duke's Palace Castle, where some of their relatives had been boiled until they turned black and then served on a silver dish. What happened after that was a mystery to them. They couldn’t even imagine what it was like to be boiled and presented that way, but it must have been something very classy and special. Neither the cockchafer, the toad, nor the earthworm they asked could provide any answers since none of their relatives had ever been cooked or served in such a manner. They knew they were the most aristocratic snails in the world—it was a fact they were proud of. The forest was planted for them, and the nobleman's castle was built just so they could be served on silver dishes.

title: THE HAPPY FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
They lived happily and in seclusion, and since they had no children of their own, they adopted a little common snail that they raised as their own. The little snail didn’t grow much since he was just an ordinary snail, but the old couple, especially the mother snail, insisted that she could see him grow. When the father said he didn’t quite see it, she encouraged him to feel the little snail's shell, and when he did, he realized she was right.

One day, it started raining heavily. "Listen to the drumming on the burdock leaves! Turn, turn, turn; turn, turn, turn," said the father snail.

"Here come the raindrops," the mother said. "They’re dripping down the stalks. It’s going to be very wet here soon. I’m really glad we have such nice homes, and that the little one has one of his own. We’ve truly been given more than any other creature; it’s clear that we are the most noble beings in the world. We have had houses since we were born, and this burdock forest has been planted just for us. I wonder how far it goes and what lies beyond it."

"There’s nothing better than what we have here," said the father snail. "I don’t wish for anything more."

"Well, I do," replied the mother. "I’d love to be taken to the palace, boiled, and served on a silver dish, like our ancestors. It must be something really special."

"Maybe the nobleman's castle has fallen into disrepair," the father snail suggested, "or perhaps the burdock forest has grown out. There’s no need to rush; you’re always so impatient, and the little one is becoming just like you. He’s been trying to crawl to the top of that stalk for three days. I get dizzy just watching him!"

"Don’t scold him," replied the mother snail. "He’s being careful. He will bring us joy; we have nothing else to look forward to. But have you thought about where we’re going to find a wife for him? Do you think there are more of our kind further out in the woods?"

title: THE HAPPY FAMILY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"There may be black snails," the father snail said, "but they don’t have houses and seem so common and proud. Let’s ask the ants for help; they’re always rushing around as if they have important business. They’ll probably know of a suitable bride for our little one."

"I do know of a beautiful bride," said one of the ants, "but I’m afraid it wouldn’t work because she’s a queen."

"That’s not a problem," said the old snail. "Does she have a house?"

"She has a palace," replied the ant, "a stunning ant palace with seven hundred passages."

"Thank you," said the mother snail, "but our son won’t be living in an ant hill. If you don’t have anything better, we’ll ask the white gnats; they fly around in both rain and sunshine and know every inch of the burdock forest."

"We’ve got a wife for him," the gnats announced. "A hundred man-steps from here, there’s a little snail with a house sitting on a gooseberry bush. She’s all alone and old enough to marry. It’s just a hundred man-steps from here."

"Then let her come to him," said the old snails. "He has the entire burdock forest; she’s only got a bush."

So the little lady snail was brought to them. The journey took her eight days, which was just as it should be, proving her to be of good breeding. They held a wedding with six glow-worms providing as much light as they could. Other than that, it was quite a subdued event, as the old snails preferred not to have celebrations or crowds. The mother snail gave a beautiful speech, while the father was too overwhelmed to speak. Then they gifted the entire burdock forest to the young snails as their inheritance and reminded them, as they often did, that it was the finest place in the world. They said that if they lived honorable lives and grew their family, one day they might be taken to the nobleman's palace to be boiled black and served on a silver dish. Once they finished speaking, the old couple went back into their homes and never came out again; they just fell asleep.

Now the young snail couple ruled over the forest and had many offspring. However, since the young ones were never boiled or served on silver dishes, they assumed the castle had fallen into ruin, and that all the people in the world were gone. Nobody contradicted this belief, so they felt justified. Rain fell onto the burdock leaves, creating a drumroll just for them, while the sun shone colors across the burdock forest, and they were incredibly happy. The whole family was completely and utterly joyful.

title: THE JEWISH MAIDEN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In a charity school, there sat a little Jewish girl among the other children. She was a bright, smart child who grasped her lessons quickly, but she wasn’t allowed to join the Scripture lesson since this was a Christian school. During that time, she worked on her geography or did math problems for the next day. Once her geography lesson was mastered, she would leave the book open in front of her but wouldn’t read a word, choosing instead to quietly listen to the Christian teacher. He soon noticed that she was paying more attention to him than most of the other kids. "Read your book, Sarah," he said gently.

But again and again, he saw her dark, shining eyes fixed on him. Once, when he posed a question, she answered even better than her classmates. She didn’t just hear; she understood his words and reflected on them deeply. Her father, a poor but honest man, had enrolled her in this school with the condition that she wouldn’t be taught the Christian faith. It could have caused confusion or resentment among the other kids if she had been sent out of the room, so she stayed. But it was clear this situation couldn’t continue. The teacher spoke to her father, advising him to either pull her from the school or allow her to become a Christian. "I can no longer stand by and watch those bright eyes filled with a sincere yearning for the gospel," he said.

The father then broke down in tears. "I don’t know much about my ancestors’ traditions," he confessed, "but Sarah's mother was steadfast in her faith as a daughter of Israel, and I promised her on her deathbed that our child would never be baptized. I must uphold that promise—it’s like a covenant with God Himself." And so, the little Jewish girl left the Christian school.

title: THE JEWISH MAIDEN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Years went by. In a tiny provincial town, there lived a poor Jewish woman who worked as a servant. Her hair was as black as ebony, her eyes dark as night, yet bright with a light unique to the daughters of the East. It was Sarah. The look on her face as a young woman was the same as when she sat listening attentively to the Christian teacher in class. Every Sunday, the sounds of an organ and hymns drifted from a nearby church. Sarah heard them while diligently completing her household tasks. "Keep the Sabbath holy," whispered her faith, but her Sabbath became a workday among Christians, which troubled her deeply. Yet there was a comforting thought: "Does God count the days and hours?" On the Christian Sabbath, she found solace in having an hour for her own prayers without interruption. The music and singing became sacred to her, and she would read from the Old Testament—the only Scriptures she could access. She had held onto her father's words to her teacher when she left the school and the vow he made to her mother that she would never receive Christian baptism. Although the New Testament remained a closed book to her, she still remembered much of its teachings, with echoes of gospel truths lingering from her childhood.

One evening, she sat in a corner of the dining room while her master read aloud. It wasn’t the gospel he was reading, but rather a storybook, so she could listen. The tale was about a Hungarian knight who had been captured by a Turkish pasha and treated cruelly. The pasha forced him to plow while yoked to oxen, whipping him until blood flowed, pushing him to the brink of exhaustion. Back home, the knight's faithful wife sold her jewels, mortgaged their castle, and gathered funds from friends to pay the exorbitant ransom. Eventually, the ransom was raised, and the knight was released from his suffering.

Not long after, another call to battle arose for the knight. He, though still alive, could not find peace or rest. With great effort, he had himself lifted onto his war-horse; color returned to his face, and his strength surged as he rode out to confront his enemies and claim victory. The very pasha who had enslaved him became his captive and was dragged to a dungeon in the knight’s castle. Soon after, the knight stood before the imprisoned pasha and asked, "What do you think awaits you?"

title: THE JEWISH MAIDEN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I know," replied the pasha. "Retribution."

"Yes, the retribution of a Christian," said the knight. "The teachings of Christ tell us to forgive our enemies and to love our neighbors, for God is love. Go in peace; return to your loved ones. But from now on, be kind and compassionate to all in need."

The prisoner broke down in tears and exclaimed, "How could I ever expect such mercy and forgiveness? I anticipated pain and torment! In my despair, I carried poison with me to end my life. I thought I was certain to die! But before I go, show me this teaching filled with love and mercy, so grand and God-like. I wish to understand it and die as a Christian!" His prayer was accepted.

This was the story the master read from the old book. Everyone in the house listened and enjoyed it, but Sarah, the Jewish girl in the corner, felt her heart race with emotion. Tears welled in her bright eyes, and as she listened with the same gentle spirit she had when hearing the gospel in school, she felt its depth, and tears rolled down her cheeks. The last request of her dying mother echoed in her mind: "Do not let my child become a Christian," followed by the commandment to "Honor your father and your mother."

"I am not accepted among the Christians," she said. "They mock me for being Jewish; just last Sunday, the neighborhood boys ridiculed me as I stood outside the church, watching the candles on the altar and listening to the singing. Ever since I was on the school bench, I have felt Christianity’s power, a light that streams into my heart, even when I try to ignore it. But I will not bring grief to you, my mother, in your grave. I won't betray my father's vow. I won’t read the Christian Bible. I trust in the God of my ancestors, and in Him, I will remain loyal."

title: THE JEWISH MAIDEN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
And again, years passed. After her master passed away, his widow found herself struggling financially and wished to let Sarah go; however, Sarah refused to leave. She became a real pillar of support in hard times, working late into the night to help put food on the table. No relatives came forward to offer assistance, and the widow became confined to her sickbed for months, growing weaker each day. Despite her own struggles, Sarah worked tirelessly but also took time to comfort her and watch over her during her illness. She was kind and devoted, truly an angel of mercy in their household of hardship.

"My Bible is over there on the table," the sick woman said to Sarah one day. "Please read something from it; the night feels so long, and my soul craves the word of God."

Sarah lowered her head. She took the book, placed her hand over the Christian Bible, and eventually opened it to read for the sick woman. Tears filled her eyes as she read, and they sparkled, for there was light in her heart.

"Mother," she whispered, "your child cannot receive Christian baptism, nor join the congregation of Christians. You wished it so, and I will honor your request. We remain united in this life; however, in the next world, we will have a deeper connection, even with God Himself, who guides His people until the end. He came down to suffer for us, so we could produce fruits of repentance. I understand this now. I don’t know how I learned this truth, except perhaps from the name of Christ." Yet she shuddered at the sound of that holy name. She fought against these truths of Christianity for days until one evening, while keeping watch over her mistress, she suddenly fell very ill; her legs gave way, and she collapsed beside the sick woman's bed.

"Poor Sarah," the neighbors said. "She’s exhausted from hard work and caring for the sick." They brought her to the hospital for the poor. There, she passed away, and they buried her beyond the churchyard of the Christians, where there was no space for a Jewish girl. They dug her grave outside the wall. And God’s sun, which shines upon the graves in the Christians’ cemetery, also graces the Jewish maiden’s resting place beyond the wall. When the Christian psalms echo across the graveyard, their sound reaches her solitary grave, and she who rests there will be deemed worthy at the resurrection, through the name of Christ, who told his disciples, "John baptized you with water, but I will baptize you with the Holy Spirit."

title: THE JUMPER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The Flea, the Grasshopper, and the Skipjack wanted to determine who could jump the highest. They invited everyone, near and far, to witness the incredible spectacle. And so, the three famous jumpers gathered together in a room.

"I’ll give my daughter to whoever jumps the highest," announced the King. "It wouldn’t be right to let them jump for nothing."

The Flea was the first to take his turn. He had charming manners and bowed in every direction, coming from a family of fine lineage, which meant he was used to mingling with humans. This was quite important.

Next up was the Grasshopper. While he was definitely heavier, he had a nice physique and sported his natural green attire. He claimed to be from a long-established family in Egypt, where he was highly respected. He mentioned he had just come from the fields and had been living in a three-story card house made of picture cards, with the faces turned inward. There were doors and windows carved into the body of the Queen of Hearts.

"I sing like this," he said, "to the point where sixteen native crickets, who have been chirping since they were young and have never had a card house of their own, would become envious and lose weight just hearing me."

Both the Flea and the Grasshopper made sure to introduce themselves, indicating that they believed they deserved to marry a Princess.

The Skipjack stayed quiet, but it was said that he thought a lot. When the Yard Dog sniffed him, he was quick to point out that the Skipjack came from a good family and was made from a genuine goose's breastbone. An old advisor, who had earned three medals for being silent, proclaimed that the Skipjack had the gift of prophecy; one could predict whether winter would be harsh or mild by examining his bones—something not everyone could do from the breastbone of the person who writes the almanac.

"I won’t say anything else," remarked the old King. "I just stay calm and always think the best."

Now it was time to jump. The Flea hopped so high that no one could see him, leading some to claim he hadn't jumped at all. That was quite unfair. The Grasshopper leaped only half as high but landed right in the King’s face, which the King deemed severely rude. The Skipjack took a long moment to think it over; eventually, people began to worry that he couldn't jump at all.

"I just hope he’s okay," said the Yard Dog, sniffing him again.

"Tap!" he finally jumped with a small, crooked leap straight into the lap of the Princess, who was seated on a low golden stool.

Then the King declared, "The highest jump was made by the one who landed in my daughter’s lap; that’s what really matters. It takes cleverness to achieve that, and the Skipjack has proven he’s clever."

"I actually jumped the highest," the Flea complained. "But it doesn’t matter. Let her have the goose-bone with its chunk of wax and stick. I jumped the highest, but in this world, you need a body to be noticed."

So the Flea went off to serve in a foreign army, where it’s said he met his end.

The Grasshopper sat in a ditch, pondering how things worked in the world. He too said, "You need a body! You need a body!" Then he sang his own sorrowful song, and from that, we’ve crafted this story, which some claim isn’t true, even though it's published.

title: THE LAST DREAM OF THE OLD OAK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In the forest, perched on a steep shore not far from the sea, a very old oak tree stood tall. It was three hundred sixty-five years old, which for the tree felt like a full year feels to us. We wake during the day and sleep at night, dreaming our dreams. But for the tree, it stayed awake through three seasons, finally resting only when winter comes. Winter is its time to recharge; it's like the night after the busy days of spring, summer, and autumn. On many warm summer days, the Ephemera—tiny flies that only lived for a single day—would flutter around the old oak, enjoying life. Whenever one of them rested on its large, fresh leaves, the tree would often think, "Poor little thing! Your whole life is just one day. How sad that must be."

"Sad? What do you mean?" the little creature would respond each time. "Everything around me is so incredibly bright and warm that it makes me happy!"

"But just for one day, and then it’s all gone.**"

"Gone!" repeated the fly. "What do you mean by all gone? Are you gone too?"

"No, I'll probably live through thousands of your days. My day lasts through whole seasons; in fact, it’s so long that you’d struggle to count it!"

"Really? Then I don't understand you. You might have many of my days, but I get to enjoy countless joyful moments. Does all the beauty of the world disappear when you die?"

"Not at all," replied the tree. "It lasts much longer—infinitely longer than I can even grasp."

"Well, then," said the little fly, "we actually have the same amount of time to live; we just measure it differently." The little creature danced and floated, reveling in her delicate wings. She celebrated in the warm breezes thick with scents from clover fields, wild roses, elderflowers, honeysuckle, and all kinds of fragrant herbs. The delightful fragrances intoxicated her. The long, beautiful day had brought her so much joy that, as the sun began to set, she felt tired from all her happiness. Her wings couldn't hold her anymore, and she gently glided down to a soft patch of grass, giving a small nod as she settled down and fell into a peaceful, sweet sleep. The fly was dead.

title: THE LAST DREAM OF THE OLD OAK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Poor little Ephemera!" the oak said softly. "Such a terribly short life!" And so, every summer day, the same dance happened—questions asked, and answers exchanged, continuing for many generations of Ephemera—all of them felt just as merry and happy.

The oak remained awake through the spring mornings, summer afternoons, and autumn evenings; its rest was approaching—winter was coming. The storms sang, "Good night, good night.**" Leaves drifted down, one after the other. "We’ll rock you and lull you. Go to sleep, go to sleep. We’ll sing you to sleep, and that will do your old branches good. They’ll crackle with delight. Sleep sweetly, sleep sweetly; it's your three-hundred-sixty-fifth night. To be honest, you’re still quite young in this world. Sleep well; the clouds will blanket you with snow, warm and cozy. Sweet dreams to you." The oak stood bare, resting throughout the long winter and dreaming many dreams of all it had experienced, like dreams of humans. Once, the great tree had been small, cradled as an acorn. Now, according to human time, it was in its fourth century. It towered above all the other trees, a landmark for sailors far at sea. Little did it know how many eyes sought it out. In its highest branches, woodpigeons built their nests, and cuckoos sang their iconic tunes echoing among the branches. In autumn, when the leaves turned a fiery copper, migratory birds visited its branches before their journey across the sea. But now it was winter, and the tree stood bare, revealing how twisted and bent its branches had become. Crows and rooks took turns resting on them, talking about the tough times ahead, and how hard it was to find food in winter.

title: THE LAST DREAM OF THE OLD OAK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Around Christmas time, the tree had a special dream. It sensed the festive season had arrived and imagined it heard bells ringing from nearby churches. Yet in its dream, it felt like a beautiful summer day. Its majestic top was adorned with vibrant green leaves; sunlight danced among the branches, and the air was rich with scents of herbs and blossoms. Colorful butterflies flitted past; summer flies buzzed around, making it feel like the world was made for them to dance and celebrate. Memories of every year in the tree's life paraded through its mind. It saw knights and noble ladies riding through the forest on spirited horses, hats adorned with feathers, and falcons perched on their wrists. The hunting horns sounded, and dogs barked joyfully. It witnessed warriors in bright clothing, glinting armor, and spears pitching and striking their tents. Campfires blazed, and men sang and rested under the shelter of the tree. Lovers met happily nearby in the moonlight, carving their initials into the tree's bark. Long ago, merrymakers had hung guitars and Eolian harps on its branches; now, those instruments seemed to reappear, filling the air with their magical sounds. The woodpigeons cooed as if sharing the tree's feelings; the cuckoo called out, counting down the summer days left in its life. Suddenly, the tree felt a surge of life pulsing through every fiber of its being, rising to the tips of its branches. It felt itself growing taller and stronger, its branches reaching wider and fuller. Along with its growth came a growing desire to reach even higher toward the warm, bright sun. Its branches pierced through the clouds like flocks of migrating birds or graceful swans; every leaf felt alive, as if it could see. The stars became visible in the daylight, glittering like gentle eyes, reminding it of the familiar sparkle found in a child's eyes or the gaze of lovers under its branches. These were magical, happy moments for the old tree—full of peace and delight. Yet, amidst all this joy, the tree yearned for all the other trees, bushes, herbs, and flowers below it—their heights and gaze—to rise up and share in the same splendor and happiness. The grand oak couldn't enjoy its blessings completely while the rest stayed behind. That longing flowed through every branch and leaf warmly, as if they had the fibers of a human heart. The top of the tree swayed as if, in silent longing, it searched for something. Suddenly, sweet fragrances of thyme mingled with the stronger scent of honeysuckle and violets wafted toward it. It fancied it heard the cuckoo's call. Finally, the waiting was over. The green tops of the forest trees began to rise through the clouds, and the oak saw them reaching higher and higher. Bushes and herbs shot upwards, some even uprooting themselves to climb faster. The birch tree was the quickest, shooting up like a bolt of lightning, with branches spreading out like green ribbons. Every woodland creature, even the feathery brown rushes, grew alongside the others while the birds soared, filling the air with joyful song. A grasshopper, sitting on a blade of grass dancing in the breeze like a long green ribbon, scrubbed its wings with its legs. May beetles buzzed, bees hummed, and birds sang, each in their unique way; the air was alive with music and joy.

title: THE LAST DREAM OF THE OLD OAK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"But where's that little blue flower by the water?" asked the oak. "And what about the purple bellflower and the daisy?" The oak wanted to have them all with it.

"Here we are, we are here!" echoed voices in song.

"But what about the lovely thyme from last summer? Where are the lilies-of-the-valley that covered the earth with their blossoms last year? Where's the wild apple tree with its beautiful flowers, and all the richness of the woods that have thrived through the years? Even the new sprouts should be here with us!"

"We are here, we are here," chorused voices high in the air, as if they had arrived ahead of time.

"This is beautiful, too beautiful to believe," the oak exclaimed joyfully. "I have everything with me, both big and small; not one has been forgotten. Can such happiness be real?" It felt almost impossible.

"In heaven, with the Eternal God, it can be imagined, and it is real," came the reply from the air.

As the old tree continued to grow upwards and onwards, it sensed its roots loosening from the earth.

"That's right, it's for the best," the tree said. "No chains hold me anymore. I can soar to the very highest point of light and glory. And everyone I love is here with me, both small and great. All—everyone is here!"

That was the dream of the old oak. While it dreamed, a powerful storm surged across the land and sea during that holy Christmas time. The sea roared with great waves crashing towards the shore. A loud cracking sounded from the tree as its roots were ripped from the ground just as it dreamed they were being freed. It fell—its three hundred sixty-five years passed as quickly as a day for the Ephemera. On Christmas morning, as the sun rose, the storm had calmed. From every church, the festive bells rang, and from every little home, smoke rose into the blue sky, much like the incense from the druids' altars. The sea regained its calm, and aboard a ship that had braved the tempest overnight, all the flags flew as symbols of joy and celebration. "The tree is down! Our old oak, our landmark on the coast!" the sailors shouted. "It must have fallen in last night’s storm. Who can replace it? Alas! No one can." This was their short, heartfelt tribute to the old tree. There it lay, stretched out on the snow-covered shore, as a song from the ship wafted through the air—a song of Christmas joy and the salvation of humanity, calling to mind eternal life through Christ’s sacrifice.

"Sing loud on this happy morning, All is fulfilled, for Christ is born; With joyful songs let’s sing out loud, 'Hallelujahs to Christ, our King!'"

This was the tune of the old Christmas carol, and everyone aboard the ship felt uplifted through the song and prayer—much like the old tree felt elevated in its final, beautiful dream on that Christmas morning.

title: THE LAST PEARL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the balance of joy and sorrow
text_chunk:
In a beautifully lavish house filled with joy, the master, the servants, and family friends are all celebrating. A son has been born, and both mother and baby are healthy. The lamp in the bedroom was dimly lit, and thick, luxurious curtains hung over the windows. The carpet felt soft and plush, like a cozy layer of moss. Everything in the room invited rest, radiating a warm sense of peace. So it was no surprise that the nurse had found a moment to doze off, surrounded by such warmth and happiness. A guardian angel stood by the bed, and above the baby, a shimmering net of stars sparkled, each one representing a pearl of joy. The best gifts of life—health, wealth, fortune, and love—seemed to be surrounding the newborn, promising everything one could wish for.

"Everything has been provided here," said the guardian angel.

"No, not everything," replied a nearby voice—the good angel of the child. "One fairy hasn’t brought her gift yet, but she will, even if it takes years; her gift is the last pearl that’s missing."

"Missing!" exclaimed the guardian angel. "Nothing can be missing here; if that’s the case, let’s find her! Let’s go to the powerful fairy and bring her here!"

"She will come, she will come someday without being sought!"

"Her pearl cannot be absent; it must be here to complete the crown. Where can she be found? Where does she live?" asked the guardian angel. "Tell me, and I’ll find the pearl."

"Will you really do that?" the good angel of the child responded. "Then I will guide you to her, no matter where she is. She has no permanent home; she moves between the emperor’s palace and the humble cottage of a peasant. She leaves a mark wherever she goes. She carries gifts, whether they're grand or small. She must come to this child. Waiting for her arrival might seem long and pointless, so let’s go get this pearl—the only one missing among all this abundance."

title: THE LAST PEARL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the balance of joy and sorrow
text_chunk:
Together, they floated hand-in-hand to where the fairy was lingering. They arrived at a large house with dark windows and empty rooms, where an unusual stillness filled the air. A row of windows stood open, allowing the cold wind to rush in, causing the long white curtains to billow. In the center of one room was an open coffin, resting in which was a beautiful woman, still youthful. Fresh roses lay scattered over her. Only her gentle hands and serene face were visible, transformed in death by an earnest expression, suggesting a passage to a better world. Around the coffin stood her husband and their children; a whole crowd, with the youngest cradled in the father's arms. They had come to offer a final goodbye to their mother. The husband kissed her hand, now as fragile as a dried leaf, which not long ago had been busy with acts of love towards them all. Tears streamed down their faces, falling heavily to the floor, but silence filled the room, speaking volumes of their grief. With quiet steps, still weeping, they left the room. A flickering light remained in the space, and a long, red wick rose high above the flame, dancing in the draft. Strangers entered and gently placed the coffin lid on the deceased, hammering it shut; the sounds echoed through the house like the wounds in their hearts.

"Where are you taking me?" asked the guardian angel. "There’s no fairy here whose pearl could be counted among life’s greatest gifts."

"Yes, she’s here; she’s here at this sacred moment," replied the other angel, pointing to a corner. There, in the very spot where the mother once sat amidst flowers and art, a strange woman now occupied her place, wearing long, flowing garments. This was the fairy, and her name was 'Sorrow.' A warm tear fell from her, forming into a pearl that shimmered in every color of the rainbow. The angel reached for it; the pearl glimmered like a star, radiant and bright. This pearl of Sorrow, the final one that was needed, added luster and gave meaning to all the other pearls.

"Do you see how the rainbow shines, connecting earth and sky?" This has built a bridge between our world and the next. Through the dark grave, we look up beyond the stars to the end of everything. Then we gaze at the pearl of Sorrow, holding within it the wings that will elevate us to everlasting happiness.

title: THE LITTLE ELDER-TREE MOTHER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once there was a little boy who had caught a cold because he went outside and got his feet wet. No one could figure out how it happened since the weather was perfectly dry. His mother took off his clothes, tucked him into bed, and had the teapot brought in to brew a nice cup of warming tea made from elder-tree blossoms. Just then, a kind old man who lived alone in the upper floor of their house came in. He was quite lonely because he had no family, but he loved other people's children, and he could tell so many fairy tales that it was always a delight to listen to him.

"Drink your tea now," said the mother. "Maybe you'll hear a story."

"Yes, if only I knew a new one," replied the old man with a smile. Then he asked, "But how did your little one get his feet wet?"

"Well, that’s a mystery to everyone," the mother replied.

"Can you tell me a story?" asked the boy.

"Sure, if you can describe how deep the gutter is on the street where you go to school as accurately as possible," answered the old man.

"It’s about half as high as my top boots," the boy explained, "but I have to stand in the deepest parts to measure it right."

"There we go, now we know how you got your wet feet," the old man said. "I should tell you a story, but unfortunately, I can't think of any more right now."

"You can make one up," the little boy suggested. "Mother says you can tell a fairy tale about anything you see or touch."

"That might sound good, but those kinds of stories aren’t very valuable! No, the best ones come to me on their own and knock on my forehead, saying: 'Here I am.'"

"Will one knock soon?" the boy asked. His mother smiled as she added elder-tree blossoms to the teapot and poured hot water over them. "Please, tell me a story."

title: THE LITTLE ELDER-TREE MOTHER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Sure, if stories showed up without an invitation; they are so proud—they only come when they feel like it. But wait," he said suddenly, "there’s one! Look at the teapot; there’s a story brewing in there right now."

The little boy gazed at the teapot, and the lid slowly lifted. The elder-tree blossoms popped out one by one, fresh and white. Long branches sprouted everywhere, even from the spout, forming a bush—a big elder tree that stretched its branches toward the bed, pushing the curtains aside. The air filled with blossoms and a sweet fragrance! In the middle of the tree sat a kind-faced old woman wearing a strange dress. It was as green as the leaves and trimmed with big white blossoms, making it hard to tell if it was real fabric or the leaves and flowers of the elder tree.

"What’s this woman’s name?" the little boy asked.

"Well, the Romans and Greeks called her a Dryad," the old man replied, "but that doesn’t really make sense. In the sailors’ quarter, they have a better name for her; they call her the Elder-Tree Mother. Now, you have to pay close attention to her and admire the beautiful elder tree."

"Just like a huge flower-covered tree outside, which grew in a small, simple yard. One sunny afternoon, two old folks sat beneath it. He was an ancient sailor, and she was his old wife; they had great-grandkids and were about to celebrate their golden wedding, but they couldn’t remember the exact date. The Elder-Tree Mother was sitting in the tree, looking just as happy as this one. 'I know exactly when the golden wedding is!' she said, but the couple didn’t hear her—they were reminiscing about their past.

"'Do you remember?" the old sailor asked. "When we were little and used to run around and play? We made a garden here in this same yard by putting little branches into the ground.'"

"'Oh yes,' replied the old woman. 'I remember it well; we watered those branches, and one of them, an elder branch, took root and grew to become the big tree we’re now sitting under.'"

title: THE LITTLE ELDER-TREE MOTHER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"'That’s right,' he said. 'And over there was a large water tub where I used to sail my boat that I carved myself—it sailed so well! But soon I had to set sail for other places.'"

"'First, of course, we went to school to learn something,' she added. 'We both cried on our confirmation day, but later that afternoon, we held hands and climbed the tall round tower to gaze out over the vast world, right over Copenhagen and the sea. Then we walked to Fredericksburg, where the king and queen were sailing on their splendid boat in the canals.'"

"'But soon I was off to travel far away for many years.'"

"'And I often worried about you because I feared you might have drowned and were lying at the bottom of the sea. Many nights, I got up to see if the weathercock had turned; it often did, but you didn’t come back. I remember one stormy day when it poured rain; the rubbish collector was at the house where I worked. I went down with the dustbin and paused in the doorway, looking at the terrible weather. That’s when the postman handed me a letter from you. Oh, how that letter had traveled! I tore it open and read it, crying and laughing at the same time, feeling so happy! You wrote that you were in a hot country where coffee grows. Those must be amazing places! You told me so much about them, and I read your letter while the rain came down and I stood there with the dustbin. Just then, someone wrapped their arm around my waist..."

"'Yes, and you gave him a big kiss on the cheek," the old man interrupted.

"'I didn’t realize it was you—you arrived as swiftly as your letter! You looked so handsome, and still do. You had a big yellow silk handkerchief in your pocket and a shiny hat on. You looked amazing, while the weather outside was just awful!"

"'Then we got married,' he continued. 'Do you remember when we had our first boy, then Mary, Niels, Peter, John, and Christian?'"

title: THE LITTLE ELDER-TREE MOTHER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"'Oh yes, they’ve all grown up now and become helpful members of society, well-loved by everyone.'"

"'And their children have had children too,' said the old sailor. 'Yes, those are our grandchildren, strong and healthy. If I remember correctly, our wedding took place around this time of year.'"

"'Yes, today is your golden wedding day,' said the little elder-tree mother, leaning her head down between the two old folks, who thought she was just a neighbor nodding at them. They looked at each other and held hands. Before long, their children and grandchildren arrived because they knew it was the golden wedding day. They had already congratulated them that morning, but the old couple completely forgot, even though they could vividly recall many events from long ago. The elder tree smelled incredibly strong, and the setting sun illuminated the two old folks' faces, making them look rosy. The youngest grandchild danced around them, joyfully announcing that there would be a feast in the evening, featuring hot potatoes. The elder mother nodded in the tree and cheered 'Hooray!' along with everyone else.

"But that wasn’t a fairy tale," the little boy who had been listening pointed out.

"You’ll understand soon enough," said the old man who told the story. "Let’s ask the little elder-tree mother about it."

"That wasn’t a fairy tale," the little elder-tree mother said, "but now it comes! Real life gives us topics for the most wonderful fairy tales, otherwise my beautiful elder bush wouldn’t have sprung up out of the teapot."

title: THE LITTLE ELDER-TREE MOTHER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Then she lifted the little boy out of bed and placed him on her lap. The elder branches filled with blossoms wrapped around them like a thick leafy bower, soaring through the air; it was stunning beyond words. The little elder-tree mother transformed into a charming young girl, still wearing the same green dress covered in white flowers. A real elder blossom was pinned to her chest, and a crown of the same flowers adorned her curly golden hair. Her eyes were huge and blue, captivating to look into. They shared a kiss, and suddenly, they felt the same age and joy. Hand in hand, they stepped out of the bower and found themselves at home in a lovely flower garden. Nearby, tied to a post, was the father’s walking stick. It became animated as soon as they sat on it; the polished knob morphed into a horse’s head, with a long black mane dancing in the breeze, and four strong legs sprouted out. The spirited creature galloped around the lawn. "Hooray! We’ll ride far away for miles!" the boy exclaimed, "Let’s head to the nobleman’s estate we visited last year." And they rode around the lawn again. The little girl, who was actually the little elder-tree mother, kept saying, "Now we’re in the countryside! Look over there at the farmhouse with the large baking stove sticking out like a huge egg into the road. The elder tree spreads its branches over it, and the cock struts around scratching for hens. Just look at how proud he is! Now we’re near the church on a tall hill under the sprawling oak trees; one of them is half dead! Now we’re at the blacksmith’s, where the fire blazes, and half-naked men hammer away, sending sparks flying everywhere. Let’s hurry to the pretty farm!" They passed everything the little girl described, and although they circled the lawn, the boy could see it all.

title: THE LITTLE ELDER-TREE MOTHER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
They then played in a side yard, marking out a small garden on the ground. She took elder blossoms out of her hair and planted them, and they grew just like those the old couple had planted in their childhoods. They walked hand in hand, just like the old couple did when they were kids, but they never went to the round tower or the Fredericksburg garden. No; the little girl grabbed the boy around the waist, and together they flew far across the land. Seasons changed; it became spring and turned to summer, then autumn, and finally winter, with thousands of images reflecting in the boy's heart and eyes. All throughout their journey, the sweet smell of the elder tree lingered; he noticed the roses and fresh beeches, but the elder tree's fragrance was much stronger since the flowers were against the little girl’s chest where the boy often rested his head.

"It’s lovely here in spring," the little girl said as they returned to the green beech wood, where thyme released a sweet fragrance at their feet, and pink anemones bloomed beautifully in the green moss. "Oh! If only it could always be spring in this fragrant beech wood!"

"It’s spectacular in summer!" she exclaimed, pointing out the old chivalric castles. The high walls and crenelated towers reflected in the ditches where swans glided, peering into the old shady avenues. The fields of grain swayed like a golden sea, and red and yellow flowers blossomed in the ditches, while wild hops and convolvulus grew abundantly in the hedges. As evening came, the moon rose—large and round—and the hayricks in the meadows released a sweet aroma. "You’ll never forget this!"

"Autumn is beautiful here!" the little girl exclaimed, as the atmosphere brightened, shining blue, while leaves glowed crimson, green, and gold. Hounds raced off, flocks of wild fowl screamed overhead, and bramble bushes climbed around the old stones. The dark-blue sea was dotted with white-sailed ships, and in the barns, old women, girls, and children picked hops into a tub, singing songs while the older folks shared fairy tales about goblins and sorcerers. It couldn’t be more wonderful anywhere else.

title: THE LITTLE ELDER-TREE MOTHER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Winter is delightful here!" the little girl proclaimed, as every tree wore a coat of hoar frost, resembling white coral. The snow crunched underfoot as if one were wearing new boots. Shooting stars darted across the sky. Inside, the Christmas tree sparkled with lights, and laughter filled the air. In the peasant's home, a violin played, and games were held for apple quarters; even the most impoverished child exclaimed, "It’s beautiful in winter!"

And indeed, it was beautiful! The little girl showed the boy everything, while the elder tree continued to give off a sweet fragrance. The red flag with a white cross fluttered in the wind—it was the flag under which the old sailor had served. The boy grew into a young man, set to travel far away to the lands where coffee grows. But as they parted, the little girl gifted him an elder blossom from her breast as a keepsake. He tucked it into his prayer book, and whenever he opened it in distant lands, it would always be at the spot where he placed the flower of remembrance. The more he gazed at it, the fresher it smelled, making him almost able to detect the fragrance of the woods back home. He vividly pictured the little girl, with her bright blue eyes peeking out from behind the petals, and heard her whispering, "It’s beautiful in spring, summer, autumn, and winter," as countless images rushed through his mind.

Many years passed. He was now an old man, sitting with his old wife under a blooming elder tree. They held hands just like the great-grandfather and great-grandmother once did, reminiscing about old days and their golden wedding. The little girl, with blue eyes and elder blossoms in her hair, sat high up in the tree, nodding at them and saying, "Today is the golden wedding!" She plucked two flowers from her crown and kissed them. At first, they shimmered like silver, then like gold, and when she placed them on the heads of the elderly couple, each flower transformed into a golden crown. They sat there like a king and queen beneath the fragrant tree, which looked just like an elder tree, and he told his wife the story of the elder-tree mother as it had been told to him when he was a little boy. They both felt the story contained many elements of their own lives, and those parallels made them the happiest.

"Yes, that's right," said the little girl in the tree. "Some call me Little Elder-Tree Mother; others call me a Dryad. But my true name is 'Remembrance.' I am the one who lives in the tree that keeps growing. I can recall things and tell stories! But let’s see if you still have your flower."

The old man opened his prayer book; the elder blossom was still inside, just as fresh as when he first put it there. Remembrance nodded, and the two old people, now crowned in gold, sat in the warm glow of the evening sun. They closed their eyes and—and—

And now, the story has come to an end! The little boy in bed couldn’t tell if he had dreamed it or if someone told him. The teapot sat on the table, but there was no elder tree growing out of it, and the old man who told him the story was about to leave the room, and he did.

"How beautiful it was!" the little boy said. "Mother, I visited warm countries!"

"I believe you," his mother replied. "After two cups of hot elder tea, it makes sense you would end up in warm countries!" She covered him snugly so he wouldn’t catch a cold. "You’ve been sleeping soundly while I debated with the old man whether it was a true story or a fairy tale!"

"And what happened to the little elder-tree mother?" the boy asked.

"She’s in the teapot," the mother said, "and that’s where she can stay."

title: THE LITTLE MATCH-SELLER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
On the last evening of the old year, it was bitterly cold and almost dark, with snow falling heavily. In the freezing air and dim light, a poor little girl wandered through the streets, her head bare and her feet exposed. Although she had left home in a pair of slippers, they were too big for her, having once belonged to her mother. In her hurry to avoid two fast-approaching carriages, she lost one and a boy grabbed the other, claiming he would use it as a cradle someday. Now, the girl continued on, her small, bare feet turning red and blue from the cold. Clutched in an old apron were a handful of matches, and she held more tightly in her hands. No one had bought a single match from her all day, nor had anyone given her even a penny. Shivering with cold and hunger, she crept along, looking like a picture of despair. Snowflakes settled on her long, fair hair, which fell in curls over her shoulders, but she didn’t even notice them.

Lights glowed from every window, and the savory aroma of roast goose filled the air because it was New Year’s Eve—she remembered that well. She curled up in a corner between two houses, one jutting out further than the other, trying to keep warm. She tucked her little feet under her, but she still couldn’t escape the chill. She was too afraid to go home because she hadn’t sold any matches and couldn’t bring home a single penny. Her father would surely be angry with her; besides, it was just as cold at home as it was out here because their roof barely kept the wind out, even though they had stuffed it with straw and rags. Her little hands were nearly frozen, so she thought maybe lighting a match could help warm her fingers. She struck one from the bundle against the wall—"scratch!"—and it sputtered to life, casting a warm, bright glow like a tiny candle as she cupped her hands around it. It was such a beautiful light. For a moment, she imagined she was sitting next to a large iron stove with shiny brass feet and ornamental details. The fire crackled and seemed so inviting that the little girl reached out her feet as if to warm them, but suddenly the flame went out, the stove disappeared, and all that was left was the end of the half-burnt match in her hand.

title: THE LITTLE MATCH-SELLER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
She struck another match against the wall. It flared up, and where its glow touched the wall, it became as transparent as a veil, allowing her to see through into a room. The table was elegantly set with a bright white tablecloth, showcasing a magnificent dinner set and a piping hot roast goose stuffed with apples and dried plums. Even more astonishing, the goose jumped off the dish and waddled across the floor, knife and fork jabbed into its breast, headed straight for the little girl. But then the match extinguished, and she was left staring at the damp, cold wall.

She lit another match, and suddenly she found herself beneath a stunning Christmas tree. It was even bigger and more beautifully adorned than the one she had glimpsed through the glass door at the wealthy merchant’s house. Thousands of candles flickered on the green branches, and colorful images, just like the ones she had seen in shop windows, looked down upon her. The little girl reached out her hand toward them, only to have the match go out.

The Christmas lights rose higher and higher until they resembled stars in the sky. Then she saw a star fall, trailing a bright streak of fire behind it. "Someone is dying," she thought, remembering that her old grandmother—the only person who had ever loved her and who was now gone—had told her that when a star falls, a soul is rising up to God.

She struck another match against the wall, and as the light illuminated her surroundings, her grandmother appeared, clear and glowing, kind and loving in her presence. "Grandmother," the little girl cried, "please take me with you! I know you’ll leave when the match burns out, just like the warm stove, the roast goose, and the beautiful Christmas tree did." Hastily, she lit the entire bundle of matches to keep her grandmother near. The matches glowed brighter than noon, and her grandmother had never looked so big or so lovely. She scooped the little girl into her arms, and together they soared upwards into the warm light and joy, far above the earth, where there was no cold, no hunger, and no pain, for they were with God.

As dawn broke, the poor little girl was found with pale cheeks and a serene smile, leaning against the wall. She had frozen to death on New Year’s Eve, and the sunlight shone down upon her lifeless body. The child remained seated, frozen in death, still clutching the matches in her hand, one bundle of which had been burned. "She just wanted to warm herself," some said. No one knew about the beautiful visions she had experienced or the glorious place she had entered with her grandmother on New Year's Day.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Deep in the ocean, where the water sparkles like a stunning cornflower and is as clear as glass, it stretches far below the surface; so deep that even the longest cable couldn’t reach. This is where the Sea King and his subjects live. It's not just empty yellow sand down there; incredible flowers and plants thrive, their soft leaves swaying gracefully in the water, almost as if they were alive. Fish, big and small, glide through the branches of these underwater gardens, much like birds fly through trees on land. In the deepest part of the ocean stands the Sea King's castle, built from coral with crystal-clear amber windows. Its roof is made of shells that open and close with the flow of water, each holding a beautiful pearl, perfect for a queen's crown.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: The Sea King had been alone for years, with his wise old mother managing the household. She took immense pride in her noble status, wearing twelve oysters on her tail while others of her rank only wore six. She was highly regarded, especially for caring for the six little sea princesses, her granddaughters. Among them, the youngest was the most beautiful. Her skin was as delicate as a rose petal and her eyes were as blue as the deepest sea; just like her sisters, she had no feet, with her body ending in a fish's tail. All day, they played in the grand castle halls or among the vibrant flowers that grew from the walls. The amber windows were always open, allowing fish to swirl in freely, just like swallows do when we open our windows; but these fish swam to the princesses, eating from their hands and letting themselves be petted. Outside the castle, a stunning garden bloomed with bright red and deep blue flowers, fiery blossoms, and golden fruit; the sandy ground sparkled blue as if lit by burning sulphur. A unique blue light surrounded it all, looking as if the air from above had spilled into the deep sea. On calm days, the sun peeked through the waves, shining like a purple flower with rays illuminating the surroundings. Each of the young princesses had their own little garden plot where they could plant whatever they wanted. One shaped hers into a whale, another made hers look like a mermaid, but the youngest chose a round design, filled with flowers as red as the setting sun. She was a curious child, quiet and thoughtful; while her sisters delighted in treasures from shipwrecks, she preferred her vibrant red flowers and a beautiful marble statue of a handsome boy that had sunk to the ocean floor. She planted a lovely rose-colored willow beside the statue, which flourished and soon draped its branches over it, nearly touching the blue sands below. The shadow it cast added a violet hue and swayed like a playful dance, as if the tree and its roots were trying to kiss. Nothing caught her attention more than stories of the world above the sea. She often asked her grandmother about the ships, towns, people, and animals. It amazed her that the land flowers had fragrance while those under the waves did not, that trees stood tall and green in forests, and that birds sang sweetly among them.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"When you turn fifteen," her grandmother told her, "you’ll get to swim to the surface and sit on the rocks in the moonlight, watching the grand ships pass by; then you’ll see forests and towns for yourself."

The next year, one of her sisters would be fifteen; but since they were born a year apart, it would be five more years before the youngest got to rise to the surface and see the world as humans do. Each sister promised to tell the others all about their first visit and what they thought was the most beautiful sight. Their grandmother could only say so much, and they were eager to learn all they could. Nobody longed for her turn to arrive as much as the youngest, who had the longest wait and the quietest disposition. For many nights, she would stand by the open window, gazing up through the dark water, watching the fish flicker their fins and tails. She could see the moon and stars shining faintly, appearing larger than they did to human eyes. When a shadow crossed her view, she knew it was either a whale swimming overhead or a ship full of people entirely unaware that a little mermaid was beneath them, reaching out her delicate hands toward their vessel.

As soon as the eldest turned fifteen, she was allowed to rise to the ocean's surface. When she returned, she couldn't stop talking about her experience; she said the most beautiful part was lying in the moonlight on a sandbank, near the shore, gazing at the nearby town filled with twinkling lights like stars. She could hear music, the sound of carriages, voices of humans, and the jingle of church bells ringing. Since she couldn’t get near any of those marvelous sights, it only made her want them more. The youngest sister listened carefully to every word, and afterward, when she stood at the open window gazing up through the dark water, she imagined the lively city with all its noise, even trying to hear the church bells echoing down under the waves.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The following year, it was the second sister’s turn to visit the surface. She swam up just as the sun was setting, claiming that it was the most stunning sight of all. The sky was golden, while violet and pink clouds floated overhead in ways she couldn’t even describe. A flock of wild swans flew swiftly toward the sunset, looking like a flowing white veil across the ocean. She swam after them, but soon the sun dipped beneath the waves, and the rosy colors faded from both clouds and sea.

Next was the third sister’s turn, who was the boldest of them all. She swam up a broad river that led out to the ocean. On the banks, she saw green hills covered in vines and palaces peeking out from the proud trees of the forest. There were birds singing, and the sun's rays were so strong that she often had to dive back under the water to cool her flushed face. In a narrow creek, she discovered a group of young children playing naked in the water. She wanted to join them, but they fled in fear. Then a little black creature came near— a dog, though she didn’t recognize it. The barking frightened her, and she dashed back to the open sea. Yet she would never forget the stunning forest, green hills, and the children who swam without fish tails.

The fourth sister was more reserved and stayed in the heart of the ocean, claiming it was just as beautiful there. She could see for miles, and the sky appeared like a glass bell. She spotted ships, but they looked like seagulls from such a distance. Dolphins frolicked in the waves, while enormous whales shot water from their blowholes, making it seem as if fountains erupted everywhere.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The fifth sister’s birthday was in winter, so when her turn arrived, she witnessed things her sisters hadn’t seen on their first outings. The sea looked green, with enormous icebergs floating by, each reminiscent of pearls but larger and grander than any human church. Their shapes were unusual, sparkling like diamonds. She perched upon one of the largest icebergs, letting the wind tousle her long hair, and noticed that all the ships sailed quickly by, steering clear of the iceberg as if they were afraid of it. As evening approached, dark clouds overtook the sky; thunder rumbled, and lightning crackled, casting a red glow on the bobbing icebergs. The sails of the ships were reefed nervously as she sat on the iceberg, watching the blue lightning dance across the sea.

At first, when the sisters rose to the surface, they were all thrilled by the new and beautiful sights. But now that they were grown-up, they could go whenever they wanted, and their excitement faded. They wished to return to the depths, and after a month, they found it much more beautiful below and preferred to be home. Yet, in the evenings, the five sisters would hold onto each other and rise to the surface, swimming in a line. They sang more beautifully than any human could, and before storms, when they sensed a ship was in trouble, they would swim to the vessel, singing sweetly about the joys of the deep sea, urging sailors not to fear what lies below. But the sailors couldn’t understand their song; they thought it was just the howling wind. Sadly, these moments were never intended to be beautiful for them since shipwrecks meant drowning, and only the lifeless bodies reached the palace of the Sea King.

When the sisters swam together in this way, the youngest remained behind, watching them and nearly crying, though mermaids couldn’t shed tears, so their suffering was deeper. "Oh, if only I were fifteen," she lamented, "I just know I would love the world up there and all the people living in it."

Finally, she reached her fifteenth birthday. "You’re all grown up now," her grandmother said, placing a wreath of white lilies in her hair, each petal embedded with half a pearl. The old lady also had eight large oysters attached to the tail of the princess to show her royal status.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"But they hurt so much," the little mermaid said.

"Pride comes with pain," replied the old lady. She eagerly wished to shake off the heavy crown of pride and lay aside the beautiful wreath! The red flowers in her own garden would have suited her much better; but there was nothing she could do. So, she said, "Goodbye" and floated gracefully to the surface. Just as the sun set and she lifted her head above the waves, the clouds blazed with crimson and gold, and as the evening star shimmered in the twilight, the sea was calm, and the air felt fresh. A large, three-masted ship lay idle on the water, with only one sail up, as the sailors lounged on deck, enjoying the stillness. Music and laughter filled the air, and as night fell, a hundred colorful lanterns were lit, making it look as if the flags of all nations were waving in the breeze. The little mermaid swam close to the cabin windows. Every time the waves lifted her, she peered through the glass panes and saw beautifully dressed people inside. Among them was a young prince, the most handsome of all, with striking dark eyes. He was sixteen years old, and tonight they were celebrating his birthday with a grand festivity. The sailors danced on deck, and when the prince emerged from his cabin, hundreds of rockets shot up into the sky, lighting everything up like day. The little mermaid was so taken aback that she dove underwater; when she resurfaced, it felt as if all the stars were falling around her, she had never seen such fireworks before. Great suns of fire illuminated the night, radiant fireflies floated into the twilight, all reflected in the calm sea below. The ship was so brilliantly lit that every person, even the tiniest rope, was distinctly visible. And how handsome the young prince looked as he smiled at everyone, warmly greeting those around him while music played through the night sky.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: The night grew late, yet the little mermaid couldn't tear her gaze from the ship or the beautiful prince. The lanterns had been extinguished, the rockets stopped bursting, and the booming cannons fell silent; but the sea became restless, a gentle groaning sound could be felt under the waves. Yet, the little mermaid stayed by the cabin window, bobbing up and down on the water to keep looking in. After a while, the sails unfurled quickly, and the grand ship continued its journey; soon, however, the waves rose high, dark clouds gathered, and lightning flashed in the distance. A dreadful storm was on the way. The sails were reefed again, and the great ship sped onward over the furious sea. The waves swelled high enough to topple the mast, but the ship soared across them like a swan, rising and falling on their frothy crests. To the little mermaid, the crashing waves were exhilarating; the sailors, however, were terrified. Suddenly, the ship creaked and groaned as thick planks fractured under the force of the crashing sea, the mainmast snapped like a twig, and the ship leaned dangerously to one side, with water rushing in. The little mermaid realized the crew was in danger; she had to be cautious of the beams and wreckage floating around her. At one point, it was pitch dark, and she couldn’t see anything until a flash of lightning illuminated the scene; in that moment, she spotted everyone aboard except for the prince. She had seen him sink when the ship broke apart, and was glad he was with her; but she quickly remembered that humans couldn’t survive underwater, so when he reached her father’s palace, he would be dead. But he must not die. So she swam around the beams and planks floating on the surface, forgetting that they could crush her. Dipping deep beneath the tumultuous waves, she rose and fell with the currents until finally, she reached the young prince, who was losing strength in that stormy sea. His limbs had gone weak, and his beautiful eyes were closed; he would have drowned had it not been for the little mermaid, who held his head above water and let the waves carry them where they wanted.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: By morning, the storm had passed, but not a single piece of the ship could be seen. The sun rose, glowing red over the water, its light returning color to the prince's cheeks, but his eyes remained shut. The mermaid kissed his smooth forehead and brushed his wet hair back; to her, he resembled the white marble statue from her garden, and she kissed him again, wishing for his survival. Soon they spotted land; she observed towering blue mountains crowned with white snow, resembling a flock of swans lying upon them. Beautiful green forests lined the coast, and beside them stood a large building, whether a church or convent she couldn't tell. Orange and lemon trees flourished in the garden, and tall palms stood proudly near the door. The sea formed a quiet bay here, deep and still; she swam with the handsome prince to the shore, which was covered with fine, white sand, and laid him gently in the warm sunshine, raising his head above his body. Then she heard bells ringing from the large white building, and soon several young girls entered the garden. The little mermaid swam further from the shore and tucked herself between some tall rocks that jutted out from the water; she covered her head and neck with sea foam so her face wouldn’t be seen and waited to see what would happen to the unfortunate prince. It didn’t take long for a young girl to come to where he lay. She appeared startled at first, but only for a moment; then she ran back to gather more people, and the mermaid watched as the prince came back to life and smiled at those surrounding him. But he offered her no smile; he didn’t even know she had saved him. This hurt her heart, and when he was led away into the large building, she sorrowfully dove back into the water, returning to her father’s castle. She had always been the quiet and thoughtful one, and now she was even more so. Her sisters asked her about her first visit to the surface, but she wouldn’t tell them anything. Many evenings and mornings, she rose to the spot where she left the prince. She saw the fruits in the garden ripen and later get harvested, the snow on the mountaintops melt away, but she never laid eyes on the prince again, so she returned home, increasingly sad each time. Her only comfort was to sit in her little garden, throwing her arms around the lovely marble statue that resembled him. She stopped caring for her flowers, and they grew wildly over the paths, wrapping their long leaves and stems around the trees' branches, making the whole space dark and dreary. Eventually, she could endure it no longer and confided in one of her sisters. Before long, all the others knew her secret, and it spread quickly among two mermaids, as one of their friends happened to know who the prince was. She had witnessed the festivities on the ship and told them where the prince lived and where his palace was.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Come, little sister," said the other princesses, and they linked their arms together, rising in a long row to the surface where the prince’s palace stood. It was made of shiny yellow stone, with a staircase leading all the way down to the ocean. Beautiful gilded domes rose above the roof, and between the pillars surrounding the structure stood lifelike marble statues. Through the crystal-clear tall windows, noble rooms could be seen with lavish silk curtains and tapestry adorning the walls, painted with scenes that were a joy to behold. In the center of the largest hall, a fountain shot sparkling jets high up into the glass dome of the ceiling, illuminated by sunlight shining down on the water and the beautiful plants surrounding the fountain’s basin. Now that she knew where he lived, the little mermaid spent many evenings and countless nights near the palace, swimming closer to the shore than any of her sisters dared. Once, she even swam beneath the narrow channel of the marble balcony, which cast a broad shadow on the water. Here she would sit, watching the young prince, who believed himself completely alone in the moonlight. Many times she saw him sailing in a lovely boat, flags waving and music playing. She would hide among the green reeds, and if the wind caught her silvery-white veil, people often mistook it for a swan spreading its wings. Many nights, when fishermen were out with their torches, she would listen to their stories of the young prince, and her heart would swell with happiness, knowing she had saved his life when he nearly drowned. She remembered how his head had rested on her chest and how she had kissed him; but he had no idea of all this, not even a dream of her. She grew more fascinated by humans and longed to walk among them, feeling that their world was so vast compared to her own. They could sail over the sea in ships and climb high mountains that reached beyond the clouds; their lands, woods, and fields stretched far outside her sight. There was so much she wanted to understand, but her sisters couldn’t answer all her questions. So, she decided to ask her old grandmother, who knew everything about the world above the ocean, which she reasonably referred to as the lands above the sea.

"If humans aren’t drowned," the little mermaid asked, "can they live forever? Do they never die like we do in the sea?"

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Yes," her grandmother replied, "they do die, and, in fact, their lives are shorter than ours. We can sometimes live for three hundred years, but when we perish here, we just become foam on the surface of the water, and we don’t even get to rest in graves beside those we love. We don't have immortal souls and can never return; just like green seaweed, once it’s cut, it can never grow again. Humans, however, have souls that live forever, even after their bodies turn to dust. They rise through the clean, pure air beyond the sparkling stars; just as we come up from the water to see all the land of earth, so do they ascend to unknown, glorious realms that we will never see."

"Why aren’t we given immortal souls?" the little mermaid mournfully asked. "I would gladly give up the hundreds of years I have to live just for one day as a human, and the hope of knowing the happiness of that glorious world beyond the stars."

"You shouldn’t dwell on that," her grandmother stated. "We feel happier and more fortunate than humans."

"So I will die," the little mermaid declared, "and become the seafoam, never to hear the waves’ music again, or see the lovely flowers or the red sun. Is there nothing I can do to gain an immortal soul?"

"No," the old woman replied, "unless a man loves you so deeply that you mean more to him than his own parents, and if all his thoughts and love are directed toward you, and a priest joins your hands in marriage, then his soul would join with yours, granting you a share of the happiness that resides in humanity. He could give you a soul and keep his own too; but this is something that can never happen. To us, your fish tail is considered beautiful, but to humans, it’s ugly. They think they have to have two sturdy legs to be seen as attractive."

The little mermaid sighed and looked mournfully at her fish tail. "Let us enjoy ourselves," urged the old woman, "and swim and play during the three hundred years we have to live, which is surely long enough; after that, we can rest all the better. This evening, we have a royal ball to attend."

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
It’s a magnificent sight, one we could never witness on land. The walls and ceiling of the grand ballroom were made of thick, yet transparent crystal. Hundreds of colossal shells, some deep red, others bright green, lit up the whole hall with blue flames, casting light through the walls so that the sea sparkled as well. Countless fish, large and small, swam past the crystal barriers; some glimmered with purple brilliance, while others shone like silver or gold. A wide stream flowed through the hall, and within it, mermen and mermaids danced to their melodious singing. No one on earth could rival their beautiful voices. The little mermaid sang sweeter than them all, and the entire court applauded her with tails and hands. For a moment, her heart felt exhilarated, for she knew she had the loveliest voice among all in the sea and on land. But soon, she thought once more of the world above her, unable to forget the charming prince or her sorrow over not having an immortal soul like him. Therefore, while the entire palace was filled with joy and song, she quietly slipped away from her father's palace and sat alone in her garden, laden with sadness.

There, she heard the bugle calling through the water, believing, "He must be sailing above me, the one whom my hopes depend on, in whose hands I want to place my happiness. I’ll risk everything for him, to gain an immortal soul. While my sisters dance in my father's palace, I shall visit the sea witch, of whom I have always been so afraid, yet she may offer me guidance and help."

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The little mermaid exited her garden and took the path toward the swirling whirlpools, where the sorceress lived. She had never traveled that way before; nothing grew there, neither flowers nor grass, just barren, gray sand leading to the whirlpool, which churned like a frothy mill wheel, dragging everything it caught into the unfathomable depths. To reach the realm of the sea witch, the little mermaid had to navigate through these fierce whirlpools, and for a fair distance, the only way lay across warm, bubbling sludge, which the witch called her turf moraine. Beyond it stood her dwelling in the heart of a bizarre forest where trees and flowers resembled polypi—half animals, half plants—growing like serpents with a multitude of heads. Their branches acted as long, slimy arms, with fingers as flexible as worms, stretching out to snatch anything that passed by. The little mermaid was alarmed by the sight, stopping momentarily with her heart pounding, nearly turning back; but thinking of the prince and the human soul she yearned for rekindled her courage. She tied her long hair around her head, so it wouldn’t be seized by the polypi, crossed her arms over her chest, and darted forward through the water like a fish, navigating between the grasping arms of the terrible polypi. Each one clasped something it had snatched with its many tiny arms, gripping them like iron chains. The white skeletons of those who perished at sea tangled with the bones of land creatures, oars, rudders, and ship chests lay firmly held by their clutches; but the image of a little mermaid they had captured and suffocated horrified the young princess the most.

Now she arrived at a marshy clearing within the woods, where hefty water snakes writhed in the mud, showing off their ugly, drab bodies. In this spot stood a hut built from the bones of shipwrecked souls. Within sat the sea witch, feeding a toad from her mouth like someone might feed a pet bird with sugar. She referred to the ugly water snakes as her little chicks, allowing them to crawl all over her.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I know what you seek," the sea witch said. "It's foolhardy of you, but you shall have your wish. Yet, be warned, my lovely princess, it will lead you to sorrow.
