You want to shed your fish tail and gain two legs like the humans on earth so the young prince will fall for you and so you can earn an immortal soul!" Then the witch let out a loud, hideous laugh, causing the toad and the snakes to fall to the ground, writhing. "You're just in time," she continued, "for after sunrise tomorrow, I won't be able to assist you for another year. I will prepare a potion for you that you must swim to shore with before dawn, and sit on the beach to drink it. Your tail will vanish and shrink until it forms what humans call legs. It will hurt immensely, as though a sword were slicing through you. But everyone who sees you will declare you the loveliest human they have ever seen. You’ll maintain the same graceful movement, and no dancer will step as lightly as you, but with each step, it will feel as though you are walking on sharp blades, with blood flowing. If you can endure all that, I will help you."

"I will do it," said the little princess in a shaky voice as she thought of the prince and the immortal soul.

"But think it over," cautioned the witch. "Once you take on human form, you can never return to being a mermaid. You will never be able to go back to your sisters or your father's palace. If you don’t win the prince’s love so that he cancels his love for his parents and dedicates himself entirely to you, permitting the priest to join your hands as husband and wife, then you will never earn an immortal soul. The very first morning after he marries another, your heart will break, and you’ll dissolve into foam on the waves."

"I don't care; I will do it," said the little mermaid, and she turned pale as a ghost.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"But I must be compensated as well," the witch replied. "You possess the sweetest voice of anyone beneath the ocean, and you believe you can enchant the prince with it, but that voice must belong to me. Your most precious gift will be my payment for the potion. My own blood will be mixed in it, making it sharp as a two-edged sword."

"But if you take away my voice," the little mermaid protested, "what will be left for me?"

"You still have your lovely form, graceful walk, and expressive eyes; surely, with these, you can capture a man's heart. Have you lost your nerve? Show me your tongue, so I can cut it off as my price, and then you shall have the potion."

The witch set her cauldron on the fire to brew the magical potion.

"Cleanliness is essential," she said, scrubbing the pot with snakes tied together in a knot; then she pricked her breast, letting black blood drip into the mixture. The steam rising formed horrific shapes, fearsome to behold. Every moment, the witch added something to the pot, and when it boiled, it sounded like the cries of a crocodile. When the magic potion was finally ready, it looked like the clearest water. "Here it is for you," proclaimed the witch. Then, she cut off the mermaid's tongue, rendering her mute, ensuring she could never speak or sing again. "If the polypi attempt to grasp you on your return through the woods," the witch warned, "cast a few drops of the potion upon them, and their arms will tear apart into a thousand pieces." The little mermaid had no need to do this, however; the polypi recoiled in terror at the sight of the shimmering potion shining in her hand like a twinkling star.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
She swiftly maneuvered through the woods and marsh, dodging the raging whirlpools. She glimpsed her father's palace, where the ballroom torches were snuffed out and everyone was asleep; yet she didn’t dare enter, knowing she was now mute and would be leaving them forever, her heart felt like it would shatter. She slipped into the garden, picked a flower from each sister’s patch, kissed her hand a thousand times towards the palace, and then surged up through the dark blue waters. The sun had yet to rise when she caught sight of the prince's palace and swam toward the beautiful marble staircase; the moon was bright and clear. The little mermaid drank the magic potion, and it felt as if a sword pierced her delicate body; she fell into a swoon, appearing lifeless. When the sun arose and beamed down upon the sea, she regained consciousness to a stinging pain, but standing before her was the handsome young prince. He gazed deeply into her coal-black eyes as if enchanted, making her instinctively lower her gaze and realize her fish tail was gone, replaced with a pair of pretty legs and small feet. Yet she had no clothes, so she wrapped herself in her long, thick hair. The prince inquired about her identity and where she came from, and she looked at him with gentle, sorrowful blue eyes but couldn’t utter a word. Each step she took felt as if she were walking on sharp needles or knives, but she endured it willingly, gliding beside the prince like a soap bubble, leaving him and everyone else awestruck by her graceful movements. Before long, she was adorned in lavish silk and muslin garments, becoming the most beautiful being in the palace; yet she remained mute, unable to speak or sing.

Beautiful handmaidens dressed in silk and gold stepped forward to sing for the prince and his royal family; one sang sweeter than all the rest, and the prince applauded, smiling at her. This brought great sorrow to the little mermaid; she longed to remind him how sweetly she had once sung and thought, "Oh, if only he could know that! I've given away my voice forever to be with him."

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The handmaidens next performed enchanting dances, accompanied by delightful music. The little mermaid raised her lovely white arms, standing on the tips of her toes, gliding gracefully across the floor, dancing like no one had ever seen before. With every moment, her beauty shone even brighter, and her expressive eyes appealed more directly to hearts than the voices of the singers. Everyone was captivated, especially the prince, who called her his little foundling; she danced again with enthusiasm to please him, though with every touch of her foot upon the floor, it felt as though she treaded on painful knives.

The prince declared she should stay with him forever, allowing her to sleep by his door on a velvet pillow. He even commissioned a page's outfit for her so she could accompany him on horseback. Together, they rode through fragrant woods, the green branches brushing against their shoulders, while little birds sang among the fresh leaves. They climbed to the peaks of high mountains, and though her tender feet bled with every step, she simply laughed and followed him until they were high enough to see clouds looking like a flock of birds traveling to far-off lands. Whenever night fell in the prince's palace, while everyone else slept, she would sit on the wide marble steps; bathing her feet in the cool seawater brought relief from her burning pain, and she'd think of all those below in the depths.

One night, her sisters swam up, arm-in-arm, singing sorrowfully as they floated above the water. She called to them, and they recognized her, expressing their sadness for her. They returned to that spot every night, and once, she spotted her old grandmother, who hadn’t surfaced for many years, and her father, the Sea King, wearing his crown. They reached out to her, but they didn’t swim close to the land like her sisters.

As the days passed, she fell deeper in love with the prince, and he cared for her as he would for a little child, yet it never crossed his mind to marry her. Unless he declared his love for her, she wouldn’t earn an immortal soul. The morning after he married someone else, she would dissolve into foam.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Do you not love me best of all?" the little mermaid's eyes seemed to ask, as he took her in his arms and kissed her gently.

"Yes, you are precious to me," the prince replied. "You have the kindest heart, and your loyalty is unwavering; you remind me of a young maiden I once saw and will never meet again. I was shipwrecked and cast ashore near a holy temple, where several young women performed rituals. The youngest discovered me and saved my life. I saw her only twice; she is the only one whom I could love; but you are so much like her that you’ve nearly erased her from my mind. She belongs to the holy temple, and my good fortune brought you to me instead; we will never part."

"Ah, he doesn't even know it was me who saved him," thought the little mermaid. "I carried him over the sea to the woods where the temple stands; I waited beneath the waves until humans came to help him. I watched the beautiful maiden he loves more than me;" and she sighed deeply, though she could shed no tears. "He says the maiden belongs to the temple, so she will never return to him. They will never see each other again; I’m beside him every day, taking care of him and loving him, giving up my life for his sake."

Word soon spread that the prince intended to marry the beautiful daughter of a neighboring king; preparations for a grand ship were underway. Although the prince claimed he only planned to visit the king, it was widely believed he was going to meet the princess. A large entourage would accompany him. The little mermaid smiled and shook her head; she understood the prince's thoughts better than anyone else.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I must travel," he told her. "I have to meet this stunning princess; my parents want it, yet they won’t force me to bring her home as my bride. I can’t love her; she isn’t like the beautiful maiden from the temple, who looks so much like you. If I had to choose a bride, I would choose you, my darling mute one, with those expressive eyes." Then he kissed her soft mouth, played with her long flowing hair, and rested his head against her heart, while she dreamt of human happiness and an immortal soul. "You’re not afraid of the sea, my mute love," he said as they stood on the deck of the grand vessel that would carry them to the realm of the neighboring king, narrating stories of storms and calms, of strange fish swimming in the depths, and of divers who had seen them. She smiled at his tales, knowing better than any what wonders hid beneath the waves.

As night fell, when everyone on board was asleep except the helmsman steering the ship, she sat on deck, gazing through the clear water. She thought she could make out her father's palace and there stood her aged grandmother, her silver crown shining, looking through the rushing tide towards the ship. Then her sisters surfaced, gazing at her with sorrowful eyes and wringing their hands. She waved to them, smiling, wanting to share how happy she was; but when the cabin boy came close, her sisters dove underwater, and he just assumed it was merely the foam of the sea.

The next morning, the ship docked in a beautiful harbor belonging to the king the prince had come to visit. Church bells rang jubilantly, and trumpets sounded from high towers; soldiers with vibrant flags and gleaming swords lined the paths they passed. Every day became a celebration, with balls and festivities happening one after the other.

But the princess had not yet appeared. People said she was being raised in a religious house, learning every royal virtue. When she finally appeared, the little mermaid, eager to see if she was truly as beautiful as everyone claimed, had to concede she had never seen someone more perfect. Her skin was delicately fair, and under her long dark eyelashes, her sparkling blue eyes overflowed with purity and truth.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "It was you," the prince said, "who saved my life when I was washed up on the beach," as he wrapped his arms around his blushing bride. "Oh, I am too happy," he said to the little mermaid. "All my dreams are coming true. You will be glad for my happiness, for you have been so devoted to me."

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: The little mermaid kissed his hand, feeling as if her heart were breaking. His wedding morning would mean her death, and she would dissolve into foam. Bell chimes sang throughout the streets, and heralds rode around the town announcing the engagement. Perfumed oils burned in elegant silver lamps on every altar. The priests waved their censers, while the bride and groom held hands and received the bishop's blessing. The little mermaid, dressed in luxurious silk, held up the bride's train; yet she heard none of the festive music, nor saw the sacred ceremony. Instead, she thought of the impending death that awaited her and all she’d lost in the world. That evening, the bride and groom boarded the ship; cannons boomed, flags waved, and a fine tent of purple and gold had been set up for them in the center of the vessel, adorned with comfortable couches for the couple to rest in that night. The ship, with full sails and a favorable wind, floated swiftly and lightly over the calm waves. As night fell, colorful lamps illuminated, and sailors joyfully danced on deck. The little mermaid could not help but remember the first time she rose from the sea, watching similar celebrations of joy as she joined the dance, moving through the air like a swallow chasing its prey, and everyone cheered for her in awe. She had never danced so beautifully before, but even with all the excitement, her delicate feet felt like they were cutting on sharp knives, and a deeper pain sliced through her heart. She knew this was the last evening she would spend with the prince, the one for whom she had sacrificed her kin and home. She had traded her beautiful voice and endured indescribable pain for him, while he knew nothing of it. This was her last night to breathe the same air as him or gaze at the starlit sky and deep sea; eternal darkness, with no thought or dream, loomed ahead of her. She had no soul, and now there was no hope of earning one. The ship roared with life and laughter until long after midnight; she laughed and danced along with everyone, all the while clutching the thoughts of death within her heart. The prince kissed his beautiful bride, who played with his raven hair, until they went arm-in-arm to rest in the splendid tent. Then silence fell upon the ship; only the helmsman remained awake at the helm. The little mermaid leaned her pale white arms over the edge of the boat, gazing toward the east, waiting for the first hint of dawn, that first ray of light that would bring her demise. She spotted her sisters rising from the sea; they appeared just as pale as her; yet their long hair waved no longer in the wind, having been cut off.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"We have given our hair to the witch," they said, "so we could get help for you, to ensure you don’t die tonight. She gave us this knife; here, see how sharp it is. Before the sun rises, you must pierce it into the prince's heart; when the warm blood falls upon your feet, they will stick together again and form back into a fish tail. You can once again be a mermaid and return to live out your three hundred years before you die and turn into salt sea foam. Hurry, either he must die, or you must die before sunrise! Our old grandmother is grieving for you, so deeply she is losing her white hair, just like ours fell to the witch's scissors. Kill the prince and come back, hurry: can’t you see the first streaks of red in the sky? The sun is about to rise, and you must die." And with that, they sighed deeply, mourning, and sank back beneath the waves.

The little mermaid drew back the curtain of the tent and saw the beautiful bride resting her head against the prince’s chest. She bent down and kissed the prince’s forehead, then gazed at the sky, which was turning rosy with dawn. Then she glanced at the sharp blade and focused her gaze again on the prince, who murmured his bride's name in his dreams. The prince was thinking of her, and the little mermaid’s hand trembled around the knife; in one swift motion, she flung it deep into the waves. The water turned crimson where it sank, and the splashes that erupted looked like blood. She cast one final, lingering glance at the prince, then leaped from the ship into the ocean, believing her body was dissolving into foam. The sun broke over the waves, and its warm rays touched the cold foam of the little mermaid, who did not feel she was dying. She saw the bright sun above and around her were hundreds of ethereal beings; she could see through them to the ship's white sails and the red clouds in the sky; their voices sang melodiously, but their words were too delicate for mortal ears, as they were also invisible to human eyes. The little mermaid realized she had a body like the ones around her, and she continued to rise high from the foam.

title: THE LITTLE MERMAID
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"You are among the daughters of the air," one of them answered. "A mermaid doesn’t have an immortal soul and can’t obtain one unless she secures the love of a human. A person's fate rests on the heart of another. However, the daughters of the air, even though they do not possess an immortal soul, can earn one through their good deeds. We fly to warm regions and cool the sultry air that brings disease to mankind. We carry the fragrances of flowers to restore health. After we have worked hard for three hundred years doing good, we receive an immortal soul and partake in human joy. You, poor little mermaid, have tried with all your might to do good; you have suffered and endured while striving toward the spirit realm. Now, by continuing these good deeds for three hundred years, you, too, may earn your immortal soul."

The little mermaid lifted her glorified eyes toward the sun and felt tears filling them for the first time. On the ship she had left, the prince and his beautiful bride searched for her; sorrow painted their faces as they gazed at the pearly foam, as if they knew she had submerged into the depths. Unseen, she kissed the bride's forehead, fanned the prince gently, and then ascended with the other daughters of the air to a rosy cloud drifting through the ether. "After three hundred years, we will ascend into the kingdom of heaven," she mused. "We may even reach it faster," whispered one companion. "Unseen, we can float through homes where there are children. For each day we find a kind child full of joy, deserving of their parents' love, we shorten our probation by one year. But when we witness a naughty child, we shed tears, and for every tear, a day is added to our time of trial!"

title: THE LOVELIEST ROSE IN THE WORLD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a great queen who had a garden filled with the most amazing flowers from every corner of the world, blooming beautifully in every season. Among them, she had a special fondness for roses, cultivating the most stunning varieties, from the wild hedge-rose with its apple-scented leaves to the gorgeous Provence rose. These roses thrived by the garden walls, climbing around columns and window frames, snaking through the hallways and hugging the ceilings. They came in every color and fragrance.

But despite the garden's beauty, sadness lingered within those walls; the queen was gravely ill, and the doctors feared she wouldn't survive. "There is still one thing that could save her," said one of the wisest among them. "If you can bring her the loveliest rose in the world, one that symbolizes the purest and brightest love, and if it’s delivered to her before her eyes close, she will live."

People from all over rushed to present roses that bloomed in their gardens, but none were the right kind. The flower had to come from the garden of love, but which rose symbolized the highest and purest love? Poets sang of this extraordinary rose, each arguing for the one they believed deserved that honor. Word spread far and wide to every heart that felt love, reaching people of all ages, backgrounds, and circumstances.

"So far, no one has named the flower," said the wise man. "No one has pointed out where it blooms in all its glory. It’s not a rose from the tomb of Romeo and Juliet, or from Walburg’s grave, even though those roses are immortalized in song. It's not one of the roses that sprouted from the brave blood of Winkelreid. The blood that flows from a hero's chest, who dies for his country, is sacred, and his memory is cherished, yet no rose can be redder than that blood. And it's definitely not the magical flower from Science, for which a man sacrifices countless sleepless nights in solitude."

"I know where it blooms," said a joyful mother who came with her beautiful child to the queen's bedside. "I know where the loveliest rose in the world is. It shines on the blooming cheeks of my sweet child, showing the pure and holy love of infancy. When my child awakens from sleep, their eyes open, and they smile at me with such innocent affection."

title: THE LOVELIEST ROSE IN THE WORLD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"This is indeed a lovely rose," said the wise man. "But there’s one that is even lovelier."

"Yes, one far lovelier," chimed in another woman. "I have seen it, and there’s no rose more lofty and pure. But it was white, like the petals of a blush-rose. I saw it on the cheeks of the queen when she took off her golden crown. Throughout the long, weary night, she held her sick child in her arms, weeping, kissing, and praying over them in a way that only a mother truly can in her hour of despair."

"Holy and powerful is the white rose of grief, but it's not the one we're looking for."

"No, the loveliest rose in the world appeared at the Lord's table," said the wise old bishop. "I saw it glow as if an angel's face had descended. A young maiden knelt at the altar, renewing her baptismal vows, her cheeks displaying both white and red roses. She looked up to heaven with all the purity and love of her youthful spirit, embodying the highest and purest love."

"May she be blessed!" said the wise man. "But still, no one has named the loveliest rose in the world."

Just then, the queen's little son entered the room. His eyes were filled with tears, glistening on his cheeks, and he held a large book bound in velvet with silver clasps. "Mother," called the little boy, "please listen to what I’ve read!" He sat beside her and read from the book of Him who suffered on the cross to save everyone, even those who hadn’t been born yet. He read, "Greater love hath no man than this," and as he spoke, a rosy glow spread across the queen's cheeks, and her eyes brightened. She could see a beautiful rose springing to life from the pages, a symbol of the one who shed His blood on the cross.

"I see it," she said. "Whoever sees this, the loveliest rose on earth, shall never die."

title: THE MAIL-COACH PASSENGERS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
It was freezing outside, the sky sparkled with stars, and there wasn't a single breeze. "Bump"—an old pot was tossed at a neighbor's door; and then came the loud "bang, bang" of fireworks, marking the arrival of the New Year. It was New Year's Eve, and the church clock struck midnight. "Tan-ta-ra-ra, tan-ta-ra-ra!" sounded the horn as the mail-coach lumbered into view. The awkward vehicle came to a stop at the town gate; all the seats were filled, as there were twelve passengers in the coach.

"Hurrah! Hurrah!" cheered the townspeople; every house was celebrating the New Year. As the clock chimed, they rose with their glasses in hand, toasting to success for the new arrival. "A happy New Year!" they exclaimed, "a lovely wife, lots of money, and no worries or troubles."

The toast circulated, and the glasses clinked together with cheerful sounds; meanwhile, the mail coach halted in front of the town gate with its twelve curious passengers. Who were these newcomers? Each of them carried a passport and their luggage; they even had gifts for me, for you, and for everyone in town. "Who are they? What do they want? What have they brought with them?"

"Good morning!" they called out to the sentry at the town gate.

"Good morning!" the sentry replied, as the clock struck twelve. "What is your name and profession?" he asked the first passenger who stepped down from the carriage.

"Check the passport if you want to know," he answered. "I am who I am;" and he looked quite impressive in his bear-skin coat and fur boots. "I’m the person many folks have high hopes for. Come to me tomorrow, and I’ll give you a New Year’s gift. I toss shillings and pennies to the crowd; I host balls—up to thirty-one of them!—that’s the max I can throw. My ships often get stuck in ice, but my offices are nice and cozy. My name is JANUARY. I’m a merchant, and I usually bring my accounts with me.”

title: THE MAIL-COACH PASSENGERS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Then the second passenger hopped out. He appeared quite cheerful. He was a theater director, a masked ball organizer, and the mastermind behind all sorts of entertainment. His luggage was a big barrel.

"We’ll dance the bung out of this barrel during carnival!" he announced; "I’ll have a festive tune ready for everyone, including myself. Unfortunately, my time is short—the shortest of my entire family—just twenty-eight days. Sometimes they grant me an extra day, but I don’t really worry about it. Hurrah!"

"You need to tone it down," said the sentry.

"Of course I can shout," the man shot back; "I'm Prince Carnival, traveling under the name FEBRUARY."

The third passenger emerged. He looked like a walking diet plan; his nose was held high because he was linked to the 'forty knights' and worked as a weather forecaster. Sadly, that job didn’t pay well, so he promoted fasting instead. He had a tiny bunch of violets pinned to his coat, but they were rather small.

"MARCH, March!" the fourth called out, giving him a light slap on the shoulder. "Don't you smell something? Quick, head to the guard room; they’re offering punch, and that’s your favorite drink. I can already smell it from here. Move it, Master March!" But that wasn’t true; the speaker was just teasing him about his name, trying to make a fool out of him for April—something the fourth often did. He looked very jolly, worked little, and enjoyed long breaks. "If only things were a bit more consistent," he said. "Sometimes I have to be cheerful, other times I’m in a foul mood—depends on the weather. Right now it's all about clouds or sunshine. I’m also a house agent and a funeral planner. I can shift from laughing to crying depending on what’s happening. I’ve got my summer clothes packed in this box, but it would be silly to wear them now. Here I am. On Sundays, I take leisurely walks wearing shoes and white silk stockings, and a fur muff."

title: THE MAIL-COACH PASSENGERS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Next came a lady from the coach. She introduced herself as Miss MAY. She wore a summer dress and overshoes; her dress was a light green, and she had anemones in her hair. The scent of wild thyme she carried made the sentry sneeze.

"Cheers, and God bless you!" she greeted him.

What a pretty sight she was! And such a wonderful singer—not a stage singer or a ballad singer; no, she sang the songs of the woods, wandering through the vibrant green forest, holding concerts just for her own enjoyment.

"Now here comes the lovely lady," announced those in the carriage, as a charming, proud, and delicate woman stepped out. It was Mistress JUNE, a time when people become lazy, happily napping for hours. She hosts a feast on the longest day of the year so her guests have ample time to enjoy all the delicious dishes she has prepared. Indeed, she has her own carriage, yet she chose to travel with the rest to show she’s no snob. But she wasn’t alone; her younger brother JULY accompanied her. He was a chubby young man, dressed for summer and sporting a straw hat. He didn’t have much luggage because carrying things in the heat was tricky, but he did bring along his swimming trunks, which were easy to manage. Then came their mother, Madame AUGUST, dressed in crinoline. She was robust and somewhat hot, a wholesale fruit dealer and owner of several fish ponds and farms. She worked hard but was always ready to serve a cool drink to the field workers. "If you want to eat bread, you need to work for it," she would say; "it’s written in the Bible." After work came leisure: dancing and enjoying the greenery during harvest time—she was a true homemaker.

title: THE MAIL-COACH PASSENGERS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Following her was a man—the painter known as SEPTEMBER. He was the master of colors, and when he arrived, the forest transformed in shades of red, gold, and brown. He could beautify everything with his amazing palette. He had a talent for whistling like a blackbird and quickly wound the tendrils of the hop plant around his beer jug, making it a lovely decoration. That was all he carried: a color pot. Then came a landowner—Squire OCTOBER, who paid attention to sowing seeds and enjoyed field sports. He brought his dog and his hunting rifle, even packing nuts in his game bag. "Crack, crack!" he exclaimed. He had plenty of gear, including an English plow. He chattered about farming, but it was hard to hear him over the coughing of his neighbor, NOVEMBER, who stepped out with a severe cough, using a handkerchief repeatedly. Yet he said he had to help servant girls settle in their new jobs and initiate them into their winter duties. He believed his cold would linger when he was out chopping wood since he was the main supplier for the entire parish. He spent his evenings crafting wooden soles for skates, saying he knew those would be needed soon for winter fun. Finally, the last passenger emerged—old Mother DECEMBER, with her cozy fire-stool. She was very elderly, but her eyes sparkled like two stars. She carried a flower pot with a small fir tree. "I’ll nurture this tree," she said, " so it grows tall by Christmas Eve, reaching the ceiling. It will be adorned with vibrant candles, golden apples, and tiny decorations. The fire-stool will be warm as a stove, and I will pull out a storybook and read until all the kids are quiet. Then, the little trinkets on the tree will come to life, and the golden-winged angel on top will fly down, kissing everyone in the room, big and small—even the children outside singing about the 'Star of Bethlehem.'"

"Well, now the coach can leave," said the sentry. "We have all twelve aboard. Let’s get the horses settled."

"First, I want all twelve to come to me," instructed the captain on duty, "one by one. I’ll keep their passports here. Each one is valid for a month; once that’s up, I’ll note how each behaved on their passport. Mr. JANUARY, please step forward." And Mr. January moved ahead.

When the year comes to a close, I think I'll be able to tell you what the twelve passengers left for you, for me, and for everyone. But for now, I don't know, and maybe even they don’t, because times are quite unusual.

title: THE METAL PIG
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In Florence, not too far from Piazza del Granduca, there’s a small street called Porta Rosa. Right in front of the market where vegetables are sold, stands a brass pig, shaped in a unique way. Its once bright color has faded to a dark green with age, but fresh water flows from its shiny snout, as if it had just been polished. Countless poor people and children grab it with their hands, bringing their mouths close to drink from it. It's quite a sight to see a half-naked boy hugging this well-shaped creature, pressing his rosy lips against its mouth. Anyone visiting Florence can easily find this spot; they just need to ask the first beggar they meet about the Metal Pig.

It was late on a cold winter evening; the mountains were blanketed in snow, but the moon shone brightly overhead. In Italy, moonlight feels different, lifting us above the earth, unlike the heavy, gloomy skies of the north. In the grand duke's palace garden, beneath one of the wings where a thousand roses bloom even in winter, a little ragged boy had been sitting all day. He was a boy who represented Italy—beautiful and smiling, yet still in pain. He was hungry and thirsty, but nobody gave him anything. When darkness fell and the gardens were about to close, the porter kicked him out. He lingered on the bridge crossing the Arno, gazing at the glittering stars reflected in the water below, looking between himself and the elegant marble bridge Della Trinita. Eventually, he walked to the Metal Pig, knelt beside it, wrapped his arms around its body, and pressed his mouth against the shiny snout, drinking deep gulps of fresh water. Nearby lay a few salad leaves and two chestnuts that would serve as his dinner. The street was empty except for him; it belonged only to him, so he boldly climbed onto the pig's back, leaned forward to rest his curly head on it, and before he knew it, he fell asleep.

title: THE METAL PIG
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
At midnight, the Metal Pig gently raised itself, and the boy distinctly heard it say, "Hold on tight, little boy, because I'm about to run!" And off they went on a magical ride. First, they reached Piazza del Granduca, where the metal horse bearing the duke's statue neighed loudly. The painted coats-of-arms on the old council house glimmered like translucent pictures, and Michelangelo's David seemed ready to toss his sling. It was as if everything had come to life. The metallic figures, including Perseus and the Rape of the Sabines, looked like real people, and their terrified cries echoed across the grand square. By the Palazzo degli Uffizi, where the nobility gather for carnival, the Metal Pig paused. "Hold tight," the pig said, "I’m going upstairs."

The boy didn’t say a word; he felt both excited and scared. They entered a long gallery, a place he had been before. The walls sparkled with paintings; statues and busts stood out in bright daylight clarity. But the most breathtaking moment came when a side room door opened; the memories of the beautiful things he had seen there flooded back. Everything sparkled in vibrant colors tonight. There was a stunning statue of a woman, carved beautifully by one of the great masters. Her graceful limbs seemed to move, dolphins danced at her feet, and immortality sparkled in her eyes. Everyone knew her as the Venus de’ Medici. Next to her were statues infused with a lifelike spirit; one depicted a man sharpening his sword, nicknamed the Grinder, while another group of wrestling gladiators prepared for a battle over the goddess of beauty. The boy was dazzled by the glitter; the walls shone with vivid hues, and everything felt like living reality.

title: THE METAL PIG
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
As they moved from one hall to the next, beauty surrounded them. With each step the Metal Pig took, the boy absorbed the sights. One stunning piece engraved itself in the boy's mind, especially because it featured happy children he had seen during the day. Many passed this painting without care, yet it held a wealth of poetic emotion, depicting Christ’s descent into Hades. The people in the painting weren’t the lost souls one might expect; they were the ancient heathens. The Florentine artist Angiolo Bronzino had painted this piece, capturing the delightful expressions on the faces of two children who looked convinced they'd eventually reach heaven. They embraced each other, and one child pointed at himself, as if saying, "I’m going to heaven." The older folks stood by, uncertain yet hopeful, bowing in humble adoration before the Lord Jesus. The boy’s gaze lingered on this painting more than any other; the Metal Pig came to a halt before it. A soft sigh was heard. Was it from the painting or the pig? The boy raised his hands toward the smiling children, and then the Pig took off with him, darting through the open vestibule.

"Thank you, thank you, beautiful animal," said the little boy, petting the Metal Pig as they raced down the steps.

"Thanks to you too," replied the Metal Pig; "I’ve helped you, and you’ve helped me. It’s only when I have an innocent child on my back that I gain the power to run. Yes, as you can see, I can even venture under the lamp’s light by the Madonna’s picture. However, I can’t go into the church; I can only peek through the open door while you’re on my back. Stay on a bit longer; if you get off, I’ll turn lifeless, just like you’ve seen me at Porta Rosa."

title: THE METAL PIG
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "I’ll stay with you, my dear friend," the little boy said. Then they sped through the streets of Florence until they reached the square in front of the Santa Croce church. The doors swung open, and brilliant light poured from the altar into the empty square. A radiant blaze illuminated one of the monuments in the left aisle, and a thousand moving stars seemed to create a halo around it; even the coat-of-arms on the gravestone shone, and the red ladder on a blue field glinted like fire. It was Galileo’s grave. The monument may have been plain, but the red ladder symbolized art, suggesting that the path to glory climbs a bright ladder, with the great minds rising to heaven like the prophet Elijah. In the right aisle of the church, every statue on the ornately carved sarcophagi appeared alive. Here was Michelangelo; there was Dante with a laurel wreath; Alfieri and Machiavelli; for here rested Italy's greats side by side. The church itself was stunning, even more so than the marble Cathedral of Florence, though it was smaller. It felt as if the carved vestments stirred with life, as if the marble figures wanted to raise their heads, gazing at the beautifully colored, glowing altar, where young boys swung golden censers, amidst music and song, while sweet incense filled the air and floated out into the square. The boy reached out his hands toward the light, and just then, the Metal Pig took off again so fast that he nearly lost his grip. The wind whipped past his ears, and he heard the church door creak as it closed behind them. For a moment, it felt like he had lost his senses—a shiver ran through him, and he awoke.

title: THE METAL PIG
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Morning had come; the Metal Pig was back in its old spot at Porta Rosa, and the boy found he had nearly slipped off its back. Fear gripped him as he thought of his mother; she had sent him out the day before to get some money, which he had failed to do, and now he was starving and thirsty. He hugged the metal pig’s neck, kissed its nose, and nodded his farewell. Then he wandered into one of the narrowest streets, barely wide enough for a donkey to pass through. An enormous iron-bound door stood ajar. He slipped through and climbed a brick staircase, its walls filthy and adorned with a rope for a banister, until he reached an open gallery filled with rags. Steps led down to a courtyard where water was drawn up by iron rollers for the multiple stories of the building. Water buckets dangled side by side, sometimes splashing water into the courtyard when they danced in the air. Another crumbling staircase branched off from the gallery when two Russian sailors barreled down almost knocking the boy over, bleary-eyed from their night out. A woman who looked middle-aged, with a harsh face and a lot of black hair, trailed behind them. "What did you bring home?" she asked when she spotted the boy.

"Please don’t be angry," he begged. "I didn’t get anything; I have nothing at all!" He clutched his mother’s dress and leaned in to kiss it. Then they entered a cramped little room. No need to describe it; just know there was an earthen fire pot, called a marito in Italy, which she took into her lap to warm her hands and then nudged the boy with her elbow.

"You must have some money," she insisted. The boy began to cry, and in frustration, she kicked him, causing him to cry out louder.

"Will you be quiet? Or I’ll break that screaming head of yours!" she shouted, swinging the fire pot she held while the boy crouched down and wailed.

Just then, a neighbor entered, also carrying a marito under her arm. "Felicita," she said, "what are you doing to the child?"

title: THE METAL PIG
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"The child is mine," Felicita shot back. "I can do whatever I want with him, and that includes you too, Giannina." And she brandished the fire pot. Giannina raised hers for defense, and the two pots clashed so hard that they shattered, sending flames and ashes flying around the room. The boy bolted at the sight, dashed across the courtyard, and escaped from the house. The poor child ran until he was breathless, finally stopping at the church whose doors had opened for him the night before. Inside, everything was bright, and he knelt by the first tomb on his right, Michael Angelo’s grave, sobbing as if his heart would break. People came and went, mass was held, but nobody noticed him—except for an elderly man who paused for a moment to regard him before going on his way, just like everyone else. Hunger and thirst overwhelmed the boy, and he began to feel faint and ill. Eventually, he crawled into a corner behind the marble monuments and fell asleep. As evening approached, he was awakened by a tug at his sleeve; the same old man stood in front of him.

"Are you sick? Where do you live? Have you been here all day?" he asked, throwing a series of questions at the boy. After hearing his story, the man took him home to a small house nearby, located in a side street. They entered a glovemaker's workshop, where a woman was busy sewing. A little white poodle, so closely shaved that its pink skin was visible, frolicked around the room, jumping all over the boy.

title: THE METAL PIG
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Innocent souls bond quickly," the woman remarked, as she affectionately petted both the boy and the dog. They kindly offered him food and drink, saying he could stay with them for the night, and that the next day, the elderly man, named Giuseppe, would talk to his mother. They made a cozy little bed for him, which felt like a royal bed to one who’d often slept on cold stones, and he drifted off to sleep, dreaming of the magnificent paintings and the Metal Pig. The next morning, Giuseppe left, and the boy felt a pang of sadness when he saw him go, knowing he was headed back to his mother, bringing the chance he might have to return home. He wept at the thought, then played with the lively little dog, kissing it, while the old woman watched kindly—trying to reassure him. And what news did Giuseppe bring back? At first, the boy couldn’t quite hear, as Giuseppe conversed with his wife, who smiled and gently stroked the boy’s cheek.

Then she said, "He’s a good lad; he can stay with us. He might become a skilled glovemaker like you. Look how delicately he sews; it seems Madonna intended him for this trade." So the boy settled in, learning how to sew from the woman. He enjoyed good meals, restful nights, and became quite cheerful. But soon he began to playfully annoy Bellissima, the little dog. This behavior frustrated the woman, causing her to scold him and threaten him, leaving him feeling very upset. He would retreat to his room, filled with sadness. This room overlooked the street where skins hung to dry and had thick iron bars across his window. That night, as he lay awake, he found himself thinking about the Metal Pig; it lingered in his thoughts. Suddenly, he thought he heard faint footsteps outside, going pit-a-pat. He jumped out of bed and rushed to the window. Could it be the Metal Pig? But nothing was there; whatever he had heard had already passed. The next morning, their neighbor, the artist, went by, carrying a paintbox and a large roll of canvas.

title: THE METAL PIG
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Help the gentleman carry his box of colors," the woman told the boy, and he eagerly obeyed, taking the box and following the painter. They walked until they reached the picture gallery, climbing the same staircase he had gone up that night with the Metal Pig. He recalled all the statues and paintings, especially the beautiful marble Venus, and once again admired the Madonna with the Savior and St. John. They stopped in front of the Bronzino painting where Christ stands in the lower world, surrounded by smiling children, all eagerly awaiting to enter heaven; the boy smiled too, for he saw his own heaven here.

"You can go home now," the painter said as the boy watched him set up his easel.

"Can I watch you paint?" asked the boy. "Can I see you put the picture on this white canvas?"

"I won’t be painting just yet," the artist replied, then he took out a piece of chalk. His hand moved swiftly, while his eye measured the grand picture, and although only a faint outline appeared, the figure of the Savior was already as clear as in the colored painting.

"Why don’t you go home?" the painter asked. The boy meandered home silently, sitting at the table, trying to learn to sew gloves. All day long, his mind was still in the gallery, which made him clumsy as he pricked his fingers. However, he did avoid teasing Bellissima. When night came and the house door was open, he slipped out into a bright, beautiful, starlit evening, although it was rather cold. He walked through the now-deserted streets until he reached the Metal Pig, bent down to kiss its shiny nose, and then climbed onto its back.

"You wonderful creature," he said, "how I’ve missed you! We need to take a ride tonight."

But the Metal Pig lay still, with fresh water bubbling from its mouth. The boy remained on its back until he felt something tugging at his clothes. He looked down to see Bellissima, the smooth-shaven little dog, barking as if to say, "Here I am too; why are you just sitting there?"

title: THE METAL PIG
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Nothing could have scared the little boy more than that little dog in this moment. "Bellissima out in the street and not dressed!" as the old lady termed it; "what could happen next?"

The dog never went outside in winter without wearing the little lambskin coat made especially for her. It was fastened around her neck and body with red ribbons and decorated with rosettes and tiny bells. Dressed like that, she almost resembled a little kid when she trailed after her mistress. Now, however, she stood out in the cold with no coat on. How could this end well? All his thoughts quickly faded, yet he kissed the Metal Pig one last time, picked up Bellissima, and ran home, trying to warm her.

"What are you running off with?" two police officers asked when they spotted him and the dog, who was barking in protest. "Where did you steal that pretty dog?" they demanded, taking her from him.

"I didn’t steal her; please give her back!" the boy cried hopelessly.

"If you didn’t steal it, you can tell your family to call the watch-house for the dog," they informed him, then directed him to where the watch-house was before walking away with Bellissima.

This caused an immense problem. The boy was torn between jumping into the Arno or returning home to confess everything. Fear of being killed by his mother flooded his thoughts.

"Well, I would gladly face death," he thought; "for at least then I’d die and go to heaven." And so, he made his way home, almost wishing for an end.

The door was locked, and he couldn’t reach the knocker. With no one else in the street, he picked up a stone and banged it loudly against the door.

"Who’s there?" someone shouted from inside.

"It’s me," he replied. "Bellissima is gone. Please open the door and kill me."

Panic erupted inside. Madame loved Bellissima dearly. She immediately checked the wall where the dog’s coat usually hung, and there it was—the little lambskin.

title: THE METAL PIG
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Bellissima is in the watch-house!" she exclaimed. "You naughty boy! How did you lure her out? Poor delicate thing, with those rough policemen! She’ll freeze!"

Giuseppe rushed off right away while his wife lamented, and the boy cried. Several neighbors joined, including the painter. He sat the boy on his lap, asking him questions, and through his broken sentences, he soon learned the entire story, including the Metal Pig and the magical journey to the gallery, which was rather hard to believe. Nevertheless, the painter comforted the little boy and tried to calm the lady’s anger, though she wouldn't be soothed until her husband returned with Bellissima, who had been in the custody of the police. Then there was great joy, the painter patted the boy on the head and gifted him a bunch of pictures. Oh, what beautiful pictures they were!—figures with whimsical heads, and among them was the Metal Pig too! Nothing was more delightful. With just a few strokes, the pig appeared on the paper, and even the house behind it was sketched out. Oh, if only he could draw and paint! Those who could do this could summon all the world before them. The next moment he had free, the boy grabbed a pencil and, on the back of one of the earlier drawings, tried copying the Metal Pig. And he succeeded! Though it was a bit crooked, with one leg thick and the other thin, it looked like a pig, and he was thrilled with his effort. He discovered the pencil wouldn’t behave exactly as he wanted, but the next day he tried again. A second pig appeared next to the first, and it looked a hundred times better. His third attempt was so good that it was easily recognizable.

Meanwhile, his progress in glovemaking was slow. The orders from local shops weren’t completed quickly because the Metal Pig had shown him that anything could be drawn on paper; Florence itself was a picture book waiting to be explored. On Piazza della Trinita, a tall pillar stood with the goddess of Justice, blindfolded and holding her scales. He soon captured her on paper, and it was the glovemaker’s boy who made her image come to life. His collection of drawings grew, but so far, they were merely sketches of inanimate objects. One day, Bellissima bounded into view: "Stay still," he called, "and I’ll draw you beautifully to add to my collection."

title: THE METAL PIG
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
However, Bellissima wouldn’t cooperate, so he decided to tie her in place. He lashed her head and tail together, but she yelped and leaped around, pulling on the string until it nearly choked her; just then, her mistress walked in.

"You naughty boy! You’re hurting the poor little creature!" she exclaimed.

She shoved the boy away, kicked him with her foot, called him ungrateful and lazy, and forbade him from ever entering the house again. Then she burst into tears, kissing her nearly-strangled Bellissima. At that moment, the painter stepped into the room.

In 1834, an exhibition took place at the Academy of Arts in Florence. Two paintings displayed side by side attracted many viewers. The smaller one depicted a little boy sitting at a table, drawing; before him was a little white poodle, closely shaved, but because it wouldn’t stay still, it had been tied with strings on both ends to keep it in position. The vividness and life captured in this painting drew everyone in. They said the young painter was a Florentine who had been found on the streets as a child and taken in by an old glovemaker. This artist had taught himself to draw; rumors said that a now-famous painter had discovered his talent just when he was about to be punished for having tied up Madame’s favorite little dog as a model. The glovemaker's boy had become an accomplished painter, as the artwork demonstrated, but the larger painting beside it was an even greater testament to his talent—it showed a handsome boy in rags, asleep, leaning against the Metal Pig in the street of Porta Rosa. All the crowd recognized that spot well. The child's arms were wrapped around the Metal Pig's neck while he slept soundly. A lamp situated before the Madonna’s picture cast a warm, impactful light on the pale, delicate face of the boy. It was a stunning piece. A large gilded frame surrounded it, and on one corner, a laurel wreath had been hung; however, a black ribbon, subtly intertwined within the green leaves, and a stream of mourning cloth dangled from it, for within the last few days, the young artist had—died.

title: THE MONEY-BOX
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In a playroom filled with scattered toys, a money-box perched on top of a tall wardrobe. Shaped like a pig and crafted from clay, it was bought from the potter. The pig had a slit in its back, which had been widened with a knife to allow dollars and coins to slip through; in fact, it held two dollars and quite a few pennies. The money-pig was so stuffed that it could no longer rattle, which is the ultimate state of perfection for a money-pig. There it stood, high and proud, looking down at everything else in the room. It knew very well that it had enough money inside to buy all the other toys, which made it think very highly of itself. The other toys recognized this fact too, though they didn't vocalize it since there were so many other happenings to discuss. A large doll, still beautiful despite her age and a few repairs on her neck, rested in one of the open drawers. She called out to the others, "Let’s play a game where we become men and women; that sounds like real fun!"

This suggestion created a lively uproar; even the framed engravings on the wall turned around in excitement, unintentionally showing their backs, though they had no intention of revealing themselves this way or contesting the game. It was late, but with the moonlight streaming through the windows, they had plenty of light to play. As the game was about to begin, everyone was invited to join in, even the children's wagon, which was definitely among the rougher toys. "Each of us has our own role," said the wagon. "Not everyone can be aristocrats; some have to do the work!"

title: THE MONEY-BOX
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The money-pig was the only one to receive a written invitation. He was placed so high that they worried he wouldn’t hear a verbal one. In response, he declared that if he had to participate, he would enjoy watching the fun from his own spot; they would have to arrange that for him, and they did. They set up a little toy theater so the money-pig could look right in. Some wanted to start with a comedy and follow it with a tea party and a discussion for mental growth, but they began with the discussion instead. The rocking horse talked about training and races; the wagon chimed in about railways and steam power, as these topics were part of their professions and made sense to discuss. The clock discussed politics—"tick, tick;" he claimed he knew what time it was, but whispers suggested he wasn’t always accurate. The bamboo cane stood nearby, looking stiff and proud, showing off its brass end and silver top, and two pretty yet dim cushions lay on the sofa. When the performance at the toy theater commenced, the rest of the toys sat and watched, asked to cheer and clap, or crack when they enjoyed what they saw. But the riding-whip remarked that he wouldn't crack for old toys, only for the young ones who weren't married yet. "I crack for everyone!" exclaimed the cracker.

"Yes, and it’s quite a noise you make," the audience thought as the play continued.

title: THE MONEY-BOX
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: While it wasn’t spectacular, it was performed well, with all the characters showing their painted sides to the audience, since they were made to be admired from only one angle. The acting was brilliant, except sometimes they stepped outside the lights because the wires were a bit long. The doll with the mended neck was so thrilled that the stitching gave way, and the money-pig decided he should do something for one of the performers since they had all entertained him so well. He thought he would remember one of them in his will to be buried with him in the family vault whenever that time came. They all enjoyed the comedy so much that they forgot about the tea party and only considered their idea of intellectual fun, which they called playing at being men and women; there was nothing wrong with it since it was just play. Throughout it all, each toy mostly thought of itself or pondered what the money-pig could be thinking. The pig contemplated what seemed like a far-off moment—drafting his will, thinking about his burial, and when that might happen. However, it was sooner than he expected—suddenly, he fell from the top of the wardrobe, crashing to the ground and shattering into pieces. The coins danced around joyfully. The smaller ones twirled like tops, while the larger ones rolled away as far as they could, particularly the big silver crown piece, who was eager to explore the world, and now he could, just like the others. The remains of the money-pig ended up in the dustbin, and the next day a new money-pig appeared on the cupboard, but it was empty inside; like the old one, it couldn't rattle. This was just the start for him, and we’ll leave it here for our story.

title: THE NEIGHBOURING FAMILIES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
You might think something significant was happening at the duck pond, but it turned out to be nothing important at all. The ducks, lazily floating on the water or turning upside down, suddenly darted to the edges. Their little feet left marks in the wet earth, and their quacking echoed all around. The water, which just moments before had been clear and still like a mirror, became quite agitated. All the beauty surrounding it—the trees, nearby bushes, the old farmhouse with its leaky roof, the swallows' nest, and especially the vibrant rosebush in full bloom—was reflected in the water. The rosebush draped over the wall and hung over the water, creating a scene that looked like a painting, even if it was upside down. But when the water disturbed, everything was mixed up, and that pretty picture was lost. Two feathers, lost by the flapping ducks, floated up and down. They started to move rapidly as if a breeze was coming, but since it didn’t, they settled still again, and the water calmed down. The roses reflected once more, looking lovely, though they were unaware of their own beauty since no one had told them. The sun shone through the delicate leaves; a delightful fragrance filled the air, and everything felt like that blissful joy we experience when thinking of our happiness.

"How beautiful life is!" each rose exclaimed. "If only I could kiss the sun, since it's so warm and bright! I’d like to kiss those roses in the water because they’re just like us, and the lovely little birds in the nest nearby too. There are some up above as well; they peek out and chirp softly, without any feathers like their mom and dad. We have wonderful neighbors, both below and above us. How beautiful life is!"

The little ones above and below—those below were really only reflections in the water—were all sparrows. Their parents were sparrows too, having taken over last year's empty swallows’ nest, now treating it like their own.

"Are those the ducklings paddling around?" the young sparrows asked when they spotted the feathers on the water.

title: THE NEIGHBOURING FAMILIES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"If you’re going to ask questions, make them sensible," their mother replied. "Can’t you see those are feathers, just like mine and soon to be yours? Ours are much finer. Still, I’d love to have those feathers in the nest; they’d keep us warm. I really wonder what startled the ducks so much; it surely wasn’t us, even though I did chirp loudly to you. Those thick-headed roses should know, but they don’t; they just look at themselves and smell good. I’m so tired of such neighbors."

"Listen to those sweet little birds up there," said the roses. "They’re starting to want to sing, but they can’t quite manage it yet. Soon they will, and what a joy that must be! It feels lovely to have such cheerful neighbors."

Suddenly, two horses came galloping up to drink. A farmer boy rode one, wearing nothing but a large black hat. He whistled like a bird and rode straight into the deepest part of the pond. As he passed the rosebush, he plucked a rose and tucked it into his hat. Now he looked all dressed up and continued on. The other roses looked after their sister, asking each other, "Where could she be going?" But none of them had any idea.

"I wish I could see the world just once," said one rose. "But I also love it here among our green leaves. The sun shines brightly and warmly all day, and at night, the sky seems even more beautiful; we can see it through all the little holes."

They meant the stars, but they didn’t know any better.

title: THE NEIGHBOURING FAMILIES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"We bring life to the house," the mother sparrow said; "and people say a swallows’ nest brings good luck, so everyone’s happy to have us. But our neighbors! A rosebush like that on the wall causes dampness. I bet it will be removed, and then we might have some corn growing here. Those roses are good for nothing but looking pretty and smelling good, or at most, being stuck in a hat. Every year, I’ve heard from my mother, they fall off. The farmer’s wife preserves them, sprinkling salt on them; then they get a fancy French name that I don't even know how to pronounce, and they’re put in the fire to make a lovely smell. That’s their life; they exist just for looks and scent. Now you know."

In the evening, as the gnats danced in the warm air and the red clouds glowed, the nightingale arrived and sang to the roses, telling them that beauty was like sunshine to the world and that beauty lasts forever. The roses thought the nightingale was singing about itself, and one could easily believe it; they had no idea the song was about them. But they enjoyed it and wondered if all the little sparrows could grow up to be nightingales.

"I understand that bird's song quite well," said the young sparrows. "There was just one word that was unclear. What does ‘the beautiful’ mean?"

"Nothing at all," replied their mother. "That's just something superficial. Up at the Hall, where the pigeons have their own house and get corn and peas every day—I’ve dined with them myself, and you will too, in time; for tell me who you associate with, and I’ll tell you who you are—up at the Hall, they have two birds with green necks and crests on their heads; they can spread their tails like a big wheel, and they shine with such bright colors it almost hurts your eyes. Those birds are called peacocks, and that's ‘the beautiful.’ If they were plucked a little, they’d look just as ordinary as the rest of us. If they weren’t so big, I would have plucked them already."

title: THE NEIGHBOURING FAMILIES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I’ll pluck them!" chirped the young sparrow, who still didn’t have any feathers.

In the farmhouse lived a young couple; they loved each other dearly and worked hard, making their home look lovely. Every Sunday, the young wife would get up early, pick a handful of the most beautiful roses, and place them in a glass of water on the cupboard.

"Now I see it’s Sunday," said the husband, kissing his little wife. They sat down, read their hymn book, and held hands while the sun shone on the fresh roses and on them.

"This sight is just so dull," said the sparrow mother, who could see into the room from her nest; and she flew away.

The same thing happened the following Sunday; fresh roses were added to the glass each week. But the rosebush bloomed just as beautifully as before. The young sparrows had now grown feathers and wanted desperately to fly with their mother, but she wouldn’t allow it, so they remained home. However, one day, while she was flying, she got caught in a horsehair net some boys had set up in a tree. The horsehair tightened around her leg, almost cutting it off; she was in excruciating pain and terror. The boys came running and seized her, not exactly gently.

"It’s just a sparrow," they said, but they didn’t let her go; instead, they took her home with them, and every time she cried, they hit her on the beak.

In the farmhouse lived an old man who knew how to make soap into cakes and balls for both shaving and washing. He was a cheerful old man, always wandering about. When he saw the sparrow that the boys had brought—claiming they didn’t want her—he asked, "Shall we make her look very pretty?"

Those words sent a chill through the sparrow-mother.

From his box, filled with beautiful colors, the old man took out shiny leaf-gold, while the boys went to fetch some egg whites to smear all over the sparrow. The gold was stuck to her, and now she shimmered all over. But she, trembling all over, didn’t think about the adornment. Then the soapman tore off a small piece from the red lining of his old jacket, cut it to resemble a rooster's comb, and stuck it on the bird's head.

title: THE NEIGHBOURING FAMILIES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Now you’ll see the golden sparrow fly," said the old man as he released her, and she flew away in sheer fright, glimmering in the sunlight. All the other sparrows, even an old crow—who was quite the character—were shocked by the sight, but they followed her to see what kind of strange bird she was.

Driven by fear and panic, she flew back homeward; she felt like she was going to faint as the flock of birds chasing her grew larger, some even trying to peck at her.

"Look at her! Look at her!" they all exclaimed.

"Look at her! Look at her!" cried her little ones when she approached the nest. "That must be a young peacock, glimmering in so many colors that it makes your eyes ache; peep! That’s ‘the beautiful.’" And they pecked at her so much that she couldn’t even squeeze into the nest; she was so worn out that she couldn’t even manage a peep, let alone say, "I’m your mother!" The other birds also swarmed in, plucking off feather after feather until she collapsed, bleeding, into the rosebush.

"Poor thing!" said all the roses. "Just be quiet, and we’ll hide you. Lean your little head against us."

The sparrow spread her wings once more, then brought them close to her body and lay dead beside the neighboring family, the beautiful fresh roses.

"Peep!" came a sound from the nest. "Where can our mom be for so long? I can’t understand it. It can’t be a trick of hers, meaning we’ve got to take care of ourselves now. She left us the house as an inheritance; but when we start families of our own, to whom will it belong?"

"It won’t do for you to stay with me when I have my own family," said the smallest.

"I bet I’ll have more wives and kids than you," said the second one.

"But I’m the oldest!" shouted the third. Then all of them got worked up; flapping their wings and pecking with their beaks, they pushed one another out of the nest. They ended up lying on the ground, backing up with their heads cocked to one side, blinking the eye that was turned upward. That was their way of looking silly.

title: THE NEIGHBOURING FAMILIES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
They could fly a little; through practice, they improved. Eventually, they agreed on a signal to recognize each other if they met again in the world. It was to be a single "Peep!" along with three scratches on the ground with their left foot.

The young one who remained behind in the nest puffed himself up as much as he could, thinking he was the owner. But this moment of grandeur didn’t last long. That night, red flames burst through the window, seizing the roof; the dry straw caught fire, and the whole house—including the young sparrow—was consumed in the blaze. The other two brothers, who wanted to marry, were fortunate enough to escape.

When the sun rose again, everything looked refreshed as if it had had a peaceful sleep. All that remained of the farmhouse were a few blackened beams leaning against the chimney, which now stood as its own master. Thick smoke still rose from the ruins, but the rosebush remained blooming, fresh, and untouched, with every flower and twig beautifully reflecting in the clear water.

"How beautifully the roses bloom in front of the ruined house," exclaimed a passerby. "It’s such a lovely sight; nothing could be better. I must capture that!" The man pulled a little book with blank pages from his portfolio; he was a painter and sketched the smoking house, the charred beams, and the leaning chimney, while in the foreground, he depicted the large, blooming rosebush that provided a charming view. For its sake alone, the whole picture was drawn.

Later that day, the two sparrows born there passed by. "Where’s the house?" they asked. "Where’s the nest? Peep! Everything is burned; our strong brother too! Look at what he’s gotten for keeping the nest. The roses came out fine; they’re still here with their red cheeks. They surely don’t mourn for their neighbors' misfortunes. I don’t want to talk to them; it looks miserable here—that’s just my opinion." And they flew away.

On a beautiful sunny autumn day—one could almost believe it was still the middle of summer—a bunch of black, white, and colorful pigeons were strutting around in the neatly swept courtyard before the main entrance of the Hall, all glimmering in the sunlight. The pigeon mothers were telling their young ones: "Stand in groups, stand in groups! It makes for a much better sight."

title: THE NEIGHBOURING FAMILIES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"What kind of creatures are those little gray ones scampering behind us?" asked an old pigeon with colorful eyes. "Little gray ones! Little gray ones!" she cried.

"They’re sparrows, and they’re good creatures. We’ve always had a reputation for being kind, so we’ll let them peck up the corn with us; they don’t interrupt our chatter and scrape the ground so nicely when they bow."

Indeed, they kept making three scratches with their left foot and chirped, "Peep!" This was how they recognized each other, for they were the sparrows from the nest on the burned house.

"This is great food!" said one of the sparrows. The pigeons strutted around one another, puffing up their chests, having their own opinions.

"Did you see that pouter pigeon?" said one to another. "Look how she gobbles up the peas! She eats too many, on top of that, the best ones! Curoo! Curoo! Look how she puffs herself up, that ugly, spiteful creature! Curoo! Curoo!" And everyone’s eyes sparkled with malice. "Stand in groups! Stand in groups! Little gray ones, little gray ones! Curoo, curoo, curoo!"

Their chatter continued on, and it would keep going for thousands of years. The sparrows pecked happily away; they listened intently and even joined in the line with the others, but it didn’t suit them at all. Feeling full, they left the pigeons, sharing their thoughts about them, slipped under the garden palings, and when they found the door into the house was open, one of them, feeling more brave because he was a little too full, hopped onto the threshold. "Peep!" he said; "I think I can dare that."

"Peep!" said another; "so can I, and maybe even more!" and he hopped into the room. With no one present, the third sparrow, seeing this, ventured even further into the room, exclaiming, "All or nothing! This curious nest is something else; what have they set up here? What is it?"

title: THE NEIGHBOURING FAMILIES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Close to the sparrows, the roses were blooming; they were reflected in the water, and the charred beams leaned against the overhanging chimney. "What is this? How did this get into a room at the Hall?" they wondered. All three sparrows wanted to fly over the roses and chimney but flew straight into a flat wall. It was all just a painting, a magnificent painting, created from an artist’s sketch.

"Peep!" said the sparrows, "this is nothing. It only looks like something. Peep! That’s ‘the beautiful.’ Do you get it? I don’t."

And they flew away as people entered the room.

Days and years passed. The pigeons often cooed and quarreled—those spiteful creatures; the sparrows grew cold in winter and enjoyed carefree summers. They either got engaged, married, or whatever you want to call it, and each of them thought their own young ones were the most beautiful and clever. One flew this way, another that way, and when they met, they recognized each other through their "Peep!" and the three scratches with their left foot. The eldest remained an old maid without a nest or kids. Her only dream was to see a great city, so she flew to Copenhagen.

There stood a large house painted in vibrant colors beside the castle and the canal, where many ships loaded with apples and pottery passed by. The windows were wider at the bottom than at the top, and when the sparrows peeked through, every room looked like a tulip, bursting with the brightest colors and shades. But in the center of the tulip were white marble men, some made of plaster; yet, to the sparrows, it all seemed the same. Perched on the roof was a metal chariot drawn by metal horses, and the goddess of Victory, also metal, was driving. It was Thorwaldsen’s Museum.

title: THE NEIGHBOURING FAMILIES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"How it shines! How it shines!" said the female sparrow. "I bet that’s ‘the beautiful.’ Peep! But here, it’s larger than a peacock." She still remembered how her mother viewed beauty as the greatest thing when she was a little bird. She swooped down into the courtyard; everything there looked exquisite. Palms and branches adorned the walls, and in the center of the courtyard stood a grand blooming rose tree, its fresh branches laden with roses above a grave. The maiden sparrow flew there, spotting several of her kind. With a "peep" and three scratches on the ground, she often greeted them throughout the year. But no one responded, as those who part ways don't often meet. So, this was a familiar greeting. But today, two old sparrows and a young one answered her with a "peep" and the familiar three scratches."

"Ah! Good day! Good day!" They were two old sparrows from the nest and a little one from the family. "What a grand place! But there isn’t much to eat. This is ‘the beautiful.’ Peep!"

Many people emerged from the side rooms where the stunning marble statues stood and gathered around the grave of the great master who created these masterpieces. All had enraptured faces around Thorwaldsen's grave, and a few picked up fallen rose leaves, preserving them. They had traveled from afar: one from mighty England, and others from Germany and France. The most beautiful ladies plucked one of the roses, hiding it in their bosoms. The sparrows thought the roses ruled here and that the house was built for them. That seemed too much for them, but since everyone displayed affection for the roses, they didn’t want to be left out. "Peep!" said they, sweeping the ground with their tails and blinking their eyes at the roses. Not long after, they were convinced that they were their old neighbors. And so they truly were. The painter who had illustrated the rosebush near the ruined house later got permission to dig it up and gifted it to the architect, for never had there been finer roses. The architect planted it on Thorwaldsen's grave, where it bloomed as a symbol of 'the beautiful' and produced fragrant red rose leaves to be taken as mementos to distant lands.

"Have you found a place here in the city?" asked the sparrows. The roses nodded; they recognized their gray neighbors and were happy to see them again. "How wonderful it is to live and bloom, to see old friends and cheerful faces every day. It feels like every day is a festival." "Peep!" said the sparrows. "Yes, they truly are our old neighbors; we remember where they came from by the pond. Peep! How well they’ve done! Some find success even in their sleep. Oh! I see a faded leaf; I can clearly spot that." And they took to pecking at it until it fell off. But the tree remained fresher and greener than ever; the roses blossomed gloriously under the sun on Thorwaldsen's grave, forever linked to his extraordinary legacy.

title: THE NIGHTINGALE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In China, the emperor was Chinese, and everyone around him was also Chinese. The story I'm about to share with you happened many years ago, so it's good to hear it now before it fades away. The emperor's palace was the most stunning in the world, constructed entirely of porcelain. It was costly, yet so delicate that anyone who touched it had to be very careful. In the garden, there were unique flowers with pretty silver bells tied to them that tinkled, making them impossible to ignore. Everything in the emperor's garden was extraordinary, stretching so far that even the gardener didn’t know where it ended. Those who ventured beyond knew there was a magnificent forest with tall trees sloping down to the deep blue sea, where grand ships sailed under their shadows. In one of these trees lived a nightingale, whose song was so beautiful that even the poor fishermen would stop to listen, despite their busy lives. Sometimes, when they spread their nets at night, they would hear her sing and say, 'Oh, isn't that lovely?' Yet, when they returned to their fishing, they forgot about the bird until the next night. Then they would hear her again and say, 'Oh, how beautiful is the nightingale's song!'

Travelers from all over the world visited the emperor’s city, amazed by the palace and gardens. However, when they heard the nightingale, they declared it the best of all. Upon returning home, the travelers shared tales of what they had seen; knowledgeable scholars wrote books describing the city, the palace, and the gardens, but the nightingale remained the greatest wonder. Poets composed beautiful verses about the nightingale that lived in a forest by the deep sea. These books traveled worldwide, and some reached the emperor himself. As he sat in his golden chair reading, he nodded in approval at the beautiful descriptions of his city, palace, and gardens. But when he came across the words, 'the nightingale is the most beautiful of all,' he exclaimed, 'What is this? I’ve never even heard of a nightingale. Is there really such a bird in my empire, perhaps even in my garden? Seems like there’s more to learn from books than I thought.'

title: THE NIGHTINGALE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
He then summoned one of his lords-in-waiting, who was so proud that whenever someone of a lower rank spoke to him or asked a question, he would just reply with a dismissive 'Pooh,' which meant nothing.

'There’s talk of an incredible bird called the nightingale,' said the emperor; 'they say it’s the most wonderful thing in my vast kingdom. Why hasn’t anyone mentioned it to me?'

'I’ve never heard of her,' replied the lord. 'She hasn’t been presented at court.'

'I want her to perform this evening,' said the emperor. 'It seems like the whole world knows what I possess better than I do.'

'I’ve never heard of her,' said the lord, 'but I’ll do my best to find her.'

But where could the nightingale be found? The nobleman searched up and down the staircases, through halls and passages, yet none of the people he met had heard of the bird. So he returned to the emperor, declaring it must be a myth invented by the book’s authors.
