'Your imperial majesty,' he stated, 'can’t believe everything in books; sometimes they’re just fiction, or what’s known as black magic.'

'But the book I read was sent to me by the esteemed Emperor of Japan, so it can’t be false. I want to hear the nightingale; she must be here tonight! She is in my highest favor, and if she doesn’t show up, the whole court shall be punished after supper!'

'Tsing-pe!' shouted the lord-in-waiting, and he dashed up and down the stairs again. Half the court ran with him because they didn’t want to face punishment. A great search began for this marvelous nightingale who was known to the whole world but unknown to the court.

Eventually, they stumbled upon a poor girl in the kitchen, who said, 'Oh, yes, I know the nightingale very well; she sings beautifully. Every evening I get to take scraps from the table home to my sick mother, who lives by the seashore. On my way back, I feel tired, so I sit in the woods to rest and listen to the nightingale's song. It brings tears to my eyes, as if my mother is kissing me.'

title: THE NIGHTINGALE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
'Little girl,' said the lord-in-waiting, 'I will find you a permanent job in the kitchen, and you can watch the emperor dine if you lead us to the nightingale, for she is invited to the palace tonight.' The girl happily led them into the woods where the nightingale sang, with half the court trailing behind her. As they went, a cow began to moo.

'Oh,' said a young courtier, 'now we've found her! What power such a tiny creature has; I feel like I’ve heard this before.'

'No, that's just a cow mooing,' said the little girl. 'We’re still a ways from her.'

Then they heard frogs croaking in the marsh.

'Beautiful,' the young courtier said again. 'Now I hear it, tinkling like little church bells.'

'No, those are just frogs,' the little girl replied. 'But I think we’ll hear her soon,' and, almost in an instant, the nightingale began to sing.

'Listen, listen! There she is,' said the girl, 'and look, there she sits!' She pointed to a little gray bird perched on a branch.

'Is it possible?' asked the lord-in-waiting. 'I never imagined it would be such a plain little thing. She must have changed color because of all the grand people around her.'

'Little nightingale,' the girl called out, raising her voice, 'our gracious emperor wishes to hear you sing.'

'I’d love to!' said the nightingale, and she began to sing beautifully.

'It sounds like tiny glass bells,' the lord-in-waiting remarked, 'and look how her little throat moves. It's amazing that we’ve never heard this before; she'll be a big hit at court.'

'Should I sing again for the emperor?' asked the nightingale, thinking he was present.

'My wonderful little nightingale,' said the courtier, 'I’m delighted to invite you to a court festival tonight, where your charming song will earn the emperor’s favor.'

title: THE NIGHTINGALE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
'I sing best in the green woods,' said the bird, but she came willingly when she heard it was the emperor's wish.

The palace was beautifully decorated for the event. The porcelain walls and floors gleamed under the light of a thousand lamps. Gorgeous flowers adorned the corridors, their little bells tinkling so loudly that no one could hear each other speak. In the center of the grand hall, a golden perch was set for the nightingale. The entire court was in attendance, and the little kitchen girl had permission to stand by the door, though she wasn't officially a court cook. Everyone was dressed to the nines, and as the emperor nodded at the little gray bird to begin, all eyes turned toward her. The nightingale sang so sweetly that tears welled up in the emperor’s eyes and streamed down his cheeks as her song touched everyone’s hearts. Overjoyed, the emperor declared that the nightingale should wear his gold slipper around her neck, but she graciously declined the honor, saying, 'I’ve already been rewarded enough. Seeing tears in an emperor's eyes is my greatest prize. Tears from an emperor hold incredible power and are more than enough honor for me.' Then she sang again, even more enchantingly.

'That singing is a marvelous gift,' the ladies of the court said to one another, as they attempted to imitate the nightingale's gurgling sounds when they spoke, hoping to fancy themselves as nightingales. Even the footmen and maids expressed their delight, which was saying a lot because they were typically very hard to please. Indeed, the nightingale's visit was a great success. She was to remain at court, given her own cage with the freedom to go out twice a day and once at night. Twelve servants were assigned to attend her, each holding a silken thread fastened to her leg. There wasn't much joy in this kind of flying.

The entire city buzzed about the incredible bird. When two people met, one would say 'nightin,' and the other would reply 'gale,' and they understood each other because it was all anyone discussed. Eleven peddlers' children were named after her, but none could sing a note.

title: THE NIGHTINGALE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
One day, the emperor received a large package labeled 'The Nightingale.' 'This must be a new book about our celebrated bird,' he said. However, instead of a book, it contained an artistic creation in a box—an artificial nightingale designed to look like a real one, adorned with diamonds, rubies, and sapphires. Once wound up, the mechanical bird could sing like the real one and move its tail up and down, sparkling with silver and gold. Around its neck was a ribbon that read, 'The Emperor of China’s nightingale pales in comparison to the Emperor of Japan’s.'

'This is stunning!' exclaimed everyone who saw it, and the person who brought the artificial bird was given the title 'Imperial Nightingale-Bringer-in-Chief.'

'Now they must sing together!' said the court, 'What a duet it will be!' But they didn’t harmonize well, as the real nightingale sang in its own style, while the artificial bird only performed waltzes.

'That’s not a flaw,' remarked the music-master. 'It’s perfect in my opinion,' so the artificial bird had to sing solo and was as popular as the real one; plus, it was prettier, glimmering like jewelry. It sang the same tunes over and over, thirty-three times, without tiring; people would gladly have listened more, but the emperor insisted that the living nightingale sing something. But where was she? Nobody noticed when she flew out the open window back to her own green woods.

'What strange behavior!' exclaimed the emperor when her absence was discovered; all the courtiers scolded her, saying she was ungrateful.

'But we still have the best bird!' said one, and they decided to have the mechanical bird sing again, even though it was the thirty-fourth time they heard the same piece, and they still hadn’t learned it because it was rather tricky. However, the music-master praised the artificial bird highly, claiming it was superior to the real nightingale—not only for its appearance and beautiful gems but also for its musical capability. 'You must understand, my chief lord and emperor, that with a real nightingale, we can never predict what it will sing, but with this bird, everything is set. It can be explained and opened, so people can understand how the waltzes are formed and why one note follows another.'

title: THE NIGHTINGALE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
'This is exactly what we believe,' everyone replied, and the music-master was granted permission to showcase the bird to the people the following Sunday. The emperor ordered everyone to attend and hear it sing. When they did, they were like people intoxicated; but it must have been from drinking tea, which is a common Chinese tradition. They exclaimed, 'Oh!' and held up their fingers in excitement, nodding along, but a poor fisherman who had heard the real nightingale said, 'It sounds nice enough, and all the melodies seem the same, yet something is missing; I can’t quite put my finger on it.'

After this, the real nightingale was banished from the empire, and the artificial bird was placed on a silk cushion beside the emperor's bed. Precious gifts of gold and jewels received with it surrounded the bird, which was now given the title 'Little Imperial Toilet Singer,' and promoted to No. 1 on the left side; for the emperor considered the left side, where the heart lies, as the most honorable, seeing that an emperor’s heart is just like everyone else’s.

The music-master wrote a lengthy, complex work, in twenty-five volumes, all about the artificial bird. It was filled with intricate Chinese phrases, yet everyone claimed they had read it and understood it for fear of seeming foolish and facing punishment.

A year passed, and the emperor, court, and all other Chinese knew every bit of the artificial bird's song. Thus, it pleased them even more. They could sing along with the bird, which they did often. Street boys sang, 'Zi-zi-zi, cluck, cluck, cluck,' and even the emperor himself could sing it, making it a great source of amusement.

title: THE NIGHTINGALE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
One evening, while the artificial bird was performing its best, the emperor lay in bed listening, when suddenly he heard a 'whizz' from inside the bird. A spring snapped. 'Whir-r-r-r' went all the wheels, and then the music stopped. The emperor jumped out of bed and called for his physician, but what could he do? They then sent for a watchmaker; after much discussion and examination, the bird was put back into working order, though he warned that it had to be handled carefully, as the mechanics were worn, and replacing them would ruin the music. There was great sadness because the bird could only be allowed to sing once a year, and even that carried risk.

Then the music-master gave a speech filled with complicated words, declaring that the bird was as good as ever; of course, no one contradicted him.

Five years passed, and then true sorrow struck the land. The Chinese genuinely cared for their emperor, who was gravely ill and not expected to recover. A new emperor was already chosen, and those in the streets asked the lord-in-waiting how the old emperor was doing; he merely replied with a dismissive 'Pooh!' and shook his head.

Cold and pale, the emperor lay in his royal bed; everyone at court believed he was dead and hurried away to pay respects to his successor. The chamberlains went out to discuss the matter while the maids invited guests for coffee. Cloths were spread out along the halls and corridors to muffle footsteps, creating an eerie silence. Yet the emperor was not dead; he lay white and still on his lavish bed, with velvet curtains and heavy golden tassels. A window stood open, allowing moonlight to shine in on the emperor and the artificial bird. The emperor struggled to breathe with a strange weight on his chest. He opened his eyes to find Death sitting there, adorned with the emperor’s golden crown, sword of state in one hand, and beautiful banner in the other. Surrounding the bed, peering through the velvet curtains, were a number of strange faces—some ugly, some gentle—representing the emperor’s good and bad deeds, staring into his soul as Death sat at his heart.

title: THE NIGHTINGALE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
'Do you remember this?' they asked one after another, bringing back memories that caused sweat to bead on his brow.

'I don’t recall any of it,' the emperor replied. 'Music! Music!' he shouted. 'The grand Chinese drum! I don’t want to hear what they’re saying.' But they continued on, and Death nodded in acknowledgment. 'Music! Music!' the emperor cried. 'You precious golden bird, sing! I’ve given you gold and precious gifts; I even hung my golden slipper around your neck! Sing! Sing!' But the bird remained silent since no one was there to wind it up, so it couldn’t produce a single note.

Death kept staring at the emperor with his cold, empty eyes, and the room was chillingly quiet. Suddenly, a sweet melody floated through the open window. Outside, on a tree branch, sat the living nightingale. She had heard of the emperor’s illness and had come to sing him a song of hope and trust. As she sang, the shadows seemed to lighten; the blood in the emperor’s veins flowed more vigorously, revitalizing his weak limbs, and even Death paused to listen, saying, 'Go on, little nightingale, continue to sing.'

'Then will you give me the precious golden sword and that rich banner? And will you give me the emperor's crown?' the bird asked.

So Death relinquished each of those treasures for a song, and the nightingale continued her melody. She sang of the peaceful churchyard where white roses bloom, where the elder tree scents the air with its fragrance, and where the fresh, sweet grass is watered by the tears of those mourning. Death longed to see that garden and floated out through the window as a cold, white mist.

'Thank you, thank you, you heavenly little bird. I know you well. I once banished you from my kingdom, yet you’ve enchanted the ugly faces away from my bed and chased Death from my heart with your lovely song. How do I reward you?'

'You’ve already rewarded me,' the nightingale replied. 'I’ll never forget that I made tears flow from your eyes the first time I sang to you. Those are the jewels that bring joy to a singer's heart. But for now, sleep and grow strong and healthy again. I will sing to you once more.'

As she sang, the emperor fell into a peaceful sleep, and it was a gentle, refreshing slumber! When he awoke, feeling restored and strong, the sun shone brightly through the window. Yet not one of his servants returned, as they all believed he was dead; only the nightingale remained by his side, singing away.

'You must stay with me always,' said the emperor. 'You shall sing only when it pleases you, and I shall break the artificial bird into a thousand pieces.'

'No, please don't do that,' the nightingale replied. 'The mechanical bird did well while it could. Keep it here. I can't live in the palace or build a nest, but let me visit when I want. I’ll sit on a branch outside your window in the evening and sing to you, sharing tales of joy and sorrow, of the good and bad hidden around you. The little singing bird travels far from you and your courts to the homes of commoners. I cherish your heart more than your crown; yet there is something sacred surrounding that too. I’ll come and sing for you, but you must promise me one thing.'

'Anything,' said the emperor, as he dressed in his royal robes, hand resting on his heavy golden sword, pressed to his heart.

'I only ask one thing,' she replied; 'let no one know you have a little bird who shares everything with you. It’s best to keep it a secret.' With that, the nightingale flew away.

The servants then entered to attend to the supposedly dead emperor; and to their amazement, he greeted them with a cheerful, 'Good morning!'

title: THE OLD BACHELOR'S NIGHTCAP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In Copenhagen, there's a street with a peculiar name: Hysken Street. The origin and meaning of the name remain quite uncertain. Some believe it to be German, but that seems unfair to the Germans, as it should be called "Hauschen," meaning a little house. For many years, Hysken Street comprised just a handful of tiny houses, hardly larger than the wooden stalls we see during fairs. They might have been slightly taller and had windows, but those windows were made with horn or bladder skins since glass was too expensive for every home back then. This was ages ago—so long that our grandparents and even great-grandparents would refer to it as "olden times." In fact, centuries have gone by since then.

The wealthy merchants from Bremen and Lubeck, who traded in Copenhagen, didn’t live in the city. Instead, they sent their clerks, who stayed in the wooden stalls on Hauschen Street and sold beer and spices. The German beer was quite good, and there were many types—from Bremen, Prussia, and Brunswick—as well as ample varieties of spices like saffron, aniseed, ginger, and especially pepper. Pepper became the main product sold here, so the German clerks in Denmark earned the nickname "pepper gentry." These clerks were conditioned to remain unmarried, which meant that those who grew old had to fend for themselves, tending to their own comforts and even lighting their own fires, if they had any. Many of them were quite elderly—lonely old bachelors with peculiar thoughts and quirky habits. In Denmark, all unmarried men of a certain age are called "pepper gentry," a detail that’s important to remember to understand this tale. These "pepper gentlemen," or as they’re termed in England, "old bachelors," often become the target of mockery. People poke fun at them, telling them to put on their nightcaps, pull them over their eyes, and go to sleep. The boys in Denmark sing a song about it:

"Poor old bachelor, cut your wood, Such a nightcap was never seen; Who would think it was ever clean? Go to sleep, it will do you good."

title: THE OLD BACHELOR'S NIGHTCAP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
So they sing about the "pepper gentleman," making fun of the lonely old bachelor and his nightcap, not realizing the reality behind either. It's a cap that nobody really wants or should mock. But why not? Well, we’ll find out in the story.

Back in the day, Hauschen Street wasn’t paved, and pedestrians would trip from one hole to another, just like they do on unfrequented paths. The street was so narrow that the booths leaned against each other so closely that, during summer, a sail would be stretched across from one booth to another. When this happened, the smells of pepper, saffron, and ginger intensified. Young men were rare behind the counters. Most clerks were old bachelors; however, they didn’t dress like old men from our vision—no wigs, nightcaps, or knee-breeches, and their coats and waistcoats weren’t buttoned up to their chins. We’ve seen portraits of our great-grandfathers looking like that, but the "pepper gentlemen" couldn’t afford portraits, although each of them would have made a fascinating picture in his own right while standing behind the counter, walking to church, or during holidays. On special occasions, they wore high-crowned, broad-brimmed hats, and sometimes younger clerks would add feathers. Their wool shirts were hidden beneath a broad linen collar, their close jackets were buttoned up, and their cloaks hung loosely. Their trousers tucked into tipped shoes, and they typically carried a table knife and spoon in their belts along with larger knives, often necessary for protection.

Anthony dressed similarly on holidays and special occasions, except he wore a kind of bonnet over a knitted cap—his nightly cap—so familiar that it was always perched on his head; he had two of them, just to clarify, not two heads. Anthony was among the oldest clerks—truly a painter’s subject. He was as thin as a lath, with wrinkles around his mouth and eyes, long, bony fingers, bushy gray eyebrows, and a tuft of thick hair hanging over his left eye which didn’t look handsome but made him stand out. People knew he hailed from Bremen, though that wasn’t exactly where he called home—his master was from there. His ancestors came from Thuringia and lived in Eisenach, near Wartburg. Old Anthony rarely spoke about Eisenach, but it lingered in his thoughts often.

title: THE OLD BACHELOR'S NIGHTCAP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: The old clerks of Hauschen Street rarely gathered; each stayed in his booth, which closed early in the evening, making the street appear dark and lonely. Just a faint flicker of light could be seen through the horn panes of their small windows above, while inside, the old clerks typically sat on their beds, softly singing evening hymns, or busying themselves with tasks late into the night. It wasn’t a particularly lively existence. Being a stranger in a foreign land is a hard fate; you become invisible to most unless you’re in their way. Often, during those dark nights, as rain or snow fell, the area seemed deserted and dreary. There weren’t any street lamps except for a small one hanging at one end of the street before a painting of the Virgin on the wall. The sounds of waves crashing against the nearby castle were clear. Such evenings felt endless and gloomy unless you had something to keep you occupied, which Anthony did. There weren’t always items to pack or unpack, bags to create, or scales to polish. So, he found tasks to occupy him: mending his clothes, patching his boots. When he finally went to bed, his nightcap—worn out of habit—stayed snugly on his head, just needing a simple pull to settle it further down over his forehead. Yet, it would soon slide back up as he checked to ensure the light was off; he’d touch it, adjust the wick, and eventually pull the nightcap back down over his eyes again, trying to sleep. Often, however, a worry would arise about whether the tiny fire in the shop below was fully extinguished. Even a tiny spark could start a fire and cause serious damage. So, he would get up from bed, carefully navigate down the ladder—hardly considered a staircase—and find no spark of fire at all. Back to bed he would go, but halfway there he might worry whether the front door was securely fastened, sending his thin legs scurrying down the ladder again. By the time he crawled back into bed, he would be so cold his teeth chattered. He’d pull his coverlet tighter around himself, tuck the nightcap low over his eyes, and try to shift his thoughts away from trade and work, drifting into memories of old times. Yet, that wasn’t exactly a comforting thought; reminiscences often peeled back the curtains of the past, sometimes piercing his heart with painful memories that brought tears to his eyes. And so it went with Anthony; often his hot tears, like sparkling drops, would roll down to his coverlet and plop on the floor, as if a piece of his heart had just cracked. At times, memory would burst forth like a bright flame, illuminating a scene from life that had never faded from his heart. If he dried his eyes with his nightcap, he’d crush both the tear and the memory; yet, the source of his tears didn’t dry up, continuing to overflow from his heart. The images didn’t come in order, as the events took place; often, the most painful ones crowded together, while the joyful ones always cast deep shadows.

title: THE OLD BACHELOR'S NIGHTCAP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Denmark's beech woods are renowned for their beauty, but to old Anthony, the beech woods near Wartburg appeared even more stunning. The majestic old oaks surrounding the grand castle appeared more dignified to him, where creeping plants cascaded over the rocky peaks; even the scent of the apple blossoms there felt sweeter than anywhere in Denmark. Vividly, as a tear rolled down his cheek, he could see a scene of two children playing—a boy and a girl. The boy, with rosy cheeks, golden curls, and bright blue eyes, was the son of Anthony, a wealthy merchant. That boy was him. The girl had chestnut eyes and glossy black hair; she was clever and brave—the mayor's daughter, Molly. The two played with an apple, shaking it to hear the seeds rattling inside. They cut it in half, sharing the pieces, and even divided the seeds, eating all but one, which the little girl suggested they plant in the ground.

"You’ll see something unexpected come from it," she said. "A whole apple tree will grow, but not right away." So, they got a flowerpot, filled it with soil, and soon found themselves excitedly working on it. The boy used his finger to poke a hole in the soil, then the little girl placed the seed in, covering it back over.

"You mustn't check it tomorrow to see if it's taken root," said Molly. "No one should do that. I did that with my flowers, but only a couple of times; I wanted to check if they were growing. I didn’t know any better back then, and all the flowers ended up dying."

Little Anthony kept the flowerpot and carefully checked on it every morning throughout winter, but all he saw was black dirt. Eventually, spring arrived, bringing warm sunlight, and suddenly, two tiny green leaves emerged from the pot.

"They are Molly and me," said the boy. "Aren't they wonderful and beautiful!"

Soon after, a third leaf appeared.

"Who does that represent?" he wondered, as more leaves appeared day by day and week by week until the plant turned into a full tree. All of this about the two children existed in a single tear from old Anthony, which could easily be wiped away but could always return from the depths of his heart.

title: THE OLD BACHELOR'S NIGHTCAP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Neighboring Eisenach features a range of stony mountains, one of which has a rounded summit, bare of trees, bushes, or grass. It’s called "Venus Mountain," and the tale goes that the "Lady Venus," one of the ancient goddesses, resides there. Children from Eisenach know her as "Lady Halle." She’s said to have lured the noble knight, Tannhauser, away from the circle of singers at Wartburg to her mountain.

Molly and Anthony often stood near this mountain, and one day Molly said, "Do you dare to knock and call out, 'Lady Halle, Lady Halle, open the door: Tannhauser is here!'" But Anthony didn’t have the courage. Molly, however, did, even though she only shouted, "Lady Halle, Lady Halle," loudly and clearly; the rest she muttered under her breath, making Anthony think she hadn’t really said anything at all. Yet, she stood boldly, just like she sometimes did in the garden with a group of other girls. They’d all gather around him, wanting to kiss him because he disliked it, pushing them away. But Molly would always feel free to resist him, declaring, "I can kiss him," as she threw her arms around his neck. She reveled in her power over Anthony, for he would let her do it and think nothing of it. Molly was charming yet a bit bold, and how she loved to tease!

They said Lady Halle was beautiful, but her beauty had a devilish allure. Saint Elizabeth, the patron saint of the land, the pious princess of Thuringia, known for her good deeds portrayed in many stories and legends, was more beautiful truly. Her portrait hung in the chapel, surrounded by silver lamps, but she didn’t resemble Molly at all.

title: THE OLD BACHELOR'S NIGHTCAP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The apple tree, which the two children planted, grew year after year until it became so large it needed to be moved to the garden, where the dew fell and the sun shone warmly. There it grew strong enough to withstand winter's cold; after braving the harsh weather, it bloomed in spring as if joyful that winter had passed. In autumn, it bore two apples, one for Molly and one for Anthony—it simply couldn’t do less. The tree thrived, growing rapidly, just as Molly did alongside it. She bloomed like an apple blossom, but Anthony wouldn’t observe this flower for much longer. Time changes everything; Molly's father left their old home, and Molly went with him far away. In our days, it would be a journey of just a few hours, but back then, it took more than a day and a night to travel from Eisenach all the way to a town still known as Weimar on the outskirts of Thuringia. Both Molly and Anthony cried, but their tears merged into one beautiful tear that shimmered with joy. Molly had confessed her love for him—loved him more than all the wonders of Weimar.

One, two, three years went by, and he received just two letters during that time. One arrived with the carrier, and the other was brought by a traveler. The journey was long and complicated, winding through towns and villages. Anthony and Molly often echoed the story of Tristan and Isolda, and Anthony believed it applied to him, even though "Tristan" means born in sorrow, which Anthony certainly wasn’t. He never thought of Molly as forgotten like Tristan did with Isolda. Yet, Isolda hadn’t forgotten her faithful friend; when both were laid to rest, one on each side of the church, the linden trees that grew by their graves intertwined their blossoms together. Anthony found it a beautiful but mournful tale; still, he didn’t fear that fate would befall him and Molly, as he passed by that spot, whistling the tune of a song composed by the minstrel Walter, called "The Willow Bird," which began,

"Under the linden trees, Out on the heath."

title: THE OLD BACHELOR'S NIGHTCAP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Through the forest, and in the vale, Sweetly warbles the nightingale."

This song often played in his mind, and he would whistle it on moonlit nights as he rode toward Weimar to visit Molly. He aimed for a surprise visit, and indeed, that’s how it turned out. He was welcomed warmly and introduced to a plethora of elegant company, where wine flowed freely. A lovely room and a comfortable bed were provided for him, yet the welcome wasn’t what he had envisioned or dreamed. He couldn’t grasp his own feelings or those of others; it’s easy to be let into a home or family without truly becoming part of them. Conversations felt like those with fellow travelers in a stagecoach—fleeting and temporary; knowing little about one another, wishing themselves or others far away. This is how Anthony felt when Molly talked about their childhood.

"I’m a straightforward girl," she stated, "and I’m going to be honest with you. A lot has changed since we were kids; everything feels different inside and out. We can’t manipulate our emotions or the feelings of our hearts through custom. Anthony, I wouldn’t dream of becoming your enemy while I’m far away. Believe me, I hold only kind wishes for you in my heart; but the feelings I now understand for another man can never be yours. You need to come to terms with this. Farewell, Anthony."

Anthony simply replied, "Farewell." Not a single tear fell from his eye; he realized he was no longer Molly's friend. Just like hot and cold iron, which feel the same pain when they touch our skin, Anthony’s kiss transformed from one of affection to one of resentment. Within 24 hours, he returned to Eisenach, though his horse was entirely spent.

"What does it matter?" he thought. "I’m ruined too. I will destroy everything that reminds me of her, or of Lady Halle, or of that heathen goddess. I’ll cut down the apple tree and uproot it; it will never bloom or bear fruit again."

title: THE OLD BACHELOR'S NIGHTCAP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The apple tree wasn't cut down; instead, Anthony was struck with a fever that laid him low in bed. But something brought him back to his feet. What was it? A medicine was offered to him, a bitter remedy that made both his body and spirit shudder. Anthony's father lost all his wealth, going from being one of the richest merchants to very poor. Dark days and heavy trials, with poverty knocking at their door, rolled in like waves crashing on the shore. The sorrow and suffering drained Anthony's father of his strength, forcing him to focus on more than his heartache and anger towards Molly. He had to step up, given orders, act decisively, and eventually venture into the world to earn a living. Anthony traveled to Bremen, where he learned the harsh realities of poverty and hard living. Often, such experiences can harden a person’s character, but they can also soften a heart too much.

How different the world and its people appeared to Anthony now compared to his childhood thoughts! The minstrel's songs? Just echoes of the past, long gone. Sometimes, he would reflect this way, yet frequently the melodies would resonate within him, bringing softness and piety to his heart.

"God's will is best," he would say. "It was fortunate I couldn’t hold onto my power over Molly's heart, and she didn’t remain faithful to me. How I would feel now, with fortune having abandoned me! She left before knowing of the changes in my life or contemplating what lay ahead. That’s a kind providence for me. Everything happened for the best. She couldn’t help it, and yet I’ve remained bitter and filled with resentment towards her."

title: THE OLD BACHELOR'S NIGHTCAP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Years went by: Anthony's father passed, and strangers took up residence in their old house. He saw it again once more after time had passed. His wealthy master sent him on business trips, and one journey took him back to his hometown of Eisenach. The old Wartburg castle stood unchanged upon the rock where the monk and nun had been carved out of stone. The grand oaks framed the scene that he remembered vividly from childhood. Venus Mountain loomed gray and bare, shading the valley below. He wished he could call out, "Lady Halle, Lady Halle, unlock the mountain. I wish to stay here always, in my homeland." That was a sinful thought, and he offered up a prayer to drive it away. Just then, a little bird in the bushes began to sing sweetly, and old Anthony recalled the minstrel's song. So much memory rushed back to him as he looked through tears at his hometown once more. The old house still stood like it did in the past, but the garden had changed significantly; a pathway moved through it, and outside the garden, beyond the path, stood the old apple tree he hadn’t destroyed, despite his earlier thoughts. The sun still shone on it, and the morning dew fell upon it as it always had; it was so laden with fruit that the branches bowed toward the ground under the weight. "That tree still thrives," he remarked, gazing at it. However, one of its branches had broken—unruly hands must have done that in passing since the tree stood in a public area. "Blossoms often get plucked," Anthony said, "the fruit is snatched away, and the branches get broken without gratitude for their abundance and beauty. It could be said about a tree, as it has been said about some men—nobody predicted at its birth that it would come to this. How brightly the story of this tree began, and what is it now? Neglected and forgotten, in a garden by a hedge in a field, and close to a public road. There it remains, exposed, plundered, and broken. It may not have withered just yet, but over the years, the number of blossoms will dwindle, and eventually, it will cease to bear fruit altogether, and with it, its story will end."

title: THE OLD BACHELOR'S NIGHTCAP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Such thoughts filled Anthony’s mind as he stood beneath the tree and during many long nights spent alone in his room on Hauschen Street in Copenhagen, the foreign land to which his Bremen employer had sent him with orders to remain unmarried. "Marry! Ha, ha!" he scoffed bitterly at the idea.

title: THE OLD BACHELOR'S NIGHTCAP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: One winter, it turned frigid early, and a snowstorm kept everyone indoors. This led to Anthony's neighbors across the street not noticing his house remained shut for two days and that he hadn’t shown himself in that time, considering who would venture out in such weather unless absolutely necessary. The days were gray and gloomy, and in a house without actual glass windows, twilight and darkness flickered in waves. During those two days, old Anthony hadn't gotten out of bed, lacking the strength to do so. The harsh weather had taken a toll on his aging limbs. There lay the old bachelor, forsaken by everyone and unable to care for himself. He could barely reach the water jug next to his bed, and now it was empty. It wasn’t fever or illness but old age that had him down. In his small corner where his bed lay, he was overshadowed by perpetual gloom. A little spider, though invisible to him, spun its web over him, creating a little banner waving above when his eyes closed. Time passed slowly and painfully. No tears fell, no pain was felt; not once did memories of Molly cross his mind. The world felt empty to him, as if he was beyond it, unnoticed. Occasionally, he experienced flickers of hunger and thirst, but no one came to tend to him. He thought of all who had once faced starvation, of Saint Elizabeth, who roamed the earth—the saint of his childhood—who cared for the poorest villages, bringing hope and aid to the sick inhabitants. Remembering her kind deeds brought a flicker of light to poor Anthony’s spirit. He envisioned her going about offering comforting words, tending to the wounded, and feeding the hungry, though often criticized for it by her stern husband. He recalled a story about her: once, while carrying a basket filled with wine and provisions, her husband had followed her, fury in his eyes. He stepped forward and angrily demanded to know the contents of her basket, prompting her to tremble as she replied, "Roses, which I picked from the garden." Then he ripped the covering off the basket, and what a surprise it was for the pious woman when everything had magically transformed into roses.

title: THE OLD BACHELOR'S NIGHTCAP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In this way, the memory of that kind lady lingered in Anthony's calm mind. In his small home in Denmark, she felt like a living presence. He uncovered his face, longing to gaze into her gentle eyes, as everything around him shifted from poverty and need to a warm rosy hue. The scent of roses filled the room, mingling with sweet apple aromas. He imagined the branches of the apple tree rising above him. It was the very tree that he and Molly had planted. The fragrant leaves brushed against him, cooling his fevered brow; on his dry lips, they felt like refreshing bread and wine. As they laid gently on his chest, a profound calm descended on him, urging him to sleep. "I will rest now," he murmured to himself. "Sleep will do me good. Tomorrow, I'll be back on my feet, healthy and strong. Glorious! Wonderful! That apple tree, planted with love, now appears in heavenly beauty." And with that, he drifted off.

The next day, on the third day his house had been closed, the snowstorm finally stopped. Then his neighbor stepped over to the house where old Anthony lived, noticing he hadn’t appeared yet. There he lay stretched out on his bed, dead, with his old nightcap tightly clutched in his hands. However, the nightcap wasn't on his head in the coffin; instead, he wore a clean white one. Where were the tears he had shed? What had happened to those precious pearls? They remained in the nightcap, for such tears cannot be washed away—even if the nightcap is forgotten. The old thoughts and dreams tied to the bachelor’s nightcap lingered on. Never wish for such a nightcap; it might make your forehead hot, cause your heart to race with anxiety, and summon dreams that feel all too real.

The first person to don old Anthony's cap felt the truth of this, though it happened half a century later. That man was the mayor himself, who had already built a cozy home for his wife and eleven children through his hard work. The moment the cap graced his head, he dreamt of heartache, bankruptcy, and dark days. "Wow! How this nightcap burns!" he exclaimed, ripping it from his head. Then a pearl rolled out, followed by another, and another—they twinkled and clinked as they hit the ground. "What could this be? Am I losing my mind, or is something dazzling my eyes?" Those were the tears Anthony shed half a century ago.

To everyone who subsequently wore that cap, visions and dreams invaded their sleep, stirring them deeply. Their stories blended with Anthony’s until they became one—and many other stories could arise from this as well, but we will allow them to tell their own tales. We've shared the first; our final word is: never wish for a "bachelor’s nightcap."

title: THE OLD CHURCH BELL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In the countryside of Wurtemburg, Germany, where acacia trees line the public roads and where apple and pear trees bend low with ripe fruit in autumn, you’ll find the small town of Marbach. Like many towns, it’s beautifully positioned along the banks of the Neckar River, which flows swiftly by, passing charming villages, ancient castles, and lush vineyards before joining the grand Rhine. It was late autumn; the vine leaves still clung to their branches but were already glowing with shades of red and gold. Heavy rains poured over the land, and the cold autumn wind blew sharply and fiercely. Not the best weather for those who struggled. The days grew shorter and gloomier, and while it was dreary outside, the inside of the quaint, old houses felt even darker. One such house, with its gabled roof and narrow windows, had a rather shabby look. The family living there was quite poor but held deep faith in God in their hearts. And now, they were about to welcome a child. It was a time of sorrow for the mother when the church tower rang out with festive bells. At that solemn moment, the joyful chimes filled the humble home with gratitude and hope. And when, amid these happy sounds, a little son was born to them, their hearts overflowed with prayers and praises, filling the town and countryside with the enchanting melodies of the church bells. The little one, with bright eyes and golden hair, was joyfully welcomed on that dark November day. His parents kissed him affectionately, and the father wrote in their Bible, "On the tenth of November, 1759, God sent us a son." Soon after his baptism, they added the names he was given: "John Christopher Frederick."

So, what happened to the little boy—the poor lad from the humble town of Marbach? Believe it or not, not even the old church bell, which had first rung for him, expected he would one day compose the beautiful song "The Bell." The boy grew quickly, just as the world kept moving forward.

title: THE OLD CHURCH BELL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
While he was still a child, his family moved away from Marbach to another town. However, their closest friends remained, so on nice days, the mother and her son would visit the little town. At this time, the boy was around six years old and already knew many biblical stories and some religious songs. Sitting in the evening on his little cane chair, he often listened as his father read from Gellert's fables or from Klopstock's grand poem, "The Messiah." He and his older sister often shed tears over the tale of the one who died on the cross for all of us.

On his first visit back to Marbach, the town felt familiar and hardly changed, close enough to not be forgotten. The house with its pointed gables, narrow windows, and overhanging walls still looked the same as before. But in the churchyard, many new graves had appeared; and there, in the grass by the wall, stood the old church bell! It had been removed from its lofty spot due to a crack that prevented it from ringing again, with a new bell taking its place. When the mother and son discovered the old bell while walking in the churchyard, they paused to admire it. She reminded her son of how this bell had served the community for many years, chiming for weddings and christenings, tolling for funerals, and sounding alarms during fires. With every important moment in life, the bell had been there to announce it. She also shared how the bell's chimes had once filled her heart with joy, a joy that coincided with the moment she received her child. The boy gazed at the old bell with great interest. He bowed his head and kissed it, cracked and forgotten, yet standing there amidst the grass and weeds. He never forgot what his mother told him, and the bell's tones echoed in his heart as he grew into adulthood. He grew tall and slender, with a freckled complexion and nearly red hair; but his clear blue eyes, as deep as the sea, held promise for a bright future. He excelled at the military academy, affiliated with a prestigious family, and wore gaiters, stiff collars, and powdered hair that marked his status. He was known by the command, "March! halt! front!"

title: THE OLD CHURCH BELL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The old church bell had long been forgotten, and no one ever thought it would be melted down to be restored. It was as improbable as believing the bell's tones still reverberated in the heart of the boy from Marbach—yet they did, and soon they would resonate loud enough to be heard across the globe. They could already be heard behind the school walls, even above the chaotic shouts of "March! halt! front!" The bell's music chimed so strongly in his heart that he sang it to his friends, causing its sounds to spread to the edges of the country. He wasn't a free scholar at the military school, nor did he have luxury in clothes or food. But he had his number and his own peg; everything was organized like clockwork, which, as we all know, helps everyone get along better when roles and responsibilities are clear. Just like how pressure is needed to form a jewel, this order and discipline shaped the future gem that the world would come to admire.

In the provincial capital, a grand festival was underway. Light poured from thousands of lamps while fireworks shot into the sky, showering the air with colorful sparks. This dazzling display would be remembered, as the military school student was overcome with tears and sadness. He had attempted to escape unnoticed to foreign lands, leaving behind his homeland, his mother, and his dearest friends, or he would be trapped in a dull life. The old church bell, standing forgotten in the church's shadow in Marbach, remained some source of comfort. The wind howled around it and could have shared tales of its history and of its sweet chimes. It could tell of the boy, who, in a nearby forest, collapsed from exhaustion, having nothing but hope for the future and a worn-out page from "Fiesco." The wind might mention how he was supported by an artist who read each page to him and how they played nine-pins together. The wind could also describe the pale boy who spent weeks in a shabby roadside inn where the landlord was often drunk and rambunctious, while the merry-makers ruled. Yet, through it all, he sang of ideals.

title: THE OLD CHURCH BELL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The heart must endure many heavy days and dark nights to withstand trials and temptations; amid it all, the minstrel would sing. Dark days and cold nights also passed over the old bell, which remained unaware. However, the bell in the man’s heart felt the weight of these gloomy times. What would happen to this young man? And what about the old bell?

Eventually, the old bell was taken far away from its home, beyond what anyone, even the bell tower's guardian, could have imagined. The bell in the young man's heart was heard in places his feet had never touched, resonating across the ocean and reaching every corner of the world.

Now, let’s trace the journey of the old bell. As mentioned, it was carried far from Marbach and sold as scrap metal, eventually ending up in Bavaria to be melted down in a furnace. So, what came next?

Years later in the royal city of Bavaria, after the bell had been removed from the tower and melted down, some metal was needed to create a monument honoring one of Germany's most celebrated figures. Here’s a wonderful twist; strange things do appear in this world.

In Denmark, on one of the green islands where the beech trees rustle in the wind and ancient Huns' graves are visible, another poor boy was born. He wore wooden shoes and carried his father’s lunch wrapped in an old shawl while his dad worked in a shipyard. This poor child grew to become the pride of his nation; for his sculpted marble work astonished the world. He was offered the honor of shaping a clay model of the figure whose name—"John Christopher Frederick"—was written by his father in the Bible. The bust, cast in bronze, included some metal from the old church bell, whose tones had faded from everyone’s memory. The molten metal poured into the mold, forming the head and bust of a statue unveiled in the square before the old castle. This statue perfectly captured the essence of the one born in poverty—the boy from Marbach, the military school student, the fugitive battling poverty and oppression, Germany's great and immortal poet who sang about William Tell, the deliverer of Switzerland, and the heaven-inspired Maid of Orleans.

It was a beautiful sunny day; flags waved from towers and roofs in Stuttgart, and the church bells rang joyfully. One bell remained silent but was illuminated by the bright sunshine pouring over the now-immortalized head and bust of its renowned figure. On this day, one hundred years had passed since the day the old church bell at Marbach had chimed, filling the mother's heart with hope and joy—the very day her child was born into poverty and a humble home. This child later became a celebrated and noble-hearted poet, a blessing to the world— the glorious, sublime, and immortal bard, John Christopher Frederick Schiller!

title: THE OLD GRAVE-STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In a house with a spacious courtyard located in a small town, a family was gathered together in their old home. It was that time of year when people say the evenings grow longer. A lamp flickered on the table, even though the weather was warm and mild. Long curtains hung down in front of open windows, while outside, the moon illuminated the dark blue sky.

But instead of talking about the moon, they were discussing a large, ancient stone resting in the courtyard near the kitchen door. The maids often set out the shiny copper pots and kitchen utensils on this stone to dry in the sun, and the children enjoyed playing on it. In reality, it was an old grave stone.

"Yeah," said the head of the household, "I think this stone came from the graveyard of the old church at the convent that was torn down. They sold the pulpit, monuments, and grave stones. My father bought some of them; most were split in half for paving stones, but this one was kept whole and placed in the courtyard."

"You can see it’s a grave stone," said the oldest child. "You can still make out the design of an hourglass and part of an angel, but the inscription underneath is almost completely worn away, except for the name 'Preben' and a big 'S' next to it. A little further down, you can easily read the name 'Martha.' But that’s about it, and you can really only see it clearly when it has rained or when we wash the stone."

"Goodness, how strange. That must be the grave stone of Preben Schwane and his wife."

The old man who spoke looked as if he could be the grandfather of everyone in the room.

title: THE OLD GRAVE-STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "Yes," he went on, "these two were among the last to be buried in the old churchyard at the convent. They were a very respectable couple, and I remember them well from my childhood. Everyone knew them and held them in high regard. They were the oldest residents in town, and people believed they had a fortune, yet they always dressed plainly in the coarsest fabric, with the whitest linen. Preben and Martha were such a lovely couple. When they sat on the bench at the top of the steep steps in front of their house, under the waving branches of the linden tree, and nodded to passersby with friendly smiles, it truly made you feel happy. They were kind to the poor; they fed and clothed them, showing both compassion and wisdom in their goodwill. The old woman passed away first; I can still vividly remember that day. I was a little boy and had gone with my father to visit the old man. Martha fell into her eternal sleep just as we arrived. Her body lay in a bedroom near where we sat, while the old man wept like a child, expressing to my father and the few neighbors there how lonely he felt now that she was gone, and how good and faithful she had been throughout their years together, sharing how they met and fell in love. I was just a boy, standing by and listening, but I felt a strange emotion listening to him as the color rose in his cheeks when he talked about their courtship, how beautiful she was, and the little tricks he pulled to see her. Then he spoke of their wedding day; his eyes lit up, and it was like he traveled back in time to that joyful occasion. Yet, there she was, lying in the next room, gone—an old woman—and he, an old man, reminiscing about those hopeful days long past. Ah, how time flies; back then I was a child, and now I've grown old, just like Preben was then. Time moves on, and everything changes. I distinctly remember the day of her burial, and how Old Preben walked closely behind the coffin.

title: THE OLD GRAVE-STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"A few years before this time, the couple had their grave stone made with their names and an inscription, but without a date. In the evening, it was taken to the churchyard and placed on their grave. A year later, it was lifted again so that Old Preben could rest beside his wife. They didn't leave much behind; certainly not the wealth people assumed they had. What little they had went to distant relatives no one had ever known. The old house, with its wicker balcony and the bench at the top of the steps under the linden tree, was deemed too old and decayed to remain standing by the road inspectors. Later, when the convent church was also torn down along with the graveyard, Preben and Martha's grave stone, like everything else, was sold to whoever would take it. So it came to be that this stone wasn’t cut in half like many others, but now lies in the courtyard below, serving as a scouring block for the maids and a playground for the children. A paved street now runs over where Old Preben and his wife once rested; no one remembers them anymore."

The old man who recounted this shook his head sadly and said, "Forgotten! Yes, everything will be forgotten!" Then the conversation shifted to different topics.

However, the youngest child in the room, a boy with large, earnest eyes, climbed onto a chair behind the window curtains and gazed out into the yard. The moon was casting a bright light on the old gravestone—the stone that had always seemed dull and flat to him. But now, in the moonlight, it felt like a significant page from a history book. Everything he had learned about Old Preben and his wife came alive as he looked at the stone, and as he watched the clear, bright moon shining in the pristine air, it seemed like the light of God’s presence was illuminating His beautiful world.

"Forgotten! Everything will be forgotten!" echoed through the room again, and at that moment, an invisible spirit whispered to the boy’s heart, "Carefully preserve the seed that has been given to you, so it can grow and flourish. Guard it closely. Through you, my child, the faded inscription on the old, weathered grave stone will be shared with future generations in clear, golden letters. This old couple will once more walk the streets arm-in-arm or sit with their youthful, healthy faces on the bench under the linden tree, smiling at both the wealthy and the poor. The essence of this moment will blossom over the years into a beautiful poem. The beautiful and the good are never truly forgotten; they live on in stories and songs."

title: THE OLD HOUSE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
There once stood a very old house on a street where all the other houses were new and pristine. The date it was built was carved into one of the beams, surrounded by scrolls of tulips and hop-tendrils. By this date, you could tell the old house was almost three hundred years old. Old-fashioned verses were written above the windows in a fancy script, and strange faces, carved with quirky grins, peeked out from under the cornices. One story of the house jutted out significantly over the other, and a lead gutter ran along the roof, with a dragon's head at the end. The rain was supposed to pour out of the dragon's mouth, but instead, water streamed out of a hole in its body. The newer houses on the street were well-constructed, with big window panes and smooth walls. Anyone could see they had nothing in common with the old house. They probably thought, "How much longer will that old heap of junk stay here, making the whole street look bad? Its parapet sticks out so far that we can't see what's happening beyond it. The stairs are as wide as a castle staircase and as steep as those to a church tower. The iron railing looks like a cemetery gate, complete with brass knobs. It's just ridiculous!"

Facing the old house were even more nice, new houses that shared the same opinion as their neighbors.

In one of those houses, a little boy with rosy cheeks and sparkling eyes sat by the window. He loved the old house, whether it was sunlit or bathed in moonlight. The boy would gaze at the wall where chunks of plaster had fallen off, imagining all sorts of scenes from long ago. He pictured how the street must have looked when all the houses had gable roofs, open staircases, and gutters with dragon spouts. He could even see soldiers marching with halberds. The old house was certainly a fascinating sight.

An old man lived in that house. He wore knee-breeches, a coat adorned with large brass buttons, and a wig that was unmistakably real. Every morning, an attendant would come to clean the rooms and take care of him, or else he would have been all alone. Sometimes, the old man would come to one of the windows and look out, and the little boy would nod at him. The old man would nod back, and although they had never spoken a word to each other, they became friends in their own special way.

title: THE OLD HOUSE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
One day, the little boy overheard his parents say, "The old man across the street is quite well off, but he is terribly lonely." The very next Sunday morning, the boy wrapped something in paper and took it to the old house. He said to the attendant, "Can you please give this to the gentleman who lives here? I have two tin soldiers, and this is one of them, and I want him to have it because I know he is very lonely."

The old attendant nodded with a smile, clearly pleased, and took the tin soldier into the house.

Later on, the attendant was sent over to ask the little boy if he would like to come visit the old man himself. His parents agreed, and that’s how he got the chance to go inside the old house.

The brass knobs on the railings gleamed more brightly than ever, as if polished for his visit; the door featured carvings of trumpeters amidst tulips, as if they were playing with all their might, their cheeks puffed out. "Tanta-ra-ra, here comes the little boy; Tanta-ra-ra, here comes the little boy!"

Then the door opened. The hall was adorned with old portraits of knights in armor and ladies in silk gowns; the armor clinked and the silk rustled as he entered. A staircase led up steeply, then down a bit, leading to a very old balcony. It was in a terrible state, with large holes and cracks where grass and leaves sprouted. The entire balcony, courtyard, and walls were overgrown, making it look like a garden. On the balcony were flower pots that had heads with donkey ears, but the flowers grew wild. One pot had pinks overflowing, as if to say, "The air has fanned me, the sun has kissed me, and I’m promised a bloom by next Sunday—really, by next Sunday!"

Then, they entered a room where the walls were covered in leather, adorned with golden flowers.

"Gilding will fade in damp weather; nothing endures quite like leather," the walls seemed to say.

In the room stood beautifully carved chairs with armrests, and tall backs that creaked. They seemed to whisper, "Sit down. Oh dear, I’m creaking. I’ll surely get the gout like the old cupboard. Gout in my back, ugh!"

title: THE OLD HOUSE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Finally, the little boy entered the room where the old man sat.

"Thank you for the tin soldier, my little friend," the old man said with a warm smile, "and thank you for coming to visit me."

"Thanks, thanks," or "Creak, creak," echoed all the furniture.

There was so much chatter that the furniture got in each other's way as they tried to catch a glimpse of the little boy.

On the wall, near the center of the room, hung a portrait of a beautiful young lady dressed in historical fashion, with powdered hair and a full, stiff skirt. She didn't say a word, but her gentle eyes looked down at the little boy. He turned to the old man and asked,

"Who is she?" The old man replied, "She once hung in a shop across the street. Many portraits there go unnoticed; the people they depict have long since passed. But I knew this lady many years ago; she has been gone for nearly fifty years now."

Beneath the picture, under glass, lay a nosegay of withered flowers that, no doubt, were just as old, looking it too.

The pendulum of the old clock swung back and forth, the hands turned as time passed, and everything in the room aged, yet no one seemed to acknowledge it.

"They say at home," the little boy mentioned, "that you are very lonely."

"Oh," the old man replied with a wistful smile, "I cherish pleasant thoughts of times gone by, and now that you’ve come to visit, that makes me very happy."

He then pulled a picture book from the bookshelf, filled with illustrations of grand processions, magnificent coaches, soldiers resembling the knave of clubs, and citizens waving banners. The tailors had a flag featuring a pair of scissors supported by two lions, and the shoemakers' flag had an eagle with two heads, all to show they could say, "This is one pair." What a delightful picture book it was! The old man then stepped into another room to fetch apples and nuts. It was indeed a lovely time to be in that old house.

title: THE OLD HOUSE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I can't stand it here," complained the tin soldier on the shelf, "it's so lonely and boring. I used to live in a family, and I can't get used to this solitude. The days seem endless, and the nights are even longer. It's not like it was in your house opposite, where your parents chatted happily, and you and all the children made such delightful noise. No, here in the old man's house, there’s only silence. Do you think he ever gets kisses or friendly smiles, or has a Christmas tree? All he has left is the grave. I can't stand it."

"But you shouldn’t only focus on the sadness," the little boy replied. "I find everything here beautiful, and all the old pleasant memories come back to visit."

"Ah, but I never see them, and I don’t know them," said the tin soldier. "It’s just too much for me to handle."

title: THE OLD HOUSE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "You must endure it," insisted the little boy. Just then, the old man returned, looking cheery, holding a plate of delicious preserved fruits along with apples and nuts. The little boy's thoughts about the tin soldier faded away. He felt so happy and delighted; after he returned home, days and weeks went by with the two houses frequently nodding to each other. Soon, the little boy went to visit the old house again. The carved trumpeters announced, "Tanta-ra-ra! Here comes the little boy! Tanta-ra-ra!" The suit of armor clattered, the silk dresses rustled, the leather continued its rhyme, and the old chairs creaked in unison. It was just like the very first visit; in that house, each day felt just like the one before. "I can't take it anymore!" shouted the tin soldier. "I've shed tears of tin; it's so dreary here. Let me go to war and lose a limb; that would at least be a change. Now I know the visits from memories and all they bring. I've had visits from my memories, and it's not entirely pleasant. I almost jumped off the shelf when I saw you all in your house across the street as if you were actually there. It was Sunday morning, and you kids were gathered around the table, singing your morning hymn. You were so serious, standing quietly with folded hands while your parents looked equally grave until the door opened and in came your little sister Maria, who isn't even two yet. She always dances to music, and she began to dance right then, even though she shouldn't have. She couldn't get the rhythm right because the tune was slow, so she wobbled from one leg to the other and dipped her head, but it just didn't fit the music. Everyone was so serious, though it was hard to stay that way; I found it so funny that I fell off the table, and I still have the bruise from that! I know I shouldn't have laughed. Everything I saw keeps playing in my mind. Are you still singing on Sundays? How is my old friend, the tin soldier? He must be so happy! I just can't stand this life."

title: THE OLD HOUSE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "You belong to the old man now," the little boy said. "You have to stay here. Don’t you see that?" Just then, the old man came in with a box filled with all sorts of curiosities to show the boy. There were rouge pots, scent boxes, and large gilded cards that looked like nothing that's made these days. There were smaller boxes to explore, and the piano was opened, its lid painted with landscapes, though when the old man played, the music was sadly out of tune. He glanced at the picture he had purchased from the broker, his eyes sparkling as he nodded and remarked, "Ah, she could sing that tune!"

title: THE OLD HOUSE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I will go to war! I will go to war!" the tin soldier shouted at the top of his lungs, leaping off the shelf. Where could he have landed? The old man and the little boy searched, but he was nowhere to be found. "I’ll find him," said the old man, but he couldn’t. The floorboards were full of cracks and gaps. The tin soldier had fallen through a crack and landed in what felt like a grave. The day went by, and soon the little boy returned home. The week passed, and then more weeks came. Winter settled in, icing the windows so the little boy had to breathe on the glass to create a hole to peek through at the old house. Snow drifts formed around the carvings and inscriptions; the steps were blanketed in snow, making it seem as though no one was home. And indeed, no one was there—the old man had died. One evening, a hearse parked outside, and the old man was placed in his coffin to be taken to the countryside for burial. No one followed him; all his friends were long gone. The little boy waved goodbye as the hearse rolled away. Days later, an auction was held at the old house. From his window, the little boy watched people take away the portraits of knights and ladies, the flower pots with long-eared heads, and the old chairs. Some went one way, others went another. The portrait of the lady that had been bought at the dealer’s returned there, as no one cared for it anymore. When spring arrived, the house itself was demolished; everyone called it a complete eyesore. From the street, you could see the room with leather-covered walls, now tattered and frayed, while the greenery on the balcony drooped over the edges. They tore it down quickly, as it seemed ready to collapse, and eventually it was cleared away entirely. "Good riddance!" the neighboring houses exclaimed. Before long, a shiny new house was built further back from the road, boasting tall windows and smooth walls. But in front of where the old house had stood, a little garden was planted, with wild vines creeping up the nearby walls and large iron railings and a grand gate that looked quite impressive. People would stop to peek through the railings. Sparrows clustered on the wild vines, chattering loudly among themselves, but not about the old house; none could remember it, for so many years had passed that the little boy was now a man, a good man, and his parents took pride in him. He had just been married and had brought his young wife to live in the new house with the garden. Standing by her side, he watched as she planted a lovely wildflower. She carefully pressed it into the earth with her hands, when suddenly she exclaimed, "Oh dear, what’s that?" Something pricked her finger. Through the soft earth, something had been sticking up—it was, can you believe it? The very same tin soldier that had been lost in the old man's room, buried under old wood and junk for who knows how long, until he settled into the earth. The young wife wiped the soldier clean first with a green leaf, then with her fine handkerchief that smelled of exquisite perfume. The tin soldier felt as though he were waking up from a long sleep. "Let me see it!" the young man said, and smiled, shaking his head. "It can hardly be the same soldier, but it reminds me of something that happened when I was a little boy with my own tin soldiers." He then told his wife the tale of the old house and the old man, and how he sent the tin soldier because he thought the man was lonely. His wife listened closely, tears forming in her eyes as she felt moved by the story of the old house and its lonely inhabitant. "It could very well be the same soldier," she said, "and I promise to take care of him and remember your story. But one day, you must show me where the old man is buried."

"I don’t know where it is," he replied. "No one knows. All his friends are gone, and I was just a little boy then."

"Oh, how dreadfully lonely he must have been," she said softly.

"Yes, incredibly lonely," the tin soldier chimed in. "But still, it is wonderful not to be forgotten."

"Wonderful indeed," echoed a voice nearby; it was no one but the tin soldier who noticed it came from a fragment of the leather that hung in tatters. Although it had lost all its gilding and looked like damp earth, it had its own thoughts and expressed them:—

"Gilding will fade in damp weather; nothing endures like leather."

But the tin soldier refused to believe any such thing.

title: THE OLD STREET LAMP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Have you ever heard the tale of the old street lamp? It might not seem very exciting, but maybe it's worth your time to listen. This lamp, which had served proudly for many years, was about to retire with a pension. That evening, it stood at its post for the last time, lighting up the street. It felt like an aging dancer performing her final act, knowing that soon she'd be in her room, alone and forgotten. The lamp was anxious about the next day because it had to go to the town hall to be examined by the mayor and the council. They would decide if it was still good enough to light one of the suburbs or a factory in the countryside. If not, it would be sent to an iron foundry to be melted down. The thought of being transformed into something else troubled the lamp. Above all, it feared the separation from the watchman and his wife, whom it considered family. It had been hung up on the same night that the watchman, a strong young man back then, took on his job. Oh, how long ago that was! Back then, the watchman's wife had a bit of pride; she rarely glanced at the lamp except when passing by in the evening, never during the day. But as years passed, and they all grew old, she tended to the lamp, cleaned it, and filled it with oil. The couple was very honest, never cheating the lamp out of a drop of its oil.

title: THE OLD STREET LAMP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
This was the lamp's last night lighting the street, and the next day meant an uncertain future—no wonder it wasn’t glowing brightly. Thoughts swirled through its mind. It had illuminated countless people on their journeys and witnessed many events, likely more than the mayor and the council combined! Yet, the lamp kept its reflections to itself because it was a good, honorable lamp, unwilling to harm anyone, especially those in authority. As memories flooded back, its light would flicker with sudden brightness, and in those moments, it felt confident it would be remembered. "I remember a handsome young man," the lamp thought, "a long time ago, holding a little note—pink paper with a gold edge. The handwriting was elegant, obviously from a lady. He read it twice, kissed it, and then gazed up at me with eyes full of happiness. Only he and I remember what that first letter said. And there was another pair of eyes I recall—how remarkable it is how thoughts connect! I saw a funeral pass by once, a beautiful young woman lay on a bier covered in flowers, surrounded by torches that outshone my light. As the crowd lined the street to join the procession, I spotted one person off to the side, leaning against my post, weeping. I'll never forget those sorrowful eyes looking up at me." These memories filled the old lamp as it prepared for its last shift.

On the bridge above the canal stood three odd figures hoping to catch the lamp's favor, believing it could choose who would replace it. The first was a herring's head that claimed it could illuminate the darkness, promising to save on oil if put on the lamp post. The second was a piece of rotten wood that also promised to shine; it swaggered about, proud of its lineage from an old mighty tree. The third was a glow-worm, whose presence puzzled the lamp, yet it, too, claimed it could provide light. But the rotten wood and the herring's head adamantly declared, with all the seriousness they could muster, that the glow-worm only shone at certain times and shouldn’t be in the running with them. The old lamp told them that none of them could provide enough light to take its place, but they didn’t believe him. When they learned he couldn't name a successor, they were pleased to hear it, believing the lamp was too old and worn out to make a good choice.

title: THE OLD STREET LAMP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Just then, the wind rushed around the corner and flowed through the old lamp's air holes. "What’s this I hear?" the lamp asked. "You’re leaving tomorrow? Is this our last evening together? I must give you a parting gift! I’ll blow into your mind, so you’ll remember everything you’ve seen and heard, and your glow will be so bright that you’ll understand everything happening around you!"

"Oh, that's an incredible gift," the old lamp replied, "thank you so much! I really hope I won’t be melted down."

"That likely won’t happen just yet," the wind assured him. "I’ll also blow a memory into you, so that when you get other gifts, your old age will be filled with happiness."

"That is, if I don’t get melted down," the lamp said. "But if that happens, will I still keep my memories?"

"Be reasonable, old lamp," the wind said, blowing lightly.

Just then, the moon broke through the clouds. "And what will you give the old lamp?" the wind asked her.

"I can't give anything," she answered. "I’m waning, and no lamps have ever lit me, though I’ve often shone on them." With that, the moon hid behind the clouds to avoid more requests. Just then a drop of rain fell on the lamp, explaining it was a gift from the gray clouds—a rather shabby gift. "I’ll soak you through," it said, "so you can rust and crumble into dust overnight if you choose."

The lamp found this gift to be quite unimpressive, and so did the wind. "Is there no one else who will give something?" shouted the wind as loudly as possible. Soon, a bright falling star streaked through the sky, leaving a shining trail behind it.

"What was that?" yelled the herring's head. "Didn’t a star just fall? I think it landed in the lamp. When such high-class beings try for a position, it’s best for us to say 'Good night' and head home."

title: THE OLD STREET LAMP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
And that’s exactly what they did, while the old lamp shined with an unexpectedly powerful light.

"What a magnificent gift!" the lamp exclaimed. "The bright stars have always been a joy to me, shining so much brighter than I ever could, despite my best efforts. Now they’ve noticed me, a humble old lamp, and sent me a gift that lets me remember everything as if it’s right in front of me, and to be seen by those who care about me. That’s true happiness because joy unshared is only half the experience."

"That’s a noble sentiment," said the wind. "But to share this joy, you'll need wax candles. Without them, your special abilities won’t reach anyone else. The stars don’t realize this; they think you and every other light need to be a wax taper. But now I must go." And with that, it settled down.

"Wax candles, really?" the lamp muttered. "I’ve never had those, nor is it likely I ever will. If only I could be certain I wouldn’t be melted down!"

title: THE OLD STREET LAMP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The next day. Well, let’s skip over that day. Evening fell, and the lamp found itself resting in a comfy grandfather chair—guess where? At the old watchman's house. He had asked the mayor and council to let him keep the lamp, honoring its years of faithful service. After all, he had hung it up and lit it the very day he started his job twenty-four years ago. It was like his child since he had none. There it sat like it was larger than life in the oversized chair beside the warm stove. The old couple shared their meal, casting warm glances toward the lamp, which they would’ve loved to sit at the table with them. Sure, they lived in a cellar, a couple of yards underground, crossed a stone passage to get to their living space, but inside it was cozy, with strips of cloth nailed around the door. Their bed had curtains, and everything looked tidy. On the window seat, two peculiar flower-pots brought back by a sailor named Christian stood proudly—two clay elephants with open backs filled with earth, blooming with flowers. One held chives, their kitchen garden, while the other, with a beautiful geranium, was their flower garden. A colorful print of the Congress of Vienna hung on the wall, featuring all the kings and emperors. There was a clock that ticked steadily, though it was always a bit fast, which the old couple preferred rather than too slow. As they enjoyed their supper, the old lamp relaxed in the grandfather chair by the stove. It felt like the whole world had rotated, but after a bit, the watchman looked at the lamp and talked about all they’d been through together—through rain and fog, during the bright summer nights and the long winter evenings, even during snowstorms when he would wish to be back home in the cellar. At that moment, the lamp felt perfectly content. It could recall each moment as if it were playing out again before its eyes. Clearly, the wind had bestowed an excellent gift upon him.

The old couple was always busy, never idle for a single minute. On Sunday afternoons, they would pull out a travel book they loved. The old man read aloud about Africa’s great forests and wild elephants while his wife listened closely, glancing occasionally at the clay elephants serving as their flower pots.

title: THE OLD STREET LAMP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I can almost picture it all," she said. And how the lamp wished it could light a wax taper to share this vision with the old woman, so she could see every tiny detail as clearly as he did—the tall trees intertwining, the bare-back riders, and herds of elephants trampling through the bamboo.

"What’s the use of my abilities," the old lamp sighed, "if I can’t use a wax candle? They only have oil and tallow here, and those won’t do." One day, some wax candle stubs ended up in the cellar. The larger pieces were burned, while the small ends were saved by the old woman for waxing her thread. Though they now had enough candles, no one thought to place even a tiny piece into the lamp.

title: THE OLD STREET LAMP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
