text_chunk: "Here I am with my rare gifts," thought the lamp, "with abilities inside me, yet I can't share them; they don’t know I could decorate these plain walls with stunning tapestries or transform them into magnificent forests or anything else they wish for." The lamp, however, was always kept clean and shining, attracting everyone’s attention. Strangers saw it as old junk, but the couple didn’t mind; they adored the lamp. One day, on the watchman’s birthday, the old woman smiled and said, "I’ll light up the place today for my old man." The lamp shook in its metal frame, thinking, "Now I’ll finally have a light within me!" But once again, no wax candles were lit; only oil was used. The lamp burned brightly through the evening and came to the painful realization that the stars' gift would remain hidden. That night, the lamp had a vivid dream; dreaming was easy for one with its abilities. It imagined the old couple had died and that it had been sent to the iron foundry to be melted down, provoking the same anxiety it felt when it had visited the mayor and council. Despite having the power to rust away at will, the lamp refrained from doing so. Instead, it found itself in a furnace, transformed into an elegant candlestick designed to hold a wax taper. This candlestick had an angel shape holding a bouquet, with the taper meant to sit in the center. It was destined for a green writing table in a lovely room adorned with scattered books and magnificent paintings. Its owner was a poet and an intellectual, surrounded by representations of everything he envisioned or wrote. Nature appeared to him in dark forests, vibrant meadows filled with storks, or aboard a ship sailing beneath a brilliantly blue sky at night, with stars sparkling overhead. "What power I possess!" said the lamp upon waking from the dream. "Maybe being melted down wouldn’t be so bad... but no! Not while the old couple is alive. They love me for who I am, they keep me bright and filled with oil. I’m as happy as the scene of the congress they cherish." From that moment on, the lamp felt at peace with itself because an honorable old lamp like it deserved nothing less.

title: THE PEA BLOSSOM
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there were five green peas in a single shell. Because both the peas and the shell were green, they believed the whole world must be green too, which made perfect sense to them. As the shell and the peas grew, they settled into their space and lined up side by side. The sun warmed the shell, and rain made it clear and shiny. During the day, everything felt mild and pleasant, while nights turned dark as usual. As they sat together, the peas grew bigger and more thoughtful, sensing there had to be more to life than just sitting.

"Are we just going to sit here forever?" one of the peas asked. "Won’t we get hard from sitting here so long? I can feel there's something outside, and I just know it!"

As weeks went by, the peas started to turn yellow, just like the shell.

"I guess the whole world is turning yellow," they said, and maybe they were right.

Suddenly, they felt a tug on the shell. It got pulled off and was held in human hands before being tossed into the pocket of a jacket along with other pods.

"We’re going to be opened soon," said one, which was exactly what they all wanted.

"I wonder which of us will travel the furthest," said the smallest pea. "We'll find out soon enough."

"Whatever is meant to happen will happen," the largest pea said.

With a loud "Crack!", the shell burst open, and the five peas rolled out into the bright sunlight. They landed in the hand of a little boy who was holding them tightly, declaring they were perfect for his pea-shooter. Without hesitation, he loaded one in and shot it off.

"Now I’m flying into the wide world! Catch me if you can!" the pea said, and in an instant, it was gone.

"I," declared the second pea, "plan to soar straight toward the sun. That shell looks inviting, and it’s just what I want!" With that, he took off.

title: THE PEA BLOSSOM
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"We’ll just take a nap wherever we land," said the next two peas, and they rolled around on the floor before getting loaded into the pea-shooter. "We might go further than the rest," they said.

"Whatever is meant to happen will happen," the last pea exclaimed as he was shot from the pea-shooter. He collided with an old board under a garret window and fell into a small crevice filled with moss and soft earth. The moss wrapped around him, so he lay there, captured but not unnoticed by a higher power.

"Whatever is meant to happen will happen," he reminded himself.

In the little garret lived a poor woman who worked hard cleaning stoves, chopping wood, and doing other tough jobs, yet she remained poor. Meanwhile, at home lay her only daughter, not fully grown and very frail. She had been bedridden for a whole year, and it felt as if she was stuck between living and dying.

"She’s going to her little sister," the woman said. "I only had the two children, and it was tough to support them both. But God helped me with my work and took one to Him, providing for her. Now, I’d love to keep the one who’s left, but I think they need to be together, and my sick girl will soon be with her sister in heaven." Despite this, the sick girl remained quietly in bed, patiently waiting while her mother worked.

As spring arrived, one bright morning, the sun shone through the little window and onto the floor. Just as the mother was leaving for work, the sick girl gazed at the lowest window pane and exclaimed, "Mother, what is that little green thing that’s peeking in? It’s moving in the wind!"

The mother walked to the window and opened it halfway. "Oh! There’s a little pea that’s taken root and is sprouting green leaves! How did it get in there? Well, now you have a little garden to enjoy," she said. So, the mother pulled the sick girl's bed closer to the window so she could see the budding plant before heading out to work.

title: THE PEA BLOSSOM
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Mother, I think I’m going to get better," said the sick girl that evening. "The sun has warmed the room so much today, and that little pea is thriving—I feel hopeful that I can enjoy the sunshine again!"

"God willing!" said the mother, though she wasn’t sure it would happen. However, she propped up the little green plant with a stick, hoping it wouldn’t get damaged by the wind. She tied a piece of string to the window sill and the top of the frame so the pea tendrils could climb it as they grew. And grow they did, almost visibly each day.

"Look! A flower is coming!" the old woman said one morning, finally beginning to nurture the hope that her daughter might really recover. She noticed her child had been speaking cheerfully lately and had even sat up in bed to gaze outside with sparkling eyes at the little garden holding just a single pea plant. A week later, the girl sat up for the first time for a whole hour, feeling bright and happy in the warm sunshine while the pea plant outside blossomed into a beautiful pink flower. The little girl gently kissed the delicate leaves. This day felt like a celebration to her.

"Our heavenly Father planted that pea and helped it grow to bring us joy and hope, my blessed child," the delighted mother said, smiling at the flower as if it were a divine gift.

But what happened to the other peas? The one who flew into the wide world and called out, "Catch me if you can," landed in a gutter on a rooftop and ended up in a pigeon’s stomach. The two lazy peas didn’t fair any better, getting eaten by pigeons as well, so at least they served a purpose. The fourth pea, aiming for the sun, tumbled into a sink where it sat in dirty water for weeks, swelling in size.

"I’m getting nice and plump," the pea said. "I’ll probably burst soon. No other pea could accomplish more than that, I believe; I’m the most remarkable of all five that were in the shell." And the sink agreed.

But the young girl stood at the open garret window, her eyes sparkling and her cheeks rosy with health, folding her thin hands over the pea blossom, thanking God for His blessings.

"I," said the sink, "will always support my pea."

title: THE PEN AND THE INKSTAND
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In a poet's room, there was an inkstand sitting proudly on the table. Someone remarked, "It's amazing what comes out of an inkstand. What do you think will happen next? It's really quite remarkable."

"Absolutely!" replied the inkstand to the pen and all the other items on the table. "I always say it's incredible what I can produce. Honestly, I have no idea what’s next when that man dips his pen into me. Just one drop is enough for half a page of paper, and think about all the ideas half a page can hold! Every amazing work of a poet comes from me—those imaginary characters that people feel like they’ve encountered. All the deep emotions, the humor, and vibrant scenes of nature come from me. I can’t even explain how it happens because I'm not familiar with nature myself, yet it's definitely within me. I've sent out descriptions of charming maidens and brave knights on galloping horses, the downtrodden, and so much more, though I assure you, none of this ever crosses my mind."

"You might be right," said the pen, "but it’s clear you don’t think at all. If you did, you’d realize you’re just the means to an end. You give me the ink so I can express what's inside me, the ideas I want to share. It’s the pen that writes, and no one can deny that. Honestly, most people know about poetry as much as an old inkstand does!"

"You've barely had any experience," the inkstand shot back. "You’ve only been in service for a week, and you're already getting worn out. Do you think you’re a poet? You’re just a tool. Before you came along, I had plenty of others—some made of goose feathers, others from England. I’m familiar with quills just as well as I am with steel pens. I’ve had both types, and I’ll have more when that man—who does the real writing—returns to capture what I provide. I wonder what he’ll create next from me."

title: THE PEN AND THE INKSTAND
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"You’re just an inkpot!" the pen scoffed.

Later that evening, the poet came home. He had just attended a concert, completely captivated by a brilliant violinist. The musician produced such rich tones, sometimes resembling gentle raindrops or shimmering pearls, sometimes echoing birds singing together, rising and cascading like the wind among the fir trees. The poet felt his heart swell with emotion that echoed beautifully, almost like a woman’s voice. It wasn’t just the strings—every part of that instrument seemed alive with sound. The performance was magnificent, challenging, yet the bow danced across the strings effortlessly, making it seem like anyone could do it with a little practice. The violin and bow looked as if they were playing independently, their melodies so compelling that the audience forgot about the musician. But the poet didn’t forget. He remembered the artist, named him, and captured his feelings on paper. "How silly it would be for the violin and the bow to take credit for their performance. Yet we humans often make that mistake. The poet, the artist, the scientist in his lab, the general—we all do this. We are merely instruments in the hands of the Almighty; the honor belongs solely to Him. There's nothing about ourselves to be proud of." This is what the poet wrote, framing it as a parable titled "The Master and the Instruments."

"That’s what you’re good for, my dear," the pen said to the inkstand when they were alone again. "Did you hear him read what I wrote?"

"Of course, that was my contribution!" the inkstand responded with a hint of sarcasm. "That was a jab at you for your arrogance. Did you really think you were being complimented? I provided that clever line. I certainly know my own satire!"

"Writing tool!" the inkstand replied sharply. Despite their bickering, both felt satisfied with their comebacks. It feels great to think you've won an argument, and they both slept soundly after that. But the poet lay awake. Thoughts stirred inside him like the sweet notes of the violin, cascading like pearls, or rushing like the strong wind through the trees. He found clarity in those thoughts—they felt like a glimpse from the mind of the Great Master of all creativity.

title: THE PHILOSOPHER'S STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the meaning of life and death
text_chunk:
In a distant place far to the east, in India, which back then felt like the edge of the world, there stood the majestic Tree of the Sun. This was a magnificent tree, unlike anything we’ve seen, and possibly something we may never see.

The top of this tree spread out for miles, resembling an entire forest. Each of its smaller branches created its own complete tree. There were palms, beech trees, pines, plane trees, and many other varieties from all over the globe, sprouting like tiny branches from the great tree. The thicker boughs twisted and turned, forming valleys and hills, blanketed in lush green and burst with flowers. Everywhere you looked, it resembled a blooming meadow or a delightful garden. Birds from all over the world gathered together here—some from America’s ancient forests, others from the rose gardens of Damascus, and many from Africa's deserts, where the elephant and lion claim dominance. Even polar birds flew here, and of course, storks and swallows were not missing. But it wasn’t just birds living here; there were also stags, squirrels, antelopes, and countless other graceful creatures that made this splendid home.

At the very top of the tree was a sprawling garden, and in its center, where the green branches formed a small hill, sat a crystal castle. From there, one could gaze out toward every corner of the sky. Each tower mimicked a lily flower, and inside, a winding staircase led up to the top, where one could step onto the leaves as if they were balconies. The flower’s calyx shaped a stunning, shining circular hall, where the only roof was the blue sky above, adorned with the sun and stars.

title: THE PHILOSOPHER'S STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the meaning of life and death
text_chunk:
Equal splendor of a different nature spread throughout the grand halls of the castle below. Here, the walls reflected images of the world, showcasing countless scenes from daily life. For anyone who wanted to see, there was no need for newspapers, and in fact, they didn’t exist in this magical place. Everything unfolded in lively pictures for those curious enough to look, but even the wisest person might find it overwhelming. This wise man resided here. His name is very difficult to pronounce, so we can leave it unsaid. He knew everything a person could possibly imagine or understand. Every invention that existed or was yet to be known was in his mind and more besides. Despite this, everything has its limits. Even the wise King Solomon couldn’t compare to him. He had mastery over nature’s forces and commanded powerful spirits; even Death had to provide him each morning with a list of those who would die that day. But even King Solomon faced death in the end, a reality that often occupied the thoughts of this great man in the castle atop the Tree of the Sun. He understood that regardless of how much higher he soared in wisdom above others, he too would eventually die. He saw that his children would fade away like leaves and return to dust. He watched humanity wither like leaves falling from a tree; new people arrived to take their place, but those fallen leaves never sprouted again; they turned to dust or were absorbed by other plants.

"What happens to someone," the wise man wondered, "when touched by the angel of death? What is death? The body decays, but what about the soul? Yes; what is the soul, and where does it go?"

"To eternal life," came the soothing voice of faith.

"But what does this change mean? Where and how will we exist afterward?"

"Above, in heaven," replied the devout man; "that is our hope."

title: THE PHILOSOPHER'S STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the meaning of life and death
text_chunk:
"Above!" the wise man reiterated, gazing at the moon and stars overhead. He realized that in this earthly realm, everything is constantly shifting. The position completely changes depending on where one finds themselves. He knew that even if he ascended to the top of the highest mountain, what seemed clear and bright below would appear dark and cloudy up there; the sun would glow a strange reddish hue and emit no rays, while the earth below would be shrouded in an orange mist. How limited our physical sight is, and how little the wisest among us truly knows about what matters most.

In the castle's most secret chamber lay the greatest treasure on earth—the Book of Truth. The wise man had read it cover to cover. Anyone could read from this book, but only in fragments. To many, the letters appeared jumbled, making the words indecipherable. On some pages, the ink was so light or blurry that they became blank. The wiser a person became, the more they would read, and those who were the wisest read the most.

The wise man could combine sunlight and moonlight with reason and the hidden forces of nature; through this heightened clarity, many things in the book became clearer to him. But in the portion titled "Life after Death," not a single concept could be clearly grasped. This troubled him. Would he never find a light that revealed everything written in the Book of Truth during his time on earth? Like the wise King Solomon, he could understand the language of animals and translate it into song, but that didn't make him any wiser. He discovered the properties of plants and metals capable of healing diseases and halting death, but none that could eliminate death itself. He sought a light among all created things that would clarify the certainty of eternal life, but it eluded him. The Book of Truth lay open before him, but its pages felt like blank paper. Christianity offered him the promise of eternal life through the Bible, but he wanted to read it in his book, where nothing about it seemed written.

title: THE PHILOSOPHER'S STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the meaning of life and death
text_chunk: He had five children—four sons, raised by a wise father to be just like him, and a daughter who was beautiful, kind, and smart but blind. Though she couldn't see, this seemed inconsequential to her; her father and brothers were her eyes, and her rich imagination painted everything clearly for her mind’s eye. The sons had never ventured farther than the branches of the trees surrounding their home, and the sister had barely stepped outside. They were happy children in their childhood home, the enchanting Tree of the Sun. Like all kids, they loved hearing stories, and their father shared tales that many other children wouldn’t understand; but these kids were as clever as most adults. He explained the pictures they saw on the castle walls—depicting human endeavors and the flow of events across the lands. The sons often wished they could take part in these significant actions. Their father would tell them that in the world, there was only hard labor and hardship; things weren't as they seemed from their beautiful home. He spoke of the true, the beautiful, and the good, teaching them that these three intertwined in the world, crystallizing into a priceless jewel, clearer than the finest diamond—a jewel valued even by God, in whose brightness all other things fade. This jewel was named the philosopher's stone. He explained that through searching, mankind could come to know the existence of God and that every person possesses the ability to discover the truth that this philosopher's stone exists. Such knowledge would have been beyond other children, but these children understood, and others would come to grasp its meaning over time. They asked their father about the true, the beautiful, and the good, and he explored these topics with them in various ways. He told them that when God made man from the earth’s dust, He touched His creation five times, instilling five profound senses. Through these, the true, the beautiful, and the good are perceived, valued, and nurtured. Five senses have been granted to us—mentally and physically, inside and outside, to both body and soul.

title: THE PHILOSOPHER'S STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the meaning of life and death
text_chunk:
The children reflected deeply upon all these concepts, pondering them day and night. Then the eldest brother had a magnificent dream. Strangely enough, the second, third, and fourth brothers all dreamt the exact same thing: each journeyed into the world to seek the philosopher's stone. They each dreamed that they found it and, riding back home on their swift horses in the morning light, the stone gleamed from their foreheads like a shining beacon. It illuminated the pages of the Book of Truth, making every word that spoke about life beyond the grave glow with clarity. But the sister never dreamt of venturing into the wide world; that thought never crossed her mind. Her world was her father's home.

"I will set out into the world," declared the eldest brother. "I need to experience what life is like among people. I will only practice the good and the true; with these, I will protect the beautiful. Much will change for the better while I’m there."

title: THE PHILOSOPHER'S STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the meaning of life and death
text_chunk: These thoughts were bold and grand, as thoughts usually are at home before we step out into the world and face its storms and challenges, its thorns and troubles. In him, and in all his brothers, the five senses were highly developed, both internally and externally; but each held one sense that was sharper and more developed than the others. For the eldest brother, that sense was sight, which he hoped would serve him well. He possessed eyes for all times and all people; eyes that could unveil hidden treasures buried deep within the earth and peer into the hearts of men as if looking through glass. He could read more than what usually shows on a blushing cheek or in a drooping or smiling eye. Stags and antelopes accompanied him to the edge of his home, where he found wild swans. Following them, he ventured far north, away from the fertile lands where his father resided. How astonished he was to see so much! Everything was different from the mere images reflected in his father's house. Initially, he nearly overwhelmed his senses with the contrasting sights and follies presented as beautiful; but he maintained focus and soon found ample opportunities to explore his understanding of the true, the beautiful, and the good. But how were these represented in the world? He noted that often what truly belonged to beauty was bestowed upon the ugly; the good frequently went unnoticed, while mediocrity received applause when it should have been shunned. People tended to admire appearances over character, fixating on names rather than on doing their duty, placing more trust in reputation than genuine service. It seemed to be this way everywhere.

title: THE PHILOSOPHER'S STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the meaning of life and death
text_chunk:
"I need to tackle these issues head-on," he resolved, and began doing just that without holding back. Yet while he was searching for the truth, the evil one, the father of lies, sought to oppose him. The fiend would have liked to blind the Seer, but that route was too straightforward for him; he operated with greater cunning. He allowed the young man to seek and discover what was beautiful and good; but while he was gazing upon these, the evil spirit blew dust into his eyes, and such mischief could weaken the strongest vision. Then he blew on the dust, turning it into beams that obscured the clarity of his sight, leaving the Seer feeling like a blind person in the world, losing faith in everything around him. He became disillusioned with life, giving up hope in both the world and himself; and when someone abandons trust in the world and in themselves, it’s often the end for them.

"It’s all over," said the wild swan, flying eastward over the sea.

"It’s all over," chirped the swallows also heading home toward the Tree of the Sun. They brought back no good news.

"I think the Seer didn’t fare well," said the second brother, "but maybe the Hearer will have more luck."

This brother possessed an exceptional sense of hearing; it was so sharp that people claimed he could hear the grass growing. He said heartfelt goodbyes to everyone at home and rode off, equipped with good intentions and talents. The swallows guided him, and he followed the swans until he found himself in the world, far away from home. But he quickly discovered that one could have too much of a good thing. His hearing was overly sharp. He heard not only the grass growing but also every heartbeat filled with joy and sorrow. For him, the world felt like a giant clockmaker's workshop, with every clock ticking away "tick, tick," and all the church bells ringing "ding, dong." It was unbearable. His ears endured for a while, but ultimately, the chaos became too much to handle.

title: THE PHILOSOPHER'S STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the meaning of life and death
text_chunk:
There were even mischievous sixty-year-old boys—because age alone doesn’t make a man—who stirred up ruckus that could have amused the Hearer, had it not been for the applause echoing through every street and house, even reaching country lanes. Falsehood boldly stepped forward and played the hypocrite; the bells on the fool’s cap jingled, proclaiming themselves as church bells, and the cacophony became unbearable for the Hearer. He shoved his fingers into his ears, but even then, he couldn't escape the false tunes, bad singing, gossip, and baseless talk—both outside and within. "Heaven help us!" He pushed deeper into his ears until, at last, his eardrums burst. Now he couldn’t hear anything about the true, the beautiful, and the good anymore, which was what he had hoped to acquire through his hearing. He withdrew into silence and suspicion, ultimately losing trust in everyone, including himself. Losing hope of finding and bringing home the treasured jewel, he surrendered, falling into despair, which was worse than anything else.

The birds carried news back towards the east, and the word reached the castle in the Tree of the Sun.

"I will give it a try now," the third brother declared. "I have an excellent sense of smell." While that wasn’t a particularly fancy way to say it, it was how he spoke, and we must take him as he is. He was a cheerful person and, moreover, a real poet; he had a knack for making many things feel poetic with how he expressed them. Ideas would strike him long before they occurred to others. "I can smell," he would often say, and he placed great importance on his highly developed sense of smell in terms of beauty. "I can smell," he would claim, "and many places have a fragrance or beauty unique to their inhabitants. One person feels at home in the tavern’s atmosphere, surrounded by flickering candles and the scent of strong drinks mingling with bad tobacco. Another prefers the overpowering aroma of jasmine or dabs of scented olive oil. One man loves the fresh sea breeze while another climbs a towering mountain to look down at the busy lives below."

title: THE PHILOSOPHER'S STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the meaning of life and death
text_chunk:
As he spoke, it seemed like he had already explored the world and mingled with its people. But this insight was instinctual—it was the poetry inside him, a gift from Heaven since birth. He said goodbye to his family in the Tree of the Sun and departed on foot, leaving the pleasant surroundings of his home behind. Once he reached the edges, he hopped on an ostrich, which could outrun a horse. Later, when he encountered the wild swans, he climbed onto the strongest of them because he loved change, and they flew off together over the ocean to distant lands filled with vast forests, deep lakes, towering mountains, and grand cities. Wherever he went, it seemed like sunshine traveled with him through the fields. Every flower and bush exuded a new fragrance as if they recognized a friend who could appreciate them and knew their worth. Even the scraggly rosebush began to sprout the most stunning roses in response; everyone noticed, and even the black, slimy wood snail acknowledged its beauty. "I will give my seal to the flower," declared the snail, "I left my slimy trail on it; that’s all I can do."

"This is how it always goes with beauty in this world," the poet remarked. He composed a song about it and sang it his own way, but no one paid attention. Then he gave a drummer some coins and a peacock feather, crafting a song for the drum, which the drummer went on to play throughout the streets of the town. When people heard it, they exclaimed, "That’s a fantastic tune!" The poet wrote numerous songs about the true, the beautiful, and the good. His songs were enjoyed in taverns filled with flickering candles, in fresh clover fields, in the forest, and on the open sea; it appeared that this brother might find success where the others had not.

But the evil spirit was displeased by this, so he set to work with soot and incense, which he could combine so artfully that they could fool even an angel, much less a poor poet. The evil one knew how to manipulate people like this. He enveloped the poet in incense until he lost his sense of purpose, forgot his mission, and ultimately vanished into thin air.

title: THE PHILOSOPHER'S STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the meaning of life and death
text_chunk:
When the little birds heard about this, they mourned and sang not a single song for three days. The black wood snail grew even darker, but not from sadness—out of envy. "They should have offered me incense," he grumbled, "for it was I who inspired him to write the most famous of his songs—the drum song of 'The Way of the World;' and I was the one who spat at the rose; I can provide proof of that!"

Yet none of this news ever reached the poet’s home in India. The birds remained silent for three days, and when their period of mourning ended, their grief had faded so much that they even forgot who they were mourning.

"Now it’s my turn to venture into the world and disappear like the others," said the fourth brother. He shared the good-natured temperament of the third, though lacked the poetic touch.

The two eldest brothers had filled the castle with laughter, and now the last spark of joy was fading away. Sight and hearing have always been considered two of the most important senses among humans, the ones they strive to keep clear, while the other senses are often viewed as less essential.

However, the youngest son had a different view; he had refined his sense of taste in every way possible, believing it to be very influential. It governed not only what a person eats but also what inputs are accepted by their minds; thus, he took it upon himself to sample everything found in jars and bottles, which he called the rough part of his work. Every person’s mind was for him like a vessel cooking something; every land became a kind of mental kitchen. "There’s nothing here of refined quality," he declared, deciding to travel the world in search of something to please his taste. "Maybe fortune will be kinder to me than it was to my brothers. Where shall I go? Are air balloons invented yet?" he asked his father, who was aware of all existing and future inventions.

Air balloons hadn’t been created yet, nor had steamships or railways.

title: THE PHILOSOPHER'S STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the meaning of life and death
text_chunk:
"Alright," he concluded, "then I will go for an air balloon. My father can tell me how to build and pilot one. No one has invented it yet, so people will think of it as a magical aerial spirit. Once I’m done with it, I’ll burn it, and for this, I need a few things from another invention that’ll come next: some chemical matches."

He got what he needed and took to the sky. The birds accompanied him further than they had the other brothers. They were intrigued by how this adventure would unfold. Many more birds swooped down, thinking this was a new species, and soon he had a flock of followers. They filled the sky, creating a cloud that darkened the air much like the swarm of locusts over Egypt.

Now he was out in the wide world. The balloon drifted over a massive city, and the aeronaut found his place at the highest point, on the church steeple. The balloon inexplicably rose again into the sky; what happened to it remains a mystery, but it doesn't matter since air balloons hadn’t been invented.

He sat atop the church steeple. No birds lingered around him anymore; they grew tired of his company, and he tired of theirs. Every chimney in town was puffing smoke.

"There are altars built in my honor," boasted the wind, eager to please him as he sat there, looking down at the crowds. One person strutted along, proud of their wealth; another took pride in the key dangling behind them, even though they had nothing to lock up. Someone else flaunted their tattered coat, while yet another boasted about their pitiful body. "Vanity, pure vanity!" he exclaimed. "I must go down there eventually, to touch and taste; but for now, I’ll enjoy the wind at my back. It’s quite nice to lounge late into the morning after a long day," the sluggard said, "so I’ll stay up here as long as the wind blows, simply enjoying the moment."

And there he remained. But while sitting on the weather-vane atop the steeple, which turned with him, he wrongly believed the same wind kept blowing and that he could remain there without any cost.

title: THE PHILOSOPHER'S STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the meaning of life and death
text_chunk:
Meanwhile, in India, in the castle atop the Tree of the Sun, it was quiet and lonely, for all the brothers had departed one by one.

"Nothing is going well for them," the father observed. "They will never bring back the precious jewel; it's not meant for me; they’ve all perished." He leaned over the Book of Truth, staring at the page that should have revealed life after death, but to him, it contained no words or lessons.

His blind daughter was his source of comfort and joy; she held him close with genuine love, wishing for his happiness and peace, longing for the day they would find the treasured jewel.

With tender emotions, she thought about her brothers. Where could they be? Where were they living? How she wished she could dream about them, yet it was odd that not even in dreams could she come near them. But at last, one night, she dreamed of hearing her brothers' voices calling out from a distant world. Unable to resist, she sought them out, yet in her dream, it felt as if she remained in her father's house. Although she couldn’t see them, she sensed a fire burning in her hand, one that didn’t hurt her, for it was the jewel she carried back to her father. Upon waking, she briefly believed she still held the stone, but instead found herself clutching the knob of her distaff.

During the long evenings, she had been continuously spinning, creating threads finer than a spider’s web; human eyes would never distinguish these strands once separated. Yet she had soaked the threads with her tears, making them as strong as rope. Rising with the feeling that her dream must be real, she was determined.

title: THE PHILOSOPHER'S STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the meaning of life and death
text_chunk:
It was still nighttime, and her father slept peacefully. She gently kissed his hand, then took her distaff and tied the end of the thread to her father's house. Without this thread, even with her blindness, she wouldn’t find her way home again. She had to hold tight to it and put her trust in it, not in others or even herself. From the Tree of the Sun, she plucked four leaves, releasing them to the wind and weather so they could reach her brothers as letters and greetings, just in case she didn’t encounter them in this vast world. Poor blind girl, what awaited her beyond? But she held on tightly to the invisible thread of her home, along with a gift that none of the others possessed—a determination to fully immerse herself in whatever she undertook, giving her the sensation of sight even at her fingertips, and she felt deeply in her heart. Quietly she stepped into the noisy, bustling, wondrous world. Wherever she went, the skies brightened, sunny rays warmed her, and she perceived a rainbow arcing across the dark world. She heard the birds sing and caught whiffs of orange groves and apple orchards so strongly that it felt as if she could taste it. Soft tones and delightful songs reached her ears alongside harsh sounds and rough words—thoughts and feelings sharply contrasting one another. Every echo of human thoughts and emotions reached deep into her heart. Then she sadly heard the following words sung,

"Life is a shadow that flits away In a night of darkness and woe."

"Life has the rose's sweet perfume With sunshine, light, and joy."

"Each mortal thinks of himself alone, Is a truth, alas, too clearly known;"

Then, on the other hand, came the response—

"Love, like a mighty flowing stream, Fills every heart with its radiant gleam."

"In the pretty turmoil here below, All is a vain and paltry show.

"Great and good are the actions done By many whose worth is never known."

And if sometimes the mocking strain reached her—

"Why not join in the jesting cry That contemns all gifts from the throne on high?"

title: THE PHILOSOPHER'S STONE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the meaning of life and death
text_chunk:
In the blind girl's heart, a stronger voice whispered—

"To trust in yourself and God is best. In His holy will forever to rest."

But the evil spirit couldn't watch this unfold without becoming restless. He was more clever than ten thousand men and found ways to achieve his goals. He made his way to the marsh and gathered bubbles from stagnant water. Then he uttered deceiving words over them to make them stronger. He mixed songs of praise with lies, boiled them in tears shed by envy, and sprinkled on rouge gathered from faded cheeks, producing another maiden—a likeness of the blind girl, the so-called angel of perfection. The evil one’s plot succeeded. The world could not discern which was genuine—indeed, how could they?

"To trust in yourself and God is best. In His holy will forever to rest."

This was the melody sung by the blind girl with unwavering faith. She entrusted her four green leaves from the Tree of the Sun to the winds, treating them as greeting letters to her brothers, and she believed wholeheartedly that the leaves would indeed find their way to them. She firmly believed that the jewel, which radiates all worldly glories, would eventually be discovered, shining upon humanity even within her father’s castle. "Even in my father's house," she echoed. "Yes, the place where this jewel is to be found is here on earth, and I shall return with more than just a promise. I can feel it glowing and swelling more in my closed hand. Every grain of truth that the wind carried up to me, I caught and cherished. I infused it with the perfume of beauty, which exists abundantly in this world, even for the blind. I gathered the echoes of good deeds and incorporated them into my treasure. All that I can offer may seem like mere dust; yet, it’s still part of the jewel we seek, and there's plenty of it in my hand."

She soon found herself back home, carried there on a wave of thought, never loosening her grip on the invisible thread tied to her father’s house. As she outstretched her hand to her father, a fierce blast of wind surged over the Tree of the Sun, rushing into the sanctuary where the Book of Truth lay.

"It’ll be swept to dust by the wind," proclaimed the father, as he clasped her open hand.

"No," she replied with quiet faith, "it’s indestructible. I can feel its warmth enveloping my soul."

At that moment, her father noticed a dazzling light shining from the white page where the shimmering dust had landed. It was there to affirm the certainty of eternal life, and on the book glowed one luminous word—just one: BELIEVE. Soon after, the four brothers returned to their father and sister. When the green leaf from home touched each of them, a longing took hold of them, driving their desire to return. They arrived accompanied by the migrating birds, the stag, the antelope, and all the woodland creatures eager to share in their joy.

We've often witnessed how a beam of sunlight breaks through a door crack into a dusty room, creating a swirling leader of dust all around. But this wasn’t ordinary, insignificant dust that the blind girl brought back; even the colors of a rainbow dimmed in comparison to the beauty radiating from the page where her offering had fallen. The shining word BELIEVE, born from every grain of truth, shone with the brightness of the beautiful and the good, far brighter than the majestic flame that led Moses and the Israelites to the promised land, and from the word BELIEVE arose a bridge of hope that reached to the immeasurable Love in the infinite realms.

title: THE PHOENIX BIRD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In the Garden of Paradise, underneath the Tree of Knowledge, a rose bush was in full bloom. From the first rose of that bush, a bird came to life. His flight was as quick as a flash of light, his feathers were stunning, and his song enchanting. But when Eve picked the fruit from the tree of knowledge of good and evil, and she and Adam were banished from Paradise, a spark tumbled from the flaming sword of the cherub into the bird's nest, igniting it instantly. The bird perished in the fire, but from the red egg in the nest, a new bird soared into the sky—the solitary Phoenix. Legend has it that he lives in Arabia and every hundred years, he sets himself ablaze in his nest; yet each time, a new Phoenix, the only one of its kind, rises from the red egg.

The bird flutters around us, swift as light, radiant in color, and lovely in song. When a mother sits by her baby's crib, he lands on the pillow, creating a halo around the infant's head with his wings. He glides through the cozy room, bringing warmth and light, making the violets on the small table smell even sweeter.

But the Phoenix isn’t just an Arabian bird. He glides through the shimmering Northern Lights over the plains of Lapland and hops among the yellow flowers during Greenland's brief summer. Beneath the copper mountains of Fablun and in England’s coal mines, he appears, taking the form of a dusty moth as he flutters over the hymn book resting on the lap of the devout miner. He drifts on a lotus leaf down the sacred waters of the Ganges, and the eye of the Hindoo maiden sparkles when she sees him.

Do you know the Phoenix bird? The Bird of Paradise, the holy swan of song! On the chariot of Thespis, he perched disguised as a chattering raven, flapping his black wings stained with wine. Across the strings of the harp from Iceland, the swan's red beak glided. On Shakespeare’s shoulder, he sat disguised as Odin's raven, whispering in the poet's ear, ‘Immortality!’ and at the minstrels' gathering, he danced through the grand halls of the Wartburg.

Do you recognize the Phoenix bird? He sang the Marseillaise for you, and you kissed the pen that slipped from his wing; he arrived in the radiance of Paradise, and perhaps you turned away from him to admire the sparrow adorned with fake jewels on its wings.

The Bird of Paradise—reborn each century—created in fire, ending in fire! Your image hangs in a golden frame in the halls of the wealthy, yet you often fly around, lonely and overlooked, a legend—the ‘Phoenix of Arabia.’

In Paradise, when you were born from the first rose, underneath the Tree of Knowledge, you were given a kiss, and your true name was bestowed upon you—your name, Poetry.

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The General occupied the grand first floor, while the porter stayed in the cellar. There was quite a divide between their two families—the entire ground floor and their different social standings. Yet, they shared the same building and could both see the street and the courtyard. In the courtyard, there was a grassy area with a blossoming acacia tree (when it bloomed), and underneath it sat the elegantly dressed nurse with the even more elegantly dressed daughter of the General, little Emily. The porter's son often danced barefoot in front of them, his large brown eyes shining and dark hair bouncing. Little Emily smiled at him and reached out her hands, and whenever the General saw this from the window, he would nod and call out, "Charming!" Despite this, the General's wife—a young woman who could easily have been mistaken for her husband’s daughter—never looked out the window that faced the courtyard. She had instructed that the boy could perform his antics to entertain her child, but he was never to touch her. The nurse strictly adhered to these orders.

The sun poured into both the grand first floor and the cellar; the acacia tree flourished with blossoms that eventually fell, only to bloom again the following year. The tree blossomed, and the porter's little son seemed to bloom too, looking fresh as a tulip.

The General’s daughter became frail and pale, resembling the petal of the acacia flower. She hardly came down to the tree anymore, as she preferred to take in the air from a carriage. When she went out with her mom, she would always nod at the porter's son, George; she even used to kiss her hand to him until her mother told her she was too old for that.

One morning, George was asked to deliver letters and newspapers that had been left at the porter’s room to the General. As he hurried upstairs, he passed the door of the sand-box and heard a faint whimpering noise. Thinking it was a lost chick calling for help, he was surprised to find it was the General’s daughter, decked out in lace and finery.

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Don’t tell Papa and Mama," she whimpered. "They’d be upset."

"What’s wrong, little lady?" George asked.

"It’s all on fire!" she answered. "It’s burning with a bright flame!" George rushed upstairs to the General's apartment, burst into the nursery, and saw the window curtain almost entirely aflame, while the wooden curtain pole was engulfed in fire. He quickly jumped onto a chair he hastily grabbed and managed to pull down the flaming materials before alarming everyone. If it weren't for him, the house would have burned down.

The General and his wife interrogated little Emily.

"I only struck one match," she confessed, "and it lit up right away, and then the curtain ignited. I spat at it to douse the flames as much as I could, but I couldn’t put it out, so I ran away and hid because Papa and Mama would be mad."

"I spat!" exclaimed the General's wife, "What a choice of words! Have you ever heard your parents talk about spitting? You must have picked that up from downstairs!"

George received a penny as a reward. Instead of spending it at the bakery, he saved it in his piggy bank. Before long, he had enough savings to buy a paint box to color in his numerous drawings. They seemed to flow effortlessly from his pencil and fingertips. His first colored creations were gifted to Emily.

"Charming!" the General said, and even the General's wife acknowledged that she could clearly see what the boy intended to draw. "He has genius," were the words that traveled down to the cellar.

The General and his wife were notable people. Their carriage displayed two coats of arms, one for each of them. The wife had those coats of arms embroidered on all their linens, even her nightcap and dressing bag. One coat of arms was particularly precious; her father had purchased it for a hefty sum because neither he nor she were born with it. She entered this world seven years ahead of the coat of arms, a fact many remembered, though the family seemed to forget. It was understandable for a man to feel overwhelmed when he bore such an emblem, and the General's wife felt particularly regal as she attended court balls.

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The General was an old man with gray hair, but he had a good ride and knew it; he went out daily on horseback, with a groom keeping a respectable distance behind him. When he arrived at a gathering, he looked as if he were entering a room on a high horse, and he carried many accolades that would astonish anyone; but then again, that wasn’t entirely his fault. In his youth, he had participated in significant autumn parades. He had a charming tale he loved to share about those days, the only one he could recall: a junior officer under his command once captured a prince, forcing the prince to ride through town alongside captured soldiers, the prince himself being a prisoner behind the General. This standout event was recounted annually by the General, who would repeat the memorable words he said when he returned the prince's sword; he remarked, "Only my junior officer could have captured your Highness; I could never have managed it!" The prince replied, "You are incomparable." In genuine warfare, however, the General had never participated; when conflict arrived in the country, he went into diplomacy instead. He spoke French so fluently that he often forgot his native language; he could dance gracefully and rode confidently, and he collected orders on his coat in truly impressive numbers. The sentries saluted him, one of the most beautiful girls had once saluted him and eventually became his wife, and together they had a lovely little girl, so captivating that it seemed she must have dropped from heaven. The porter's son danced for her as soon as she could understand and gave her all his colorful drawings, but little Emily merely looked at them with delight before tearing them apart. She truly was beautiful and delicate.

"My little Roseleaf!" exclaimed the General's wife, "You are destined to marry a prince."

The prince was already at the door, but they were unaware of it; people often overlook what lies just beyond their threshold.

"Just the other day, our boy shared his bread and butter with her!" the porter's wife exclaimed. There was neither cheese nor meat on it, but she enjoyed it as much as if it were roast beef. There would have been quite an uproar had the General and his wife witnessed this feast, but they did not.

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
George had shared his bread and butter with little Emily, and he would have shared his heart with her if it would have pleased her. He was a good kid—energetic and smart—and he was now attending night classes at the Academy to learn proper drawing. Little Emily was advancing in her studies as well; she was learning French from her nurse and had a dance instructor.

"George will be confirmed at Easter," said the porter's wife, as George had progressed to that stage.

"It would be best to have him become an apprentice," his father suggested. "He should have a good profession—and then he could move out of the house."

"He would have to sleep away from home," George's mother replied. "It’s not easy to find a master who can accommodate him at night, and we must provide him with clothing as well. The little bit of food he needs can be managed; he’s content with just some boiled potatoes, and he’s getting free education. Let him follow his own path. You can say he’ll bring us pride one day, and even the professor says so too."

The suit for confirmation was ready. His mother had sewn it herself, but the tailor who did repairs had cut it out, and he was an excellent cutter.

"If he had upper status and the ability to run a workshop with apprentices," the porter's wife noted, "he might have been a court tailor."

The outfit was complete, and the candidate for confirmation was ready. On that day, George received a fancy watch from his godfather, the wealthiest of them, who worked at the ironmonger's shop. The watch was a reliable one—though it always ran fast, it was better than being slow. It was an expensive gift. From the General's apartment came a morocco-bound hymn book sent by the little lady George had gifted pictures to. Inside the book, his name was written along with hers, stating she was his "gracious patroness." These words had been penned at the General’s wife’s behest, and the General proclaimed its inscription "Charming!"

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"What a lovely gesture for a family of such esteem," remarked the porter's wife; and George was sent upstairs to present himself in his confirmation suit, holding the hymn book.

The General's wife was bundled up and complaining of the bad headache she often had when her time dragged on. She looked at George with warmth, wishing him all the best and hoping he never faced her headache. The General paced about in his robe, wearing a cap with a long tassel and Russian boots with red tops. He walked back and forth three times, lost in his own thoughts and memories before finally stopping to say:

"So, little George is now a confirmed Christian. Be a good person and respect those in authority over you. Someday, when you’re an old man, you can tell others that the General gave you this advice."

That was the longest speech the General was used to making, and then he returned to his thoughts, looking as aristocratic as ever. But throughout everything George heard and saw, it was little Miss Emily who remained most vividly in his mind. She was so graceful, gentle, and pretty. If he were to draw her, it would have to be on a soap bubble. The fragrance of a fresh rose lingered in her dress and in her golden curls. It amazed him that he had once shared his bread and butter with her, and that she had enjoyed it with utmost delight, smiling at him at every second bite! Did she remember? Yes, she definitely did, for she had given him the beautiful hymn book as a keepsake. So when the first new moon of the following year came, he took a piece of bread, a penny, and his hymn book, and went outside, flipping the book open to find a psalm. It was a psalm of praise. Then he turned to find something that would be for little Emily. He carefully avoided opening to the section with funeral hymns, yet landed on one that mentioned the grave and death. But he thought to himself, this is not something one must truly believe; still, he was startled when, shortly after, the lovely girl had to stay in bed, and the doctor's carriage appeared at the gate every day.

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"They won’t keep her with them," the porter's wife sighed. "God knows whom He will call to Himself."

But they did keep her after all; and George kept sending her drawings. He drew the Czar's palace; the old Kremlin in Moscow, as it stood, its towers and domes looking like enormous green and gold cucumbers in George's rendition. Little Emily was thrilled, so when a week passed, George sent her more pictures, all featuring buildings, because she could imagine so many things inside the windows and doors.

He sketched a Chinese house with bells hanging from each of its sixteen stories. He drew two Greek temples with slender marble columns surrounding them. He illustrated a Norwegian church, clearly made entirely of wood, carved and beautifully assembled; every story appeared to have rockers like a cradle. But the most beautiful creation was the castle, drawn on one of the pages, which he called "Emily’s Castle." This was the kind of place she deserved. That’s what George envisioned, and so in this structure, he incorporated everything he thought was beautiful. It featured intricately carved wooden details, marble columns like the Greek temple, bells on every floor, and topped with green and gilded domes reminiscent of the Kremlin. It truly looked like a child’s castle, and under each window was a description of the room inside; for example: "Here Emily sleeps;" "Here Emily dances;" "Here Emily plays at hosting guests." It was a joy to behold, and undoubtedly, the castle was admired just as it deserved.

But the old Count—who was even more distinguished than the General and owned a castle himself—said nothing at all. He learned that it had been designed and drawn by the porter's little son. Not that he was especially little anymore, for he had already been confirmed. The old Count inspected the pictures and contemplated quietly as he did.

One gloomy day, when the weather was bleak, a bright day dawned for George; the Professor at the Academy summoned him to his office.

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Listen closely, my friend," said the Professor. "I need to talk with you. The Lord has blessed you with talents and placed you among good people. The old Count over there has been speaking highly of you. I’ve also seen your sketches, but we don’t need to discuss now, for they have a lot of room for improvement. From now on, you may attend my drawing class twice a week, and soon you’ll learn to improve. I think there’s more architect in you than painter. Take your time considering that; but today, go to the old Count and express your gratitude for having sent you such a friend."

The corner house belonged to the old Count. Around the windows were carvings of elephants and dromedaries from ancient times; yet the old Count preferred the present and everything it had to offer, whether it came from the first floor, or the cellar, or the attic.

"I believe," the porter's wife said, "the grander people are, the fewer airs they put on. How kind and direct the old Count is! He speaks just like you and me, unlike the General and his lady. George was absolutely thrilled yesterday after being welcomed so warmly by the Count, and I’m still delighted today after speaking with such a great person. Wasn’t it fortunate we didn’t bind George as an apprentice to a tradesman? He has his own abilities."

"But others must step in to help," the father added.

"That support he has now," the mother replied, "for the Count spoke to him very clearly and kindly."

"But I think it all started with the General," the father observed, "and we must express our gratitude to them as well."

"Let’s do so with all my heart," the mother declared, "though I doubt we have much to thank them for. I will thank God; and I will also give thanks for little Emily’s recovery."

Emily was doing well, and George was also thriving. Over the year, he earned the little silver medal at the Academy and later won the big one too.

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"It might have been better if he had been apprenticed to a tradesman," the porter's wife lamented, "because then we could have kept him close. What will he do in Rome? I might never see him again, not even if he comes back; but he probably won’t return, the dear boy."

"It’s fortune and fame awaiting him," the father commented.

"Yes, thank you, my friend," the mother replied, "but you’re saying what you don’t really mean. You’re just as heartsick as I am."

And it was all true about the sadness and the departure. Yet everyone spoke of it as a tremendous piece of good fortune for the young man. He had to bid farewell, including to the General. The General's wife avoided meeting him, citing her headache. On this occasion, he shared his only anecdote about the prince, repeating how the prince had said, "You are incomparable," as he extended a weary hand to George.

Emily also offered George her hand, her expression almost sorrowful, and George felt the saddest of all.

Time passes quickly when one is busy, and also drags when there’s nothing to do. Though it felt long, it was more meaningful for George than for the General's family; he contemplated the good folks from upstairs and downstairs. How were they managing? Yes, there was so much to write about, and letters could convey bright moments as well as dark days. Both sentiments were present in the letter announcing his father’s death and that his mother was now alone.

George’s mother wrote that Emily had visited her, acting like an angel of comfort during her time of grief; she added that she was allowed to keep her position as the porter.

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The General's wife kept a diary, logging every ball she attended and every visit she received. The diary was embellished with the visiting cards of diplomats and noble families, and it was a source of pride for her. Throughout the years, amid many headaches and many late nights spent at court balls, Emily finally attended her first court ball. Her mother had donned a bright red dress with black lace, styled in Spanish fashion; Emily wore delicate white, adorned with fluttering green silk ribbons among her golden locks, and on her head, she had a crown of water lilies. Her eyes sparkled a bright blue, her small mouth a delicate red; she resembled a water spirit, the epitome of beauty. Princes danced with her, one after the other, and for a week thereafter, the General's wife didn’t have a single headache.

However, the first ball wasn’t her last; Emily couldn't handle it all. Luckily, summer brought relief and outdoor activities. The old Count had invited the family to his castle, which featured a beautiful garden. Part of the garden was designed in an old-fashioned style, with rigid green hedges forming walls that sheltered peeping holes. Box trees and yew trees were trimmed into star and pyramid shapes, and fountains lined with shells cascaded from large grottos. All around stood beautifully sculpted figures that were evident in both their clothing and their expressions. Each flowerbed represented a different shape, be it a fish, a coat of arms, or a monogram, representing the French section of the garden. From there, visitors could transition into the lush, wild forest, where trees grew freely and splendidly. The grass was vibrant, easy to walk on, and was well-maintained. That represented the English portion of the garden.

"Old and new blend seamlessly here," remarked the Count. "In two years, the building itself will take a proper form, a stunning transformation will occur. I’ll show you the drawings, and I’ll present you to the architect, who’s coming to dine here today."

"It’s like paradise here," exclaimed the General's wife, "and over there stands a knight’s castle!"

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"That’s my poultry house," replied the Count. "The pigeons reside in the tower, the turkeys on the first floor, but old Elsie rules the ground floor. She has rooms in all directions. The sitting hens each have their room, the mother hens with chicks have theirs, and the ducks have a special door leading to the water."

And everyone set out to see these extraordinary sights. Old Elsie was in the room at ground level, with Architect George beside her. This was the first time he and Emily had met again after so many years, and it occurred in the poultry house.

Yes, there he stood, handsome enough for admiration. His face displayed an energetic sincerity; his dark hair shone, and a smile hinted at the thought, "I have a brownie sitting in my ear who knows all of you inside and out." Old Elsie had slipped off her wooden shoes, standing in her stockings to honor the noble visitors. Hens clucked, roosters crowed, and ducks waddled about, quacking away. But the lovely pale girl—the childhood friend of his, the General's daughter—stood there, a rosy blush illuminating her typically pale cheeks, her eyes wide, her mouth seeming to speak without a sound; this silent greeting from her was the most beautiful a young man could hope for from a lady, especially one he hadn’t danced with before.

The Count greeted him with a handshake, introducing him.

"He’s not exactly a stranger, our young friend George."

"Our little Master George!" remarked the General. "Old friends! Charming!"

"You’ve become quite Italian," said the General's wife. "I assume you speak the language like a native?"

"My wife sings the language, but doesn’t speak it," noted the General.

During dinner, George sat next to Emily, whom the General had escorted, while the Count guided the General's wife in.

George animatedly spoke about his travels; he was a great conversationalist and the life of the table, though the old Count could have held that title too. Emily remained quiet, but she listened intently, her eyes brightening, though she didn’t utter a word.

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In the flower-filled veranda, she and George found themselves together, concealed among the rose bushes. George took the lead in conversation now.

"Thank you for the kindness you've shown my mother," he said. "I know you visited her the night my father passed, you stayed until the end. I truly appreciate it!"

He gently took Emily's hand and kissed it—it was appropriate for such a moment. She blushed deeply, returning his gesture with a hand squeeze and a loving look from her beautiful blue eyes.

"Your mother was so lovely!" she said. "She adored her son! She let me read all your letters, so I almost feel as if I know you. You were so kind to me when I was little! You used to give me pictures."

"No, I still have your drawing of the castle," George replied.

"It's my turn to build that castle for real now," George declared, his heart warming with his own words.

The General and his wife discussed the porter's son in their room—how well he conducted himself and expressed his thoughts with perfect propriety.

"Intellect!" the General's wife remarked, but she had nothing more to add.

During that lovely summer, Mr. George visited the Count at his castle several times; he was quite missed during his absences.

"How much has God given you that He hasn’t given to us mere mortals?" Emily asked him. "Are you sure you’re truly grateful?"

George felt flattered that the lovely girl held him in such high regard, and he believed Emily must have extraordinary potential. The General was increasingly convinced that George was no mere cellar-dweller.

"His mother was a remarkable woman," he observed. "It’s only right I give her her due now that she has passed."

As the summer faded, winter arrived, bringing talk of Mr. George once more. He was held in high esteem and welcomed in elite circles; the General had seen him at a court ball.

Now the General's house was to host a ball for Emily, and could Mr. George be invited?

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Anyone the King invites can also be invited by the General," the General declared, puffing himself up to stand an inch taller.

Mr. George received an invitation and arrived; princes and counts graced the event, dancing perfectly. However, Emily could only dance one—the first—because she stumbled, causing her foot to hurt, which forced her to sit out the rest and watch. So she sat, observing as the architect stood beside her.

"I suppose you’re telling her the entire history of St. Peter’s," the General said as he passed by, smiling like the personification of patronage.

With the same patronizing smile, he welcomed Mr. George a few days later. The young man had come, no doubt, to express gratitude for the invitation to the ball. Or so it appeared—for he had something truly astounding to share; he spoke words of pure madness, an offer so irrational the General could scarcely believe his ears. It was "the height of absurdity," a marriage proposal—Mr. George was asking for Emily’s hand!

"Man!" the General exclaimed, his mind in turmoil. "I can't comprehend you at all. What are you saying? What do you want? I'm unfamiliar with you. Sir! Man! What possesses you to intrude into my home? Do you expect me to stand here and listen to you?" He retreated into his bedroom, locking the door behind him, leaving George standing there in silence.

"Has my father responded?" Emily asked, her voice trembling.

"He brushed me off," he said, "but a better time will come."

Tears welled in Emily’s eyes, but George’s reflected courage and hope; sunlight streamed through the window, casting its blessings upon them.

The General remained in his room, simmering. Yes, he was still boiling, ready to explode with an outburst of, "Madness! Porter! Lunacy!"

In less than an hour, the General's wife learned of it straight from him. She called Emily in and spent time alone with her.

"You poor child," she said. "To insult you so! To insult us both! There’s also tears in your eyes, and they suit you well. You appear lovely when you cry. You resemble how I looked on my wedding day. Go ahead and shed those tears, my sweet Emily."

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Then I must cry," Emily said, "if you and my father do not agree to this."

"Child!" the General's wife screamed. "You’re unwell! You’re saying foolish things, and I’m about to have a terrible headache! What a disaster is about to befall our family! Please don’t let your mother die, Emily, or you’ll end up with no mother!"

The General's wife’s eyes glistened with tears; she couldn't bear the thought of her own demise.

The newspapers carried an announcement: "Mr. George has been appointed Professor of the Fifth Class, number Eight."

"It’s a pity his parents are gone and can’t read this," remarked the new porters living in the cellar under the General's apartments. They were aware that the Professor had been born and raised within their walls.

"Yes, but that’s not much for a poor child, is it?" asked the woman.

"Eighteen dollars a year," said the man. "That’s a substantial amount of money."

"No, I mean the honor of it," the wife replied. "Do you think he cares about the money? Those few dollars he can earn many times over, and he’ll likely secure a wealthy wife too. If we had children of our own, husband, we would want our child to be an architect and a professor as well."

George was well thought of in the cellar, and equally well regarded on the first floor. The old Count himself made sure of that.

The childhood drawings he created inspired the talk. But how did the conversation turn to those pictures? They had been discussing Russia and Moscow, which led to mentioning the Kremlin that little George had once drawn for Miss Emily. He had illustrated many pictures, but the Count remembered one especially—"Emily’s Castle," where she would sleep, dance, and host her guests.

"The Professor is a true gentleman," remarked the Count, "and before he passes, he might become quite the privy councillor; he could indeed design a real castle for the young lady before that time comes—why not?"

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"That’s an odd jest," the General's wife commented once the Count had left. The General shook his head in thought and headed out for a ride, sitting even prouder on his high horse.

It was Emily's birthday. Gifts of flowers, books, letters, and visiting cards poured in. The General's wife kissed her on the lips, while the General kissed her forehead; they were affectionate parents, who also had to greet important visitors—two princes. They discussed balls and theaters, diplomatic missions, and the governance of empires; then the conversation shifted to talent and natural ability—leading to discussions about the young architect.

"He is building a legacy for himself," one remarked, "and he will surely secure a place among our foremost families."

"One of our foremost families!" echoed the General and later the General's wife. "What do they mean by that?"

"I know for whom it was meant," the General's wife said. "But I won’t disclose it. I don’t believe it—Heaven has its plans, but I will be amazed."

"I’m astonished as well!" the General exclaimed. "I can’t wrap my head around it!" And he fell into a reverie, waiting for ideas to surface.

There is a power—a nameless power—in the favor from above, the favor of Providence, and this was something little George possessed. But let’s not forget about the birthday.

Emily’s room was filled with the scent of flowers, sent by friends both male and female; on the table lay precious gifts for well-wishing and remembrance, but none arrived from George—none could. It didn’t matter, though, as the whole house was flooded with memories of him. From the ash-bin, the bloom of memory emerged, reminding Emily of the moment she sat there crying on the day the window curtains had caught fire, when George rushed in as a makeshift firefighter. A glance from the window led her to the acacia tree, reminding her of their childhood days. The flowers and leaves may have fallen, but the tree still stood, covered in frost, resembling a massive coral branch, while the moon shined brightly among the twigs, unchanged just as it had been when George shared his bread and butter with little Emily.

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
From a box, the girl retrieved drawings of the Czar’s palace and her own castle—reminders of George. She examined the artwork, stirring memories. She recalled the day she had secretly visited the porter's wife, who lay dying. It felt as if she were sitting beside her again, holding the woman’s hand during her last moments and hearing the last words: "Bless George!" The mother thought of her son, and now Emily interpreted her words differently. Yes, George was undoubtedly with her on her birthday.

The next day marked another birthday in that house: the General's birthday. He had been born the day after his daughter, but many years earlier. Numerous presents arrived, and among them was an exquisitely crafted saddle—one just like the prince had. Now, who could have sent this saddle? The General was delighted. Along with the saddle came a little note. Had it said, "thank you for the lovely reception yesterday," we could have easily guessed who sent it. Instead, it read, "From someone the General does not know."

"Who in the world don’t I know?" the General challenged. "I know everyone;" and his thoughts flitted through society, as he had connections with everyone present. "This saddle must have come from my wife!" he concluded. "She’s teasing me—charming!"

But she wasn't teasing him; those days were long gone.

Once again, there was a celebration, though it wasn’t in the General's house; it was a masquerade ball at the prince’s, where masks were permitted.

The General dressed as Rubens, in a Spanish costume, complete with a ruff around his neck, a sword at his side, and an air of superiority. The General's wife wore a black velvet gown made high around her neck—exceedingly warm—and with an enormous ruff around her neck—authentically styled after a Dutch painting that the General greatly admired for its portrayal of hands.

Emily was dressed as Psyche. In white crepe and lace, she looked like a gliding swan, needing no wings aside from a few that symbolized Psyche.

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Brightness, opulence, light, and flowers, wealth, and taste filled the ball; there was so much to take in that the lovely hands of Madame Rubens garnered no attention at all.

A black domino, with an acacia flower in his cap, danced with Psyche.

"That’s His Royal Highness," the General replied, without a doubt. "I recognized him immediately by his handshake."

General Rubens had no doubts. He approached the black domino and penned royal letters in the mask's hand. Though the domino denied this, he still offered a hint.

The words that accompanied the saddle read: "One whom you do not know, General."

"But I know you," the General declared. "You were the one who sent me the saddle."

The domino raised his hand and vanished among the other guests.

"Who was that black domino you were dancing with, Emily?" the General's wife inquired.

"I didn’t ask his name, since I thought you knew it. It’s the Professor, Count!" she went on, directing her comment at the nobleman standing nearby. "A black domino, adorned with acacia blossoms in his cap."

"Very likely, my dear lady," the Count replied. "But one of the princes wears the exact same costume."

"I recognized his handshake," the General declared. "The saddle was sent by the prince. I’m confident of it, to the point that I could invite that domino to dinner."

"Do so. If it is the prince, he will undoubtedly accept," the Count replied.

"And if it’s the other person, he won’t come," the General remarked as he approached the black domino, who was in conversation with the King. The General respectfully extended an invitation to meet and smiled, so certain of the individual he was inviting. His voice was loud and clear.

The domino lifted his mask, and it was George. "Do you care to extend your invitation again, General?" he asked.

The General did seem to stand an inch taller then, adopting an even more dignified demeanor; he took a couple of backward steps and then two forward, as if performing a dance, letting a gravity of expression fill his face as much as he could muster. But he answered,

title: THE PORTER'S SON
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I never take back my words! You are invited, Professor!" He bowed, ensuring the King must have overheard the entire exchange.

The General hosted a dinner, but only the old Count and his protégé received invitations.

"I’ve secured my position at his table," George thought. "That’s laying the first brick of the foundation."

And the foundation stone was indeed laid, with great ceremony, at the General’s home.

The man had arrived and spoke as someone from proper society, making himself quite likable, prompting the General to exclaim, "Charming!" often. The General even discussed this dinner with a court lady, which led her to request an invitation to meet the Professor the next time he came. Naturally, he was invited again, and when he arrived, he was charming again, even having the skills to play chess.

"He’s not just from the cellar," the General remarked. "He’s a distinguished fellow. There are indeed many distinguished personalities of that type, and it's no fault of his."

Since the Professor was welcomed into the King’s palace, it was only proper that he was also received by the General; but the idea of him becoming part of their family was simply inconceivable, even if the entire town buzzed about it.

He continued to rise in status. The blessing of favor fell from above, so no one was shocked when he eventually became a privy councillor, with Emily as a privy councillor’s lady.

"Life can either be a tragedy or a comedy," the General mused. "In tragedies, people die, in comedies, they marry."

In this instance, they did marry. They had three clever boys—but not all at once.

The delightful children rode their hobbyhorses throughout the house whenever they visited their grandparents. The General, too, rode on his stick, trailing behind them as the groom to his small privy councillors.

And even the General's wife smiled at them while relaxing on her couch, despite her worst headaches.
