So far did George go, and much further; otherwise, it wouldn’t have been worth telling the tale of THE PORTER'S SON.

title: THE PORTUGUESE DUCK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
A duck made her way from Portugal, but some said she came from Spain, which was nearly the same thing. Regardless, everyone called her the "Portuguese duck." She laid eggs, was killed, cooked, and that was the end of her. However, the ducklings that hatched from those eggs were also named "Portuguese," which might be up for debate. Of all the ducklings, only one remained in the duckyard, which could easily be called a farmyard since the chickens were allowed in, and the rooster strutted around with an air of authority. "He really annoys me with his loud crowing," the Portuguese duck sighed, "but I can't deny he's a handsome bird, even if he’s not a drake. He should tone down his voice, much like those little birds singing in the lime trees over in our neighbor's garden. That kind of singing is an art learned in polite society. How sweetly they sing! I call it Portuguese singing. If I had such a little songbird, I’d be the most caring mother to him—it's just in my nature, part of my Portuguese blood."

As she spoke, one of the little songbirds tumbled off the roof and landed in the yard. The cat chased after him, but he’d escaped with a broken wing and ended up crashing down. "That's just typical of the cat; she's a villain," the Portuguese duck remarked. "I remember those ways from when I had my own ducklings. How can such a creature roam freely on the rooftops? I doubt they’d allow that in Portugal." She felt sorry for the little bird, and so did all the other ducks that weren’t Portuguese.

"Poor little thing!" they said one after another as they gathered around. "We may not be able to sing, but we have something inside us that resonates, even if we don’t verbalize it."

"But I can talk," the Portuguese duck declared, "and it’s my duty to help this little one." She hopped into the water trough and flapped her wings so vigorously that the little bird was nearly soaked. But her intentions were good. "That was a kind act!" she said. "I hope the others will take inspiration from it."

title: THE PORTUGUESE DUCK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Tweet, tweet!" chirped the little bird, still struggling with his broken wing and unable to properly shake himself dry. He understood that the bath was meant to help, so he said, "You are very kind, madam, but I don’t want another bath."

"I’ve never really thought about my heart," the Portuguese duck replied, "but I know I care for all my fellow creatures, except for the cat; and who could blame me for that? She ate two of my ducklings. But please, make yourself at home—it's easy to get cozy here. I’m from another country, as my feathers show. My drake is a local, not of my kind, but I’m not proud about that. If anyone here can understand you, it’s definitely me."

"She’s full of 'Portulak,'" chimed in a witty little common duck. All the common ducks found it funny, since "Portulak" sounded like Portugal. They elbowed each other and said, "Quack! That was clever!"

The other ducks began to take notice of the little bird. "The Portuguese has quite a way with words," they said. "To be honest, we don’t want to overwhelm our beaks with such long phrases, but we sympathize with you just as much. If nothing else, we can walk around with you, and that feels like the best support we can offer."

"You have a beautiful voice," one of the older ducks said. "It must feel great to spread so much joy with your singing. I can't judge, so I’ll keep my beak shut, which is better than babbling nonsense like the others."

"Don’t bother him too much," interjected the Portuguese duck. "He needs rest and care. Little songbird, would you like me to prepare another bath for you?"

"Oh no! Please, just let me dry off," the little bird begged.

"The water cure is the only remedy I know when I’m not feeling well," the Portuguese replied. "Having fun is also beneficial. The neighborhood hens will be visiting soon. A couple of Cochin Chinese will join us; they have feathers on their legs and are very refined, having traveled from afar. That’s why I treat them with more respect than the others."

title: THE PORTUGUESE DUCK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
When the hens arrived, the rooster was unusually polite today and didn’t act rude. "You’re quite the songster," he said. "You do so much with your little voice, but to be known, one really needs to be louder and sharper."

The two Chinese fowls were captivated by the singing bird’s appearance. His feathers were a bit ruffled from the bath, making him look quite like a tiny Chinese fowl. "He’s delightful," they whispered to one another, conversing in the most refined Chinese dialect. "We are of the same esteemed lineage as you. Ducks, even the Portuguese, are all waterfowl, as you must have noticed. Very few know us or bother to get acquainted, even among the fowls. We were born to belong to a higher tier of society than most. But that doesn’t trouble us; we continue our way among those with entirely different viewpoints. We focus on the positive and only speak of what’s good, though that’s often tough when there's not much of it around. Besides us and the rooster, no one else in the yard is truly talented or polite. Watch out for that one over there with short tail feathers; she’s cunning. The oddly patterned one with the crooked wing stripes is a troublemaker and hates losing, even when she’s wrong. That hefty duck over there speaks ill of everyone, which goes against our principles. If we can’t say something good, we keep it to ourselves. The Portuguese is the only one among us who has had an education to socialize with, but she can be passionate and talks too much about 'Portugal.'"

"I wonder what those two Chinese are whispering about," one duck said to another; "they’re always doing it, and it drives me crazy. We never say a word to them."

Then the drake walked up and mistook the little singing bird for a sparrow. "Honestly, I can’t see the difference," he said. "They all seem the same to me. He’s just a toy, and if people want playthings, let them have them, I say."

title: THE PORTUGUESE DUCK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Don’t pay any attention to him," the Portuguese advised quietly. "He’s competent in business matters, but for him, business is everything. Now, I’m going to lie down and rest. We owe it to ourselves to be nice and plump for when they prepare us with sage, onions, and apples." She settled down in the sun, half-closing one eye, comfortable enough that she dozed off. The little singing bird tended to his broken wing for a while, then lay down close to his protector. The sun was bright and warm, and he found it to be a perfect spot. Meanwhile, the neighboring fowls were awake, and honestly, they came to the duckyard only to scavenge for food. The Chinese were the first to leave, followed by the rest of the hens.

The witty little duck remarked about the Portuguese, calling her a "doting ducky." All the other ducks laughed at this. "Doting ducky," they whispered. "Oh, that’s too clever!" They then repeated the earlier joke about "Portulak," deciding it was hilarious. Afterward, they all settled down for a nap.

After snoozing for a while, something suddenly landed in the yard with a loud thud, making all the ducks flap their wings in surprise. The Portuguese woke up too and hurried to the other side, accidentally stepping on the little singing bird.

"Tweet!" he exclaimed. "You stepped on me pretty hard, madam."

"Well, why are you lying in my path?" she shot back. "Don’t be so sensitive. I have nerves too, but I don’t cry 'tweet.'"

"Don’t be upset," the little bird said. "The 'tweet' slipped out of my beak without me meaning to."

The Portuguese ignored him and dove into the food, eating as quickly as she could. When she was done, she lay down again. The little bird, wanting to be friendly, started to sing.

"Chirp and twitter,
The dew-drops shimmer,
In the sunny hours of spring.
I’ll sing my best,
Until I rest,
With my head beneath my wing."

title: THE PORTUGUESE DUCK
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Now I want to rest after my meal," the Portuguese duck said. "You have to follow the house rules while you’re here. I’m going to sleep now."

The little bird was taken aback; his intention had been kind. When she woke up later, he was there with a little kernel of corn he’d found, placing it at her feet. But since she hadn’t slept well, she was in a bad mood. "Give that to a chick," she told him, "and stop standing in my way."

"Why are you upset with me?" the little singing bird asked. "What have I done?"

"Done?" the Portuguese duck echoed. "Your way of expressing yourself isn't very polite. I should draw your attention to that."

"It was sunny here yesterday," the little bird replied, "but today it's gloomy and the air feels stuffy."

"You don’t seem to know much about the weather," she shot back. "The day isn’t over yet. Don’t stand there looking so clueless."

"But you’re looking at me just like the wicked eyes stared at me when I fell into the yard yesterday."

"Impertinent creature!" the Portuguese duck exclaimed. "You wouldn’t dare compare me to the cat—she’s a predator! I don’t have a malicious bone in my body. I’ve looked after you like a mother, and I have a good heart!" With that, she pecked at the little singing bird, and he fell lifeless to the ground. "What’s going on here?" she wondered. "Could he not withstand even a tiny peck? Clearly, he wasn’t made for this world. I tried my best to care for him."

Just then, the rooster from the neighboring yard poked his head in and crowed energetically.

"You’ll deafen me with your crowing!" she yelled. "It’s your fault he’s lost his life, and here I am, close to losing mine too!"

"There’s not much of him left there," the rooster pointed out.

"Speak of him respectfully," the Portuguese duck said. "He had manners and education, and yes, he could sing. He was kind and gentle—qualities that are as rare in animals as they are in those who consider themselves human."

The other ducks gathered around the lifeless little bird. Ducks can be very passionate, whether out of envy or empathy. Since there was nothing to envy here, they were all filled with compassion, even the two Chinese ducks. "We will never have another singing bird among us; he was almost one of us," they whispered, and they wept noisily, causing all the other fowls to cluck in unison, although the ducks walked away with redder eyes. "We have hearts of our own," they said. "No one can deny that."

"Hearts?" the Portuguese chuckled. "You have hearts indeed, almost as tender as the ducks in Portugal."

"Let’s focus on getting something to eat now," said the drake. "That’s the most important thing. If one of our toys breaks, we have plenty more."

title: THE PRINCESS AND THE PEA
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a prince who dreamed of marrying a princess—but she had to be a true princess. He journeyed all over the world in search of one, but no matter where he looked, he couldn't find what he desired. There were plenty of princesses around, but he always sensed something wasn't quite right about them. So, he returned home, feeling sad, because deep down, he really wanted a genuine princess.

One evening, a fierce storm rolled in; thunder cracked, lightning flashed, and the rain came down in sheets. Suddenly, there was a knocking at the city gate, and the old king went to open it.

Standing at the gate was a princess. But oh my, the rain and wind had made her look a mess! Water dripped from her hair and clothes, soaking into her shoes and pooling at her heels. Despite her disheveled appearance, she claimed to be a real princess.

"Well, we'll find out for sure soon enough," thought the old queen. But she kept her thoughts to herself, went into the bedroom, stripped the bed of all its bedding, and placed a pea at the very bottom. Then she stacked twenty mattresses on top of the pea, followed by twenty fluffy eider-down beds.

The princess had to sleep on this setup all night. The next morning, they asked her how she had slept.

"Oh, very poorly!" she replied. "I hardly got any sleep at all. I don’t know what was in that bed, but I was lying on something hard, and now I'm bruised all over! It's awful!"

Now they were certain that she was a genuine princess because she had felt the pea even through the twenty mattresses and the twenty eider-down beds.

No one but a true princess could be that sensitive.

So the prince took her as his wife because now he knew he had found a real princess. The pea was put in a museum, where it can still be seen today, unless someone has taken it.

title: THE PSYCHE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the conflict between dreams and reality
text_chunk:
In the fresh morning light, a brilliant star gleams like the brightest beacon of dawn. Its rays dance on the white wall, almost as if it wants to share the stories it has gathered over thousands of years in our ever-rolling world. Let’s listen to one of its tales.

"A little while ago"—though my "little while" stretches into centuries for you—"my rays followed a young artist. This was in the city of the Popes, in the vibrant metropolis of Rome. Much has changed there over time, but nothing compares to the shift from youth to old age. Back then, the palace of the Caesars was already in ruins, much like today; fig trees and laurel bushes grew among the fallen marble columns, and in the abandoned bathing halls, where remnants of gilding clung to the walls, the Coliseum stood as a grand ruin. Church bells rang, wafting incense filled the air, and processions marched through the streets, carrying flaming torches and glowing canopies. The Holy Church was alive, and art was revered as something sacred. In Rome lived the world’s greatest painter, Raphael, and the finest sculptor, Michelangelo. Even the Pope honored them both, making sure to visit them. Art was recognized and celebrated, rewarded for its greatness. Yet still, many magnificent creations went unnoticed.

"In a narrow alley, there was an old house that used to be a temple; now, it was home to a young sculptor. He was unknown, just starting out. He had friends, fellow artists, sharing the same youthful dreams and hopes, who told him he had a wealth of talent. But they also said he was foolish for lacking faith in his own abilities; he would often break the clay sculptures he created and never finish anything. They insisted that a work needed to be complete to be appreciated and to earn money.

"'You’re a dreamer,' they said to him, 'and that’s your downfall. The reason is simple: you’ve never truly lived. You haven’t experienced life fully and enjoyed it as you should. In your youth, you need to mix your personality with life, let them become one. Just look at the esteemed master Raphael, whom the Pope admires and the world praises. He enjoys life, wine, and bread.'

title: THE PSYCHE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the conflict between dreams and reality
text_chunk:
"'And he even fancies the baker’s daughter, the lovely Fornarina,' added Angelo, one of the livelier friends.

"Yes, they said plenty like this, according to their own understanding. They wanted to pull the young artist into the thrilling, chaotic life they lived, and sometimes he felt tempted to join them. He had warm blood, a vivid imagination, and could join in their laughter and conversation; but what they called 'Raphael's joyous life' faded away in his mind when he glimpsed the divine brilliance in the great master's paintings. Standing in the Vatican, in front of the beautiful figures sculpted from marble ages ago, he felt a swell in his chest, a sense of something profound, something sacred, something uplifting. He yearned to create similar forms from the rough stone. He wanted to depict what stirred within him, rising from his heart into the infinite; but how? In what shape? The soft clay yielded beautiful forms under his hands, yet the next day he would smash them, just like before.

"One day, he walked past one of Rome's grand palaces. He paused at the vast open gate and found a garden surrounded by arched walkways. The garden bloomed with radiant roses. Brilliant white lilies stood tall from a marble fountain where clear water splashed, and then he saw her glide by—the daughter of the noble family, graceful, delicate, and breathtakingly fair. He had never seen such beauty in real life before—yet wait! He remembered seeing a depiction of her painted by Raphael as a Psyche in one of those very palaces. Yes, he had seen her in paint, but now she passed him in living form.

"The memory of her lingered in his mind and heart. He returned to his modest room and sculpted a figure of Psyche from clay. It became that enchanting Roman girl, the noble maiden; and for the first time, he registered satisfaction with his work. It meant something to him because it was her. When his friends saw his creation, they cheered with joy, declaring that this work truly demonstrated his artistic talent, which they had known all along, and that the world should recognize it too.

title: THE PSYCHE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the conflict between dreams and reality
text_chunk:
"The clay figure was lifelike and lovely, but it lacked the bright whiteness and permanence of marble. So they declared that Psyche had to be crafted in marble from then on. He already had a prized block of stone, a gift from his parents, lying in the courtyard for years. Fragments of glass, climbing weeds, and wilted plants cluttered around it, tarnishing its purity, but beneath the grime, the block was as white as mountain snow. It was from this very block that Psyche would emerge.

One morning, it happened—though the bright Star remained silent on this matter, we know it did occur. A noble Roman gathering entered the narrow lane. Their carriage halted at the top of the street, and they walked toward the young sculptor's home to see his work, having heard whispers about him. And who were these esteemed guests? A poor young man, or perhaps a fortunate one. The noble lady stood in the room, radiantly smiling when her father remarked, "It’s your living image." That smile was one that could never be replicated, just like the sparkling gaze she gave the artist, a look that lifted him up and yet felt like it could crush him.

"The Psyche must be sculpted in marble," the wealthy patrician declared. Those words breathed life into the lifeless clay and heavy marble block—and into the deeply moved artist. "I’ll buy it when it’s finished," the nobleman continued.

A new era seemed to dawn in the humble studio. Life and cheer filled the space, and industrious creativity thrived. The bright Morning Star watched as the sculpting progressed. The clay itself appeared inspired since her presence arrived, molding itself into an even finer likeness of her recognizable features.

"Now I understand what life is," the artist exclaimed joyfully; "it’s Love! It’s the selfless devotion to allow beauty to blossom from the soul! What my friends call life and enjoyment is a fleeting illusion; it’s like bubbles in murky water, not the pure, heavenly wine that connects us to existence."

title: THE PSYCHE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the conflict between dreams and reality
text_chunk:
The marble block was carefully positioned. The chisel struck it powerfully, carving away large pieces; measurements were taken, points and lines sketched—slowly, the stone transformed into a human female form, a figure of beauty, becoming Psyche, exquisite and divine—a celestial being in human shape. The once heavy stone looked like a gliding, dancing Psyche, complete with the innocent, heavenly smile that had mirrored in the artist's soul.

The Morning Star watched and understood the emotions churning within the young man, reading the color shifts on his cheeks and the spark of light in his eyes as he busily worked, faithfully recreating the visions that inspired him from above.

"You are a master, akin to the greats of ancient Greece," his excited friends exclaimed; "soon, the entire world will admire your Psyche."

"My Psyche!" he echoed back. "Yes, she’s mine. I, too, am an artist like those revered figures of the past. Providence has blessed me and made me her equal."

He knelt down in gratitude, whispering a prayer to Heaven, but then he forgot about Heaven for her—his focus was on her likeness in marble—the Psyche standing there, seemingly made of snow, glowing in the morning light.

He hoped to see her in person, the graceful, living Psyche, whose voice echoed like music in his ears. He was ready to tell the noble family that the marble Psyche was complete. He made his way there, walking through the open courtyard where water splashed from the dolphin's mouth into marble basins, blooming roses and snowy lilies surrounding him. He entered the grand hall, its walls and ceilings embellished with gold and vibrant colors. Dressed-up servants, outfitted like they were going to a festival, moved about, while some lounged on carved oak furniture as if they were the masters. He shared his purpose for visiting, and they led him to the noble lady’s chamber, where she wanted to see him too. The servants guided him through more lavish halls and rooms into her space, where she stood as the centerpiece of beauty.

title: THE PSYCHE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the conflict between dreams and reality
text_chunk:
She spoke to him. No hymn or holy chant could touch his heart like the sound of her voice. He took her hand and gently raised it to his lips. No rose was softer, yet a fire surged within him from this delicate bloom—a surge of power overcame him, and words poured out of him, yet he didn’t even know what he was saying. Did a volcano know it was unleashing molten lava? He confessed his feelings for her. She stood before him, stunned, offended, proud, her face twisted in contempt, as if she had just brushed against a cold, repulsive snake. Her cheeks flushed red, her lips whitened, and her eyes sparked with fury, dark as the depths of night.

And she turned away from him. Her beautiful face bore a stone-like expression, reminiscent of a statue with snaky locks.

Like a man struck down, he staggered down the staircase and into the street. Stumbling like one in a dream, he found his way back to his home. Then he was struck with madness and pain; he grabbed his hammer, raised it high, and rushed forward to destroy the beautiful marble statue. But in his turmoil, he didn’t notice that Angelo stood beside him. Angelo caught his arm with a firm grip, crying out,

They wrestled together. Angelo was stronger, and with a deep sigh of fatigue, the young artist collapsed into a chair.

"What happened?" Angelo asked. "Pull yourself together. Speak!"

But what could he say? How could he explain? Since Angelo couldn’t make sense of his friend’s hurried words, he held back on questioning him further and simply said,

"You’re losing your mind with all this dreaming. Be a man, like others, and stop living in fantasies, or you might just drive yourself to madness. An enjoyable feast will let you sleep peacefully and happily. Trust me—your time will come when you’re old, your body shrinks, and one sunny day, while everything laughs and rejoices, you’ll find yourself an old, withered plant that’s done growing. I live in reality, not in dreams. Come with me. Be a man!"

title: THE PSYCHE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the conflict between dreams and reality
text_chunk:
Angelo pulled the artist along with him. In that moment, the urgency to break free from his old ways took hold; he felt a pull to escape his past self—to forget his individuality if at all possible; this urge prompted him to follow Angelo.

In a tucked-away part of Rome, there was a tavern popular with artists. Built on the ruins of ancient baths, great yellow citrons dangled from dark green leaves, partially covering the old reddish-yellow walls. The tavern held a vaulted chamber, almost cave-like, where a lamp flickered before the Madonna’s image. A warm fire blazed on the hearth, roasting and boiling food; outside, beneath the citron and laurel trees, a few tables stood covered.

Their friends welcomed the two artists with cheers. Little food was eaten, but much was sipped, lifting the spirits of the gathering. They sang songs, strummed guitars, and soon the Salterello tune played, prompting everyone to dance. Two young Roman girls, who modeled for the artists, joined in the celebration. They were charming Bacchantes—not Psyches, but fresh, vibrant, and lively.

The heat was intense that day! Even after the sun went down, the warmth lingered. There was fire in the blood, fire in every gaze, fire all around. The air felt golden and rosy, and life seemed to shimmer with beauty.

"Finally, you’ve joined us!" his friends exclaimed. "Now let yourself be swept away by the currents around you!"

"I’ve never felt so good, so joyful!" cried the young artist. "You’re all right—I was a fool, a dreamer. A person belongs to reality, not fantasies."

With melodies and lively guitars, the young people returned that evening from the tavern, strolling through the narrow streets; the two radiant flowers, the daughters of the Campagna, accompanied them.

In Angelo's studio, amidst a chaos of colorful sketches and vibrant paintings, the laughter was warm, but no less lively. There lay numerous sketches resembling the Campagna daughters, showcasing their fresh, lively charm, though the originals outshone their portraits. The flames from the six-armed lamp flickered intensely, illuminating the atmosphere where the essence of humanity radiated, almost divine.

title: THE PSYCHE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the conflict between dreams and reality
text_chunk:
"Apollo! Jupiter! I feel as though I’m soaring up to your heavens—experiencing your glory! I feel the blossom of life unfurling in my veins right now!"

Indeed, the blossom unfurled, then burst and fell, leaving a miasma that clouded vision and twisted thoughts; the sensory fireworks dimmed, plunging everything into darkness.

Suddenly, he found himself back in his own room. He sank onto his bed and attempted to gather his thoughts.

"Get out!" The words tumbled from his lips, ringing with sorrow from the depths of his heart. "Wretched soul, go away!" A heavy sigh heaved from his chest.

"Go away!" Her words, the living Psyche’s words, echoed in his heart, slipping from his lips. He buried his head in his pillows, his thoughts swirling, and soon fell into a deep sleep.

At dawn, he bolted upright, trying to gather his thoughts again. What had happened? Had everything before been a dream? The visit with her, the feast at the tavern, the evening spent with the vibrant Campagna flowers? No, it was all real—a reality he had never experienced before.

In the purple dawn, the bright Star glimmered, its beams falling on him and the marble Psyche. As he gazed at his immortal creation, he felt tainted, so he draped cloth over the statue but soon lifted it again to unveil it—yet he couldn't bring himself to look again at his own work.

Gloomily, silently, absorbed in his thoughts, he remained throughout the long day; he heard nothing of the world around him, and no one suspected the turmoil within this human soul.

Days and weeks slipped by, but the nights stretched longer than the days. One morning, the shining Star bore witness as he rose, pale and trembling with fever, from his sorrowful bed; he approached the statue, removed the cloth, gazed long and sorrowfully at his work, and then, almost collapsing under the burden, dragged the statue outside into the garden. In that space stood an old dry well, now just a hole. He cast the Psyche inside, poured dirt over her, and concealed the spot with twigs and nettles.

title: THE PSYCHE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the conflict between dreams and reality
text_chunk:
"Away! Go away!" was the brief epitaph he uttered.

The Star witnessed all of this from the pink morning sky, its rays trembling upon two large tears that rolled down the young man's feverish cheeks. Soon, it was said he was gravely ill, lying on a bed of suffering.

Brother Ignatius, a monk, visited him as both physician and friend, bearing words of comfort, sharing tales of faith, and discussing the peace to be found in the church, as well as the sinful nature of humanity—an offering of rest and mercy in heaven.

His words fell like soothing sunbeams on a barren field. The soil reacted, transforming into misty images of reality, allowing him to glimpse human life as something meaningless and deceptive—vanity and delusion that had clouded his existence. They told him art was a trickster, leading people into vanity and base desires, making us untrue to ourselves, unfaithful to friends, and disloyal to Heaven, while echoing the serpent’s promise, "Eat, and you shall become like God."

It felt as if he had finally discovered himself, uncovering the path that led to truth and serenity. In the church lay light and the glow of God—inside the monk’s cell, he could find the solace that would help the tree of human life flourish into eternity.

Brother Ignatius fueled his desires, solidifying his resolve. A world-bound youth became a church servant—the young artist renounced the earthly realm and withdrew into the monastery.

The brothers welcomed him warmly, treating his initiation like a Sunday celebration. To him, it felt as if Heaven shone brightly within the church, illuminating him through the holy images and the cross. That evening, as he stood in his small cell, gazing out over ancient Rome—seeing the crumbling temples and the grand Coliseum draped in spring’s embrace, where acacias blossomed, ivy flourished, and roses burst into bloom—he felt a deeper emotion than he had ever known. The serene Campagna spread out towards the snowy blue mountains, melting into one another, breathing peace and beauty—a dreamlike vision!

Yes, this world felt like a dream—a fleeting illusion that could return again and again; but life in the monastery was measured in years—long years, and many years.

title: THE PSYCHE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the conflict between dreams and reality
text_chunk:
Much that arises from within makes people sinful and impure. He felt the truth of this deeply. What flames sometimes ignited within him! What a source of corruption swelled endlessly! He disciplined his body, but the real struggle came from within.

One day, many years later, he ran into Angelo, who recognized him.

"Man!" Angelo exclaimed. "It’s you! Are you happy now? You’ve sinned against God and cast aside His gifts—you’ve ignored your purpose in this world! Remember the parable of the entrusted talent! The MASTER, who spoke that parable, spoke the truth! What have you gained? What have you discovered? Are you creating a religion and a dreamy life of your own imagination, like nearly everyone else? What if this is all a dream—a beautiful delusion!"

"Get away from me, Satan!" the monk replied, and walked away from Angelo.

"There is a devil, a real devil! Today, I faced him!" the monk thought. "Once, I extended my hand to him, and he took my whole arm. But now," he sighed, "the evil resides in me, and in him too; yet he walks with head held high, reveling in his comfort, while I sought solace in the comforts of faith. What if all of this is nothing more than a consolation? What if everything here, just like the world I left, is merely a beautiful illusion—like the magnificent evening clouds or the hazy blue of far-off hills? When you approach them, they are different! Oh, eternity! You beckon like a vast, calm ocean, filling us with hope—and when we set out on your waters, we sink, disappear, and cease to exist. Deception! I’m done with this!"

Tearless but absorbed in bitter thoughts, he sat on his hard bed, then knelt down—before whom? Before the stone cross on the wall? No, it was a mere habit that made him adopt this posture.

The deeper he peered into his own heart, the greater the darkness seemed. "Nothing within, nothing beyond—this life wasted and discarded!" This thought swelled like a snowball, crushing him under its weight.

title: THE PSYCHE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the conflict between dreams and reality
text_chunk:
"I have no one to share my grievances with. I cannot speak about the nagging worm inside me. My secret is my captive; if I allow it to escape, it will consume me!"

The divine power that lived within him suffered and struggled.

"Oh Lord, my Lord!" he cried out in despair, "be kind and grant me faith. I threw away the gift you bestowed upon me—left my mission unfulfilled. I lacked strength, and strength you did not give me. Immortality—the Psyche within me—away with it! It shall be buried just like that Psyche, the brightest spark of my life; it will never rise from its grave!"

The Star blazed in the rosy sky, a Star destined to be extinguished while the soul continues to live; its flickering rays fell onto the white wall, but did not inscribe any hope of mercy or reliance on the divine love that invigorates the heart of those who believe.

"The Psyche within can never perish. Will it exist in my consciousness? Can the unimaginable happen? Yes, yes. My existence is unfathomable. You, Lord, are unfathomable. Your entire world is a wonder of strength, glory, and love."

His eyes sparkled, then closed in death. The tolling of the church bell was the last sound that echoed above him as he lay dead, buried with earth from Jerusalem, mingled with the dust of countless pious souls.

After many years, they unearthed his skeleton, as they had done with the skeletons of the monks who had come before him; it wore a brown robe, a rosary clasped in its bony hand, and the remains were placed among the other skeletons in the convent cloisters. Outside, the sun shone, while inside, the censers swung and mass was said.

The bones crumbled into dust, merging with others. Skulls piled up, forming an outer wall around the church, and his head lay in the blazing sun, among many nameless dead. And look! Something wriggled in the sunshine within the hollow eye sockets! What could that be? A shimmering lizard darted through the empty orbs. This little creature represented the last flicker of life left in that skull, once filled with grand thoughts, bright dreams, love of art, and where hot tears had flowed. The lizard scampered away and vanished, as the skull itself crumbled into dust among dust.

title: THE PSYCHE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the conflict between dreams and reality
text_chunk:
Centuries flowed by. The bright Star shone just as brightly, unchanged and radiant, as it had for thousands of years, while the air blazed rose-red, as fresh as roses, crimson as blood.

Where there once stood the narrow lane with its temple ruins, a convent was now built. This morning, they began to dig a grave in the convent garden for a young nun who had passed away. The spade struck something hard—an object that dazzled in white. Soon, a block of marble appeared, a rounded shoulder exposed; then, with careful hands, the workers revealed a female head and delicate butterfly wings. From the grave, into the rosy morning light, they unearthed a stunning statue of Psyche, intricately carved in white marble.

"How beautiful, how perfect it is!" cried the onlookers. "A remnant of the finest artistic era!"

But who could the sculptor have been? No one knew; no one remembered him, except for the bright star that had shone through the ages. The star had witnessed that earthly life and acknowledged the man’s struggles and weaknesses—after all, he was only human. The life of the man faded away, his dust scattered like so much earth; yet the evidence of his noble attempts, the magnificent creation that revealed his divine essence—the Psyche that cannot die—continued to shine beyond time and was seen, acknowledged, and cherished.

The bright Morning Star cast its rays downward on the Psyche, and upon the glowing faces of the admiring crowd, who beheld the image of the soul captured in marble.

What is earthly will fade away and be forgotten, and the Star in the vast sky knows this truth. What is heavenly will shine brightly through generations, and when the ages have passed, the Psyche—the soul—will still live on!

title: THE PUPPET-SHOW MAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the collision of art and science
text_chunk:
On a steamer, I once met an older man with such a joyful face that if it reflected his mind, he must have been the happiest person around. And he believed that was true, as I heard him say it himself. He was a Dane, the owner of a traveling theater, and had his entire company with him in a big box because he ran a puppet show. He mentioned that his natural cheerfulness had been tested by someone from the Polytechnic Institution, and the results had left him utterly happy. At first, I didn't grasp what he meant, but he later explained the whole story to me.

"I was performing," he said, "in the hall of the post office in the small town of Slagelse. The audience was made up mostly of children, except for two respectable women. Suddenly, a man dressed in black, looking like a student, walked in and took a seat. He laughed at the funny parts and applauded right on cue. It was quite unusual for me to have such a spectator, and I was curious about who he was. I learned he was a member of the Polytechnic Institution in Copenhagen, sent out to share lectures with people in the provinces. I wrapped up my show right at eight o'clock because kids need to head to bed early, and a manager must think of the audience's needs."

"At nine o'clock, the lecturer began his lecture and experiments, and I became part of his audience. It was amazing to both hear and see. Much of it was beyond my understanding, but it made me think that if we can learn so much, we must surely be meant to last longer than the brief time we spend hidden under the earth. His experiments were like little miracles, and yet his explanations flowed naturally from his lips. In the days of Moses and the prophets, such a man would have been deemed a sage, while in the middle ages, they would have burned him at the stake."

"I couldn't sleep a wink that night. The next evening, when I performed again with the lecturer in attendance, I was in a fantastic mood."

title: THE PUPPET-SHOW MAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the collision of art and science
text_chunk:
"I once heard of an actor who, when playing the part of a lover, always focused on one lady in the audience; he played only for her, forgetting everyone else. That Polytechnic lecturer was my lady—my only audience, for whom I performed exclusively."

"After the performance and once the puppets were tucked behind the curtain, the Polytechnic lecturer invited me to his room for a glass of wine. We talked about my shows, and I about his science, and it felt like we were equally delighted. However, I held the upper hand because there were many things he did that he couldn't fully explain to me. For example, why a piece of iron becomes magnetic when it’s rubbed on a cylinder. How does that work? The magnetic sparks come to it—but how? It’s like people in the world; they're pushed around on this spherical planet until the electric spark hits them, and voila, you have a Napoleon or a Luther, or someone like that."

"'The whole world is just a series of miracles,' the lecturer said, 'but we're so used to them that we call them everyday occurrences.' He kept explaining things to me until it felt like my skull was being lifted from my brain. I mentioned that if I weren’t so old, I would join the Polytechnic Institution right away, just to learn how to see the bright side of everything, even though I was already one of the happiest men alive."

"'One of the happiest!' the lecturer replied, clearly intrigued. 'Are you really happy?'"

"'Yes,' I said. 'I'm welcomed in every town when I arrive with my company. But there's one wish that sometimes weighs on my cheerful spirit like a heavy weight. I would love to manage a real theater and direct an actual crew of men and women.'"

"'I see,' he said. 'You want to breathe life into your puppets so they can become real actors, with you as their director. Would that truly make you happy?'"

title: THE PUPPET-SHOW MAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the collision of art and science
text_chunk: "I thought it would. But he disagreed, and we debated it in every way possible, yet we couldn't reach a conclusion. Still, the wine was fantastic, and we clinked our glasses as we drank. There must have been magic in it, because I felt perfectly clear-headed, with a kind of warm glow filling the room, radiating from the Polytechnic lecturer's eyes. It reminded me of old stories where gods wandered the earth in their eternal youth, visiting mortals. I mentioned this to him, and he smiled, making me believe he might be one of those deities in disguise or at least belonged to a divine race. Our conversation ended with the arrangement that my greatest wish would come true: my puppets would be given life, and I would become the manager of a real company. We toasted to my future success and clanked our glasses together. Then he packed all my puppets into the box, secured it on my back, and I felt like I was spinning in circles before I found myself on the floor. I remember that clearly. Before long, the whole company leaped from the box. The magic was upon us; the puppets had transformed into distinguished actors—at least, that’s what they claimed—and I was their director."

title: THE PUPPET-SHOW MAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the collision of art and science
text_chunk:
"As everything was set for our first show, the entire company asked to speak with me before going public. The dancing lady insisted she could only perform if she stood on one leg because she was a true genius and needed to be treated as such. The actress playing the queen expected to be treated like royalty offstage too, or she insisted she would lose her edge. The actor delivering letters had as many airs as the leading man, arguing that every role, big or small, deserved equal respect as part of an artistic ensemble. The hero would only take on roles that would guarantee applause, while the prima donna would only act under red lights, claiming that blue didn’t suit her complexion. It felt like a swarm of flies trapped in a bottle, and there I was in that bottle with them because I was their director. I was overwhelmed, my head spinning, and I felt as miserable as a person could be. I longed to have them all back in my box and regret ever becoming their director. So, I boldly told them they were nothing but puppets, and that's when they turned on me. Eventually, I woke up in my bed, but I couldn’t recall how I ended up there or how I got away from the Polytechnic professor. The moonlight illuminated the floor, and the box lay open, with the dolls strewn about in chaos. But I didn’t waste a moment; I jumped off the bed and started putting them back in the box, some upside down, others right side up. I shut the lid and sat on top: 'Now you all have to stay in there,' I declared, 'and I’ll think twice before wishing you back to flesh and blood.'"

"I felt light again; my cheerfulness returned, and I was the happiest person alive. The Polytechnic professor had cured me completely. I was as joyful as a king and fell asleep on the box. The next morning—actually, it was noon since I slept in really late—I found myself still sitting there, realizing my previous wish had been quite foolish. I asked about the Polytechnic professor, but he had vanished, like the ancient Greek and Roman gods. From that moment forward, I became the happiest man in the world. I am a joyful director; none of my company ever complain, nor do the audiences, because I keep them entertained. I can arrange my shows exactly how I like. I choose the best bits from every comedy, and nobody gets offended. The plays that have fallen out of favor today were once beloved thirty years ago, and the audiences cried over them back then, just like their parents did years later. These are the very shows I put on for the kids, who weep over them just like Mom and Dad used to. But I tailor them for shorter attention spans, because kids don’t enjoy drawn-out speeches, and if there’s something sad, they want it over quickly."

title: THE RACES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Two prizes were awarded for the fastest runner of the year—a big one and a small one.

"I won the first prize," said the hare. "Justice must be upheld, even if you have friends on the award committee; but giving the second prize to the snail feels like an insult to me."

"Not so fast," replied the fence-rail, who had seen the prize distribution. "We must also consider hard work and determination. I’ve heard many respectable folks say this, and it makes sense. The snail took half a year just to cross the doorstep; he broke his shell in the rush. He put his all into the race, dragging his house along, which is quite commendable. That's why he earned the second prize."

"I think I deserve some recognition too," said the swallow. "No one is faster in the sky than I am, and just think about how far I’ve traveled! Really far!"

"Well, that’s your downfall," said the fence-rail. "You’re too flighty and always off to foreign lands when winter hits here. You’ve got no love for your homeland. There’s no way you can expect any consideration."

"But if I spent the whole winter dozing in the moor," said the swallow, "wouldn’t that count for something?"

"Get a certificate from the old moor-hen," replied the fence-rail, "to prove you spent half your time in your homeland, and maybe then you’d earn some recognition."

"I should have won the first prize, not the second," declared the snail. "At least I know the hare raced out of fear, thinking delay was dangerous. Meanwhile, I’ve dedicated my life to racing and ended up a cripple in the process. If anyone deserves the first prize, it’s me. But I don't get into bragging or nonsense. In fact, I look down on it." The snail spat in their direction with disdain.

title: THE RACES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I can swear that each prize I voted for was given with careful thought," said the old boundary post in the woods, a member of the judging committee. "I always make decisions with order and purpose. This was my seventh prize distribution, and finally, I exercised my right. I award the first prize by counting from A to Z, and the second in reverse. So listen up: the eighth letter is H, so I gave the first prize to the hare; the eighth letter from the end is S, awarding the snail the second prize. Next year, it will be I for the first prize and R for the second."

"I would have voted for myself," said the mule, "if I hadn’t been part of the judging committee. It’s not just speed that should be considered, but other qualities too, like how much weight a candidate can pull. I didn’t mention that, nor did I highlight the hare’s cleverness or the way he fakes people out. No, something else needs more attention: beauty. I’m particularly drawn to beauty. The hare’s well-shaped ears reminded me of my youth, so I voted for him."

"Buzz," interjected the fly. "I won’t ramble on, but I have a point about hares. I’ve caught up to more than one hare while riding on a train. It’s an easy way to judge my own speed. Just the other day, I ran over a young hare who had been trying to keep up with the train. He was clueless that I was behind him, and when he finally stopped, the train rolled over his back legs. I left him there and rode on. I consider that a victory, but I don’t want the prize."

"It honestly seems to me," thought the wild rose, though she kept quiet, which was her nature, "that the sunbeam should have won the first prize. The sunbeam travels from the sun to us in just minutes. It’s so powerful that all nature springs to life; we roses blush and release our fragrance around it. The judges clearly overlooked this. If I were the sunbeam, I might just give them a sunburn, but that would only make them angrier, and they’re already mad enough. I just hope," the rose continued, "that peace reigns in these woods. It’s beautiful to bloom, to emit fragrance, and to truly live—live in stories and songs. The sunbeam will outlast us all."

"What’s the first prize?" asked the earthworm, who woke up late and just surfaced.

"It’s free entry to a cabbage garden," replied the mule. "I suggested it as one of the prizes. The hare absolutely deserves it, and as a dedicated committee member, I made sure it benefits him. Now, the snail can chill on the fence, enjoying some moss and sunshine. He’s also been named one of the lead judges for speed in racing. It’s quite something to know that one of the judges has real talent. I have high hopes for the future; we’re off to a great start."

title: THE RED SHOES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a little girl named Karen, who was pretty and delicate. Sadly, during the summer, she had to go barefoot because her family was poor, and in winter, she wore large wooden shoes that made her little feet turn quite red.

In the center of the village lived an elderly shoemaker's wife. She sat down and, with whatever scraps she could find, crafted a simple pair of little shoes from old red cloth. They weren't perfect, but her heart was in the right place because she made them for Karen.

Karen received the shoes and wore them for the first time on the day of her mother's funeral. They weren't ideal for the occasion, but they were her only option, so she slipped her bare feet into them and followed the modest coffin.

At that moment, a big, old carriage rolled by, carrying a kind-looking elderly woman. Seeing the little girl, the woman felt sorry for her and said to the clergyman, "Look, if you let me take her, I'll take care of her."

Karen thought this was all because of the red shoes, but the old lady actually found them ugly, and they were eventually burned. Despite this, Karen was dressed neatly and clean, learned to read and sew, and people often complimented her beauty. The mirror, however, told her, "You’re not just pretty—you’re beautiful."

One day, the Queen passed through the area with her little princess. Everyone, including Karen, rushed toward the castle to see the royal family. The little princess stood in a window, letting everyone gawk at her. She wore lovely white clothes but most striking were her beautiful red morocco shoes—much more exquisite than the ones the shoemaker's wife had made for Karen. Nothing in the world could compare to red shoes!

Now that Karen was old enough for confirmation, she received some new clothes, and she was to get new shoes. The rich shoemaker in town measured her tiny foot in his shop, which was filled with beautiful shoes and sparkling white slippers. Everything looked stunning, but the old lady couldn’t see well, so she didn’t truly appreciate it. Among the shoes, there was a magnificent pair of red ones—like the princess's. They were gorgeous! The shoemaker mentioned they had been made for a count's daughter but hadn't fit her.

title: THE RED SHOES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I assume they’re shiny leather?" asked the old lady. "They look so shiny."

"Yes, they do shine," Karen replied. They fit her perfectly, and they were bought. The old lady didn’t realize they were red; otherwise, she would never have allowed Karen to be confirmed in them.

Everyone noticed her shoes, and from the church door to the choir, it felt like even the old figures in statues were staring at her red shoes. Throughout the ceremony, she couldn’t stop thinking about them while the clergyman spoke about the holy baptism, the covenant with God, and becoming a grown-up Christian. The organ played solemnly, and the sweet children's voices filled the air, but all Karen could think about were her red shoes. By the afternoon, the old lady heard from others that Karen wore red shoes. She was outraged, calling it inappropriate and declaring that Karen should wear black shoes to church from now on, even if they were old.

The next Sunday, there was Communion. Karen looked at the black shoes, then back at the red ones—she glanced at the red ones again and decided to wear them.

The sun was shining brightly, so Karen and the old lady took a dusty path through the cornfields.

At the church door, a crippled old soldier leaned on a crutch. He had a beard that was more red than white and bowed to the ground, asking the old lady if he could wipe her shoes. Karen extended her little foot too. "Well, what pretty dancing shoes!" said the soldier. "Be sure to sit tight when you dance!" he told the shoes, giving their soles a slap.

The old lady gave the soldier some money, and then she and Karen entered the church.

Inside, everyone looked at Karen's red shoes, and all the figures glanced at them too. When Karen knelt at the altar and took the golden goblet to her lips, she solely fixated on the red shoes. It felt as if they were swirling in the goblet, and she forgot the psalm and the "Lord's Prayer."

title: THE RED SHOES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
As everyone exited the church, the old lady got into her carriage. Just as Karen lifted her foot to enter too, the old soldier remarked, "What pretty dancing shoes!" Unable to resist, Karen danced a few steps. Once she started, her legs just wouldn’t stop—it was as if the shoes had taken control. She twirled around the church corner, unable to halt; her coachman had to chase after her and grab her. He lifted her into the carriage, but her feet kept dancing, kicking the poor old lady. Eventually, they took off her shoes, and her legs finally rested.

At home, the shoes were tucked away in the cupboard, but Karen couldn't help sneaking glances at them.

Unfortunately, the old lady fell ill and was said to be near death. Karen was tasked with nursing her back to health, but a grand ball was happening in town, and she received an invitation. Looking at the red shoes, she told herself there was no harm in wearing them, and she slipped them on, heading to the ball and starting to dance.

But when she wanted to move to the right, the shoes danced her to the left. When she aimed to go up the room, they pulled her down, out the stairs, down the street, and out through the town gates. She danced uncontrollably deep into the dark woods. Suddenly, she saw something shining among the trees—she thought it was the moon, but it was the old soldier with the red beard, nodding his head and saying, "What pretty dancing shoes!"

Fearful, she tried to get rid of the red shoes, but they were stuck tight. She tore off her stockings, but the shoes remained glued to her feet. She danced and was forced to dance, through fields and meadows, in sun and rain, day and night—but at night, it was the most terrifying.

She ended up dancing in the churchyard, but the dead weren't dancing; they had better things to do. She wanted to rest on the pauper's grave where bitter ferns grew, but she found no peace or rest. As she passed the open church door, she saw an angel in long white robes with wings reaching down to the ground. His face was serious, and he held a broad shining sword.

title: THE RED SHOES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"You will dance," he said. "You’ll dance in your red shoes until you're pale and cold until your skin shrivels up and you become a skeleton! You will dance from door to door, so that proud and wicked children might hear you and fear you! Dance you shall, dance—!"

"Have mercy!" cried Karen. But she couldn’t hear the angel’s response, as the shoes carried her through the gate, along highways and byways, and she had to keep dancing without pause.

One morning, she danced past a familiar door where they were singing a psalm inside, and a coffin covered with flowers was being carried out. In that moment, she realized she was abandoned by everyone and condemned by the angel of God.

She continued to dance through the dark night, the shoes dragging her over thorns and stumps until she was bleeding and torn. Finally, she found herself at a lonely house where the executioner lived. She tapped on the window and said, "Come out, come out! I can't come in, because I must dance."

The executioner replied, "I don’t think you know who I am. I cut off the heads of the wicked, and my axe is itching to do its work."

"Please don’t cut off my head!" said Karen. "Then I couldn’t repent for my sin. Just cut off my feet with the red shoes instead."

After confessing all her sins, the executioner took off her feet with the red shoes, but the shoes danced away, carrying her little feet across the field and into the deep forest.

He fashioned her a pair of wooden feet and crutches, teaching her a psalm sung by sinners. She kissed the hand that wielded the axe and wandered away across the heath.

"I've suffered enough for the red shoes," she said. "Now I want to go to church so everyone can see me." She hurried to the church door, but when she arrived, the red shoes began dancing in front of her, making her frightened and causing her to turn back.

title: THE RED SHOES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
All week long, she felt sad and shed many tears. But when Sunday came again, she thought, "I’ve suffered and struggled enough. I’m as good as many who sit in church and act superior." So she boldly moved forward but barely reached the churchyard gate when she spotted the red shoes dancing ahead of her. Terrified, she turned back and sincerely repented for her sins.

She went to the parsonage, pleading to be taken into service. She promised to work hard and do everything she could; the wages didn’t matter, just having a roof over her head among good people. The pastor's wife had compassion and took her in. She worked diligently and thoughtfully. She sat quietly, listening to the pastor read from the Bible in the evenings, and the children adored her. Yet, whenever they discussed fashion, luxury, or beauty, she would shake her head.

The following Sunday, everyone was going to church, and they asked if she wanted to join them. With tears in her eyes, she looked sorrowfully at her crutches. While they went to hear God's Word, she alone returned to her tiny room—barely big enough for a bed and a chair. Sitting with her hymn book, she read earnestly, while the wind carried the notes of the organ from the church to her. With tears streaming down her face, she said, "O God! Help me!"

Then the sun shone brightly, and right in front of her stood an angel of God in white robes—the same one she had seen outside the church that night. He no longer held a sharp sword but a beautiful green branch full of roses. With it, he touched the high ceiling, and where he did, a golden star appeared. He touched the walls, which swung wide open, revealing the organ playing beautifully, pictures of old pastors and their wives, and the congregation sitting in polished chairs, singing from their hymn books. The church had come to the poor girl in her narrow room, or perhaps her room had become part of the church. She found herself sitting in the pew with the pastor's family, and when the hymn ended and they looked up, they nodded, saying, "Good for you for coming, Karen."

The organ played, and the children's choir sounded soft and lovely. Warm sunlight streamed through the window into Karen's pew, filling her heart with peace and joy until it could bear no more. Her soul soared on sunbeams to Heaven, and no one there questioned her about the red shoes.

title: THE SAUCY BOY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was an old poet—one of those truly good old poets.

One evening, while he was relaxing at home, a terrible storm raged outside; rain poured down, but the old poet was snug in his chimney-corner, where the fire crackled and the apples roasted.

"There won’t be a dry stitch left on the poor souls out in this weather," he remarked.

"Oh, please open the door! I’m so cold and soaked through!" called a little child from outside. He was crying and knocking at the door, while the rain hammered down and the wind rattled the windows.

"Poor thing!" said the poet as he got up and opened the door. Standing there was a little boy; he was naked, and water streamed from his long, fair hair. He was trembling with cold, and if he hadn’t been let in, he surely would have perished in the storm.

"You poor little thing!" the poet said, taking him by the hand. "Come here; I’ll warm you up in no time. You can have some wine and an apple, since you’re such a pretty boy."

And he truly was. His eyes sparkled like bright stars, and even though water dripped from his fair hair, it still curled beautifully.

He looked like a little angel, but he was pale from the cold and shaking all over. In his hand, he clutched a gorgeous bow, but it was completely ruined by the rain, and the colors of his lovely arrows had blended together.

The old man settled by the fire, lifting the little boy onto his lap, wringing out the water from his hair, and warming his hands in his own.

He then made him some hot spiced wine, which quickly revived him. With rosy cheeks, he jumped off the floor and danced around the old man.

"You’re such a cheerful boy," the poet said. "What’s your name?"

"My name is Cupid," he replied. "Don’t you know me? That’s my bow right there. I shoot with that! Look, the weather is clearing up—the moon is shining!"

title: THE SAUCY BOY
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"But your bow is ruined," the old poet pointed out.

"That would be unfortunate," the boy said as he picked it up and examined it. "Oh, it’s perfectly dry and not damaged at all. The string is tight; I’ll give it a try." He drew it back, knocked an arrow, aimed, and shot the good old poet right in the heart. "See? My bow isn’t broken at all!" he said before bursting into laughter and running away. What a naughty boy to shoot the old poet after he had taken him into his warm home, been so kind, and offered him the best wine and the tastiest apple!

The good old man lay on the floor crying; he really was shot in the heart. "Oh!" he exclaimed, "what a mischievous boy this Cupid is! I’ll tell all the good children about this so they remember never to play with him, lest he hurt them."

All the good children, both girls and boys, whom he warned were on their guard against wicked Cupid. But he manages to trick them anyway, because he’s very clever. When students leave school, he walks beside them with a book under his arm, dressed in a black coat. They don’t recognize him. And then, if they take him by the arm, thinking he’s a student too, he shoots an arrow right into their chest. When the girls go to church for their confirmation, he’s among them too. In fact, he’s always around. He hides in the big chandelier of the theater, shining like a lamp; but soon enough, people realize their mistake. He wanders in the castle gardens and along the promenades. Yes, he even shot your mom and dad in the heart once. Just ask them, and they’ll tell you about it. Oh, he’s a bad boy, this Cupid, and you must never engage with him because he’s out to get everyone. Just think, he even shot an arrow at your grandmother, but that was a long time ago. The wound has healed, but such things are never forgotten.

Now you know just how naughty this wicked Cupid really is.

title: THE SHADOW
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: In extremely hot climates, where the sun shines relentlessly, people tend to have the color of rich mahogany; in the hottest regions, they're often black-skinned. A brilliant scholar traveled from the cold north to one of these warm places, thinking he could go about his usual activities. But he quickly changed his mind. He found that, like any wise person, he had to stay indoors all day with every window and door shut, making it seem as if everyone inside was either asleep or absent. The tall houses on his street trapped the sun’s heat from morning until evening, becoming almost unbearable. This young, clever scholar felt as if he were sitting in an oven; he grew weak and thin, and his shadow shrank until it was way smaller than it had been back home. The sun seemed to take away even what little shadow he had left, and he only saw it again after sunset. It truly felt good to see his shadow stretch against the wall, reaching all the way to the ceiling when the lights were brought in; it really needed that stretch to regain its power. The scholar would sometimes step onto his balcony to stretch, and as soon as the stars appeared in the lovely night sky, he felt refreshed. At this hour, people began to emerge from all their balconies in the street, as every window in warm climates has a balcony for enjoying the cool evening air, even for those whose skin was brown like mahogany. It made the street come alive with activity. Shoemakers, tailors, and all sorts of folks were chatting away. Below, tables and chairs were set out, candles flickered by the hundreds, and people were laughing and singing. Carriages and mules trotted along with their bells chiming, 'tingle, tingle.' Funerals passed by with solemn music and the tolling of church bells. It was a vibrant scene down below. Only one house, directly across from the scholar’s, was quiet and still, though flowers bloomed beautifully in its balcony, clearly tended to. This meant someone was indeed inside. The balcony doors stood half-open in the evening, and while the front room was dark, sweet music drifted from inside. The scholar found the music delightful, but maybe he was just imagining it; everything pleased him in this warm climate, except for the sun’s heat. His landlord didn’t know who lived in the opposite house; no one was ever seen there, and as for the music, he found it dreadfully monotonous.

title: THE SHADOW
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"It sounds like someone is practicing a piece they can't quite nail. It's always the same tune. I guess he thinks he’ll get it one day, but I doubt it no matter how long he keeps playing."

One night, the scholar woke up. He had left the balcony door open, and the wind had stirred the curtain, revealing a dazzling brightness in the opposite balcony. The flowers there looked like flames of vibrant colors, and amidst them stood a beautiful, delicate girl. It felt like light radiated from her, making him squint as he had just opened his eyes. He leaped out of bed and quietly crept behind the curtain, but she was gone—the brilliant light vanished, and while the flowers were still beautiful, they no longer resembled flames. The door stood slightly ajar, and from within came music so lovely it filled him with enchantment and fired up his imagination. Who could possibly live there? What was the real entrance? The entire ground floor was made up of shops, both on the street and down the side lane, making it hard for people to access the house freely.

One evening, the scholar relaxed on his balcony, with a light glowing in his room just behind him. Naturally, his shadow fell upon the wall of the opposite house. As he sat among the flowers, whenever he moved, his shadow moved along with him.

"My shadow seems to be the only living thing visible over there," the scholar mused. "Look how nicely it lounges among the flowers. That door is only slightly open; the shadow should be clever enough to sneak in, have a look around, and then come back to tell me what it saw. You’d be useful like that," he joked, addressing the shadow. It nodded back as if in agreement. "Go on now, but don’t take too long!"

The scholar stood up, and the shadow on the opposite balcony also stood. When he turned to face the other way, the shadow copied his movement. If anyone had been watching closely, they might have seen the shadow slip right through the half-open door of the opposite balcony just as the scholar went back inside his own room and let the curtain fall. The next morning, he stepped out for his coffee and to read the news.

title: THE SHADOW
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"What’s this?" he exclaimed, standing in the sunlight. "I’ve lost my shadow! It really did leave last night and hasn’t come back. This is so frustrating!"

And it did bother him, not just because the shadow was missing, but because he was aware of the tale about a man without a shadow. Everyone back home knew that story; when he returned and recounted his adventures, they’d surely say he was just copying that tale. He didn’t want anyone to say that about him, so he decided not to mention it at all, which was quite a wise choice.

Later that evening, he returned to his balcony, being careful to place the light behind him because he knew that shadows always need their owner for coverage. But he still couldn't coax it out. He tried shrinking and enlarging himself but to no avail; the shadow didn’t appear. He said, "Ahem, ahem," but nothing worked. It was quite frustrating, but in warm climates, things grow quickly. A week later, he noticed, to his immense relief, that a new shadow was forming from his feet while he walked in the sunshine. Clearly, the root must have stayed behind. After three weeks, he had a respectable shadow again, which continued to grow as he traveled back to the northern lands, eventually becoming so large he could have easily shared half of it. When the scholar arrived home, he wrote books about truth, goodness, and beauty in the world, and so days and years passed, many years.

One evening, as he sat in his study, he heard a gentle knock at the door. "Come in," he called, but no one entered. When he opened the door, he found a man standing there, so incredibly thin that he felt quite concerned at his appearance. The man was well-dressed, looking like a gentleman. "To whom do I have the pleasure of speaking?" he asked.

title: THE SHADOW
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I hoped you would recognize me," replied the elegant stranger. "I've done quite well; I have a body and clothes now. You never expected to see me like this, did you? Don’t you recognize your old shadow? I never imagined I would return to you like this. Everything has turned out splendidly since I left; I've become rich and could easily buy my freedom from servitude if I wanted to." He jingled some expensive trinkets hanging from the thick gold watch chain around his neck. Diamond rings sparkled on his fingers, and everything was real.

"I can't believe my eyes!" said the scholar. "What does all of this mean?"

"Something quite unusual," said the shadow. "But you, too, are an extraordinary man. You know I’ve been following you ever since you were a child. Once I felt I had traveled enough to manage on my own, I took my own path. Now I'm living in splendid circumstances. But I longed to see you again before you passed away and to revisit this place because there's always a connection to one's birthplace. I see you’ve acquired another shadow; do I owe you anything? If so, please let me know."

"No! Is it really you?" said the scholar. "This is truly remarkable; I never thought it was possible for a shadow to become a living person."

"Just tell me what I owe you," said the shadow, "because I don't like being in debt to anyone."

"How can you talk like that?" replied the scholar. "What kind of debt could there possibly be between us? You are as free as anyone else. I’m thrilled to hear about your good fortune. Please, sit down, my old friend, and tell me all about how this happened and what you saw in the house across from mine while we were in those hot countries."

"Yes, I'll share everything," said the shadow as he took a seat. "But you must promise me never to reveal in this city, no matter where you might bump into me, that I was once your shadow. I'm considering getting married since I now have more than enough to support a family."

title: THE SHADOW
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Don’t worry about it," said the scholar. "I won’t tell anyone who you really are. Here’s my hand—I promise, and a word is enough between friends."

"Between a man and a shadow," said the shadow, unable to resist commenting.

It was quite astonishing how much he had taken on a human appearance. He wore a suit of fine black cloth and polished boots, topped off with a foldable opera hat, displaying only the crown and rim of it. The trinkets, gold chain, and diamond rings made him look very much like a man. "Now, I’ll tell you everything you want to know," said the shadow, placing his polished boot firmly on the arm of the new shadow of the scholar, which rested at his feet like a docile poodle. Whether out of pride or to make the new shadow want to cling to him, the prostrate shadow lay still, eager to hear how a shadow could be sent away by its master and become a man. "Do you know," said the shadow, "the most glorious creature in the world lived in the house across from you? It was poetry. I spent three weeks there, which felt like three thousand years, for I read everything ever written in poetry or prose, and I can honestly say I saw and learned it all."

"Poetry!" exclaimed the scholar. "Yes, she’s like a hermit in large cities. Poetry! I caught a fleeting glimpse of her once while sleep weighed down my eyelids. She appeared to me from the balcony like the radiant northern lights, surrounded by flowers that flickered like flames. Tell me, you were on the balcony that evening, you went through the door, and what did you see?"

"I found myself in an ante-room," the shadow explained. "You were still sitting across from me, peering into the room. It was dimly lit, or at least it felt that way because many brilliantly lit rooms were open. The brightness would have overwhelmed me had I approached too close to the maiden, but I was cautious and took my time, which everyone should do."

title: THE SHADOW
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"And what did you see?" asked the scholar.

"I saw everything, as you’ll hear. But—this isn’t pride on my part, as a free man who possesses my own knowledge and wealth—I would appreciate it if you could address me as ‘you’ instead of ‘thou.’"

"I apologize," said the scholar. "It’s an old habit that’s hard to break. You’re absolutely right. I’ll make an effort to remember. But now please tell me everything you saw."

"Everything," the shadow replied. "For I have seen and know all there is to know."

"What were the inner rooms like?" asked the scholar. "Were they like a cool grove or a holy temple? Did the chambers resemble a starry sky seen from a high mountain?"

"It was all that you describe," said the shadow. "But I didn’t go all the way in—I stayed in the softly lit ante-room—but I had a good view; I could see and hear everything happening in the realm of poetry."

"But what did you observe? Did ancient gods wander through the rooms? Did old heroes reenact their battles? Were there lovely children playing who shared their dreams?"

title: THE SHADOW
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I tell you I’ve been there, so you can be sure I saw everything that was worth seeing. If you had gone there, you wouldn't have remained human, whereas I became one; and at that moment, I discovered my true nature, my natural affinity for poetry. I admit I didn’t think much about it while I was with you, but you’ll recall I was always much larger at sunrise and sunset, and even more visible than you under the moonlight, but at that time, I didn’t understand my inner self. In the ante-room, it all became clear to me. I turned into a man; I reached my full maturity. But you had left the warm climates. As a human, I felt embarrassed to wander around without a coat or clothes, those outward signs of humanity. So, I ventured out on my own; I can tell you this because I know you won’t write about it. I hid beneath the cloak of a street vendor, and she had no idea who she was concealing. By evening, I dared to roam around in the moonlight. I stood tall against the walls, tickling my back with joy. I dashed about the streets, peeking through the highest windows into homes and over rooftops. I looked in and saw what no one else could, or should see; in truth, it’s a harsh world, and I had little desire to be human, yet people hold significance. I’ve seen the most miserable things happening between husbands and wives, parents and children—sweet, adorable children. I've seen what no one else can know, though everyone wishes they could—the bad deeds of their neighbors. If I had published a newspaper, it would have been eagerly devoured! Instead, I wrote directly to those involved, causing panic throughout the towns I visited. They were terrified of me, but they loved me dearly. Professors made me a professor. Tailors provided me with new clothes; I’m well-off in that regard. The overseer of the mint even minted coins for me. Women proclaimed me handsome, and thus I became the man you see before you. And now, I must bid you farewell. Here’s my card. I live on the sunlit side of the street, and I always stay indoors when it rains." With that, the shadow departed.

"This is incredibly remarkable," said the scholar.

title: THE SHADOW
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
As the years passed, the shadow returned once more. "How are things going?" he asked.

"Ah!" replied the scholar. "I’m writing about the true, the beautiful, and the good; but no one seems to care to listen. I’m feeling quite despairing about it."

"I never let myself feel that way," the shadow replied. "I’m getting quite plump and prosperous, which everyone ought to be. You just don’t understand the world; you’ll only make yourself ill over it. You should travel; I plan on a trip this summer. Will you join me as my shadow? I would love a travel companion, and I’ll cover all expenses!"

"Are you traveling far?" asked the scholar.

"That depends on how you look at it," the shadow replied. "At the very least, a journey will do you good, and if you’ll be my shadow, all your expenses will be taken care of."

"This seems quite absurd," the scholar said.

"But it’s how the world works," countered the shadow, "and it always will be." Then he left.

Everything turned chaotic for the scholar. Gloom and confusion chased him, and what he said about good, beautiful, and true was as useful to most people as a nutmeg would be to a cow. Eventually, he fell ill. People remarked, "You look just like a shadow," which sent a chill through him because he had his own thoughts on that matter.

"You really need to go to some spa," the shadow suggested on his next visit. "There’s no other option for you. I’ll take you with me out of old friendship. I’ll pay for your trip, and you can write a description of it to entertain us along the way. I’d love to visit a spa; my beard isn’t growing as it should because of my weakness, and I need my beard. Please, be sensible and accept my offer; let’s travel as close friends."

title: THE SHADOW
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Eventually, they set off together. The shadow took the lead, becoming the master while the scholar became the shadow.
