They traveled together, whether driving, riding, or walking, side by side, or one in front and the other behind, depending on the sun. The shadow always knew when to take the honor position, but the scholar didn’t notice since he had a kind heart and was exceptionally gentle and friendly.

One day, the master said to the shadow, "We grew up together from childhood, and now that we are traveling companions, shall we not celebrate our friendship and start saying ‘thee’ and ‘thou’ to each other?"

"What you’re suggesting is very straightforward and well-meaning," said the shadow, who was now truly in charge. "I will be equally kind and straightforward. You’re a learned man, and you know how wondrous human nature is. Some men can’t stand the smell of brown paper; it makes them feel ill. Others feel a shiver run through them, down to their bones, when a nail scrapes against a window pane. My own response is similar when someone addresses me as ‘thou.’ I feel crushed by it, just as I felt in my previous role with you. You’ll see this is a matter of feeling, not pride. I can’t allow you to say ‘thou’ to me; I’ll gladly say it to you, so your wish will be half fulfilled." So, the shadow addressed his former master as ‘thou.’

"This seems a bit much," the scholar protested, "that I have to call him ‘you’ and he calls me ‘thou.'" Still, he had no choice but to accept.

Finally, they arrived at the baths, full of visitors, including a beautiful princess, whose real issue was being too sharp-sighted, which made everyone anxious. She noticed immediately that the newcomer was different from everyone else. "They say he’s here to help his beard grow," she thought. "But I know what the real reason is: he isn’t able to cast a shadow." Intrigued by him, she approached him one day while on the promenade, unencumbered by the usual formalities due to her royal status. "Your illness is the inability to cast a shadow," she stated matter-of-factly.

title: THE SHADOW
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Your royal highness must be on the fast track to recovery," he said. "I understand your ailment comes from being too sharp-sighted, and in this case, you've missed the mark completely. You see, I have an exceptionally unusual shadow. Haven’t you seen the person who’s always by my side? Often, people give their servants better fabric than they use for themselves. I’ve dressed my shadow like a gentleman; in fact, you might notice I’ve even gotten him a shadow of his own; it’s a bit pricey, but I enjoy having unique things around me."

"What’s going on?" thought the princess. "Am I really cured? This must be the best spa ever! Water must have some incredible power these days. But I won’t leave just yet; things are starting to get interesting. This foreign prince—he must be a prince—fascinates me. I hope his beard doesn’t grow too quickly or he’ll leave at once."

That evening, the princess and the shadow danced together in the grand ballroom. She was light, but he was even lighter; she had never encountered such a dancer. She told him where she came from, and he revealed he had been there, although not during her stay. He had peeked through both the upper and lower windows of her father’s palace and saw many things, allowing him to respond to the princess’s questions and make references that astonished her. She thought he must be the cleverest person in the world and felt immense respect for his knowledge. As they danced again, she fell in love with him, a fact the shadow quickly recognized because she looked at him intensely. They danced another round, and she almost confessed her feelings but exercised discretion, considering her kingdom and the many people she would someday rule. "He’s a clever man," she thought to herself, "which is a great quality, and he dances beautifully, another plus. But does he have truly deep knowledge? That’s an important question I must explore." So, she asked him a challenging question she herself couldn’t answer, and the shadow made a bizarre grimace.

title: THE SHADOW
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"You can’t answer that," the princess noted.

"I learned a bit about it back in my childhood," he replied, "and I have faith that even my shadow over there by the door could answer it."

"Your shadow," the princess exclaimed; "that would be fascinating!"

"I can’t say this with certainty," the shadow replied, "but I have a feeling he could. He has followed me for so long and heard so much from me, it’s quite probable. However, your royal highness must keep in mind that he takes great pride in being acknowledged as a man, so to put him in a good mood and ensure the correct answer, he must be addressed as one."

"I’ll gladly do that," the princess replied. She walked over to the scholar standing in the doorway and conversed with him about the sun, the moon, the green forests, and people both near and far, and the scholar spoke with her sensibly and pleasantly.

"What a remarkable man he must be to have such an intelligent shadow!" she thought. "If I were to choose him, it would be a true blessing for my country and my people, and I plan to do so." Soon, the princess and the shadow were engaged, but no one was to be told until she returned to her kingdom.

"No one shall know," the shadow affirmed. "Not even my own shadow;" and he had very particular reasons for saying this.

Eventually, the princess returned to her land, accompanied by the shadow.

"Listen, my friend," said the shadow to the scholar, "now that I am as fortunate and powerful as anyone could be, I will do something great for you. You can live in my palace, ride in my royal carriage, and receive a hundred thousand dollars a year; but you must let everyone call you a shadow and never claim that you were once a man. Once a year, when I’m basking in the sunshine on my balcony, you must lie at my feet as a proper shadow should; for I must inform you that I’m about to marry the princess and our wedding will happen tonight."

title: THE SHADOW
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"This is utterly ridiculous!" protested the scholar. "I can’t and won’t comply with such nonsense. That would cheat the whole country and the princess too. I’ll expose everything and declare that I’m the man and you’re just a shadow dressed up in human clothes."

"Nobody would believe you," the shadow retorted. "Be reasonable, or I’ll alert the guards."

"I’ll go straight to the princess!" shouted the scholar.

"But I’ll get there before you," replied the shadow, "and you’ll be sent to prison." It turned out just like that, as the guards were quick to obey him. They knew he was marrying the king’s daughter.

"You seem shaken," the princess remarked when the shadow approached her. "Has something happened? You mustn’t be ill today, as our wedding takes place this evening."

"I’ve just gone through the most dreadful ordeal imaginable," said the shadow. "Imagine this—my shadow has lost his mind; I suppose such a frail mind couldn’t handle much. He thinks he has become a real man and that I am his shadow."

"How terrible!" exclaimed the princess. "Is he locked up?"

"Oh yes, absolutely; I fear he may not ever recover."

"Poor shadow!" the princess lamented. "It’s unfortunate for him; it would truly be kind to free him from his tenuous existence; and come to think of it, given how people often side with the lower class over the higher in these times, it would be wise to quietly remove him from the equation."

"It’s rather unjust to him, as he was a faithful servant," remarked the shadow, pretending to sigh.

"You have a noble character," said the princess, bowing before him.

That evening, the entire town lit up, cannons boomed, and soldiers saluted. It was a magnificent wedding. The princess and the shadow stepped onto the balcony to greet the crowd and receive a final cheer. Meanwhile, the scholar remained oblivious to these celebrations; he had already been executed.

title: THE SHEPHERD'S STORY OF THE BOND OF FRIENDSHIP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Our little home was made of clay, with doorposts crafted from fluted marble sourced nearby. The roof sloped down almost to the ground. At that time, it looked dark and shabby, but it had once been adorned with blooming olive and laurel branches brought from across the mountains. The house sat in a narrow gorge, where steep rocky walls shot up high, stark and black. Sometimes, clouds gathered around the peaks like ghostly white figures. There was never a singing bird or the sound of dancing feet. This place was sacred, a remnant of the past—once known as "Delphi." Back then, the dark sacred mountains were capped with snow, and Mount Parnassus glowed in the evening light. The brook that flowed nearby was also sacred. I can vividly recall every spot in that deep, sacred silence! A fire crackled in our small hut, with hot ashes glowing red, baking our bread. Sometimes, snow piled so high around our home that it almost disappeared, and that’s when my mother seemed the happiest. She’d hold my head in her hands and sing songs she rarely performed, as our Turkish masters had forbidden it. She sang,

"On the summit of Mount Olympus, in a forest of dwarf firs, there lay an old stag. His eyes were heavy with tears, shimmering like dewdrops. A roebuck came trotting by and asked, 'What’s wrong? Why are your tears blue and red?' The stag replied, 'The Turks have come to our city; they hunt with wild dogs, a fierce pack.' The young roebuck exclaimed, 'I’ll chase them away across the islands!' But by evening, the roebuck was slain, and before night fell, the hunted stag was dead."

When my mother sang those words, her eyes would shimmer with tears, which she tried to hide as she kept an eye on the black bread baking in the ashes. I'd clench my fists and shout, "We’ll defeat these Turks!" But she would echo the song, "I’ll chase them across the islands to the deep sea; yet by that evening the roebuck was slain, and before night fell, the hunted stag was dead."

title: THE SHEPHERD'S STORY OF THE BOND OF FRIENDSHIP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
We’d been feeling lonely in our hut for days when my father returned home. I eagerly expected him to bring shells from the Gulf of Lepanto or maybe a shiny knife. But this time, beneath his sheep-skin cloak, he carried a little girl—a half-naked child wrapped in fur. When this fur was peeled away, three silver coins were found nestled in her dark hair; they were all she owned. My father told us her parents had been killed by the Turks, and he spoke so much about them that I dreamed of Turks all night. He himself had been injured, and my mother bandaged his arm. It was a deep wound, and his once soft sheep-skin cloak was stiff with dried blood. The little girl was to be my sister. She looked so sweet and bright that even my mother’s eyes didn’t shine more gently than hers. Her name was Anastasia, and she was to be my sister because her father and mine had sworn brotherhood many years ago, a tradition we still honored. A beautiful and virtuous girl was chosen to bless this bond of friendship. So now, this little girl was my sister. I’d sit her in my lap, bring her flowers, and pluck feathers from the birds of the mountains. We drank from the waters of Parnassus and lived together under the laurel roof of the hut, while my mother sang her song about the stag who shed red tears. But back then, I didn’t understand that my own people’s sorrows were reflected in those tears.

One day, some Franks arrived at our hut—men from a distant land, dressed unlike us. They brought tents and beds carried by horses, and were followed by over twenty Turks, all armed to the teeth. These Franks were friends of the Pasha and had letters requesting an escort. They were only there to explore our mountain, to climb Parnassus amid the snow and clouds, and to gaze at the eerie black rocks near our home. Unable to fit inside the hut or tolerate the smoke that billowed along the ceiling, they set up their tents just outside. Lamb and bird were roasted, along with strong, sweet wine, which the Turks were forbidden to drink.

title: THE SHEPHERD'S STORY OF THE BOND OF FRIENDSHIP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
As they left, I walked partway with them, carrying my little sister Anastasia wrapped in a goat-skin on my back. One of the gentlemen from the Frankish group had me pose in front of a rock while he sketched us together, so we looked like one creature. I didn’t realize it then, but Anastasia and I truly were one. She was always on my lap or riding on my back, and in my dreams, she was always there.

Two nights later, more armed men entered our tent. They were Albanians, brave warriors, as my mother described them. They didn’t stay long, yet my sister Anastasia sat on one of their laps, and after they left, she had only two silver coins in her hair—one had vanished. They rolled tobacco in strips of paper and smoked it; I remember they were unsure about which road to take. But eventually, they had to leave, and my father went along with them. Soon after, we heard gunfire. The sounds continued, and before long, soldiers stormed our hut, taking my mother, Anastasia, and me as prisoners. They claimed we had entertained robbers and that my father had acted as their guide, so we had to go with them. They brought in the bodies of the robbers and my father. I saw my poor dead father and cried myself to sleep. When I awakened, I found myself in a prison cell. The room wasn’t worse than our hut, and they fed me onions and stale wine from a tarred barrel. But at home, we were used to worse. I don’t know how long we were in prison, but many days and nights slipped by. We were freed around Easter. I carried Anastasia on my back as we walked slowly; my mother was weak, and the journey to the sea, to the Gulf of Lepanto, was long.

title: THE SHEPHERD'S STORY OF THE BOND OF FRIENDSHIP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
When we arrived, we entered a church filled with beautiful pictures in golden frames. The paintings depicted bright, fair angels, yet I thought little Anastasia looked just as lovely. In the center of the floor was a coffin full of roses. My mother told me the roses represented Lord Jesus Christ. Then the priest announced, "Christ is risen," and everyone exchanged greetings. Each person held a lit candle, and I received one for myself and little Anastasia. Music played as people left the church hand-in-hand, filled with joy. Outside, women were roasting the paschal lamb, and as I sat by the fire, a boy older than me hugged me and said, "Christ is risen." That was the first time I met Aphtanides.

My mother made fishermen's nets, which were in high demand here by the bay. We lived beside the beautiful sea, which tasted like tears and reminded me of the stag who wept red tears, for the water would sometimes be red, other times green or blue. Aphtanides knew how to handle our boat, and often I’d sit in it with my little Anastasia while it glided over the water, swift like a bird in flight. When the sun set, the mountains would take on a deep, beautiful blue hue, one rising above the other in the distance, with Mount Parnassus towering over them like a magnificent crown. Its peak sparkled in the evening light like molten gold, as if shining from within; long after the sun disappeared below the horizon, the mountaintop would glow against the deep blue sky. White birds skimmed the water’s surface as everything remained calm and still, just like amid the black rocks at Delphi. I lay back in the boat, while Anastasia rested against me, gazing at the stars shining brighter than the lamps in our church. They were the same stars that hung over me when I sat in front of our hut at Delphi. I almost believed I was still there when suddenly I heard a splash; Anastasia had fallen in. In an instant, Aphtanides leaped in after her, lifting her up to me. We dried her clothes as best we could and stayed on the water until they were dry, not wanting anyone to know how frightened we’d been or the danger that little Anastasia had faced, a life that Aphtanides had now played a part in.

title: THE SHEPHERD'S STORY OF THE BOND OF FRIENDSHIP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Summer arrived, and the scorching sun painted the leaves of the trees with golden lines. I thought about our cool mountain home and the fresh water flowing nearby; my mother longed for it too. One evening, we decided to wander back home. It was peaceful and quiet as we walked through thick, wild thyme, still fragrant despite the heat. We didn’t encounter a single herdsman or even a lonely hut—everything felt deserted. Only a shooting star hinted that there was still life up in the heavens. I couldn’t tell if the clear blue sky glimmered on its own or if the light came from the stars, but we could clearly see the mountains’ outlines. My mother lit a fire and roasted some roots she’d brought. Anastasia and I slept among the bushes without fear of the ugly smidraki, with its fiery throat, or of wolves and jackals. My mother was there with us, and I felt her presence was enough protection.

We reached our old home, but the cottage was in ruins, so we had to rebuild. With help from some neighbors, mostly women, we erected the walls in just a few days and quickly covered them with a roof made of olive branches. My mother made a living crafting bottle cases from bark and skins, while I tended to the priests’ sheep, who were sometimes farmers, while my playful companions were Anastasia and the turtles.

One day, our beloved Aphtanides visited. He said he had been longing to see us and stayed for two happy days. A month later, he returned to say goodbye, bringing my mother a large fish. He told us he would be setting sail for Corfu and Patras and could share many stories, not only about the fishermen from the Gulf of Lepanto but also about kings and heroes who once ruled Greece, just like the Turks do now.

I once saw a bud on a rosebush gradually unfold its leaves until it blossomed into a perfect rose. Before I realized it, Anastasia had bloomed too, having grown into a beautiful young woman, while I had become a strong young man myself. The wolf-skins that covered the bed where my mother and Anastasia slept were ones I had taken from wolves I’d shot myself.

title: THE SHEPHERD'S STORY OF THE BOND OF FRIENDSHIP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Years passed, and one evening, Aphtanides walked in. He had grown tall and slender like a reed, with powerful limbs and a sun-kissed, brown skin. He greeted us warmly and had so many tales to share about the great ocean, Malta’s fortifications, and the incredible tombs of Egypt that I looked up to him with admiration. His stories were as fantastic as the legends of ancient priests.

"You know so much!" I exclaimed. "What wonders you tell!"

"I think what you once shared with me is the finest of all," he replied. "You told me about the treasured custom of 'the bond of friendship'—a tradition I’d love to honor. My brother, let’s go to church, just as your father and Anastasia’s father did. Your sister Anastasia is the most beautiful and innocent maiden, and she can bless this bond."

Anastasia blushed like a fresh rose, and my mother kissed Aphtanides.

About two miles from our cottage stood a little church, nestled on a hill protected by scattered trees, with a silver lamp hanging before the altar. I wore my best clothes: a white tunic that draped elegantly, a fitted red jacket, a silver-tassel fez, and my belt held a knife and my pistols. Aphtanides was dressed in the blue garb of Greek sailors, a silver medal of the Virgin Mary hanging around his neck, and his scarf was as fine as those worn by wealthy lords. Everyone could see we were about to partake in something meaningful. As we entered the humble church, the evening sunlight streamed through the opened door, illuminating the burning lamp and shimmering off the golden picture frames. We knelt together at the altar steps while Anastasia came close to us. She wore a flowing, white garment that fell gracefully over her delicate figure, and around her neck and on her chest hung a chain of old and new coins, making for a beautiful collar. Her hair was styled into a knot, secured by a headdress made of coinage found in an ancient temple. No Greek girl adorned herself more beautifully than that. Her face glowed, and her eyes sparkled like two stars. We all three offered a silent prayer, and then she addressed us, "Will you promise to be friends in life and in death?"

title: THE SHEPHERD'S STORY OF THE BOND OF FRIENDSHIP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Will you remember to say, no matter what happens, 'My brother is a part of me; his secret is my secret, his happiness is mine; self-sacrifice, patience—everything belongs to me as it does to him?'"

We replied, "Yes." Then she joined our hands and kissed us on the forehead before we prayed silently again. Afterward, a priest emerged from a door near the altar and blessed us three. A song was sung by other holy men behind the altar screen, confirming our bond of eternal friendship. When we stood up, I noticed my mother at the church door, weeping.

Everything felt so joyful now in our little cottage by the Delphian springs! The night before his departure, Aphtanides sat beside me on the mountainside, deep in thought. His arm was around my shoulder, while mine rested around his neck. We discussed the sorrows of Greece and the trustworthy men in our country. Every thought we shared was open and clear. Suddenly, I took his hand and said, "Aphtanides, there’s something I need to share with you—something I've kept secret between myself and Heaven. My heart is filled with a love—stronger than the love I feel for my mother and you."

"And who do you love?" he asked, his face turning bright red.

His hand trembled in mine, and he went pale as if he’d seen a ghost. I noticed it and understood the reason; my own hand trembled as well. I leaned towards him, kissed his forehead, and whispered, "I’ve never told her, and maybe she doesn’t love me back. But brother, think about this—I've seen her every day. She’s grown beside me and become a part of my soul."

"And she shall be yours," he said. "Yours! I won’t wrong you. I love her too, but I’ll be leaving tomorrow. We will meet again in a year, but by then you’ll be married, right? I have a little gold saved; it's yours. You must have it."

We walked home silently through the mountains. It was late when we got to my mother’s door. Anastasia held the lamp as we entered; my mother wasn’t home. She looked at Aphtanides with a sweet but sad expression. "Tomorrow you leave us," she said. "I’m really sorry."

"Sorry?" he replied, his voice filled with a sorrow that mirrored my own. I couldn’t find the words; he took her hand and said, "Our brother loves you, and isn’t he dear to you? Even his silence shows his affection."

Anastasia shivered and burst into tears. In that moment, I noticed no one but her. I wrapped my arms around her and pressed my lips to hers. As she embraced me tightly, the lamp slipped from her hands and shattered, leaving us engulfed in darkness.

Before dawn, Aphtanides rose, kissed us all, and said, "Farewell." He had given all his money to my mother for our sake. Anastasia became my betrothed, and a few days later she became my wife.

title: THE SHEPHERDESS AND THE SHEEP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Have you ever seen an old wooden cupboard darkened by time, decorated with intricate carvings of foliage and curious figures? Well, there was just such a cupboard in a parlor, passed down to the family from their great-grandmother. It was entirely covered in carved roses and tulips, with strange scrolls all over it, from which little stag heads with antlers peeked out. Right in the center of the cupboard door was a carved figure of a man who was quite funny to look at. He grinned at you, though it couldn't really be called laughing. With goat's legs, tiny horns on his head, and a long beard, the kids in the room had dubbed him "Major-general-field-sergeant-commander Billy-goat's-legs." That was certainly a tough name to say, and very few get such titles, but still, it was remarkable that he had been carved at all. And there he was, always staring at the table under the looking glass, where a lovely little shepherdess in china stood. Her shoes were gilded, and her dress had a bright red rose ornament. She wore a hat and carried a shiny crook, looking vibrant and charming. Next to her stood a little chimney-sweep, as black as coal but just as clean and neat as any other china figure. He only represented a black chimney-sweep and could have just as easily been made a prince if the china makers had chosen to do so. He held his ladder with ease, and his face was as rosy and fair as a girl's; in fact, that was somewhat of a mistake since he should have had some black marks on it. Positioned closely together, they seemed engaged to each other, perfectly matched since they were both made of china and equally delicate. Nearby was another figure, three times their size, also made of china. He was an old Chinaman who could nod his head and liked to pretend he was the grandfather of the shepherdess, even though he had no proof. He acted as if he was in charge, so when "Major-general-field-sergeant-commander Billy-goat's-legs" asked to take the little shepherdess as his bride, he nodded his head to show his approval. "You will have a husband," said the old Chinaman to her, "who I truly believe is made of mahogany. He'll make you the lady of Major-general-field-sergeant-commander Billy-goat's-legs. He has a whole cupboard filled with silverware locked up in secret drawers."

title: THE SHEPHERDESS AND THE SHEEP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I won’t go into that dark cupboard," the little shepherdess replied. "I’ve heard he already has eleven china wives in there."

"Then you shall be the twelfth," said the old Chinaman. "Tonight, as soon as you hear a clattering from the old cupboard, you’ll be married, as surely as I am a Chinaman;" then he nodded off and fell asleep.

The little shepherdess began to cry and looked at her beloved, the china chimney-sweep. "I beg you," she said, "let's go out into the wide world, because we can’t stay here."

"I’ll do anything you want," replied the little chimney-sweep. "Let’s leave right away; I think I can support you with my profession."

"If only we could get safely down from this table!" she said. "I won’t be happy until we're truly out there in the world."

He reassured her and showed her how to place her little foot on the carved edge and the gilded decorations of the table. He brought his little ladder to help, and together, they reached the floor. But when they looked back at the old cupboard, chaos erupted. The carved stags poked their heads out, raised their antlers, and twisted their necks. The major-general jumped up and shouted to the old Chinaman, "They’re escaping! They’re escaping!" Startled, they quickly jumped into the window seat drawer. Inside were a few incomplete packs of cards and a carefully constructed doll's theater. A play was in progress, featuring all the queens of diamonds, clubs, hearts, and spades sitting in the front row, fanning themselves with tulips, while behind them stood the knaves, showing their heads above and below as playing cards do. The play was about two lovers who weren’t allowed to marry, and the shepherdess cried as it reminded her of her situation. "I can’t stand it," she said. "I have to get out of this drawer!" But as they reached for the floor and looked at the table, the old Chinaman was awake, shaking his entire body, and then he plummeted to the floor with a loud crash."

title: THE SHEPHERDESS AND THE SHEEP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"The old Chinaman is coming!" the little shepherdess cried in panic, dropping to one knee.

"I have a plan," said the chimney-sweep. "Let’s hide in the big potpourri jar in the corner; we can lie on rose petals and lavender, and throw salt in his eyes if he gets too close."

"No, that won’t work," she said. "I know that the Chinaman and the potpourri jar were once lovers, and there’s always lingering affection between those who have been so close. No, we’ve got to venture out into the wide world."

"Do you really have enough courage to step out into the wide world with me?" the chimney-sweep asked. "Have you thought about how vast it is, and how we can never come back here?"

Seeing her determination, the chimney-sweep said, "My way out is through the stove and up the chimney. Do you have the guts to crawl with me through the firebox and the iron pipe? Once we’re in the chimney, I’ll know how to navigate. We’ll soon climb so high that no one will be able to reach us, and we can come out through a hole at the top into the wide world." So he led her toward the stove door.

"It looks very dark," she remarked. But still, she followed him into the stove and through the pipe, where it was pitch black.

title: THE SHEPHERDESS AND THE SHEEP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Now we’re in the chimney," he said. "Look, there’s a beautiful star shining down!" It was a real star sparkling above them, as if guiding their way. They crawled and crept, ascending a steep incline, but the chimney-sweep helped and supported her until they climbed higher and higher. He pointed out the best places for her to set her little china foot, and eventually they reached the top of the chimney. Exhausted, they sat down, as you can imagine. Above them were stars in the sky, and below them were the rooftops of the town. They could see far out into the wide world, but the poor shepherdess rested her head on her chimney-sweep’s shoulder and cried, washing the gold off her sash. "This is too much," she said. "I can’t handle this; the world is too big. Oh, how I wish I were safely back on the table, under the looking glass! I won’t be happy until I’m back there. Now that I’ve followed you into the wide world, you’ll take me back, if you love me."

The chimney-sweep tried to reason with her, talking about the old Chinaman and the Major-general-field-sergeant-commander Billy-goat's-legs, but she sobbed so heart-wrenchingly and kissed him so desperately that he had no choice but to grant her wish, foolish as it was. So, after struggling quite a bit, they climbed down the chimney and crawled through the pipe and stove, which were definitely not pleasant journeys. Then they stood in the dark firebox, listening at the door to hear what was happening in the room. It was quiet, so they peeked out. Alas! there lay the old Chinaman on the floor; he had toppled off the table while trying to chase after them and broken into three pieces. His back had completely split, and his head had rolled into a corner of the room. The major-general was back in his usual spot, looking lost in thought.

"This is awful," cried the little shepherdess. "My poor grandfather is shattered, and it’s our fault! I can’t live with this;" and she wrung her little hands.

"He can be repaired," the chimney-sweep said. "Don’t be so quick to despair. If they fix his back and put in a solid rivet, he’ll be as good as new, able to say as many annoying things as ever."

"Do you really think so?" she asked, and then they climbed back up to the table, standing in their old positions.

"Since we didn’t accomplish anything," the chimney-sweep noted, "we might as well have stayed here instead of going through all that trouble."

"I hope grandfather does get repaired," said the shepherdess. "I wonder how much it will cost?"

And her wish came true. The family had the Chinaman’s back fixed and a sturdy rivet put through his neck; he looked as good as new, but he could no longer nod his head.

"You’ve gotten a bit proud since your fall," said Major-general-field-sergeant-commander Billy-goat's-legs. "You have no reason to act so high and mighty. Am I going to have her or not?"

The chimney-sweep and the little shepherdess looked at the old Chinaman with concern, worried he might nod; but he couldn’t, and besides, it was tiresome to keep telling strangers about the rivet in his neck.

And so the little china couple stayed together, grateful for their grandfather’s rivet, loving each other until they broke apart.

title: THE SHIRT-COLLAR
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a fine gentleman who owned many things, including a boot jack and a hairbrush, but his pride and joy was the finest shirt collar anyone had ever seen. As the collar grew old, he started thinking about getting married. One day, he found himself in the same washing tub as a garter. "Wow," the shirt collar exclaimed, "I've never seen anything so slim, delicate, neat, and soft! Can I ask your name?"

"I’m not going to tell you," the garter replied.

"Where do you live when you're at home?" asked the shirt collar. But the shy garter didn’t know how to respond to such a question.

"I assume you're a girdle, a kind of undergarment," said the shirt collar. "You seem both useful and pretty, little lady."

"You shouldn’t talk to me," the garter said. "I don’t think I've given you any reason to do so."

"Oh, come on, when someone looks as beautiful as you do, isn’t that reason enough?" the shirt collar said.

"Step back; don’t come too close," the garter replied. "You seem just like a man to me."

"I am indeed a fine gentleman," the shirt collar boasted, "I own a boot jack and a hairbrush." This wasn’t true since those belonged to his master, but he loved to brag.

"Just keep your distance," said the garter, "I’m not used to this."

Afterward, they were taken out of the wash tub, starched, and hung over a chair in the sunlight, then laid on the ironing board. Soon, the glowing iron appeared. "Mistress iron," the shirt collar said, "little mistress iron, I’m feeling quite warm. I’m changing, losing all my creases. You’re burning a hole in me. Ugh! I propose to you!"

"You old rag," said the flat iron, gliding over the collar with pride, as if she were a steam engine on a railway. "You old rag!" she scoffed.

title: THE SHIRT-COLLAR
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Since the edges of the shirt collar were a bit frayed, the scissors were brought in to smooth them out. "Oh!" exclaimed the shirt collar, "you'd make a fantastic dancer; you extend your leg so beautifully! I’ve never seen anything so charming; I bet no human could do the same."

"I doubt that," the scissors replied.

"You should be a countess," said the shirt collar, "but all I have is a fine gentleman, a boot jack, and a comb. I wish I owned an estate for your sake."

"What! Is he about to propose to me?" the scissors said, and she became so furious that she snipped sharply at the shirt collar, rendering it useless.

"Looks like I have to propose to the hairbrush next," thought the shirt collar. So, one day, he said, "It’s amazing how beautiful your hair is, my lady. Have you ever considered getting engaged?"

"As if I would think about it!" the hairbrush answered. "I’m already engaged to the boot jack."

"Engaged!" cried the shirt collar. "Now there’s no one left for me to propose to!" He then pretended to scoff at love.

title: THE SHIRT-COLLAR
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Time passed, and the shirt collar was taken in a bag to the paper mill. In the mill, he found a large gathering of rags, the finer ones separated from the coarser ones, just as it ought to be. Each rag had plenty to share, especially the shirt collar, who couldn’t stop boasting. "I’ve had tons of love affairs," said the shirt collar. "No one ever left me alone. Sure, I was a fine gentleman; quite conceited, really. I owned a boot jack and a brush I never used. You should have seen me back then when I was fresh. I still remember my first love—she was a charming girdle who jumped into the wash tub for me. There was also a widow who loved me deeply, but I ignored her and she turned quite black. The next had a passion for dance; she gave me a cut that I still carry. Even my own hairbrush was in love with me and lost all her hair from being neglected. Yes, I’ve had a lot of experiences, but my biggest regret is for the girdle—the garter I meant to say—that jumped in the wash tub. I have many regrets, and it’s time I became white paper."

In the end, all the rags were turned into white paper, and the shirt collar became the very same piece of paper on which this story is printed. This was a punishment for his excessive bragging about things that weren't true. It serves as a warning to all of us to be mindful of our actions, for we might one day find ourselves in the rag bag, turned into white paper, where our entire history, even the secretive parts, could be written. And it wouldn’t be pleasant to wander the world as a piece of paper, revealing everything we’ve done, just like the boastful shirt collar.

title: THE SILVER SHILLING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, a shiny shilling burst out of the mint, cheering, "Hooray! I'm off into the big wide world!" And indeed, it set off into that vast world. Children held it with warm hands, a miser grabbed it tightly and coldly, while older folks examined it countless times. Soon enough, young people let it roll away. This shilling was made of silver with just a hint of copper, and it felt quite worldly after a year of being used where it was minted. One day, it truly ventured out into the world, belonging to a gentleman ready to travel abroad. Unbeknownst to him, the shilling was sitting at the bottom of his purse when he set out, until one day he found it between his fingers. "Wow," he exclaimed, "a shilling from home! This has to join me on my travels!" The shilling leaped with joy when it was tossed back into the purse.

Inside the purse, the shilling mingled with various foreign coins who were constantly coming and going. Each coin took turns being spent, but the shilling from home was always put back, marking it as special. Weeks went by, and the shilling traveled far within the purse, oblivious to its surroundings. It learned that the other coins were French and Italian; one claimed they were in this town, another insisted they were in that, but the shilling couldn't grasp what they meant. A person can't see much of the world when stuck in a bag, and that was the shilling's fate. One day, as it lay in the purse, it noticed the bag was slightly open. Curious, it inched toward the opening to get a glimpse of the outside world. Little did it know what would happen next; curiosity often leads to trouble. It leaned too close and slipped into the pocket of the gentleman’s trousers. By evening, when the purse was taken out, the shilling dropped onto the floor, unnoticed. The next morning, the gentleman got dressed again for his journey, leaving the shilling behind. Eventually, someone found it and, thinking it was a good coin, tossed it with three others. "Ah!" the shilling thought, "This is nice! I’ll finally see the world, meet new folks, and learn about different customs!"

title: THE SILVER SHILLING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Is that really a shilling?" someone exclaimed just then. "That's not a genuine coin from here—it's worthless!"

This is where the story truly begins, as recounted by the shilling.

"'Worthless! Good for nothing!' That remark pierced me like a dagger. I knew I had a true ring and that I was a genuine coin. They must be mistaken; they couldn't possibly mean me. But yes, it was me they called 'worthless.'

"'Then I'll just have to pass it off in the dark,' said the man who had received me. Great, so I was to be disposed of under cover of darkness, only to be insulted in daylight again.

"'Worthless! Good for nothing!' Oh, I must find a way to get lost, I thought. I trembled as hands tried to pass me off as a local coin. What use was my silver, my stamp, or my real value in a place where those qualities meant nothing? In the world's eyes, a person is valued based on others' opinions. It must be hard to have a guilty conscience, sneaking around due to wrongdoings. I was innocent, yet I shuddered whenever I was brought out, fearing I’d be rejected again as an imposter. Finally, I was handed over to a poor old woman as payment for her hard work that day. But she couldn't get rid of me either; nobody wanted me. To her, I was an unlucky shilling. 'I have to pass this shilling to someone,' she said. 'I can't keep a bad shilling. The rich baker can handle the loss better than I can. But still, it's not right.'"

title: THE SILVER SHILLING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"'Ah!' I sighed inwardly, 'Am I really such a burden on this poor woman’s conscience? Have I changed so much in my old age?' The woman offered me to the rich baker, but he was too savvy with money and tossed me back at her almost disdainfully. She couldn’t buy bread with me, and I felt heartbroken that I caused her so much trouble, being treated like a cast-off coin. I once felt joyful and certain of my worth; now I was as miserable as a poor shilling no one wanted. The woman took me back and, studying me closely, declared, 'No, I won't trick anyone with you again. I’ll bore a hole through you so everyone knows you’re a worthless and false shilling. But why should I do that? Maybe you're lucky after all. Yes, I’ll make a hole through this shilling,' she decided, 'and put a string through it, giving it to my neighbor’s little girl to wear as a good luck charm.' So, she drilled a hole in me.

"Having a hole bored through me wasn't pleasant, but we can tolerate a lot when it’s done with good intentions. A string was threaded through the hole, and I became a sort of medal. They hung me around the neck of a child, who giggled and kissed me, allowing me to rest for a whole night on the warm, innocent chest of a child.

"The next morning, the child's mother picked me up and had her own thoughts about me, which I quickly sensed. First, she searched for scissors and cut the string.

"'Lucky shilling!' she said, 'This must be worth a try!' Then she soaked me in vinegar until I turned green, filled the hole with cement, and polished me a bit before heading out at twilight to the lottery ticket seller to buy a ticket with a shilling she hoped would bring good fortune. Everything seemed to bring me trouble. The lottery collector squeezed me so tightly I thought I'd crack. I had been labeled worthless, discarded, and now surrounded by other coins flaunting their fancy stamps. Knowing how proud they were, I avoided them from sheer embarrassment. The collector was accompanied by several men who seemed very busy, causing me to fall unnoticed into a chest filled with other coins.

title: THE SILVER SHILLING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I can't say whether the lottery ticket won a prize, but in just a few days later, I was recognized as a bad shilling and set aside. Each incident only added to my misery. Even a person with a solid reputation struggles to defend themselves against others' claims, as they aren't seen as impartial judges.

"A year rolled by, and during this time, I was passed from hand to hand—always mistreated and regarded with disdain, trusted by no one. But I kept faith in myself, losing trust in the world. Yes, those were very dark days.

"Finally, one day, I was handed to a traveler, a foreigner—the very same man who had taken me away from home. He was simple and true-hearted enough to accept me as genuine. Would he try to pass me off? Would I again endure cries of 'Worthless! Good-for-nothing!'? The traveler examined me closely and smiled; it was the brightest smile I had ever seen. 'How curious!' he said, 'It's a coin from my homeland—a good, true shilling! Someone’s bored a hole through it, and people have likely dismissed it as false. How fascinating that it’s come back to me. I’ll take it home to my family.'"

"Joy surged through me at these words. Once again, I was deemed a worthy shilling, set to return home where everyone would see I was made of fine silver with a genuine stamp. I wanted to shine with delight, but that’s not the nature of silver. I was wrapped in fine white paper to avoid mingling with the other coins and, on special occasions when people from my homeland were around, I was brought out and spoken of kindly. They called me interesting, though interesting folks often have nothing to say for themselves.

"When I finally got home, all my burdens were lifted. Joy overwhelmed me! After all, I was good silver with a genuine stamp! No more insults or disappointments to bear. Sure, there was still a hole in me, as if I were false, but suspicions mean nothing when you're truly genuine. People should keep striving to act honestly, for in time, all will be made right. That's what I truly believe," declared the shilling.

title: THE SNAIL AND THE ROSE-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Surrounding the garden was a hedge of hazel bushes. Beyond that, there were fields and meadows filled with cows and sheep. But right in the middle of the garden, a beautiful Rose-tree stood blooming, underneath which sat a Snail, whose shell held a lot—basically, it was just him.

"Just wait until my moment comes," he said. "I’m going to do more than just grow roses, produce nuts, or provide milk like the hazel bush, cows, and sheep do."

"I have high hopes for you," replied the rose-tree. "Can I ask when that will happen?"

"I take my time," the snail said. "You’re always in such a rush. That doesn’t build any excitement."

The next year, the snail lay in almost the same spot, basking in the sun under the rose-tree, which had once again sprouted roses that were just as fresh and lovely as ever. The snail crept halfway out of his shell, stretched out his feelers, and then pulled them back in.

"Everything is just like it was last year! No progress at all; the rose-tree keeps blooming but doesn’t do anything more."

Summer and autumn rolled by. The rose-tree continued to blossom with roses and buds until the snow came and the weather turned cold and wet. Eventually, it drooped its head, and the snail crawled back into the ground.

A new year began, and the roses came back, along with the snail.

"You’re just an old rose-tree now," the snail said. "You better hurry up and die. You’ve given everything you have to the world; whether it mattered much is a question I haven’t had time to contemplate. But one thing is clear: you haven’t done anything to grow inside yourself, or else you would have created something new. Do you have any defense for that? Soon you’ll just be a stick. Do you understand what I mean?"

"You’re scaring me," said the rose-tree. "I’ve never thought about that before."

title: THE SNAIL AND THE ROSE-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"No, you haven’t bothered to think at all. Have you ever considered why you bloom, and how your blooming happens—why it occurs the way it does and not in any other way?"

"No," the rose-tree replied. "I bloom happily because I can’t do otherwise. The sun shines and warms me, and the air refreshes me. I drink the clear dew and the invigorating rain. I breathe and I live! A power arises from the earth inside me, and from above, I receive strength. I feel an ever-growing joy, and that’s why I keep blooming. That’s my life; I couldn’t act differently."

"You’ve had an easy life," the snail commented.

"Absolutely. Everything was provided for me," said the rose-tree. "But even more was given to you. You have one of those deep-thinking natures, a brilliant mind that amazes the world."

"I have no intention of doing that," the snail replied. "The world means nothing to me. What does it matter to me? I have enough to focus on with myself, and that’s enough for me."

"But don’t we all have to share our best parts with others and give as much as we can? It’s true I’ve only given roses, but you—so richly gifted—what have you offered the world? What will you give?"

"What have I given? What am I going to give? I spit on it; it’s worthless and has nothing to do with me. As for you, keep producing roses; that’s all you can do. Let the hazel bush provide nuts, and the cows and sheep give milk; they each have their purpose. I have mine within myself. I withdraw into myself, and that’s where I stop. The world means nothing to me."

With that, the snail retreated into his shell and blocked the entrance.

"That’s really sad," said the rose-tree. "I can’t retreat into myself, no matter how much I might want to; I have to keep producing roses. Then the petals fall, and they get carried away by the wind. But I once saw a rose placed in a hymn-book, and how one of my roses was held close by a beautiful young girl. Another was kissed by a child in pure joy. That brought me happiness; it was a true blessing. Those are my memories, my life."

And the rose-tree continued to bloom in innocence, while the snail idled in his shell—the world didn’t mean anything to him.

The snail had turned into earth beneath the earth, but so had the rose-tree. Even the rose in the hymn-book had faded, yet in the garden, there were other rose-trees and other snails. The latter crawled into their homes and spat at the world, for it didn’t matter to them.

Should we read the story again? It’ll be just the same.

title: THE SNOW MAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"It's wonderfully cold out here," said the Snow Man. "It makes my whole body crackle. This is exactly the kind of wind that brings life to one. Look at that big red thing in the sky; it’s staring at me!" He was talking about the sun, which was just setting. "But I won’t blink; I’ll keep myself together."

He had two triangular pieces of tile for eyes; his mouth was made from an old broken rake, complete with teeth. He had come to life amidst the joyful shouts of children, the sound of jingling sleigh bells, and the crack of whips. The sun dipped below the horizon, and the full moon rose—large, round, and bright—in the deep blue sky.

"Here it comes again from the other side," the Snow Man said, thinking the sun was peeking out once more. "Ah, I’ve cured him of his staring; now he can just hang up there and shine while I admire myself. If only I knew how to move from this spot—I’d love to glide over there on the ice like the boys do. But I have no idea how; I don’t even know how to run."

"Go away, go away!" barked the old yard-dog. He was quite hoarse and couldn’t say "Bow wow" properly. He used to be an indoor dog and lay by the fire, and his hoarseness had lingered ever since. "One day, the sun will make you run. I saw it make your predecessor run last winter, and the one before him too. Go away, go away; they all end up leaving."

"I don’t get you, friend," said the Snow Man. "Is that thing up there supposed to teach me to run? I saw it running just a little while ago, and now it’s creeping up from the other side."

"You don’t know anything," replied the yard-dog, "but you’ve just been put together. What you see up there is the moon, and what you saw before was the sun. It will come back tomorrow, and it will probably teach you to run right down into the ditch by the well; I sense a change in the weather. I can feel these pricks and stabs in my left leg, which means something is definitely changing."

title: THE SNOW MAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I don’t get him," the Snow Man thought to himself. "But I have a feeling he’s talking about something unpleasant. The one he called the sun isn’t my friend; I can feel that too."

"Go away, go away," barked the yard-dog, then he turned around three times and crawled into his kennel to sleep.

There was indeed a change in the weather. By morning, a thick fog covered the entire landscape, and a sharp wind arose, making the cold feel like it could freeze your bones. But when the sun rose, the view was breathtaking. The trees and bushes were covered in hoar frost, resembling a forest of white coral, while frozen dew-drops sparkled on every twig. The delicate shapes hidden by summer's lush greenery were now clearly visible, appearing like sparkling lace. Every twig glimmered with a white glow. The birch danced in the wind, full of life as if it were summer, and its beauty was astonishing. And where the sun shone, everything sparkled as if diamond dust had been scattered everywhere; the snowy ground seemed adorned with diamonds, with countless glimmers brighter than the snow itself.

"This is truly beautiful," said a young girl who had come into the garden with a young man. They both stood near the Snow Man, admiring the dazzling scene. "Summer couldn’t possibly show us anything more beautiful," she exclaimed, her eyes shining.

"And we can’t have something like this during the summer," the young man said, pointing to the Snow Man. "He’s fantastic."

The girl laughed, waved at the Snow Man, and then skipped away over the snow with her friend. The snow creaked and crackled beneath her feet as if she were walking on starch.

"Who are those two?" the Snow Man asked the yard-dog. "You’ve been here longer than I have; do you know them?"

"Of course I know them," said the yard-dog. "She has petted me many times, and he’s fed me a bone. I never bite those two."

title: THE SNOW MAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"They are lovers," he said. "They’ll go live together soon, sharing their bones. Go away, go away!"

"Are they the same kind of beings as you and me?" the Snow Man asked.

"Well, they belong to the same master," replied the yard-dog. "Of course, anyone who was just created knows very little. I can see that in you. I have age and experience. I know everyone in this house, and I remember when I didn’t have to stay out here in the cold, chained up. Go away, go away!"

"The cold is delightful," said the Snow Man, "but please tell me, just don’t jingle your chain so much; it bothers me."

"Go away, go away!" barked the yard-dog. "I’ll tell you. They said I was a cute little fellow once; I used to lie in a velvet chair at my master’s house and sit in the mistress’s lap. They would kiss my nose and clean my paws with an embroidered handkerchief, calling me 'Ami, dear Ami, sweet Ami.' But as I grew bigger, they sent me to the housekeeper’s room, so I ended up living on the ground floor. You can look into the room from where you stand; see where I used to be the master! It was certainly a smaller room than those upstairs, but I was much more comfortable since I wasn’t always being pulled and poked by the kids like before. I got just as good food, if not better. I had my own cushion and a stove—oh, it’s the best thing in winter! I used to curl up under it, lying there contentedly. I still dream of that stove. Go away, go away!"

"Does a stove look beautiful?" asked the Snow Man. "Is it anything like me?"

"It’s the exact opposite of you," said the dog. "It's black like a crow, with a long neck and a brass knob. It burns firewood, spewing flames from its mouth. We should stay away from it to stay comfortable. You can see it through the window from where you are standing."

title: THE SNOW MAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Then the Snow Man looked and saw a shiny object with a brass knob, flames dancing from the bottom. A strange sensation washed over him—it was odd, and he couldn’t explain it. But there are some who aren’t made of snow who know what it is. "And why did you leave it?" asked the Snow Man, feeling the stove must be female. "How could you give up such a cozy place?"

"I had no choice," replied the yard-dog. "They put me outside and chained me up here. I bit the youngest of my master’s sons in the leg because he kicked my bone away. 'Bone for bone,' I thought, but they were furious, and since then, I’ve been chained and lost my bone. Don’t you hear how hoarse I am? Go away, go away! I can’t speak like other dogs anymore. Go away, go away; it’s the end of it all."

But the Snow Man had stopped listening. He was gazing through the window into the housekeeper's room where the stove stood on its four iron legs, looking almost as big as the Snow Man himself. "What a strange crackling I feel inside me," he said. "Will I ever get in there? It’s such an innocent wish, and innocent wishes always come true. I must go in there and lean against her, even if I have to break the window."

"You must never go in there," said the yard-dog. "If you get close to the stove, you’ll melt away, away."

"I might as well go," said the Snow Man. "I think I’m breaking apart regardless."

All that day, the Snow Man stood looking through the window, and when dusk hit, the room looked even more inviting. The stove gave off a warm glow, not like the sun or the moon—just the kind of bright light that shines from a well-fed stove. When the door of the stove opened, flames flickered out; that's normal for all stoves. The light from the flames hit the Snow Man’s face and chest with a rosy gleam. "I can’t stand it any longer," he said. "It looks so beautiful when it stretches out its tongue!"

title: THE SNOW MAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The night was long but didn't feel that way to the Snow Man, who was too busy enjoying his own reflections and crackling in the cold. In the morning, the window panes of the housekeeper’s room were covered in ice—beautiful ice flowers that any Snow Man could wish for, but they concealed the stove. The panes wouldn’t thaw, and he couldn’t see the stove he imagined as if it were a lovely human being. The snow crackled, and the wind whistled around him; it was just the kind of cold a Snow Man should enjoy. Yet he couldn’t—they say he was "stove sick.""

"That’s a terrible disease for a Snow Man," said the yard-dog. "I’ve had it too, but I got over it. Go away, go away," he barked, then added, "the weather is changing." And change it did; it started to thaw. As the warmth grew, the Snow Man began to shrink. He said nothing and made no complaints, which is a definite sign. One morning, he finally fell apart completely, and lo and behold, where he once stood, a stick was stuck in the ground. It was the pole the boys had used to build him. "Ah, now I see why he longed for the stove," said the yard-dog. "And there’s the shovel used for cleaning the stove, right next to the pole." The Snow Man had a stove scraper inside him—that’s what made him feel this way. "But it’s all over now. Go away, go away." And soon, winter ended. "Go away, go away," barked the hoarse yard-dog. But the girls in the house sang,

"Come from your fragrant home, green thyme; Stretch your soft branches, willow-tree; The months are bringing the sweet spring-time, When the lark in the sky sings joyfully. Come gentle sun, while the cuckoo sings, And I'll mock his note in my wanderings."

And no one thought about the Snow Man anymore.

title: THE SNOWDROP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
It was winter. The air was cold and the wind biting. But inside, behind closed doors, it was warm and cozy, and nestled within the earth was a flower bulb hiding beneath the snow.

One day, rain started to fall. The droplets made their way through the snowy blanket down to the bulb, whispering about the bright world above. Soon, a sunbeam broke through the snow and warmed the root, stirring it awake.

"I can't do it," said the Sunbeam. "I'm not strong enough to unlock the door! But when summer comes, I will be strong!"

"When will summer arrive?" asked the Flower, repeating the question each time a new sunbeam reached her. But summer was still far away; the snow remained on the ground, and ice covered the water each night.

"How long will it take? How long!" lamented the Flower. "I feel something moving inside me; I need to stretch, to unlock the door, to greet summer! Oh, what a joyful time that will be!"

With determination, the Flower stirred and stretched within the softened shell that the water had created, warmed by the earth and the snow, and nudged by the Sunbeam. It broke through the snow, revealing a greenish-white blossom on a green stalk, with thick, narrow leaves that seemed to shelter it. Though the snow was cold, the Sunbeam's increasing strength made it easier to get through.

"Welcome, welcome!" sang each ray of sunlight, as the Flower emerged from the snow into the vibrant world. The Sunbeams kissed it, causing it to fully open, white as snow, with green stripes. It bowed its head in joy and humility.

"Beautiful Flower!" said the Sunbeams. "You are so elegant and delicate! You are the first and the only one! You are our love! You are the herald of summer, spreading joy across country and town. Soon all the snow will melt and the cold winds will vanish. We will reign, and everything will be green. But you, dear Flower, are the first—so graceful, so delicate!"

title: THE SNOWDROP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
It was a magical moment. It felt as if the air was singing, and beams of light were dancing through the flower's leaves and stalk. Despite being fragile and easily broken, the Flower stood strong in its youthful beauty, wearing its white dress with green stripes and creating a summer atmosphere. Yet, summer was still a while away. Clouds covered the sun, and bitter winds blew.

"You've come out too early!" the Wind and Weather exclaimed. "We still hold the power, and you will feel it. You should have stayed quietly at home instead of showing off. Your time hasn't come yet!"

It was freezing cold! The following days brought no sunbeams at all, just weather that could easily snap such a tiny Flower in half with its chill. But the Flower had more strength than it realized. It was filled with joy and faith that summer would come soon, confirmed by its deep longing and the warmth of sunlight. So it stood tall, trusting in the snow, in its white attire, bowing its head even as thick snowflakes fell and icy winds swept over.

"You’ll break!" they warned. "You’ll wither away! What were you thinking out here? Why did you let yourself get lured in? The Sunbeam was just toying with you. Now you’ve got what’s coming to you, you summer fool!"

"Summer fool!" the Flower echoed in the cold morning.

"O summer fool!" cheered some delighted children. "Look over there! How beautiful, how beautiful! The first one, the only one!"

Their words lifted the Flower's spirits like warm sunbeams. In her joy, she didn’t even notice when she was plucked. She lay in a child's hand, kissed by little lips, brought into a warm room, gazed upon by gentle eyes, and placed in water. It felt so revitalizing! The Flower thought she had finally encountered summer.

title: THE SNOWDROP
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The daughter of the household, a lovely little girl, was celebrating her confirmation, and her friend was too. He was preparing for an exam for a job. "He shall be my summer fool," she said, taking the delicate Flower and wrapping it in scented paper covered in verses beginning and ending with "summer fool.” "My friend, you’re a winter fool now." She had teased him about the summer. Yes, all this was in the verses, and the paper was folded like a letter, with the Flower tucked inside. It grew dark for her, as dark as when she lay hidden in the bulb. The Flower set off on her journey, lying in the post bag, getting squished, which wasn’t very pleasant. But that didn’t last long.

The journey came to an end; the letter was opened and read by the dear friend. He was thrilled! He kissed the letter, tucking it away with its verses in a box full of beautiful poems, all without flowers. She was the first and only one, just as the Sunbeams had declared, and that made her feel special.

She had plenty of time to ponder this while summer rolled on, winter came and went, and another summer blossomed again before she showed up once more. But now, the young man wasn’t pleased at all. He handled the letter rough, tossing the verses aside, causing the Flower to fall to the ground. Flattened and faded, she wondered why she had been discarded. Still, it was better to be here than in the fire, where the verses and paper were being burned to cinders. What had happened? All too often, the Flower had become a joke to him, but the girl had truly played a prank on him; she had chosen another friend over the summer.

The next morning, sunlight shone down on the little crumpled Snowdrop, looking as if it had been painted on the floor. The servant girl, sweeping the room, picked it up and placed it in one of the books on the table, thinking it must have fallen during cleanup. Once more, the flower lay among verses—printed verses—better than handwritten ones, at least because more money had gone into them.

And so years passed. The book remained on the shelf until it was taken down, and someone read from it. It was a wonderful book, filled with verses and songs by the old Danish poet, Ambrosius Stub, worth reading. The man reading the book turned a page.

"Look, there's a flower!" he exclaimed, "a snowdrop, a summer fool, a poet fool! This flower must have been placed here with a meaning! Poor Ambrosius Stub! He was a summer fool too, a poet fool who came too early and had to face the harsh winds, wandering as a guest from one noble estate to another, like a flower in a glass of water, a flower in rhymed verses! Summer fool, winter fool, fun and folly—but the first, the only, the fresh young Danish poet of those days. Yes, you shall remain as a reminder in this book, little snowdrop: you were placed here for a reason."

And so the Snowdrop was tucked back into the book, feeling honored and pleased to know it was a symbol within a cherished collection of songs, aware that even the first to sing and write had also been a snowdrop, a summer fool, seen in winter as a fool. The Flower understood this in its own way, just as we interpret everything in our unique ways.

title: THE STORKS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
In the last house of a small village, the storks had made a nest, where the mother stork sat with her four little ones. The chicks stretched their necks and pointed their black beaks, which hadn’t turned red yet like their parents'. A little distance away, on the edge of the roof, stood the father stork, upright and stiff. To avoid being completely still, he lifted one leg and balanced on the other, so still it looked like a wooden carving. "It must look impressive," he thought, "to have a guard for my wife’s nest. They won’t know I’m her husband; they’ll think I was ordered to stand here. That’s quite noble." So he stood on one leg.

Down in the street, a group of children were playing, and when they spotted the storks, one adventurous boy began singing a song about them. Soon, the others joined in, singing bits they could remember in their own way.

"Stork, stork, fly away, Please don't stand on one leg today, See your wife in her nest, With her little ones at rest. They will hang one, And fry another; They will shoot a third, And roast his brother."

"Did you hear what those kids are singing?" asked the young storks. "They say we'll be hanged and roasted."

"Don't worry about what they say; you don’t have to listen," the mother reassured them. "They can't hurt you."

But the boys continued to sing, pointing and teasing the storks. One boy named Peter, however, thought it was unfair to mock animals and refused to join in. The mother stork comforted her chicks, saying, "Look at your father; see how he stands so quietly, even on one leg."

"But we're really scared," the young storks replied, drawing their heads back into the nest.

The next day, when the children spotted the storks again while playing, they sang the song once more.

"Will we be hanged and roasted?" asked the young storks.

title: THE STORKS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Absolutely not," the mother said. "I’ll teach you to fly, and once you’ve learned, we can soar to the meadows to visit the frogs. They'll bow to us in the water and croak. Then, we can feast on them—that'll be fun."

"Then," she continued, "all the storks in the land will gather for a grand autumn exercise, so it's crucial for you to learn how to fly well. If you don't, the general will poke you with his beak, and you could be in trouble. So, it’s important to practice and be ready for drill time."

"What if we really do end up getting hurt like the boys say? And hey! They’re singing again!"

"Focus on me, not on them," said the mother stork. "Once the big event is over, we’ll fly far away to warm lands, away from here. We’ll go to Egypt and see the wonderful pyramids that reach the clouds. They’ve been around longer than any stork can imagine. There’s a river there that floods and then recedes, leaving mud behind, and we can wander around and feast on frogs there—plenty of delicious frogs."

"Oh, that sounds lovely! There, we can eat to our hearts' content. While we enjoy our time there, in this land, the trees will stand bare without any leaves, and the weather will be icy, dropping clouds that look like little white rags on the ground." The mother stork meant snow, but couldn't explain it any other way.

"Will those naughty boys freeze and fall apart?" asked the young storks.

"No, they won’t freeze and break into pieces," the mother clarified. "But they will be very cold, stuck in a dark, gloomy room while we fly in lands filled with blooming flowers and warm sunlight."

As time passed, the young storks grew big enough to stand upright and look around from their nest. Every day, their father brought them delicious frogs, little snakes, and various stork delicacies. It was entertaining to watch him perform tricks to amuse them. He would twist his head around his tail and clatter his beak like a rattle, then share tales about the marshes and swamps.

title: THE STORKS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Alright," said the mother one day, "it’s time for you to learn how to fly." All four young ones had to step out onto the roof. At first, they wobbled, struggling to balance on their wings to avoid falling off.

"Watch me," said the mother. "Angle your heads like this and place your feet there. One, two, one, two—that’s it! Now you’ll be able to take care of yourselves in the world."

Then she flew a short distance away, and the young ones jumped to follow her. Unfortunately, they tumbled to the ground because they were still too heavy.

"I don’t want to fly," whined one of the young storks, retreating back into the nest. "I don’t want to go to warm places."

"Would you rather stay here and freeze when winter comes?" replied the mother. "Or wait until the boys plan to hang or roast you? Well, do you want me to call them?"

"Oh no, no," exclaimed the young stork, hopping back onto the roof with the others. Now, they were all focused, and by the third day, they managed to fly a little. They even thought they might be soaring, so they attempted to rest on their wings while quickly realizing they were falling and had to flap as fast as they could. The boys returned, singing their song again.

"Are we going to fly down and peck their eyes out?" asked the young storks.

"No, just leave them be," said the mother. "Listen to me; that’s much more important. Alright, one-two-three. Now to the right. One-two-three. Now to the left, around the chimney. Good job! That last flap was so graceful that I'll allow you to fly with me tomorrow to the marshes. There will be many fancy storks and their families there, and it's up to you to show them that my chicks are the most well-mannered storks around. Strut confidently—it'll earn you respect."

title: THE STORKS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"But can we get back at those naughty boys?" asked the young storks.

"No, let them scream as much as they want. You can now fly high among the clouds, and while you’re enjoying the land of the pyramids, they'll be freezing, with not a single green leaf or apple to eat on the trees."

"We’ll get our revenge," whispered the young storks to one another as they resumed their practice.

Among all the boys in the street who sang the teasing song, none were more determined than the one who started it—he was a tiny fellow, barely six years old. To the young storks, he seemed ancient, especially since he was much larger than their own parents. Clearly, storks aren’t aware of how old children and adults are. They decided this boy, who began the song and kept it going, would be the target of their revenge. The young storks were incredibly upset, and their anger intensified as they grew, eventually leading their mother to promise they could get back at the boy, but only the day they were set to leave.

"We first need to see how you perform at the grand review," she said. "If you don’t do well, the general will poke you with his beak, and you might meet your end like the boys said—just not in the same way. So, we’ll wait and see."

"You’ll see," the young storks replied. They practiced hard every day, and soon, it was a joy to watch them fly gracefully. As autumn rolled in, all the storks began to gather before heading off to the warm countries for winter. The review began, and they flew over forests and villages to showcase their skills because they had a long journey ahead. The young storks excelled, earning honors along with treats of frogs and snakes, the best part being they could eat those treats, which they did with gusto.

"Now can we get back at them?" they cheered.

"Of course," replied the mother stork. "I have the perfect plan for our revenge. I know of a pond where tiny little children wait for the storks to take them to their parents. The sweetest little babies lie there dreaming, happier than they will ever be in the future. All parents love having a child, and children are thrilled to have a little brother or sister. So, let's fly to the pond and bring back a baby for each of the kids who didn’t tease us."

"But what should we do about the naughty boy who started the song?" asked the young storks.

"There’s a little dead baby in the pond who dreamed too long," the mother said. "We’ll take that one to the naughty boy, and he’ll cry because we brought him a dead little brother. But we won't forget the good boy who thought it was wrong to mock animals. We’ll bring him his little brother and sister, too, since he was nice. From now on, he’ll be called Peter, and you will all be named Peter, too."

So they all followed their mother’s plan, and from that day forward, all storks have been called Peter.

title: THE STORM SHAKES THE SHIELD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Back in the days when my grandfather was just a little boy, he would run around in his bright red breeches and coat, complete with a feather in his cap—just like all the other boys when they dressed their best. Life was quite different then, with lots of vibrant displays in the streets that we don’t see anymore since they’ve been deemed too old-fashioned. Listening to grandfather share those stories is always fascinating.

It must have been such a magnificent sight back then when the shoemaker brought over the shield to celebrate the change at the court-house. The silken flag waved gracefully, the double eagle on the shield gleamed, and a big boot was proudly displayed. The youngest boys carried the ‘welcome’ signs and the chest of the workmen's guild, their shirt sleeves festooned with red and white ribbons. The older boys armed themselves with drawn swords, each topped with a lemon. A full band filled the air with lively music, featuring the ‘bird’—the grand stick with a crescent at the top, adorned with all sorts of jingling trinkets, creating a delightful clamor that dazzled the eyes under the sunlight glinting off its gold, silver, and brass.

Leading the procession was the playful Harlequin, decked out in a patchwork of colorful fabric sewn together, his face painted black, bells jingling atop his head like a horse's bridle. He would tap the crowd with his bat, creating a ruckus without causing any harm, causing the people to jostle together, only to retreat and surge forward again. Children stumbled over their own feet into the gutter, elderly women elbowed their way through the crowd with sharp expressions, and snuff in hand. Laughter erupted, conversation flowed, and people crowded around windows, doorsteps, and even lounged on rooftops. Despite a little rain, which was a blessing for the farmers, they considered the wet weather only a minor inconvenience.

Oh, the stories grandfather could tell! As a boy, he experienced all these extravagant events in their full glory. The oldest policeman would deliver a speech from the platform where the shield was hung, reciting verses that sounded as if composed by a poet—because they were! Three friends collaborated on it, largely inspired after enjoying a bowl of punch to get their creativity flowing.

title: THE STORM SHAKES THE SHIELD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The crowd cheered enthusiastically for the speech but erupted even louder when the Harlequin pranced in front of the platform, making funny faces in delight.

The entertainers played their parts perfectly, sipping mead from spirit glasses, which they tossed into the crowd to be caught. Grandfather was lucky enough to snag one of these glasses, gifted to him by a hardworking mason who had caught it mid-flight. It truly was a joyous spectacle, with the shield at the new court-house draped in flowers and green wreaths.

"You never forget a feast like that, no matter how old you become," grandfather would say. And he wasn't mistaken, for even after witnessing many grand events in his lifetime, it was always the tale of the shield being moved from the old to the new court-house that delighted him most.

One day, when he was just a little boy, grandfather went with his parents to see this festive occasion. It was his first visit to the capital, and the streets were so crowded he thought everyone was there to carry the shield. There were shields everywhere—if you could hang pictures inside and outside, they could fill a hundred rooms! The tailor displayed images of all sorts of clothes, showing off his talent for stitching from the simplest to the finest garments. Meanwhile, the tobacco shop showcased charming little boys puffing on cigars, just like in real life. There were signs featuring painted butter, herring, clerical collars, coffins—everything imaginable! A person could stroll through the streets all day, soaking in the sights and learning who lived where simply by looking at the shields they hung outside.

Grandfather recounted what happened with those shields when he first arrived in town. Trust me, he was definitely not spinning tales—he had no reason to as he had no mischievousness on his back, as mother used to claim.

The first night he spent in the town was marked by a terrible gale—one so fierce it was recorded as the worst ever in the newspapers. The skies darkened with flying tiles, old woodwork splintered and crashed, and a wheelbarrow rolled down the street by itself, trying to avoid the chaos. The air was filled with groaning and howling, creating a scene of utter destruction. The canal overflowed, unsure where to go, while the storm swept through town, taking chimneys along for the ride and forcing proud weathercocks on church steeples to bow lower than ever.

title: THE STORM SHAKES THE SHIELD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
There was a little sentry-house where the wise old fire brigade superintendent lived, always the last to arrive on the scene.
