The storm ripped at the sentry-house, rolling it down the street, landing right in front of the home of a dirty journeyman plasterer who had saved three lives during the last fire. But the sentry-house didn’t recognize his heroism.

The barber’s shield, a huge brass plate, was swept away and landed squarely in the garden of the local councillor of justice. The whole neighborhood saw it as a nasty jest, since they, along with many of the councillor's wife's friends, called her "the Razor" for being sharp-tongued and knowing more about everyone's affairs than they knew about themselves.

One shield decorated with a painted dried fish sailed right in front of the door of a Newspaper Writer. The storm seemed to forget that a newspaper writer is someone who, after all, holds great sway in his own paper and isn't one to take jokes lightly.

Meanwhile, a weathercock took refuge on a nearby house, looking as though it were enjoying the tropical storm—so said the neighbors.

A tub belonging to the cooper ended up lodged under a ‘ladies' costumes’ sign.

The menu of the local eatery, framed heavily and previously hanging at the door, was posted at the theatre entrance, a place that rarely saw patrons. "What a ludicrous menu—horse-radish, soup, and stuffed cabbage—" but suddenly, people flocked in!

The fox's skin, the respected symbol of the furrier, was found hanging from the bell-pull of a young man who always attended early lectures, appearing quite like a furled umbrella. He prided himself on seeking the truth and was hailed by his aunt as a "model and an example."

The sign stating 'Institution for Superior Education' ended up near the billiard club, which boasted with the phrase, 'Children raised by hand.' It wasn't clever, yet, as storms go, it was just another one of those unpredictable moments.

It was a dreadful night, and when morning came—would you believe it?—most of the shields had swapped places! Some of the inscriptions were so cheeky that grandfather refused to discuss them, even though I caught him chuckling quietly, and perhaps his descriptions weren’t entirely accurate.

The poor townsfolk, and especially the newcomers, constantly confused whom they were trying to find based on the mismatched shields. For instance, those wanting to attend an important gathering of dignified elders often found themselves amidst a raucous boys’ school where everyone was jumping from chairs to tables.

Some even mixed up the church and the theatre, and that was a truly terrible mistake!

Such a storm has not graced us in our time; it only swept through when grandfather was but a small child. Perhaps we’ll never witness a storm of that degree, but maybe our grandchildren will—let's just hope they stay indoors while the storm dances the shields around.

title: THE STORY OF A MOTHER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
A mother sat beside her young child, feeling incredibly sad as she feared it might not survive. The child looked pale, its eyes were shut, and every once in a while it let out a deep, almost sigh-like breath. The mother's heart ached as she looked at her precious little one. Suddenly, there was a knock at the door, and in walked a frail old man. He was wrapped in what seemed like a large, heavy blanket, which he truly needed to stay warm, for outside it was cold and wintery, with snow and ice all around, and the wind bit at her face. The child had briefly dozed off, and noticing the old man shivering, the mother stood up and warmed a small mug of beer for him on the stove. While the old man rocked the cradle, she took a seat nearby, watching her sick child, who continued to breathe heavily, gently holding its tiny hand.

"You believe I will keep him, don’t you?" she said. "Surely our all-merciful God won't take him away from me."

The old man, who was actually Death himself, nodded in a way that was strange enough to mean either yes or no. The mother lowered her gaze, tears rolling down her cheeks. Exhausted from not sleeping for three days and nights, she dozed off for just a moment. Startled by the cold, she woke up and looked around the room. The old man was gone, and so was her child! He had taken the child with him. As the old clock in the corner began to chime, the chains rattled, the heavy weight fell to the floor, and the clock stopped. The poor mother dashed out of the house, calling for her child. Outside in the snow, there sat a woman dressed in long black garments. She told the mother, "Death has been in your home. I saw him hurriedly leaving with your little child; he moves faster than the wind and never brings back what he has taken."

"Just tell me which way he went," pleaded the mother. "Please, let me know the direction; I will find him."

title: THE STORY OF A MOTHER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I know the way," replied the woman in black. "But first, you must sing to me all the lullabies you sang to your child; I love those songs, and I have heard them before. I am Night, and I saw the tears flow as you sang."

"I will sing them all for you," the mother responded. "But please don't delay me now. I must catch up to him and find my child."

Yet, Night remained silent and still. The mother cried and sang, wringing her hands. So many songs poured out, along with even more tears, until finally Night spoke: "Go to the right and into the dark fir forest; I saw Death take that path with your child."

As she entered the woods, the mother approached a crossroads and was unsure of which way to go. Nearby stood a thorn-bush, bare of leaves and flowers, with icicles hanging from its branches in the frigid winter air. "Have you seen Death pass by with my little child?" she asked.

"Yes," said the thorn-bush, "but I won't tell you which way he went until you warm me in your arms. I'm freezing to death, turning to ice."

So, she pressed the thornbush against her chest to thaw it, but the thorns pierced her flesh, and blood dripped down. Yet, the thornbush burst into fresh green leaves and blossomed into flowers on that cold winter night; that's how warm a grieving mother's heart can be. Then the thornbush revealed the path she needed to take. Eventually, she reached a vast lake, where neither ship nor boat was in sight. The ice wasn't thick enough for her to walk on, nor was the water shallow enough for her to wade through, but she needed to cross it to find her child. Desperate, she laid down, thinking maybe she could somehow drink up the water, even though that was impossible for any human. But the sorrowful mother hoped for a miracle. "You won't succeed in that," said the lake. "Let's make a deal instead. I collect pearls, and your eyes are the purest I've ever seen. If you will weep all your tears into my waters, I will take you to the large greenhouse where Death cultivates flowers and trees, each representing a human life."

title: THE STORY OF A MOTHER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Oh, what wouldn’t I give to see my child again!" cried the mother as she continued to weep, her tears falling into the depths of the lake, turning into two precious pearls.

Then, the lake lifted her and carried her to the other side as if she were on a swing, arriving at a remarkable structure that seemed to stretch on for miles. No one could tell if it was a mountain filled with forests and caves or a grand building. But the mother couldn’t see anything clearly since she had wept her eyes into the lake. "Where can I find Death, who took my child away?" she asked.

"He hasn’t arrived yet," said an old gray-haired woman, busy watering Death’s greenhouse. "How did you make it here? Who guided you?"

"God has helped me," the mother answered. "He is kind; won’t you be kind as well? Where can I find my little child?"

"I don't know the child," said the old woman, "and you are blind. Many flowers and trees have wilted tonight, and Death will soon come to transplant them. You know every person has a life-tree or a life-flower, just like what’s meant for them. They look like other plants, but they have hearts that beat. Children’s hearts beat too; maybe you can recognize your child that way. But what will you give me if I tell you what you must do next?"

"I have nothing to offer," said the grieving mother, "but I would travel to the ends of the earth for you."

"I can't ask for anything else from you there," replied the old woman. "But you can give me your long, beautiful hair. You know it’s lovely, and I admire it. In exchange, you can take my white hair, which will be something in return."

"Is that all you ask for?" she said. "I’ll gladly give it to you."

title: THE STORY OF A MOTHER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
So, she traded her beautiful hair for the old woman’s white locks. Then they entered Death's extensive greenhouse, where flowers and trees grew in stunning abundance. There were hyacinths blooming under glass domes and peonies that looked like sturdy trees. Water plants, some healthy and others wilted, were wrapped around by water-snakes, while dark crabs clung to their stems. Towering palm trees, oaks, and plantains stood tall, surrounded by blooming thyme and parsley. Every tree and flower had a name that represented a human life, belonging to people still living, some in China, others in Greenland, and every corner of the globe. Some large trees were stuck in small pots, seeming cramped and ready to burst, while many delicate flowers thrived in rich soil, surrounded by moss, carefully tended and cherished. The sorrowful mother leaned down toward the tiny plants and could hear the heartbeats within each, recognizing her child’s heartbeat among millions of others.

"That’s it!" she shouted, reaching out for a little crocus flower that hung its head weakly.

"Do not touch the flower," warned the old woman. "Stand over here. When Death arrives—I expect him at any moment—don’t let him uproot that plant. Threaten him that if he does, you'll harm the other flowers in the same way. This will frighten him; he must answer to God for each one. No plant can be uprooted unless he gets permission."

An icy chill swept through the greenhouse, and the blind mother sensed that Death had come.

"How did you find your way here?" he asked. "How could you arrive faster than I did?"

Death reached out for the delicate flower, but she clasped her hands around it tightly, holding it with utmost care to avoid touching any of its leaves. Death breathed down upon her hands, and she felt his breath colder than the winter wind, making her hands weaken.

"You can’t defeat me," said Death.

"I only carry out His wishes," Death replied. "I’m His gardener. I take all His flowers and trees and transplant them into the gardens of Paradise in an unknown land. I can't describe how they flourish or what that garden looks like."

title: THE STORY OF A MOTHER
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Please, give me back my child!" the mother begged, crying. She grabbed two stunning flowers and shouted at Death, "I’ll uproot all your flowers; I'm in despair!"

"Don’t touch them," said Death. "You claim to be unhappy; would you make another mother feel as miserable as you do?"

"Another mother!" cried the distraught woman, releasing the flowers from her grasp.

"Here are your eyes," said Death. "I fished them out of the lake for you. They were shining brightly, but I didn’t realize they were yours. Take them back—they are clearer now than before—and look into the well nearby. I’ll tell you the names of the two flowers you wanted to pull up, and you will see the future of the people they represent, giving you a glimpse of what you were about to destroy."

So, she peered into the well, and it was a beautiful sight to see how one flower brought blessings to the world while spreading happiness and joy. Yet, she saw that the other flower’s life was filled with struggle, poverty, pain, and sorrow.

"Which flower is the one that suffers, and which one brings happiness?" she asked.

"I can’t tell you that," said Death. "But you may learn this much: one of those flowers represents your own child. You’re witnessing the destiny of your child—their future."

Frantic, the mother cried out in fear, "Which one belongs to my child? Please, tell me. Free the unhappy child from all this misery. I'd rather it be taken away. Take it into the Kingdom of God! Forget my tears and pleas; forget everything I’ve said or done."

"I don’t understand," said Death. "Do you want your child back, or should I take them to a place you don’t know?"

Then the mother wrung her hands, fell to her knees, and prayed to God, "Do not grant my wishes if they go against Your will, which is always for the best. Oh, ignore my requests!" And her head hung low on her chest.

Then, Death carried her child away to the unknown land.

title: THE STORY OF THE YEAR
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
As January was winding down, a heavy snowstorm was blasting the streets, swirling around in chaotic gusts. Snow piled up on the windows, clinging to them like frosting, and huge clumps fell from rooftops. Everyone was rushing about, darting through the flurries, sometimes bumping into one another and clinging together just to steady themselves. Coaches and horses looked like they’d been dipped in sugar. The footmen braced themselves against the carriages, turning away from the biting wind. Pedestrians used the carriages as shields, trudging slowly through the deep snow. Finally, when the storm eased, a narrow path was cleared in front of the houses. When two people met on this path, they hesitated, both reluctant to step into the deeper snow to let the other pass. They stood there, silent and still, until they silently decided to sacrifice one leg each and plunge it into the snow. By evening, the weather calmed down. The sky, now free from snow, appeared clearer and higher, while the stars shone with renewed brightness. The frozen snow crunched underfoot, firm enough for the sparrows, who flitted about in the morning light. They searched the cleared path for food, but found barely anything, and they were freezing. One chirped to another, "Tweet, tweet; they call this a new year, but honestly, I think it’s worse than the last. We might as well have stuck with the old year. I have every reason to feel unhappy."

"You’re right about that," chimed in a tiny shivering sparrow. "Yet people are running around, shooting off guns to celebrate the new year. They throw things at doors and act like they’re overjoyed that the old year is gone. I was happy too because I thought we might finally have some warm days ahead, but my hopes have gone up in smoke. It’s colder than ever; I think humans have messed up their sense of time."

"You’re totally right," said another sparrow, an older one with a frosty white head. "They created something called a calendar. It’s their own invention, but it doesn’t work. The year should actually start when spring arrives. That’s what nature says, and I go by that."

title: THE STORY OF THE YEAR
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"But when is spring coming?" asked the others.

"It will be here when the stork returns, but he’s pretty unreliable, and no one in town knows when that will happen. The people in the countryside know more about it; should we fly there and wait? We’d be closer to spring for sure."

"That sounds nice," said another sparrow who had been hopping around and chirping but hadn’t really said anything important. "But I’ve found a few comforts here in town that I might miss in the countryside. There’s a family nearby who has sensibly placed several flower pots against their wall, the openings turned inside. The holes are big enough for me to come and go. My partner and I built our nest in one of those pots, and all our chicks, who have since flown away, were raised there. The people set this up so they could enjoy seeing us, or they wouldn’t have bothered. They even sprinkle bread crumbs for us, so we have food, and we feel taken care of. So I think my partner and I will stick around, even though we’re not completely happy."

"And we’re heading out to the country," the others said, "to see if spring is on its way." And off they flew.

In the countryside, it truly felt like winter, a few degrees colder than in town. The sharp winds whipped across the snow-covered fields. A farmer, bundled up in warm clothes, sat in his sleigh, crossing his arms to fend off the cold. The whip rested on his lap. The horses ran hard, kicking up clouds of steam. The snow crackled as the sparrows flitted around in the wheel tracks, shivering as they cried out, "Tweet, tweet; when will spring arrive? It seems to be taking forever."

"Taking forever indeed," echoed a voice from the nearest snow-covered hill. It could have been the echo people hear, or perhaps it was the voice of an extraordinary old man, sitting atop a pile of snow, unfazed by the wind and the weather. He was dressed in a coarse white coat, with long white hair, a pale face, and large, clear blue eyes. "Who is that old man?" asked the sparrows.

title: THE STORY OF THE YEAR
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I know who he is," said an old raven perched on the fence, taking a rare moment to acknowledge that even little birds were equal in the eyes of Heaven, which is why he spoke to the sparrows and shared the information they sought. "That old man is Winter, the guardian of last year. He isn't dead, despite what the calendar says; he’s watching over young Prince Spring who is on his way. Winter is still in charge around here. Ugh! Doesn’t the cold make you shiver, little ones?"

"See? I told you!" piped up the smallest sparrow. "The calendar is just a human invention, and it doesn’t align with nature. They need to leave these matters to us; we’ve been created much smarter than they are."

A week passed, then another. The forest felt dark, the frozen lake lay still as a sheet of lead. The mountains had faded away, cloaked in misty, icy dampness. Large black crows flew silently around; it was as if nature were taking a deep sleep. Finally, a sunbeam glided over the lake, making it shine like polished silver. But the snow on the fields and hills didn’t sparkle as it once did. The white figure of Winter remained there, with his gaze unfocused on the south. He didn’t notice that the snowy blanket seemed to sink into the earth; little patches of green grass began appearing here and there, and those spots were sprinkled with sparrows.

"Tee-wit, tee-wit; is spring finally coming?"

Spring! The cry echoed across the fields, meadows, and through the dark-brown woods, where the vibrant green moss still glowed on the trees’ trunks. From the south flew the first two storks, each carrying a lovely child—a boy and a girl. They greeted the earth with a kiss, and wherever they stepped, white flowers sprang up from beneath the snow. Hand in hand, they approached the old Winter man, embraced him, and held him close; and in that instant, all three were enveloped in a thick, damp mist that wrapped around them like a veil. The wind roared with a mighty rustle, clearing away the mist, and then the sun burst through warmly. Winter vanished, and the lovely children of Spring took their place as the rulers of the year.

title: THE STORY OF THE YEAR
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Now this is truly a new year!" cried the sparrows. "We’ll finally get what’s ours and have some reward for all we endured during winter."

Wherever the two children roamed, green buds burst forth on bushes and trees. The grass spread higher, and the cornfields transformed into a lovely delicate green.

The little girl scattered flowers in her path, holding her apron wide open—it brimmed with blooms as if new flowers had sprung to life just for her. The more she scattered around, the more flowers her apron seemed to hold. Excitedly, she sprinkled snowy blossoms over apple and peach trees, making them look stunning even before their green leaves had fully unfurled. Then the boy and girl clapped their hands, and flocks of birds flew in from nowhere, chirping and singing, "Spring has arrived!" Everything looked beautiful! Many an old woman emerged from her home into the sunlight, shuffling and grinning at the golden flowers that sparkled across the fields as they had in her youth. The world felt fresh and young again as she remarked, "What a wonderful day to be out here!" The forest was already adorning itself in dark green buds. The thyme released its fresh scent, primroses and anemones burst forth, and violets bloomed in the shade, while each blade of grass was filled with energy and life. Who could resist sitting down on such a beautiful carpet? Then the young children of Spring sat together, holding hands, singing, laughing, and growing. A soft rain began to fall from the sky, but they were unaware of it, as the raindrops turned into joyful tears of happiness. They kissed each other and were engaged; at that moment, the buds on the trees unfurled, and when the sun rose, the forest transformed into a vibrant green. Hand in hand, the two wandered under the new leafy canopy, with the sun’s rays filtering through the gaps, casting shimmering hues all around. The fresh young leaves filled the air with delightful scents. The clear brooks and streams gurgled merrily between the lush green rushes and colorful pebbles below. Nature spoke of abundance everywhere. The cuckoo sang, and the lark chirped happily; it was indeed beautiful spring.

title: THE STORY OF THE YEAR
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: However, the careful willows had cautiously covered their blossoms with woolly gloves, and that watchfulness could be a bit tiresome. Days and weeks flew by, and the heat intensified. Warm breezes swayed the golden corn as it ripened in the sun. The elegant northern lily spread its broad green leaves over the clear woodland lake, where fish sought shade underneath. In a sheltered corner of the woods, the sun illuminated the walls of a farmhouse, enhancing the blooming roses and ripening the dark, juicy berries hanging off the cherry trees. Here sat the beautiful Summer lady, the same one we had seen as a child and as a bride; her gaze was fixed on dark clouds that were accumulating in swirling shapes of black and deep blue like towering mountains. They came from all directions, steadily building up like an incoming wave. Then they swooped down toward the forest, where every sound was silenced as if by magic, every breath held, and every bird went mute. Nature stood still, holding its breath. Meanwhile, along the lanes and roads, pedestrians and carriages hurried to find shelter. Then came a flash of light, as if the sun had burst forth from the sky in a flaming rush, and darkness returned with a rumble of thunder. The rain flooded down in sheets—darkness, then blinding light—now a thrilling silence, and then a deafening roar. The brown reeds on the moor swayed in the stormy wind; the branches of the forest swirled in a watery mist, as light and darkness continued to chase each other, with silence following the thunderous sounds, while the corn and grass were battered down and soaked, seemingly overwhelmed and unable to rise again. But soon the rain softened to a gentle fall, the sun's rays pierced through the clouds, and water droplets sparkled like pearls on leaves and stems. The birds sang, the fish jumped up to the water's surface, the gnats twirled in the sunshine, and there, on a rock by the rolling salt sea, sat Summer himself. He was a strong man with sturdy limbs and long, wet hair. Refreshed from his cool bath, he basked in the warm sunshine while all around him, nature bloomed anew, robust, and stunning; it was summer, beautifully warm summer.

title: THE STORY OF THE YEAR
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The fragrance wafting from the clover fields was sweet and inviting, where bees buzzed around the decaying tower. Brambles twisted themselves around the old altar, which sparkled in the sunshine after the rain; the queen bee flew in with her swarm to prepare wax and honey. But Summer and his lovely companion viewed this scene differently; to them, the altar was covered in nature's bountiful offerings. The evening sky shimmered like gold; no church dome could ever gleam as brilliantly, and between the golden glow of sunset and the rosy dawn, the moon illuminated the world. This was indeed summer. And days and weeks passed by, with the bright scythes of the harvesters shining in the cornfields, the branches of apple trees bending low under the weight of red and golden fruit, and the hop vines hung in fragrant clusters. Beneath the hazel bushes, where nuts grew in great quantities, a man and woman rested—Summer and his serious bride.

"Look at all this wealth, the blessings surrounding us!" she exclaimed. "Everything is cozy and good, but even so, I can't shake this feeling of longing for peace. I can hardly express what I feel. They are already plowing the fields again; people desire more and more gain. Look, the storks are gathering and trailing behind the plow. They are the birds from Egypt, who brought us through the skies. Do you remember when we were children coming to this northern land? We brought flowers, sunshine, and greenery, but the winds have been harsh, and now everything has grown dark and brown like the southern trees—but they don’t bear golden fruit like the others."

"Do you really want to see golden fruit?" asked the man. "Then rejoice!" he said, lifting his arm. The leaves of the forest turned vibrant shades of red and gold, bright colors splashed across the woodlands. The rose bushes glittered with scarlet hips, and the elderberry branches hung low with dark, full berries. The wild chestnuts dropped ripe from their green shells, and violets bloomed again in the woods. Yet still, the queen of the year became quieter and more pale.

title: THE STORY OF THE YEAR
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"It’s getting cold," she said. "The nights bring damp mist; I long for my homeland." As she watched all the storks take off, she reached out her hands toward them. She gazed at the empty nests; in one of them grew a tall stalk of cornflower, while in another was mustard seed, as if the nests had been placed there just for their comfort and protection, with sparrows fluttering around them.

"Tweet, where did the master of the nest go?" cried one. "I guess he couldn't handle it when the wind blew, so he left this country. I wish him a pleasant trip!"

The forest's leaves turned more and more yellow; leaf by leaf fell down as the autumn winds howled. The year was now deep into its cycle, and lying on the fallen yellow leaves was the queen of the year, gazing up with gentle eyes at a shining star, while her husband stood beside her. A gust of wind swept through the foliage, and the leaves showered down. The summer queen was gone, but the last butterfly of the year fluttered through the chilly air. Wet fogs rolled in, icy winds blew, and the long dark nights of winter approached. The ruler of the year appeared with hair as white as snow, but he didn't realize it; he thought the snowflakes that fell from the sky were just covering his head, like they cloaked the green fields with a thin veil of white snow. And then, church bells rang out to mark Christmas.

"The bells are ringing for the new-born year," said the ruler. "Soon a new ruler and his bride will be born, and I shall find rest with my wife among yonder twinkling stars."

In the fresh green fir wood, where the snow blanketed the ground, stood the Christmas angel, blessing the young trees that would adorn his celebration.

"May joy fill the rooms beneath the green boughs!" exclaimed the aged ruler of the year. In a matter of weeks, he had aged into a frail old man with hair as white as snow. "My time of rest is approaching; soon the young pair of the year will claim my crown and scepter."

title: THE STORY OF THE YEAR
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"But the night still belongs to you," replied the Christmas angel, "for power, but not for repose. Let the snow lie gently upon the tender seeds. Learn to endure the thought that another is adored while you still reign. Learn to tolerate being forgotten while still alive. Your moment of freedom will come when Spring arrives."

"And when will Spring arrive?" Winter asked.

With his snowy locks and beard, cold and bent but as strong as a winter storm, old Winter sat on a snowdrift-covered hill, looking toward the south, where he had sat before, staring longingly. The ice sparkled; the snow crunched as skaters glided over the frozen lakes. Ravens and crows contrasted sharply against the white ground, and not a breeze stirred the still air. Old Winter clenched his fists as the ice lay thick between the lands. Then the sparrows returned from town and asked, "Who is that old man?" The raven remained perched, or perhaps it was his son, which was essentially the same, and he told them,

"That is Winter, the old man of last year; he isn’t dead, as the calendar claims; he is the guardian of Spring, who is on her way."

"When will Spring arrive?" asked the sparrows. "When Spring comes, we’ll have better times, a better rule. The old days are worthless now."

In quiet contemplation, old Winter gazed at the bare forest, where the elegant shapes of trees were silhouetted against the sky. While Winter slept, icy mists streamed from the clouds, and the ruler dreamed of his youth and manhood. By dawn, the entire forest shimmered with frost, which the sun shook free from the branches—this was Winter’s summer dream.

"When will Spring come?" asked the sparrows. "Spring!" The echo returned from the snowy hills. The sunshine warmed the landscape, melting the snow, and the birds chirped, "Spring is on the way!" As the first stork soared high in the sky, the second followed; a beautiful child sat on each of their backs. They landed in an open field, kissed the earth, and gave a warm hug to the quiet old man; as they did, mist drifted away from the mountaintop, and he vanished. And thus, the story of the year came to a closing.

"This is all well and good," the sparrows chirped, "and it’s undoubtedly beautiful, but it doesn’t match the calendar, so it must all be wrong."

title: THE SUNBEAM AND THE CAPTIVE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
themes: the duality of good and evil
text_chunk:
It's autumn. We're standing on the ramparts, gazing out over the sea. We spot numerous ships and the Swedish coast on the other side of the sound, rising majestically above the water that reflects the warm colors of the evening sky. Behind us, the woods are defined sharply, with towering trees all around, and yellow leaves fluttering down from the branches. Below us, at the base of the wall, there's a gloomy building surrounded by palisades. The space in between is dark and narrow, and it must be even drearier behind the iron grates that cover the small loopholes or windows, where the most hardened criminals are locked away. A ray of the setting sun breaks through into the stark cells of one of the captives, shining for both the good and the wicked. The hardened criminal shoots an annoyed glance at the bright ray. Then, a little bird flits toward the grating, chirping happily for both the just and the unjust. It simply sings, "Tweet, tweet," before settling near the bars, fluttering its wings, plucking a feather from its body, puffing itself up, and fluffing its feathers around its chest and throat. The chained man watches the bird, and a softer look crosses his usually hardened face. A thought stirs within him that he can’t quite grasp, but it feels connected to the sunbeam, the bird, and the scent of violets that bloom abundantly in spring at the base of the wall. Just then, the cheerful sound of the hunter's horn fills the air. The little bird takes flight, the sunbeam slowly fades away, and once again, darkness falls in both the room and the heart of that troubled man. But for a moment, the sun has touched his heart, and the bird's song has reached him.

Play on, you glorious notes of the hunter's horn; keep your lively melody going, for the evening is gentle, and the sea, gently swaying and calm, is as smooth as a mirror.

title: THE SWAN'S NEST
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Nestled between the Baltic Sea and the North Sea, there’s an ancient swan's nest, a place where swans are born and have always been born, and they will never fade away.

Long ago, a flock of swans flew over the Alps, landing in the lush plains near Milan, a truly enchanting place to live. These swans were known to people as the Lombards.

Another group, with glossy feathers and sincere eyes, glided south to Byzantium. There, they took up residence near the Emperor’s throne, spreading their wings like shields to protect him. They became known as the Varangians.

On the coast of France, a cry of fear echoed, for blood-stained swans arrived from the North, carrying fire in their wake. The people prayed, "Heaven, save us from the savage Northmen."

On the bright grass of England, the Danish swan stood proud on the open shore, wearing the crown of three kingdoms on his head. He raised his golden scepter over the land, and the heathens on the Pomeranian coast knelt before him as the Danish swans approached with the Cross banner and drawn swords.

"That was a long time ago," you might say.

In more recent times, two mighty swans have been seen soaring from the nest. A brilliant light shined far into the sky, over the lands of the earth. With powerful wingbeats, one swan cleared the twilight mists, revealing a starry sky that felt closer to the earth. This was the swan Tycho Brahe.

"Yes, but what about our current times?" you ask.

We've witnessed one swan after another soaring beautifully through the skies. One glided its wings over the strings of a golden harp, sending melodies echoing through the North. Norway’s mountains seemed to rise higher in the golden sunlight of yesteryear; rustling among the pine trees and birches revealed the gods, heroes, and noble women of the North in the deep forest shadows.

We’ve seen a swan strike the marble crag with its wings, causing the stone to shatter and release magnificent forms of beauty into the sunshine, making people around the lands look up in awe.

We’ve also seen a third swan weaving the thread of thought that connects lands around the globe, allowing words to travel with lightning speed from one nation to another.

And our Lord cherishes the ancient swan's nest between the Baltic and the North Sea. Even when fierce birds come rushing to harm it, the young ones gather bravely around the edge of the nest. Though they might be hurt and bleed, they endure and fight back with their wings and claws.

Centuries will pass, swans will fly from the nest, and people will see and hear them throughout the world, long before it will ever be said in spirit and truth, "This is the last swan—the final song from the swan's nest."

title: THE SWINEHERD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a poor prince. His kingdom was small, but it was just big enough for him to think about marriage, and he was determined to marry. Boldly, he approached the emperor's daughter and asked, "Will you marry me?" His name was known far and wide, and many princesses would have gladly accepted him. But would she?

On his father's grave grew a rose tree, the most stunning of its kind. It bloomed only once every five years, offering just a single rose, but what a rose it was! Its sweet fragrance made anyone who inhaled it forget all their sorrow and sadness. He also had a nightingale that could sing as if every beautiful melody was in its throat. He wanted to give both the rose and the nightingale to the princess, so they were placed in large silver cases and sent to her.

The emperor ordered the gifts to be brought into the grand hall where the princess was playing "Visitors are coming" with her ladies-in-waiting. When she saw the large cases holding the gifts, she clapped her hands in delight.

"I wish it were a little cat," she said. But then the rose tree with the beautiful bloom was unpacked.

"Oh, how beautifully made it is!" exclaimed the ladies.

"It's more than just beautiful," said the emperor, "it's charming."

The princess touched it and nearly started to cry.

"How shameful, Father!" she said. "This isn’t artificial; it’s natural!"

"How shameful, it’s natural!" echoed all her ladies.

"Let's first see what the other case holds before we get upset," said the emperor. Then, when the nightingale was revealed, it sang so beautifully that no one could say anything negative about it.

"Superb, charming," said the courtiers, all of whom chatted away in French, with some speaking more poorly than others.

"That bird reminds me of the late empress's music box," said an old courtier. "It has the same tone, the same skill."

title: THE SWINEHERD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"You're right," the emperor agreed, starting to cry like a small child.

"I hope this isn’t natural," said the princess.

"Yes, it’s definitely natural," replied those who brought the gifts.

"Then let it fly, I refuse to see the prince," said the princess.

But the prince wasn’t discouraged. He painted his face, donned ordinary clothes, pulled his cap low over his forehead, and returned.

"Good day, emperor," he said. "Could you offer me some work at the court?"

"There are so many applying for positions that I currently have no openings, but I’ll keep you in mind. However, I do need someone to take care of my pigs, as I have a lot of them."

So, the prince became the emperor's swineherd and lived in a tiny room next to the pigsty; he worked all day long, and at night, he crafted a lovely pot. It had small bells around the rim, and when the water boiled, the bells played an old tune:

"A jolly old sow once lived in a sty, Three little piggies had she," and so on.

But what was truly amazing was that, when someone dipped a finger into the steam rising from the pot, they could instantly smell whatever meal was cooking on every stovetop in the entire town. That was far more remarkable than the rose. When the princess passed by with her ladies and heard the tune, she stopped and smiled, as it was the only tune she could play, and she played it with one finger.

"That’s the tune I know!" she exclaimed. "He must be a talented swineherd. Go find out how much the instrument costs."

One of the ladies was sent to ask, but she wore platform shoes.

"What do you want for your pot?" asked the lady.

"I want ten kisses from the princess," said the swineherd.

"Well, I can't sell it for less," replied the swineherd.

"I really can’t tell you," replied the lady.

title: THE SWINEHERD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"That’s very cheeky," said the princess, and walked away.

But as she walked a little further, the bells chimed again sweetly:

"A jolly old sow once lived in a sty, Three little piggies had she," and so on.

"Ask him if he’ll settle for ten kisses from one of my ladies," said the princess.

"No, thank you," replied the swineherd. "Ten kisses from the princess, or I’ll keep my pot."

"That's so annoying," said the princess. "But you have to stand in front of me, so no one else can see it."

The ladies gathered in front of her, spread out their gowns, and she gave the swineherd ten kisses in exchange for the pot.

What a delight that was! Day and night, the water in the pot boiled away; they knew what every fire in town was cooking, from the chamberlain’s to the shoemaker's. The ladies danced and clapped their hands in excitement.

"We know who's having soup and pancakes! We know who's having porridge and cutlets! Oh, how fascinating!"

"Very fascinating, indeed," said the housekeeper. "But you mustn't tell anyone, for I'm the emperor's daughter."

The swineherd—that is to say, the prince—though they thought he was just a real swineherd, didn’t waste any day without creating something. He made a rattle that, when spun quickly, played all the waltzes, galops, and polkas known since the beginning of time.

"That’s incredible," said the princess when she passed by. "I’ve never heard a more beautiful piece. Go down and find out what it costs; but I won’t kiss him again."

"He wants a hundred kisses from the princess," said the lady who went down to ask.

"I think he's lost his mind," said the princess, walking away. But soon she paused. "Art must be encouraged," she said. "I am the emperor's daughter! Tell him I’ll give him ten kisses, as I did before; the rest can come from one of my ladies."

title: THE SWINEHERD
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"But we don’t want to kiss him," said the ladies.

"That's nonsense," the princess said. "If I can kiss him, you can too. Remember, I provide you with food and jobs." And the lady had to go down once more.

"A hundred kisses from the princess," the swineherd insisted, "or everyone keeps their own."

"Stand before me," said the princess, and they did as she commanded. She kissed him.

"What’s that crowd near the pigsty about?" the emperor said, just stepping out onto his balcony. He rubbed his eyes and put on his glasses.

"The ladies of the court seem to be up to something mischievous. I’ll have to check it out." He quickly put on his shoes, which had fallen down at the heels. When he reached the courtyard, he tiptoed, and the ladies were so caught up in counting the kisses to be fair that they didn’t notice the emperor. He stood on tiptoe.

"What’s going on here?" he asked upon seeing his daughter kissing the swineherd, just as the swineherd received his sixty-eighth kiss.

"Get out of my sight!" said the emperor, who was furious; and both the princess and the swineherd were banished from the empire. There she stood, crying, while the swineherd scolded her, and the rain poured down.

"Alas, what a miserable creature I am!" the princess lamented. "I wish I had accepted the prince. Oh, how wretched I am!"

The swineherd stepped behind a tree, wiped his face, shed his shabby clothes, and revealed his princely attire; he looked so handsome that the princess couldn’t help but bow to him.

"Now I’ve learned to disdain you," he said. "You rejected an honest prince; you didn’t appreciate the rose and the nightingale, yet you had no issue kissing a swineherd for his toys. You have no one to blame but yourself!"

Then he went back to his kingdom, leaving her behind. Now she was free to sing at her leisure:

"A jolly old sow once lived in a sty, Three little piggies had she," and so on.

title: THE THISTLE'S EXPERIENCES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The lordly manor had a stunning, well-kept garden filled with rare trees and flowers. Guests often praised its beauty, and people from nearby towns and villages would come on Sundays and holidays to ask for permission to visit. Even entire schools made trips to enjoy the garden.

Outside the garden, by the fence along the road, stood a large, mighty Thistle, sprawling out from its root, almost like a thistle bush. No one paid it any attention, except for an old donkey that pulled the milkmaid’s cart. This donkey would stretch his neck toward the Thistle and say, "You’re beautiful; I’d love to eat you!" But his tether was too short to reach it.

There was quite a gathering at the manor, with some very noble guests from the capital; among them were young, pretty girls, including one young lady who had traveled from far away. She hailed from Scotland and was of high birth, rich in both lands and gold—a bride worth winning, according to more than one young gentleman, which their mothers echoed as well.

The young people were enjoying themselves on the lawn, playing ball and wandering among the flowers. Each girl picked a blossom to pin in the buttonhole of a young gentleman. However, the young Scottish lady was indecisive and looked around for a long time. None of the flowers caught her eye, until she spotted the big thistle bush outside with its sturdy, reddish-blue flowers. She smiled and asked the son of the house to pick one for her.

"That’s the flower of Scotland," she said. "It blooms in the emblem of my country. Please give me that flower over there."

He picked the finest blossom, oblivious to the fact that he pricked his fingers as thoroughly as if he had touched the sharpest rose bush.

She placed the thistle flower in the young man’s buttonhole, and he felt incredibly honored. Each of the other young men would have been pleased to give up their own beautiful flower to wear this one, gifted by the fair hand of the Scottish maiden. If the son of the house felt honored, what must the Thistle bush have felt? It was as if dew and sunshine were streaming through it.

title: THE THISTLE'S EXPERIENCES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I’m a lot more special than I realized," the Thistle thought to itself. "I must belong inside the fence, not outside. It’s funny how life places us in unexpected spots; but now I’ve actually managed to get one of my own people inside the fence, and into a buttonhole!"

The Thistle eagerly shared this event with every flower that bloomed, and not long after, it wasn't just people or the chirping birds it heard from; the very air around it carried the news from the most hidden corners of the garden and the open windows of the manor. The young gentleman who had received the thistle flower from the fair Scottish lady had also accepted her heart and hand. They looked like a beautiful couple—it was a match made in heaven.

"I made that match happen!" said the Thistle, thinking fondly of the flower he had given for the buttonhole. Every flower that bloomed heard about this occurrence.

"I’m definitely going to be moved into the garden," thought the Thistle, "maybe even put into a pot, which is considered quite an honor."

It imagined this so vividly that it finally said with conviction, "I’m going to be placed in a pot."

Then, it promised every little thistle flower that bloomed that they, too, would find themselves inside a pot and maybe even in a buttonhole, the highest honor one could achieve. But none of them ended up in a pot, let alone a buttonhole. They soaked up sunlight and fresh air, living off the sunlight by day and the dew by night, flourishing as bees and hornets visited, attracted by the flower’s nectar, taking it away and leaving the blossoms where they were.

"Those thieving pests!" the Thistle exclaimed. "If only I could jab every single one of them! But I can’t."

The flowers hung their heads and withered, but eventually, new ones appeared.

"You’ve arrived just in time," said the Thistle. "I’ve been expecting to leap across the fence at any moment."

A few innocent daisies and a tall dandelion listened in silent awe, believing everything they heard.

title: THE THISTLE'S EXPERIENCES
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The old donkey from the milk cart stood at the edge of the dirt road, gazing at the blooming thistle bush, but its tether was too short for it to reach.

The Thistle pondered for so long about the thistle from Scotland, claiming kinship with it, that it eventually believed it had originated from Scotland, dreaming that its ancestors were represented in the national emblem. It was a big thought; after all, a great thistle is entitled to grand ideas.

"You know, sometimes one belongs to a prestigious family without even being aware of it," the Nettle, who grew nearby, commented. He entertained the notion that he could be made into fine linen if treated well.

Summer passed, and autumn arrived. The leaves fell from the trees, and the few remaining flowers grew deeper in color but lost their fragrance. The gardener's boy sang happily in the garden, his voice carried over the fence:

"Up the hill, down the dale we travel, That’s life, from start to finish."

The young fir trees in the forest began to look forward to Christmas, which still felt far away.

"I’m still here!" the Thistle said. "It feels as if no one thinks of me, yet I orchestrated the match. They’ve been engaged and have already married; it’s been a week. I won’t take a single step—because I can’t."

A few more weeks passed. The Thistle stood tall with its last single flower, robust and full. This flower sprouted from deep in the roots; the cold wind swept over it, fading its colors, yet it grew larger, resembling a silvery sunflower.

One day, the newlywed couple strolled into the garden. They walked along the fence, and the young wife looked over.

"Look at that big thistle still growing," she commented. "It doesn’t have any flowers now."

"Oh, yes, the ghost of the last one is still there," he replied, pointing to the silvery remnants of the flower that looked like a flower itself.

"It’s quite pretty," she remarked. "Such a thing should be carved on the frame of our picture."

Then, the young man had to climb over the fence again to break off the thistle's calyx. It pricked his fingers, but he had already called it a ghost. The thistle calyx joined them in the garden, then into their home, and into the drawing-room. There, a painting titled "Young Couple" displayed a thistle flower in the bridegroom’s buttonhole. They talked about it and the thistle flower they brought, the last thistle flower glimmering like silver, whose picture was engraved on the frame.

The breeze carried their words far away.

"What a life I’ve led!" exclaimed the Thistle Bush. "My firstborn was placed in a buttonhole, and my youngest has been framed. Where will I go next?"

Meanwhile, the donkey stood by the side of the road, gazing at the Thistle.

"Come to me, my nibbly darling!" he called. "I can’t reach you."

But the Thistle did not respond. It grew more and more contemplative, thinking until near Christmas, when finally a thought-flower blossomed.

"If the children are good, the parents don’t mind standing outside the garden fence."

"That’s an honorable thought," said the Sunbeam. "You deserve a good place too."

"In a pot or in a frame?" asked the Thistle.

title: THE THORNY ROAD OF HONOR
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
There's an old story about the "Thorny Road of Honor," featuring a marksman who achieved great status and respect, but only after battling through a lifetime of challenges. When we read this tale, who doesn't relate it to their own struggles and countless difficulties? The story feels quite real; however, it also offers a sense of resolution here on Earth, while life’s reality often leads us to think about what lies beyond our earthly existence. The history of humanity is like a magic lantern, illuminating the dark moments of the present with snapshots of the great benefactors, the heroes of creativity, who traveled along the thorny road of honor.

Throughout history and across the globe, these brilliant snapshots appear before us. Each one lasts only a brief moment, yet each tells a story of a lifetime, filled with struggles and triumphs. Let's take a moment to reflect on one of these heroes—the kind of individuals who will continue to inspire even until the end of time.

We gaze down at a packed amphitheater. From Aristophanes’ "Clouds," satire and humor rain down upon the audience; on stage, Socrates—the most extraordinary man in Athens, who defended the people against the thirty tyrants—is mocked and ridiculed in a way that’s both physical and mental. Socrates, who once saved Alcibiades and Xenophon in battle, stands proud, towering over the crowd, inviting the laughing Athenians to notice just how similar he is to the caricature they see.

Oh, poisonous hemlock, cast your shadow over Athens—but not you, olive tree of glory!

Seven cities fought over the title of Homer's birthplace—though they only did so after his death! Let's take a look at him during his lifetime. He wandered through the cities on foot, reciting his poems to earn a living, his worries about tomorrow turning his hair gray! This legendary seer, blind and struggling, faced the sharp thorns that tore at the cloak of the king of poets. Yet, his words endure, allowing us to remember the heroes and gods of old.

Images flash before us from the east and the west, separated by time and distance, yet all a part of the thorny road of honor. Here, a thistle blooms, but only as a decoration for a grave.

title: THE THORNY ROAD OF HONOR
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Under the palm trees, camels laden with indigo and other treasures cross paths, sent by the ruler of the land to the one whose songs bring joy to the people—the pride of the nation. The caravan is approaching the little town where their artist, driven to exile by envy and deceit, has been hiding. As a funeral procession emerges from the town gate, the caravan comes to a stop. The deceased is the very person they've been searching for—Firdusi—who traveled the Thorny road of honor right to the end.

A man with strong features, thick lips, and curly hair sits on the marble steps of a palace in Portugal, begging. He is the loyal servant of Camoens, and without his help, along with the small coins given to him by passersby, his master—the author of the "Lusiad"—would face starvation. Now, a grand monument marks Camoens' grave.

Behind the iron bars, a pale man with a long, unkempt beard appears.

"I’ve made a breakthrough," he exclaims. "The greatest discovery in centuries, and they’ve kept me locked up for more than twenty years!"

"Just a madman," retorts the madhouse guard. "What silly ideas these lunatics have! He thinks you can power things with steam."

This is Solomon de Cares, the inventor of the steam engine, whose theory, conveyed through confusing words, goes unrecognized by Richelieu, leading to his tragic end in the asylum.

Now, here’s Columbus, once chased and mocked by street kids for dreaming of discovering a new world; and now he has made that dream a reality. Cheers erupt from everyone, bells ring to celebrate his glorious return, but soon, the sound of envy drowns it out. The man who uncovered a new world—who presented the riches of America to his king—was rewarded with chains. He wishes these chains to be placed in his coffin, a testament to how society rewards its true heroes.

Another image emerges; the thorny path of honor and fame is overflowing.

In the darkness, a man sits—Galileo, who measured mountains on the moon and navigated limitless space among stars and planets. Now, blind and deaf, he’s aged, suffering from pain and neglect. He struggles to lift the very foot that once stamped the ground, crying out in despair, "Yet it moves!"

title: THE THORNY ROAD OF HONOR
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Here stands Joan of Arc—a woman full of childlike wonder, yet rich with faith and inspiration. She carries the flag in front of the fighting army, bringing victory and hope to her nation. Cheers erupt, and flames rise high; they are burning the accused witch, Joan of Arc. Yes, a future generation will mock the White Lily. Voltaire, the satirist of human thought, writes "La Pucelle."

At the Assembly in Viborg, the Danish nobles burn the king’s laws. The flames rise high, illuminating both the era and the lawgiver, casting a radiant glow on a shadowy prison tower. Inside, an old man, once ruler of three kingdoms—a friend to the common people—grows gray and frail. It is Christian the Second. His enemies have told his story. But let’s remember his improvements over twenty-seven years, as we try to forget his failings.

A ship sails away from the shores of Denmark. A man leans against the mast, casting a final glance back at the Island Hueen. It is Tycho Brahe. He lifted Denmark's name to the stars, yet was met with injury, loss, and sadness. He is setting off for a new land.

"The vault of heaven surrounds me wherever I go," he says. "What more could I want?"

And off sails the renowned Dane, the astronomer, heading to live honored and free in a foreign country.

"Yes, free—if only from the unbearable suffering of the body!" comes a sigh that echoes through time. What a powerful image! Griffenfeldt, akin to a Danish Prometheus, chained to the rocky island of Munkholm.

We find ourselves in America, alongside one of the largest rivers, where a massive crowd has gathered because it’s rumored that a ship is about to sail against the wind and weather, defying nature. The man who thinks he can crack this challenge is named Robert Fulton. The ship begins its journey, but suddenly it halts, igniting laughter, whistling, and hissing from the crowd, including even his own father.

"What arrogance! What nonsense!" the crowd cries. "It’s exactly what he deserves. Lock up that crazy fool!"

Then, out of nowhere, a tiny nail breaks loose—what had kept the machine stuck for a moment. Suddenly, the wheels begin to move again, the paddle wheels push through the water, and the ship resumes its journey. The invention shortens the distance between lands from hours to mere minutes.

Oh, human race, can you comprehend the joy that comes with that single moment of realization? That deep understanding of purpose when sorrow and every wound—even those self-inflicted—transform into strength and clarity; when discord is replaced by harmony; that instant when humanity recognizes the divine grace within one person who shares it with all?

Thus, the thorny path of honor reveals itself as glorious, wrapping the world in its light. Happy indeed is the one chosen to travel this road, to stand between the architect of the bridge and the Earth—between Providence and humanity.

The spirit of history soars through the ages, offering courage and comfort, awakening kind thoughts with dazzling images—showing us the thorny path of honor that, unlike a fairy tale, does not culminate in happiness here on Earth but stretches on into eternity.

title: THE TINDER-BOX
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
A soldier was marching along the main road, counting his steps: "Left, right—left, right." He had a knapsack slung on his back and a sword at his side; he had just returned from the wars and was heading home.

As he continued on his way, he encountered a very scary-looking old witch in the road. Her lower lip drooped all the way to her chest, and she stopped him, saying, "Good evening, soldier; you've got a fine sword and a big knapsack, and you're a real soldier. That means you can have as much money as you want."

"Thank you, old witch," replied the soldier.

"Do you see that big tree?" said the witch, pointing at one nearby. "It's hollow inside. You need to climb to the top, where you'll find a hole to let yourself down into the tree. I'll tie a rope around you so I can pull you back up when you call for me."

"But what will I do down there in the tree?" asked the soldier.

"You'll get money," she answered. "When you reach the bottom, you'll find a big hall lit by three hundred lamps. There are three doors, and the keys are in the locks, so they're easy to open. In the first chamber, you'll find a chest in the center of the room, with a dog sitting on it that has eyes as big as teacups. Don't be afraid; I'll give you my blue checked apron to spread out on the floor. Just grab the dog and put him on the apron. Then you can open the chest and take as many copper coins as you want. If you prefer silver, go into the second chamber, where there's another dog with eyes as big as mill-wheels. Put him on my apron, and you can take all the silver you wish. If you want gold, enter the third chamber, where there's a chest full of it. The dog on this chest is truly frightening; his eyes are as big as towers, but if you put him on my apron, he won't harm you, and you can take all the gold you want."

title: THE TINDER-BOX
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"That's quite a story," said the soldier. "But what do you want in return, old witch? Surely you don't mean to tell me all this for free."

"No," said the witch. "I don't ask for any money. Just promise to bring me an old tinder-box that my grandmother left behind the last time she went down there."

"Alright, I promise. Now tie the rope around my waist."

"Here it is," said the witch, handing him both the rope and her blue checked apron.

Once the rope was secured, the soldier climbed up the tree and lowered himself through the hollow to the ground below. Just as the witch had said, he found a large hall filled with hundreds of burning lamps. He opened the first door. "Wow!" he exclaimed; there was the dog with eyes as big as teacups, staring right at him.

"You're quite the sight," the soldier said, grabbing him and placing him on the witch's apron while he stuffed his pockets with as many coins as they could hold. After shutting the lid, he placed the dog back on it and moved into the next chamber. Sure enough, the dog with eyes as big as mill-wheels was there.

"You might want to stop staring at me like that; you're going to make your eyes water," the soldier teased, placing him on the apron too before opening the chest. When he saw how much silver money was inside, he quickly tossed all the copper he had taken and filled his pockets and knapsack with only silver.

He then went into the third room, where, indeed, the dog looked hideous; his eyes were as big as towers, spinning around in his head like wheels.

"Good morning," said the soldier, tipping his cap, since he had never seen such a dog before. After observing him closely, he thought he had been polite enough, so he set the dog on the floor and opened the chest. Wow, there was a mountain of gold! Enough to buy all the candy, toy soldiers, whips, and rocking horses in existence—or even the whole town! It was an incredible amount. So the soldier tossed aside the silver and loaded his pockets, knapsack, cap, and even his boots with gold, making it hard to walk.

title: THE TINDER-BOX
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Now he was truly wealthy, so he put the dog back on the chest, closed the door, and called up the tree, "Now pull me up, old witch!"

"Did you get the tinder-box?" asked the witch.

"No, I completely forgot it." So he went back and retrieved the tinder-box, after which the witch pulled him up from the tree. He stood once more on the main road, his pockets, knapsack, cap, and boots brimming with gold.

"What will you do with the tinder-box?" the soldier asked.

"That’s none of your concern," the witch replied. "You have your money; now hand over the tinder-box."

"I'll tell you this," said the soldier, "if you don’t tell me what you're going to do with it, I will draw my sword and cut off your head."

Without hesitation, the soldier beheaded her. Her body fell to the ground. He then gathered all his money in her apron, slung it on his back like a bundle, put the tinder-box in his pocket, and headed to the nearest town. It was a delightful place, and he checked into the best inn, ordering a feast of all his favorite foods since he was now wealthy and had plenty to spend.

The servant who cleaned his boots thought they were quite shabby to be worn by such a rich gentleman, considering he hadn't bought any new ones yet. The next day, however, he got himself some nice clothes and proper shoes, so he soon became known as a dapper gentleman. People flocked to visit him, sharing stories about all the wonders in town and telling him about the king’s beautiful daughter, the princess.

"How can I see her?" the soldier asked.

"You can’t see her at all," they replied. "She lives in a large copper castle, surrounded by towering walls. Only the king can go in or out since there's a prophecy that she'll marry a common soldier, and the king can't stand that thought."

title: THE TINDER-BOX
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I really want to see her," the soldier thought, but he couldn't get permission to do so. Meanwhile, he was having a great time, visiting the theater, strolling through the king's garden, and generously giving money to the poor, which he felt good about; he remembered how it felt to be broke. Now he was rich, dressed nicely, and surrounded by friends who all regarded him as a fine gentleman, which pleased him greatly. But money doesn't last forever, and as he spent and gave away more each day without receiving any in return, he eventually found himself with only two coins left. Thus, he had to leave his fancy room and move into a small attic under the roof, where he had to clean his boots and even mend them with a big needle. His friends stopped visiting him, as there were too many stairs to climb. One dark evening, he didn't even have a penny to buy a candle; then suddenly, he remembered there was a piece of candle stuck inside the tinder-box he'd brought from the old tree where the witch had helped him.

He found the tinder-box, and as soon as he struck a few sparks with the flint and steel, the door flew open, and there stood the dog with eyes as big as teacups, the one he'd seen while in the tree, asking, "What are your orders, master?"

"Well, this is a lucky tinder-box if it brings me everything I desire," the soldier said.

"Bring me some money," he instructed the dog.

In a flash, the dog disappeared and soon returned with a large bag of copper coins in his mouth. The soldier quickly discovered the true value of the tinder-box. If he struck the flint once, the dog who guarded the chest of copper money would appear; if he struck it twice, the dog from the chest of silver would come; and if he struck it three times, the dog with eyes like towers, who watched over the gold, would show up. With this newfound wealth, the soldier returned to his elegant rooms, dressed in his fine clothes once more, and his friends recognized him right away, treating him with the same admiration as before.

title: THE TINDER-BOX
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
After a while, he began to think it was odd that no one could catch a glimpse of the princess. "Everyone claims she's incredibly beautiful," he pondered. "But what's the point if she's stuck behind walls in a copper castle? How can I see her? Wait! Where's my tinder-box?" He quickly struck a spark, and in an instant, the dog with eyes as big as teacups appeared.

"It's midnight," said the soldier, "but I would love to see the princess, even if just for a moment."

The dog vanished instantly and before the soldier could blink, he returned with the princess. She was asleep on the dog’s back, looking so beautiful that anyone who saw her would instantly know she was a true princess. The soldier couldn't help but kiss her, as was fitting for a true soldier. Then, the dog raced back with the princess; however, the next morning, while having breakfast with the king and queen, she shared a strange dream she had about a dog and a soldier who had kissed her while she was riding on the dog's back.

"What a lovely story," said the queen. So, the next night, one of the court ladies was assigned to watch over the princess's bed to find out whether it was a dream or something else.

The soldier longed to see the princess again. That night, he called for the dog once more, instructing it to bring her quickly. But the old lady put on water-resistant boots and chased after them, managing to track down the dog as he took the princess to a large house. To help herself remember where the place was, she drew a big cross on the door with a piece of chalk. Afterward, she returned home to bed. The dog soon came back with the princess. But seeing the cross marked on the doorway, he grabbed a piece of chalk and drew crosses on every door in town so the lady-in-waiting wouldn’t be able to find the right house.

Early the next morning, the king and queen accompanied the lady and all the royal attendants to see where the princess had been.

title: THE TINDER-BOX
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Here it is," the king said when they reached the first door marked with a cross.

"No, dear husband, it must be that one," said the queen, pointing to another door that was also marked.

"And here’s one! And there’s another!" they all exclaimed, as they found crosses on doors in every direction.

They realized it would be pointless to continue searching. But the queen was quite clever; she was capable of more than just sitting in a carriage. She took out her gold scissors, cut a piece of silk into squares, and crafted a neat little bag. She filled it with buckwheat flour and tied it around the princess's neck, making a small hole so the flour could spill out as she walked. That night, the dog came again, carrying the princess on his back and running swiftly to the soldier, who loved her dearly and wished he were a prince so he could marry her. The dog didn’t notice how the flour fell to the ground, marking a trail from the castle wall to the soldier's house and even up to the window where he had climbed in with the princess. So the next morning, the king and queen discovered where their daughter had been, and the soldier was arrested and thrown in prison. Oh, how dark and gloomy it was as he sat there, with people telling him, "Tomorrow, you'll be hanged." That was tough news to hear, especially since he'd left the tinder-box at the inn.

In the morning, he could see through the iron bars of his small window how people hurried out of town to watch him get hanged. He heard the drums beat and saw soldiers marching. Everyone rushed out to see what was happening when a shoemaker's boy, wearing an apron and slippers, hurried by so fast that one of his slippers flew off and hit the wall where the soldier sat watching through the bars. "Hey, shoemaker's boy, no need to rush," called the soldier. "Nothing's happening until I get there, but if you run to where I've been staying and bring me my tinder-box, I'll give you four coins, but you need to move fast."

title: THE TINDER-BOX
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The shoemaker's boy, excited about the four coins, ran quickly to fetch the tinder-box and gave it to the soldier. Now we can see what happened next. Outside the town, a large gallows had been set up, with soldiers and thousands of people standing around. The king and queen sat on magnificent thrones, opposite the judges and the entire council. The soldier was already on the ladder, but just as they were about to tie the noose around his neck, he asked if a poor criminal could have a final request before facing death. He wanted to smoke one last pipe, since it would be his last. The king couldn’t refuse, so the soldier took out his tinder-box and struck it, once, twice, three times—and in a moment, the three dogs appeared: the one with eyes as big as teacups, the one with eyes the size of mill-wheels, and the third with towering eyes. "Help me now, so I won't be hanged," cried the soldier.

The dogs pounced on the judges and all the council members; they grabbed one by the legs, another by the nose, throwing them high into the air until they came crashing down.

"I won't be touched," said the king. But the largest dog seized him and the queen and tossed them after the others. Fear spread among the soldiers and the crowd, who shouted, "Good soldier, you'll be our king, and you'll marry the beautiful princess!"

They placed the soldier in the king's carriage, and the three dogs raced in front, barking, "Hurrah!" while the children whistled and the soldiers saluted. The princess emerged from her copper castle, happy to become queen. The wedding celebrations lasted an entire week, and the dogs sat at the table, staring with wide eyes.

title: THE TOP AND BALL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
A spinning top and a little ball were nestled together in a box filled with other toys. The top turned to the ball and said, "What do you think about us getting married since we’re both in this box together?"

But the ball, dressed in a fancy morocco leather, thought very highly of herself and didn’t even bother to respond.

The next day, the little boy who owned the toys came along. He painted the top in bright red and yellow, and drove a brass nail into the center so that, as the top spun, it looked spectacular.

"Check me out now," the top called out to the ball. "What do you think? Should we get engaged? We’d be a perfect match; you bounce, and I spin! No one would be happier than us."

"Really? You actually think so? Well, maybe you don’t know that my parents were morocco slippers, and I have a Spanish cork inside me."

"But I’m made of mahogany," responded the top. "The major himself crafted me. He has his own lathe, and it’s a real hobby of his."

"I swear, I’m telling the truth," said the top. "You have my word on that!"

"You certainly know how to express yourself," said the ball, "but I can’t accept your proposal. I’m almost engaged to a swallow! Each time I soar through the air, he pokes his head out of the nest and asks, 'Will you?' and I’ve silently said 'Yes' to myself, which is basically like being half engaged. But I promise I won’t forget you."

"That’s not much comfort to me," sighed the top, and they stopped talking to each other.

The next day, the boy took the ball outside. The top watched as the ball soared high into the sky, like a bird, until it almost disappeared from sight. Each time it came back down, it leaped even higher than before, either because it wanted to fly or because of the Spanish cork inside it. But on the ninth leap, it stayed away and didn’t come back. The boy searched everywhere for it, but to no avail; it was lost.

title: THE TOP AND BALL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I know exactly where she is," the top sighed. "She’s in the swallow’s nest and has married the swallow."

The more the top thought about this, the more he missed the ball. His feelings for her only deepened because she was no longer his, and the fact that someone else had won her made it even worse. The top kept spinning and humming, but his mind was full of thoughts about the ball; the more he thought of her, the more beautiful she seemed.

Years passed, and his love for her aged like a fine wine. The top wasn’t young anymore either, but one day he looked more impressive than ever, completely gilded. Now he was a golden top, twirling and dancing until he hummed with excitement, really worth watching. But one day, he jumped too high, and then, he too vanished. They searched everywhere, even in the cellar, but he was nowhere to be found. Where could he have gone? He had landed in the dust-bin, surrounded by all sorts of rubbish: cabbage stalks, dirt, and rainwater that had dripped down from the roof.

"Well, this is a lovely place to be," he said. "My gilding is going to wash off soon. Oh, what a bunch of rubbish I’m stuck with!" Then, he noticed a curious old round thing, resembling a shriveled apple, lying next to a long, leafless cabbage stalk. It wasn’t an apple at all, but an old ball that had been sitting in the gutter for years, completely soaked.

"Thank goodness, another soul of my kind to talk to," said the ball, examining the shiny top. "I’m made of morocco, too. A young lady sewed me together, and I’ve got a Spanish cork in me, but you wouldn’t guess it by looking at me now. I was once engaged to a swallow, but I fell into the gutter and have been here for over five years, soaking wet. Believe me, that’s a long time for a young lady."

The top remained silent but thought of his long-lost love. The more the ball spoke, the more it struck him that this was really her.

Just then, a servant came to clean out the dust-bin.

"Oh, wow! Here’s a golden top!" she exclaimed. So the top was pulled back into the light and noticed again, but no one ever mentioned the little ball again. He didn’t speak of his old love; it faded away quickly. When something you love has spent five years in a gutter and is all soaked, no one wants to talk about it when they find it in a dust-bin.

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
John felt utterly heartbroken; his father was so ill that he could hardly bear to think about it. He sat alone with the sick man in their small room, the lamp flickering and nearly burnt out as the late hour wore on.

"You've been a wonderful son, John," his father said softly, gazing at him with kind, sincere eyes. He sighed deeply and then, as if he were drifting off to sleep, he died.

John cried uncontrollably, feeling utterly alone in the world with no family left—no father, mother, brother, or sister. He knelt beside his father’s bed, kissed his lifeless hand, and shed countless tears. Eventually, exhaustion took over as he rested his head against the hard bedpost and fell asleep. In his dreams, he imagined the sun shining brightly on him, his father healthy and laughing as he did when he was happy. A beautiful girl with a golden crown and long, radiant hair reached out to him, while his father said, "Look at the lovely bride you’ve won. She’s the most beautiful maiden on Earth." But then he woke up, the beautiful visions faded, and he found himself alone with his father’s body. Poor John!

The following week, they buried John's father. John walked behind the coffin, feeling the heavy weight of his grief, knowing he would never see his beloved father again. As the earth hit the coffin lid, he watched until only a corner remained visible, then that too vanished. His heart felt like it was breaking under the weight of sorrow. But as those around the grave began to sing a psalm, the sweet, comforting tones brought tears to his eyes, easing his pain. The sun beamed down on the green trees, as if to say, "John, don’t be so sad. See the beautiful blue sky above? Your father is up there, praying to the loving Father of all for your future happiness."

"I will always be good," John promised, "and then I’ll be with my father in heaven again. What joy it’ll be to see him! I’ll have so many things to share, and he’ll explain the delights of heaven to me, just like he did when I was on Earth. Oh, how glorious it will be!"

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
He imagined it so vividly that a smile crept across his face, even as the tears continued to fall.

The little birds in the chestnut trees chirped, "Tweet, tweet," appearing to celebrate, even after witnessing the funeral. They seemed to know that the deceased was now in heaven, with wings larger and more beautiful than their own, finally happy because he had been good on Earth. John watched them fly off into the vast world, wishing he could join them. But first, he carved a large wooden cross to place on his father’s grave. When he arrived at the grave that evening, he found it decorated with gravel and flowers—a loving tribute from strangers who had known and cherished the kind old man.

The next morning, John packed his small bag of clothes and tucked away all his money—fifty dollars and some coins—into his belt. He decided to venture into the world. But first, he stopped by his father’s grave, said a prayer, and whispered, "Goodbye."

As he walked through the fields, the flowers appeared fresh and radiant in the warm sunlight, swaying in the breeze as if saying, "Welcome to the bright green wood where everything is lively and joyful."

Just before he reached the town, John turned for one last glimpse of the old church where he had been baptized, and where his father had taken him every Sunday for worship and singing. He spotted the bell-ringer at a narrow opening, wearing a pointed red cap and shading his eyes from the sun.
