John waved goodbye, and the bell-ringer waved his cap back at him, placing his hand over his heart and blowing kisses, showing he cared and wished him well on his journey.

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
John traveled on, dreaming of all the amazing sights he would see in the vast, beautiful world. He found himself farther from home than he had ever been. The names of the towns and people he passed were unfamiliar, and the language was strange in this new land. That first night, he slept on a haystack in the fields because he had no other place to rest. But he found it quite cozy, more comfortable than a king's bed. The field, the stream, and the haystack provided a beautiful bedroom, with the blue sky above acting as a ceiling. The soft grass with its red and white flowers was the carpet, while elder bushes and wild rose hedges framed the walls. The clear brook offered water for a refreshing wash, while the reeds bowed gently, wishing him good morning and good night. The moon, like a big lamp, hung high above him, and he wasn’t worried about it setting fire to his blankets. John slept soundly the entire night, and when he awoke, the sun was shining, and the little birds were singing around him, "Good morning, good morning! Are you still asleep?"

It was Sunday, and the church bells were ringing. As people entered, John followed, listening to God’s word, joining in the hymns, and paying attention to the preacher. It felt just like his own church where he had been baptized and sung with his father. Outside in the graveyard, some graves were overgrown with tall grass. John remembered his father's grave, which he knew would eventually look like those, since he couldn’t care for it. So he got to work, pulling up the tall grass, setting upright the fallen wooden crosses, and replacing the wreaths that had been blown away by the wind. He hoped that perhaps someone was doing the same for his father’s grave, since he couldn’t be there to do it himself.

Outside the church door stood an old beggar, leaning on his crutch. John gave the man his silver coins and continued on his journey, feeling lighter and happier than he had in a while. Later that evening, the weather turned stormy, so he hurried to find shelter, but it was dark by the time he arrived at a little lonely church perched on a hill. "I’ll go in here," he decided, "and sit in a corner. I’m quite tired and need to rest."

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
He stepped inside, took a seat, folded his hands, and said his evening prayer before drifting off to sleep while thunder rumbled and lightning flashed outside. When he awoke, it was still night, but the storm had passed, and the moonlight streamed in through the windows. That's when he saw an open coffin in the center of the church, holding a dead man awaiting burial. John wasn’t scared; he felt at peace knowing that the dead can’t harm anyone. It’s the wicked among the living who create problems. However, two wicked-looking men stood beside the coffin with plans that were anything but honorable—they intended to toss the corpse outside the church instead of allowing it to rest properly.

"Why are you doing this?" John asked when he noticed their intentions. "That’s wrong. Let him rest in peace, in Christ’s name."

"What nonsense," replied the two dreadful men. "He cheated us! He owed us money he couldn’t repay, and now that he’s dead, we won’t get anything. We’re going to take our revenge and leave him to rot outside like a dog!"

"I only have fifty dollars," John offered, "it’s all I have in the world. But I’ll give it to you if you promise to leave the dead man in peace. I can manage without the money; I’m strong and healthy, and God will help me along the way."

"Well, of course," the men grinned. "If you pay his debt, we won’t bother him. You can count on that," they laughed as they took John’s money, mocking his kindness before leaving.

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
John gently laid the body back in the coffin, folded the man's hands together, and said his goodbyes before he walked away, feeling lighthearted as he passed through the great forest. All around him, he could see tiny elves dancing in the moonlight filtering through the trees. They seemed unbothered by his presence, recognizing that he was kind and harmless. Only wicked souls are blind to the beauty of fairies. Some of the tiny elves were no taller than a finger's width, with golden combs in their long yellow hair. They rocked back and forth on large dew drops that dripped from the leaves and tall grass. Sometimes, the dew drops would roll away, causing laughter to erupt among the little folks. Watching them play was utterly charming. They sang songs John remembered learning as a boy. Large, spotted spiders spun beautiful bridges and palaces from one hedge to another, and as tiny drops fell upon them, they sparkled in the moonlight like glass. This continued until sunrise, when the little elves slipped into the flower buds as the wind swept the bridges and palaces into the air like cobwebs.

As John was leaving the woods, a strong voice called out to him, "Hey there, fellow! Where are you headed?"

"To explore the wide world," he replied. "I’m just a poor boy now, with neither father nor mother, but I believe God will help me."

"I’m also headed into the wide world," the stranger replied. "Shall we travel together?"

"Absolutely!" John said, and together they continued their journey. They quickly became friends, sharing a bond of goodness. John soon realized that the stranger was much more knowledgeable, having traveled far and wide and had tales of nearly everything. As the sun climbed higher in the sky, they settled under a large tree for breakfast when an elderly woman approached them, almost doubled over. She leaned on a stick and carried a bundle of firewood on her back, with ferns and willow twigs peeking out from her apron. Just as she reached them, her foot slipped and she tumbled to the ground, screaming in pain; poor woman had broken her leg!

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
John immediately suggested they help the old woman back to her cottage. But the stranger opened his knapsack, revealing a box containing a salve that would quickly heal her leg, allowing her to walk home as if nothing had happened. All he asked for in return were the three ferns she had gathered.

"That’s a bit steep," said the old woman, shaking her head. She seemed hesitant to give up the ferns, but lying there in agony wasn’t pleasant, so she eventually relented and handed them over. To her amazement, as soon as he applied the ointment, she stood up and walked even better than she had before. Yet, such a miraculous balm couldn't be found at any pharmacy.

"What do you want with those three fern stems?" John asked his traveling companion.

"Oh, they make great brooms," he replied. "Sometimes I have strange whims, you know." With that, they walked on together for quite some time.

"The sky is getting so dark," John remarked, "and look at those thick, heavy clouds. What do you think they are?"

"Those aren’t clouds," the stranger answered. "Those are mountains—tall, high mountains. Once we reach their peaks, we’ll be above the clouds, breathing in fresh air. Believe me, it will be delightful to climb so high. We’ll reach them by tomorrow." However, the mountains were further away than they appeared, requiring a full day of travel and leading them through deep, shadowy forests and rock piles the size of small towns. After such a demanding trek, John and his companion stopped at an inn to rest and gather their strength for the journey ahead.

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Inside the inn’s main room, a crowd gathered to watch a puppet show. The showman had just set up his stage, and the audience was eagerly seated around, ready to see the performance. Right in front, occupying the prime spot, sat a hefty butcher with a bull-dog who seemed eager to bite. Everyone, including him, was completely absorbed. The show began, featuring a charming tale with a king and queen seated on a magnificent throne, adorned with gleaming crowns. Their dresses trailed extravagantly, and the most exquisite wooden dolls with glass eyes operated the doors to let fresh air in. It was a whimsical play until the moment the queen got up and crossed the stage, at which point the ravenous bull-dog lunged forward and bit her arm, snapping it in two! This awful misfortune left the showman distraught, as she was the finest doll he owned, and now she lay damaged. However, after the crowd dispersed, John’s companion assured the showman that he could fix her. He took out his box, rubbed the doll with some of the same salve he had used on the old woman, and soon, to everyone’s astonishment, the doll was as good as new, moving effortlessly on her own!

Later that night, when the inn was quiet and everyone had gone to bed, a deep sigh echoed through the room, so distressing that everyone awoke to investigate. The showman dashed to his puppet theater and discovered that the sighs were coming from the dolls, all lamenting on the floor, wishing to be restored to life, just like the queen. The queen knelt, took off her beautiful crown, and holding it in her hands, pleaded, "Please take this from me, but do help my husband and his courtiers!"

The showman, in a state of despair, addressed John’s companion, pleading for assistance. He promised him all the revenue from the next show if he would merely apply the ointment to four or five of the dolls. The traveler replied that he sought nothing in return except the sword the showman wore at his side. Once he received the sword, he happily anointed six of the dolls with the magical ointment, and instantly they came alive, dancing so gracefully that all the girls present couldn’t resist joining in. The coachman spun with the cook, while the waiters twirled with the maids, including even the tongs and fire-shovels attempting to move—but they flopped down after just one leap. It was a truly delightful evening!

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The next morning, John and his companion set out to continue their journey through tall pine forests and across majestic mountains. They finally climbed to such heights that towns and villages lay beneath them, with the church steeples appearing like tiny dots among the foliage. They could gaze out over miles of unexplored territories, discovering more beauty in the world than John had ever imagined. The sun shone brightly in the clear blue sky, and the sound of a huntsman’s horn carried through the crisp mountain air, bringing tears of joy to John’s eyes. He exclaimed, "How wonderful and loving God is to bless us with such beauty in this world to bring us happiness!"

His companion stood beside him, hands folded, admiring the dark woods and the villages basking in the warm sunlight. At that moment, they heard enchanting music overhead. Looking up, they saw a large white swan soaring gracefully through the sky, singing as no other bird could. But then the song began to fade, the bird’s head drooped, and it descended slowly, landing dead at their feet.

"What a beautiful bird," the traveler remarked. "Those majestic white wings are worth a fortune! I’ll take them with me. A sword has its uses after all."

With one swift slice, he severed the swan’s wings and carried them away.

They pressed on over the mountains for many miles until they finally reached a bustling city with hundreds of towers glistening in the sun. In the center of the city stood an exquisite palace of marble, topped with pure red gold, where the king resided. John and his friend chose not to enter the town right away; instead, they stopped at an inn outside the city to change into respectable attire before strolling through the streets. The innkeeper informed them that the king was a kind man who never harmed anyone, but about his daughter, "Heavens help us!"

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
She was indeed a striking beauty, yet she was also a wicked witch. Many noble princes had lost their lives due to her cruel games. Anyone, whether prince or beggar, could seek her hand, only to face a dreadful challenge. She possessed three riddles and would be married to the one who could guess them, while failure meant execution. The old king, heartbroken over her deeds, had long given up trying to control her—weary of lamenting the fate of her suitors. Every year, he prayed with his soldiers for her goodness, yet she remained unchanged. Women consuming brandy mourned in black, showing their sadness; what more could they do?

"What a hideous princess!" exclaimed John. "She deserves punishment! If I were the king, I would find a way to discipline her."

At that moment, they heard shouts of "Hurrah!" Outside, the princess progressed on horseback, stunningly beautiful, causing everyone to forget her wickedness and cheer. Twelve lovely maidens in white silk performed maneuvers on their coal-black horses beside her, while the princess rode a snow-white steed adorned with diamonds and rubies. Her golden dress gleamed, and the whip she held sparkled like shimmering sunlight. Her crown twinkled like heavenly stars, and her cape was made of countless butterfly wings sewn together. Yet, she herself was the most breathtaking of all.

Upon seeing her, John’s face flushed bright red, leaving him nearly speechless. The princess looked just like the enchanting girl in the golden crown from his dream the night his father died. She was so lovely that he found himself deeply enamored.

"Surely it can't be true," he thought. "How could she be the same wicked witch who orders executions for failed guesses? Every suitor, even the humblest beggar, is welcome to propose to her. I have to go see the palace!"

Everyone warned him against it, fearing he would meet the same fate as the others. Even his companion suggested he reconsider, but John felt confident in his chances. He polished his shoes and coat, washed his face and hands, combed his soft flaxen hair, and boldly stepped out alone into the town, heading toward the palace.

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Come in," the king said when John knocked. John opened the door, and the old king, in a dressing gown and embroidered slippers, approached him. Wearing a crown, he carried the royal scepter and orb. "Hold on a moment," he said, tucking the orb under his arm to shake John’s hand. But when he realized John's intentions as yet another suitor, he began to sob violently, dropping the scepter and orb. Poor old king! "Leave her be," he moaned. "You’ll meet the same fate as the others. Come, I’ll show you." He led John to the princess’s garden, revealing a horrifying sight—three or four young princes hung from every tree, unable to solve her riddles, their skeletons rattling in the wind, preventing even birds from entering the garden. The flowers stood on human bones instead of sticks, and skulls grinned from flowerpots. It was truly a grim garden suited for a witch. "Look at all of this," said the king. "You’ll meet the same end if you try your luck. Please don't! It troubles my heart so much."

John gently kissed the old king’s hand, assuring him it would be alright since he was enchanted by the beautiful princess. Moments later, she rode into the courtyard, accompanied by her ladies, and he greeted her with a smile. She appeared more stunning than ever, which only intensified his feelings. How could she really be a witch? He followed her inside, and servants greeted them with sweet treats, but the old king was too upset to eat. It was settled that John would return the next day when judges and advisors would be present, tasked with guessing the first riddle. If successful, he would have to return for a second. But if not, he risked losing his life—no one had ever guessed even one successfully. Yet, John wasn’t worried; instead, he felt joyful, thinking only of the beautiful princess. He believed that somehow he would receive help, though he chose not to ponder how. He danced along the road back to the inn, eager to tell his friend of the princess’ grace and beauty, longing for the next day when he could attempt the riddles.

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
That evening, it became known throughout the town that another suitor had arrived. Everyone mourned, the theater closed its doors, women selling sweets donned black ribbons around their sugar-sticks, and the king and priests knelt in prayer. There was an intense sorrow, as few had hope that John would fare better than his predecessors.

When night fell, John’s companion prepared a large bowl of punch, saying, "Let’s celebrate and toast to the princess’s health!" However, after just two glasses, John began to feel drowsy and soon fell fast asleep. The traveler gently lifted him from his chair and laid him onto the bed. When the night turned completely dark, he took the large wings he had cut from the dead swan and tied them securely to his shoulders. Then he pocketed the largest fern stem from the old woman and opened the window, taking flight over the town straight towards the palace. He settled himself in a corner just beneath the princess’s window.

The town was silent as the clock struck a quarter to midnight, when the window opened and the princess—her own wings black and a long white coat flowing behind her—flew out into the night sky, gliding toward a tall mountain. The traveler, now invisible, followed her, using the fern rod to whip the princess whenever he struck, causing blood to flow with each slash. It was an unusual flight through the air, with the wind catching her garment, spread wide like a ship’s sail, as the moon illuminated her path. "What heavy hail!" she cried out every time the rod struck her—serving her well for her misdeeds.

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Eventually, she reached the mountain and knocked. The mountain erupted, opening with a sound akin to thunder, and she entered. The traveler followed, remaining unseen as they traversed a long, wide corridor where a multitude of sparkling spiders scurried along the walls, making them shine as if lit by flames. They entered a vast hall of silver and gold adorned with vibrant flowers, however, the stems were poisonous snakes, and the flowers emitted fire. Glowing glow-worms dotted the ceilings while translucent bats flew overhead, contributing to the eerie ambiance. In the center stood a throne resting on four skeletal horses, their harness crafted from fiery spiders. The throne, made of milk-white glass, had cushions made of black mice biting each other's tails. Hanging above it was a canopy of luxurious spider silk, adorned with glistening little green flies like jewels. Seated on the throne was an old sorcerer, ugly and crowned, who greeted the princess with a kiss before inviting her to sit beside him as the music began. Enormous black grasshoppers played music, while an owl thumped its body as a drum, creating an utterly absurd concert. Goblins danced in the hall, but the traveler remained unseen, positioned just behind the throne to listen in on everything. The nobles who entered appeared regal, yet they were merely broomsticks dressed in fine clothing by the sorcerer. After a brief dance, the princess informed the sorcerer that she had a new suitor and inquired about what riddle he should present him the following day.

"Listen carefully," the magician advised, "choose something simple; it’s less likely he’ll guess correctly. Think of one of your shoes—he would never suspect that. Then take his head! And don’t forget to bring me his eyes tomorrow night for supper!"

The princess curtsied and promised not to forget the eyes.

The magician opened the mountains, and she returned home, but the traveler whipped her once more, causing her to lament about the heavy hail and speeding back to her bedroom through her window. The traveler then went back to the inn where John still slept, removed his wings, and retired to rest, thoroughly exhausted. The next morning, John awoke, and as his companion rose, he told John he had a revealing dream about the princess’s shoe and suggested he should ask her if she had considered her shoe as part of her riddle. His companion already knew what the magician intended.

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I might as well go with that idea," John said. "Maybe your dream will come to fruition. But I’ll say farewell, for if I guess wrong, I may never see you again."

They embraced each other tightly, and John ventured into the town toward the palace. The grand hall bustled with people; judges perched in plush chairs with cushy headrests due to the weight of their thoughts. The old king stood nearby, dabbing his eyes with a white handkerchief. When the princess appeared, she looked even more radiant than before, greeting everyone graciously—all except John, to whom she said, "Good morning."

Now it was time for John to guess what she was thinking. She looked at him with such kindness that it warmed his heart. But the moment he whispered the word "shoe," she went pale as a ghost; even her wisdom couldn’t protect her, for he had guessed correctly. The old king was overjoyed, celebrating the moment, and the crowd erupted in applause for both him and John. His companion felt joy as well, pleased to see John's success. But John closed his hands in thanks to God, confident that divine help would aid him again, knowing he still had two more riddles to solve. The evening passed pleasantly, much like the night before. As John slept, the traveler flew once again after the princess, this time carrying two rods with him, while readying to punish her for each failed guess.

That night was dark and stormy, with tiles flying off roof tops and the trees of the sinister garden bending before the wind. Lightning flashed, and thunder boomed relentlessly. The castle window flung open as the princess emerged, her face pale as a ghost, yet she laughed amid the storm. Her white cloak billowed out behind her like a great sail. The traveler lashed her with the rods, blood dripping down from the hits, but she persevered and ultimately reached the mountain. "What a hail-storm!" she commented, "I’ve never experienced such fury before!"

"Indeed, too much of a good thing can sometimes be overwhelming," said the magician.

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The princess then mentioned that John had guessed correctly the second time, and if he succeeded tomorrow, she would be lost to him, never returning to the mountain or practicing magic again, making her very upset. "Don’t fret; I’ll create a riddle so difficult he’ll never guess it unless he’s better than me! But let’s enjoy ourselves for now."

The magician took the princess's hands and they danced with the goblins and Jack-o'-lanterns in the room. Red spiders danced merrily across the walls, blending sparks from the fiery flowers, while the owl provided rhythmic drumbeats and crickets whistled along. It was a truly absurd ball. As the night wore on, the princess realized she must return before being missed in the palace. The magician offered to accompany her, ensuring they’d stay together on the way home. They took off into the turbulent night, with the traveler close behind, shattering the rods across their backs, catching the magician off-guard with the heavy storm.

Just outside the palace, the magician paused to bid the princess farewell, whispering, "Tomorrow, think of my head."

But the traveler overheard this threat, and as the princess slipped through the window into her quarters, he seized the magician by his long black beard and with one swift stroke of his sword, severed the wicked conjurer’s head, leaving him with no way to recognize his attacker. He tossed the dead body into the sea for the fishes, dipping the head to cleanse it, tying it up in a silk handkerchief before carrying it back to the inn to rest.

The next morning, he presented John with the head, advising him not to untie it until the princess inquired about her thoughts. The palace was packed with spectators standing shoulder to shoulder. The council lounged in ornate armchairs, the old king donned new robes while looking sharp in his golden crown and scepter. However, the princess was pale, dressed in black as if preparing for a funeral.

"What have I been thinking of?" the princess asked John. He quickly unfastened the handkerchief, and was astonished to find the head of the wicked magician inside. The room gasped in horror; the sight was horrifying! But the princess remained frozen like a statue, unable to speak. Finally, she arose and took John’s hand, for he had guessed correctly.

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
She avoided eye contact, sighing deeply as she declared, "You are my master now; our marriage must take place this evening."

"I’m delighted to hear that!" the old king exclaimed. "It’s exactly what I wish!"

The crowd erupted with cheers. Music played throughout the streets, bells rang, and those selling confectionaries finally removed their mourning colors from the treats. Joy enveloped the town. Whole oxen stuffed with ducks and chickens cooked in the market square, allowing everyone to help themselves. The fountains flowed with exquisite wine, and anyone who bought a penny loaf received an extra six raisin-filled buns. That evening, the entire town glowed with lights, soldiers fired cannons, and children ignited firecrackers, filling the air with excitement. In the palace, nobles and ladies danced joyfully together, their voices echoing in the distance with the melody:

"Here are maidens, young and fair,
Dancing in the summer air.
Like two spinning-wheels at play,
Pretty maidens dance away—
Dance through spring and summer fair,
Till the soles come off your shoes."

title: THE TRAVELLING COMPANION
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
But the princess remained under the magician's spell, unable to love John. His companion had foreseen this, providing John with three feathers from the swan’s wings and a small bottle filled with magical drops. He instructed John to prepare a large bath of water beside the princess's bed, adding the feathers and drops while waiting for her to approach. Just as she was about to climb into bed, he would give her a gentle shove, plunging her into the water three times to break the sorcerer’s hold; then she would fall madly in love with him. John followed his friend’s advice. The princess shrieked as he dipped her for the first time, even transforming into a great black swan with fiery eyes. When she resurfaced a second time, she was a white swan circled by a black ring. He submerged her once more, and as she emerged, she transformed into the most stunning princess he’d ever seen. More beautiful than ever, she thanked him, tears sparkling in her eyes for breaking the sorcerer's spell. The next day, the king arrived with the entire court to congratulate them, staying far longer than expected. Lastly, John’s traveling companion showed up, staff in hand and backpack slung over his shoulder. John showered him with gratitude, insisting he stay since he was the reason for all his fortune. But the traveler gently shook his head. "No, my time here is done. I’ve merely repaid my debt to you. Remember the dead man whom you saved from being discarded? I am he," he revealed, and as he spoke, he vanished from sight.

The wedding festivities went on for an entire month. John and his princess loved one another dearly, and the king enjoyed many moments with his grandchildren, allowing them to play with his scepter. John ultimately became the king of the entire land.

title: THE UGLY DUCKLING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
It was a beautiful summer day in the countryside, with golden fields of corn, lush green oats, and haystacks dotting the meadows. A stork walked around on its long red legs, chattering in the Egyptian language it learned from its mother. The cornfields and meadows were bordered by thick forests, which contained deep, serene pools. It was truly delightful to stroll through the countryside. In a sunny corner stood a cozy old farmhouse near a deep river, where large burdock leaves grew tall enough that a small child could stand upright beneath the tallest one. It was as wild as a place deep in a thick forest. In this snug hideaway, a duck sat on her nest, eagerly waiting for her young ones to hatch. She was growing weary of her wait; it was taking a long time for the little ones to emerge, and she rarely had any visitors. The other ducks preferred to swim in the river instead of clambering up the slippery banks to gossip under a burdock leaf. Finally, one shell cracked, and then another. Each egg hatched a little creature that lifted its head and chirped, "Peep, peep." "Quack, quack," said the mother, and they all quacked as best as they could while looking around at the vast green leaves. Their mother let them enjoy the sights as much as they liked, since green is good for the eyes. "Wow, the world is huge!" the little ducklings exclaimed, realizing they had so much more room now compared to when they were in their egg shells. "Do you think this is the whole world?" their mother asked. "Just wait until you see the garden; it stretches way beyond to the parson's field, though I’ve never ventured that far. Is everyone out?" she asked, getting up. "No, it looks like the biggest egg is still here. I wonder how much longer this will take; I’m getting really tired of it," she said as she settled back down on her nest.

"So, how's it going?" asked an old duck who came to pay her a visit.

"One egg hasn’t hatched yet," replied the duck. "It won’t break open. Just look at the rest of them; aren’t they the cutest little ducklings you’ve ever seen? They all look just like their unkind father, who never comes to see us."

title: THE UGLY DUCKLING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Let me see the egg that won’t break," said the old duck. "I bet it’s a turkey's egg. I tried hatching some once, and despite all my care and trouble, they were scared of the water. I quacked and clucked, but it didn’t help. I couldn’t get them to take the plunge. Let me see that egg. Yep, that’s definitely a turkey’s egg; trust me, leave it be and teach the others to swim."

"I think I’ll sit on it a little longer," the duck said. "I’ve been at it this long already, so a few more days won’t matter."

"Suit yourself," said the old duck, and she waddled away.

At last, the large egg broke open, and a young one emerged, crying, "Peep, peep." It was big and quite ugly. The mother duck stared at it and exclaimed, "That one is massive and doesn't look at all like the others. I wonder if it’s really a turkey. We’ll find out soon enough when we go to the water. It has to swim even if I have to push it myself."

The next day was lovely, with the sun shining down on the green burdock leaves. So the mother duck took her brood down to the water and jumped in with a splash. "Quack, quack!" she called, and one by one, the little ducklings hopped in. The water closed over their heads, but they quickly resurfaced, swimming gracefully with their little legs paddling beneath them, and the ugly duckling was swimming alongside them too.

"Oh," said the mother, "that's not a turkey; look at how well he swims and how upright he holds himself! He’s my own child, and if you look closely, he’s not so ugly after all. Quack, quack! Come on, let’s go meet society and I’ll introduce you to the farmyard. Just stay close to me, or you might get stepped on; and, above all, watch out for the cat!"

title: THE UGLY DUCKLING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
When they reached the farmyard, there was quite a commotion—two families were fighting over an eel’s head, which ended up being taken by the cat. "Look, kids, that’s how the world works," said the mother duck, sharpening her beak because she would have liked that eel’s head for herself. "Now, show me what you’ve got; let’s see how well you behave. Make sure to bow to that old duck over there; she’s the highest-born of them all and has Spanish blood, so she’s well-to-do. Do you see that red flag tied to her leg? That’s a big deal and a great honor for a duck; it means everyone wants to keep an eye on her, since both humans and animals recognize her. Now, don’t turn your toes in; a well-bred duckling spreads his feet wide like his mother and father. Now, bend your neck and say, ‘quack.’"

The ducklings did as they were told, but the other duck stared and said, "Look, here comes another brood, as if we didn’t already have enough! And what a weird-looking one that is; we don’t want him here!" Then one duck swooped in and pecked him on the neck.

"Leave him alone," said the mother. "He’s not bothering anyone."

"Yeah, but he’s so big and ugly," the nasty duck replied. "So he has to be kicked out."

"The others are lovely little ones," said the old duck with the rag on her leg, "except for that one; I wish his mother could improve him a bit."

"That’s impossible, your grace," the mother replied. "He’s not pretty, but he’s got a good heart and swims as well, if not better, than the rest. I believe he’ll grow up to be handsome, maybe even smaller; he’s just been in the egg too long, and that’s why he’s not quite right;" and then she stroked his neck and smoothed his feathers, saying, "He’s a drake, so he’s not as important. I think he’ll be strong and able to fend for himself."

title: THE UGLY DUCKLING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"The other ducklings are graceful enough," the old duck remarked. "Now make yourself at home, and if you find an eel’s head, bring it to me."

And so they settled in, but the poor duckling, who had been the last to crawl out of his shell and looked so ugly, was bitten, shoved, and mocked, not just by the ducks but by all the other poultry too. "He’s too big," they all said. Even the turkey, who strutted around like a king with his spurs, puffed his chest and charged at the duckling, turning bright red with anger. The poor little thing didn’t know where to go and felt completely miserable because of his ugliness and the laughter he faced from the entire farmyard. Day after day, it just got worse. He was bullied even by his brothers and sisters, who would say, "Oh, you ugly creature, I wish the cat would get you," and his mother even wished he had never been born. The ducks pecked him, the chickens chased him, and the girl who fed the animals kicked him with her feet. Eventually, he ran away, startling the little birds in the hedges as he flew over the fences.

"They’re scared of me because I’m ugly," he said. So he closed his eyes and flew further until he landed on a vast moor with wild ducks. There he stayed the whole night, feeling tired and sad.

In the morning, when the wild ducks took to the sky, they stared at their new companion. "What kind of duck are you?" they all asked, gathering around him.

He bowed politely but didn't answer their question. "You’re really ugly," said the wild ducks, "but that won’t matter as long as you don’t want to marry one of us."

title: THE UGLY DUCKLING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Poor thing! He wasn’t thinking about marriage; all he wanted was the chance to lie among the reeds and drink some water from the moor. After he had been there for two days, two wild geese came along, or rather goslings, as they had just hatched. They were quite cheeky. "Hey, friend," said one of them to the duckling, "you’re so ugly that we actually kind of like you. Want to join us and become a traveler? Not far from here, there’s another moor with some pretty wild geese, all unmarried; you might just find a wife, despite your looks."

title: THE UGLY DUCKLING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Then, out of nowhere, a loud "Pop, pop" rang through the air as two wild geese fell dead among the reeds, and the water turned red with blood. "Pop, pop," echoed all around, and flocks of wild geese took to the air. The sound continued as hunters surrounded the moor, some even perched in trees, watching over the reeds. The blue smoke from the guns rose like clouds over the dark trees, and as it drifted across the water, sporting dogs crashed into the reeds, bending them down as they rushed through. This terrified the poor duckling! He turned his head to hide under his wing just as a large, fearsome dog lumbered nearby. Its jaws were open, its tongue lolled out, and its eyes were fierce. It shoved its nose right in front of the duckling, revealing its sharp teeth, then splashed into the water without touching him. "Oh," sighed the duckling, "thank goodness for my ugliness; even a dog won’t bite me." So he stayed still while the shots continued to ring through the reeds and went off one after the other. It was late in the day before things quieted down, but even then, the poor creature didn’t dare to move. He waited several hours until he could safely look around and then hurried away from the moor as fast as he could. He dashed over fields and meadows until a storm kicked up, making travel nearly impossible. By evening, he reached a dilapidated little cottage that seemed like it could fall apart at any moment, unsure of which side to collapse on first. The storm raged on so fiercely that the duckling couldn’t go any further; he settled down by the cottage and noticed that the door wasn’t completely shut because one of the hinges had come loose. There was a narrow gap at the bottom where he could slip through quietly and find shelter for the night. A woman, a tom cat, and a hen lived in this cottage. The tom cat, whom the woman called "My little darling," was a favorite. He could arch his back, purr, and even shoot sparks from his fur if it was stroked the wrong way. The hen had short legs, earning her the name "Chickie Short Legs." She laid good eggs, and her mistress loved her as if she were her own child. In the morning, the strange visitor was discovered, and the tom cat began to purr while the hen clucked.

title: THE UGLY DUCKLING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"What’s all that noise?" asked the old woman, looking around the room. Her eyesight wasn’t great, so when she spotted the duckling, she thought it was just a fat duck that had strayed from home. "What a catch!" she exclaimed, "I hope it’s not a drake; then I might get some duck eggs. I’ll wait and see." So the duckling was allowed to stay on trial for three weeks, but there were no eggs. The tom cat was the head of the house, and the hen acted like the mistress. They always insisted, "We and the world," as they believed they were half of the world, and the better half at that. The duckling thought that others might disagree, but the hen wouldn’t entertain such thoughts. "Can you lay eggs?" she asked. "No." "Then please be quiet." "But can you arch your back, purr, or make sparks fly?" asked the tom cat. "No." "Then you have no right to speak when sensible animals are talking." So the duckling sat in a corner, feeling pretty down until the sunshine and fresh air filtered into the room through the open door. That’s when he started to feel a strong urge to swim in the water and couldn’t help but mention it to the hen.

"What a ridiculous dream," said the hen. "You have nothing else to do, so you’re filled with silly ideas. If you could purr or lay eggs, these thoughts would fade away."

"But swimming is such a joy!" exclaimed the duckling. "It feels so refreshing to have the water close over my head while I dive down to the bottom."

"Delightful, indeed!" scoffed the hen. "You must be out of your mind! Ask the cat; he’s the cleverest animal I know. Ask him how he’d feel about swimming or diving underwater, and I won’t even mention my own opinion. Ask our master, the old woman—there’s no one cleverer than she is. Do you think she would want to swim or let the water cover her head?"

title: THE UGLY DUCKLING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"You’re not understanding me," the duckling replied.

"We don’t understand you? Who can? Do you really think you’re smarter than the cat or the old lady? I won’t even mention myself. Don’t waste your mind on such nonsense and be grateful you’re accepted here. Aren't you in a warm home, surrounded by company where you can learn something? You talk too much and your company isn’t very pleasant. Believe me, I say this for your own good. I might tell you unflattering truths, but that just shows I care. So I advise you to start laying eggs and learning to purr as soon as possible."

"I think I need to go back out into the world," said the duckling.

title: THE UGLY DUCKLING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "Go ahead," said the hen. So the duckling left the cottage and soon found water to swim and dive in, but other animals continued to avoid him due to his ugly looks. Autumn arrived, and the leaves in the forest turned shades of orange and gold. As winter approached, the wind swept the falling leaves into the cold air. Dark clouds, heavy with hail and snowflakes, loomed in the sky, and the raven croaked from the ferns, making one shiver to look at him. All of this was very disheartening for the poor little duckling. One evening, just as the sun set amidst radiant clouds, a large flock of beautiful birds appeared from the bushes. The duckling had never seen anything like them. They were swans, elegantly curving their graceful necks, their soft feathers gleaming with dazzling whiteness. They made a unique sound as they spread their magnificent wings and flew away from those cold areas to warmer lands across the sea. As they soared higher and higher, the ugly little duckling felt an unusual sensation while watching them. He whirled in the water like a wheel, stretched out his neck toward them, and let out a cry so strange it startled him. How could he ever forget those beautiful, happy birds? When they finally disappeared from view, he dove underwater and resurfaced nearly overwhelmed with excitement. He didn’t know what those birds were called or where they had flown, but he felt a longing for them that he hadn’t felt for any other bird in the world. He didn’t envy those magnificent creatures; he only wished he could be as lovely as them. That poor ugly creature would have happily lived even with the ducks if they had just given him some encouragement. The winter became colder and colder; he had to keep swimming to prevent the water from freezing, but each night the area where he swam became smaller and smaller. Finally, it froze so solidly that the ice cracked beneath him as he moved, and the duckling had to paddle as hard as he could to keep the space from closing. Exhausted at last, he lay still and helpless, trapped in the ice.

title: THE UGLY DUCKLING
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Early the next morning, a peasant passing by noticed what had happened. He broke the ice into pieces with his wooden shoe and carried the duckling home to his wife. The warmth revived the poor little creature, but when the children wanted to play with him, the duckling panicked, thinking they would hurt him. He jumped up in fear, flapped into the milk pan, and splashed milk all over the room. The woman clapped her hands, which made him even more scared. He flew into the butter cask, then into the meal tub, and out again. What a mess he was in! The woman screamed and swatted at him with the tongs; the children laughed and shouted, tumbling over each other as they tried to catch him. Luckily, he escaped. The door was open, and he managed to slip out into the bushes, lying down completely exhausted in the freshly fallen snow.

It would be very sad to recount all the hardship and suffering the poor little duckling faced throughout the brutal winter; but when it finally ended, he found himself waking one morning in a moor surrounded by reeds. He felt the warm sun shining, heard the lark singing, and saw that spring had returned beautifully all around him. The young bird realized his wings were strong as he flapped them against his sides, lifting himself high into the air. They carried him onward until he unexpectedly found himself in a large garden. The apple trees were in full bloom, and the fragrant elder trees bent their long green branches down to the stream that flowed around a smooth lawn. Everything looked stunning in the freshness of early spring. From a nearby thicket, three beautiful white swans emerged, rustling their feathers and swimming gracefully over the calm water. The duckling recognized the lovely birds, feeling strangely unhappy again.

"I’ll go to those royal birds," he thought. "They might kill me because I’m so ugly and dare to approach them; but it doesn’t matter: better to be killed by them than to be pecked by ducks, beaten by hens, pushed around by the girl who feeds the poultry, or starve during winter."

So he swam towards the beautiful swans. The moment they saw the stranger, they rushed to meet him with outstretched wings.

"Kill me," said the poor bird, bowing his head to the water and waiting for death.

But what did he see in the clear stream below? His own reflection; no longer a dark, gray bird, ugly and unappealing, but a graceful and stunning swan. Being born in a duck’s nest in a farmyard doesn’t matter if one hatches from a swan's egg. He felt thankful for all his suffering, as it allowed him to appreciate the joy and happiness around him so much more. The great swans swam around the newcomer, gently stroking his neck with their beaks as a sign of welcome.

Moments later, some little children came into the garden and tossed bread and cake into the water.

"Look," cried the youngest, "there’s a new one!" The rest of them cheered and ran to their parents, dancing and clapping their hands, shouting joyfully, "Another swan has arrived! A new one!"

They tossed more bread and cake into the water, saying, "The new one is the most beautiful of all; he looks so young and pretty." The old swans bowed their heads before him.

Feeling shy, he hid his head under his wing; he didn’t know how to respond. He was so happy but not at all proud. After being pursued and ridiculed for his ugliness, now he heard them say he was the most beautiful of all birds. Even the elder tree bent its branches into the water before him, and the sun shone warm and bright. Then he ruffled his feathers, arched his slender neck, and joyfully cried from the bottom of his heart, "I never dreamed I could experience such happiness when I was just an ugly duckling!"

title: THE WICKED PRINCE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once upon a time, there was a wicked prince whose heart and mind were fixated on conquering every country in the world and instilling fear in its people. He ravaged lands with fire and sword, trampling crops underfoot and burning down peasants' homes until flames devoured the green leaves and left the fruit shriveled on the blackened branches. Many poor mothers fled with their naked babies in their arms, seeking refuge behind the still-smoldering walls of their cottages. But the soldiers pursued them, and when they caught up, those mothers became new prey for their cruel pleasures; no demons could have done worse than these soldiers! The prince believed all of this was justified, thinking it was just the natural order of things. Each day, his power grew, his name struck fear in hearts, and fortune smiled upon his deeds.

He brought back incredible riches from the towns he conquered, amassing treasures in his palace that were unmatched anywhere. He built grand palaces, churches, and halls, and everyone who saw these magnificent structures and immense wealth marveled, saying, "What a mighty prince!" Yet they had no idea about the endless suffering he had inflicted on other nations, nor did they hear the sighs and cries rising from the ruins of the destroyed cities.

The prince often gazed happily at his gold and splendid buildings, thinking, like everyone else, "What a mighty prince! But I need more—much more. No power on earth should equal mine, let alone surpass it."

He waged war against all his neighbors and defeated them. The conquered kings were shackled in golden chains behind his chariot as he paraded through the streets of his city. These kings had to kneel at his feet and his courtiers’ at mealtimes, surviving on the scraps they left behind. Eventually, the prince had his own statue erected in public squares and on the royal palaces. He even wanted it placed in churches on the altars, but the priests resisted, saying, "Prince, you are indeed powerful, but God's power is far greater than yours; we cannot follow your orders."

title: THE WICKED PRINCE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Well," replied the prince, "then I will conquer God too." In his arrogance and foolish pride, he ordered the construction of a magnificent ship that could sail through the sky. It was beautifully decorated in vibrant colors, adorned with thousands of eyes, which were actually cannon barrels. The prince sat in the center of the ship, ready to release a barrage of bullets by simply touching a button, while the cannons reloaded automatically. Hundreds of eagles were attached to this vessel, and it soared swiftly towards the sun. The ground soon vanished below, reducing the earth to a patchwork of fields and meadows, until it faded into mist and clouds. Higher and higher, the eagles climbed into the sky, when suddenly, God sent one of his countless angels to confront the ship. The wicked prince fired thousands of bullets, but they bounced off the angel's shining wings like hailstones. Just one drop of blood, a single drop from the angel's feathers, landed on the ship, burning into it and weighing it down like a mountain, dragging it rapidly back to earth. The eagles' strong wings faltered, the wind howled around the prince, and the clouds swirling around—were they formed from the smoke of burned cities?—shaped themselves into massive crabs stretching their claws toward him, rising like enormous boulders that unleashed rolling lumps and turned into fire-spitting dragons.

The prince lay half-dead in his ship as it crashed with a terrible thud into the branches of a large tree in the woods.

"I will conquer God!" the prince declared. "I have sworn it: my will must be done!"

He spent seven years building incredible ships to fly through the air, creating darts from the hardest steel to break through heaven’s walls. He gathered warriors from every land, enough to stretch for miles. Just as they boarded the ships and the prince was nearing his own, God sent a swarm of gnats—just a swarm of tiny gnats. They buzzed around the prince, stinging his face and hands. Infuriated, he drew his sword and swung it wildly, only to hit nothing but air. In frustration, he commanded his servants to bring lavish coverings to protect him from the gnats. They obeyed, yet one lonely gnat managed to slip beneath the fabric and sting him in the ear. The pain burned like fire, and the poison surged into his blood. Mad with agony, he ripped off the coverings and his clothes, tossing them aside, and began to dance around in front of his fierce soldiers, who mocked him, the crazy prince who dared fight God but was brought down by a single tiny gnat.

title: THE WILD SWANS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: In a distant land where swallows fly during winter, there lived a king who had eleven sons and one daughter named Eliza. Each of the eleven brothers was a prince, and they attended school with a shining star on their chests and a sword by their sides. They wrote with diamond-tipped pencils on golden slates, quickly mastered their lessons, and read effortlessly, making it clear to everyone that they were royalty. Their sister Eliza, however, sat on a little stool made of glass and had a picture book that cost as much as half a kingdom. Although the children were incredibly happy, this wasn’t destined to last. Their father, the king, married a wicked queen who didn't care for the children at all. They sensed her indifference right from the first day of her marriage. During the palace’s grand celebrations, while the children played pretend, the queen served them sand in a teacup instead of cake, insisting they pretend it was a treat. The following week, she sent little Eliza to stay with a peasant and his wife before telling the king a dozen lies about the young princes that made him dismiss any concerns for them.

title: THE WILD SWANS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "Go out into the world and fend for yourselves," said the queen. "Fly away like dumb birds!" But instead of becoming ugly as she wished, they transformed into eleven magnificent wild swans. With a strange cry, they soared through the palace windows, glided over the park, and headed toward the forest. It was early morning when they flew past the peasant's cottage where Eliza slept. They circled above her house, twisting their long necks and flapping their wings, but no one noticed them. Eventually, they had to fly high into the clouds, traveling across the wide world until they arrived at a dense, dark forest that stretched all the way to the seashore. Meanwhile, poor little Eliza was alone in her room, playing with a green leaf—her only toy. She poked a hole through it and peered through at the sun, imagining it was her brothers’ bright eyes. Warm sunlight kissed her cheeks, reminding her of all their tender kisses. Days passed with a similar rhythm: occasionally, the wind would rustle through the rose bushes, whispering to the flowers, "Who’s prettier than you?" But the roses would shake their heads and reply, "Eliza is!" On Sundays, when the old woman read her hymn book at the cottage door, the wind would flutter the pages and ask, "Who’s more devout than you?" The hymn book would answer, "Eliza." The roses and the hymn book were speaking nothing but the truth. When Eliza turned fifteen and returned home, the queen grew bitter and resentful at her beauty. Deep down, she longed to turn Eliza into a swan like her brothers, but she hesitated because the king still wanted to see his daughter.

title: THE WILD SWANS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
One early morning, the queen entered the marble bath, decorated with soft cushions and beautiful tapestries. She brought three toads with her, kissed one, and said, "When Eliza comes to bathe, sit on her head, so she becomes as foolish as you!" To another, she whispered, "Position yourself on her forehead, so she turns ugly—her father won't recognize her!" And to the last, she murmured, "Rest on her heart, so that she develops wicked desires and suffers from them." She submerged the toads in the clear bathwater, which turned them green immediately. When Eliza came in, the queen helped her undress and enter the bath. As Eliza dipped her head underwater, one toad sat on her hair, another on her forehead, and the last on her chest. But she didn’t seem to notice them. When she emerged, three red poppies floated on the surface. If the toads hadn’t been enchanted or kissed by the witch, they would have transformed into red roses. Regardless, they bloomed into flowers because of their brief resting on Eliza’s head and heart. She was pure and innocent enough that witchcraft couldn’t harm her. When the wicked queen saw this, she smeared her face with walnut juice to make it brown; she tangled Eliza's lovely hair and coated it with a foul ointment, making it impossible to recognize the beautiful girl.

When her father saw her, he was horrified and declared she was not his daughter. Only the watchdog and the swallows recognized her, and they couldn’t say a word. Poor Eliza wept, missing her eleven brothers. Heartbroken, she left the palace and wandered all day across fields and moors until she reached the vast forest. Uncertain of where to go, she felt desperate and longed for her brothers, who had also been cast out into the world. Not long after she entered the woods, night fell, and she lost the path. She lay down on soft moss, offered her evening prayer, and rested her head against a tree stump. The night was still, and the cool, gentle breeze caressed her forehead. Hundreds of glowworms twinkled in the grass and moss like little green stars, and when she lightly touched a twig, the glowing insects fell around her like shooting stars.

title: THE WILD SWANS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: All night long, she dreamed of her brothers, returning to childhood days when they played together. She imagined them writing with diamond-tipped pencils on their golden slates while she looked at her beautiful picture book. Instead of writing letters, they penned tales of the noble deeds they had accomplished and all the adventures they had experienced. In her vivid dreams, everything in the picture book came to life. The birds sang, and characters stepped out to speak with Eliza and her brothers. Yet, as the pages turned, they rushed back to their places, ensuring everything remained in order.

title: THE WILD SWANS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
When she finally awoke, the sun was high in the sky, but she couldn’t see it because the towering trees spread their leaves thickly above her. Still, beams of sunlight danced through the branches, creating a golden glow. The sweet scent of fresh greenery filled the air, and birds almost alighted on her shoulders. She heard the gentle sound of water bubbling from springs that flowed into a lake with golden sands. Dense bushes surrounded this lake, but a deer had made a path that allowed Eliza to reach the water’s edge. The lake was so clear that, had it not been for the wind shaking the branches, it would have seemed as though the reflections were painted on its surface. Every leaf mirrored perfectly in the water, be it in shadow or sunlight. When Eliza finally glimpsed her own reflection, she was startled by how brown and unattractive she appeared. But as she wet her hand and rubbed her eyes and forehead, her fair skin shone again. After undressing and immersing herself in the fresh water, she became the most beautiful princess anyone could find. Once she dressed and braided her long hair, she went to the bubbling spring and drank water from her cupped hand. Then, without knowing where she was headed, she wandered deep into the forest. She thought of her brothers, confident that God would never abandon her. After all, God was the one who made wild apples grow in the woods to feed hungry wanderers and had guided her to a heavily laden tree. She sat beneath it, grateful for her midday meal. Then she ventured into the darkest parts of the forest. It was so quiet that she could hear her own footsteps and the crunch of every dry leaf underfoot. No birds were visible, and no sunlight pierced through the dense foliage. The trees grew so close together that it felt like she was in a woven trellis. She had never experienced such solitude before. That night was pitch black, and not a single glowworm lit the moss.

With a heavy heart, she lay down to sleep. After a while, she felt as if the branches of the trees parted above her and that gentle angelic eyes looked down from heaven. Upon awakening in the morning, she wasn't sure if it had been a dream or reality. Continuing her journey, she hadn’t walked far when she came across an old woman carrying a basket of berries. The woman offered her some to eat, and Eliza asked if she had seen any princes riding through the forest.

title: THE WILD SWANS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "No," replied the old woman, "but yesterday I saw eleven swans with golden crowns swimming on the river not far from here." She guided Eliza a bit further to a gentle slope leading down to a small river. The trees along the banks stretched their leafy branches across the water, intertwining like long arms, and where they couldn't meet, their roots pulled away from the earth to mingle. Eliza thanked the old woman and walked along the flowing river until she reached the open sea. Before her eyes lay the majestic ocean, but not a single sail was in sight—no boat to be found. How was she to move forward? She noticed how the countless pebbles lining the shore had been smoothed and rounded by the water’s embrace. Glass, metal, stones—everything mixed together had been shaped by the same force, feeling as smooth, or even smoother, than her delicate hand. "The water keeps rolling in without pause," she said, "until all that’s rough becomes smooth; I must persevere in my efforts too. Thank you, rolling waves; my heart knows you will lead me to my dear brothers." Spotting eleven swan feathers among the frothy seaweed, she gathered them and placed them together. Drops of water rested on them, but whether they were dew or tears was uncertain. Alone as she was on the beach, she hardly noticed, since the ever-changing sea revealed more transformations in a few hours than a lake would show in an entire year. When a heavy black cloud rolled in, it was as if the sea declared, "I can be wild and furious too!" But then, when the wind died down, and the clouds shifted with the sun's crimson rays, the sea looked like a delicate rose leaf. Even when the calm white surface seemed still, there was an ever-present rhythm as the waves ebbed and flowed like a child's gentle breath. As the sun began to set, Eliza spotted eleven white swans with golden crowns soaring downwards, one by one, like a long white ribbon. She hurried down the gentle slope and hid behind some bushes. The swans landed close to her and fluttered their impressive wings. As soon as the sun dipped below the horizon, their feathers shed, transforming into eleven handsome princes, her brothers. With a loud cry, she recognized them instantly despite their change. She jumped into their arms, calling each of their names. Joy flooded over the princes as they reunited with their beloved sister, marveling at how she had grown tall and beautiful. They laughed and cried, quickly understanding the cruel actions of their mother. "We brothers," the eldest shared, "are cursed to roam as wild swans during daylight; when the sun sets, we regain our human form. We must always find a place to rest before sunset, or if we’re still airborne when we change, we would sink into the sea. We don’t live here but in a land equally beautiful beyond the ocean. We have no islands along our way to rest, just a small rock rising from the water barely enough for us to stand together. If the sea is rough, waves crash over us, yet we thank God for that rock; it's allowed us to survive many nights or we’d never reach home. We’re permitted to visit our fatherland once a year for eleven days, flying across the forest to see the palace of our birth and the church where our mother rests. This land feels like our home, and it’s drawn us back to you, our dear sister. We have just two days left here before we must fly to a beautiful place that isn’t our home; how can we bring you with us? We don’t have a ship or boat."

title: THE WILD SWANS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "How can I break this spell?" Eliza asked. They spent nearly the entire night discussing it, only dozing off for a few hours. At dawn, Eliza awoke to the sound of the swans’ wings as they soared above, and her brothers were again transformed into swans, flying in larger and larger circles until they disappeared from view. But one of them, the youngest swan, stayed behind, resting his head in Eliza’s lap while she gently stroked his wings. They spent the whole day together. As evening approached, the rest returned, and when the sun set, they regained their human forms. "Tomorrow," one said, "we’ll have to leave and won’t come back for a year. But we can’t leave you here. Do you have the courage to come with us? My arms are strong enough to carry you through the woods, and our wings can lift you over the sea!"

title: THE WILD SWANS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "Yes, take me with you," Eliza replied. They spent the entire night weaving a sturdy net from soft willow and rushes. It was large and strong. Eliza laid down on the net, and at sunrise, when her brothers turned back into wild swans, they lifted the net with their beaks and flew high into the clouds, carrying their beloved sister who was still asleep. Sunbeams danced on her face, so one of the swans flew above her, using his broad wings to shield her from the rays. They were far from land when Eliza woke, feeling as though she must still be dreaming. It felt so surreal, soaring high over the sea. Beside her was a branch full of delicious ripe berries and a bundle of sweet roots that the youngest brother had gathered for her. Gratefully, she smiled at him, knowing he was the swan who had shielded her. They flew so high that a large ship below looked like a tiny sea gull skimming the water. A great cloud hung behind them, resembling a gigantic mountain, and on it, Eliza saw their shadows—hers and the eleven swans—enormous against the backdrop. The scene was more stunning than anything she had ever witnessed, but as the sun ascended and the clouds drifted away, the shadows faded. They flew across the sky all day like a winged arrow, slower than usual since they were carrying Eliza. Dark stormy weather approached, and Eliza watched with anxiety as the sinking sun slipped below the horizon, still no land in sight. She felt the swans struggling to keep up the pace, and guilt gnawed at her—was she the reason for their troubles? When the sun set, they would turn back into men and fall into the sea. Eliza prayed from the depths of her heart for guidance, but the rock still hadn’t appeared. Gathering dark clouds drew nearer, and gusts of wind warned of an impending storm. Lightning flashed violently from the thick, heavy clouds. Just as the sun kissed the water, the swans plummeted downward so rapidly that Eliza felt her head spin; she thought they were crashing but they soared higher again. Soon, she spotted the rock below. By this time, the sun was halfway hidden in the waves. The rock looked no larger than a seal's head peeking out of the water. They dove down swiftly, and as their feet touched the rock, it shone like a star, vanishing like the last spark from a burned piece of paper. Eliza saw her brothers tightly gathered around her, their arms linked. There was barely enough room for them, no space left at all. The sea splashed against the rock, drenching them in spray. The sky crackled with lightning and the rolling thunder echoed. Yet, the siblings held hands tightly, singing hymns that brought them hope and courage. At dawn, the air grew calm and still, and at sunrise, the swans took flight once more with Eliza. The sea remained choppy, and from their lofty vantage point, the breaking waves appeared like numerous swans gliding on the dark green water. As the sun rose higher, Eliza beheld mountains floating in the air, their peaks capped with shining ice. In the center stood an enormous castle that seemed to stretch for miles, adorned with columns rising tier upon tier, while around it, palm trees swayed and flowers bloomed as large as wheelbarrows. Curious, she asked if they were heading towards that land. The swans shook their heads; what she saw were the ever-changing cloud castles of "Fata Morgana," which no human could ever enter. Eliza kept gazing at the view until mountains, forests, and castles faded and were replaced by twenty majestic churches, tall towers and pointed Gothic windows. She fancied she heard organ music but realized it was simply the sea’s soothing sounds. As they flew closer to the churches, they morphed into a fleet of ships sailing below her. Yet a second glance revealed it was merely a shimmering mist gliding over the ocean. The scenery before her eyes shifted so continually, until finally she saw the real land they were destined for—a land with blue mountains, cedar forests, and its cities and palaces. Long before sunset, she settled on a rock in front of a grand cave, where lush climbing plants carpeted the floor like an exquisite tapestry. "Now we’ll see what you dream about tonight," her youngest brother said, showing her to her room.

title: THE WILD SWANS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "Heaven, grant that I may dream of how to save you," she replied. This thought consumed her as she fervently prayed to God for guidance, even as she slept, her prayers continued. In her dreams, she soared high towards the cloudy palace of "Fata Morgana," where a radiant fairy resembling the old woman from the woods greeted her. "You can free your brothers," the fairy said, "if you possess courage and determination. Although water feels softer than your delicate hands, it can polish stones. Water feels no pain, unlike what you'll endure. Do you see the stinging nettle I’m holding? Similar nettles thrive in the cave where you sleep, but only the ones growing in graveyards will serve you. Pluck those while they sting your skin. Crush them with your hands and feet, and they’ll turn into flax, from which you must weave eleven long-sleeved coats. Throw these coats over the eleven swans to break the spell. But remember, from the start of this task until it's completed—no matter how long it takes—you mustn’t speak. One word will pierce your brothers' hearts like a dagger, and their lives depend on your silence. Keep all this in mind.” As she finished, she lightly touched Eliza’s hand with the nettle, setting fire to her skin.

title: THE WILD SWANS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
When daylight broke, just beside where she had slept lay a nettle like the one from her dream. She knelt down and thanked God. Then she ventured out of the cave to begin her work with her fragile hands. Braving the painful nettles that left blisters on her skin, she resolved to endure for the sake of her brothers. Crushing the nettles with her feet, she spun the flax. When her brothers returned at sundown, they were alarmed to find her silent; they feared she had fallen victim to their mother's curse again. Spotting her hands, they quickly realized what she was sacrificing for them, and the youngest brother wept. Where his tears fell, the pain eased, and the blisters faded. She worked tirelessly through the night, determined to free her dear brothers. The next day, while they were away, she sat alone, but time flew faster than ever. One coat was finished, and she was starting on the next when she heard the huntsman's horn, and fear gripped her. The sound drew nearer, and she could hear the dogs barking, so she fled in terror into the cave. She hurriedly gathered the remaining nettles and sat on the bundle. Within moments, a large dog bounded toward her from the ravine, barking loudly, followed by more. Soon the huntsmen gathered outside, led by the king himself, who stepped forward, having never seen a maiden as beautiful as she.

"How did you end up here, my sweet child?" he asked. But Eliza only shook her head. She couldn’t speak, knowing it could cost her brothers their lives. She hid her hands under her apron so he wouldn’t see her suffering.

title: THE WILD SWANS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Come with me," he insisted, "you can’t stay here. If you possess as much goodness as beauty, I’ll dress you in silk and velvet, crown you in gold, and you shall rule from my finest castle." He lifted her onto his horse. She wept and wrung her hands, while the king said, "I only want your happiness. You’ll thank me for this one day." As they galloped away over the mountains, the hunters followed closely. As the sun began to set, they approached a splendid royal city with churches and domes. Once at the castle, the king led her into marble halls adorned with ornate fountains and lavish paintings. But Eliza was too consumed with grief to notice any of it. She allowed the attendants to dress her in royal garments, weave pearls into her hair, and cover her blistered fingers with soft gloves. Standing among them, she looked so stunningly beautiful that the entire court bowed before her. The king declared his intentions to marry her, but the archbishop shook his head, whispering that she was merely a witch who had bewitched the king's eyes and heart. Still, the king was unwavering; he called for musicians and exquisite food to be served while the fairest maidens danced. Afterward, he toured her through fragrant gardens and grand halls, yet not once did a smile grace her lips or sparkle in her eyes. She wore a mask of sadness. The king opened the door to her sleeping chamber, decorated with rich green tapestries reminiscent of the cave where he found her. On the floor lay the bundle of flax she had spun, and dangling from the ceiling hung the coat she had made. A huntsman had collected them as curiosities.

"Here, you can dream back to the old cave," said the king. "You’ll find entertainment in remembering those simpler days."

title: THE WILD SWANS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Seeing those cherished items so close to her heart, a smile graced Eliza’s lips, and rosy color returned to her cheeks. Thoughts of her brothers flooded her mind, and the prospect of their release filled her with joy, prompting her to kiss the king's hand. Instinctively, he pulled her close. Soon, jubilant church bells rang, announcing the wedding celebration, proclaiming that the enchanting mute girl from the woods would become the queen. The archbishop, meanwhile, whispered malicious accusations to the king, but they failed to resonate with him. The marriage preparations continued, and even the archbishop had to place the crown on her head. In a fit of petty spite, he pressed the narrow circlet tightly onto her forehead, causing her pain. However, a deeper ache encircled her heart—a sorrow for her brothers. Though she felt no physical discomfort, her spirit ached. With one simple word, she could save her brothers' lives. But she was so deeply in love with the kind and handsome king, who tirelessly tried to make her happy day by day. Her affection for him blossomed, shining through her eyes, though she kept her secret burdens locked away. If only she could confide in him, expressing her pain! But silence was her fate until her task was complete. Thus, at night, she escaped to her cozy chamber, adorned to resemble the cave, and quickly wove one coat after another. When she started the seventh, dread enveloped her as she realized she had run out of flax. She knew that the nettles she needed grew in the graveyard, and she had to gather them herself. How would she accomplish this? "What are my hand’s pains compared to the torment in my heart?" she questioned. "I must take the risk; heaven will surely aid me." That night, with trembling resolve, she crept into the garden under moonlight, navigating the narrow paths and deserted streets until she reached the churchyard. To her horror, she saw a band of ghouls atop one of the gravestones. These ghoulish figures shed their rags, as if preparing to bathe, then clawed open freshly interred graves to feast on the remains! Eliza had to walk past them, and their wicked gazes fell upon her, but she silently prayed and bravely gathered the burning nettles to carry back to the castle. Only the archbishop witnessed her, awake while everyone else was asleep. Convinced that something was awry with the queen, he decided she was a witch, having ensorcelled the king and the people. Quietly, he informed the king of his suspicions, and as he spoke the harsh words, the carved figures of saints seemed to shake their heads knowingly to say, "This isn’t true; Eliza is innocent."

title: THE WILD SWANS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: Yet the archbishop spun the words differently, believing that the saints were witnessing her wrongdoings. Two large tears streamed down the king's cheeks as he returned home, burdened by doubt. That night, he pretended to sleep; still, true rest eluded him as he observed Eliza sneaking out every night. Day by day, he grew more suspicious, and Eliza sensed his distrust, which alarmed her and made her heart ache for her brothers. Her tears fell like pearls onto her regal attire, while others enviously longed to be queens. Meanwhile, she was close to finishing her task; only one coat remained, yet she had no flax left, not a single nettle. For one last time, she needed to venture into the churchyard for just a couple handfuls. Terrified of the graveyard and the phantoms, she steeled herself with faith in a higher power. Eliza set out, and the king and archbishop followed her. They saw her disappear through the gate into the graveyard. As they approached, they found the ghouls she had encountered earlier. The king turned his head away, believing she was involved with them—she who had lain on his breast just the evening before. "The people must judge her," he decided, and she was swiftly condemned to burn at the stake. They led her away from the extravagant royal halls to a dark, dreary cell, where the wind whistled through iron bars. Instead of the velvet and silk gowns, they clothed her in the coats of mail she had woven, using the nettles for a pillow—but nothing else could please her more than that. She pressed on with her work, praying for help while the street boys mocked her with their songs, and no one offered her a kind word. In the early evening, she heard the rustle of a swan’s wings at the grating—it was her youngest brother! He had found his sister, and despite her immense joy, she recognized that this could be her last night alive. Nonetheless, hope persisted, for she was nearly done, and her brothers were by her side. The archbishop came as promised to be with her at the end, but she begged silently for him to leave; she knew she must finish her work that night or else all her tears and sleepless nights would be for nothing. The stubborn archbishop retreated, growling words against her while Eliza focused on her task.

title: THE WILD SWANS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Mice scurried around the floor, dragging nettles to her feet in a show of support. Outside her barred window, a thrush sang sweet melodies to keep her spirits high throughout the night.

Before dawn, at least an hour before sunrise, the eleven brothers arrived at the castle gate, demanding to be seen by the king. The guards informed them it was too dark and they couldn’t disturb the king’s sleep. They shouted and pleaded, alarming others until the guards appeared, followed by the king who wanted to know what all the commotion was. Just then, the sun began to rise. The eleven brothers vanished, and eleven wild swans took flight over the castle.

As a crowd gathered outside the city’s gates, eager to witness the witch's execution, an aging horse pulled the cart carrying her. Dressed in coarse sackcloth, her beautiful hair fell loose around her shoulders. Her cheeks turned ghostly pale, and her lips moved soundlessly as her fingers continued weaving the flax. Even on the path to her death, she refused to abandon her task. The finished ten coats lay at her feet while she diligently worked on the last one.
