The crowd taunted her, chanting, "Look at the witch muttering! She has no hymnbook! Let’s tear her apart!"

The mob surged forward, intent on destroying the coats, but at that moment, eleven wild swans flew overhead, landing on the cart. They flapped their massive wings, causing the crowd to step back in fear.

"It’s a sign from heaven! She’s innocent!" many whispered, but none dared say it aloud.

As the executioner grabbed her hand to pull her from the cart, she quickly threw the eleven coats over the swans. In an instant, they transformed back into eleven handsome princes, except for the youngest, who had a swan's wing instead of an arm because she hadn’t completed the final sleeve.

"Now I can speak," she exclaimed. "I am innocent!"

The crowd who witnessed the transformation worshiped her like a saint, but Eliza collapsed, overwhelmed with anxiety, anguish, and pain, falling lifeless into her brothers' arms. "She is indeed innocent," said the eldest brother, recounting everything that had transpired. As he spoke, a sweet fragrance filled the air, reminiscent of blooming roses. Every stick in the fire pit took root, growing into a high hedge covered in thick rose bushes, crowned by a single shining white flower that sparkled like a star. The king picked the flower and placed it in Eliza’s bosom, and she awoke from her faint with peace and joy flooding her heart. The church bells rang joyfully, and a flock of birds filled the sky. A grand wedding procession made its way back to the castle, celebrating like never before.

title: THE WILL-O-THE WISP IS IN THE TOWN, SAYS THE MOOR WOMAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once there was a man who used to know many stories, but they had slipped away from him—or so he claimed. The Story that used to visit him without fail had stopped coming to knock at his door. Why had it stopped? It's true that for days and years, he hadn't thought about it or expected its arrival; and if he had expected it, it definitely wouldn't have come. Outside, there was war, and inside, there was the worry and sorrow that war always brings.

The stork and the swallows returned from their long journey, believing there was no danger. But when they arrived, they found the nest burned, the homes of people in ashes, the hedges disheveled, and everything seemed lost. The enemy's horses trod upon forgotten graves. Those were tough, dark times, but they would eventually come to an end.

People said those times were now behind them—but still, no Story came to knock at the door or provide any signs of its presence.

"I guess it must be dead, or maybe it just left along with many other things," the man said.

But stories never die. Over a whole year passed, and he missed the Story oh so much!

"I wonder if the Story will ever come back and knock again?"

He remembered it vividly in all the different forms it had taken throughout the years—sometimes young and vibrant, like the essence of spring, sometimes as a stunning maiden with thyme in her hair, a beech branch in her hand, and eyes that sparkled like deep lakes in the sun.

Other times, it appeared as a traveling peddler, opening its box to reveal silver ribbons fluttering out, lined with old verses and memories.

But it was the most enchanting when it took the form of an old grandmother with silvery hair and wise, large eyes. She had a knack for recounting tales from long ago—before princesses spun with golden spindles and dragons guarded castles. Her stories held such a ring of truth that black spots danced before the eyes of those who listened. They were haunting, filled with human blood—a shocking sight and sound, yet so captivating because so much time had passed since those events.

title: THE WILL-O-THE WISP IS IN THE TOWN, SAYS THE MOOR WOMAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Will it ever knock at my door again?" he wondered, staring at the door until black spots clouded his vision; he couldn't tell if it was blood or mourning cloth from those dark days.

As he sat there, a thought struck him: maybe the Story had hidden itself away, like a princess in an old tale. He decided he would go looking for it; if he found it, it would shine again, more beautiful than ever.

"Who knows? Maybe it’s hidden in the straw that balances on the edge of the well. Carefully, carefully! Perhaps it lies within a special flower—like the flower in one of the big books on my shelf."

The man opened one of the newest books to find out, but there was no flower to discover. He read about Holger Danske and found that a monk in France had invented that tale, which was a romance, "translated into Danish and published in that language;" that Holger Danske never existed, and so could never return, as had been sung and cherished. William Tell was treated the same way. These were mere myths—nothing to rely on—but it was all written in a very learned book.

"Well, I’ll believe what I choose!" replied the man. "Nothing grows where no one has walked."

He closed the book, placed it back on the shelf, and moved to the fresh flowers by the window. Maybe the Story had hidden among the vibrant red tulips with golden edges, the fresh rose, or the brightly blooming camellia. The sunshine danced among the flowers, but there was no Story.

The flowers that thrived during those dark times had been far more beautiful, but one by one, they had been cut down to make wreaths for coffins, with flags flying over them. Maybe the Story had been buried with those flowers, but the flowers would have known, and the coffin would have heard it, and every blade of grass would have revealed it. The Story never dies.

Maybe it had come before and knocked but who had eyes or ears for it during those tough times? People gazed darkly and angrily at the spring sunshine, at the singing birds, and all the cheerful green around them. Their tongues couldn’t bear the old joyful songs—they were placed in a coffin along with so much that the heart held dear. The Story might have knocked but received no warm welcome, and so it left.

title: THE WILL-O-THE WISP IS IN THE TOWN, SAYS THE MOOR WOMAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I’ll go out and find it. Out in the country! In the woods! On the open beach!"

Out in the countryside lies an old manor house, with red walls and pointed gables, topped with a red flag waving from the tower. The nightingale sings among the delicately fringed beech leaves, gazing at the blooming apple trees in the garden, believing they bear roses. Here, during the summer, bees buzz busily around their queen. Autumn brings tales of wild chases, fallen leaves, and races of men that are vanishing together. Wild swans sing at Christmas on the open water, while in the old hall, guests by the fireside gladly listen to songs and old legends.

The man searching for the Story wandered into the older part of the garden, where the grand avenue of wild chestnut trees tempted passersby into its shady embrace. Here, the wind had once whispered to him about 'Waldemar Daa and his Daughters.' The Dryad in the tree, who was the very essence of the Story, had once shared with him the 'Dream of the Old Oak Tree.' In the days of the first ancestors, well-kept hedges stood here, but now only ferns and nettles ruled the ground, hiding remnants of sculpted figures. Moss had covered their eyes, but they still saw clearly—unlike the man, who couldn’t find the Story anywhere. Where could it be?

Crows flew past him in flocks, cawing, "Krah! da!—Krah! da!"

He left the garden and crossed the grassy yard into the alder grove, where a little six-sided house stood, complete with a coop and duck yard. Inside sat an old woman who managed everything, knowing exactly about every egg laid and every chick that emerged. But she was not the Story he sought, as she could confirm with a baptism certificate tucked away in her drawer.

Not far from the house was a hill covered in red-thorn and broom. An old gravestone lay there, brought from the churchyard of a provincial town—remembrance of one of the most respected councilors there; his wife and five daughters, all with folded hands and stiff ruffs, stood around him. One could gaze at them for so long that their thoughts began to echo back at them from the stones, as if they were sharing memories of old times. At least, that was how it felt to the man searching for the Story.

title: THE WILL-O-THE WISP IS IN THE TOWN, SAYS THE MOOR WOMAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
As he drew closer, he noticed a living butterfly sitting on the forehead of the sculpted councilor. It fluttered its wings, flying a little farther before returning, tired, to land on the gravestone, as if pointing out what was growing there. Four-leaved shamrocks thrived; he found seven clumped together. Good fortune truly comes in bunches. He plucked the shamrocks and tucked them into his pocket.

"Fortune is nice, but a new captivating story would be even better," he mused, but he couldn’t find it there.

The sun set, round and large, as mist enveloped the meadow. The moor woman was busy brewing.

It was evening. He stood alone in his room, gazing out over the sea, meadow, moor, and coast. The moon shone brightly, and a mist covered the meadow, making it appear like a vast lake. Legends claimed it had once been so, and in the moonlight, he envisioned those myths.

Then he thought about what he had read in town—that William Tell and Holger Danske never truly existed, yet they lived on in stories, like the lake before him, a living testament to such myths. Yes, Holger Danske will return!

As he pondered, something suddenly thudded against the window. Was it a bird, a bat, or an owl? Those creatures don’t get let in, even when they knock. The window swung open by itself, and an old woman peeked in at the man.

"What do you want?" he asked. "Who are you? Why are you looking in at my first-floor window? Are you standing on a ladder?"

"You've got a four-leaf shamrock in your pocket," she replied. "In fact, you have seven, and one of them is six-leaved."

"I'm the Moor-woman," she continued. "The Moor-woman who brews. I was working when one of the mischievous little moor imps pulled the bung from the cask and flung it into the yard, causing it to bang against the window. Now the beer’s spilling out of the cask, which isn’t good for anyone."

title: THE WILL-O-THE WISP IS IN THE TOWN, SAYS THE MOOR WOMAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Just wait a moment," the Moor-woman responded. "I have other things to do right now." And just like that, she vanished.

The man was about to close the window when she appeared before him once again.

"Now it’s finished," she said. "But I’ll have to brew half the beer again tomorrow if the weather permits. Well, what do you want to ask me? I’ve come back because I always keep my word, and you have seven four-leaf shamrocks in your pocket, one of which is six-leaved. That’s impressive because that’s a plant that grows along sandy paths; not everyone finds them. What do you want to know? Don’t just stand there like a silly fool, I have to return quickly to my bung and my cask."

The man inquired about the Story, asking if the Moor-woman had encountered it during her travels.

"By the big brewing barrel!" the woman exclaimed, "haven’t you heard enough stories? I believe most folks have enough already. There are more important things to focus on, different things to examine. Even the kids have moved past that. Give the little boy a cigar, and the little girl a new dress; they prefer that! Listening to stories? No, there are far more pressing matters to attend to!"

"What do you mean?" the man asked. "What do you know about the world? All you see are frogs and Will-o'-the-Wisps!"

"Yes, beware of the Will-o'-the-Wisps," the Moor-woman cautioned, "because they’re out—let loose—that’s what we need to talk about! Come meet me in the moor, where my presence is essential, and I’ll tell you everything; but you must hurry and come while your seven four-leaf shamrocks, especially the six-leaved one, are still fresh and the moon is high!"

It struck twelve in town, and just before the last chime faded, the man was out in the yard, in the garden, and standing in the meadow. The mist had disappeared, and the Moor-woman stopped her brewing.

title: THE WILL-O-THE WISP IS IN THE TOWN, SAYS THE MOOR WOMAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"You took your sweet time!" the Moor-woman said. "Witches always get there faster than men, and I'm glad to be one of the witch folk!"

"What do you want to share with me now?" the man asked. "Is it something about the Story?"

"Can you ever stop asking about that?" the woman shot back.

"Can you tell me anything about the poetry of the future?" the man persisted.

"Don’t get all high and mighty with me," the crone replied, "and I’ll give you an answer. All you think about is poetry, constantly needing to know about that Story, as if she's the queen of everything. She’s the oldest among us but seems to overshadow the younger ones. I know her well. I was once a lovely elf-maiden, dancing in moonlight, listening to the nightingale, wandering in forests and encountering the Story-maiden who was always out running about. Sometimes she’d rest for the night in a half-bloomed tulip, or within a flower in the fields; other times, she’d sneak into the church, wrapping herself in the mourning cloth that hung down from the altar candles."

"You really know your stuff," the man remarked.

"I should know at least as much as you," the Moor-woman replied. "Stories and poetry—yeah, they're like two yards of fabric; they can lie wherever they want, and one can brew all their chatter—which is cheaper and easier to manage. I have plenty of poetry stored in bottles. It creates essences—that's the best part! I have everything people want from poetry, kept in bottles, so I can dab a bit on my handkerchief for special occasions to smell it."

"Wow, that’s incredible!" the man exclaimed. "You really have poetry in bottles?"

"More than you’ll ever need," the woman answered. "You must know the story of 'the Girl who Trod on the Loaf so she wouldn’t dirty her shoes.' That’s been written down and published too."

title: THE WILL-O-THE WISP IS IN THE TOWN, SAYS THE MOOR WOMAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I shared that story myself," said the man.

"Well, then you must know it; and you should also know that the girl sank into the earth straight to the Moor-woman, just as Old Bogey’s grandmother was visiting the brewery. She caught sight of the girl slipping down and decided to take her as a souvenir from her visit. Meanwhile, I received a gift that’s useless to me—a traveling druggist’s shop—a cupboard full of poetry in bottles. Grandmother instructed me where to place the cupboard, and it still stands there. Just look! You have your seven four-leaf shamrocks in your pocket, including the six-leaved one, so you’ll be able to see it."

Indeed, in the middle of the moor lay what looked like a massive knotted block of alder, and that was the old grandmother’s cupboard. The Moor-woman explained that it was always accessible to her and to anyone in the land, if they just knew where it was located. It could be opened from the front, back, sides, and corners—a masterpiece of craftsmanship—though it appeared just like an old alder stump. Poets from everywhere, especially from our own land, had their works arranged here; their spirits were extracted, refined, criticized, and then stored in bottles. With a certain flair—or perhaps genius—the grandmother had taken the essence of various poets, added a touch of mischief, and corked the bottles for use in times to come.

"That’s fascinating! But there are more important things to hear," the Moor-woman responded.

"But now we’ve reached the cupboard!" the man said, looking inside. "Here are bottles of all shapes. What’s in this one? And what about that one over there?"

"This one contains what they call may-balm," the woman remarked. "I haven’t tried it myself. However, I haven’t yet told you the important news you needed to hear. THE WILL-O'-THE-WISP'S IN TOWN! That matters far more than poetry or stories. I really should keep quiet, but something is pressing on me, something that demands to come out. Be cautious, you mortals!"

title: THE WILL-O-THE WISP IS IN THE TOWN, SAYS THE MOOR WOMAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"I don’t understand any of this!" the man exclaimed.

"Please take a seat on that cupboard," she shot back, "but be careful not to fall through and break the bottles; you know what’s inside them. I must share this crucial event. It happened just the day before yesterday. It didn’t happen earlier, and it has now three hundred sixty-three days to make its rounds. You do know how many days make up a year, don’t you?"

"There was quite a stir out here in the marsh yesterday! There was a christening! A little Will-o'-the-Wisp was born here—actually, twelve of them were born at once! They have permission, if they choose, to mingle among humans, acting just like they were born mortals. That was a major event in the marsh, and, of course, all the Will-o'-the-Wisps, both male and female, began dancing like little lights across the moor. Some even belong to the dog family, but they’re not worth mentioning. I sat there on the cupboard, with all twelve newborn Will-o'-the-Wisps on my lap. They sparkled like glow-worms. They began to hop and grew in size every minute; before long, each one matched the size of their father or uncle. Now, there’s an old tradition that when the moon is in the same position it was yesterday, and the wind blows just as it did then, all Will-o'-the-Wisps born at that moment can become mortals, and for a year, they can have their own influence.

title: THE WILL-O-THE WISP IS IN THE TOWN, SAYS THE MOOR WOMAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"The Will-o'-the-Wisp can dance through the country and the world, as long as it's not afraid of falling into the sea or being blown out by a strong storm. It can inhabit a person, speak for them, and move however it likes. The Will-o'-the-Wisp can take on any form—of man or woman—and can act in their spirit and disguise to accomplish whatever it desires. However, they must manage to lead three hundred sixty-five people astray, in a dramatic fashion, within the year. They must steer them away from truth and righteousness; if they succeed, the Will-o'-the-Wisp can gain the honor of running before the devil’s coach, dressing in fiery yellow garments while breathing flames from its throat. That’s something that a simple Will-o'-the-Wisp would love. But there’s danger involved, and it’s a tough job for one aspiring to such a prominent role. If the man opens his eyes to what the Will-o'-the-Wisp truly is, and if he blows it away, it’s all over for the Wisp, and it must return to the marsh. Or if, before the year ends, the Will-o'-the-Wisp gets the urge to visit its family and returns home, it can no longer shine brightly and will eventually be extinguished, unable to relight again. When the year is up and it hasn’t led three hundred sixty-five people astray from the truth and honorable virtues, it faces imprisonment in rotting wood, flickering there without movement—that’s the worst punishment a lively Will-o'-the-Wisp could endure.

"Now, I know all this, and I shared it with the twelve little Will-o'-the-Wisps on my lap, who were bubbling with excitement.

"I advised them that doing nothing was the simplest route, but the little flames didn’t agree; they already envisioned themselves dressed in fiery clothes, breathing flames.

"'Stay with us,' suggested some of the older ones.

title: THE WILL-O-THE WISP IS IN THE TOWN, SAYS THE MOOR WOMAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"'Join us in our fun with mortals,' urged the others.

"'The mortals are drying out our meadows; they’ve started draining the land. What will our future generation do?'"

"'We want to blaze; we will blaze—flame!' cried the newborn Will-o'-the-Wisps.

"They threw a party, lasting only a minute; it couldn't be any shorter. The little elf maidens twirled with the others three times so as not to seem boastful, but they'd rather dance together.

"Then came the sponsors' gifts, and presents were tossed to them. These gifts flew like pebbles across the water. Each little elf-maiden gave a piece of her veil.

"'Take this,' they said, 'and you’ll learn the higher dance, the most challenging moves and turns—at least if you think you’ll need them. You'll know the right way to present yourself in the best of circles.'"

"The night raven taught each young Will-o'-the-Wisp to say, 'Goo-goo-good!' in just the right way; that’s a big advantage and comes with rewards.

"The owl and the stork—but they deemed it unworthy of mention, so we won’t discuss them.

"King Waldemar’s wild hunt was just sweeping across the moor, and when the great lords caught wind of the celebrations, they sent a couple of handsome dogs that hunt the scent of the wind as gifts; these could carry a few Will-o'-the-Wisps. A pair of old Alpas—spirits skilled in Alp-pressing—were also present at the festivities, and the young Will-o'-the-Wisps learned how to slip through any keyhole, as though the doors were wide open before them. These Alpas offered to take the little ones to the town, a place they knew well. They typically rode through the sky using their own hair, which they knot together for a sturdy seat; but this time, they sat sideways on the wild hunting dogs, carrying the eager young Will-o'-the-Wisps in their laps, all itching to go into town and mislead humans, and away they went! This is what unfolded last night. Today, the Will-o'-the-Wisps are in town, taking action—but where and how? Ah, can you tell me?

title: THE WILL-O-THE WISP IS IN THE TOWN, SAYS THE MOOR WOMAN
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"This sounds like a full-fledged story," the man exclaimed.

"Yes, but it’s just the beginning," she replied. "Can you explain how the Will-o'-the-Wisps behave and in what forms they’ve appeared before, leading people astray?

"I believe," the man replied, "that it would be possible to tell a grand romance about the Will-o'-the-Wisps in twelve parts; or better yet, it could make for an entire popular play."

"You could write that," the woman said, "but it’s better if we leave it alone."

"Yes, that sounds better and much more pleasant," the man agreed, "because then we can avoid the newspapers and not be stuck with them, which feels as confining as it is for a Will-o'-the-Wisp to linger in rotting wood, glowing but unable to move."

"I don’t care either way," the woman exclaimed. "Let those who can write, do so, and those who can’t should also have a go. I’ll give you an old bung from my cask that will open the cupboard holding the poetry in bottles, and you can take whatever you need. But as for you, my good sir, it looks like you’ve stained your hands enough with ink, and you’ve reached that age where you should not be scurrying around every year for stories, especially since there are far more important things to address. You must have realized what’s afoot?"

"The Will-o'-the-Wisp is in town," the man replied. "I’ve heard about it and understood. But what do you think I should do? I’ll get into trouble if I went to the folks and said, 'Look, there goes a Will-o'-the-Wisp in his finest clothes!'"

"They also go in casual attire," she countered. "The Will-o'-the-Wisp can take on all kinds of forms and appear anywhere. It may enter the church, but not for the service; perhaps it may even possess a priest or two. It speaks in Parliament, not for the country's good, but solely for itself. It's a performer with the brush and in the theater—but when it grabs all the power, then the paint pot’s empty! I babble on, but I must let this out, even at the risk of my own family’s pains. Yet, I must now be the one to save many people. I don’t do this for my own pleasure or for a medal; I perform the craziest things I can, then tell a poet about it, and before you know it, the whole town hears!

"The town won’t take that to heart," the man noted; "it won’t disturb anyone since they’ll think I’m just spinning a tale if I say, 'The Will-o'-the-Wisp is in town, says the Moor-woman. Be cautious!'

title: THE WINDMILL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
A windmill perched confidently on the hill, standing tall and proud.

"I’m not proud at all," it said. "But I feel very enlightened, both outside and inside. I have the sun and moon to help me shine, and I’m stocked with stearine candles, train oil, lamps, and tallow candles for my needs. I can honestly say I'm enlightened. I think deeply and am wonderfully constructed; it’s really delightful. I have a good windpipe in my chest, and four wings arranged just beneath my hat. While birds have to carry their two wings on their backs, I’m like a flying Dutchman by birth—though I’m quite natural. I have a gallery around my chest and a cozy space beneath it where my thoughts reside. The strongest thought, the one who rules me, goes by 'The Man in the Mill.' He knows what he wants and is in charge of the meal and bran, but he has a companion who calls herself 'Mother.' She is my very heart. Unlike being frantic or clumsy, she knows her purpose, she knows her capabilities—she’s as gentle as a breeze and as fierce as a storm. She knows how to start things carefully and get her way. She represents my gentle side, while the father represents my tougher side. Together, they are two halves of one whole, calling each other 'My half.' They even have little boys—young thoughts—that are always growing. Those little ones help keep everything organized. Recently, I let the father and the boys check out my throat and the hole in my chest, as I sensed something was off and it’s good to self-examine. The little ones made quite a ruckus. One of the youngest jumped into my hat and yelled until I was tickled. I know those little thoughts can grow, and out in the world, there are other thoughts too—not just mine. As far as I can see, I don’t notice anything quite like me, but those silent houses, with no noise from their throats, have thoughts as well. They mingle with my thoughts in a delightful dance. It’s amazing—something has shifted in me, or maybe in the mill itself. It seems like the father has changed, becoming more patient with a kinder partner—young, gentle, and good, yet still the same. What was once bitter has faded, and now I feel much more at ease.

title: THE WINDMILL
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"The days roll on, getting closer to clarity and joy; there will come a day when I’ll be no more, but not completely lost. To be taken apart so I can be rebuilt; to become something entirely new, yet still keep my essence! It’s a bit challenging for me to grasp, despite all my enlightenment from the sun, moon, stearine, train oil, and tallow. My old woodwork and brickwork will rise again from the dust!"

"I hope to hold on to my old thoughts—the father in the mill, the mother, the big and little ones—all my thoughts. I can’t help but refer to them as my thought family, because it feels right to me."

"And I must stay 'myself,' with my throat inside my chest, my wings on my head, and that gallery around my body; otherwise, I wouldn’t recognize myself, nor would others, and they wouldn’t be able to say, 'Look at the mill on the hill, proudly standing yet not proud at all.'"

That’s what the mill had to say. It said a lot more, but that was the core message.

Days passed, and time kept moving forward until yesterday marked the end.

Then, the mill caught fire. Flames leapt high, dancing in and out, gnawing at the beams and planks, consuming everything. The mill collapsed, leaving nothing but a pile of ash. Smoke billowed across the scene of destruction, whisked away by the wind.

What had once lived in the mill remained, but what had been gained from it isn’t part of this story.

The miller’s family—one soul made up of many thoughts, yet still just one—built a new and magnificent mill that fulfilled its purpose. It looked a lot like the old one, and people exclaimed, "Look, there’s the mill on the hill, proudly standing!" But this mill was better organized, reflecting the times, allowing progress to flourish. The old beams had become rotten and decayed; they lay in dust and ashes. The body of the mill didn’t rise from the dust as many hoped; they took that too literally—and not everything should be taken literally.

title: TWO BROTHERS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
On one of the Danish islands, where ancient Thingstones, the historic seats of justice from our ancestors, still stand in the cornfields, and towering beech trees rise in the forests, there’s a small town with low houses topped with red tiles. Inside one of these homes, something unusual was happening over the glowing coals of the open hearth; liquids were boiling in glass jars, and a mix of herbs were being chopped and ground. An older man supervised it all.

"You just have to do the right thing," he said. "Yes, the right—the correct thing. It’s essential to uncover the truth behind everything and stick to that."

In the room with their caring mother were her two sons. Though they were still young, they had big ideas. Their mother always emphasized the importance of doing what’s right and urged them to stay true to the truth, which she said represented the Lord’s face in the world.

The older boy had a mischievous streak and a sense of adventure. He loved reading about nature's forces, the sun and the moon; no fairy tale could compare. Oh, how magical it would be, he thought, to go on great adventures, or to figure out how to mimic birds' wings and take to the sky! Yes, discovering that was the right thing. His father was right, and so was his mother—truth is what holds everything together.

The younger brother was quieter, completely absorbed in his books. When he read about Jacob disguising himself in sheep-skins to trick Esau and steal his brother's birthright, he would clench his little fist in anger at the deceiver. When he encountered tales of tyrants and the injustices of the world, tears would fill his eyes, and he would be consumed with thoughts of the justice and truth that must prevail.

One evening, he lay in bed, the curtains still drawn open, letting the light pour in. He had smuggled his book into bed because he wanted to finish reading about Solon. His mind took off, carrying him to a wondrous distance; it felt as if his bed had transformed into a ship sailing swiftly along. Was he dreaming, or what was happening? The bed glided over rolling waves and across the ocean of time, and he heard the voice of Solon, spoken in a strange language but clearly understandable to him, echoing the Danish motto: "By law, the land is ruled."

The spirit of humanity hovered in the humble room, leaned over the boy's bed, and kissed his forehead: "Be strong in your fame and courageous in life’s battles! With truth in your heart, soar toward the land of truth!"

The older brother wasn’t in bed yet; he stood at the window, gazing out at the mist rising from the meadows. They weren’t fairies dancing around as their old nurse had said; he knew they were vapor trails warmer than the air that caused them to lift. A shooting star lit up the night sky, and in an instant, his thoughts shifted from Earth’s mist to the brilliant meteor. The stars sparkled above, and it seemed as if long golden threads dangled from them down to Earth.

"Fly with me," sang a voice that the boy felt deep in his heart. And the great genius of humankind, faster than a bird and swifter than an arrow—swifter than anything from our world—carried him away into space, where the stars are bound together by rays connecting them. Our Earth spun in the thin, clear air, and its cities seemed so close together. Through the cosmos echoed the words:

"What is near, what is far, when you are lifted by the mighty mind?"

And again, the boy stood by the window, looking out, while his younger brother lay in bed. Their mother called them by name: "Anders Sandoe" and "Hans Christian."

Denmark, and indeed the entire world, knows them—the two brothers Oersted.

title: TWO MAIDENS
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Have you ever seen a maiden? I’m talking about what the pavers refer to as a maiden—it's the wooden tool they use to pack down paving stones in the roads. This kind of maiden is entirely made of wood, wider at the bottom and surrounded by iron rings. At the top, it tapers down, with a stick going across its waist, making it look like it has arms.

In a shed, there were two Maidens like this. They were surrounded by shovels, hand-carts, wheelbarrows, and measuring tapes. Recently, word had spread that the Maidens were no longer going to be called "maidens," but rather "hand-rammers," which was the latest term the pavers had decided was correct for what everyone used to call "the maiden."

Now, among us humans, there are people known as "emancipated women," like principals of schools, professional dancers who can balance on one leg, milliners, and nurses. These two Maidens in the shed decided that they belonged to this group of liberated women. They were considered "maidens" among the paver community and were determined not to lose this respectable name, refusing to let themselves be called "rammers."

"Maiden is a human name, but hand-rammer is an object, and we won’t be called objects—that’s insulting," one of them declared.

"My fiancé would be ready to break off our engagement," said the younger maiden, who was engaged to a paver’s hammer. The hammer is the tool that drives heavy piles into the ground, similar to how ten maidens work together. "He wants to marry me as a maiden, but I'm not sure he would if I became a hand-rammer. So, I won’t let my name change."

"And I," said the older maiden, "would prefer to have both my arms chopped off!"

However, the Wheelbarrow had a different perspective. It was regarded as somewhat important since it fancied itself a quarter of a coach, rolling around on one wheel.

"I need to point out that the name 'maiden' is quite common and not nearly as elegant as 'hand-rammer' or 'stamper,' which has also been suggested. Being called a stamper would put you in the same category as seals, and just think of the royal seal that validates laws! In your case, I would gladly give up my maiden name," the Wheelbarrow said.

"No way!" exclaimed the elder. "I’m too old for that."

"I assume you’ve never heard of 'European necessity,'" the honest Measuring Tape chimed in. "One must adapt to time and circumstances. If the law states that 'maiden' must now be called 'hand-rammer,' then so be it! No amount of sulking will change that, because everything has its measure."

"Well, if there has to be a change," the younger one responded, "I’d prefer to be called 'Missy' because that reminds me a bit of maidens."

"But I would rather be chopped to bits," the elder replied.

Eventually, they all got to work. The Maidens ended up riding in a wheelbarrow, which was a bit of a distinction, but they were still referred to as "hand-rammers."

"Mai—!" they said as they bounced along the pavement. "Mai—!" They almost managed to finish the word "maiden," but they stopped short and swallowed the last syllable, deciding it was beneath them to protest. Still, they always called each other "maiden" and reminisced about the good old days when everything had its rightful name, and those who were maidens were called maidens. They stayed true to themselves because the hammer truly ended his engagement with the younger one—nothing would satisfy him but to have a maiden as his bride.

title: UNDER THE WILLOW-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: The area around the small town of Kjoge is pretty bleak and chilly. The town sits along the beautiful seashore, but its beauty could be more pronounced because the surrounding fields are flat, and the forest is quite a distance away. Yet, people who call a place home can always find something lovely about it, even if it's in the most charming place that isn't home. It's true that there are some modest gardens on the outskirts of town beside a little stream flowing towards the sea, and during summer, those gardens look lovely. That was definitely how two children, whose families were neighbors, felt as they played in these gardens, often squeezing through the gooseberry bushes that separated them. One garden had an elder tree, and the other had an old willow tree where the kids loved to play. Their parents allowed it, even though the tree was close to the stream and there was a risk of falling in. But the kids were careful; the boy, Knud, was especially afraid of the water. While other kids splashed in the sea during summer, he would rather not join them, no matter how much they teased him. Once, Joanna, the neighbor’s little girl, had a dream where she sailed in a boat, and Knud waded into the water to join her, only for the water to rise up and cover him completely. When Knud heard about this dream, he felt like he could never face the laughter again; how could he ever venture into the water after Joanna's dream? He decided he wouldn’t, and that decision stuck with him. The children’s parents, both poor, often sat together while Knud and Joanna played around them. Along the road separating their homes, there were willow trees planted to keep a ditch at bay. They weren’t the most attractive trees since their tops had been trimmed, but they served a purpose rather than just looking pretty. The old willow in the garden, however, was much more appealing, which is why the children loved playing beneath it. The town had a big market square, and during fairs, there were rows of tents and booths filled with silks, ribbons, toys, cakes, and everything anyone could wish for. Crowds of people filled the square, and sometimes rain poured down, soaking the wool jackets of the peasants, but it couldn’t wipe out the wonderful smell of honey cakes and gingerbread from one particular booth. Best of all, the man who sold those cakes stayed with Knud's parents during the fair, so he often received gingerbread treats, which Joanna inevitably shared with him. To make things even better, that gingerbread seller knew all kinds of stories, and one evening, he told one so captivating that Knud and Joanna never forgot it. So, we might as well hear it too since it's not very long.

title: UNDER THE WILLOW-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: "Once upon a time," he said, "there were two gingerbread cakes on my counter: one shaped like a man in a hat and the other like a lady without a bonnet. Their faces showed on the upper side, while the other side looked quite different. Just like people, most have a 'best side' to their personalities that they choose to show the world. Right where the heart is, the gingerbread man had an almond stuck in, while the lady was made entirely of honey cake. They sat on the counter for a long time before they finally fell in love, but neither spoke about it, which they should have if they wanted something to come from it. 'He’s a man; he should speak first,' thought the gingerbread lady, but she felt happy—certain her love was returned. Meanwhile, his thoughts were more ambitious, like a man's often are. He dreamed of being a real street boy, having four actual pennies, and buying the gingerbread lady just to eat her up. Days and weeks went by, and they grew harder and drier. The lady’s feelings became more tender and womanly. 'Well, I’m just happy to have lived on the same counter as him,' she said one day, and then—crack—she broke in two. 'If only she had known of my love,' thought the gingerbread man, 'she would have held together a little longer.' And here they are; that’s their story," the cake man said. "You might find their silent love story quite remarkable. And here you go!" He gave Joanna the gingerbread man, still intact, and handed Knud the broken lady, but the children were so touched by the story they didn't have the heart to consume the lovers.

title: UNDER THE WILLOW-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The next day, they took the two gingerbread figures to the churchyard and sat beneath the church wall, which was covered in lush ivy in both summer and winter, looking like a rich tapestry. They placed the gingerbread figures in the sunlight among the green leaves and shared the story about their silent love with a group of other kids. They named it 'love' because the story was so sweet, and the other kids agreed. But when they turned back to admire the gingerbread couple, the broken lady was gone! A big boy had eaten her out of meanness. At first, the children cried about it, but then, thinking the poor lover shouldn't be left alone, they decided to eat him too, though they never forgot the story.

Knud and Joanna continued to play beneath the elder tree and the willow. The little girl would sing beautiful songs with a voice as clear as a bell. Knud didn't have a musical bone in his body, but he knew all the lyrics, which was something. The townsfolk of Kjoge, even the wealthy shopkeeper’s wife, would stop and listen to Joanna sing, remarking, "She truly has a lovely voice."

Those were happy days, but happiness can’t last forever. The neighbors had to separate; Joanna’s mother had passed away, and her father considered remarrying and moving to the capital, where a promising job awaited him. The farewell was bittersweet, filled with tears from both children. Yet, parents promised they would keep in touch, writing at least once a year.

title: UNDER THE WILLOW-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
After that, Knud became an apprentice to a shoemaker, transitioning into a big boy who couldn’t roam free anymore. He was also preparing for his confirmation. Oh, how joyful he would have been to share that special day in Copenhagen with little Joanna, but he stayed back in Kjoge and had never seen the big city, even though it was just five miles away. On clear days, he could even glimpse the towers of Copenhagen across the bay, and on his confirmation day, he distinctly saw the golden cross atop the main church shining in the sunlight. He often thought about Joanna, but did she think of him too? Yes. Around Christmas, Joanna's father wrote a letter to Knud's parents, mentioning their progress in Copenhagen and how Joanna’s beautiful voice was likely to bring her a bright future. She had even gotten a gig singing at a concert, earning money she sent back to her beloved neighbors in Kjoge as a Christmas gift to share some cheer, specifically asking them to raise a toast in her honor. She even added a note saying, "Kind regards to Knud."

Knud's family cried when they read the cheerful news, but those were tears of joy. Knud had thought about Joanna every day, and now he knew she thought of him too. As his apprenticeship came to an end, he realized he loved Joanna and wanted her to be his wife. That thought brought a smile to his face, and while stitching, he got so carried away that he pricked his finger with an awl. But he didn’t care; he refused to play the silent lover like the gingerbread cakes had. He’d learned a good lesson from their tale.

title: UNDER THE WILLOW-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Eventually, he became a journeyman, and for the first time, he planned his journey to Copenhagen, with his knapsack packed and ready. A master in the city awaited him, and he couldn't help but think of how happy Joanna would be to see him. She was now seventeen, and he was nineteen. He wanted to buy her a gold ring back in Kjoge, but then he remembered how much lovelier such things would be in Copenhagen. So, he said goodbye to his parents and set off on a rainy day in late autumn from the town where he was born. The leaves were falling from the trees, and by the time he reached his new master in the bustling capital, he was drenched. He planned his first visit to Joanna's father for the upcoming Sunday. When that day arrived, he put on his journeyman’s clothes and a new hat he had brought from Kjoge. The hat suited him well, as he’d always worn a simple cap before. He found his way to the desired house with ease, though he had to climb many stairs that left him a bit dizzy; he was surprised to see how people lived so close together in this enormous town.

Once inside the room that radiated affluence, Joanna's father greeted him warmly. The new wife was unfamiliar to him, but she welcomed him with a handshake and offered him some coffee.

title: UNDER THE WILLOW-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Joanna will be thrilled to see you," her father said. "You’ve grown into quite a handsome young man. You shall see her soon; she’s such a good girl and my pride and joy, and with God's grace, she will remain just that. She even has her room now and pays rent for it." He knocked politely on a door, as if he were a visitor, then they both walked in. What a lovely room it was! You wouldn’t find a more beautiful space in all of Kjoge; even the queen would be pleased to have such accommodations. Soft carpets, flowing curtains that touched the floor, paintings, and flowers were beautifully arranged everywhere. There was even a velvet chair and a large mirror that could easily trick someone into thinking they were entering through a door. Knud took in everything quickly, but truly, his eyes were only for Joanna. She had grown up, looking far different from the little girl he remembered, and way more beautiful. There was no girl like her in all of Kjoge, and she seemed so graceful, even if her first glance at him felt unfamiliar. But that lasted only a moment before she rushed toward him as if she wanted to kiss him; she didn’t, though, but she was clearly overjoyed to see her childhood friend again, her eyes brimming with tears. She asked about Knud's parents and everything, all the way down to the elder tree and the willow, which she referred to as "elder-mother and willow-father" as if they were real people, which in a way, they were, much like the gingerbread cakes. Then she reminisced about their childhood, their shared story of silent love, and how they lay on the counter together until they split in half. That made her laugh heartily, but blood rushed to Knud's cheeks, and his heart raced. Joanna wasn’t proud at all; he noticed her parents invited him to stay and spend the entire evening, and she poured tea for him herself. Afterward, she read aloud a book, and it felt like the story mirrored his own emotions. Then she sang a simple song that turned into a true tale through her voice, pouring out her heart.

"Oh," Knud thought, "she knows I love her." Tears streamed down his face, and he felt speechless.

title: UNDER THE WILLOW-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
When it was time to leave, Joanna squeezed his hand and said, "You have such a kind heart, Knud; stay as you are now." What a night of joy it had been; falling asleep afterward was impossible for Knud.

As they parted, Joanna's father said, "Now, promise you won't forget us; don’t let an entire winter pass without visiting us again," which made Knud feel free to return the following Sunday evening, and indeed he did. But each evening after work, especially when the candles were lit, he would wander through the town and stroll past the street where Joanna lived, glancing up at her window. It was usually lit up, and one evening he clearly saw the shadow of her face upon the blind; that was a splendid evening for him. His master's wife was not pleased with his nightly outings, believing he was wasting time, and often shook her head disapprovingly.

His master merely smiled and replied, "He’s a young man, my dear, you know."

"This Sunday, I will tell her I love her with all my heart and soul, and that she must be my little wife. I know I’m just a poor shoemaker's apprentice now, but I’ll work hard and eventually become a master. Yes, I’ll speak to her; nothing comes from silent love. I learned that from the gingerbread story," Knud promised himself.

Sunday came, but upon his arrival, he was met with the unfortunate news that they had been invited out for the evening and couldn’t take him in.

title: UNDER THE WILLOW-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Joanna squeezed his hand and offered, "Have you ever been to the theater? You must! I’m singing there on Wednesday, and if you have time, I’ll send you a ticket; my father knows where your master lives." How kind of her! On Wednesday afternoon, Knud received a sealed envelope with no return address, but inside was the ticket. That evening, he went to the theater for the very first time, and what did he see? He saw Joanna, and she looked radiant and enchanting! He even saw her acting a wedding scene with a stranger, but he understood that was just part of the play, nothing real. He felt certain she wouldn’t have invited him if it were actual. So, he watched on, cheering with the crowd, shouting "hurrah!" He could see that even the king smiled upon Joanna and seemed captivated by her song. Knud felt so small, yet filled with affection for her, believing she held feelings for him too, and reminded himself that it was the man who must initiate their love, just as the gingerbread maiden had thought. Ah, how much he learned from that childhood tale!

Once Sunday arrived, he returned with a sense of sacredness. Joanna was there to greet him alone; it couldn’t have gone better. "I'm so glad you're here," she exclaimed. "I was thinking of sending my father to fetch you, but I had a feeling you would come tonight. Actually, I wanted to tell you that I’m heading to France. I leave this Friday. It’s essential for me if I want to become a top performer."

Poor Knud! It felt like the entire room was spinning around him. His courage sank, and he felt as if his heart would shatter. He fought back tears, but his sorrow was evident.

"You honest and faithful soul," she exclaimed. That made Knud speak up and reveal how deeply he loved her and that she must be his wife. As he declared this, he noticed Joanna's color drain from her face and her hand slipped away. With a serious and somber tone, she replied, "Knud, don’t make us both unhappy. I’ll always be a good sister to you, someone you can trust, but I can never be anything more." She gently stroked his warm brow and said, "God gives us strength to endure, if we only strive to face our challenges."

title: UNDER THE WILLOW-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Just then, her stepmother entered the room, and Joanna quickly explained, "Knud is feeling down because I’m leaving;" and it seemed they had merely been discussing her journey. "Be brave," she urged, placing her hand on his shoulder. "You’re still a child at heart, and you must be good and reasonable, like when we were kids playing under the willow tree."

Knud listened, but it felt like the world had tilted off its axis. His thoughts fluttered like a loose thread dancing in the wind. He stayed, uncertain whether she welcomed him to remain. Yet, she treated him kindly, pouring tea and singing for him. But her song no longer held that old magic; while it was beautiful, it left his heart feeling heavy. As it was time to go, he didn’t offer his hand, but she grasped it and said,

"Why won’t you shake hands with your sister before we part, my old playmate?" She smiled, even as tears streamed down her face. Again, she called him "brother," which offered some comfort; and thus, they parted ways.

Joanna sailed to France, while Knud wandered through the muddy streets of Copenhagen. The other apprentices asked him why he seemed so downcast, urging him to join them for some fun, reminding him he was still a young guy. So, he followed them to a dance hall. He saw many lovely girls there, but none compared to Joanna, and wherever he looked, he felt her presence more vividly than ever. "God gives strength to endure, if we strive to do our best," she had told him, which filled him with a solemn sense of spirituality, and he found himself praying. When the violins played and the girls danced, he suddenly felt as if he were in a place where Joanna should not be because her spirit accompanied him. He quickly left. As he walked through the streets swiftly, he passed the house where she once lived; it looked dark and empty like a lonely shell. But life continued around him, and Knud had to keep moving forward.

title: UNDER THE WILLOW-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Winter arrived; the water was frozen, and everything seemed concealed under a cold blanket. But when spring rolled back around and the first steamer set sail, Knud was filled with an urge to venture forth into the world, though not to France. He packed his bag and traveled through Germany, visiting town after town, yet finding neither solace nor peace. Only when he reached the magnificent old city of Nuremberg did he finally feel a sense of control over himself, and so he settled there.

Nuremberg is a fantastic old city that appears as if it was taken straight from a storybook. The streets seem to have arranged themselves whimsically, as if the houses resisted standing in rows. Gables with tiny towers, decorative columns, and statues lead to the city gate, where waterspouts shaped like dragons and long, lean dogs extend into the streets. Knud stood in the market square, his knapsack on his back, beside one of the beautifully adorned fountains featuring scriptural and historical figures. A pretty maid was filling her buckets nearby; she offered Knud a cool drink. Holding a bouquet of roses, she gifted him one, which felt like a good omen for the future. From a nearby church came the sound of music that reminded him of the organ back at home in Kjoge. He entered the grand cathedral, where sunlight streamed through the stained-glass windows, creating a serene atmosphere. As he stood between two tall pillars, peace filled his soul, and he began to pray. He sought out and found a good master in Nuremberg and stayed to learn the German language.

title: UNDER THE WILLOW-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: The old moat surrounding the town had transformed into several little kitchen gardens, although the stout walls and heavy towers still stood tall. Within those walls, a rope maker twisted his ropes along a path resembling a gallery, with elder bushes stretching their green branches over the small houses below. In one of those houses lived Knud's new master, and right outside the garret window where he worked, the elder tree waved its branches. He spent one entire summer and winter there, but as spring returned, he couldn't put up with it any longer. The elder was in bloom, and its sweet scent reminded him so much of home that he felt he had returned to Kjoge’s gardens. So, he left his master to work with another craftsman further into town, where no elder tree grew. This workshop was close to a bridge near a watermill, where a rushing stream always gushed and roared; the neighboring houses, with their crumbling balconies, seemed on the verge of collapsing into the water. No elder plant sat near him, nor even a flower pot with a tiny green plant, yet across from his workshop stood a grand willow tree that clung to the house as if trying not to be swept away by the water. Its branches hung over the stream just as those of the willow in Kjoge had stretched over the river. Yes, he had indeed migrated from elder-mother to willow-father. There was something about that tree, especially on moonlit nights, that spoke directly to his heart, yet it wasn’t really the moonlight, but the tree itself. Still, he could not endure it, and why? Ask the willow, ask the blossoming elder! Regardless, he left Nuremberg and pressed on. He never spoke of Joanna to anyone; his heartache was well concealed. The childhood story of the two cakes carried a profound meaning for him. He now understood why the gingerbread man had the almond of bitterness hidden in his left side—he felt that same bitterness, while Joanna, with her sweet and friendly demeanor, represented the honey cake maiden. Reflecting on this, the strap of his knapsack pressed against his chest, making it hard to breathe, so he loosened it but felt no relief. He saw only half of the world surrounding him; the other half he carried deep within his thoughts as he departed from Nuremberg. It wasn’t until he caught sight of the towering mountains that the world felt liberated once again; his thoughts shifted to the beauty around him, and tears welled in his eyes. The Alps seemed to be the wings of the earth folding together; when unfolded, they bared vibrant images of dark forests, rushing waters, drifting clouds, and snowy peaks. "On the last day," he mused, "the earth will spread its great wings and soar into the sky, bursting like a soap bubble in the radiant gaze of the Deity. Oh," he sighed, "if only the last day could come soon!"

title: UNDER THE WILLOW-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Silently, Knud wandered through the land of the Alps, which felt like a fruit garden blanketed in soft grass. Young lacemakers waved to him from the wooden balconies of their homes as he passed. The mountain peaks glowed in the brilliant red sunset, while green lakes nestled beneath the overshadowing dark trees reflected that glow. Then, he thought of the seaside town of Kjoge, filled with a longing that was devoid of pain. There, where the Rhine rolls onwards like a massive wave that dissolves into snowflakes, and where the shimmering clouds endlessly shift as if this were their birthplace, and the rainbow flutters around them like a colorful ribbon, Knud remembered the watermill at Kjoge with its always rushing waters. He felt a bittersweet desire to stay in the tranquil Rhenish town, but there were still too many elders and willows to contend with.

So he continued onward over a stunning, lofty mountain range, across rugged, rocky cliffs, and along roads that hung precariously on the mountainsides like swallows' nests. The waters churned below him. The clouds floated beneath him. He walked on, stepping on Alpine roses, thistles, and patches of snow, with the summer sun shining down, until finally, he left the northern lands behind. Soon, he passed under the shade of blooming chestnut trees, through vineyards and fields of sweet corn, becoming increasingly aware that the mountains loomed like a barrier between him and his childhood memories, which he longed for.

Before him lay a grand, magnificent city named Milan, where he found a German master who hired him as a worker. The master and his wife, who employed him, were an elderly, devout couple. They quickly took a liking to this quiet apprentice, who spoke little but worked diligently, living a faithful Christian life. He started to feel as though God had lifted the heavy burden from his heart. His greatest joy was climbing onto the rooftop of the magnificent church built from white marble, where the pointed towers, ornate cloisters, stately columns, and white statues smiled down on him from every corner. Above him was the vast blue sky; below lay the bustling city and the expansive plains of Lombardy; and towards the north, the towering mountains remained capped with snow. He thought back to Kjoge's church, with its red, ivy-covered walls, but he no longer yearned to return; here, across the mountains, he would live and eventually pass away.

title: UNDER THE WILLOW-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Three years had passed since he left his hometown; out of those, he had spent one year in Milan.

One day, his master took him into town, not to see the circus with all the riders, but to the opera, a grand building worth the visit itself. The seven tiers of boxes reached dizzying heights and were adorned with rich, silky curtains. Elegantly dressed ladies held bouquets in their hands, while the gentlemen donned formal attire, many showcasing medals of gold and silver. The venue was lit so brilliantly that it felt like sunshine, and grand music flowed throughout. Everything appeared more beautiful than at the theater in Copenhagen, but Joanna had once performed there—could she be? Yes, it was like magic; she was here too! When the curtain rose, there stood Joanna, dressed in silk and gold with a golden crown atop her head. He thought she sang like an angel; then she stepped forward, smiling the smile that only Joanna could give, and looked directly at Knud. Poor Knud! He grabbed his master's hand and cried out, "Joanna!" but no one could hear him due to the music that drowned out all sounds.

"Yes, yes, that’s Joanna," his master replied, pulling out a printed bill and pointing out her name. It was no dream! The entire audience applauded and showered her with flowers; each time she left, they called for her return, making her come and go repeatedly. Outside, crowds gathered around her carriage, pulling it along themselves instead of letting the horses do the work. Knud stood at the front, cheering as joyfully as the rest, and when her carriage stopped before an elegantly-lit house, he positioned himself by the door. It swung open, and she stepped out into the light; he could see her smile as she thanked the crowd, though she appeared quite overwhelmed. Knud locked eyes with her, but she didn’t recognize him. Another man with a shiny star on his chest offered her his arm, and whispers of their engagement filled the air. That night, Knud returned home and packed his knapsack; he felt an undeniable need to return to the place of his childhood, to the elder tree and the willow. "Ah, underneath that willow!" A man can live a lifetime in a single hour.

title: UNDER THE WILLOW-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk: The elderly couple urged him to stay, but he couldn’t be swayed. They pleaded that winter was fast approaching and that snow had already started falling on the mountains. He told them he would easily find the path cleared for the carriages moving slowly along. With just his knapsack on his back and leaning on his walking stick, he could manage to move swiftly. So he turned his steps towards the mountains, ascending one side and descending the other, still heading north until he began to tire, and all signs of civilization disappeared. The stars twinkled above him, and down in the valley, lights sparkled like stars as if another sky lay beneath him. But his head spun, his feet stumbled, and illness weighed him down. The valley lights grew brighter and more numerous, suggesting a village up ahead. Summoning his waning strength, he pushed forward and finally found refuge in a humble inn. He stayed there that night and the following day, giving his body the rest it needed while rain and thaw swept through the valley. By early morning on the third day, a man arrived with an organ, playing a melody reminiscent of home. After that, Knud knew he could no longer stay, so he resumed his journey northward. He walked for several days, stepping quickly as if he aimed to reach home before everyone he knew passed away; yet, he never shared this longing. No one would understand or believe this profound grief in his heart; such sorrow wasn’t entertaining even to friends, and poor Knud had none. He was just a wanderer traversing unfamiliar lands towards home.

title: UNDER THE WILLOW-TREE
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
One evening, as he walked along the public roads, the landscape grew flatter with fields and meadows, the air feeling brisk. A willow tree lined the roadside, stirring memories of home. After a long day, he felt weary, so he settled beneath the tree and quickly dozed off, drifting into sleep. Still, he sensed the willow stretching its branches over him; in his dreams, the tree transformed into a strong, old man—the "willow-father," who had taken his exhausted son into his arms, returning him to his homeland, to the garden of his youth alongside the bleak, open shores of Kjoge. In his dream, he envisioned the very willow tree from Kjoge embarking on a journey across the world to find him and finally bring him back to that little garden by the stream, where he would find Joanna, adorned in all her beauty, wearing a golden crown as he had last seen her, welcoming him. Then appeared two curious figures that looked far more human than when he first saw them in childhood; they had changed, but he recognized them as the two gingerbread cakes, the man and the woman, who had shown their finest sides to the world, looking so refined.

"We thank you," they spoke to Knud, "for you have given us a voice; you’ve taught us that thoughts should be freely expressed, or nothing will come of them. Because of you, our hopes have become real; we are engaged to be married." Then they strolled away, hand in hand through the streets of Kjoge, looking quite respectable, as one ought to when on display. They headed toward the church, and Knud and Joanna followed, also hand in hand; the church stood as it once did, with its red walls draped in green ivy.

The great church doors swung wide open, and as they made their way up the broad aisle, soft melodies from the organ filled the air. "Our wedding first," declared the gingerbread pair, inviting Knud and Joanna to kneel at the altar. As they prayed, Joanna bowed her head over Knud, and icy tears fell from her eyes onto his face. They were indeed tears of ice; her heart melted towards him through his overwhelming affection, and as they dripped onto his flushed cheeks, he awakened. He found himself still perched beneath the willow tree in a distant land on a bitter winter evening, as snow and hail battered against his face.

"That was the happiest hour of my life," he reflected, "even if it was just a dream. Oh, let me dream again!" He squeezed his eyes shut once more and fell asleep.

As dawn approached, snow began to fall heavily; the wind drifted it over him, but Knud continued to sleep peacefully. Villagers going to church discovered a worker seated by the roadside but found that he was dead, frozen to death beneath the willow tree.

title: WHAT ONE CAN INVENT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Once there was a young man who dreamed of becoming a poet. He wanted to achieve this by Easter, get married, and support himself through poetry. He understood that writing poems was all about being able to invent something—but he struggled with inventing anything. He felt he had arrived too late; everything had been spoken and written long before he entered the world.

"How lucky the people who were born a thousand years ago are!" he lamented. "It must have been easy for them to achieve immortality. Even those who were born just a hundred years ago had more to write about. Now, the world feels exhausted of new ideas, and what can I possibly write poetry about?"

He studied so hard that he grew sick and miserable. No doctor could help him, but maybe the wise woman could. She lived in a little house by the roadside, where she would open the gate for travelers. But she could do so much more than just that—she was wiser than the doctor who rode around in his carriage and paid for his station.

Her house was small and tidy but rather dreary to look at; it was devoid of flowers or trees. Near the door, there was a bee-hive, which proved very useful. She also had a little potato field, and a bank of earth with sloe bushes on it that had finished blooming. Those bushes bore sour little fruits that would pucker your lips if you tasted them before frost touched them.

"That’s a fitting picture of our poetry-less time," thought the young man. At least he found a thought, a little nugget of inspiration, just outside the wise woman’s door.

"Write that down!" she instructed. "Even the smallest crumbs are like bread. I know why you’ve come here. You can’t invent anything, yet you want to be a poet by Easter."

"Everything has already been written," he replied. "Our time isn’t like the old days."

title: WHAT ONE CAN INVENT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"No," the woman said. "In the old days, wise women were burned, and poets roamed with empty stomachs and ragged clothes. This is a good time—a great time—but you’re looking at it all wrong. Your ear isn’t tuned in, and I suspect you don’t say the Lord’s Prayer in the evening. There’s plenty here to inspire poems and stories for anyone who knows how. You can find it in the fruits of the earth and the waters—those who know how to catch a sunbeam will see it! Now, try on my spectacles and use my ear-trumpet, then pray to God and stop thinking so much about yourself."

Doing that last part was a pretty tough challenge, more than any wise woman should ask.

He took the spectacles and the ear-trumpet and stood in the middle of the potato field. She handed him a big potato. Sounds came from within it; it began to sing and share the potato's story—an everyday tale in ten parts, surprisingly interesting. And it only took ten lines to tell it.

She sang about herself and her family, how the potato came to Europe, and the misunderstandings it faced before being recognized as greater than gold.

"We were sent out, by royal command, from the council chambers to various towns, proclaiming our true worth. But no one believed us or even knew how to plant us. One person dug a hole and dumped in an entire bushel of potatoes, while another planted each potato separately, expecting them to grow into perfect trees that would shed potatoes. They did grow, and produced flowers and watery fruit, but that all withered away. Nobody recognized the true treasure buried in the ground—the potato. Yes, we endured and suffered; our ancestors did, and so do we—it’s all one."

"Well, that’s enough," the woman said. "Now, look at the sloe bush."

"We have some relatives from the potato family, but they grow further north," the Sloes said. "There were Norsemen from Norway who navigated through mist and storms to an unknown land, where they discovered plants and meadows hidden behind ice and snow, as well as bushes with dark blue fruit—the sloe bushes. The frost ripened the grapes just like us. They named the land 'wine-land,' also known as 'Groenland' or 'Sloeland.'"

title: WHAT ONE CAN INVENT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"What a romantic story!" the young man exclaimed.

"Absolutely! But now come with me," the wise woman said, leading him to the beehive.

He peered inside, astonished by the busy life. The bees moved through the passages, flapping their wings to keep a fresh breeze flowing through their factory. They were hard at work. Other bees flocked in with little baskets on their legs, bringing in flower dust, which they sorted and processed into honey and wax. They buzzed in and out. The queen bee wanted to escape, but all the workers had to follow her, and it wasn't time yet, so they nipped her wings, forcing her to stay.

"Now climb up on the earth bank," the wise woman instructed. "Look out over the highway and watch the people."

"What a crowd!" the young man exclaimed. "Stories everywhere! It swirls and swirls! It’s a complete frenzy before my eyes. I’ll head back instead."

"No, go straight ahead," the woman said. "Dive right into the crowd of people. Look at them properly—listen with an open ear and feel with the right heart, and soon you'll invent something. But first, return my spectacles and ear-trumpet."

"Now I can't see a single thing," the young man said, "and now I can’t hear anything at all."

"Then you can't become a poet by Easter," the wise woman replied.

"When will I be able to?" he asked.

"Not by Easter or Whitsuntide! You’ll never learn how to invent anything."

"What do I need to do to earn my bread through poetry?" he questioned.

"You can manage that by Shrove Tuesday. Hunt down the poets! Destroy their works, and you'll take them down. Don't be discouraged. Strike boldly, and you'll have carnival cake to support you and your future wife."

"What one can invent!" the young man shouted. And so, he boldly struck down every second poet, since he couldn’t be one himself.

We heard this from the wise woman. She knows WHAT ONE CAN INVENT.

title: WHAT THE OLD MAN DOES IS ALWAYS RIGHT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
Let me share a story that was told to me when I was a young boy. Each time I think of this tale, it feels more delightful; stories, like many people, often get better with age.

I’m sure you’ve seen a quaint farmhouse in the countryside, with a thatched roof and moss growing freely on it. A stork has made its nest on the gable, because we definitely need the stork around. The house has sloping walls, low windows, and only one that opens. The baking oven juts out from the wall like a big knob. An elder tree hangs over the fence, and under its branches, there’s a little pool where some ducks are splashing about. A yard dog barks at everything it sees. Just like this, there was a farmhouse nestled in a country lane where an old couple, a farmer and his wife, lived. Despite their meager possessions, they owned one thing they couldn’t part with: a horse that grazed by the roadside. The old farmer rode this horse to town often, and their neighbors would borrow it, providing a service in return. Eventually, the couple thought they might sell the horse or trade it for something more useful. But what could that be?

"You know best, dear husband," said the wife. "Today is fair day; ride into town and sell the horse for money or barter it; either way, I trust your judgment, so go to the fair!"

She tied his neckerchief for him, doing it better than he could, and she made it look nice with a double bow. She also smoothed his hat with her hand and gave him a kiss before he headed off on the horse meant for sale or trade. He had a good sense about him. The sun blazed overhead, and there was not a cloud in sight. The road was dusty from all the people heading to the fair by horse, cart, or on foot. There was no shade from the hot sun. Among them was a man walking along, leading a stunning cow.

"That cow looks like it gives great milk," thought the farmer. "Exchanging the horse for the cow would be a good deal. Hey you with the cow," he called out. "I know a horse is generally worth more than a cow, but honestly, a cow will be much more useful to me, so if you’re interested, let’s trade."

title: WHAT THE OLD MAN DOES IS ALWAYS RIGHT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
The swap went smoothly, and now that the deal was settled, the farmer could have turned around, having accomplished what he intended. But curious about the fair, he decided to continue on to the town with his new cow. As he walked, he soon caught up with a man driving a sheep—a fat, fluffy one with a beautiful fleece.

"That sheep would be great for us," thought the farmer. "There’s enough grass by our fence for it, and in winter, we could keep it inside with us. Maybe having a sheep is better than a cow. Should I trade?" The sheep owner was agreeable, and they quickly made the deal. The farmer continued along the road now leading a sheep.

Not long after, he spotted another man who had come from a field carrying a large goose under his arm.

"That goose is quite hefty!" remarked the farmer. "It has plenty of feathers and fat—perfect for me, we could make all kinds of things. My wife often said, 'If only we had a goose!' This is my chance! How about an exchange? I’ll give you my sheep for your goose, and I’ll throw in my thanks too."

The man agreed without hesitation, and the farmer happily became the owner of the goose. By this time, he was nearing town. The crowd along the road was growing more hectic, with people and animals all around. Cattle walked close to the fence, and at the tollgate, they even wandered into the tollkeeper’s potato patch, where a hen was strutting around with a string tied to its leg to keep it from getting frightened and running off. The hen’s tail feathers were short, and it blinked its two eyes, looking quite clever as it clucked away. I’m not sure what that hen was thinking, but the moment our farmer spotted it, he thought, "That’s the finest hen I’ve ever seen! It’s better than our parson’s hen for sure. I’d love to have that hen! They can scrounge for food and almost take care of themselves. It would be a fantastic trade if I could get it for my goose. Should I ask the tollkeeper?"

title: WHAT THE OLD MAN DOES IS ALWAYS RIGHT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Trade?" the tollkeeper echoed. "Well, it wouldn’t hurt to swap one for the other."

And just like that, they made the trade—the tollkeeper kept the goose, and the farmer walked away with the hen. After all this bartering, he was hot and worn out, ready for something to eat and a beer to relax with, so he headed toward an inn. As he approached the entrance, he bumped into the innkeeper, who was carrying a sack.

"What’s in that sack?" the farmer asked.

"Rotten apples," the innkeeper replied. "A whole sackful. Perfect for feeding the pigs."

"What a shame to waste them!" the farmer exclaimed. "I’d like to take them home for my wife. Last year, our old apple tree only produced one apple, and we kept it dried up in the cupboard. It felt like a treasure to my wife. Imagine how impressed she’d be with a sackful!"

"What will you give me for the sack?" the innkeeper asked.

"What will I offer? I’ll give you my hen in exchange."

So, he gave up the hen and received the apples, carefully leaning the sack against the stove inside the inn. Then, he headed to the table. But the stove was warmed up, and he hadn’t taken that into consideration. The inn was packed with guests—horse traders, cattle drivers, and even two wealthy Englishmen. The Englishmen looked so rich that their pockets seemed ready to burst, and they were in the mood for a bet. "What’s that hissing?" asked one of them.

"Oh, that?" said the farmer. And he went on to recount his journey, starting from the horse he had exchanged for the cow, right down to the apples.

"Oh, your wife is going to have words with you when you get home!" chuckled one of the Englishmen. "Isn’t there going to be a ruckus?"

"What? What will she say?" asked the farmer. "She’ll kiss me and tell me, 'What the old man does is always right.'"

title: WHAT THE OLD MAN DOES IS ALWAYS RIGHT
author: Hans Christian Andersen
text_chunk:
"Let’s make a bet on this," declared the Englishmen. "We’ll wager you a ton of gold, a hundred pounds for a hundred-weight!"

"No thanks; a bushel is enough," responded the farmer. "I can only offer a bushel of apples, and I’ll toss myself and my wife into the mix—that should weigh it down nicely!"

At that moment, the landlord’s coach arrived, and the Englishmen and the farmer hopped in. They drove off quickly and soon arrived at the farmer’s little hut. "Evening, dear wife," called the man. "I made the exchange."

"Well, you certainly know what you’re doing," said the woman as she embraced him, paying no mind to the strangers or the sack.

"Thank goodness!" she said. "Now we can have plenty of milk, butter, and cheese. That was a fantastic trade!"

"Yes, but I traded the cow for a sheep."

"Oh, even better!" shouted the wife. "You always consider everything; we have just enough pasture for a sheep. We can have cheese, jackets, and socks! The cow wouldn’t give us all that; she would just shed her fur! How thoughtful of you!"

"But I exchanged the sheep for a goose."
