"So, we’ll get to have roast goose this year! You dear old man, you always think of ways to make me happy. This is wonderful! We can let the goose roam with a string on her leg, and she’ll get fatter before we cook her!"

"A hen! Well, that was smart too," replied the wife. "The hen will lay eggs, and soon we’ll have some chicks; we’ll have our own little flock! This is just what I wished for!"

"Yes, but I traded the hen for a sack of dried apples."

"What? I must kiss you for that!" exclaimed the wife. "Oh, my dear husband, when you left me this morning, I started thinking about what I could cook for supper tonight, and I wanted to fry some eggs and bacon with fresh herbs. I had the eggs and bacon ready, but I needed the herbs. So I went over to the schoolmaster’s, knowing they had a lot. But the schoolmistress is so stingy, despite her sweet smile. When I asked to borrow some, she huffed, 'I have nothing to spare, not even a shriveled apple! But now I’m happy to lend you ten apples, or even a whole sackful, which makes me chuckle!" After that, she gave him a great kiss.

"I really love this," laughed the Englishmen, "It’s all about going downhill yet staying cheerful; it’s worth every penny to witness it." So they paid the farmer a hundred-weight of gold, who, no matter what he did, received kisses rather than scolding.

Indeed, the best outcome comes when a wife sees her husband has wisdom, and whatever he does is right.

That’s the story I heard when I was a child; now you’ve heard it too, and remember, "What the old man does is always right."

title: The Story Of Creation
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth. While the earth was still a blank canvas, God declared, "Let there be light," and light appeared. God saw that the light was good, so He separated the light from the darkness, calling the light Day and the darkness Night. This marked the evening and the morning of the first day.

Then God said, "Let there be a sky to divide the waters below from the waters above." And it happened. God named the sky the Heavens. This marked the evening and the morning of the second day.

God announced, "Let the waters under the heavens gathered together, and let dry land appear." And it happened. God named the dry land Earth and the gathered waters Seas. He saw that it was good.

God said, "Let plants and trees grow from the earth." And it happened. Plants and trees sprouted, each with their own seeds and fruits, and God saw that it was good. This marked the evening and the morning of the third day.

Then God said, "Let there be lights in the heavens to separate day from night. They will serve as signs for seasons, days, and years, and illuminate the earth." And it happened. God created two great lights (the sun and the moon): the greater to govern the day and the lesser to govern the night. He also made stars and placed them in the heavens to shine on the earth. God saw that it was good. This marked the evening and the morning of the fourth day.

God proclaimed, "Let the waters teem with living creatures, and let birds fly across the sky." God created great sea creatures and all kinds of marine life, along with various birds. He saw that it was good and blessed them, saying, "Increase and fill the seas, and let the birds multiply on land." This marked the evening and the morning of the fifth day.

God said, "Let the earth produce all kinds of living creatures, including livestock, crawling things, and wild animals." And it happened. God created all the various wild animals, livestock, and everything that crawls on the ground. He saw that it was good.

God said, "Let us make man in our image. He will rule over the fish in the sea, the birds in the sky, the livestock, the wild animals, and everything that crawls on the ground." So God made man in His image; He created them male and female.

God blessed them and said, "Be fruitful, multiply, fill the earth, and take charge of it; govern the fish of the sea, the birds of the sky, and every living thing that crawls on the ground."

God said, "Look, I give you every plant that grows on the earth and every tree with fruit that has its own seeds. This will be your food. And to every wild animal, every bird of the sky, and everything that crawls on the earth, I give every green plant for food." And it happened.

When God looked at everything He had made, He saw that it was very good. This marked the evening and the morning of the sixth day.

The heavens and the earth were completed, along with everything in them. On the seventh day, when God finished His work, He rested. God blessed the seventh day and made it holy because it was the day He rested from all the work He had done.

title: God'S Good Gifts To Man
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
In the beginning, when God created the earth and the sky, there weren't any trees or plants on the ground because God hadn't sent any rain yet, and there was no one to tend to the soil. But a mist would rise from the earth, watering everything.

Then God shaped a man from the dust of the earth and breathed life into him, making him a living being. God planted a beautiful garden in Eden, far to the East, and from the soil, He caused all sorts of trees to grow that were appealing to the eye and good to eat. Among these was the tree of life and the tree that granted the knowledge of good and evil.

After that, God placed the man in the Garden of Eden to cultivate and take care of it. He gave the man this instruction: "You can eat freely from any tree in the garden, except for the tree that gives knowledge of good and evil; if you eat from it, you will surely die."

God then said, "It’s not good for man to be alone; I’ll create a companion for him." So from the ground, God made all the wild animals and birds and brought them to the man to see what he would name them. Whatever the man named each creature would become its name. However, he found no suitable companion among them.

Then, God caused the man to fall into a deep sleep. While he slept, God took one of his ribs and closed up the flesh at that spot. From the rib He had taken, God made a woman and brought her to the man. The man exclaimed, "Since she was taken from my body, she will be called Woman."

title: The First Disobedience
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
The serpent was more cunning than any other creature God had made. It approached the woman and asked, "Did God really say you can’t eat from any tree in the garden?" The woman replied, "We can eat the fruit from any tree except the one in the middle of the garden. God said, ‘You shall not eat from it or even touch it, or you will die.’" The serpent responded, "You won’t die! God knows that when you eat it, your eyes will be opened, and you’ll be like Him, knowing good and evil."

When the woman saw that the tree was good for food, beautiful to look at, and that it could make her wise, she picked some of the fruit and ate it. Then she gave some to her husband, who was with her, and he ate it too.

Suddenly, their eyes were opened, and they realized they were naked, so they sewed together some fig leaves to cover themselves. When they heard God walking in the garden during the cool of the day, they hid among the trees.

God called out to the man, asking, "Where are you?" The man replied, "I heard you walking in the garden, and I was afraid because I was naked, so I hid." God asked, "Who told you that you were naked? Did you eat from the tree I told you not to eat from?" The man said, "The woman you gave me—she gave me the fruit, and I ate it." God turned to the woman and asked, "What have you done?" She answered, "The serpent deceived me, and I ate."

God then said to the serpent, "Because of what you’ve done, you will be cursed more than all animals. You will crawl on your belly and eat dust for the rest of your life. You and humans will always be enemies; you will strike their heel, and they will crush your head."

To the woman, He said, "I will make your pains in childbirth very severe. You will long for your husband, and he will rule over you."

And to the man, He said, "Because you listened to your wife and ate from the tree I commanded you not to eat from, you will toil for your food all your life. It will be hard work to grow what you eat. You will return to the ground, for you were made from dust, and to dust you will return!"

Then God made garments out of animal skins for the man and his wife and clothed them. He sent them out of the Garden of Eden to work the ground.

title: Cain And His Brother Abel
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Adam named his wife Eve because she was the mother of all living things. They had two sons, Cain and Abel. Abel became a shepherd, while Cain worked as a farmer.

One day, Cain decided to bring an offering to God, presenting some fruit and grain he had grown. Abel also made an offering, bringing the best animals from his flock and sacrificing their fatty portions to God. God was pleased with Abel's offering but did not accept Cain's.

This made Cain really angry, and it showed on his face. So God said to him, "Why are you angry? Why are you downcast? If you do what is right, won’t your offering be accepted? But if you do wrong, sin is waiting to pounce on you; you must control it."

Cain said to his brother Abel, "Let’s go out to the field." While they were there, Cain attacked Abel and killed him.

Then God asked Cain, "Where is your brother Abel?" Cain replied, "I don’t know. Am I my brother’s keeper?" God responded, "What have you done? Listen! Your brother’s blood is crying out to me from the ground. Now you are cursed, and the ground is no longer going to yield good harvests for you. You will be a restless wanderer on the earth."

Cain said to God, "My punishment is more than I can handle. You’ve driven me away from this land, and I won’t be able to worship you anymore. I’ll be a wandering outcast, and anyone who finds me will kill me."

But God replied, "If anyone kills you, they will be punished seven times as much." So God put a mark on Cain to protect him, ensuring that no one would kill him. Then Cain left God’s presence and became a wanderer, far from Eden.

title: Noah And The Great Flood
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
When God saw that people were becoming more wicked and that their thoughts were filled with evil, He felt deep sorrow and regretted creating humanity. So God declared, "I will wipe out all living beings from the earth because I regret making them."

However, Noah had found favor with God. God spoke to Noah, saying, "I’ve decided to put an end to all living beings because the earth is full of their wickedness. I’m going to destroy them. Build yourself an ark from cypress wood. Create rooms in the ark and coat it inside and out with pitch. Here’s how you should construct it: the ark shall be five hundred feet long, eighty feet wide, and fifty feet high. Make a roof for it and put a door on the side. It should have three levels: lower, middle, and upper. I’m about to bring a flood to wipe out every living creature that breathes. Everything on earth shall perish."

Then God told Noah, "Bring your whole family into the ark because you alone are righteous among everyone else alive. From the animals that are clean for food and sacrifice, take seven pairs, the male and female; from the unclean animals, take one pair, male and female; and from the birds that are clean for food and sacrifice, take seven pairs to keep each kind alive on the earth. After seven days, I will send rain for forty days and nights, and I will destroy every living thing that I have made."

Noah did everything God commanded him. When the floodwaters came, he, his sons, his wife, and his sons' wives, along with the animals suitable for food and sacrifice, the other animals, and the birds, all entered the ark to escape the flood.

It rained for forty days and nights, and God sealed Noah and the others inside the ark. The waters kept rising, lifting the ark high above the ground. All the creatures on land perished. Only Noah and those with him in the ark survived.

Then God remembered Noah and all the animals with him in the ark. He sent a wind to blow over the earth, causing the floodwaters to recede. The rain stopped and the waters gradually withdrew from the land.

After forty days, Noah opened a window from the ark and released a raven, which flew back and forth until the waters dried up. He also sent out a dove to see if the waters had receded. But the dove couldn’t find anywhere to land and came back to him in the ark because the waters still covered the earth. So Noah gently took her back inside.

Noah waited another seven days and sent the dove out again from the ark. This time, the dove returned at dusk with a fresh olive leaf in her beak. So Noah realized that the waters had gone down. He waited another seven days and sent the dove out once more, but this time she didn’t come back.

So Noah removed the covering of the ark and saw that the ground was dry. Then he, along with his sons, his wife, and his sons' wives, stepped out of the ark.

Noah built an altar for God and took one of every clean animal and bird to offer as burnt offerings. God said to Himself, "I will never curse the ground again because of man, nor will I destroy every living creature like before. As long as the earth lasts, the seasons of planting and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night will continue."

Then God said, "This is the sign of the everlasting covenant I’m making with you and every living creature: I’ve placed my rainbow in the clouds, and it will be the sign of this agreement between me and the earth. Whenever I bring a cloud over the earth and a rainbow appears, I will remember this covenant between us, and the waters will never again become a flood to destroy all life."

title: The Story Of The Tower Of Babel
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Everyone on Earth spoke the same language. As they traveled west, they discovered a vast valley in Babylonia and decided to settle there.

Then they said to each other, "Let’s make bricks and bake them well." They used bricks instead of stones and asphalt for mortar. They added, "Let’s build a city, along with a tower that reaches the heavens, so we can create a landmark and avoid being scattered across the Earth."

But when the Lord came down to look at the city and the tower the people had built, He said, "Look, they are all one people and speak one language. This is just the beginning, and soon nothing they try to do will feel impossible for them. Let’s go down and confuse their language so they won't understand each other."

So the Lord scattered them from there all over the Earth, and they stopped building the city. That’s why it was called Babel, which means Confusion, because that’s where the Lord confused everyone’s language and scattered them across the world.

title: Abraham, The Friend Of God And Man
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Noah's sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth, stepped out of the ark together. Terah, who was a descendant of Shem, was the father of Abraham, Nahor, and Haran; and Haran was Lot's father.

God spoke to Abraham, saying, "Leave your country, your family, and your father’s house, and go to the land I will show you. I will make you into a great nation; I will bless you and make your name famous so that you will be a blessing. All the families on earth will seek blessings through you."

So, following God’s command, Abraham packed up and left, taking Lot with him. Abraham was seventy-five years old when he departed from Haran. He brought along his wife, Sarah, and Lot, along with all their belongings, and headed for the land of Canaan.

As Abraham traveled through Canaan, he arrived at a place called Shechem, near the oak of Moreh. There, God appeared to Abraham and declared, "I will give this land to your descendants." Abraham built an altar to God, who had revealed Himself. After that, he moved to the hill near Bethel, set up his tent with Bethel on one side and Ai on the other, and built another altar there to pray to God.

Abraham was very wealthy, with lots of cattle, silver, and gold; Lot, who accompanied him, also had many flocks, herds, and tents. The land couldn’t support both of them, so when a dispute arose between their herdsmen, Abraham said to Lot, "I want us to avoid quarreling—after all, we’re family. The land is before you; please separate from me. If you go left, I’ll go right, and if you go right, I’ll go left."

Lot surveyed the area and saw that the Jordan plain was lush and well-watered, like a garden. He decided to pick the entire Jordan valley for himself, settling near the cities of the plain, all the way to Sodom. However, the men of Sodom were notoriously wicked and sinned greatly against God.

title: Abraham, The Friend Of God And Man
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
After Lot had parted ways, God said to Abraham, "Look around you! Lift your eyes up and see north, south, east, and west. All the land you see, I’m giving to you and your descendants forever. I’ll make them as numerous as the dust of the earth, and if you can count the grains of dust, then you can count your descendants. Get up and walk throughout the land, for it will belong to you."

Then Abraham moved his tent once more and settled in the oak grove of Mamre, located in Hebron, where he built another altar to God.

God appeared to Abraham at the oaks of Mamre when he was sitting at the entrance of his tent during a hot day. He looked up and saw three men standing nearby. When he noticed them, he hurried from the entrance to meet them, bowed down, and said, "Gentlemen, if you’re willing, please don’t pass by your servant. Since you’re here, let me get you some water to wash your feet and you can rest under the tree. I’ll also prepare some food for you to regain your strength before you continue on your journey." They replied, "Do as you’ve said."

Abraham quickly went to Sarah's tent and asked, "Hurry up and make three measures of fine flour; knead it, and make some bread." He then ran to the herd, picked out a tender calf, and gave it to a servant to prepare. After that, Abraham brought out curds and milk, along with the prepared calf, and served them as they ate under the tree.

They asked him, "Where is your wife?" He answered, "She’s inside the tent." One of them said, "I’ll come back to you in about nine months, and at that time, your wife Sarah will have a son."

title: Lot'S Escape From A Wicked City
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Abraham accompanied the visitors to send them on their way. God said, "I’ve heard that the people of Sodom and Gomorrah have committed terrible sins. I will go down to see if their actions are as bad as the reports suggest; if they're not, I'll find out."

The men then turned and looked toward Sodom.

Abraham approached God and asked, "Will you really sweep away the righteous with the wicked? What if there are fifty righteous people in the city? Will you destroy it and not spare it for the fifty who are in it? It wouldn’t be right for you to do that—killing the righteous along with the wicked, treating them the same! Shouldn't the Judge of all the earth do what is just?" God replied, "If I find fifty righteous people in Sodom, I will spare the whole city for their sake." Abraham responded, "I’ve taken the liberty to speak to you, even though I’m just dust and ashes. What if there are only five less than the fifty righteous? Will you destroy the whole city for just five?" God said, "I won’t destroy it if I find forty-five there."

Abraham continued to speak to Him, saying, "What if I find forty there?" God replied, "For the sake of forty, I won’t do it." Then Abraham asked, "Please don’t be angry, but let me speak. What if there are thirty found there?" God answered, "I won’t destroy it if I find thirty there." Abraham said, "Since I’ve dared to speak to you, what if twenty are found?" He replied, "For the sake of twenty, I won’t destroy it." Then Abraham asked, "Please don’t be upset, but just let me speak once more. What if there are ten?" God said, "For the sake of ten, I won’t destroy it." After that, God went on His way, and Abraham returned home.

title: Lot'S Escape From A Wicked City
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Two angels in human form arrived in Sodom that evening while Lot was sitting at the gate. When Lot saw them, he stood to greet them, bowing with his face to the ground. He said, "Sirs, please turn aside to your servant’s house, spend the night, and wash your feet. In the morning, you can rise early and continue your journey." They answered, "No, we’ll stay in the street." But Lot insisted so much that they agreed to go with him and entered his house. He prepared a feast for them, baking unleavened bread, and they ate.

Before they had gone to bed, the people of Sodom, both young and old, surrounded the house from every side. They called out to Lot, "Where are the men who came to you tonight? Bring them out so we can have our way with them!"

Lot went out to them at the entrance of his house and shut the door behind him. He said, "Please, my friends, don’t do this wrong. Don’t hurt these men, for they have taken refuge under my roof." But they replied, "Back off! Or we’ll treat you worse than them!" They pressed in on Lot and tried to break the door. But the men reached out, pulling Lot back inside the house and shutting the door. Then they struck the men outside, leaving them all blind, so they couldn’t find the door.

The angels said to Lot, "Do you have anyone else here? Bring your sons-in-law, your sons, your daughters, or anyone else you have in the city out of this place, because we’re about to destroy it. The complaints against the people have reached God, and He sent us to destroy it." Lot went out to speak to his sons-in-law, saying, "Hurry, get out of this place! God is going to destroy the city!" But they thought he was joking.

At dawn, the angels urged Lot, saying, "Get up! Take your wife and your two daughters so you won’t be swept away in the destruction of the city." When he hesitated, the men grabbed his hand and the hands of his wife and daughters, leading them safely outside the city because God was merciful to him.

Once they were outside, the men said, "Run for your lives! Don’t look back or stop anywhere in the plain. Escape to the mountains so you won’t be swept away!" But Lot replied, "Oh no, my lords! Please, your servant has found favor in your eyes, and you have shown great mercy in saving my life. I can’t escape to the mountains; I might not make it and die. Look, there’s a small town nearby. Let me escape there, and my life will be saved." God replied, "I’ll grant you this request as well; I won’t destroy that town you mentioned. Hurry, escape there, for I can’t do anything until you reach it."

The sun had risen when Lot arrived at Zoar. Then God rained down burning sulfur on Sodom and Gomorrah, destroying those cities and the entire plain, along with everyone who lived there and everything that grew. But Lot’s wife, who was following behind him, looked back and turned into a pillar of salt.

Early the next morning, Abraham got up and went to the place where he had stood before God. He looked toward Sodom and Gomorrah and all the plain and saw smoke rising from the land like smoke from a furnace.

title: God'S Care For The Boy Ishmael
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
God remembered what he had promised Sarah, and he fulfilled his word. So, Sarah had a son, and when he was older, Abraham threw a huge party to celebrate his weaning. But Sarah noticed Hagar's son, the Egyptian boy, playing with her son Isaac. She turned to Abraham and said, "Send this slave girl and her son away. The son of this slave girl shouldn’t share in the inheritance with my son Isaac." Abraham was upset by her request because that boy was also his son. But God reassured Abraham, saying, "Don’t be upset about the boy or your slave girl. Do what Sarah says, because it’s through Isaac that your family line will continue. However, I will also make a great nation from the son of the slave girl, since he is your son too."

The next morning, Abraham got up early, took some bread and a container of water, and gave it to Hagar. He placed the boy on her shoulder and sent them away. They wandered in the desert of Beersheba. When the water ran out, Hagar laid the child down under one of the desert bushes and went a little way off to sit down. She said to herself, "I can't bear to watch my child die."

As she sat there, the boy began to cry. God heard the boy's cry and called out to Hagar, "What’s wrong, Hagar? Don't be afraid. I have heard the boy's cry. Get up, pick him up, and hold his hand, because I will make him a great nation." Then God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water. She went over, filled the container, and gave the boy a drink.

God took care of the boy, and as he grew older, he lived in the wilderness of Paran, becoming a skilled bowman. His mother found him a wife from Egypt.

title: Abraham'S Loyalty To God
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Later, God tested Abraham by calling out to him, "Abraham!" and he replied, "I'm here." God instructed him, "Take your son, your only son Isaac, whom you love, and go to the land of Moriah. There, offer him as a burnt offering on one of the mountains I will show you."

Early the next morning, Abraham got up, saddled his donkey, and took two of his servants along with his son Isaac. After cutting the wood for the burnt offering, he set out for the place God had told him about. On the third day, Abraham looked up and saw the place in the distance. He said to his servants, "Stay here with the donkey while Isaac and I go over there. After we worship, we will come back to you."

Abraham then took the wood for the burnt offering and placed it on Isaac's back. He carried the fire and the knife while they both walked together. Isaac turned to Abraham and said, "Father!" and Abraham replied, "Yes, my son?" Isaac asked, "Here is the fire and the wood, but where is the lamb for the burnt offering?" Abraham answered, "God will provide a lamb for the burnt offering, my son." They continued on together.

When they reached the place God had mentioned, Abraham built an altar and arranged the wood on it. He then tied up his son Isaac and placed him on the altar on top of the wood. Abraham reached out his hand and took the knife to sacrifice his son. But an angel from heaven called out, "Abraham! Abraham!" He answered, "I'm here." The angel said, "Do not harm the boy or do anything to him. Now I know that you truly fear God because you did not withhold your son, your only son, from Me."

Abraham looked up and saw a ram caught in the bushes by its horns. He took the ram and offered it up as a burnt offering instead of his son. He named the place Jehovah-jireh, meaning "The Lord Will Provide."

The angel of the Lord called to Abraham again and said, "The Lord declares, 'Because you have done this and have not withheld your son, your only son, I will bless you and your descendants will be as many as the stars in the sky and the sand on the seashore. They will conquer their enemies, and all nations on earth will be blessed through them because you have obeyed my command.'"

title: How Rebekah Became The Wife Of Isaac
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
When Abraham was very old and had been blessed by God in every possible way, he called his most trusted servant, who managed all his affairs. "Put your hand under my hip," he said, "and promise me by the Lord, the God of heaven and earth, that you won’t let my son marry any of the Canaanite women where I live. Instead, go to my own country and to my relatives to find a wife for my son Isaac." The servant asked him, "What if the woman doesn’t want to come back with me? Should I take your son back to the land you came from?" Abraham replied, "Make sure you don’t take my son back there! The Lord, the God of heaven, who brought me from my father’s house and my homeland, will send His angel ahead of you to guide you in finding a wife for my son. If the woman refuses to come with you, you’re free from this promise, but you must never take my son back there." So the servant put his hand under Abraham's hip and made the vow.

Then the servant loaded up ten of his master’s camels with valuable gifts and set out for the town of Nahor. He made the camels kneel down by the well outside the town in the evening, which was the time when the women come to draw water. He prayed, "O Lord, the God of my master Abraham, please grant me success today and show loyalty to my master Abraham. I’m standing here by the well, and the daughters of the townsfolk are coming out to draw water. Let the young woman who I say, 'Please lower your jug so I can drink,' and who replies, 'Drink, and I’ll water your camels too,' be the one You’ve chosen for Your servant Isaac. That will be my sign that You’ve been kind to my master."

Before he finished speaking, Rebekah, the granddaughter of Nahor, Abraham's brother, appeared with her water jug on her shoulder. She was stunningly beautiful and still single. She went down to the well, filled her jug, and returned. The servant hurried to meet her and said, "Can I have a little water from your jug?" She replied, "Drink, sir," and quickly lowered her jug from her shoulder to give him a drink.

title: How Rebekah Became The Wife Of Isaac
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
After he finished drinking, she said, "I’ll draw water for your camels too, until they’ve had enough to drink." So she emptied her jug into the trough and ran back to the well to draw water, while the man watched her silently, trying to determine if the Lord had made his journey successful.

Once the camels had finished drinking, the man took a gold ring weighing five ounces and placed it in her nose. He also put two golden bracelets weighing five ounces on her arms. He then asked, "Whose daughter are you? Please tell me, is there room in your father’s house for us to stay?" She answered, "I’m the granddaughter of Milcah and Nahor. We have plenty of straw and feed, and there’s a place for you to stay."

Then the man bowed his head and worshiped the Lord, saying, "Blessed be the Lord, the God of my master Abraham, who has been kind and faithful to my master. As for me, the Lord has guided me to the house of my master’s relatives."

The young woman ran home to share the news with her family. Rebekah had a brother named Laban, and when he saw the bracelets on her hands and the ring on her nose, he hurried out to the servant at the spring. When he heard Rebekah say, "This is what the man said to me," he approached the servant, who was still standing by the camels, and said, "Come in, you who are blessed by the Lord! Why are you standing outside? I’ve cleared a space in the house and have room for the camels." He welcomed the man in, unpacked the camels, and provided straw and feed, along with water to wash his feet and those of the other men with him.

title: How Rebekah Became The Wife Of Isaac
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
But when food was served, he said, "I won’t eat until I explain why I’m here." They answered, "Please, go ahead." He said, "I’m Abraham’s servant. The Lord has greatly blessed my master, making him very wealthy. He has flocks, herds, silver, gold, servants, camels, and donkeys. My master’s wife Sarah bore him a son when she was old, and he has given everything to him. My master also made me swear, saying, 'Do not let my son marry a Canaanite woman, but go to my father’s family to find a wife for my son.'

"When I asked my master, 'What if the woman won’t come with me?' he replied, 'The Lord will send His angel with you and grant you success. You will find a wife for my son from my relatives. If they don’t give her to you, you’ll be free from your promise.' So today I came to the well and said, 'O Lord, the God of my master Abraham, I’m standing here by the well. If you will make my mission successful, let the young woman who comes to draw water, to whom I say, "Please give me a little water from your jug to drink," and who replies, "Drink, and I’ll also draw water for your camels," let her be the one You’ve chosen for my master’s son.'

"Before I finished speaking, Rebekah came out with her jug on her shoulder and went down to the well to draw water. I asked her, 'Can I have a drink?' She quickly lowered her jug and said, 'Drink, and I’ll also water your camels.' So I drank, and she watered the camels too. Then I asked her, 'Whose daughter are you?' and she answered, 'I’m the granddaughter of Nahor and Milcah.' I put the ring in her nose and the bracelets on her arms. I bowed my head and worshiped the Lord, blessing the God of my master Abraham for guiding me to find my master’s brother’s daughter for his son. Please tell me if you’ll be kind and truthful to my master so I know what to do!"

title: How Rebekah Became The Wife Of Isaac
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Laban and his family replied, "This matter is up to the Lord. We can’t say either 'yes' or 'no.' Rebekah is here; take her and go, let her be the wife of your master’s son, as the Lord has said."

When Abraham’s servant heard their answer, he bowed down to the ground before the Lord. Then he took out gold and silver jewelry and clothing, presenting them to Rebekah. He also gave costly gifts to her brother and mother. They all ate and drank, and spent the night there.

In the morning, the servant said, "Send me back to my master." But Rebekah's brother and mother insisted, "Let her stay with us a month, or at least ten days; after that she can go." He urged them, "Don’t delay me! The Lord has granted me success. Please send me on my way to my master."

They said, "Let’s ask the young woman." They called for Rebekah and asked her, "Will you go with this man?" She replied, "Yes, I will go." So they sent Rebekah away with her nurse and Abraham's servant along with his men.

They blessed Rebekah, saying, "Our sister, may your descendants become countless!" Then Rebekah set out with her maids, riding on camels, following the servant. He took Rebekah and left.

Abraham had given everything he had to Isaac and had passed away at a good old age, satisfied with his life. One evening, Isaac went out into the field to meditate, and he looked up to see camels approaching. Rebekah also looked up, and when she saw Isaac, she quickly got down from her camel and asked the servant, "Who is that man walking toward us in the field?" The servant replied, "He’s my master." She then covered her face with her veil. The servant went on to tell Isaac everything he had done. Isaac then brought Rebekah into his mother Sarah’s tent, and she became his wife; he loved her.

title: How Jacob Deceived His Father
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Isaac prayed to God for his wife, who couldn’t have children, and God heard his prayer. Soon, Rebekah became the mother of twin boys. They named the first one Esau and the second one Jacob.

As they grew up, Esau became a skilled hunter, spending his time outdoors, while Jacob was more of a homebody who liked to stay close to the tents. Isaac favored Esau because he loved the game his son hunted, while Rebekah had a special affection for Jacob.

One day, Jacob was making a stew when Esau returned from the fields, famished. "Let me have some of that red stew, I’m starving!" Esau said to Jacob. Jacob replied, "First, sell me your birthright as the firstborn." Esau scoffed, "I’m about to die! What good is my birthright to me?" Jacob insisted, "Promise me first." So, Esau swore an oath and sold his birthright to Jacob. Jacob then gave Esau some bread and lentil stew, and after eating and drinking, Esau got up and left. That’s how Esau lost his birthright.

Years later, Isaac was old and nearly blind. He called for Esau, his oldest son, saying, "My son." "Here I am," Esau replied. Isaac continued, "I’m old and might not have much time left. Take your bow and quiver, go into the fields, hunt some game for me, and prepare a meal that I love. Bring it to me so that I can bless you before I die."

Rebekah overheard Isaac talking to Esau. When Esau went off to hunt, she turned to Jacob and said, "I just heard your father tell your brother Esau, 'Bring me game and prepare a delicious meal so I can bless you before I die.' Now listen to me: Go to the flock and get two young goats. I’ll cook them up the way your father likes. Then you can take it to him so he can bless you before he dies." Jacob hesitated, saying, "But Mom, Esau is hairy, and I’m smooth-skinned. If Father touches me, he’ll realize I’m deceiving him and I’ll get in trouble instead of being blessed." Rebekah firmly replied, "Let the blame be on me, my son! Just do what I say. Go get the goats." Jacob obeyed and brought them to her, and she prepared the meal.

title: How Jacob Deceived His Father
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Rebekah took Esau’s best clothes from the tent and dressed Jacob in them. She covered his hands and neck with the skins of the goats so he would feel hairy like Esau. Then she handed him the food and bread she had made, and he went to his father, saying, "Father?" Isaac responded, "Here I am; who are you, my son?" Jacob answered, "I’m Esau, your firstborn. I’ve done what you asked. Now sit up and eat my game so you can bless me." Isaac said, "How did you find it so quickly?" Jacob replied, "The Lord your God helped me find it."

Isaac then said, "Come here, so I can touch you to see if you’re really my son Esau." Jacob approached his father, and Isaac touched him, saying, "The voice is Jacob’s, but the hands feel like Esau’s. Are you really my son Esau?" Jacob answered, "I am." Isaac was still unsure because Jacob's hands felt hairy like Esau's. So, he blessed Jacob. Isaac then said, "Bring me the food, so I can eat and bless you." Jacob brought it to him, and Isaac ate. He also brought some wine for Isaac to drink.

Isaac then said, "Come closer and kiss me, my son." As Jacob approached and kissed him, Isaac caught the scent of his clothes and blessed him.

Just after Isaac finished blessing Jacob and he was about to leave, Esau returned from his hunt. He had prepared a meal and brought it to his father. "Father, get up and eat some of the game I’ve hunted so that you can bless me," Esau said. Isaac asked, "Who are you?" Esau replied, "I’m your son, your oldest, Esau." Isaac started shaking and said, "Who is this that hunted game and brought it to me? I’ve already eaten and blessed him, and surely he will stay blessed!"

When Esau heard his father’s words, he let out a loud, bitter cry and pleaded, "Bless me too, Father!"

Isaac said, "Your brother came deceitfully and took your blessing." Esau cried out, "Is it because he’s called Jacob, which means Supplanter, that he has tricked me twice? First, he took my birthright, and now he’s taken my blessing! Do you have any blessing left for me?" Isaac answered, "I’ve made him your master and given him all your relatives as servants, and grain and wine as his food. What can I do for you, my son?" Esau asked, "Is that really the only blessing you have for me?" And he began to cry.

Isaac then said, "You will live away from fertile land, and far from the dew of heaven. You will live by the sword, and you will serve your brother."

title: Jacob'S Dream
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Esau hated Jacob because of the blessing their father had given him. Esau thought to himself, "My dad won't be around much longer; once he's gone, I'll take my revenge on Jacob."

When Rebekah heard what her older son Esau was plotting, she called for her younger son Jacob and said, "Your brother Esau is planning to kill you. So listen carefully, my son: flee to my brother Laban in Haran and stay there for a while until your brother calms down and forgets what you've done. Then I’ll let you know when it’s safe to come back. I can't bear the thought of losing you both in one day."

Jacob then left Beersheba and headed toward Haran. As night fell, he found a spot to rest. He took one of the stones lying nearby, placed it under his head, and fell asleep. That night, he dreamed of a ladder reaching from the ground to the sky, with angels of God ascending and descending on it.

The Lord stood beside him and said, "I am the Lord, the God of Abraham and Isaac. This land where you’re lying, I will give to you and your descendants. I’m with you, and I’ll protect you wherever you go. I won’t leave you until I fulfill my promise to you."

When Jacob woke from his sleep, he exclaimed, "God is truly in this place, and I didn’t realize it!" He was filled with wonder and declared, "This is none other than the house of God, and this is the gateway to heaven."

So Jacob got up early the next morning, took the stone he had used as a pillow, set it up as a pillar, and poured oil on top of it. He named the place Bethel, which means 'House of God.' Jacob then made a vow: "If God will be with me and protect me on this journey, providing me with food and clothing, and if I return safely to my father’s house, then the Lord will be my God, and this stone I’ve set up as a pillar will be the house of God. I will give you a tenth of everything you give me."

title: The Deceiver Deceived
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Jacob continued on his journey and reached the land of the people from the East. He spotted a well in a field where three flocks of sheep were lounging nearby; they came to the well to drink water, but a heavy stone covered the opening. When all the flocks arrived, they would roll away the stone to water the sheep before putting it back in place.

Jacob approached the men nearby and asked, "Hey friends, where are you from?" They replied, "We’re from Haran." He then inquired, "Do you know Laban, the son of Nahor?" They responded, "We know him." Jacob followed up with, "Is everything okay with him?" They assured him, "Yes, all is well; in fact, here comes Rachel, his daughter, with the sheep." Jacob exclaimed, "Look, it’s still bright out! It’s too early to gather the cattle. Water the sheep and give them something to eat." However, they replied, "We can’t do that until all the flocks are gathered and the stone is rolled away from the well; then we’ll water the sheep."

While he was still talking, Rachel arrived with her father’s sheep, since she was a shepherdess. When Jacob saw Rachel, Laban’s daughter, and the sheep, he went over to the well, rolled away the stone, and watered Laban's flock. Overwhelmed with emotion, Jacob kissed Rachel and cried. He told Rachel that he was related to her father and that he was Rebekah's son, prompting her to dash off and inform her father.

As soon as Laban heard about Jacob, his sister's son, he ran to greet him, embraced him, kissed him multiple times, and took him back to his home. After Jacob shared everything that had happened, Laban declared, "You are truly my own flesh and blood." Jacob stayed with him for a whole month.

Then Laban said to Jacob, "Should you work for me for free just because we're relatives? Tell me what wages you'd like." Laban had two daughters: the older was named Leah, and the younger was named Rachel. Leah had weak eyes, but Rachel was stunningly beautiful. Jacob loved Rachel and replied, "I will work for you for seven years to earn Rachel, your younger daughter." Laban agreed, saying, "It’s better for me to give her to you than to anyone else. Stay with me." Jacob worked those seven years for Rachel, and they felt like just a few days to him because of his love for her.

After the seven years, Jacob said to Laban, "Give me my wife, for my time is up, and I'm ready to marry her." So Laban gathered all the local men and held a feast. That evening, he brought Leah, his daughter, to Jacob, and Jacob accepted her as his wife.

In the morning, when Jacob discovered it was Leah, he exclaimed to Laban, "What have you done to me? Didn’t I work for you to marry Rachel? Why have you deceived me?" Laban replied, "In our culture, we don’t marry off the younger daughter before the older one. Stick with Leah for the wedding week, and then we’ll give you Rachel too, in exchange for another seven years of work." Jacob agreed, staying with Leah for the wedding week. Afterwards, Laban gave Rachel to Jacob as his wife, but Jacob loved Rachel more than Leah, leading him to serve Laban for an additional seven years.

title: Meeting A Brother Who Had Been Wronged
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Over time, Jacob became quite wealthy with big flocks, servants, and donkeys. But then he overheard Laban's sons saying, "Jacob has taken everything belonging to our father, and he has built up his wealth with it." He also noticed that Laban was treating him differently than before. So, Jacob got up, placed his sons and wives on camels, and gathered all his cattle. He tricked Laban by not telling him he was leaving, and then he fled across the Euphrates River, taking everything with him and heading toward Mount Gilead.

Next, Jacob sent messengers ahead to his brother Esau. He instructed them, "Tell my lord Esau: 'Your servant Jacob says, I’ve been living with Laban and have stayed until now. I have oxen and donkeys, flocks and servants, and I’m sending this message to my lord to win your favor.'" The messengers returned, saying, "We found your brother Esau, and he’s coming your way with four hundred men."

This news made Jacob extremely anxious. So, he split his people, flocks, herds, and camels into two groups and said, "If Esau attacks one group, the other can escape."

Jacob then prayed, "O God of my father Abraham and my father Isaac, please save me from my brother Esau’s hand. I fear he will come and harm me, along with the mothers and children."

To win Esau over, Jacob prepared a hefty gift—two hundred female goats, twenty male goats, two hundred ewes, twenty rams, thirty milking camels with their young, forty cows, and ten bulls, along with twenty female donkeys and ten young donkeys. He organized them into separate herds, each under the guidance of his servants, and told them, "Go ahead of me and keep space between the herds."

title: Meeting A Brother Who Had Been Wronged
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
He instructed those at the front, saying, "When my brother Esau meets you and asks, 'Whose cattle are these? Where are you going? Who owns all these in front of you?' you should reply, 'They belong to your servant Jacob; it’s a gift for my lord Esau, and Jacob himself is right behind us.'" He gave the same instructions to the next groups as well, saying, "Jacob is right behind us." He thought to himself, "I’ll soften him up with this gift ahead of me; maybe when I finally see him, he’ll welcome me." So, he sent the gift ahead while he stayed behind in the camp for the night.

Later that night, Jacob got up and took his two wives, two maidservants, and his eleven children, sending them across the Jabbok River.

Now alone, Jacob found himself wrestling with a mysterious figure until dawn. When the figure realized he wasn’t winning against Jacob, he struck Jacob’s hip, leaving it wrenched as they continued to wrestle. The figure said, "Let me go, for dawn is coming." Jacob replied, "I won’t let you go unless you bless me." The figure asked, "What is your name?" Jacob answered, "Jacob." Then the figure said, "From now on, you’ll be called Israel, which means Struggler with God, because you have struggled with God and with people and have prevailed." So, he blessed Jacob there. Jacob named the place Penuel, meaning Face of God, for he said, "I’ve seen God face to face, and my life is spared."

As Jacob looked up, he saw Esau approaching with four hundred men. He arranged the maidservants and their children in the front, followed by Leah and her children, with Rachel and her son Joseph at the back. Jacob went ahead of them, bowing down seven times as he got closer to his brother. Esau ran to him, embraced him, and kissed him, and they both wept.

Esau then noticed the women and children and asked, "Who are these with you?" Jacob replied, "These are the children God has blessed me with." The maidservants and their children came forward and bowed down. Leah and her children bowed too, and finally, Joseph and Rachel approached and also bowed before Esau.

Esau asked, "What was the purpose of all these animals I encountered?" Jacob answered, "I wanted to find favor with you, my lord." Esau replied, "I have plenty, my brother; keep what you have." But Jacob insisted, "Please accept this gift as a sign of our friendship. I hope you’ll take it, because God has been generous to me, and I have more than enough." He kept urging Esau until he finally accepted it.

Esau then said, "At least let me leave some of my men with you." Jacob replied, "What’s the need? Just having your friendship is enough for me, my lord." So Esau went back to Seir that day.

title: Joseph Sold As A Slave By His Brothers
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
When Joseph was seventeen, he and his brothers worked as shepherds. However, Joseph angered them by telling their father a bad report about their behavior. Jacob loved Joseph, his son born in his old age, and made him a beautiful coat with long sleeves. When his brothers saw that their father favored Joseph above all of them, they grew to hate him and wouldn't speak to him nicely.

Joseph had a dream that he shared with his brothers, and it only made their hatred for him grow. He said, "I dreamed that while we were binding sheaves in the field, my sheaf stood tall, and your sheaves came around and bowed before mine." His brothers responded, "Are you really going to rule over us?" They despised him even more because of his dreams and what he said.

Later, Joseph had another dream and told his brothers, saying, "I had another dream where the sun, the moon, and eleven stars were bowing down to me." When he shared this with his father and brothers, his father admonished him, saying, "What does this dream mean? Are your mother and I and your brothers really going to bow down to you?" His brothers were jealous of him, but his father kept the dream in mind.

When his brothers went to take care of their father's flocks in Shechem, Jacob told Joseph, "Go see if everything is alright with your brothers and the flocks, and bring me back news." Joseph set out, and while wandering in the fields, a man found him and asked, "What are you looking for?" Joseph replied, "I'm looking for my brothers. Can you tell me where they are pasturing the flock?" The man informed him, "They've gone to Dothan." So Joseph followed his brothers to Dothan.

As they spotted Joseph from a distance before he reached them, they conspired to kills him. They said to each other, "Look, here comes that dreamer! Let’s kill him and throw him into one of the pits. Then we can say that a wild animal devoured him, and we’ll see what happens to his dreams!"

Judah, overhearing the plan, stepped in to save Joseph's life, saying, "Let’s not kill him." Reuben also urged them, "Don’t shed any blood. Just throw him into this pit here in the wilderness, but don’t hurt him." Reuben intended to rescue Joseph later and take him back to their father. So when Joseph arrived, they stripped off his colorful coat and tossed him into the empty pit, which had no water.

Then they sat down to eat. Looking up, they saw a group of Ishmaelites coming from Gilead, their camels loaded with spices and goods destined for Egypt. Judah suggested to his brothers, "What will we gain if we kill our brother and cover up his blood? Let’s sell him to the Ishmaelites and not harm him; after all, he's our brother, our own flesh and blood." They agreed, and they pulled Joseph up and sold him for twenty pieces of silver to the Ishmaelites headed to Egypt.

Later, his brothers took Joseph's coat, killed a goat, and soaked the coat in its blood. They brought it to their father and said, "We found this. Is it your son’s coat?" Jacob recognized it and exclaimed, "It is my son’s coat! A wild animal has attacked and devoured him! Joseph is surely torn to pieces!" Jacob tore his clothes, put on sackcloth, and mourned for his son for many days. All his children tried to comfort him, but he refused to be consoled, saying, "I will go to my grave mourning for my son." Thus, Jacob grieved deeply for Joseph.

title: Joseph'S Eagerness To Help Others
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Joseph was brought down to Egypt, where Potiphar, an Egyptian officer and the captain of the guard, bought him from the Ishmaelites.

Jehovah was with Joseph, allowing him to thrive in his master's house. When Potiphar noticed that Jehovah was with Joseph and that everything he did was successful, he placed his trust in him and made him his personal servant. He appointed Joseph as overseer of his household, putting all his possessions under Joseph's care. Ever since Joseph took charge, Jehovah blessed Potiphar's home, and all he owned—both in the house and the field—was blessed because of Joseph. Potiphar left everything he had in Joseph's hands, only paying attention to the food he ate. And Joseph was good-looking and charismatic.

After Joseph received these honors, his master's wife attempted to seduce him. But he declined, saying to her, "My master trusts me completely and doesn't know what goes on in the house. How could I betray him and sin against God?" Day after day she pressed him, but he remained steadfast. One day, when he entered the house to do his work and none of the household staff were around, she grabbed him by his garment and tried to seduce him again. He left his garment in her hand and ran out of the house.

She kept his garment until her husband came home. Then she said to him, "That Hebrew slave you brought here tried to assault me. When I screamed, he left his garment with me and ran away."

When Potiphar heard his wife's accusation, he was furious. He took Joseph and threw him into prison, where the king’s prisoners were held. So, Joseph ended up in prison. But Jehovah was with Joseph, showing him kindness and helping him win the favor of the prison keeper, who then put all the prisoners under Joseph's supervision, entrusting him with everything they did.

Sometime later, Pharaoh's butler and baker both offended the king of Egypt, and he was so angry that he imprisoned them in the same place where Joseph was held. The captain of the guard assigned Joseph to serve them, and they remained in prison for a while.

title: Joseph'S Eagerness To Help Others
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
That night, both the butler and the baker had dreams, each with a different interpretation. When Joseph came in to see them the next morning, he noticed they looked troubled. So he asked Pharaoh's officials, "Why do you look so down today?" They replied, "We had dreams, and there’s no one to interpret them." Joseph said, "Don’t interpretations belong to God? Share them with me, if you’d like.

Then the chief butler shared his dream with Joseph, saying, "In my dream, I saw a vine in front of me. It had three branches that budded, blossomed, and produced ripe grapes. Pharaoh's cup was in my hand, and I squeezed the grapes into the cup and handed it to Pharaoh."

Joseph responded, "This is its meaning: The three branches represent three days. Within three days, Pharaoh will lift you out of prison and restore you to your position, and you will hand Pharaoh his cup just as before. But when things go well for you, please remember me and mention me to Pharaoh so he can release me from this prison. I was taken from the land of the Hebrews, and here, I haven’t done anything to deserve being locked up in a dungeon."

When the chief baker heard that the butler’s interpretation was positive, he said to Joseph, "I had a dream too: there were three baskets of white bread on my head, and in the top basket, I had all kinds of baked goods for Pharaoh, but the birds were eating them out of the basket on my head." Joseph replied, "This is what it means: The three baskets are three days. Within three days, Pharaoh will remove your head and hang you on a tree, and the birds will eat your flesh."

Three days later, on Pharaoh's birthday, he held a feast for all his servants. He released the chief butler and the chief baker. The chief butler was restored to his position, allowing him to serve Pharaoh again, while the chief baker was hanged, just as Joseph had predicted. However, the chief butler forgot all about Joseph.

title: A Prisoner Who Became A Mighty Ruler
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Two years later, Pharaoh had a dream. As he stood by the Nile River, he saw seven healthy, fat cows coming up from the water, grazing on the lush river grass. Then, seven other cows emerged from the Nile, skinny and weak, and they stood by the healthy cows on the riverbank. The thin cows gobbled up the seven fat ones. Then Pharaoh woke up.

Later, he fell asleep again and had another dream. He saw seven ears of grain, plump and good, growing on a single stalk. After that, seven thin, withered ears sprouted, damaged by the east wind. The skinny ears swallowed the seven healthy ones. Pharaoh woke up again, realizing it was just a dream.

In the morning, Pharaoh was anxious. He called all the magicians and wise men of Egypt to interpret his dreams, but no one could explain their meanings.

Then the chief butler spoke up, saying to Pharaoh, "I remember my mistakes now. You were really angry with your servants and put me and the chief baker in prison with the captain of the guard. We both had dreams the same night, each with a different meaning. There was also a young Hebrew, a servant of the captain. We told him our dreams, and he interpreted them accurately. Just as he said, I got my job back, but the chief baker was executed."

Pharaoh then sent for Joseph, and they quickly brought him out of the dungeon. He shaved his face, changed his clothes, and came before Pharaoh. Pharaoh said to Joseph, "I've had a dream, and no one can tell me what it means. I've heard that you can interpret dreams." Joseph replied, "It’s not me; only God can provide a real answer for Pharaoh."

Pharaoh continued, "In my dream, I was by the Nile and saw seven fat, well-fed cows grazing on the river grass. Then seven more cows, thin and worse than any I'd seen in Egypt, came up after them, and the skinny cows ate the fat ones. After they had eaten, you couldn't even tell they had eaten anything since they still looked so thin. Then I woke up."

title: A Prisoner Who Became A Mighty Ruler
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
"Again, I dreamed I saw seven ears of grain, plump and good, growing on one stalk. Then seven thin ears, dried up by the east wind, sprang up after them, and the thin ears swallowed the good ones. I told my dream to the magicians, but no one can explain what it means."

Joseph then said to Pharaoh, "Both of Pharaoh's dreams are interconnected; God has revealed to Pharaoh what He plans to do. The seven healthy cows represent seven years, and the seven good ears also represent seven years. The seven skinny and thin cows are seven years, and the seven withered ears represent another seven years of famine. That’s why I said to Pharaoh, 'God has shown Pharaoh what He is about to do.' There will be seven years of abundance throughout Egypt, followed by seven years of famine, so severe that people will forget all the abundance. The famine will consume everything, and it will be extremely harsh."

"The fact that Pharaoh had the same dream twice shows that the famine is inevitable, and God is about to make it happen soon. Therefore, Pharaoh should choose someone wise and sensible to oversee Egypt. He should act quickly to set up overseers to collect one-fifth of everything that grows during those seven plentiful years. They should store it away in cities for food, so the people won’t starve during the coming famine."

Pharaoh and all his advisors were impressed with Joseph's plan. He turned to his people, saying, "Can we find anyone like this man, someone in whom is the spirit of God?" Then he told Joseph, "Since God has shown you all this, there is no one as wise as you. You will be in charge of my country, and my people will take commands from you. The only thing I will keep is my throne."

Pharaoh told Joseph, "Look, I have made you ruler over all of Egypt." He removed his signet ring from his finger and placed it on Joseph's hand, dressed him in fine linen, and put a gold necklace around his neck. He made Joseph ride in the second chariot and commanded everyone to shout, "Attention!" So Joseph became the ruler over all of Egypt. Pharaoh added, "I am Pharaoh, but no one in this land shall move a hand or a foot without your permission." Pharaoh also gave Joseph a wife, Asenath, the daughter of Potiphera. Joseph was thirty years old when he became the ruler of Egypt.

During the seven years of abundance, the harvests were huge. Joseph gathered all the food from those good years and stored it in the cities, keeping the grain from each region nearby. He collected so much grain that it was like counting the sand on the seashore, so he stopped keeping track because there was too much to measure.

Once the seven years of abundance ended, the seven years of famine began, just as Joseph had said. There was famine throughout all the lands, but Egypt had food. When people were hungry, they cried out to Pharaoh for bread, and Pharaoh instructed all the Egyptians, "Go to Joseph and do what he tells you." When the famine spread across the region, Joseph opened the storehouses and sold food to the Egyptians. The famine was severe everywhere, and people from all lands came to Joseph in Egypt to buy grain.

title: The Testing Of Joseph'S Brothers
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
When Jacob heard there was grain for sale in Egypt, he said to his sons, "Why are you just standing around? I’ve heard there’s grain in Egypt; go buy some so we can live and not starve." So, Joseph's ten brothers headed off to Egypt to buy grain. However, Jacob didn’t send Benjamin, Joseph’s other brother, along because he was worried that something might happen to him. Therefore, Jacob’s sons went with others to buy grain, since there was a famine in Canaan.

At that time, Joseph was the governor of Egypt, overseeing the sale of grain to everyone in need. When Joseph's brothers arrived, they bowed down to him, faces to the ground. As soon as Joseph spotted his brothers, he recognized them, but he pretended not to know them and questioned them harshly, asking, "Where do you come from?" They replied, "We’ve come from Canaan to buy food." While Joseph knew who they were, they had no idea he was their brother.

Joseph remembered the dreams he had about them and accused them, saying, "You’re spies! You’ve come to see how vulnerable this land is." They protested, "No, my lord; we're just here to buy food. We're all the sons of one man; we’re honest men, not spies!" But Joseph insisted, "No, you’ve come to see how defenseless the land is." They responded, "We're your servants, twelve brothers, the sons of the same father in Canaan. The youngest is with our father now, and one of us is no longer living." Joseph declared, "Just as I said, you are spies. Here’s how I’ll test you: as Pharaoh lives, you won’t leave unless your youngest brother comes here. One of you must go back and bring him, while the rest of you stay in prison, so I can see if you’re telling the truth or not. Otherwise, as sure as Pharaoh lives, you are indeed spies." He imprisoned them for three days.

title: The Testing Of Joseph'S Brothers
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
On the third day, Joseph said to them, "Listen, and you’ll live, because I fear God: if you are honest men, let one brother stay in prison while the rest of you take grain for your families. Bring back your youngest brother so that I know you’re telling the truth, and you won’t die."

They complied with Joseph’s orders but began discussing among themselves, saying, "We’re guilty for what we did to our brother; we didn’t listen when he begged us for help, and that’s why this trouble has come upon us." Reuben chimed in, "Didn’t I tell you not to hurt him? You wouldn’t listen."

Unbeknownst to them, Joseph understood everything they were saying, for he had been speaking through an interpreter. However, he turned away and cried. Afterward, he returned and took Simeon from among them, binding him in front of their eyes. Joseph then ordered his servants to fill their bags with grain, return their money to each sack, and provide food for the journey; and that’s exactly what happened. They loaded their donkeys with grain and set off.

Once they reached their father Jacob in Canaan, they recounted everything that had happened. They said, "The man who runs the land spoke harshly to us and threw us in prison, accusing us of being spies. We told him, 'We’re honest men, not spies; we’re twelve brothers, all sons of the same father. One is no longer living, and the youngest is with our father.' But the man insisted, 'This is how I’ll know you’re honest: leave one brother with me and take grain for your families, then bring your youngest brother back to me. Only then will I believe you and return your other brother to you.'"

title: The Testing Of Joseph'S Brothers
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
As they emptied their sacks, each brother discovered his money had been returned, and when they saw the coins, fear filled them. They turned to each other, asking, "What has God done to us?" Jacob lamented, "You’ve robbed me of my children! Joseph is gone, Simeon is gone, and now you want to take Benjamin too! All this is too much for me!" Reuben responded, "If I don’t bring him back to you, you can put my two sons to death. Trust me with him, and I’ll ensure he comes back." Jacob replied, "My son won’t go with you; his brother is dead, and he’s all I have left. If anything happens to him on your journey, it will break my heart and bring me sorrow until I die."

The famine continued to ravage the land. Once the grain they had brought from Egypt was gone, Jacob told his sons, "Go back and buy us more food." Judah reminded him, "The man was very clear: 'You won’t see me again unless your brother is with you.' If you send Benjamin, we’ll go down and get you food; but if you don’t, we won’t go because he said, 'You won’t see my face without your brother.'" Jacob asked, "Why did you tell him you had another brother?" They explained, "The man asked us about our family and we answered his questions honestly. How were we supposed to know he would demand to see our brother?"

title: The Testing Of Joseph'S Brothers
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Then Judah spoke to his father Jacob, saying, "Send the boy with me, and we’ll go at once, so that we, and you, and our little ones can live. I’ll be responsible for him; if I don’t bring him back, you can blame me forever. If we hadn’t waited so long, we would have gone back already." Jacob finally agreed, saying, "If it must be done, then do this: take some of the best products from the land in your bags and bring a gift to the man—some balm, syrup, spices, nuts, and almonds. Take twice as much money with you, returning the money that was previously put in your sacks; perhaps it was a mistake. Also, bring your brother and go back to the man. May God Almighty show mercy and free Benjamin and Simeon. But if I lose my sons, I’ll be heartbroken!" So, they took the gifts, double the money, and Benjamin, setting out for Egypt.

When Joseph saw Benjamin with them, he said to his steward, "Take these men to my house, prepare a feast, because they will be dining with me at noon." The steward did as he was instructed and brought them into Joseph’s house. The brothers were scared, thinking they were being brought in because of the money from their last visit, fearing they’d be punished and taken as slaves along with their donkeys.

As they approached Joseph’s steward, they said, "Sir, we came the first time just to buy food. But when we got home and opened our sacks, we found each man’s money in the mouth of his sack; we’re not sure how it got there. We’ve brought more money to buy food this time." The steward reassured them, "Don’t worry, your God and the God of your father has given you the treasure in your sacks; your money was returned to me."

Then he brought Simeon out to them. The steward led them into Joseph's house, offered them water to wash their feet, and provided fodder for their donkeys. They prepared the gifts for Joseph, eager to present them at noon.

title: The Testing Of Joseph'S Brothers
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
When Joseph arrived, they presented him with the gifts they had brought and bowed low before him. He asked them about their well-being, saying, "How is your father, the old man you spoke of? Is he still alive?" They responded, "Your servant, our father, is well; he is still alive." They bowed down again.

When Joseph looked up and saw Benjamin, his younger brother, he asked, "Is this the younger brother you mentioned?" He added, "God be gracious to you, my son." His heart ached with longing, and he sought a private place to cry, leaving the room.

After composing himself, he returned and ordered, "Bring on the food!" They served a meal for him alone, a separate meal for his brothers, and another for the Egyptians, as Egyptians wouldn't eat with Hebrews. Joseph's brothers were seated in order—oldest to youngest—and were astonished when they noticed. Joseph served portions to them, giving Benjamin five times more than anyone else. They laughed and enjoyed their time together.

Joseph then instructed the steward, "Fill the men’s sacks with as much food as they can carry, and place my silver cup in the mouth of the youngest’s sack, along with the money he paid for his grain." The steward followed Joseph’s orders.

As the morning sun rose, they were sent away with their donkeys. Just outside the city, Joseph told his steward, "Follow them and, when you catch up, ask them, 'Why have you repaid good with evil? Why have you stolen my silver cup, the one my master drinks from? This is wrong!"

The steward caught up with them and said these words. They replied, "Why would my lord say such things? We’d never do that! Remember, we returned the money we found in our sacks from Canaan. Why would we steal silver or gold from your house? If you find the cup with one of us, let that one die, and we will be your servants!" The steward replied, "Let it be as you say: the one who has it will be my slave, but the rest of you will be free." They quickly opened their bags, and the steward searched them, starting with the oldest and ending with the youngest; the cup was discovered in Benjamin's sack. They ripped their clothes in despair, and each man loaded his donkey and returned to the city.

title: Joseph'S Forgiveness Of His Brothers
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
When Judah and his brothers returned to Joseph's house, he was still there, and they threw themselves on the ground in front of him. Joseph asked, "What have you done? Don't you know that a man like me can always tell what's going on?" Judah answered, "What can we say to my lord? How can we explain or defend ourselves? God has revealed our guilt. See, both we and the man the cup was found with are your slaves." But Joseph replied, "No way! The man with whom the cup was found will be my slave, but you guys can go back home in peace to your father."

Judah stepped closer and said, "My lord, please let me speak just a moment. Don’t be angry with me, for you are like Pharaoh himself. You asked us, 'Do you have a father or a brother?' and we told you, 'We have an old father and a young brother who is the only son left from his mother. His father loves him dearly.' You insisted we bring him to you. We explained, 'The boy can't leave his father; if he does, his father might die.' But you said we wouldn’t see you again unless our youngest brother came with us.'

When we went back to our father, we shared what you said, and he told us, 'Go buy some food.' We replied, 'We can’t go unless our youngest brother is with us. We can’t see the man again without him.' Our father said, 'You know I had two sons with my wife; one went away, and I’m sure he’s gone forever. If you take this one too and something happens to him, it will break my heart.' If I go back to my father and Benjamin isn’t with us, our father will die of sorrow. I promised my father that I would take care of the boy, saying I would bear the blame forever if I didn’t bring him back. Please, let me stay here as your slave instead of the boy, and let him go back with his brothers. I can’t face my father without him; the pain would be too much for him."

title: Joseph'S Forgiveness Of His Brothers
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Joseph was so overwhelmed that he couldn’t hold back his tears anymore. He shouted, "Everyone leave me!" So, no Egyptians were around when Joseph revealed his identity to his brothers. He cried so loudly that the Egyptians and Pharaoh's court could hear him.

Then Joseph said to his brothers, "I am Joseph. Is my father still alive?" His brothers couldn’t reply because they were too ashamed to look him in the eye. Joseph continued, "Come closer to me, please." They stepped forward, and he said, "I am Joseph, your brother, the one you sold into Egypt. Don’t be worried or angry with yourselves for selling me here, because it was God who sent me ahead of you to save lives. The famine has already lasted two years, and there are still five years ahead without any plowing or harvest. God sent me ahead to ensure your survival through this great crisis and to keep your family alive. So really, it wasn’t you who sent me here, but God. He made me like a father to Pharaoh and ruler over all Egypt.

"Hurry, go back to my father and tell him, 'Your son Joseph says that God has made me master of all Egypt. Come to me quickly. You will live in the land of Goshen, close to me with your children and grandchildren, with all your flocks and herds, so that you will never be in need. I will take care of you during these next five years of famine.' You and my brother Benjamin can see that it’s really me speaking to you. Tell my father about my high position in Egypt and all that you have witnessed, and make sure you bring him here as fast as you can."

Then he embraced his brother Benjamin and wept. Benjamin cried on Joseph's shoulder too. He also kissed all his brothers and cried on them. After that, his brothers chatted with him.

title: Joseph'S Loyalty To His Family
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
News spread throughout Pharaoh's palace that Joseph's brothers had arrived, and this delighted Pharaoh and his servants. Pharaoh instructed Joseph, "Tell your brothers this: ‘Load up your animals, head to Canaan, bring your father and families back with you, and I will offer you the best of Egypt’s land; you'll enjoy the finest food available. You need to take wagons from Egypt for your children and wives and bring your father along too. Forget about your household items; everything in Egypt's the best is yours.’" Following Pharaoh's orders, the sons of Jacob got to work.

Joseph provided them with wagons just as Pharaoh had ordered, along with everything they needed for the journey. He also gave each of them a new outfit, but for Benjamin, he gave three hundred pieces of silver and five new sets of clothing. For his father, Joseph sent an incredible gift: ten donkeys loaded with the finest products of Egypt, plus ten donkeys filled with grain, bread, and provisions for the journey.

As he sent his brothers off, he warned them, "Be careful not to argue on the way!" They traveled back to Canaan to their father Jacob and shared the news: "Joseph is alive! He’s the ruler over all of Egypt!" Jacob was stunned and couldn't believe it. But once they shared everything Joseph had told them and showed him the wagons Joseph had sent to help, Jacob's spirit lifted. He exclaimed, "It’s true! My son Joseph is alive! I’m going to see him before I die!"

Jacob then set out on his journey, taking everything with him. He stopped first at Beersheba to offer sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac. That night, God spoke to him in a vision, calling, "Jacob! Jacob!" Jacob replied, "Here I am." God reassured him, "I am God, the God of your father. Don’t be afraid to go down to Egypt, because there I will make you into a great nation. I’ll go with you into Egypt, and I’ll bring you back again; and Joseph will be there to close your eyes when you die."

title: Joseph'S Loyalty To His Family
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
As Jacob left Beersheba, his sons loaded him, their children, and their wives into the wagons sent by Pharaoh. Jacob also sent Judah ahead to guide them to Goshen.

When they arrived in Goshen, Joseph prepared his chariot and went to meet his father. As soon as they saw each other, Jacob embraced Joseph tightly, and they shared a long, emotional moment.

Jacob told Joseph, "Now I can die happy, because I’ve seen you and know you’re alive." Joseph then said to his brothers and his father's household, "I’ll go talk to Pharaoh and tell him, ‘My brothers and my father’s family from Canaan have come to me. They are shepherds by trade and have brought their flocks and herds with them.’ When Pharaoh asks you what you do, say, ‘Your servants have been shepherds since we were young, just like our fathers.’ This way, you can settle in Goshen, since Egyptians don’t really respect shepherds."

Joseph went to Pharaoh and reported, "My father and brothers, with their sheep and cattle and everything they have, have come from Canaan, and they are now in Goshen." He chose five of his brothers and brought them to Pharaoh. Pharaoh asked them, "What is your business?" They replied, "We’re your servants, both we and our fathers, are shepherds." They went on to explain, "We’ve come to live in your land because the famine is severe in Canaan, and there’s no pasture for our flocks. Please let us stay in Goshen." Pharaoh told Joseph, "Let them settle in Goshen; and if you know of any capable men among them, put them in charge of my livestock."

Joseph then brought Jacob his father in to meet Pharaoh, and Jacob blessed Pharaoh. Pharaoh asked him, "How old are you?" Jacob replied, "I've lived 130 years; my years have been few and troubled compared to my ancestors.' After Jacob blessed Pharaoh, he exited the palace. Joseph then arranged for his father and brothers to live in Goshen, in the best part of Egypt, just as Pharaoh had instructed.

Joseph made sure to provide food for his father, brothers, and their families, according to the needs of the children. So, the Israelites settled in Egypt, in Goshen, where they thrived and multiplied.

title: The Boyhood And Training Of Moses
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
After Joseph and his brothers passed away, the Israelites multiplied rapidly, becoming so numerous and powerful that they filled the land. A new king came to power in Egypt who didn’t know Joseph. He told his people, "Look, the Israelites are growing too many and strong for us. Let’s deal wisely with them, or they might join our enemies if war breaks out and fight against us, leaving our land.”
