So, the Egyptians appointed taskmasters over the Israelites to impose heavy burdens on them. They built store-cities for Pharaoh, named Pithom and Rameses. But the more the Egyptians oppressed them, the more they multiplied and spread, which made the Egyptians fear what they might do. The Egyptians treated them harshly, enslaving them and making their lives miserable with hard labor in brick and mortar, as well as all kinds of tough work in the fields.

Pharaoh also ordered all his people, "You must throw every newborn Hebrew boy into the river, but let every girl live."

A man from the tribe of Levi married a woman from the same tribe, and they had a son. When she noticed that he was an exceptionally beautiful child, she hid him for three months. But when she could no longer keep it a secret, she made a basket from papyrus reeds, sealed it with tar, and placed the baby inside. Then she set the basket among the reeds along the bank of the Nile, while his sister stood nearby to see what would happen.

Pharaoh's daughter came down to the Nile to bathe, and while her maids walked along the riverbank, she spotted the basket among the reeds and sent a maid to fetch it. When she opened the basket and saw the baby crying, she felt compassion for him and said, "This must be one of the Hebrew children."

Then his sister asked Pharaoh's daughter, "Should I find one of the Hebrew women to nurse the child for you?" Pharaoh's daughter replied, "Yes, go ahead." The girl ran and called the child's mother. Pharaoh's daughter then said to her, "Take this child and nurse him for me, and I will pay you for it." The woman took her son and nursed him. When he was older, she brought him back to Pharaoh's daughter, who adopted him as her son and named him Moses, saying, "I drew him out of the water."

Years later, when Moses had grown up, he went to check on his people. As he watched them working hard, he saw an Egyptian beating a Hebrew, one of his own kin. He looked around and, seeing no one, struck down the Egyptian and buried him in the sand.

The next day, Moses went out again and saw two Hebrews fighting. He said to the one in the wrong, "Why are you hitting your fellow worker?" The man retorted, "Who made you our ruler and judge? Are you planning to kill me like you did the Egyptian?" Moses was scared and thought, "What I’ve done is known!" When Pharaoh found out about this, he sought to kill Moses, so Moses fled the country and settled in Midian.

While sitting by a well, the seven daughters of the priest of Midian arrived to draw water and fill the troughs for their father’s flock, but some shepherds came and drove them away. Moses stood up for the women and watered their flock.

When the daughters returned to their father, he asked, "How did you come back so soon today?" They said, "An Egyptian saved us from the shepherds, and he even drew water for us and watered the flock." He said to his daughters, "Where is he? Why did you leave the man? Invite him to have a meal with us." So, Moses stayed with the man, and he gave Moses his daughter Zipporah as a wife. They had a son, and Moses named him Gershom.

title: The Voice From The Bush
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
After a long time, the king of Egypt passed away. Moses was taking care of Jethro’s flock, his father-in-law. One day, he led the flock to the far side of the pasture and arrived at Horeb, the mountain of God. There, the angel of the Lord appeared to him in a flame of fire coming from the middle of a thorn bush. Moses noticed the bush was burning, but it wasn't consumed. He said, "I need to check this out! Why isn't the bush burning up?"

When the Lord saw Moses stop to look, He called out from the bush, "Moses, Moses." Moses replied, "Here I am." God said, "Don’t come any closer; take off your sandals because you are standing on holy ground." He added, "I am the God of your ancestors—the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob." At this, Moses hid his face, afraid to look at God.

But the Lord said, "I have definitely seen the suffering of my people in Egypt and heard their cries because of their harsh overseers. I know their pain. I've come down to rescue them from the Egyptians and lead them to a good and spacious land, a land flowing with milk and honey. I’ve heard the Israelites’ cries and witnessed their suffering at the hands of the Egyptians. So now, I’m sending you to Pharaoh to bring my people, the Israelites, out of Egypt."

Moses replied to God, "Who am I to go to Pharaoh and bring the Israelites out of Egypt?" God answered, "I will be with you, and this will be the sign that I have sent you: when you have brought the people out of Egypt, you will worship God on this mountain."

title: The Voice From The Bush
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Then Moses asked God, "If I go to the Israelites and tell them, 'The God of your ancestors has sent me to you,' and they ask me, 'What is His name?' what should I tell them?" God replied, "I AM WHO I AM." He said, "Tell the Israelites: 'I AM has sent me to you.' Gather the leaders of Israel and say to them, 'The Lord, the God of your ancestors, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, has appeared to me and said, I have certainly remembered you and seen what is happening to you in Egypt. I will bring you out of your suffering in Egypt to a land flowing with milk and honey.' They will listen to you, and you and the leaders of Israel will go to the king of Egypt and say to him, 'The Lord, the God of the Hebrews, has met with us. Please let us go three days’ journey into the wilderness to offer a sacrifice to the Lord our God.' I know that Pharaoh won’t let you go unless he’s forced to, so I will perform powerful wonders in Egypt. After this, he will let you go."

Moses said to the Lord, "But I’m not good at speaking! I’m slow to talk and struggle to express what I mean." The Lord replied, "Who gave people the ability to speak? Who makes someone deaf or unable to speak, or blind or able to see? Is it not I, the Lord? Now go; I will be with you and teach you what to say. Your brother Aaron will help you by speaking to the people on your behalf."

Then Moses returned to Jethro, his father-in-law, and said, "Let me go back to my people in Egypt to see if they are still alive." Jethro responded, "Go, and may you have my blessing."

title: Pharaoh The Stubborn Ruler
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Then God told Aaron, "Go into the wilderness and meet Moses." So he went and found Moses on the mountain of God, greeting him with a kiss. Moses shared everything that God had instructed him to say. Together, Moses and Aaron gathered all the leaders of the Israelites, and Aaron communicated all of God's words to Moses. The people believed; when they heard that God had remembered them and seen their suffering, they bowed their heads and worshipped.

Then Moses and Aaron approached Pharaoh and said, "God, the God of Israel, commands, 'Let my people go so they can hold a feast in my honor in the wilderness.'" But Pharaoh replied, "Who is God that I should listen to him and let Israel go? I don’t know God, and I'm not letting them go." They said, "The God of the Hebrews has appeared to us; allow us to journey three days into the wilderness so we can offer a sacrifice to God, or he might strike us with disease or the sword." The king of Egypt responded, "Moses and Aaron, why are you trying to distract the people from their work? Get back to your tasks!"

That same day, Pharaoh ordered the taskmasters in charge of the people, saying, "You will no longer provide straw for the people to make bricks as before. Instead, let them gather straw for themselves. But you must require them to produce the same number of bricks as before; do not reduce their quota at all. They are just being lazy, which is why they cry, 'Let us go and offer a sacrifice to our God.' Increase their workload so they’re too busy to pay attention to these false claims."

So the taskmasters went out and told the people, "This is Pharaoh's command: 'No more straw will be given to you. Go and find straw wherever you can, but your workload must remain the same.'" The people scattered throughout all of Egypt to collect scraps of straw. The taskmasters hurried them along, insisting, "You must complete your daily tasks just as you did when we provided straw." The overseers, whom Pharaoh's taskmasters had appointed to supervise the Israelites, were also beaten and were asked, "Why haven’t you produced as many bricks today as you did yesterday?"

Then the overseers of the Israelites went to Pharaoh and said, "Why are you treating your servants like this? No straw is given to us, yet you demand that we make bricks. Look how your servants are being beaten and how you are mistreating your people." But Pharaoh responded, "You are lazy, purely lazy; that’s why you say, 'Let us go and offer a sacrifice to God.' Now go to work, for no straw will be supplied, but you must continue to make the same number of bricks."

Then Moses turned back to God and said, "God, why have you brought such trouble upon this people? Why did you even send me? Ever since I went to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has made things worse for these people, and you haven’t done anything to help them."

God replied to Moses, "Now you will see what I will do to Pharaoh. With a powerful hand, he will surely let them go and even drive them out of his land."

title: The Cost Of Being Cruel And Stubborn
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Then God spoke to Moses, saying, "Pharaoh is really stubborn; he isn't going to let the people go. Go to Pharaoh early in the morning as he’s heading out to the water, and stand by the bank of the Nile to meet him. Tell him, 'The God of the Hebrews sent me to you with this command: Let my people go so they can worship me in the wilderness. But so far, you haven't listened. This is how you will know that I am God: I will strike the waters of the river with the staff in my hand, and they will turn to blood. The fish in the Nile will die, and the river will be so foul that the Egyptians will hate to drink its water.'"

Moses raised his staff in front of Pharaoh and his officials, striking the waters of the Nile, and just like that, all the water changed to blood. The fish in the Nile died, and the river became so stinky that the Egyptians couldn’t drink from it anymore, so they dug around the Nile trying to find water.

Seven days later, God instructed Moses, "Go to Pharaoh and say to him, 'God commands: Let my people go so they may worship me. If you refuse to let them go, I will bring a plague of frogs all over your land. The Nile will teem with frogs that will invade your home, your bedroom, your bed, the houses of your servants, and even your ovens and kneading bowls; the frogs will be everywhere, even on you and your people and all your servants.'"

Then God told Moses, "Tell Aaron to stretch out his hand with his staff over the rivers, canals, and ponds, causing frogs to cover the land of Egypt." Aaron did as he was instructed, and frogs came up and covered the entire land.

title: The Cost Of Being Cruel And Stubborn
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Pharaoh summoned Moses and Aaron, saying, "Pray to God to remove the frogs from me and my people, and I will let the people go to offer sacrifices to God." Moses replied, "Do you want to tell me when I should pray to God for you, your servants, and your people, so that the frogs are only left in the Nile?" Pharaoh answered, "Tomorrow." Moses said, "Just as you said; this way you'll know there's no one like God our Lord. The frogs will leave you, your palaces, and your servants, and will only remain in the Nile."

After Moses and Aaron left Pharaoh, Moses prayed to God to remove the frogs He had brought upon Pharaoh. God did as Moses asked; the frogs died in homes, courtyards, and fields, and the people piled them in heaps. The entire land stank. But when Pharaoh saw that the relief had come, he hardened his heart, just as God had said, and wouldn’t listen to Moses and Aaron.

Then God told Moses, "Get up early in the morning and stand before Pharaoh as he goes out to the water, and say to him, 'God commands: Let my people go so they may worship me. If you refuse to let my people go, I will send swarms of flies upon you, your servants, and your people. Your palaces will be filled with flies, and the ground will be covered with them. But at that time, I will protect the land of Goshen where my people live, so no flies will be there, and you will know that I am God, present in the land.'"

And God did just that: a huge swarm of flies invaded Pharaoh's palace and the homes of his servants; the whole land of Egypt was ruined because of the flies.

Pharaoh summoned Moses and Aaron, saying, "I will let you go to offer sacrifices to your God in the wilderness; just don’t go too far. Pray for me." Moses answered, "I will go out and pray to God that the flies leave you, your servants, and your people tomorrow. Just don't be deceitful again by refusing to let the people go to offer sacrifices to God."

title: The Cost Of Being Cruel And Stubborn
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Moses left Pharaoh and prayed to God, and God did what Moses asked; yet again, Pharaoh was stubborn and refused to let the people go.

Then God said to Moses, "Tell Pharaoh, 'God, the God of the Hebrews, commands: Let my people go so they may worship me. If you refuse to let them go and keep them, then the power of God will bring a severe plague on your cattle in the fields, including horses, donkeys, camels, herds, and flocks. But God will make a distinction between the cattle of Israel and the cattle of Egypt; not a single animal belonging to the Israelites will die.'"

God set a specific time, saying, "Tomorrow, God will do this in the land." The following day, God did exactly that—all the cattle of the Egyptians died, but none of the cattle of Israel perished. Pharaoh checked and found that not a single animal owned by the Israelites had died, but he remained stubborn and wouldn’t let the people go.

Then God told Moses, "Get up early in the morning and stand before Pharaoh, and tell him, 'God, the God of the Hebrews, commands: Let my people go so they may worship me. Are you still opposing my people by refusing to let them go? Tomorrow, around this time, I will send a heavy hail that has never occurred in Egypt since it became a nation.'"

God sent hail upon the land of Egypt, and the lightning that flashed through the hail was severe—unlike anything before in Egypt’s history. The hail destroyed everything in the fields, striking down both people and animals, ruining all the crops and breaking every tree. However, in the land of Goshen, where the Israelites were, there was no hail.

Pharaoh again summoned Moses and Aaron and said, "I have sinned this time; God is right, and I and my people are in the wrong. Pray to God; there has been enough of this thunder and hail. I will let you go, and you won't stay any longer." Moses responded, "As soon as I leave the city, I will spread out my hands in prayer to God; the thunder will stop, and there will be no more hail, so you will know the earth belongs to God. But I know that even then, you and your servants will not fear God."

title: The Cost Of Being Cruel And Stubborn
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Moses went out of the city from Pharaoh and spread out his hands toward God; the thunder and hail stopped, and the rain halted. But when Pharaoh saw that the rain, hail, and thunder had stopped, he sinned again, hardening his heart, and he wouldn’t let the Israelites go.

Moses and Aaron went back to Pharaoh, saying, "God, the God of the Hebrews, commands: 'How long will you refuse to listen to me? Let my people go so they can worship me. If you keep refusing to let them go, I will bring locusts into your land tomorrow, and they will cover the ground so no one will see it. They will eat everything left after the hail and all your trees in the fields.'"

Moses and Aaron were pushed out of Pharaoh’s presence, but Moses stretched out his staff over the land of Egypt as God commanded. A strong east wind blew all day and night, and in the morning, the wind brought locusts, filling the land of Egypt. There were more locusts than ever seen before or will be again, covering the ground so thoroughly that it looked dark, leaving not one green plant or tree anywhere in Egypt.

Then Pharaoh urgently called for Moses, saying, "I have sinned against God and you. Please forgive my sin just this once and pray to God to remove this deadly plague from me." Moses went out from Pharaoh and prayed to God, and God sent a strong west wind that drove the locusts into the Red Sea; not a single locust was left anywhere in Egypt. But God allowed Pharaoh’s heart to remain hard, so he didn’t let the Israelites go.

Then God said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand toward heaven, so there may be darkness over the land of Egypt, darkness so thick that it can be felt." Moses stretched out his hand, and complete darkness settled over Egypt for three days; no one could see their neighbor or move for three days, but the Israelites had light in their homes.

Then Pharaoh summoned Moses and said, "Go worship God, but let your flocks and herds stay behind; your little ones can go with you." Moses replied, "You must provide us with animals for sacrifices and burnt offerings for God because we have to take our cattle with us. Not a hoof will be left behind, as we don’t know what we need to offer to God until we arrive there."

But God stiffened Pharaoh's heart, and he didn’t let them go. Pharaoh said to him, "Leave me; be sure you never come back, or the day you do, you will die." Moses replied, "You have spoken truthfully; I will never see you again."

Moses told Pharaoh, "God says: 'At midnight, I will go through the land of Egypt. All the first-born sons in Egypt will die, from the first-born son of Pharaoh who sits on the throne to the first-born son of the slave girl at the mill, including all the first-born of the cattle. There will be a terrible cry of anguish throughout the land of Egypt, unlike anything ever heard before or will be again. But not a single dog will bark at the Israelites or their animals, so you will know that God distinguishes between the Egyptians and the Israelites. Your servants will come to me, pleading, 'Leave, along with all the people who follow you.' After that, I will go." Moses left Pharaoh in a fury.

title: The Escape From Egypt
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Moses gathered all the leaders of Israel and told them, "Choose lambs from your flocks according to your families and prepare the Passover lamb. Take a bunch of hyssop, dip it in the blood from the basin, and mark the top and sides of your door frames with it. And make sure none of you leaves your house until morning, because the Lord will pass through to strike down the Egyptians. When He sees the blood on the door frames, He will pass over your homes and keep you safe from the destroyer. You and your children should remember this event as a lasting tradition."

"When your children ask you, 'What does this ceremony mean?' you should tell them, 'It is the Passover sacrifice to the Lord, who passed over the houses of the Israelites in Egypt, sparing us when He struck down the Egyptians.'"

The people bowed their heads and worshipped, and the Israelites did exactly what the Lord had instructed Moses and Aaron.

At midnight, the Lord struck down all the firstborn in Egypt, from Pharaoh's firstborn who sat on the throne to the firstborn of the prisoner in the dungeon. Pharaoh awoke that night along with all his servants and the Egyptians, and there was a loud wail in Egypt, for every household had lost someone. He summoned Moses and Aaron during the night and said, "Get out of my people, both you and the Israelites! Go, worship the Lord as you’ve requested. Take your sheep and cattle with you, and please ask a blessing for me too." The Egyptians urged the Israelites to leave quickly, saying, "We’re all going to die!" So the people took their dough before it had risen, wrapping their kneading bowls in their clothing, which they carried on their shoulders.

The Israelites traveled on foot from Rameses to Succoth, and a mixed crowd went with them, along with numerous flocks and herds. They baked unleavened bread with the dough they had brought from Egypt because there was no yeast in it; they had been hastily driven out and hadn’t prepared any food for the journey.

They moved from Succoth and set up camp at Etham on the edge of the wilderness. The Lord went ahead of them during the day in a pillar of cloud, guiding their way, and at night, He appeared as a pillar of fire, providing light so they could travel both day and night; the pillar of cloud by day and the pillar of fire by night stayed ahead of the people.

title: The Escape From Egypt
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
When the king of Egypt learned that the people had escaped, his attitude and that of his servants changed. They said, "What have we done? We let the Israelites go free from serving us!" He got his chariot ready and took his troops with him, along with six hundred of the best chariots and all of Egypt's chariots, commanded by their officers. The Lord made Pharaoh stubborn, so he pursued the Israelites, determined to bring them back.

As Pharaoh approached, the Israelites looked up and saw the Egyptians marching after them. They were terrified and cried out to the Lord. They said to Moses, "Why did you bring us out of Egypt? Didn’t we tell you in Egypt to leave us alone so we could serve the Egyptians? It would have been better for us to stay and serve them than to die in the wilderness." But Moses replied, "Don’t be afraid. Stay calm, and you will see how the Lord saves you today; the Egyptians you see now, you will never see again. The Lord will fight for you; you just need to be still."

Then the angel of God who had been leading the Israelite army moved and stood behind them. The pillar of cloud changed its position from in front and took its place behind them, coming between the Egyptian and Israelite armies. The cloud was dark on one side and lit up the night on the other, so neither army approached the other all night.

Moses raised his hand over the sea, and the Lord drove the sea back with a powerful east wind that blew all night, making the sea dry. The Israelites walked through the middle of the sea on dry ground. The Egyptians followed them with all of Pharaoh’s horses, chariots, and horsemen. Just before dawn, the Lord looked out from the pillar of fire and cloud at the Egyptian army and threw them into confusion. He disabled their chariot wheels, making it hard for them to drive. The Egyptians shouted, "Let’s get away from the Israelites! The Lord is fighting for them against us!"

Then the Lord told Moses, "Stretch out your hand over the sea, so that the waters can return to the Egyptians—their chariots and horsemen." Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and at dawn the waters returned to normal, while the fleeing Egyptians were caught in the waves. The Lord swept them into the sea, and not a single one of them survived. That day, the Lord saved the Israelites from the Egyptians, and when they saw the bodies of the Egyptians washed up on the shore, they were filled with awe and believed in the Lord and in His servant Moses.

Then Miriam, the prophetess and sister of Aaron, took a tambourine and led all the women with tambourines and dancing as she sang with them:

"Sing to the Lord, for He has triumphed gloriously:
Both horse and rider He has hurled into the sea."

Then Moses and the Israelites sang this song to the Lord:

"I will sing to the Lord, for He has triumphed gloriously:
Both horse and rider He has hurled into the sea.
The Lord is my strength and song, He has delivered me;
He is my God, and I will praise Him; my father’s God whom I will honor."

title: God'S Commands To The People
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Moses led the Israelites away from the Red Sea and into the wilderness of Sinai, where they set up camp at the base of the mountain.

Moses climbed the mountain to be in the presence of God, and Jehovah called out to him, saying, "Tell the Israelites: 'You have witnessed what I did to the Egyptians, how I lifted you up on eagles' wings and brought you to myself. Now, if you listen to my voice and keep your promises to me, you will be my treasured people, set apart from all others, because the entire earth belongs to me. You will be a nation of priests, dedicated to my service.'"

So Moses gathered the leaders of the people and shared these words as Jehovah had instructed him. The people responded in unison, "We will do everything Jehovah has commanded."

When Moses reported the people's response to Jehovah, He said, "Look, I will come to you in a thick cloud so that the people can hear me speak and always trust you." Jehovah then told Moses, "Go to the people and make sure they are prepared today and tomorrow. They need to wash their clothes and be ready on the third day, because on that day I will come down on Mount Sinai for everyone to see."

On the third day, as morning broke, there were loud thunderclaps, flashes of lightning, and a thick cloud enveloped the mountain. A powerful trumpet blast echoed, making everyone in the camp tremble. Moses brought the people out of the camp to meet God, and they stood at the base of the mountain. Mount Sinai was completely shrouded in smoke, for Jehovah had descended upon it in fire. The smoke billowed up like the smoke from a furnace, and the entire mountain shook violently.

Then God declared, "I am Jehovah your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt, from the place where you were enslaved."

"You shall have no other gods but Me."

"Do not create a graven image or any likeness of anything in the heavens above, on the earth below, or in the waters beneath the earth. Do not bow down to them or serve them, for I, Jehovah your God, am a jealous God, punishing the sins of parents upon their children for the third and fourth generation. However, I show kindness to the thousandth generation of those who love me and follow my commands."

"You shall not misuse the name of Jehovah your God, for Jehovah will not hold him guiltless who misuses His name."

"Remember the Sabbath day and keep it holy. You shall work for six days and do all your tasks, but the seventh day is a Sabbath dedicated to Jehovah your God. On that day, neither you, nor your son, daughter, male servant, female servant, cattle, or guests should do any work. For in six days Jehovah made the heavens, the earth, the sea, and everything in them, and rested on the seventh day. That’s why Jehovah blessed the Sabbath day and made it holy."

"Honor your father and your mother so that you may live long in the land that Jehovah your God is giving you."

"You shall not murder."

"You shall not commit adultery."

"You shall not steal."

"You shall not bear false witness against your neighbor."

"You shall not covet your neighbor’s house; you shall not covet your neighbor’s wife, or male servant, or female servant, or ox, or donkey, or anything that belongs to your neighbor."

Then Jehovah said to Moses, "Write down these words, for I have established a solemn agreement with you and with Israel based on these words."

title: Our Duties To God And Man
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Listen, Israel: The Lord our God is the one true God. You should love the Lord your God with all your heart, all your soul, and all your strength.

Look, the heavens above, the earth, and everything in it all belong to the Lord your God. He loved your ancestors more than any other people, choosing their descendants, including you, from all nations. So, open your heart to Him and let Him guide you. The Lord your God is the most powerful of all gods, the mighty and wonderful God who shows no favoritism and accepts no bribes. He ensures justice for orphans and widows, loves those from other lands, and provides them with food and clothing. Love the Lord your God and always follow His laws and commands.

Do not deceive one another.

Do not lie to each other.

Do not make false promises in my name.

Do not harm or cheat your neighbor.

Do not insult the deaf.

Do not place a stumbling block before the blind.

Do not spread rumors about one another.

Do not harbor hatred toward anyone.

Do not seek revenge or hold a grudge against anyone.

Love your neighbor as you love yourself.

Stand up in the presence of those who are older and show respect to the elderly.

If a foreigner lives in your land, do not mistreat them. Treat them like one of your own people and love them as yourself.

title: The Report Of The Hebrew Spies
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Moses sent some men to scout out the land of Canaan. He said to them, "Go up into the southern region and head into the highlands. Explore the land and see if the people living there are strong or weak, if they are few or many. Check out whether the land is good or bad, and what kind of cities they live in—do they have camps or strongholds? Look to see if the land is fertile or barren, and if there are trees in it. Be brave and bring back some of the land's fruit," since it was the time when the grapes were just starting to ripen.

So they ventured into the southern region and arrived at Hebron. When they got to the valley of Eshcol, they cut down a branch bearing a single cluster of grapes, carrying it on a pole that two men lifted. They also collected some pomegranates and figs. That place became known as the valley of the Grape Cluster because of the large grapes the Israelites harvested there.

After that, they returned to Moses, Aaron, and the entire Israelite community at Kadesh, bringing back word and showing them the land's fruits. They reported to Moses, "We went to the land you sent us to, and it truly flows with milk and honey; here’s some of its fruit. However, the people living there are strong, and the cities are massive with tall walls surrounding them."

Caleb then quieted the crowd and said, "We should go up at once and take the land; we are more than capable of conquering it." But the other men with him responded, "We cannot defeat those people; they are stronger than we are. The people we saw there are extremely tall, and we even saw giants. We felt like grasshoppers next to them, and I'm sure they saw us the same way."

That night, all the people cried and complained, "Why did the Lord bring us to this land only to fall by the sword? Our wives and children will be taken as captives! Wouldn’t it be better for us to go back to Egypt?" They went on to say to each other, "Let's choose a leader and return to Egypt."

title: The Report Of The Hebrew Spies
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Moses and Aaron fell to the ground in front of all the Israelites gathered there. Then Joshua and Caleb, who were among those who explored the land, tore their clothes and spoke to the people, saying, "The land we explored is truly amazing. If the Lord is pleased with us, He will bring us into this land and give it to us—a land flowing with milk and honey. Don’t rebel against the Lord; don’t be afraid of the people in the land because they will be our food. Their protection is gone, and the Lord is with us; don’t fear them!" But the people refused to trust the Lord.

Then the Lord spoke to Moses, saying, "How long will this people continue to despise me? How long will they refuse to trust me, despite all the miracles I have shown them? I will bring sickness upon them and wipe them out, and I will make you and your family a greater nation than they are."

Moses replied to the Lord, "If the Egyptians hear about it, they will say, 'The Lord killed them in the wilderness because He couldn't bring them to the land He promised them.' Please forgive this people's sin, for Your love is great, just as You have forgiven them since the day we left Egypt."

The Lord answered, "I have forgiven them as you asked; but as surely as I live and as surely as the entire earth will be filled with my glory, none of the men who saw my glory and the wonders I performed in Egypt and the wilderness—yet still tested me ten times and did not listen to my voice—will see the land that I promised their ancestors. However, I will bring my servant Caleb into the land he explored, for he has a different spirit and has faithfully followed me. His children will possess it. But your little ones, whom you said would be taken captive, I will bring in, and they will inherit the land you rejected. Your dead bodies will fall in this wilderness, and your children will wander there for forty years, suffering for your unfaithfulness until all of your bodies decay in the wilderness."

title: The Last Words Of Moses
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
As Moses grew older, he addressed all the Israelites, saying, "Today, I am a hundred and twenty years old. I can no longer move about freely, and God has told me, 'You will not cross the Jordan River.' The Lord your God is going ahead of you. He will defeat these nations for you, and you will drive them out. Joshua will lead you, just as I have commanded. Be courageous and strong; don’t be afraid of them, because the Lord your God is with you. He will never fail you or abandon you."

Moses then called Joshua and spoke to him in front of all Israel: "Be courageous and strong, for you will bring this people into the land that the Lord promised their ancestors. You will lead them there. God is going ahead of you; He will be with you. Don’t be afraid or terrified."

After that, Moses climbed the plains of Moab to Mount Nebo, to the peak of Pisgah opposite Jericho. The Lord showed him all the land and said, "This is the land I promised with an oath to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, saying, 'I will give it to your descendants.' You can see it with your own eyes, but you won't cross over into it."

So Moses, the servant of God, died there in Moab as the Lord had said. God buried him in a valley in Moab, but to this day, no one knows the exact location of his burial. Moses was a hundred and twenty years old when he passed away, yet his eyesight was clear, and he hadn’t lost his strength. The Israelites mourned for Moses in the plains of Moab for thirty days, and then their period of mourning came to an end.

Joshua, son of Nun, was filled with a spirit of wisdom because Moses had laid his hands on him. The Israelites listened to Joshua and followed the commands that God had given to Moses. At that time, no prophet had arisen in Israel who was as close to God as Moses was.

title: Crossing The River Jordan
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
After Moses passed away, Jehovah spoke to Joshua, his assistant, and said, "Moses my servant is dead. Now get up and lead all these people across the Jordan River into the land that I am about to give to the Israelites. No one will be able to stand against you for as long as you live. Just as I was with Moses, I will be with you; I will not fail you or abandon you. Be strong and courageous, because you will help this people take possession of the land that I promised to their ancestors. Just make sure to be strong and courageous in following all the teachings that Moses my servant gave you. Don't stray from them, either to the right or to the left, and you will be successful wherever you go. Haven't I commanded you? Be strong and courageous; don't be afraid or disheartened, for Jehovah your God is with you wherever you go."

Joshua then instructed his officers, "Go through the camp and give this command: 'Get your supplies ready, because in three days, you will cross the Jordan River to take possession of the land that Jehovah your God is giving you.'"

While Joshua was at Shittim, he secretly sent two men to scout the land, specifically Jericho. They entered the home of a woman named Rahab and stayed there.

The king of Jericho heard reports, saying, "Some men from the Israelites came here tonight to spy on the land." So the king sent a message to Rahab: "Bring out the men who came into your house, for they have come to explore the entire land."

However, the woman had taken the two men and hidden them. She told the king's messengers, "It's true, some men came to me, but I don't know where they were from. When night fell, the gate was shut, and the men went out. I don’t know where they went. If you hurry, you might catch up with them." In reality, she had hidden them on the roof under some stalks of flax that she had laid out. The men of Jericho pursued them toward the Jordan fords, and as soon as they left, she shut the gate.

title: Crossing The River Jordan
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
The spies had barely settled down when Rahab came up to them on the roof and said, "I know that Jehovah has given you this land, and we are all terrified of you. Everyone in this land is losing heart because of you. Now swears to me by Jehovah that since I've shown you kindness, you will show kindness to my family. Promise to spare the lives of my father, my mother, my brothers, my sisters, and all their possessions, and that you won’t execute us." The men replied, "We promise to save your lives if you keep our mission a secret; when Jehovah gives us the land, we will treat you kindly and honorably."

She then lowered them down by a rope through her window, as her house was built into the city wall. She advised them, "Go into the hills so the men searching for you won’t find you. Stay hidden there for three days until they return. Then you can continue on your journey."

The men told her, "We will be free from our promise unless you tie this scarlet cord in the window where you let us down and gather your family into your house. If anyone leaves your house and goes outside, they will be responsible for their own death, and we will be innocent. But if anyone stays with you in this house, we will be responsible for their safety if any harm comes to them. If you disclose what we’re doing, we will be released from our promise to you." She said, "I accept your terms." After they left, she tied the scarlet cord in the window.

The two men went into the hills and stayed hidden for three days until those searching for them had returned. They looked for them in every direction but couldn’t find them. Finally, the two men came down from the hills, crossed the river, and returned to Joshua to report everything that had happened.

title: Crossing The River Jordan
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Joshua woke up early the next morning and set out from Shittim. He and all the Israelites arrived at the Jordan, where they spent the night before crossing. Joshua addressed the people, saying, "Consecrate yourselves, for tomorrow Jehovah will perform amazing wonders among you. Come and listen to the words of Jehovah your God. By this, you will know that the living God is with you: the ark of the Lord of all the earth is about to lead you into the Jordan. When the priests who carry the ark step into the river, its waters will be cut off, and the waters flowing down from upstream will stand still."

When the people left their tents to cross the Jordan, the priests carrying the ark led the way. As the bearers of the ark reached the river, and their feet dipped into the water (which was overflowing its banks during harvest), the waters upstream stopped flowing and piled up in a heap near Adam, the city close to Zarethan. Meanwhile, the waters flowing down toward the Dead Sea were completely cut off, allowing the people to cross right in front of Jericho. The priests carrying the ark of Jehovah stood firm on dry ground in the middle of the Jordan while all the Israelites crossed over on dry ground until the entire nation had made it across.

Once everyone had crossed, Jehovah instructed Joshua, "Tell them to take twelve stones from the center of the Jordan, where the priests had stood, and carry them to your campsite. This will serve as a reminder for them. When your children ask you in the future, 'What do these stones mean to you?' you will tell them, 'These stones are a reminder that the waters of the Jordan were cut off before the ark of Jehovah when it crossed the Jordan.' These stones will always remind the Israelites of this miracle."

So the Israelites did exactly as Joshua had commanded, taking twelve stones from the middle of the Jordan, corresponding to the number of tribes of the Israelites. They carried them to their campsite and laid them down.

Then the waters of the Jordan returned to normal, overflowing its banks as it had before.

title: The Capture Of Jericho And Ai
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Jericho had locked its gates because of the Israelites, and no one was allowed in or out. But the Lord said to Joshua, "Look, I have delivered Jericho into your hands, along with its king and its mighty warriors. You are to march around the city with all the soldiers, circling it once. Do this for six days, and on the seventh day, the people will attack, each man going straight ahead."

Joshua then told the people, "March around the city and let the armed men go ahead of the Ark of the Lord. You must not shout or raise your voices; not a single word should come out of your mouth until the day I say, 'Shout!' Then you can shout!"

So, he had the Ark of the Lord carried around the city once, and then they went back to camp for the night. On the second day, they marched around the city again and returned to camp. They did this for six days. On the seventh day, they woke up at dawn and marched around the city in the same way, but that day, they circled the city seven times. On the seventh time, the priests blew the trumpets, and Joshua told the people, "Shout! The Lord has given you the city. The city and everything in it are devoted to the Lord; only Rahab and those with her in her house will be spared, because she hid the messengers we sent."

So the people shouted, the walls fell down, and they charged into the city and took it over. Joshua saved the lives of Rahab, her family, and everything she had because she hid the spies he sent to explore Jericho; they have lived among the Israelites ever since.

Then Joshua and all his warriors set out to attack Ai. He chose thirty thousand brave soldiers and sent them out at night with this plan, "Hide somewhere near the town, but not too close. Be ready to spring into action. I and all the people with me will approach Ai, and when they come out to fight us, we will act like we're fleeing. They will chase after us, thinking we are retreating. Then you will rise up from your hiding place and take the town, and once you capture it, set it on fire."

Joshua sent them out, and they hid on the west side of Ai. He spent the night among the people and got up early the next morning to gather them. Then he led the rulers of Israel up to Ai. The warriors with him approached the town, and when the king of Ai saw them, the townspeople quickly got ready to fight against the Israelites, unaware that men were hiding behind the town ready to attack.

Then Joshua and the Israelites pretended to retreat and fled toward the wilderness; the townspeople were called to follow them. Leaving the town unguarded, they pursued the Israelites. Meanwhile, the men who had been hiding sprang into action and set the town on fire. When the men of Ai looked back, they saw smoke rising from the town, and there was no escape for them, as the Israelites who had been pretending to flee turned back to face those who were chasing them. As the smoke billowed from the town, the rest of the Israelites came out to attack, surrounding the men of Ai so that none could escape.

title: Women Who Saved A Nation
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Later, Sisera, who commanded nine hundred iron chariots, cruelly oppressed the Israelites for twenty long years. Then, the prophetess Deborah, the wife of Lappidoth, stepped forward to deliver Israel. She would sit beneath the palm tree that bears her name between Ramah and Bethel in the highlands of Ephraim, and the Israelites came to her for decision on their disputes.

She called for Barak, the son of Abinoam, from Kadesh Naphtali, and said to him, "Doesn't Jehovah, the God of Israel, command you: 'Go, march to Mount Tabor and take with you ten thousand men from Naphtali and Zebulun? Then, I will lead Sisera along with his chariots and troops to the Kishon River, and I will hand him over to you.'" Barak replied, "If you go with me, I will go; but if you don't, then I won't go." She answered, "I will definitely go with you, but you won't receive glory for this battle, for Jehovah will deliver Sisera into the hands of a woman."

So, Deborah got up and went with Barak to Kadesh. Barak then summoned the Zebulunites and Naphtalites to assemble at Kadesh, and ten thousand men followed him; Deborah went up with him as well.

Now, Heber the Kenite had separated from the Kenites, who were descendants of Jethro, Moses' father-in-law, and had pitched his tent near the oak close to Kadesh.

When Sisera learned that Barak, the son of Abinoam, had gone up to Mount Tabor, he gathered all his chariots—those nine hundred iron ones—and all his soldiers from the foreign city of Harosheth to the Kishon River. Then Deborah said to Barak, "It's time to attack! Today, Jehovah has given Sisera into your hands. Hasn't Jehovah gone out ahead of you?"

So Barak charged down from Mount Tabor, followed by ten thousand men. As Barak's troops charged, Jehovah scattered Sisera and his chariots and all his forces, causing Sisera to abandon his war chariot and flee on foot. Barak pursued the chariots and soldiers all the way to Harosheth, and not a single member of Sisera's army was left standing.

title: Women Who Saved A Nation
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: That day, Deborah and Barak, the son of Abinoam, sang this song:

title: Women Who Saved A Nation
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
"O Jehovah, when you moved from Seir,
Marching from the region of Edom,
The earth trembled, the heavens rocked,
The clouds dripped with rain;
The hills quaked as you approached,
Mount Sinai, before Israel's God.
"In the time of Shamgar, Anath's son,
And in the days of Jael, the roads were deserted,
And travelers walked through byways.
Leadership had vanished in Israel,
Until you, O Deborah, arose,
Until you became a mother in Israel.
"My heart goes out to the commanders of Israel,
Who volunteered among the people. Bless Jehovah!
You who ride on sleek donkeys,
You who sit upon luxurious saddles;
You who walk along the roads, tell of this.
Far from the echoes of dividing spoil,
At the wells where water is drawn,
Let them share Jehovah's righteous deeds,
And the just acts of his leaders!
"Then the people of Jehovah
Broke into the gates, crying:
'Awake, awake, O Deborah,
Awake, awake, sing a battle song!
Rise up, rise up, O Barak,
Take your captives, son of Abinoam!'
"And they went down to face the powerful,
The Lord's people against the mighty.
From Machir, commanders descended,
From Zebulun, standard-bearers,
With Deborah were Issachar's princes,
And with Barak, the men of Naphtali;
Into the valley they charged after him.
"Zebulun risked their lives,
Naphtali stood firm in the heights of battle.
Rulers came together, they fought,
The rulers of Canaan fought
At Taanach by the waters of Megiddo.
"They seized no silver as loot,
For from heaven, the very stars fought,
They fought against Sisera from their courses.
The Kishon River swept them away,
That ancient stream, the Kishon.
O my soul, march on with strength!
Then their horse hooves pounded
To the gallop of swift steeds.
"Blessed above all women shall Jael be,
The wife of Heber the Kenite,
More blessed than all nomad women!
He asked for water, and she gave him milk,
Curdled milk in a lavish bowl!
She took the tent peg in her hand,
And with the hammer she struck Sisera,
Crushing his skull, shattering his head.
He fell at her feet and lay still,
At her feet he sank, and there he fell,
Victim to her hand!
"Through the window, she gazed and cried,
Through the lattice, Sisera's mother asked:
'Why is his chariot taking so long?
Why the delay in the hoofbeats of horses?'
Then the wisest of her ladies answered,
And she even answered her own question,
'Aren't they dividing the spoil?
One woman or two for each warrior,
A splendid array of dyed cloths for Sisera,
Beautifully embroidered fabrics,
And some lovely pieces for his neck?'
"So may your enemies perish, O Jehovah!
And those who love you be like the sun,
Shining in unstoppable brilliance!"

title: Gideon'S Brave Band
author: The Children's Bible
themes: the effects of fear and courage
text_chunk:
Over time, the Midianites defeated the Israelites. To protect themselves, the Israelites hid in caves, mountains, and strongholds. After the Israelites planted their crops, the Midianites would sweep in and steal everything—sheep, oxen, donkeys—taking their animals and tents. The Israelites were so devastated by the Midianites that they cried out to God for help.

Then, the angel of God came and sat beneath the oak tree in Ophrah, which belonged to Joash from the Abiezer clan. His son, Gideon, was threshing wheat in a winepress to keep it hidden from the Midianites. The angel appeared to him and said, "The Lord is with you, brave warrior!" Gideon replied, "Sir, if the Lord is with us, why has all this happened? Where are all the miracles our ancestors told us about, saying, 'Did the Lord not bring us out of Egypt?' But now the Lord has abandoned us and handed us over to the Midianites."

The Lord turned to Gideon and said, "With the strength you have, go and save Israel from the Midianites. I am sending you!" But Gideon responded, "But Lord, how can I save Israel? My family is the weakest in Manasseh, and I am the least in my father's house." The Lord assured him, "I will be with you, and you will defeat the Midianites as if they were one man."

Gideon was filled with the Spirit of the Lord, and he blew the trumpet, gathering the Abiezerites to join him. He also sent messengers throughout the land of Manasseh, and they rallied to him. He reached out to the Asherites, Zebulunites, and Naphtalites, and they came to join his cause. But the Lord told Gideon, “You have too many people with you; if I let the Midianites fall into their hands, they will claim, 'We saved ourselves!' So announce to your troops, 'Anyone who is afraid can go home.'"

Gideon separated the troops, and twenty-two thousand of them went home, leaving only ten thousand. But the Lord said to him, "There are still too many. Take them down to the water, and I will test them for you. Anyone I say, 'This one can go with you,' will go; and anyone I say, 'This one cannot go with you,' will not go."

title: Gideon'S Brave Band
author: The Children's Bible
themes: the effects of fear and courage
text_chunk:
So Gideon led the remaining people to the water. The Lord instructed him, "Put aside those who lap the water like dogs and those who kneel down to drink separately." The ones who lapped with their hands to their mouths numbered three hundred men, while the rest knelt down to drink. Then the Lord told Gideon, "With the three hundred men who lapped, I will save you and deliver the Midianites into your hands. Let the others go home." So they took their provisions and trumpets, and Gideon sent everyone else away, keeping only the three hundred.

Gideon crossed the Jordan with his three hundred men, who were exhausted but still in pursuit. He asked the people of Succoth, "Please, give some loaves of bread to my men, as they are weary and I am chasing after Zebah and Zalmunna, the kings of Midian." The rulers of Succoth responded, "Are Zebah and Zalmunna already in your power that we should provide bread for your troops?" Gideon replied, "Once the Lord delivers Zebah and Zalmunna into my hands, I will punish your people for this insult with thorns and briers from the desert." He continued to Penuel and made the same request, receiving the same reply. He warned them, "When I return victorious, I will demolish this tower."

Zebah and Zalmunna were camped at Karkor with about fifteen thousand men. Gideon moved stealthily along the caravan route and caught the enemy by surprise. He divided his three hundred men into three groups, giving each of them horns and empty jars. Each jar held a torch. He instructed them, "Follow my lead! When I reach the edge of the camp and blow my horn, you also blow your horns on every side of the camp and shout, 'For the Lord and Gideon!'"

Gideon and his hundred men reached the edge of the camp just as the guards were being posted. They blew their horns and shattered the jars they had. The other two groups did the same, holding their torches in one hand and swords in the other, shouting, "The Sword of the Lord and of Gideon!" The entire camp woke up in panic, sounding the alarm and fleeing. Zebah and Zalmunna tried to escape, but Gideon chased after them and captured the two Midianite kings.

title: Gideon'S Brave Band
author: The Children's Bible
themes: the effects of fear and courage
text_chunk:
When Gideon returned from battle, he captured a young man from Succoth. At Gideon's request, the young man wrote down the names of the rulers and prominent men of Succoth, totaling seventy-seven. When Gideon confronted the men of Succoth, he said, "Here are Zebah and Zalmunna, the ones you mocked, asking, 'Are they already in your power to give food to your weary men?'" He then punished the leaders of Succoth with desert thorns, and he demolished the tower in Penuel, executing the men there.

Gideon asked Zebah and Zalmunna, "What kind of men did you kill at Tabor?" They answered, "They looked just like you; each one was a prince." Gideon replied, "Those were my own brothers, my mother's sons. As surely as the Lord lives, if you had spared them, I would not kill you now."

He then turned to his eldest son, Jether, and said, "Kill them!" But Jether hesitated and didn't draw his sword because he was afraid; he was still just a boy. Zebah and Zalmunna urged, "If you want to kill us, do it yourself, for a man should do what a man can!" So Gideon rose and killed Zebah and Zalmunna, taking the ornaments from their camels.

The men of Israel said to Gideon, "Rule over us, you and your sons after you, for you have saved us from the Midianites." Gideon replied, "I will not rule over you, nor will my son; the Lord will rule over you. However, I do have one request: each man should give me the earrings from their plunder," for they had golden earrings as desert dwellers. They responded, "Of course, we will give them." They spread out a blanket, and each man dropped their earrings into it until the total weight reached nearly seventy pounds of gold. Gideon then made a priestly garment from the gold to wear while seeking guidance from the Lord, placing it in his hometown, Ophrah.

Gideon passed away at a ripe old age and was laid to rest in the tomb of Joash, his father, in Ophrah belonging to the Abiezerites.

title: Jephthah'S Foolish Promise
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Jephthah, a skilled warrior from Gilead, was known for his prowess in battle. However, he was also the son of a woman of ill repute, which led him to flee from his family and settle in the land of Tob. There, he gathered a group of outlaws who joined him on various raids.

Eventually, the Ammonites declared war on the Israelites. The elders of Gilead traveled to find Jephthah in Tob and said to him, "We need your leadership to fight against the Ammonites. Will you come and be our commander?" Jephthah responded, "Aren't you the same people who hated me and drove me out of my father's house? Why are you asking for my help now that you're in trouble?" The elders assured him, "That’s exactly why we’ve come to you. If you lead us in battle against the Ammonites, you will be our chief over all of Gilead." Jephthah replied, "If you bring me back to fight the Ammonites and the Lord gives me victory, I will be your chief." The elders agreed, saying, "The Lord will be our witness; we promise to do as you say."

So Jephthah returned with the elders of Gilead and was appointed chief and commander. He then made a vow to the Lord, saying, "If you grant me victory over the Ammonites, the first person who comes out of my house to greet me upon my return will belong to you, and I will offer that person as a burnt offering."

Jephthah set out to battle the Ammonites, and the Lord granted him victory, delivering the enemy into his hands. As he returned home to Mizpah, his daughter rushed out to meet him with tambourines and joyful dances. She was his only child; he had no other son or daughter. When he saw her, he tore his clothes in despair and exclaimed, "Oh, my daughter, you’ve brought me such sorrow! I’ve made a vow to the Lord that I cannot break!" She responded, "Father, you’ve made a solemn promise to the Lord; do what you've vowed, for the Lord has given you victory over your enemies. Please grant me one favor: let me go into the mountains for two months with my friends to mourn my fate, since I will never marry or have children." He agreed, saying, "Go."

She spent two months with her friends on the mountains, mourning her fate of never becoming a wife or mother. After the two months, she returned to her father, and he fulfilled his vow, even though she had never been married. This led to a tradition in Israel: every year, the women of Israel would go out for four days to grieve the death of Jephthah's daughter.

title: Samson Who Did To Others As They Did To Him
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
In a town called Zorah, there lived a man named Manoah from the Danite clan. He and his wife had no children. One day, an angel of the Lord appeared to his wife and said, "You may not have kids now, but you need to be careful. Don’t drink any wine or strong drink, and steer clear of anything unclean, because you’re about to have a son. He’ll be special; no razor will ever touch his head, because he’ll be dedicated to God from the moment he’s born." So, she had a son and named him Samson.

Samson once visited Timnah and saw a Philistine woman there. When he got back, he told his parents, "I found a Philistine woman in Timnah. I want her to be my wife." His parents replied, "Are there no women from our own tribe or among our people? Why do you want to marry someone from the Philistines?" But Samson insisted, "Get her for me. She’s the one I want."

Samson went with his parents to Timnah. As they approached the vineyards, a young lion suddenly roared at him. The spirit of the Lord came upon Samson, and even though he didn’t have anything in his hands, he tore the lion apart like it was a small animal. He didn’t tell his parents what he had done.

Later, he went down to speak with the woman, and he liked her. When he returned to marry her, he took a moment to look at the lion’s carcass and found a swarm of bees with honey inside. He scooped some honey into his hands and ate as he walked. When he got to his parents, he shared the honey with them but kept it a secret that it came from the lion.

Samson then returned to the woman and threw a feast, as was the custom for grooms. When the Philistines saw him, they sent thirty companions to celebrate with him. Samson said to them, "I have a riddle for you. If you can solve it within the week of the feast, I’ll give you thirty fine linen robes and thirty suits of clothes. But if you can’t figure it out, you owe me thirty fine linen robes and thirty suits of clothes." They replied, "Go ahead, tell us your riddle, and we’ll listen."

title: Samson Who Did To Others As They Did To Him
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
"Here’s the riddle: Out of the eater came something to eat, and out of the strong came something sweet," he said.

On the seventh day, they went to Samson’s wife and said, "You need to get your husband to tell us the answer to the riddle, or we’ll burn you and your father’s house down. Did you invite us here just to make us poor?" So, Samson’s wife cried in front of him and said, "You don’t love me at all! You told a riddle to my people, but you won’t tell me what it is." He replied, "I haven’t told my father or mother, so why would I tell you?" She cried and kept at him until he finally revealed the answer to her on the seventh day, and she shared it with her fellow countrymen.

Just before sunset on the seventh day, the men of the city confronted him, saying, "What’s sweeter than honey? And what’s stronger than a lion?" He responded:

"If you hadn’t plowed with my heifer, you wouldn’t have cracked my riddle."

Suddenly filled with divine strength, he went down to Ashkelon, killed thirty men, and took their belongings, giving the confiscated clothes to those who solved the riddle. Furious, he returned to his father's house, and his bride was given to his best man.

Some time later, at the wheat harvest, Samson returned to visit his wife with a young goat as a gift. When he said, "Let me go to my wife in the bedroom," her father refused him, saying, "I figured you must have hated her, so I gave her to your best man. Isn’t her younger sister prettier? Just take her instead." Samson replied, "Now I’m justified in hurting the Philistines!" He then caught three hundred foxes, tied them tail to tail, and put a torch between each pair. After lighting the torches, he released them into the standing grain of the Philistines, destroying all their crops and olive trees.

title: Samson Who Did To Others As They Did To Him
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
The Philistines asked, "Who did this?" Someone answered, "Samson, the son-in-law of the Timnite; that man took Samson’s wife and gave her to his best man." Furious, the Philistines went and burned her and her father. Samson declared, "If this is how you treat me, I won’t stop until I get my revenge!" He fought violently and killed many Philistines, then hid out in a cave on the cliff of Etam.

When the Philistines camped in Judah and raided Lehi, the men of Judah asked, "Why are you coming against us?" They replied, "We’re here to bind Samson and pay him back for what he did to us." Three thousand men from Judah went to the cave and said to Samson, "Don’t you know the Philistines are our rulers? What are you doing?" He answered, "I’m just doing to them what they did to me." They said, "We’ve come to tie you up and hand you over to the Philistines." Samson responded, "Promise me you won’t attack me yourselves." They replied, "No, we’ll only bind you securely and deliver you to them, but we won’t kill you." So they tied him up with two new ropes and brought him up from the cliff.

When he arrived at Lehi, the Philistines shouted as they approached him. Then Samson received a sudden burst of divine strength, and the ropes on his arms became like burnt flax, melting right off. He grabbed a fresh jawbone of a donkey and killed a thousand men with it. After that, Samson exclaimed:

"With the jawbone of a donkey, I’ve piled them up, mass on mass; A thousand warriors have I slain with the jawbone of a donkey."

title: A Strong Man Who Lost His Strength
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Later, Samson fell in love with a woman named Delilah who lived in the Valley of Sorek. The leaders of the Philistines approached her and said, "Find out how he has such incredible strength, and how we can overpower and tie him up. If you do this, we’ll each give you eleven hundred pieces of silver." Delilah then asked Samson, "Can you tell me how you’re so strong and how someone could bind you?" Samson replied, "If they tie me up with seven fresh bowstrings that haven't been dried, I would be as weak as any other person."

The Philistine leaders brought Delilah seven fresh bowstrings, and she tied him up with them. She had men hiding in the other room, and when she shouted, "Samson! The Philistines are here!" he broke the bowstrings as if they were nothing more than a thread near a flame. They still didn't uncover the secret of his strength.

Delilah confronted Samson, saying, "You’ve tricked me and lied to me! Now please tell me how you can be securely bound." He answered, "If they tie me up with new ropes that haven't been used for any work, I’ll be as weak as any other person." Delilah took new ropes and tied him, then called out, "Samson! The Philistines are upon you!" Men were waiting in the other room, but he tore the ropes off his arms like thread.

Again, Delilah asked, "So far you’ve deceived me. How can you be securely tied?" He said, "If you weave the seven braids of my hair into a loom and tighten it with a pin, I would become weak like any other man." While he was asleep, she braided his hair into the loom and pinned it down. Then she exclaimed, "Samson! The Philistines are here!" He woke up and pulled the loom and the pinned hair right up.

Delilah said to him, "How can you say you love me when you don’t trust me? You’ve tricked me three times and still haven’t revealed the secret of your strength!" After a while, she kept pushing him so hard that he became sick of it and finally told her everything. "No razor has ever touched my head; I’ve been dedicated to God from birth. If my hair is cut, I'll lose my strength and become weak like any other man."

title: A Strong Man Who Lost His Strength
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
When Delilah realized he had told her the entire truth, she called the Philistine leaders and said, "Come quickly! He has revealed everything to me!" The leaders came with the money in hand. After making Samson sleep on her lap, she had someone shave off the seven braids of his hair. She started mocking him, and at that moment, his strength left him. She shouted, "Samson! The Philistines are coming!" He woke up thinking, "I’ll break free as usual," but didn’t realize the Lord had abandoned him. The Philistines grabbed him and gouged out his eyes. They took him to Gaza, chained him with brass, and forced him to grind in prison. However, his hair started to grow back once it was shaved.

Then the Philistine leaders gathered to offer a big sacrifice to their god, Dagon, celebrating because they believed, "Our god has delivered Samson, our enemy, into our hands." Seeing him, the crowd praised their god, saying:

"Our god has brought down our foe,
He who caused our country woe,
He who defeated us with countless blows."

In their excitement, they said, "Bring out Samson so he can entertain us!" They called for him, and when he was brought from prison, he amused them, standing between two pillars.

Samson then asked the boy guiding him, "Let me lean against the pillars that support the building." The building was packed with men and women, including all the leaders of the Philistines, and about three thousand people were watching from the roof as he entertained them. Samson cried out to the Lord, "O Lord, remember me and please give me strength just this once, so I can take revenge on the Philistines for the loss of my eyes."

Samson grabbed the two middle pillars that held up the building, one in his right hand and the other in his left, and pushed against them. He declared, "Let me die with the Philistines!" He exerted all his strength, and the entire house collapsed on the rulers and everyone inside. The number of people he killed when he died was greater than all those he had defeated during his life.

Later, his brothers and all his family came to retrieve his body and buried him between Zorah and Eshtaol, in the grave of Manoah, his father.

title: Samuel The Boy Prophet
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Elkanah, a man from Zuph in the hilly region of Ephraim, lived in Ramah with his two wives, Hannah and Peninnah. Peninnah had children, but Hannah was unable to have any.

Every year, Elkanah would travel from his village to worship and offer sacrifices to the Lord at Shiloh. On those sacrifice days, he would give portions of the meal to his wife Peninnah and their children. But because he loved Hannah, he only gave her one portion, since the Lord had not blessed her with children. Peninnah often taunted Hannah, which only fueled her sorrow. Year after year, whenever Hannah went up to the Lord's temple, Peninnah's mockery made her so upset that she cried and wouldn’t eat. Elkanah, her husband, would ask her, "Hannah, why are you crying? Why won't you eat? Why are you so downcast? Don’t I mean more to you than ten sons?"

After they had eaten in Shiloh, Hannah got up and stood in front of the Lord’s temple, while Eli the priest was sitting near the entrance. With tears streaming down her face, she prayed to the Lord earnestly.

"O Lord Almighty! If you will look upon my misery and remember me, and not forget your servant, but give me a son, then I will give him to you all the days of his life, and no razor will ever touch his head."

As Hannah continued to pray before the Lord, Eli observed her mouth. She was moving her lips, but her voice wasn't audible, so Eli thought she was drunk. He said, "How long will you act like a drunken woman? Put away your wine from you!" Hannah responded, "No, my lord, I am a woman troubled in spirit. I haven't drunk wine or strong drink; I have been pouring out my soul before the Lord. Don’t take your servant for a wicked woman; I have been speaking out of my great anxiety and grief." Eli replied, "Go in peace, and may the God of Israel grant what you have asked of him." She said, "May your servant find favor in your eyes!" Then Hannah went away and ate, and her sadness was gone.

title: Samuel The Boy Prophet
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Early the next morning, she and her husband got up and worshiped the Lord before returning home to Ramah. In time, Hannah conceived and bore a son, naming him Samuel, saying, "I asked the Lord for him."

When it was time for Elkanah and his family to offer their yearly sacrifice, Hannah didn’t go with them. She told her husband, "Once the child is weaned, I will take him to the Lord's temple, and he will serve there his whole life." Elkanah responded, "Do what you think is best; stay here until you’ve weaned him. May the Lord help you keep your promise."

So Hannah nursed her son until he was weaned, and then she took him, along with a three-year-old bull, a bushel of flour, and a skin of wine, to the Lord's temple at Shiloh. They sacrificed the bull, and Hannah brought the boy to Eli, saying, "Oh, my lord, as surely as you live, I am the woman who stood here praying to the Lord. This is the boy I prayed for, and the Lord has granted my request. I dedicate him to the Lord, and he will belong to the Lord for his entire life."

Elkanah returned home, but the boy stayed to serve the Lord under Eli’s care. Samuel, a young boy dressed in a linen robe, carried out his duties as a servant in the temple. His mother made him a special robe and brought it to him each year when she came with Elkanah to make their annual sacrifice.

Eli blessed Elkanah, saying, "May the Lord give you more children through this woman for the gift she has given to the Lord." They returned home, and Hannah had three more sons and two daughters. Meanwhile, the boy Samuel grew up serving the Lord.

Eli's sons were corrupt; they had no respect for the Lord and treated the sacrifices with contempt. Eli was old, and when he heard about his sons’ wrongdoings, he told them, "Why do you do such things? I hear such terrible things about you from all the people. No, my sons, it’s not good what I’m hearing about you!" But they ignored their father's warnings.

title: Samuel The Boy Prophet
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
As Samuel grew, he found favor with both the Lord and the people. He continued to serve under Eli's guidance, but during that time, not many messages came from the Lord.

One night, Eli was lying down in his room. His eyesight had dimmed, and he could hardly see. Meanwhile, the lamp of God was still burning, and Samuel was lying in the temple near the ark of God when the Lord called, "Samuel! Samuel!" Samuel responded, "Here I am!" He ran to Eli and said, "Here I am; you called me." Eli replied, "I didn’t call you. Go back and lie down." So Samuel went back and lay down. Again, the Lord called, "Samuel! Samuel!" Samuel got up and went to Eli, saying, "Here I am; you called me." Eli answered, "My son, I didn’t call you. Go lie down again."

Samuel didn’t yet know the Lord, and the Lord hadn't spoken to him before. So when the Lord called Samuel for the third time, he got up and went to Eli again, saying, "Here I am; you called me!" Then Eli realized that the Lord was calling the boy. He told Samuel, "Go lie down, and if He calls you, say: 'Speak, Lord, for your servant is listening.'" So Samuel went back to his place.

Once more, the Lord came and called, "Samuel! Samuel!" Samuel replied, "Speak, for your servant is listening." The Lord said to Samuel, "I am about to do something in Israel that will make everyone’s ears tingle. On that day, I will fulfill everything I promised Eli’s family, from the beginning to the end. I have warned him that I will punish his family forever for the sin his sons committed, and he didn’t stop them."

Samuel lay there until morning, then opened the doors of the Lord’s temple, afraid to share the vision with Eli. But when Eli called out, "Samuel, my son," he replied, "Here I am." Eli asked, "What did the Lord say to you? Do not hold anything back; may God deal with you if you hide from me what He said." So Samuel told Eli everything, withholding nothing. Eli said, "It is the Lord; let Him do what He thinks is best."

title: The Ark Among The Philistines
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Back in those days, the Israelites faced off against the Philistines, and after a tough battle, the Israelites were defeated.

When the people returned to their camp, the leaders of Israel asked, "Why has God allowed us to be defeated by the Philistines today? Let's bring the ark of our God from Shiloh. If we do, He might go with us and rescue us from our enemies."

So, the people sent for the ark of the Lord of hosts from Shiloh. When it arrived at the camp, the Israelites erupted in such loud cheers that the ground shook.

The Philistines heard the uproar and wondered, "What does all this shouting in the Hebrew camp mean?" When they learned that the ark of the Lord had arrived, fear gripped them. They said, "Their god has come into their camp! This is bad news for us; we've never faced anything like this before. Be brave and fight like men!" So the Philistines fought bravely, but the Israelites were defeated again and fled to their tents.

On that same day, a Benjamite warrior rushed to Shiloh, ripping his clothes and covering his head in dust. Eli, who was old and anxious about the ark of God, was sitting by the gate and watching the road.

When the man arrived and told the news to the townspeople, everyone broke into loud cries. Eli heard the commotion and asked, "What’s happening?" The messenger hurried to Eli and said, "I'm the one who fled from the battle." Eli asked, "What happened, my son?" The messenger replied, "Israel ran away from the Philistines, many were killed, your two sons are dead, and the ark of God has been captured!" Hearing about the ark, Eli fell backward off his seat by the gate, broke his neck, and died, because he was old and heavy.

The Philistines took the ark of God to the temple of Dagon in Ashdod, placing it right next to Dagon. The next morning, the people of Ashdod went to the temple and found Dagon had toppled over, face down before the ark of the Lord. They picked Dagon up and set him back in place. But when they checked again the next morning, Dagon was flat on the ground again, this time with his head and hands broken off at the threshold, leaving only his body.

title: The Ark Among The Philistines
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
God struck the people of Ashdod with painful boils. When the men of Ashdod realized what was happening, they declared, "The ark of the God of Israel can’t stay with us because He’s punishing us and our god, Dagon." They called together all the rulers of the Philistines and asked, "What should we do with the ark of the God of Israel?" They decided, "Let’s send it to Gath."

So they moved the ark of the God of Israel to Gath. But God punished the people of that city, young and old alike, with painful boils as well. Therefore, they sent the ark to Ekron, but when it arrived, the Ekronites panicked and cried out, "They’ve brought the ark of the God of Israel here to kill us and our people!" They gathered all the rulers of the Philistines and pleaded, "Send the ark of the God of Israel back to its own place so it won't bring death to us and our people!"

The Philistines summoned the priests and diviners and asked, "What should we do with the ark of the Lord? How can we send it back home?" They replied, "If you send the ark of the God of Israel back, don’t send it back empty. Make sure to include a guilt offering to compensate for what you have done to it. Then you’ll be healed and understand why He has continued to punish you." They asked, "What kind of offering should we send back?"

They agreed, "Let’s send five golden boils and five golden mice, the same number as the Philistine rulers, since the plague struck both you and your leaders. Prepare a new cart and take two cows that have never been yoked. Attach the cows to the cart, but leave their calves at home. Place the ark of the Lord on the cart along with a box containing the golden objects you're returning as an offering. Then send it off. If it goes straight to its own territory, to Bethshemesh, we’ll know it’s the Lord who has done this great harm to us. But if it doesn’t, we’ll know it was just a coincidence."

They did exactly as instructed. The cows took a straight route along the Bethshemesh road, lowing as they went, never veering to the right or left. The rulers of the Philistines followed them as far as Bethshemesh.

The people of Bethshemesh were in the fields, harvesting wheat. When they looked up and saw the ark, they celebrated with joy. The ark came to rest in the field of Joshua, a Bethshemeshite. There was a large stone there, so they split up the wood of the cart and used the cows as a burnt offering to the Lord. When the five Philistine rulers saw this, they returned to Ekron that same day.

title: How Samuel Found A Leader
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
In a prosperous town called Gibeah, there lived a wealthy man from the tribe of Benjamin named Kish. He had a son named Saul, a tall and strikingly handsome young man—no one in all of Israel was more attractive than him. Saul stood taller than anyone else in the crowd.

One day, Kish realized that his donkeys were missing. He called Saul and said, "Take one of the servants with you and go look for the donkeys." They traveled through the hills of Ephraim and the land of Shalishah, but they didn’t find them. After that, they ventured into the land of Shaalim, but still no luck. Finally, they searched through Benjamin, but the donkeys were nowhere to be found.

As they reached the land of Zuph, Saul said to his servant, "Let’s head back now, or my dad will start worrying more about us than the donkeys." The servant replied, "There’s a man of God in this town who’s highly respected; everything he says comes true. Let’s go see him, maybe he can help us figure out where to look next."
