Saul responded, "But what can we give the man? Our provisions are gone, and we have nothing to offer the man of God." The servant replied, "I have a quarter of a silver shekel. We can give that to him so he can guide us." Saul thought it over and said, "That’s a good idea; let’s go." So they set off to the town where the man of God was.

As they approached the town, they met some young women coming out to fetch water and asked them, "Is the seer here?" They replied, "Yes, he’s just ahead of you. Hurry, he just entered the town, and today the people are having a sacrificial feast on the hilltop. Once you enter the town, you’ll find him before he goes up to the high place to eat, because the people won’t eat until he arrives and blesses the sacrifice. So hurry up, you’ll catch him!"

title: How Samuel Found A Leader
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
They followed the women into the town, and just as they entered the gate, Samuel was about to head up to the high place. The night before, God had told Samuel, "Tomorrow about this time, I will send you a man from the land of Benjamin. You’ll anoint him as a leader over my people Israel. He will rescue them from the Philistines, for I’ve seen their suffering and heard their cries."

When Samuel saw Saul, God said to him, "Here’s the man I told you about! He will be the ruler over my people." As Saul approached Samuel at the gate and asked, "Can you please tell me where the seer’s house is?" Samuel replied, "I am the seer. Come up with me to the high place; you’ll eat with me today, and I’ll let you go in the morning, telling you everything on your mind. Don’t worry about the donkeys you lost three days ago; they’ve already been found. And why shouldn’t you care about what’s best in Israel? It’s meant for you and your family." Saul said, "But am I not from Benjamin, the smallest tribe in Israel? And is my family not the least of all the families in Benjamin? Why are you speaking to me this way?"

Samuel took Saul and his servant and brought them into the hall, seating them at the head of the guests, who numbered about thirty. Samuel then instructed the cook, "Bring the portion I told you to set aside." The cook brought out the leg and its accompanying dish and placed it in front of Saul. "See what has been saved for you! Eat it, as it was reserved for this occasion," Samuel said. So that day, Saul ate with Samuel.

After descending from the high place back to the town, they prepared a bed for Saul on the roof, and he lay down. At dawn, Samuel called out to Saul on the roof, saying, "Get up; I have something to send you off with." Saul got up, and he and Samuel headed out into the street. As they were leaving town, Samuel instructed Saul, "Tell the servant to go on ahead, but you stay back for a moment so I can share God’s message with you."

title: How Samuel Found A Leader
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Samuel took a flask of oil, poured it on Saul’s head, kissed him, and said, "Isn’t it the Lord who has anointed you as a leader over His people Israel? You’re going to govern the people and save them from their enemies all around. This will be the sign that God has chosen you: when you leave me today, you will meet two men at Rachel’s tomb. They will say, 'The donkeys you were searching for have been found, and now your dad is worried about you, not the donkeys.' Then head on from there to the oak of Tabor, where you will meet three men on their way to God at Bethel. One will have three young goats, another will have three loaves of bread, and the last will carry a skin of wine. They’ll greet you and give you two loaves of bread, which you should accept. After that, proceed to Gibeah. As you enter the city, you’ll run into a group of prophets coming down from the high place, playing instruments and prophesying. The Spirit of God will rush upon you, and you’ll prophesy with them, becoming a new person. When these signs happen, do whatever you can, for God is with you." When Saul turned to leave Samuel, God gave him a new heart, and all those signs came true that day.

When Saul returned home, his uncle asked him and the servant, "Where did you go?" Saul replied, "We went to look for the donkeys, and when we couldn’t find them, we went to see Samuel." His uncle urged, "What did Samuel say to you?" Saul responded, "He assured us that the donkeys were found," but he didn’t mention that Samuel had declared he was to be the new leader.

About a month later, Nahash the Ammonite attacked Jabesh in Gilead. The men of Jabesh pleaded with Nahash, saying, "Make a treaty with us, and we will serve you." But Nahash replied, "I will make a deal with you, but only if you let me gouge out the right eye of each of you to bring disgrace on all Israel." The elders of Jabesh said, "Give us seven days to send messengers throughout Israel. If no one comes to rescue us, we will surrender to you."

The messengers arrived in Gibeah, where Saul lived, and shared the news with the people, causing them to weep loudly. Just then, Saul was returning from the field behind his oxen and asked, "What’s going on with the people? Why are they crying?" They explained what the men of Jabesh had said. As Saul listened, the Spirit of God came upon him, and he became very angry. He took a pair of oxen, cut them into pieces, and sent the pieces throughout Israel by messengers, saying, "Whoever doesn’t come out to follow Saul and Samuel, this will be done to their oxen!"

A great fear of the Lord fell upon the people, and they all gathered together. Saul told the messengers, "Tell the men of Jabesh in Gilead that by the time the sun gets hot tomorrow, help will come for you."

The messengers went and relayed Saul’s message to the men of Jabesh, who were filled with joy. The men of Jabesh then told the Ammonites, "Tomorrow we will come out to you, and you can do whatever you want with us." The next day, Saul divided the people into three companies; they attacked the Ammonite camp early in the morning and fought until noon. The remaining Ammonites were so scattered that not two of them remained together.

Afterward, everyone went to Gilgal, where they made Saul their ruler before the Lord and offered sacrifices there. Saul and all the men of Israel celebrated happily.

title: Jonathan'S Brave Deed
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Saul gathered three thousand men from the Israelites. Two thousand stayed with him in Michmash and on the Bethel highlands, while one thousand went with his son Jonathan in Gibeah. The rest of the people were sent home.

Jonathan then launched an attack against the Philistine outpost at Gibeah, and the Philistines caught wind of it. Saul rallied everyone in the land by declaring, "Let the Hebrews know!" As a result, all of Israel heard that Saul had struck the Philistines and that Israel had become their target.

The Philistines banded together to fight Israel, arriving with three thousand chariots, six thousand horsemen, and foot soldiers that numbered like the grains of sand on the shore. They set up camp in Michmash. When the Israelites realized they were in a tough spot (the pressure was mounting), they hid in caves, holes, rocks, tombs, and pits. Many even crossed the Jordan River to escape to Gad and Gilead.

Saul took a roll call and found only about six hundred men were with him. He and Jonathan, along with those with them, stayed in Gibeah while the Philistines camped in Michmash. The Philistines split into three groups and moved out from their camp to plunder: one team went to Ophrah in the Shual region, another towards Bethhoron, and the last toward the hill overlooking Zeboim Valley. Meanwhile, the Philistine garrison moved to the Michmash pass.

That day, Jonathan, the son of Saul, said to his armor-bearer, "Let's cross over to the Philistine garrison on the other side." But he kept this plan from his father.

Meanwhile, Saul was sitting just outside Gibeah under a pomegranate tree close to the threshing floor, with about six hundred men around him. No one knew that Jonathan had left.

On the path Jonathan chose to approach the Philistines, there were steep cliffs on either side; one was called The Shining and the other The Thorny. One cliff stood to the north of Michmash, and the other to the south of Geba.

title: Jonathan'S Brave Deed
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Jonathan told his armor-bearer, "Let’s go over to the camp of these godless Philistines. Maybe the Lord will help us; nothing can stop the Lord from saving us, whether we are few or many." His armor-bearer replied, "Do whatever you want; I’m with you all the way." Jonathan continued, "We'll reveal ourselves to them. If they tell us to wait, we’ll stay put. But if they say to come up, that will be the sign that the Lord has given them into our hands."

When they showed themselves to the Philistines, the Philistines shouted, "Look! Here come the Hebrews crawling out of their hiding spots!" They called to Jonathan and his armor-bearer, "Come up here, and we’ll show you something!" Jonathan replied to his armor-bearer, "Let’s climb up, for the Lord has given them into the hands of Israel."

Jonathan climbed up on his hands and feet, with his armor-bearer right behind him. As they charged in, the Philistines fell before Jonathan, and his armor-bearer followed, finishing them off. In their first strike, Jonathan and his armor-bearer took down about twenty men with their spears and stones. This stirred panic among the Philistines, spreading confusion in their camp and out in the field; even those who were robbing became frightened, and the earth shook with a panic sent by God.

The watchmen on the lookout for Saul in Gibeah noticed the large Philistine army falling apart and scattering in all directions. Saul then asked the people with him, "Count and see who’s missing!" Upon checking, they discovered that Jonathan and his armor-bearer were gone. Saul ordered Ahijah, "Bring the ark of God here," since it was with the Israelites at that time. While Saul was still talking to the priest, the chaos among the Philistines intensified, prompting him to say, "Forget consulting God!"

title: Jonathan'S Brave Deed
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Then Saul and his men joined the battle. Each Philistine’s sword turned against his fellow, creating great turmoil among their ranks. The Hebrews, who had previously sided with the Philistines, switched allegiances back to Saul and Jonathan. Additionally, all the Israelites who had been hiding in the hills of Ephraim rushed into the fight when they heard the Philistines were retreating. That day, the Lord delivered Israel, and the battle carried on beyond Bethhoron. However, Saul made a serious mistake by commanding the people, "Anyone who eats until evening and my revenge on my enemies is complete will face punishment." As a result, no one ate anything.

There was honey on the forest floor, and when the people reached the woods, they spotted a stream of honey but dared not eat any, fearing the punishment. Jonathan hadn’t heard his father’s order, so he took the end of his staff, dipped it in the honeycomb, and tasted it, instantly feeling invigorated. Then one of the soldiers pointed out, "Your father commanded everyone not to eat today, or they would be punished." Jonathan responded, "My father has brought trouble upon the land. Look how revitalized I am after tasting just a bit of this honey! If only everyone had eaten from the spoils today, even more Philistines would have fallen!"

Saul suggested, "Let’s pursue the Philistines at night and gather their spoils until dawn, leaving none behind." The men agreed, "Do as you see fit." However, the priest said, "Let’s ask God first." Saul prayed, "Should I chase the Philistines? Will you deliver them into Israel’s hands?" God didn’t answer him that day. So Saul called for a gathering of the leaders and declared, "By God, the deliverer of Israel lives! If today’s sin is mine or Jonathan’s, he must die." No one spoke up.

Saul told all of Israel, "You stay on one side, and I and Jonathan will be on the other." The people insisted, "Do what you think is right." Then Saul prayed, "Lord, God of Israel, why haven’t you answered me today? If the sin is mine or Jonathan’s, show it with the Urim lot; if the sin lies with the people, show it with the Thummim lot." The lot fell on Jonathan and Saul, not on the people. So Saul ordered, "Cast lots between me and Jonathan. Whoever God selects must die." The people protested, "No way!" But Saul forced them to comply, and the lot fell on Jonathan.

Saul then asked Jonathan, "What did you do?" Jonathan answered, "I only tasted a little honey with the end of my staff. Here I am! I'm prepared to die." Saul replied, "May God do this to me and more; Jonathan, you must die!" The people defended Jonathan, saying, "Should Jonathan be punished for bringing such great victory to Israel? Not a chance! As surely as the Lord lives, not a hair on his head will be harmed—he did what God had planned today." The people saved Jonathan from execution.

title: David'S Victory Over The Giant
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
As long as Saul was alive, there was a fierce conflict with the Philistines. Whenever Saul spotted a strong or capable person, he would bring them into his service.

Now, the spirit of God had left Saul, and instead, an unsettling spirit troubled him. His servants said, "Look, an evil spirit is bothering you. Let us find a skilled musician who can play the lyre. Whenever the spirit attacks you, he can play, and you will feel better." Saul replied, "Find me someone who plays well and bring him to me."

One of the young men said, "I know a son of Jesse from Bethlehem. He’s a talented musician, a strong and brave man, a warrior, careful in his words, and he’s good-looking. God is with him too." So Saul sent messengers to Jesse with a request: "Send me your son David, who is tending the sheep."

Jesse packed ten loaves of bread, a skin of wine, and a young goat, sending them with David to Saul. David arrived at Saul's court and began serving him. Saul grew fond of David, and he became his armor-bearer. Saul then sent a message to Jesse, saying, "Let David stay with me; I’m really pleased with him." Whenever the unsettling spirit would come upon Saul, David would play the lyre and Saul would feel relief and calm, and the spirit would depart.

Meanwhile, the Philistines gathered their forces for battle and set up camp between Socoh and Ezekah in Ephesdammim. Saul and the Israelite soldiers gathered and camped in the valley of Elah, ready to face the Philistines.

The Philistines positioned themselves on one hill, while the Israelites stood on the opposite hill, with a valley in between. From the Philistine ranks, a giant named Goliath emerged, towering about ten feet tall. He wore a bronze helmet and a heavy bronze breastplate that weighed around one hundred and fifty pounds. He also sported bronze leg armor and a bronze back-plate. His spear's shaft was as thick as a weaver's beam, and the iron spearhead alone weighed about twenty pounds. A shield-bearer marched ahead of him.

title: David'S Victory Over The Giant
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Goliath shouted to the Israelite troops: "Why have you come out to fight? I’m a Philistine, and you’re just servants of Saul. Pick a man to face me in combat. If he defeats me, we’ll become your servants; but if I win, you’ll serve us! Today, I defy the armies of Israel. Send me a challenger!"

When Saul and the Israelites heard Goliath's challenge, they were filled with fear. But David boldly said to Saul, "Don’t let your courage fade; I’ll fight this Philistine!" Saul replied, "You can’t go against this Philistine; you’re just a boy, and he’s been a warrior since he was young." David answered, "I’ve been taking care of my father’s sheep. If a lion or bear came and took a lamb, I’d chase it down, fight it, and save the lamb. If it attacked me, I’d grab it by the fur and kill it. I’ve killed both lions and bears, and this uncircumcised Philistine will be the same, for he’s mocked the armies of the living God. The Lord who rescued me from the lion and bear will also save me from this Philistine." Saul replied, "Go, and may the Lord be with you."

Saul dressed David in his own armor, putting a bronze helmet on his head and a coat of mail on him. But when David tried to walk, he felt weighed down and clumsy—he wasn’t used to them. He said to Saul, "I can't go out like this; I’m not familiar with these." So David took the armor off.

Instead, he picked up his staff, chose five smooth stones from the stream, packed them in his bag, grabbed his sling, and approached the Philistine. When Goliath saw David, he sneered because David was just a young, red-cheeked boy. Goliath shouted to David, "Am I a dog that you come at me with a stick?" Then he cursed David by his gods, saying, "Come here, and I'll feed your flesh to the birds and wild animals!"

David replied to Goliath, "You come to me with a sword, spear, and javelin, but I come at you in the name of the Lord of hosts and the God of Israel, whom you’ve insulted. Today, the Lord will hand you over to me, and I’ll take you down and cut off your head!"

As Goliath moved to attack him, David reached into his bag, took out a stone, and flung it from his sling. The stone struck Goliath in the forehead, and he fell face-first to the ground. David ran over, drew Goliath's sword from its sheath, and killed him by cutting off his head.

When the Philistines saw that their champion was dead, they ran in fear. The men of Israel and Judah rallied, shouting with encouragement, and chased the Philistines all the way to Gath and the gates of Ekron, so that the wounded Philistines fell across the battlefield up to Gath and Ekron.

title: Saul'S Mean Jealousy
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
When the Israelites and David returned from defeating the Philistines, women from all the cities of Israel came out singing and dancing to welcome Saul. They carried tambourines and celebrated with joyful cries and cymbals. The women joyfully sang to each other,

"Saul has slain his thousands, and David his tens of thousands."

Saul became very angry because he didn't like what they were saying. He muttered, "They credit David with ten thousands, but only give me thousands; what’s to stop him from getting the throne?" From that day on, Saul kept a close watch on David. He started to fear David and kept him at a distance. Saul appointed David as a commander over a thousand men, and David led his soldiers in and out. Whatever David did, he did wisely and experienced success because God was with him. The more success David had, the more afraid Saul became. Yet all of Israel and Judah loved David because he led them so well.

Michal, Saul's daughter, also loved David. When Saul heard this, he was pleased. He thought, "I'll give her to him; maybe she’ll lead him to his downfall and the Philistines will capture him." So, Saul commanded his servants, "Tell David secretly: 'The king likes you, and all his servants love you; so here’s your chance to become his son-in-law.'" When Saul's servants conveyed this message to David, he replied, "Do you think it’s easy for me to become the son-in-law of a king? I’m poor and have no reputation!" When Saul’s servants reported his response, Saul instructed them to say to David, "The king requires no dowry for his daughter, just the evidence that you’ve killed a hundred Philistines;" Saul thought that the Philistines would end up killing David.

David went out with his men and killed a hundred Philistines. So, Saul gave Michal to David as his wife. At that point, Saul realized that God was with David and that the people truly loved him, which made Saul even more fearful.

title: Saul'S Mean Jealousy
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Next, Saul told his son Jonathan and all his servants to kill David. But Jonathan cared deeply for David. He defended David to his father, saying, "Please don’t betray your servant David; he hasn’t done anything wrong to you, and he has always acted honorably. He risked his life to defeat the Philistine, which saved all Israel. You saw that and were happy. So, why would you want to kill an innocent person?"

Saul listened to Jonathan and made a solemn promise: "As surely as God lives, David won’t be killed."

Jonathan called David and informed him of everything that had happened. Then he took David back to see Saul, and David was in Saul's presence again, just like before.

But then war broke out again, and David went out to battle the Philistines. He defeated so many of them that they ran away. Meanwhile, an evil spirit from God came over Saul while he was sitting in his house with a spear in his hand, and David was playing music. Saul tried to pin David to the wall with the spear, but David dodged out of the way, causing Saul’s spear to hit the wall instead. David escaped.

That night, Saul sent messengers to David’s house to watch him, planning to kill him in the morning. However, Michal, David’s wife, warned him, "If you don’t escape tonight, you’ll be killed tomorrow." So, she let David down through the window, and he fled away. After that, Michal took the household idol, placed it in the bed, put a pillow of goat's hair under its head, and covered it with a blanket. When Saul sent messengers to capture David, she told them, "He’s sick."

Saul then sent the messengers back with the command, "Bring him here on the bed, so I can kill him." When the messengers arrived, they found the household idol in bed with the goat's hair pillow. Saul asked Michal, "Why did you deceive me and let my enemy escape?" Michal replied, "He told me, 'Let me go; why would I kill you?"

title: Jonathan'S Love For David
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
David hurried to find Jonathan and asked, "What have I done? What’s my guilt, and what sin have I committed in your father’s eyes that he’s trying to kill me?" Jonathan replied, "No way! You’re not going to die. You know that my father always informs me about everything, big or small. He wouldn’t keep something like this from me!" David insisted, "Your father knows how much you care for me. He’s probably saying to himself, 'I can’t let Jonathan know, or he’ll be upset.' But I swear, as surely as the Lord lives and as you live, I’m just a step away from death."

Jonathan then asked, "What do you want me to do for you?" David said, "Tomorrow is the New Moon festival, and I should be at the table with Saul, but I need to hide out in the field until evening. If your father notices I’m missing, tell him, 'David asked for my permission to go to Bethlehem, his hometown, for the yearly sacrifice with his family.' If he responds positively, then it’s all good for me. But if he gets angry, you’ll know he means to harm me. Please be kind to me, as you made a solemn promise before the Lord. But if I’m guilty, go ahead and kill me yourself. Why should you bring me to your father?" Jonathan firmly replied, "That will never happen! If I find out that my father intends to hurt you, I’ll let you know."

title: Jonathan'S Love For David
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
David asked Jonathan, "Who will let me know if your father reacts harshly?" Jonathan responded, "May the Lord, the God of Israel, be my witness. By tomorrow around this time, I’ll figure out how my father feels. If he’s in a good mood towards you, I’ll send you a message. But if he means to harm you, may God deal with Jonathan severely if I don’t inform you and help you escape in peace. May the Lord be with you, just as He has been with my father. And if I’m still alive, please keep being kind to my family. If, when the Lord wipes out all David’s enemies, my family is destroyed, may the Lord hold David’s enemies accountable for it." Jonathan renewed his promise to David, loving him as much as he loved his own life.

Jonathan then said, "Tomorrow is the New Moon festival, and you’ll definitely be missed because your seat will be empty. On the third day, when you’re really missed, go to the spot where you hid when my father tried to attack you, and sit by the pile of stones. I’ll shoot three arrows as if aiming for a target. Then I’ll send the boy, saying, 'Go, find the arrows.' If I call out to the boy, 'The arrows are this way; pick them up!'- then come, because everything is fine with you. But if I call, 'The arrows are beyond you,' then you must go, for the Lord is sending you away. And may the Lord witness the promises we’ve made to each other forever."

David hid in the field, and when the New Moon festival began, Saul sat down to eat at the table. He took his usual seat by the wall, with Jonathan across from him and Abner sitting beside Saul, but David’s seat was empty. Saul didn’t say anything that day, thinking, "Maybe it’s just an oversight."

title: Jonathan'S Love For David
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
But the next day, when David’s place was still empty, Saul asked Jonathan, "Why hasn’t the son of Jesse come to the table, either yesterday or today?" Jonathan said, "David asked for my permission to go to Bethlehem. He said, 'Let me go because my family is having a sacrifice in the town, and my brother told me to be there. If it’s okay with you, I’ll go see my family.' That’s why he hasn’t joined us at the table."

Saul’s anger flared at Jonathan, and he exclaimed, "You son of that rebellious servant! Don’t you realize you’re bringing shame on yourself and your mother by befriending the son of Jesse? As long as he’s alive, neither you nor your rule will be safe. Go and get him for me; he’s destined to die!"

Jonathan responded to his father, saying, "Why should he be put to death? What has he done?" In a fit of rage, Saul threw his spear at Jonathan to strike him. It became clear to Jonathan that his father was determined to kill David. Furious, Jonathan got up from the table and didn’t eat on the second day of the month, feeling hurt by his father’s insult towards David.

The next morning, Jonathan headed out to the field to the designated meeting spot with David, bringing a young boy along. He told the boy, "Run and find the arrows I shoot." As the boy ran, Jonathan shot an arrow beyond him. When the boy reached where the arrow lay, Jonathan called out, "Isn’t the arrow beyond you? Hurry! Don’t stop!" The boy collected the arrows and brought them back to Jonathan, unaware of the message being conveyed. Only Jonathan and David understood the signal.

title: The Cost Of A Lie
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
David arrived in Nob, where he found Ahimelech the priest, who approached him with concern. "Why are you here all alone?" he asked. David replied, "Saul sent me on a mission and instructed me, 'Don’t let anyone know about the mission I'm sending you on.' I’ve also told my companions to meet me at a specific location. So, if you have five loaves of bread or anything else, please give it to me." The priest said, "I don’t have regular bread, only the holy bread available." He handed David the holy bread, since it was the only bread there, meant to be replaced with fresh loaves.

That same day, Doeg, the Edomite, the chief of Saul's herders, was present. David asked Ahimelech, "Do you have a spear or sword here? I left in such a hurry that I didn’t grab any weapons." The priest replied, "I only have the sword of Goliath the Philistine, the one you defeated in the valley of Elah. It’s wrapped in a cloth. If you want it, take it; it’s the only one I have." David said, "There's nothing like it. Give it to me!"

David then left and escaped to the stronghold at Adullam. When his brothers and the rest of his family heard about it, they came down to him. People in distress, those in debt, and others who were unhappy started to gather around him, and he became their leader. Soon, about four hundred men were with him.

title: The Cost Of A Lie
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Meanwhile, Saul learned that David and his men had been located. He was sitting under a tamarisk tree at the high place in Gibeah, holding his spear, surrounded by his servants. Saul addressed his attendants, saying, "Listen up, Benjamites! Will the son of Jesse give you fields and vineyards? Will he make you commanders of thousands and hundreds? Isn’t it true that you’ve all conspired against me, and none of you have told me that my son has made a pact with Jesse’s son? It’s like none of you pity me or tell me my servant David has become my enemy!" Just then, Doeg the Edomite, standing near Saul’s servants, spoke up, "I saw the son of Jesse at Nob, talking to Ahimelech, the son of Ahitub. The priest asked God for him and gave him food and the sword of Goliath the Philistine."

Saul then summoned Ahimelech the priest and all the priests from Nob, and they came to him. Saul said, "Listen, son of Ahitub!" Ahimelech replied, "Here I am, my lord!" Saul accused him, "Is it true that you and David plotted against me? You gave him bread and a sword and sought guidance for him, so he could rebel against me?" Ahimelech answered, "Who among your servants is as trustworthy as David, your son-in-law, the commander of your guard, honored in your household? I’ve been inquiring about him for a long time. I assure you, I mean no disloyalty! I had no idea about any of this!" Saul's response was harsh: "Ahimelech, you will surely die, you and your whole family!"

Saul then turned to his guards and commanded, "Kill the priests of Jehovah, because they helped David, and yet didn’t inform me, even though they knew he was fleeing!" But Saul’s servants refused to harm the priests of Jehovah. Then Saul ordered Doeg, "You kill the priests." So Doeg, the Edomite, killed them, taking the lives of eighty-five men who wore the priestly robes that day.

One of Ahimelech’s sons, Abiathar, managed to escape and ran to David. When he told David that Saul had killed the priests of Jehovah, David replied, "I knew something bad would happen because Doeg the Edomite was there. I feel responsible for the deaths of your family. Stay with me, and don’t be afraid, for anyone who wants to take your life will have to face me first, since you’re under my protection now."

title: A Soldier Who Spared His Enemy
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
When David heard that the Philistines were attacking Keilah and stealing from the threshing floors, he asked God, "Should I go and fight these Philistines?" God replied, "Yes, go and attack the Philistines to save Keilah." However, David's men expressed their fear, saying, "We're already scared here in Judah; how much more terrified will we be if we go to Keilah against the Philistines?" After asking God again, He told David, "Go to Keilah, for I will hand the Philistines over to you." So David and his men went to Keilah, fought the Philistines, drove away their livestock, and defeated many of them. In this way, David saved the people of Keilah.

When Abiathar, the son of Ahimelech, fled to David in Keilah, he brought with him the priestly robe used for consulting God. When Saul heard that David was in Keilah, he declared, "God has delivered him into my hands, for by hiding in a town with gates and bars, he has trapped himself."

Saul called all his men to arms to march against Keilah and surround David and his men. But when David found out that Saul was planning something evil against him, he said to Abiathar the priest, "Bring me the priestly robe.” Then David prayed, "O God of Israel, your servant has heard that Saul is coming to Keilah to destroy the town because of me. Will Saul really come down?" God answered, "Yes, he will." David then asked, "Will the people of Keilah hand my men over to Saul?" God replied, "They will," so David and his roughly six hundred men left Keilah and roamed from place to place. Once Saul found out that David had escaped, he stopped pursuing him. David then lived in the Wilderness of Ziph and stayed in the mountain strongholds.

Then the Ziphites came to Saul at Gibeah and said, "Isn't David hiding in the hill country of Hachilah?" Saul descended into the Wilderness of Ziph with three thousand of his men to hunt for David. Saul camped in the hill country of Hachilah, while David remained in the wilderness. Realizing that Saul was following him, David sent scouts and discovered that Saul had camped very close. David then went to the place where Saul was camping and saw where he lay, with Abner, the commander of his army, beside him; Saul was protected by a barricade, and his soldiers were all around.

title: A Soldier Who Spared His Enemy
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
David turned to Ahimelech the Hittite and to Abishai, the brother of Joab, asking, "Who will go with me to Saul's camp?" Abishai replied, "I'll go with you." So David and Abishai approached the camp by night, where they found Saul lying asleep inside the barricade, with his spear stuck in the ground beside his head and Abner and the soldiers around him.

Abishai whispered to David, "Today, God has delivered your enemy into your hands. Let me pin him to the ground with one strike of my spear; I won't need to hit him twice!" David replied, "As surely as God lives, either God will strike him down, or his time will come to die in battle. God forbid that I harm the one God has chosen as ruler! Now take the spear next to his head and the jug of water, and let's go." So David took the spear and the jug of water from Saul's head, and they quietly left. None of the soldiers noticed them because they were all asleep, a deep sleep sent from God had fallen upon them.

David went over and stood on a distant hilltop, giving them plenty of space. He called to the soldiers and Abner, the son of Ner, saying, "Hey, Abner! Are you going to answer me?" Abner replied, "Who are you calling?" David said to Abner, "You're a mighty warrior, aren't you? Why haven't you guarded your king, the Lord's chosen one? One of the people came to kill him, and you didn't do your duty. As God lives, you deserve to die for not protecting your master! Look where his spear is and the jug of water that was at his head!"

Saul recognized David's voice and asked, "Is that you, my son David?" David replied, "Yes, it's me, my lord." He continued, "Why are you chasing your servant? What have I done? What am I guilty of? Please listen to me. If God has stirred you against me, let Him accept my offering. But if it's men stirring you up, may they be cursed before God, for they have driven me away today, saying, 'Go serve other gods,' robbing me of my share in the land God has given His people. I don't want to die far from God's presence, for the king of Israel is out to take my life, as if I were just a hunted bird on the mountains."

Saul said, "I've done wrong. Come back, my son David; I won't harm you again, because you've spared my life today. I made a foolish mistake." David replied, "Here is Saul's spear! Let one of the young men come and take it. May God reward everyone who does what is right and stays faithful; for today, God delivered you into my hands, but I won’t harm someone chosen by Him. Just as your life matters greatly to me, so may my life be precious in God's sight, and may He rescue me from all my troubles."

Saul said to David, "Bless you, my son David! You will achieve great things and surely succeed!" Then David went his way, while Saul returned home.

title: Abigail'S Sensible Advice
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
David left for the Wilderness of Maon. In Maon, there was a wealthy man living in Carmel. He owned three thousand sheep and a thousand goats and was busy shearing his sheep. His name was Nabal and his wife's name was Abigail. Abigail was sensible and beautiful, while Nabal was rude and rough; he belonged to the Calebite clan.

When David learned that Nabal was shearing his sheep, he sent ten young men with the message, "Go to Carmel and greet Nabal for me. Tell him, 'Peace and prosperity to you and your family! I've heard that you have sheep-shearers. Your shepherds were with us, and we didn’t harm them; nothing was missing while they were in Carmel. Ask your young men, and they'll confirm this. Since it's a festive occasion, please be generous and provide whatever you can for my servants and for your son David.'"

The young men went to Nabal and delivered David's message, then waited for his response. Nabal replied to David's servants, "Who is David? And who is the son of Jesse? Nowadays, many slaves run away from their masters! Should I take my food and water and the meat I prepared for my shearers and give it to men I don’t even know?" When David's men returned and reported this, he told them, "Every man, grab your sword!" They all strapped on their swords, with David leading about four hundred men while two hundred stayed back to guard the supplies.

One of Nabal's young men informed Abigail, "David sent messengers from the wilderness to greet our master, but he insulted them. The men have been very good to us; we haven't been harmed, and nothing went missing while we were with them. They protected us day and night while we tended the sheep. You need to decide what to do because bad things are coming for our master and his household. Nabal is such a hothead that no one can talk to him."

title: Abigail'S Sensible Advice
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Abigail hurriedly gathered two hundred loaves of bread, two skins of wine, five roasted sheep, five baskets of parched grain, a hundred bunches of raisins, and two hundred cakes of figs. She loaded them onto donkeys and instructed her young men, "You go ahead; I'm coming after you." She didn’t tell Nabal anything about her plans. As she rode down a hillside, she encountered David and his men, who were coming toward her. David had just declared, "I wasted my time protecting all this man's properties in the wilderness, and he has paid me back with evil. By morning, I swear, not a single man belonging to Nabal will be left alive!"

When Abigail saw David, she quickly got off her donkey and bowed low to the ground. Falling at his feet, she said, "My lord, let the blame fall on me. Just let me speak, and please listen to me. Don’t pay any attention to that mean man Nabal; he truly lives up to his name. He is a fool, and his foolishness controls him. I didn’t see the young men you sent. As surely as the Lord lives and as you live, since God has kept you from murdering and seeking revenge, may your enemies and those who seek to harm you be like Nabal. Please, let this gift I’ve brought be given to the young men following you. Forgive the wrongdoing of your servant because the Lord will certainly make your family strong. You’re fighting for the Lord, and you shouldn’t be guilty of wrongdoing. If someone comes to chase you and take your life, God will protect you, but He will take care of your enemies like stones slung away. When God fulfills His promises and makes you ruler over Israel, you won’t regret shedding blood for no reason or seeking revenge yourself. When good comes to you, remember me."

David replied to Abigail, "Blessed be the Lord, the God of Israel, who sent you to meet me today. And blessed is your wisdom! You’ve saved me from murder and from taking revenge myself. As surely as the Lord lives, I would have wiped out Nabal's men by dawn if you hadn’t come swiftly to stop me."

So David accepted all that Abigail brought and told her, "Go back home in peace. I’ve listened to your advice and done what you asked."

When Abigail returned to Nabal, he was throwing a big feast, feeling like a king and drunk. She didn’t tell him anything until the morning. When the wine wore off, she told him what she had done, and his heart sank; he turned as cold as a stone. About ten days later, he suffered a stroke and died.

When David learned that Nabal was dead, he said, "Thank you, Lord, for punishing Nabal for insulting me and keeping me from wrongdoing. God has dealt with Nabal's crime; it’s on his own head."

David then sent word to Abigail to ask her to be his wife. When his servants arrived and said, "David wants you to come with us to be his wife," she rose, bowed her face to the ground, and replied, "Your servant is willing to be even a servant, washing the feet of my lord's servants." Abigail quickly got on a donkey, with five of her maids following, and went with David's messengers to become his wife.

title: Making The Best Of Trouble
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
David thought to himself, "Someday, Saul will kill me. My best option is to escape to the land of the Philistines. That way, Saul will stop searching for me throughout Israel, and I can get away from him." So, David, along with the six hundred men who were with him, went to Achish, the king of Gath. They settled in Gath with their families. When Saul heard that David had fled to Gath, he stopped looking for him.

David asked Achish, "If you’re willing, please give me a place in one of the towns out in the countryside so I can live there; your servant doesn't need to stay in the royal city with you." Achish granted him Ziklag, and David lived in the open country of the Philistines for a year and four months.

David and his men went out and attacked the Geshurites, Girzites, and Amalekites, who lived in the area stretching from Telem to Egypt. Each time David raided that land, he didn’t leave any man or woman alive. Instead, he took their sheep, oxen, donkeys, camels, and clothes and returned to Achish. When Achish asked, "Where did you raid today?" David replied, "In the South Country of Judah, or the South Country of the Jerahmeelites, or the South Country of the Kenites." Achish believed David, thinking, "He's made the people of Israel dislike him, so he’ll be my servant forever."

During that time, the Philistines gathered their forces to wage war against Israel. Achish told David, "You and your men will go with me into battle." David replied, "You'll find out what your servant can do." Achish then said, "Great! From now on, I'll make you the captain of my bodyguard."

The Philistines assembled their troops at Aphek, while the Israelites camped by the spring in Jezreel. As the leaders of the Philistines marched by in groups of hundreds and thousands, David and his men followed behind Achish. The commanders of the Philistines asked, "What are those Hebrews doing here?" Achish responded, "Isn’t this David, the servant of Saul, the king of Israel? He’s been with me for two years, and I've found no fault in him since he came to me."

title: Making The Best Of Trouble
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
However, the commanders of the Philistines were not pleased. They said to Achish, "Send him back to the place where you've stationed him. We can’t let him go into battle with us, or he might win back the favor of his master by taking us out. Isn’t this the David they sang about, saying:

"Saul has slain his thousands,
And David his ten thousands?"

Achish then called David and said, "As surely as the LORD lives, you are a good man. Your behavior has been commendable since you’ve been with me, and I’ve found nothing wrong with you up to now. But the other rulers don’t trust you. So, I think it’s best for you to go back home, so you don’t upset the Philistine leaders." David replied, "What have I done? What have you found in me since I joined your service that would prevent me from fighting the enemies of my lord the king?" Achish answered, "I know you’re as loyal as an angel of God, but the Philistine commanders have said, ‘He shouldn’t go to battle with us.’ So, you and your men need to rise early in the morning and return to the place where I’ve assigned you. Don’t plan any trouble, because I trust you, but just leave at dawn."

So, David and his men got up early in the morning to head back to the land of the Philistines, while the Philistines marched up to Jezreel.

On the third day, when David and his men returned to Ziklag, they discovered that the Amalekites had raided the South Country and Ziklag, burning it to the ground and taking away everyone captive, including the women and children. They had not killed anyone but had taken them all with them. David and his companions wept loudly until they could weep no more.

David was in a tough spot, as his men talked about stoning him, feeling bitter over the loss of their sons and daughters. But David found strength in trusting the LORD his God. With his six hundred followers, he continued on to the Brook Besor, where those who were too exhausted to cross the brook stayed behind.

title: Making The Best Of Trouble
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
There, they found an Egyptian in the open field and brought him to David. They gave him food and water. Then David asked him, "Who do you belong to, and where are you from?" The young man replied, "I’m an Egyptian servant of an Amalekite. My master left me behind because I fell sick three days ago. We raided the South Country of the Cherethites, Judah, and the South Country of Caleb, and we burned Ziklag." David asked, "Will you lead me to that raiding party?" He said, "Swear to me by your God that you won’t kill me or return me to my master, and I’ll guide you to them."

When they reached the Amalekites, they were scattered all over the land, enjoying their feast and celebrating the great plunder they had taken from the lands of the Philistines and Judah. David launched an attack against them from dusk until the next evening, and only four hundred young men, riding camels, managed to escape.

David reclaimed everything the Amalekites had taken, rescuing his two wives, and nothing was missing. He gathered all the flocks and herds and drove them ahead of his followers, declaring, "This is David's spoil."

When David met the two hundred men who had been too exhausted to go with him, the selfish, mean-spirited men in his group said, "Because they didn’t go with us, we shouldn’t give them any of the spoil we took—only their wives and kids can go home." David replied, "My brothers, don’t do that with what the LORD has given us. He has kept us safe and delivered this raiding party into our hands. Those who stayed with the baggage will share equally with those who went into battle." From that day forward, he established this as a law and rule in Israel.

When David returned to Ziklag, he sent some of the spoil to the leaders of Judah and to his relatives, saying, "Look! Here’s a gift for you from the spoils of the enemies of the LORD."

title: The Death Of Two Brave Warriors
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Samuel had passed away, and all of Israel mourned for him, burying him in his hometown of Ramah. Saul also removed all the mediums and those who communicated with spirits from the land.

Then the Philistines gathered at Shunem, and Saul rallied the Israelites to camp at Gilboa. Upon seeing the Philistine army, fear gripped him. He inquired of Jehovah whether he should confront them, but there was no answer—no dreams, no lots, no prophets spoke to him. So Saul turned to his attendants, saying, "Find me a woman who acts as a medium so I can ask her about this." His servants replied, "There’s a medium in Endor."

Saul kept his identity hidden, donned different clothes, and took two men with him. They visited the woman at night. He said, "Bring up for me the spirit of someone I want to consult." The woman replied, "You know what Saul has done; he has expelled all mediums and spiritists from the land. Why are you trying to get me killed?" Saul swore to her by Jehovah, saying, "As surely as Jehovah lives, you won’t be punished for this." She asked, "Whom do you want me to bring up for you?" Saul replied, "Bring up Samuel."

When the woman saw Samuel, she screamed and exclaimed, "Why have you deceived me? You are Saul!" He reassured her, "Don’t be afraid! What do you see?" She answered, "I see a spirit rising from the earth." Saul inquired, "What does he look like?" She said, "An old man is coming up, wrapped in a cloak." Recognizing it was Samuel, Saul bowed down and worshiped.

Samuel said to Saul, "Why have you called me?" Saul replied, "I’m in deep trouble. The Philistines are waging war against me, and God has turned away from me. He doesn’t answer me anymore—no prophets, no dreams. So I’ve called you to tell me what to do." Samuel responded, "Why do you consult me when Jehovah has turned against you? He has taken away your kingship and given it to David. Tomorrow, you and your sons will die, and Jehovah will hand Israel over to the Philistines."

title: The Death Of Two Brave Warriors
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Saul fell flat on the ground out of fear at Samuel's words, feeling utterly drained because he hadn’t eaten for the entire day and night. The woman noticed his distress and said, "I risked my life to help you. Please, let me prepare some food for you so you can regain your strength for the journey ahead." Initially, Saul refused, saying, "I won’t eat," but his servants and the woman insisted, and he finally agreed. He got up and sat on the couch. The woman quickly killed a fat calf and made unleavened bread. She served it to Saul and his attendants, and they ate before leaving that night.

The Philistines fought against Israel, causing the Israelites to flee and many to fall dead on Mount Gilboa. The Philistines pursued Saul and his sons, killing Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchishua. With the battle turning against him and archers closing in, Saul was gravely wounded. He ordered his armor-bearer, "Draw your sword and kill me so these uncircumcised Philistines won’t mock me." But his armor-bearer was too frightened to do it. So Saul took his own sword and fell on it. When the armor-bearer saw Saul was dead, he also fell on his sword and died beside him. On that same day, Saul, his three sons, and his armor-bearer all perished.

When the Israelites in the lowlands and across the Jordan learned that the Israelites had fled and that Saul and his sons were dead, they abandoned their towns, and the Philistines took over.

The next day, the Philistines came to loot the dead and discovered Saul and his three sons had fallen on Mount Gilboa. They cut off his head, stripped him of his armor, and sent messengers throughout the land to announce their victory to their idols and their people. They placed his armor in the temple of Ashtarte and hung his body on the wall of Bethshan.

When the people of Jabesh in Gilead heard what the Philistines had done to Saul, their brave warriors rose up and marched all night to retrieve the bodies of Saul and his sons from the wall of Bethshan. They took them back to Jabesh, mourning for them there. They then buried their bones under an oak tree in Jabesh and abstained from food for seven days.

On the third day after David returned to Ziklag from defeating the Amalekites, a man approached him from Saul's camp, his clothes torn and dirt on his head. He fell to the ground before David. David asked him, "Where have you come from?" He replied, "I escaped from the Israelite camp." David further asked, "How did the battle go? Tell me everything." The man responded, "The people fled, and many were killed, including Saul and his son Jonathan!"

David and all the men with him tore their clothes in sorrow, mourned, wept, and went without food until evening because Saul, his son Jonathan, and the people of Jehovah had fallen by the sword.

David then sang a mournful song for Saul and Jonathan:

"Weep, O Judah!
Grieve, O Israel!
Your heroes lie slain on the heights!
How the mighty have fallen!
"Saul and Jonathan, loved ones in life and death!
They were inseparable;
Faster than eagles,
Stronger than lions.
"O Jonathan, your death wounds me deeply,
O Jonathan, my brother, how I lament for you.
You were a friend beyond compare,
Your bond meant more to me than any love!
"How the mighty have fallen,
And the weapons of war are gone!"

title: A Shepherd Boy Who Was Called To Lead A Nation
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
After that, David asked the Lord, "Should I go up to one of the towns in Judah?" The Lord replied, "Yes, go up." David then asked, "Which town should I go to?" The Lord answered, "To Hebron." So David went up with his two wives, Ahinoam and Abigail. He also brought his men with him, each with their families, and they settled in the towns around Hebron. Soon after, the men of Judah came to Hebron and made David their ruler.

When David heard about the men of Jabesh in Gilead who had buried Saul, he sent messengers to them saying, "You are blessed by the Lord for showing kindness to your master Saul by giving him a proper burial. May the Lord show you kindness and faithfulness in return. I will also reward you for this good deed. Be brave and strong; Saul is dead, and the people of Judah have made me their ruler."

Meanwhile, Abner, the commander of Saul's army, had taken Ishbaal, Saul's son, to Mahanaim and made him ruler over Gilead and all Israel. However, the people of Judah remained loyal to David.

There was constant conflict between the followers of Saul and those of David. However, David continued to grow stronger while Saul's followers became weaker.

One day, Rechab and Baanah, the sons of Rimmon, visited the palace of Ishbaal around noon while he was taking a nap. The doorkeeper had fallen asleep while cleaning wheat, so Rechab and Baanah sneaked in, attacked Ishbaal, killed him, and then cut off his head.

Eventually, all the tribes of Israel came to David at Hebron and said, "Look, we are your relatives. When Saul ruled over us, you were the one who led the Israelites, and the Lord has said to you, 'You will shepherd my people Israel and be their leader.'" So, all the leaders of Israel gathered at Hebron, and David made a covenant with them in front of the Lord, officially becoming the ruler over Israel. David was thirty years old when he began to reign and ruled for forty years.

title: A Shepherd Boy Who Was Called To Lead A Nation
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
When the Philistines heard that David had been made ruler over Israel, they went up to search for him. David, hearing this, went down to the stronghold.

Three of David's thirty warriors went to the top of the rock near the fortress of Adullam while a group of Philistines camped in the Valley of Rephaim. David was in the fortress at that time, and a company of Philistines was in Bethlehem. He sighed and said, "Oh, how I wish someone would bring me a drink of water from the well in Bethlehem by the gate!" The three famous warriors broke through the Philistine camp, filled their water jug from the well by the gate, and brought it back to David. But he wouldn't drink it; instead, he poured it out as an offering to the Lord and said, "The Lord forbid that I should drink this! It’s like the blood of these men who risked their lives." So he refused to drink it.

When the Philistines spread out over the Valley of Rephaim, David asked the Lord, "Should I go out against the Philistines? Will you hand them over to me?" The Lord replied, "Yes, go; I will certainly deliver the Philistines into your hands." David went to Baal-perazim and defeated them there, stating, "The Lord has broken through my enemies like a flood."

Later, the Philistines came up again and spread out over the Valley of Rephaim. David inquired of the Lord, who instructed him, "Don't attack directly. Instead, circle around behind them and strike them near the balsam trees. When you hear the sound of marching in the treetops, act quickly because the Lord will have gone out ahead of you to defeat the Philistine army." Following the Lord's command, David drove the Philistines back from Gibeon all the way to Gezer.

title: Jerusalem Made The Capital City
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
David and his men marched into Jerusalem to confront the Jebusites, the original inhabitants of the land. The Jebusites mocked David, saying, "You won’t get in here; even the blind and the lame could hold you back," convinced that David couldn't possibly overcome their defenses.

However, David successfully captured the fortress of Zion and settled there. He also constructed a wall around it and named it the City of David.

David continued to grow stronger because the Lord of Hosts was with him. Hiram, the king of Tyre, sent messengers along with cedar trees, carpenters, and masons to build a palace for him. David recognized that the Lord had made him the ruler over Israel and had strengthened his kingdom for the sake of the people.

Once again, David gathered all the important leaders of Israel—thirty thousand in total—and set out with the people to Baal-Judah to retrieve the ark of God. They loaded the ark onto a new cart and took it from the house of Abinadab, which was on a hill. Uzzah and Ahio, the sons of Abinadab, were guiding the cart, with Uzzah walking beside the ark and Ahio leading the way. David and all the people of Israel danced passionately before the Lord, playing music on harps, lyres, drums, castanets, and cymbals.

When they reached the threshing floor of Nachon, Uzzah reached out to steady the ark of God when the oxen stumbled. However, the Lord's anger flared against Uzzah, and He struck him down right there because he touched the ark; he died in the presence of God. David was terrified of the Lord that day and wondered, "How can I bring the ark of the Lord to my home?" So, he decided not to take the ark to the City of David but instead placed it in the house of Obed-edom, the Gittite, where it stayed for three months. During that time, the Lord blessed Obed-edom and his family.

When David heard the news, "The Lord has blessed Obed-edom and his entire household because of the ark of God," he joyfully brought the ark up from Obed-edom’s house to the City of David. After every six steps the bearers of the ark took, David sacrificed an ox and a fat animal. He danced wholeheartedly before the Lord, wearing a linen priestly garment. David and all the people of Israel celebrated as they brought the ark of the Lord in with cheers and the sound of trumpets.

Once the ark of the Lord was brought in and placed in the tent that David had constructed for it, he offered burnt offerings and peace offerings to the Lord. After finishing these sacrifices, David blessed the people in the name of the Lord of Hosts, distributing rolls of bread, portions of meat, and cakes of raisins to every Israelite present—men and women alike. Then everyone returned home.

The Lord spoke to Nathan, the prophet, and said, "Tell my servant David: 'The Lord of Hosts says: I took you from the pasture where you tended sheep to be the leader of my people Israel. I have been with you every step of the way, defeating all your enemies before you, and I will make your name great, like that of the most famous men on earth. When your days come to an end and you are buried with your ancestors, I will raise up your son to succeed you, establishing his kingdom. I will be a father to him, and he will be my son. Should he stray, I will correct him gently, but my favor will not be taken from him as it was from Saul. Your dynasty and kingdom will be secure before me; your throne will be established forever.'"

title: David'S Kindness To Jonathan'S Son
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
David asked, "Is there anyone left from Saul's family whom I can show kindness to for Jonathan's sake?" There was a servant of Saul named Ziba. When he was brought before David, the king asked, "Are you Ziba?" Ziba replied, "Yes, I am your servant." David continued, "Is there anyone else from Saul's family that I can show kindness to, just like God shows us?" Ziba answered, "Jonathan has a son who is still alive, but he's lame in both feet." David asked, "Where is he?" Ziba replied, "He's at the house of Machir in Lodebar."

David then sent for him and brought him from the house of Machir. When Meribaal, Jonathan's son, arrived, he bowed deeply before David. David said, "Meribaal!" He answered, "Here I am, your servant!" David reassured him, "Don’t be afraid, for I will show you kindness because of your father Jonathan. I’m going to restore to you all the land that belonged to your grandfather Saul, and you will always eat at my table." Meribaal lowered his head and said, "What is your servant that you should look at me with such kindness? I feel unworthy."

David then called Ziba, Saul’s servant, and told him, "I’ve given your master’s son everything that belongs to Saul’s family. You and your sons and servants will farm the land and bring in the harvest, so your master’s son has food. But Meribaal, your master’s son, will eat at my table regularly." Ziba had fifteen sons and twenty servants, and he replied, "Your servant will do whatever my lord commands."

So Meribaal ate at David's table like one of his own sons. Meribaal also had a young son named Mica. Everyone who lived in Ziba's household became Meribaal's servants. Meribaal lived in Jerusalem, and even though he was lame in both feet, he always had a place at David's table.

title: A Rich Man Who Was A Thief
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
One evening, while Joab was laying siege to Rabbath Ammon, David got up from his bed and walked around on the roof of the royal palace. From up there, he saw a woman taking a bath, and she was incredibly beautiful. Curious, David asked about her, and someone informed him, "Isn't this Bathsheba, the wife of Uriah the Hittite?" David then sent messengers to bring her to him, and she came. Afterward, she returned to her home.

David wrote a letter to Joab and sent it with Uriah. In that letter, he instructed, "Put Uriah on the front lines where the fighting is fiercest, then pull back from him so that he gets struck down and dies." Joab followed these orders, placing Uriah in a location known for its danger. When the city's soldiers fought against Joab, several of David's men were killed, and Uriah the Hittite met his end.

Joab then sent a report to David about the battle, giving specific instructions to the messenger: "After you tell the king everything about the war, if he gets angry and asks, 'Why did you go so close to the city to fight? Didn’t you know they would shoot from the wall? Who killed Abimelech, the son of Jerubbaal? Didn't a woman drop a millstone on him from the wall, so he died at Thebez? Why would you go near the wall?' then you should say, 'Your servant Uriah the Hittite is dead too.'"

The messenger from Joab went to Jerusalem, delivering all of Joab's messages to David. David responded to the messenger, saying, "Tell Joab, ‘Don't let this thing bother you. The sword takes one and then another. Keep fighting against the city and capture it,’ and reassure him."

When Bathsheba found out that her husband Uriah had died, she mourned for him according to custom. After her mourning period was over, David sent for her, and she became his wife, eventually giving birth to a son.

title: A Rich Man Who Was A Thief
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
What David did did not sit well with God, so He sent the prophet Nathan to speak with David. Nathan approached and said, "There were two men in a city: one rich and the other poor. The rich man owned many flocks and herds, but the poor man had nothing except for one little ewe lamb he had bought. He nurtured it, and it grew up with him and his children. It even ate from his plate, drank from his cup, and lay in his arms like a daughter to him."

"Then a traveler came to the rich man. Instead of taking from his own flocks or herds to prepare a meal for the guest, he chose to take the poor man's lamb and cooked it for the visitor who had come."

David was outraged and exclaimed to Nathan, "As surely as the Lord lives, the man who did this deserves to die! He must repay fourfold the value of the lamb because he showed no compassion."

Nathan replied to David, "You are that man! The Lord, the God of Israel, says: ‘I made you king over Israel, and I rescued you from Saul’s hand. I gave you your master’s house and wives, and I granted you the kingdoms of Israel and Judah. If that weren’t enough, I would have given you even more. Why have you despised the Lord by doing what is wrong? You killed Uriah the Hittite with the sword and took his wife as your own. You killed him with the sword of the Ammonites. Because of this, the sword will never leave your house, since you despised me and took Uriah’s wife as your own.’"

David confessed to Nathan, "I have sinned against the Lord!" Nathan replied, "The Lord has forgiven your sin; you won’t die. However, because you’ve shown such contempt for the Lord, the child born to you will surely die." Then Nathan went back to his own home.

The Lord struck Bathsheba’s child with illness, and David prayed for the child. He ate no food and lay all night on the ground in sackcloth. His servants tried to help him get up from the ground, but he refused to rise or eat with them. On the seventh day, when the child died, David’s servants hesitated to tell him because they said, "While the child was still alive, we spoke to him, and he didn’t listen. How can we tell him that the child has died? He might hurt himself!"

But when David noticed that his servants were whispering, he realized the child was dead. He asked his servants, "Is the child dead?" They answered, "Yes, he is dead." David then got up from the ground, washed himself, anointed his body with oil, changed his clothes, and went to the Lord's temple to worship. After that, he returned home and asked for food, and when it was served to him, he ate.

His servants asked him, "What have you done? While the child was alive, you refused to eat and cried desperately, but now that he’s dead, you’ve risen and eaten bread." David replied, "While the child was still alive, I fasted and cried because I thought, 'Who knows if the Lord will be gracious and let the child live?' But now that he’s dead, why should I fast? Can I bring him back? I’ll go to him, but he won’t return to me."

title: Absalom The Ungrateful Son
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Some time later, Absalom, King David's son, prepared a chariot and hired horses, along with fifty men to run ahead of him. He would wake up early and stand by the road leading to the city gate. He called out to everyone coming for a judgment from the ruler, asking, "Where are you from?" When someone replied, "I’m from one of the tribes of Israel," Absalom would say, "Your case is valid and right, but there’s no one appointed to listen to you. I wish someone would make me a judge in the land so that everyone with a complaint could come to me, and I would ensure they got justice!" If anyone approached to bow before him, he would reach out, take their hand, and kiss them. This is how Absalom gained the hearts of all the Israelites who sought justice from David.

After four years, Absalom told his father, "I’d like to go to Hebron to fulfill a vow I made to Jehovah." David replied, "Go in peace." So, he went to Hebron, but he secretly sent messengers throughout all the tribes of Israel to announce, "When you hear the trumpet, shout, ‘Absalom has become king in Hebron!’" Absalom took two hundred men from Jerusalem with him—men who came innocently, unaware of his plan. He also called for Ahithophel, David's adviser, who was offering sacrifices in Giloh. The plot was gaining strength as more people began to side with Absalom.

When a messenger arrived to tell David, "The hearts of the Israelites have turned to Absalom," David turned to all his servants in Jerusalem and said, "Get up, let’s flee, or we won’t escape from Absalom. We need to go now, or he’ll catch us and bring disaster upon us, killing everyone in the city." His servants replied, "We’ll do whatever you say, our lord."

So, David and all the people with him left and stood at the last house, while all the officers, the royal bodyguard, and the six hundred men of Ittai the Gittite, who had followed him from Gath, moved on ahead.

title: Absalom The Ungrateful Son
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
David then asked Ittai, "Why are you coming with us? Go back and stay with the new ruler, since you’re a foreigner from your own land. You just arrived yesterday—should I make you wander with us today? Go back and take your men with you; may Jehovah show you kindness and faithfulness." Ittai responded, "As surely as Jehovah lives, and as my lord the king lives, I’ll be wherever you are, whether in life or death!" David said, "Then march on!" So, Ittai and all his men, along with their families, continued on.

The people were all weeping loudly as David stood in the Kidron Valley, and they passed by on their way to the wilderness. Zadok and Abiathar arrived carrying the Ark of God and set it down until everyone had passed. Then, David told Zadok, "Take the Ark of God back to the city. If I find favor with Jehovah, He will bring me back to see it and its dwelling place. But if He says, ‘I have no confidence in you,’ then here I am; let Him do what He thinks is best." So, Zadok and Abiathar took the Ark of God back to Jerusalem and stayed there.

Meanwhile, David was climbing the Mount of Olives, weeping as he went, with his head covered and his feet bare. The people traveling with him also covered their heads and wept as they climbed.

When David reached the top where people worship God, Hushai the Archite met him, his clothes torn and dust on his head. David said to him, "If you come with me, you’ll be a burden. But if you go back to the city and tell Absalom, ‘Your father's men have left, and I've followed him; I will serve you as I served your father,’ you can help counter Ahithophel's advice. Don’t you have Zadok and Abiathar the priests with you? They have their sons, Ahimaaz and Jonathan, who can send me updates on everything you hear." So Hushai, David's friend, went back to the city just as Absalom was coming to Jerusalem.

title: Absalom The Ungrateful Son
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
David and the people with him finally reached the Jordan River, exhausted, but there he took a moment to refresh himself.

Meanwhile, Absalom and his followers arrived in Jerusalem, and Ahithophel was with him. When Hushai, David's friend, met Absalom, he greeted him, saying, "Long live the king!" But Absalom replied, "Is this how you show love for your friend? Why didn’t you go with him?" Hushai answered, "No! I will support the one whom Jehovah and his people have chosen, and whom I will serve. Shouldn’t I be serving his son? Just as I served your father, I will serve you now."

Ahithophel’s advice was seen as coming directly from God by both David and Absalom. He suggested to Absalom, "Let me choose twelve thousand men to go after David tonight. I’ll catch him when he’s tired and weak; his army will flee. I’ll only kill the king, and the rest will return to you, just as a bride returns to her husband. Focus only on one man’s life, and the rest will be at peace." Absalom and the leaders of Israel found this advice appealing.

Then Absalom said, "Let’s hear what Hushai has to say as well." When Hushai arrived, Absalom asked him, "Ahithophel has advised this; should we go with his plan, or do you have a different suggestion?" Hushai replied, "The advice of Ahithophel doesn’t seem wise this time. You know your father’s men are mighty warriors and are driven by rage, like a bear robbed of cubs. Your father is a skilled soldier and wouldn’t stay with the army at night. He’s probably hiding in one of the caves or somewhere else. If any of his people fall, it will be reported that your army is in chaos. Even the bravest will lose heart, for everyone knows your father is a great warrior and his men are valiant. My advice is to gather all Israel to you, from Dan to Beersheba, as numerous as the sand along the seashore, and you lead them. This way we’ll find him, and we’ll strike him down as the dew falls; not even one man with him will survive. If he retreats to a city, we’ll pull it down until not even a stone is left."

Absalom and all the people of Israel declared, "Hushai’s advice is better than Ahithophel’s." For Jehovah had decided to frustrate the good advice of Ahithophel in order to bring disaster upon Absalom.

Hushai then told Zadok and Abiathar the priests, "This is what Ahithophel advised Absalom and the leaders; this is my advice. Quickly get word to David: ‘Don't spend the night at the wilderness fords; cross over immediately to save yourselves, or David and the people will be killed.'"

At this time, Jonathan and Ahimaaz were hiding at Enrogel. A maid was to go and bring them news, but they couldn’t be seen entering the city. A boy spotted them and reported to Absalom. They quickly escaped to the house of a man in Bahurim, where there was a well in the courtyard. They descended into it, and the woman covered the well with a cloth and scattered dried fruit on top so nothing appeared amiss. When Absalom’s men came to the woman and asked, "Where are Ahimaaz and Jonathan?" she said, "They’ve crossed the brook." When they couldn't find them, they returned to Jerusalem.

As soon as the men left, Ahimaaz and Jonathan emerged from the well and hurried to tell David, "Get up and cross the water quickly; that’s Ahithophel’s advice for you!" So, David and all his people rose and crossed the Jordan. By daybreak, not a single person was left behind.

title: A Broken-Hearted Father
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
After Absalom and all the men of Israel crossed the Jordan River, David counted the troops with him and appointed commanders over thousands and hundreds. He divided the troops into three groups: one led by Joab, another by Abishai, and the last by Ittai. David said to the people, "I’ll definitely go out with you." But the people replied, "You shouldn’t go out with us; if we are defeated or if a lot of us die, it won’t matter because you’re worth ten thousand of us. It’s more important for you to be ready to help from the city." David agreed, saying, "I’ll do what you think is best!" He then stood by the gate while all the troops marched out in groups of hundreds and thousands.

David instructed Joab, Abishai, and Ittai, "Be gentle for my sake with the young man, Absalom!" Everyone heard him give this order about Absalom.

The troops then headed out to fight against Israel. The battle took place in the forest of Ephraim, where David's loyal soldiers defeated the men of Israel, resulting in a significant loss of life—twenty thousand men. The battle spread throughout the region, and many lives were lost in the thick underbrush, surpassing those killed by the sword.

While riding his mule, Absalom encountered David's soldiers, but as the mule went under the thick branches of a large oak, Absalom's head got caught, leaving him hanging helplessly between heaven and earth while the mule continued on. A man saw this and informed Joab, saying, "I saw Absalom hanging in an oak." Joab replied, "You saw him! Why didn’t you take him down? I would have rewarded you with ten pieces of silver and a belt." The man responded, "Even if I had a thousand pieces of silver in my hands, I wouldn’t raise a finger against the king’s son. Remember, he ordered you, Abishai, and Ittai to ‘Take care of the young man Absalom.’ If I had killed him, nothing would have been hidden from you, and you wouldn’t have tried to protect me." Joab retorted, "I’m done talking to you."

So Joab took three spears, pierced them into Absalom's heart while he was still alive in the oak, then said to a servant, "Go and tell the king what you saw." The servant bowed and hurried off.

David was sitting between the two gates when the servant arrived. He said, "Let my lord hear the good news: God has delivered you from all who rose up against you today." David asked the servant, "Is my young Absalom okay?" The servant replied, "May your enemies and everyone who rebels against you end up like that young man!"

David was heartbroken and went up to the chamber over the gate to weep. As he cried, he said, "My son Absalom, my son, oh my son Absalom! I wish I had died for you, Absalom, my son, my son!" Meanwhile, Joab was informed, "The king is mourning for Absalom." The victory turned to mourning for the people because they realized David was grieving for his son. So, the people quietly returned to the city, feeling ashamed, as if they had fled from battle. David covered his face and cried loudly, "My son Absalom, Absalom, my son, my son!"

title: How Solomon Became The Ruler Of Israel
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
When David grew old, Adonijah decided, "I’m going to be the ruler of Israel." So he prepared chariots, horsemen, and fifty men to run ahead of him. David, his father, had never questioned him about his actions. Adonijah was handsome and was the next youngest son after Absalom. He made an alliance with Joab and Abiathar the priest for support. However, Zadok the priest, Benaiah, Nathan the prophet, and David's well-known warriors were not on his side.

Adonijah threw a feast and sacrificed sheep, oxen, and fat animals by the Serpent's Stone next to Fuller's Spring. He invited all his brothers and the royal officials of Judah, but he didn’t invite the prophet Nathan, Benaiah, the famous warriors, or his brother Solomon.

Nathan approached Bathsheba, Solomon's mother, and said, "Haven't you heard that Adonijah has been made king without David knowing? Here’s what you need to do to save yourself and your son: Go to David and say, 'Did you not, my lord, promise that Solomon would be the ruler after you? Why, then, has Adonijah taken that position?' While you’re speaking, I’ll come in and reinforce your message."

Bathsheba entered David's room; he was very old, and Abishag the Shunamite was looking after him. When David asked, "What do you want?" she replied, "My lord, you promised me by Jehovah: 'Solomon will rule after me.' But now Adonijah has become king without your knowledge! All the Israelites are waiting for you to tell them who will be their ruler. If you don’t speak up, my son Solomon and I will be treated as criminals when you’re gone."

title: How Solomon Became The Ruler Of Israel
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
While she was still talking to David, Nathan the prophet came in. They announced, "Nathan the prophet is here." Nathan entered and bowed before David. He said, "My lord, did you really say, 'Adonijah will be the ruler after me?' Because he’s gone and sacrificed many oxen, fat animals, and sheep. He’s invited all your sons, the military commanders, and Abiathar the priest, and they’re celebrating, saying, 'Long live King Adonijah!' But he hasn’t invited me, your servant, or Zadok the priest, or Benaiah, or Solomon. If you’ve allowed this, my lord, you haven’t shown your servants who should be the next ruler after you."

David said, "Bring Bathsheba to me." She entered and stood before him. Then he made a solemn promise: "As surely as Jehovah lives, who has delivered me from all my troubles, I promise you by Jehovah, the God of Israel, that Solomon your son will be the next ruler. I will do this today." Bathsheba then bowed down and said, "May my lord live forever."

David instructed, "Get Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah the son of Jehoiada for me." When they arrived, he said, "Take my servants with you. Let Solomon ride on my mule and bring him down to Gihon. There, Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet will anoint him as ruler over Israel. Blow the trumpet and shout, 'Long live King Solomon!'
