Then you can follow him up, and he will take my throne. I have appointed him to lead Israel and Judah." Benaiah responded, "So be it! May Jehovah uphold your words, and as He has been with you, may He be with Solomon as well. May Solomon’s reign be even greater than yours!"

Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah, along with the royal bodyguards, went down and placed Solomon on David's mule, then they took him to Gihon. Zadok the priest poured oil on Solomon’s head and blew the trumpet, and everyone shouted, "Long live Solomon!" The people started following him, playing flutes and celebrating so loudly that it felt like the ground shook from the noise.

Adonijah and his guests heard the commotion just as they finished eating, and they were terrified, each one of them getting up and leaving. In his fear, Adonijah grasped the horns of the altar. When Solomon was informed, "Adonijah is afraid of you and is holding onto the altar, asking for a promise that he won’t be killed with the sword," Solomon replied, "If he shows himself to be a good man, not a hair on his head will be harmed. But if he’s found guilty of treason, he will die." So Solomon had him brought down from the altar. He came and bowed before Solomon, who said, "Go home."

After this, David died and was buried in the City of David, having ruled over Israel for forty years.

title: A Young Man'S Wise Choice
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Solomon traveled to Gibeon to make a sacrifice, as it was the main high place. He offered a thousand animals as a burnt offering on the altar.

At Gibeon, God appeared to Solomon in a dream during the night and said, "Ask for anything you want, and I'll give it to you." Solomon replied, "You showed great kindness to my father David, and now, Jehovah my God, you've made me king in his place, even though I'm just a young man who doesn't know much about leading. Please give me the wisdom to rule your people well so that I can tell right from wrong, because who is capable of governing such a vast nation?"

God was pleased that Solomon asked for wisdom, and He said, "Since you've asked for this and not for a long life, wealth, or the lives of your enemies, but for understanding to discern what is right, I will grant your request. I've given you a wise and discerning mind, and I'll also provide you with the riches and honor you didn't ask for." When Solomon awoke, he realized it was just a dream, and he returned to Jerusalem.

Two women came to Solomon and stood before him. One woman said, "My lord, this woman and I live in the same house. I gave birth to a child while we were alone, and three days later, she had her own child. But during the night, her baby died because she accidentally laid on it.

"In the middle of the night, she got up and took my son from beside me while I was asleep. She laid him on her chest and put her dead child beside me. When I woke up in the morning to feed my baby, I saw that he was dead, but when I looked closely, I realized he wasn't my son." The other woman replied, "No, the live one is my son, and the dead one is yours!" They began to argue in front of Solomon.

Solomon said, "One of you claims that the living child is hers, while the other says the dead child is yours. Bring me a sword." They brought him a sword. Then he declared, "Cut the living child in two and give each of them half." The woman who was the real mother immediately spoke up, her love for her son evident in her voice. She said, "Please, my lord, give her the living child. Don't kill him!" The other woman said, "Neither of us should have him! Cut him in two!" Solomon then said, "Give the first woman the living child and do not kill him; she is his mother."

When all of Israel heard about Solomon's decision, they were in awe of him because they recognized that he possessed divine wisdom for making fair judgments.

title: Building A Great Temple
author: The Children's Bible
themes: the importance of place in worship
text_chunk:
In the fourth year of Solomon's reign over Israel, he constructed the temple for Jehovah. The temple measured ninety feet long, thirty feet wide, and forty-five feet high. The porch in front of the main area of the temple was thirty feet wide and fifteen feet deep. Solomon added windows to the temple, designed with broad casings on the inside and narrow ones on the outside.

The temple was built using stone that had been prepared at the quarry, and not a single hammer, chisel, or iron tool was heard during its construction. On the outside of the temple, Solomon built wings that surrounded both the main room and the inner sanctum, along with side chambers all around the temple.

The entrance to the lower side chambers was located on the south side of the temple. Winding stairs led up to the second floor, and then from the second to the third. Solomon constructed the wings on the sides of the temple, each standing seven and a half feet tall; they were connected to the temple with cedar beams.

He covered the inner walls of the temple with cedar planks from the floor up to the rafters, and the floor itself was made of cypress boards.

Solomon also created a thirty-foot square room at the back of the temple, made entirely of cedar, reaching from the floor to the rafters. This room served as the inner sanctuary, resembling the most holy place. The main area of the temple, in front of this inner room, was sixty feet long. Inside, cedar adorned the temple walls, intricately carved with designs of gourds and open flowers. The beauty of cedar surrounded everything, leaving no stone visible. Solomon prepared the inner room specifically for the ark.

In the inner room, Solomon crafted two winged bulls out of olive wood. Each stood fifteen feet tall, and their wings spanned seven and a half feet wide, extending fifteen feet from one wingtip to the other. He positioned them in the inner sanctuary of the temple with their wings outstretched, one wing touching one wall and the other wing touching the opposite wall, while their wings touched in the center. He covered them in gold.

Solomon then gathered the leaders of Israel in Jerusalem to bring up the ark of Jehovah from Zion, the City of David, during the autumn festival in September. Once all the leaders had arrived, the priests lifted the ark, along with the tent of meeting and all the sacred items inside it. The priests placed the ark of Jehovah in its designated spot in the inner room of the temple, under the wings of the winged bulls. The only contents of the ark were the two stone tablets that Moses had put there at Horeb. When the priests exited the inner room, a cloud filled the temple of Jehovah, making it impossible for them to continue their service because the glory of Jehovah filled his temple.

Then Solomon spoke:

"Jehovah has set the sun in the heavens, but has declared that He will dwell in thick darkness. So I have built You a temple as a grand dwelling, a place for You to reside forever."

While standing before the altar of Jehovah in front of the entire assembly of Israel, Solomon stretched out his hands toward heaven and said, "O Jehovah, God of Israel, there is no other God like You in heaven above or on earth below. You keep Your promises and show kindness to Your servants who serve You wholeheartedly. You have upheld the promise You made to Your servant David, my father."

"But can God truly dwell on earth? Even heaven and the highest heavens cannot contain You; how much less can this temple I have built!"

title: A Ruler Who Wronged His People
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Solomon spent thirteen years constructing his palace, and he also built the throne hall where he presided over his people. This grand space, known as the Hall of Judgment, was beautifully lined with cedar from the floor all the way up to the ceiling.

His palace, where he lived a bit deeper inside from the Hall of Judgment, matched the craftsmanship of the hall. Solomon even created a similar palace for his wife, Pharaoh's daughter. All of these impressive structures were made from expensive stones, meticulously shaped and polished both inside and out.

Solomon amassed a large fleet of chariots and horsemen, totaling one thousand four hundred chariots and twelve thousand horsemen. These forces were stationed in various chariot cities and also with him in Jerusalem. Additionally, he appointed twelve officers across Israel, responsible for supplying food to him and his household; each man was tasked with providing a month's worth of food each year.

When the queen of Sheba learned about Solomon's renowned wisdom, she decided to visit him and challenge him with tricky questions. She arrived in Jerusalem with a huge entourage, bringing camels loaded with spices, gold, and precious stones. Upon meeting Solomon, she shared everything on her mind, and he responded to all her inquiries; nothing was too tough for him to answer.

After seeing Solomon's wisdom, his magnificent palace, the lavish spread of food on his table, the housing of his officials, the way his attendants served him, their attire, and even the burnt offerings he made at the temple of Jehovah, the queen was utterly amazed. She said to Solomon, "What I heard in my homeland about your deeds and your wisdom is true. I couldn't believe it until I witnessed it myself; but honestly, the reality exceeds what I was told! Your wisdom and wealth are even greater than I imagined."

Solomon had a deep fondness for women and married many foreign wives—Moabites, Canaanites, Edomites, Sidonians, Hittites, and Ammonites. He had seven hundred wives of noble lineage and three hundred concubines. As he grew older, his wives led him away from loyalty to Jehovah, and he began to worship other gods. Solomon built shrines for Chemosh, the god of Moab, on a hill facing Jerusalem, and for Milcom, the god of the Ammonites. He did this for each of his foreign wives, burning incense and making sacrifices to their deities.

As a consequence, God raised up an adversary against Solomon: Rezon, the son of Eliada, who had escaped from his master, King Hadadezer of Zobah. Rezon gathered a band of followers and took control of Damascus, ruling as a foe to Israel for the remainder of Solomon's life.

Jeroboam, the son of Nebat, was a skilled and capable man. Noticing his industrious nature, Solomon put him in charge of all the men from the tribe of Joseph who were working for him.

One day, while Jeroboam was on his way out of Jerusalem, the prophet Ahijah from Shiloh encountered him and took him aside. Ahijah was wearing a new garment, and while they were alone in the field, he tore it into twelve pieces. He then said to Jeroboam, "Take ten pieces for yourself; for Jehovah, the God of Israel, says, 'I will take the kingdom from Solomon and give ten tribes to you, but he will keep only one tribe.'" That's when Jeroboam decided to rebel against Solomon.

As a result, Solomon intended to kill Jeroboam, but Jeroboam managed to escape to Egypt, where he stayed until Solomon's death.

title: Rehoboam'S Great Mistake
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
After Solomon passed away, his son Rehoboam took over the throne. As soon as Jeroboam, who was still in Egypt, heard about Solomon's death, he rushed back to his hometown, Zeredah in Mount Ephraim.

Rehoboam headed to Shechem, where all the Israelites had gathered to make him their king. They approached him and said, "Your father put a heavy burden on us. Please lighten the tough work and heavy yoke he imposed, and we’ll serve you willingly." He replied, "Give me three days, and then come back to me." So the people went home.

Rehoboam then sought advice from the old men who had served under his father Solomon. He asked, "What do you think I should say to these people?" They replied, "If you’re kind to them and respond favorably, they will be your loyal servants forever."

However, Rehoboam dismissed their advice and consulted the young men he had grown up with, who had also served him. He asked them, "What should I tell these people who say, 'Make your father’s yoke lighter'?" The young men advised him, "Tell them this: 'My little finger is thicker than my father's waist! My father made your yoke heavy, but I will make it even heavier; he punished you with whips, but I will punish you with scorpions.'"

So, when the people returned on the third day as he had told them, he responded harshly, ignoring the advice of the elders. He told them, "My father made your workload heavy, but I will make it heavier; my father used whips, but I will use scorpions." Rehoboam had no regard for the people's request.

When all of Israel realized he wasn’t listening to their demands, they replied, "What do we have in common with David? We have no share with the son of Jesse! To your tents, Israel! Look after your own house, David!"

And so the Israelites went back to their homes.

Rehoboam then sent Adoniram, who was in charge of forced labor, to the people. But when the Israelites stoned him to death, Rehoboam quickly jumped into his chariot and fled to Jerusalem. From then on, Israel refused to follow the house of David.

Once all of Israel learned that Jeroboam had returned, they called him to the assembly and made him king over all Israel. Only the tribe of Judah stayed loyal to the house of David.

title: Elijah And The Widow'S Son
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
After Asa had ruled Judah for thirty-one years, Omri took over as the ruler of Israel, a position he held for twelve years. He bought the hill of Samaria from Shemer for two talents of silver and built a city on that hill, naming it Samaria after Shemer, the previous owner.

When Omri died, his son Ahab stepped in as ruler. However, Ahab angered Jehovah more than any of the kings before him. He married Jezebel, the daughter of Ethbaal, the king of the Sidonians, and began to worship the Phoenician god Baal. Ahab even built an altar for Baal in the temple he erected in Samaria.

Then Elijah from Tishbe in Gilead confronted Ahab, saying, "As surely as Jehovah, the God of Israel, lives, the God I serve, there will be no dew or rain for years unless I announce it."

Next, Jehovah spoke to Elijah: "Leave this place and hide near the Brook Cherith, east of the Jordan. You’ll drink from the brook, and I have commanded the ravens to feed you there."

Elijah obeyed Jehovah and stayed by the Brook Cherith. The ravens brought him bread in the mornings and meat in the evenings, while he drank from the brook. But eventually, the brook dried up because there was no rain in the land.

Then Jehovah instructed him, "Get up and go to Zarephath, which belongs to Sidon, and live there. I've instructed a widow to take care of you." So Elijah went to Zarephath.

title: Elijah And The Widow'S Son
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
When he arrived at the city gate, he saw a widow gathering sticks. He called to her, "Please bring me a little water in a jar so I can drink." As she went to get it, he added, "Also, bring me a piece of bread." She answered, "As surely as Jehovah your God lives, I don’t have any baked bread, just a handful of flour in a jar and a little oil in a jug. I’m gathering a few sticks to prepare a meal for my son and me so we can eat it and then die." Elijah replied, "Don't be afraid. Go ahead and do what you've said, but first make a small loaf for me and bring it to me. Then make something for yourself and your son. For Jehovah, the God of Israel, says: 'The jar of flour won’t run out, and the jug of oil won’t go dry until Jehovah sends rain on the earth.'"

She went along with Elijah's instructions, and she, her son, and Elijah all had enough food. From that day on, the jar of flour never ran out, and the jug of oil didn’t dry up, just as Jehovah had promised through Elijah.

Later on, the woman's son fell seriously ill and stopped breathing. Upset, she said to Elijah, "What do you want from me, man of God? Did you come here to remind me of my sins and to take my son’s life?" Elijah replied, "Give me your son." He took the boy from her arms, brought him upstairs, and laid him on his own bed. Then he prayed fervently to Jehovah, saying, "O Jehovah, my God, have You brought tragedy on this widow, with whom I'm staying, by taking her son?" He stretched himself over the child three times, praying, "O Jehovah, my God, I urge You, let this child's life return to him."

Jehovah heard Elijah’s prayer, and the child's life returned. The boy sat up, and Elijah took him downstairs, handing him back to his mother. He said, "Look, your son is alive!" The woman responded, "Now I know you are a man of God, and that the word of Jehovah that you speak is true."

title: The Prophet Of Fire
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
During the third year of the famine, Jehovah spoke to Elijah: "Go present yourself to Ahab. I'm going to send rain on the earth." So Elijah went to meet Ahab.

The famine hit Samaria hard, prompting Ahab to call for Obadiah, the palace overseer. Obadiah was very faithful to Jehovah; when Jezebel attempted to kill the prophets, he hid a hundred of them in a cave, providing them with food and water. Ahab said to Obadiah, "Let’s check the countryside for springs and brooks. Hopefully, we can find some grass to save the horses and mules; we can’t afford to lose them all." They split up the territory, with Ahab heading one way and Obadiah going the other.

As Obadiah was on his way, he suddenly encountered Elijah. Recognizing him, Obadiah fell to the ground and exclaimed, "Is that you, my lord Elijah?" Elijah replied, "Yes, go tell your master: 'Elijah is here.'" Obadiah responded, "What sin have I committed that you would put your servant in danger with Ahab? As surely as Jehovah your God lives, there isn’t a nation or kingdom where my lord hasn’t sent search parties looking for you. When they reported back that you weren’t found, Ahab made everyone take an oath that you weren’t here. And now you’re telling me to go back and say, 'Elijah is here!'? The moment I leave, the spirit of Jehovah might carry you away to some unknown location, and when I tell Ahab and he can’t find you, he will kill me, even though I’ve been loyal to Jehovah since I was young! Haven’t you heard what I did when Jezebel killed the prophets of Jehovah? I hid a hundred of them in caves, fifty at a time, and kept them fed with bread and water." Elijah replied, "As surely as Jehovah of Hosts lives, before whom I stand, I will present myself to Ahab today."

title: The Prophet Of Fire
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Obadiah went to Ahab and informed him, and Ahab went to meet Elijah. Upon seeing him, Ahab said, "Is that you, the one who’s caused trouble for Israel?" Elijah responded, "I didn’t cause trouble for Israel; you and your family did, because you failed to follow Jehovah’s commands and chased after the gods of the Phoenicians. Now, gather all the Israelites and the four hundred and fifty prophets of Baal who eat at Jezebel’s table, and bring them to Mount Carmel."

Ahab summoned all the Israelites and assembled the prophets at Mount Carmel. Elijah approached the people and questioned, "How long will you waver between worshipping Jehovah and Baal? If Jehovah is the true God, follow Him, but if Baal is, then follow him." The people remained silent. Elijah then declared, "I am the only prophet of Jehovah left, but there are four hundred and fifty prophets of Baal. Let’s each take two oxen; you choose one for yourselves and prepare it, but don’t set fire to it. I’ll prepare the other ox and lay it on the wood, and I won’t light a fire either. You call on your god, and I’ll call on Jehovah. The god who answers with fire is the true God." The people agreed, saying, "That sounds fair."

Elijah instructed the prophets of Baal, "Pick one of the oxen and prepare it first, since there are so many of you. Call on your god, but don’t light a fire." They took the ox he gave them, prepared it, and called out to Baal from morning until noon, begging, "O Baal, hear us!" But there was no response, and they even danced around the altar they had built.

At noon, Elijah started mocking them, saying, "Shout louder! He’s a god, after all. Maybe he’s deep in thought, or on a journey, or maybe he’s asleep and needs to be woken up!" They shouted even louder, cutting themselves with swords and lances according to their custom, until the blood flowed. They continued this frenzy until it was time for the evening sacrifice, but still, there was no voice, no answer, and no one paid attention.

title: The Prophet Of Fire
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Then Elijah said to the people, "Come closer to me." They all approached, and he repaired the altar of Jehovah that had been torn down. He made a trench around the altar big enough to hold about two bushels of seed. Once the wood was arranged, he cut the ox into pieces and laid it on the wood. "Now fill four jars with water and pour it over the offering and the wood," he instructed. Then he said, "Do it again," and they did it a second time. He instructed, "Do it again a third time," and they did so, until water ran around the altar and filled the trench.

When it was time for the evening sacrifice, Elijah stepped forward and prayed, "O Jehovah, God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, let it be known today that you are God in Israel, and that I am your servant, and I’ve done all these things at your command. Answer me, O Jehovah, answer me, so these people will know that you, O Jehovah, are God, and that you are turning their hearts back again."

Then the fire of Jehovah fell from heaven and consumed the burnt offering, the wood, the stones, and even the dust, lapping up the water in the trench. When the people saw this, they fell to the ground and cried, "Jehovah is God! Jehovah is God!" Elijah then ordered, "Seize the prophets of Baal; don’t let any of them escape!" They seized them and took them to the Brook Kishon, where they put them to death.

Elijah turned to Ahab and said, "Go eat and drink, because the rain is coming." Ahab went off to eat and drink. Meanwhile, Elijah climbed to the top of Mount Carmel, bent down to the ground, and put his face between his knees. He told his servant, "Go look toward the sea." The servant went up and looked but said, "There’s nothing there." Elijah told him, "Go back and check again," seven times. Finally, the seventh time, the servant said, "I see a small cloud, like a man’s hand, coming up from the sea." Elijah told him, "Go tell Ahab, 'Prepare your chariot and head down, so the rain doesn't stop you.'" Soon, dark clouds gathered, the wind picked up, and the rain started pouring. As Ahab drove toward Jezreel, Elijah was given supernatural strength, so he tucked his cloak into his belt and outran Ahab to the entrance of Jezreel.

title: God'S Low Whisper
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
When Ahab reported to Jezebel that Elijah had killed the prophets, she sent a message to him saying, "I swear, Elijah, that if I don't make your life just like one of those prophets by this time tomorrow, may the gods punish me severely!"

Fear gripped him, and he fled to save his life. He arrived in Beersheba, which was part of Judah, and left his servant there. Then he traveled a whole day into the wilderness, sat under a tree in the desert, and wished he could die. "I've had enough, Jehovah. Just take my life, because I'm no better than my ancestors," he said.

He lay down and fell asleep under that desert tree. But then an angel touched him and said, "Get up and eat!" When he looked around, he found a loaf of bread baked on hot stones and a jar of water right by his head. So he ate, drank, and lay down again. The angel came back a second time, touched him, and urged, "Get up and eat, or the journey will be too much for you." So he got up, ate and drank, and then, fueled by that food, traveled for forty days and forty nights to Horeb, the mountain of God.

Then Jehovah passed by, and a mighty wind ripped through the mountain, shattering rocks before Him, but Jehovah wasn't in the wind. After the wind came an earthquake, but Jehovah wasn't in the earthquake. Next came a fire, but Jehovah wasn't in the fire either. After the fire, there was a gentle whisper. When Elijah heard it, he wrapped his face with his cloak and stepped out to the entrance of the cave. He heard a voice asking, "What are you doing here, Elijah?" He answered, "I've been very devoted to Jehovah, God of the armies, but the Israelites have abandoned you, torn down your altars, and killed your prophets. I'm the only one left, and they're trying to take my life too."

Jehovah replied, "On your way back, head to the wilderness of Damascus. When you get there, anoint Hazael as king over Aram, Jehu, son of Nimshi, as king over Israel, and Elisha, son of Shaphat, to take over your role as prophet. Anyone who escapes Hazael's sword, Jehu will kill; those who escape Jehu's sword, Elisha will kill. But I'll save seven thousand in Israel—everyone who hasn't bowed to Baal or kissed his image."

After that, Elijah found Elisha, son of Shaphat, plowing with twelve pairs of oxen. When Elijah approached him and threw his cloak over him, Elisha left the oxen and ran after Elijah, saying, "Let me say goodbye to my parents, then I’ll follow you." Elijah replied, "Go back; what have I done to you?" Elisha returned, took one pair of oxen, sacrificed them, and used the wooden plowing equipment to cook the meat, sharing it with the people. Then he got up and followed Elijah, serving him from that day on.

title: Ahab The Thief
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Naboth, a man from Jezreel, had a vineyard right next to King Ahab's palace in Jezreel. One day, Ahab approached Naboth and said, "Sell me your vineyard so I can turn it into a vegetable garden since it's close to my palace. I can offer you a better vineyard in exchange, or if you prefer, I'll pay you its full value in cash." But Naboth replied, "God forbid that I should give you what has been passed down to me from my ancestors!"

Feeling dejected and upset about Naboth's refusal, Ahab returned home in a foul mood. He lay down on his bed, covered his face, and refused to eat.

His wife, Jezebel, came in and asked, "Why are you so down that you won’t eat?" He answered, "I asked Naboth to sell me his vineyard for cash, or swap it for another, but he said no." Jezebel replied, "Aren't you the king of Israel? Get up, eat, and cheer up. I’ll make sure you get Naboth’s vineyard."

Jezebel wrote letters in Ahab's name, sealed them with his seal, and sent them to the leaders and officials of Naboth's town. In her letters, she instructed, "Announce a fast and put Naboth in front of the people. Create a situation where two dishonest men accuse him of cursing God and the king. Then, take him out and stone him to death."

The leaders of Naboth's town followed Jezebel's orders. They declared a fast and presented Naboth to the crowd. Two dishonest men then stood up and said, "Naboth cursed both God and the king." So, they took him outside the city and stoned him to death. Afterward, they reported to Jezebel, "Naboth has been stoned to death."

As soon as Jezebel heard about Naboth’s death, she told Ahab, "Get up and take possession of Naboth’s vineyard that he refused to sell you; he’s dead now." When Ahab found out Naboth was dead, he went to take over the vineyard. But God spoke to Elijah the Tishbite, saying, "Go meet Ahab, the king of Israel, who is in Naboth’s vineyard to take it. Tell him this message from God: 'Have you murdered and then taken his vineyard? Just like the dogs licked up Naboth's blood, they will lick up your blood too.'" When Ahab saw Elijah, he exclaimed, "So, you’ve found me, my enemy?" Elijah replied, "I have found you. And God has announced: 'Dogs will eat Jezebel within the borders of Jezreel.'" Hearing this, Ahab tore his clothes, put on sackcloth, and refused to eat.

title: Micaiah'S Courage In Telling The Truth
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
For three years, there was peace between Aram and Israel. But in the third year, when Jehoshaphat, the king of Judah, visited Ahab, the king of Israel, Ahab turned to his advisors and said, "Don’t you know that Ramoth in Gilead is ours? Yet here we are, sitting idly instead of taking it from the king of Aram!" Then he asked Jehoshaphat, "Will you join me in attacking Ramoth in Gilead?" Jehoshaphat replied, "I’m with you; my people are your people, and my horses are your horses."

So Jehoshaphat said to Ahab, "Let’s ask what the Lord has to say about this." Ahab then gathered about four hundred prophets and asked them, "Should I go to fight against Ramoth in Gilead or not?" They all answered, "Go up; the Lord will hand it over to you!" But Jehoshaphat questioned, "Is there no other prophet of the Lord we can consult?" Ahab replied, "There is one more, Micaiah, son of Imlah, but I can’t stand him because he never says anything good about me, only bad things." Jehoshaphat said, "Don’t say that!"

Then Ahab called a servant and commanded, "Quickly bring Micaiah, son of Imlah." The messenger sent to fetch Micaiah told him, "All the prophets are promising success to the king of Israel. You should go along with them and predict success too." But Micaiah replied, "As surely as the Lord lives, I will only speak what He tells me."

When Micaiah finally arrived, Ahab asked him, "Micaiah, should we go to Ramoth in Gilead to fight, or not?" Micaiah responded, "Go ahead and conquer! The Lord will deliver it into your hands!" Ahab said, "How many times do I need to tell you to tell me nothing but the truth in the name of the Lord?" Micaiah replied, "I saw all the Israelites scattered on the hills, like sheep without a shepherd. The Lord said, 'These people have no leader; let each one go home in peace!''

title: Micaiah'S Courage In Telling The Truth
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Ahab then turned to Jehoshaphat and said, "Didn’t I tell you he only prophesies bad news for me?" Micaiah continued, "Listen to the Lord’s message: I saw the Lord sitting on His throne with all the heavenly hosts standing around Him. The Lord asked, 'Who will deceive Ahab so he will go up and fall at Ramoth in Gilead?' One spirit suggested one thing and another spirit suggested something else, until a spirit stepped forward and said, 'I will deceive him.' The Lord then asked, 'How will you do that?' and the spirit replied, 'I will be a lying spirit in the mouths of all his prophets.' The Lord said, 'You will succeed in deceiving him; go out and do that.' So, the Lord has put a lying spirit in the mouths of all your prophets because He has decided to bring this evil upon you.'

At that point, Zedekiah approached Micaiah and slapped him on the cheek, saying, "How did the Spirit of the Lord leave me to speak to you?" Micaiah answered, "You’ll find out on the day you go from one hiding place to another." Then Ahab ordered, "Take Micaiah back to Amon, the governor of the city, and to Joash, the king’s son, and tell them, 'This is the king’s command: Put this man in prison and give him only a small ration of bread and water until I return victorious.'" Micaiah replied, "If you return victorious, then the Lord hasn’t spoken through me."

So Ahab, the king of Israel, and Jehoshaphat, the king of Judah, marched up to Ramoth in Gilead. Ahab said to Jehoshaphat, "I’m going to disguise myself in battle, but you wear your royal robes."

As the battle began, a random archer shot an arrow that struck Ahab between the sections of his armor. Ahab said to his charioteer, "Turn around and take me out of the battle, for I’m wounded." But the fighting grew fierce, and Ahab remained propped up in his chariot, visible to the Arameans, with blood draining from his wound into the chariot. By evening, he died.

At sunset, word spread through the army: "Each of you go back to your town and land, for the king is dead!" They returned to Samaria and buried Ahab there. While washing his chariot by the pool of Samaria, the dogs came and licked up his blood, just as the Lord had predicted.

title: The Mantle Of Elijah
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
As Jehovah swept Elijah up to heaven in a whirlwind, he was on his way with Elisha from Gilgal. Elijah told Elisha, "Stay here, because Jehovah has sent me to Bethel." But Elisha replied, "As surely as Jehovah lives and as you live, I won’t leave you." So, they both headed down to Bethel.

When they reached Bethel, the local prophets approached Elisha and said, "Do you know that today Jehovah is taking your master away from you?" Elisha answered, "Yes, I know; don’t talk about it anymore." Then Elijah said to him, "Stay here, because Jehovah has sent me to Jericho." Elisha replied, "As surely as Jehovah lives and as you live, I won’t leave you." So, they both set off to Jericho.

As they continued on to Jericho, the prophets there came up to Elisha and said, "Do you realize that today Jehovah will take your master from you?" He said, "Yes, I know; please stop mentioning it." Elijah then said, "Stay here, because Jehovah has sent me to the Jordan." Elisha insisted, "As surely as Jehovah lives and as you live, I won’t leave you." So, they both went on together.

Fifty prophets stood at a distance, watching while Elijah and Elisha stood by the Jordan. Elijah took off his mantle, rolled it up, and struck the waters with it, parting the river so they could walk across on dry ground. Once they crossed, Elijah turned to Elisha and asked, "What do you want me to do for you before I’m taken away?" Elisha replied, "I want a double portion of your spirit." Elijah said, "You’ve asked for something quite challenging; if you see me when I’m taken from you, it will be yours; if not, it won’t be."

As they were walking and talking, a fiery chariot with horses of fire suddenly appeared and separated them. Elijah was taken up to heaven in a whirlwind. Elisha cried out, "My father, my father! The chariots and horsemen of Israel!" After that, he no longer saw Elijah, and in grief, he tore his own robes in two. Then he picked up the mantle that had fallen from Elijah.

title: A Slave Girl Who Helped Her Master
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Naaman was the commander of the king of Aram's army, a man cherished by his master and held in high esteem because Jehovah had granted victory to Aram through him. He was skilled at his job, but he had leprosy.

The Arameans had raided the land of Israel and captured a young girl who became a servant to Naaman's wife. She told her mistress, "If only my master could see the prophet in Samaria! He would cure him of his leprosy." Naaman told the king what the girl from Israel had said, and the king of Aram replied, "Go now, and I will send a letter to the king of Israel."

Naaman set off with a thousand pounds of silver, six thousand gold pieces, and ten sets of fine clothes. He also brought a letter to the king of Israel that read, "This letter is to let you know that I’ve sent my servant Naaman to you so that you can heal him of his leprosy." When the king of Israel read the letter, he tore his clothes in distress and exclaimed, "Am I a god who can give life and take it away? Why is this king sending a man to me to cure him of his leprosy? Clearly, he’s trying to provoke a conflict!"

When Elisha, the man of God, heard that the king of Israel was tearing his clothes, he sent a message: "Why are you upset? Have Naaman come to me—he will learn that there is a prophet in Israel!" So Naaman arrived with his horses and chariots and stood at Elisha's door. Elisha sent a messenger to tell him, "Go wash seven times in the Jordan River, and your skin will be restored and made clean."

title: A Slave Girl Who Helped Her Master
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
But Naaman stormed off in anger, saying, "I expected him to come out and call on the name of Jehovah, wave his hand over the spot, and cure the leper. Aren't the rivers of Amana and Pharpar in Damascus better than any water in Israel? Couldn’t I wash in them and be clean?" So he turned away, furious. However, his servants approached him and said, "If the prophet had asked you to do something big, wouldn't you have done it? So why not trust him when he says, 'Wash and be clean'?" After some thought, Naaman went down and dipped himself seven times in the Jordan as the man of God had instructed, and his skin returned to that of a young child—he was healed of his leprosy.

Naaman then returned with all his attendants to the man of God. When he reached him, he declared, "Now I know there is no god anywhere but in Israel. Please accept a gift from your servant." But Elisha replied, "As surely as Jehovah lives, whom I serve, I won’t accept anything." Despite Naaman urging him to take it, he refused. Naaman then said, "If you won’t accept anything, at least let me take enough soil from here for two mules; from now on, I will offer burnt offerings and sacrifices to no one but Jehovah." Elisha replied, "Go, and may you find peace on your journey."

However, after Naaman left, Gehazi, Elisha’s servant, thought to himself, "My master let this Aramean go without taking anything! I swear by Jehovah, I’ll chase after him and get something." So Gehazi ran after Naaman, and when Naaman saw someone running after him, he got down from his chariot to greet him, asking, "Is everything okay?" Gehazi replied, "All is well. My master sent me to say that two young men from the prophets are here, and he needs a hundred pounds of silver and two suits of clothes for them." Naaman insisted, "Take twice that amount of silver." He pressed Gehazi until he accepted two hundred pounds of silver in two bags and two suits of clothes, which his servants carried for him. Once Gehazi was out of sight on the hill, he took the silver and clothes from them and hid them in his house before sending the men away.

Gehazi then approached Elisha, but when he stood before his master, Elisha asked, "Where have you been, Gehazi?" He replied, "Your servant hasn’t gone anywhere." But Elisha said, "Wasn’t I with you in spirit when the man got down from his chariot to meet you? You have received money and clothing, olive groves, vineyards, sheep, oxen, and servants, but now the leprosy of Naaman will cling to you and your descendants forever." As a result, Gehazi left Elisha, his skin white as snow with leprosy.

title: Elisha'S Way Of Treating Enemies
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Once, while the king of Aram was at war with Israel, he told his officers, "We’ll hide in a certain spot and catch them off guard." But Elisha, the man of God, warned the king of Israel, saying, "Be careful not to go near that place because the Arameans are hiding there." So, the king of Israel sent troops to the location Elisha had mentioned. He received multiple warnings, which helped him stay alert.

The king of Aram was really frustrated by this, so he summoned his officers and asked, "Can anyone tell me who has betrayed us to the king of Israel?" One of the officers answered, "No one, my lord. It's Elisha, the prophet in Israel. He tells the king of Israel everything you say, even your conversations in your own bedroom." The king replied, "Go find out where he is so I can send men to capture him." They informed the king, "Elisha is in Dothan right now."

So, the king sent horses, chariots, and a large army there. They arrived at night and surrounded the city. When the man of God got up early the next morning and stepped outside, there was an army with horses and chariots all around the city. His servant said to him, "Oh no, what are we going to do?" Elisha replied, "Don’t be afraid! The ones with us are more than the ones against us." Then he prayed, "Lord, open his eyes so he can see." The Lord opened the eyes of the young man, and he saw that the hills around Elisha were filled with horses and chariots of fire.

When the Arameans came closer, Elisha prayed to the Lord, saying, "Make these people blind." So the Lord made them blind, just as Elisha requested. Then Elisha said to them, "You’ve taken a wrong turn; this isn’t the right city. Follow me, and I’ll lead you to the man you’re looking for!" And he guided them to Samaria.

title: Elisha'S Way Of Treating Enemies
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Once they arrived in Samaria, Elisha prayed, "Lord, open their eyes so they can see." The Lord opened their eyes, and they realized they were in Samaria. When the king of Israel saw them, he asked Elisha, "Should I kill them, my father?" Elisha replied, "No, don’t kill them. Would you kill those you haven’t captured with your sword or bow? Just set food and water in front of them so they can eat and drink and then return to their master." So he prepared a big feast for them; after they had eaten and drunk, he sent them back to their master. After that, the raiding parties from Aram stopped invading the land of Israel.

Later on, Benhadad, the king of Aram, gathered his whole army and laid siege to Samaria. The famine in Samaria was so severe during the siege that a donkey's head was sold for eighty pieces of silver.

One day, as the king of Israel was walking along the wall, a woman cried out to him, "Help me, my lord!" He replied, "If the Lord doesn’t help you, how can I help you? With grain from the threshing floor or wine from the press?" He then asked her, "What’s troubling you?" She responded, "This woman said to me, 'Give me your son so we can eat him today, and we’ll eat my son tomorrow!' So we cooked my son and ate him, but when I asked her the next day to give me her son, she had hidden him."

When the king heard the woman’s words, he tore his clothes. As he walked along the wall, the people looked and saw that he was wearing sackcloth underneath.

Meanwhile, Elisha was sitting in his house with the elders. While he was speaking with them, the king of Israel came down and said, "This disaster is from the Lord! Why should I keep hoping in the Lord any longer?" Elisha replied, "Listen to the word of the Lord: ‘Tomorrow at this time, a seah of the finest flour will sell for a shekel and two seahs of barley for a shekel at the gate of Samaria.’" Then the officer assisting the king said, "Even if the Lord opened the floodgates of heaven, could this happen?" Elisha replied, "You will see it with your own eyes, but you won’t eat any of it."

title: Elisha'S Way Of Treating Enemies
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
There were four lepers sitting at the entrance of the city gate, and they said to each other, "Why sit here until we die? If we enter the city, we’ll die there because of the famine, but if we stay here, we’ll die too. So, let’s go over to the army of the Arameans. If they spare our lives, we’ll live; if they kill us, we’ll die."

That evening, they set out to go to the Aramean camp. When they reached the edge of the camp, no one was there, for the Lord had caused the Aramean army to hear the sound of chariots and horses, as if a great army was approaching. They said to each other, "The king of Israel has hired the kings of the Hittites and Egyptians to attack us!" So they got up and fled in the twilight, leaving their tents, horses, and donkeys behind. The lepers reached the camp and went into a tent where they ate and drank. They also took silver, gold, and clothes and hid them. They then went into another tent and did the same.

Then they said to one another, "We’re not doing right. Today is a day of good news! If we wait until morning, we're going to get punished. Let’s go tell the people in the palace." They called out to the watchmen at the city gate, saying, "We went to the Aramean camp, and there’s no one there! No sound of people! The horses and donkeys are still tied, and the tents are as they were."

The watchmen shouted the news to those in the palace. The king got up during the night and told his servants, "I’ll explain what the Arameans have done: they know we’re hungry, so they’ve gone out of the camp to hide in the fields, thinking, ‘When they come out of the city, we’ll capture them alive and get into the city.’"

But one of his servants suggested, "Let’s take a couple of the horses left here. If they die, they’ll be like most of the Israelites who are dying! Let’s send them to check it out." So they took two men on horseback, and the king sent them after the army of the Arameans with the instruction, "Go see what’s happening." They followed the trail to the Jordan River, where they found clothes and weapons the Arameans had discarded in their haste. The messengers returned and reported back to the king.

Then the people rushed out and looted the camp of the Arameans. Just as the Lord had said, a seah of the finest flour was sold for a shekel, and two seahs of barley for a shekel.

title: The End Of Ahab'S Selfish Family
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
The prophet Elisha called one of his followers and said, "Tighten your belt, grab this flask of oil, and head to Ramoth in Gilead. When you get there, look for Jehu, the son of Jehoshaphat. Take him into an inner room, away from everyone else. Pour the oil on his head and say, 'God says, I have anointed you to be king over Israel.' Then, open the door and run away without wasting any time."

The young man went to Ramoth in Gilead. When he arrived, he found the army officers gathered together. He approached one and said, "Commander, I have a message for you." Jehu asked, "For which of us?" He replied, "For you, Commander." Jehu stood up and went into the house, where the young man poured oil on his head and said, "The God of Israel says, 'I have anointed you to be the ruler over God's people, Israel!'" Then he quickly opened the door and ran away.

When Jehu came out to the servants of his master, they asked, "Is everything alright? Why did that crazy guy visit you?" He replied, "You know the man and his message." But they insisted, "You have to tell us!" So Jehu explained, "He told me, 'God says, I have anointed you to be king over Israel.'" Immediately, the servants took their garments, laid them on the stairs, blew the horn, and shouted, "Jehu is the king of Israel!" Jehu then plotted against Joram.

Joram, along with all the Israelites, had been defending Ramoth in Gilead against Hazael, the king of Aram. However, Joram had gone back to Jezreel to recover from injuries he had received in battle with Hazael. Jehu proclaimed, "If you want this, don't let anyone escape to deliver news to Jezreel." He then jumped into his chariot and rode toward Jezreel.

title: The End Of Ahab'S Selfish Family
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
As the watchman stood on the tower in Jezreel, he saw a cloud of dust rising from Jehu's approach and said, "I see a cloud of dust coming." Joram ordered, "Send a horseman to meet him and ask if he comes in peace." A horseman went out and asked, "The ruler of Israel wants to know, 'Do you come in peace?'" Jehu replied, "What does peace have to do with you? Just turn around and follow me." The watchman reported, "The messenger went to them but hasn’t returned." So Joram sent a second horseman who asked the same question. Jehu responded again, "What does peace have to do with you? Turn and follow me." The watchman said, "He didn’t return either, but the driving is definitely Jehu’s. He drives like a man on a mission."

Joram then ordered, "Get my chariot ready." Once it was prepared, he set out to meet Jehu and found him in the field of Naboth the Jezreelite. When Joram saw Jehu, he asked, "Jehu, do you come in peace?" Jehu answered, but Joram decided to flee. Jehu quickly drew his bow, shooting Joram between the shoulders, and the arrow pierced his heart. Joram fell down in his chariot.

Jehu then commanded his charioteer, Bidkar, "Lift him up and throw him into the field of Naboth the Jezreelite. I remember when we rode together after Ahab, his father, and God proclaimed this judgment: 'I saw yesterday the blood of Naboth and his sons, and I will punish you on the same piece of land.' So, throw him onto this land, just as God said."

When Jehu arrived at Jezreel, Jezebel heard about it. She colored her eyes, styled her hair, and looked out the window. As Jehu entered the gate, she shouted, "Is all well, you traitor, murderer of your master?" Jehu looked up at the window and called out, "Who is on my side?" A few servants looked down, and he commanded, "Throw her down!" They did, and the horses trampled her. After Jehu went inside and had something to eat and drink, he ordered, "Look after this woman and bury her, for she is a king's daughter." But when they went to bury her, they found only her skull, feet, and hands. When they reported back to Jehu, he said, "This is what God declared through his servant Elijah: 'On the land at Jezreel, dogs will eat Jezebel's flesh, and her body will be like refuse on the ground, so nobody can say, This is Jezebel.'"

title: The Boy Joash On The Throne Of Judah
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
After Joram had been king of Israel for twelve years, Ahaziah, the son of Jehoram, took over Judah. He went to Jezreel to visit Joram, who was sick. When Jehu attacked Joram, Ahaziah saw what happened and fled toward Beth-gannim. Jehu pursued him, ordering, "Shoot him down in the chariot!" They managed to strike him as he was heading toward Gur, near Ibleam, but he managed to escape to Megiddo, where he ultimately died.

When Athaliah, Ahaziah's mother, found out her son was dead, she immediately rose up and killed all of Ahaziah's children except for Joash. Joash's aunt, Jehosheba, secretly took him and hid him with his nurse in a safe place. She protected him from Athaliah, and for six years, Joash stayed hidden in the temple of Jehovah while Athaliah ruled the land.

In the seventh year, Jehoiada the priest gathered the officers of the royal guard and brought them to the temple of Jehovah. After making them promise an oath, he revealed to them Ahaziah's son and gave them instructions, saying, "Here's the plan: One-third of you who work on the Sabbath to guard the palace will watch over it. The other two groups, including everyone who goes out to guard the temple of Jehovah, will surround Joash, armed and ready. Any intruder should be put to death. You must protect Joash at all times as he comes and goes."

The officers followed Jehoiada's orders. He brought out Ahaziah's son and placed the crown and royal insignia on him, making him the king of Judah. They anointed him with oil and cheered, shouting, "Long live the king!"

When Athaliah heard the people cheering, she rushed to the temple of Jehovah. Upon seeing Joash standing by the pillar, as tradition dictated, along with the officers and trumpeters, and the entire crowd celebrating, she ripped her clothes and screamed, "Treason! Treason!" But Jehoiada commanded the officers, "Take her out and kill anyone who tries to help her with the sword," insisting, "She shouldn’t be put to death in the temple of Jehovah." They captured her, and she was killed as she tried to flee through the palace's horse entrance.

Jehoiada made a serious covenant between Jehovah, the new ruler, and the people, committing them to be Jehovah's followers. This agreement was also established between the king and the people, and Joash went on to reign for forty years in Jerusalem.

title: A Young Man Who Said, "Send Me"
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Uzziah, the grandson of Joash, was just sixteen when he began to rule over Judah. He ruled for fifty-two years in Jerusalem.

He battled the Philistines, tearing down the walls of Gath, Jabneh, and Ashdod. He built cities near Ashdod and among the Philistines. God supported him in his fight against the Philistines, and the Ammonites also paid tribute to Uzziah as he grew stronger.

Uzziah constructed towers in Jerusalem at the Corner Gate, the Valley Gate, and at the corners of the wall, reinforcing them. He also built towers in the wilderness and dug numerous cisterns, as he had many herds in the lowlands, farmers in the plains, and vine-dressers in the mountains and fertile fields, since he had a passion for cultivating the land. However, he struggled with leprosy until his death. He lived in his own home while his son Jotham took charge of the royal household and governed the people. Uzziah passed away and was buried with his ancestors in the City of David, and his son Jotham succeeded him.

Isaiah mentioned that it was the year Uzziah, the king of Judah, died when he saw the Lord sitting on a high and majestic throne, with the train of His robe filling the temple. Guardian angels hovered above Him, each with six wings: one pair covering their faces, another pair covering their feet, and a third pair for flying. They called out to one another:

"Holy, holy, holy, is the Lord of hosts, the entire earth is filled with His glory."

The foundations of the temple trembled at the sound of their voices, and the place was filled with smoke. Then I exclaimed, "Oh no! I’m doomed! I’m a man of unclean lips, living among people with unclean lips, and my eyes have seen the King, the Lord of hosts!" At that moment, one of the guardian angels flew to me with a hot coal he had taken from the altar. He touched my mouth with it and said, "Look, this has touched your lips; your guilt is removed, and your sin is forgiven."

Then I heard the voice of the Lord asking:

"Whom shall I send, and who will go for us?"

title: Jeremiah'S Call To Do A Hard Task
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
This was the message I received from God: "Before you were born, I knew you and prepared you for your mission. I've chosen you to be a prophet for the nations." But I, Jeremiah, replied, "Oh Lord! I’m not great at speaking in public; I’m just a kid." And God said to me, "Don’t say, 'I’m just a kid,' because you will go wherever I send you, and you’ll say whatever I tell you. Don’t be afraid of them; I’m right here with you to keep you safe."

Then God reached out and touched my mouth, saying, "Look, I’m putting my words in your mouth. Today, I’m appointing you over nations and kingdoms, giving you power to tear down and destroy, as well as to rebuild and plant."

Again, the message came from God: "What do you see?" I responded, "I see a big pot boiling, and it's tilting from the north." Then God explained, "From the north, trouble is coming for everyone in the land. I’m about to summon all the kingdoms of the north; they will each set up their throne at the gates of Jerusalem and surround its walls and the cities of Judah. I will judge Jerusalem and these cities for their wickedness, as they have betrayed me by offering sacrifices to other gods and worshipping what their own hands have made."

"So get ready and speak everything I command you. Don’t be afraid of them, because I will make you like a sturdy city today, like a bronze wall standing against the rulers of Judah, its leaders, its priests, and the common people. Even though they will fight against you, they won’t succeed, because I am with you to protect you."

title: The Young Josiah And The Book Of The Law
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Josiah was just eight years old when he started his reign, and he ruled for thirty-one years in Jerusalem. During the eighteenth year of his leadership, he sent Shaphan, the scribe, to the temple of God with a directive: "Go to Hilkiah, the chief priest, and make sure that when he collects the money brought into the temple and what the doorkeepers have gathered from the people, it's given to the workers responsible for the temple. They should then provide it to the carpenters, builders, and masons to fix up the temple and buy timber and cut stone for its restoration." They didn’t have to account for the money given to them because they were honest.

Then Hilkiah, the chief priest, told Shaphan, the scribe, "I have found the Book of the Law in the temple of God." Hilkiah handed the book to Shaphan, who read it. Shaphan then went to Josiah and reported, "Your servants have collected the money found in the temple and given it to the workers overseeing the temple of God." Shaphan also informed Josiah, "Hilkiah, the priest, has given me a book," and he read it to the king.

As soon as Josiah heard the words from the Book of the Law, he tore his clothes in distress. Then he commanded Hilkiah the priest, Ahikam, son of Shaphan, Achbor, son of Micaiah, Shaphan the scribe, and Asaiah, his servant, "Go and ask the Lord for me, for the people, and for all Judah about what’s written in this book that’s been found; God must be really upset with us because our ancestors didn’t listen to what’s written here or follow the commands we've been given.”

So Hilkiah, Ahikam, and Achbor went to Huldah, the prophetess, who lived in Jerusalem, and spoke with her. She told them, "This is the message from the Lord God of Israel: 'Tell the man who sent you to me, the Lord says, I’m about to bring trouble upon this place and its people, just as is written in the book that the king of Judah has read. But you should tell him who sent you to ask the Lord, the God of Israel, states: Because you listened and humbled yourself before Me and wept before Me, I have heard you.'" They returned to Josiah with this message.

title: The Young Josiah And The Book Of The Law
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
At Josiah's command, they gathered all the leaders of Judah and Jerusalem. Josiah went up to the temple of God, and with him were all the men of Judah, everyone from Jerusalem, the priests, the prophets, and even the children. He read to them all the words of the Book of the Covenant found in the temple of God. Standing by the pillar, Josiah made a solemn promise before the Lord to follow all the commands and rules written in this book. And everyone agreed to do the same.

Next, Josiah ordered Hilkiah, the chief priest, along with the second priest and the doorkeepers, to remove from the temple of God all the items made for Baal, the Canaanite goddess of fortune, and for the Babylonian star gods. He burned these items outside Jerusalem in lime kilns by the Kidron Valley and carried the ashes to Bethel. He also removed the idolatrous priests appointed by the leaders of Judah who were sacrificing at high places around Judah and in Jerusalem, including those who offered sacrifices to Baal, the sun, the moon, the stars, and all heavenly bodies.

He destroyed Topheth, which is in the valley of Ben-Hinnom, so that no one would ever again make their son or daughter pass through fire to Molech. He took away the horses at the entrance of the temple of God that the leaders of Judah had dedicated to the sun and burned the sun chariots. Josiah broke apart and smashed the altars the leaders of Judah had constructed on the rooftops.

Josiah also brought down the altar and the ancient temple at Bethel, smashed its stones into pieces, and turned it to dust.

Then he commanded all the people: "Celebrate the Passover to the Lord your God, as written in this Book of the Covenant." This Passover had not been celebrated since the days of the judges who governed Israel and throughout the times of Israel and Judah’s leaders; the Passover was observed in honor of the Lord in Jerusalem for the first time during the eighteenth year of Josiah's reign.

Josiah removed all mediums, wizards, idols, and every evil thing discovered in the land of Judah and in Jerusalem to ensure the words of the law, written in the book that Hilkiah the priest found in the temple of God, were carried out. Josiah was the first ruler who wholeheartedly turned to the Lord with all his heart, soul, and strength, aligning exactly with the law of Moses, and none of the rulers who came after him were like him.

title: The Writing Of An Ancient Book
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Four years into Jehoiakim's reign, the son of Josiah, a message from Jehovah came to Jeremiah: "Grab a parchment roll and write down everything I’ve told you about Jerusalem, Judah, and all the nations since Josiah’s time up to now. Maybe the people of Judah will realize the consequences of their actions and turn away from their wrong ways, so I can forgive their sins and guilt."

Jeremiah then called on Baruch, the son of Neriah, and instructed him to write down all the words from Jehovah. Jeremiah told Baruch, "I can’t go to the temple of Jehovah myself, so you need to go and read the words from the roll I asked you to write on the fast day. Read them to all the people of Judah who have come from their towns. Maybe they’ll pray to Jehovah and turn from their bad ways, for Jehovah is really angry with these people." Baruch did exactly as Jeremiah had commanded and read the words of Jehovah in the temple.

The following year, during the fast in the temple courts, Baruch read the words of Jeremiah to everyone who had come to Jerusalem from the towns of Judah.

When Micaiah, the grandson of Shaphan, heard the words of Jehovah, he went down to the palace where all the court officials were gathered and shared everything he had heard from Baruch's reading.

The nobles then sent Jehudi, the son of Nethaniah, to Baruch with this message: "Bring the roll you read to everyone and come here." Baruch took the roll and went to them. They told him, "Have a seat and read it to us." So Baruch read to them. After hearing all of it, they looked at each other in fear and said to Baruch, "We have to inform Jehoiakim about this." They then asked Baruch, "How did you manage to write all this?" Baruch replied, "Jeremiah dictated it to me, and I wrote it down in ink." The nobles then urged Baruch, "You need to hide, along with Jeremiah, and don’t let anyone know where you are."

After they stored the roll in Elishama’s room, the chancellor, they went to Jehoiakim and told him everything. Then he sent Jehudi to fetch the roll, which Jehudi brought from Elishama’s room. He read it to Jehoiakim and all the leaders present.

Jehoiakim was sitting in his winter house with a fire going in front of him. After Jehudi read three or four columns, Jehoiakim took a paper knife and cut the scroll, tossing it into the fire on the brazier until the whole roll was burned. Neither he nor his attendants showed any signs of distress or tore their clothes. Elnathan, Delaiah, and Gemariah urged Jehoiakim not to burn the scroll, but he refused to listen. He also instructed his son Jerahmeel, along with Seraiah, son of Azriel, and Shelemiah, son of Abdeel, to capture Baruch the scribe and Jeremiah the prophet, but Jehovah kept them hidden.

Then Jeremiah took another roll and gave it to Baruch, the scribe. Baruch wrote down everything as Jeremiah spoke, including all the words of the book that Jehoiakim had burned in the fire, along with many similar additional words.

title: A Prophet Who Saved A Great City
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
One day, the king of Assyria sent a high-ranking official with a massive army to Jerusalem. When they arrived, they called out for Hezekiah, the ruler of Judah, along with Eliakim, the son of Hilkiah, Shebnah the scribe, and Joah, the son of Asaph. The officials stepped outside to meet them. The high official said, "Why do you feel so confident? Who are you relying on for help that made you rebel against me? You think Egypt will rescue you, but Pharaoh is just as weak as a broken reed. If you’re claiming, 'We trust in Jehovah our God,' isn’t He the one whose high places and altars Hezekiah has torn down? So make a deal with my master, the king of Assyria, and I’ll provide you with two thousand horses, if you can find riders to mount them. How can you hope to defeat even the least of my master's servants? Didn't I come here to destroy this place without Jehovah's approval? It was Jehovah who told me, 'Go up against this land and destroy it.'"

Eliakim, Shebnah, and Joah replied to the official, "Please speak to us in Aramaic; we understand that language. But don’t speak to us in Hebrew, in front of the people on the wall." The high official answered them, "Was my master sent to speak only to your master and you? No, he’s speaking to the men sitting on the wall, who will be the ones suffering the most from this siege!"

Then the official stood up and shouted loudly in Hebrew, "Listen to the message from the great king of Assyria! 'The king says, don’t let Hezekiah trick you; he won’t be able to save you from my power. Don’t let Hezekiah convince you to trust in Jehovah by saying, Jehovah will surely save us, and this city won’t fall into the hands of the king of Assyria.'"

title: A Prophet Who Saved A Great City
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
"Don’t listen to Hezekiah, for the king of Assyria says, 'Make peace with me and surrender; then each of you can eat from your own vine and fig tree, and drink from your own cistern, until I take you to a land similar to your own. A land full of grain and new wine, full of bread and vineyards, abundant with olive trees and honey, where you will live and not die. But don’t let Hezekiah deceive you by saying, Jehovah will save us! Has any of the gods from other nations saved their land from the power of the king of Assyria? Did the gods of Samaria save it from my power? Which of all the gods of these countries has saved their land from my power, that Jehovah should save Jerusalem from my hands?'"

The people listened in silence and didn’t respond, because the ruler had commanded, "Do not answer him." Eliakim, the palace steward, Shebnah the scribe, and Joah, the son of Asaph, went to Hezekiah, their clothes torn, and reported the high official’s words. As soon as Hezekiah heard the news, he tore his clothes, put on sackcloth, and went into the temple of Jehovah. He sent Eliakim, who was in charge of the palace, and Shebnah the scribe, and the oldest priests, all dressed in sackcloth, to Isaiah the prophet. They said, 'Hezekiah sends this message: This is a day of trouble, discipline, and shame. Perhaps Jehovah your God will listen to the words of the high official that his master, the king of Assyria, has sent to insult the living God, and will take revenge. So please lift up a prayer for the people.'

When Hezekiah’s servants reached Isaiah, he said to them, "Return to your master and tell him: Jehovah says, 'Don’t be afraid of the words you’ve heard from the servants of the king of Assyria, who have insulted Me. I will put a spirit in him, so he’ll hear bad news and return to his own land, and I will cause him to be killed by the sword there.'"

The high official went back and found the king of Assyria fighting against Libnah, for he heard that the king had left Lachish. Then the king received news that Tirkakah, the king of Ethiopia, was preparing to battle against him. That very night, the angel of Jehovah went into the Assyrian camp and struck down 185,000 soldiers. When people woke up the next morning, they found all those soldiers dead.

Sennacherib, the king of Assyria, then left and returned to Nineveh. While he was worshipping in the temple of his god, Nisroch, his own sons attacked him and killed him with the sword, then fled to the land of Ararat. Esarhaddon, his son, succeeded him as king.

title: Jeremiah'S Courage In Danger
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
God spoke to Jeremiah, saying, "Stand at the entrance of the temple and deliver this message: 'Listen to the word of God, all you people of Judah who come through these gates to worship. The God of Israel says: Change your actions and your ways, and I will allow you to live in this place. Don't be fooled by misleading words, thinking, 'This is the temple of the Lord.' If you truly change your behavior, ensure that justice is done among your neighbors, treat the foreigners, orphans, and widows with kindness, and never shed innocent blood in this place or follow harmful gods, then I will let you stay in this land forever, the land I gave to your ancestors.'

"But right now you're relying on false words that won't help you at all. Will you steal, murder, lie, offer sacrifices to Baal, and follow gods you don’t even know? Then come stand before me in this house that bears my name and say, 'We're free to do all these disgraceful things'? Do you think this house, which is called by my name, is just a den of thieves? I have seen these shameful acts,' declares the Lord.

"Go to my temple in Shiloh, where people first worshiped me, and see what I did there because of the wickedness of Israel. Because you have done all these things and ignored my warnings, even though I spoke to you earnestly and often, I will destroy the temple that bears my name, which you trust in, and the place I gave to you and your ancestors, just like I did in Shiloh. I will send you away from my sight, just as I sent away your relatives from the Northern Kingdom.'"

When Jeremiah had delivered everything God commanded him to say, the priests and prophets grabbed him and shouted, "You must die! Why are you proclaiming in the name of the Lord that this temple will be like Shiloh, and this city will be deserted?" A crowd had gathered around Jeremiah in the temple.

When the officials of Judah heard about this, they came from the palace to the temple and gathered at the entrance, by the new gate. The priests and prophets told the officials and the crowd, "This man deserves death for prophesying against this city, just as you heard!" But Jeremiah replied to the officials and all the people, "It was God who sent me to prophesy against this temple and this city. So now, change your ways and listen to the Lord your God, and He will not carry out the threats against you. As for me, I’m in your hands; do what you think is right. Just remember, if you put me to death, you will bring guilt for innocent blood upon yourselves and this city, because the Lord truly sent me to you to deliver this message."

Then the officials and all the people said to the priests and prophets, "This man does not deserve to die, for he has spoken to us in the name of our God." Some of the elders stood up and addressed the gathering, saying, "Micah prophesied during King Hezekiah's reign, and he said to the people of Judah, 'The Lord Almighty says:

"Zion will be plowed like a field, Jerusalem will become a ruin, and the temple mount will become an overgrown hill.'"

"Did King Hezekiah and the people of Judah put him to death? No, they feared the Lord and begged Him for forgiveness, and because of that, He did not bring the destruction He had threatened. But we might be making a grave mistake ourselves.'"

title: The Sad Fate Of A Guilty Nation
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
In the eleventh year of Zedekiah's reign, on the ninth day of the fourth month, Jerusalem fell. An opening was made in the city walls, and all the officers of the Babylonian king gathered at the middle gate. When Zedekiah, the ruler of Judah, and his soldiers saw them, they fled the city under the cover of night, escaping through the royal garden and the gate between the two walls, heading toward the Arabah. However, the Chaldean army chased them down and caught Zedekiah in the plains of Jericho. They brought him before Nebuchadrezzar, the king of Babylon, who was staying at Riblah in the land of Hamath. There, the king executed Zedekiah's sons right in front of him and killed all the nobles of Judah. To top it off, he blinded Zedekiah and chained him up to take him to Babylon.

In the nineteenth year of Nebuchadrezzar's reign, Nebuzaradan, the commander of the king’s guard, arrived in Jerusalem. He set fire to the temple of Jehovah, the royal palace, and all the houses in the city. The Chaldean soldiers with him smashed down the walls around Jerusalem. The rest of the people who remained in the city, along with those who had defected to Babylon, were taken away as captives. Only the poorest people were left behind to tend to the vineyards and farms.

The Chaldeans shattered the bronze pillars from the temple of Jehovah, alongside the stands and the large bronze basin, taking the metal back to Babylon. They also grabbed pots, shovels, snuffers, bowls, and all the brass vessels used for sacrifices in the temple. The commander of the guard melted down the silver and gold fire-pans and basins before bringing them away as well.

The commander of the body-guard took Seraiah, the chief priest, Zephaniah, the second priest, and three doorkeepers, bringing them to the king of Babylon at Riblah. There, the king had them executed in the land of Hamath. This was how the people of Judah were led away from their homeland.

Nebuchadrezzar appointed Gedaliah, the grandson of Shaphan, as governor over the people that remained in the land of Judah.

Nebuchadrezzar had instructed Nebuzaradan, the commander of the guard, about Jeremiah, saying, 'Take good care of him and don't harm him; do whatever he tells you.' So, the commander said to Jeremiah, 'Today, I set you free from the chains on your hands. If you'd like to come with me to Babylon, I'd be happy to help you. But if you prefer not to come with me, you can go back to Gedaliah, whom the king of Babylon has made governor over the cities of Judah, and live with him or go wherever you think is best.' The commander then provided Jeremiah with food and a gift before sending him away. Jeremiah chose to go to Gedaliah, who was at Mizpah, and he settled in with the people who remained in the land.

title: The Courage Of Four Captives
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
King Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon directed Ashpenaz, his chief servant, to gather some young Israelites, including princes and nobles. He wanted young men who were strong, attractive, well-educated, and eager to learn, to serve in his palace. They were to be trained in the ways and language of the Chaldeans. The king provided them with his choice of food and wine every day. He ordered that they be instructed for three years so they could join his royal service afterward.

Among those young men were Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah. However, the chief servant renamed them: Daniel became Belteshazzar, Hananiah was called Shadrach, Mishael became Meshach, and Azariah was renamed Abednego.

Daniel made a firm decision not to compromise his beliefs by eating the king's rich food or drinking his wine. He respectfully asked the chief servant not to force him into such harm. God granted Daniel favor and compassion from the chief servant.

The chief servant replied to Daniel, "I'm worried that my lord, the king, will notice your faces looking sadder than those of the other young men your age, and it could cost me my life."

Daniel proposed to the steward assigned to him and his friends, "Why not test us for ten days? Let us eat only vegetables and drink water. After that, compare how we look with the young men who eat the king’s lavish food. Then you can decide what to do next." The steward agreed and put them to the test for ten days. At the end of that period, they appeared healthier and better nourished than all the young men who had consumed the king's food. So, the steward removed the rich food and wine, giving them only vegetables.

God blessed these four young men with knowledge, understanding, and wisdom, and Daniel was able to interpret all kinds of visions and dreams.

When the time came for them to be presented to King Nebuchadnezzar, the chief servant brought them in. The king spoke with them and found none equal to Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah. They began to serve the king, and regarding every topic that required wisdom and understanding, the king discovered that they were ten times more capable than all the wise men and magicians in his entire realm.

title: A King'S Strange Dream
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
In the second year of his reign, King Nebuchadnezzar was troubled by dreams that kept him awake at night. Feeling anxious, he called for the magicians, wise men, and astrologers to help him decipher the meaning of his dreams. When they gathered, he told them, "I've had a dream that troubles me, and I need to know what it means."

The astrologers responded, "Long live the king! Please tell us your dream, and we'll explain its meaning to you." The king firmly replied, "Let me make this clear: if you can't tell me both the dream and its interpretation, I will have you cut to pieces and your homes turned to rubble. However, if you can reveal the dream and its meaning, you will be rewarded with gifts, honors, and riches. So tell me the dream and what it means." They repeated, "If you share the dream, we will explain it to you." The king realized they were stalling and said, "I know you're trying to buy time because you understand that what I’ve stated is final. If you don’t reveal the dream to me, you will all face the same fate. You’re attempting to deceive me until the deadline passes. So, tell me the dream, and I’ll know you can explain its meaning." The Chaldeans replied, "No human can do what you ask. No king, no matter how mighty, has ever requested this from any wise man or magician. What you are demanding is impossible. Only the gods can reveal this, and they don’t dwell among humans." This infuriated the king, who commanded that all the wise men of Babylon be executed.

The order was given for the wise men to be killed, and Daniel and his friends were also sought for execution. Daniel then spoke wisely to Arioch, the captain of the king's guard, who was out to carry out the king's orders. He asked, "Why is the king's command so severe?" After Arioch explained the situation, Daniel requested, "Give me some time to interpret the dream for the king."

title: A King'S Strange Dream
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Daniel returned to his home and shared what was happening with his friends, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah. He asked them to pray to the God of heaven for mercy and to reveal the secret to him, so they could avoid being executed along with the other wise men. That night, Daniel received a revelation in a vision, and he praised God, saying:

"Praise be to the name of God forever and ever!
For wisdom and power belong to Him.
He gives wisdom to the wise,
And knowledge to those who seek insight.
He reveals profound and hidden things;
He knows what lies in darkness,
And light dwells with Him.
I thank You and praise You,
For You have given me wisdom and strength,
And revealed to us what we asked;
You’ve shared the king's secret!"

Then Daniel approached Arioch, the officer tasked with executing the wise men of Babylon, and said, "Don’t destroy the wise men of Babylon. Take me to the king, and I will explain what his dream means."

Arioch quickly took Daniel to the king and said, "I’ve found a man among the captives from Judah who can interpret your dream." The king asked Daniel, who was also known as Belteshazzar, "Can you tell me what my dream means?" Daniel replied, "The mystery the king is asking about is beyond the capabilities of any wise man, magician, or astrologer. But there is a God in heaven who reveals mysteries, and He has informed King Nebuchadnezzar about what is to come. Your dream and the visions you experienced during sleep are as follows: You, O king, saw a massive statue. It was dazzling and terrifying as it stood before you. The head of the statue was made of gold, its chest and arms were silver, its body and thighs were bronze, its legs were iron, and its feet were part iron and part clay. You watched until a stone was cut from a mountain without human hands; it struck the statue’s feet of iron and clay, smashing them to pieces.
