The iron, clay, bronze, silver, and gold were all shattered and became like chaff that the wind blew away, leaving no trace behind. However, the stone that struck the statue grew into a great mountain and filled the entire earth."

title: A King'S Strange Dream
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
"This is the dream, and now I will explain its meaning: O king, you are the king of kings, to whom the God of heaven has granted the rule, power, strength, and glory. He has placed everything—people, wild beasts, and birds—under your control. You are the head of gold."

"After you, another kingdom will arise that isn't as strong as yours, followed by a third kingdom of bronze that will rule over the entire earth. A fourth kingdom will be as strong as iron, since iron breaks and shatters everything. Just as iron crushes, it will dismantle all things. The statue's feet and toes, made of both clay and iron, depict a divided kingdom. It will possess some strength of the iron, as you noticed iron mixed with clay. The feet's toes have parts of iron and clay—that's how this kingdom will be. Although strong, it will also be weak, and as rulers unite through marriage, they won’t truly bond, just like iron can’t bind with clay."

"During the reign of these kings, the God of heaven will establish a kingdom that will never be destroyed or handed over to another people. It will crush and put an end to all these kingdoms, and it will stand forever. This is symbolized by the stone that was cut from the mountain without hands, smashing the iron, bronze, clay, silver, and gold."

"The great God has revealed to the king what is to occur, and the dream is true, as is its interpretation."

At that, King Nebuchadnezzar fell to the ground and worshiped Daniel, commanding that offerings and incense be presented to him. The king said to Daniel, "Your God is the God of gods and the Lord of kings, who reveals secrets to His servants, and you've been able to explain this great mystery." The king then promoted Daniel to a high position, showering him with gifts, and made him the ruler over all of Babylon and the chief over all the wise men there. At Daniel’s request, the king appointed Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego to oversee Babylon, while Daniel remained at the king's court.

title: The Test By Fire
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
King Nebuchadnezzar made a giant golden statue, standing ninety feet tall and nine feet wide. He placed it in the plain of Dura, in the province of Babylon. After that, he called for all the officials, governors, judges, treasurers, and rulers from every province. They gathered together to stand before the grand statue that King Nebuchadnezzar had erected.

Then the herald shouted, "Listen up, everyone! You are commanded, O nations and peoples: As soon as you hear the sounds of the trumpet, flute, lute, harp, bagpipe, and all other instruments, you must fall down and worship the golden statue. Anyone who refuses to worship will be thrown into a blazing furnace!" When the people heard the music play, they all fell down and worshipped the golden statue that King Nebuchadnezzar had set up.

But some Chaldeans approached the king and made an accusation against the Jews. "Long live the king! You have ordered that every person who hears the music must worship the golden statue. But there are some Jews—Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego—whom you've appointed to oversee the province of Babylon. They refuse to obey your command; they don't serve your gods or worship the golden statue you created."

In a fit of rage, Nebuchadnezzar called for Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego to be brought in. When they were presented to him, he asked, "Is it true, Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, that you don’t serve my gods or worship the golden statue? If you are ready to worship when the music plays, then all is well; but if you refuse, you will be thrown into a fiery furnace immediately. And tell me, what god can save you from my power?" Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego replied, "O king, we don’t need to defend ourselves. Our God can save us from the furnace, and He will rescue us from your hands. But even if He doesn't, we still won't serve your gods or worship the golden statue you have set up."

title: The Test By Fire
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Nebuchadnezzar was furious, and his face twisted with anger as he stared at Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego. He ordered the furnace to be heated seven times hotter than usual. He demanded that some of his strongest soldiers bind Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego and throw them into the fiery furnace. The men were tied up in their cloaks, tunics, and other clothes and thrown into the blazing fire. Because the king's order was so urgent and the furnace was intensely hot, the flames consumed the soldiers who brought them to the fire. But Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego fell into the furnace, bound.

The king was so shocked that he jumped up and asked his advisors, "Did we not throw three men into the fire, bound?" They responded, "Yes, O king." "But look!" he said. "I see four men unbound and walking in the fire, and they aren't hurt! The fourth one looks like an angel."

Nebuchadnezzar approached the entrance of the fiery furnace and called, "Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, servants of the Most High God, come out!" The three men stepped out of the fire, and the officials, governors, and advisors gathered around them. They saw that the fire had not harmed their bodies—their hair wasn't singed, their clothes were intact, and there was no smell of smoke on them. Nebuchadnezzar proclaimed, "Blessed be the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, who sent His angel to rescue His servants. They trusted in Him and defied my command, choosing not to serve other gods or worship the statue. Therefore, I decree that anyone who speaks against the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego will be cut into pieces, and their homes will be turned to ashes, for there is no other god who can save like this one." Then the king rewarded Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego with high positions in Babylon.

title: The Handwriting On The Wall
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
King Belshazzar threw a massive banquet for a thousand of his nobles, celebrating with wine in front of them all. Feeling tipsy, he ordered the gold and silver goblets that his father Nebuchadnezzar had taken from the temple in Jerusalem to be brought in. The king, along with his nobles, wives, and everyone else in the palace, drank from these sacred vessels. They sipped wine and praised the gods made of gold, silver, bronze, iron, wood, and stone.

Suddenly, a hand appeared and began writing on the plaster wall right across from the lampstand in the palace. Belshazzar watched in shock as the fingers moved to form the writing.

The king turned pale, his thoughts racing, and he felt his legs tremble while his knees knocked together. He called for the magicians and stargazers and said to Babylon's wise men, "Whoever can read this writing and explain its meaning will be dressed in purple, wear a gold chain around his neck, and be made the third ruler in the kingdom." All the wise men came in, but no one could decipher the writing or tell the king what it meant. Belshazzar became terrified, his face paling, and his nobles were thrown into chaos.

The queen, hearing about the king's distress, entered the banquet hall and said, "Long live the king! Don’t let your thoughts alarm you or your face turn pale. There’s a man in your kingdom who has the spirit of the holy gods within him. In the days of your father, he was found to have intelligence, insight, and wisdom as if it were divine. Bring Daniel in, and he will explain the writing to you."

So Daniel was brought before the king, and Belshazzar asked him, "Are you Daniel, one of the captives from Judah that my father brought here? I’ve heard that the spirit of the gods is in you and that you possess insight and extraordinary wisdom. The wise men and magicians I summoned couldn’t read this writing or explain its meaning. But I’ve been told that you can interpret dreams and solve difficult questions. If you can read the writing and explain its meaning, I’ll dress you in purple, put a gold chain around your neck, and make you the third ruler in the kingdom."

title: The Handwriting On The Wall
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Daniel replied, "Keep your gifts and give your rewards to someone else. I’ll read the writing for you and explain what it means. Your Majesty, the Most High God gave your father Nebuchadnezzar the kingdom, power, glory, and majesty. Because of the power He bestowed upon him, people, nations, and languages trembled in his presence. He could kill or spare anyone as he wished; he raised up and brought down those he chose. But when he became arrogant, he was stripped of his throne, his glory taken away. He was driven from human society and lived like a wild animal, eating grass like an ox and soaking in the dew until he understood that the Most High God is the true ruler over the kingdoms of men and sets over them whoever He wills."

"But you, Belshazzar, his son, have not been humble, even though you knew all this. Instead, you’ve defied the Lord of heaven. You brought the goblets from His temple to drink wine from, and you and your nobles, wives, and guests praised the gods made of silver, gold, bronze, iron, wood, and stone—gods that cannot see, hear, or understand. You did not honor the God who holds your very life and everything you do in His hands."

"The hand was sent from Him, and it wrote this inscription:

"This is what it means: Mene: God has numbered your kingdom and brought it to an end. Tekel: You have been weighed on the scales and found lacking. Peres: Your kingdom is divided and given to the Medes and Persians."

Then, at Belshazzar’s command, Daniel was dressed in purple and given a gold chain around his neck, and he was proclaimed the third ruler in the kingdom. However, that very night, Belshazzar the Chaldean king was killed, and Darius the Mede assumed the kingdom.

title: Daniel In The Lions' Den
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Darius was pleased to appoint one hundred and twenty officers to oversee his kingdom, with three chief officials supervising them, and Daniel was one of these officials. Daniel stood out among the others because of his exceptional spirit, and the king planned to promote him over the entire empire.

The chief officials and officers tried to find a way to accuse Daniel of failing in his duties, but they couldn't find anything wrong, because he was trustworthy and had done nothing to deserve blame.

These officials said, "We won’t find a way to accuse Daniel unless it involves the law of his God." So, they approached the king and said, "Long live, King Darius! All the chief officials, counselors, officers, judges, and governors have come together to ask you to establish a law. We want a strong decree that anyone who prays to any god or man for thirty days—other than you, O king—shall be thrown into a den of lions. Please sign this command so it cannot be changed, just like the laws of the Medes and Persians." King Darius agreed and signed the decree.

When Daniel learned that the decree had been signed, he went home. His windows were open toward Jerusalem, and he knelt down three times a day to pray, giving thanks to God, just as he had always done. The officials rushed in and caught Daniel praying and calling out to his God. They went to the king and reminded him about the royal command: "Did you not sign a decree that anyone who prays to any god or man within thirty days, except you, O king, will be thrown into the den of lions?" The king replied, "The law is fixed according to the Medes and Persians, which cannot be changed." Then they told the king, "That Daniel, who is from Judah, doesn't pay any attention to you, O king, or to the command you signed. He still prays three times a day."

When the king heard this, he was very upset and tried his hardest to save Daniel, working until sunset to find a way. But the officials reminded the king, "Know, O king, that it’s the law of the Medes and Persians that no command or law that you give can be changed."

title: Daniel In The Lions' Den
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Reluctantly, the king gave the order, and Daniel was brought and thrown into the den of lions. However, before Daniel was thrown in, the king told him, "Your God, whom you always serve, will rescue you." A stone was placed over the entrance to the den, and the king sealed it with his own ring and the rings of his nobles to ensure that Daniel couldn't escape. After that, the king went to his palace and spent the night fasting.

At daybreak, the king got up and rushed to the lions’ den. When he reached the den, he shouted out in a sorrowful voice, "Daniel, servant of the living God, has your God, whom you always serve, been able to save you from the lions?" Daniel responded, "O king, live forever! My God sent an angel to shut the lions' mouths, and they haven't hurt me, because I was found innocent in his sight. And I haven’t done anything wrong against you, O king." The king was overjoyed and commanded that Daniel be lifted out of the den. Daniel was taken out and found to be unharmed, for he had trusted in God.

Then the king commanded that those men who had accused Daniel be brought in and thrown into the den of lions.

King Darius wrote to all the peoples, nations, and languages throughout his kingdom: "May your peace abound! I issue a decree that all must tremble and fear before the God of Daniel; for he is the living God, enduring forever. His kingdom will not be destroyed, and his dominion will last without end. He saves and rescues, performing wonders in heaven and earth; it is he who has saved Daniel from the lions." As a result, Daniel thrived and prospered during the reign of Darius.

title: Queen Esther'S Love For Her People
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
After King Xerxes had ruled Persia for three years, he hosted a lavish feast for all his officials, officers, and servants. The military commanders of Persia and Media, as well as the nobles and governors, were gathered around him. For one hundred and eighty days, he showcased the incredible riches of his kingdom and the splendor of his royal regalia.

When the feast came to an end, the king organized a seven-day celebration in the enclosed garden of the royal palace. This event was for everyone present in the royal palace at Susa. Queen Vashti also held a separate feast for the women in the royal palace belonging to King Xerxes.

On the seventh day, after having enjoyed some wine, King Xerxes commanded his seven attendants to bring Queen Vashti before him, wearing her royal crown. He wanted to show off her beauty to everyone present, as she was truly stunning. However, Queen Vashti refused to come when the king called for her, which made him extremely angry.

Fuming, the king turned to his wise men and asked, "What should we do to Queen Vashti, according to the law?" Memucan, one of the seven high officials, spoke up in front of the king and his officers. He said, "Vashti has wronged not only the king but all the officials and people across the provinces. If word gets out about her defiance, other women will follow her example and disrespect their husbands. If it pleases the king, he should issue a royal command that will be written into the laws of Persia and Media, stating that Vashti can never again come before King Xerxes, and that another queen, someone better than her, shall take her place. This way, all the wives will respect their husbands when they hear of the king's command!"

The plan satisfied the king and the officials, so he followed Memucan's advice. Then his attendants suggested, "Let's search for beautiful young girls for the king. The king should appoint officials in all the provinces to gather these girls at the palace in Susa. They should be given everything they need to look their best, and the one who pleases the king will become queen in Vashti's place." The king agreed to this proposal.

title: Queen Esther'S Love For Her People
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
In the royal palace at Susa, there was a Jew named Mordecai who had been taken captive from Jerusalem by King Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon. He had adopted Esther, his uncle's daughter, who had no father or mother. Esther was beautiful and lovely, and after her parents passed away, Mordecai took her in as his own daughter.

When the king's command was announced, many girls were brought to the royal palace in Susa, and Esther was among them. She was put in the care of Hegai, the king's eunuch who managed the women. The girl pleased Hegai and earned his favor, so he quickly provided her with beauty treatments, food, and seven maids chosen from the king's household. He even moved her and her maids to the best place in the women's quarters. Esther had not disclosed her heritage or family background, as Mordecai had advised her to keep that secret. Every day, Mordecai would walk in front of the court of the women's quarters to check on her well-being.

When it was Esther's turn to meet the king, he loved her more than all the other women. She became his favorite, and he placed the royal crown on her head, making her queen instead of Vashti. To celebrate, the king threw a grand feast in honor of Esther.

One day, while Mordecai was sitting at the king's gate, two of the king's servants, who were guarding the entrance, became furious and plotted to kill King Xerxes. Mordecai discovered the plan and alerted Queen Esther, who in turn informed the king, citing Mordecai's name. When the plot was exposed, both men were hanged on a tree, and it was recorded in the daily events that the king had kept.

After these events, King Xerxes promoted Haman, the Agagite, and granted him a higher position than all the other officials. All the king's servants at the gate were required to bow down to Haman, just as the king had commanded. However, Mordecai refused to bow.

title: Queen Esther'S Love For Her People
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
The king's servants at the gate approached Mordecai, saying, "Why do you disobey the king's command?" They pressed him day after day, but he did not listen. So, they reported him to Haman to see if his actions would allow for punishment since Mordecai had shared that he was a Jew. When Haman noticed that Mordecai didn’t bow or show respect, he became furious. Knowing Mordecai was a Jew, Haman decided not to punish him alone but to devise a plan to destroy all the Jews in King Xerxes' kingdom.

Haman spoke to King Xerxes, stating, "There are certain people scattered throughout your provinces whose customs are different from everyone else's, and they do not follow the king's laws. It would not be wise for the king to ignore them. If it pleases you, issue an order to eliminate them, and I will pay ten thousand silver talents into the royal treasury as compensation."

The king removed his ring from his hand and gave it to Haman, saying, "The money is yours, and the people too. Do as you wish with them." News was dispatched by mounted messengers to all the provinces, commanding that all Jews, young and old, including children and women, be killed on the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, and that all their possessions be seized. Then the king and Haman relaxed with a drink, unaware that the people of Susa were in turmoil.

When Mordecai heard about everything that had happened, he tore his clothes, put on sackcloth, and covered himself in ashes as he walked through the city, crying out in sorrow. He reached the king's gate, but no one could enter the gate dressed in sackcloth. Throughout every province to which the king's command reached, there was widespread mourning, fasting, weeping, and wailing among the Jews; many were clothed in sackcloth and ashes.

Esther's maids and attendants informed her about Mordecai's distress, which deeply troubled her. She sent clothes for him to wear, hoping to comfort him and remove his sackcloth, but he refused to accept them. Then Esther summoned Hathach, one of the king's servants assigned to her, and instructed him to go to Mordecai to learn what was happening and why.

title: Queen Esther'S Love For Her People
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Hathach went to Mordecai in the city square in front of the king's gate. Mordecai detailed everything that had transpired and the exact amount of money Haman had promised to contribute to the royal treasury. He also provided Hathach with a copy of the decree that had been issued in Susa to destroy the Jews, so Esther could understand the situation. He urged her to approach the king and plead for mercy on behalf of her people.

When Hathach relayed Mordecai's message to Esther, she instructed him to reply, "All the king's servants and the people of the king's provinces are aware that if anyone appears before the king in the inner court without being summoned, the punishment is death—unless the king extends his golden scepter to them, allowing them to live. I've not been called to see the king for the last thirty days."

Mordecai received Esther's message and sent back a reply, saying, "Don’t think that just because you live in the king's palace, you will be spared while the rest of the Jews are in danger. If you keep silent now, help will come from somewhere else, but you and your family will perish. Who knows? Perhaps you were chosen for this very moment to be in the position you are now!"

Esther then sent this message to Mordecai: "Gather all the Jews in Susa and have them fast for me for three days and nights. I will fast as well, and then I will approach the king, even though it is against the law. If I perish, I perish." So, Mordecai went away and did everything Esther had directed.

On the third day, Esther dressed in her royal robes and stood in the inner court of the palace, directly across from the king's throne. When the king saw Queen Esther standing in the court, he found favor in her sight and extended the golden scepter toward her. Esther approached and touched the tip of the scepter. The king asked her, "What is your request, Queen Esther? It shall be granted, even if it’s half of my kingdom." Esther replied, "If it pleases the king, let him and Haman come today to the feast I have prepared for them." The king said, "Bring Haman quickly, so that Esther's wishes can be fulfilled."

title: Queen Esther'S Love For Her People
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
So the king and Haman went to the feast Esther had prepared. While they were drinking wine, the king asked Esther, "Whatever you wish will be granted, even if it takes half of my kingdom." Esther responded, "If I have found favor in the king's eyes, let the king and Haman come to the feast that I will prepare tomorrow; then I will answer the king’s question."

Haman left that day feeling overjoyed, but when he saw Mordecai at the king's gate and noticed that he did not rise or show him any respect, he boiled with rage against Mordecai. However, Haman restrained his anger and returned home. He gathered his friends and his wife Zeresh to boast about his wealth, many children, and how the king had honored him by promoting him over the officials and royal servants. Haman exclaimed, "Queen Esther invited no one except me to join the king at the feast she prepared, and she also asked me to dine with her tomorrow. Yet nothing satisfies me as long as I see Mordecai the Jew sitting at the king's gate!"

Zeresh, his wife, and all his friends advised him, "Build a gallows seventy-five feet high and in the morning, ask the king to have Mordecai hanged on it. Then go to the feast with the king in good spirits." This suggestion pleased Haman, so he had the gallows constructed.

That night the king was unable to sleep, so he ordered the records of memorable deeds to be brought and read to him. As they were reading, it was noted how Mordecai had informed about the two guards who had plotted to kill him. The king then asked, "What has been done to honor Mordecai for this?" His attendants replied, "Nothing has been done for him." The king then inquired, "Who is in the court?" At that moment, Haman had just entered the outer court to speak to the king about hanging Mordecai on the gallows he had built. The king's attendants informed him, "Haman is standing in the court." The king said, "Let him enter."

title: Queen Esther'S Love For Her People
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
As Haman entered, the king asked him, "What should be done for the man whom the king wishes to honor?" Haman thought to himself, "Whom could the king want to honor more than me?" So he replied, "For the man the king wishes to honor, let a royal cloak be brought, the one the king has worn, along with the horse the king has ridden with a royal crown on its head. Let someone noble provide the clothes and horse to the man the king desires to honor, and let him be paraded through the city, proclaiming, 'This is what is done for the man whom the king delights to honor!'"

The king said to Haman, "Hurry and get the cloak and the horse, as you have suggested. Do this for Mordecai the Jew who sits at the king's gate. Don’t leave out anything you have recommended!" Haman took the cloak and the horse, dressed Mordecai, and led him through the city square, proclaiming, "This is what is done for the man whom the king wishes to honor!"

Afterward, Mordecai returned to the king's gate, but Haman hurried home, grieving and with his head covered. He shared everything that had happened with his wife Zeresh and his friends. They said to him, "If Mordecai, before whom you have fallen, is of Jewish descent, you will not prevail against him; you will surely fall before him."

As they were still talking, the king's servants arrived and quickly took Haman to the feast that Esther had prepared. The king and Haman sat down to drink with Queen Esther. During the meal, the king asked, "What is your request, Queen Esther? It shall be granted, even if it takes half of my kingdom." Esther replied, "If I have won your favor, O king, let me and my people be granted mercy at my request; we have been sold to be destroyed, killed, and eliminated!"

title: Queen Esther'S Love For Her People
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
King Xerxes turned to Queen Esther and asked, "Who is he, and where is he who dares to do such a thing?" Esther replied, "The adversary and enemy is this wicked Haman!" Haman was terrified in front of the king and queen. Then Harbonah, one of the king's attendants, said, "There is a gallows standing at Haman's house, seventy-five feet high, which he had prepared for Mordecai, who had spoken well of the king." The king commanded, "Hang him on it!" So they hanged Haman on the gallows he had constructed for Mordecai, and the king's anger was calmed.

At that moment, King Xerxes gave Haman's estate to Esther, the enemy of the Jews. Mordecai was appointed as one of the king’s advisors since Esther had revealed their family connection. The king removed his signet ring, which he had taken from Haman, and gave it to Mordecai. Esther then placed Mordecai in charge of Haman's property.

Esther again approached the king, fell at his feet, and pleaded with him in tears to stop the evil Haman had devised against the Jews. The king extended his golden scepter to her, and she rose and stood before him. King Xerxes instructed both Queen Esther and Mordecai, saying, "Write a decree for the Jews as you see fit in my name, and seal it with the king's ring; for whatever is written in the king's name and sealed with his ring cannot be undone."

Mordecai wrote a decree in King Xerxes' name and sealed it with the king’s ring. He sent messengers riding the king's swift horses, mules, and camels with the king’s order for Jews in every city to gather and protect their lives.

The command was issued throughout the royal palace in Susa; Mordecai emerged from the king’s presence dressed in royal garments of violet and white, wearing a great crown of gold and a robe of fine linen and purple. The citizens of Susa rejoiced! For the Jews, there came light, gladness, joy, and honor. Throughout every province, wherever the king’s command reached, there was joy and celebration among the Jews, marking a holiday.

On the fourteenth day of the month Adar, the Jews took a day of rest, celebrating with feasting and joy. That’s why the Jews living in the countryside observe the fourteenth day of Adar as a day of joy, feasting, and exchanging gifts. However, the Jews in Susa rested on the fifteenth day of the same month, celebrating with feasting and rejoicing.

The Jews established this custom for themselves, their children, and all those who wished to join them, ensuring these two days remained sacred feasts each year. This celebration arose because Haman had plotted to annihilate the Jews, casting lots—known as Pur. Thus, these days are commemorated as Purim.

title: Rebuilding The Temple
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
To fulfill the promise made by Jehovah through Jeremiah, Jehovah moved Cyrus, the king of Persia, in the first year of his reign to issue this decree across his entire kingdom: "Cyrus, king of Persia, says: 'Jehovah, the God of heaven, has given me all the kingdoms of the earth and has tasked me to build a temple for Him in Jerusalem, Judah. Anyone from His people who wants to return should have their God with them. They are welcome to come to Jerusalem in Judah and rebuild the temple of Jehovah, the God of Israel. (He is the God who dwells in Jerusalem.) In every place where any of Jehovah's people are now living, let the men of that area help with silver, gold, goods, and animals, in addition to any contributions each person wants to offer for the house of God in Jerusalem.'"

Then, the leaders of the families of Judah and Benjamin, along with the priests and the Levites, including everyone whom God inspired to rebuild Jehovah's temple in Jerusalem, began their journey back. Their neighbors helped them by providing silver, gold, goods, animals, and precious items, as well as any donations each person wanted to give.

Cyrus, king of Persia, ordered the sacred vessels of Jehovah’s temple, which Nebuchadnezzar had taken from Jerusalem, to be brought out under the care of Mithredath, the treasurer, and handed over to Sheshbazzar, the prince of Judah. Sheshbazzar brought them back to Jerusalem, alongside the people returning from captivity in Babylon. God instructed them through Haggai, the prophet, "Go up to the mountains, gather wood, and rebuild the temple. Then I will be pleased with it and show my glory."

Then Zerubbabel and Joshua, the high priest, along with everyone else, responded to the command of Jehovah their God. They gathered and worked on the temple of Jehovah their God. They completed the structure just as the God of Israel had commanded.

title: Nehemiah'S Answer To A Call For Help
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
In the twentieth year of King Artaxerxes' reign, in November, I, Nehemiah, was in the royal palace at Shushan when my brother Hanani and some men came from Judah. I asked them about Jerusalem and the Jews who had survived the captivity. They told me, "The people living there in the province are facing serious trouble and disgrace. The wall of Jerusalem is broken down, and its gates have been damaged by fire."

When I heard this, I sat down, weeping and mourning for several days. I began to fast and pray to the God of heaven, saying, "Please listen, O Jehovah, God of heaven, who shows kindness to those who love you and follow your commands. Open your ears to hear my prayers day and night for your servants, the Israelites, as I confess our sins. We are your servants and your people, whom you saved by your great power and strong hand. O Lord, please pay attention to my request and to the requests of those who delight in worshiping you. Give me success today, and let me find favor in this man's eyes."

At that time, I was the king's cupbearer. In March of King Artaxerxes' twentieth year, I was serving the king wine. Up until then, I hadn’t been sad. So, the king asked me, "Why do you look so down? You’re not sick; it must be sadness of heart." I felt a wave of fear wash over me, and I replied, "Long live the king! Why shouldn’t I be sad? The city where my ancestors are buried lies in ruins, and its gates are destroyed by fire!" The king responded, "What do you want?" So, I prayed to the God of heaven and told the king, "If it pleases you, and if I have found favor in your sight, please send me to Judah, to the city where my ancestors are buried, so I can rebuild it." The king, with the queen sitting beside him, asked, "How long will your journey take, and when will you be back?" I told him when I would return, and the king agreed to send me.

title: Nehemiah'S Answer To A Call For Help
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
I then continued, "If it pleases the king, please provide me with letters to the governors of the province west of the Euphrates, so they will let me pass through until I get to Judah. Also, give me a letter to Asaph, the keeper of the king's park, so he will provide me with timber to make beams for the gates of the temple fortress, the city wall, and my own house." The king granted all my requests, because my God had been looking out for me.

I traveled to the governors of the province and delivered the king's letters. The king had sent officers and horsemen with me. When Sanballat the Horonite and Tobiah the Ammonite heard about it, they were very upset that someone had come to care for the welfare of the Israelites.

So, I arrived in Jerusalem. After three days there, I went out at night with a few of my followers. I hadn’t told anyone what my God had put in my heart to do for Jerusalem. I rode out by night through the Valley Gate, toward the Dragon’s Well and to the Dung Gate. I carefully examined the broken-down walls of Jerusalem and the damaged gates. I made my way to the Fountain Gate and the King’s Pool, but there was no way for my animal to pass.

I continued up along the Brook Kidron at night, checking out the wall. Then, I returned through the Valley Gate. No one knew where I had gone or what I was doing, and I hadn’t yet shared my plan with the Jews, priests, nobles, or anyone else involved in the work.

Then I said to them, "You see the terrible situation we’re in: Jerusalem lies in ruins, and its gates are burned down. Let’s come together and rebuild the wall of Jerusalem, so we won’t be a disgrace anymore." I shared how God had taken care of me and the words the king had spoken to me. They replied, "Let’s rise up and build!" They were excited and committed to the good work.

title: Overcoming Great Difficulties
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
When Sanballat found out we were rebuilding the wall, he was furious and mocked the Jews. He stood before his fellow tribesmen and the army of Samaria, saying, "What do these weak Jews think they're doing? Are they just going to leave it to God? Are they going to make a sacrifice? Do they really think they can finish this work in one day? Can they possibly recover the stones from all this rubble after they've been burned?"

Tobiah, the Ammonite, joined him and added, "That stone wall they're building—if a fox even climbs on it, it'll fall down!"

Despite the mockery, we kept rebuilding the wall. Before long, it was halfway finished because everyone was eager to get to work. But when Sanballat, Tobiah, the Arabs, the Ammonites, and the Ashdodites heard that the walls of Jerusalem were being rebuilt and the broken sections were being filled in, they were furious. They all plotted to come and attack Jerusalem and scare the people. So we prayed to our God and set up a watch for protection against them, day and night.

When our enemies realized that we were aware of their scheme and that God had put a stop to it, we went back to work on the wall, each person at their own station. From that point on, while half of my men worked, the other half held lances, shields, bows, and armor, with the leaders standing behind the people of Judah. Those who were building the wall and those who carried supplies were also armed, each using one hand to work and the other to hold their weapon. Each builder had a sword at their side. The man who sounded the alarm was by me, and I told the nobles, the rulers, and the rest of the people, "The work is extensive and spread out, and we’re far apart on the wall. Whenever you hear the trumpet, gather around us; our God will fight for us."

So we worked hard while half of them holding lances from early morning until the stars came out. I then told the people, "Let each man and his servant stay in Jerusalem so we can defend ourselves at night and work during the day." None of us—my relatives, my servants, or the guards with me—took off our clothes; we kept our weapons ready at all times.

title: Overcoming Great Difficulties
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
After fifty-two days, the wall was completed. When our enemies heard about it, all the surrounding nations were terrified, realizing that this incredible work had been accomplished with the help of our God.

Once the wall was built and the doors were set up, I appointed the porters, singers, and Levites. I put my brother Hanani, the commander of the castle, in charge of Jerusalem because he was trustworthy and more God-fearing than most. I instructed them, "Don’t open the gates of Jerusalem until the sun is up. At night, while the watchmen are still guarding, shut and bar the doors. And let the people living in Jerusalem be on alert at their posts, right by their homes."

The city was large and spacious, but there were few people living in it, and the houses were still in ruins. So God inspired me to gather the nobles, officials, and others. The officials were already in Jerusalem, while the rest of the people drew lots so that one out of every ten would live in the sacred city, with the others remaining in the villages.

I had the officials of Judah take their places on the wall, and I organized two large processions. One marched to the right toward the Dung Gate, followed by Hoshaiah and half of the officials of Judah. At the Fountain Gate, they climbed the steps of the City of David and moved along the wall above the House of David to the Water Gate on the east side of the city.

The other procession went left on the wall above the Tower of the Furnaces, and I followed them with half of the people to the broad wall, above the Gate of Ephraim, and past the Old Gate, the Fish Gate, the Tower of Hananel, and the Tower of the Hundred to the Sheep Gate. Then they took their position at the Gate of the Guard. So the two processions assembled in the temple, and I was with half of the leaders.

Then the singers sang out loud, and the people offered many sacrifices that day and celebrated, for God had given them much to be joyful about. The women and children joined in too, and the joyful sounds of celebration in Jerusalem could be heard from far away.

title: A Brave Knight
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
The common people and their families raised a loud outcry against their fellow Jews. Some said, "We have to give our sons and daughters as pledges to get grain so we can eat and survive." Others chimed in, "We have to give up our fields, vineyards, and homes to get grain because there’s barely any food left." Still others said, "We’ve borrowed money to pay the king's taxes. Even though we share the same flesh as our brothers and our children are just like theirs, we’re forced to sell our sons and daughters as slaves. Some of our daughters have already been sold into slavery, and there’s nothing we can do to stop it because our lands and vineyards belong to the nobles."

When I heard their cries and their words, I felt a surge of anger. After some reflection, I confronted the nobles and the rulers, saying, "You make each of your fellow Jews pay for what you loan them."

So I organized a big meeting to protest their actions. I said to them, "We’ve done everything we could to buy back our fellow Jews who have been sold to foreigners. Would you really sell your own people, and should they be sold to us?" They fell silent, unable to respond. I continued, "What you’re doing is wrong. Shouldn’t you live in fear of God to avoid bringing shame upon ourselves in front of our foreign enemies? I, along with my relatives and my servants, lend money and grain to the people as well. Let's stop charging anything for what we lend. Give back their fields, vineyards, olive groves, and houses immediately, and cancel any payments made for the money, grain, new wine, and oil you've given them."

They replied, "We’ll return everything and ask for nothing in return; we’ll do exactly as you’ve instructed." Then I called the priests and made them solemnly promise to follow through with their word.

For twelve years, since I was appointed as their governor in Judah, I and my relatives didn’t consume the food that was my entitlement as governor. Previous governors burdened the people, taking bread, wine, and forty pieces of silver every day. Their servants also treated the people poorly. But I didn’t do that because I feared God. I committed myself to working on the wall, and we didn’t purchase any land. All my servants were gathered there to work. Additionally, I provided meals for a hundred and fifty Jews and rulers, plus those who joined us from other nations. Every day, I prepared one ox, six choice sheep, and poultry at my own expense, and every ten days there was plenty of wine for everyone. Even with all these costs, I didn’t demand the food due to me as governor because this public work was a heavy burden for the people. Remember, O my God, all that I’ve done for them!

title: The Story Of Job
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
In the land of Uz, there was a man named Job. He was known for being honest and good, deeply respecting God and steering clear of evil. Job had seven sons and three daughters. He owned an impressive 7,000 sheep, 3,000 camels, 500 pairs of oxen, and 500 donkeys. With so many servants, he was the wealthiest man in all the East.

One day, when the angels gathered before God, Satan joined them. God asked Satan, "Where have you come from?" Satan replied, "I've been roaming the earth, wandering here and there." Then God said, "Have you noticed my servant Job? There's no one like him: he is blameless and upright, always fearing God and avoiding evil." Satan responded, "Does Job fear God for no reason? You’ve put a protective barrier around him, his household, and all that he owns. You've blessed everything he does, making his possessions grow. But if you take everything away from him, he will certainly curse you to your face." God told Satan, "Alright, everything he has is in your control, but don’t harm Job himself." So Satan left God's presence.

While Job's sons and daughters were enjoying a feast at their oldest brother's house, a messenger rushed to Job and said, "The oxen were plowing and the donkeys were close by when the Sabeans attacked and took them. The workers were killed, and I’m the only one who escaped to tell you!"

While that messenger was still speaking, another one arrived with more news: "Fire has fallen from the sky and burned up the sheep and the servants! I’m the only one who escaped to tell you!"

Before that messenger could finish, yet another one arrived and said, "The Chaldeans attacked in three groups, raiding the camels and driving them away. The workers were killed, and I’m the only one who escaped to tell you!"

While he was speaking, another messenger ran in and said, "Your sons and daughters were eating and drinking at their oldest brother’s house when a powerful wind swept through the desert and knocked the house down. It crushed the young people, and I’m the only one who escaped to tell you!"

title: The Story Of Job
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Then Job stood up, tore his robe, shaved his head, fell to the ground, and worshipped God, saying:

"God gave, and God has taken away; blessed be the name of God!"

On another day, when the angels gathered again, Satan appeared among them. God asked him, "Where have you come from this time?"

Satan answered, "I've been roaming the earth, walking around it." God said, "Have you noticed my servant Job? There's no one like him: he's blameless and upright, fearing God and avoiding evil; he still holds strong to his integrity, even after all you've done to him without cause." Satan replied, "A man will do anything to save his life. Just touch his skin, and he’ll surely curse you to your face!" God said to Satan, "Alright, he’s in your hands, but you must spare his life."

So Satan departed from God's presence and afflicted Job with painful sores from head to toe. Job took a piece of broken pottery to scrape himself while sitting in the ashes.

As he was sitting there, his wife said to him, "Are you still holding onto your integrity? Just curse God and die!" But Job replied, "You sound like a foolish woman. Should we only accept good from God and not trouble?" In all of this, Job did not say anything wrong.

When Job's three friends heard about the troubles he was facing, they came from their homes: Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite. They had agreed to meet and offer their sympathy to Job in his time of need. However, when they saw him from a distance, they barely recognized him. They began to weep and mourn aloud, tearing their robes and throwing dust on their heads. For seven days and nights, they sat with him on the ground without saying a word, for they saw how much he was suffering.

"Why didn’t I die at birth, or perish as I came from the womb? Then I would have been at rest, lying quietly with kings and counselors who built themselves great pyramids. I would be at peace with the wicked and the weary, where captives rest together and the masters’ voices are never heard anymore."

title: The Story Of Job
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
"If someone thinks I’m going to be silent about their troubles, will it upset you? But how can I keep quiet? You’ve taught so many, giving strength to the weak. Your words have supported those who were falling, but now that trouble has come to you, you seem impatient and lose heart. Isn’t your faith what gives you hope? Remember, no innocent person has ever perished, right? Blessed is the one whom God corrects; do not despise the Almighty's discipline. He causes pain to comfort, to heal. That’s how he works."

"What strength do I have that I can endure? What is my future that I should stay patient? Am I as strong as a stone, or is my body made of steel? Friends should be kind to those in pain, even if they doubt. Teach me, and I will listen. Show me where I have sinned."

"Is God unjust? Can the Almighty do wrong? If your children sinned against him, they faced the consequences. But you should sincerely seek God and beg the Almighty for mercy. If you are pure and upright, he will surely answer your prayers and bless your home."

"I know it’s true, but how can a person be righteous before God? He’s all-knowing and all-powerful. Who can stand against him and thrive? I am blameless; I don’t value my own life. I loathe my existence; it’s all the same to me. That’s why I say openly: He destroys both the innocent and the wicked."

"If you would cleanse your heart and reach out to God, removing sin from your life, you’d be able to lift your face without shame, confident and fearless."

"You all think you’re the wise ones, but I too have understanding. Don’t think I’m unaware of what’s happening. I wish my words could be written down, inscribed in a book with an iron pen and lead, carved in stone forever! I know that my Redeemer lives; in the end, he will stand upon the earth. Even after my skin has been destroyed, in my flesh, I will see God! I will see him for myself, my own eyes will behold him, and no one else’s."

title: The Story Of Job
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
"Oh, to be like those days when God was guiding my steps, when his light shone above my head, and I walked through darkness with his guidance. During my prosperous days, God protected my tent and my children gathered around me. When I went to the city gate to take my seat in public, young men would withdraw, and the elders would rise and stand. The noble leaders would be quiet, covering their mouths because they respected me. People spoke well of me and regarded me highly, for I helped the poor and the orphan with no one to assist them. I made the widow’s heart rejoice. I was eyes for the blind and feet for the lame; I was a father to the needy. I defended the helpless and rescued those in trouble. People listened to me, eagerly waiting for my counsel. My words fell on them like gentle rain. But now, they mock me, and my name is a joke among them. "Oh, how I wish someone would listen to me! Look at my defense, it’s all confirmed! I want the Almighty to answer me, let God write down his charges! I would bear it like a crown, proudly entering his court!"

"Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth? You claim to have wisdom; so tell me, who set the measurements? Who decided the dimensions? On what were its foundations built? Who set the cornerstone, when the morning stars sang together and the angels shouted for joy? "Can you call the clouds to pour down rain? Can you command lightning on its missions, can you listen when they respond, ‘Here we are’?"Does the hawk soar beyond your wisdom, or does the eagle fly high at your command?"Are you going to argue with God? If you challenge him, what will you say? Will you reject my judgment and condemn me to justify yourself?

"How small I am! What can I say in response? I place my hand over my mouth. I spoke once, but won’t say more; I spoke twice, but won’t go further. I know you can do all things, and nothing is impossible for you. I spoke without understanding, of things too wonderful for me to know. I had heard of you from others, but now I’ve seen you for myself; therefore, I take back my words and repent in dust and ashes."

Then God restored Job, giving him twice as much as he had before. God blessed the latter part of Job's life even more than the former; he had 14,000 sheep, 6,000 camels, 1,000 oxen, and 1,000 donkeys, along with seven sons and three daughters. Job then lived for 140 more years.

title: The King Of Glory
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: The earth and everything in it belongs to the Lord. He created it and laid its foundations in the seas, setting it firmly upon the waters. Who can ascend the hill of the Lord? Who has the right to stand in His holy place? It’s the person with clean hands and a pure heart, someone who has no evil intentions and doesn’t deceive with their promises. That person will receive a blessing from the Lord and approval from God, their Savior. This is the kind of person who can truly worship Him, who can come into Your presence, O God of Jacob. Lift up your heads, O gates, and be lifted up, everlasting doors, so the King of Glory can enter! Who is this King of Glory? It’s the Lord, strong and mighty, the Lord, mighty in battle! Lift up your heads, O gates, and be lifted up, everlasting doors, so the King of Glory may come in. Who is this King of Glory? The Lord, the God of hosts—He is the King of Glory!

title: God The Loving Creator
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Praise the Lord, my soul! 
O Lord, my God, You are magnificent; 
You wear glory and majesty like a robe. 
You wrapped Yourself in light like a blanket;  
You spread the heavens out like a tent. 
You built your lofty rooms above the waters, 
You ride on the clouds like a chariot, 
You soar on the wings of the wind, 
You send the winds as Your messengers, 
And fire flames as Your servants. 
You set the earth on firm foundations, 
So it won't be shaken forever. 
You covered it with water like a garment. 
The waters towered high above the mountains, 
But at Your command, they retreated; 
At the sound of Your thunder, they hurried away, 
Never to break the boundary You set, 
Never to cover the earth again. 
Mountains rose up, valleys sank, 
All to the place You had prepared for them. 
You send springs into the valleys, 
They flow between the mountains, 
Providing water for every wild animal, 
The wild donkeys quench their thirst. 
Birds build their nests nearby, 
Singing happily among the branches. 
You water the mountains from Your high places; 
The earth is filled with the fruit of Your creations. 
You make grass grow for the cattle, 
And fresh herbs for people to use, 
Bringing forth food from the ground, 
Wine to make hearts happy, 
Oil that brightens faces, 
And bread to give strength to the heart. 
The trees You planted are full of sap, 
Like the cedars of Lebanon, where birds build their nests; 
The stork settles in the fir trees. 
High mountains are home to wild goats, 
And the rocks provide shelter for marmots. 
You created the moon to mark the seasons, 
And the sun knows when to set. 
You bring on the darkness, and night falls, 
When wild animals come out to roam; 
Young lions roar for their prey, 
Searching for food from You. 
When the sun rises, they vanish, 
And curl up in their dens. 
People head out to work, 
To labor until evening falls. 
O Lord, how numerous are your creations! 
You made them all so wisely; 
The earth is filled with Your creatures. 
There’s the sea, vast and teeming, 
With countless living things, both big and small; 
There are sea monsters, playing in the surf. 
All these creatures wait for You, 
To provide their food at the right time. 
When You give it to them, they gather it up; 
When You open Your hand, they are satisfied. 
When You hide Your face, they are terrified; 
You take away their breath, and they die, 
Returning to the dust from which they came. 
You send forth Your Spirit, and they are created; 
You renew the earth with life. 
May the Lord’s glory be forever; 
Let the Lord rejoice in His works. 
He gazes at the earth and makes it tremble; 
He touches the mountains and they smoke. 
I will sing to the Lord as long as I live; 
I will praise my God while I have breath. 
May my thoughts be pleasing to Him; 
I find my joy in the Lord.

title: God The Loving Father
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: Praise the Lord, my soul, and everything inside me, praise His holy name. Praise the Lord, my soul, and don't forget all His amazing gifts. He forgives every one of your mistakes and heals all your illnesses. He saves your life from the grave and wraps you in love and compassion. He fills you with good things, renewing your youth like that of an eagle. The Lord is known for doing right and bringing justice to those who are oppressed. He revealed His laws to Moses and showed His wonders to the people of Israel. The Lord is kind and compassionate, slow to anger and full of love. He won’t always scold us, nor will He hold onto His anger forever. He hasn’t treated us as our sins deserve or given us what we truly deserve for our wrongdoings. Just as the sky stretches far above the earth, so great is His love for those who honor Him. As far as the east is from the west, that’s how far He has removed our wrongs from us. Just as a father loves his children, the Lord loves those who respect Him because He understands our nature; He knows we are just dust. Humans are fragile—our days are like grass; they blossom like flowers in a field, but when the wind blows over them, they’re gone, and their place remembers them no more. Yet, the Lord's love lasts forever, and His goodness extends to the children of those who keep their promises to Him and remember to follow His commands. He has set up His throne in the heavens, and His authority covers everything. Praise the Lord, you angels, mighty ones who obey His commands. Praise the Lord, all His heavenly hosts, you who serve Him. Praise the Lord, all His creations in every place He rules. Praise the Lord, my soul.

title: God From Whom All Good Things Come
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: If the Lord doesn't build the house, then the builders are just wasting their time. If the Lord doesn’t protect the city, then the guards are on duty for nothing. It’s pointless to wake up early and eat your meals late, working hard just to put food on the table; God gives rest to those He loves. Children are also a blessing from the Lord; they're a reward. Like arrows in a warrior’s hand, children born in your youth are a treasure. Truly, a man is fortunate if his quiver is full of them; he won’t be embarrassed when he stands up to his opponents in court.

title: God'S Goodness And Mercy
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: The Lord is kind and full of compassion, patient, and overflowing with love. He is good to everyone, showing mercy to all of His creations. All your works, O Lord, give you thanks, and those who love you praise you. They celebrate your glorious reign and shout about your incredible power, so that everyone may know about your amazing deeds and the stunning beauty of your kingdom. Your reign lasts forever, and your authority never fades. The Lord supports everyone who stumbles and lifts up those who are burdened. Everyone looks to you for help, and you provide them with food at the right time. You open your hand and satisfy the desires of every living thing. The Lord is just in all His ways and generous in everything He does. He is close to all who call out to Him, to those who seek Him sincerely. He fulfills the wishes of His worshippers, hears their cries, and rescues them. He cares for everyone who loves Him but puts an end to those who do wrong. I will talk about the greatness of the Lord. Let every living being praise His holy name forever.

title: God The All-Seeing
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: Lord, You search me and know everything about me. Whether I'm sitting or standing, You know it. You can read my thoughts from far away. You know when I walk or lie down; You know all my paths. There isn’t a word on my lips that You don’t know! You surround me from behind and in front; Your hand is on me. Such knowledge overwhelms me—it's too amazing for me to understand. Where can I go to escape Your spirit? Where can I flee from Your presence? If I were to soar high into the heavens, You would be there. If I were to make my bed in the depths, You would be there too. If I took the wings of the morning and settled on the farthest shores of the sea, even there Your hand would guide me, Your right hand would hold me tight. If I said, "The darkness will hide me, and night will cover me," even the darkness isn’t dark to You; the night shines like the day. You created my inmost being; You knitted me together in my mother’s womb. I praise You because I am wonderfully made; Your works are amazing and I know that full well. How precious are Your thoughts about me, God! If I tried to count them, they would outnumber the grains of sand. When I wake up, I’m still with You. Search me, God, and know my heart; test me and know my thoughts. See if there’s any wicked way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting.

title: God Who Protects From All Danger
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: Let's give thanks to the Lord for his goodness; his love lasts forever. Everyone he has saved should shout it out—those he rescued from their enemies and gathered from many different places, from the east and the west, from the north and the south. Some wandered through a desolate wilderness, struggling to find a city to call home, hungry and thirsty, their lives fading away. But they cried out to the Lord in their trouble, and he rescued them from their struggles. He guided them the right way until they arrived at a city where they could live. They should praise the Lord for his love and the amazing things he has done for people! He fulfills the desires of their hearts and fills the hungry with good things. Others lived in darkness and despair, bound by their suffering and struggling against heavy chains because they rebelled against God and ignored the advice of the Most High. He softened their hearts with sorrow; when they stumbled, no one was there to help them. Yet they cried out to the Lord in their trouble, and he rescued them from their struggles. He led them out of darkness and despair and shattered their chains. They should praise the Lord for his love and the incredible things he has done for people! He broke down the bronze gates and cut through the iron bars. They acted foolishly because of their wrong choices, and their sins brought them pain; they loathed all kinds of food and were on the brink of death. Then they cried out to the Lord in their trouble, and he saved them from all their struggles. He sent his command to heal them and saved their lives from ruin. They should praise the Lord for his love and the fantastic things he has done for people! They should offer him a thank-offering and joyfully recount his deeds. Those who sail the seas in ships and do business on the deep waters witness the works of the Lord and his wonders in the vast ocean. When he speaks, the storms rise, and the waves crash high. Up to the heavens and then down they go; their courage fades in the face of danger, and they sway like drunkards, their skills totally spent. They cry out to the Lord in their trouble, and he saves them from their struggles. He calms the storm and stills the waves of the sea. They rejoice when the waves settle down; he brings them to their longed-for haven. They should praise the Lord for his love and the wonderful things he has done for people. In the gathering of the people, let him be honored; let him be praised in the council of elders.

title: God'S Protection Of Those Who Trust In Him
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: I will praise the Lord at all times, and His praise will always be on my lips. My soul celebrates in the Lord; let all the troubled hear and find joy. Come, let’s lift His name together. I sought the Lord, and He answered me, rescuing me from all my fears. When you look to Him, you’ll shine with joy, and you won’t feel ashamed. This troubled person cried out, and the Lord listened; He saved him from all his struggles. The angel of the Lord surrounds those who honor Him and protects them. Try and see how good the Lord is; blessed are those who find safety in Him. Honor the Lord, all you who are holy, because those who respect Him have everything they need. Even young lions may go hungry, but those who seek the Lord won’t lack any good thing. Come, kids, listen to me; I’ll teach you what true faith is: Who among you wants to live and enjoy a long, happy life? Then watch your words, avoid speaking evil, and turn away from wrong to do what is good; seek peace and go after it. The Lord stands against wrongdoers to wipe their names from memory. But He watches over the righteous; He hears their cries. When they call on the Lord, He listens and saves them from all their troubles. The Lord is close to those whose hearts are broken; He rescues those whose spirits are crushed. The troubles of the righteous are many, but the Lord saves them from every single one. He protects all their bones, not one will be broken. Trouble will bring down the wicked, and those who hate the righteous will face judgment; but the Lord redeems His servants, and anyone who seeks refuge in Him will never be condemned.

title: God'S Loving Care
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: The Lord is my shepherd; I have everything I need. He lets me rest in lush green fields, and He leads me to calm waters that refresh my spirit. He shows me the right path to take, all for His glory. Even when I walk through the darkest valley, I won't be afraid of anything bad, because You're right here with me. Your stick and staff—they bring me comfort. You prepare a feast for me in front of my enemies; You bless me, making my heart overflow with joy. Surely, goodness and love will be with me every day of my life, and I will live in the Lord's house forever.

title: Trusting In God'S Care
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: Don't be upset by the actions of the wicked, or envy those who do wrong. They’ll fade away like grass and vanish like the green plants. Put your trust in the Lord and do what’s right; live in the land and stay true to your word. Then the Lord will bring you joy and grant your heart's desires. Give your plans to the Lord, trust in Him, and He will support you. He will shine a light on your honesty and make it obvious like the midday sun.

title: A Prayer Of Trust
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: God, I lift my soul to You. I wait for You all day long. I trust in You, so please don’t let me be ashamed. Don’t let my enemies celebrate my downfall, and let no one who hopes in You be disappointed. Instead, let the wicked and treacherous be put to shame. Show me Your ways, Lord. Teach me how to live. Lead me in Your truth and guide me, because You are the God who saves me, and I hope in You every day. Remember Your kindness, Lord, and Your love, because they last forever. Don’t hold my youthful sins against me, but remember me in Your love because of Your goodness, Lord. The Lord is good and fair, so He teaches sinners the right path. He guides the humble and teaches the meek. All His rules are loving and true to those who follow His path. For Your name’s sake, Lord, forgive my guilt, which is great. Who is the person that respects the Lord? He will show him the right way to choose. That person will prosper, and his children will inherit the land. He is close to those who respect Him and makes His promises known to them. My eyes are always on the Lord, for He saves me from traps. Turn to me and be gracious, because I’m alone and hurting. Ease my troubled heart and rescue me from my distress. Look at my suffering and pain and forgive all my sins. Consider my many enemies who hate me intensely. Oh, save my life and deliver me; I trust You, so don’t let me be ashamed. Let my innocence and honesty protect me, for I’m waiting for You, Lord.

title: Safety In God'S Care
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: If you find your place in the shelter of the Most High, resting comfortably in the shadow of the Almighty, and if you call out to the Lord, "You are my safe haven, my stronghold, my God whom I trust," then you can be confident that He will rescue you from any traps set for you, even from the deepest pits of despair. He will wrap you in His feathers, and under His wings, you will find a safe place. His faithfulness will protect you like a sturdy shield, so you don’t need to fear the terrors of the night or the arrows that fly during the day, the sickness that lurks in the dark, or the destruction that strikes at noon. Even if a thousand fall to your left and ten thousand to your right, it won’t come near you. Just look around, and you will see how the wicked face their consequences. Since the Lord is your refuge and you have made the Most High your home, no harm will come to you, and no sickness will approach your space; because He will command His angels to guard you in everything you do. They will lift you up in their hands so that you won’t even stub your toe on a rock. You will overcome fierce lions and snakes, and trample on young lions and dragons. "Because of His love for me, I will rescue him; I will honor him because he knows my name. When he calls out to me, I will be there. In times of trouble, I will stand by him, I will save him and give him the honor he deserves. I will bless him with a long life and reveal the power of my salvation."

title: God Our Refuge
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
God is our safe place and our source of strength,
Always there to help when we’re in trouble.
So we’re not afraid, even if the earth shakes,
And mountains fall into the depths of the sea;
Even if the waters roar and churn,
And the mountains tremble at the noise.
The Lord of all is with us,
The God of Jacob is our fortress.
Just like streams refresh the city of God,
The Most High makes His home a holy place;
God is right there in the middle of her, she won’t be shaken,
God will support her as dawn breaks.
Nations were in chaos, kingdoms crumbled,
He spoke, and the earth melted away.
The Lord of all is with us,
The God of Jacob is our fortress.
Come, witness the amazing works of the Lord,
Who stops wars all over the world.
He snaps the bow and shatters the spear,
And burns up the chariots with fire.
"Be still, and know that I am God,
Elevated above the nations, elevated across the earth."
The Lord of all is with us,
The God of Jacob is our fortress.

title: The Joy Of Worshipping God
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: Come on, let’s sing to the Lord! Let’s shout out to the Rock who saves us. Let’s step forward with gratitude and lift our voices in joyful songs. The Lord is truly a mighty God, a great King over all gods. He holds the depths of the earth in His hands and the heights of the mountains are His. The sea belongs to Him because He created it, and He also formed the dry land. So, let’s worship and bow down; let’s kneel before the Lord, our Creator. He is our God, and we are His people, the sheep in His care. Shout out with joy to the Lord, everyone on earth! Serve the Lord with happiness, come into His presence singing. Know that the Lord is God; He made us and we are His, the flock He looks after. Enter His gates with thanksgiving and His courts with songs of joy. Give thanks to Him and praise His name. He is good; His kindness lasts forever, and His faithfulness goes on for all generations.

title: What God Asks Of Those Who Worship Him
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
O Lord, who can be a guest in your tent?
Who can live on your holy mountain?
It's the person who lives with integrity and does what’s right,
And speaks the truth from the heart.
They don’t slander with their words,
They treat their friends well,
And don’t make false accusations against their neighbors.
In their eyes, the wicked are rejected,
But they honor those who respect the Lord.
They keep their promises no matter what,
They don’t lend money at interest,
And can’t be bribed to harm the innocent.
That person will always stand firm.

title: A Call To Praise God
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk:
Let’s sing a new song to the Lord! 
Everyone on earth, join in to praise Him. 
Celebrate His name and share the good news of His salvation every day. 
Tell His story to everyone around you, 
Show them His wonders and what He has done.
God is amazing and deserves our praise. 
He should be honored above all other gods 
because all the other gods are just pieces of wood and stone. 
Only the Lord has created the heavens. 
His presence is filled with honor and majesty; 
His strength and beauty shine in His sanctuary. 
All you people from the nations, give to the Lord what He deserves. 
Bring Him glory and strength. 
Bring the glory that belongs to Him; 
Come with offerings and enter His courts. 
Let’s worship the Lord in our best clothes, 
And tremble before Him, everyone on earth.
Tell all the nations, "The Lord reigns! 
He will lead the people fairly."
Let the heavens be happy, and let the earth celebrate. 
Let the sea and everything in it roar with joy. 
Let the fields jump for joy along with everything in them. 
Let the trees of the forest sing happily in the presence of the Lord 
because He is here now, 
because He is here to rule the earth. 
He will govern the world with justice, 
And He will be faithful to all the nations.

title: The United Song Of Praise
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: Let’s give a shout of praise to the Lord from the heavens! He deserves all the glory from the heights. Angels, join in with praises; all the heavenly hosts, sing along! Shine on, sun and moon; twinkle bright, stars of light! Heavens above and waters high, praise the name of the Lord! He spoke, and everything came to be; he set boundaries that will last forever. Give glory to the Lord from the earth, sea monsters and deep waters included! Fire, hail, snow, and mist; stormy winds that carry out his commands! Mountains and hills, fruit trees and mighty cedars, wild creatures and cattle, everything that crawls and flutters in the sky—let them all raise their voices! Kings and people everywhere, rulers and judges, young men and women, old folks and children, let everyone lift up the name of the Lord! His name is the highest of all, reigning over heaven and earth. He empowers his people; everyone who’s faithful praises him, especially the Israelites who are close to him. Let’s celebrate God in his sacred space! Praise him for the greatness of his power and the beauty of his creation. Sound the trumpet and play the lyre and harp; dance with joy and strum the strings! Let the cymbals clash together! Let everything that breathes give praise to the Lord!

title: Man'S Place In God'S World
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: O Lord, our God, how magnificent is Your name across the whole earth! You've displayed Your brilliance throughout the heavens. When babies and infants cry out, You’ve set up a defense against Your enemies, silencing those who oppose You. When I look at the skies, crafted by Your hands—the moon and the stars You’ve made—I wonder: What is humanity that You pay attention to us? Or the child of humanity, that You care for him? Yet, You’ve made us just a little lower than divine, surrounding us with glory and honor, making us stewards of all creation, with everything under our control. The sheep and cattle, every animal in the field, the birds soaring in the sky, and the fish swimming in the sea's paths—all are under our watch. O Lord, our God, how magnificent is Your name across the whole earth!

title: The Reward Of Doing Right
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: A person is truly happy when they don’t follow the advice of those who do wrong, or hang out with people who make poor choices, or sit with those who mock others. Instead, they take joy in the teachings of the Lord and think about them day and night. They are like a tree planted by flowing water, producing fruit at the right time, with leaves that never fade, and everything they do is successful. But the wicked? They are like the chaff blown away by the wind. When it comes to judgment, the wicked won’t stand a chance, nor will the sinners be found among the righteous. The Lord shows the way for the righteous, but the path of the wicked leads to destruction.

title: A Prayer For Forgiveness
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: Please have mercy on me, God. Because of your loving kindness and the depth of your mercy, wipe away all my wrongs. Cleanse me completely from my wrongdoing and wash away my sins. I know my misdeeds too well, and my sins are always in front of me. I've only sinned against you and done what’s wrong in your eyes. So, you are right when you speak, and just when you judge. I was born into wrongdoing; my mother conceived me in sin. Yet, you want truth in my heart; teach me wisdom in my inner self. Cleanse me with hyssop so I can be clean; wash me until I’m whiter than snow. Fill me with joy and happiness so that even my broken spirit can rejoice. Please hide your face from my sins and erase my wrongs. Create in me a pure heart, God, and renew my spirit within me. Don't cast me away from your presence or take your holy spirit from me. Give me back the joy of your salvation and support me with a willing spirit. Then I will share your ways with others, and those who have gone astray will turn back to you. Save me from shedding blood, God, so that my tongue can sing of your righteousness. Lord, open my lips so I can declare your praise! You don’t take pleasure in sacrifices or burnt offerings. The offerings that please you are a broken spirit and a contrite heart.

title: The Rewards Of Listening To The Advice Of The Wise
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: Listen closely, my son. Keep my words close to your heart. Pay attention to wisdom and be careful to think things through. If you search for wisdom like it's silver and look for it like it's hidden treasure, you will come to understand true faith and get to know God. Wisdom will fill your mind, and knowledge will be a delight to you. Discretion will protect you, and understanding will keep you safe, helping you avoid wrongdoing and steering clear of those who speak evil, leaving the right path for dark ways. They find joy in doing wrong and take pleasure in wicked actions. Happy is the person who finds wisdom and gains understanding. In her right hand, she offers a long life, and in her left hand, she brings riches and honor. Her ways are enjoyable, and all her paths lead to peace. She gives life to those who seek her, and those who hold on to her are truly happy. You will walk your path confidently, and you won’t stumble. When you sit down, you won’t have to worry, and at night, you’ll sleep soundly.

title: What God Likes And Dislikes
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: God sees everything, keeping an eye on both the good and the bad. People often believe their actions are perfectly fine, but God looks deeper into their intentions. While someone may think they know their path, it’s God who truly guides their steps. God detests dishonest scales, but he finds joy in fairness. Doing what’s right and just is far more pleasing to Him than just making a sacrifice. The behavior of the wicked repulses Him, but He loves those who are eager to do the right thing. God hates evil minds, but He takes pleasure in those who live with integrity. He cannot stand the sacrifices offered by the wicked, but the prayers of the righteous bring Him joy! God keeps His distance from the wicked, yet He listens to the prayers of those who are upright. Lying is an abomination to Him, but those who act with honesty are His delight. There are six things God truly hates, and even seven that disgust Him: proud eyes, a lying tongue, hands that shed innocent blood, a wicked mind that plots evil, feet that rush to do wrong, a false witness who lies, and anyone who stirs up conflict between others.

title: God'S Care For Those Who Try To Do Right
author: The Children's Bible
text_chunk: God’s curse is on the homes of those who do wrong, but He blesses the households of those who do what’s right. He laughs at those who mock others, yet He shows kindness to the humble. The Lord ensures that good people don’t go hungry, while the desires of the wicked will be disappointed. God acts as a refuge for those who live honorably, but He brings ruin to those who choose the wrong path. The name of the Lord is like a strong tower; the righteous run to it and find safety. Every word from God is true and serves as a shield to those who trust in Him. Don't say, 'I’ll get back at someone for wronging me'; wait for the Lord, because He will rescue you. The Lord offers wisdom, and from Him comes knowledge and understanding. He keeps solid wisdom stored up for those who do what’s right, and He protects the path of those who stay faithful. Respect for the Lord is the starting point of true knowledge, while foolish people disregard wisdom and discipline. Respect grows from wise teaching, and true honor follows humility. Trust the Lord with all your heart, don’t rely on your own knowledge. In everything you do, make Him your partner, and He will guide your journey.
